Khalili who studied at the then Bombo Sudanese Primary School says he dropped out of school after Primary Seven and opted to join the Police force in 1979 because he did not love books much.
Call him a school dropout, but he speaks fluent English well enough to pass for a graduate. His accent is also clear, actually British inclined probably because of close interaction with his employers.
The light-skinned, medium-size father of seven is talkative and full of emotion. Khalili gives you the impression of an honest man who loves his job.
He speaks with a rich body language, often waving his hands in the air, shrugging and occasionally laughing. He loves to engage the people he is talking to and cares to know if you really understand what he is saying.
"I learnt to drive while in the police force and in 1985 joined the British High Commission as a driver," he reveals. One year later, the commission, like it does for all its drivers, sent him to the UK for a special driving course.
Even when the second and third training-which he undertook in July this year came, Khalili did not know he was destined to drive the queen until about one month to her coming.
"They first took me for a refresher course in the UK, telling me that I was going to drive a very important person, but I never imagined it would be the queen," he says.
When his supervisor called him on his return to announce the 'important guest', Khalili could not believe it. "I thought I was dreaming. I asked my supervisor over and over again, 'are you sure? who am I to drive the queen?"', he recalls happily.
Khalili says he emerged from the office with a very wide smile, talking to himself only to arouse the curiosity of all his colleagues who admired him for his luck.
His worry was how he was going to approach the queen.
I went and asked the commissioner and he tipped me telling me, 'almost the same way you have been with me. Only that you will have to be more careful and respectful', he recounts.
'I went and told my wife about my pending assignment and she was so proud of me. She assured me that I was fit enough for the job," he says.
But when he told his grandfather about it, his stake in the community's history went higher.
Apparently, in 1954, when the Queen first visited Uganda, it is still a man from his Nubian community, Idi Asuman, who was driving the then governor Sir Andrew Cohen in state house, who chauffeured her.
Asuman reportedly drove the queen from the airport, to Owen falls dam (Nalubale), which she commissioned and Queen Elizabeth National Park that is named after her.
This was historic for the minority Muslim group, which migrated from southern Sudan in the early 1900, predominantly settling in Bombo.
In 1962, another Nubian-Sebi Farusi Kamjara, lowered the British union Jack flag and hoisted the Ugandan flag when Uganda was getting her independence.
"I have been an addition to those achievers in my community.
In Bombo SS, my children are now popularly known as the queen's kids and everyone wants to shake my hand because I drove the queen," Khalili boasts.
And as an honour, the community gathered to have a special meal called Garusa; a mixture of maize flour and a lot of baking flour with a bit of sugar, kasera (mixture of maize flour, baking flour and cassava flour), crowning it with a big cock.
Nevertheless, every special occasion never misses a dark cloud.
"I feel sad that my mother, who passed away in 1971, could not be around to celebrate my life achievement. Even my dad is bedridden right now. I'm praying so hard he gets well and gets to know that I drove the queen," he says.
But what does he remember most about his 'royal stunt'? "Driving from Entebbe to Kampala felt like forever. The roads were so jammed. But the crowd was so cheerful, welcoming and well-behaved. I felt proud being Ugandan.
"I remember being so conscious and praying for no mishaps. If a tyre burst, or an accident. it would have been very shameful for me, but I thank God that it all went well. I feel so brave to have handled such a mighty person successfully," he says.
John Hamilton, the 2nd secretary at the British High Commission in Kampala, says that their decision to hire Khalili to drive the queen was entirely dependent on his experience.
"He is one of the most experienced drivers we have," Hamilton says, adding, "otherwise most of our drivers often undertake those special courses in basic driving skills, how to drive in convoys, invasive and defensive driving, and he did the job perfectly."
And while the rest of us scramble to buy the queen's picture on streets, Khalili boasts of an autographed photo of the couple, proudly hanging on his wall.
"They gave it to me in the car, after their dinner in Serena Hotel in Kampala," he says.

Original_Search_Title: Fed By the Bus
Headline: Uganda: Fed By the Bus
Body_Text:
Kampala — From drivers and conductors to cleaners and gatemen, dozens of Ugandans depend on the bus industry for their livelihood, Harriet Birungi discovered
RUNNING after a Gaso bus which is just arriving at the Baganda Bus Park in Kampala, Alex Twinamatsiko, a luggage carrier, tries to catch the attention of the alighting passengers. Banging his hand on the side of the bus he calls out:
suburb of Kampala, he is a married man with four children back in the village in Bushenyi District.
"I send them money twice a month," he reveals after a bit of probing.
How much he earns a day ferrying cargo to and from the bus park depends on how far he has to carry the luggage and the number of customers.
"From the bus park to the new taxi park, I charge sh500 while to the old park I charge sh1,000," he says. "If I have to go a bit further I charge sh2,000 or more," he says, adding that his fees also vary with the weight of the luggage and the amount of traffic on the roads.
"For instance, to carry a sack of matooke to Kisenyi, I charge sh4,000 as there is heavy traffic in that area," he says. "My reasoning is that I will spend longer on the trip and miss out on other customers at the bus park."
When setting his rates, Twinamatsiko also has to take into consideration the fact that he needs to pay the sh15,000 registration fee that allows him to operate from inside the bus park.
From his unstable returns, on top of the registration fee, he has to pay the park operators a sh1,500 retainer per week to make sure his job is secure.
Twinamatsiko's meals consist of energy-giving foods.
He is always careful not spend more than sh1000 on food. "I mainly eat posho, cassava, potatoes and beans," he says.
Twinamatsiko is not the only one earning a living off buses.
As a Kalita Transporters bus pulls into a washing bay on Rubaga road, a young man, with great excitement, assembles the tools of his trade - a yellow jerrycan which has been cut in half, water, detergent and a piece of foam tied up in an old T-shirt.
Then he quickly gets to work washing the bus. He will be paid sh10,000 for washing the exterior and sh20,000 if he does the interior as well.
Luckily, he does not have to pay any fees to make sure his job is secure. He simply has to show up the following day, or else someone new will be recruited.
"I only need to pay the boss a certain percentage of my earnings," he says.
"It's like some kind of rent for using his land," the cleaner, who does not want to be named, says, as colleagues warn him not to reveal the exact percentage they pay.
The cleaner adds that he is not married, but has many dependants who are AIDS orphans. Three of these dependants stay with him in Ndeeba, a suburb of Kampala while eight are in Rakai. "With the money I earn, I buy maize flour and send it to them by bus," he says as tears form in his eyes.
From hawkers, cargo handlers, brokers, security guards, receipt issuing officers, gate controllers, bus drivers and owners to turn boys, bus inspectors, park managers, garage service providers and the director of the bus terminal, a bus feeds many people.
Charles Namanya is the bus terminal manager. He says a bus officially employs four people - the turn boy, conductor, inspector and bus driver.
These get their pay from the business profits, on terms agreed upon with the bus owner.
A turn boy is in charge of loading and offloading passengers' cargo. "He must be registered with that particular bus so as to ensure trust amongst all parties that serve and are served by the bus," explains Namanya.
There are as many turn boys as buses. If a bus travelled without one, there would be chaos, with some people taking other people's property.
James Lubo, who has been a gateman for five years, compares a turn boy to a store- keeper or logistics officer. A turn boy's memory has to be very sharp, for him to remember which luggage belongs to whom.
However, like anyone else they make mistakes. "Many times passengers have come to us complaining that they have lost their luggage. The safety of cargo requires the combined effort of both passenger and turn boy," Lubo adds.
At the end of the day, a turn boy takes home sh3,000 per trip the bus makes. Depending on the season, he will on average take home sh6,000 a day.
Edward Ssabiti started out as a hawker dealing in electronics before he became a turn boy. He says to become a turn boy you have to prove yourself.
To become a turn boy, he says, you have to lobby the bus conductor, usually by paying him a commission. As a turn boy, one has to pay the operators of the bus terminal sh20,000 and they in turn issue you with an identification card. In addition, you pay a sh3,000 retainer monthly.
Namanya says the bus conductor, who issues receipts, also earns per trip. However, he is not paid until the bus is full and ready to set off. If he is a miser and unwilling to pay brokers to convince passengers to board the bus, he may never leave the terminal.
"You have to give the broker between sh500 and sh1,000 as an incentive. That way, the bus will fill quickly and the conductor will earn his sh5,000," explains the manager.
However, the payment is not immediate. The conductor has to wait until the sh25,000 park fee charged on each bus is paid. If there is not enough money to raise the park fee, the conductor has to forfeit his pay, says Namanya.
Conductors pay sh20,000 as registration fee to the terminal operators. The bus driver and inspector are meant to make sure that every passenger has a seat.
They are also in charge of servicing the bus and making sure it is roadworthy. They are paid sh20,000 per route and also have to pay a sh20,000 registration fee.
Unlike the others, however, they do not have to pay a monthly retainer. "Their daily contribution of transporting passengers safely and in keeping the bus in a good condition, is proof enough that they want their jobs," says Ssabiti.
To ensure that every bus pays the required fees and nothing is stolen in the bus park, the terminal operators employ security guards and receipt-issuing officers. Namanya says these are paid on a weekly basis. "Security officials employed at the terminal receive their sh15,000 pay every Saturday."
Before they leave the bus park, passengers often buy goods from the hawkers who sell anything from electronics and medicine to cosmetics, food and bread.
The hawkers are only allowed to operate after paying a registration fee to the terminal chairman, says Lubo. Their fees vary according to the products they sell. For instance, a hawker selling non-perishables pays an initial fee of sh20,000 and a weekly fee of sh3,000 to keep their positions, while food and bread vendors pay sh10,000 and a weekly fee of sh1,500.
Namanya says as the terminal manager, he takes home sh200,000 monthly while the bus owners pocket sh300,000 after all daily bus expenses have been met. Expenses include fuel, washing, servicing and repairing.
"To say that it's only the bus owners that reap from a bus would be wrong. More than six categories of people live off a bus," says Namanya.

Original_Search_Title: Aftermath of Chogm
Headline: Uganda: Aftermath of Chogm
Body_Text:
Kampala — NOW that we are post-CHOGM we can get back to 'normal', but what should we judge as normal?
Is traffic chaos normal? Is disposing of plastic bottles and rubbish by throwing them out the car window normal? Are potholes normal? One of the intangible benefits of CHOGM was that it demonstrated to us what life could be like if we tried.
Unfortunately, we tend to operate at the lowest common denominator, but during CHOGM we were on our best behaviour, showing what we are capable of.
Unfortunately, it did not take long for us to get back to normal and crash into the roundabouts and drive over the newly planted flowerbeds. The Mayor, Haji Nasser Sebaggala, has been quick to pronounce that he is appointing an outside company to ensure that everything in Kampala continues to be shipshape. I am sure that we are all grateful for his sentiments, but most people will have a degree of cynicism and will wait to see what actually happens over the next few months.
We do not have reserves and since we have just blown our whole year's budget on CHOGM, where will we find the money to maintain standards for longer than a week? It could be said that in Africa most people operate without reserves.
Let me illustrate from everyday observation - I was following a vehicle in Kampala along a road which had been liberally endowed with road humps and each time the vehicle went over a hump it stalled. I wondered why this should happen and a Ugandan colleague pointed out that there were several very good reasons.
The first was that the vehicle probably had very little routine maintenance, such that when it hit a bump, a battery cable might jump off, or something else could jiggle loose.
Apart from that possibility, the owner of the vehicle would be running it on the accepted Ugandan principle that the 'E' in the petrol gauge signifies 'Enough' and not 'Empty' as it does in other countries. Hence when there is a sudden bump at the front, the petrol runs to the back of the tank and the car stalls.
It is because of this that taxi drivers charge more if you live on the top of a hill, since they have to put in more fuel to keep the car running up the hill. I happen to live on a hill and have witnessed trucks and taxis break down on a regular basis, because the fuel has run to the back of the tank. It seems to be the norm in Africa that we live on the edge - we have just enough to get us by. And this applies not only to taxi operators, but to society as a whole.
Apart from living without reserves, people do not budget or plan their expenditure, but take life as it comes and somehow get by. A poor man can survive on the equivalent of a dollar a day, but he does not have to budget; he just plants some cassava and matooke and there is always something to eat. He lives on the edge, but the environment is kind and he survives.
But the community, represented by the LC5s, or KCC, or Makindye Division of KCC, has to know how to budget the little they have and since they have no reserves, if it is misspent, essential services will suffer.
Generally, they don't budget, hence the garbage handlers go on strike, because they have not been paid, the potholes multiply and the street lights don't get fixed.
The moral of the story is that we have to accept that we live in a society where there is very little money in the first place and no reserves when that runs out. If we don't want to stall on every hump and break down on every hill, then we need to plan and budget with what we have. We may not get any more money, but we can be more effective with what we have. But will we? Time will tell.

Original_Search_Title: Lifeline for the North As Peace Returns
Headline: Uganda: Lifeline for the North As Peace Returns
Body_Text:
Kampala — Two women return home with their first harvests.
A BABY crawls out of a small mud and bamboo hut sporting a smile, but swiftly disappears back into the hut, when she sees me. A few minutes later, her mother - Grace Achayo - appears from the opposite direction pushing an ox-plough, her two giggling 10-year-old twin daughters, in tow.
Next to the hut, two black saucepans sit on top of badly smoking fires and under a nearby tree two teenage mothers take shelter from the scorching sun. This is Madi Kiloc camp in Lamwo County in Kitgum. It is one of the satellite camps that were created in the district in an effort to decongest the original camps which were very big and had poor sanitation facilities.
In the villages a few kilometres from the camp people have resettled in their former homes. Gardens of maize, sorghum and cassava grace the countryside. There are more gardens than houses here. The residents have also started rearing cattle, pigs and goats.
"With agriculture we shall not only fight hunger, but also boost the economy. Many of us cannot start up businesses, but this will be our entry point into development," Achayo says.
"As peace returns to the area, we can now concentrate on cultivating our land in order to have a brighter future," she adds.
According to the inter-agency standing committee (IASC) working group, as of June 2007, more than 900,000 people remained displaced in camps across northern Uganda. This is half of the estimated 1.8 million internally displaced persons (IDPs) reported in December 2005.
Since April 2007, the ongoing peace negotiations between the Government and the Lord's Resistance Army (LRA) have significantly improved the security situation in northern Uganda, and this has encouraged IDPs to move closer to their home areas. The IASC working group reports that more than 920,000 IDPs in northern Uganda have voluntarily left camps to settle close to their original home areas.
However, some of these vulnerable people, particularly in Gulu, Amuru, Kitgum, and Pader districts, have no access to essential services.
Northern Uganda is blessed with vast tracts of land that have not been exploited. Therefore agriculture is considered the lifeline that will take the region out of poverty.
The region lies in the tropical savannah belt, where the mean annual rainfall ranges from 1,000 to 1,500mm. Except for the clay soils in areas near Lake Kyoga, the land in northern Uganda can easily be cultivated using ox-ploughs and simple hand tools.
The agricultural pattern throughout the area is homogeneous, with cotton as the main cash crop and maize, millet, groundnuts, cassava, beans, sorghum and bananas as food crops. There are over one million head of cattle in the region.
Marcello Oraca, the chairman of Agweng Animal Ox-cultivation project, says when the food relief agencies for one reason or another delay to deliver aid, the locals are driven to the point of starvation.
"As people leave the camps, it is important for them to produce their own food, with the help of the relief agencies," Oraca adds. He, however, notes that farms in the area find it hard to access markets for their produce. "It is useless to produce tonnes of produce which you cannot sell. People here have to carry their produce on bicycles to Kitgum."
Bonnex Odoch, an aid worker, says most people in Lagwel camp are engaged in rearing pigs. Cotton, which is one of the main cash crops in the area, has also been given lots of attention. The average cotton farmer has approximately nine acres of land.
Although the IDPs have increased access to agricultural land, the delayed onset of the rains in April and May followed by sporadic rains and flooding greatly affected the harvests in July, August, and September.
Flooding has also undermined the second planting season, damaged roads and bridges, thus further undermining the efforts of IDPs to return to their farmlands. Floods will go and the people will fall back to cultivating their homeland.
Communal land tenure system
Land ownership in northern Uganda is vested in the clan, which gives rights to individual clan members. This means that there is no absolute security of land tenure. Grazing areas and water resources are communally owned.
"We do not sell land here. Do not even repeat that. That is totally unacceptable here. I would rather give you a piece of it, other than selling it," says Mathew Antoyon, a former resident of Lagwel camp who has just returned to his home area. Long-term forced displacement and lack of access to land for cultivation have adversely affected food security in northern Uganda.
However, security improvements have allowed IDPs to embark on farming again. This will result in greater food security and stimulate the recovery process of the region. This year, USAID funded the Food and Agriculture Organisation and Catholic Relief Services to distribute seeds and farm implements to more than 60,000 families just before the May planting season.
This measure is geared at enhancing food security in the region. However, according to USAID's Famine Early Warning Systems Network, as of August 2007, 2.1 million people remained food insecure and IDPs depended on food aid for nearly 50% of their humanitarian needs.

Original_Search_Title: A New Publisher, Creating a New Industry
Headline: Nigeria: A New Publisher, Creating a New Industry
Body_Text:
Abuja — When Bibi Bakare-Yusuf holds her two reviewers' copies of Kemi's Journal—one printed in India and one in Nigeria—side by side, the difference is night and day.
Abidemi Sanusi's 2005 release looks vibrant with its red Indian-printed cover depicting Kemi's world—her thoughts, her person, her attitude. The same book, printed in Nigeria, looks a bit dull. The blue hues fade into the surroundings and the thinner paper inside smears slightly from ink that is not quite refined enough.
Cassava Republic, Nigeria's newest literary publishing company, prints in India. Bakare-Yusuf, its founder, says she wants its books to rival those published in London and the United States—not just in literary quality, but in appearance as well.
Cassava Republic is unique in Nigeria, not only for its quality printing but because it exists at all. When Bakare-Yusuf returned to Nigeria after a period of living in the United Kingdom, she was astonished. Literature and its publishers had all but disappeared, leaving religious works, self-help books, and textbooks to fill the shelves.
Follow us on WhatsApp | LinkedIn for the latest headlines
The mood had changed, too. "I found it upsetting," she said. "Someone will spend 5,000 naira on a self-help or management book, but ask them to pay 850 naira for a novel and they will moan and groan."
Nigeria was once home to a flourishing market, but years of military rule pushed international publishers out; not a single one remains in the country today. Economic decline hit the pockets of average Nigerians hard and their budget for luxuries such as books even harder. Thousands from the chattering middle class left the country, including the intellectuals and consumers of Nigerian literature.
"It was the Sahara Desert period of Nigerian literature," says Ken Ike Okere, a co-founder of the Abuja Literary Society. "We didn't know if people were writing because we were not seeing it." Newspapers suffered too; while any of three large papers could sell 500,000 copies a day in the 1970s, today all the papers together are lucky to sell 350,000.
With no retail market and no capital, most publishers now survive by printing textbooks or religious publications, which have guaranteed sales to schools and churches. The market for literature is tougher, and most works that do appear are self-published. Distribution is limited and costly. Most publishers focus on urban centers because the cost of transport and the small market make sales elsewhere unprofitable.
With the exodus of intellectuals and the books they read, many aspiring writers in Nigeria have had no opportunity to learn from their peers. It is no surprise then, that Bakare-Yusuf has struggled to find good manuscripts from the 50 or so she receives every week.
"Think about Yoruba drum training," she says as an example. "It takes years to be a master drummer, years of listening to others, so why should writing be any different?"
Today, Bakare-Yusuf thinks she might just know how to reverse the decline.
"I'm interested in the bottom of the pyramid; the top can go to London to buy books. If I can get books to the masses, conversations about literature will start, a questioning spirit will develop," she explains.
So Cassava Republic is cutting prices and boosting quality. Books like Kemi's Journal that stand out on the shelf cost just 650 naira, compared to the average price of 1,000 naira.
That has meant taking a smaller profit—or none at all. But it's the future market in which Bakare-Yusuf and her company are interested. Once the masses start reading, demand will pick up, she says.
Elite book stores with proper shops are out. Instead, books are being bought in rackety shops outside city walls. In Abuja, where Cassava Republic is based, that means leaving the tidy streets of the capital and heading for the suburbs, where most people live in mud and metal houses. It is on the winding streets of Maraba, Kuje, and Nyanya villages that Nigerians are buying books.
Cassava Republic's raw material is different also. Its books are new, catchy, and accessible. Sanusi is just one of a handful of writers that Bakare-Yusuf has found herself. Another, Teju Cole, author of the forthcoming Every Day is for The Thief, had a blog that caught the company's attention.
And while some titles, including Cain Prize winner Helon Habila and Orange Prize winner Diana Evans, are reprinted from abroad, Cassava Republic has turned down offers from international publishers to reprint.
"I'm interested in authors who engage with the African experience—it's not enough just to have an African author," she explains. "They must be able to speak to the complexities of African realities." Soon, Cassava Republic hopes to re-publish The Hidden Star, by the young South African author, K. Sello Duiker.
Someday soon, more of those authors may come from Nigeria itself. As part of her work, Bakare-Yusuf invites authors whose manuscripts are turned down to take part in writing workshops and join a mailing list of tips and events. Elsewhere in Nigeria, others are doing the same. The Abuja Literary Society also edits manuscripts and helps authors with their work.
All this, says Bakare-Yusuf, is about getting deeper than literature: "When you ask someone to read literature, you are asking them not only to look into the past but to look into the future—to suspend their beliefs today." That just might help Nigeria think its way out of more than just the end of a novel.

Original_Search_Title: "The Blood Keeps Flowing"
Headline: Congo-Kinshasa: "The Blood Keeps Flowing"
Body_Text:
Masisi — Semivumbi Ntawiheba never used to be interested in politics. Growing up on a mountain farm close to Masisi town in the Democratic Republic of Congo's restive North Kivu, Ntawiheba lived only for his cattle.
Then, two months ago, soldiers loyal to dissident General Laurent Nkunda attacked his village and took away some of the men aged between 12 and 50 for "conscription". "I didn't want to be one of them," Ntawiheba says. "So I decided to flee."
Ntawiheba ended up in a camp for the internally displaced, a squalid settlement near Masisi. Conditions are tough, he says as he hops over the muddy brooks that crisscross the camp, built on the only piece of flat land in town. "I never used to care about politics, but now it's been shown to me in real life - now I'm interested," he says.
Young people such as Ntawiheba are among the worst-hit in North Kivu's escalating humanitarian crisis. When his village was attacked, the 25-year-old's cattle farm was doing well; he was almost self-sufficient when the conflict reversed all his gains. Now he lives with 1,500 others in the IDP camp, relying on agencies to bring him what little food there is.
Masisi - one of the most isolated towns in North Kivu - is cut off from the rest of the region by political insecurity and poor road conditions. It is just 58km from North Kivu's main town Goma, but the mountain pass is crowded with soldiers from the competing factions and muddy throughout the rainy season. Until the World Food Programme (WFP) convoy arrived in Masisi on 21 November, there had been no food delivery to the camp for three weeks.
"We stayed alive. But there wasn't a lot to eat and there wasn't anything to do," says Ntawiheba. Women living at the camp set up a modest market, selling yams, some goat meat, a bunch of bananas, a single pineapple. Even the strip of land adjacent to the camp that once served as the town's football pitch can no longer be used because it has to function as an emergency landing strip for the UN Mission in the DRC (MONUC) helicopters that sporadically fly in and out.
"Conditions have been terrible at the camp. For two weeks there was no food - absolutely nothing. And life is probably no worse here than for Masisi's general population," said Philippe Havet, project coordinator for Médecins sans Frontières (MSF) in Masisi.
In any other town, MSF would probably be dedicating all its resources to medical needs. But as few NGOs are operating in Masisi, MSF has been forced to expand, handing out non-food items such as blankets and tarpaulins as well as coordinating free healthcare at the 120-bed Masisi hospital and adjacent nutrition centre.
"Often cut off from the rest of the region, the situation here is exceptional," Havet said.
Circulation has been helped by a new MONUC mobile base in Mushake, established in late November on the road between Goma and Masisi. "The base will help goods and people to move more freely," said Sylvie van Wildenburg, spokeswoman for MONUC.
A checkpoint set up by forces loyal to dissident general Laurent Nkunda, le Congrès National pour la Défense du Peuple (CNDP) in Mushake partially cut off the Goma-Masisi route, taxing trucks and passenger vehicles passing through the town.
Malnutrition rising
The hospital is perched on a cliff top overlooking the valley that is home to most of Masisi's 26,000 people. Specialists at MSF's nutrition centre see 40 cases a day - mainly children younger than five, who have been surviving on a diet of sorghum wheat, cassava and maize, which lack the nutrients they need.
Gakaru's mother is one of them. She and her son walked through persistent rain for 30km to reach MSF's nutrition centre. At two years old, Gakaru ought to weigh 7kg. He is 1kg lighter and shows signs of oedema, a build-up of excess fluid characterised by a puffy face, belly and ankles. It is a textbook case of malnutrition.
"He is one of twins. The other baby died shortly after birth," his mother says, looking on anxiously as Gakaru rejects a spoonful of Plumpy'nut, the peanut derivative often given to malnourished babies in sub-Saharan Africa.
Inside the hospital, Save the Children runs a feeding centre for the sickest children. Sekabamdu Utamuleza is inside, a shadow of an eight-year-old girl cradled by her mother. Three weeks of treatment have failed to improve her condition, and she is taken to a main ward by a nurse.
"She throws up her food every night," her uncle, Musanga Khami, says. "We just don't know what to do any more." Khami says there are not enough nurses at the centre to care for all the children; Sekabamdu's mother had to choose between staying at home with her other children, who have had little to eat since the conflict came close to their farm, or coming to the centre with the girl.
"If these mothers leave their homes, they don't know whether there will be an attack while they are gone. That's the choice they have to make," says Havet.
Localised fighting has meant many local people are unable to reach their cattle farms, restricting food supplies further. Road conditions and spontaneous clashes prevent access to MSF's nutrition centre, so the organisation also manages an ambulatory feeding programme targeting 1,200 vulnerable children in the district.
Inside the hospital are the other victims of the war - wounded rebels, Congolese army soldiers and civilians caught in the crossfire. An attack on the neighbouring town of Lushebere killed seven civilians on 13 November. MSF brought the survivors here.
Among them is Namagi Kima Nizaine, a widow in her 40s, who lost all her children in the DRC's most recent war, which ended in 2003. "She was caught in the fighting and sustained a bullet injury to her arm," says nurse Anne Koudiakoff.
Masisi has become a restaurant for flies. Everyone keeps getting hurt and the blood keeps flowing
In the surgical ward, James is recovering from a leg injury sustained during an attack by CNDP soldiers on his village on 16 October. For two days he hobbled along some of the most dangerous roads to reach the hospital.
"Masisi has become a restaurant for flies. Everyone keeps getting hurt and the blood keeps flowing. They feast on it. After the elections in 2006, everyone in Masisi cried out for peace. But look at Masisi now," he says.
By the end of November, MSF will have been working in Masisi for nearly four months; longer than its emergency remit of two. "Usually emergency operations cease after two months. But how could we possibly leave when there is hardly anyone else here?" asks Havet, rhetorically.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: North West - Haman Adama Best School Farm Project Launched
Headline: Cameroon: North West - Haman Adama Best School Farm Project Launched
Body_Text:
Bamenda (Mezam)- The sum total is to inspire and introduce agriculture to youths as a noble and highly rewarding occupation. The North West Delegation of Basic Education is to thank for initiating the "Haman Adama Prize for Excellence in Agriculture" with the declared intention to establish a good and strong agricultural education programme for primary schools in the province.
The provincial Delegate, Boma Nde Soh Christopher told Cameroon Tribune that the Prize will stimulate interest in agriculture, mobilize schools to play active roles in the promotion of Agriculture, develop positive attitudes towards agriculture, prepare children for a career in agriculture, develop a scientific outlook and make schools in the province more productive.
The welcome initiative will reward the five best school farms per division and the best school farm in each division will receive the Haman Adama Prize for Excellence in Agriculture. The exercise is expected to culminate with an Agricultural show to display farm products at the divisional level.
Prizes will be available for the award in May 2008 and crops to feature in the farms include Maize, vegetables like cabbage, parsley, celery, carrots, tomatoes, spices, tubers like cassava, Irish Potatoes, Sweet Potatoes, Yams, Cocoyams, beans etc. The Award Committee which comprise the Provincial and Divisional Delegates, Divisional Officers, Inspectors of Basic Education and Sub Delegates of Agriculture are expected to keep records showing the names of children participating in the farming activities and the school farms will be labelled. The competition was initiated to kick off by the end of November 2007 stressing the importance of Agriculture in the school system.

Original_Search_Title: South West - IRAD Showcases Latest Farming Techniques
Headline: Cameroon: South West - IRAD Showcases Latest Farming Techniques
Body_Text:
The Regional Director of the 50-year-old Institute of Agricultural Research for Development (IRAD-Ekona), Dr. Joseph Mafeni Mase, has observed that, in spite of Government significant investment, food self- sufficiency has not been commensurately due to Cameroon's high population growth.
Dr. Mafeni Mase was speaking while presiding at the Open Field Day in Ekona recently. The Day was organized to vulgarise IRAD's expertise in seed production and multiplication. The Regional Director explained that more assistance and realistic approaches in the agricultural sector are still required to attain food security. One main strategy, he said, was to strengthen the seed production and supply sector to optimize yield and encourage self-employment potentials through processing and marketing.
Opening the Day Mrs. Christine Poubom, Provincial HIPC Supervisor, presented an overview of the seed situation in the South West Province. She quoted a study carried out by the World Bank between 1998 and 2002 showing an acute lack of seeds. She said the study equally proves that only 35 percent of farmers benefited from IRAD's work and that up to 11 million tons of seeds were being imported and could rise to 25 million tons in 2025. For that reason, IRAD embarked on seed improvement covering maize, cassava, cocoyams, yams, fruit-trees and plantains. The IRAD's effort has been spread to all the six administrative divisions of the South West Province. Mrs Poubom also pointed out that the Seed Law intended to put order in the sector was still at the sensitization stage. The good news, she stated, was however, that there exist some 775 seed producers' Associations in the area showing an ascent in farmers' interest to improve on food production.
The hundreds of stakeholders including students from the Sasse and Bishop Rogan Colleges who turned out to benefit from the Open Day, were shown round fruit seedling nurseries and schooled on improved techniques of multiplication, budding, grafting and "marcotting". They moved to the cassava nursery to learn rapid methods of multiplication. At the Yam "minisett" plot, harvesting and mother-yam sizing for seedling techniques were demonstrated. While at the maize farm fertilization dosage and other management techniques were taught to visitors.

Original_Search_Title: NGO Cares for Cerebral Palsy Kids
Headline: Zambia: NGO Cares for Cerebral Palsy Kids
Body_Text:
Ndola — BOY Bwalya does not know any of the young children in the neighbourhood because he is always indoors at his parents' home in Ndola.
When his parents receive visitors, Bwalya who is only five years old is locked up in the bedroom until the visitors leave.
Bwalya's predicament comes forth because he is mentally retarded as a result of the cerebral palsy he has been suffering from since he was born.
In many parts of the world, and Zambia included, there are many children who are denied the right to good living because of their conditions as cerebral palsy victims.
Cerebral palsy (sometimes known as little's disease) is a condition characterised by weaknesses of certain muscles commonly in the legs coupled with stiffness and awkward jerking movements.
There may be convulsions, impaired speech and mental deficiency but in many cases mentality remains quite normal.
The cause of cerebral palsy is not always clear. Damage to the brain accruing prior to birth or during the birth process may cause it.
Because of the stigma associated with children born with this affliction, a physiotherapist, Archie Hinchcliffe who worked in Zambia in the late 1980s formed a non-governmental organisation based in the United Kingdom (UK) known as Cerebral Palsy Africa (CPA) to improve the lives of the children with this ailment.
Cerebral Palsy Africa is active in Zambia and two weeks ago, a group of physiotherapists from Scotland came to Lusaka and Ndola to train physiotherapists advanced methods of care for the children with the ailment.
According to physiotherapists, manifestations of the disease may be noted first when the child is about six months old, the evidence being a delay in the ability to sit up, crawl and stand.
At Ndola Central Hospital where the training was conducted for two weeks, the hospital's executive director, Dande Malawo said the course which was on for the past two weeks entailed trainers going out to the communities to teach parents how to take good care of their sick children and there was also actual training for physiotherapists.
"This Cerebral Palsy Africa was started by Mrs Hinchcliffe in Scotland in 2005. Mrs Hinchcliffe who was the wife of the British High Commissioner in Zambia, Peter Hinchcliffe, was a physiotherapist who worked in Zambia in the late 1980s. She decided to form the Cerebral Palsy Africa for the children with emphasis on improving mobility so that they could engage themselves in activities of daily living. Cerebral Palsy Africa carries out activities in various parts of Africa which include training physiotherapists advanced methods of care for the children," said Dr Malawo.
The handling of the case also include speech training, muscle re-education, the wearing of braces, special tutoring in school and vocational guidance.
The main aim of Cerebral Palsy Africa is to encourage local people to support children with the disease and providing them with training, awareness raising and to help them with skills.
The training also involves the community making assistive devices using cardboards and cassava flour.
Zambia Society of Physiotherapy, northern region vice president, Mary Mumba said society was working with cerebral palsy children for the programme to progress well.
"Our society is working with cerebral palsy children to see to it that there is a link between the community and health facilities to ensure early intervention of help by making sure that physiotherapists are posted to local clinics and rural areas so that people there benefit," Ms Mumba said.
She appealed to the Government to assist in workshops like the one physiotherapists held in Lusaka and Ndola in order to fight the stigma associated with cerebral palsy.
Community Based Rehabilitation (CBR) members who attended the courses at NCH were pleased that there were people who were concerned and were willing to assist the children with cerebral palsy problems.
One of the members, Godfredah Bilali of Ndola's Chifubu Township said it was important that people who had children suffering from cerebral palsy took them to centres for attention.
"People should bring to centres for CBR, mentally retarded and cerebral palsy children. Many parents prefer keeping their children indoors for fear of the unkown. These children are not a curse. They are just like any other children and they need to be cared for," Ms Bilali said.
The Kitwe based community, Wukwashi wa nzambi, which helps in making assistive devices and wheelchairs for children with deformities said parents with cerebral palsy children willing to be assisted with these equipment could contact them at Chimwemwe Christian Brethren or Chritian Mission in Many Lands in Kitwe or call Henry Mutembu on 097-9233545.
Esther Mundia Muimo who has a cerebral palsy child said it was good for CPA to teach care-givers how to make simple assistive equipment for the cerebral palsy children.
"We parents with cerebral palsy children are grateful because they are teaching us how to be self-reliant and finding ways and means to help the children improve in their sitting positions. It is good that people are coming on board to help us.
It is forums like these where we can share our problems and try to break the stigma associated with cerebral palsy. We want the people in the community we live to get involved and together go forward and integrate the children in society," said Mrs Muimo.
A cerebral palsy child, Temba Mundia Muimo, a grade 11 pupil at Kabulonga Boys in Lusaka who spoke on behalf of other cerebral palsy children appealed to people to stop stigmatising them and instead see what they can do to help them.
Temba also appealed to the Government to create facilities that were user-friendly for the people that are wheelchair- confined and people who use clutches because in the end they must realise that they were also stakeholders in society.
"Our appeal to the Government is to see what they can do to help in the education sector for some of us who are in Government schools. They should introduce teachers who have done special education to help us because in most cases, we are slow learners and we need time to catch up in lessons," said Temba.
CPA's efforts to help children with cerebral palsy needs the support of all.

Original_Search_Title: NNDC Gets N5 Billion Fresh Capital
Headline: Nigeria: NNDC Gets N5 Billion Fresh Capital
Body_Text:
Kaduna — The once colossal New Nigeria Development Company [NNDC] is set for a comeback as the Northern Governors Forum (NGF) has approved its recapitalization to the tune of N5 billion.
NNDC, founded in the 1950s by the old Northern Regional government, is owned by the 19 Northern states today.
Chairman of NNDC's board of directors Professor Halidu I. Abubakar, who disclosed this at the corporation's 39th Annual General Meeting at Unity Hall, Hamdala Hotel, Kaduna at the weekend, said there will be an additional injection of N250 million by each of the 19 owner states.
Professor Halidu said seventeen of the nineteen governors have already signed irrevocable standing payment orders (ISPO) for the money to be deducted at source from their share of the Federation Account. The ISPOs have been forwarded to the Federal Ministry of Finance, he said.
Commenting on the company's investment profile, Professor Halidu said NNDC has embarked on the pursuit of its telecommuni-cation project in earnest. He said a company, Gamji Telecom Limited, has been incorporated with Corporate Affairs Commission and an operating license obtained from Nigeria Communications Commission (NCC). He said as at March 2007, NNDC's total investment in telecommunication stood at N330 million.
The chairman also said NNDC has completed feasibility studies on the cassava project, which is expected to be set up in five owner states as soon as land is made available. A request for the provision for land will be sent to selected govern-ments and this is to boost the Federal Government's initia-tive on cassava production as well as to create employment opportunities for the teeming youths in the region, he said.
According to Halidu, the challenges of the economic reforms, which are a relatively harsh operating environment and increase in the cost of operations, affected NNDC's results for the period under review. He said gross earnings stood at N360 million. A loss of N1.4 billion on ordinary activities after taxation, exceptional and extra -ordinary items of was recorded during the year under review, as compared to last year's figure of N107.96 million.

Original_Search_Title: N70 Billion Textile Fund - 300 Companies Queue Up
Headline: Nigeria: N70 Billion Textile Fund - 300 Companies Queue Up
Body_Text:
Abuja — More than 300 textile companies have signified interest to borrow from the Federal Government's N70 billion Textile Rehabilitation Fund, the Nigeria Export Import Bank's (NEXIM) Executive Director (Operations) Mr. Edet Ekere, said in Abuja yesterday. He spoke to the News Agency of Nigeria (NAN).
"The response to our publication in the news-papers for expression of interest to draw from the fund was tremendous, with more than 300 expressions of interest received by NEXIM," Ekere said.
The Obasanjo admini-stration had set up the fund to revive the nation's ailing textile industries and NEXIM was charged with the responsibility of disbursing the fund.
According to Ekere, NEXIM has almost concluded negotiations with United Bank for Africa [UBA] on how to raise the N70 billion. He said UBA was now engaged in efforts to raise the fund, which would be disbursed as loans to textile manufacturers at single digit interest rate.
Ekere said, "We have reached agreement with UBA (the fund arran-gers); we have accepted their terms; what we are now waiting for is for the Federal Government to authorise what it mentioned in its terms of guarantee. This is what the Minister of Finance is working on right now. UBA says it can arrange the fund within six weeks of getting the guarantee from government." According to the director, if government accedes to UBA's guarantee terms, NEXIM will begin disburse-ment by next January.
Former President Oluse-gun Obasanjo had in 2006 inaugurated an inter-ministerial committee headed by the former Minister of Finance to revive the country's ailing textile sector. The committee had recommended that N70 billion was needed to revive the sector, Nigeria's second largest employer.
Ekere said First Bank of Nigeria (FBN) and Merrill Lynch, a U.K.-based financial outfit, were initially appointed by government to source the N70 billion on behalf of NEXIM.
He said that FBN and Merrill Lynch had agreed to source the fund on the condition that the govern-ment entered into an "explicit" guarantee with them.
"Initially we thought FBN and Merelich will raise the fund with an "implicit" guarantee (a guarantee not as tight as explicit). But they said that they would only raise the fund with an explicit guaran-tee, which the government could not issue at that time because of some IMF conditionality," Ekere said.
The director also said that NEXIM provides finance for the export of sesame seed, cassava and rubber. "We finance these commodities because they are in line with government's efforts to develop agricultural export. We have facility for cassava, sesame seed, and rubber, but the facility is only meant for buying and processing. We encourage those who want to benefit from the facility to process and add value to them," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Country of True Wonder And Beauty
Headline: Uganda: Country of True Wonder And Beauty
Body_Text:
Kampala — UGANDA, a land gifted with breathtaking sights, is popularly referred to as the Pearl of Africa. It is described as a place gifted by nature, hospitable people with over 40 tribes with distinct cultural attributes, 10 national parks, warm climate and mind-blowing landscapes. Raphael Okello explores some of the attractions, arguably referred to as Uganda's wonders.
NATURAL WONDERS
The Rwenzori Mountains - snow in the tropics
The splendid world of snow and glaciers on the Rwenzori Mountains in Kasese, 270 kms from Kampala, has been a tourist attraction for hundreds of years. Since its re-opening in 2001, tourist figures have steadily risen.
In 2005, there were 800 visitors. Rwenzori is home to one of four remaining tropical ice fields outside the Andes (the highest mountain range in South America). In 1994, the United Nations Education Scientific Cultural Organisation gazetted it as a world heritage site.
Emerging from the Rift Valley floor to a wintry altitude of 5,109m, the Rwenzori's 120km-long ranges (the longest in Africa), support large tracts of evergreen vegetation and bamboo forests. The higher moorland zone is dressed with a unique cover of giant heathers, lobelias and groundsels. Its highest peak, the Margherita (5,109m), is the third highest in Africa and the mountain is considered the highest non-volcanic mountain in Africa.
Sempaya Hot springs - field of boiling water
The Sempaya Hot springs in Semiliki National Park in Bundibugyo, about 450km from Kampala, eject a cloud of steam seen as far as two kilometres. According to Uganda Wildlife Authority, Sempaya's water temperature -over 100ºC- is above the maximum temperature of most hot springs in the world (about 50ºC).
Tourists have been seen boiling eggs, cassava and green bananas in the two geothermal heated springs. The first one is a pool measuring 12 metres in diameter and the second is a field of geysers, both oozing steamy sulphur-scented waters reputed to have healing powers.
The River Nile - the world's longest river
In 1862, John Speke and Grant discovered the source of the Nile, the world's longest river (4,180 miles long) at Jinja, 87km east of Kampala. "It was a sight that attracted one to it for hours - the roar of the waters, the thousands of fish leaping at the falls with all their might ... hippos and crocodiles lying sleepily on the water ... as interesting a picture as one could wish to see," Speke described it.
More than 140 years later, the site has changed significantly. The source may have lost its sparkle, but the Nile has given birth to some of the world's most breathtaking and powerful rapids and waterfalls.
Bujagali and Itanda in Jinja are considered among the world's most spectacular white-water rafting locations and Murchison Falls, because of its deadly ferocity, is unchartered.
MAN-MADE WONDERS
Kasubi Tombs
This architectural masterpiece, built with wood, thatch, reed, wattle and daub, was used as a palace in 1881 by Kabaka Mutesa I of the Buganda Kingdom. After his death in 1884, the thatched dome at Kasubi Hill in Kampala, was converted into a royal burial ground for three other kings of Buganda - Daudi Chwa, Mwanga and Mutesa II.
With a diametre of 31 metres and a height of 12 metres, it has arguably been regarded as the largest mausoleum in Africa.
This colossal cultural monument, a prominent feature on the catalogue of travel agencies and tour operators, sits on 30 hectares. The site's main significance is in the spiritual value that the Baganda attach to it.
Nyero Rock Paintings
Widely recognised as some of the finest and most dramatic rock paintings in East Africa, the Nyero Rock paintings in Kumi district (eastern Uganda) are surrounded by intrigue and mystery. No one knows who drew the paintings or why they were drawn.
What is sure is that the geometric paintings in red, white and purple pigments, were drawn about 400 years ago. Some people have put claim to ancient yellow hairy bushmen whom they say once lived in the area.
They are also considered a thread of evidence proving that South African Bantu speakers migrated from Africa's inter-locustrine region as similar paintings have been sighted down south.
Either way, the paintings are a silent voice seeming to tell us about the life and traditions of our ancestors.
Kabaka's Lake - a 'mad' king's project
A king's unfeasible whims gave birth to a lake said to be the only excavated lake in East Africa and one of the biggest on the continent - the Kabaka's Lake at Ndeeba, Kampala. It covers two square kilometres and is about 200 feet deep.
Kabaka Mwanga II (1884-1888), the architect of this small lake wonder, had a penchant for swimming, canoeing and fishing. Whenever he desired to engage in any of these water sports, he was carried 11kms from his official palace in Mengo to his lakeside palace at Munyonyo.
A story is told that in 1887, during his return from Munyonyo, the king thought that if a lake were to be constructed linking the two palaces, the journey would be more direct and less cumbersome.
The lake would also serve as an escape route in the event of armed conflict with the British or other territorial enemies. It seemed like a wild conception, but with handheld hoes, everyone from chiefs to their subjects, were involved in the intensive labour.
Many died due to hunger, accidents, disease and fatigue. Eleven months after the start of construction, a rebellion by Christian converts in 1888 led to abrupt stoppage of work that had reached Najjanankumbi, two kilometres from Mengo. The lake has continued to exist in more or less the same state since 1888. It contains several species of fish like tilapia and lungfish as well as reptiles.
The Bahai Temple - Africa's mother temple
Built more than 40 years ago, the Bahai Temple is like a crown on the crest of Kikaya Hill, four miles from Kampala, off Gayaza Road. The soaring temple, located on 70 acres of tree-studded gardens, is one of a kind in Africa.
The splendid dome-shaped temple is among nine of the only Bahai temples in the world. Like other Bahai temples, the temple is nine-sided with nine corresponding doors to signify nine religions of the world. It rises 124 feet high and covers a diametre of 44 feet. Its green conical dome is made of tiny glazed mosaic tiles.
Inside, nine big pillars and 27 smaller ones help to support the temple. It took four years of building before it was opened to the public on January 15, 1962. It accommodates up to 800 people.
Others
The mountain gorillas of Bwindi Impenetrable Forest, Lake Bunyonyi (a crater lake) and Queen Elizabeth National Park, all in western Uganda.=

Original_Search_Title: The Deserted Village - a Casualty of North Kivu's Continuing Conflict
Headline: Congo-Kinshasa: The Deserted Village - a Casualty of North Kivu's Continuing Conflict
Body_Text:
Kimoka — It may only be a short walk from its bustling neighbour Sake, but Kimoka is a ghost town. Homes are abandoned, the school is deserted and the brand new church empty. Light streams in through the open door onto huge leather drums beside the pulpit. When the congregation fled, the drums became redundant. Now the only percussion comes from the small arms fire echoing through the mountains.
Outside, dirty scraps of cloth and one child's shoe are strewn across the black volcanic earth - signs that there was once a community here. Now, there is nothing. No children laughing, no dogs barking.
Perched on the frontline of the conflict in eastern Democratic Republic of Congo's North Kivu province, Kimoka suddenly emptied one day in early September when dissident general Laurent Nkunda's forces launched an attack on government positions.
All 2000 of the village's residents fled. Most are now housed in camps on the outskirts of Goma, the province's capital, or sheltering in Sake, where the UN mission in Congo, MONUC, has established a buffer zone between soldiers loyal to Nkunda and government forces.
"Kimoka is a dangerous place to live now. Only a few weeks ago, Nkunda's rebels refused to let people go back to their homes to collect their belongings. Things are better now, but everyone who passes through the village feels in danger," said former Kimoka resident Azariah Haawli Awhmrua.
When Nkunda's forces tried to take Sake on 4 September, MONUC pushed them back, avoiding heavy fighting. A thick blue line of peacekeepers lies between the two towns, with Nkunda's rebel movement le Congrès National pour la Défense du Peuple (CNDP) controlling Kimoka and MONUC and the national army (FARDC) in Sake.
"Nkunda's soldiers are still occupying the region. People are scared to return to Kimoka and the other smaller villages," said Lieutenant Colonel SS Oberai of MONUC's Sake battalion.
Although most of Kimoka's residents are too afraid to go home, some feel they have no choice but to cross checkpoints manned by Nkunda's soldiers to collect charcoal from Kingi Kachacha market.
They may chat animatedly as they walk through Sake, but when the women reach Kimoka, the gossip abruptly stops. It takes around 15 minutes to move through the town on foot and nobody wants to be seen there. Women bow their heads and quicken their pace.
The road curves around rolling green hills, past cassava plantations. By motorbike, the journey from Kimoka to the market takes an hour. For the displaced women of Kimoka, it takes all morning.
Soldiers loyal to dissident General Laurent Nkunda rebel man a checkpoint in Kimoka.
High in the mountains, the market is unusually quiet; distant gunfire explains the lull in trade. "Before September, the market always used to be busy. But now there's hardly anyone here," said charcoal trader Ki Mengu-Jiles Hotalele. Sacks of charcoal sell for $15 there and make $20 in Sake. But for many, $15 is too expensive now, and the journey through rebel territory feels too dangerous to be worthwhile.
According to the UN, fighting between troops loyal to Nkunda, FARDC and other groups, such as Rwandan Hutu rebels, the FDLR, has displaced more than 170,000 civilians since the end of August.
The majority live in IDP camps such as Mugunga I and II, on the edge of Goma, where anxiety is endemic. When gunshots were heard in the mountains above Mugunga on 13 November, some 40,000 previously uprooted people took flight yet again, into Goma, fearing the worst.
In Kimoka, checkpoints on both sides of town are heavily defended by up to eight armed CNDP soldiers. CNDP Lieutenant Jardin D'Amour, who guards the second checkpoint, calls the situation in Kimoka "relatively calm".
Locals, however, are constantly on edge. They say that calm periods never last long in North Kivu. "We never know what's going to happen tomorrow," said Azariah Haawli Awhmrua.
"Nkunda's rebels are behaving now, but you don't want to see them when they misbehave," said a MONUC officer with the Sake battalion. He added that MONUC is considering moving the base closer to Kimoka to reassure civilians and ease tensions.
As long as there are armed men of any stripe in Kimoka, for residents like Awhmrua, peace will always feel a long way off. "Whether they are FARDC, FDLR, UN or Nkunda, we will still feel like we are in the middle of conflict. Things will return to normal only when there are no soldiers," he said.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: I Will Win 60 Percent of Votes
Headline: Ghana: I Will Win 60 Percent of Votes
Body_Text:
Accra — One of the leading contenders in the ruling New Patriotic Party (NPP) presidential race, Mr Alan Kojo Kyerematen, has stated that he would sweep over 60% of the votes at their December 22 Congress in Accra. "I say this with all humility that anybody who knows my track record, background, will confirm that when I talk confidently like this, I know why I am saying that."
Though Alan does not rule out a possible alliance with other candidates should there be a run-off, he strongly believes he has what it takes to win in the first round. To him, "there is ample evidence which assures me that I am going to win the race. Opinion polls have put me ahead of my opponents," he said. At a maiden media interaction in Takoradi, Mr Alan Kyerematen popularly referred to as 'Alan Cash' noted that his government would promote grass root capitalism where wealth would be created for the majority to enjoy. "The issue of poverty will be a thing of the past under Alan's presidency," he noted, adding that his leadership would transform Ghana's economy. He contended that he had what it takes to catapult Ghana to a middle-income status.
He promised to focus on key areas such as industrialization, Information Communication and Technology, Infrastructural development and tourism promotion. These four areas according to him were interrelated to his slogan 'Job for the people - Cash in your pocket' which would help his government achieve what he calls 'grass root capitalism, creating wealth for the majority of the people.' He noted, this meant that he would be moving from the social sector to that of productive where the country seemed to be performing poorly. "So it will be counter productive talking about educational transformation, health, macro economic development and good governance since that has already been achieved under the Presidency of Mr John Kufour".
Turning his attention on the Presidential Special Initiative, which was under his Ministry, Mr Alan Kyerematen noted that the achievement of the sitting government could not be counted without the mention of the PSI, in which he played a major role. He therefore denied that the initiative had failed saying, "If there is a significant achievement of this government that it will be proud of, it is the PSI."
According to him the primary objective of the PSI was to diversify the economy, which was concentrated on cocoa, gold and timber. "The PSI was meant to expand the economy through a diversification portfolio and to some extent that has been achieved." He mentioned the revival of the garment factories, the transformation of both the salt and the cassava industries, which has led to the creation of jobs for the majority of Ghanaians.
This is evident in the expansion of the production of cassava, which rose to 60,000 hectares and the mining of about 3.5 million tones of salt, annually. The continuous claim by his detractors to the effect that the PSI failed is never correct, he argued. Meanwhile reports reaching us at press time yesterday indicated that the publisher of the Chronicle group of newspapers, Nana Kofi Coomson met with Mr. Alan Kyerematen at the Goil filling station in Cape Coast on Tuesday, this week. Alan Cash was on his way from Takoradi with his campaign team. The publisher who had earlier apologized to Alan for failing to extend his sympathy to him after his recent accident in the Brong-Ahafo had fruitful discussions about the latter's candidature.
Dr. Osei, one of Alan's campaign team members did not however take kindly to the decision by his boss to meet with Kofi. Dr. Osei accused the publisher of writing dirty things about, Alan promising that he would never allow him to see (Kyerematen) when he assumes office as President of Ghana in January 2009.
Present at the meeting were Messrs. Partact, Efo Philipo of north Dayi constituency, Micheal Ampong among others. Mr. Coomson confirmed that he met with Alan and the above names when he was reached on his cell phone.

Original_Search_Title: President's Initiative Transforms Farmers
Headline: Uganda: President's Initiative Transforms Farmers
Body_Text:
Kampala — RUHARO Parish is located a few kilometres from Bushenyi town. It has 933 households, of which 650 are participating in the Presidential Initiative Against Hunger and Poverty. The farmers have used the little initial capital from the President so well that one is left lamenting about the poor attitude of some of our people towards work. A big section of Ugandans would rather hang along Kampala streets than engage in farming in the village.
Ruharo farmers were poor with barely enough food to feed their families. Now, they boast of too much to eat and enough for the market. They have transformed their lives. Under the initiative, the Poverty Alleviation Department headed by Mrs. Joan Kakwenzire, the Senior Presidential Advisor on Poverty Alleviation, started working with the parish to improve food production, homes, soil fertility and household incomes. Several items were distributed including 60,000 cassava cuttings, 60,000 sweet potato vines, 6,000kgs of maize, 330,000 cabbage seedlings, 330,000 onion seedlings, 450kgs of millet, piglets and 77 cows. Subsequently, the farmers engaged in zero grazing cows, pigs, goats, rabbits and poultry. They also engaged in growing bananas, beans, pineapples, avocados, onions and green vegetables.
The impact of the project is evidenced by smokeless fire places and clean kitchens, good manure disposal, neatly tended banana plantations and improved household incomes.
Through the "pass on" system, many other farmers have acquired cows and pigs. This has increased the number of beneficiaries of the project.
The farmers have started a Savings and Credit Scheme. Their micro-finance institution has sh4m. They need support to upgrade it to a SACCO.
Ruharo farmers do not use artificial fertilisers like most farmers in the country. They are aware of the long-term effects of artificial fertilisers on the soils.
MPs from other areas should take their farmers from their constituencies to Ruharo to get lessons from their counterparts. If the skills are spread across the country, grassroot poverty will be reduced and "prosperity for all" will no longer be a dream.
Ruharo farmers have no regrets for embracing the initiative.
The writer works with the Uganda Media Centre

Original_Search_Title: PDP Has Robbed Citizens, Says Senator Matori
Headline: Nigeria: PDP Has Robbed Citizens, Says Senator Matori
Body_Text:
Bauchi — A strong member of the ANPP national board of trustee and former member of the upper chamber of the national assembly in the 4th republic,senator Salisu Musa Matori has attributed the current political and economic wranglings in the country, to the poor leadership style of the PDP in the last eight years led by Olusegun Aremu Obasanjo.
Senator Salisu Matori disclosed this to LEADERSHIP correspondent in Bauchi in an interview where he said PDP has seriously demonstrated high sense of poor leadership in the last eight years of their governance,hence the decision of many people to dump the party.
He said Nigerians have seriously suffered from the bad governance of the Obasanjo led administration and it's only God Almighty that saved us from total collapse as they have bastardised every economic sector in the country and shared the remaining goodies of the country like father christmas.He stated that PDP robbed Nigerians of their voting right and resources while Obasanjo at the helm of affairs has destroyed the nation, and Nigerians who spoke the truth like Harry Marshall were assassinated with any impunity.
"We have seen the sharing of our money in the last eight years or so,we've seen the outright robbery from PDP,I just wondered why are we prosecuting armed robbers when we have pen robbers in the PDP that claimed to be fighting corruption? They have destroyed our resources under the name of privatisation where they sold our assets and even the colonial masters who came to rule could not do such shameless exercise"he added.
The Danmasanin Bauchi berated Obasanjo's government for contributing towards the collapse of many industries in the country,adding that Obasanjo went into a wasteful venture by pumping billions of naira into their pockets in the name of cassava farming which yielded nothing instead of him concentrating in improving the agricultural sector.
He praised the efforts of President Umar Yar'adua in reverting the sales of refineries in the country and urged him to also revisit the sales of federal government houses which were sold to PDP members and their friends leaving the civil servants at their own mercy.He said its amazing that even the house of Inspector General of police was sold to PDP loyalist.
Commenting on the differences between the party and its presidential candidate,Muhammadu Buhari,senator Matori explained that it's part of democracy to have differences of opinion but blamed some individuals for over blowing the issue.He blamed the ruling party for trying to create confusion within the party but warned that anyone who has the intention to use the party and destroy it would end up destroying himself,just like Mahmoud Waziri,Don Etiebet and others ended up without any political relevance.

Original_Search_Title: No Pieces to Pick in Teso After Floods As Minister Speaks of Hope
Headline: Uganda: No Pieces to Pick in Teso After Floods As Minister Speaks of Hope
Body_Text:
Katakwi — I recently travelled to Palam village, Ngariam Sub-County in Katakwi District bordering Moroto District. To reach Soroti from Mbale, I crossed Awoja Bridge by boat. The water had not completely subsided. It was a bit of fun. On either side of the bridge, taxis waited for passengers.
From Soroti to Katakwi, the road is now dry and dusty. I could see the havoc caused by floods on bridges along that road. Some bridges were swept deep into the swamps.
The water level is still high on either side of the road.
The locals were busy fishing. I could see that their harvest was good. The fishermen wore smiles on their faces.
Travellers occasionally stopped to buy the fish. I asked one of the fishermen what they felt about the whole experience of floods.
"Although the floods caused us problems, now we have a lot of Nusaf. All we need is posho now, because for sauce, we have a lot of Nusaf," he said. He declined to give me his names because he suspected my motives.
What do you mean by Nusaf? I asked. He laughs. "You mean you have not seen?" he asked me. The driver of the car I had hired volunteered an explanation.
Here, Nusaf means the now abundant fish in the waters, he said.
I got the point. Officially Nusaf means Northern Uganda Social Action Fund, a programme under the Office of the Prime Minister meant to improve the welfare of people in conflict affected areas of the north and east.
The locals in Teso coined the word because while Nusaf from the government was reportedly difficult to access, the "Nusaf" in the waters was accessible to whoever went for it.
After the exchange with "Nusaf" men, I continued withmy journey.
While the ride from Katakwi Township to Ngariam Sub-County headquarters was easy, the journey to Palam village was a nightmare.
On the way to this remote village, the driver of the car I had hired nearly abandoned me.
He was bitter that his car was passing through the bushes. I had to pay him handsomely. It was like moving in the wilderness.
Finally, we arrived in the compound of the four-classroom block Palam Primary School.
The sky is clear. It's neither hot nor cold. I see the beautiful hills from Karamoja. Children run toward me. In a short time, elderly people arrived.
I spot an old man seated on the verandah of one of the classrooms. I move to greet him and we exchange pleasantries. How did you survive the floods? I ask.
"I had never seen such floods before. You children of these days should be careful, it appears the heavens are angry with you," said the 90-year-old Mzee Ipiyo Atujai.
"My home has been destroyed and I now sleep in a classroom. Eh! Eh! The rains were heavy. Can you imagine that even Imusugun (white men) came here to see us?
"The plane landed here to bring food. I have never seen a plane land in our area," Mzee Atujai narrated. In contrast to other districts like Amuria, Bukedea, Kaberamaido and parts of Katakwi, which also suffered the invasions by LRA rebels, the residents of Palam have never known of any other calamity.
Mzee Atujai said: "Our problem here has not been rain. It is the incessant cattle raids by Karimojong thieves. I have lost my children to Karimojong. Since 1987, we have lived in this camp."
"I lost my wife two years ago and remained alone in the home.
The teachers in this school transferred me into the classroom after the floods damaged several homes. Part of the wall of my hut also collapsed, so the classroom is now my home," he said
Mr John Omulalu Akol, a Primary Two teacher offers to take me around the school.
In some classrooms, anthills were popping up. Others had cow dung in them, implying that they acted as kraals at night while traditional cooking stones also stood in some corners.
"When the ground was wet, women could not cook anywhere, so they used the classrooms," Mr Akol said.
The local defence unit soldiers whose detach was nearby, had also shifted into some classrooms. Mr Akol took me to the school gardens. All the onions, cabbages, tomatoes, cassava , oranges and pineapples that had been cultivated with support from the World Lutheran Federation had been destroyed.
The crops were to support the school-feeding programme. Mr Akol told me that Palam Primary School had between 18-20 pupils in P.5, 22 in P.4, 50 in P.3 and 15 in P.2. He didn't have figures for P.1 but said,
" They are always many in P1." He said usually after P.5, many pupils drop out while a few shift to other schools like Ngariam Primary School, 10km away.
Children lament
Merab Aluku, 12, a P.3 pupil who has hearing problems, said she wants to read up to Makerere University. When I asked her what she would like to study at Makerere, she held her cheek.
Florence Amongin, 13, of P.5 also said she wants to read up to university.
"I like maths and SST but I don't know how to speak English. I also can't read properly," she said. The children tell me they have never put on slippers and they eat one meal a day.
After speaking to the little ones, I turned my attention to Mr Donato Okello, a former UNLA soldier. He is now the Palam camp leader.
"Our biggest problem here is Karimojong rustlers nothing else. The floods just came recently and have gone. "But the Karimojong have made us live in this camp for 20 years. We cannot cultivate big gardens because when people return to villages, the Karimojong sneak in to kill.
"When they get you, they want you to show them where the animals are or else they kill you. We have chosen to stay in the camp, so that if Karimojong get one person, at least the neighbours will know.
"Recently, the government deployed Anti-Stock theft Unit soldiers near Iising hill but we are not sure how long they will take".
"Every year they take our children to join the army. They say, come and join the army to save your people. Our children join saying we want to protect our people but after training, they are taken away leaving us to suffer under Karimojong".
"You children who are in Kampala, tell the government that we are not happy with them taking away our children. When these children go, they never come back home. Some of them who came said when they went back, they were tortured," he said
After the floods
Mr Okello said: " UN came here with an helicopter and gave us food. Sometimes when relief is brought, we walk 10km to Ngariam to pick it but they have taken time".
"We had cultivated food in gardens near the camp but the floods have destroyed everything. We even don't have seeds to plant".
"Minister Musa Ecweru sometimes comes here. When he comes, he brings food but when he goes back, we don't receive anything".
"That man (Ecweru) is good. He parks his vehicle and walks up to our camp. Our MP is Charles Ojok also came here." "Some of the posho NGOs brought us was black and bitter. When people ate it, they developed diarrhiea".
"You tell those people that when they are bringing us something, let them bring something good. They should not bring things like they are giving to pigs."
"We just eat what is given to us because of poverty. The only good posho was the yellow one, which was given by UN." Other residents also complained that local leaders reportedly gave a lot to their families and sold some of the relief items.
Okello continued: "Since they stopped bringing relief, we are now surviving on selling poles and grass for building huts.
"We sell grass at Ocorimongin market, 20km away. When we get Shs1,000, we budget that for food. People from Soroti town come to buy grass and poles."
"We also have a problem of water. The only borehole we had is spoilt. One day, big worms were pumped out together with the water. We told the district people and they came and closed it. Now we suffer a lot getting water."
I met Anna Grace Akudo, 40, a houseless mother of eight children.
She told me how her husband, Simon Peter Choda joined the army 14 years ago and never returned home until November last year.
She said her husband spent most of his life in the Congo.
"We thought he had died but he came back home. He spent only four days and left; he is now in Sudan," Akudo narrated.
"When he came back, he was an old man but he has sent another wife home from Congo. There is no food at home and I am now struggling to help her.
"I sell grass and sometimes dig in other people's gardens to get money to buy food. The relief they give us is not regular."
After touring the camp and speaking to several residents, I returned to where my hired car was parked in the school compound.
I found a woman standing by. Her story is a typical African one. She wanted a lift to Soroti town to escape from her husband. This woman (names withheld), is a mother of five.
She tells of how hours before I arrived in, her husband had beaten her. Reason? The floods had damaged the door to their hut. This door had since remained un-repaired. So, the lady asked another man in the village to help her fix it. She pleaded that she had no money immediately but she would pay up later.
The man accepted to repair the door on condition that she takes him to a nearby home where waragi (local gin) was sold, to get it on credit. She took him there. But when they arrived, the woman finds her husband taking waragi.
She informs him that the door had been repaired and thecarpenter wanted waragi. Her husband immediately left the waragi joint and hurried home without answering her. The wife secured waragi on credit for the carpenter and also went back home.
No sooner had she arrived than her husband asked for food.
She said she had delayed to prepare it. The man beat her up.
