She told me: " I am tired of this man. Help me to go to Soroti, I have relatives there."
After thinking of what would befall the children if I helped the mother escape, I painfully turned down her request.
Malaria attacks
On my way back to Katakwi town, I stopped at Priscilla Comprehensive Girls' School. This was the collection centre for all Ngariam Sub-County candidates for the primary leaving examinations. There were 133 candidates from the 12 schools.
The candidates had spent a week at the centre. Unicef had donated tents to act as classrooms while classroom blocks acted as dormitories.
"We have hope of producing first grades. Some schools have not covered the syllabus but teachers have tried their best," said Mr Joseph Charles Ekume, the centre head.
Although Mr Ekume was optimistic, the environment appeared hopeless.
Several children had broken down with suspected malaria and flu yet the examinations were to start the next day.
"There is no medicine, that is the major challenge. I have had to spend my own little money," Mr Ekume said. "I can't just look at a sick child like that. I can't send them away when exams start tomorrow"
Mr Ekume found solace in prayer. "God has been with us. We shall overcome," he said. One candidate, Kevin Amongin was bedridden (or is it tarpaulin ridden?) and couldn't even talk.
She had not eaten any meal as her appetite was bad. Her teachers were visibly sad because they had expected her to perform well.
"It is very sad that we have no health worker around and there are no anti-malarial drugs. We don't know whether she can sit exams tomorrow," Ekume sorrowfully said.
In the "dormitories", the children slept on tarpaulins donated by Unicef. Some had sheets, others tattered blankets while others had nothing.
They had three pressure lumps for reading at night. Katakwi District had donated posho and beans while World Food Programme gave biscuits. Water was a problem, as the only borehole was not functioning to capacity.
But with all the misery, some candidates were excited. Joel Okello, 13, said: " I like maths and I want to get 10 aggregates. I don't speak English but I can read." For Agnes Okure, 15, her desire was to become a nurse although she likes arts subjects.
"I like SST and English. I want to join Ngora Girls' School and I will pass in second grade with 22 aggregates," she said.
Ecweru speaks
"I don't want to disagree with what the people have told you. They are the recipients of the aid and are best placed to give a better assessment.
"All NGOs that responded to the floods were clustered into three: the Food cluster (to give emergency relief aid), Health cluster, and the Water and sanitation cluster," who I met in Soroti on November 4." he said.
"The food cluster is coordinated by the Prime Minister's office and headed by WFP with World Vision and Red Cross as prominent members.
World Health Organisation (WHO) chaired the health cluster with Unicef, Director of District Health Services and other NGOs while FAO led NGOs trying to help revive food production.
"These groups meet every week to review the situation. But if people have complained, I can't rule out the supply of inferior goods."
"I am aware that the major complaint is the selling of relief food by local leaders. I have got reports from Ngora, Magoro and Kolir Sub-Counties. When food is delivered, the leaders delay to distribute it."
"I have directed that food should not stay for more than three days without distribution. Nobody is allowed to flout that. In fact food should be distributed the same day it's delivered.
"My main concern now is for the government and development partners to focus on the post flood era. There is need to rebuild the road network with a view of making bridges and roads better than before to make them withstand future calamities. Mr Ecweru said Teso needs to revive the culture of using traditional granaries for storing food.
"We need to make it mandatory for each home to have granaries. Income generation must not remain in theory. Can you imagine Iteso now generate income through cutting and selling grass?
"Because of the persistent chaotic situation, the people are resigned to drinking as a way to forget the suffering," he observed.
Indeed several people told me that they are in a difficult situation. While they grow fruits in abundance, there is no market with 10 oranges going for only Shs200.
In most cases, the fruits rot away in gardens. A sack of potatoes is sold for between Shs4,000-6,000 although the same goes for at least Shs30, 000 in Kampala.
The floods visited more suffering to Teso where Karimojong cattle rustling and incursions by LRA rebels have all conspired to make life a misery.
Formerly vibrant with large herds of cattle, Teso is now a shadow of its former self with abject poverty being the lowest common multiple. There was always little or nothing for the large majority of people to go back to even after the flood waters have receded.

Original_Search_Title: People Consuming Less - Museveni
Headline: Africa: People Consuming Less - Museveni
Body_Text:
Kampala — UNDER-consumption is the greatest enemy of business in Africa, President Yoweri Museveni has said. He told a gathering of hundreds of senior executives from companies like Coca-Cola and Chevron, in South Africa, that if all the 900 million people in Africa had reached average consumption of all commodities and services, investors would be richer.
Museveni made the remarks on Friday at a dinner hosted in his by the Corporate Council on Africa in his honour and that of other dignitaries who attended the US-Africa Business Summit that ended yesterday in Cape Town.
To illustrate his point, Museveni cited Uganda where an individual is expected to take 210 litres of milk per year but consumes only 30 litres.
He also said Ugandans are expected to consume 50kg of meat per year but on average they eat 5kg.
A State House statement said the President emphasised the importance of a human being as the most important economic resource in the world.
Japan, Museveni noted, does not have natural resources like oil or gold, but is the second greatest economy at $4.5 trillion behind the US, because of its human resource.
"Human beings are capable of reasoning and using brains to acquire skills that are crucial in transforming natural resources into wealth."
Museveni said industrialised countries were wrong to place a higher value on Africa's raw materials than on its 900 million people, who should no longer be considered aid recipients but potential consumers.
Uganda and other African countries, Museveni stressed, owed the West no favours. "Africa has been heamorrhaging resources. It is a misnomer that the West is helping Africa. This is not true."
He said Uganda, the world's fourth biggest coffee exporter, receives $1 per kilogramme of coffee and yet after the beans are roasted and ground in London, they go for $20 a kilogramme.
"For each kilogramme of coffee, we are donating $19. We have been doing this for a long time. But we are fighting it."
Museveni noted that lack of infrastructure was Africa's biggest obstacle to investment.
He added that if Western governments and companies failed invest in the construction of dams for hydroelectric power and other infrastructure, the continent would go it alone.
Museveni hailed US Presidents Bill Clinton and George Bush for signing and implementing the African Growth Opportunity Act, which has enabled Africa access the American market - tax and quota free.
Museveni encouraged members of the business community in South Africa to invest in Uganda, especially in agro-processing as well as in the manufacture of electronic appliances.
The entrepreneurs, led by the president of the Corporate Council on Africa, Stephen Hayes, expressed interest in investing in the manufacture of goods for the Ugandan market and COMESA.
They were also willing to produce water and biogas from cassava and sugarcane.
Other speakers were US secretary of treasury Henry Paulson, South Africa's trade minister Mandisi Mpahlwa and Hayes.

Original_Search_Title: PPP As Tonic for Niger's Development
Headline: Nigeria: PPP As Tonic for Niger's Development
Body_Text:
After 27 years of statehood, Niger State is yet to be set on a steady path to real development. Practical indices of development reveal that the state has either continually stagnated or has often been driven back to the starting point at the end of the tenures of successive administrations.
Nothing indeed underscores the bad state of things in the state than the practical breakdown of virtually all public utilities. A careful analysis of the situation shows that much of the problem lies with the sad departure from the conventional ways of conducting the business of governance. Sustained through the successive dispensations, the bitter trend had over time thrown the polity into serious socio-moral crisis.
The incumbent government, determined to make the desired differences, decided to make a clean departure from the old method of service delivery. But prior to setting out for business unusual, the leadership had made certain to clearly spell out its vision and mission. A key component of the vision which generally aims at enduring growth and development of Niger State, includes selfless service interlaced with transparency, accountability and due process.
Beyond this is the determination of the government to tailor her fresh development drive along the tested and trusted patterns of Europe, America and lately South Africa. This was the deep quest that compelled the government to settle for the public private partnership (PPP) initiative.
In opting for the PPP as the perfect option suitable to our peculiar situation, the steering team did a thorough job of analyzing the pros and cons. The one compelling factor is the fact that the initiative offers multiple advantages and gains in favour of the masses, being the targeted beneficiaries. Essentially, the public private initiative is a long term but sustainable programme between the government and pro-profit private business or investment institutions in which the latter is relied upon to provide the needed capital and technical resources in the quest by the engaging party [government] to deliver better services to the people. It is also a risk sharing programme. It offers the leverage of using available capital resources of the private partner to improve social infrastructure as well as enhance value for public asset and money.
However, the government has specific responsibilities which include supervising the activities of the private sector partners, providing moral support, instilling confidence in the investing partners as well as putting her home front in order such as would guarantee a healthy business tie and bargain. This way, an enabling environment for the investors to operate freely would have been guaranteed. The sub-factors here include total security, allotting enough land space for the investors to operate and avoiding undue interference.
At the risk of exaggeration, one makes bold to say that the social security incentives in the state are as attractive as they can possibly be. Beside the enviable reputation of a most peaceful state, we boast of an extensive and agrarian land mass, a good portion of which the government has since resolved to offer our prospective private partners. A key object of interest here is the need for them to enjoy soft facilities, particularly price liberalisation and capping.
The extent to which the Chief Servant keeps faith in the PPP policy initiative, to the point he appreciates the fact that this and indeed all other programmes of his government are unlikely to succeed where strict measures are not employed to discourage any form of sharp practices. It is in keeping with such fact that the government set out early to establish the due process office. This office is responsible for analysing, evaluating and scrutinising all proposals and propositions with the aim of cutting expenses, discouraging corrupt tendencies and, consequently, retaining more resources in the treasury.
The public/private partnership (PPP) unit has its functions clearly spelt out. It shall among other things formulate and co-ordinate business policy programmes by way of sourcing for credible donor agencies and private partners as well as establishing and coordinating both bilateral and multi-lateral business ties. Beside the added task of forging an enduring partnership with other Local Government Councils, the unit also has the all-important task of attracting to the state such international intervention organs as the World Bank, UNDP, UNESCO, UNIDO, DFID and several others of their ilk.
The unit has a further crucial task, one that it has begun to carry out, to establish PPP Committee at the ministries, extra-ministerial departments and agencies. The PPP unit collaborates with the Economic Council as well as the Committee of the Organised Private Sector (OPS) for effective and speedy realization of its goals. The status of the state Ministry of Justice as an imperative component of the collaboration, partnership and agreement deals can not be over emphasised.
The key component parts of the public sector where investment collaboration is being sought with the organised private sector include agriculture, health, industrial, housing and urban planning, transportation, tourism and hospitality, Water and sanitation, education, information and communication technology, housing and planning, commerce as well as energy, particularly solar power.
The quest to lure private investors to the agricultural sector in the state is noble in every respect. The sector, with its streak of edible investment components, offers a ray of hope for fair economic prosperity for the state in the short run, given appropriate annexation and management strategies. There is a score of edible cash crops, which if given appropriate handling, can rake in millions of dollars in export returns, for the state. The PPP shall seek collaboration in fertilizer production, conservation, high breed plantation as well as specialized farming.
The PPP unit has identified a number of edible cash crops produced around the state and which could very well boost her economy, given the necessary aid and handling. They include the Baddeggi Rice Mill as well as other expansive Fadama projects in senatorial zone A, the cassava and yam projects, cotton, the expansive rice production initiative, etc.
Niger State is endowed with extensive and attractive tourism centres that can put her on an equal footing in terms of both patronage and revenue generation as the most notable African tourist state, Kenya. It's an open fact that the West African country is today sustained substantially by her rich tourism sector. Our claim to such tourist centres as the Zuma Rock, Gurara Falls and the Games Park at Kainji readily portray ours as a state with some of the best tourism potentials.
In taking full advantage of these rare gifts of nature, plans are a foot to partner the organised private sector with a view to getting these and several other viable centres of tourism across the state fully functional. The PPP is working out a broad policy plan aimed at putting in place tourism centres such as tourist village, medical tourism as well as cultural tourism events. However, it wont be out of place to resolve the riddle around the Zuma Hotel project which has reportedly been on the cards for a long time. Doing so could serve to further boost tourism activities around the amazing Zuma rock.
Without doubt, a legion of challenges dot our path to enduring development. Evidently, the most conspicuous is that of successfully wooing very credible private investors, particularly the foreign ones, to the state. Our trip is rendered the more challenging by the extra wave of caution and fear sent across the business world by the unfortunate militant activities in the Niger Delta. But we hold a veritable ace in the fact that ours is a state with the proud reputation of being the most peaceful and investment-friendly in the country today.
Mohammed, is Senior Special Assistant on Public Private Partnership (PPP) to State Governor Muazu Babangida Aliyu.

Original_Search_Title: "Book Minister" Inaugurates Read Campaign Committee
Headline: Nigeria: "Book Minister" Inaugurates Read Campaign Committee
Body_Text:
The Minister of State for Education (I), Dr. Anthony Jerry Agada on Tuesday 13th November inaugurated a 24-man National Action Committee on Read Project (NACORP).
In his opening remarks before the inauguration, the Minister commended the committee for their commitment and all the work they have done so far, from the conception of the project which is one of the Ministry's reform programme till date. He charged the committee not to rest on their oars but to deliberate extensively on strategies to employ to advance the course of the Read Project. Recounting his own experience as Permanent Secretary in Benue State where he had to give the needed support to all activities geared towards improving the reading culture, the "Book Minister as he is now fondly called reiterated the Ministry's desire to give the committee a 100% support to achieve their objectives. Dr. Mrs. Makoju, a Director in the Ministry in her address informed the committee that its activities will not be driven completely by the Ministry.
Hence, the need to work out ways of collaborating with the organized private sector to fund the read campaign activities. She however assured the committee that while no specific financial provision was made for the read project in the 2006/2007 budget, a moderate provision has been made for it in the 2008 budget to ensure that the project is not abandoned. Having been officially inaugurated, the committee re-assembled to deliberate on the work plan drawn up after the children and youth launch of the read campaign carried out by the administration of Dr. Oby Ezekwesili. After an extensive deliberation, the committee broke into five sub-committees namely: School Activities, Media/Advocacy, Professional Associations, Private Public Partnership and the Federal Ministry of Education.
Each group was charged with the responsibility of coming up with suggestions and strategies necessary to advance the course for wish they were assembled. The Nation Action Committee on Read Project (NACORP) comprises of all major stakeholders in the education and book industry among which are, the Association of Nigerian Authors, Nigerian Educational Research Development Council, Reading Association of Nigeria, Nigerian Publishers Association, Nigerian Booksellers Association, the National Library of Nigeria, Universal Basic Education Commission, the Nigerian book Project, Peniel Library, Cassava Republic Press, Pressfield Communications Limited, Education Trust Fund, Arojah Concepts and Culture Afrique among others.

Original_Search_Title: Taming the Garrison Commander
Headline: Nigeria: Taming the Garrison Commander
Body_Text:
Lagos — Yesterday, the self-styled strongman of Ibadan politics, Chief Lamidi Ariyibi Adedibu, was held by the Police and quizzed for about two hours.
He was subsequently charged to Abuja Magistrate Court in Abuja for alleged breach of public peace. He was granted bail and released.
The move by the Police to get at the controversial politician who for decades imposed his political agenda on Oyo State through intimidation and thuggery is a departure from the past. Adedibu had always been treated by officialdom as an outlaw who would get away with anything.
During the eight years of Obasanjo's administration, the notorious strongman reached the apogee of his political influence. He called the shots not only in his native Oyo State but also in some other states in the Southwest zone. In fact, the former President who is now the Chairman of Board of Trustees of PDP recently hailed Adedibu as 'the only father' of PDP. The party's national chairman, Dr. Ahmadu Ali similarly eulogised him as the 'Garrison Commander' of Ibadan politics. He declared that anyone who was not prepared to take orders from the revered kingmaker should pack his bag and baggage and leave town.
It was such statements that emboldened Adedibu in throwing caution to the wind to impose his warped political authority on the people. The fear of Adedibu was indeed the beginning of wisdom in Oyo State during the past nine years.
Alhaji Adedibu usually gave little or no regard for decorum while aiming for a victim. Former governor of Oyo State, Alhaji Rasheed Ladoja, got more than a fair dose of Adedibu's voodoo politics as he was literarily haunted and chased around until he was impeached. When the court later returned him, Adedibu and his henchmen could not give him any breathing space until Adedibu's anointed godson and Ladoja's deputy, Otunba Christopher Alao-Akala, was installed governor.
Feeding the Multitude
Adedibu was popular among the masses. Those who are hungry often go to his Molete residence and are fed. The regular meal served is amala, a popular Yoruba food made from cassava or yam flour. His generosity in feeding the multitude made him popular among the hungry masses. For this reason, there was always a crowd at his residence which was always at his beck and call. Many of them are miscreants and motor park touts known as "agbero" whom he easily mobilises. Thus Adedibu became known as an apostle of amala politics.
The politician has been variously extolled by his admirers. Praise singers are never in want around his residence and at any function he is attending. Some local musicians even composed songs in his praise. For instance, a prominent Ibadan musician described him as "an individual who is equal to half of Ibadan". The same musician described him as a man who would forcefully seize another man's chicken and as the owner of the chickens is crying and looking for his chicken, he (Adedibu) would be smiling and grinding pepper with which he would roast the chicken.
Adedibu's regular day begins like this. He wakes up at his Molete residence and takes majestic stroll to his expansive sitting room. There he holds court meeting his protégés, admirers, supporters and visitors. It was from that daily routine that he derived the title, 'Alaafin Molete'. At the Molete palace, the needy converged seeking one form of favour or the other from him.
He diligently attends to their requests one after the other. The usual requests range from introductory letters for contracts or employment, phone calls to people in government, financial assistance and the like. For those seeking elective or appointive positions in the state or even at the federal level, an anointing by Adedibu was vital.
Lawlessness
Adedibu typifies lawlessness, brigandage and terrorism. His thugs are dreaded throughout Oyo State and particularly in the state capital, Ibadan. He has an assemblage of broad-chested thugs who served as his private army. Some of them bore such nicknames as Tokyo, Sir K, Elewe-Omo, Destroyer and so on. They enforced his will as his word was their command. Some major lawless acts of his supporters include the following:
The Ladoja Experience
Adedibu made no pretence about the fact that he installed Rasheed ALadoja as governor. However, he fell out with Ladoja when he laid claim to a certain percentage of the state's monthly allocation of revenue. When Ladoja refused his demand and began to assert his independence of the Molete chief, he withdrew his support and declared war against him. He threatened to unseat him boasting that he had single-handedly installed and removed governors and speakers at whim in the state. He said he had limitless powers.
He was proved right as the group of State Assemblymen loyal to him mobilised sooner and impeached Ladoja. Before then, his hefty thugs had once sacked the Governor's office while the security personnel seemed helpless. Until yesterday, it might not be an overstatement to say that Adedibu was running a parallel government in Ibadan and was treated as somebody virtually above the law of the land.
NAFDAC
About two months ago, the Director-General of NAFDAC, Professor Dora Akunyili, lamented that Adedibu was frustrating the efforts of her agency in the fight against counterfeit drugs. According to Akunyili, who spoke in Ibadan, the octogenarian was using his thugs to shield importers of fake drugs in the state. She said each time NAFDAC officials sealed off a warehouse of fake drugs or other consumables, Adedibu would mobilise his men to reopen the warehouse by force. She said NAFDAC was simply helpless over the ugly trend and challenged the government and Police to do something.
Invasion of TV station
Adedibu's dreaded thugs once sacked a TV station in Ibadan while broadcast was in progress. The action was to forestall the announcement of the results of the local government election conducted by Ladoja. The election was not approved by Adedibu. It was later voided by his protege, Alao Akala, immediately he assumed power to the satisfaction of Adedibu.
Curiously, the Police never made any arrest following the TV station invasion. Over the years, particularly since the advent of the democratic dispensation, many people had been killed or seriously maimed in clashes with these thugs. Interestingly, there has been no reported arrest or prosecution of any of the culprits.
Abductions
Abductions and torture were among the methods employed by the thugs to beat his opponents into line. There was, for instance, the case of his men's abduction of Alhaji Layi Oladoja, a former director of O'odua Development Company. He was whisked away by the Adedibu men in his vehicle from the Eid praying ground in Ibadan. He was later rescued by the police. However, the police exonerated Adedibu saying he was not involved in the criminal act.
In all those lawless acts by Adedibu's me,n neither the Ibadan chief nor his men were arrested or interrogated by the Police. The only exception was the recent half-hearted arrest, interrogation and subsequent release of his chief helmsman, Tokyo (Lateef Akinsola), the chairman of NURTW, Oyo State chapter.
That Adedibu has all along been able to hold Ibadan and Oyo State to ransom is as a result of the support he enjoys from the Federal and State governments. Is his present ordeal a signal that the honeymoon is over or is it a mere window dressing to deceive Nigerians? With his immediate release after the charge, the likelihood is that he would return to a hero's welcome at Ibadan. He would likely continue to thumb-chest claiming that he cannot be dared by the Police.
For President Umaru Musa Yar'Adua, this may be the acid test of his trumpeted rule of law policy.

Original_Search_Title: LWF/WS Empowers Many in Bong
Headline: Liberia: LWF/WS Empowers Many in Bong
Body_Text:
The Lutheran World Federation Department of World Service (LWF/WS), with support from an international church-based organization in Finland, (FINN CHURCH AID), has empowered thousands of citizens and residents of Panta and Zota Districts in Bong County to carry out activities in the areas of agriculture, and the construction of bridges, hand pumps, latrines, among others.
The citizens and residents, who spoke to the press in Bong County, praised the Lutheran World Federation/World Service and the FINN CHURCH AID for empowering them to be able to carry out these activities, which now serve as sources of livelihood for them.
The citizens said the LWF/WS and FINN CHURCH AID empowered them in water and sanitation education wherein they would become knowledgeable about the construction and maintenance of hand pumps, bridges and latrines and in agricultural activities; the provision of family implements and knowledge in crops production were also assets the people attained.
Most of those spoken to last week during an assessment and evaluation tour made by a team from the LWF/WS and FINN CHURCH AID, said the projects were divided into two communities, namely the Watson and Agriculture communities.
The Watson Community include Joe Tow, Koryah, Moanga, Gbansu, Woumeh, Mbellequah, Yowee and Farvey, while the Agriculture Communities include; Garmu, Kpo, Boway, Gbainyear, Jorwah, Gbaita, Paquelleh, Woumeh, Koryah, Mbellequah.
The beneficiaries of the two districts later appealed to the LWF/WS and the FINN CHURCH AID, including humanitarian groups and well-meaning individuals to assist them with cassava machines for the production of 'gari,' another name for farina and other basic foods acquired from cassava.
Speaking to the press after a 3-day assessment and evaluation tour of Panta and Zota Districts in Bong County, Lutheran World Federation/World Service Project Manager, Madam Mary Leesolee said the projects in the two districts started in April 2007 and these have benefited thousands of the citizens and residents, most of whom are returning IDPs and refugees.
Madam Leesolee said the projects include activities that deal with Watsan, which she said has a component of hygiene promotion, pump, latrine, bridge construction, and agricultural assistance to returning families. The project will also encompass health protection issues with emphasis on HIV/AIDS, child protection, women and children protection and sexual exploitation.
She described the projects in the two districts as effective and that they are having positive results on the lives of the people and the LWF/WS was the very first organization to reach to their aid since their return from IDP and refugee camps.
Madam Leesolee estimated the cost of the projects to be two hundred and ninety-one thousand United States Dollars for both administrative and project implementation.

Original_Search_Title: Measures to Ease Fertilizer Distribution Identified
Headline: Nigeria: Measures to Ease Fertilizer Distribution Identified
Body_Text:
Abuja — The difficulties associated with the fertilizer supply programme of the federal ministry of agriculture in Nigeria will soon become a thing of the past as some measures have been put in place to prevent the problems encountered during the 2007 season exercise.
These measures, according to reliable source from the ministry, include timely procurement process when the international prices of fertilizers are less competitive, timely deliveries before the raining season in order to ease road transportation problems, tangible funding arrangement with payment to contractors, selection of few, competent and reliable companies that have good records of performance over the years to execute the contract.
Meanwhile, the proposal for 2008 season of the programme has been slated to include: quantity to be procured for 2008, the procurement plans funding requirements, bidding and evaluation plans, distribution/access module and remittance of sales proceeds.
The source disclosed that the quantity to be procured will be based on the following consideration; report of the socio-economic study conducted in 2006 on fertilizer use, which put the agronomic requirement at 2.60 million tonnes per annum; expected high demand for fertilizer to implement the various crop development initiatives in rice, cassava, maize and sustain the National Food Security Programme, need to achieve the target of 50kg per hectare nutrient use of farmer's level from the present 10kg per hectare by 2015 in order to attain the targets of the MDGs.
Others are the need to move growth in the agricultural sector from the present 9 per cent to 13 per cent by next year; the need to improve the fertilizer supply situation in 2008 season in order to continue to stabilise the market, and enhance farmers' access to the input, among others.
It would be recalled that these measures are coming on the heels of some circumstances that led to low performance of the programme in the 2007 season.

Original_Search_Title: Environmental Law Court Arrests 10 in Kwara
Headline: Nigeria: Environmental Law Court Arrests 10 in Kwara
Body_Text:
Ilorin — Ten persons believed to have violated environmental law in Kwara State were recently arrested and fined N1, 000 each .
The state government had recently banned street trading in unhygienic places in the state.
Eight of the offenders were arrested along Ahmadu Bello Way, while two were arrested at Ipata area.
The presiding judge of the mobile Grade I Area Court, Alhaji Ibrahim Abdullahi, released those who paid their fines while defaulters were ordered to be remanded at 'C' Division Police Station, Oja-Oba.
One Mrs Maria Peter was arrested for allegedly preparing local cassava food (fufu) in an unhygienic environment, and littering the main road with waste. She was also alleged to have rained abuses on health officers which made the judge to fine her N1, 500.00
Abdullahi said the fine became imperative to serve as a deterrent to those who do not keep healthy environment.
He therefore implored Kwarans to complement government's efforts by keeping their environment clean, and also dump refuse in roro bins provided by the waste management company.

Original_Search_Title: Why Kampala is the Heart of Illegal Ivory Trade
Headline: Uganda: Why Kampala is the Heart of Illegal Ivory Trade
Body_Text:
Kampala — UWA enforcement unit supervisor Steven Kimpwedde displays ivory pieces estimated at sh277m at their head offices. They were recovered by UWA in December 2006
ONLY elephants are supposed to wear ivory, so goes a popular slogan. But Wang Xiuli, a youthful Chinese, disrespected it. She was caught smuggling ivory recently. Wang was restless as customs officials checked her overweight bag in Busia.
Desparate, Wang gave a $20 (sh36,000) bribe to Nice Byarugaba, a customs official. Byarugaba declined it. Sensing danger, Wang ran and attempted to board a Kampala-bound taxi. But the officials caught up with her. They took her to Police and later to court, where she was charged for smuggling ivory.
"Court ordered Wang to pay a fine of sh10m," says Vincent Opyene in Uganda Wildlife Authority (UWA) law enforcement department. So far, Wang has paid sh2m. The court is holding her passport until she completes payment.
In the last six years, four large consignments of ivory were intercepted by UWA. However, "There is great suspicion that most traffickers have become smarter and ivory could be getting out of the country undetected," says Opyene.
About five years ago, a tonne of ivory was ground and mixed with cassava flour and smuggled to China. It was impounded in Shanghai.
Why is the trade continuing unabated?
According to a UWA official, in some cases, officials in charge of checking illegalities, turn a blind eye. "Such cases of illegal transactions in ivory have swallowed some of the security personnel who are supposed to be checking it," says a source that preferred anonymity. "There is no way such quantities can pass the check points without being detected."
Unclear circumstances
In the latest case, a consignment of ivory allegedly entered Uganda from the DR Congo through Burundi, but the trafficker and the ivory remained elusive until last week. According to a source, over 300 pieces of ivory were impounded three months ago and the trafficker arrested. They also arrested the trafficker suspected to be a Burundian, but he was released under unclear circumstances.
Poaching and corruption
Kampala has become the heart of illegal trade in ivory. The trade is masterminded by businessmen mainly from West Africa and China, who wield a lot of influence in the Uganda Police, the army and diplomatic circles.
Once, UWA used ivory and fake traders to penetrate the criminal gangs. "We thought we had got evidence and arrested the suspects on Friday and took them to Central Police Station, Kampala but after the weekend, the suspects were already out and mocking UWA," says a source. "There was no sufficient evidence to incriminate the suspects."
The hunters are hunted
All roads of illegal ivory from the DR Congo lead to Uganda because of the strong network of traders and security agencies.
According to sources in the wildlife trade, a former law enforcement officer in UWA, Sam Tugume, was on the verge of exposing the traffickers, but was arrested as a rebel of the People's Redemption Army.
In another incident last year, security personnel and the Uganda Revenue Authority impounded about 300kg of ivory at Kabusu in Kampala. But the culprits, believed to be part of rackets masterminded by West African businessmen, disappeared.
UWA and Government institutions building capacity
Over 30 officials recently convened at Kireka Sports View Hotel in Kampala at a meeting organised by UWA and Interpol on wildlife to share experiences with the Police, customs and immigration officials from Busia and Entebbe.
"We have to share experiences to break down the criminal gangs," said Karl Karugaba, working with the Lusaka Task Agreement. Karugaba, who worked with UWA for over a decade, remembers how frustrating it was to weed out criminals.
However, things are better and UWA is hot on the heels of the traders implicated in the illicit trade in ivory. "It is almost impossible to poach in the protected areas in Uganda," says Moses Mapesa, the executive director of UWA.
Mapesa attributes the illicit trade, which has left many elephants dead, to the political unrest in the DR Congo.
He says, this has created a safe haven for criminals disguised as investors to deal in ivory. As a result, endangered animals like elephants and rhinos have been butchered by gunmen in eastern DR Congo.
Fortunately, wildlife authorities in DR Congo are making most of the protected areas secure, according to Mapesa.
UWA's law enforcement department has increased its manpower, bringing on board people with skills in legal and intelligence gathering.
UWA now has an ivory detector that can be used to check the ivory in case it is mixed with other commodities like maize flour.
Byarugaba deserves a pat on the back because she did not accept Wang's $20 bribe. She let the law take its course and led to the recovery of ivory and Wang's arrest. Many people would be proud of being Ugandan if other Government officials acted like Byarugaba.

Original_Search_Title: Niger Govt Builds N37.5b Ethanol Plant
Headline: Nigeria: Niger Govt Builds N37.5b Ethanol Plant
Body_Text:
Niger — Niger State Government has signed a N37.5 billion agreement with OSPREY Investment Company Ltd for the construction of an ethanol factory in the state.
The state deputy governor, Hon. Ahmed Ibeto, while signing the agreement on behalf of the state government, said the agreement was in furtherance of the state's industrial policy, which places a high premium on public-private-partnership for the rapid socio-economic development of the state.
Ibeto opined that the state's quest to be among the three most industrialised states in the country by the year 2020 requires a sound economic base as well as viable sources of revenue.
While noting that the project would be of immense benefit to the state, the deputy governor explained that the project would provide employment opportunities to hundreds of Nigerlites, boost the state's economic and revenue base and impact positively on the lives of the people.
He enjoined OSPREY to adhere strictly to the terms of the agreement and assured them that the state government would fulfil its own obligation to ensure the realisation of the project.
General manager of the company, Mr. Geoff Hughes, said the ethanol factory will come to life within two years. Hughes explained that if completed, the plant would have an annual production capacity of 15,000 tons of ethanol fuel and a large scale plantation to provide feed stocks. He added that Niger State stands to benefit in areas like direct and indirect employment opportunities, 25 percent share holding and private financial investors. In addition, the state will be placed on the Nigeria Stock Exchange with a pre-investment fund of N100 million.
Accordin g to him, the variety of crops to be used for the production of ethanol are cassava, maize, wheat, rice, sweet potatoes, sugar beet and sugar cane.
The general manager explained that ethanol products are mostly used in the US, Europe, Asia, South-Africa and other parts of the world.

Original_Search_Title: Government Gives Shs50m to Kayunga Storm Victims
Headline: Uganda: Government Gives Shs50m to Kayunga Storm Victims
Body_Text:
Kayunga — THE Ministry of Disaster Preparedness has donated relief items worth Shs50m to residents in Kayunga District whose homes and crops were ravaged by a storm.
The items, which included 470 bags of maize flour, 200 bags of beans and 250 bags of improved cassava cuttings.
Officials from the ministry of Disaster Preparedness handed over the donation to the Cao, Mr Sam Kateihangwa and district officials at Ntenjeru District head quarters on Friday.
A terrible storm, in September, destroyed crops and razed over 100 homes in the sub-counties of Kayunga, Kangulumira and Nazigo.
At least 200 residents were left homeless while three residents sustained injuries when their houses collapsed on them.
While receiving the donation, Mr Kateihangwa thanked the government for rescuing the affected residents some have reportedly been going without food.
He, however, said there was need to provide building materials is to the victims because most of them don't have to live after their homes were destroyed by the strom.
"We appeal to the government to consider giving building materials like iron sheets to the affected people so that they can reconstruct their houses,"Mr Kateihangwa said.
He warned the beneficiaries against selling the donation and urged LCs to ensure that the seeds and cassava cuttings are planted.
The district Director for production, Mr David Mugabi, warned political leaders against influencing the distribution of relief items. He said the items should benefit only those who were affected by the storm and not those who have political connections to big offices in the district.
Mr Mugabi said the items would be distributed by LC3 leaders of the three affected sub-counties.
Meanwhile, the over 50 residents who had illegally built along the banks of River Ssezibwa in Kayunga D istrict have appealed to the district authorities to give them a grace period of five months before eviction.
They said they need some time to look for where to relocate and that evicting them at this time would render them homeless.
However, the district environment officer Mr Patrick Musaazi, said they would not give them more time.

Original_Search_Title: U.S Spends $15m Annually On Country's Agriculture -Mission Director
Headline: Nigeria: U.S Spends $15m Annually On Country's Agriculture -Mission Director
Body_Text:
The United States Agency for International Development (USAID) spends over $15 million annually on Nigeria's agriculture, the agency's mission director has said.
Sharon Cromer made the remarks weekend when she visited the Ministry of Agriculture and Water Resources Abuja. She said USAID is investing the money as Nigeria's development partner to improve the livelihoods of the citizens.
She said the USAID MARKETS Project which focuses on improving rural livelihood by expanding economic opportunities in the agricultural sub-sector , especially for women and youth is investing over $25 million between 2005 and 2010 to support Government of Nigeria in promoting private sector investment in agriculture. She said the intervention will complement government efforts in devising appropriate technologies in production.
"Post harvest loses are high, appropriate processing and value adding technologies are not available, a commercial orientation is lacking and market information and linkages are weak" she said.
Cromer said USAID spends roughly $3million a year on policy development and capacity building in a number of ministries, agencies and departments, Ministry of Agriculture and Water Resources inclusive.
"USAID sponsored National Stakeholder Workshop on Fertilizer Policy and Strategy Implementation as well as the African Fertilizer Summit which eventually led to adoption of a National Fertilizer Policy, approved by the Federal Executive Council in June 2006" she said.
The Mission Director also said USAID has been the major donor towards addressing Avian Influenza in Nigeria with more than $1.6 million invested during the first year of the outbreak.
"In 2007, another $4 million has been committed towards supporting Nigeria to containing and eradicating the disease" Cromer said.
She said the Cassava Enterprise Development Project which is an $11.7milion five year partnership between USAID/Nigeria, Shell Petroleum Development Corporation and the International Institute of Tropical Agriculture has yielded positive result.
The Minister of Agriculture and Water Resources, Dr. Sayyadi Abba Ruma thanked the USAID in their efforts on agriculture and call on them to partner with the Government on improving processing and storage of crops.
"Nigeria despite been the highest producer of Cassava has poor processing and storage facilities" he said.

Original_Search_Title: Why Mechanisation of Agriculture Still Remains a Dream
Headline: Uganda: Why Mechanisation of Agriculture Still Remains a Dream
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE Government's call for modernisation of agriculture is stilla myth. Mechanisation cannot be achieved when farmers are still using hoes and relying on rainfall to work on their farms," the Mukono district LC5 chairman, Francis Lukooya observes.
Recently, Pastor Samuel Kakande unveiled state-of-the-art agricultural equipment worth sh3b.
The phrase, mechanisation of agriculture, has remained a myth, with nothing much to show that the country is moving in that direction. Policy makers may argue that it is difficult to mechanise the sector because there are no funds to buy equipment. However, the truth is, the policy makers have failed to identify priorities and stop distributing small amounts of money among hundreds of small farmers.
This financial year, sh60b will be spent under the National Agricultural Advisory Services (NAADS) programme. In the last four years, over sh150b has been spent on agricultural development.
Every district receives at least sh400m annually for NAADS activities. NAADS is not the only multi-billion project that has failed to produce tangible results.
Under the Northern Uganda Social Action Fund (NUSAF) on which over sh150b was spent in the last few years, the targeted groups have not benefitted much. The main agriculture budget has consumed around sh600b in the last five years, but the impact remains invisible. Hundreds of non-governmental organisations have also pumped billions of shillings in the sector.
The problem is, the money is distributed among subsistence farmers, many of who cannot make a significant impact. For example, under the NAADS, some farmers got as little as sh100,000.
While meeting the visiting Sasakawa Global officials last week, the minister of Agriculture, Hillary Onek, pointed out that another sh600m has been earmarked for every sub-county to develop agriculture. This may sound good, but the problem is that this amount will be distributed to about 350 families per sub-county. In the end, there will be no impact.
According to analysts, such programmes fail because of poor planning. For example, billions of shillings are spent on workshops in hotels around the city. More money is lost in renting lavish offices, while more is spent on expensive vehicles. Less than 25% of the money find its way to the grassroot farmers.
A typical agricultural seminar is held in a hotel, where the presenters and participants are paid hefty allowances. The target group leaves the hotel with nothing practical to take to their farms.
On many occasions, President Yoweri Museveni has said there is no need to spend billions hosting seminars in air-conditioned hotels in Kampala. This money should directly be channelled into mechanisation or value addition to products.
There is also the problem of duplication of responsibilities at the district level. On top of NAADS, every district has a department of production and a district farmers' association, all under the Ministry of Agriculture.
All these groups are paid differently and yet, the work that they do can be done by one group.
In addition, teaching farmers how to plant bananas or maize has to move to another level. "Telling us how to plant cassava or maize is no longer the main point. We know how to do that. We only lack the means to produce them in larger quantities," says Samson Ocen, a farmer in Kaberamaido.
A few farmers have tried to hire tractors from individuals, but this is costly. A farmer has to part with sh150,000 to sh200,000 to have an acre of land opened up.
"But if these tractors were owned by the farmers association, a farmer would only pay for fuel and maintenance," Frank Tumwebaze, the president of the Uganda Farmers Federation, says.
According to a prominent farmer, Brig. Kasirye Gwanga, without mechanisation, we shall never move from the subsistence agriculture.
Recently, in Wakiso, the minister of agriculture, Onek, launched tractors privately acquired at sh3b by Pastor Kakande.
Each of the tractors can plough over 400 acres of land at any one time.
The Government does not have to go for this particular type. There are cheaper ones at sh7m, while the most common one cost between sh15-25m. With at least sh150m for mechanisation, a district can acquire at least five tractors every year.
The Luweero district, LC5 chairman, Ronald Ndawula, says the NAADS funds should have been used to buy farm implements.
Under the Prosperity for All programme, a lot of the money should be spent on mechanisation and value addition. Under the programme, the country has been divided into 15 regions, and each region is to receive sh2b first batch. This amount is enough to start up a tractor scheme.
"Farmers groups can effectively manage these tractors. We have groups in all districts," Tumwebaze says.

Original_Search_Title: Grinding Mill Products Cause Cancer - Study
Headline: Uganda: Grinding Mill Products Cause Cancer - Study
Body_Text:
Kampala — CONSUMPTION of groundnuts, maize, cassava chips, soybeans and millet processed by local grinding mills could lead to cancer, a Usaid funded research done by Makerere University has shown.
Dr Archileo Kaaya, a senior lecturer in the Department of Food Science and Technology at the university, conducted the research from 2000-2007 across the country.
He said the findings showed that locally fabricated grinding mills were partly responsible for the increase in cancer levels.
"Mild steel is always pilling off particles of hammers or blades of blenders within 6-8 months and this coupled with the rotten groundnuts, maize, cassava chips, soybeans and millet will increase cancer levels by 10 times the recommended standards," Dr Kaaya said.
The research measures the quantity in terms of parts per billion (ppb) or microgrammes. The report shows that while developed countries have the capacity to handle the aflatoxins intake by their people, it was worse in developing countries because they have no capacity to detect the recommended Aflatoxins intake.
Aflatoxins are chemicals produced by fungi known as Aspergillus flavus. When contaminated food is cooked, the fungi will be killed but not the toxins because they are heat stable, thus causing cancer.
"While the US and EU has strict laws limiting aflatoxin levels in human nutrition to 20ppb and 4ppb respectively, few developing countries have enforceable laws in place," the report says.
"Aflatoxins are dangerous and may cause diseases such as liver cancer when these particles collect around the liver. Other sicknesses among children include retard growth and immune suppression," he said.
Dr Sam Zaramba, the director general of health services confirmed the findings.

Original_Search_Title: Purging  Peanuts of Deadly Aflatoxin
Headline: Malawi: Purging  Peanuts of Deadly Aflatoxin
Body_Text:
Local efforts to put an end to aflatoxin outbreaks are helping groundnut farmers back to prosperity, reports Charles Mkoka.
For Amos Katosa, a subsistence farmer in Malawi's central region district of Mchinji, the groundnut -- popularly known as the peanut-- has been his main source of income for the past 40 years.
He describes how the crop helped to pay school fees for his four children and to buy household necessities for his family.
But over the past few decades, peanut sales have been declining at commodity markets.
Little did farmers like Katosa know, but the groundnut, once their best income earner, was facing a major challenge that would jeopardise Malawi's contribution to the international export market.
The challenge was aflatoxin, a highly potent poison contaminating crops and rocking Malawi's groundnut industry, which could no longer meet international standards of quality.
Cost of the problem
Aflatoxins are a waste product from the fungus Aspergillus, which grows on food crops such as maize, groundnuts, sorghum and cassava.
The fungus grows on harvested crops under warm, moist conditions that can occur during transit and storage. Unripe crops are also prone to aflatoxin contamination during drought.
Because of the toxicity of aflatoxins, the European Union -- an important trading partner for Malawi -- effectively banned the import of groundnuts from the country in the early 1990s because contamination exceeded acceptable levels.
The European Union limits aflatoxin contamination to four parts per billion, a standard that has cost some developing countries hundreds of millions of dollars in export losses.
The problem, according to Rodomiro Oritz, from the International Maize and Wheat Improvement Centre, is that monitoring for toxins and enforcement of standards are rarely effective in the developing world, particularly in Sub-Saharan Africa.
Cost to health
In developing countries such as Malawi, the best-quality foods tend to be exported, whereas poor-quality food -- often contaminated with aflatoxins -- is kept for local consumption.
Children exposed to aflatoxins can suffer from restricted growth and from immune suppression, making them more susceptible to HIV and malaria. Aflatoxins are also strongly associated with the development of liver cancer.
According to Charles Dzamalala, a pathologist at the University of Malawi, acute aflatoxicosis is widespread in developing countries.
The syndrome can be fatal and is characterized by vomiting, abdominal pain, pulmonary oedema, convulsions and coma, and damage to the liver, kidneys and heart.
"Conditions increasing the likelihood of acute aflatoxicosis in humans include limited availability of food, environmental conditions that favour fungal development in crops and commodities, and lack of regulatory systems for aflatoxin monitoring and control," he said.
In 2004 in Kenya, 300 people became ill after consuming aflatoxins, leading to the deaths of 125 people.
Lasting solutions
In Malawi, the challenge presented by aflatoxins has seen the launch of a nongovernmental organisation, Facseat-Tropical.
The organisation promotes food-safety measures, including the prevention of fungal contamination of crops, sorting and removal of contaminated grains, and increasing awareness of state-of-the-art facilities for grain storage.
The organisation plans to assess these measures to determine whether they can be linked to any changes in the pattern of liver-cancer incidence, food poisoning or toxicity in the population.
The National Smallholder Farmers Association of Malawi (NASFAM) is collaborating with the International Crop Research Institute for Semi-Arid Tropics (ICRISAT) and the United States Agency for International Development to help farmers meet the aflatoxin safety requirements for export markets.
This involves promoting good agricultural practices, including early planting to prevent groundnuts from maturing during potential drought conditions at the tail end of the season -- which can encourage growth of Aspergillus on ripening crops -- and appropriate harvesting and drying techniques to reduce the possibility of Aspergillus infection.
NASFAM is also encouraging the use of hand-operated or mechanised shelling equipment to ease the task of shelling. Much of the aflatoxin contamination in Malawi arises from moistening the unshelled nuts to make them easier to shell. The association also encourages farmers to sell their groundnuts immediately after shelling because storage increases the risk of contamination.
NASFAM has recommended organising a national conference to initiate awareness on issues surrounding aflatoxins and their effects on agriculture, health and trade.
The association has so far trained 803 staffers and farmers as part of its capacity-building process to fight aflatoxin contamination, and is setting up a system to trace contamination back to the source.
Catching the culprit
Detection of trace amounts of aflatoxins has recently become much easier for developing countries, thanks to the development of inexpensive enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) kits (see Farmers use cheap technology to fight fungus).
At about US$1 per sample, ELISA is a testing tool that individual farmers can afford; the exorbitant cost of assay by high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), used by pre-shipment inspection agents costs around US$230 per sample.
The ELISA kit uses monoclonal and polyclonal antibodies to detect aflatoxins in crop and feed samples. Even remote rural farms can use a small, mobile kit to monitor grains and nuts and to improve storage techniques.
The kit also enables crops for export to be screened rapidly, reducing the risk of infection of other crops.
ICRISAT has established diagnostic laboratories in India, Malawi and Mozambique, which can assay up to 300 samples daily versus the HPLC methods, which can analyse between 20-40 samples a day. This has resulted in safer products for consumer and higher returns for African farmers.
A safer future
Zuberi Seguni, principal agricultural research officer at the Mikocheni Agricultural Research Station in Tanzania, says the technology represents a real saving for farmers.
It should eventually benefit local populations too, as crop quality and storage methods improve.
These new developments have prompted Amos Katosa and many other farmers to give groundnuts another chance, wise to the potential value of the crop both locally and internationally.
"During this crop season, which commences in October, I am certain to reserve two hectares of my garden for groundnuts; this is the crop that has supported me since time immemorial," he says.

Original_Search_Title: Smallscale Farmers to Benefit From Peanut Plant
Headline: Kenya: Smallscale Farmers to Benefit From Peanut Plant
Body_Text:
Small scale farmers from Nyanza are set to benefit from the establishment of a peanut processing factory in the region.
The plant has already been set up at the offices of Mich, a community based organisation in Kisumu.
The organisation has also embarked on supplying farmers with quality peanut seeds.
The founder of Mich, Ms Fidelis Wainaina, yesterday said that Mich had mobilised farmers groups in Siaya district and was training them on tending to the crop and marketing it.
The organisation has introduced a system whereby the farmers would be shareholders in the company, reaping profit made from the integrated chain.
"When we buy two kilogrammes at Sh130, we make peanut butter which is sold at Sh400 and since we encourage the farmers to be shareholders in the organisation, they are set to benefit from the profit," said Ms Fidelis.
Mich is likely to benefit from a partnership with a Swedish organisation International Christian Chamber of Commerce (ICCC) which is keen to help farmers venture into agribusiness activities.
ICCC chairman Gunner Olson toured the farmers projects in Siaya on Tuesday. ICCC has nurtured business start ups in 107 countries across the World.
Mr Olson said he would share business ideas with Mich with a view to eradicating poverty.
Currently Mich is working with groups of farmers in Siaya district who are involved in production of cassava, millet, sorghum, pea nuts, groundnuts and maize among other cereals.

Original_Search_Title: Chinese Team to Discuss Agricultural Centre
Headline: Mozambique: Chinese Team to Discuss Agricultural Centre
Body_Text:
Maputo — A Chinese technical team will shortly visit Maputo to negotiate the final details for the project to build an Agricultural Technology and Development Centre.
An agreement in principle to set up such a centre was reached during the visit of Chinese President Hu Jintao to Mozambique in February. The initial idea was to establish the centre in the northern province of Nampula, but it was subsequently decided to move it to Boane in Maputo province.
According to the economic consellor at the Chinese Embassy in Maputo, Cheng Changhe, the Centre will cover an area of 30 hectares, but it has not yet been decided how much money will be invested in the project.
"After the centre has been installed, the Chinese agricultural technicians will come to Mozambique to start developing hybrid varieties of rice, soya, cassava and other products", said Cheng.
As for the Chinese pledge to build Mozambique a new national sports stadium, preparations are moving ahead, and construction work is expected to begin in the first half of 2008.
Further projects that might be supported by China include the expansion of Maputo airport, and the creation of a high technology park. The Chinese Exim Bank is studying these projects before taking any decision on offering preferential loans.
Trade between Mozambique and China is steadily growing. In 2006, it reached 208 million US dollars, 26 per cent more than in 2005.
In the health and education areas, China has offered to set up a malaria treatment centre in Mavalane Hospital in Maputo, and to supply anti-malarial drugs to the value of 250 million dollars.

Original_Search_Title: Muhwezi Trial - Beyond the Prosecution
Headline: Uganda: Muhwezi Trial - Beyond the Prosecution
Body_Text:
The trial is like the field court martial of 1981--86 where the comrades compare contribution in a particular battle, ask why an individual is being punished for running away with some cassava tuber he uprooted yet the other who took a village girl and some chicken has been let free
When the Inspector General of Government (IGG), Justice Faith Mwondha, walked into the chief magistrates court on Buganda Road Monday last week, she was sure she had plugged the holes in her first major trial.
Having quietly brought on board senior prosecutor Michael Wamasebu, IGG Mwondha did not expect the walk-out of the lawyers for the suspects she is trying, former health minister Jim Muhwezi, his former junior colleagues Alex Kamugisha and Mike Mukula, and former State House youth-desk officer Alice Kaboyo.
The four are being jointly charged for various offences including abuse of office, causing financial loss, embezzlement, theft, obtaining money by false pretence, uttering false documents and other charges relating to the mismanagement of an immunisation fund worth $4m. Mwondha also did not anticipate that they would soon be running to the High Court to challenge the decision of the lower court's initial rulings on day one of the trial. But what does this mean for Ms Mwondha and the office she holds?
A former investigator in the IGG's office says the Muhwezi case is "a test case for the IGG." Mr Reagan Okumu, who has since joined active politics and is now a senior member of the opposition Forum for Democratic Change (FDC), believes Mwondha has put her office and personal credibility on the wire in this case. "The IGG is playing Museveni's game of whipping the people growing horns within the NRM," says Okumu.
His perception is shared by many but most importantly, the speed and commitment of the IGG to see it through is telling. She has for the moment ignored another file where all the three ministers are implicated relating to the Global Fund for HIV/Aids Malaria and Tuberculosis partly because, sources claim "it is a more complicated case."
Public expects conviction
"The public expects conviction and recovery; anything short of that will significantly undermine public confidence in the office of the IGG. If Mwondha succeeds in getting a conviction or a recovery then she will gain some credibility," Okumu said. Okumu, who is now MP for Aswa, worked in the IGG office before Mwondha came in.
Another official who worked at a senior level within the IGG's office during its formative years agrees with Okumu. The official, who preferred to remain anonymous for professional and ethical reasons, says he believes the conclusion of the case will be crucial as to whether Mwondha can retain whatever credibility she has. The public view of the IGG's office depends on the character of the person holding it, this official asserts.
The IGG office has walked a long road so far. Founded in 1986 shortly after the NRM came to power, Mr Augustine Ruzindana, was appointed first IGG via a radio announcement. Sources close to the appointing authority tell Inside Politics that there was never a formal appointment and public service worked backwards to formalise it at least two months after the initial public announcement. Ruzindana was deputised by Mr Waswa Lule, who was at the time still in exile in London and did not report to office until three years later. But even after he returned, he served only for a short time and resigned. Mr David Psomgen replaced him.
In 1996, Mr Ruzindana sought elective office and was elected MP for Ruhama County in Ntungamo District; he had resigned the office but the President was for a while reluctant to appoint a new office holder, which put Ruzindana in a dilemma to leave without handing over to anyone.
Later Mr Jotham Tumwesigye was appointed and Psomgen stayed as his deputy. Tumwesigye served two terms and quit in 2003. Again the President took his time before appointing a replacement until he finally announced the appointment of current office holder, Justice Mwondha, who had been Chief Magistrate in eastern Uganda.
Mr Ruzindana was for most of his 10 years in-charge pre-occupied with setting up the office, giving it traction both in the law and its public image. A source familiar with Mr Ruzindana's time as IGG tells Inside Politics that he worked more like the conscience of the young NRM government at a time when it was still filled with idealism, a hate for the way those before them had conducted the affairs of the country, a desire to do things differently and in disdain for many of the African leaders of the time.
He was therefore conscious not to wash all the dirty linen in public, preferring to avoid bare-knuckle battles. Ruzindana's time was also defined by a government still keen on building institutions and still frowned upon corruption which allowed him lee-way to formulate it out of the woodwork into an institution of international repute. Those who crossed Ruzindana's path testify that if a complaint was raised against you, he would summon you for a discussion and ask that you sort the matter out without much of a fuss. And they were willing to listen.
Tumwesigye largely carried on from where Ruzindana stopped but sought to assert the authority of that office a little more. He caused ripples when he allowed the media to publish declarations of leaders' assets and liabilities, opening a new chapter in the demand for leaders to explain their known and unknown wealth as the general public often raised debate over some leaders' known properties that were never made part of the official record.
He also cracked the whip especially among the lower rank civil servants, with reports that caused many district accounting officers to lose their jobs. But it was also during Tumwesigye's time that some of the people in government had realised that soft-talk never actually got them to lose anything and corruption had generally become part of normal life and leaders competed to have more knowing the repercussions were nil. The system was also still knit together.
Ms Mwondha came in at a different time demanding different approaches to deal with existing challenges. Her time coincided with the campaign to remove term limits and the visible discomfort of some the bush war heroes.
Unlike her predecessors, Ms Mwondha finds herself fighting more than just corruption, and the opposition she encounters is beyond guilty or not guilty but some sort of a vengeance by those who feel by going after the only space comrades in the struggle is also being invaded now.
Evoking the bush war every time the whip comes hard on those alleged to be corrupt is partly to say "why can't you keep the power and let us make some money" a doctrine that seemed to thrive over the years. Like Okumu says, action on sources of internal disharmony must be dealt with in a manner that will appeal to two distinct audiences; the internal audience of the ruling NRM and the external particularly the donor community.
Unfortunately for Mwondha, and what will make her case against the Muhwezi's a test-case and a game of ping-pong at the same time, is that those she is seeking to prosecute know they can use the political goals President Museveni is seeking to score to divert attention from the criminal aspects.
Justice Mwondha's person does not help matters either. Known to have wrecked the internal structures since the day she set foot on the sixth floor of IPS building; widened the rift with other players as well as alienating the media, her style of work makes her a character many in her firing line see as susceptible to being blown by the wind should the tide change and the political front is shifted.
Not spared of criticism
Like a former official at the IGG's office said, the holder of the office shapes its character and Mwondha will not be spared criticism as being hostile in her approach. She will not avoid accusation of being vindictive, especially given how she has pursued the likes of Ken Lukyamuzi and the Solicitor General (SG) Lucien Tibaruha and his immediate successor.
Does she attract admiration or disdain? The 'big fish' she has managed to drag out of office, through jail and now to court, will continue to try and show they are victims of an individual's desire to have his cake and eat it at the same time.
What Mwondha must be conscious of is that for many strugglists of the early 80s, the current trial is like the field court martial of 1981--86 where the comrades compare contribution in a particular battle, ask why an individual is being punished for running away with some cassava tuber he uprooted yet the other who took a village girl and some chicken has been let free. Unfortunately, the camaraderie that some people share obscures the bigger issues that concern the majority of Ugandans.

Original_Search_Title: Border Opens for Country Maize
Headline: Namibia: Border Opens for Country Maize
Body_Text:
Windhoek — The closed border period between Namibia and Zambia opened last month with Namibia's largest miller, Namib Mills importing thousands of tonnes of maize from that country.
It is the first time that Namibia is importing maize from Zambia in huge quantities following Zambia's bumper harvest last season.
From May to September each year, Namibia suspends imports of crop produce such as maize to enable local millers to purchase and use local produce. The period opens in October.
Frans Meyer, Namib Mills Operations Manager, said, about 800 tonnes of maize were imported from Zambia. The company intends to bring in between 3000 and 4000 tonnes from one of Zambia's smallest towns - Kalomo in the Southern Province.
Namib Mills usually imports about 60 percent of white maize from South Africa while 80 percent of wheat comes from Europe and the United States of America.
Established in 1982, Namib Mills has been an integral part of revolutionising the local food industry.
Before Namib Mills, Namibia would import all maize meal, wheat flour, pasta and animal feed in trainloads from South Africa.
Given Namibia's semi-arid state, farmers are challenged to devise new methods of farming that will allow them to intensify crop production.
"Our country has limited resources to produce food. The challenge which future generations of farmers have to face is to intensify crop production, to become precision farmers and be highly innovative," said founder of Namib Mills, Pieter Van Niekerk.
The most notable Namib Mills brands are Top Score and Pasta Polana.
Ricky Lilami from the Board of Directors at the Namibian Agronomic Board in Caprivi told New Era yesterday that the borders especially Wenela are open and about 2000 tonnes of maize has been brought into the country from Sesheke.
In light of the poor harvest during the last farming season and the drought sweeping through Namibia, the country's millers face a deficit hence the imports.
Although Lilami could not state the margin of the deficit, he said, "There is a deficit. Production cannot run from local produce. Our production is very low compared to the consumption."
He added that for the past three weeks, trucks loaded with bags of maize have crossed the borders, mainly off-loading at Otavi.
The importation of maize from Zambia comes after the Zambian High Commissioner to Namibia, Griffin Nyirongo, offered Namibia the opportunity to import maize from Zambia. The High Commissioner, during Zambia's 43rd independence anniversary last month, announced that the Namibian Government had accepted the offer.
Nyirongo assured a continued consolidation of economic relations between the two countries.
With the anticipated creation of the Southern African Development community Free Trade Area 2008, trade between the two countries especially in the area of agricultural produce is likely to grow.
Last year, Namibia's exports to Zambia totalled U.S.$6.86 million while Zambia's exports to Namibia were valued at U.S.$9.58 million.
"Zambia is poised to regain her position as a major copper producer and processor. Zambia is equally poised to become the grain basket of the Southern African region", Nyirongo said.
Apart from maize, potential Zambian products that can be imported by Namibia include refined sugar, chitenge material, canned vegetables, fruit and other cereals, seeds, cassava, molasses, vegetables, cotton, maheu drinks and UTH milk.
Early this year, Minister of Agriculture, Water and Forestry, Dr Nickey Iyambo, and his Zambian counterpart, Ben Kapita, met in Katima Mulilo to discuss ways in which the two countries could boost food exports and address food insecurities.
The consultations were based on the Namibia/Zambia agriculture venture proposed by Zambian President, Levy Mwanawasa and Founding President, Sam Nujoma, five years ago.
In 2002, the two leaders agreed on bi-lateral economic co-operation and that ministers of agriculture establish a joint technical committee that would investigate possible joint ventures in food production.
Mwanawasa proposed that the project be established on 10000 hectares in the Kaoma district but the Namibian Government wanted the project moved to the Sesheke-Senanga area near Namibia's military bases close to the border. Nujoma said locating the project near the Namibian border would ease the movement of heavy equipment from Namibia to the project site in Zambia.
The Zambian government then identified 5000 hectares in Sesheke across the Zambezi River.
The area will be used for the cultivation of maize, wheat, groundnuts, sunflower and tropical fruit such as mangoes and guavas.
Namibia and Zambia will have a 50-50 share in the project.

Original_Search_Title: Agriculture, Pivot for Devt
Headline: Nigeria: Agriculture, Pivot for Devt
Body_Text:
Lagos — The latest World Development Report calls for greater investment in agriculture in developing countries and warns that the sector must be placed at the center of the development agenda if the goals of halving extreme poverty and hunger by 2015 are to be realised.
Titled 'Agriculture for Development', the report says the agricultural and rural sectors have suffered from neglect and under-investment over the past 20 years. While 75 percent of the world's poor live in rural areas, a mere 4 percent of official development assistance goes to agriculture in developing countries.
In Sub-Saharan Africa, a region heavily reliant on agriculture for overall growth, public spending for farming is also only 4 percent of total government spending and the sector is still taxed at relatively high levels.
The World Bank Group is advocating a new 'agriculture for development' agenda. According to the WDR, for the poorest people, GDP growth originating in agriculture is about four times more effective in reducing poverty than GDP growth originating outside the sector.
"A dynamic 'agriculture for development' agenda can benefit the estimated 900 million rural people in the developing world who live on less than $1 a day, most of whom are engaged in agriculture," said Robert B. Zoellick, World Bank Group President.
To him, "we need to give agriculture more prominence across the board. At the global level, countries must deliver on vital reforms such as cutting distorting subsidies and opening markets, while civil society groups, especially farmer organisations, need more say in setting the agricultural agenda".
According to the report, agriculture can offer pathways out of poverty if efforts are made to increase productivity in the staple foods sector; connect smallholders to rapidly expanding high-value horticulture, poultry, aqua-culture, as well as dairy markets; and generate jobs in the rural non-farm economy.
"Agricultural growth has been highly successful in reducing rural poverty in East Asia over the past 15 years," said Francois Bourguignon, World Bank Chief Economist and Senior Vice President, Development Economics.
Bourguignon said the challenge is to sustain and expand agriculture's unique poverty-reducing power, especially in Sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia where the number of rural poor people is still rising and will continue to exceed the number of urban poor for at least another 30 years.
For its part, the Bank intends to continue increasing its support for agriculture and rural development, following a decline in lending in the 1980s and 1990s. Commitments in FY07 reached $3.1 billion, marking an increase for the fourth straight year.
Report of Findings
The report also warns global food supplies are under pressure from expanding demand for food, feed, and biofuels; the rising price of energy; and increasing land and water scarcity; as well as the effects of climate change. This in turn is contributing to uncertainty about future food prices.
Agriculture consumes 85 per cent of the world's utilised water and the sector contributes to deforestation, land degradation, and pollution. The report recommends measures to achieve more sustainable production systems and outlines incentives to protect the environment.
The report says in agriculture-based countries (home to 417 million rural people, 170 million of whom live on less than $1 a day) the agricultural sector is essential to overall growth, poverty reduction, and food security. Most of these countries are in Sub-Saharan Africa, where the sector employs 65 percent of the labor force and generates 32 percent of GDP growth.
