For Sub-Saharan Africa's development, the report highlights issues to be urgently confronted: too little public spending on agriculture; donor support for emergency food aid with insufficient attention to income-raising investments; rich-country trade barriers and subsidies for key commodities such as cotton and oilseeds; and the under-recognised potential of millions of women who play a dominant role in farming.
In transforming countries such as China, India, and Morocco, agriculture contributes on average only 7 percent to GDP growth, but lagging rural incomes are a major source of political tensions.
Dynamism in the rural and agricultural sectors is needed to narrow the rural-urban income gap and reduce rural poverty for 600 million poor while avoiding falling into subsidy and protection traps that will stymie growth and tax poor consumers, the report said.
In urbanised countries, mainly in Latin America and the Caribbean and Eastern Europe and Central Asia, agriculture contributes just 5 percent of GDP growth on average. However, rural areas are still home to 45 percent of the poor, and agribusiness and food services account for as much as one third of GDP.
The report revealed that the broad goal is to link smallholders to modern food markets and provide remunerative jobs in rural areas.
It also says rich countries need to reform policies which harm the poor. For example, it is vital that the United States reduces cotton subsidies which depress prices for African smallholders. In the emerging area of biofuels, the problem is both restrictive tariffs and heavy subsidies in rich countries, which drive up food prices and limit export opportunities for efficient developing country producers.
The report also asserts that industrialised countries that were the major contributors to global warming urgently need to do more to help poor farmers adapt their production systems to climate change.
Productivity Growth in S'Saharan Africa
The World Development Report calls for greater investment in agriculture in Africa and says the need for action is especially urgent in Sub- Saharan Africa, where agricultural productivity growth has lagged behind other regions. Agriculture in Sub-Saharan Africa employs 65 percent of the labor force and generates 32 percent of GDP growth.
"In Sub-Saharan Africa, home to 229 million extremely poor rural people, agriculture is about much more than simple food security. A greater focus on agriculture will help boost overall economic growth and can offer multiple pathways out of poverty," said Zoellick.
According to the report, the share of official development assistance going to agriculture in developing countries is a mere 4 percent. This is also the same insufficient share that governments in Sub-Saharan Africa spend from their budgets on agriculture, far short of the 11-14 percent share of national budgets invested in agriculture that fueled the Asian green revolutions.
The report calls for an 'agriculture for development' agenda for Africa that will improve the investment climate as well as make optimal use of markets, technology, sustainable water and soil management, and institutional services.
In addition, member countries were enjoined to deliver on issues such as a level playing field for trade, while farmer organisations and other local groups were encouraged to have more say in setting relevant policies.
According to the WDR, for the poorest people, GDP growth originating in agriculture is about four times more effective in reducing poverty than GDP growth originating outside the sector.
It cites the example of smallholders successfully producing crops for export markets, such as coffee, cotton and green beans. With rapid population growth and urbanisation, it says demand for local food staples and livestock products is growing fast, offering expanding market opportunities.
"African agricultural growth increased from 2.3 percent in the 1980s, to 3.3 percent on average in the 1990s, to 3.8 percent annually from 2000 to 2005. Rural poverty rates have started to decline in 10 of the 13 countries for which data were analysed. Further accelerating and sustaining this growth is needed if key development goals are to be met," said Bourguignon, World Bank Senior Vice President, Development Economics.
The report says demand for food in Sub-Saharan Africa is expected to double by 2015 from its level in 2000. But food markets in the region already do not serve millions of people adequately, especially those in remote areas with poor infrastructure. More needs to be done, it canvassed.
The World Bank restated its commitment to increasing its support for agriculture and rural development. Commitments averaged $2.9 billion per annum during FY05-07, equivalent to 12 percent of overall Bank commitments during that period. In FY07 total commitments reached $3.1 billion, of which $580.5 million were directed to Sub-Saharan Africa.
While agriculture in Africa holds promise, the report says current challenges are daunting and require new roles for the state, the private sector, and civil society; a new mix of centralised and decentralised services to serve rural people.
It also seeks improved coordination among such entities as the Consultative Group on International Agricultural Research (CGIAR), the Food and Agriculture Organisation (FAO), the World Bank, a wide range of development partners and non-governmental organisations, regional organisations and national governments.
The agriculture for development agenda also requires rich countries to amend policies that harm the poor. For example, it is vital that the rich countries reduce subsidies that hinder African cotton exports. And the report says rich countries that have been the major contributors to global warming urgently need to do more to help poor farmers to adapt to climate change.
"Africa's own agricultural revolution must cater to very diverse rain-fed farming systems and simultaneously improve technologies, institutions, and markets," said Alain de Janvry, Co-Team leader of the report and professor of agricultural economics at UC Berkeley.
He cited irrigation in Nigeria (small scale) and Mali (large scale), multiple uses for cassava in West Africa, cotton in Zambia, and horticulture and dairy in Kenya as good examples of local successes which can be scaled up.
"Today, environmentally friendly technologies like conservation tillage, integrated pest management, and new varieties such as 'Nerica' rice (known as New Rice for Africa) hold promise. Science and technology are a linchpin to Africa's future productivity growth" said Derek Byerlee, Co-Team leader of the report.
According to the authors, beyond agriculture, greater geographic mobility and improved skills are central to reducing rural poverty in Africa. Because of HIV/AIDS and malaria, better health care and education must be an integral part of a broader set of safety nets that protect the assets of poor people.
Useful Lessons
The World Development Report has outlined in detail what needs to be done to kick-start rapid development through increased investment in agriculture in Nigeria and Africa.
Useful lessons can be garnered from the report, which holds the potential of generating huge foreign revenue and resultant growth for Nigeria and other African countries. Not to talk of the leverage which will help steer the current trade negotiations in favour of the regions in Africa.
The main challenge here is whether the concerned countries have the political will to invest in and subsidise agriculture massively like some developed economies; and to pump in huge accessible funds to both the mechanised farmers and small-holders. A word for the wise leaders.

Original_Search_Title: Country, China to Boost Trade Ties
Headline: Nigeria: Country, China to Boost Trade Ties
Body_Text:
The Nigerian Export Promotion Council (NEPC) has said that the provision of accurate trade statistics between Nigeria and China will enable the Council focus more on specific products for export to China.
The acting executive director and chief executive officer, Mr. Aliyu M. Lawal stated this in Abuja during a courtesy visit by the Economic and Commercial Counselor of the Chinese Embassy, Mr. Huang Jie.
A statement from NEPC indicated that, the objective of the visit by the Chinese envoy is to fashion out ways to increase imports from Nigeria by match-making Chinese businessmen and companies with their Nigerian counterparts.
According to Lawal, the Council would further provide a list of genuine and performing exporters to the Chinese Economic Counselor for onward transmission to interested Chinese importers of Nigerian products. Towards this end, he said NEPC will ensure that all areas of cooperation between the Council and China will be harmonized particularly as it relates to trade policies for the mutual benefits of both countries.
Mr. Lawal commended the Chinese government for training some NEPC staff on capacity building programme aimed at enhancing staff efficiency in promoting the nation's non-oil exports. He however urged the Counselor to assist Nigerian exporters by removing the 10 per cent tariff on Sesame Seed export to China in order to make the Nigerian product more competitive in the Chinese market.
In his remarks, Mr. Jie said that there are several products from Nigeria which they hope to work on with the cooperation of the Council for export to China.
The products, he said are cassava, cocoa, solid minerals, sesame seed among others. The envoy said the objective of his visit is to fashion out ways to increase imports from Nigeria by match-making Chinese businessmen and companies with their Nigerian counterparts.
In another development, Mr. Aliyu M. Lawal has said that the NEPC's will participate at the 2008 Sudanese International Trade Fair. He gave the assurance; recently, during a courtesy visit by the Sudanese envoy to Nigeria, Ambassador Awad Mursi Taha in Abuja, Jalla noted that Sudan is one of the biggest markets for Nigeria products. He also explained why the Council stopped attending the fair since 2003 principally due to some logistical problemes.
Mr. Lawal who also said that Nigeria has signed a Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) with their Sudanese counterpart at one of the Trade Mission facilitated said that the MoU centered on the establishment of the Export Trade Warehouse in Sudan.
In his remarks, Ambassador Taha commended Nigeria for her positive role in maintaining peace in Sudan in spite of the tragic killing of Nigerian soldiers who were on peace keeping mission to the war torn country.
According to him, "Nigeria has played positive role towards peace talks for two years which materialized in the signing of the peace agreements between warring factions.
He however solicited Nigeria's participation at the 25th edition of the fair adding that Nigeria being the most populous black nation in Africa has a huge potential in terms of trade and investments. A member of the Board of NEPC, Alhaji Mohammed Abdulsalam who was also in attendance implored the Ambassador to provide the Council with a list of exportable products to Sudan and business outfits that are willing to market Nigerian products.

Original_Search_Title: World Bank Says Govt on Track on Agriculture
Headline: Rwanda: Government On Track in Agriculture Transformation - World Bank
Body_Text:
Kigali — African governments are spending too little on agriculture and to some extent wastefully - a trend that needs to change if the sector is to be an engine to growth. However, Rwanda is way ahead in prioritizing expenditures in the same sector that other countries need to learn from, a World Bank official said on Tuesday.
Mr. Michael Morris - who was presenting findings of the 2008 World Development Report, told officials from government and donor agencies that despite agriculture contributing significantly to revenue, Africa is spending just under 4% on it. Mr. Michael was part of the team of researchers for the report.
Donor allocation for agriculture development has also moved from 12% in 1990 to less than 4% of total recent aid injections. In a situation where you have more than 75% of people depending on agriculture for livelihood, these figure do not sound very attractive, Michael said.
"GDP growth from agriculture benefits the income of the poor 2-4 times more than GDP growth from non-agriculture sectors", he said but added that there is not a standard unit of how much governments need to put into the sector because countries differ in dynamics.
The World Bank report released last month in Washington, is the first on agriculture since 1982, and comes at a time when critics also say donor agencies including the World Bank have neglected agriculture in Africa. Over the past 15 years, financial support for the sector has plummeted but was renewed upwards only last year.
Figures from the World Bank Rwanda office indicate that Agriculture and rural development covers 20% of the total aid program for Rwanda under a 5-year plan ending next year. Infrastructure development and capacity building both consume 15.3% each. Governance on its part - the lowest - has covered 5.6%.
The report calls for increased public investment in scientific research, rural roads, irrigation, credit, fertilizer and seeds. Africa, the report suggest has to focus on the basics of an agricultural economy - that is crucial to helping poor farmers grow more sorghum, corn, millet, cassava and rice on their small plots.
Rwanda leads
Agriculture in Rwanda contributes some 36% to total production (GDP) and employs 80% of the workforce, according to latest government figures.
The report also recognizes efforts in some countries to push up investment into agriculture but says governments need to 'invest smartly'. Mr. Michael singles out Zambia that put a lot of resources in agriculture in 2004/05 but with a 37% allocation to fertilizers with just about 6% on infrastructure and irrigation.
Investing that much on fertilizers may be good for the short-term but cannot be sustainable, Michael said, adding: it should not just be how much we spend but the quality of the spending is what matters.
"Looking at the various government programs targeting rural development and agriculture, it is fair to say that Rwanda is a leader in Africa on the level and type of spending into agriculture", Michael noted.
The World Bank agricultural economist said government is on track with developing a commendable immediate-term expenditure framework and long-term investment strategy for agriculture. I think Rwanda - in that sense - is a good example of best practice of what the World Development Report is saying, he said.
The Secretary General in the Ministry of Agriculture Ms. Agnes Kalibata concurs that investment in agriculture is lagging even in Rwanda but that programs are underway to reverse the trend. She told RNA on the sidelines that government is looking at intensification to move production to another level with quality at centre-stage.
"We do not have so much land and are looking at investing in it productively.making sure every inch is used productively.we are also thinking about environmental issues because agricultural production cannot ignore the ecosystem as well" Kalibata said.
In the 2008 budget plan, government put agriculture among the four critical sectors along with communication and infrastructure, education and health. Increasing agricultural output was allocated about Frw26 billion ($48m) compared to Frw10 billion in the previous years.
Availing fertilizers and selected seeds for agriculture as well as improved breeds of livestock was given Frw3.7 billion ($6.5m). The war on crops and livestock diseases was allocated Frw1.7 billion. Rainwater tapping infrastructure for agricultural purposes was given Frw1.5 billion.
Hillside irrigation was earmarked some Frw1.6 billion - probably showing important government attaches to increased production with the view that the country has a hilly terrain. Terracing was given Frw2.1 billion.
The World Bank report also calls for countries to focus on priority crops. Rwanda is targeting rice, maize, sorghum, cassava and potatoes got Frw2.8 billion in 2008.
High value produce
The livestock restocking program by giving each needy household a cow (One cow-One Family) was allocated Frw1.4 billion. Under this, planners believe families will diversify incomes from milk sells as well as free compost manure from dung.
Marketing agricultural products both in the domestic and foreign markets is to receive Frw900 million. This is targeting produce such as coffee, tea and horticulture that bring in much needed forex but require low intensity.
The World Bank report says Africa needs to put effort into high-value, labor-intensive products for external, regional and domestic markets because they offer strength growth opportunities. African countries have already started going more into horticulture and meat.
In 2003, the African Union summit promised to more than double the very low public spending on agriculture. In 2005, the UN Millennium Project started advocating for major investments to increase the productivity of poor farmers in Africa.
But the World Bank economist Mr. Michael Morris says to meet the development goals that world leaders set - to halve poverty by 2015, governments and donors must invest more and better in sustainable agriculture.

Original_Search_Title: Abuja Farmers Decry Late Govt Assistance
Headline: Nigeria: Abuja Farmers Decry Late Govt Assistance
Body_Text:
Farmers in Abuja have decried the manner in which government attend to their farming needs late.
The farmers spoke to the News Agency of Nigeria (NAN) under the umbrella of the Federal Capital Agro-Input Dealers Association (FECAIDA).
They said in separate interviews that they usually received agricultural inputs like seedlings, fertilisers and pesticides from the area councils late into the planting season.
According to them, late delivery of the inputs is of no benefit to the farmers.
Some of the farmers said they had expanded the scope of their business to become agro-input dealers as the income realized from their farms was not commensurate with their financial input.
Mr Abdullahi Mohamm-ed, a farmer and agro-input dealer in the Gwagwalada area, said he had produced 5,000 heaps of yams and one hectare of maize.
He said he had sold some of the yams and left the maize for domestic consumption.
"So l decided to farm mainly to feed my family. There is no assistance from the federal government and when it provides subsidised inputs, the inputs do not get to farmers on time."
According to him, accessing agro- inputs during the farming season is a major challenge to farmers.
He added that: "what farmers need from the government are improved variety of seedlings and fertilizers at the right time, agro-chemicals, tractors and good road network to get to the farmlands."
Another FECAIDA member, Mr John Duwoyi, said farmers could not access inputs like seedlings and pesticides through government at subsidised prices.
Duwoyi, who is an official of the Abuja Municipal Area Council (AMAC), said extension workers were not provided with adequate facilities to address the needs of farmers.
He said in his official capacity, he had been able to liaise for inputs for the benefit of FECAIDA members.
According to him, his farm at Kabusa village produces one tonne of yam yearly and about 10 tonnes of cassava.
Duwoyi expressed regret that in spite of the federal government promise to make farmers have access to cassava market locally and abroad, it had not fulfilled that promise.
He also said that the government also failed to purchase excess produce as it had promised.
This, he said, made many cassava farmers to incur losses due to the lack of storage facilities.
"FCDA assistance comes very late. For instance, government was to give farmers animal traction loan of N250,000 in place of tractors in March 2007.
"The loan eventually came between September and October 2007 when the farming season was already over. Did we make use of it effectively this planting season?
"Even at that, instead of N250,000, each farmer got N100,000 and when you remove the N2,500 insurance money, it means that each farmer received less than N100,000 for animal traction loan in September instead of March the planting season," the farmer said.

Original_Search_Title: Malnutrition - Silent Child Killer in Gulu
Headline: Uganda: Malnutrition - Silent Child Killer in Gulu
Body_Text:
Gulu — THREE decades ago, malnutrition related conditions like kwashiorkor, marasmus, starvation in children and adults were minimal.
Foods like millet, yams, cassava, sweet potato, shear nut oil, vegetables such as 'Malakwang', peas, beans, meat, honey, simsim and groundnuts paste among others were in plenty.
And to parents in this part of the country providing a balanced diet and ensuring food security were taken seriously in every homestead. Families that did not have enough food to sustain themselves always survived on their neighbours' harvest.
But today, malnutrition is one of the leading causes of death among children in northern Uganda. It is silently killing children below five years in Gulu.
According to the World Health Organisation, malnutrition essentially means "bad nourishment". It concerns not enough as well as too much food, the wrong types of food, and the body's response to a wide range of infections that result in malabsorption of nutrients or the inability to use nutrients properly to maintain health.
Clinically, malnutrition is characterised by inadequate or excess intake of protein, energy, and micronutrients such as vitamins, and the frequent infections and disorders that result.
Staying in camps for internally displaced persons is hugely to blame for the situation in Acholiland. Access to farmlands has been almost impossible for over two decades.
Moving through the camps and satellite camps in Gulu, one notices children with stunted growth, falling grey hair, running noses and swelling of the stomach and other body parts, all symptoms and signs of lack of food among these children.
The medical superintendent of the Gulu Regional Referral Hospital, Dr. Tom Otim says malnutrition is the leading cause of death among children in Gulu. He, however says it's not being noticed because of not easily diagnosed like other illnesses like pneumonia or diarrhea.
"Malnutrition is the underlying cause of many deaths among children in Gulu, but lacks public recognition because it does not kill children directly, as does pneumonia or diarrhea," says Mr Otim.
He says so many children are dying of malnutrition and related illness because the child's immune systems are left weak to resist other infections. According to statistics from the nutrition unit of the children's wing in the Gulu referral hospital, in the past five years a total of 2,608 children in Gulu have been admitted with severe malnutrition at the unit's therapeutic feeding center.
But Dr Otim says about 10 per cent of the children who are admitted normally end up dying. Dr Otim attributes the high rate of death to ignorance of the mothers. "Mothers bring children to the hospital at a later stage when children are very weak and their immunity weak to resist secondary infection," says Dr Otim.
None-the-less, sanitation in IDP camps is also very poor exposing children to diarrhea and dysentery infections, indirectly leading to malnutrition, according to Dr Otim. He explains that most children who fall sick tend to lose appetite, thus making them weak and malnourished.
He, however, adds that they are addressing the problem in partnership with Unicef and the World Food Programme among others. He says they provide the unit with food rich in nutrients like milk, corn soy blend, vegetables and other relevant foods.
Ms Beatrice Atuku (20), a resident of Pabbo IDP camp almost lost her third child if it was not for the therapeutic centre nutrition ward in Gulu hospital.
"My baby fell sick with malaria and lost appetite for several days. The baby could not suckle or drink and lost a lot of weight," says Ms Atuku. She adds that the baby was admitted at Gulu hospital recovered.

Original_Search_Title: Ogun Broadens Farmers' Knowledge On Cash Crops
Headline: Nigeria: Ogun Broadens Farmers' Knowledge On Cash Crops
Body_Text:
As part of measures to give prominence to six cash crops including Cocoa , Cotton, Cassava, Rice, Oil Palm and Kolanut, the Ogun State government has put together a workshop to further broaden the knowledge of farmers about the crops.
Declaring open the workshop with the theme:" Sustaining the State Leadership in Long Staple Cotton Production in Nigeria,' the state commissioner for agriculture, Dr. Iziaq Kunle Salako, highlighted the importance of the training and its timeliness in helping to resuscitate the ailing textile industry.
Dr Salako noted that the training programme was crucial to the socio- economic growth and development of the agricultural sector, stating that the workshop will pass technical and practical knowledge to cotton farmers.
He affirmed the commitment of the Otunba Gbenga Daniel led -government to the boosting of the agriculture sub-sector through assistance to farmers, including distribution of motorcycles to agricultural extension workers and the granting of credit facilities to farmers by OSAMCA.
Dr Salako assured farmers that the state government will purchase any surplus cotton from farmers and pay immediately.
He gave an assurance that some of the problems being faced by them will be looked into, particularly bad road network.
In his submission, the permanent secretary in the Ministry, Rev. Aderemi Onajole, enjoined participants to tap from the knowledge of the guest lecturer, Professor Yahya Adeoti, of the University of Agriculture , Abeokuta on new improved techniques.

Original_Search_Title: Floods Recede But Life Still a Challenge
Headline: Uganda: Floods Recede But Life Still a Challenge
Body_Text:
Kampala — "DURING the three months of heavy rains and floods I was left in this hut. I would have been washed away by the floods but I am alive due to God's mercy," says the 88 year-old in Ateso. Susana Eyakolit could not commute to dry areas like the others in her village because she is not only old but also blind.
"She can't walk easily and at times we have to cross small swamps at high risk. So we have no option but to leave her behind and check on her in the mornings," says her brother Kupalyana Awupal, 77.
Awupal is one of many that have been commuting to higher, drier grounds to avoid sleeping in a flooded home. For James Peter Amuge aged 37, life with his three wives and 19 children has become so harsh that they look on in disbelief as he explains the situation.
"We have no food and can't get any. This place is inaccessible for relief distribution. We are surviving on God's mercy," he says outside his water-logged compound.
He says he has to set off at 4pm daily with his family of 24 to sleep at a school located six kilometers away. "At exactly 6am we start trekking back here to allow room for children to study. We leave all our beddings outside the classrooms," he explains.
The three months rain that hit the districts of Kumi, Amuria, Katakwi, Bukedea, Kaberamaido and Soroti as well as Lira, Pader, Gulu and Kitgum has left over 300,000 people homeless.
Hundreds of huts collapsed, the people's cassava, millet and groundnuts rotted in the water logged gardens. The displaced people have been taking refuge in nearby schools, hurches and health centers. Only those like Eyakolit stayed.
Abaim Solome, 73 a camp leader in Amuria says that most of the people who were staying in the low lying areas were affected. A total of 13 children were born in classrooms in Okocho village, Amuria district during the period of the floods.
"The health centers were hardly accessible.The roads were washed away and there was lack of transport means. These mothers had no choice but to give birth in these classrooms," said James Peter Amuge, chairperson Okocho village.
But now that the floods have subsided, local and international relief agencies like Actionaid have started helping the affected societies by distributing non-relief food items to the displaced persons.
"Relief items such as food are good but will not last for long. People have lost their harvest in the floods and the planting season has also passed.
We feel it is important to set them ready by giving non-relief food items such as seeds and agricultural implements to give them a soft-landing," says Actionaids' information officer, Rebecca Musoke.
Musoke, who led a team of officials and the media during the flood impact assessment tour in the districts of Bukedea, Kumi, Soroti, Amuria and Katakwi, adds that joint relief distribution is needed urgently.
Soroti district mayor, Stephen Ochola, says: "The food basket in Teso region started to get affected right from the planting season. The main issue now is lack of cassava, the staple food in the region. A home without cassava in Teso is like a home hit by famine."
"We have given out 1000 mosquito nets, 710 pieces of tarpaulin, 2,730 meters of plastic sheeting and assorted drugs worth sh8m," Harriet Gimbo Actionaids' Katakwi programme coordinator says.
The areas that have received support include Amuge camp in Amuria, Achowa, Amusia parish, Omodoi, Magoro and Usuk sub-counties in Katakwi.
While touring the flood affected districts last week Gimbo said Actionaid had committed sh120m as a long term food security recovery programme for the affected areas.
"The next harvest is expected around July because most people have lost out on the planting season and their fields are still affected by moisture," she said. Lack of access due to the damaged roads and washed bridges has made distribution of relief hard.
And as one walks through the water-logged gardens that are beginning to dry up, the issue of acute malnutrition is eminent. No crops, no harvest for the communities, no food.
But as the floods recede, relief distribution is starting to trickle in. The locals are now praying that rain stops in other parts of the country because water flows towards their area due to the flat terrain.
As humanitarian agencies like World Food Programme, Oxfam and Red Cross respond to the demands of the affected people, government has committed sh22b towards rehabilitation of the districts.

Original_Search_Title: New Outfit Pulls in Crowds Through Proper Play Choice
Headline: Kenya: New Outfit Pulls in Crowds Through Proper Play Choice
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Festival of Creative Arts seems to have settled well into their new home, the Alliance Francaise auditorium in Nairobi, if the full houses they have been pulling are any yardsticks.
But more importantly, the organisers have not lost the art of selecting good comedies such as Robin Hawdon's Birthday Suite, which opened Friday and ends Saturday.
The farce has all the potential cocktail for a humorous melee. Put four impulsive people with very different reasons for being in two hotel rooms with an adjoining door that should be locked.
Add to it an excitable waiter and you have a recipe for explosive humour as the couples attempt to untangle themselves from their meshed relationships, tugging the waiter along with them.
But for the Festival, it will just be just another weekend since setting up shop two years ago. And the group has gradually made a name for itself as a formidable amateur theatre outfit about town since opening in 2005 with the comedy by Ray Galton and John Antrobus - When Did You Last See Your Trousers? directed by Sam Otieno and staged at the Kenya National Theatre (KNT).
"The Festival of Creative Arts was formed as a response to demands for quality play by local theatre-goers. We therefore identified plays that the public yearned for, and engaged experienced actors and qualified directors to sustain quality productions by investing in all aspects of production," says Eliud Abuto, the chairman.
To get a foothold in the precarious world of performing arts, Festival depended on the success of previous hit dramas like When Did You Last See Your Trousers?
"We began by adding professionalism to previously staged but popular plays as a way of pulling in crowds before staging plays by good, new playwrights," adds Abuto, who doubles up as the group's executive producer.
He has produced and adapted comedies and thrillers such as Eric Chapel's It Can Damage Your Health, a medical comedy directed by Sam Otieno at KNT in February last year, Jimmy Makotsi's She Ate the Female Cassava directed by Victor Ber at KNT and Giles Cole's Suspects, a thriller directed by Carole Odongo and Derek Benfield.
Another is A Fly in the Ointment that Arabron Osanya directed and staged at the Alliance Francaise where the group moved after two shows at KNT.
Since then, all their other plays including, Ray Cooney's Bedside Manners, Marc Carmoletti's Boeing Boeing and Derek Benfield's Look who's Talking, have been staged at the Alliance, also the home of Heartstrings Kenya, a trail-blazer in amateur theatre for 11 years, and still counting.
"Now we're staging new playwrights," says Abuto, referring to Dave Freeman's Kindly, Keep It Covered that the group staged in September, and Birthday Suite, their fifth production this year.
To keep the audience flocking for more, Festival casts a star-studded assembly from the pool of the best thespians and directors.
Examples are, in Birthday Suite, the indelible talents of Angel Waruinge, Nick Ndeda, Derrick Amunga, Mbeki Mwalimu (she first made her name in Where Did You Last See Your Trousers? and is the group's treasurer) and Francis Amos in the five hander farce.
"We hold quarterly auditions to pick actors that we may use over time. And those who have worked with us before stand a better chance, depending on the type of play," explains Abuto on cast selection.
Like most of their plays, its directed by veteran thespian Carole Odongo whose involvement in theatre dates back to 1995 in plays such as Meja Mwangi's The Plague, staged at the defunct Miujiza Players theatre, Nairobi.
"Directors are selected depending on the type of play and experience. We have set high standards and choosing the artistic director is carefully done. Currently though, Carole Odongo is the best artistic director in Kenya. She's very creative and is an excellent drama trainer," says Abuto, explaining why Carole has been their choice director in the bulk of the plays.
And if you enjoy watching plays, remember that they cost quite some money to produce. And although each play is unique and requires different types of stage sets, costumes, numbers of actors and props, on average, a quality show consumes up to Sh300,000, including publicity and other overheads.
Festival has, however, benefited from private companies who have recognised the quality of shows and the potential of theatre as a marketing tool. "Previous theatre groups took patrons for granted," says Abuto. "And companies wouldn't sponsor plays due to dismal turnouts, but currently, the patronage has increased, courtesy of professionalism as well as staging interesting, quality plays that theatre-goers want."
Kindly, Keep It Covered by Dave Freeman, was sponsored by IPAL who met the production cost, while the Nation Media Group took care of part of the advertising budget. "Festival was formed as a response to audience demands for quality and consistency," says Abuto; "and we've had a successful year. We see ourselves putting up plays every month from next year."
After Heartstrings and Unicorn Arts Productions, Festival is the third amateur theatre outfit offering quality shows and thus creating the much needed competition for good scripts, top talent and consistent audience.

Original_Search_Title: Foodstuff Prices Increase
Headline: Rwanda: Foodstuff Prices Increase
Body_Text:
Kigali — Consumers in Kigali City are digging deep into their pockets to afford meals as prices of foodstuffs have increased, some by 40 per cent.
A mini survey curried out in some markets and shops indicates that peas now cost Frw1,400 from Frw 1200. While groundnuts have hiked to Frw700 from Frw500, this is the highest price city consumers are paying for foodstuffs.
But retailers warn-the prices may hike more during in Christmas festivity. A kilo of meat now costs Frw1,200 increased.
Alice Mutoni, a vendor at Kimironko market says she was forced to increase prices partly because her suppliers had increased the prices too.
"Our suppliers have increased their prices on every commodity. So we had to do the same if we are to remain in business", she said.
The survey shows that tomatoes, fish, Irish potatoes, cassava, green bananas, sorghum, beans, rice had also registered upward prices.
Jean Gahene, a vendor at Biryogo market in Nyarugenge says high transport costs from farms to Kigali have partly contributed to the price hikes.
"We buy our foodstuffs from upcountry. Import some from Uganda. So you can imagine the transport we incur to deliver these foodstuffs to Kigali.
Bosco Uwimana, a supplier of peas, beans, Irish potatoes and other assorted food stuffs in Nyabugogo market however blames price increase to climatic changes.
He said parts in the countryside that produce these foodstuffs were hit by heavy rains that destroyed crops, thus resulting to low production.
He identified some of these areas as Gitarama, Byumba and some parts of Kibungo.

Original_Search_Title: NGO Aims to Produce 18 Million Tonnes of Rice for Continent
Headline: Africa: GTZDO Sets to Produce 18 Million Tonnes of Rice for Continent
Body_Text:
Global Trade Zone Development Organisation, (GTZDO), a non governmental organization under its Global Food production initiative has decided to venture into agriculture, cultivates rice and expects to get about 18million tons of rice for Nigeria and African Market. Mr. Chris Timehin, a Mechanical and Chemical Engineer with local and oversea training and the vice President of the organization spoke to Vanguard on how they intend to dismantle food shortage in Nigeria and Africa.
Excerpts.
This organization Global Trade Zone Development Organization was founded by British and Nigeria scientific and economic firms to paddle with the government of the 3rd world and the advance countries who are committed to actualizing the millennium development goal of the united Nations. and its resolution to eradicate universal hunger and starvation. We intend to achieve this through agriculture.
You know productivity has three stages - primary, which is the agriculture produce; the secondary productivity sed goods while the tertiary has to do with the utilization of the secondary or processed goods. Your sugarcane for example, when you cultivate it, you processed it into semi product utilization and final utilizzation.
At the primary level, the GTZDO we set the stage for cultivation and at the secondary level, we also go to involve in the mechanism where the primary level product will be utilized. At the tertiary level, which may not be immediate because of the complexities, we also consider these area as viable. If we have to cultivate sugarcane, we also know that this will be converted to sugar and in the process of sugar, we should know we're going to solid meneral where we use lime which is one of the major material for sugar production. So GTZDO in general amplifies production and creates jobs and sets out the scenario for production at industrial level.
Partners of GTZDO
We work with Federal Ministry of Tade and Industry to se that they provide enabling law that will give our pfoject the needed support. They agreed and asked us to meet the states to make land available for our project, we will bring the funds and technology while the United Nations Food and Agricultura Organisation will provide the needed support when it takes off.
Land Area & Acquisition
First we are targeting three states. Two in the north and one in the south west. for a start and we are asking the state government to provide the at least 300,000 hectares of land. The area we are going are areas where rice is already being cultivated. So in each area, 6million tons will be cultivated. Each area would not have more than two locations because of facility utilization and to reduce cost of production.
We have chosen Bauchi and Kaduna states and arrangment are in top gear to meet the governors of these states for talks. They have already indicated interest and another state in the south west has shown tremendous support for the project.
Other Support
We do not have any specific industry for now but we know that we have them at the fence with their funds in their pockets waiting to invest it as soon as the projects takes-off. The British government will come in the area of technical support in terms of equipment.
Technology is not easily given out as you can see what it takes for our president to sign an agreement with German government. But when your government now commit that country in exchange of certain things, only on that basis you can be sure of technical support. As for the UN, they may come in where we need additional support, they will consider it under the World Food Programme Unit much as they want to see that it meets the millenium development goal of 2015. So the UN support is moreorless a back-up support.
When it will take off
Once we get the needed land area, the project will take off within three month. What will then follw is the seedling. We intend to use the 'Derica type-2' which is the best African rice seedling which can yield 20tons per hectares and with 300,000 hactares of cultivated land, we would get 6million tons. And since we are using three states, it means we would ge 18million tons of rice for the African market.
Nigeria cannot consume that much, some can be put in reserve while some will earn some foreign exchange. We chose rice because Nigeria and Africa in general consume rice a lot and it is one of the products Nigeria spends foreign exchange on. The bye products are also many, the shaft can be used for petroleum product because it contains oil. Coutries like Brazil and Japan use it.
We can also use it for soap making and other things. The starch content is even better than the cassava starch which is more of acidic content. The paddy residue extracted during milling could also be processed to obtain rice flour, vegetable oil, furfural and fertilizer. Besides, it can also help the CBN on its liquidity problem. So if we have 18millioon tons of rice, no matter how we eat, we cannot consume all and the other ones will for exports and if we sell at N2000 per bag, compared to the present price of N5,000 - N7,000 per bag, we will be making as much as N2.7bn and when you sell it for about $20 per bag, you save a lot of foreign exchange.
And remember when you processed the rice, the paddy residue (breakables) to produce industrial starch which you can also export. So, with this, African countries, even Europe when they consider it cheaper, with good standard guaranteed, they will come to buy and we will earn a lot of foreign exchange. So this initiative will benefit the whole of Africa in terms of food supply.

Original_Search_Title: Cost of Living Shoots Up
Headline: Uganda: Cost of Living Shoots Up
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE cost of living rose faster in October than at any time since the beginning of the year and is likely to stay up following increases in prices of staple foods, which constitute the largest percentage of consumer expenditure.
An official of the Uganda Bureau of Statistics (Ubos) told journalists at a press briefing on Tuesday that when the cost of living goes up, people are forced to adjust their budgets and in the end they spend more than they were doing previously to cope up with increasing prices of goods and services.
The current high cost of living, which is already squeezing family budgets and raising the burden of double spending on the same item, is also being exacerbated by high utility costs Ugandans are incurring in domestic and industrial use.
CPI figures
According to the Consumer Price Index for October released by Ubos, Ugandans spent more in October than they did in September because of the increased prices of staple foods and reduced supply from rural to urban centres.
According to Ubos, the trend has led to food prices going up by 3 per cent.
"The cost of living is an important thing for people in this country because it is the difference between what they earn and what they can spend on the rest of their family," said Mr Matthew Ssewanyana, the director of Macroeconomics at Ubos.
However, he pointed out that a rise in cost of living does not mean that there is a fall in standard of living because cost of living may rise while the standard of living may remain at the same level.
Inflation up
Mr Ssewanyana said the higher price levels for staples, meat and poultry, rent, fuel and utilities pushed up the inflation rate during the month by 0.4 per cent.
Food prices went up in most centres with significant contributions coming from matoke, Irish potatoes, cassava, fresh fruits and vegetables especially Passion fruits, pineapples cabbages, tomatoes, onions and dried beans.
Mr Ssewanyana said increases in prices of food items were due to reductions in supplies to the markets resulting mainly from the coming to an end of the main harvesting season.
The CPI figures show that the annual headline inflation rate for the year ending October went up to 5.0 per cent - almost at the same level of what was registered for the year ended September.
On the other hand the annual core inflation rate, which excludes food crop items, fuel, electricity and metered water from the CPI basket, also went up from 5.8 per cent to 6.4 per cent for the same period.
During October increases in the prices of paraffin, charcoal and petrol were recorded in most centres countrywide, indicating that life has generally become harder for Ugandans.

Original_Search_Title: Catholic Church Organizes Forum for the Salvation of Northern Economy
Headline: Ghana: Catholic Church Organizes Forum for the Salvation of Northern Economy
Body_Text:
The Catholic Church of Ghana Ecclesiastic Province Pastoral Conference (TEPPCON) has organized a forum for the three northern regions aimed at mapping out a strategy to bridge the gap between the north and south.
The Dean of student University for development studies Professor Saa Dittoh in his address said it is a fallacy that Northern Ghana contributes marginally to Ghana's economic development.
"It is a fallacy because a chunk of the Ghana's resources are created by northern Ghanaians in and outside northern Ghana. It is no secret that Ghana's cocoa farms, cassava farms, maize farms, oil palm plantations, rubber plantation and others are largely maintained by people of northern extraction. It is also a fact that the mines survive on the labour of northern Ghanaians", he pointed out.
Prof Dittoh argued that though the area is bogged by various problems , salvation is in the hands of people from the north.. "It is unfortunate that the average northern Ghanaian believes in the ability of everybody except the northern Ghanaian. We are too much preoccupied with our egos and the result is the destruction of our common goals and aspirations", he pointed out.
In his view the educated few in the north tend to blind and divide the people rather than enlightening and uniting the people.
The Most Rev Gregory E.Kpiebyya on his part said the church will continue to do its best to improve the socio-economic conditions of the people and urged privileged northerners to help the less privileged.
The Vice President, Alhaji Aliu Mahama who was the guest speaker said the area has the potential for poverty reduction, especially in the agricultural sector, where the north has competitive advantage in land and sunshine.
According to him Good conditions exist for production of crops like mangoes, cashew, Sheanut, groundnuts, Soya beans, cotton, rice, millet, sorghum and yam, okra, guinea and corn on a large scale to improve standards of living.
Alhaji Mahama added that, what is needed to modernize agriculture is the right approach involving credit, appropriate technical assistance and marketing.
Alhaji Mahama also stressed the need to eliminate all forms of discrimination based on gender, tribe, religion or political opinion. "We need to unite as a people to enable us confront these development challenges. All manner of litigations and chieftaincy disputes divert our energies from our main enemy which is poverty", he advised.

Original_Search_Title: Inflation Rate Increases to 5.5 Percent
Headline: Uganda: Inflation Rate Increases to 5.5 Percent
Body_Text:
Kampala — RISING food prices pushed the October inflation rate to 5.5% from 5% in September, the Uganda Bureau of Statistics (UBOS) has said.
"Food prices went up in most centres with significant contributions coming from Matooke, Irish potatoes, cassava, fruits and vegetables like passion fruits, pineapples, cabbages, tomatoes and dried beans," the monthly consumer price index from UBOS said.
The increase in the food prices was attributed to reduction in supply as the main harvesting season wound up.
The annual core inflation, which excludes foods, fuel and metered water, rose to 6.4% from 5.8%.
The monthly headline inflation rate also increased by 1.2%, the same as what was recorded in September.
Prices of meat, chicken and milk went up as well in all centres countrywide.
Arua and Gulu had the highest headline inflation rate at 15.9% and 8.7% respectively.
Prices of paraffin, charcoal and petrol rose in most centres due to high oil prices on the world market. The price of a bag of charcoal is now sh25,000 in Kampala.
The oil price on the international market averaged $80 per barrel last week before hitting $90. It is expected to reach $100.
Matthew Ssewanyana, the director of macro-economic statistics, predicted price increases in the remaining two months due to the Commonwealth meeting and end-of-year shopping.

Original_Search_Title: Floods Leave Thousands On Verge of Starvation
Headline: Uganda: Floods Leave Thousands On Verge of Starvation
Body_Text:
In Katakwi, Soroti and Bukedea districts, several affected people say they have never received any food relief despite the fact that the heavy rains destroyed their crops
Before the floods hit his village, Amuge in Amuria District, Mzee Fasinu Okumur, 77 was a happy man. His family could afford two meals a day. But after his groundnuts, cassava and sorghum crops were destroyed by the floods, his family hardly gets what to eat.
"The groundnuts were washed away, the sorghum germinated and the cassava which was submerged for weeks is now rotten and we really don't know what to do," he says.
Okumur says life after the floods is so harsh that one cannot predict what they are going to have for lunch or supper. He says since the floods started, he has never received any food relief.
"One time we saw a plane coming to our village and we were excited that they had brought us food only to see the food being distributed in schools," he says.
They now depend on the few groundnuts that he had stored in a granary that survived the floods. His worry is that the supply will not last for a week. He exchanges the groundnuts with people willing to give him flour.
More food needed
He says four of his children who are at school survive on porridge provided by the school. In Katakwi, Soroti and Bukedea districts, several affected people also say they have never received any food relief despite the fact that the heavy rains destroyed their crops.
Those who have been able to get some food from relief agencies say they need more assistance because what they got is finished. Mr James Abarimo of Okocho village in Katakwi says he has got two wives and 10 children but he was given two kilogrammes of beans and five kilogrammes of maize flour but it could not last three days.
While meeting officials from ActionAid International Uganda in Soroti on October 17, some of the LCV Chairpersons in Teso also admitted that some of the affected have not yet received food.
They urged the government to expedite the distribution of food relief to flood victims. Soroti District Chairman, Mr Stephen Ochola said although President Museveni declared a state of emergency, the government has not done much to respond to the food needs of the affected people.
"About 80 percent of the food relief has been provided by Non Governmental Organisations and the United Nations World Food Programme. If it were not for the contribution of these good Samaritans, the situation would be worse," Ochola said.
About 300,000 people who have been displaced by the floods are in need of food. But the UN World Food Programme, the major supplier of food relief has said it is running out of food to feed these people.
Ochola says scarcity started in May when the floods first hit five sub-counties in Soroti destroying crops. The worst affected crops include cassava, groundnuts and millet. He says millet bread is the staple food for people in Teso and if cassava and millet are not there, it means there's food insecurity.

Original_Search_Title: Cassava to Fight Famine
Headline: Uganda: Cassava to Fight Famine
Body_Text:
Kampala — NAKAPIRIPIRIT district has embarked on multiplication and distribution of cassava stems to farmers to ensure food security.
The National Agricultural Advisory Services (NAADS) coordinator, Simon Okello, recently said 65 acres of cassava multiplication plots had been established in various sub-counties.
Lack of a reliable food crop, he said, had often left the people susceptible to famine.
He said growing cassava as a food security crop would enable them live on their own. The NAADS have also supported the growing of both paddy and upland rice and used the established farmer groups to promote the cultivation of groundnuts, sweet potatoes, maize, beans, egg plants, water melon, millet and the promotion of apiary, goat-rearing and vegetable-growing.

Original_Search_Title: Become a Millionaire Through Pig-Rearing
Headline: Uganda: Become a Millionaire Through Pig-Rearing
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE demand for pork is steadily growing, making pig-rearing a lucrative business in the country. Several pork joints have opened in the city and upcountry, leading to the rise in the price of the commodity. A kilogramme of pork now goes for between sh3,500 and 4,500, up from sh2,800 a few months ago.
According to dealers, the supply is low due to a variety of problems. The main problem is lack of a fully commercialised pig-rearing culture. According to statistics from the Ministry of Agriculture, there are about six million pigs countrywide.
However, only a very small percentage is kept on serious commercial basis. Most of the farmers in the villages keep one or two pigs, which they sell off whenever they have a problem. Unlike crops, pigs have no seasons and no geographical regions, meaning they can be kept all the year around anywhere across the country.
"They bring in a lot of profit because they grow up quickly and multiply faster," says Edward Luswata, a farmer who keeps at least five pigs.
For example, a pig gives birth to a litter of 8 to 12 piglets twice every year, while a goat gives birth to one or two kids once a year. At six months, a goat is still too young to be sold, yet the pig is old enough for sale.
Experts advise that one should start with a few pigs, and gradually increase the number as he gains experience. It is not very expensive to start. For example, if one starts with 10 piglets, the cost includes at least sh400,000 for the piglets and sh500,000 for the shelter.
The shelter should have a cemented floor of at least 8 by 8 feet. The walls may be constructed with blocks. If one is operating on a tight budget, timber can be used. Each of these shelters should hold not more than three piglets. There should be a section for feeding and another for resting.
There are various breeders countrywide. On Mityana Road, for example, Muva Farm produces quality pig breeds. On Masaka Road, there is Equator Farm at Nkozi in Mpigi district and St Jude Farm in Masaka.
There are also National Agricultural Advisory Services (NAADS) breeders in every sub-county countrywide.
"I started with three pigs on a trial basis," says Ssemazzi Zake, a farmer who owns a five-acre farm at Komamboga in Kawempe Division.
Today, Ssemazzi earns handsomely from what used to be a trial farm. On the same farm, Ssemazzi grows bananas, cassava and vanilla, but his emphasis is on pigs.
Information about pig-rearing can be obtained from experienced farmers or from the Ministry of Agriculture.
"I trained at Kabanyolo Farm and the training showed me that pig-rearing is a profitable venture," Ssemazzi says.
At first, Ssemazzi was rearing pigs for pork, but with time, he realised that having high quality pigs for breeding purposes was more profitable.
"I realised that if you have a breeding stock, you earn good money," he says.
An organised piggery will give quality animals. Semazzi's farm is well-organised. It has two huge brick shades roofed with iron sheets. Each of the pigs has its own cubicle. Because pigs need both water for wallowing in and sunshine for sunbathing, the cubicles have reservations for both.
Ssemazzi dismisses common fears that pigs are dirty and that they breed jiggers.
"Pigs are as clean as their owner wants them to be. If the floor is concrete and well-maintained, the pigs will, of course, be clean," he says. Ssemazzi says the cubicles at his farm are cleaned regularly. A modest breeder, who keeps at least 30 sows at any one tim,e can get 420 piglets per year.
Each piglet costs between sh30,000 and 40,000. On average, such a farmers will earn over sh13m every year.
If one is rearing for pork, the pigs are kept for at least five months and then sold off. At this time, if well-fed, each weighs between 40 and 50kg. A pig that weighs 40kg goes for sh90,000 to sh100,000.
A goat at the same age is not ready for sale. In fact, a one-year-old local breed goat fetches half what a six-month-old pig fetches.
"To maintain high quality, one has to give pigs the right diet," Ssemazzi says. Pigs, just like human beings, need proteins and lots of clean water. They can feed on raw potato vines or cassava leaves. "The good thing is that pigs can eat anything, from peelings to real food.
However, pigs are vulnerable to a number of diseases. To avoid infection, it is advisable never to share farm equipment with another farm or you risk bringing swine fever to your stock," Ssemazzi says.
To avoid diseases, caution should be taken. No one enters the piggery without disinfecting his feet at the door. It is also important to de-worm regularly.

Original_Search_Title: 35kg Water Melon Excites Show Goers
Headline: Uganda: 35kg Water Melon Excites Show Goers
Body_Text:
Kampala — A water melon weighing about 35kg was one of the items exhibited on Saturday at Amudat Trading Centre during the World Food Day celebrations for Nakapiripirit district. Lokoona Keebu, a farmers group supported by the National Agricultural Advisory Services (NAADS) in Kamaturu village, Lorengedwat sub-county, said they picked just one fruit for the exhibition.
"We have even bigger ones. If it was not for the weight, we would have brought bigger ones," the chairperson, Andrew Lokee said.
He said three energetic men lifted the fruit from the garden to a waiting car.
The commanding officer of the UPDF 3rd division, Col. Patrick Kankiriho, paid sh30,000 for the water melon.
A UPDF soldier had to first do a warm-up before he could pick up the fruit, which he placed onto his chest and walked with difficulty to the vehicle that transport it to Mbale.
"I will to show this water melon to the president so that he can see the potential in Karamoja," Kankiriho said.
The exhibition attracted 31 farmers' groups who exhibited a variety of crops grown in the different parts of the district.
The crops included onions, beans, maize, cassava, egg-plants, upland rice, paddy rice, cabbages and potatoes.
Farmers who were facilitated to start livestock rearing also exhibited improved goats and chicken.
The onions exhibited by the Nakapiripirit Town Council LC3 chairman, Festo Tebanyang, also attracted the attention of the showgoers who scrambled for them.
Tebanyang said the exhibition was an indication that the people of Karamoja were able to feed themselves and also sell food to other parts of the country.
"How long shall Karamoja continue to be fed by the World Food Programme(WFP) when you also have a green belt that can be used to cultivate food?" Kankiriho asked. He said 10 such fruits harvested every three months would generate enough money to buy a cow.
"If you embarked on the cultivation of water melons alone, you would raise enough money to buy more cows instead of risking your lives in cattle raids," he said. He appealed to the Karimojong to utilise the green belt to engage in agriculture in order to improve their livelihood.
He said areas around Mt Kadam, Lokales in Kariita sub-county, Kamusalaba on Nakapiripirit-Moroto road, Lokirimo near Nakapiripirit Town Council and
Napak on the Moroto-Nakapiripirit-Katakwi border were ideal for crop production.
He, however, observed that insecurity by armed warriors had made it difficult for the community to settle in those areas.
"I have my own strategy to bring peace to Nakapiripirit. If we plan better, I am ready to offer UPDF trucks to help the farmers transport their produce to markets in Mbale and Soroti," Kankiriho said.
The district agriculture officer, Mario Tengei, said Nakapiripirit was endowed with fertile soils ideal for crop production.
"We need to move away from the traditional hand hoe to animal traction to increase the area under cultivation and from subsistence to commercial agriculture," he said. He called for the growing of high value crops that could be sold as finished products.

Original_Search_Title: IFAD Invests $200m On Rural Poverty Schemes
Headline: Nigeria: IFAD Invests $200m On Rural Poverty Schemes
Body_Text:
Abuja — International Fund for Agricultural Development has invested about $200million in projects and programmes aimed at alleviating rural poverty, since Nigeria started using IFAD loan and grant facilities in 1985.
The Fund has also approved over $70 million for two new programmes, namely the Rural Finance Institution-Building Programme (RUFIN) and the Rural Micro-Enterprise Programme (RUMEDP).
Vice President of the Fund, Dr Kanayo Nwanze, disclosed this in Abuja, while briefing newsmen on activities of his organisation.
Nwanze, who was in Nigeria to meet with key officials of the Federal Government, as well as representatives of United Nations agencies, said his visit to Nigeria was to share views on how to strengthen collaboration among the UN Family in Nigeria, in the framework of the UN Reforms.He expressed delight at the excellent cooperation that exists between IFAD and Nigeria, and explained that the mandate of IFAD remained to tackle rural poverty in all its ramifications.
According to him, IFAD's mandate and activities have been designed to tackle poverty which afflicts more than 60 per cent of the rural population in the country through wealth creation, because without creating conditions for the rural poor to generate wealth out of their daily activities, it would be difficult to lift them out of poverty.
IFAD programmes currently being implemented in some states in Nigeria include the Community-Based Agricultural and Rural Development Programme (CBARDP), the Community-Based Natural Resource Management Programme in Niger Delta (CNRMP) and the Roots and Tuber Expansion Programme (RTEP).
Previously, IFAD has financed and completed four other projects namely, the Cassava Multiplication Programme, the Artisanal Fisheries Development Project and two area-based Community Demand-driven Rural Development projects in Katsina and Sokoto states.

Original_Search_Title: Swedes Eye Energy Sector
Headline: Rwanda: Swedes Eye Energy Sector
Body_Text:
Kigali — ABB, a Swedish firm and international leader in power and automation technologies is scouting Rwanda for investment opportunities in the energy sector.
Officials from the ABB Group of companies that operates in about 100 countries and employs 100,000 people were in Kigali last week together with a group of Swedish investors seeking opportunities in the energy sector.
During a trade meeting organized by the ministry of commerce and the Rwanda Investment and Export Promotion Agency (RIEPA), the area sales manager, southern Africa substations Mr. Stefan Kullander told East African Business Week that ABB is in position to help fill the gap in the energy deficit.
We are presently looking at the possibility of interconnecting countries so that the energy deficits in one country can be complemented by the surplus of another. We are looking at connecting Zambia, Tanzania and Kenya. Very soon I will be meeting the energy minister in Tanzania." said Kullander. The use of ethanol as a substitute for fossil fuel was discussed and one of the Swedish businessmen said his company can produce stoves and cooking utensils that run on ethanol.
Most African countries have the potential of producing ethanol not just from sugar cane waste but cassava by products and other waste.
According to RIEPA's director general, Mr. Francis Gatare, Lake Kivu has the potential of producing 700MW and its exploitation will benefit Rwanda and EAC members
The chairman of the private sector federation Mr. Robert Bayigamba who is a also a leading business man in the country called for the Swedes to consider investment in different sectors.
Rwanda, a country of 8.5million people seeks to turn itself into a middle income country by 2020.

Original_Search_Title: UN Agriculture Fund Announces New Project in Gabon
Headline: Gabon: UN Agriculture Fund Announces New Project in Gabon
Body_Text:
New York — The United Nations International Fund for Agricultural Development (IFAD) today announced the launch of a new project aimed at helping farmers in Gabon diversify their incomes by developing and marketing new products from staple crops.
The agreement for the $5.7 million loan and $300,000 grant was signed in Rome today and will receive co-financing from the OPEC Fund for International Development, IFAD said. "The Gabon economy has been heavily dependent on the oil and timber sectors for some time," said Abdoul Barry, IFAD's country programme manager for Gabon. "This project will offer concrete opportunities to do that by helping small farmers and their organizations get better access to value chains for products with significant market potential," said Mr. Barry of the effort to help market products from crops like banana, cassava and peanut.
The project aims to directly benefit 28,000 farmers, half of whom are women and a third young people.

Original_Search_Title: Assembly Outlines Programme to Discourage Youth
Headline: Ghana: Assembly Outlines Programme to Discourage Youth
Body_Text:
Accra — To curtail the growing of marijuana, the Tain District Assembly in the Brong-Ahafo Region is providing alternative livelihood for the youth in the district.
The Assembly is offering free tractor ploughing services during the farming season and has drawn up a scheme to supply inputs on credit to the youth for the cultivation of cash crops. The executive committee of the Assembly outlined the new initiatives at its second session meeting at Nsawkaw.
Mr. Emmanuel Simon Kwadwo Owusu, District Chief Executive, expressed concern about the notoriety that the district had gained in "wee" cultivation and stressed the need to redeem its image.
Mr. Owusu urged Assembly Members to educate the people about the adverse effects of narcotics on society and advised them to rather cultivate maize, millet, yam, cassava, groundnuts and vegetables.
The District Chief Executive decried the protracted chieftaincy disputes among five paramountcies in the area and called on the regional House of Chiefs, as well as the Minister for Chieftaincy Affairs, to ensure their speedy resolution.
He commended the Assembly Members for their co-operation and active participation in the decision-making process and not getting involved in chieftaincy disputes.
He urged security agencies in the area and officers at the Bui dam projects site to execute their duties with professionalism to maintain peace and order in the communities.
On the district mutual health insurance scheme, Mr. Owusu said the people had shown an increasing interest in the programme and were registering in their numbers.
He said about 26,699 persons had registered with the scheme, representing an increase of 42 percent over last year's enlistment.

Original_Search_Title: Rakai's Aids Affected Get Seeds
Headline: Uganda: Rakai's Aids Affected Get Seeds
Body_Text:
Rakai — Ms Scovia Nnassali and Ms Clevencia Nakate were among over 150 residents who braved hot weather in Kibaale Village on October 22 for the distribution of seeds and cassava stumps.
It was a long wait and many residents sought shelter on the verandas of the few shop buildings. A mini-bus, loaded with maize, beans, and ground nuts seeds as well as cassava stumps was packed in front of the chairman's office.
The Youth Action International (YAI) Uganda team from Kampala was also waiting, comfortably seated by the office. Ms Nnassali and Ms Nakate sat, discussing Richard who recently turned 19 and acquired a wife. He became head of the family at 16.
Richard had gone off to an unknown place as they sat waiting and they feared he risked missing the seeds should the officials begin the distribution. Ms Nnassali, who is in her late 50s, takes care of three grand children, all orphans. Ms Nakate has five children of her own as well as several orphans.
Both women said due to the heavy rains in April and May, they had harvested little beans and maize. "Yet a long dry period followed and we ate even the little that we should have saved for the planting season," Ms Nnassali said.
The prolonged drought in the sub-county coupled with the big numbers of vulnerable families, some of them child headed, attracted the compassion of Yai, an American funded NGO.
The NGO made an assessment of the families and identified their immediate needs.
It was discovered that residents needed seeds but were too poor to buy them.
It was also discovered that there was a need for granaries and for education on food preservation and food security measures.
In due course the people also needed a saving and low interest loan scheme to start engaging in commercial agriculture. "We have similar programmes in Jinja, Kampala, Gulu, and in Karamoja Districts, among people living in extreme difficulties such as post war conditions and other calamities. The Aids epidemic in Rakai that has placed such a heavy burden on the many families is one such condition," said Ms Memory Bandera, the regional director of Yai in East Africa.
"We work in collaboration with the district leaders, with whom we have to sign a memorandum of understanding. After one year we intend to identify another sub-county or community in the district that might be in need of our help and we will expand."
Before the distribution of the seed, Yai was launched and a memorandum of understanding between the district and the NGO was signed.
Rakai District Vice Chairperson Resty Tusiime signed on behalf of the district.
Each family got an average of five kilogrammes of maize, beans or ground nuts as specified in the needs assessment report. "The seeds are valued at over Shs2 million," said Ms Memory Bandera.
Yai country representative Agnes Amooti Namiyingo warned the recipient families against consuming the seeds. "They are treated with a chemical that can be harmful when eaten," she said.
"The chemical is intended to protect the seeds from pests such as weevils. And please wash your hands after touching them especially after planting them." The beneficiaries were asked to give back a small portion of their harvest to Yai for the benefit of other communities.

Original_Search_Title: Abandoned Bridge Makes Businesses Suffer in Sapele
Headline: Nigeria: Abandoned Bridge Makes Businesses Suffer in Sapele
Body_Text:
Lagos — Is it not ludicrous that government would embark on a project to assuage the suffering of the people, only for the said predicament it set out to ameliorate be multiplied times over by acts of omission and commission of the same government?
This is exactly the question the people of Sapele in Delta State have been asking themselves since the state government, three years ago, 2004 to be precise, awarded a contract for the construction of a multi-culvert bridge on the main strategic link road in the town, only to increase their woes by abandoning the project.
Indeed, in 2004, when the Delta State Government awarded the contract for the construction of the multi-culvert bridge on the Amuogodo area of the Sapele-Warri road, a vital access road , linking the different parts of the ancient city, the people rolled out the drums in celebration, thinking that the end of their nightmares had come.
Sapele city
Sapele, also known as the electricity town lies along the Benin River just below the confluence of the Ethiope and Jameson rivers, 98 miles or 158 kilometers from the Escravos Bar and entrance to the Bight of Benin.
The town also lies on a road with a branch to Warri, Ughelli, Asaba and Benin-City. It was founded in the colonial period on land traditionally inhabited by the Urhobos, and has been a saw-milling centre since 1925. Its plywood and veneer manufacturing plants is one of the largest in the West-Africa sub-region.
It is also known for its rubber plantations and its industries became more diversified in the 1960s with factories for making shoes. It also has large market centres for cassava, fish, palm oil and kernels, yams and plantains.
A port town in the eyes of some people, it is one of the oldest cities in the country that has lost its glory. Development started many years ago in Sapele before some new cities in the country but due to neglect, the city is crawling.
Nightmares
Saturday Vanguard was told that non-completion of the road is causing serious problem for security agents in Sapele, as the impassable road leads to the police station and other government establishments in the town.
For example, if there is an armed robbery operation at the Amuogodo area of Sapele, the Police could not easily access the place because of the abandoned culvert bridge and before they would snake-in, using other roads from their station, the armed robbers would have escaped.
Besides the security importance of the road, which has now been cut off, swift access to the African Timber and Plywood (AT&P), Sapele, Lonestar Drilling Company, Sapele Stadium, Sapele Hospital, all the courts in the town and other government and private establishments have been affected by the impassable Sapele-Warri road.
Because of the impassable state of the road, heavy-duty vehicles going to the AT&P for business now take Okpe and New Ogorode roads, causing not only long traffic jams, but extensive damage to the roads.
Worse hit is the Akintola Road, which now has many failed portions. It is the same story at the Apollo Hotel junction, Sapele, Market road to New Ogorode and several other roads in Sapele.
A source said the contract for the multi-culvert bridge was awarded to a former top official of the government who sub-contracted it to another contractor but neither the names of the former official nor the contractor he gave the job to is known.
"Except if the contractor was not mobilized by the government, I don't see why that project should be abandoned and the government keeps sealed lips over it. There must be something wrong," he added.
Who is the contractor?
But from obtainable indications, three years after, the contractor just disappeared from the site, and the impudence of it is that nobody seems to know who the contractor is and why the state government, which awarded the contract during the second tenure of the administration of the former governor, Chief James Ibori has not shown much interest regarding its completion.
Former military governor of defunct Mid-Western Region, Brigadier-General Samuel Ogbemudia (rtd.) whose government constructed the bridge in the first instance because of the importance of the road to the political, economic and social life of Sapele, many years ago, would surely palpitate at the neglect of central artery.
How it started
Investigation by Saturday Vanguard showed that the problem of the bridge became glaring when the surrounding area of the multi-culvert started caving in, ostensibly due to the flow of water in the channel, which is a natural water course, linking most parts of the town. Clearly, Sapele is battling with a civilized but poor shot by the government at controlling a normal water route.
The first job on the bridge was executed by Edok-Itah (Mandilas), which constructed the multi-ring culvert bridge to evacuate the water from the town into the river under the Ogbemudia administration.
However, the culvert failed over time and whenever it rained, the Amuogodo area of the road was usually flooded, as it was no longer discharging water into the river but back to the town.
The attendant flooding nevertheless cleared some hours after each downpour but caused rigorous transportation hitches.
Ibori sets up committee
Sapele people bawled and in 2004, a delegation, led by the former council chairman, now a member of the Delta State House of Assembly, Chief Monday Igbuya met with then governor, Chief James Ibori, who inaugurated a committee, headed by a top official in his administration to inspect the bridge and other affected areas, including Amukpe in Sapele, which also had drainage problems.
But because of lean funds, the government decided to tackle the multi-culvert bridge on Sapele-Warri road.
To the chagrin of the people, the job, which they thought would not exceed a year, which is 2005, at most, has lasted for three years with the job practically abandoned. In fact, the road, which was motorable before the contract was awarded, is now impassable.
What Uduaghan should do
A resident, Mr. John Okorotie, however, said, "This road is too sensitive for anybody to play politics with; it is the most important road in Sapele and for three years now, they have left it worse than they met it.
I think Governor Emmanuel Udughan should find out who the contract was awarded to, why it was abandoned and pay the contractor if it is government that is owing him so that the job can be completed."
It was observed that the contractor had commenced job at the site before it was abandoned but the reasons for the stalled position of the project was not immediately known.
Why lawmakers dismissed Igbuya's motion
The member representing Sapele Constituency in the Delta State House of Assembly, Chief Igbuya raised the issue of the abandoned culvert in a motion, not too long ago, on the floor of the House, but his colleagues, apparently unaware of the importance of the road to the entire Sapele, shot down the motion. Some described it as too personal and too selective of the mover of the motion, as there were other abandoned projects in the state, which were also crying for attention.
Leaders may storm Government House
