And that sort of experiences is invaluable and I've had to stop that and that's frustrating. But it's been very stimulating and great fun.
The former administration of Tony Blair, especially towards the end of his tenure, placed Africa as his priority but from the pronouncements of Gordon Brown, that momentum doesn't seem to be there. What do you think about that?
Quite the opposite. Gordon Brown was a member of the Commission for Africa, where Fola Adeola who is from here used to assist the majority of Africans. And then a mixture of Latin Americans and Chinese and three of our cabinet ministers - Prime Minister, Hillary Benn (who was our Development Minister) and Gordon Brown. And we found that he got into that work very energetically. He then went several trips (very focused, he doesn't do trips for trips' sake); he went to Mozambique and he came here in May last year (nearly 18 months ago) when he was still Finance Minister and they talked to a whole group of Education and Finance Ministers. Gordon Brown said to them as our Finance Minister - if you, (education and finance ministers), in six months' time, can come up with ten years plan of what you want the donors to do in education with the time-scale and the budget - we would finance it. And he gave them up till September (just about a year ago) when all the financial wizards were meeting in Singapore (the autumn meeting of the World Bank and IMF) and they did. And he went back there and that interest is still continuing, very strongly from where he is now. I think the fact that we've always had amongst the ministers of state someone who comes as African, this time that person's been put in cabinet - its never happened before. Lord Mallock Brown who used to be a UN official has now been put in the House of Lords and is Minister for Africa with a seat in cabinet. And that was Gordon Brown saying 'look, I am serious about Africa', so, I think we'll see strong focus from here.
So, how then do you see the controversy over the EU-Africa Meeting and his refusal to attend if Mugabe attends?
Oh am sure they'll have a very interesting meeting without him.
Without Gordon Brown?
Yes.
Because Mugabe is attending.
If he attends our Prime Minister won't. Cause we've made ourselves very clear on that. If you had agreed after a lot of sorting and deliberations that you'll have a sanctions policy which includes not giving visas to senior leadership- then you've got to implement it. You chose it, you mean it. The idea of sitting down with Dr. Mugabe in Portugal or anywhere else doesn't work.
Your Excellency, I am aware of some recent direct recruitment of Nigerians into the British army, is there a direct recruitment?
I am not aware. I don't think they're doing it through newspaper adverts or television spots as you claimed but we've got a very long tradition of Nigerians and other Commonwealth citizens recruitment into the British Army. Indeed a lot of Nigerian boxers who then went on to win medals for their country started when they were soldiers in the British Army in years gone by. We do not discriminate. We have a whole section of the British Army with people from Nepal for years and years even though now, there's just one brigade. I hope we're not depriving the Nigerian Army of people, but having Nigerians in the British Army is something we've always felt comfortable with. I mean, how many thousands and thousands of Nigerians served in the Second World War in forces far away as Burma.
My father did.
Your father did?
I was just wondering whether there is any reason behind the fact that the DFID has reduced its funding or part of its funding of projects at micro level in Nigeria and sub-Saharan Africa as a whole. Is there a deliberate policy or is just a problem of budgetary policy or economic reality? I find it a bit ironic that Nigerians tend to recognise the activities of the USAID more than it does DFID, don't you find that ironic?
We had a very interesting minister before Hillary Benn called Clare Short. She was our Minister for Aid and Development for five years. She was very clear that what we needed to do was use British money where it most effectively make the difference. And it didn't matter whether people knew it is British money or not. No Union Jack flags waving or such things, (she wasn't interested in all that) she was more interested in where the money could be used - say for funding UNICEF to have a programme across northern Nigeria, to encourage more girls to go into schools - that's what we do. We're spending $50 million on that (£25 million) funding UNICEF as part of the programmes going on in different LGAs in the north to encourage the girls to go to school. Which means - talking to the parents; talking to the traditional rulers; to the Imams and everyone else because we all know that the numbers of the girls who go to school are very small. Now that doesn't have a DFID label on it, if it works, that's what matters to us and without us, UNICEF wouldn't be able to do it.
British entry visa. From the general public, we still have complaints on the cost and other things.
On the cost question, we are bound by law. A law has to come and we won't change it, whereby our visa operation has to cover its cost. It mustn't make a profit; it mustn't make a loss around the world. So the visa fee has to pay for all the people involved in the UK and here. Now we have here about 90-visa officers brought from the UK to Nigeria on full time working in Lagos and Abuja. The additional cost now come in because we had to start collecting fingerprinting and so worldwide, we had to increase fees. The fees are no different here than in Australia or Brazil. We've thought hard about this and we've decided that we'll increase fees for people on two or five year visas and we would increase the visa fees scarcely at all for people on six months visas or student visas.
And in fact in Nigeria it didn't increase at all for the student visa and six month visas because at the same time that we changed the fees, the local agent we use - VFS - they abolished their fees that they used to add on, so the net effect is no increase at all. And we did this because we think that well to do, well-travelled people such as your esteemed publisher can afford to pay a lot more than the students. The six-month visa is what the young traveller gets and we've held that down because we would encourage people who want to come as students or first time travellers. And the people who will pay some of the cost of that would be the more established. I mean we'll all like to charge less fees but it wouldn't work because we'll be breaking our law.
Don't you see a decline in British influence in terms of investments and all that in Nigeria and an increase in Chinese and Indian investments?
Well you've clearly got an increase in Indian and Chinese investments. I hope that you need that investment and that people here can manage that in a way that it doesn't undermine or undercut other things you want to do. Clearly that's only possible because the Chinese and Indians will come and invest under your rules. In terms of the amount of British investments, it is actually going up. Shell brought in that big offshore rig; that cost $4 million all paid for by Shell.
That's directly the point. That investment has no direct linkage to the Nigerian and its economy beyond the oil economy.
But its about to start paying huge royalties.
Yeah, to the federal government.
To the federal government. And federal government, we hope would use that to build schools and good clinics - that's what taxes are for.
So when would it start paying?
Ask Shell that. Sooner than expected. And BG are coming, the old British Gas, they're gonna go into that LNG plant in One, Ondo State; and they're doing offshore things also. Am going down this afternoon to Equatorial Guinea because BG are buying all the Liquefied Natural Gas from Equatorial Guinea and a new plant is being inaugurated tomorrow in Malabo. And that's a big change that's taking BG in - we haven't had a big British industry coming to the oil and gas industry for many many years. And in other areas, there are some other areas of interest. ACTIS is a British development which puts a big money into the palms.
I thought it's a Commonwealth investment.
ACTIS is part of the old Commonwealth investment, its an offshoot and that was very successful and they are now putting money into things like the Ibom Power in Akwa Ibom near Eket, pretty sizeable investment there to boost the electricity in that part of the country on a commercial basis. So I find that very encouraging because that's a sign of people re-engaging in investment outside the oil and gas. The more adventurous companies are now prepared to be engaged outside the oil and gas industry.
That's why April is important. Because it was in April that you've completed a political cycle for the first time ever and having a handover from one more or less one elected government to another. Maybe slightly more or less elected civilian government. That has provided a predictability to policy and investors especially domestic investors. I can think of no country in the world where manufacturing industry is picked up by foreign investment.
It is kicked off by local investment and then the foreign investors' come in, quite unlike oil and gas which is kicked off by foreign investment. Your oil palm plantation won't give you much money for the first five, six years. Even sugar takes more serious investment, maintaining the land, the whole way that you have to re-arrange the fields and have those irrigation systems and canals. It is the sort of investment that you won't get back in your first or maybe second year.
I don't know. So you need to have that confidence that the business climate would be equally propitious in three, four or five years time, and that's what I hope the effect of April would be. Handing over from one elected government to another elected government at the end of that eight years makes people think - ah maybe I can start making some assumptions about what the business environment would be in about four or five or six years' time. Otherwise people go back to what they are used to doing in Nigeria which is trading with turnover and stocks.

Original_Search_Title: Country to Get a Biofuel Plant
Headline: Uganda: Country to Get a Biofuel Plant
Body_Text:
A local investor is planning to set up a plant which will use foodstuff like maize, cassava and sugarcane to manufacture oil and gel for lighting and cooking respectively.
The investor, who currently imports the biofuels (synthetic oil and gel) from their South African-based parent company Liquifier Pty Limited, hopes to set up the plant in east and western Uganda, by the end of June next year.
According to the General Manager of Liquifier Uganda limited, Mr Michael Musoke, the plan is to reduce carbon emissions in the atmosphere through reduced deforestation and consequent charcoal burning.
"Plans are underway to set up a local manufacturing (biofuel manufacturing) plant in Uganda. It will be the only Gel manufacturing plant in Uganda, the East and Central African region,"Musoke told Sunday Monitor.
"There is a lot of garden surplus in Uganda, which we shall use," Musoke adds, to justify where the feedstock (crops to be used to produce the fuels) will come. He says since their operations will depend mainly on farm produce "we shall also support the farming sector by offering them more seeds to plant, provide farm inputs and provide the necessary advisory services".
Liquifier Uganda limited became operational in Uganda two years ago but its products were only launched last month.
Today, their products with a brand name Liquifier have found their way in most super markets in Kampala.
Among their products are synthetic oil, which burns in specially designed lamps (liquilamp) made of durable, hard plastic, which does not get destroyed when used for lighting.
The Liquilamp, which takes half a litter of synthetic oil, goes for Shs26,000 giving 60 hours of burning or lighting. According to Musoke, the synthetic oil has been mixed with a chemical called citronella, which is a mosquito repellant.
"When you use the liquilamp, you can also be sure you are well protected from mosquitoes and malaria,"he says of the Liquifier product. Other products are gel, which comes with specially designed stoves, made of mild steel.
A litre of gel, which burns in the stove, goes for Shs3,600. A five-litre gel pack goes for 18,000 and according to Musoke, it burns for a period of three to four weeks for light cooking.
A double plate stove goes for Shs55,000 while a single plate stove goes for Shs 42,000.
The gel is packed in consumer friendly quantities ranging from one litre to 200 litre drums, which caters for big institutions like schools, hotels, restaurants, and hospitals.
For hotels that have long been using spirit for warming foodstuffs during the buffet method of serving, Musoke says the gel is a better option as it burns longer.
Musoke describes the products as smokeless, odourless, highly portable, leaves minimal residue after use and produces twice as much energy, compared to gas and paraffin.
"It's also none poisonous and can be used as anti-septic on the skin," he says.
"Research on our products found Liquifier is a better alternative compared to other products (such as gas and paraffin)," he adds.
Biofuels are gaining firm ground in Uganda with many companies and individuals opting to convert arable land to enable production of the feedstock used to manufacture fuel from the crops.
Last year, Sugar Corporation of Uganda Ltd (Scoul) requested the government to provide 7,100 hectares of land from within Mabira Central Forest Reserve to enable it expand it's sugar production from the current 50,000 tonnes to 100,000 tones per annum, in line with their plans to increase sugar production and produce power alcohol.
In her cabinet paper which appeared in the New Vison, the Minister of Water and Environment Ms Maria Mutagamba said if Scoul was given part of the forest reserve, it would produce power alcohol, which can be mixed with petrol to the extent of 10-15percent, to diversify energy production.
Mutagamba said Scoul would produce additional 10-12 MW of electricity which can be supplied to UGMA, cable and to the national grid. But Scoul is not the only company intending to produce biofuel.
In an earlier interview, the executive director of Oil Palm Uganda Limited Mr Kodey Rao, said if additional land was given to his company in Bugala Island, Kalangala District, more palms would be planted and also used to manufacture biofuel.
However, biofuel critics warn governments against adapting biofuels at face value, saying converting land for green fuel production will increase food shortage and soar food prices and, above all, threaten fragile ecosystems like wetlands and forests.
According to the website http://www.swissinfo.org, the United Nations Special Rapporteur on the Right to Food Jean Ziegler was reportedly demanding an international five-year ban on conversion of land for the production of biofuels,to combat soaring food prices.
According to the website, a study commissioned by the Swiss authorities in May, concluded that biofuels might not be the panacea for the world's fossil-fuel woes. Such fuels, touted as an ecologically friendly source of energy, might be more harmful for the environment than their fossil counterparts, it said.
According to the authors, while it was true that biofuels might emit less greenhouse gases than fossil fuels when consumed, producing them is generally more stressful on the environment.
Growing and processing crops for energy purposes or feedstock can have the heaviest environmental impact, as soil quality can be affected adversely, for example through fertiliser overuse, the swissinfo reported.

Original_Search_Title: World Bank Calls for Leap in SSA Agricultural Renewal
Headline: Nigeria: World Bank Calls for Leap in SSA Agricultural Renewal
Body_Text:
The World Bank has called for greater investment in agriculture in Africa and warns that the sector must be placed in the centre of the region's development agenda if the goals of halving extreme poverty and hunger by 2015, are to be realized.
Titled 'Agriculture for Development,' the report says the need for action is especially urgent in Sub- Saharan Africa, where agricultural productivity growth has lagged behind other regions. Agriculture in Sub-Saharan Africa employs 65 percent of the labor force and generates 32 percent of GDP growth.
"In Sub-Saharan Africa, home to 229 million extremely poor rural people, agriculture is about much more than simple food security," said Robert B. Zoellick, World Bank Group President. "A greater focus on agriculture will help boost overall economic growth and can offer multiple pathways out of poverty."
According to the report, the share of official development assistance going to agriculture in developing countries is a mere 4 percent. This is also the same insufficient share that governments in Sub-Saharan Africa spend from their budgets on agriculture-far short of the 11-14 percent share of national budgets invested in agriculture that fueled the Asian green revolutions.
The report calls for an 'agriculture for development' agenda for Africa that will improve the investment climate as well as make optimal use of markets, technology, sustainable water and soil management, and institutional services. In addition, countries must deliver on issues such as a level playing field for trade, while farmer organizations and other local groups need more say in setting policies.
According to the WDR, for the poorest people, GDP growth originating in agriculture is about four times more effective in reducing poverty than GDP growth originating outside the sector .
It cites the example of smallholders successfully producing crops for export markets, such as coffee, cotton and green beans. With rapid population growth and urbanization, it says demand for local food staples and livestock products is growing fast, offering expanding market opportunities.
"African agricultural growth increased from 2.3 percent in the 1980s, to 3.3 percent on average in the 1990s, to 3.8 percent annually from 2000 to 2005. Rural poverty rates have started to decline in 10 of the 13 countries for which data were analyzed," said Francois Bourguignon, World Bank Chief Economist and Senior Vice President, Development Economics. "Further accelerating and sustaining this growth is needed if key development goals are to be met."
The report says demand for food in Sub-Saharan Africa is expected to double by 2015 from its level in 2000. But food markets in the region already do not serve millions of people adequately, especially those in remote areas with poor infrastructure. More needs to be done.
For its part, the World Bank is committed to increasing its support for agriculture and rural development, following a decline in lending in the 1980s and 1990s. Commitments averaged $2.9 billion per annum during FY05-07, equivalent to 12 percent of overall Bank commitments during that period. In FY07 total commitments reached $3.1 billion, of which $580.5 million were directed to Sub-Saharan Africa.
While agriculture in Africa holds promise, the report says current challenges are daunting and require new roles for the state, the private sector, and civil society; a new mix of centralized and decentralized services to serve rural people; and improved coordination among such entities as the Consultative Group on International Agricultural Research (CGIAR), the Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO), the World Bank, a wide range of development partners and non-governmental organizations, regional organizations and national governments.
The agriculture for development agenda also requires rich countries to amend policies that harm the poor. For example, it is vital that the rich countries reduce subsidies that hinder African cotton exports. And the report says rich countries that have been the major contributors to global warming urgently need to do more to help poor farmers to adapt to climate change.
"Africa's own agricultural revolution must cater to very diverse rain-fed farming systems and simultaneously improve technologies, institutions, and markets," said Alain de Janvry, Co-Team leader of the report and professor of agricultural economics at UC Berkeley. He cited irrigation in Nigeria (small scale) and Mali (large scale), multiple uses for cassava in West Africa, cotton in Zambia, and horticulture and dairy in Kenya, as good examples of local successes which can be scaled up.
"Today, environmentally friendly technologies like conservation tillage, integrated pest management, and new varieties such as 'Nerica' rice (known as New Rice for Africa) hold promise. Science and technology are a lynchpin to Africa's future productivity growth" said Derek Byerlee, Co-Team leader of the report.
According to the authors, beyond agriculture, greater geographic mobility and improved skills are central to reducing rural poverty in Africa. Because of HIV/AIDS and malaria, better health care and education must be an integral part of a broader set of safety nets that protect the assets of poor people.

Original_Search_Title: This Year's Idd Was Like an Oasis
Headline: Kenya: This Year's Idd Was Like an Oasis
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Idd ul Fitr celebrations have provided the much needed respite from the packed political menu embellished by opinion polls and reeling from ODM Kenya presidential candidate Kalonzo Musyoka's "miracle" at Uhuru Park, Nairobi, last weekend.
The festivities broke to louder-than-normal calls by the Muadhin and as dawn broke on Saturday and as the bleating of goats being led to their slaughter filled the air in the Mombasa estates.
Huge pots of sumptuous biriani (a rice dish) served in huge platters and shared by groups, together with boiled goat meat and soup, was the order of the day as Muslims marked the breaking of the fast.
Idd is characterised by feting neighbours, irrespective of whether they are muslims or not. It is an occasion of one of the most remarkable outpourings of love I have ever witnessed.
This Idd was no different as my neighbours, the Alis, woke us up quite early in the morning with the Swahili sweet bread (mkate wa sinia) and home-baked doughnuts for our breakfast, and followed it up at lunch-time with biriani ya maini (rice with liver).
My eight-year-old son had his own version for the the joy: He spent the day at his friend Kipruto's birthday party next door alongside playmates.
His sister, on a half-term school holiday, was shooting the channels with remote controls in both hands, while their mother was busy with her own kitchen specials for the occasion.
On my part, I was catching up on my reading and nursing a king-sized one from a previous heavy night. Almost overnight, it was like a whistle had been blown to give the local residents some time off from the political hullabaloo to show some humanity.
Even at the Eid Baraza, which President Kibaki attended at Treasury Square, the Chief Kadhi was firm: No political campaigns - at least not overtly.
The only time we realised that the president was in town was when I and my "tribe" were diverted from State House Road as we drove to a popular family corner at Mama Ngina Drive as his motorcade left town. As we quaffed our madavu (coconut milk) and munched cassava crisps and roast maize, we were amazed at the serenity and hospitality of the day.
After days of rantings about political possibilities, Idd ul Fitr was like an oasis in the Kalahari.
My biggest surprise was when my new colleague, Mubarak, drove 15km to deliver to my family some sumptuous beef biriani cooked by his wife and mother.
Now the people are looking forward to the Diwali (a Hindu occasion) next week which, despite the extended fireworks, makes me realise how great and multicultural my immediate community is.
When my friends were fasting, I missed the "civilised" community who gather at the 100-year-old A.C. D'souza Bar at Kilindini, where nobody is interested in one's origin.
Kenyatta and Jamhuri Days as well as the voting day are on the way and I hope the people's Kenyanness will continue to flourish and show its superiority over the base sectarianism that threatens to consume us all.
Kenyans should not squander the goodwill that has made them what they are today.
On Wednesday, I met Mzee Pekeshe Ndeje, he of the "Similani!" chants at political rallies, and I gave him a lift to a rendezvous in a sudden rainstorm. Obviously enjoying his new-found popularity and accompanied by his two sons, he summed up the political scenario and how it should be approached:
"If you had eight children and five of them were complaining about the way you were treating them, it would be wise to listen to them since their disaffection might lead to the instability and failure of the whole homestead and even lead to death. Listen to all your children."

Original_Search_Title: Post-Harvest Losses
Headline: Uganda: Post-Harvest Losses
Body_Text:
Kampala — GRACE Ineza, 40, owns an acre of land in Kisoro district and is considered to be one of the most successful farmers in her village. But in the last season's sorghum harvest, she lost nearly half of what she reaped.
"I cut the stems, tied them up in bundles and left them to dry in the field. When I went back, most of it had been eaten by rats and birds.
In our culture, when you want your sorghum to turn from red to black, it is put in a sack and kept in a moist place. I took mine to the river bank. When I went back, it had all been washed away!"
Ineza's experience is typical of Ugandan farmers, who toil to produce food only to throw away a significant proportion.
About 5-15% of the cereals harvested either rot or are eaten by pests, according to statistics at the Kawanda Agricultural Research Institute. Post-harvest loss is even higher. For instance, 20-25% and over 35% of the cereals get spoilt after harvesting.
These losses are not limited to crops.
The Food and Agricultural Organisation estimates that in Uganda, approximately 27% of the milk produced is lost; 6% is wasted at the farm level, while 11% and 10% of production is either lost to spillage or spoilage during transport or marketing, respectively.
FAO estimates this loss to be $23m a year.
Experts blame these losses on poor storage and lack of proper technology for preserving food.
"Some farmers just uproot the crops, but never go back to check whether there is anything that has remained," says Arinaitwe Rwakajara Katambuka, an official with the Uganda National Farmers Federation.
"Someone just puts the food in a sack and stores it anywhere and it is affected by moisture, weevils and so on."
Farmers, however, blame the losses on natural calamities, pests, rodents, birds and theft.
In 2001, research scientists from Kawanda, Makerere University, and the Natural Resources Institute selected 120 banana farmers to talk about post-harvest losses. 81.5% of the farmers cited wind breakage as the main cause of loss, 36.1% cited theft and 35.2% ripening, while other losses were attributed to rodents.
Others blame poor transportation. "Whenever I am harvesting beans, I hire people to carry the harvest from the fields. The same happens when taking the produce to the market. Some times in the process, some seeds fall in mud and everything is wasted,"says Alex Womanya, a farmer in Mpigi.
"Sometimes matooke ripens before reaching the market."
Statistics show that about 25% of the rural feeder roads are impassable during the rainy season.
In rural Uganda, markets generally lack essential facilities like all-weather structures, secure storage, safe water and healthy sanitation structures.
These amenities also have significant impact on the food.
What to do

Original_Search_Title: Africa Cries in the Midst of Plenty - Bekoe
Headline: Ghana: Africa Cries in the Midst of Plenty - Bekoe
Body_Text:
Professor Daniel Adzei Bekoe, Chairman of the Council of State on Tuesday expressed regret that though Africa had enough land and human resources, adequate food supply continue to be the worst of the problems hindering socio-economic development.
He said Africa needed to make technologies available to grow improved, high yielding crops to meet the food security problems of the growing populations and ensure that Africa attained self-sufficiency in food production.
Speaking at the opening of a 10-day international training on "Advanced Plant Tissue Culture and Dihaploid Techniques" in Accra, Prof Bekoe said there was the need to train more scientists to equip them with modern techniques and skills in plant breeding to solve the food insecurity.
The course attended by 13 countries was aimed at equipping scientists to develop new, more efficient and cheaper large-scale production protocols on haploid production to improve plant-breeding programmes.
Organised by the Plant and Soil Science Department of the Biotechnology and Nuclear Agriculture Research Institute (BNARI) of the Ghana Atomic Energy Commission (GAEC) in collaboration with International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA), the training programme would also enhance plant breeding and agricultural improvement in the sub-region.
Prof. Bekoe noted that the use of dihaploids in plant breeding had shown to shorten the breeding cycle and that technology could be used to improve staple foods most of which were classified as "orphans" in terms of research.
He urged the participants to avail themselves to be equipped with the technologies and the needed skills to ensure food security on the continent.
Prof Dominic Fobih, Minister of Education, Science and Sports in a speech read for him by Nana Yaa Osei-Brimpong, Director in Charge of Tertiary Education of the Ministry commended BNARI for using biotechnological tool to disseminate crops of highly economic value to numerous farmers in the country.
He noted that the BNARI was producing carotene-rich sweet potato for vegetable producers and exporters association of Ghana using tissue culture whilst the Department of Plant and Soil Sciences was in the process of establishing a national Vitro Repository for all under utilized staple food crops as well as crops threatened with genetic extinction be conserved in Vitro gene banks to serve as duplicate for traditional conservation of food crops.
"It will provide readily plant materials for crop improvement programmes in the country", he added.
He said the production of most stable crops such as plantain, cassava, yam, cereals, and grain legumes were constrained by lack of improved planting materials, diseases and pest, declining production levels though population continued to increase.
He recommended the use of tissue culture to provide year-round disease free planting for farmers and that government would give maximum support to research institutions to equip them with these new technologies.
Dr Yaw Serfoh-Armah, Deputy Director General of GAEC reading the speech of the Director-General Prof Edward Akaho outlining the numerous achievements of BNARI said it had developed swollen shoot virus resistance in cocoa with Cocoa Research Institute and good cooking cassava mutant namely "TEK Bankye" with the Kwame Nkrumah University of Science and Technology. GNA

Original_Search_Title: The Long Road to Delivering Food Aid
Headline: Congo-Kinshasa: The Long Road to Delivering Food Aid
Body_Text:
Goma — The three World Food Programme (WFP) trucks laden with 34 tonnes of cornmeal, salt, and flour bound for 20,000 displaced civilians in Masisi, a town in eastern Democratic Republic of Congo's embattled North Kivu province, had just begun to snake up a steep mountain pass when the convoy ground to a halt - yet again.
Armed peacekeepers from the UN Mission in the Congo (MONUC) quickly fanned out across the narrow road, eyeing the dense jungle to the south and waiting tensely for an explanation for the delay.
It soon became clear that one of the trucks was stuck in the mud. A rather mundane setback, it seemed, given that North Kivu is the theatre of clashes between forces loyal to dissident General Laurent Nkunda and the regular Congolese army.
Then the shooting began. A battle had broken out just a few kilometres away.
The convoy had come across clear signs of fighting earlier that morning, when it encountered a stream of villagers fleeing the town of Karuba, most with little more than the clothes on their backs. The lucky ones had managed to grab a bag of cassava plants, a few goats, an umbrella.
Fura Nyamuriha had only her children, scurrying to keep pace with her brisk clip down the mountain. "Fighting began the day before yesterday," Nyamuriha told IRIN. "The soldiers of Laurent Nkunda are looting food from people's houses. I have nine children. If this keeps up, I'm finished."
She and the others were headed for the squalid, teeming camps that have sprung up on the outskirts of Goma, North Kivu's capital.
The UN estimates that 370,000 people in the province have fled their homes since fighting began between Nkunda's forces and the army in December 2006.
However, many have decided to brave it out and stay in their villages, a choice that further complicates the delivery of food aid.
Tense talks
At Mushake, a village sympathetic to the Nkunda rebellion, the WFP convoy was stopped by a posse of young men in sunglasses.
They demanded to know why the village had not been given food aid.
After an hour of tense negotiations with various elders, Theodore Kapuku, head of programmes with the WFP in Goma, assured residents that a consignment of food was scheduled to leave Goma for Mushake the following day.
The beleaguered convoy continued on its way, until the soggy, cliff-clinging road brought it to a standstill. After toiling to the sound of gunfire for nearly three hours, mud-spattered truck drivers managed to free the vehicle.
Later that same day, another truck blew a tire, bringing yet another delay.
"The road condition is very bad and the security situation is very bad," said Kapuku, summing up the problems that have so hampered humanitarian aid in North Kivu since fighting resumed in earnest in late August.
The WFP estimates as many as 150,000 vulnerable people may be cut off from assistance because of the clashes and rain-sodden roads.
As drivers struggled to change the flat tire, MONUC peacekeepers received more reports of fighting along the road to the west. It began to look like the convoy would not make it before nightfall.
It didn't.
Just outside Masisi, one of the lumbering trucks got stuck in the mud again. A small group of MONUC peacekeepers settled in for the night to guard the food.
The following day, the sacks of aid finally delivered to a distribution centre in Masisi, the convoy was preparing to head back down the mountain when fighting broke out anew, making it impossible to return to Goma.
Mob action
Five days later, with the convoy still trapped in Masisi, an anti-UN riot erupted in the town centre.
Children streamed out of their homes and schools, shouting and pelting MONUC and WFP trucks with rocks. Several young men wielding machetes roamed the streets.
"MONUC is giving ammunition and uniforms to Laurent Nkunda," protester Kibanga Kibuya told IRIN, before rushing off to join a crowd of irate youths streaming up the hills towards MONUC local headquarters.
The Congolese police fired into the air to quell the riot but not before the mob reached the MONUC building and demanded to speak to officials.
After tense discussions punctuated by gunshots, most residents returned home.
Officials said it was unclear who incited the riot, but suggested it might have been a militia group known as the Mayi Mayi, one of numerous armed groups of shifting loyalties active in North Kivu. Mayi Mayi units are organised around small communities and describe themselves as a sort of civil protection force.
Amid such a confusion of combatants, MONUC, whose UN Security Council mandate includes providing logistical support to the Congolese army, is often accused of taking sides. Nkunda's followers claim the peacekeepers have fought alongside the regular army, while the dissident general's foes claim MONUC has given Nkunda military supplies.
MONUC strongly denies any involvement in activities that go beyond its mandate: to prevent violence and promote security; protect civilians and aid workers; monitor military activity; and help to disarm and demobilise foreign and domestic fighters.
Six days after the convoy set off from Goma for a planned two-day round trip, the fighting eased and the trucks made their way down the mountain and back into the provincial capital.
WFP's Kapuku, displaying a talent for understatement, summed up the task of delivering humanitarian aid in a province riddled by sporadic eruptions of violence and awful roads: "It's a big job," he said.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Bayelsa Govt to Assist Flood Victims
Headline: Nigeria: Bayelsa Govt to Assist Flood Victims
Body_Text:
The Bayelsa government has pledged to assist communities affected by flood in Ekeremor Local Government Area in the state.
The Secretary to the State Government (SSG), Dr Bolere Ketebu-Nwokeafor, made the pledge during an assessment tour of 21-communities devastated by the disaster on Wednesday.
She said government would do its best to ameliorate their suffering through the provision of relief materials.
The communities affected are Amanagbene, Amalka-Zion, Obrigbene, Oboloseria, Lalagbene, Kusi, Angalawei-gbene I & II, Foutorugbene I & II, Bolougbene, Ekeremor, Peretorugbene, Tamogbene, Isampou, Aleibiri, Ayamasa, Azagbene, Okpegbene, Kunou and Ama-bulu.
A News Agency of Nigeria (NAN) correspondent who visited Tungbo, Sagbama, Adagbabiri and Agbere communities in the Sagbama LGA, reports that flood ravaged crops such as cassava, plantain and yams.
The flood, which did not spare Ammasoma and its farmlands, forced the people to construct makeshift wooden bridges to get home.
Mr Martins Suwah, the South-South Zonal Coordi-nator of NEMA, described the flood as the worst in the area. Suwah said they would liaise with the federal and state governments to assist the communities.
(NAN)

Original_Search_Title: UNBS Tightens Grip On Fake Goods
Headline: Uganda: UNBS Tightens Grip On Fake Goods
Body_Text:
Kampala — A person who imports counterfeit products is to pay three times the cost of the goods, the Uganda National Bureau of Standards' (UNBS) quality assurance manager, Deusdedit Mubangizi, has said.
"If you imported goods worth sh20m and they are found to be counterfeit, you will have to pay a penalty of sh60m," Mubangizi told reporters at a training session about the operations of the bureau at Nakawa, Kampala.
The training was part of activities to mark this year's World Standards Day under the theme "Standards and the Citizens: Contributing to Society."
He said archaic laws are responsible for the increase in the importation of counterfeit products.
"The law gives a penalty of between sh3,000 and sh30,000. We recently impounded 300 cartons of expired beers and the person paid only sh30,000," Mubangizi said.
He said a survey carried out by the bureau showed that problems in Uganda's market including sale of expired food products, tempering with expiry dates on food and food products labelled in foreign languages especially Arabic.
Others include products with high pathogenic organisms, sale of products that do not function and those that can injure consumers.
"We have "enterprising" Ugandans who pick empty bottles of Vim and fill them with cassava flour."
Meanwhile, 26 cartons (15,580 pieces) of sub-standard padlocks worth $9,862 (sh17m) are to be re-exported to Dubai, the bureau's head of import inspection has said.
Gyaviira Musoke said: "The consignment was inspected in July. It was found that a key could open several different padlocks, a threat to security, contrary to the requirement that padlocks shall be individually keyed."
According to the UNBS imports inspection and clearance regulations 2002, the 50mm padlocks specified as FF Italy are to be re-exported through the Malaba station.
The goods, which attract about sh8.6m in taxes, would be re-exported through the Uganda Revenue Authority's (URA) customs process and witnessed by the bureau's import officials.
The clearing agent, Novelty Agencies, will lodge a re-export entry with URA proving that the goods are re-exported in order to be refunded the taxes.
Sehil Kumar, the general manager of Max Imports and Exports, said: "In an effort to avoid total loss to an importer, we made an effort to take the samples to the country of origin for confirmation of the irregularities in the supplied products."
"We shall compel the supplier to meet the re-exportation costs and refund the money originally paid for the goods," Kumar said in a letter written to the UNBS executive director received on September 25.
The consignment was imported in container number FCIU 24105/3 by Max Imports and Exports whose offices are at Nabugabo Road in Kampala.
Vincent Ochwo, the UNBS head of engineering, said a genuine padlock should be resistant to rust when exposed to heat or cold.
Ochwo said the plug where the key enters should be firm (shouldn't come out of the padlock case when the key is pulled out).
Other products that were re-exported last month include 400 portable immersion water heaters and 100 cartons of Bint el Sudan Petroleum Jelly. The products had been imported by Robanne (U) Ltd and Makoha Peter respectively.

Original_Search_Title: Insecurity Hinders Intervention in Northern Delta
Headline: Nigeria: Infrastructure - Insecurity Hinders Our Intervention in N'delta - Envoy
Body_Text:
Lagos — Netherland Ambassador to Nigeria, Arie van der Wiel, has said that the level of security in the Niger Delta is the major impediment to his country's attempt to provide social amnenities in the area.
According to him, militants activities make it difficult to embark on infrastructural and different development works in the area, rather, his country gives frequent travel warnings concerning the zone where Royal Dutch Shell carry out its activities.
Speaking during a courtesy visit to THISDAY yesterday, the envoy also said the volume of trade with Nigeria is low at present, but said the country remains the largest trading partner with his country in Africa.
According to him, despite the low trade, his country comes behind China and United States, pointing out that Netherlands exports petroleum products to Nigeria.
He said Nigeria is the highest beneficiary of Dutch aids through Organisation for Economic Cooperation and Development (OECD) which stand at EU2 billion.
Many of the investments, he said, are by private sector investors citing examples like Unilever, Nigeria Breweries, Shell Petroleum and KLM Airlines.
He said his country has a strong presence in the shipping subsector of the economy adding that more cooperation would come on the area of agriculture.
"There are cassava processing projects done by Dutch private companies in Benue, Taraba and Niger states. Cassava has secrets in terms of potentials, there is big future for it and Nigeria is by far the biggest producer in Africa," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Gov Gbenga Daniel Commends News Agency of Nigeria
Headline: Nigeria: Gov Gbenga Daniel Commends NAN
Body_Text:
Abuja — Ogun State Governor, Gbenga Daniel, said Wednesday in Abuja that the state had done well, "at least beyond average" in achieving the targets of the Millennium Development Goals (MDGs).
Daniel made the declaration as a guest of the News Agency of Nigeria (NAN).
Some of the eight MDGs aim at addressing extreme poverty, halting the spread of HIV/AIDS and providing universal primary education by the target date of 2015.
From a blueprint agreed to by UN member- countries and development partners, the MDGs have galvanised unprecedented efforts to meet the needs of the world's poorest.
Daniel told the NAN forum that from an unemployment figure of more than 300,000 in 2003, his administration had been able to reduce the number of unemployed to 50,000 by April 2007.
"There is a downward trend in the rate of unemployment in Ogun State," Daniel stressed.
He also told NAN that there were 17,000 employees in the public service of the state in 2003.
He added that two years later, his administration had employed an additional 7,000 workers, 7,000 teachers and another set of 2,500 employees in the local government councils.
"In four years, we have more than doubled our workforce, created additional farm settlements, trained and provided credit for graduates and other unemployed people in the state.
"We trained them in poultry and fish farming, animal husbandry and cash crop farming. More than 50 per cent of them are still in farming , while others have gone into white collar jobs," Daniel said.
Daniel also said that there had been an unprecedented cassava revolution in the state, which had made Ogun State the largest producer of cassava in the country.
He said that the state also trained youths in other skills like paint-making, garri processing, custard and beads making after which they were provided with equipment to start off the trades.
He said that most young people had also been trained in road construction because the state had been doing its construction by direct labour.
Daniel said that he believed that his administration's empowerment had translated to security of lives and properties of the residents of the state.

Original_Search_Title: Agriculture - Gov't to Collaborate With Firm
Headline: Nigeria: Agriculture - Gov't to Collaborate With Firm
Body_Text:
Akure — To harness the synergy between the public and private sector in agriculture, Ondo State is collaborating with a private company, Shepherd Agriculture Nigeria Ltd, to introduce large scale commercial mecahnised farming mainly in the area of food production.
The state Governor, Olusegun Agagu, disclosed this yesterday at an event marking the World Food Day, adding that government had spent over N2 billion on agriculture in the last four years.
Represented by Secretary to the State Government (SSG), Honourable Isaac Kekemeke, Agagu said the huge investment in the sector was as a result of government's resolve to make agriculture the centre piece of its development efforts, adding that the synergy between the private and public sector must be strenghtened for greater food production.
According to Agagu, government's huge investment in the sector was aimed at generating employment, poverty alleviation, food production, security and provision of raw materials for agro-based industries in the state.
He said the money was spent on various government programmes like the Youth in Agriculture, provision of subsidised inputs, livestock and food production programme, while no less than 1,800 hectares of land had been cultivated for the production of maize and cassava in 86 locations across the state.
The governor said the aim of the programme in which at least 2000 youths were participating was to enhance food production and create a pool of successful generation of farmers.Commissioner of Agriculture, Fisheries and Forestry, Dr Jibayo Oyebade, said the event was celebrated annually to create awareness about the need to boost food production all over the world

Original_Search_Title: Omehia to Launch Operation 100 Millionaires Next Week
Headline: Nigeria: Omehia to Launch Operation 100 Millionaires Next Week
Body_Text:
Abuja — Governor Celestine Omehia of Rivers State says his much awaited programme aimed at turning out 100 millionaires yearly among youths in the state will be unveiled next week.
He made the declaration on Tuesday at the Isaac Boro Park, Port Harcourt at a ceremony marking the 2007 World Food Day in the state. He said the school to land programme will be revitalized and that the youths will undergo training there and in other places for skills acquisition after which they will be empowered with N1 million each to start off their businesses.
Omehia therefore called on youths in the state who have been misled into cultism and militancy to come back and embrace the programme instead of squandering their future on non-productive ventures. He also advised sponsors of the cultists and militants to sponsor them on agriculture and agro-allied ventures.
The governor who was represented by his Deputy, Mr Tele Ikuru, said in order to show the high premium placed by his administration on agriculture, 27 persons sponsored by the Rivers State government last month undertook a study tour to Benin Republic to see the success of Songhai farms with a view to replicating it in the state.
He said his government has bought five new fishing trawlers for fishermen in the state in order to boost the quantity of fish produced for local consumption and for export. He said the achievement of government target in fish production will depend on the fishermen themselves and urged them to form themselves into cooperatives in order to benefit maximally from the government initiative.
He also announced that government intends to establish a starch and cassava processing factory where farmers in the state can process all their cassava into starch. He said as much cassava the farmers can produce, government is ready to buy the excess so as to enable them continue to produce more.

Original_Search_Title: Delta - Beyond the Oil Windfall
Headline: Nigeria: Delta - Beyond the Oil Windfall
Body_Text:
Lagos — Concerns about the future relevance of oil are not new. Neither is the fact that the developed countries are bent on discovering new sources of energy for two major reasons - the unstable supply of oil (and its consequent high cost) and the environmental pollution associated with the use of petroleum products as sources of energy.
As prosperity spread round the world and the use of automobiles keep increasing, the use of petroleum products for individual and industrial needs keep increasing. The attendant pollution is worrying to the developed countries, which have discovered that they are spending billions of Dollars annually on the health bills of their people.
Oil & Delta
Oil was not a major factor in the economy of Delta State or this part of the country until the mid seventies. The principal economic activity was agriculture. There were plantations for palm oil, rubber, while rice and cassava prospered. Fishing boomed, in line with the people's environment.
All these changed as oil exploration increased. From zero revenue from oil, we have depended overwhelmingly on it for the running of our economy. Right now Delta State produces about 30% of the country's oil and gas output. Overtime as more exploration took place, many farmlands and fishing areas gave way to oil pipelines or became oil wells. The economic activity of the people went with it, increasingly cheap money from oil contractors distracted the people from education and other activities that would have helped in securing the future of their children, even our own future. The struggle for ownership of areas with oil led to intra and inter-tribal crisis.
Environmental Devastation
An area that has suffered irreparable damage from oil exploration is the environment. In recognition of the importance of the environment, the United Nations has declared the environment a human right. Many of us are witnesses to the ugly impact and consequences of oil exploration. The resources from oil may be palliatives, in relation to the damage done, but we all admit they are not enough. Part of the reason, if not the main reason for the rise of militancy is the neglect of the environment. Everyone one of us has suffered from it; oil pipelines were vandalised, hostage taking became rife, and there was general breakdown of law and order.
For us in Delta State, all these militant activities undermined oil production and exploration with the attendant decline of our revenue profile. From being the first two states with the highest oil revenue from federal allocation, we became the fourth, losing a third of our revenue as a result. Oil has changed our thinking, our habits, our attitudes, and our culture. Oil now dictates who we are. Strange illnesses and loss of whole communities have become some of the known consequences of oil and gas exploration. Even experts admit that it will take years to determine the full impact of oil exploration in our communities.
Threats To Oil
Researches on use of solar energy for industrial, domestic uses, and for automobiles, have been on for decades. Ethanol, from alcohol, is one of the biggest hits as alternative source of fuel. Researches in its uses are on going. Energy from wind and sun date further back and there has been resurgence in exploring them, as safer sources of energy while the world grapples with the depletion of the ozone layer with its indeterminate effect on the future of mankind. On our own part, we cannot carry on as if we are not part of the global changes that are taking place. The threats to the relevance of oil are manifold, but the two major ones I will discuss with some details.
With instability in the Middle East ,China 's burgeoning industralisation, growing concerns and stiffer environmental laws for oil and gas exploration, the instability of oil supplies from once secure sources, and consistently depleted US reserves, oil prices have been at some of the highest prices ever. There are fears that prices of $100 per litre are within sight. The industrialised countries have also set some prices at which they think commerce, industry, transportation (whether air, land or seas) will come to great danger. Serious threat to these sectors will affect banking, insurance, health, as the inability of their citizens to meet the daily demands of existence will fatally damage their economy.
Industries will produce at such high prices that nobody will purchase their goods, nor will the ships and aircrafts be able to operate at the anticipated price of fuel. Ordinarily, this will sound like good news to oil producing communities, but it is not. The communities are also tied to the global economy.
We have seen airfares going up - the simple explanation has been the increases in price of oil. For the first time in many years, many airlines are declaring losses and wondering how they can continue in business. Unresolved conflicts with oil producing communities can also mean that oil prices could be high without our communities benefiting from the boom, if production is not taking place in those communities.
Delta Without Oil
How can Delta State survive without oil? Some of us think it is inconceivable. This administration from the beginning has thought in that direction. We do not work for the money that comes from oil. It is easy money; it has changed our orientation about hard work. Our young people are growing up in expectation of an easy life from oil.
The criminal activities around oil (and the rightful agitations of the communities for a role in the exploration of their natural resources) have sustained the impression that there will be no Delta State without oil. As proof of our belief that Delta State can do better without oil, we are in the process of harnessing the other non-oil businesses in the State. Our plan is to leverage on the oil revenue for purposes of regenerating wealth. We can no longer afford to squander what little money we get from oil. This rule applies to government, in the same manner as it applies to our oil producing communities, which get a direct share of the oil revenue that comes to the State.
The future lies in investments that will make the State more prosperous without oil. That future starts today, if we start acting as if the State does not have to depend on oil money for survival. The cardinal points of my programme include massive integrated development of road transportation, rails, ports, telecommunication, and airport. We are planning an eight- lane road round the State like a ring, with spots for four ports - sea port, oil and gas terminal, fishing terminal and general cargo. There will be a rail line linked to it. This plan is crucial to the development of all parts of the State. It will in addition to our peace and security efforts provide the enabling environment for industralisation.
The funding of this massive development is more than N3.5 trillion. The money cannot come from oil and gas resources. The private sector has to be actively involved. The execution of these projects will create massive employment and at the same time raise the competitiveness of Delta State as a haven for business.

Original_Search_Title: Sh50b Needed to Restore Agriculture After the Floods
Headline: Uganda: Sh50b Needed to Restore Agriculture After the Floods
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE north and north-eastern region will need massive efforts if the agricultural sector is to be restored to its pre-floods level. Agriculture is the source of livelihood in this part of the country, with over 95% of the population depending on it.
However, even before the floods, the region was regarded the most food insecure in the country becuse of insurgency. The floods made the situation worse.
"The crop harvests that may be realised this year may not last to the next harvest in July 2008," says Amuria LC5 chairman, Julius Ochen.
"The fact that the floods came at the harvesting time means that farmers will not have seeds for the next planting season," says the Food and Agriculture Organisation (FAO) country representative, Percy Misika. FAO has already earmarked sh1.7b to buy planting materials for the next season. However, this is a drop in the ocean.
About sh50b is needed to bring the agricultural sector in the region back on its feet.
In Amuria alone, at least sh2.1b is needed to buy emergency planting materials. Katakwi and Lira need at least sh2b each.
The floods struck at the time when the Government was at the peak of its sh19b resettlement programme for internally displaced people (IDPS).
The FAO had put in place projects such as the cassava multiplication to help the people in Lira and parts of Teso to engage in agricultural activities. Crops like millet, maize and cassava were blossoming. Unfortunately, all these were destroyed by the floods.
"We were approaching a season of plenty, where many people would have enough to sustain them," laments Godwin Ongom from Lira.
The floods changed all this. In Amuria district, over 65,000 acres, about 44% of the agriculture sector were destroyed, leaving about 14,000 homesteads helpless. The total value of affected crops is sh28b. In Katakwi, an estimated 8,500 acres of crop had been destroyed by early this month.
In Bukedea, over 7,000 households had lost their food by early this month. In Kumi, over 2,500 homesteads had lost their crops. Overall, about 50,000 households will need new farm inputs to get back on track.
By early this month, at least 130,000 acres of crops including cassava, ground nuts, beans, millet, sorghum, sweet potatoes, peas and maize had been lost to floods across the region.
The people here had started serious agriculture after the return of peace and there was hope that food aid would be a thing of the past," says Gulu LC5 chairman, Norbert Mao. Mao's despair is echoed by many farmers. "I had six acres of assorted crops. I thought I had enough to feed my family and to sell and get money, but everything was destroyed," says Paul Ochen in Lira.
Because they use mainly rudimentary tools like hand hoes and pangas to prepare the farms, their efforts in terms of calories can never be measured.
To make matters worse, the floods struck at the time of harvest. "We had started harvesting millet," says Ongom.
However, everything was destroyed by the floods.
The items needed per homestead include at least two bags of cassava cuttings, 1kg of ground nuts, 5kgs of sorghum, 5kgs of millet, 5kg of cow peas and 2 bags of sweet potato vines. These can be got from various research centers in the north and other parts of the country.
At the moment, out of the planned 50,000 bags of cassava cuttings, only 5,000 bags have been distributed in the north. The Government was still in the process of implementing the sh18b IDPs resettlement kit programme.
"This money should be used to assist the farmers," Mao says.
In the 2005/06 budget, the Government talked of introducing tractors to work on farms in the north. The farmers hope the tractors could come as soon as possible. Farmers need oxen and ox-ploughs to prepare their land. A set of oxen and ox-plough in this part of the country costs about sh400,000.
"If there is no money to give us these things free of charge, the Government should give us loans," says Herman Kilama, a farmer in Agur, Soroti district.

Original_Search_Title: Boom Time in Gulu
Headline: Uganda: Boom Time in Gulu
Body_Text:
Gulu — Two years ago Gulu was a war town. Every morning the people would wake up to the sound of hundreds of children making their way back home after a night seeking refuge from the Lord's Resistance Army in the town's shelters. In the evening, buses would race up from Kampala to make the city limits before nightfall.
But Gulu is changing fast. With peace talks progressing in Juba, capital of Southern Sudan, its air of desolation is fast being replaced by a hustle and bustle as energetic as that of any Ugandan market town. Developers are competing to get their buildings up, with a string of new constructions set to break the three-storey mark - skyscraper territory in downtown Gulu.
Its band of motley but charming bicycle taxis has been replaced by motorcycle boda-bodas. The streets are full of traffic and the first jam can be only months away.
The streets near the bus park provide an inkling of what is fuelling the boom. Tall Sudanese sit sipping their sodas next to their trucks. Around the corner, traders scramble aboard an impossibly loaded truck as their driver finally bounds out of the customs post.
Hundreds of trucks are braving the pot-holed road north, taking their wares to Southern Sudan, a country enjoying the sort of rejuvenation only possible when an oil boom combines with peace after civil war.
Market prices have shot up as traders destined for the border buy up produce. Just as northern Ugandans are once again starting to sow their seeds, farmers' harvests are suddenly worth a lot more.
Market power
While government and agencies bicker about livelihood projects and empowering returnees, it is likely that the biggest project of all has nothing to do with aid or charity - it is Southern Sudan, which savvy returnees are already realising can do more than any number of workshops.
The first money I made was US$400 in 16 hours. I was so excited...In the camp you are lucky to get 5,000 Uganda shillings [$3]
Simon Peter Akena decided to try his luck and hitched a ride with a truck up from his home in Pabbo, northern Uganda's biggest displacement camp, to Juba. He started finding casual building work and is now running government contracts. "The first money I made was US$400 in 16 hours. I was so excited," he remembers, having returned to Pabbo for a break with his family. "In the camp you are lucky to get 5,000 Ugandan shillings [$3].
"Now I have contracts pending for $60,000 - you can really make yourself in South Sudan."
Akena hit the jackpot but for any number of entrepreneurial young Ugandans the returns are worth the trip north. Susan Abio has also just returned from Nimule, the closest town to the border, where she sells maize, sorghum and cassava. "The money is a lot more than you can ever earn here, so many of my friends are going," the 25-year-old says. "I can earn $100 a day - so I'll definitely be going back."
But riches come at a price and Ugandans returning from Sudan are full of stories of the difficulties they have suffered in a land where the gun is king.
Rashid Olanga, 32, spent several months trading and driving in South Sudan but says the brutality of life north of the border made him happy to get back to Kitgum. "If anyone has a problem he just goes and gets his gun," he says, shaking his head. "A friend of mine was shot by an SPLA soldier because he said he had no money when the guy asked for a beer. He came back with his AK-47. What they admire, they take."
Sudanese moving in
Sudanese are also coming in increasing numbers to buy goods in Uganda.
Denis Ogwal is riding the wave at his Aganadiro "Beauty Shop" in Kitgum. It is more of a wholesaler, stacked high with everything from cooking oil to cassava flour, although Ogwal insists the Sudanese are starting to develop a taste for his body lotion.
"Half of our business is now with the Sudanese," he says watching the hustle and bustle of Kitgum town from his shop. "We've tripled earnings on last year. In a day we can bank 10 million shillings [$5,700] - sometimes more than 20m a day. And that's down to the Sudanese.
"These people don't bargain, they just open their wallets. Ten come in at a time so if one bargains, another will jump in and pay. If you cheat them, they don't mind. They have increased the prices themselves because they want to leave so fast."
There are uglier aspects to the new money pouring in. "They are buying a lot of women here. One woman here in Kitgum just stole 25m shillings [$14,250] from a Sudanese guy she met at a bar - it caused a real scandal - and now the girls are targeting them."
The cross-border demand has also driven up prices. Sam Nambasi stands over piles of beans and fruit on his stall in Kitgum market and says his customers complain all the time about prices."They can go up every 24 hours - but this is the market price - I sympathise with them, but what can you do?"
He says red beans have gone up by 50 percent on the year and sugar by almost the same, from 1,400 shillings/kg (80 cents) to 2,000 ($1.15). A bunch of bananas, which used to cost 100 shillings, now goes for 2,000 shillings a bunch.
Back in Pabbo, Akena labels it "the South Sudan effect", and says the price rises can be positive for the north. "It's good for our lives here - it means we can make more money from small produce. Before a bag of rice would be 80,000 shillings [$46] but now you can get more and that buys you a new bicycle."
Price hikes may be an opportunity for those able make a surplus but can also make life harder for the vulnerable - often widows and landless orphans - unable to grow enough food.
Trade potential
Noah Opwonya, chairman of the Gulu branch of the Ugandan Chamber of Commerce, estimates "at least 100 trucks cross the border every day". He says he can see a time when Acholiland once again becomes the "bread basket of Uganda. Upland rice was one of our major crops and with people now going back to the villages I think we can soon be selling it back to the World Food Programme and why not the Middle East and Europe - it's organic after all!"
In Gulu, Charles Openy, 32, is trading in crops from local farmers and says his business has gone up by 50 percent since people started going back to the land. "Two years ago there might have been two or three trucks a week - now it's 10 or 20," he says, overseeing a dozen or so men loading up a truck. "Some go to Kampala, some to Juba, Nimule or Torit. About a third of my business is now with Sudan.
"This is changing our lives. I used to ride a bicycle into the village and now I drive a small truck. There are many more like me. We are free. Now I can pay for all my children to go to school and I'm extending my house. Once peace comes this will get even bigger."
Construction is one of Gulu's boom areas, with Openy's neighbour, building merchant Cosmas Oola, reporting he has run out of cement. "We'd sell about 50 bags of cement a day before and now that's doubled. The factory just can't give us enough cement to keep them happy.
"Now we can't meet the demand. There is a boom in hotels and people want bigger houses.
"When they start to make more cement it will go through the roof."
Oola says a year ago he had no business with Sudan but now it makes up 20 percent. "The South Sudanese are starting from nothing. No hospitals, no schools, no shops, no administration blocks. And everything that is being built is coming up from Uganda and Kenya.
"The economy is really changing. When people have money in their pockets they are buying cars, motorbikes; look outside - next year we will even start to get traffic jams!"
Don't try to change the way people live during their return process. Just let them go home
Several banks have opened in Gulu, hoping to take advantage of what they see as a big growth market. A huge billboard on the Kampala road announces Barclays' arrival, which is closely tied to the South Sudan factor, says branch manager Henry Otto. He says he wants traders to bank their money near the deal rather than in Kampala.
Otto also says there is a lot of money coming into the local economy from NGO workers who base their families in northern Uganda while working in Southern Sudan. "There are a lot of people - mainly Ugandan nationals - who have their families here and work in South Sudan. They have good salaries and need to bank."
Downsides
But some of the north's economic boom is beginning to attract controversy, with mounting unease over plans to attract investment into areas that have yet to experience returns from displacement camps. Arguments have broken out in Amuru district, home to some of the north's most fertile land, over proposals to establish a sugar factory and plantation.
The national government and Kakira Sugar argue that large projects are needed to spur development and start closing the gap with the rest of the country. But others caution that hasty land allocations now could lay the seeds for future strife when people return home and discover their land has been annexed.
Craig Hollingsworth of the Norwegian Refugee Council cautions that any development plans need to be "conflict sensitive. Don't try to change the way people live during this return process. Just let them go home," he says, voicing concerns that politicians may be tempted to use the return home as an opportunity to reorganise Acholi society away from traditional subsistence patterns. "If you try to change the manner in which people live you are going to cause trouble."
Opwonya agrees that the government needs to be circumspect with land issues and says he fears some local leaders will take bribes for pushing through investors' plans. But he also says that living in camps has changed the Acholis' attitude to business and money.
Once a man's wealth was measured by the size of his herd of cattle but cattle raids and general insecurity stripped Acholiland of its cattle and led to a change in attitudes, he says. "People are now looking to permanent assets," he says. "I am a civil engineer but I didn't look to building. And then my 800 cows were all looted. Instability meant I had to find new, more secure ways to invest.
"With cows I can employ my uncle and his family but with the same money invested in something concrete I can employ many more people."
Magnificent horned cattle are once again becoming a familiar sight in Acholiland but Opwonya says he expects the Acholi to diversify from subsistence farming increasingly to trade, providing more work for a booming population. "The conflict has changed the way people invest and measure wealth - in this case war opened our eyes."
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Janvière Congera - 'Do They Want to Make US Refugees in Our Own Country?'
Headline: Burundi: JanviÃ¨re Congera - 'Do They Want to Make US Refugees in Our Own Country?'
Body_Text:
Bujumbura — Janvière Congera, 30, was repatriated from Tanzania last July after spending four years as a refugee in Kibondo district. With her husband and five children, she chose to settle at Giharo in the southeastern province of Rutana, fearing their original home in Gitega province, in central Burundi, was still unsafe.
"Before we fled to Tanzania, we were living at Gitega; we had a small piece of land but we cannot go back there. I cannot speak for my husband but I think we won't go back. When we left, members of our family had been killed, we have nothing left there.
"When we arrived here, we bought this plot from our neighbour [also a returnee]. It is only enough to erect this hut but nothing else. We have now to work in the farms of our neighbours to earn a living. Sometimes we get 500 francs [50 US cents] with which we buy cassava flour. There are times when nobody needs our labour. Then we go to bed on empty stomachs.
"In Tanzania, we were given food aid, but here we have to look for it ourselves. Not that I regret coming back. You cannot be a refugee all your life.
"We cannot get enough money to buy land but we are now working hard to get money to rent a piece of land for a season or two and grow beans or maize.
"Now, it seems we have to move again from this place. We heard on the radio that we are living on a site where refugees will be accommodated. [The land in the Gakungu zone of Giharo commune has been earmarked by local authorities for a camp for refugees from the Democratic Republic of Congo.]
"Nobody has directly informed us about this [the refugee camp].
"Where do they want us to go? Do they want to make us refugees in our own country?"
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Woman Charged for Child Cruelty
Headline: Sierra Leone: Woman Charged for Child Cruelty
Body_Text:
Freetown — Massah Alpha, 23 was last week charged to court by the Family Support Unit (FSU), Kenema for allegedly burning the right hand of Regina Kallon, 11.
According to Police Constable 2531 Kabbia, the suspect, residing at Saama village, Dama chiefdom, Kenema district was reported to have forcefully burnt the right hand of Kallon.
Alpha who reportedly confessed committing the act said Kallon stole her cooked cassava.
Eyewitnesses account revealed that the relationship between Alpha and Kallon has not been cordial.
However, Kallon is responding to treatment at the Kenema Government Hospital.

Original_Search_Title: One of the Most Favourite Tourist Spots
Headline: Seychelles: One of the Most Favourite Tourist Spots
Body_Text:
Kampala — SEYCHELLES, one of the most beautiful tropical islands in the world, is home to the love-nut, the world's largest seed. Officially known as Coco de mer, the love-nut was nick-named so because of its suggestive shape. It is a species of palm that grows only on two islands of Seychelles, Praslin and Curieuse.
It is the natural heritage of the Seychelles that make it one of the world's favourite tourist destinations. It is home to spectacular marine life and endemic plant, some of the largest seabird colonies in the world and animal species like the Seychelles giant tortoises.
Geography
Located in the Indian Ocean, The Seychelles lies Northeast of Madagascar, east of Kenya, west of Zanzibar, South of Mauritius and Reunion and southwest of Comoros and Mayotte. Seychelles is a group of 115 islands and 40 islets, covering over 1.3 million square kilometres of the western Indian Ocean, and a land area of 444 square kms. They are either coral atolls or granitic in origin with narrow coastal strips and hills of over 900m high.
Economy
The smallest nation in the world issuing its own currency, Seychelles has reaped from its beauty and location. Tourism employs 30% of the islands' population and provides over 70% of hard currency earnings. This has formed the basis of the islands' economic growth since independence, making Seychelles the richest country in Africa in terms of GDP per capita.
World Bank records, however, rank Seychelles as the world's most highly indebted country per capita with a total public debt of 122.8% of GDP. This is a direct result of its overvalued exchange rate. It is now into arrears with most of its international creditors and is living beyond its means by borrowing more.
The government is working to reduce the country's dependence on tourism by putting more emphasis on farming, fishing and manufacturing. This followed the blows suffered by the tourism industry during the Gulf War and the 2001 September 11 attacks on America.
A good number of public enterprises are still under government control, including petroleum product distribution, banking, imports of basic products and telecommunications.
Seychelles main export partners are the UK, France, Italy and Germany, while the import partners are Saudi Arabia, South Africa, Spain, France, Italy, Singapore and the UK.
History of Seychelles
Arab traders were the first to inhabit Seychelles.
They were first sighted by Vasco Da Gama in 1502, a Portuguese Admiral who named the islands after himself. He called them Amriantes, which means Islands of the Admiral. The islands became a transit point between Asia and Africa.
They were used by Pirates until the French took control in 1756 and named them Seychelles in honour of their finance minister, Jean Moreau de Séchelles.
By 1794, the islands were a cause for conflict between France and Britain. Britain persistently sent heavy warships to the island, until she finally took full control from France in 1812.
Chinese, Indian tradesmen and former slaves settled on the island during the 19th Century.
Seychelles gained independence on June 29, 1976 as a republic within the Commonwealth, with James Mancham as the first president. He was, however, ousted in 1977 and replaced with France Albert Rene.
A constitution declaring the island as a socialist one-party state was drafted in 1977 and was not amended until 1992.
Today, the President of Seychelles serves as both the head of state and the head of government. The current president is Rene's former vice-president, James Michel.
Basic facts
Capital City: Victoria
Major Islands: Mahe, Praslin and La Digue.
Currency: The Seychelles Rupee.
Official languages: Seychelles Creole (kreol seselwa), English and French
Population: (2002 est.) 80,098
Elevation: Morne Seychellois at 905 m.
Agriculture: Coconuts, cinnamon, vanilla, sweet potatoes, cassava, bananas, broiler chicken, tuna fish.
Compiled by Elizabeth Namazzi

Original_Search_Title: Food Security, Still a Challenge, Says FAO
Headline: Nigeria: Food Security, Still a Challenge, Says FAO
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Food and Agriculture Organisation Representative in Nigeria, Dr. Helder Muteria, said in spite of the significant agricultural growth in Nigeria, food security is still a challenge.
Muteia, who made this assertion in an interview with the News Agency of Nigeria yesterday in Abuja, called for increased budgetary allocation to agriculture and research to address the situation.
He also disclosed that Nigeria, FAO and United Nations are collaborating in designing a strategy to address cassava issues in asustainable and competitive manner.
According to him, the initiative will help to identify the areas that have huge investment potential in cassava and how to improve production.
He was speaking on the sideline of preparations for the forthcoming World Food Day celebration billed for on October16, 2007. The FAO representative also stressed the need for investments in what he referred to as the right spots.
"Agriculture has been growing by six to seven per cent in the last five years. That's a great, great achievement.
