FAO has commended the government and the farmers themselves because it was achieved through commitment by the government especially in the areas of maize and rice production, livestock and fishery," he said.
He said that investments should be specific, such that the different ecological zones in the country would concentrate on areas that they had comparative and competitive advantage. He also advocated research that would help to identify the right technology needed to double productivity.
The FAO representative also said that the provision of infrastructure including access roads, storage and processing facilities and value addition were critical to achievingfood security.
Citing Indonesia, which he noted, had similar problems like Nigeria, Muteia said the countryhad been able to reduce food insecurity from 50 per cent to 14 per cent over a period of 10 years.
On the continental level, he expressed regretover the non-implementation of agriculturalreforms endorsed by African leaders in Maputo,and Sirte, Libya, in 2003 and 2004respectively. "In the Maputo declaration, we had about15 actions, one of which calledfor 10 per cent of budgetary allocationto agriculture and 14 other strongrecommendations.
"In Sirte, we had more than 30 recommendations,and in Abuja at the fertiliser summit, therewere 20 recommendations and 30 on the FishSummit."We are not lacking in formulas fordevelopment. We need to develop in areasof budget allocation, research,infrastructure, marketing and storage.
"If these elements are addressed, it willmake a huge difference in achieving foodsecurity in Sub-Sahara Africa."Sub-Sahara Africa is the only regionwhere food security has been regressing,"Muteia noted.

Original_Search_Title: Child Labour Frustrating Education in Yumbe
Headline: Uganda: Child Labour Frustrating Education in Yumbe
Body_Text:
Yumbe — Looking at other children going to school, 12- year Rashid Asiku could not hold back his tears as his father shouted at him to go and weed the cassava field. Asiku slowly walked back into the room, changed from his school uniform, picked up a hoe and headed to the garden.
The young boy might have felt worthless and hollow as he watched playmates go to school, but Asiku is not alone. Child labour has been identified as the worst cause of high school drop outs in Yumbe District.
Children not only provide labour in their homes but also get employed by other families.
Early this month the District Chairperson, Al-haji Rashid Govule Iyiga, instructed his guards to arrest Mr Geoffrey Ayimani a site foreman and three pupils he had employed to level murram on a community access road in Drajini Sub-County.
"This is criminal. We want our children to go to school and some people are here abusing the government programme of UPE. We can not allow this," Mr Govule said.
The pupils identified as; Stephen Andruga,14, in Primary four at Yiba Primary School; Lemeriga Twaha,16, in Primary six and Draga Mudasiri,12, of Primary four at Lodonga Black Primary School were driven to Lodonga Police Post.
Mr Ayiman maintained that the children had been sent by their parents. But Mr Govule said the police would sermon the parents to explain why their children were not in school that day. The boys claimed they were earning Shs3,000 daily and that they give the money to their parents.
Child labour is the employment of children under the age of 18. The LC3 Chairperson for Drajini Sub-sounty, Mr Linus Kayie said his council would soon pass a by-law on compulsory school enrollment. "This by-law was passed by the previous council and we think there are some parts that need to be amended," he said.
But in other sub-counties such as Apo, the authorities have been mounting operations to arrest children who do not go to school. Their parents are fined Shs10,000. The Education Officer in charge of administration, Mr Jamal Abdi, says about half of the pupils in primary schools drop out.
He says inspection reports indicate that out of 5,574 pupils who were in Primary six last year only 2,778 registered for Primary Leaving Examinations this year.
Who is to blame?
"Parents do not encourage their children to stay in school. If all the stakeholders played their part we would go along way in addressing the issue of quality education ...certainly our challenge remains improving the quality of education," Mr Jamal says.
He says although early marriages pose a challenge to education in the district, child labour tops the list. "It is worse during planting and harvest seasons. Children are sent to work in the fields. Sometimes they go to chase away vermin and eventually, they are retained at home," Mr Jamal says.
He said some parent say they are successful despite not having attended school.
Despite limited resources, Mr Jamal says his department started sensitising residents on the value of education and training of school management committees.
"By training these stakeholders we feel we are empowering them to perform their roles effectively and ensure that schools are managed in line with the government policy," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Agric Biotechnology - Mixed Blessing
Headline: Zimbabwe: Agric Biotechnology - Mixed Blessing
Body_Text:
Harare — While  Zimbabwean scientists are upbeat about the need to use biotechnology to improve agricultural productivity, agronomists and environmentalists argue that emerging technologies could have serious implications on the operations of smallholder farmers in Zimbabwe and Africa as a whole.
Agronomists and environmentalists who attended a three-day workshop on the implications of Economic Partnership Agreements and the need for the protection of farmers' rights which was held last week in Harare said modern biotechnology is very expensive in terms of access, affordability and suitability for smallholder farmers in developing countries.
"Developing countries do not have the resources to conduct research in modern biotechnology and the seed, the technology and other input requirements cannot be afforded by smallholder farmers," said Mr Andrew Mushita, the director of the Community Technology Development Trust.
Prominent Zimbabwe agronomist Mr Roger Mpande said although Zimbabwean scientists were excited about biotechnology and its potential in the agriculture sector there is need to conduct research on the implications of modern biotechnology on the livelihoods of smallholder farmers who constitute more than 80 percent of the population in most developing countries.
"Zimbabwean scientists are so excited about modern biotechnology but they haven't told us about the possible impact of growing genetically modified (GM) crops on farmers," he said.
"Biotechnology research on GM crops is very expensive and smallholder farmers cannot afford to buy GM seed. Scientists want to promote the growing of GM crops at the expense of local and indigenous varieties which are easily accessible, cheap and suitable for local soils and climate."
Mr Mushita, an expert on agrobiodiversity and environmental issues told participants that even though modern biotechnology offered some opportunities it has some demerits which affect the interests of and livelihoods of smallholder farmers.
Modern biotechnology, he said, was largely in the hands of multinational corporations that enjoyed exclusive rights over the technologies as they have the resources and capacity to develop new GM seed varieties.
He said unlike local seed varieties such as 'Mutode' (maize seed), indigenous small grains, beans and other vegetables that could be saved as on-farm seed over years, new GM seed hybrids could only be used once.
"Farmers will depend heavily on seed from these multinationals leading to loss of biodiversity. The hybrid seed cannot germinate again once you harvest it whereas local seed types can grow," Mr Mushita said.
He told smallholder farmers, policy makers; agronomist and other experts that smallholder farmers in Zimbabwe and the entire African continent were unlikely to reap the benefits attributed to advances in agricultural biotechnology.
Instead, Mr Mushita argued that the advances in and adoption of new agro biotechnologies were likely to worsen rather than mitigate existing inequalities, undermine rather than protect African biodiversity, and facilitate dependence rather than development.
According to Mr Mushita some of the disadvantages of modern agro-biotechnologies include:
l Technologies and GM seed types owned and controlled by multinationals
l Technologies and GM seed crops protected by Intellectual Property Rights
l GMOs contaminate biodiversity
l Seeds are converted from being self-propagating to laboratory products
l Plant Breeders and patent holders gain monopoly rights over seeds
l Farmers become dependent on biotechnology industry over seeds
Agronomists and environmentalists who attended the workshop which was organised by CTDT said it is better for local scientists to embrace biotechnology in other fields such as medicine, veterinary, chemical and other fields which have limited adverse effects on the livelihoods of smallholder farmers.
Scientists who attended a workshop organised by the National Biotechnology Authority two weeks ago argued that biotechnology could be used to find solutions to some of Zimbabwe's pressing challenges.
Other scientists also unanimously agreed that Zimbabwe should fully embrace biotechnology and tread the path that India, China, Cuba, Brazil and South Africa have taken.
Given the importance of agriculture in Zimbabwe and the entire African continent biotechnology has been greeted with a mix of hope and suspicion making it a hot bed of fierce debate.
The sharp differences are reflected both in public and academic spheres in their approach on biotechnology and in legislative frameworks that deal with its introduction and regulation.
"In the future, bananas engineered to include malarial provolactics could prove to have great health benefits for the region.
"Bt cotton could reduce pesticide use, freeing much-needed foreign exchange and reducing the need for farmers to handle dangerous chemicals, and drought tolerant crops could reduce losses associated with bad weather," Mr Abisai Mafa, acting chief executive of the National Biotechnology Authority remarked in 2001.
"At the same time, there are many unknowns, which will take time to resolve. DDT, for example, was used for years before its cumulative effects were realised. We should therefore not move recklessly towards the adoption of GM crops."
Supporters of the expansion of production of GM crops and related products argue that emerging technologies can modernise agricultural production in the developing world.
Increasing yields, they argue, will end hunger and malnutrition, and biotechnology will make agriculture more sustainable by reducing chemical inputs.
Mr Mpande said a number of factors limit the applicability of these new and emerging technologies to the African context.
He said GM crops have failed to be adopted in Zimbabwe and a few other African countries.
"The Bt Cotton project in Zimbabwe was a failure. Farmers were not keen to adopt the new seed technology, in Malawi and Tanzania there are similar reports," he said.
Critics of biotechnology say the limitations are, in large part, a function of Africa's position within the global political economy.
They say current biotechnology research is not generally informed by local conditions in Zimbabwe and across the entire African continent.
In many ways this reflects the classic problem of development and "appropriate technology."
Agronomists and environmentalists called for the promotion of appropriate technology that is sensitive to the local conditions in the developing world. They said appropriate technology would be small-scale, labour-intensive, more subject to local mastery, repair and control and would meet particular cultural and ecological demands of the communities where it would be applied.
Such technology, Mpande and other agronomists and environmentalists believed, would be more likely to result in improved smallholder farm out and development.
In a paper titled, "Biotechnology and Rural Development: Implications for Southern Africa Agriculture," Noah Zerbe, a political scientist said the current generation of GM crops under commercial production reflects the problem of inappropriate technology.
"They were designed for North American (and to a lesser extent European) farmers, not African smallholders, and focused on crops not usually grown by and adopt traits of secondary importance to most smallholder African farmers.
"Soyabeans, for example, one of the dominant commercial biotech crops, are almost exclusively cultivated by large-scale commercial farmers. The GM maize currently available is yellow maize, grown primarily as animal feed and considered unpalatable by many Africans.
"Similarly, the GM canola/rape currently on market is designed for high oil output, as the crop is grown for oil production in the West," he said.
African farmers, he said, however grow rape as a household vegetable for domestic consumption.
"While some research is now being directed into other crops of importance to smallholder farmers--crops such as cassava and sorghum--the vast majority of research remains confined to crops cultivated by commercial farmers in Europe and North America," Zerbe argued.
Opponents of biotechnology say because farmers in developing countries could produce and save their own seed from season to season, corporations argued that there was little reason for them to fund research into open pollinated varieties (OPV) seed and instead focused on hybrid crops such as maize.
Zimbabwean agronomists said the selection of hybrid crops for research and the deliberate exclusion of OPVs from research was not based on the potential of such crops nor the needs of farmers but instead on the potential profits that multinational seed companies could reap.
As a result, they said, there was limited funding into research on OPV seed which are important smallholder farmers who grow crops such as groundnut, sorghum and millet which are drought tolerant and can adapt to semi-arid regions of the country.
Studies by agronomist show that across Southern Africa, informal seed networks remain the primary source of seed for the vast majority of smallholder farmers.
In Sadc region, the studies show, less than 10 percent of seed sown by smallholder farmers is obtained from the formal sector.
Farmers rely extensively on farm saved seed (60-70 percent of total seed planted) and social networks (30-40 percent). Informal, community-based seed networks -- saving seed on-farm, or acquiring seed from relatives, neighbours, or other community sources through barter or social obligation, they say, represent a key component of agricultural production in Southern Africa.
In the end, there is need for Zimbabwean scientists, agronomists and environmentalists to weigh what is best for the country in terms of biotechnology application, adoption and the implications of its use on smallholder farmers' livelihood.
Zerbe rightly concludes: "Agricultural biotechnology thus represents, at best, a mixed blessing for Southern Africa, holding both promise and peril for African smallholders in particular. The application of molecular techniques to agriculture could lead to increased yields and crops expressing many desirable traits.
"At the same time, however, the social, economic and environmental consequences of the new technology must be considered. The benefits of biotechnology will not be felt evenly. Some will benefit, others will not. Or perhaps more accurately, in Southern Africa, some will benefit, most will not."

Original_Search_Title: Enugu Parleys South African Businessmen On Fish Production
Headline: Nigeria: Enugu Parleys South African Businessmen On Fish Production
Body_Text:
Talks have reached advanced stage between the Enugu State government and some businessmen from South Africa on fish production.
The State commissioner for agriculture and natural resource, Prof. Hilary Edeoga, made this known to newsmen in Enugu last Friday.
Edeoga said that the state government was looking beyond the country for partners to bring agriculture to the front burner in the state.
The commissioner said that the state-owned fishery project located at Nike at the outskirts of the state capital would form the pilot scheme of the fish project.
He said that a memorandum of understanding would be signed between the state government and the South African businessmen before the end of this year.
Edeoga also said that a cassava processing plant was being sited at Nkerefi in Nkanu East local government area of the state.
He said the project, being executed in collaboration with the Korean government was meant to ease the glut being experienced by cassava farmers in the state.
The commissioner said that each of the 17 local government areas had initiated arrangement for the acquisition of graders to make the rural roads motorable.
He said the state government had distributed 42 tractors to the 17 local government headquarters to assist farmers.

Original_Search_Title: WFP Airlifts Relief to North
Headline: Uganda: WFP Airlifts Relief to North
Body_Text:
Kampala — The World Food Programme (WFP) has started airlifting 3,000 metric tonnes of relief food items to 248,000 internally displaced persons in Acholi and floods victims in Lango.
The food which includes maize, beans and corn soya, is being airlifted from the UPDF military airbase in Gulu using a WFP helicopter.
Most displaced persons have not received their food rations for July and August due to the bad roads caused by the floods, according to Bai Monky Sankoh, the head of WFP Gulu office.
"In general WFP will airlift 1,165 metric tonnes of food to Pader to serve 116,000 IDPs living in Kalongo, Amyel, Omiya Pachua, Wol and Kokil camps.
"We will also deliver 70 metric tonnes of food to Olam nyu, Pawel and Palokere camps in Gulu; 335 metric tonnes of food to Kitgum and 500 to Lira," Sankoh explained.
Also, the WFP on Friday boosted its emergency relief operations in Teso region by flying in three helicopters from the Republic of Ukraine.
The helicopters that landed at Soroti Flying School can each carry more than three tonnes, according to the UN agency's head of the logistics cluster, Hugo Van Vuuren.
The UN agency has been using one helicopter to airlift relief to victims living in areas that have been hard-hit by floods with the roads and bridges destroyed.
Meanwhile, the United Nations under the Central Emergency Response Fund has donated $6million (sh10.4b) for emergency life-saving activities in flood-hit areas of northern and eastern Uganda.
The grant is in response to a $41m appeal launched last month by the humanitarian organisations comprised of UN agencies and NGOs.
An appeal launched in Soroti solicited support to address the devastation caused by the floods and extend assistance to over 300,000 affected people.
Flooding has damaged houses, water and sanitation facilities.
Most water sources including boreholes have been contaminated, further increasing the possibility of disease outbreaks, especially cholera.
The humanitarian community has been providing food aid, established temporary learning structures and plastic latrines for worst-hit schools.
They have also distributed cassava cuttings and water purification tablets to households.
Theophan Nikyema, the UN humanitarian coordinator in Uganda, in a statement, said: "These funds will be put to immediate use to benefit the thousands who require aid to cope with the effects of the floods.
"We cannot afford to think a few days of sun may solve the harm caused by the rains.
"Instead, we must recognise that only continued support for food security and disease prevention will help to rebuild people's livelihoods."
The response plan will prioritise food security, prevention of disease outbreaks and ensuring capacity to respond to health emergencies.
The plan will also address re-opening of flood-affected schools, needs of vulnerable groups and facilitate early recovery for victims of floods.

Original_Search_Title: What Experts Say of the Floods Ravaging Us
Headline: Uganda: What Experts Say of the Floods Ravaging Us
Body_Text:
Kampala — Climate scientists have confirmed that the climate is actually changing. For us in Uganda, climate change is no longer a story but rather a reality as most parts of the country, especially the north and east are already feeling the impacts of heavy rains in form of floods.
Climate change is any noticeable change in the earth's climate such as a shift in seasons, increased temperature in one area, increased rains and consequent floods, storms in other regions.
According to the Meteorology Department, there is an increased likelihood of more near normal to above normal rainfall over most parts of the country.
"The intensity of the rains is expected to be more pronounced during the months of October, except over most parts of the western region, where the intensity is expected to be more pronounced during the months of November," principal meteorologist Alloysius Kagoro wrote, in the September to December, seasonal rainfall forecast issued to Daily Monitor.
The minister of State for Agriculture, Israel Ssebunya Kibirige's, recent statement to farmers also warns that flooding and water logging similar to those currently being experienced in Teso and Lango is still expected to occur in all low lying areas.
"The most vulnerable are areas lying in the catchment areas of lakes, where the water table is already raised by the cumulative heavy rains experienced since June. Other areas include districts that lie within the cattle corridor," Mr Ssebunya said.
The likely adverse effects of flooding and water logging may hence include rotting of root crops like cassava and potatoes within the soil, flower abortion such as in beans and groundnuts, pasture for live stock being submerged and water pollution, Mr Ssebunya said.
Other likely impacts, he added, include disease epidemics for both livestock and crops, rise of fungal and bacterial diseases for especially vegetables like tomatoes, Irish potatoes and beans.
The resulting mud and stagnant water due to heavy rains may favour development of foot rot, foot and mouth disease and liver flukes, he warned. Leaching, water run-off and flash floods will most likely render soils less fertile for agriculture.
While in the highland areas especially in south western and eastern central, the intensity and frequency of the rains are most likely to influence landslides.
In its fourth overall assessment report, United Nations Frame work Convention on Climate Change(UNFCC) suggests that it is "probably too late" to avoid some impacts in developing countries because about one degree centigrade of warming is already in the climate system.
"Extreme weather events" are likely to become more intense and more frequent, while higher global temperatures could affect crops, water supplies and spread disease.
"If it is not kept below 2C - which "currently looks very unlikely to be achieved" - up to 3.2 billion people will face water shortages and up to 600 million will face hunger, UNFCC's Working Group II, which examines global warming's impact on the environment and people predicted.
"Although there is little we can do now to avert climate change, at least we cannot sit back and watch but rather devise means to adapt and mitigate climate change," assistant commissioner for meteorogy, Phillip Gwage, said in Kampala at a training of journalists in Uganda on how to report climate change issues.
How to adapt
Farmers are being advised to go to their nearest agricultural extension workers for advice on how to clear all waterways and dig up channels and trenches to allow drainage of water from their gardens.
Government has also advised farmers in highland areas to avoid practices that encourage soil erosion -- the washing away of top fertile soil by wind or water, such as planting downslope but rather plant across the hill and in terraces.
To help your soils gain fertility after the floods,, Dr Louis Verchot, of the Kenya-based World Agroforestry Centre says agroforestry, which involves growing trees and rearing animals in the same piece of land, has the potential to provide multiple products including fruits good for your health and a range of environmental services like cleaning the air of carbondioxide, the greenhouse gas most blamed for causing climate change.
Dr Verchot also advises that you plant leguminous crops which are also cover crops like beans, soya and groundnuts.
Leguminous crops, he said, fix nitrogen in the soil, thereby improving its fertility and prevent soils from being washed away in case any more rains fall again.
A senior lecturer at Makerere University's faculty of forestry and nature conservation, Dr Gerald Eliu, advises that you plant trees too since trees check the speed of the would-be disastrous run off and hold soil particles together.
To government, Miambo Malesu, regional coordinator for the World Agroforestry Centre's water management programme advises that to avert floods: Design large community reservoirs in flood prone areas and utilise the stored water for irrigation and livestock management.
"Design multiple small reservoirs in non-flooding zones to be used at household level for domestic and agricultural use," he said. Also, "design groundwater recharge structures to boost groundwater levels and reduce ponding".
To control malaria, Mr Malesu advises government to design reservoirs with steep slopes to impede thriving of mosquito larvae.
Malesu also advised citizens to install run-off catchment systems from open grounds and store the water in pans, ponds and underground reservoirs.

Original_Search_Title: Humanitarian Agencies Receives $6m for Flood Interventions
Headline: Uganda: Humanitarian Agencies Receives $6m for Flood Interventions
Body_Text:
Kampala — HUMANITARIAN agencies operating in Uganda have received a US$ 6m (Shs10.4 bn) grant from the Central Emergency Response Fund (Cerf) for emergency relief activities in response to the flooding that hit sevaral parts of eastern and northern Uganda.
The grant is in response to a US$41 million Flash Appeal launched by the relief agencies last month.
"These funds will be put to immediate use to benefit the thousands of who require aid to cope with the effects of the floods," said Mr. Theophane Nikyema, the United Nations Humanitarian Co-ordinator for Uganda.
He said: "We cannot afford to think a few days of sun may solve the harm caused by the rains. Instead we must recognise that only continued support for food security and disease prevention will help to rebuild people's livelihoods."
The floods have left property worth millions of shillings destroyed and hundreds of people displaced.
The displaced people are in dire need of safe drinking water, food, clothing, medicine and shelter.
Heavy showers have persisted in eastern and northern Uganda since July 2007, destroying much of the first season harvest and disrupting second season planting, hence raising concerns about food security.
Many families will require food aid to cover a ten-month hunger gap.
The flooding has also damaged shelter, water and sanitation facilities.
Many homes have collapsed, while most that remain standing have damp floors and crumbling walls due to excessive moisture.
Most pit latrines have collapsed while those remaining are not safe for human use. Rather than risk their lives, residents have chosen to defecate in the bush.
Most water sources like boreholes, have been contaminated, further increasing the possibility of water borne diseases epidemics.
Many health centres in the affected areas had little or no supply of essential drugs at the start of the emergency, while staffing levels are chronically low.
The government recently declared a state of emergency in the areas affected by the floods. The police estimate that 20 people have so far been killed by the rampaging waters since August.
To arrest the situation, the humanitarian agencies have been delivering relief supplies in the the affected areas.
The agencies have distributed food aid to at least 120,000 people; established temporary learning spaces and supporting approximately 10,000 Primary Leaving Examinations (PLE) candidates.
The relief organisations have constructed plastic latrines for 20 worst hit schools in every affected district.
5,000 bags of cassava cuttings have been distributed to 4000 households; supplied water purification tablets to over 25,000 households as well as non-food items like suace pans and plates to over 18,345 households.
The relief agencies appealed for the US$ 41 million to help mitigate the effects on floods on the communities.
A statement dated October 11th, from the office of Coordination Humanitarian affairs at the United Nation said UN agencies and non-governmental organisations have formed a response plan about the floods.
"That forms the basis of the appeal prioritises urgent action to stabilise the food security situation; prevent outbreaks of epidemic disease and ensure capacity to respond to health emergencies; re-open schools affected by the floods; ensure access to the most vulnerable and respond to their needs; and undertake activities to facilitate the early recovery of those affected by the floods, the statement reads in part.
The funding received from the Cerf is directed at answering the most urgent needs identified in the Appeal.

Original_Search_Title: 200 Children Rescued from Pastors
Headline: Uganda: 200 Children Rescued From Gulu Pastors
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE POLICE on Friday stormed a new church for born-again Christians in Gulu district and rescued over 200 children who were under illegal custody.
The children have been reunited with their parents.
Three pastors of the church, Healing Outreach Ministries in Bungatira sub-county, Aswa county, were arrested in the raid that followed complaints by parents about their missing children.
"We mounted an operation and managed to rescue over 200 school-going age children who were under illegal custody," the Gulu Police community liaison officer, Johnson Kilama, said.
He identified the pastors as Lucy Anek Kigozi from Kampala, Alfred Kanyama, a teacher from Gulu, and Saverino Okello, a peasant in Gulu.
Kilama said they were shocked to find children from over 15 primary schools in Gulu and Amuru districts under the pastor's custody.
"The children claimed the Church invited them to register for scholarships," Kilama said.
Kanyama said the children started reporting to the Church on October 7. The children were being fed on boiled cassava, according to the resident district commissioner, Col. Walter Ochora.
"The church didn't have any means to give the children proper meals but decided to keep them there on boiled cassava," Ochora said. "The Police started searching for the children following a tip from Bungatira residents that a fake religious organisation was keeping children illegally."
Both Ochora and the Police said the Church duped the children that a Canadian donor would pay their school fees and provide them basic needs.
"The managers of the church claimed that they were looking after some children and had got a promise of funding from a Canadian religious organisation.
"So they rushed to the district and started inviting and keeping children whom they wanted to show to the Canadian when he comes to Uganda," Ochora said.
The actions of the church, Ochora said, were tantamount to abduction.
He noted the incident was one of the negative things coming up following the return of peace to the region. "Now that peace is returning to the district, all sorts of fake NGOs are coming here.
"We have passed a by-law requiring that all guests in the district should announce their presence to the office of the district internal security officer within 24 hours. "They have to state how long they will be in the district and the purpose of their visit," Ochora said. The Police spokesman, Asan Kasingye, yesterday said investigations into the incident were still going on and Pastor Anek Kigozi Anek had been released on bond.
Kigozi, the Church leader, claimed that Johan, a Canadian, had asked her to register orphans and widows who needed support.
"We are not a cult and we did not steal the children. The church invited the children who are orphans to come and be registered for the scholarships."
Last month, security agencies arrested 11 members of a cult, the Jerusalem Temple, which was preaching the end of the world. The Police warned the public against starting and joining cults and urged them to be vigilant and report such cases.

Original_Search_Title: Forget Helicopters; Instead Start Rearing Horses And Asses
Headline: Uganda: Forget Helicopters; Instead Start Rearing Horses And Asses
Body_Text:
Although the rains are still falling and the floods still bothering our people in different parts of Uganda, time to stop blaming anybody has come. What is needed is to assess our capability to deal with such disasters and move on to lay a foundation upon which the ministry responsible for disaster preparedness can base its future operations.
Today's disaster preparedness is nothing to write home about and there is no point crucifying the gentlemen who run the shambolic ministry. After all, how many helicopters are there in this country for instance? Two and a half or three.
That is what is shared between all the agencies and sectors that require the use of helicopters like health (for evacuation of patients and casualties), forestry (for monitoring encroachment and illegal felling activities), the wildlife authority (for chasing poachers and inspecting movement of herds), police (for chasing getaway criminals), media (for covering some events up close when roads are jammed with traffic) and of course the ministry of disaster preparedness, for getting close to disaster sites and helping the victims quickly.
I am told the health sector has half a helicopter because the one used by a private sector operator is reportedly often parked outside Uganda in a neighbouring country, and it may not always be readily available at a few minutes notice.
Aviation technology has in the recent couple of decades proved elusive and so let us forget the '70s when Uganda Air Force had dozens of supersonic MiG 21s and a host of other fighter planes, scores of helicopters and several Hercules transporter aircraft. That is the time when the Uganda Police Airwing had innumerable small fixed wing planes, choppers and airfields of its own which today are covered with cassava gardens and haphazard dwellings.
The time to focus on is today and tomorrow. Aviation aside, we need to accept that even vehicular road means of responding to disaster are not our strongest point. It is not clear if there is a single ambulance in the country that measures up to ambulances elsewhere which are in effect casualty wards in which an accident victim is treated by paramedics and made ready for further treatment including surgery en route to hospital.
In case of traffic jams and distance, someone does not have to die from bleeding, obstruction of air passages or wrong handling. Let us stop lamenting at our new public 'ambulances' that are really double cabin pick-up trucks that cannot conceivably accommodate a patient lying in horizontal position.
We should also not complain that many accident victims at night are taken to Mulago in police patrol pickups, thrown down there under bench with robbery suspects who are being carted to the cells.
What we need is to look at alternative solutions that are within our capabilities. Since our capacity to acquire, man and maintain machines for disaster action has proven inadequate, a time has come to try out alternative methods that do not require foreign exchange and large budgetary outlays to finance. We need to look at nature.
Let us call up all the veterinary graduates who are doing business in Kikubo or teaching Biology in secondary schools and immediately put them to work breeding animals that can be trained to operate in difficult terrain.
We need to develop mounted squads that can reach where wheeled vehicles will not dare and deliver relief and medical attention to our people. If we start breeding horses and donkeys now, by the end of next year we would be seeing the beginning of a well trained disaster and rescue unit that would have the capability to take medical personnel, foodstuffs, drugs and blankets to people in areas that cannot be accessed by cars.
It also time to remember that a fifth of Uganda's surface area is covered by water. This tends to increase when there are floods like these days, Therefore our capacity to move over water is highly inadequate when you consider that 20 percent of our territory cannot be covered using wheels or on foot, but by boat and swimming.
We therefore need to revive the boat building section of the fisheries department in Entebbe. Any other agencies that used to build boats need to be revisited. Our capacity to move on water must be boosted using available raw materials.
As for fire, it is rather hard to find local solutions to cover the gap created by our lack of fire engine trucks. It is tempting to suggest sprinklers for every building since we have no shortage of water. But our aversion to acquisition and maintenance of modern technology may not allow automated sprinklers.
Maybe we should require every building to keep several huge drums of water and some kanyamas (muscle men) around to start tendering the water when fire breaks out.
We are by no means advocating for sticking to ancient means of disaster handling. These are stop gap measures. By the time we buy enough choppers and fire engines several decades from today, these natural methods could have saved many lives in the interim.
Mr Buwembo is managing editor, Daily Monitor

Original_Search_Title: Mombasa Gets a Rare Makeover
Headline: Kenya: Mombasa Gets a Rare Makeover
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The surrounding caves, which served as holding grounds for slaves waiting to be shipped to the Middle East and other destinations more than 400 years ago, were used to commit some of the vices.
Gangs used caves as hideouts to pounce on unsuspecting visitors.
Many residents had resigned to fate perhaps because they never expected any action from the Government or the local authority.
The choice of Mombasa as the host city for this year's 35th edition of the International Association of Athletics Federations (IAAF) cross-country championships brought with it some good tidings.
The hosting of the event on 24th March marked a turning point for the town and the popular public beach in particular. After successfully hosting the IAAF festival, all has changed and the historic site continues to get a new face.
According to the Mombasa Municipal Council, Mama Ngina Drive was set aside for visitors to sit and relax while watching ships coming to dock or sail out of the port of Mombasa.
The area, which extends from the Likoni ferry to the Coast police headquarters, is today the number one choice of many visitors.
Prior to hosting the IAAF event, a number of ministries were given the mandate to develop the centre into a world-class venue.
After hosting the event, the ministries decided to extend the beautification programme to cover other areas.
Today, Mombasa is undergoing a makeover and once it is complete, it will transform the coastal city into one of the most modern tourists hub in East Africa.
Boosting world class beach hotels, Mombasa hosts more than 70 per cent of the nearly one million tourists who visit Kenya each year.
In recognition of Mombasa as a gateway to East Africa, a major beautification project pioneered by the Office of the Government Spokesman, Dr Alfred Mutua, has already transformed the area into a well-lit city at night. Previously, Mombasa used to be among the darkest towns in Kenya at night.
Driving along the streets of Mombasa at night is no longer a nightmare but a leisure to motorists as the town continues to get a face-lift.
For the last three months, trenches have been dug along the busy streets, electric poles put and underground cables laid.
This is part of the multi-million Mombasa improvement project undertaken by the inter-ministerial committee, headed by Mutua, in partnership with the private sector.
The committee is working on an elaborate programme to install streetlights.
In a move aimed at decongesting the island of vehicles, the committee plans to construct a bus park at Kengeleni for Malindi and North Coast bound public service vehicles.
The project allows partner companies to advertise their products on the illuminated boards and generate some revenue for the Mombasa Municipal Council.
The concessions will last for five years and if the companies wish to continue, they can apply afresh or have other companies take over the management and advertisement.
"The arrangement is such that 30 per cent of the proceed will go to the Mombasa Municipal Council while the rest will go towards maintaining the infrastructure for five years," Mr Francis Mwaka, who represents Mutua in overseeing the works, said.
Design work is already in progress to put up a footbridge at Likoni ferry to ease the flow of passengers and reduce congestion at the vital crossing.
"Once complete, passengers will walk on the foot bridge and get to the ferry without the usual queues ," Mwaka said.
The committee also intends to put up a police station at the Mama Ngina Drive. The station will have an office block where officials from the Mombasa Municipal Council, Tourism and National Museums of Kenya will be stationed and act as customer care officers.
The office will be a one-stop shop where a visitor will be given information about Mombasa and what it offers.
It will also have four cells and five blocks of toilets. The site for the toilets has already been identified and work has started.
A 'perfume garden' has also been established. This will create an environment that will give a natural welcoming scent to visitors.
The attractive trees that give out the sweet smell have been planted. These trees are now an attraction.
To bring some order, the committee has banned driving schools from using the eight-hectare area as a training ground.
Mwaka says designer shelters are being put in place for Madafu sellers and cassava crisps (kachiri) to ensure that the history of the garden is maintained.
The committee will also install seats to ensure holidaymakers and residents have an ideal place to relax.
The ongoing Sh8 million landscaping is expected to give Mombasa a new face .
Two modern restaurants will be constructed close to the sea and tour operators will have an opportunity to bring tourists to these facilities every time they have town tours.
A modern walkway is also being put up in place from Likoni ferry to Fort Jesus. The walkway, measuring about five kilometres, will have lights and this will allow visitors to enjoy the beach front at night. Fifty per cent of the work on this pathway is complete.
On traffic flow, a modern parking yard has been put up at the Mama Ngina Drive to ensure there is organised parking. Previously, motorists parked cars on the grass and on any available open space. A well-designed parking has been put up at the garden.
"This will not happen again, motorists are advised to park at designated places. Police will be on standby to ensure order and security throughout," Mwaka said.
Three points have been identified and will be constructed at strategic areas for the public to view ships. Transparent material will be used but will be reinforced to ensure safety.
"We want Kenyans visiting Mama Ngina Drive to be proud of their country. We want them to visit again because of the high standards in place. We want to ensure that everything done here is environment friendly," Mwaka said.
The committee has also sunk a borehole to ensure there is water at the garden throughout. Some of the water will be used to water the grass and ensure cleanliness of the ablution block.
A 10,000 litres water tank is under construction at the park. It is expected to ease the water shortage and ensure the park has enough water throughout. The tank is expected to be ready this month.
At the Pamba Roho football ground, Fort Jesus, the committee has issued a notice to close down the ground for at least six months and plant grass to make it conducive for the youth to play football and carry out other activities.
Lights have already been put up around the ground and there are plans to put more. This is designed to give the youth a facility to carry out day and night activities.
"Our aim is to make the youth busy and avoid being lured into the harsh world of drugs," Mwaka said.
Similarly, all roads in Mombasa are being re-branded. A brief history of each of the roads or personality named after the road will be engraved.
"For instance, Nyerere Road will have a picture and brief history of former Tanzanian president, Julius Mwalimu Nyerere, and role played he played," Mwaka said.
The project will involve school children as part of a learning process. This is designed to help them to know their heroes and the contribution they made to the country's independence or development.
Design work for the road-branding project is complete and will be launched soon.
Mombasa mayor, Mr Khamisi Musa Mwidani, said the project will make Mombasa the darling of many Kenyans.
Working with the road safety department, all the major roads have been marked with zebra crossings to ensure safety of pedestrians and schoolchildren.
At the North Coast, the inter-ministerial committee has picked Jomo Kenyatta Public Beach for improvement.
An additional road to the beach is being put up to ease congestion and allow more motorists and visitors.
A police station is already being put up to beef up security at the recreation centre.
Design work for model kiosks is already in place.
Jomo Kenyatta is today a well-lit recreational facility. Beach operators have ensured discipline is maintained.
Several groups have come up with uniforms and nametags for easy identification. This will also make it easy for visitors to identify the people that they deal with.
In the past, Mwaka says, visitors to Jomo Kenyatta feared losing their property when swimming. This will now be history as personal effects will be put in private lockers.
Mr Anthony Masinde, a beach operator, said the introduction of nametags and uniforms has helped to reduce theft.
Photographers, life savers and those who rent out floaters have different uniforms," he said.
Mr Samuel Mbudza, also a beach operator, said the inter-ministerial committee will put up a watchtower and a quick response team to deal with emergencies.
"There will be three watchtowers equipped with life saving equipment to ensure visitors in distress are assisted immediately," he said.
A parking yard has also been developed to ensure vehicles are parked at a safe distance.
A police station with four cells is being built.
Mwaka says his committee will put up five toilets and an equal number of bathrooms.
A team of experts at the Town Hall has also gone out to survey the possibilities of creating several fire sub stations. The areas identified are Shika Adabu, Kongowea, Bombolulu, Mlaleo and Changamwe. The sub stations will ensure there is a quick response to emergencies.
"We cannot talk of development if we have not taken care of our emergency response, we are thinking ahead to ensure we can respond to any kind of disaster when it occurs," Mwaka said.
In its long term plan, the team has been exploring the possibilities of building an alternative road to the existing Mombasa/Nairobi highway through Mwakirunge to ease congestion and decongest the town.
Design work has already started and is expected to be complete by end of the year.
In the town centre, road crossings have been marked clearly to ensure pedestrians' safety. To ease the traffic congestion at Kibarani, all lorries have been directed to keep to the left. The long-term solution, the police says, is to create a climbing lane at Kibarani.

Original_Search_Title: Agriculture Minister Warns West On Impending Heavy Rains
Headline: Uganda: Agriculture Minister Warns West On Impending Heavy Rains
Body_Text:
Mbarara — THE State Minister for Agriculture, Dr Kibirige Ssebunya, has appealed to farmers in the south western region to prepare for impending heavy rains that are likely to cause floods and landslides.
In a letter to district production departments, Dr Ssebunya said forecasts show the region will get heavy rains which may be above normal starting mid October and lasting up to December. He says these rains will affect crop and livestock production and hence poor incomes and low food security.
Mbarara District Production Officer Beatrice Byarugaba told a news conference at her office on Thursday that the rains are as a result of climatic changes and depletion of the ozone layer.
Rains started early in the region with some devastating effects. Hailstorms have already hit areas of Mwizi Sub County in Isingiro and Bihanga Sub County in Bushenyi leading to loss of houses and food crops like banana plantations, cassava, beans and potato gardens, in the last week. Both are hilly areas.
She says while the east has been severely affected by floods, the west may experience land slides in hilly areas while flat areas will be hit by floods. She advises farmers to dig across hills not uphill in hilly areas and also to use contour farming. "Crops may be damaged by hailstorms while pastures may be affected by floods," she says.
Not only will livestock get affected but the entomology department will be affected.
"In the event of floods we are likely to have poor honey harvests as flowers will be destroyed by the cold weather and bees will starve. Beehives should be sheltered and bees given supplementary feeds, she says. On homesteads she advises that big trees which do not sprout should be cut and others trimmed to avoid them falling on houses and also to open waterways so as flood do not damage homesteads.
The district veterinary officer, Dr John Barigye, at the same news conference said livestock are likely to experience outbreak of cattle diseases. "To minimise on the occurrence of diseases, farmers are advised to de-worm the animals. Spry regularly after rains, follow instructions from veterinary officers and instructions of manufacturers of drugs," he said.
To reduce on death of calves and goat kids Dr Barigye advised that animal shelters be roofed. He, however, says the fisheries department will benefit from the increased waters but damming of ponds should be improved to avoid fish being taken by floods.

Original_Search_Title: Niger Economy, the PPP And Vision 2020
Headline: Nigeria: Niger Economy, the PPP And Vision 2020
Body_Text:
Minna — At any rate, Niger state epitomises the economic backwardness in the northern part of Nigeria where majority of its people live below the stipulated $1 dollar a day. Yet successive governments have made frantic efforts to break the yoke of ravaging poverty with little success recorded.
Critics are of the view that little achievements have been recorded in the first 100 days of the new government. They accused the governor of talking much and doing less in terms of redeeming his pledges to turn the state around.
In the face of the criticsm, the Secretary to the Niger state Government (SSG) Alhaji Mohammed Kuta Yahaya expressed optimism that the state is on the path of economic recovery.
Yahaya told the Daily Trust in Minna that the governor had embraced the Public/partnership initiative as part of the re-engineering strategy to transform the state to the third best economy in the country by 2020.
For now, it would appear that there is apathy towards the economic development policies of former governments, having failed to provide the necessary incentives to woo investors to the state.
But few days after his inauguration, the state governor Muazu Babangida Aliyu tried to put paid to the recurring poverty cycle by opening what many have described as the windows of potential opportunities to prospective investors through the Private-Public Partnership (PPP) initiative.
They say it is a more rational way of sourcing for funds to execute developmental projects without the usual huge commitment of government money.
What is clear is that for ages the people of Niger have had over dose of unfulfilled economic promises that they give no exception to the new governor coming with dreams of a change and unusual business that will bring socio-economic development to the state.
The SSG said the government only need to provide conducive environment for private investors to invest their money. Yahaya told the Business Trust in Minna that the governor had embraced the partnership initiative as part of the re-engineering strategy to transform the state to the best third place economy in the country in 2020.
He said that the people would soon start to reap the dividends of the PPP initiative as government had sealed agreement with investors in the Housing and Agricultural sector. Under the agreements, according to him, a private firm has begun the construction of a 500 housing units named after the former Chief of Army Staff, General Mohammed Wushishi. This, he said was the first step in the construction of 500 houses across the state to curb the acute shortage of accommodation in the state and provide good shelter to the people.
The SSG pointed out that though the General Wushishi housing estate was estimated to cost about N1.4 billion, government is only expected to provide the land and necessary facilities which, he said represented about 25 percent of the total cost of the entire project. On completion, Yahaya said the houses would be allocated to civil servants on owner-occupier basis while others will be sold to the public to recoup the money invested in the project. He described the benefits derivable from this partnership as the first step of dividends of democracy to the people of the state.
Another sector from where government will tap the advantage of the public private partnership, the SSG said, is agriculture. The government, he explained, had reached agreement with foreign partners for the establishment of an ethanol plant and mass production of cassava in the state.
"The overall development strategy of the state is to use the agricultural endowments through private public partnership to develop, industrialize and modernize the state economy for the improvement of the welfare of the people. Agro-based industrialization implies that agriculture and allied industries and services will form the fulcrum of the state's development", he said.
Perhaps, the success story of the General Wushishi Housing Estate may have encouraged the government to go into partnership with another private firm in tackling the health problems in the state.
The government has rolled out a holistic approach to meet the health needs of the people with the construction of three standard hospitals in the rural parts of the senatorial zones of the state to be financed through the PPP initiative.
In addition, the existing general hospitals will be renovated and stocked with drugs while all rural hospitals across the state will be upgraded and equipped to the status of general hospitals. The private firm is expected to release over N5.8 billion for the execution of these projects as interest free loan.
No doubt the PPP initiative has come to the front burner in the socio-economic re-engineering programmes of the Aliyu-led government. The governor had announced that government public/private partnership programme is paying off, explaining that his administration was now in the position to effectively deploy resources to areas that need urgent attention. Addressing the state executive council and other political appointees at a retreat at Maizube farms, the governor explained that the participants were expected to be educated on what the PPP initiative is all about and their role in its successful implementation in the state as a new development strategy.
Aliyu said government had opened discussions with international tourism experts for the development of the Gurara water falls to an international tourism site. He explained that the Gurara waterfall had been laying waste in the past even though it could compete favourably with any tourism site in the world. The governor hoped that the development of the site would attract tourists from within Nigeria and abroad.
Speaking to journalists after inspecting the decayed facilities at the site, the governor explained that the tourism sector was another area where government was going to partner with the private sector. He said that government had compiled an inventory of tourism sites including the Kainji parks in Borgu area of the state for development through the Public/Private Partnership.
The governor said that his administration had gone back to the drawing board on its plan to activate the dormant Airport in Minna with the creation of a free cargo zone. This, he said will allow for efficient utilization of the Airport considering its proximity to the Abuja.
Perhaps the beauty of the PPP initiative admired by government, apart from the long-term benefits is the fact that government can now ensure equal distribution of resources among competing demands for basic amenities and poverty alleviation programmes. However, many people are yet to understand the wisdom behind this partnership initiative. Their expectation was that the governor should have begun the settlement of campaign pledges with the award of contracts on political patronage.
The Senior Special Assistant to the governor on Media and communications, Alhaji Suleyman Jibrin told the Daily Trust that the governor would not pick quarrel with any person who do not know and would want to know the activities of government.
Jibrin said that the transformation of the rural areas form parts of the reform agenda of the governor, explaining that in line with this the governor has ordered the direct allocation of funds to the councils to trigger off and enhanced development at the grassroots level. He pointed out that the previous administration operated a state and local government council joint account system that left the councils with no funds for capital projects, adding that for the first time in eight years, the local governments were last month elevated with over N700 million grants from the state for the execution of capital projects. According to him, government had embarked on the renovation of about 150 primary and post primary schools across the state, adding that the chairmen of the councils were directed to consult with the people in selecting the dilapidated schools that need urgent attention for the renovation exercise in their respective areas.
He said that the fact that government has ordered that statutory allocations should be released directly to the councils would soon activate the execution of people oriented programmes in the rural areas of the state.
Jibrin explained that government determination to develop the state into the best three economies of the country in 2020 was unshakable, adding that it was the pursuit of the realization of this dream that government last week inaugurated the Economic and Social Advisory Council of Niger State (ESACON). The advisory council, he said has been mandated to act as the think- tank of government in the formulation of its policies.
Daily Trust learnt that the ESACON comprises of eminent citizens and elder statesmen including former heads of state General Ibrahim Babangida and General Abdulsalami Abubakar as life patrons while former Secretary to the Government of the Federation Alhaji Shehu Musa will serve as the chairman.
He said that the governor was ready to give the required political will to the economic and social transformation of the state in order to meet the target of repositioning it among the three best economies in the country by 2020.

Original_Search_Title: Independence in France with Juliana
Headline: Uganda: Independence in France with Juliana
Body_Text:
Like any other city, Paris is full of life. The witty conmen who sell fake merchandise, creepy characters that give suspicious glares and tricksters who do everything in their means to unwillingly relieve you of your possessions.
You could know it all, but unlike in other cities like Nairobi , you get tempted to walk on the lonely streets simply because of the allure of Paris.
On Friday October 5, many Ugandans from all over France and living as far as the Sarregue Mines (France - Germany border) and Geneva - Switzerland braved the not so friendly circumstances and biting cold in Paris as the Ugandan Community savoured an early celebration of their country's 45th Independence Anniversary.
Over 600 guests including diplomatic corps accredited to France, permanent delegates to Unesco, friends of Uganda in Paris and the business community crammed the 7th floor of the Unesco headquarters building located on Plot 7, Place de Fontenoy Street to be part of the celebrations.
The Charge d'Affaires of the Ugandan Embassy in France Ms Samantha Sherurah with two Ugandan girls studying in Paris - Agnes Kushemerwerwa and Sandra Nambooze ushered in the guests before unexpected songbird Juliana Kanyomozi got the evening started with the National Anthem.
Known to depart early, Juliana's contagious voice and slick dancing moves forced the diplomats to stay longer.
She had most of the crowd that comprised former Cranes custodian Livingstone Kyobe, Princess Ryan Keza Barigye (Prince John Barigye's daughter), Burundi's Princess Kamatari, Mr Pierre Lys (President of the Uganda-France Friendship Association), Professor Eriabu Lugujja (member of Unesco Executive Board) and Mr Omare Okurut (Secretary General of Uganda National Commission of UNESCO) singing along when she sang Kibaluma and Nkyanoonya.
During her performance Juliana made a killing when she sold over 50 CDs of her new Kanyimbe Album at 15 Euros (Shs37, 200) each.
It was a typical Ugandan night! There was a scramble for the preferred Uganda Waragi and home made cassava chips, samosas and local tunes dominated airplay during the night.
Hadn't it been for the stringent laws, the Independence celebrations would have ended later than midnight when the curtain was drawn on the memorable evening.
As the guests said their 'Au revoirs,' each was handed a Ugandan gift bag with a folder containing history, tourism, trade and investment information on Uganda.
The bag also had a desktop calculator courtesy of SN Brussels, a VCD of Uganda's flora and fauna, instant coffee pods and tots of Uganda Waragi. Real proof of Independence!
Betty Mpologoma goes solo
EDGAR R. BATTE
After years of musical collaborations, Betty Mpologoma feels she is ready to go it alone and give fans her 'exclusivity.' She has set out on a her first musical trip with her Mpaliriza track, off her Big Deal album. She has also shot a video for the song, which she expects to use as a precursor in promoting this album.
Big Deal has songs like Kyaterekera, Nandigumye, Todula and Teli Polisi and the title track Big Deal.
Mpologoma explained her change of career direction on draw backs. "At times, when you are in a band, you feel like you're putting in so much but working for general expenses of the group. You'll work for transport, costumes and generally survival. I guess I can now direct my efforts to my personal career," she said. The singer famed for her other songs like Akasiimu tugende nkakyajinge, Body Guard, Deejay nsomerayo and ekirango, attributes her musical background to her roots.
"Music runs through my family. My grandfather was an artiste, my sister too is one of the budding artistes. I grew up singing in the choir in an Adventist church as early as my Sunday school days," Mpologoma who is also a sister to artiste Phoebe Nassolo adds.
She got her first break through first by voicing ads under VCL, as well as miming with The Ebonies and also featured in their dramas like The Boss and Diamond Ring.
She was with The Ebonies for three years before Diamonds Production took her on in 2000. In 2003 she joined Kat Production and subsequently Jungle Production which she left in July this year.
She has worked with Dream Studio and Kasiwukira for her new album and expects to start off her new solo career with live weekly performances at a place she is yet to confirm.

Original_Search_Title: Bio Fuel - Proda Needs N1b for Upgrading
Headline: Nigeria: Bio Fuel - Proda Needs N1b for Upgrading
Body_Text:
The Project Development Institute {PRODA} has produced a bio fuel from ethanol for the movement of automobiles and other heavy machines in the country
Director general of the institute, Prof Goddy Nkem Onuoha , who made this known yesterday while conducting newsmen round the facilities at the headquarters of the institute in Enugu, disclosed that the PRODA requires about N1 billion for the upgrading of its facilities .
According to him, the bio fuel was produced from the distillation of ethanol to find an alternative source of energy for automobiles that rely heavily on fuel for movement.
Dr. Onuoha explained that the ethanol itself was produced from cassava .
He disclosed that the institute has also manufactured a cassava processing machine for the production of ethanol from cassava.
The DG called on the federal government to release a grant of N1 billion to the research institute to enable it upgrade its facilities to meet up with the challenges posed by globalisation and modern technology.
While lamenting the obsolete nature of most of the machines used in the research institute, he stressed the need to acquire modern machines for the federal government-owned center .
Apart from the production of bio fuel by the PRODA, Onuoha stated that the PRODA has also utilised the local technology and raw materials to produce many other things including briquette from coal used as alternative for kerosene and firewood , Kero Heated Brand Owen, Palm Producing Plants , among others.
He commended the federal government for promising to assist the institute to fully actualise its mandate of giving value to local raw materials and technology.

Original_Search_Title: Use of Cassava for Ethanol Production Defended
Headline: Mozambique: Use of Cassava for Ethanol Production Defended
Body_Text:
Maputo — Mozambican scientists and researchers told an International Symposium on Tropical Roots and Tubers that they are determined to develop varieties of cassava appropriate for the production of bio-fuels.
They were speaking in Maputo on Wednesday, the third day of the meeting, on the theme "Roots and Tubers for the production of bio-fuels: Challenges and Opportunities".
The national coordinator of the Roots and Tubers Programme, Fernando Chitio, said that research is being prepared to identify varieties of cassava for the production of ethanol.
"We are interested in using cassava for the production of bio-fuels, because that will add value to cassava itself", he said.
With annual production of about six million tonnes, Mozambique is the sixth largest producer of cassava in the world. Despite the apparent abundance of cassava, and of land on which it can be grown, there are voices saying that the production of bio-fuels will endanger Mozambican food security and biodiversity.
The fear is that farmers in countries with tropical climates may dedicate their full attention to the cultivation of plants used for the production of bio-fuels to the detriment of their own food security.
Chitio dismissed such fears, arguing that farmers will not cut their own throats, but will only sell surplus cassava for biofuels, after taking what they need for their own consumption.
As raw material for industry, cassava would attract investment and stimulate productivity, he said, and it also offers the farmers an opportunity to sell it as a cash crop.
The executive director of the Kenya-based African Agricultural Technological Foundation (AATF), Mpoko Bokanga, said that there is no reason to fear that the use of cassava for the production of ethanol might jeopardise food security and increase poverty in Africa. "On the contrary, people should be more optimistic, because opportunities will be opened to solve the problem of poverty", he said.
He further added that time will come when only refuse, such as the peel, and the leaves will be used to produce ethanol, and the most important part will be for human consumption.
"The African Continent has major potential to become a true actor in the issue of bio-fuels", said Bokanga, adding that ethanol should be seen as a stage in the cycle of development, because new technologies will be developed and the reality will be different within the coming 10 to 20 years.
Eduardo de Sousa, and Marco Patino, from Brazil, said that each country should not only determine its capacity to produce raw materials for bio-fuels, but should also continue using the land reserved for agriculture to produce food.
In most cases, including Brazil, they claimed, there is enough land for agriculture to produce raw materials for bio-fuels without endangering food security.
"Another important aspect is the fact that industry is the driving force to reduce hunger. As jobs are being created in service sectors to support industry, this will help people out of poverty", said Sousa.
He added that "small farmers may sell their cassava and generate financial resources to buy other foodstuffs and still have enough of it for their normal diet".

Original_Search_Title: Beyond Oil And Gas - Options for Development of a Robust Ijaw
Headline: Nigeria: Beyond Oil And Gas - Options for Development of a Robust Ijaw
Body_Text:
Abuja — Decades ago, when Isaac boro, the legendary Ijaw patriot took up arms to liberate his people, he envisaged a future in which the Ijaws would be in control of not just their political but also their economic destiny. His rebellion failed and four decades later, the Ijaws and other people of the Niger Delta, have not made economic progress commensurate with their resources and contributions to the Nigerian nation. Oil and gas from the Niger Delta have built brand new cities like Abuja , produced Nigerians who will be billionaires in any currency and transformed the economic landscape of the country. However, Ijaw land has, in some parts, remained much what it was in the 60's, so much that most parts of the Ijaw land are accessible only by water. Apart from the appropriation of the petroleum resources of the Ijaw land by the Nigerian nation, the people have enough resources to transform themselves into a formidable economic group. Besides, the tragedy of Nigeria 's economic development is that no one thinks of the "oil box".
With gratitude to Mother Nature, Ijaw land is well endowed by a mix of natural resources and an enterprising population. The Niger Delta, home to the Ijaws, is a treasure trough. As the world's third largest wetland, it is also an area of scenic beauty. It has a beautiful coastal line and a network of rivers and creeks. It is pointless mentioning that oil was first drilled from Oloibiri in Bayelsa State, in 1958.
What is important is that the area is rich in arable lands which can sustain the production of rice, plantain, cassava, sugar, oil palm, rubber and coconut. The pristine swamp allows for the cultivation of raffia palm, which produces sap from which the popular 'kakai' is distilled. The gin was such a threat to whisky produced by the colonialists that they had called it' illicit gin'. The waters of Ijaw land and the adjoining continental shelf are rich in fishery resources. Undernealth the land are deposits of salt, clay and fine sand.
What is needed today is the harnessing of these resources and the empowerment of the people to transform Ijaw land into an economic powerhouse. Given where we are today, this is possible in another ten years. However, as the Toyota advert goes, 'the foundation is investing in infrastructure, investing in people and developing the potential.
According to Moffat Ekoriko of News Africa who spoke in London, it is a known fact that there is a need for sustainable infrastructure. Most of the Ijaw lands lack basic infrastructure like power and transportation. This state of affairs is hampering investment and economic development. Given the terrain of the area, there is a competitive disadvantage to any entrepreneur vis-à-vis other parts of the country. The area therefore, needs superior infrastructure and incentives to overcome this.
In the past, the state government lacked the resources to tackle infrastructural problems. Everyone waited on the federal government, which has failed the people of the area in this respect. With the introduction of the 13 percent derivation, the state governments are in position to execute major infrastructural projects. The emphasis should be on power and developing a transport network to make the area accessible. The area's riverine terrain can be turned into an advantage, if the focus is on developing water transportation to complement the existing road network. Availability of power will have a revolutionary economic effect. It will lure investors, empower artisans and improve the quality of life. The Niger Delta Development Commission can also play a key role here. At present, the commission is saddled with doing everything from the construction of roads to clearing of refuse. However, the commission will have a better legacy if its resources are concentrated on the development of infrastructure.
In the 1950's, Chief Obafemi Awolowo, the revered Yoruba leader embarked on a free education programme. At the time, his policy was derided by the World Bank as unworkable and scorned by other regional leaders. In a matter of decades, the impact of the policy transformed not just the social, but also the economic landscape of the Yoruba land. Today, the Yorubas are in commanding heights of the Nigerian economy, ahead of all other groups in social indexes and have spawned a formidable diaspora. What this illustration means is that there is no investment, which can transform Ijawland like social infrastructure: equipping the human resources to master their environment and innovate for tomorrow. The Bayelsa State government's policy of local and foreign training of qualified indigenes is a laudable move, which must be sustained. Ijaw land should focus on the mass education of its people. Education should be free and qualitative. Free education means all the talents of Ijaw lnd irrespective of their parental background can be tapped and developed. With the will and right investment, a literacy level of 70 per cent is possible in the next ten years.
In the ongoing reforms of the Nigerian economy, the Ijaws have fared poorly and assuming vantage economic positions. The core reason that the Ijaws have been excluded from the commanding heights of the economy is due to what appears to be a deliberate federal policy. One is yet to recall any Ijaw man benefiting from oil lifting contracts or the allocation of oil Blocs.
Unfortunately, these patronages are the shortest cuts to building up capital. The Ijaws have to create economic champions either through a deliberate policy of the state government patronage or the pulling together of capitals. Even at the national level, the need to create economic champions has been recognised. This informs the setting-up and patronage of Transcorp by the presidency. With the political heights the Ijaws had recently attained, creating the Ijaws as economic champions should be the priority for the Ijaws in federal politics.
The future, beyond oil and gas, also lies in developing the economic potentials of Ijaw land. Top on the list is the tourism potential. Owing to the ecosystem of the area, Bayelsa State, for instance, offers enormous potentials for ecotourism. This type of tourism, based on conservation, needs very litle investment. Instead of five-star hotels, you need huts; instead of a good road network, you need bush paths; instead of electricity, you need bush lamps. The multiplier effects are however, enormous. It will empower communities, create jobs and guarantee that the environment yields economic benefits at no cost to its future. The Ijaws can also capitalise on their coastlines by building holiday resorts.
Another ignored potential is the distillery industry, which is a core vocation of the people. This cottage industry has the potential to transform Ijaw land. All the that is needed is packaging, excellent branding and sleek marketing of the gin produced across the Russian, their vodka, there is no reason why kakai cannot assume the same status. Just by forming an international marketing cooperative support by the government, this cottage industry can become a big international business.
With its excellent climate and arable land, the Ijawland can become an agricultural country. Projects like the Peremabiri rice project can lead the way to large cultivation of various crops. Ijaw economy can also be built by developing the traditional occupation of the people: fishing. The industry needs support for fishermen to acquire vessels. It also needs investment in the canning plant and cold storage for the preservation of catch.

Original_Search_Title: Agriculture to Aid Flood-Hit Farmers
Headline: Uganda: Agriculture to Aid Flood-Hit Farmers
Body_Text:
Kampala — The Ministry of Agriculture, Animal Industry and Fisheries has earmarked Shs4.8 billion to enable flood-hit farmers in the north and eastern regions resume food production as soon as the floods recede.
"The money will be used, among other things, to acquire high yielding and fast growing crops for staple food like cassava stems, potato vines, cow peas, and upland rice," said Mr Okaasai Opolot, the commissioner for crop production and marketing.
Mr Opolot was addressing reporters at the Media Centre yesterday ahead of the 27th World Food Day/Tele food celebrations to be held on October 16, in Kalangala District.
The Assistant Representative of the Food and Agricultural Organisation said nearly 300,000 people have been affected by the floods. The most affected are Amuria, Katakwi, Soroti, Kumi, Bukedea, Lira and Kaberamaido districts.
In his speech read by Mr Opolot, the Minister for Agriculture, Animal, Industry and Fisheries, Mr Hillary Onek, said the National Agricultural Research Organisation (Naro) has developed crop varieties and other technologies to boost food production in the country.

Original_Search_Title: Another Boy Killed in Bomb Blast
Headline: Uganda: Another Boy Killed in Bomb Blast
Body_Text:
Kampala — A bomb exploded yesterday in Mbogwe village Buhanika sub-county in Hoima district, killing a 13-year-old boy. The second victim, a sister to the deceased, was rushed to Hoima Hospital in critical condition following the incident at Buhanika, about 10kms from Hoima town.
The officer in charge of crime at Hoima Police Station, John Bahemuka, identified the deceased as Bernard Atuhairwe, a Primary Five pupil at St. Petero Paul Primary School in Buhanika. His sister, Angella Nsungwa, 15, is a Primary Six pupil in the same school.
Their mother, Christine Kinura, 50, said: "My children told me that somebody had uprooted our cassava. I asked them to go and check, only to hear a loud sound. When I rushed there, I found my children in a pool of blood," she said, praying that at least her daughter survives.
