This is the second bomb to explode in two days in Bunyoro region. A bomb exploded in Bulisa on Sunday, killing one man and injuring his two brothers, who are now admitted in Hoima Hospital. The three were sons to Leuben Kasumba, a herdsman.
The mid-western regional criminal investigations department chief, James Bangirana, said the bomb was a tortoise grenade. "We are investigating the incident because cases of bomb blasts are becoming common in the region. We shall use radio messages to sensitise the public about this problem."
The area MP, Kiiza Rwebembera, appealed to the Government to investigate the source of the grenades.

Original_Search_Title: 'Five Million Citizens Malnourished'
Headline: Uganda: 'Five Million Citizens Malnourished'
Body_Text:
Kampala — ABOUT five million people in Uganda are malnourished, according to the agriculture ministry. Okaasi Opolot, the commissioner for production and marketing, said most of them live in the rural areas.
About 7.75 million in the countryside face food insecurity, he said.
Briefing journalists about the World Food Day preparations, he said another four million of the rural population were moderately vulnerable to food insecurity.
Chronic malnutrition among children is widespread in Uganda, he added. "Stunting affects a third of the children in rural areas."
Okaasi appealed to Ugandans to embrace the Government programmes aimed at eradicating poverty through increased agricultural production and productivity to ensure food security and increased household incomes.
He repeated the Government's commitment to the bonna baggaggawale (Prosperity-for-All) programme aimed at increasing farmers income by transforming the peasant households from primitive, unplanned production methods to commercial production.
Okaasi announced that the Food and Agriculture Organisation (FAO) had mobilised $891,000 (about sh1.6b) for the purchase of improved early maturing sweet potato vines, cassava cuttings, cow peas and agricultural implements in the north and eastern flood-hit parts of the country.
He encouraged Ugandans to take on aquaculture (fish growing) to avoid eating fish skeletons, which are rejected by fish factories.
He blamed the drop in fish stocks in Uganda's lakes and rivers to the introduction of bigger species, which eat others as well as encroachment on the wetlands.
"Let every Ugandan grow fish and we shall not complain again about eating fish bones, he advised.
Charles Owach, the assistant FAO representative, appealed to Ugandans to avoid being complacent as the number of malnourished people had reduced from 26% in 1995 to 19% in 2004.
"This is still a very high figure and a lot is still needed to improve the situation."
The World Food Day, slated for October 16, will be held in Kalangala district with the theme: "Right to Food."

Original_Search_Title: FG Told to be Firm on Cassava Initiative
Headline: Nigeria: FG Told to be Firm on Cassava Initiative
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Federal Government has been told to be firm in the implementation of the presidential initiative on cassava. This is with a view to ensuring that flour millers in the country make use of five per cent cassava content in their products as stipulated in the initiative.
The Chairman of All Farmers Association of Nigeria (AFAN), Dr. Tunde Arosanyin, made the call in Lokoja, in an interview. He traced the glut that characterised the production of cassava in many states of the federation to a lack of effective implementation of the objectives of the policy. According to him, the absence of enforcement on the flour millers to comply with the mandate as contained in the initiative has brought a negative effect on cassava processing plants.
"The situation is such that most flour millers in the country have poor responses to the initiative to include cassava in their products because of the lapses in its implementation," Arosanyin said.
This development, he further argued, had led to the under-utilisation of cassava for industrial products thereby causing glut in the face of the increasing production of cassava by farmers.
The AFAN chairman also stressed the need for the Federal Government to encourage small-scale business operators to identify with cassava producers in the utilisation of cassava by helping them to establish cottage industries.
According to him, such assistance can be in the form of granting of soft loans to enable them establish industries that will process cassava, "from the raw stage tointermediate raw materials for the bigger industries to process for finished industrial products".
He assured that such process would lead to massive production of industrial products like starch, cassava chips and cassava cake among others which could be consumed locally and internationally.
Arosanyin said that apart from earning enormous foreign exchange, the effort would equally lead to employment of thousands of employable but jobless youths, roaming the streets of the nation.
"When this is done, you will see that more small-scale industries will spring up to make use of the cassava products, many of which now rot away," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Flood Renders 4 Communities Homeless
Headline: Nigeria: Flood Renders 4 Communities Homeless
Body_Text:
Abakaliki — No less than 2,700 people in four communities of Ikwo Local Government Area, Ebonyi State have been rendered homeless by a rampaging flood which also destroyed crops including rice and yam worth about N100 million.
The overflow of a river in Cross River State after torrential rain caused the flood, which swept the border communities of Ndiagu Amagu, Ndiagu Igbudu, Ndiagu Inyimagu and Ndiagu Achara in the Ikwo council area, home of the state governor, Chief Martin Elechi.
Residents of the four border towns between Cross River and Ebonyi states have reportedly deserted the area, some of them taking refuge in the neighbouring communities while others are in some churches at the state capital, Abakaliki.
Although no life was lost at the time of filing this report some of the communities Leaders, Elder Joseph Chukwu of Odom Owo village, S.O. Nwuze of Akawuju Inyimagu and John Nte of Ndiagu Achala called for quick intervention of the state and federal emergency agencies saying that if urgent measures were not taken, the state of affairs could lead to the extinction of the communities.
Elder Joseph Chukwu, visibly disheartened by the situation explained that their major source of livelihood being rice and yam farming but with devastation of all that, it would be difficult to start life afresh.
A widow, Agnes whose hut was covered by the flood said it took magnanimity of the youths in the area to build the shelter for her and that with the development; she had neither means nor place to go with her four siblings.
The state Commissioner for Loans and Grants, Elder Fred Udeogu who hails from the area was met at the scene with officials of State Emergency Management Agency (SEMA).
Udeogu lamented that the victims of the flood are major rice and yam farmers in the state expressing worries that with the disaster, there was likely going to be acute food scarcity in the state and the neighbouring ones who rely on them for supply of food.
He told reporters that, "This is one of worst flood disaster, there is massive destruction of rice, yam, cassava and other crops, and the major problem is that the water is stagnant, meaning that the crops will all be destroyed, so, it is already a colossal loss, you can see it, the damages is worth close to N100 millions".
"The problem is that there is no immediate solution, the only solution is the dredging of the Cross River because this has been an annual incident but that of this year is terrible, the state government is not insensitive to the problem but no state government can alone handle this, so, the obvious thing is that the federal government should come in to help address the problems," Udeagu stated.
He said, "the people living within this riversides are the agricultural base of Ebonyi State, they have the most fertile soil, they can feed the nation if the things we are planning sail through, but should this situation persist, there could be acute shortage of rice and yam in this part of the country"
The commissioner explained that the state government was already putting into effect provision of feeder roads in the areas as to cause easy movement of farm produce from the communities to the urban markets but that they would not be deterred by the disaster.

Original_Search_Title: Heavy Rains Hit Bushenyi, Destroy Crops
Headline: Uganda: Heavy Rains Hit Bushenyi, Destroy Crops
Body_Text:
Bushenyi — RESIDENTS of Buhweju County in Bushenyi District have appealed to the government for relief assistance after heavy rains destroyed their homes and gardens.
The rains that fell on Saturday lasted several hours, destroying banana, cassava, peas and bean gardens and the roofs of schools and homes in Bihanga Sub-county.
The Bihanga LC 5 councilor Mr Godfrey Kabandize, told Daily Monitor on Monday that residents are afraid of an outbreak of famine in the area.
The some of the affected families are in the villages of Rutehe, Kashambya, Karembe, Ryabihogo, Nyakishenyi and Rukiri among others.
On resident, Mr Robert Jaluwa told Daily Monitor that he lost about three acres of banana plantation and his cassava garden were destroyed.
"I have six children to feed. After a week this [small] matooke [garden] will have been exhausted," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Flood Displaces 3,000
Headline: Nigeria: Flood Sacks 3,000 in Ebonyi
Body_Text:
Abakaliki — Not less than 3000 inhabitants in four boarder communities of Ikwo local government area, Ebonyi State have been sacked by surging flood from the banks of Cross River that demarcates Ebonyi from Cross River State.
Consequently, food crops estimated at over N100 million were equally lost to the torrent that overflowed the communities of Ndiagu-Amagu, Ndiagu-Igbudu, Ndiagu-Inyimagu and Ndiagu-Achara, all in the Ikwo, home council area of the Ebonyi State Governor, Chief Martin Elechi.
Inhabitants of the areas have therefore fled, some of them taking refuge in the neighbouring communities while others are temporarily located in churches in Abakaliki, the state capital.
Leaders of the communities have in effect pleaded with the state and federal governments to come to their aid before the four communities go into extinction. One of the elders of the communities, Joseph Chukwu, said that with the devastation on their major source of livelihood which is rice and yam farming, it would be difficult to start all over again.
Ebonyi State Commissioner for Loans and Grants, Elder Fred Udeogu, was seen meeting with officials of State Emergency Management Agency (SEMA) over the tragedy.
Udeogu told newsmen that the victims of the flood are major rice and yam producers in the state and expressed fears that the disaster is likely to cause acute food scarcity in Ebonyi and other neighbouring states that rely on Ebonyi for supply of food.
Udeogu said: "This is one of worst flood disaster, there is massive destruction of rice, yam, cassava and other crops, and the major problem is that the water is stagnant, meaning that the crops will all be destroyed, so, it is already a colossal loss, you can see it, the damages is worth close to N100 millions.
"The problem is that there is no immediate solution, the only solution is the dredging of the Cross River because this has been an annual incident but that of this year is terrible, the state government is not insensitive to the problem but no state government can alone handle this, so, the obvious thing is that the federal government should come in to help address the problems."

Original_Search_Title: Flood Renders 3000 Homeless
Headline: Nigeria: Flood Renders 3000 Homeless
Body_Text:
Abakaliki — No fewer than 3000 persons, most of them widows and indigent children were yesterday rendered homeless, while properties, including food crops said to be worth over N100 million were totally destroyed, following an early morning flood that swept through four communities in Ikwo Local Government area of Ebonyi State.
The torrent, according to eyewitness account, was an over flow from the bank of a river in Cross River State that swept Ndiagu Amagu, Ndiagu Igbudu, Ndiagu Inyimagu and Ndiagu Achara, which indidentally is the home council of Governor Martin Elechi.
Already, indigenes of the once peaceful towns have fled the areas, some of them taking refuge in the neighbouring communities, while others are in Churches at the state capital, Abakaliki.
Some of the communities' leaders, Elder Joseph Chukwu of Odom Owo Village, S.O. Nwuze of Akawuju Inyimagu, John Nte of Ndiagu Achala have cried out to both federal and state governments, saying unless urgent steps were taken to check the situation, the communities may go into extinction.
Elder Joseph Chukwu, visibly disheartened by the situation explained that their major source of livelihood is rice and yam farming and that with devastation of all that, it would be difficult to start life afresh.A widow, Agnes who was met watching her hut covered by the flood said it took magnanimity of the youths in the area to build the shelter for her and that with the development; she had neither means nor place to go with her four siblings.When, reoporters visited the place, officials of the State Emergence Management Agency, (SEMA) were seen being conducted round by the Commissioner for Loans and Grants, Elder Fred Udeogu who hails from the area.
Udeogu lamented that the victims of the flood are major rice and yam farmers in the state expressing worries that with the disaster, there was likely going to be acute food scarcity in the state and the neighbouring ones who rely on them for supply of food."This is one of worst flood disaster that we've witnessed in recent time, there is massive destruction of rice, yam, cassava and other crops, even properties, and the major problem is that the water is stagnant, meaning that the crops will all be destroyed, so, it is already a colossal loss, you can see it, the damages is worth close to #100 millions.
"The problem is that there is no immediate solution, the only solution is the dredging of the Cross River because this has been an annual incident but that of this year is terrible, the state government is not insensitive to the problem but no state government can alone handle this, so, the obvious thing is that the federal government should come in to help address the problems" Udeagu stated.Continuing, the Commissioner stated;"the people living within this riverside are the agricultural base of Ebonyi state, they have the most fertile soil, they can feed the Nigerian nation if the things we are planning sail through, but should this situation persist, there could be acute shortage of rice and yam in this part of the country" The Commissioner explained that the state government was already putting into effect provision of feeder roads in the areas as to cause easy movement of farm produce from the communities to the urban markets but that they would not be deterred by the sudden development.

Original_Search_Title: Heavy Rains Destroy Four Mbarara Schools
Headline: Uganda: Heavy Rains Destroy Four Mbarara Schools
Body_Text:
Kampala — HEAVY rains on Saturday hit Mwizi sub-county in Mbarara district damaging four schools, 30 houses, a church, parish offices and crops. The sub-county chairman, Vanansi Rurema, said the schools were St. Jude's Bushweere, Kikunda and Kyonyo primary schools and Bushweere United Secondary School.
At St. Judes, six classrooms and a library were damaged. The roofs of some teachers' quarters, school kitchen and store were blown off, he said.
A classroom and staff room at Kikunda Primary School were damaged.
Rurema said a classroom at Kyonyo Primary School was also damaged, while three classrooms and an office at Bushwere Secondary School were damaged. Laboratory and teaching aids were also destroyed.
Rurema added that Kyonyo Catholic Church was also destroyed.
Over 200 hectares of banana plantations, beans, cassava, sweet and Irish potatoes and sorghum were destroyed, he noted.
The Mbarara district inspector of schools, Chris Mbabazi, expressed concern that the pupils did not have classrooms.
Dennis Tumusime, a resident of Mwizi whose children study at Bushwere Secondary School, said: "We are distressed! We started the secondary school to help children whose parents could not afford to take them to far-away schools but now the school is destroyed."
The LC2 chairman of Bushwere parish, Anthony Tumukunzi, called upon the Government to assist the victims.
The Mbarara district LC5 chairman, Yaguma Rutashokwa, accompanied by district leaders visited the area on Monday.
Yaguma contributed 100 iron sheets and other building materials to St. Jude's Primary School and promised that the district would contribute sh30m to re-build the classrooms. He said a report on the issue would be sent to the Government for more assistance.
Yaguma advised the residents to use strong building materials for their houses and schools because they are located in hilly places, which are prone to disasters.

Original_Search_Title: Scientific Research Must Guarantee Food Security
Headline: Mozambique: Scientific Research Must Guarantee Food Security
Body_Text:
Maputo — Mozambican Agriculture Minister Erasmo Muhate said in Maputo on Monday that scientific research must contribute to guaranteeing the food security of subsistence farmers.
He was speaking during the opening session of an International Symposium on the Cultivation of Tropical Roots and Tubers, in Maputo, attended by scientists from several African countries.
'Agriculture in Africa is the main socio-economic activity representing a source of income that satisfies the basic needs of the people', said Muhate.
He added that roots and tubers play an important role in guaranteeing food and nutritional security, and hence poverty relief among the rural population.
In Mozambique, cassava is second only to maize as a staple food, and it is the mainstay of the rural diet in areas prone to drought.
Cassava production in the country is estimated at six million tonnes a year, of which 70 per cent is consumed by the family sector.
During the five day meeting, the participants will discuss, among other issues, progress in scientific research on roots and tubers, and will define the effective implementation of strategies to increase agricultural production.
The main aim is to ensure that the results of research are translated into improved living conditions of communities, using roots and tubers not only in their own diet but also as a source of income.
Muhate expressed hope that the results to be presented during the meeting will be transmitted to agricultural services, and through them to the farmers in order to increase agricultural production and improve nutrition.
'We encourage participative and multidisciplinary research, taking into account agro-ecological and socio-cultural differences, the regional advantages, and the impact of the added value of the agricultural products', he said.

Original_Search_Title: Osunbor Decries Neglect of Cassava Plant
Headline: Nigeria: Osunbor Decries Neglect of Cassava Plant
Body_Text:
Benin — Edo State governor, Professor Oserheimen Osunbor has decried the state of neglect of the ultra modern Cassavita plant at Uromi, saying that it was a tragedy that the state-owned processing factory, was being allowed to remain domant after attaining 90 per cent completion.
It would be recalled that the administration of former Governor Lucky Igbinedion, had initiated and completed the factory as part of its industrialization programme.
It was designed to process cassava into finished Industrial starch for textile factories and other allied companies.
The governor during his maiden inspection visit to the factory explained that it didn't make any sense to sink so much money into a project and allow it to remain in a state of neglect.
He observed that a lot of equipment was on ground with a number of structural facilities, noting that it was unfortunate that no further progress had been made since July 2006 when the Plant had a successful test run.
The governor disclosed that funds would immediately be provided to enable the state government put the factory to use soon and directed the contractors to mobilize to site, stressing that it was the people's expectation that the facility which had been on the drawing board for the past eight years would have been functional by now.
While expressing the determination of government to make it one of the best cassavita processing factories in the country, Professor Osunbor assured the community that the Uromi-Amendokhian-Ugboha road would be reconstructed with adequate drainages.
The governor, who was conducted round the premises by the company's Production Engineer, Mr. Emmanuel Oghudu, was told by the State Commissioner for
Commerce and Industry, Dr. Mrs. Abieyuwa Osemwenkha that the establishment still needed vital equipment especially in the staff clinic and the Quality Control Laboratory. Mrs. Osemwenkha also informed the governor that the salaries of the workers had not been paid for the past 16 months.

Original_Search_Title: President Opens Aids Drugs Factory
Headline: Uganda: Museveni Opens Aids Drugs Factory
Body_Text:
Kampala — PRESIDENT Yoweri Museveni has said some people within the Government tried to sabotage the construction of Africa's first plant to manufacture the full triple therapy combination of anti-retroviral medicines.
"I supported the construction of this plant immediately because we have a big number of our people with malaria and AIDS," the President said yesterday at the inauguration of the $30m hi-tech pharmaceutical factory in Luzira that will also manufacture anti-malarial drugs.
"It is very embarrassing and annoying that there are some saboteurs who were opposed to this project. They prefer to import. We cannot keep importing drugs."
Without naming them, the President said there were people in the system who did not see the "wisdom" of such a factory in Uganda. "They dragged their feet to sabotage it," Museveni said.
The plant, which will in a first phase produce two million tablets per day, is a partnership project between the Government, Quality Chemical Industries, a local company, and Cipla, one of the world's leading generic manufacturers.
The President was responding to complaints by Emmanuel Katongole of Quality Chemical Industries that the Government had not met its side of the agreement.
Katongole said the Government would provide a guarantee to buy the drugs when production started.
"The Government of Uganda owes us this favour because we fulfilled our side of the agreement. We delivered the plant in the agreed one year. We request that the Government buys the drugs as agreed," he said as hundreds of workers cheered.
In the absence of a Government guarantee, the company initially experienced difficulties raising the necessary funds, he added.
"We had to desperately look for $25.8 million. We have a $4.8million funding gap."
He also lamented that the construction of the plant was hampered by frequent electricity failures, the increase of cement and fuel taxes, the rise in the price of steel, labour, construction materials, and bank rates from 8% to 9.5%.
"You see in the government side we also have opposition that sabotages programmes. But they have no choice but to do what is expected of them because ours is an elected government," Museveni said.
He denounced leaders whom he described as "blind, ignorant and defiant" and praised Vice-President Prof. Gilbert Bukenya for following the plant project to the end.
He assured the company that the Government would fulfill its obligations. "We are going to buy the drugs you will manufacture. But I must stress that the drugs should be of comparable quality, price and time-bound. There is no way I can refuse to drink my own mubisi (banana juice)."
On allegations that he had given away Uganda Prisons land, Museveni triggered laughter when he said: "These 15 acres that I gave for this ultra-modern plant will earn an estimated $36m per year. Could we earn that amount of money from the potatoes, cassava and maize that were here?"
He urged the company to build an active pharmaceutical ingredient factory to consume locally grown anti-malarial products like Artemisia in Kabale, stressing that this move would create more employment, especially in the countryside.
Quality Chemical Industries director Fred Mutebi Kitaka said the production of ARVs in Uganda would help lower the cost of sh28,000 per dose per month for a patient.
"Of the 300,000 people in Uganda who need anti-retroviral treatment, about 100,000 have access to the lifeprolonging drugs. With time this plant will help lower the cost for ordinary patients. At some point the cost of the drug was an exorbitant $1,500 per dose per month."
The Cipla managing director, Amar Lulla, pointed out that the new plant had sophisticated technology and should be looked after carefully.
"I know that infant mortality is very high in Africa, but I request the Government of Uganda to nurture this baby so that you can get the real fruits for your society," he cautioned.
Museveni, accompanied by his wife Janet, cut a tape and planted a cinnamon tree to inaugurate the plant before watching the first tablets roll off the belt.

Original_Search_Title: The Lawyer as Brand Consultant
Headline: Nigeria: The Lawyer as Brand Consultant
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Alder Group, consisting Alder Consulting, Alder B&M, Alder IT and Alder Research is well known in the country today.
Amongst its well known and sometimes controversial programmes are: mind the_gap, as well as the Heart of Africa project. Alder was also behind the successful Orange Rules campaign of GT Bank recently. But not many know that the founder of this policy and strategy firm, Leke Alder, is also a lawyer. FUNKE ABOYADE spent an evening with him recently, to find out just what makes this extraordinary man of ideas tick
For Oluwaleke Alder, 'Every Nigerian is a walking billboard!' Not a surprising summation from Leke, he is (what else?) a brand consultant. He runs Alder, the policy, strategy and brand consulting firm, arguably, the country's number one branding firm.
But it hasn't always been life on easy street for the 43 year old bundle of energy who at 10 knew he definitely wanted to become a lawyer. At that young age, he'd been fascinated by a TV Docudrama, The Main Chance, featuring a brilliant, confident, articulate and charismatic Attorney. 'You see a dramatisation of a successful life and you just want to identify with that'.
Taking JAMB at 16, straight out of Kings College, Lagos and coming second out of thousands of candidates, Leke headed for the University of Ife for his LL.B which he obtained in 1985. The following year, he achieved his childhood dream and became a lawyer.
He practised law in Lagos for some two or three years post-NYSC and then, one day finally faced up to the fact that the drudgery of practice was not for him and that his arty, creative side would not be suppressed any longer. He'd lost interest in law progressively.
As a child, Leke was already doing professional art work for people. 'I was getting paid to design banners and the like' he recalls. At University he designed wedding invitation cards. He also had a successful art exhibition. During his NYSC year, he often found himself buying expensive art equipment rather than law books. The artist in Leke Alder was clear for all to see. 'None of my friends' parents ever believed I'd practise law, because of the many other things I could do'.
On that fateful day in chambers, he'd asked himself, 'If I die now, will I be a happy person? I realised it would be a total waste so I picked up a piece of paper and resigned there and then. I knew if I thought about it, I would never do it'.
He started a greeting card company with two other friends, but the restless young ex-lawyer felt stifled. They also did cartoons, graphics, production and the like. 'We were doing very well, but I found it limiting for me. I needed to start business, on my own this time, so I could fully express myself'.
He pulled out and shortly afterwards, decided to get hitched aged just 25. 'At that time, I had N20 left'.
And so, armed with N17.50 out of that amount and a bride, and with nowhere to go but up, Leke Alder took a giant step of faith and began the journey into the rest of his life and career. He purchased some cardboard, did some cartoons, put them in frames the newly weds had received as gifts, took them to Church and they were an overwhelming success! The young man made N200; not bad for a start. But, he says, 'it wasn't the money; it was the fact that I could do something that people loved'.
His art works were soon fetching decent sums, especially as he was one of the few people who could do airbrush painting since the equipment required a great degree of dexterity to manipulate.
The turning point in his career came when he was approached by a telephone company for marketing strategies. They'd seen a business card size marketing he'd done for a struggling company. The cards said, If Brother Typewriters had existed at the time of Moses, he would have used it to write the Ten Commandments.
'They said, "Who did this?". It is a prophetic question that has dodged his footsteps all his life.
Leke came up with a three-page marketing plan for the UK-based company, supported with an advert design he'd done himself. 'It was conversational marketing; you engage the customer at the visceral level. The Managing Director, a Nigerian, understood. He offered me a six figure sum. To put things in perspective, a Mercedes Benz cost N50,000 then'.
Within six months, the company had made N100m, on the strength of Leke's three pager; they doubled their retainer fee and commissioned him for the following year's advert.
Another defining moment for him was when a Lebanese client of his approached him to do a low budget Invitation Card for a classy exhibition. Talented Leke had no difficulty in coming up with a well designed letter, content and all. 'He marketed the Italian Embassy who told him to go and bring me.'
By the end of that year, Leke had signed on the Italian establishment in Nigeria - the Italian Embassy, Italian School and Italian Cultural Institute. 'I remember getting back home one day after a meeting with the Italian Embassy and seeing a car from what I thought was the Embassy waiting for me, and I thought, "these people are confused!" I didn't realise the car was from another Italian establishment!'
Wondering one day about how to expand his business, he had a flash of inspiration to go into full time consulting. There was just one snag - he'd never worked in a consulting firm. 'I didn't even know what they did!'
What he did however know was that whenever someone brought a problem to him, he solved it.
'Image consulting was my inspiration. The question was, what was image consulting? You had advertisers and marketers, but no image consultants'.
He decided to explore the subject and in no time, came to the realisation that 'what we term "image" is the external manifestation of inner realities. It meant you could only change a company from the inside. If you could marry the change from the inside with the manifestation of outside change, if you got the internal fundamentals right, you could express it creatively on the outside. Simply put, a nexus between the organisation and its publics.You had a recipe for success! '
And so was born, Alder Consulting, where the strategic is married to the creative. In Alder's employ are found mathematicians, lawyers, medical doctors, computer scientists, sociologists, psychologists and just about any other professional under the sun, all happily and harmoniously working together.
So what exactly do they all do? 'Alder is a policy strategy and brand organisation, with capabilities in research, IT, media and consulting' he explains.
Until recently, they signed off their letters with, Passionate About Progression. Not for them, the usual Yours Faithfully. These days, their letters are more likely to be signed off, Cogito Ergo Sum (I think, therefore, I am) - a Latin phrase all lawyers are familiar with. Perhaps then, it's safe to say once a lawyer always a lawyer as the lawyer turned brand consultant seems to have his profession deeply ingrained in him after all.
But passionate about the progression of what?
'Nation states and brands, about nation building and the development of economies.'
How so? 'Every six months or so, we review our vision and mission. We came to the conclusion that no matter how successful we became, if we didn't do something about our country, not only was our success diminished, even our lives would become greatly diminished. A rich man on a street full of beggarly elements is a poor man still.'
Concerned about the state of the nation some years ago, Alder staff locked up themselves at The Sofitel, Ikoyi and decided they would brainstorm and find a contributory solution to Nigeria's problems.
'We resolved three things: what became known as The Heart of Africa Project; the introduction of branding as a discipline in Nigeria and a heavy social responsibility focus'.
Alder began its annual branding seminars - five so far; instituted a half a million Naira prize, the Alder Prize, for any idea that can move the nation forward - a UNIBEN student won the prize last year; started the Alder Internship Programme, with students from Nigeria, the UK, US and Belgium taking part so far; founded the Alder Foundation aimed at relief for the poor.
Every year, Alder holds a photo exhibition, with Leke's photos from all over the world exhibited and sold. Leke is a keen photographer and has an extremely well equipped studio in his home.
On a regular basis, the Foundation supports orphanages and refugee camps such as the recently closed Liberian Refugee Camp in Ogun State. Twice weekly, the destitute who live under Lagos bridges are fed breakfast.
They also have an Extreme Life Makeover programme, in which an indigent family is relocated, their rent paid for two or three years and the house kitted with furniture, gadgets and even the little things, even tampons! 'Sometimes' says this extraordinary man, 'people just need that shove to make them see beyond the limitations that life has set them in'.
They also have a micro finance programme for youths who are collected in a large convoy of buses chartered by Alder, from all nooks and crannies of the state and taken to Church. Those who show promise are given scholarships.
'Some of these programmes are coordinated by the parents of our staff who consider themselves part of the company' he also informs me, beaming.
For critics of the Heart of Africa project, he says, 'What they fail to realise is that we did a strategic blueprint, the advert was handled by different firms. People can't see the full programme; the purport was to turn Nigeria into a positively branded product, and that goes beyond marketing'.
As we sit chatting in his den, Leke jumps up excitedly. Passionate about Nigeria he certainly is. 'We asked, what are the brand assets? What are the brand eroders? We did an extensive study of all the nation brands in the world and created a map of Nigeria's brand assets and discovered that Nigeria has 14 major brand assets that were under-exploited and under-marketed. For example, personality brands like Wole Soyinka; locational brands like Obudu Cattle Ranch and educational brands like LASU (Lagos State University)'.
'On the other hand, we had brand eroders like 419, drugs, the Niger Delta problem, political adventurism and so on.'
The project, he explains, was two-pronged. 'To promote what you're good at, in which you have competitive advantage over other countries, and to work on your eroders'.
'A country's brand is not about CNN adverts, that's very stupid! Then, some people said that no amount of promotion of your brand assets will mitigate your eroders, that's crap!' One of Leke's attributes is his bluntness, in case you haven't noticed by now. For Leke, a spade must be called a spade.
'After all' he continues, 'China had Tiananmen Square, the US had slavery, and until recently, Made in Taiwan was a by-word for low quality goods'.
Not done, he illustrates, 'For example, people say you're a thief, you know you're not a thief and they say you shouldn't say anything; that's dumb!'
'My belief in this country is unassailable. If we put hands together, we can live without oil. The top 20 agricultural commodities in the world can be produced in Nigeria' he points out.
'The climatic conditions of all the nations that produce these top 20 crops are resident in Nigeria. The exportable value of these top 20 commodities, as per 2006 statistics, is $70billion! We may be the largest producer of cassava in the world, but we consume more than 80%, that is subsistence economy.'
'Part of the objectives of the Heart of Africa project is information substitution, where the image of the country is substituted from that of 419 and brigandage to the point at which we are promoting our business brands, personality brands, locational and infrastructural brands. For example, Dubai promotes the Dubai Airport'.
'Secondly, obvious from all this' he explains 'there are two sides to this issues - external and internal. The internal is crime policing and value orientation. The Heart of Africa project is a holistic blueprint to tackling Nigeria's image project and promotion of its brand assets. The green passport had to be seen as something beyond negative; it must not be seen as a red alert. I must be able to stand tall as a citizen of this country in any clime, because intellect and excellence have no geographical demarcation or distinction, neither do they have ethnic or colourative bias'.
'Every Nigerian is a Brand Ambassador!' he declares, waxing strong.
Yeah, I like the sound of that, Her Excellency, Funke Aboyade, the Ambassador of Brand.
In the same vein, mind the_gap was a national conference focused on Brand Nigeria and economic development. Initially meant for business executives and enterpreneurs, it was progressively expanded to include the public sector and diplomatic missions.
We move on to other things and I mention Alder's hugely successful Orange Rules campaign for GT Bank. A man who leaves no stone unturned and for whom ideas are second nature, his firm set out to design a campaign that would reinforce the bank's leading position in the post-Soludo consolidation era. The result was first, the titillating teasers on most major city streets and key meeting places - large orange boxes artfully tossed around; then came the newspaper teasers and adverts and unveiling of the bank's new logo which had undergone a complete rebranding.
What inspires his ideas? He recalls of another bank which was having problems in its systems some years back. 'In trying to determine why they had so many inefficiencies in their system, they had no answers and at that point, called us in'.
'Every organisation is basically a production system. We realised that we needed to create a model to apply to the system; but it needed to be unusual. The easiest through-put system we could look at was the flow of traffic on Third Mainland Bridge'.
So, off Alder staff went to Third Mainland Bridge, to sit there and observe the flow of traffic! They discovered eight causes of inefficiencies on the bridge. One of such they called Osa Straight phenomenon, a reference to the ubiquitous Molue buses which rather too regularly for comfort, plunge off Lagos bridges into the Lagoon's murky depths, passengers, driver, conductors, goods et al!
'Everything, traffic, then stops on both sides. The question was: what would constitute that phenomenon in the bank?' continues the creative genius, quite now in his element, eyes twinkling.
They returned to their office, drew graphs and ran statistics of the eight identified causes of traffic and phenomena.
'Once we were able to identify those issues, that bank was able to tackle their inefficiencies. Their profitability shot up - because of the simple rule that an organisation is as efficient as a most inefficient factor in its work flow process; ie, inefficiencies determine efficiencies.
This brand consultant is also a prodigious author who has five publications to his name, including the best selling lifeasiseeit, which he describes as a philosophical book. So quickly did the book fly off the book shelves that it is now in its second print.
Taking the saying, Charity Begins at Home, to heart - some might be tempted to say, too far - even his dogs are branded! He owns five Lhasas which, mercifully, are kept outside and were persuaded to retreat to the back of the house when I'd arrived earlier. Dogs, I very definitely do not like around me, no matter how cute or fetching. Brandy (as in brands, duh), Ideo (as in ideas), Puff (as in Diddy), Diddy (again?! The original Diddy would be honoured, twice over!). As for the fifth, he's still trying to determine whether to call him Prez (as in President)!
What should one make of this, er, oddball? Previous visits to his Victoria Island offices reveal that he excites loyalty from his staff who are passionate about and dedicated to both him and the company and are quite protective of him.
I remark that fact to him and he is humbled as he confirms with wonderment, 'Yes, they are very dedicated and passionate about what we do; they would stake their lives on the company and in the end, the sincere vision of an ordinary man. That is what amazes me!'
Well, on that score I disagree, ordinary he certainly is not.
There's still so much that he wants to do. And after 43 years, he's just warming up!
'If I die now, I won't be happy, but I know at least I'll die engaging all my gears!'
Indeed. In the next 43 years, who knows what this lawyer turned brand analyst will add to his already impressive resumé? What would he surprise us all with? We can only wait and watch.
I take my leave. It's been a most delightful evening in the company of Oluwaleke Alder: brand consultant, lawyer, philosopher, patriot, philanthropist, prodigy, photographer, poet, author, multi-talented, creative, enigma, oddball, ahead of his time perhaps? You decide.

Original_Search_Title: Ohakim And Challenge of Governance
Headline: Nigeria: Ohakim And Challenge of Governance
Body_Text:
Lagos — Early months of any administration provide an ample time for the public to form impressions of that administration. Such impressions are natural in the process of governance. Impressions are fluid, they never remain the same. And in politics, impressions assume a far reaching implication. This is so because it enables doubts or confirm them. A lot of things happen in politics and we cannot but say, "God moves in mysterious ways". It's a maxim we ponder about without actually assimilating its full import. The mysteries of God cannot be understood by mortal man. God knows our needs more than we do. We pontificate on those too, but when things turn out contrary to our expectations, we grieve. We lament and wonder why our carefully laid plans did not work out. We often forget that maybe, just maybe God wants to do something better than that which we had planned.
When the governorship election in Imo state went contrary to expectations the people wondered aloud. There was confusion all over the place. People were perplexed, not knowing what to expect. The greatest worry was whether the Anambra example was now to play out in Imo. What else could the populace think when Chief Ikedi Godson Ohakim of the Peoples Progressive Alliance (PPA) was declared Governor-elect in a state whose House of Assembly was dominated by the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP). The worst-case scenario haunted the poor masses and when considered against the backdrop of the myriad of problems that preceded the elections in the state then such fears expressed by the people would be understood.
Again, there was the matter of the fact that the governor-elect coming from a little known party in the state was not among the top contenders of the post prior to the election. Because of this, people were unfamiliar with his programmes and manifesto. This brought about a kind of unease among the citizenry as to what was to unfold, and what they should expect from the new administration.
Well, Chief Ohakim did not keep anyone guessing for long, neither did he keep the people waiting. He went straight into the business of governance of the state. He effectively took charge with the confidence of one who had been waiting for just such an opportunity of a life time. He knew his own mind and what he wanted to achieve for the state. When he rolled out his 13-point agenda on how he intends to handle the affairs of the state for the next four years, there was no doubt left in the minds of the people that maybe this time, God has all by Himself chosen a leader for His people.
Ohakim left no one in doubt as to his mission and vision for the state. He made all to understand that he was capable of steering the ship of state in the right direction. He made the people to understand that what was needed to move the state forward was a leader who has abiding faith in his mission to use public office to do good.
Governance is all about leadership, the ability and capacity of one at the helm of affairs to connect with the people. This connection sometimes comes with shaking up the doubts that had kept the people hostage in order to meet some of their aspirations. This lack of leadership ability has been one of the problems besetting the state and the country at large. It does not come easy. There is always a prize to pay. Only the courageous can plunge into it since it could mean stepping on some toes and inflicting some kind of pain.
This leadership quality Ohakim has continued to demonstrate since he took office as governor of Imo state. He recognized that good leadership is vital in the affairs of any nation or state. It can change everything if a leader realizes that he can use the potentialities of politics to do good for himself and for those who put him into power. The very excellent and harmonious relationship existing between the State House of Assembly and the governor is a testimony to the leadership quality of the man controlling the affairs of the state. Contrary to fears and speculation that a House of Assembly dominated by a party different from that of the governor is a prescription for chaos, this is not the case in Imo state. Such fears have been rested with the vote of confidence passed on the governor by the state assembly on Wednesday, October 3, 2007.
Ohakim's approach to governance so far has gone beyond political gimmick. It is a transformation process that is bound to lead to positive change. Without wasting time he set out to first of all clean the state of the heaps of refuse which had been struggling with the people over who should occupy the land. With his Clean and Green Initiative, Chief Ohakim let the people know that it would not be business as usual. Over the years, the city of Owerri known for its cleanliness had all but been submerged by refuse. The master plan had been effectively buried and forgotten. Shops and kiosks of various kinds sprang up wherever and however they were conceived by whosoever cared. Markets sprouted over night in all corners of the capital, distorting the landscape and giving off the idea that that there was no rule existing in the state.
Things of course have been like that for years and many had come to believe that it was the way there were meant to be. Change has never been embraced as an acceptable fact of life but when that change touches on the economic life of the people adversely, albeit at the outset, then it pitches the proponent against the masses. Ohakim it appears knew all about that but he chose to go the hard way. He believed that in the end, the people would come to appreciate what he had done.
In assembling his cabinet members, Ohakim did not go the usual way of selecting an all-new cabinet. Instead, he chose to retain some of the trusted hands that had served in the former administration in the state. He also injected some new blood to make for a more robust cabinet. By choosing them, he did not go for mediocre. Dr Catherine Okparaeke, former ICAN president was appointed as commissioner for Finance. Prof Ben Obinali, is the Commissioner for Commerce and Industries. He (Obinali) is reported as one of the best hands in information and communication Technology in Africa. Immediate past Vice Chancellor of the Federal University of Technology, Owerri (FUTO), Prof Jude Njoku is commissioner for Lands, Industries and Urban Survey. While Chief Longers Anyanwu former national chairman of Accord Party is at the helm at the Agriculture Ministry as commissioner.
One of the governor's ideas for change is to make Imo the food basket in the country by 2008. This is a huge responsibility that falls squarely into the hands of the Chief Longers Anyanwu. The commissioner has been tasked among other things, to increase rice production in the state from its present 21 million tons per year to 250 million tons per year. Cassava, which the state currently produces at 718 million tons per year, is to be upped to 848 million tons per year. Obviously, the governor is in a hurry to get the state to the next level in agricultural productivity. In this manner, the state will leap from being a mere subsistent producer of food items to a force that could be reckoned with in the area of agriculture.
These are achievable especially since the state is blessed with fertile land and capable hands. Chief Anyanwu himself concurs to that. He noted that all that was needed to get things moving in the right direction is commitment. He observed that with such clear-cut agenda and a focused leadership Imo state is headed to a very bright future. The commissioner maintained that such a plan is a confirmation of the governor's determination to transform Imo to a state that every citizen will be proud of within his first tenure.
As one of those who represents the "New face of Imo", as shown through the governor's government of unity, Chief Longers Anyanwu says the state has become a modern state for other states to emulate. Ohakim's government, Chief Anyanwu maintains, has demonstrated what it means to bring people of different political persuasions together through genuine reconciliation by ensuring that after every election, those elected must go into the business of governance other than politicking. What is happening in Imo State, Chief Anyanwu added shows that a people can follow a leader that has a vision. The gritty truth is that poor leadership remains perhaps the biggest problem that alienates the governed from the government. My interest in what Ohakim's government is doing in Imo, which many people in the state are yet to fully realize, is that he is leading change through transformation. It is a process, not an event. It must advance through stages while avoiding the corresponding pitfalls.
To understand these stages, to borrow from Prof. John P. Kotter who teaches the Art of Leadership at the famous Harvard Business School in Boston, USA, there is need to establish a sense of urgency in government at the early stages. The pitfall however is that many people won't like to be driven from their comfort zones. You can then begin to understand why quite most of people in Owerri, the state capital, are complaining about the pulling down of shanty shops and bill boards. But that is exactly why the government has formed what some people have called unity government, which for me, is simply an assemblage of people with shared commitment and ideas to change things, while the governor is a team captain leading the change through a creation of a vision that must direct the change effort. He has not disappointed so far.
He needs to communicate this vision more lucidly and empower the people to act on his vision that must recognize and reward hardwork. If he (Ohakim) sustains the momentum and institutionalizes the vision that carries him into office, he surely will change Imo and the citizenry to take them to where he wants them to be.
But the governor must keep this in view always: that life is a marathon and politics the test of it. In it you can find governance, and governance is a journey in itself. All that matters in governance is that the electorate want to see the "servant leader" (apologies to President Yar'Adua) moving and taking them in the right direction. The truth in all of this, is that in modern politics, while a political figure depends on friends or (as it were in our case) on a sorry pack of cronies to succeed, a governor for instance, rises or falls as a result of his own decisions or errors in office.
Mr. Onwukwe, a journalist, wrote in from Owerri, Imo State.

Original_Search_Title: Economic Opportunism
Headline: Nigeria: Economic Opportunism
Body_Text:
Days of sharp and long knives are here. So, watch your sides, your front, your back and even the air above you, as well as the very ground on which you walk. No one is safe anymore from anyone. Nigerians, by and large, worship at the altar of Mammon and do go on to taunt God with their raucous religious hypocrisy and this is precisely why our economic mess compounds itself by the day.
For, if the millions of Nigerians who today claim to be Christians and Muslims were sincerely true and faithful to their confessions, would there not have emerged in Nigeria the critical mass of staunch and ecumenical people of God to bring down, through fasting and prayers, the justice and peace of God upon the whole land so that we may become citizens all and subjects none in Nigeria, this amazingly endowed heartbeat of the black race? There surely would. But here we are, full of deceitful and heartless talk and hardly ever loyal to any act of intellectual or spiritual faith, because we are opportunism writ large; and this is why we are so loudly as empty as empty can be.
Indeed, we are mostly walking accidents of dishonesty: intellectual, spiritual, material, social, etc., primed and waiting to happen at the one or the other feckless instant. We are, thus, children of the impending doom and chaos, because we mostly do not care to have any discernible and unchanging handle to life. And this is why we mostly live a stinking lie and, of course, we die a stinking and miserable lie. Can you beat this?
In fact, nothing can better tell us Nigerians that strange and entropic days of crass opportunism and ethical relativism are here with us than the quite surprising short-shrift which Prof. Charles C. Soludo's hare-brained naira redenomination plan received in the hands of those Nigerians who one, with a large measure of truth, could call his intellectual and economic policy parents in Nigeria. Such are Dr. Kalu Idika Kalu, Dr. Chu Okongwu, Chief Olu Falae and other like-minded intellectual and economic policy magicians from the hey and inglorious days before September 26, 1986, when the floating rate variant of the currency board debt standard, otherwise known as the SAP, arrived to stay in Nigeria. Was it not these gentlemen, with Chief Omowale Kuye and Alhaji Abubakar Alhaji, (Triple A), in tow, who terrorised the whole country with the saying that there is no alternative (TINA) to SAP! These were, indeed, the men. And they eventually got their way on that dark day of September 26 1986 and we Nigerians, in return, got our woes of the past two decades of starvation, disease, death and embarrasing ethical disintegration in the form of the ubiquitous and chameleonic 419 business. For hell hath no fury than a naira emptied of value and mismanaged by the SAP!
It follows from this that Soludo's naira reform plan could only, with decency, be critiqued by these floating - rate SAP incubators of 1986, if it was so that they did not know that their floating rate variant of the SAP also has a fixed rate variant or sibling which Mr. Soludo proposed for Nigeria on August 14, 2007. And if they did not know that their own floating rate variant of the SAP also has a fixed rate variant, then what was their business being in office in 1986 as the seasoned and knowledgeable helmsmen of Nigeria's economic management? Ask them!
What is certain is that our economic managers who led us to the desert land of the SAP in 1986, were not and are not rounded masters of the theory and practice of money and, either by omission or by commission, they allowed the West to get Nigeria back on the slippery path of economic re-colonisation through their floating rate variant of the SAP. And once Nigeria found itself once again on the open colonial market train it was only a question of time before another SAP enthusiast like Mr. Soludo would conclude Nigeria's journey to economic re-colonisation by springing the fixed-rate variant of the SAP on Nigeria. And this is precisely what Prof. Charles C. Soludo did to Nigeria on August 14, 2007. So, by coming out to make short-shrift of the Soludo plan, Dr. Kalu and his class of 1986 also made short-shrift of their own reputations as full-fledged, patriotic and down to earth economists. The floating-rate economic kettle has no business calling the fixed-rate economic pot black. For, they are all the neo-liberal merchants of the economic variant of St. Vitus' Dance - the dance of economic disarray, morass and death.
I know that Dr. Kalu will hurry to say that I am talking arrant nonsense, but this, I am afraid, is very far from being so. For on page 146 of my book of 2004: NEPAD and The Common Good, I had this to say about how to get the management of the Nigerian economy right:
"So, two things must be done to get the Nigerian economy back on its feet. First, the domestic public debt mass must be liquidated and no tier of government in Nigeria may contract any form of domestic debt as government business will be conducted only on cash basis in the manner of Dr. Kalu Idika Kalu's Imo Formula of 1984-1985. And, second, the international reserves of Nigeria must be kept at home in Nigeria in the form of above-ground and underground gold reserves. In other words, as soon as the naira goes onto the gold commodity standard so soon will Nigeria be on her way to a blazing recovery into growth."
Now, the point I repeatedly make to Dr. Kalu whenever we run into each other these days is that if he had been privileged to do his Imo Formula at the federal level in the Nigeria of 1986, he would either have led Nigeria back to the colonial fixed rate currency board debt standard of the Soludo variety, or straight to the gold commodity naira of the gold payments standard. Dr. Kalu has not come out to tell me where he always wanted to take Nigeria in this regard.
But, I now tell him that his Imo Formula is exactly the Soludo colonial plan at the federal level. Why is this so? Simply because Dr. Kalu has not, on any occasion in man's memory, argued for the total elimination of the interest phenomenon in the structure of financial intermediation in Nigeria through the total liquidation of the Nigerian domestic public sector debt mass, and thus, for the elimination of the Nigerian public sector borrowing requirement, PSBR, and, importantly also, for the establishment of a Nigeria-wide network of naira-trading commodity exchanges for gold, oil, grains, cassava etc, etc. It is this domestic environment of equity finance in Nigeria's financial intermediation that, inexorably, would create the inward-looking, gold convertible and good servant naira. But this is not what Dr. Kalu's Imo Formula is all about. Rather, the Imo Formula would sustain the peripheral and extractive capitalism of NEITI in Nigeria, with the aid of the fixed rate colonial convertibility of the bad master naira.
In other words, the SAP has a floating rate variant and a fixed rate variant as well. Dr. Kalu's Imo Formula of 1984 - 1985 is the fixed rate variant of the SAP at the federal level and is, therefore, the same as Mr. Soludo's naira redenomination plan. So, how can Dr. Kalu and company, who introduced the floating rate variant of the SAP to Nigeria on September 26 1986, now turn around to excoriate, in August 2007, their dutiful, logical, intellectual son, Prof. Charles C. Soludo, for doing what an obedient, loyal and intellectual son should do, by carrying out the economic thought and action plans of his intellectual Babas to their logical and colonial conclusion? If you ask me, I would say that this form of intellectual atilogwu dance on the part of Dr. Kalu and company is the bane of sound economic management in Nigeria. They find it extremely difficult to talk the domestic economic talk of equity finance, because they are so easily distracted by the sweet, but deadly, and unserious talk of debt finance.
In fact, these gentlemen owe Prof. Charles C. Soludo loads of heartfelt apology for having
failed to recognise the fact that in Soludo's American Wonder, one, indeed, finds the fixed rate variant of the mess of an economic policy plan which they foisted on Nigeria on September 26 1986. Intellectual arrogance and error begets intellectual arrogance and error. It was the intellectual arrogance and error of September 26 1986 that gave birth to the intellectual arrogance and error of August 14 2007. This is the legacy of the ethically barren and mechanical mumbo - jumbo of the neo-liberal Washington Consensus, which is the ideological pedestal of the SAP. Soludo is descended from these Nigerian official purveyors of this ideology in 1986. And as a dutiful son, Soludo has done extremely well to carry the barren ideas of his intellectual parents to their logical and colonial conclusion. Are these not very strange days of sharp and long knives where intellectual kinsmen, fathers and sons for that matter, are at daggers drawn with each other over nuances of the same ideological and economic jagaa - jagaa? Na waa - O!
But this is not all. For it is on the record that in late August 1987, the Nigeria which was then under the economic watch of the floating-rate, SAP enthusiasts like Chief Olu Falae, Dr. Chu Okongwu, Alhaji Abubakar Alhaji, - (Triple A), Chief Omowale Kuye and Dr. Kalu Idika Kalu gave the sack ,on the grounds of irreconcilable differences, to two top parastatal banking executives in the persons of Prof. Ibrahim Ayagi, at the defunct Continental Bank, and Oba Oladele Olashore, at First Bank, for having the truthful guts to say that the SAP has no human face and, besides, that its economic rationale does not quite add up. In local lingo, we would say that the SAP dey one kind! Then some ten to twelve years ago, Prof. Sam Aluko, my good friend Dr. Charles Njoku and myself got an ear-full of a tongue-lashing from Dr. Kalu Idika Kalu. For in a full page and self-eulogising interview, in the Guardian newspaper, he called us this and that from Cambridge and Harvard who had the impudence to say in 1986 and thereafter that their floating rate variant of the SAP is the same as a plan for economic genocide. We were obviously in the right, no matter what Dr. Kalu may say about us, because just one look at the stinking open sewer that Lagos has become since 1986 tells all the story about the dehumanising properties of the SAP. Perhaps Dr. Kalu does not see that with time and with the SAP, Lagos has become a pen for animals, rather than a habitat for human beings.
But, this is not really the point here. Rather, the point is the wonder of wonders that did happen in August - September 2007. Prof Sam Aluko, Prof. Ibrahim Ayagi and myself found that we had the quite uncomfortable company of Dr. Chu Okongwu, Dr. Kalu Idika Kalu and Chief Olu Falae in making our different cases against Soludo's fixed rate variant of the SAP! How times and minds do change! However, one thing is as clear as clear can be. Neither Prof. Sam Aluko nor Prof. Ibrahim Ayagi nor myself is either a floating rate, or a fixed rate, SAP enthusiast. We have kept faith with what we thought of SAP in 1986 and thereafter. But this much one cannot say for Dr. Kalu Idika Kalu, Dr. Chu Okongwu and Chief Olu Falae.
This form of intellectual opportunism which the proposed Soludo naira reform plan has thrown up in Nigeria is also evident in the economic management of most African economies. So let us, for example, look at the economic woes that have befallen Ghana, our Anglophone neighbour, since 1984. It came, for those of us like the late Prof. Claude Ake, Prof. Okwudiba Nnoli, Prof. Osita Eze, the current DG of the NIIA, and myself, who were the professional contemporaries of Dr.Kwesi Botchway at the University of Dar es Salaam in the early mid- nineteen seventies, as a very rude shock for us to learn that this middling law don at the Law Faculty of the University of Dar es Salaam, who had this disconcerting way of babbling some Marxist inanities and clichés, which everyone knew he did not believe in, was transformed in the early nineteen eighties into the seemingly cracking economic gu-guru of Ghana! It was in Kwesi Botchway that the IMF and the World Bank found the human conduit for getting their SAP policy of economic genocide into Ghana. Thus was it that, from 1984, Ghana began its own St. Vitus Dance of economic death.
At this time the Cedi exchanged at the rate of 2.75 to one US dollar. But by July 2007 many thousands of Cedi exchanged for one US dollar. And we glean from the experience of Ghana, from 1984 to 2007, the cast-iron procedure which the IMF and the world adopt in worsen the currencies of African economies into the total subject-hood of colonial convertibility. First, they get African economic opportunists to use the floating rate currency board debt standard to de-industrialise the African nation completely. And second, they get African economic opportunists to use the fixed rate currency board debt standard to return the currency of the African nation back to the colonial convertibility of a CFA Franc or a Hong Kong dollar. Ghana completed this procedure in July 2007. And this was what Nigeria, according to Soludo, was billed to conclude come August 1, 2008.
Thus, one must not overlook what the re-denomination of the Cedi in July 2007 stands for in symbolic and practical terms. Symbolically, the West took their revenge on the name and ghost of Kwame Nkrumah, who had the temerity of destroying the fixed-rate Anglophone West Africa Currency Board in July 1957 by creating the Cedi and withdrawing it from this West African fixed-rate currency board debt standard. But fifty years on, precisely after Botchway's Judas' economic programme of de-industrialisation had led to the total destruction of the infant industries of Ghana, the Cedi was returned to colonial and fixed-rate currency board debt standard with the currently reeling and bottomless US dollar as its anchor! It is as if Kwame Nkrumah had not lived and worked so hard for the political, economic and, above all, spiritual, emancipation of the black race now that the West is fully in charge of Ghana's economy all over again.
And practically, the Ghana of Kwame Nkrumah, which had upset the colonial currency applecart of the West African Currency Board in July 1957 would, 50 years on, become the Anglophone West African nation to re-introduce the fixed- rate currency board debt standard to the West African Anglophone nations. This is precisely what the re-denomination of the Ghanaian Cedi in July 2007 was set to do. Of course, after the Cedi, the next victim of this re-colonising monetary rout of West Africa by the West was naturally to be Nigeria come August 1 2008. Such was and is the plan for the birthing of the ECO Common Currency, as forged by the hired minds of the African economic opportunists at the West African Monetary Institute, WAMI, at Accra, Ghana. Quite a whammy lot!
However, as they say, man proposes, but God disposes. For God used President Musa Yar'Adua to save Nigeria from the grabbing hands of the West. Yar'Adua saw through the colonial trap in Soludo's naira reform plan and said an emphatic BABU to it. Nigeria will no longer be subject to the debt financing excesses of the West's economics of apartheid.
What lesson do we, then, learn from the above? That we must look everyone of our so-called African economic experts very closely in the mouth. For intellectual opportunism seems to have eaten deep into their minds and marrows. Thus, any African economic expert who claims that debt finance at home and abroad is the path to Africa's economic emancipation must be seen as the colonising agent of the West. But any African economic expert who says that equity finance at home and abroad is the narrow road to Africa's sustainable growth in social and material plenty, is the true intellectual heir or heiress of Kwame Nkrumah, Sekou Toure, Julius Nyerere, Amilcar Cabral and the other great African liberators.
Thus, the message of economic life in any society of any scale is jobs, food and social security for the people. And the messenger of economic life in any society of any scale is money. However, economic opportunism does not subscribe to the idea that money should work, with increasing returns, for the people, rather it says that people should work, with diminishing returns, for money. Accordingly, economic opportunism describes the mindset of the African economic mercenary who, in the service of the West, pied-pipes his people, through debt finance, into the slavery of the floating-rate and fixed-rate currency board debt standard.
For, where the domestic and foreign debt financing regime in Africa causes the dislocating and massive rural-urban drift of resources in Africa, the domestic and foreign equity financing regime in Africa arrests and reverses the rural-urban drift of resources as it makes rural and cottage entrepreneurs of most Africans. More specifically, it is the equity financing regime of micro-finance-banks, MFBs, which arrests and reverses the flow of resources: human, material and financial, from the rural areas of Nigeria to its urban areas. And the market framework which enables MFBs to create a de-centralised structure of economic enterprise in Nigeria is the country-wide and electronic network of back-to-back stock and commodity exchanges.
In effect, Nigeria shrinks today in home-made jobs, goods and services, because the country labours under the debt financing regime of the bad master and interest-based variant of the naira. But Nigeria will grow and grow tomorrow in home-made jobs, goods and services, because the country will be woven together, in work and reward, by the emerging equity financing regime of the good servant and interest free variant of the naira. And this is, indeed, the financial secret of, and the open sesame to, the phenomenal growth of Nigeria in social and material plenty which will soon follow in the wake of the imminent roll out of RIROMAN, Rural Industrialization Roadmap for Nigeria, the electronic market development and management programme for Nigeria.

Original_Search_Title: Gov't Authorizes Rice Price Increment, Sortage Imminent
Headline: Liberia: Gov't Authorizes Rice Price Increment, Sortage Imminent
Body_Text:
A few month ago, the Ministry of Commerce informed the public that there were six importers of rice in Liberia, hence there will be no shortage.
But recent investigation conducted by this paper revealed that stocks at the Freeport of Monrovia have dropped to 16, 000 metric tons and some importers have expressed fear of looming rice shortage.
The Analyst Newspaper has gathered that only 8, 000 metric tons of butter rice are actually on hand and that significant consignment of the country sure diet is not expected from China any time soon.
Liberians, in spite of other products - cassava, yam , plantain, eddoes and corn, consume 5, 000 metric tons of rice each month, food analysts have revealed.
Based on the unfolding development, there is a strong possibility that the supply of rice on the local market in coming weeks will be adversely affected and it's feared that butter rice will soon start leaving the market, giving way to the US parboiled rice, which is once in a blue moon preferred.
It's understood that the price has increased by US$1.00 per a 100lb bag and the government has also authorized retail price of US$23.00 per 100lb bag of butter rice.
Our investigation revealed that the supply is compounded by price bickering between a major importer and the Ministry of Commerce regarding the wholesale price of butter rice.
The investigation, which also took us to the warehouses of major importers of rice in the country at the Freeport of Monrovia, established that there are depleting stocks of rice in the country as we speak.
"Retailers are now selling 100lb bag of butter rice at US$24.00 (L$1, 450), which is US$1.00 above the government stipulated price", commented one of the six major importers.
But in actual market terms, as we speak, a 50kg bag of butter rice is being sold by retailers at US$23.50 and a 100lb bag is US$27.50 (L$1, 650).
Butter rice, according to the government retail approved price is US$25.00, but it's being sold by retailers in Monrovia at $30.00 (L$1, 800), which is US$5.00 more.
With this uncontrollable development in the country's rice market, it seems that the market forces have taking over the government officious and attempt to regulate the rice market.
When the Director of Division for Price, Marketing and Analysis at the Commerce Ministry, Mr. Charles Bennie, was contacted , he confirmed the increase in price on the local market.
He told this paper that the increment is due to the number of vessels conveying butter rice and that other vessels are refusing to come to Liberia.
Whatever the structural reasons of price increases are, do we take that to mean that the price of rice is going to enter into another price wind, reinforced by disruption in the steady supply of rice on the market base on stocks that are less than one month to exhaustion, one wonders.
The government should be the one to stabilize the rice market by helping the importers provide a remedy of sufficient rice on the market.
Like the Ivory Coast, the government puts in for reserve in order to fight the shortage of the most popular product on the market.
Investigation continues.

Original_Search_Title: Kuje Council Distributes Palm Seedlings
Headline: Nigeria: Kuje Council Distributes Palm Seedlings
Body_Text:
Kuje Area Council of FCT has distributed more than 1,000 palm seedlings to boost production.
The supervisory councillor for Agriculture, Mr Samson Barde, made this knownWednesday in Kuje in an interview with the News Agency of Nigeria (NAN).
He said the council would also procure another 500 seedlings next year for the second phase of the programme.
"We are determined to free ourselves from dependency on palm oil from the southern part of the country," he said. According to him, the weather in Kuje is conducive for the planting of the seedlings.
Barde said the two phases of the project was estimated to cost more than N2.5 million.
He said two of the 10 wards had been selected for the first phase while the remaining eight wards would come up next year.
When fully established, the project would provide not only revenue to the council but also improve the income of the people, Barde added.
The councillor also said the council was considering introducing cassava and bee-keeping by the end of the year.
"Already, some farmers have indicated interest to participate in the projects," he said.
He appealed to farmers to form cooperative societies for easy access to loans and other benefits from government. (NAN)

Original_Search_Title: UN Launches Flash Appeal to Aid 75,000 Flood Victims
Headline: Ghana: UN Launches Flash Appeal to Aid 75,000 Flood Victims
Body_Text:
The United Nations and partner organizations today launched a $10-million flash appeal for 75,000 people in northern Ghana, where entire communities have been hit by floods after heavy and persistent rain in late August and mid-September.
Farmers in the already vulnerable Northern, Upper East and Upper West Regions have lost their crops, vast tracts of land, food storage and processing facilities are submerged, and houses, bridges, schools and health facilities have been destroyed by the waters, which are part of a wider flood crisis across a whole swath of sub-Saharan Africa.
"Although floods are common in Ghana, this year's abnormally heavy rain has resulted in flooding that is stretching the ability of affected communities to cope," UN Emergency Relief Coordinator John Holmes said. "Food security is a particular concern in areas of the country where people's lives were already precarious. The generosity of Member States and other donors can help prevent living conditions from deteriorating even further."
Preliminary assessments by the Ministry of Food and Agriculture reveal an estimated loss of 144,000 metric tons of crops, including maize, sorghum, millet, peanuts, yam, cassava and rice. In addition, prices of all staples have doubled since the flooding, and not all food commodities are readily available at markets due to flooded roads and submerged bridges.
This leaves around 75,000 people at risk of malnutrition until next year's harvest, which will take place only if irrigated crops can be planted on schedule. Seeds, fertilizer and tools must be supplied by March 2008 for that to happen. Funds from the appeal will also ensure that those cut off by floodwaters have access to health services through outreach and mobile clinics.
Partners with logistics expertise will enable mobile teams to deliver essential drugs and medical supplies to inaccessible areas by the use of helicopters or boats. The teams will also be able to provide insecticide-treated bed-nets to prevent an increase in malaria as the flood waters recede, as well as chlorine tablets to make household water safe to drink.
Funds are also needed to fix damaged public and household latrines. River boats will be used throughout the relief operation, estimated at six months, or until the floods have receded following the end of the rainy season.
The humanitarian community will also distribute tents and basic household items such as blankets, sleeping mats, jerry cans and kitchen-sets to those deemed the most vulnerable, some 18,000 people who have lost their homes.

Original_Search_Title: Airtime Tops Spending List
Headline: Uganda: Airtime Tops Spending List
Body_Text:
Kampala — UGANDANS generally spent more on telephone airtime in September than they did on food items, a survey by the Uganda Bureau of Statistics has shown.
According to the Consumer Price Index (CPI) released by the Uganda Bureau of Statistics (Ubos) on Tuesday, the highest expenditure for most people was on telecommunications services.
The second highest expenditure was on the food sub groups, which have overall weight of 27.2 per cent of the total index.
Releasing the CPI figures at a news conference in Kampala, the Director of Macroeconomics at Ubos, Mr Matthew Ssewanyana said the annual headline inflation rate for the year ending September went up to 4.9 per cent from 4.3 per cent that was registered for the year ended August 2007.
"Ugandans are increasingly spending more money on telecommunication services especially on mobile phones," he said.
People in Kampala, Jinja and Mbarara spent most on telephone airtime, on matoke in Masaka, on charcoal in Gulu and on cassava in Arua.
