Mr Ssewanyana said during September, food prices went up by 3.2 per cent in all centres, with a significant contribution coming from Irish potatoes, cassava, fresh fruits especially passion fruits and pineapples.
Likewise, prices of milk, some vegetable like cabbages, tomatoes, onions and dried beans also went due to low supplies.
Higher price levels in rent, fuel and utilities group in most centres were also registered.
Arua, Mbarara Kampala high and Kampala low centre registered the highest increase in food prices during the month.
Increase in the prices of paraffin, charcoal, petrol and transport fares were recorded in most centre.
In a new development, Ubos has come up with a new methodology of excluding items with unstable prices from the CPI basket.
Mr Ssenwanyana said the exclusion of food items, fuel, electricity and metered water from CPI basket will now help Uganda to have stable inflation rate.
Inflation figures
Following the exclusion of the listed items, Uganda's core inflation (originally known as the underlying inflation), which now excludes food items, fuel, electricity and metered water from CPI basket, was at 5.7 per cent in September.
The upward changes in the CPI comes at a time when Ubos has completed re-basing exercise of the CPI, which began in March 2006 and ended in September 2007.
It has seen Ubos computing 273 items and six urban centres chosen from the four regions to measure inflation in the country.

Original_Search_Title: Omehia Pledges Support for Small Scale Businesses
Headline: Nigeria: Omehia Pledges Support for Small Scale Businesses
Body_Text:
Port Harcourt — Governor Celestine Omehia of Rivers State has said that every Rivers person wishing to embark on small or medium scale business "will be empowered through direct funding or by laying solid foundation for the people to be economically self-reliant."
Accordingly, he said the eradication of poverty will be given adequate attention because the "objective of the National Poverty Eradication Programme (NAPEP) is in synergy with that of the state government."
Omehia who met with the Senior Special Assistant to the President on Economic Matters, Professor Magnus Kpakol who called on him in Port Harcourt, lauded the new NAPEP initiative, of the Village Economic Development Solutions (VEDS).
He said it was a viable alternative "since it is grassroots oriented" and expressed the hope that it would reduce the poverty level of those in the rural communities. He said the state government had decided to develop human capital in the state insisting that without developing the human beings, economic empowerment would be meaningless.
To achieve this, Omehia said an institution to train those interested in agriculture had been established in the state, explaining that graduates of the programme would be assisted with loans to go into agricultural production.
He announced that the "state government has entered into partnership with a Dutch company to build a cassava processing industry as well as paid for five fishing trawlers to be given to cooperative fish farmers."
We have entered into an arrangement with banks on how to assist the people to develop individual skills as well as deposited One billion Naira with Oceanic Bank to economically empower the people".
While congratulating Kpakol on his new appointment, he praised President Umaru Musa Yar"Adua for finding an indigene of the state worthy for such a position.
On his part, Prof. Magnus Kpakol said he was in the state to launch a new poverty reduction initiative known as the Village Economic Development Solutions saying that "the programme is aimed at eradicating poverty from the rural communities".
Kpakol who is also the Coordinator, National Poverty Eradication Programme, (NAPEP), gave kudos to Omehia for the construction of a shopping mall, Agricultural Training Institute as well as the proposed construction of flyover bridges at Eleme, First Artillery and Garrison Junctions, and other projects of poverty reduction measures that would open the state to economic activities. He described lack of access to information, capital and markets as factors that inhibit economic growth, stating, "it is the objective of NAPEP to break these barriers and improve the growth of the Nation"s economy".

Original_Search_Title: Kuje Area Council Distributes Palm Seedlings to Farmers
Headline: Nigeria: Kuje Area Council Distributes Palm Seedlings to Farmers
Body_Text:
Abuja — The Kuje Area Council of the FCT has distributed more than 1,000 palm seedlings to boost production.
The Supervisory Councillor for Agriculture, Mr Samson Barde, made this known yesterday in Kuje in an interview with the News Agency of Nigeria (NAN).
He said the council would also procure another 500 seedlings next year for the second phase of the programme.
"We are determined to free ourselves from dependency on palm oil from the southern part of the country," he said.
According to him, the weather in Kuje is conducive for the planting of the seedlings.
Barde said the two phases of the project were estimated to cost more than N2.5 million. He said two of the 10 wards were selected for the first phase while the remaining eight wards would have theirs next year.
When fully established, the project would provide not only revenue to the council but also improve the income of the people, Barde added.
The councillor also said the council was considering introducing cassava and bee-keeping by the end of the year.
"Already, some farmers have indicated interest in participating in the projects," he said.
He appealed to farmers to form cooperative societies for easy access to loans and other benefits from government.
(NAN)

Original_Search_Title: Districts Not Prepared for Disasters
Headline: Uganda: Districts Not Prepared for Disasters
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE current floods in northern Uganda have brought into question the ability of districts to handle disasters. Local leaders in the affected districts are wondering about what they can do to help the population. Julius Ochen, the LC5 chairman of Amuria district, says they need about sh50b to help the flood victims.
"This is a disaster," says Kaberamaido LC5 chairman, Robert Engulu.
"We need a master plan," says Kitgum LC5 chairman Komakech Ogwok. None of the districts in Uganda has a clear disaster preparedness programme.
"We do not have the money for the programme. Almost all our budget is funded by the central government. Without any reliable sources of revenue, it is difficult to set aside funds for the disaster programme," says Steven Ochola, the LC5 chairman of Soroti district.
His colleague in Amuria agrees: "The disaster management committee, which we set up at the beginning of this rainy season has not been funded at all," Ochen says.
"We have no funds for disaster programmes. The only kind of emergency fund is the sh18.6b that was set up to facilitate the return of the internally displaced persons home. I request that some of that money be diverted to help the flood victims," says Norbert Mao, the Gulu LC5 chairman.
Because of these inadequacies, districts have to depend on the central government and NGOs whenever there is a disaster. So far, an estimated sh10b has been spent on relief services and another sh20b has been approved by the Government for the same cause. "But sh20b is inadequate," Mao says.
NGOs say at least sh75b is needed for relief services. However, about sh400b is needed to improve the roads and the agriculture sector. The annual budget for all the 25 affected districts averages about sh250b. Most of this is used to pay salaries, with little left for development purposes.
The most affected districts are Soroti, Katakwi, Amuria, Kaberamaido and Kumi in Teso region, Lira and Dokolo in Lango region and Pader, Kitgum and parts of Gulu in Acholi region.
Agriculture gone
With two years of relative peace, agriculture had started thriving in the north and north-east. People had planted huge shambas of cassava, maize, millet and groundnuts. Most of these crops were about to be harvested when the rains started.
Local leaders say all the crops have been destroyed by floods. It is not easy to make a clear estimation, but over 300,000 acres of cereals, cassava and potatoes are rotting.
For example, Amuria district has lost over 70,000 acres of crops. Local leaders estimated the loss at over sh250b.
"The Government and NGOs should come to our rescues," Komakech says.
Before the rains, local leaders had distributed farm implements, including seeds and tools worth over sh8b to the farmers in the region, but all these have been lost.
With the return of peace, people in the north had started growing their own food and many of them were no longer depending on NGOs like the World Food Programme (WFP) and the Food for African Organisation. However, local leaders say, with the floods, residents will have to turn to the NGOs for help.
Sh250b for roads
Local leaders are wondering where funds to renovate the roads in the area will come from. Although the Ministry of Works and Transport has approved at least sh10b for emergency road repairs, the leaders estimate the overall cost at over sh250b.
Almost all major roads in Katakwi, parts of Lira, Kitgum, Amuria and Kaberamaido have been destroyed. Local leaders have estimated the damage to be over 3,000kms. These include over 40 on rivers in the region.
Even tarmac roads like Soroti-Kumi-Mbale are flooded. Awoja bridge on this road also collapsed. According to local leaders, this bridge needs not less than sh1b to be re-constructed.
"Our worry is that the only remaining bridge at Agu may also collapse," says Ochola. Ochen says Amuria district needs at least sh4.3b to work on the roads in the district, while Gulu needs about sh9b.
None of the districts had an emergency road fund. News that over sh42b meant for working on roads is being diverted to prepare for the Commonwealth Heads of Government Meeting makes matters worse.
All districts had already passed their budgets, each with an average of sh200m for road repairs. This money had been allocated to specific roads yet on average, each district will need about sh2b to repair the roads. Given the budgetary constraints, "Only the Government can help us with an emergency road reconstruction plan," Komakech says.
The Gulu-Atiak-Juba Road is now impassable because of the floods and travelling on the Gulu-Pader-Kitgum Road, especially at the River Aswa crossing, has turned into a nightmare. At one time, this crossing had a standard bridge. However, it was destroyed in the 1970s by retreating UNLA soldiers, who did not want Amin's soldiers to cross into Acholi. Since then, residents used a makeshift bridge made of steel angles and logs to cross the river.
"We have been lobbying to have this bridge re-constructed and now is the time for this to happen," Komakech said. About sh3b is needed for the construction of the bridge. Another bridge over the same river as one crosses the Lira-Pader border, is also sub-merged and might need to be replaced. Others are Unyama bridge and Atyanga bridge in Gulu.
To repair these roads after the rains, the entire region will need a master plan. Had it not been for the regulations governing the construction of roads, Kitgum district would by now be working on some of its roads.
"Our equipment is in good condition, we would have perched some of these spots, but regulations do not allow. This work has to be contracted," says Komakech. Several other districts have road units that are in good condition.

Original_Search_Title: Gbagyi Farmers Urged to Adopt New Technique
Headline: Nigeria: Gbagyi Farmers Urged to Adopt New Technique
Body_Text:
Abuja — The Gbagyi communties in the Federal-Capital Territory (FCT) and neighbouring states have been advised to adopt new approach to farming for economic empowerment.
Speaking during a one-day seminar and peace music concert for enlightenment dialogue entitled "Empowering the Masses for Agricultural Revolution" the chairman of the occasion, Alhaji Abubakar Wodi advised Gbagyi farmers to organize themselves into cooperatives or enterprise groups and adopt new approach to production, processing and marketing.
"The Gbagyis in the past used agricultural practices to sustain their economic wellbeing.
There is therefore the need for the Gbyagis to revolutionise agricultural practices.
The Gbagyis sho-uld adopt in planting crops with cosmopolitan taste than local crops, and crops that mature easily."
The convener of the seminar, Col. Jonah Isa Bawa (Rtd) who is a chief research officer at the Institute of Peace and Conflict Resolution, Abuja, said the vision of the seminar is to mobilize Gbagyis' agriculturally so that they can be economically empowered.
"The focus is that instead of us to sell our lands, we should rather use it to improve our agricultural capabilities."
He said the aim is for the Gbagyis to access the loan facility available by the African Foundation in order to extend their farming abilities in Cassava as required by the Federal Government.
"This will help them invest and expand their farms and at the same time employ other people which will give them the economic advantage and make them more competitive in the present socio-economic dispensation." he said.
The event had in attendance the Esu Karu, HRH Luka Baba Panya, as the chief host and several dignitaries including traditional Gbyagyi dance by different troops, a drama, a presentation to a Gbyagyi model farmer and paper presentations by different resource persons.

Original_Search_Title: These Names On Plantains
Headline: Cameroon: These Names On Plantains
Body_Text:
If you say plantains are not the most coveted of food items these days (especially in the southern parts of Cameroon); then you've got it wrong.
A sizeable bunch can't cost less than 2,000 francs. And as wholesalers from other CEMAC countries flood into our local markets for plantains in particular, our economists tell us demand outweighs supply. Simply put, those involved in plantain farming are therefore in lucrative business.
Ha... far from it. Stealing in villages like Tole, Sasse, Small Soppo and other areas in Fako division; discourages many to take the initiative. Hardly can you find mature plantains on sale as those who don't want to labour, make hay on others sweat. So what solution ?
Name initials or painting plantains is thus in vogue to keep thieves at bay. Though with relative success, not surprising if soon we have identification name tags on cocoyams, cassava, pepper, okro; and you name the rest.

Original_Search_Title: Foodstuffs Prices Increase Countrywide
Headline: Rwanda: Foodstuffs Prices Increase Countrywide
Body_Text:
Kigali — Climatic change and external factors are to blame for the current increase in prices of foodstuffs in the country, a Central bank official has said.
A mini survey of markets and shops in the city indicates that prices of some foodstuffs and other essential commodities have continued to increase, some by as much as 50 percent, the highest in the country in past three months.
But the bank official who requested to remain anonymous refuted claims that the price increase was the result of a unstable economy. "If we import a commodity from a country that is experiencing inflation, the effect is transferred to our economy. But our monetary policy on local produce is still stable," the official revealed. The 2007 monetary policy report indicates that inflation slowed down in response to significant fall in prices of foodstuffs. Last month the Governor of the National Bank of Rwanda (BNR) François Kanimba revealed that the bank would contain inflation to less than 10 per cent. "Compared to the beginning of 2006, inflationary pressure significantly dropped during the first half of 2007", Kanimba said. Rose Mukarwego, a vendor at Muhima market in lower Kiyovu says she increased her prices due to high price from his suppliers. "Our suppliers have increased their prices on every commodity they supply us, so we have to do the same if we are to remain in the business", she said.
She said that the smallest cluster of yellow bananas costs Frw200-250 compared to previous months when it was selling at Frw150-200. The price Sugar has increased to Frw 700 from 600-650, a sack of charcoal now costs Frw4,700 an increase from Frw4,500 last month. Consumers of Irish potatoes, cassava, green bananas, sorghum, beans, rice, and groundnuts have to dig deeper into their pockets these days if they are to afford a meal.
"I know our clients are complaining about the price increment, but I have nothing to do. I also get them at high prices compared to previous months," Mukarwego explained.

Original_Search_Title: Ministry Spearheads Projects for Women
Headline: Zimbabwe: Ministry Spearheads Projects for Women
Body_Text:
Harare — Government is committed to improving the standards of living for women in Zimbabwe through self-help projects to fight drought-induced hunger and poverty in rural areas, the Minister of Women's Affairs, Gender and Community Development, Cde Oppah Muchinguri, has said.
She said her ministry was in the process of establishing self-help projects at the grassroots level to cater for marginalised women.
"Our ministry is spearheading as many income-generating projects as possible across the country to ensure that women and children get enough assistance from Government," she said in a speech read on her behalf by the ministry's Permanent Secretary Dr Perpetua Gumbo in Chipinge on Saturday at Maunganidze Congregational Centre during the St Luke's Jekenishen Women Fellowship 12th anniversary.
She expressed hope that the recently introduced food processing projects would alleviate drought-induced hunger in rural areas. "We have realised that we have abundant fruits here in Chipinge that could be processed and benefit a lot of families in terms of income and food. Our women should be involved in these projects as they could also earn foreign currency for the country," added Cde Muchinguri. She urged people to form groups to ease the distribution of inputs and other resources provided by the Government.
Cde Muchinguri said her ministry was working closely with other stakeholders in the agricultural industry with the objective of promoting food processing enterprises to process cassava and sweet potatoes into flour.
"I want to challenge ward co-ordinators to ensure that inputs are put to good use and to work together as a team for the development of the country," the minister said. She warned that the Government would not hesitate to prosecute those who abuse strategic facilities and resources that are meant to turn around the economy. The St Luke's Jekenishen Women Fellowship is undertaking different self-help projects that include baking, interior décor and horticulture. The church is also looking after poor children that it equips with skills to develop themselves.
Cde Muchinguri donated $200 million to the fellowship towards the sustenance of existing projects and the establishment of new ones. The leader of the women's fellowship, Mrs Rumbidzai Pferedzai, praised Cde Muchinguri for the donation, saying this was clear testimony of the ministry's commitment to improving the lives of women in the area.
"We welcome the donation as it would go a long way in changing the lives of many women here in Maunganidze. As a church, we are going to make sure that all the projects prosper as is the mandate from Government," Mrs Pferedzai said.

Original_Search_Title: SOS From Odoni Kingdom
Headline: Nigeria: SOS From Odoni Kingdom
Body_Text:
THE lack of a motorable road and the alarming insecurity on the waterways have become a source of serious concern to the people of Odoni Kingdom. However, my first year of reign as the Pere of Odoni Kingdom has been quite eventful. One of the notable activities which took place is the signing of a memorandum of understanding (MOU) with the Shell Petroleum Development Company (SPDC) at Yenagoa. This was after some Odoni indigenes were trained for this purpose. In addition to this, construction work at a World Bank-assisted cottage hospital through the Bayelsa State Ministry of Health has commenced.
Also significant was the awareness campaign against the dreaded HIV/AIDS. Another remarkable and significant event in the first year of my reign was the peaceful elections which led to the emergence of a democratically-elected civilian Government headed by President Umaru Musa Yar'Adua with His Excellency, Goodluck Jonathan as Vice-President on 29th May, 2007.
The emergence of the new Bayelsa State Governor, His Excellency, Timipre Sylva and his Deputy, Peremobowei Ebebi, was also peaceful. Also worthy of note was the appointment of the Niger-Delta Development Commission (NDDC) Managing Director, Chief Timi Alaibe and Chief Aginiyan as the Commission's new Director of Finance. There is no doubt that these divinely selected men of integrity will definitely bring succour and deliver the dividends of democracy to the grassroots, particularly the very impoverished and deprived communities in this part of Bayelsa State, the core Niger-Delta Region.
Another remarkable event that took place in the kingdom in the last one year was that, for the first time in the annals of Odoni history, 200 out-going youth corps members serving in Bayelsa State chose Odoni Community for their annual three-day crusade which held from 7th to 9th June, 2007. It was a very exciting, inspiring and a successful crusade. Odoni Community felt their impact. The peaceful and successful celebration of the 2007 Odoni Seigbein Festival, cannot be over emphasised.
The festival is a period to show-case the rich, unique and enviable culture of Odoni Kingdom. Culture, they say, identifies a people. So, to preserve our identity, is to uphold and preserve our unique culture and value systems, including the lzon language and our peculiar Odoni dialect. And so, the diversity of all the traditional, cultural and other social activities of the three towns of Odoni - Gbekebor, Opu-Odoni and Tamu-Odoni, were displayed in their true colours during the festival which lasted from 26th May to 1st June, 2007. All the traditional dances of the three towns which include the Dein-Ogele, the lye-Ogele and the Pere-Ogele, paid homage to the Pere of Odoni Kingdom, Abubu II in his Palace.
Nevertheless, there were few unpalatable events which included the frequent siege and attacks on Odoni and Agbere communities by pirates. These pirates came at will on more than three occasions, shot sporadically into the air and carried away over 10 speed boats owned by indigenes of Odoni and Agbere communities. These speed boats are the only means of transportation to and from Odi, because the only motorable road terminates there.
Since the river and waterways are now unsafe and insecure, and economic and commercial activities in Odoni Kingdom and its environs have been paralysed.
Odoni, Agbere, Abuotor, Osifo, Osekenike, Asamabiri, Ekperiware and Elemebiri communities which occupy the eastern axis of Bayelsa State, have a lot of cash and food crops such as plantain, cassava, yam, ogbono, pepper, garden eggs, palm products and timber, etc, at different seasons. But, this economically important axis of Bayelsa State has been completely cut off without a link to the all-important East/West Road.
The effort to connect these communities to the East/West Road was first initiated by the then Niger-Delta Development Board (NDDB) but did not take off. In 1983, Chief Melford Okilo's civilian government of the then Rivers State responded favourably to the persistent demands of these communities by surveying, designing and bulldozing this link-road of 22 kilometers only to Odoni-Agbere. This good gesture by Chief Okilo's government was terminated by the military coup d'etat in 1984.
Between 1989 and 1990, the Directorate of Food, Road and Rural Infrastructure (DFRR1) cleared this important link-road of 22 kilometers only from the East/West Road to the same Odoni-Agbere. As a result this access road which connected Odoni-Agbere to the East/West Road became motorable only in the dry season for few years. Lastly, OMPADEC embarked on this road project with a view to tarring it, but it did not take off before OMPADEC was scrapped .
This road project which could be regarded as the oldest in Bayelsa State even though short in distance, has not been executed in spite of all the previous attempts made. The execution of this road project would mean a lot to the economic development and advancement of commercial activities in the area.
I therefore, plead with the NDDC, the Bayelsa State Government and the Federal Government and the Agip Oil Company operating in the area to come to the aid of these communities for the execution of this vital road project in order to link Odoni-Agbere to the Port Harcourt East/West Road. This will check the menace and curb the excesses of the pirates, and also serve as an economic and commercial gateway to and from Bayelsa State to the South-Eastern states and beyond. And if micro agricultural loans are judiciously provided for small scale industries in this naturally rich and arable land, it will create an enabling environment to engage our vibrant youths in gainful enterprises. Then sea/river piracy and youth restiveness will become things of the past in this part of Niger-Delta.
•His Royal Majesty, Godfrey Douebi Dakoru, Abubu II, is the Pere of Odoni Kingdom in Bayelsa State.

Original_Search_Title: Brazilian Investors Plan Mega Cities for Some States in Nigeria
Headline: Nigeria: Brazilian Investors Plan Mega Cities for Some States in Nigeria
Body_Text:
Lagos — A consortium of Brazilian investors that is interested in building mega cities in some states in Nigeria is expected in the country next week to seek land for the project.
The delegation, which will be led by the Governor of Central Bank of Brazil, would meet with some state governors, especially those with whom they had earlier commenced discussions in Brazil. The states governors include those of Lagos, Ogun, Imo, Abia, Gombe, Delta and Osun states.
The multi-billion naira project, to be known as Agrovilla, are to come with industries and independent power plant (IPP) which would be powered by ethanol.
The Nigerian Co-ordinator of the trip, Mr. Erymoore Ibekwe, who dropped hint of the visit in Johannesburg, South Africa, told Vanguard that the team would arrive the country on October 7, 2007, to seek the co-operation of the state governments in question on how to acquire land for the project.
According top him, "the Brazilian businessmen, most of whom are my business partners, urgently want land to commence work on the project.
"They have already started discussions in Brazil with some of the state governors who invited them over to Nigeria for further talks."
Ibekwe said the cities would also integrate low-cost houses for the people who would be engaged to work in the industries to be located in them, adding that the essence of the project was to urbanise some rural communities in different parts of the country.
He said the investors were particularly interested in planting and processing of sugarcane and cassava with which they hope to produce ethanol that has become an alternative source of fuel in Brazil.
"These people are coming to Nigeria to create jobs for the teeming unemployed youths in the country. One would only hope governments in the country move fast to take advantage of the opportunities these people are coming with.
"At the moment, the federal government is preaching public-private partnership in efforts to develop the economy of the country. The coming of the Brazilian investors aptly fits into this plan," said Ibekwe, who is also a major importer and supplier of ceramic tiles to construction companies in South Africa.
He said the president of the Nigerian community in Brazil who has seen the project work effectively in Brazil is in the forefront of those who encouraged the investors to look in the direction of Nigeria because of federal government's policy discouraging mass movement of youths from the rural to the urban areas.
Ibekwe said, although the delegation was coming mainly to have talks with state governments, it would equally have some interface with President Umaru Musa Yar'Adua on other possible areas of investment in the country's economy.

Original_Search_Title: Workshop to Celebrate Country's Achievements
Headline: Zimbabwe: Workshop to Celebrate Country's Achievements
Body_Text:
Harare — THE National Biotechnology Authority will this week hold a one-day workshop to celebrate Zimbabwe's achievements, capabilities and talents in the field of biotechnology.
NBA registrar and acting chief executive officer Mr Abisai Mafa said the high profile workshop will be held on Thursday and is aimed at celebrating and promoting biotechnology research, development, application and management.
"We hope that when we meet we can identify the kinds of projects that we can embark on to translate the national vision into reality," he said.
"The national vision that we want to translate, will seek to create and sustain a biotechnology sector which is capable of contributing at least 20 percent to Gross Domestic Product by 2020."
NBA will host the workshop in partnership with the Ministry of Science and Technology Development, the National Economic Consultative Forum, Finealt Engineering, the Scientific Industrial Research and Development Centre and SeedCo.
"The main idea behind this workshop is to celebrate Zimbabwe's achievements, capabilities and talents in the field of biotechnology," said Mr Mafa.
"We want to identify priority areas for biotechnology research and development in five strategic areas, which include: agriculture, medicine, food, industry and the environment."
Scientists, policy makers and other private sector players will also explore options for scaling up biotechnology research, development and commercialisation.
Participants will be drawn from industry, research and academic institutions, key government institutions, industry and those in biotechnology related areas.
Key speakers include, Prof Christopher Chetsanga, one of the pioneers of the recombinant DNA technology and current president of the Zimbabwe Academy of Sciences, Prof Gordon Chavunduka -- a traditional medicine researcher and president of the Zimbabwe National Traditional Healers' Association, Prof Lameck Chagonda -- one of the country's leading pharmacologists in traditional medicine and Dr Dahlia Garwe, a young gifted woman scientist who scored a first in DNA-based drought tolerant maize crop research.
Other leading biotechnology experts and research and development experts will also make presentations.
Dr Garwe will lead a session on the meaning of biotechnology and its conceptual techniques to help participants to better understand the methods of biotechnology.
In simple terms, biotechnology may refer to the use of living things to make products or any technique that uses living organisms, or parts of organisms, to make or modify products, improve plants or animals, or to develop microorganisms for specific uses.
Biotechnology is a useful process in agricultural production, hybrid plant development, environmental research, medicine, drug and vaccine development, food and chemical industries and many others.
The process is employed to produce beer, dairy products, beverages, vaccines and many other products.
Prof Chetsanga will attempt to explain the importance of biotechnology in the country's five strategic areas -- agriculture, medicine, food, industry and the environmental sector while Mr Mafa's presentation will focus on the role of the National Biotechnology Authority in research development, application and management of biotechnology.
Dr Olivia Muchena, the Minister of Science and Technology Development will officially open this workshop which aims to push science, technology and innovation to be at the centre of rapid and sustainable economic development.
There will also be presentations by various experts from the food processing industry, seed companies and academic and research sectors.
Group sessions will discuss priority setting and networking issues in five strategic areas -- agricultural biotechnology, food biotechnology, medical biotechnology, environmental biotechnology and industrial biotechnology.
"Science, technology and innovation is central to rapid and sustained economic development," said Mr Mafa.
"China, India and Cuba have advanced their biotechnology sectors. These countries are doing well in the biotechnology field. We need to learn from these countries' experiences.
"India's Shanta Biotech company was contracted by the World Health Organisation to produce the DNA-based Hepatitis B vaccine, Cuba is also producing medical vaccines for children and China is also expanding its biotechnology base. There is need for us to think in a strategic manner and put more resources into biotechnology," he said.
If more resources were put into the biotechnology sector, the country could make huge savings in terms of foreign currency used in the importation of biotechnology products.
"We missed the industrial revolution, we missed the agricultural revolution but we cannot afford to miss the biotechnology revolution," Mr Mafa said.
"We have a good intellectual pool of scientists. We have not been able to tap into the intellectual capacity of our scientists. People are taking our genetic materials to make money and they are poaching our people to make more money again.
"The National Biotechnology Authority believes Zimbabwe can also invest resources into biotechnology research, development and application to put the national economy on a recovery path," he said.
The workshop will be useful to seed breeders, food processing industries, academic and research institutions, regulatory authorities and others involved in the marketing of biotechnology crops and products.
The workshop will also be valuable to growers, biology teachers, extension specialists, businesspeople in industries related to food and agriculture.
The National Biotechnology Authority was established through an Act of Parliament.
In June this year, the National Biotechnology Authority held a training workshop on Biosafety Clearing House to help scale up efforts to safely manage genetically modified organism and to fulfill the country's obligation under the Cartagena Protocol on Biosafety.
Zimbabwe has over the years produced a number of high profile and gifted scientists who have gone to conquer the world through their intellectual capabilities and innovations in various fields of biotechnology.
The Zimbabwe "Biotechnology Hall of Fame" include, among others:
He has more than 47 published articles, 15 abstracts and has contributed 21 papers to research seminars and more than 40 public addresses on science and technology. In 1990, he received a Presidential award for distinguished research in the area of cancer in Zimbabwe, whilst the Unesco honoured him with the Gold Medal award in 1999.
The University of Zimbabwe conferred him with a Doctor of Science degree in 2000.
Prof Idah Sithole-Niang, an accomplished professor and a major driving force in the development of agricultural biotechnology in Zimbabwe.
A number of sorghum and millet varieties that he developed were released and adopted.
These include two white sorghum varieties -- SV1 and SV2, pearl millet variety PMV-2, the country's first sorghum hybrid ZWSH-1 and Zimbabwe's first finger millet varieties PMV-1 and PMV-2.
He made an immense contribution to the collection of sorghum and millet germplasm, helping to save Zimbabwe's drought crop diversity.
Dr Mushonga has more than 40 publications covering work on sorghum and pearl millet.
In 1992, he was awarded the Seed Co prize in recognition for his contribution to the development of the country's cereal industry.
He has also made meaningful contributions to the country's food security and nutritional issues, grain quality development and into sorghum and millet research.
He is a plant breeder par excellence and this year won the Research Council of Zimbabwe award in recognition of his distinguished contribution to the country's agricultural sector.
He was one of the long-serving professors at the University of Zimbabwe's Department of Anatomy until his retirement in 2006.
He joined the then University of Rhodesia in 1968 as a lecturer in Neurosurgery and rose through the ranks to become chairman of the department.
Prof Levy served in various local and international boards giving advice and service helping to advance knowledge, expertise and experience in the country's medical field.
He was a recipient of the Merit Award, the Zimbabwe Medical Association award, the President's Award for Contribution to Neurosurgery and the Paul Harris Award of the Rotary Foundation, all for his distinguished service in the medical field.
He was a former director of the Blair Research Laboratory (now National Institute of Health Research).
Prof Chandiwana's exceptional knowledge in research on water-borne diseases such as malaria and bilharzia, diarrhoea, HIV and Aids, public health technologies and health systems led him to play a leading role in transforming the thinking of parasitologists and epidemiologists not only in Africa but all over the world.
He played a major role in the development of public health strategies at both continental and international level.
He was a keen promoter of African networking and in developing competencies of young researchers to be competitive in applying for global research funds.
The renowned public health expert was one of the founder directors of the Harare-based non-governmental research organisation -- the Biomedical Research and Training Institute.
This maize variety is on demand in Zimbabwe and in the Sadc region as it addresses challenges of lack fertilizers, droughts and food security.
There are numerous others like the late Dr Joseph Gopo who worked as director-general of the Bio-Technology Research Institute of the Scientific and Industrial Research and Development Centre in Zimbabwe and was instrumental in promoting biotechnology in the country.
Ian Robertson, a biotechnologist, developed improved farm varieties of cassava and sweet potato varieties and farming methods while Prof Lameck Chagonda and Prof Gordon Chavunduka have distinguished themselves in the promotion and development of traditional medicine as a science in the country.
Apart from individuals, research and academic institutions have also made important contribution to the country's biotechnology field.
There is no doubt that the workshop will help the country to exploit new and emerging research opportunities that address the social and economic challenges.
Within this context, it will also be important promote the country's biotechnology research agenda given the fact that Zimbabwe has skills base in the agricultural sciences, is endowed with rich biological resources critical for the biotech revolution and a good pool of scientists to propel the country's biotechnological revolution forward.
Optimising the use of biotechnology in various sectors of the economy will enhance the country's scientific and technological advancement.

Original_Search_Title: Country at 47
Headline: Nigeria: Country at 47
Body_Text:
Lagos — AS Nigeria celebrates its 47 years of Independence tomorrow, eminent Nigerians have blamed the pace of the country's economic development on the absence of patriotism among the people and lack of commitment to the Nigerian project.
Among the eminent Nigerians who spoke on the Nigeria's 47 years of nationhood are former Petroleum Resources Minister, Senator Jibirl Aminu, former president of Nigeria Labour Congress (NLC), Comrade Pascal Bafyau, Secretary-General of the Nigeria Supreme Council for Islamic Affairs (NSCIA), Alhaji Abdulateef Adegbite, former Attorney-General and justice Minister, Prince Bola Ajibola and an Ogun State-based Islamic leader, Alhaji Munirudeen Nusirudeen.
They spoke at different just as a cross section of other Nigerians declared that visionless leadership was still the bane of leadership.
In an exclusive interview with Sunday Champion in Yola, Adamawa State capital weekend, both Senator Aminu and Bafyau lamented that Nigeria was grappling with the problems of poor health facilities, high maternal and infant mortality, high unemployment rate as well as low capacity utilization and corruption.
According to Senator Aminu, while Ghana, which will soon be 50 years has something to celebrate as a nation, Nigeria is yet to have a clearly defined area of focus despite the huge resources the nation is endowed with.
"Ghana will soon be 50 years old and they have mapped out an area which they want to celebrate but which area do we have here to boast of; can we say we want to celebrate uninterrupted power, excellence in the health sector, energy, or is it self sufficiency in food supply or literacy level; where," he stated.
He called on Nigerians to be wary of celebrating mediocrity and corrupt behaviour but rather imbibe the spirit of hard work, honest and patriotism if the nation must develop. He urged Nigerians to give the government of President Umaru Musa Yar'Adua a chance while also calling for support and cooperation for the Economic and Financial Crimes Commission (EFCC) on the current moved to rid Nigeria of corruption.
Alhaji, Nusirudeen has stressed the need for Nigerian leaders to make the fear of God their guiding principle in the day-to-day running of the nation.
Alhaji Nusirudeen who is the Seriki Musulumi Ajanosi of Egba stated this in a special Jumat service to mark the 47th National Day Celebration in Ogun State held at the Central Mosque, Kobiti Abeokuta.
He noted that God's favour was upon Nigeria, adding that the independence anniversary should be used to reflect on such divine goodness with a view of drawing closer to God.
Alhaji Nusirudeen who commended the peaceful atmosphere in Ogun State , urged the Governor Gbenga Daniel not to relent on pursuing the path of unity and tranquility to attain greater heights.
Speaking on the occasion Governor, Daniel attributed the various successes recorded in the area of massive investment flow to the prevailing peace in the state.
"While our government has continued to create an enabling environment for business to thrive, we also strive hard in the area of religious harmony," Daniel said.
He described the state as one of the most stable in terms of religious and business growth, saying that credibility had also been restored in government through the establishment of Financial Investigation Control Unit (FICU).
The Secretary General, NSCIA, Alhaji Adegbite who also spoke on the occasion, threw his weight behind the fight against corruption, urging the religious leaders to join the anti-corruption crusade.
Adegbite said: "The world will continue to laugh at Nigeria if we only pay lip service to the so called anti-corruption war, treating corrupt officials with leniency or sweeping blatant acts of corruption under the carpet, be it within the legislature, Executive or Judiciary".
While stressing the need for good governance at all levels, Adegbite stressed the need to enforce religious instructions in the nation's educational institutions.
He described the Yar' Adua's administration as a government of law, driven by the fear of God.
Alhaji Adegbite commended the administration of Governor Daniel in the state, pointing out that he had maintained the momentum of good governance characterized by progressive measures, "the impact of which is evenly felt in every area and segment of the state".
Also speaking at the Jumat Service, an eminent Jurist, Prince Bola Ajibola (SAN) urged government at various levels to focus more on agriculture as the mainstay of the economy.
"With our experience in Ogun State which has made it one of the leading cassava producing states, we need to focus more on agriculture in Nigeria Prince Ajibola stated.

Original_Search_Title: Freedom Fight Hideout Cries for Recognition And Protection
Headline: Kenya: Freedom Fight Hideout Cries for Recognition And Protection
Body_Text:
Nairobi — It is one of the numerous breath-taking sites on the picturesque Taita Hills. Nestling on the foot of one of the hills, the Kenyatta Cave, known to the locals as the Kino cave, is at the tiny Mwanguwi village, about 6km from Wundanyi, the headquarters of the larger Taita-Taveta.
The villagers talk excitedly about the ancient cave as they recall and savour its long and eventful history. It is believed to have been one of the hideouts of Mzee Jomo Kenyatta at the height of the struggle for independence.
Other celebrated political figures said to have called at the cave include the doyen of the Kenyan opposition politics, Jaramogi Oginga Odinga, Mr Bildad Kaggia, Mr Achieng' Oneko, Mr Ronald Ngala and Mr Paul Ngei.
Mzee Zephania Mkuji Nyambu, a political activist and founder member of political lobby group, the Taita Hills Association, and who owned the land on which the cave stands, played host to the freedom struggle luminaries.
Mzee Kenyatta also consorted at the site with well known politicians such as Mr Mengo Woresha and Mr Fredrick Maseghe.
The founding father of the nation and other freedom heroes who met inside the cave, are said to have slept on dried banana leaves as part of the price to pay for the fight for independence.
Mr Nyambu's only surviving widow, Mrs Silvia Manga, 92, whom we traced to Landi village of Mwatate division where the family acquired additional land, remembers meeting and entertaining Mzee Kenyatta and other freedom fighters. She says she and two co-wives enjoyed treating the gallant Kenyans, strictly inside the cave, to traditional dishes composed of cassava, sweet potatoes and meat.
"Our role as women was just to take the food into the cave," recalls Mrs Manga who seems to have good memory despite her advanced age. "We then left immediately, thus we were not privy to whatever transpired among the men led by Kenyatta."
But nothing about the lanky, almost completely toothless woman, whose only complaint is failing eyesight, attests to the fact that she once rubbed shoulders with Kenya's high and mighty. Poverty is rife in her family, and she appeals to the Government to come to their aid, saying that her husband contributed to the fight for freedom.
"My husband died a poor and broken man after his attempt to see the President at State House, Mombasa, for assistance failed as his visit coincided with Kenyatta's death," says the mother of eight.
Mzee Nyambu is reported to have collapsed in the street as he prepared to see his former comrade-in-arms on receiving news of his death.
But in spite of its remarkable history, the cave's role as Mzee Kenyatta's popular hideout remains unknown to many people. It has existed in obscurity for long despite having been the founding father's quiet place for prayer, thought and strategising how to win in the struggle.
Mzee Kenyatta and his colleagues sought refuge here to avoid arrest, and used it as a base to conduct the war against the colonialists.
The need to have it documented as a historical monument was raised recently during a public hearing organised by the Task Force on Criteria and Modalities of Honouring National Heroes and Heroines chaired by Prof Vincent Simiyu. A local political activist, Mr Christopher Kayanda, asked the Government to establish a heroes square at the district level to honour people who could not be honoured nationally.
It was felt also that the exceptional cave should be preserved and recognised as an important historical site. In fact, when we arrived at the quiet Mwanguwi shopping centre looking for a sign that would lead us to it, we were surprised to find none.
Fortunately, however, on making inquiries we were directed to a shop belonging to one of Mzee Nyambu's sons, Mr Julius Righa, 65, who accompanied us to the cave.
As we viewed the cave his brother joined us and the two gave us an account of how their father had turned it into a place of refuge for freedom fighters.
"It also acted as an assembly point where the liberators met in secret to lay strategies of driving the colonialists out of our land," he said. "The cave was an impregnable fortress for Kenyatta and company as it was hidden under a thick, impenetrable forest which colonial security agents could not easily suspect to be harbouring anybody."
He recalled how they were frequently awakened late at night during the many police raids at their home in security operations targeting their father and his "mystery" guests.
But luckily, the liberators were never found as they allegedly sought "protection" from traditional medicinemen who performed rituals that enabled them to detect approaching danger.
It is also claimed that it was at this cave that local seers predicted that Mzee Kenyatta would be jailed by the colonialists and ultimately ascend to the presidency.
For instance, a special Taita ritual in which a goat was slaughtered, would be used in arriving at the prediction. Traditional medicinemen, sorcerers as well as witches and wizards held sway in Taita before the advent of Christianity, and the area has produced some of the most famous witchdoctors in the coast region.
Notable among them is Mzee Mwakishaluwa Mkamba, whose exploits and fame spread far and wide. Due to his uncanny powers, his home became a place of pilgrimage for people seeking traditional healing and protection.
While other Taita leaders had the reputation of fearsome fighters who represented the militant campaign against colonial rule, Mzee Mkamba used "black magic" to overcome it. "Taita had been turned into a place of pilgrimage by people from far and wide who came to consult our seers and medicine men," says Righa.
As a result of Mzee Kenyatta's early interaction with the Taita a strong bond of friendship developed.
His liking for the amiable community continued into his presidency, as attested to by his appointment of locals, Dr Eric Mngola as his personal physician, and retired army major Marsden Madoka, now Mwatate MP, as his bodyguard.
The Kenyatta family has also invested heavily in the district where it owns the vast Gicheha farm in Taveta.
Beehive of activity
But, once a beehive of activity, the large cave has been abandoned and neglected. It is now a study in desolation and dereliction.
It has been encroached upon and the once lush green vegetation around it has been cleared by the Nyambu family for farming.
The local residents speak fondly of their long association with Mzee Kenyatta. A local elder, Mzee Scaver Kiwoi, accuses the past governments of neglecting the cave despite its historical importance.
Its preservation as a historical and tourist site, he says, would not only restore its past glory, but also earn the residents revenue to uplift their living standards.
Caves used to enjoy a special place among the Taita as they used them as traditional shrines.
But they are today forgotten and neglected, victims of modernity and Western religion.
They have been turned into dens of criminals and illicit brewers and a place where children play the hide-and-seek game.
And Kenyatta is not the only cave in this area. Taita Hills are dotted with a vast array of spectacular ones which are unexploited for tourism purposes.
Recently discovered in Taita are caves into which the legendary "man-eaters of Tsavo" dragged and ate 130 railway workers about a century ago.
Caves used as hideouts by the Mau Mau freedom in the Mount Kenya forests have been rehabilitated and preserved as a historical site by a group of youths called the Dark Caves Expedition.

Original_Search_Title: Brothers Held Over Rape of Stepmother
Headline: Uganda: Brothers Held Over Rape of Stepmother
Body_Text:
Kampala — TWO brothers in Atiak internally displaced persons' cam in Amuru district have been arrested for raping their 60-year old stepmother, the Police has said.
On September 15, Joseph Okumu, 21 and James Akena, 34, followed their stepmother to the field, where they raped her, the community liaison officer, Robinal Oceng, told journalists.
The two brothers were arrested on September 17.
"They followed their elderly stepmother who had gone to uproot cassava in the garden and raped her in turns, one after the other," he said.
Rape and defilement are rampant in the displaced persons' camps, with most of them never reaching the Police, he added.
"I want to ask people not to keep quiet about any case of rape.
"They should all be reported to the police so that the perpetrators are punished," he said.
Oceng added that the two brothers, who are being held at Gulu police Station, would appear in court as soon as their file was sanctioned by the resident state attorney.

Original_Search_Title: Gulu's Brighter Side
Headline: Uganda: Gulu's Brighter Side
Body_Text:
Kampala — I WILL never be the same again. That is one thing I know for sure. I have grown up hearing about nothing else about this region but the war and it had never occurred to me that I could travel to northern Uganda.
Its not that I didn't feel for these innocent people who have suffered the war for two decades.
Rather, I was afraid that I could be killed by the rebels or abducted and that fear kept me glued here. Talk about cowards!
And even after I heard that people in northern Uganda were returning home with some amount of peace prevailing, I still didn't feel the urge to tour this place. Why? Because there were stories of landmines blowing off people's limbs!
But journalism, does take you to places you hitherto thought you would never go. As long as you are a reporter, it is your job to travel and go to the ground for good articles. And so I could not hide any longer. A time came and I was asked to take on an assignment in Gulu.
I thought it would take me more than 10 hours from Kampala, for the sheer fact that Gulu, literally means something that is far high, may be out of reach. Surprisingly, it took me about four hours to get there.
If it were not for the endless potholes and the humps, somewhere after Karuma, I believe it would have taken me only three hours to get there. So Gulu wasn't in heaven after all.
I could imagine idle, dark-skinned men sipping local brew from a large pot day in, day out, no permanent structures, let alone get a decent hotel to eat or sleep and that the place must be as dry as the Sahara desert!
But how wrong I was! For rain it did, until I couldn't bear it. For the three days I was there, it rained all through. I froze, but luckily, I had carried my jumper. The fields were green, as if to convince me that this place has never been dry at all. You could see one or two men carrying baskets of cassava or fresh fruit for sell; a constant reminder that this is a fertile place.
I had images of children begging on the streets like the ones in Kampala. I pictured young mothers with over 10 toddlers living in small dirty shacks.
But instead of beggars, I saw children in uniform, heading to school. Even their less fortunate counterparts who are not going to school, appeared to be doing just fine. I did not see anyone coming up to beg for sh100, like the children on Kampala's streets.
Many of them spoke some little English. They knew what to answer when you asked them "How are you?" "What is your name" or "Where do you go to school?" They were laughing and playing; content inspite of their situation.
Going deep into the camps, it was soggy due to an earlier downpour. But gallantly, the barefoot children danced in the mud, clapped and sang joyfully as 'good Samaritans" brought them handouts.
This got me asking how many times I pity myself when things aren't moving really well? Yet, here were children who grew up during the war and were living in filthy squalid camps or homesteads, unsure of what they would eat the next day, but are amazingly happy.
Across the town, I saw women trying the best they could to fend for their families. Everywhere I turned I saw one or two women with babies on their backs riding bicycles loaded with a sack of maize a panga or a hoe.
I was fascinated. While it is very typical for a woman in the city to drive or take a taxi to work, women in this region ride bicycles to go and cultivate their land. Once or twice I looked away, afraid that the babies would fall off the women's backs, if they lost balance. But on looking back, they were steadily riding on. I expected to see a group of boda-boda men or idlers cheering such women on; but everybody seemed to mind their own business.
I also discovered that Gulu is not only for the dark-skinned men. There are as many races as there are in the city, especially the Asians, most of whom speak fluent Acholi. And the natives seemed to have embraced them, as occasionally you meet one or two seated with them recounting tales of war times.
By the time I returned to Kampala, I had a totally different image of Gulu. I feel I could go back and do something for the wives without husbands, mothers with fatherless children and children without parents who are struggling to start all over again; to heal wounds, ease the pain and stabilise families pulled apart by the 20-year LRA war.

Original_Search_Title: Plant Oil for Vehicles
Headline: Namibia: Plant Oil for Vehicles
Body_Text:
Windhoek — International University of Management - Namibia Oil contributes immensely to the human advancement. Furthermore, oil keeps our automobiles, trains, ships, airlines and entire civilization on the move.
However, the world's oil wells are going to dry within a few decades. Meanwhile, new economic giants -- Brazil, China, India, wealthy America, Japan, and some European countries - are prepared to buy oil at any price.
Therefore, to survive and thrive, the developing countries should consider diverse energy options. This article appraises the practicability of another energy resource - bio-diesel - that can contribute to the national energy mix.
Bio-Power
Bio-diesel refers to fuel that originates from biological sources such as vegetables or plants such as soya, corn, maize, rice, cassava, palm fruits, sugarcane, sunflower seeds and jathtropa seeds. Bio-diesel can be used in unmodified diesel. On August 10, 1883, Rudolf Diesel in Augsburg used peanut oil to power his engine. In remembrance of this technological breakthrough, 10th August has been declared International Bio-Diesel Day.
With greater confidence in the future of vegetable oil, in 1912 Rudolf Diesel said: "The use of vegetable oils for engines may seem insignificant today, but such oils may become, in the course of time, as petroleum and coal-tar products of the present time."
Presently, the renowned German automobile manufacturer Volkswagen is working with researchers on the next generation of bio-diesel that would emit less carbon dioxide and make automobile engines more efficient.
Research Progress
In 1977, Brazilian scientist Expidito Parento's discovery helped to produce bio-diesels by converting (trans-esterification) ethanol. In addition, Parento's bio-diesel is validated by international automobile manufacturers.
Currently, Parento's Tecbio Bio-diesel Company is conducting research with the National Aeronautical Space Agency (NASA) and Boeing Airbus Company in the USA on another product known as bio-kerosene, produced by Parento.
In 2005, global bio-diesel production was 3.8 million tons and currently it is used to power automobiles.
Bio-diesel can also be utilized to heat household and industrial boilers. Andrew Robertson, during 'Bio-diesel Expo -2006' in the United Kingdom, tabled a research paper that explained the process of converting exiting boilers to operate by bio-diesel.
Asian Trends
To deal with energy security, since 2002 China has been promoting a fuel-ethanol programme that mandates the use of a 10% blend of bio-ethanol.
Chinese ethanol facilities use vegetable sources such as corn, sorghum, rice and cassava.
Malaysia is the world's largest palm oil exporter in the world. The country has produced 200 million litres of bio-diesel in 2006. Malaysian palm oil has become a key raw material for European bio-diesel stations. I am of the opinion, that by 2010, Malaysia will become the main bio-diesel producer in the world.
Bio-ethanol contributes moderately to Japan's national energy mix. The country imports vast volumes of bio-ethanol from Brazil. Currently, Japan is planning the 'E-3 Program' that promotes the use of a 3% blend of bio-ethanol to petrol countrywide in 2012.
The government of Indonesia has planned to obtain 17% national energy supply from renewable sources including bio-diesel that has already attracted US$12 billion foreign investment and US$3 billion domestic investments.
Chinese state-owned National Offshore Oil Corporation, Hong Kong Energy Company, Japanese Mitsubishi and Brazilian Petrobas are key investors in Indonesian bio-dieses ventures.
African Projects
Inadequate power across the continent has created debilitating effects on the economies of African countries. To face the threat of oil crises and reinforce the national energy mix, now they are leaning towards bio-energy.
Duelco, a South African renewable energy company, has signed a memorandum of understanding with Petromac, a state oil company, to establish a bio-diesel project.
Bob Geldof, a Western poverty reduction activist, has established 400 hectares of jathropa plantation, which is situated 200 km from the capital city of Swaziland. To add bio-fuels to the national energy mix, a few months ago Namibia imported seeds from India to promote jathropa plants among farmers in the northern regions.
Dangote Sugar Company in Nigeria that owns the largest sugar factory complex has expressed its willingness for an ethanol plant. Ernest Janovsky, head of the agricultural unit of the First National Bank in Johannesburg, is of the opinion that African countries within the high rainfall belt, such as Angola, Zambia and Mozambique, own huge potential to produce crops that are suitable for bio-diesel.
Feedstock Yield
The plant-based raw materials used to produce bio-diesel are known as feedstock. The feedstock-yield efficiency per hectare is a key factor that decides the cost-effectiveness of bio-diesel as an energy source. With the current technology, 0.4 hectare of soya beans can produce 375 litres of clean-burning bio-diesel fuel.
Algae give the highest yield of feedstock - 250 times of bio-diesel litres per hectare than soya beans.
A New Zealand company uses municipal sewerage waste to culture algae to produce bio-diesel. Micheal Briggs, an American scientist, believes that oil-rich algae can be grown in sewerage ponds at municipal wastewater treatment facilities.
The feedstock yield (litres of oil per hectare) of some crops are as follows: Algae=>47, 500,Palm Oil=>5,950,Coconut=>2689, Jathropa seeds=>892, Peanuts=>1,059, Sunflower seeds=>952,Pumpkin seeds=>534,Soya beans=> 446,Cotton seed=>325, Corn (maize)=>172.
Some Arguments
Many African countries cannot allocate adequate arable lands for bio-fuel plantations without jeopardizing the national food supply. For instance, the bio-farm boom can threaten food security on the African continent.
According to Nampa/Reuter, in South Africa, the prices of staple maize have risen around 40% this year.
Land occupied by Malaysian palm oil plantations have raised from 54 00 hectares in 1975 to 4 million in 2004 and encroached on thousands of hectares of tropical forests.
Rising palm oil prices have increased the food prices in Malaysia and Indonesia, punishing the poor. Moreover, the world sugar price is predicted to increase with the expanding use of sugarcane for bio-ethanol.
Future of Bio-diesel
Fossil fuels - coal, natural gas and oil - have been the energy workhorses that have powered modern civilization for the last 100 years and will continue to play a decisive role in the next 100 years too.
In relation to fossil fuels, renewables can help to reduce global warming.
Nevertheless, with our current technological knowledge, renewables energy sources like bio-diesel could never replace conventional fossil fuel entirely. However, bio-diesel can be a considerable part of the energy mix.

Original_Search_Title: Alliance for a Green Revolution - a Blunt Philanthropic Arrow
Headline: Africa: Alliance for a Green Revolution - a Blunt Philanthropic Arrow
Body_Text:
Rather than proposing techno-fixes to problems of agricultural development in Africa, donors could better assist in the development of rural infrastructure such as roads and water supplies, and education to empower the younger generation in the study of useful science. African farmers, along with peasants around the world, are seeking respect for their right to decide on what to plant and how to plant it, as well what to eat and how.
It is a common saying that when a man has a hammer in his hand every problem appears to be a nail. It takes a wise man to know that a hammer is just one of the tools in the craftsman's box. The Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation made money from technology. It is understandable that they should think that problems can always be solved with a technological fix. Nor is it surprising that the Rockefeller and Gates Foundations should plan to jointly plough $150,000,000 into their so-called Alliance for a Green Revolution in Africa (AGRA). Tragically, the biotech solutions proposed by AGRA are likely to deepen rather than solve problems of hunger, poverty and malnutrition in Africa.
The Gates Foundation has recently taken on scientists from the biotech industry. It is expected to fund projects in areas such as biotechnology to improve seeds and crop yields; fertilizer, irrigation and other farm management systems; access to markets; and advocacy for improved agricultural policies. They may claim otherwise, but the idea of AGRA is anchored around agricultural modern biotechnology or genetically modified organisms (GMOs). Yet, genetically modified crops, on the admission of the US Department of Agriculture, do not give better yields than conventional crops. In addition, the plan's entire framework would turn African farm practices on their heads, wiping out local knowledge and creating more poverty, hunger and strange new diseases.
What is not being said is that people are not going hungry today because of insufficient food production. Indeed, it is generally agreed that there is enough food in the world to meet everyone's basic needs. An action plan adopted in March by ministers of the Economic Community of West African States admits that food production in West Africa has doubled over the last 20 years and that only 19 per cent of food needs are met from imports.
So what is the real reason behind the emphasis on biotechnology? The biotech industry has invested hugely in attempts to penetrate Africa - through food aid channels and other channels of assistance, as well as through commercial routes. However, the food aid channel blew up in the face of the industry and that of the World Food Programme in 2002 when Zambia rejected genetically modified corn as food aid.
AGRA's biotech thrust is wrong-headed: rather than solving problems of hunger and poverty in Africa, it will deepen them. Genetically modified crops create dependence on chemicals such as herbicides as some varieties are engineered to be herbicide tolerant, which often leads to the emergence of super-weeds. Efforts at popularising GMOs have been carried out by both USAID and the International Institute for Tropical Agriculture in circles that have excluded critical opinion. Wherever contrasting views have been elicited, local people and farmers generally reject this technology. AGRA's suggestion that Africa needs a 'green revolution' does not appear to have considered the many pitfalls of that revolution.
Efforts at introducing GMOs in Africa have so far yielded poor returns. To take just one example, that of cassava engineered to overcome the cassava leaf mosaic disease. This has so far failed. There are already non-GM varieties that do withstand the disease. Why waste resources that could be better used to strengthen agricultural production in Africa, drawing on the rich pool of local knowledge and ensuring food sovereignty, as demanded by farmers and civil society groups at the recent forum in Selingue, Mali? Africa is not seeking handouts in order to improve its agricultural production systems. And certainly not a push towards a so-called green revolution baptised in chemical fertilizers and other imported inputs. African farmers, along with peasants around the world, are seeking respect for their right to decide on what to plant and how to plant it, as well what to eat and how.
Agriculture means far more than the mechanical multiplication of seeds. It is the basis of the African's life. It provides the platform for cultural, religious, economic and even political relations. If the Gates and Rockefeller Foundations wish to extend the hand of fellowship to the African continent, they should move away from strategies that favour monoculture, lead to land-grabs, and tie local farmers to the shop-doors of biotech seed monopolies. Instead, they can assist in the development of rural infrastructure such as roads and water supplies, and education to empower the younger generation in the study of useful science.
This article was first published in Alliance [http://www.alliancemagazine.org/]
* Nnimmo Bassey is Executive Director of Environmental Rights Action, Nigeria. http://www.eraction.org/

Original_Search_Title: Big Potential And Challenges for Biofuels
Headline: Africa: Big Potential And Challenges for Biofuels
Body_Text:
Durban — Biofuels offer Africa the chance to supply itself with alternative energy sources, and also to become a major supplier of these sources for developed markets. Yet, challenges -- from creating the relevant infrastructure to competition for biofuel crops from food markets -- remain.
"There is huge room for exploitation of clean sources of energy at a time when the entire world is confronted with global warming," said Salvador Namburete, Mozambique's minister of energy.
He was speaking at the First Annual Africa Biofuels Conference & Expo, held in the South African coastal city of Durban; the event concludes Thursday.
The European Union (EU) has mandated 10 percent biofuel use by motorists and industry within this region by 2020, and its commissioner for agriculture has told the press several times that 20 percent of that use would probably come from imports.
Europe has preferential trade agreements with Least Developed Countries (LDCs) under the Everything But Arms (EBA) initiative, enabling biofuels from these nations to enter the European market duty- and quota-free.
The LDC category was created by the United Nations in 1971 in recognition of how the world's poorest states need special assistance; it presently comprises 50 countries -- including Mozambique -- most in Africa. Certain LDCs have land suitable for growing biofuel crops, and offer prospects to investors who could import the technology for transforming crops into fuel.
In addition, opportunities exist for African-produced biofuels to enter the U.S. market under the African Growth and Opportunities Act, Washington's answer to EBA. Import markets are developing in other major global economies like Japan, and a handful of African countries are looking at biofuel use as well.
In the face of these opportunities, however, questions are being raised across Africa about the shortage of infrastructure to cope with biofuel production.
"Lack of infrastructure in African countries weighs down opportunities for biofuel use. You can produce it, but if you can't get it to the users at a reasonable price there's no point," said Vinesh Moodly, refinery and deployment manager for D1 Oils Africa Plc, a Johannesburg-based company.
Brazil, the world's largest exporter of ethanol, a fuel produced from sugarcane, is also struggling with infrastructure difficulties. These are not only putting pressure on supplies available for export, but also lowering prices -- because exporters are forced to sell due to a lack of port facilities.
Brazil's state-owned petroleum company, Petrobras, and local industry are exploring the development of several intra-state ethanol pipelines to avoid transporting fuel via the country's poor highway network.
A 450-kilometre pipeline from Mozambique's capital, Maputo, to South Africa is already under development, says Namburete, as well as several liquid fuel storage tanks in the country's second-largest city -- Beira -- which is close to where much of Mozambique's biofuel development is currently underway.
Many of Africa's most viable biofuel producers -- in terms of production costs for sugarcane, maize or cassava -- are landlocked, however, and don't have the transportation infrastructure in place to enable export of the fuels, or immediate plans for infrastructure investment like Mozambique.
"Another question is how to make sure that the land concession process is monitored in such a way that all land is being used for the purpose that it has been allotted for. We have 36 million hectares of arable land, and until two years ago, only five million hectares were under production," said Namburete.
"Since then, we have land requests for five million hectares just for biodiesel. The challenge is to make correct allocations and make sure they truly produce biofuels."
Furthermore, "We have to find out how to ensure an adequate balance between biofuel production and food production."
Namburete said Mozambique's 36 million hectares of arable land could be used for biofuel production "without threatening food production", while another 41 million hectares of marginal land would be suitable for raising jatropha, a tree that produces non-edible seeds which can be used for making biodiesel.
The food versus fuel debate is an especially important one in Africa, where harsh climatic conditions, civil disturbance and poor land use patterns cause millions to go without their daily food requirements.
When moving forward with biofuel projects, it is vital to ensure the food supply isn't in jeopardy in the region where the project is being developed, according to Justin Vermaak, chief executive officer of the Durban-based Verus Company Group.
"Can you imagine my doing a biofuel project in Zimbabwe? No way. How can we produce biofuels where there isn't enough food?...So we need to produce from (crops) that don't compete with food."
According to the U.N's Food and Agriculture Organisation, more than a third of Zimbabweans suffer from food shortages. While the country has been gripped by drought in recent years, its food crisis is mostly ascribed to political and economic mismanagement on the part of the government.
Vermaak says biofuels have not created new agricultural production worldwide -- only diverted existing production.
"All we've done is taken stock out of the system. There has been no new development, just a new consumer of the same product, putting more stress on the same system."
Several countries in Africa are developing biofuel policies to help state-owned and commercial companies alike bring about biofuel production, and some are creating local markets. Small countries like Rwanda, which are not only landlocked but suffer from high petroleum import costs, are looking at biodiesel production for the local market.
"People developing biofuels in Africa hold their information close to their heart, thinking that one tiny bit of information will give them a commercial edge. That's bull. Share it and we'll all do better together," said Vermaak.

Original_Search_Title: They Raise School Fees By Roasting Maize
Headline: Uganda: They Raise School Fees By Roasting Maize
Body_Text:
Kampala — ROSEMARY Nansambu, 9, would not be studying at Shalom Primary School, Mulago, if she was not selling maize on the streets.
She began selling maize last year after the death of her father, Charles Muyondo. Nansambu cannot recall the name of her mother, who died when she was younger but says: "Whenever I think of my mother, I cry."
Nansambu sells roasted and boiled maize in the evening near her home. Her three siblings do not go to school; they stay at home with their grandmother.
"I used to go with my grandmother to Kalerwe Market to buy the maize. When she started falling sick very often, I began the maize business. I had to do everything on my own," Nansambu says.
Nalongo, Nansambu's grandmother, says she is worried about the future of the orphans. "These children are not in school. I cannot afford to pay their fees. Nansambu is in school because she raises her school fees," she adds.
The grandmother of four says if it was not for lack of school fees and other basic needs, her granddaughter would be about to complete P.7. However, she says, there is a shortage of funds when there is a scarcity of maize.
During such times, Nansambu and her grandmother, sell roast cassava and sweet potatoes to earn a living.
However, many other children face Nansambu's plight. At Makerere, many children, Nansambu's age, sit in groups, selling food. Most of them, stay in the surrounding areas of Mulago, kikoni, Kawaala, Nakulabye and Katanga.
One Meddie Mubiru, 13, goes to Makerere University in the evening to sell maize. He studies at All Saints Primary School in Mulago.
He began selling maize at the age of eight, when his father abandoned him and his siblings.
"One day, my father came back home jobless. From that time, whenever we asked him for money to buy sugar and bread, he would say he was broke. He left us helpless. Since then, I have not seen him, although I'm told he lives in Kalerwe," Mubiru adds.
When his mother became ill, Mubiru started selling maize to raise school fees. "Whenever my mother was sick, I assisted her to run the business. I had to sell maize at Makerere University. At the university, I made friends with a cleaner, who advised me to sell maize in halls of residence and hostels around the campus," he says. Mubiru, who is now in P.6, wants to become a pilot.
Gladys Ayikoru, 12, studies at Makerere Church of Uganda Primary School. She also sells maize to raise money for her upkeep. However, Ayikoru stays with her parents. "My mother prepares the maize for me to sell. She stays home, while I go to Makerere university, where my father works as a security guard," Ayikoru says.
Florence Masia, who goes to school at Makerere Church of Uganda Primary School, says she was forced to start hawking maize at a tender age after her father died. "I get money for my school fees and other scholastic materials from selling maize on the streets in the evening.
Ten-year-old Olivia Gasi's mother died in April this year. On top of raising her school fees, Gasi has to take care of her three-month-old sibling. However, Gasi has managed to return to school because her aunt took the baby with her to Wakiso.
A report by the Save the Children's Fund in 2002, a national coverage survey shows that the majority of pupils aged six to 18 have dropped out of school at the primary level.
The report adds that the cost of uniforms, textbooks and exercise books was the main reason they dropped out of school.
According to the report released by Platform for labour Action in 2004, child labour continues to be a major problem of the developing world.
Platform for labour action is an NGO that strives to prevent child labour, by rehabilitating children. It aids children by giving them primary, secondary and vocational education.
