In Uganda, it is estimated that children make up 62% of the poor; 2.7 billion of these children are working and more than half of them are aged between 10-14 years. The report reveals that with such statistics, it is inevitable that many of these children will end up doing hazardous, dangerous and exploitative work. According to the report, 227 children have been removed from work they were engaged in, 150 of these were in high-risk work environments. Seventy-six children have been placed in school and provided with basic scholastic materials like uniforms, exercise books and pens.

Original_Search_Title: Teso Floods Damage Estimated at Shs120b
Headline: Uganda: Teso Floods Damage Estimated at Shs120b
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE cost of the damage caused by floods in Teso sub-region has been put at Shs120 billion. The figure covers six districts of Amuria, Katakwi, Bukedea, Kaberamaido, Kumi and Soroti.
According to a report compiled by LC5 chairpersons in the areas, Amuria District requires Shs50.8 billion for reconstruction while Bukedea needs Shs9.6 billion, Kaberamaido Shs4.3 billion, Katakwi Shs25.3 billion, Kumi Shs12.1 billion and Soroti Shs17.6 billion. Most of the funds would go towards restocking food, relocation of IDP camps, rehabilitation and improvement of school environment, reconstruction of roads, disease out breaks, shelter and safe water among others. The region local leaders are appealing for national and international intervention.
"The region appeals for immediate intervention from government, UN agencies, international and national humanitarian organisations, embassies and Ugandans in the diaspora," the report reads. The heavy rains that have hit the country for the last three months caused flooding in many parts of the country. Teso sub region has been the most affected, limiting access to many areas and affecting the provision and delivery of social services and humanitarian services.
Most sub-counties in the districts have been deserted due to the floods while roads and bridges were damaged, making it difficult to reach the affected people. Seventy Three schools have not been able to open for the third term that started on September 17.
The government and a host of humanitarian organisations have been sending food consignments to the region. Food aid that includes posho, beans, cassava stems and potato veins were also sent to Amuria, Bukedea and Katakwi. According to the report, out of 958,617 people in the Teso sub region, 246,394 people, representing 26 per cent, have been affected by the floods.
The report says the situation could get worse. "Ahead of the region is a prolonged hunger period because of the loss of crops. The main source of livelihood is agriculture and livestock, which have been destroyed," the report said.
Because many roads are becoming inaccessible, transport fares and the cost of available food stuffs have also gone up. The report also says 14 IDP camps in Katakwi and Amuria need relocation. Ealier during a press conference, the Teso flood Relief Task Force announced a charity walk that would take place on Saturday from the National Theatre to Kololo Air strip.
The walk, to be led by President Yoweri Museveni, is to create awareness and call upon well wishers, international and national cooperate organisations to come to the aid of Teso.
Meanwhile, essential drugs in Katakwi are continuing to run out, especially in areas that were cut off. According to a snap survey conducted by Daily Monitor yesterday, anti-malaria drugs have run out in most of the far off health centres that serve IDP camps in the district.
Health centres that have been affected most by the drug shortage are the lower units in Ngariam and Magoro sub counties.
ADDITIONAL REPORTING BY RICHARD OTIM & AL-MAHDI SSENKABIRWA

Original_Search_Title: Widows Cashing in On Garbage
Headline: Rwanda: Widows Cashing in On Garbage
Body_Text:
Kigali — Buoyed by the increasing volume of garbage in Kigali City, the Dusabane Association is cashing in on making and selling manure from the rubbish.
Each home is charged at least Frw1, 000 monthly in order to keep Kigali clean from generating too much garbage.
Women workers process the solid waste into fertilisers.
The current market price is Frw1500-2000 per wheelbarrow, while a 5 tonne truck costs Frw30, 000.
Madina Uzamushaka, the president of the association said they received assistance from the government and a Canadian Cooperation to start the fertiliser business.
"We got wheelbarrows, bags, gloves, computers, chairs and a house where we conduct our business," she said.
Uzamushaka said they are planning to install a machine for processing cassava, potatoes and banana peels into charcoal.
There are about 115 members in the association but only 79 members are involved in the garbage disposal business.
"The number of members is increasing due to profitability of the business," said Uzamushaka.
"Saving was a dream among our members before, but now we have started to smile."

Original_Search_Title: Projects to Resettle IDPs Set Up
Headline: Uganda: Projects to Resettle IDPs Set Up
Body_Text:
Kampala — They clapped and chanted as their group leader received oxen and ox-ploughs.
"Don't use them here in the camp. Go and use it in your village," said Jimmy Owani of the Food and Agricultural Organisation (FAO), who was handing over the implements at Unyama in Gulu.
For many years, agricultural development in the north and north eastern Uganda has been based in camps. However, because donors and the Government are encouraging the people to go back to their homes, they are giving out implements to organised groups that are ready to return home.
During the 20 years of insurgency in the region, the land in this part of the country has remained untilled and so is very fertile and fit for agricultural production.
A lot of money is being pumped into revitalising agricultural systems in this region. The Government has been running an agricultural capacity-building project, worth sh18b. Though there has been controversy in Gulu and some parts of Teso where rotting seeds and poor quality pangas were given to farmers, the Government is trying to ensure the people resettle in their homes and engage in income-generating activities.
One such form of intervention is the cassava project run by the FAO. This project targets over 2,300 families. Under the project, cassava cuttings will be given to those farmers who have already returned to their homes. "I have already prepared 10 acres in my home at Okigayere village," says Alex Eyala, a farmer, who says he needs at least 10 sacks of cuttings to start off.
The returnees also have the rice project to fall back to. The beneficiaries are given rice seeds, hoes, jerry-cans, tape measures, protective gears, pangas and sprayers.
Fish ponds are being set up in the region. According to Moses Kilama, an extension eorker in charge of the ponds, one of the fish multiplication ponds is in Ocokean, Parak in Gulu district.
"The fish bred in this pond will help reduce the shortage of food in the region," he says.
"If we give them this aid under the groups they formed within their camps, many of them may not want to return to their areas," says Owani. There is also a project to help orphans and people living with HIV in Kitgum district.
Most of these interventions are done after sensitising the beneficiaries on how to use them
Post-harvest handling programmes are incorporated in most of the projects.
FAO has, for example, set up several flour mills across the region, to enable farmers produce food for their families.

Original_Search_Title: Sunflower - New Hope for Bukedea
Headline: Uganda: Sunflower - New Hope for Bukedea
Body_Text:
Kampala — While most crops in Teso are rotting in the fields due to floods, a farmers group in Malera sub-county in Bukedea District hopes to bag sh441m from sunflower.
Michael Ikara, the deputy chairperson of the Popular Kumi Women's Initiative (PAKWI), a grassroots group says: "Unlike most crops, the sunflower can be grown on the same piece of land four times in a year. The crop is neither affected by heavy rains nor prolonged drought.Besides, it takes only two days to dry after harvest.
"Once we satisfy the local market and start exporting the crop, our living standards will improve. We shall also shift to modern farming methods in line with the Government's plan for modernising agriculture," Ikara says.
Sunflower is considered a first-priority crop in Bukedea and the district leaders are promoting it because the local people have a lot of experience in its production.
"The district enjoys two favourable seasons for agricultural production," says Sam Ebukalin, the LC5 chairman.
In addition, the local leaders are promoting cassava growing and poultry farming.
Unlike in the past when the farmers were discouraged by low prices and inaccessibility, Ebukalin says today the market is available, especially, with the help of the National Agricultural Advisory Services (NAADS).
Out of the 460 NAADs farmers, 336 (about 73%) are engaged in poultry farming, which depends on sunflower for feeds.
Partners like the Teso Private Sector have also embarked on sunflower production.
"We are working with Serere Research Institute to produce varieties of sunflower to broaden the market," he says.
However, the farmer are faced with one challenge - opening up the roads to promote rural industrialisation.
Ebukalin agrees that the district, which is recovering from the ravages of war, cannot improve the infrastructure because they have limited resources. The problem has been compounded by the floods in the region.
Rachel Nandelenga of the Food and Agriculture Organisation (FAO) applauds the project. She says whereas sunflower farming has been successful in all their pilot districts, the group in Bukedea stands out.
The project which started in 1993, with 12 members striving to improve education standards and welfare of internally displaced persons (IDPs), now boasts of a membership of 450, with 1,050 acres of sunflower crop.
The project equips small-scale farmers with improved techniques of processing the crop. It is funded by FAO through the Office of the Vice President and the ministry of agriculture.
It will soon embark on community-based nutritional education and communication programmes and oil seed processing.
Ebukalin says FAO recently gave them a motorised milling machine for processing edible oil.
"We have a big market in Kampala where we are required to supply over 250 tonnes of seeds monthly. Serena Hotel wants 300 litres of cooking oil per week. The demand is high," Ebukalin says.
With this machine, Safia Aseku, a member of the group, estimates they will process 800 litres of cooking oil per day, up from 80 litres per week.
"We can satisfy the demand," she says.
She says FAO will train extension workers to advise the farmers on sunflower cultivation, seed-crushing, animal-feed production and soap-making methods.

Original_Search_Title: Food Production to Halve By 2020
Headline: Africa: Food Production to Halve By 2020
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — Food security in Africa is likely to be "severely compromised" by climate change, with production expected to halve by 2020, according to climate change experts.
The projections are contained in a report launched last week in London by the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC), which was followed by an experts' panel discussion.
"The discussions [last week] concluded that Africa is likely to be the most affected [by climate change] partly because of the increasing aridity in the north [the Sahel] and Southern Africa: and these are the most populous parts of the continent," said Martin Parry, the co-chair of the IPCC's working group which authored the report. He also listed the lack of technology available to adapt to environmental change as other aspects which would make the region vulnerable.
About 25 percent of Africa's population - nearly 200 million people - do not have easy access to water; that figure is expected to jump by another 50 million by 2020 and more than double by the 2050s, according to the report.
Over 95 percent of Africa's agriculture depends on rainfall, according to the UN's Food and Agriculture Organisation (FAO). "Models indicate that 80,000 square km of agricultural land in sub-Saharan Africa currently deemed constrained will improve as a result of climate change. However, 600,000 square km currently classed as moderately constrained will become severely limited", according to the FAO.
As the global average temperature rises by one to three degrees Celsius, northern industrialised countries may well gain in food production, but in lower latitudes - especially in subsistence sectors, where rain fed agriculture is the norm - even with a minimal rise in global temperature crop potential will most probably decline, according to the FAO.
This year drought-affected parts of southern Africa - Zimbabwe, Swaziland and Lesotho - experienced a 40 percent to 60 percent reduction in maize production, for which global warming was partly to blame, according to the World Meteorological Organisation (WMO).
But the IPCC report was more cautious. "The contribution of climate to food insecurity in Africa is still not fully understood, particularly the role of other multiple stresses that enhance impacts of droughts and floods and possible future climate change".
However, the report warned that crop revenues in Africa could fall by as much as 90 percent by 2100 and predicted that wheat production was likely to disappear from Africa by the 2080s.
Mitigate
"It is too late to avoid all climate change impact", warned Parry. "Our choice now is between a damaged world and a severely damaged world...and if we do not restrict carbon emissions now then we may well embark on a pathway to [a global warming] well above two degrees Celsius".
Our choice now is between a damaged world and a severely damaged world...and if we do not restrict carbon emissions now then we may well embark on a pathway to [a global warming] well above two degrees Celsius
Scientist have speculated that even holding the temperature increase to two degrees Celsius, a target adopted by the European Union, was too high and enough to trigger melting of the Greenland ice sheet.
Human drivers within Africa are also affecting its ecosystems. Dependency on fuelwood and charcoal as sources of energy in rural areas contribute to about 80 percent to 90 percent of the residential energy needs in low-income households. Besides, fire incidents represent a huge threat to tropical forests in Africa, according to the IPCC report. "An estimated 70 percent of detected forest fires occur in the tropics, with 50 percent of them being in Africa."
Parry called on the industrialised countries participating this week in the UN's annual general assembly debate, which is focusing on climate change this year, to help the developing world by providing them with technology to develop greener energy alternatives such as solar power.
Build resilience
Faced with extreme poverty and lack of resources, African countries should also concentrate on building it people's resilience, said Parry. "Countries must attempt to reduce the vulnerability of its people and draw up adaptation plans".
The IPCC report suggests water-harvesting system to supplement rain-fed farming, weather insurance, national grain reserves, cash transfers and school feeding schemes as adaptation measures. It also highlights the benefits of biotechnology research in Africa if it could lead to drought and pest-resistant rice, drought-tolerant maize and insect-resistant millet, sorghum and cassava.
A summary of the IPCC report was published in April this year.
The IPCC set up by the WMO and the United Nations Environment Programme in 1988 is the world's most authoritative voice on climate change. The panel comprising leading world experts assesses scientific, technical and socioeconomic information to understand the risk of climate change and draws up options for adaptation and mitigation.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: LWF/WS, Finn Church Aid Assist Farmers
Headline: Liberia: LWF/WS, Finn Church Aid Assist Farmers
Body_Text:
The Lutheran World Federation/Department of World Service (LWF/WS) with support from an International Church Based Organization in Finland (FINN CHURCH AID), has empowered thousands of citizens and residents of Panta and Zota Districts in Bong County in the areas of Agriculture, and the construction of bridges, hand pumps, latrines, among others.
The citizens and residents, who have been speaking to the press in Bong County, praised the Lutheran World Federation/World Service and the FINN CHURCH AID for empowering them through the various productive activities, which now serve as a source of livelihood.
They said the LWF/WS and FINN CHURCH AID empowered them in Watson education through the construction and maintenance of hand pumps, bridges and latrines, and in Agriculture activities, which include the provision of family implements and knowledge in crops production.
Most of those spoken to last week during an assessment and evaluation tour by a team from the LWF/WS and FINN CHURCH AID, said the projects were divided into two communities, namely the Watson and Agriculture communities.
The Watson Community includes; Joe Tow, Koryah, Moanga, Gbansu, Woumeh, Mbellequah, Yowee and Farvey, while the Agriculture Communities include; Garmu, Kpo, Boway, Gbainyear, Jorwah, Gbaita, Pa;;aquelleh, Woumeh, Koryah, Mbellequah.
The beneficiaries of the two districts however appealed to the LWF/WS and the FINN CHURCH AID, including humanitarian groups and individuals to assist them with cassava machines for the production of 'gari' and other basic food acquired from cassava.
Speaking to the press after a 3-day assessment and evaluation tour to Panta and Zota Districts in Bong County, Lutheran World Federation/World Service Project manager, Madam Mary Leesolee, said the projects in the two districts started in April of 2007 and have benefited thousands of the citizens and residents, most of whom are returning IDPs and refugees.
Madam Leesolee said activities of the projects include: Watson, which she said has a component of hygiene promotion, pump, latrine and bridge construction, and Agriculture assistance to returning families, along with protection issues with emphasis on HIV/AIDS, Child Protection, Women and Children Protection, and sexual exploitation.
She described the projects in the two districts as effective and that they are having positive effects on the lives of the people because the LWF/WS was the very first organization to reach to their aid since their return from IDP and refugee camps, in rebuilding their lives.
Madam Leesolee estimated the cost of the projects as two hundred and ninety-one thousand United States Dollars for both administrative and project implementation.
For her part, FINN CHURCH AID Programme Coordinator on Humanitarian Aid, Madam Mari Paajanen, said her organization remains supportive to initiatives designed to improve the lives of Liberians in need. She said, once there are funds, FINN CHURCH AID is prepared to decentralize its activities across the country, especially to provide assistance to less fortunate rural dwellers.
Madam Paajanen called on Liberians to take their destiny in their own hands by managing peace and engage in productive ventures.
The Administrative Assistant at the Lutheran World Federation/World Service, Madam Musu Mulbah, who is also part of the assessment team, called for the construction of more schools in rural Liberia to help accommodate the thousands of school-going children currently out of school due to lack of schools.
Madam Mulbah earnestly called on representatives and senators of Bong County to direct their attention for the provision of education in Panta and Zota Districts.

Original_Search_Title: The Battle of the Floods
Headline: Uganda: The Battle of the Floods
Body_Text:
Amaserniko — Francis Egoliam believed the downpour was a one-off in this north-eastern region of Uganda that is usually very dry. But then waves of water started swirling through his homestead and the houses of unbaked mud bricks started melting, leaving his family homeless and hungry.
"We all thought it was going to be short-lived but we realised it had become a daily phenomenon and the floods started. Our crops have been destroyed by the water and houses have collapsed," says Egoliam of his village's ordeal at Amaseniko camp in Amuria district.
Local leaders said that up to 147 houses had collapsed in the camp while most of the latrines were overflowing. "But the main problem is that our children are not attending classes," Egoliam said, because the school block has become a haven for 500 people displaced by the floods.
The residents had returned to the gardens to retrieve whatever food remained. "But all we get is a rotten harvest. Groundnuts, cassava tubers and sweet potatoes have all rotted," said mother of eight, Margaret Akol, 36.
Some people braved the pools of water to cross to drier areas where government and relief agency officials are trying to assess the situation.
"The waves took my bicycle when I tried to cross a flooded area from Tukumu [a village some distance from Amaseniko] to go to school 22km away. Now I cannot go to school because it is a long distance. We are completely cut off. It is like we are in a cell," said 17-year-old Henry Epau, a student of St Francis Secondary School.
Malaria on the rise
Water and sanitation facilities have been severely damaged, and with many latrines collapsing, there are fears of disease as mini-lagoons separate houses in the camp.
Florence Asege, the local nurse at a government health centre 5km away, told reporters that incidences of malaria, diarrhoeal diseases and acute respiratory infections were on the increase. "We need more drugs, but we are cut off. We are only eight at the centre, but we have now to attend to up to 76 patients a day and 60 of these are malaria and acute cough cases," she said.
"The general situation is very bad and rough. Two days ago we lost two children who drowned. We sleep in very appalling conditions because we fear the houses collapsing on us because many of them have collapsed," Robert Osujo, the Amaseniko camp leader, said.
The UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs (OCHA) has launched an appeal for US$43 million in emergency assistance for the flood-affected Ugandans.
Officially, up to 18 people have died since the floods started, but local leaders put the figure higher at 45, though government representatives would not be drawn on the discrepancy.
Emergency measures
Most bridges in the area have been washed away and Musa Ecweru, the minister in charge of disaster preparedness, relief and refugees, who has been coordinating the humanitarian response in the region, said on 24 September that Katakwi district, for example, had been completely cut off and could only be accessed by air.
He said a dry spell that lasted three days was broken on 23 September as rains resumed for 24 hours. "We now need to rely on air transport to deliver food items to the districts, but we are just praying that it stops raining," Ecweru said by phone from Soroti.
The crisis prompted the government last week to declare a state of emergency in affected areas of the east, the first time President Yoweri Museveni has resorted to such a measure in his 21-year tenure. Forecasts predict more rain in many parts of Africa over the coming days.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Ethanol Production Next Year
Headline: Ghana: Ethanol Production Next Year
Body_Text:
Accra — Caltech Ventures Ghana Limited could begin the production of ethanol from cassava at Hodzo near Ho next year when its 6.5 million dollars production plant would be ready.
Managing Director, Mr. Chris Quarshie, told newsmen on Saturday that the company's total investment in the venture is 10 million U.S. dollars. The company has put 400 acres under cassava cultivation with plans to expand to 1,200 acres next year. He said 60 percent of the 6 million litres of ethanol to be produced yearly would be exported.
Mr. Quarshie said the company, which has an out-growers' scheme, has the potential to provide 600 jobs up from the current 140 when in full production.
He described the relationship between the company and the people of Hodzo as excellent and has donated five computers with accessories to five junior high schools in the area for computer training.

Original_Search_Title: Teso Transport Fares Soar
Headline: Uganda: Teso Transport Fares Soar
Body_Text:
Kampala — TRANSPORT fares and commodity prices have gone up in most parts of flood-hit Teso. The chairman of the Teso Region District Chairpersons Forum, Robert Ekongot, said moving from the sub-counties to the districts' towns costs over sh15,000 from between sh2,000 and sh3,000.
Ekongot said a sachet of salt costs sh1,000 from sh200. Earlier on, the Teso chairpersons met the Teso Parliamentary Group to discuss the effect of floods in the area.
The chairpersons included Julius Ochen of Amuria, Robert Engulu of Kaberamaido, Stephen Ochula of Soroti, Ismail Orot of Kumi and Rev. Sam Ebukalin of Bukedea.
The leaders attributed the increase in prices and fares to the floods that have rendered most roads inaccessible. The leaders said most bridges leading to major towns had been destroyed by the floods.
"The devastation of road networks has left most fares increased since there is a lot of hiring. Saloon and other small cars can no longer reach some of the sub-counties. Whereas it used to take only two hours to Soroti from Amuria, it now takes over six hours," Ekongot explained.
"Acurimongi, the biggest market in Katakwi, has not operated for the last two days. This is the place where people buy commodities like vegetables and other foodstuffs. We are in problems," he added.
Ekongot predicted that the situation might get worse with the increasing rains.
Ebukalin said 14,000 acres of cassava worth sh14.5b had been destroyed by the floods and 106 houses worth over sh100m had collapsed.
"The situation is not getting better. The rains started when we were about to harvest. Right now, all the gardens of cassava, groundnuts, sorghum and other crops have been destroyed. After the rains, it will be famine," he predicted.
The leaders called on the works minister, Eng. John Nasasira, to quickly dispatch a team to repair some of the roads and collapsed bridges.

Original_Search_Title: Agriculture - Thailand Partners Investors
Headline: Nigeria: Agriculture - Thailand Partners Investors
Body_Text:
Abuja — The Thai Charge d'affairs, Mr Niyom Wattammawut has urged Nigerian businessmen to explore the many investment opportunities, especially in agriculture, available in Thailand which is the fastest growing economy in South East Asia.
This was at a seminar themed "Thai-Nigerian Partnership in Globalization" aimed at showcasing the economic potentials which Thailand holds for intending investors.
Wattammawut added that cassava which is in abundance in Nigeria is also peculiar to Thailand and a lot of profit can be made by the two countries through the production of industrial starch, animal chips and cassava flour, if an exporting partnership is entered into.
He disclosed that the country has an open trade investment policy and has free trade agreement with other Asia and some South American countries.
He further explained that the Sufficiency Economic Philosophy which was introduced by His Majesty King Bhumidol Adulyadej of Thailand saved the country from the economic crises of 1997 which almost crippled the economy.
Sufficiency Economy is a policy which preaches moderation, reasonableness and self immunity for sufficient protection from impact arising from internal and external changes.
Nigeria's former Ambassador to Thailand, Prince Ademola Adenrele stated that Nigeria hopes to become an industrialized economy like Thailand by the year 2020. He added that "the bi-lateral relationship between the two countries will offer Nigeria the opportunity to learn from Thailand's experience, in the area of small and medium scale, agro-industrial sector of our economy as well as tourism and hospitality businesses".
Documentaries by Nigerian journalists who visited Thailand recently on a familiarization tour were shown to the audience.
One of the journalists, Mr Ike Abonyi, associate Editor of THISDAY newspapers, said Nigeria has a lot to learn from Thailand especially in the fight against corruption. He told the audience that the anti corruption body in Thailand is more powerful than anybody and that this has aided has helped to record many successes in curbing the scourge.

Original_Search_Title: Govt, Foreign Farmer Plan N2.6bn Farm
Headline: Nigeria: Govt, Foreign Farmer Plan N2.6bn Farm
Body_Text:
Lagos — Ondo State government and a Nigeria-based South African agricultural company, have concluded arrangements to spend N2.6 billion to establish mechanised farms in the state.
The partnership is part of the state government's efforts to boost food production, revenue generation and create abundant job opportunities.
Already, government has provided about 500 hectares of land near the Owena Multi-purpose Dam and another 1,500 hectares of land at Owo, while necessary arrangements have been concluded for the signing this week, of a Memorandum of Under-standing between the state government and the firm.
In the partnership, the state government has 49 per cent equity; the South African firm (Shepherd Agriculture), 36 per cent equity and individual contributions stand at 15 per cent.
Managing Director, Shepherd Agriculture, Chief Jan Hepburn, said after a meeting with government representatives in Akure, weekend, that his company would engage in the cultivation of mechanised fruit farming, crop farming and livestock.
He said, "we shall focus on the cultivation of fruits like banana and pawpaw and crops like maize and cassava in large quantities mainly for export."
According to him, the land provided by government near the Owena Multi-purpose Dam will be used for fruits planting, while that at Owo will be for cultivation of crops and rearing of livestock.
Agriculture, Fisheries and Wildlife Commissioner, Dr Jibayo Oyebade, described the project as a catalyst for development of agriculture in the state. He assured that government would not relent in playing its part as the private partner is expected to spend its counterpart fund on the supply of necessary equipment with the assistance of South African government.

Original_Search_Title: Osunbor On Scale
Headline: Nigeria: Osunbor On Scale
Body_Text:
Lagos — SINCE the live appearance of Senator Oserhiemen Osunbor, governor of Edo State, during the September 5 celebration of the first hundred days on television, his image has soared widely. The event has afforded the former Senate Committee chairman on Judiciary, Law and Justice, a rare opportunity to display a great oratorical skill which pundits believe was strong enough to rubbish many town criers.
Osunbor from a dwindling image has risen to become the rave of the state. And no doubt many reactions so far has given the lawyer turned politician a nod of approval which is considered as a great punch on the otherwise strong camp of the opposition led by Adams Oshiomhole of Action Congress, AC.
Shortly before the ceremonial hundred days, the state government was embroiled in the decision to probe the sale and allocation of many government houses and lands by the immediate past administration under former governor, Chief Lucky Igbinedion. It had been alleged that some houses under the administration were given away as gifts to friends and relatives to the extent that a former governor in one of the South-south states got one for one thousand naira.
Forest reserves in the state were also said to have been allocated to party leaders who quickly fenced them for future development. As a result of this, Osunbor was said to be finding it difficult to win popular support to execute the probe. The commissioner of lands and survey, Mr. Anthony Iyalekhue, came under pressure from politicians who benefitted from the deals. The governor's confirmation of how the state industries were sold or privatized by the Igbinedion administration sent cold shivers across, that the past eight years were a hell.
Corroborating the governor, his special adviser on communication and public relations, Mr. Tony Ikpasaja, disclosed that the Ikpeshi company was established for over N500 million and became a big hope for the state which was just finding its feet in the mining sector. "The outright sale of Ikpeshi and Ehor Fruit Juice factories, under a very suspicious circumstance by the Igbinedion administration, has only taken the hand of clock backwards for the state," said Ikpasaja.
"For now, we are awaiting the faceless new owners of the factories, especially the Ehor Juice Factory, which have been overgrown by weeds, to take off. If these factories have been purchased by people and they are not coming out to operate them, that means there is either a problem somewhere or the transaction may have been fraught with filth and fraud." The cassava factory in Uromi, started five years ago, and which was also loudly sung by former Governor Igbinedion, also came shockingly to be a yet-to-be-completed project.
More worrisome is the fact the cement factory is no more operating at full capacity. And the people are feeling short-changed by the sudden change of ownership. The revelations by Osunbor about the sold properties of the state have whipped up so much sentiments that anger is welling up among the Edo people. Musa Igbadumhe, a native of Okpella, confessed that since the Igbinedion regime, the cement factory no longer attracts business to the town.
Other state companies which the governor did not speak much about but inviting curiosity from the public are the Edo Line, Edo City Transport Service, Bendel Brewery, Edo Broadcasting Service and Nigerian Observer. For what it seems, Osunbor is faced with a Herculean task of reviving many of these companies which in the past years were scandalized as existing for some selfish interests than those of the public. If laws are made for man and not man for law, the Osunbor government may be compelled to reverse some of the suspected deals especially the sale of government houses, privatization and change of ownership or management of the public utilities in the state.
Idugbor, a lawyer, lives at 134 Sapele road, Benin City.

Original_Search_Title: Former Street Children Out to Change Life in the City Slums
Headline: Kenya: Former Street Children Out to Change Life in the City Slums
Body_Text:
Nairobi — With the election season under way, Kenyans are daily subjected to an endless succession of photo ops, press conferences and sound bytes. With all the sound and fury, it's easy to forget what any of these men are actually doing for the people they claim to represent.
Emmanuel Boys Centre founder Daniel Muiruri Nduati with Aids orphans Samuel Kamau, 9, and Kevin Kinyanjui, 14. Photos/ARNO KOPECKY
But a new generation of leaders is quietly emerging that is far removed from the wealthy dynasties now orbiting State House. Working below the radar and without the benefit of a public purse, the youths are all the more impressive for coming from the most disenfranchised corners of society. Unlike so many who make good and never look back, they have stayed on to help lift their communities from the hopelessness of poverty.
"I know how people struggle," says one of them, Mr Daniel Muiruri Nduati; "because I went down that road myself."
Mr Nduati, a soft-spoken 26-year-old, left an abusive home when he was 14 and entered life in the streets. "I started hustling," he remembers; "stealing when I could, doing odd jobs for a few days at a time. I was taking drugs everyday, whatever I could lay my hands on - brown sugar, marijuana, alcohol, glue - I went crazy for years."
Articulate founder
It's difficult to equate this story with the articulate founder of Emmanuel Boyz Centre standing before me now. But it is precisely his experience of life in the streets that gave Mr Nduati the drive and compassion to start up Emmanuel in 2000. The youth centre has so far taken 300 children off the streets, providing them with shelter, food and a productive environment in which to focus on self-development rather than merely surviving.
At present, the centre houses 40 boys, including three brothers orphaned by Aids. They were all at school the afternoon I dropped in, leaving the gated compound quiet and serene. Mr Nduati explained the religious vision he had at 17 which shook him back to life. He returned home and completed high school with such distinction that he was offered a European scholarship. Although he never graduated from university, the international contacts he made soon led to the funding that enabled him to start up Emmanuel Boyz Centre.
"One of the benefits of having the centre at Dagoretti is that it's too isolated for the children to sneak back to the streets," he says. "When they first come here, many can't think of anything other than finding a way to get high. But once they're here it's just about impossible, and gradually they learn to focus on other things."
At length the gates open and a van comes through, disgorging an unruly but cheerful crowd of children. Most have been here for months now, some for years; in fact, some are just a few years younger than Mr Nduati.
"I used to run with some of these guys when I was in the streets, you know? I go back and look for old faces, buy them lunch, see how it's going. If I find someone who really wants to make a change in his life, I'll take him in. But of course, I can't take them all - there are just so many new children on the street every year."
People who are too old for school are offered vocational training instead. We leave the boys in the yard and go visit one of them, Samuel, now employed as a carpenter at a nearby workshop.
"Daniel totally changed my life," Samuel, 22, says, stepping away from the bandsaw where he's carving out a bed frame. "I would still be on the streets, or worse, if it wasn't for him."
Various programmes
The success of Emmanuel Boyz Centre inspired another project nearby: Dagoretti 4 Kids, or D4K as the locals know it. Founded two years ago by four men in their early 20s, D4K provides shelter and various programmes for orphans and street children while at the same time working to find jobs for impoverished parents and single mothers.
"I used to know Daniel," says Mr Michael Mungai, one of D4K's founders. "He was one of the people who kept telling us we could make it if we wanted to." Now 23 and studying on a scholarship at St Joseph's University in the US, Michael recalls the days when he too lived in the streets, selling drugs and working in brothels.
Mr Mungai rehabilitated himself with help from the Pamoja Child Trust, where he met another young man named Peter Nduchu. The two teamed up with Mr Elijah Waweru and Mr James Njoroge and got D4K off the ground. They are now busy planting maize, cassava, beans and other crops on the one-hectare compound. When not busy on the farm, they are heavily involved in community outreach programmes.
"Drunkenness, prostitution, HIV/Aids - these are the major problems," says Mr Njoroge. "These are the reasons so many children keep showing up in the streets. If we don't tackle the issue at the root, more will just keep coming." Much of their energy is spent finding the street children's parents. When they do, D4K tries to engage them, ideally funnelling some of the money they receive from international donors into small business projects to help them to get back on their feet so they can take their children back. But he admits that day is some way off.
Their efforts have attracted the attention of such notables as Nobel laureate Wangari Maathai, Al Gore, and Jeffrey Sachs. The high profile helps when it comes to fund-raising, says Mr Mungai.
At the other end of town is another inspiration - Carolina For Kibera - which is run by the energetic Mr Salim Mohammid. Carolina was started six years ago when Mr Mohammid was 25, and has grown into a four-tier community-based organisation affecting the lives of thousands of Kiberans.
One of its principle foundations is the Tabitha health clinic where the locals may go for free treatment and testing. "We have over 20,000 people in our data base," he tells me. "Can you imagine what these people would do without Tabitha? I don't even want to. None of them could afford to go to the hospital."
He takes me for a walk through Kibera, spreading smiles and handshakes by the dozen as we go. In this neighbourhood, he is probably more popular than its MP, Mr Raila Odinga. But he laughs when I ask if he would ever consider running for politics. "Are you kidding?" he says; "they would eat me alive."
Mr Mohammid grew up at Mathare and was a professional footballer in his youth. He's translated his passion into another of Carolina For Kibera's outreach programmes. Thousands of Kibera children are now part of the football league he created, but before they can join a team, they must take part in the community clean-ups he organises each weekend.
The programme ties into Mr Mohammid's "Trash for Cash" project, whereby young men and women are taught how to make a profit from recycling and collecting garbage before it reaches the sewer.
And the fourth aspect of Carolina for Kibera is the Binti Pamoja project, which allows young women to gather, study and talk about issues that affect them. Last year, the girls published a book of photography and personal narratives depicting life at Kibera from the perspective of adolescent women.
The project lured US senator and presidential hopeful Barack Obama to drop in during a visit to Kenya in 2006.
At Dandora, children are learning to break-dance. Kangethe Ngigi, or MC Kah, is teaching them, his back-pack still on as he shows a half-dozen wide-eyed youths how to prolong a hand stand.
We are on the graffiti-covered compound that serves as the headquarters for Ukoo Flani Mau Mau, the Kisima-award winning hip-hop collective that is a branch of MC Kah's Maono project.
Kah is elusive about his age - "let's just say I'm old," he laughs, though it's clear he's not past his mid-20s. His older brother, Kamau, is 29, a member of hip-hop group Kalamashaka. Together with friend Joshua Maina they founded Maono in 1999 as a way of "helping youths develop skills to become independent."
Maono, an acronym for Mikasi, Anzisha, Onyesha, Njia, Okoa, offers much more than just break-dance lessons. There is also a film and photography school, music and dance lessons and an acrobatics programme. And like his friend Mohammid in Kibera, Kah has harnessed a passion for football to keep children off the street.
Maono operates under a simple philosophy: Each one teach one. Every member of the group is responsible for passing his or her learning onto someone younger.
Emos Okulo, 21, is a long-time Maono team captain about to become a full-time assistant coach. "I remember when they found me," he says; "I was playing football with some friends using a ball made of plastic bags. The next thing I knew we were winning tournaments." He has become a passionate member, putting in 20 hours a week and more on a volunteer basis.
This year, Maono brought in two trophies - one for the under-13 group at the Mombasa youth tournament, and the other for under-12s at the Dandora youth tournament. The children get an additional push from knowing that two of them will be selected each year to represent Kenya at the Norway Cup.
Unlike its counterparts at Kibera and Dagoretti, however, Maono doesn't get large donor funding and relies exclusively on the contributions of members and friendly people.
Much of the money earned through record sales from the hip-hop branch, Ukoo Flani, is channelled back into the group. Kah's latest projects are a library and a recording studio.

Original_Search_Title: City Food Prices to Increase
Headline: Uganda: City Food Prices to Increase
Body_Text:
Kampala — FOOD prices are likely to go up as famine takes root in flooded eastern Uganda. The situation is likely to get worse even in Kampala, as the floods cripple one of Uganda's biggest food baskets.
Kevin Ocen, a Kampala groundnut dealer who normally gets his produce from Soroti, says his supply routes have now been cut off. Not only have the roads become impassable, but the crops are also rotting in the floods.
"I should be driving to the village for more groundnuts, but where do I pass?" says Ocen, who has a produce store at Kampala's Arua Park.
Ocen says he has no option but to hike prices for his remaining produce. "I would buy as much as I could and sell a kilo at around sh2,000. Now I am considering pushing the price to sh3,000. For the meantime, I am trying to get another supplier," he says.
Ocen's neighbour is quick to defend him: "It is not that we want to use the situation to cheat our clients. Look, I have lost everything. Even for the little seeds you get, you pay a lot of money for someone who is helping you to cross a flooded bridge. So I might double the price."
Already, prices of most of the foods that come from the far east of Uganda are rising. A survey of Owino market showed that 100kg of simsim cost about sh180,000, up from about sh160,000 a few months ago.
A sack of groundnut seeds goes for sh200,000 compared to an earlier sh150,000 - sh180,000. A sack of dry cassava is stagnating between sh30,000 to sh35, 000 and sweet potatoes are going for sh40,000 - sh50,000, an increase from sh35,000 a sack.
"Everybody who is getting food from Teso has had to increase the price of especially groundnuts, sweet potatoes and simsim by about sh10,000 or sh20,000 a sack. It is only those who are buying from other regions that have not yet increased prices," says Ocen.
The flooding in eastern Uganda will have far-reaching consequences because the region is one of Uganda's leading food producers.
For instance, according to the 2004 District Information Portal on Agriculture prepared by the Uganda Communications Commission, the total production of maize in the country was estimated at 739,000 tonnes from an area of 665,000hectares, of which 55% was in eastern Uganda. Uganda's total production of sweet potatoes was estimated at 2,620,000 tonnes, of which 1,029,000 were from the east.
However, it is not clear how much of this comes from the now flooded Teso region.
Although statistics are scanty, the Ministry of Agriculture says there is clearly "too much damage on crops," which will certainly lead to famine. Major staple crops have been affected, including ground nuts, potatoes, maize, millet and sorghum.
In Teso alone, approximately 2,498 gardens have been destroyed since the beginning of the torrential rains. Of these, 60% are groundnuts gardens, 35% cassava, 2% sorghum and 2% sweet potato gardens, according to the Christian Children's Fund.
In Bukedea, district officials estimate that about 14,000 acres of cassava have been destroyed, while in Nebbi, over 40 acres of maize, 10 acres of cassava and 36 acres of simsim, groundnuts and various vegetables that had just germinated also perished.
"We foresee prices of potatoes, groundnuts and millet going up," says Okasayi Opolot, the Commissioner for Crop Production in the agriculture ministry. "It is too early to estimate how much has been destroyed so far, but we have our field officers on the ground."
"There is definitely going to be a huge loss," says Eng. Hillary Onek, the agriculture minister. "Even fruits which are high up in the tree cannot grow well when there is too much water. The potatoes, millet and sorghum have all been submerged and are now rotten."
"So for places like Kampala or other neighbouring districts which have been buying especially groundnuts from there, the prices may go up; that is if at all one even manages to harvest," Onek adds. "What we need now is to give our people relief food immediately. But the problem remains big because there is no where to pass."
The ministry plans to distribute quick-maturing crops that people can plant when the floods recede.
There are new varieties of cassava that take only six to eight months and potatoes that mature in three months.
Other quick-maturing crops are cow peas and beans. Where flooding is not too much, farmers are also advised to grow crops like yams and rice that can survive in water logged soils.
What to do
-Don't throw away or waste food anyhow. Prepare only what you can finish.
-Find out other producing districts. For instance, if you are interested in maize, other maize-growing districts are Iganga, Kamuli, Bushenyi, Masindi, Kasese and Luweero.
-Farmers should plant quick-maturing crops once the floods recede.
-Individuals can buy some dry foods and store them in a clean, dry and safe place. They could help you when prices go high.
lWhere flooding is not too much, farmers can plant crops like yams and rice

Original_Search_Title: Famine Imminent as Crops Rot
Headline: Uganda: Famine Imminent as Crops Rot
Body_Text:
Kampala — Many former IDPs have returned to camps after their houses collapsed
Please leave that cassava for us. Don't buy it because we want it seriously. We have been starving here for the past 24 hours.
"You are coming from Soroti town and must have had something in your stomach. If you insist on buying it, we will fight over it," cried out Joseph Okolany, one of the hundreds of passengers stranded at Alak Aituk in Amuria district.
Alak Aituk, near the border between Amuria and Katakwi districts, is one of the areas most affected by the on-going floods in Teso. The roads have been impassable for weeks now.
Vehicles from Katakwi stop about a mile away and drop off passengers who daringly wade through the waters that are at their waist level.
Whereas the floods are peaceful in most places, it is quite violent at the spot where the bridge collapsed.
Maria Alupo has been standing here for three hours contemplating whether to cross or not. With a baby strapped onto her back and a bag on her head, Alupo moves closer to the spot and backwards several times. She hesitates to go, but she has to collect her children from Katakwi and take them to school in Soroti. Finally, she makes a daring move, joining a group of passengers that is just starting to cross.
Nervously, she takes a deep breath, and then dips her right foot. Slowly but steadily, she moves into the water but she cannot see where her feet are stepping.
Staggering, she reaches out to grab the person next to her. This is a journey that can only be made with a helping hand.
The scene is characterised by different kinds of people; idlers, small-time money makers, stranded parents taking children to school and students carrying luggage.
There are also offloaded bags of UN emergency food, trucks, graders and lorries. Shoes and gumboots are of no use because water enters them and they become a burden.
Whereas the floods terrorise passengers, snakes have fun in it. Hardly 30 minutes pass before a snake bobs through the flowing water.
In Katakwi district, many former internally displaced persons have returned to camps after their houses collapsed or cracked.
Their crops have been washed away, making hunger inevitable.
Martin Okwii, a resident of Dadas in Katakwi and his family, narrowly escaped death when their mud and wattle house collapsed at night.
"It was at 6:00pm. As I arrived home, it started to rain. My wife was in a different hut with all three children, cooking. The rains intensified and darkness fell suddenly. Lightning flashed and thunder roared repeatedly. It was terrifying," he recalls.
"The kitchen, where my wife sat with children, crumbled. The children screamed in the darkness. I dashed out to rescue them. The whole place was flooded and slippery. I called for help but the neighbours could not hear because of the heavy rain. I managed to rescue them but they had some injuries," he continues.
Metres away from Okwii's homestead, Simon Iculet's hut had collapsed the previous night.
In the same village, seven grass-thatched houses broke down within two days.
As if that is not bad enough, the village wells have been messed up by the floods, yet many people do not have containers for collecting enough rain water for use.
Martha Amuro faced a multiple tragedy. She lost her cassava in the garden and after spending a whole day digging trenches to divert the floods, she fell ill, probably due to exhaustion. The following day, as she rode to a health centre, she had an accident on the bicycle and hurt her leg. Now because of the floods, the wound cannot heal easily.
Amuro panics whenever it rains. She doesn't know whether to stay in and the hut collapses on her, or be out in the rain and risk being struck by lightning. "I don't know where to hide," she says.
Margaret Acangada cries only for one blanket to share with her four grand children. All her beddings were destroyed by the floods. The homestead is flooded, leaving the family with nowhere to sleep.
In Angopet, Katakwi town council, over 20 mud and wattle houses are sinking into the ground. When you step on a seemingly dry area, water oozes out.
James Okwii, a second year student of Busitema University, rents a house in Katakwi town council.
When the floods hit his village, his siblings and parents moved to Katakwi town to live with him. Suddenly, the house collapsed during heavy rain and the whole family became homeless again. Moreover, Okwii cannot return to university because there is nowhere to pass.
He is bitter with Teso leaders, accusing them of ignoring their people. He accuses (disaster preparedness state) minister Musa Ecweru for not giving them audience when they wanted to explain their condition. "He just came in a helicopter and landed at Katakwi Technical [institute]. He met district leaders whom I think are not conveying our plight adequately. We have been reporting our problems to the LCs but we do not see what they are doing to help," he fumes.
Ecweru says the Government and development agencies like UNICEF and the World Food Programme are working around the clock to contain the situation. He says the Prime Minister's office has so far distributed 1,860 bags of maize flour and 460 bags of beans. WFP is committed to feeding 300,000 people in Teso and 15,000 people in Lira. UNICEF and the government gave 5,000 families mosquito's nets and shelter materials, and another 1,000 water treatment tablets.
In addition, over 17,000 households in Teso, Lira and Mt. Elgon areas have received standard family kits, including blankets, tarpaulin, soap and household items.
But while they appreciate efforts being made by the Government and other development agencies to rescue them, the flood victims say the support they have so far received is far from sufficient.

Original_Search_Title: Nigeria Loses $5 Billion Annually On Cassava Export
Headline: Nigeria Loses $5 Billion Annually On Cassava Export
Body_Text:
Abeokuta — Flour Millers in the country yesterday received knocks for frustrating federal government's move to generate $5 billion annually from foreign revenue.
The attack came from a professor of agricultural economics, Prof. Dupe Olatunbosun, who argued that the whooping sum would have been generated through exports of cassava derivatives and import savings on wheat.
Addresing a press conference in Abeokuta, he stressed:"If not for the deliberate sabotage by the flour millers, the cassava initiative was meant to generate at least 5 million jobs for the unemployed youths in Nigeria ."
Olatunbosun, who is the chariman, Ogun State Cassava Revolution Programme, however,called on President Umar Musa Yar'Adua to immediately ban importation of wheat and wheat bread into the country.
According to him,doing so, would help conserve the nation's foreign exchange earnings and generate five million jobs for the unemployed youths in the country, just as he alleged that the imported wheat does not meet the minimum standard prescribed by the regulatory body, Standard Organisation of Nigeria (SON) as it is low in quality "to the detriment of the health of our people."
The university don warned President Yar'Adua that, without the rapid development of the nation's agricultural sector, the country quest to be one of the 20 leading economies in the world by year 2020 would be a mirage.
To underscore the seriousness of the loss in foreign revenue from cassava, Olatunbosun said that in Ogun State alone, with efforts put in place by Governor Gbenga Daniel administration, N36 billion would be injected into the state's economy this year.
His words:"Our projection in Ogun State this year, 2007, is to produce about 4.5 million tons of cassava with a conservative income of about N36 billion to be pumped into the state's economy.
"Because of this, an industrial revolution is in the offing in Ogun State. Many factories producing cassava flour, starch, glucose syrup, ethanol and other cassava derivatives are springing up in the state."
Olatubosun maintained that if the cassava revolution programme had been faithfully implemented, about $400 million was expected to be saved under the single project, stressing that the flour millers, instead of complementing government efforts to develop the country, devised ways to frustrate the policy to introduce cassava flour in the country, even with ridiculous prices being offered cassava processors.
In similar vein, he criticised the recent rise in prices of flour, thus, leading to hike in prices of bread, saying it was absolutely uncalled for. "A policy that was meant to provide the vehicle for socio-economic development of our nation is now gradually hitting the rock."
"I urge President Umaru Musa Yar'Adua to resist the blackmail of the flour millers. If they were to close down because they do not want to use blended flour - so be it. Nigeria will not collapse. Nigeria will not die", he said.

Original_Search_Title: Flour Millers Accused of Frustrating Cassava Production
Headline: Nigeria: Flour Millers Accused of Frustrating Cassava Production
Body_Text:
Abeokuta — The Chairman of the Ogun State Cassava Revolution Programme, Prof. Dupe Olatunbosun has raised alarm over the efforts by flour millers in the country to frustrate cassava producers and processors by giving excuses to reject the cassava flour in producing flour.
The development came, even as some of the millers have been accused of stock piling their warehouses to deceive the Standard Organization of Nigeria (SON), the regulatory body checking the compliance of the millers on the use of the 5% cassava flour in their production.
He also hinted that to check this anomaly, a law that would make it mandatory for millers to include the cassava flour in their production would soon be table before the National Assembly.
Olatunbosun, who addressed a press conference at the Secretariat of the Ogun State Council of the Nigeria Union of Journalists (NUJ), in Abeokuta on Wednesday, said that with the new fraudulent practice of the millers in the country, the efforts of the Federal government at ensuring total compliance to the use of cassava flour or tubers could be a mirage.
The Chairman remarked that with increase in the number of processors to about 300 per cent since inception, the flour millers were fingered as having been responsible for not meeting the about $400m expected to be saved under the project.
According to Olatunbosun, "the number of processors rose by about 300 per cent since inception, but the flour millers are deliberately frustrating the producers and the processors. To recapitulate,if the policy had been faithfully implemented, about $400M was expected to be saved under this single project
"Under the scheme, 300, 000 metric tones of High Quality Cassava Flour (HQCF) or 10,000 trailer load of HQCF or 33 trailer loads per day or 1,000mt or 165 trailers of fresh cassava tubers per day was expected to be consumed."
Olatunbosun, who is also the Chairman of the Ekiti state Policy and Strategy Committee noted further that the flour millers devised ways to frustrate the federal government policy, among which was the price for the purchase of the cassava flour, which has not been honoured by them.
"As at March 2007, almost a year later, only 5,000Mt of HQCF was supplied to flour mills by processors. Obviously, the flour millers had devised ways to frustrate the policy. A March 30, 2006 agreement fixing cassava flour price at N75,000 per ton-ex factory was not honoured.
Others gave the ridiculous excuse that the wheat being imported into the country had low protein content-consequently mixing this with local cassavas flour would rob the finished product of adequate protein content. Some of the flour miller even stock plied to deceive Standard Organization of Nigeria (SON) and their fellow Nigerians."

Original_Search_Title: Osei-Wusu Foundation Grants Loans to Farmers
Headline: Ghana: Osei-Wusu Foundation Grants Loans to Farmers
Body_Text:
Bekwai-Ash — The Osei-Owusu Foundation for Indigenous Business Development, a micro-financing service has granted ¢54 million (GH¢5,400) as loans to 54 members of a local Plantain, Cassava and Cocoyam Growers Association at Koniyaw, a farming community in the Amansie East district of the Ashanti Region.
Each farmer received an initial amount of ¢1,000,000 (GH¢100) to enable them procure plantain suckers for cultivation during this farming season and thus produce plantain and other foodstuff in large quantities.
The beneficiaries would also be provided with agro-chemicals to enable the farmers to produce in abundance and provide raw material for a plantain processing plant yet to be installed in the area by the Foundation.
The Foundation aims basically at developing and sustaining business acumen among residents of the Bekwai local community as well as engaging the people in socio-economic ventures that would improve the well being of the people.
Areas to which the Foundation gives priority for assistance are small and medium scale businesses, talent hunting, agriculture, agro processing, education and sports development.
The founder of the Foundation, Mr. Joe Osei-Owusu indicated that the Foundation would soon go into plantain processing and produce fufu powder.
As part of its social responsibility, the Foundation has sponsored a month of extra classes organized by 45 students from the various universities for JSS and SSS students in the Poano zone of the Bekwai constituency.
The secretary of the Foundation, Mr. Frank Owusu Awuah indicated that it cost the Foundation ¢7.2 million (GH¢720) to run the vacation classes and that the exercise would be sustained in the future.
Meanwhile, founder Osei-Owusu, who is also the CEO of the Driver and Vehicle Licensing Authority (DVLA), has announced a ¢2 billion (GH¢20,000) package under the Foundation's micro credit facility from which loans would be advanced to about 150 people who cannot raise the premium for the National Health Insurance Scheme (NHIS).
He told The Chronicle that the Foundation had already started negotiations with some local banks to pay premiums of about 150 interested beneficiaries to enable them register with the NHIS by the close of September this year.
Beneficiaries were expected to be issued with their insurance cards within two weeks of registration, he assured.
All monies paid to beneficiaries were disbursed through two local banks, namely the Amansie West Rural Bank and the Odotobiri Rural Bank.
Last year, the Amansie West Bank disbursed a total of ¢1.6 billion to 201 members of the Osei Owusu Foundation for Indigenous Business Development under the bank's micro-financing scheme.
Over 600 members drawn from various groups have undergone savings and management training as part of empowerment to expand their businesses since the Foundation was established in May 2006. It now has over 6,000 members in 35 towns and villages across the district.

Original_Search_Title: Country Gets $847 Million U.S. Grant to Implement Needs
Headline: Nigeria: Country Gets $847 Million U.S. Grant to Implement Needs
Body_Text:
THE United States Agency for International Development {USAID} disclosed Tuesday that of the total $845 Million it promised to support Nigeria's development till 2009, with Niger Delta as a major focus, it had granted $477 Million to the Nigerian government.
Speaking during the signing ceremony of the Amendment to the Strategic Objectives Agreement Grant between the USAID and the National Planning Commission {NPC}, Ms Sharon Cromer, USAID Mission Director noted that the American government through the agency was committed basically to prevent 1.1 million new AIV/AIDS infections, provide care and support to 1.7 million HIV affected individuals and to assist more than 400,000 orphans as well as other vulnerable children in Nigeria.
According to her, the USIAD had redeemed the $162 million commitment for 2007 to complete its $847 million grant for the implementation of the country's National Economic Empowerment and Development Strategy {NEEDS}, amounting to a total of $477 million so far paid.
Ms. Cromer who explained that the agency will increase local treatment of Tuberculosis by 77% through the establishment of 102 new facilities, make available provision for the immunization of 4 million children against polio, said this would reduce the annual number of wild polio cases from 246 to zero.
"We shall distribute more than 91,000 long lasting insecticide treated mosquito nets through private sector and provide 600,000 doses of medicine to treat malaria as well as train 6,000 primary health service providers in 800 hospitals and clinics across the country.
"I am pleased to say that USAID's current strategy for Nigeria is closely aligned with the objectives of NEEDS. Our strategy strives to improve the lives of Nigerians through strengthening democratic institutions, reducing conflict, enhancing agriculture and economic growth, improving social sector service delivery and reducing poverty.
"In the past, USAID has had a significant impact on the lives of Nigerians. Over the next year, USAID will work in collaboration with all tiers of government, civil society and the private sector, to ensure that the funds we are granting today continue to be used to improve the lives of Nigerians."
"We will distribute more than 91, 000 long lasting insecticide treated mosquito nets in 300 public facilities, and sell more than 3 million nets through the private sector. We will also market nearly 600, 000 does of medicine to treat malaria. Finally in health, we will train close to 6, 000 primary health care service providers in 800 hospitals and clinics", she said.
The USAID Director who noted that other areas of intervention by the agency include the strengthening of democratic institutions, encourage development of the hostile Niger Delta region, and enhance transparency and economic reform, stressed that it would also in its move to boost the economy, increase the productivity and marketing of Cassava, Cowpea and daily products and improve agro- processing to increase at least 500,000 farmers that would generate more than 200,00 new jobs.
According to her, in the area of education, USIAD would support 1, 400 primary schools, including 466 Islamic schools in 37 local government areas, adding that this include include the training of over 22, 000 teachers and 1, 400 Parent Teacher Associations in an effort to improve literacy and numeric skills in Nigerian primary school age children.
Signing on behalf of the Federal government, Minister and Deputy Chairman, National Planning Commission{NPC}, Senator Mohammed Sanusi Daggash said the amended agreement had been done within the framework of the statutory responsibilities of the National Planning Commission which include management and programmes of multilateral and bilateral external assistance to Nigeria among others.

Original_Search_Title: Alliance Funds Next Cohort of Crop Breeders
Headline: Malawi: Alliance Funds Next Cohort of Crop Breeders
Body_Text:
The Alliance for a Green Revolution in Africa (AGRA) is to fund a new initiative to train the next generation of African crop scientists, aiming to improve agricultural productivity and food security in the region.
The funding, which will allow African PhD students to study staple African crops, was announced today (19 September).
AGRA will provide US$8.1 million and US$4.9 million respectively to the African Centre for Crop Improvement (ACCI) at the University of KwaZulu-Natal in South Africa, and the West African Centre for Crop Improvement (WACCI) at the University of Ghana.
ACCI's programme, started in 2002, currently has 46 students from southern and eastern Africa studying 13 crops.
"The new funding from AGRA allows us another five intakes of eight students a year, starting this year," says Pangirayi Tongoona, deputy-director of ACCI.
"Students focus on different crops, depending on what is more pressing in their countries," Tongoona adds. Crops include sorghum, millet, cassava, groundnut and pigeon pea.
From January WACCI will admit eight students a year from western and central Africa. Eric Danquah, director of WACCI, said they were in the process of making the final selection for this first intake.
Students will spend the first two years of their five-year programme studying at one of the universities, before returning to their local research institutions to complete their thesis.
"AGRA has committed itself to continued funding of the research programmes of our graduates after they get a PhD. So they are not abandoned after graduating, but instead get the funding they need to continue their plant breeding projects," says Mark Laing, director of ACCI.
He adds that the centre is confident that the academic and practical training the students receive will equip them to successfully breed new crop varieties, addressing the specific needs of the local environment. Some ACCI graduates have already started to release new varieties.
AGRA will also give US$1.7 million to the US-based Cornell University, which will provide services and resources such as assessing students' doctoral proposals, distance learning opportunities and library access.
AGRA was founded in 2006 by the Rockefeller Foundation and the Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation.

Original_Search_Title: President Declares State of Emergency
Headline: Uganda: Museveni Declares State of Emergency
Body_Text:
Kampala — President Yoweri Museveni is to declare a state of emergency in northern and eastern Uganda, where floods have displaced thousands, destroyed crops and property worth billions and washed away bridges and roads, cutting off entire sub-counties and districts.
During a Cabinet meeting yesterday morning, chaired by the President, it was agreed that the floods constituted a natural disaster and a state of emergency be declared, Prime Minister Apolo Nsibambi told Parliament yesterday.
It will be the first time President Museveni invokes article 110 of the Constitution. The previous Parliament pressurised the Government to declare the North a disaster area because of the prolonged insurgency but this was rejected by the Government.
Nsibambi explained that the Constitution gives powers to the President to declare a state of emergency if he is "satisfied that circumstances exist in any part of Uganda that is threatened by war, internal insurgency, natural disaster, the need to maintain law and order or to secure public safety."
The Constitution requires that a proclamation declaring a state of emergency is tabled in Parliamentary for approval within 14 days.
The Leader of the Opposition in Parliament, Prof. Ogenga Latigo, welcomed the decision, saying it would address the crisis in northern and eastern Uganda and the looming crisis in other areas such as Mukono.
"We sought a lot of clarification from the Government regarding the flooding. The party to act is the Government. We are ready to work with the Government to ensure people get the best services," Ogenga asserted.
Fred Ruhindi, the deputy attorney general, explained that the ministry of justice was preparing a declaration's instrument to be published in the Gazette. He said Museveni was expected to receive the draft today.
Tarsis Kabwegyere, minister for disaster preparedness, said declaring a state of emergency would help to get external support. While the proclamation was being issued, the Government would continue to assist the affected populations, he assured.
The declaration by the Prime Minister closed the debate on the floods report, which had been presented on Tuesday by Musa Ecweru, the disaster preparedness minister in the Office of the Prime Minster. The MPs argued that the Government had shown seriousness in dealing with the disaster.
Earlier, six local leaders under the Teso Region Chairpersons Forum told the press at Parliament that they had declared their districts a disaster area "because the Government has failed to do so".
The district chairpersons of Amuria, Kaberamaido, Soroti, Bukedea, Kumi and Katakwi said 80% of Amuria district and 50% of Katakwi had flooded.
According to Rev. Sam Ebukalin of Bukedea, 14,000 acres of cassava, worth sh14.5b, had been destroyed by the floods and 106 houses had collapsed.
The leaders also revealed that 35 camps needed to be evacuated. They called upon the Government to speed up the relief effort.

Original_Search_Title: Trekking Towards a People's Theatre
Headline: Kenya: Trekking Towards a People's Theatre
Body_Text:
Who said theatre business is all fun without funds in return? Ask Eliud Abuto and he will tell you that theatre is as good business as any other.
Three years ago, the Festival of Creative Arts- a local theatre company he runs in the city-rose from the ashes of the now defunct Mbalamwezi Theatre Society.
Even with the renewed spirit with which it entered the market, the company could not manage to attract enough fans to watch plays it was staging.
Then staging most of their shows at the Kenya National Theatre, the company's best moment was an audience of 40 thespians.
But things have gradually changed especially on matters of business strategy and product development.
Today, anyone intending to watch a theatre performance by the group must book well in advance to watch a production.
Last weekend, the theatre company staged its version of Kindly Keep It Covered, a play written by Dave Freeman. Starring Derrick Amunga, Lydia Gitachi and Nice Githinji, the play staged at the Alliance Française attracted full houses through its run.
The auditorium has a sitting capacity of 210; there were six runs-after audiences requested for an extra show on Sunday evening.
"When we initially decided to venture into theatre, we knew that the theatre terrain in the country has been very rough. But armed with our professionalism, we were ready to trek on," says Abuto who is one of the group's director.
"Initially, we were just staging plays we thought were good; most of this time, we felt we knew what the audiences wanted. But we were wrong. When most of the productions failed to attract theatre fans, we decided to inject professionalism into the show."
Every time the group staged a play they sought to survey on consumer preferences. Using exit polls, the producer has a chance to get into the minds of those who frequent theatres, something that had eluded theatres for so long.
"We also make regular visits to different companies to understand what kind of plays Kenyans with some disposable income would be pay to watch on stage," he adds.
Going by the number of fans queuing for tickets to watch productions by this group lately, the strategy is already bearing fruits.
"Very soon, we might be looking for a more spacious theatre to accommodate all the fans," quips Abuto.
Crystal Mbuga and Godfrey Odhiambo in Kindly Keep It Covered that was staged by Festival of Creative Arts at the Alliance Française two weeks ago.
While still based at the Kenya National Theatre, Festival of Creative Arts suffered several cancellations thanks to orders from the Department of Culture.
There was also lack of continuity in terms of standards at the theatre that does not currently vet productions before they are staged.
"The problem in this case comes in when a group stages a poor production as this will cut off the fan base for other more serious groups," admits Abuto.
Lighting, acoustics and even seats at the theatre are a put- off. During the run of She Ate The Female Cassava, rains disrupted the show forcing the producer to refund money to the audience.
This is a hilarious Kenya play written by Jimmi Makotsi that revolves around a school girl entangled in a love web.
To escape from the National Theatre problems, the group sought a base elsewhere at the Alliance Française auditorium, which has become one of the favourite spots for the growing number of theatre goers.
"We have focused on changing perception that theatres are idle spaces for lay-abouts; make it a respectable profession that people can trust to offer quality entertainment," says Abuto.
While most theatre groups rarely engage the services of professionals in their act, Creative Arts has a team of marketers, managers, front office managers and even public relations officials who are professionally trained and with a passion for the arts. Most of these also double as cast to get a taste of the stage.
Mr Eliud Abuto, who is the glue holding this theatre dream, is a biochemist working at the Kenyatta National Hospital.
With an MBA degree from Moi University, the man is currently pushing the theatre company to a level where they can sustain a repertory theatre.
After experimenting with a number of genres, the group has now gone back to staging more farces that the audiences seem to follow keenly.
Some of these are When Did You Lastly See Your Trouser where a man finds himself locked in a space after losing his pants, Bedside Manners and even Boeing Boeing where a man has different women to serve him on different days depending on their schedules.
Even though farces are most popular with the audiences, says Abuto, the theatre company will be dropping an African play, satire along the way to spice up their act-when they must.

Original_Search_Title: 'Presidential Initiatives Are Economic Windows for Growth'
Headline: Nigeria: 'Presidential Initiatives Are Economic Windows for Growth'
Body_Text:
The agriculture-related initiatives of the immediate past administration, if well implemented, are windows for steady economic growth, a commodity association president has said.
Mr Mohammed Magaji, the national president of the Cassava Producers, Promoters and Exporters Association of Nigeria (CASPEAN) made this known in an interview with newsmen yesterday in Abuja.
Magaji said: "The cassava initiative was not President Obasanjo's political programme but an economic window identified by his administration.
"The potential of its success is there with or without government's support and entrepreneurs must continue to embrace it because it will never be a failure."
Magaji said whether the present administration embraced it or not, entrepreneurs would not abandon it because "it is not a political programme and therefore should not 'die' with the initiator".
