According to him, the Obasanjo administration created a "wider window" for cassava and it has become a commercial raw material that is globally acceptable.
He said Nigeria could have done better in its cassava export drive but for what he termed the "missing link" in negotiation by the government marketers during the export of the first consignment to China.
Magaji said the negotiation in China was not market-oriented but directed towards gaining entry for the commodity in the international market.
He said the inability of government to provide funding to ensure adequate development of the cassava initiative also hampered the project.
The CASPEAN president noted that with the global emphasis on renewable energy sources, Nigeria cannot but embrace the initiatives mostly related to cassava.
"The renewable energy goes along with the use of raw materials like cassava, corn, millet and sugar cane.
"Cassava also serves as raw materials for foods, and industrial starch among others, the importance of which cannot be overemphasised," he added.
He, therefore, urged the government put in place proactive measures on various agricultural policies, adding that it is the only way to ensure sustainable growth.
It would be recalled that former President Olusegun Obasanjo initiated several intervention programmes designed to boost the production of key agricultural commodities with export potentials.
They included the presidential initiatives on rice, cassava, cotton, cocoa, vegetable oil, tropical fruits and livestock among others.
The initiatives resulted to significant increase in the contribution of the sector to the GDP.

Original_Search_Title: Teso Leaders Speak On Govt Response to the Raging Floods
Headline: Uganda: Teso Leaders Speak On Govt Response to the Raging Floods
Body_Text:
Flooding in many parts of Eastern Uganda, especially Teso have displaced thousands of people and destroyed property. How well has the government responded to the disaster? Agness Nandutu spoke to State Minister for Relief and Disaster Preparedness Mr Musa Ecweru and Soroti Woman MP Ms Alice Alaso.
As a leader in the region, how do you rate government's handling of this disaster?
Ecweru
As government, we have done our best given the resources available to handle the calamity. The fact that I am on the ground to coordinate the crisis is evidence that we are committed. We had not anticipated the crisis of this magnitude.
Despite the fact that we are overwhelmed, it's our responsibility to protect Ugandans and that is what we are doing. We are disaster prepared but this was beyond our capacity. Just like America is disaster prepared but Osama bin Laden (al Qaeda) attack was beyond their capacity, so is Uganda. There are calamities that are a little bit beyond any government's capacity to handle.
Alaso
The government has been very slow in handling this crisis. The government was aware of the change of weather but they did nothing. Candidly speaking, this government is never prepared for disasters and has never handled any disaster effectively.
Am sure government was notified of change of weather. Government did not explore how to mitigate the consequences. The floods started in August but the assessment has been slow.
The intervention has been slow and uncoordinated. Evacuation of the population is very pertinent when a country envisages a calamity. As we speak now, schools are closing down. What has the government done or has in place to have training centres? The government should have curved learning centres as floods engulfed the region; there was no government plan of how to transport food to the affected areas. Government lacks both long term and short term planning.
Why do leaders drag in politics at the expense of their people in times like this?
Ecweru
There has been a lot politics but I appeal to our leaders not to politicise the situation. Dragging in politics will not help us. Leaders should not take advantage of the disaster to say that the NRM government is doing nothing. We are doing our best. I ask our political leaders to work together with government to address the problem instead of criticising all the time. Politics should stay out of such calamities.
Alaso
I do not see any politics. If asking this government to fulfil its constitutional mandate of caring and protecting citizens is what they call politics, then we are guilty of politicising the disaster. But if they think we shall sit back and look at them leave our people to suffer and be given rotten beans, they are wasting time.
The government should not run away from its responsibility. They should address the plight of the people of Teso. We shall continue to talk and mobilise our people until we see action.
Does the government has the capacity to sustain relief supplies during and after the crisis?
Ecweru
We are prepared and have the capacity, with the support of our partners, to handle the situation in terms of food supplies. We have been overwhelmed by the floods but we shall feed the population. As I speak now, we have stored food that can feed more than 100,000 people for three months (the flooding has affected between 200,000-300,000 people in Teso alone).
We are also going to prepare for the recovery of Teso sub region and other affected areas. We shall supply cassava stems of quick maturity variety and other seeds that mature quickly.
Alaso
The government is already defeated and that's why we are shouting. This government has never had the capacity to feed Ugandans in times of disaster. I'am not optimistic that the government will sustain food supplies.
This government has never had the needs of Ugandans on the list of its priorities. Even when people were in the camps, the government failed to feed them. Where will the zeal come from this time round?
How much money has the government allocated for disasters in the national budget?
Ecweru
We have a budget but it is inadequate to handle disasters. But we must appreciate that as a country, we have limited resources. We have many competing demands and the resources can never be adequate. We also have a small vote for emergency but the challenges are overwhelming.
Alaso
This government has never had a budget for disasters. As an MP who scrutinises sector budgetary estimates, I have never seen that budget in Parliament. I was a chairperson of the Parliamentary select committee on the humanitarian situation in northern Uganda.
One of the recommendations was for the government to have a budget for disasters, but it has never been considered. We recommended for the emergency vote but the government says they cannot plan for what they do not see.
Uganda is 100 per cent relying on donors for relief and that scenario is very bad. We cannot leave the destiny of our people in the hands of well wishers. What if they run out of relief supplies?
The government has secured Shs153b as additional funding for Chogm. But given this disaster, would you say the government has its priorities right?
Ecweru
Securing resources for Chogm does not mean that the government has no priorities. Chogm is a priority on its own. While you prepare for a visitor, you cannot prevent a child from getting sick. And the child's sickness does not mean that you abandon the visitor. We shall handle the disaster and have a good Chogm.
Alaso
That is the problem with this government. They never set their priorities right. They relocated all the money for roads to Chogm. Our people pay taxes to this government but nobody cares about them. The welfare of the people of Uganda should be a priority. The people of Uganda and their needs are not this government's priorities.
How is the situation now?
Ecweru
There is nothing to hide. The situation is very bad and worrying. It is not just submerging and water logging; it has even reached the level of breaking river banks. We have to plan to airlift drugs and food supplies to the affected areas. The ministries of education, health and works have to extend help.
Alaso
The government should know that the situation is bad and people are helpless. The rains are continuing and more households are getting affected. Every drop of rain is a disaster to the people of Teso. Our people are in dire need of food, shelter, clothing and beddings. We ask well-wishers to help us. Teso was just recovering and this calamity is dragging us backwards. The government alone cannot fulfil our expectations.

Original_Search_Title: Country Marks Science Week
Headline: Uganda: Country Marks Science Week
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE Vice-President, Prof Gilbert Bukenya, and many other government officials and politicians joined in the Science Walk on Monday with a call for innovation. Many of the solutions to problems brought about by changes in the environmental lie in the knowledge of some of these scientists who marched in Kampala.
With about $30m received earlier this year from the World Bank towards the cause of science and technology, the Government must by now be thinking of real solutions to intercept the effects of environmental disasters like floods.
In the right direction so far is the Uganda Millennium Science Initiative, which is an important step towards the creation of a stronger science and technology system.
President Yoweri Museveni set up an incentive where he will reward scientists who come up with lucrative innovations that can save Ugandans from poverty.
The science week has come at the right time when we need to get solutions to our problems. There is need to look for local technological innovations, which the Vice-President said would promote development. One activity highlighting exposure to crucial technologies is the ongoing science and technology exhibition at the Uganda Manufacturers Association showgrounds in Lugogo.
On Monday, the exhibition hall was full of exhibitors ready to explain the scientific methods used to arrive at their products. For instance, university students with the Uganda Gatsby Trust explained how farmers can use a prototype machine to produce banana wine. One needs sh500,000 to acquire this machine.
Aloe vera farmers who cry out against middlemen can avoid being fleeced by using the new manual Aloe Vera Gel extractor, which goes for sh500,000.
By having such machines, farmers will be able to add value to their produce such that they compete favourably on the market. Another machine is the Cassava grater which reduces processing time. All these machines are necessary for Uganda.

Original_Search_Title: Why Corrupt Rulers Hate History, Political Science
Headline: Uganda: Why Corrupt Rulers Hate History, Political Science
Body_Text:
Mr Wafula Oguttu's article, Save UPE Before It Turns a Chronic Disaster, (Daily Monitor on Monday) was extremely depressing reading. Of course, many people foresaw that the Universal Primary Education (UPE) would end up as a gulag of ill-educated children of the poor and working class, but no one would have imagined it would be as bad as Wafula described it.
The scary thing is that even the gloomy picture Wafula paints is not the worst, going by accounts one reads on the Internet about the state of primary school education in the more remote parts of the motherland. I remember almost seven years ago, we were driving on a weekend to a funeral in the western part of Tororo district and took a shortcut off Tirinyi Road.
Deep in the village we came upon a place that had a relatively new UPE school, iron roof and all shining on one side of the road. On the other was another school, a private one. It was built of grass-thatch and mud walls.
The shiny government school was dead. The grass-thatched one was full of life. We stopped under a mango tree nearby to buy roast maize and cassava, and asked the vendor why the government school was closed.
He told us the teachers were all across the road teaching in the private grass-thatched school. With its mud walls and all, it still had something the shining government school didn't. Parents paid, not much, the equivalent of about Shs50 a week.
With that, the enterprising headmaster paid the teachers a few thousand bob a month, bought chalk, put up blackboards, and so forth. In the government school, they went for months without pay.
For those who follow these matters, it's the same story, from Kenya's sprawling Kibera to the great slums of Mumbai. In these slums, the schools where parents pay one dollar a month, offer a better education than the government and, sometimes, donor-funded ones with their glass windows, which are "free".
It would be woefully wrong to conclude from this that "free" primary schooling cannot work. It can, of course. In the Nordic and Scandinavian countries, for example, they offer some of the best education in the world - and it is "free". We quote "free" because, really, there's nothing free. Someone pays for it - a taxpayer at home, or abroad if like UPE a large chunk of it is paid for from donor aid, or philanthropy if it's funded by some charity organisation. Someone always pays.
Anyhow, the bigger point is that a military dictatorship can offer free decent education; a theocratic state can; functioning democracies too can. However, no corrupt half-democracy where the gap between the poor and rich grows with every other day has been able to offer a UPE worth its name anywhere in the world.
This explains why UPE in Uganda is the mess it is. In the early stages, the government will push it as a populist programme to get legitimacy and win votes.
But UPE, even when it still produced mostly below average students as it did in the early stages in Uganda, eventually increases the number of slightly more enlightened people who understand things better than the totally illiterate peasant of old.
Before you know it, the first graduates become more aware voters. And soon, they are a problem for the government.
They begin seeing that it is corrupt, that only a few people are doing well, and instead of voting for it in gratitude, they vote against it. That forces the government to resort to more and more brazen vote stealing to win as we have seen in Uganda over the last two elections.
Eventually, the government becomes stuck. It can't scrap the programme, because there would be a huge backlash. But also it can't do the things Wafula is asking for to improve it, because it would be digging its political grave. Therefore, it does the next big thing - it turns UPE into a limbo; it's there, but its products are as good as people who didn't go to school.
Actually if you want to know what exactly is a government's intention, don't look at what it does at the primary school level. Look at what it does at university level.
Usually, it will stop funding the Arts and Social Sciences, and concentrate on Science and "practical" subjects as the Museveni government has done.
The reason for that is that historians, political scientists, and psychologists are the greatest danger to a decaying autocracy. First, they can profile the mental state of the strongman. They can read social unrest. They can make comparisons with other societies.
But the biggest nuisances of them all are historians and literature people. When the autocrat tries to rewrite the history of the country, and his own years in power, the historians keep the accurate record. And the writers and playwrights capture the true story for posterity.
So state scholarships for the Arts have to go. There are no prizes for what will be next - Law, of course.

Original_Search_Title: Farmers Learn New Cassava Preservation Method
Headline: Uganda: Farmers Learn New Cassava Preservation Method
Body_Text:
Kampala — FARMERS in Pallisa district have taken to preserving their cassava using a grater. The motor-driven machine can process one metric tonne of cassava per hour, using only 0.8 litres of petrol.
"I used to sell 4kg of cassava at sh800. I feared it would rot. I now get sh1,200 a kilogramme," says George Owere."
Gertrude Kikyolongo, the Pallisa Agri-business Training Association spokesperson, said the machine is popular. "We sell each at $900 (about sh1.4m), but there is already high demand. Sometimes we get as many as four orders a week," she said.
Kikyolongo said the machine is mostly bought buy commercial farmers. It grinds raw cassava into a paste, which is sieved and dried by frying.
This enables it to stay for five years without going bad.
"The cassava can be used to make cakes, buns, doughnuts, pancakes and to mix in millet flour to make millet bread," Kikyolongo says.

Original_Search_Title: 'No Value Addition Without Money'
Headline: Uganda: 'No Value Addition Without Money'
Body_Text:
Kampala — FRANK Tumwebaze was elected the new president of the Uganda National Farmers Federation (UNFF) in May. He takes over an institution that has made several achievements, but still has serious challenges to handle. Tumwebaze is also MP Kamwenge County. Joshua Kato talked to him about his aspirations:
What inspired you to become the chairman of this group?
Answer: I am a farmer in Kamwenge. I derive part of my income from farming. Whatever I get from Parliament, I invest it in farming. When I became chairman of Kamwenge District Farmers Association, I found out that there was need for a stronger voice to articulate farmers' issues. Farmers are different from other private groups. I thought they have to be represented at the highest level.
What is the national membership of UNFFE?
There over 5 million members in 91 farmers groups. Farmers are organised in district farmers associations. We also have commodity associations. We have a big network that runs up to the parish level. UNFFE are the organisers of the annual agricultural fair at Jinja. They organise it to showcase what they are doing and to make contacts with each other.
For many years, the Government has been advocating mechanisation of agriculture, but this is not being implemented. What are your plans?
We want the Government to know the land holding capacity in Uganda. Not all farmers in Uganda need tractors. These include those with less than five acres. But there are people with over 10 acres of land. Ploughing them with a hoe is difficult. It will not take us anywhere. They need tractors. Currently one needs at least sh240,000 to plough one acre of land for the first and second ploughing.
This is too high for most farmers. We have already proposed that the Government sets up a tractor hire scheme managed by the farmers.
The Government can give us a seed grant or a subsidy to help up secure tractors. There are tractors of between sh15m and 35m.
Sh1b can give us 30 to 40 tractors. We can give these tractors to the district farmers groups to run them. They can hire them out to farmers at half the current price.
How about small but effective farm tools?
There are so many small farm tools but our farmers do not have them.
The problem is lack of an agro-processing and value addition fund. We talk about value addition, but we never put in any money. Value addition is now an inbuilt component of National Agricultural Aadvisory Services (NAADS).
But it needs to be scaled up. We are now teaming up with NAADS to ensure that value addition is separately put in the budget. Once there is funding, farmers groups will start writing proposals for whatever tool they need - cassava mills, hand-held tractors and many others.
Uganda's farm produce is very high quality, but this quality is affected by poor post-harvest handling. How will you work to improve this?
We have to invest in construction of regional storage facilities. These can even allow us to operate the Warehouse Receipt System. But the Government and donors have assist. We are training people in good post-harvest practices, but training alone is not enough if we don't have proper storage facilities.
The Government introduced zoning a few years ago as one of the ways to improve agriculture. How have your farmers been involved in this process?
I don't know the progress because our farmers were not officially involved. For this programme to succeed, we should have been directly involved. The Government now has a challenge to work with us because we have the ability to mobilise all farmers countrywide.
How should the Bonna Bagaggawale component be incorporated into the agricultural sector?
We have written to the Government about this. We proposed that the funds be channelled to farmers, but hinged on things like acquiring tractors, construction of storage facilities, agro-processing and value-addition.
The annual agricultural show ground and the fair have got challenges, how are you handling them?
We have already asked the Government to help us improve this structure. We are organising the show privately without receiving any money from the Government, but it is also the Government's role to ensure that new technologies in agriculture are showcased. Furthermore, the show is under threat from the Uganda Revenue Authority(URA).
We had problems this year when URA officials closed off the show demanding taxes from our gate collections. URA should realise that this show is not like the Uganda Manufacturers Association fair, which is attended by established manufacturers. Gate collections are our only source of income. If it is taxed, the next thing we shall do is to close the fair and this will be a very big loss to the country.

Original_Search_Title: Mixing Wheat And Cassava to Reduce Cost of Bread
Headline: Mozambique: Mixing Wheat And Cassava to Reduce Cost of Bread
Body_Text:
Maputo — The Eduardo Mondlane University (UEM), the largest higher education institution in Mozambique, has successfully conducted a study on using a mixture of wheat and cassava flours to produce bread.
The study was ordered by the government, seeking solutions to the repeated increases in the price of wheat flour by the milling companies, which subsequently increases the price of bread.
The results of the first study approved a mixture of 7.5 kilos of cassava flour for every 42.5 kilos of wheat flour. The researchers found that the resulting bread has the same characteristics of that made of wheat flour only, with the advantage that the new type offers better guarantees of conservation.
Happy with these results, the study group increased the proportion of cassava to 25 per cent - 12.5 kilos of cassava flour to 37.5 kilos of wheat flour. The idea is to advance to a type of bread that is at least 60 per cent cassava flour and only 40 per cent wheat flour.
The Minister of Science and Technology, Venancio Massingue, met on Monday with representatives of the milling companies, bakers, researchers and other food specialists to present publicly the project and ask for the necessary support from the bakers, who must be persuaded to adopt the new type of bread. The cassava- wheat mixture has already been approved by the health services.
Cited in Tuesday's issue of the Maputo daily "Noticias", Massingue explained that the idea of seeking alternative types of bread, in response to rising international wheat prices, was first advanced by President Armando Guebuza in May, during a meeting of the government.
"After that, we called a meeting of the Science and Technology Council, that quickly started work in the field", said Massingue.
UEM Engineering Faculty research director Carlos Lucas, who heads the working group, explained that the experiment was undertaken in three bakeries, selected on the basis of a questionnaire circulated to bakers in Maputo and Matola concerning the quantity of bread they produce and the complexity of their equipment.
After the proven success of the mixture of the wheat and cassava flours (the new type of bread was tested by 500 people), the idea is to advance to other experiments, with maize meal and soya.
Mozambique imports almost all the wheat it consumes. Only on the Angonia plateau, in the western province of Tete, is a small amount of wheat grown. But cassava is the most abundant staple in the country, and it should not be difficult to increase cassava production to meet demand from bakers.
Despite Massingue's insistence on knowing whether or not the bakers are prepared to produce this new type of bread, the response was lukewarm. Far from enthusiastically rising to the challenge of producing a healthy product using Mozambique's own raw materials, conservative bakers preferred to lament the lack of home-grown wheat.
Victor Manuel, of the Maputo Bakers Association, said the most important measures that could be taken would be for the University to increase the production of wheat in Mozambique, taking advantage of potential which he alleged exists in various parts of the country. (In fact, the climatic conditions in most of Mozambique are not propitious for wheat).
The millers also said that they need some time before they can give any informed answer on their real capacity to embark on this project.
The researchers believe that the country has enough cassava flour to produce the new bread. The only problem they saw was the lack of any tradition in the urban centres of eating bread made from anything other than wheat.

Original_Search_Title: UN Begins Food Security Drive in North
Headline: Uganda: UN Begins Food Security Drive in North
Body_Text:
Lira — Internally displaced persons and those returning home from the camps in the north may not have to worry about food since the UN Food and Agriculture Organisation has launched a food security drive in the area.
The Fao-funded cassava planting programme that began in November 2006 covers Lango, Acholi and Teso regions.
The residents prefer cassava for food security crop since it can be stored for long.
Other crops in the programme include maize, rice, sweet potatoes, groundnuts, sorghum, millet, sim-sim and improved banana species which are still under testing to make sure that they can adopt to the climate in the north.

Original_Search_Title: Zimbabwean Farmers to Build N400m Dairy
Headline: Nigeria: Zimbabwean Farmers to Build N400m Dairy
Body_Text:
Zimbabwean commercial farmers operating in Shonga Edu local government area of Kwara State, have concluded arrangements to establish a N400 million dairy factory.
The commissioner for Agriculture, Prof. Gana Yisa, made this known to newsmen yesterday in Ilorin, stating that the factory would produce about four million litres of milk annually.
He revealed that for this purpose, about 900 specially bred dairy cows would be imported from South Africa.
Yisa pointed out that the factory would be located in Shonga, which would be completed cand production would start by early next year. He added that the factory would produce cheese for export while some of the 13 farmers would also set up poultry farms.
"The farmers intend to produce two million broilers per annum once the poultry farms are in place," the commissioner said. He added that Soya bean crop would be used for poultry feed.
About 5,000 hectares of farmland with a variety of crops including maize, rice, soya bean, and cassava had been cultivated by the farmers.
The commissioner further said that a flow producing company from Benue State had already placed an order for the cassava.
Meanwhile, products from the farms were sold to merchants from other parts of the country and exported. Only produce from local farms are available in Shonga village market.
In Shonga village in Kwara, locals said that they do not have access to the produce of the white Zimbabwe farmers.
Malam Usman Etsudiko who is the head of the market, said the people and traders did not even know what the produce of the white farmers looked like.
"What we sell here is from our local farmers. If anybody tells you we are buying or eating cheap commercial produce from Zimbabwean farmers, it is a lie," Usman said.
Another resident, Mallam Aliyu added that the few workers employed by the white farmers also had no access to the produce.
However, the wife of Mr. Hunter Coetzee, another commercial farmer, confirmed that buyers came from as far as Lagos to buy their produce.

Original_Search_Title: White Farmers Plan N400m Dairy Factory
Headline: Nigeria: White Farmers Plan N400m Dairy Factory
Body_Text:
Kwara — Kwara State Commissioner of Agriculture, Professor Gana Yisa, has said Zimbabwean commercial farmers operating in Shonga, Edu Local Government Area of the state, will soon establish a N400-million dairy factory
Yisa said in Ilorin yesterday, that the factory would produce about four million litres of milk annually and would so on be completed, while production will start early 2008.
He said the state government has awarded a N200-million electricity contract for the connection of Shonga Village to the national grid.
Yisa said about 900 specially bred dairy cows would be imported from South Africa for the purpose.
According to him, the factory would produce cheese for export, while some of the 13 farmers would soon set up poultryfarms. "The farmers intend to produce two million broilers per annum once thepoultry farms are in place," he said, adding that soya bean crop would be used for poultry feed.The farmers had cultivated about 5,000 hectares of farmland with a variety of crops, including maize, rice, soya bean and cassava. "As a matter of fact, each of them has 320 hectares of cassava with an estimated yield of 60,000 tonnes per hectare," Yisa said, adding that a flour producing company from Benue had placed order for the cassava.
The commissioner said when completed, the cargo shed at the Ilorin International Airport now under construction, would be used to store produce from the farms.
Prof Gana Yisa, said the electricity project would enable the farmers to have access to electricity, the lack of which he noted,was a major problem. According to him, contractors are now on site installing electricity transformers."In a couple of months, the commercial farms will be linked to the national grid.You cannot use diesel to run a factory,"Yisa said, pointing out that the farmershad been using generators. He observed that infrastructure wasspringing up in Shonga due to thepresence of the white farmers now called "new Nigerian farmers" by the state government. On employment opportunities, Yisa said that some 3,000 persons from Shonga area were now employed both as casual and permanent workers. Investigations , showed that only 100 of the workers were on permanent employment, while the rest were casual workers hired during harvest. Meanwhile, Alhaji Amuda Kannike, theSpecial Assistant to the Governor onAgriculture, in August disclosed that five Nigerian banks had assisted the farmers with a total of N2.5 billion. The banks according to him are: Intercontinental Bank, First InlandBank, Bank PHB, Guarantee TrustBank and First Bank.

Original_Search_Title: The Enterprise Sector is Emerging to Cage Unemployment and Sustain Development - Adelaja, DG SME
Headline: Nigeria: The Enterprise Sector is Emerging to Cage Unemployment and Sustain Development - Adelaja, DG SMEDAN
Body_Text:
Lagos — Mrs. Dupe Adelaja, the indefatigable Director-General of the Small and Medium Enterprises Development Agency of Nigeria SMEDAN, speaks in this interview held in her office in Abuja, of her determination to grow the enterprise sector to a level it can drive the nation's economy. Excerpts:
HOW can you leverage on the MSMEs national policy recently approved by the Federal Executive Council (FEC) to realise your determination to shape the SMEs sector vibrant enough to sustain economic development?
Thank you very much. I thank God that we were able to come up with the fourth national policy on MSMEs. The policy as a document on its own means not much, but a step in the journey. So, to ensure that there is effective policy implementation, we have developed policy implementation strategy, in the national policy, there is provision for committee of stake holders for implementation. Once the committee sits, we'll map out landmark areas we're going to achieve within a specific period of time. On our part, we have already started extracting certain critical elements of implementation from the document and we are now reaching out to state governments to commence the implementation in key areas like infrastructure, finance, human capacity development and marketing. So, we developed implementation strategy on our own because we are the umbrella implementation body. The state governments are responding positively by given their commitments to the counter part funding to get it going.
The economic aspiration of the present administration is to build an economy strong enough to be counted among the 20 best in the world in 2020, how would you contribute towards it?
Our role towards achieving the vision of Mr. President is promotional and facilitating the development of micro, small and medium enterprises through information provision, gathering, designing intervention programmes in terms of entrepreneurial training and education and encouraging state governments to set up the necessary infrastructure like parks, work space common facility where those who have gained information on vocational and entrepreneurial skills can put it into practice. Our own role is very crucial and that we'll continue to play.
As a critical institution in growing or nurturing enterprises which are of course key to sustaining the nation's economic development, how would you plug in the financial and economic sector reforms for further growth?
Well this is an important question. The reforms touch on improving factors of production, without those elements including human reform in the civil service to reposition the service to better perform its bureaucratic functions. The reforms help to drive socio-economic development and we key in straight away because it helps the SMEs to develop faster because they are now able to have access to factors of production. Like the financial sector reform, which has produced micro finance banks.
How would you react to the observation that since 2003 you assumed office as DG, your activities have concentrated on city centres only, thus denying the grassroots entrepreneurs access to your agency's activities, whom indeed deserve your impact because micro and small enterprises are scattered in the rural areas of the country?
Once again I thank you for this credible question. Let me answer it by saying Nigeria is huge. And it is a wrong perception that we concentrate on cities. We actually concentrate on rural areas. Because our mandate is to reduce rural-urban migration and most of the people that we deal with are in the rural areas. The city is already served by business development service providers. The main jobs that we do like fish foundation, enterprise development services of Lagos Business School, LEAP, etc, etc. So, we know right from the beginning that our mandate is in the rural area. In order to deepen the awareness and get the message to the rural area, we are requesting for partnership, because we want sustainability. We don't want to embark on projects that the people don't have ownership in. Because of sustainability, sometimes it looks as if we are not deeping our intervention or support sufficiently enough in the rural area.
We have 774 local government areas. What we did was to conduct studies on what each local government can best convert comparative advantage into competitiveness. Definitely we are not an implementation agency, we tried to market it to the local government who should also be partners in the programme. Because our programme is private sector- private sector partnership or private sector-public sector partnership for sustainability.
In order to ensure that we are dealing with the rural area, one local government, one product, by the time each state starts accepting that you will have activities in the local governments. The state governments would have no choice but to build infrastructure around the products that they are promoting, then employment is created and wealth is distributed and rural-urban migration is reduced. Through our information centres, we are partnering with communities apart from local governments.
Our interest and efforts are actually in the rural areas and we have been implementing programmes in the rural areas, through the information centres and carrying out focus group discussion among agro processing areas/industrialists - cassava, farmers, rice millers, and shea butter producers in Kwara. This is not in Abuja or Lagos. They are in the rural area and we reach out to those ones. But it is like having pockets of people rather than the critical mass. There is what we call rural-micro enterprise development which is still on-going. And which International Federation of Agricultural Development (IFAD) in Rome is trying to promote if Nigerian government accepts, then those rural areas will benefit more.
How many local governments have you reached out?
There are not many. But we have 33 business development information centres space based amongst communities. The insincerity of our people is causing a lot of set back.
You expressed intention to regulate business development service provider. What are your reasons and how far have you gone?
We have gone very far. We have in our data bank the ones that have gone through our check list. The reason why we are doing that is because we want to protect MSMEs community from those who may exploit them as a result of entrepreneurial capacity that we are emphasising on. The law gives us the power to regulate.
What is your candid assessment of the enterprise sector, does it have the potential to position as the nation's economic heartbeat in five years, if not what are the challenges?
The enterprise sector is emerging. There is a lot of awareness being created about the potential of self-employment in boosting the economy. And based on that, I see as crave by most young Nigerians, in the next five years if we can maintain this tempo, the SMEs sector would be the real engine to make our economy vibrant and a solid GDP. If you talk of growing the agro sector, you need to encourage enterprises to add values so that our economy can earn more income. We are going to move from necessity entrepreneurs to opportunity entrepreneurs. I am seeing the signs that SMEs will contribute a lot to wealth creation in this country. We are moving in the right direction. In terms of challenges, we have had in the past few years insufficient support and commitment by state governments to partner to drive this process.

Original_Search_Title: Unrealised Dreams in the Luwero Triangle
Headline: Uganda: Unrealised Dreams in the Luwero Triangle
Body_Text:
Luweero — At times I ask myself whether this is the government I fought to bring into power. The people up there seem to have forgotten that we once shared a piece of cassava in the bush - former NRA fighter
For Isirayiri Bugembe, 52, a resident of Semuto, Luweero District when the National Resistance Army/Movement (NRA/M) shot its way to power in 1986, it was like reaching the promised land. To him, free education for his eight children, a good job, though he dropped out in primary two, a huge village house and respect from his peers is what lay ahead because of what he says was his role as one of the founding fighters of the NRA under Mr Yoweri Museveni.
Two decades down the road, Bugembe, looking weary and haggard after falling on hard times, is thinking twice. "At times I ask myself whether this is the government I fought to bring into power. The people up there seem to have forgotten that we once shared a piece of cassava in the bush," Bugembe, now a casual labourer and charcoal trader despite his failing health, told Sunday Monitor when this reporter met him early this month.
Bugembe's point of disillusionment came on May, 23, 2006. On that day Bugembe says a senior government official, who he says hid in his house for two weeks during the bush war, came to Luweero claimed he did not remember him.
"How can that person (official) claim he does not remember me when I used to hide him in my house. My wife and children looked after him, we ate from the same plate," he said. Bugembe, however, refused to divulge the official's name to avoid embarrassing him but blamed that attitude for the reason some former bush war fighters supported either Forum for Democratic Change (FDC) leader, Kizza Besigye, in the February 2006 elections or the Democratic Party's (DP) Ssebaana Kizito.
Bugembe, a known supporter of President Museveni according to his drinkmates in a bar selling local potent gin (waragi) on the fringes of Semuto Town represents thousands of NRA's unsung heroes who are living a life of deprivation today in what was then known as the Luweero Triangle. They live the hard life while some of their former colleagues today drive posh cars, own mansions, educate their children in affluent schools abroad and in Kampala.
It is people like him who attended the President's meet-the-people meeting at Mijinji, one of the former NRA guerilla bases, as he toured the former triangle in what was officially labelled a campaign to fight household poverty. Some observers, however, have seen the makings of presidential re-election campaign in the tour.
Even though the people he met still laughed at his jokes about the past, the President appeared ready to confront the issues of the poverty head on. He chose to divert from the general line that had been carried in one of his landmark speeches delivered in Jinja on February 9, 2002, about the "Evolution and Modernisation of Society". In that speech, he had blamed the failure of African leadership to improve the people's lot, but now he was castigating the people for their sad state affairs.
President Museveni addressing residents of Masulita in Luwero District, on August 30. Photos by Wandera W'Ouma & PPU
"If you take the case of Karamoja, for instance, is it fully employed or utilised? If you go to Karamoja you can see that everybody is asleep. The land, the forests, the human beings, and the cows are all asleep!" he mused at on rally while noting that this is characteristic of backward countries -- the under utilisation of the factors of production.
But former fighters like Bugembe would have thought that once they were in power it was an outright guarantee of the end of poverty. They thought they would receive hand-outs from the State, especially when the government established a ministry for Luweero affairs.
This was not to be, and this is the more reason they cannot reconcile President Museveni's theory about employing "full factors of production" and their difficult situation.
One felt that there is indeed a level of bitterness hanging in the air as we followed the President throughout his tour. He might have also been aware of this because unlike the 200km 'long march' from Luweero through Birembo in Mubende that marked the beginning of his re-election campaign in 2001, this time he is pressed deeper into the villages.
There were fewer rallies held at district headquarters this time. Mr Museveni also moved with a smaller entourage of ministers and hangers-on. In fact, it was interesting to see him cutting them off. At Kapeeka, the minister for Luwero, Nyombi Tembo, suffered this embarrassment just like Ruth Nankanbirwa (state for defence) at Ddwaniro, when they wanted to make long-winded speeches.
Instead, the President insisted on interacting directly with the people at a personal level.
He visited homes of the former fighters, some who like Bugembe might be feeling that they have been forgotten, received donations and pledges. For those who have died, the President promised to give assistance to their families.
However, those we spoke to still felt the President should go beyond the handshake he willingly extended. "I fought with him (Museveni). I lost a wife and my eldest son during the war. My goats and cows were taken by the NRA soldiers. I tried getting compensation from the President, his people blocked me," Joseph Lukabya of Lwamata, Kiboga said.
"Until I get what was taken from me, I think shaking of hands with the President makes no difference in my life," he added. Lukabya said he voted for DP's Ssebaana in the last presidential election.
It is people like this President Museveni might have been addressing when he said at the rally in Mijinji that: "All these ministers you see here are your servants. The other time I was a freedom fighter but you elected me into office, now I am your servant. If you don't vote me into office, I will not complain. I will retire to my home."
But he is not leaving everything to chance because cash donations of Shs10 million were doled out to at least every three villages per sub-county we visited from the presidential purse, ostensibly to help the people start up income generating projects.

Original_Search_Title: Vietnamese Govt Offers to Invest in Ekiti
Headline: Nigeria: Vietnamese Govt Offers to Invest in Ekiti
Body_Text:
Lagos — Efforts of the Engr. Segun Oni led Ekiti State government to attract local and foreign investors to the state have continued to yield results as the Vietnamese Government has joined the league of investors wishing to invest in the state.
Mr. Van Pham Cong, Trade Counselor of Vietnam Trade Office in Nigeria, expressed this position when he visited the governor yesterday. The Counselor said he was in the state to explore possibility of investment collaboration bet-ween Ekiti State and Vietnam.
He disclosed that his country is interested in investing in the agric sector, especially cassava plantation for local production of starch, ethanol.
Other areas of interest include cashew plantation, building and construction to involve production of building materials from local raw materials, skill acquisition and vocational training for setting up of cottage industries in areas like paint making, clay burning, wood processing and so on.
"On behalf of the Vietnam Trade Office of Socialist Republic of Vietnam in Nigeria, I am particularly calling on the Governor, Engr. Segun Oni, to join hands with me, to chart a path forward for the Nigeria-Vietnam Bilateral Investment collaboration; generally we shall be expecting cooperation from the State Government in identifying areas which the Vietnam Technical expertise and Funding could be sourced for the mutual benefit of Ekiti State people and Vietnam investors," Cong said.
Responding, Oni thanked the Vietnamese government for taking interest in the state. He said the focus of his administration is to open up Ekiti state to local and foreign investors and that explains why he has taken the task of creating an enabling environment very seriously.
His words; "This administration is passionate about opening up of the state to local and foreign investors. We want to create jobs for our people and also increase our Internally Generated revenue so as to reduce dependence on the federation account.
"Therefore, we shall continue to make Ekiti State investors friendly by removing all identified obstacles."
"Already, the process of securing Certificate of Occu-pancy (C of O), which used to be cumbersome has been so simplified that it is now possible to get C of O within 10 working days. We are also making efforts to improve power supply while roads are being constructed to make all town and villages in the state accessible."

Original_Search_Title: Global Markets Boost Incomes of Small-Scale Farmers
Headline: Zambia: Global Markets Boost Incomes of Small-Scale Farmers
Body_Text:
Lusaka — Struggling with low prices for their produce in the domestic market, a growing number of small-scale farmers in Zambia have managed to turn their lives around by supplying international markets.
Luke Mbewe, chief executive officer of the Zambia Export Growers Association, an umbrella organisation of small-scale and commercial farmers cultivating vegetables and cut flowers, said returns were high for farmers who targeted the international market, especially the European Union (EU).
Under the Cotonou agreement for least-developed countries, the EU offers duty- and quota-free access to imports from African and Caribbean-Pacific countries.
"Many of our farmers are able to work themselves out of poverty within just a short period of time. In totality, our farmers are earning a gross income of about US$60 million every year from the exports of over 7,000 metric tonnes of fresh fruits and vegetables, and from about 4,000 tonnes of cut flowers," Mbewe told IRIN.
"Of this amount, about half is realised from fresh vegetables that we export mainly to South Africa through the Woolworths chain stores, as well as to New Zealand and the EU. The major market for our cut flowers still remains the European market, particularly the Netherlands, where they are auctioned," he said.
Joseph Mulenga, 62, took up growing organic maize, fruit and vegetables chiefly for export on his 14-hectare of land, 25km east of the capital, Lusaka, after he lost his job in the formal sector a few years ago. A huge demand for organically produced food in neighbouring South Africa has made him comparatively rich.
"I now own a state-of-the-art $10,000 worth bore-hole that provides my family with clean water for home use and for irrigating the crops," he said. "I also have a solar panel system, which propels the machine to constantly supply water to the tank and to the fields without using diesel or electricity, thereby cutting on the costs."
Mulenga uses green manure, a cover crop grown for a specific period and then ploughed under to add nutrients to the soil, or simple techniques like rotating crops every other year to maximise production.
Little domestic incentive
Over 75 percent of Zambia's working population is engaged in agriculture, largely as subsistence farmers growing cassava, vegetables, fruit, and maize, the country's staple crop, according to the Ministry of Agriculture.
President Levy Mwanawasa's administration has attempted to promote agricultural production to reduce the economy's over-dependence on diminishing mineral resources, but analysts say not much has been done to make farming profitable, especially for maize growers, who have had to sell their produce at low prices set by the government.
Many of our farmers are able to work themselves out of poverty within just a short period of time. In totality, our farmers are earning a gross income of about US$60 million every year from the exports of over 7,000 metric tonnes of fresh fruits and vegetables, and from about 4,000 tonnes of cut flowers
In the 2006/07 harvest season, a 50kg bag of maize was pegged at a ceiling price of $9. The Food Reserve Agency, the government body responsible for purchasing produce from farmers, is often late with payments.
"Whatever I am growing and selling on this farm has a premium, unlike my friends who are practising conventional farming and, therefore, only targeting the Food Reserve Agency," said Mulenga. "While their 50kg bag of maize is sold for 38,000 kwacha [$9], as dictated by the government, I can sell mine for as much as K400,000 [$100.88] because the demand is high [on the international market]".
Interest rates of up to 60 percent, expensive diesel and steep electricity tariffs have also left Zambian farmers who focus on the local market with slim profit margins.
Guy Robinson, president of the Zambia National Farmers Union, told IRIN: "For us to have some of our produce selling in countries like South Africa and the UK is a very big achievement; it's a very good development for the farming sector in Zambia, especially since recently we even exported some maize to South Africa, in particular ... I think our farmers are producing better quality products than in the past."
Already a precedent
Small-scale farmers elsewhere in Africa have been selling to external markets for some years. Kenya-based Vegpro, sub-Saharan Africa's third largest vegetable purchaser, has been in existence since the late 1970s, buying produce from small-scale farmers and selling it overseas.
In 2000, US charity CARE pre-financed production and provided technical support to small-scale farmers in Kenya's western region. "We were driven by the fact that, instead of whining about globalisation, which was undermining the poor across the world, we reasoned, 'why not make the markets work for the poor?'" said CARE's George Odo. "With the IT revolution, markets have been brought to our doorstep; we thought, 'why shouldn't our farmers benefit?'"
Although the project was a successful initiative, it was not commercially viable, so CARE linked up with Vegpro to provide a market for the farmers, Odo said.
A rural commercial enterprise has since been formed, known as VegCARE, which helps more than 600 farmers produce vegetables for supermarkets in the UK, such as Tesco and Sainsbury's.
"It was one of the first such initiatives in sub-Saharan Africa to supply supermarkets in Europe," said Odo. The lives of the farmers have improved dramatically, and "annual incomes have shot up from $200 to up to $3,000 a year."
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Pollution On the Rise
Headline: Uganda: Pollution On the Rise
Body_Text:
Kampala — The increasing number of second hand vehicles in Uganda has seen several informal garages come up to take advantage of mechanical problems associated with used vehicles. These garages are found in almost every corner of the city and its suburbs.
Whereas the garages have created jobs and helped several car owners to get back on the road, poor disposal of waste oil and other effluents has put the health and livelihood of thousands of inhabitants at risk. The executive Director of National Environment Management Authority (NEMA), Dr Aryamanya Mugisha says some wetlands where a variety of staple food crops are illegally grown in the city have been turned into dumping sites for waste products from factories and garages among others.
Some of the effluents are carried from the garages into water bodies by infiltrating water whenever it rains. Kampala has several wetlands covering about 16 percent of the district.
Massive contamination
Even the major wetlands that are connected to Lake Victoria and Kyoga have been heavily contaminated by both industrial and domestic waste. Some of the wetlands include Nsoba, Nalukolongo, Kansanga, Kiwembo, Lubigi, Nakivubo and Kirombe swamp system. Mugisha says the waste usually containS toxic chemicals, metals and oils.
When they get into wetlands where crops are grown, they are extracted and absorbed by the crops. Mugisha explains that even if these effluents are dumped into a water source or on a dry land, they will still end up in the food chain. He says when chemicals get into the lake, they are ingested by the fish while those that are dumped on dry land will sink into the soil and contaminate it.
Mugisha says that however much contaminated food or fish is cooked, it will still contain the chemicals. He says a person who eats food or takes water contaminated by these chemicals may end up getting cancer and other skin diseases, adding that a recent study conducted by the Department Zoology and Botany of Makerere University proved it.
Some people in Kampala have gone to the extent of growing crops in wetlands like Nakivubo that are used as effluent treatment centers due to limited agricultural land.
The ever-increasing population in the city has exerted pressure on land and forced some people to reside or farm in wetlands. Kampala's population stands at about 2 million people.
Danger to health
Mugisha says all crops that are grown in city wetlands are contaminated and therefore dangerous to people's health. "Some of these wetlands absorb a lot of effluent and I don't encourage anyone to grow crops in such areas because the crops will be contaminated," he says. Illegal farming going on in such places has forced some city dwellers to discard some foodstuffs grown by city dwellers who are practicing urban farming.
Consumers assume that whatever is grown in the city is contaminated with either chemicals or faecal matter. Several people have sworn never to eat certain foods while in Kampala because they believe they are grown in places that are contaminated with toxic chemicals.
"I have never bought yams from Kampala because they are grown in areas where factories and garages are empting untreated effluent, which is then absorbed by the crops and when you eat them, you end up getting health complications," says the Spokesperson of Kampala City Council, Mr Simon Muhumuza, adding that it has been so difficult for the City Council to protect city dwellers from eating contaminated foodstuffs due to interference from people he declined to mention.
"When you attempt to close industries that are empting raw effluent into wetlands and those that are unhygienic, you get so many calls from different people. Recently, we attempted to close unhygienic abattoirs in the city but we had to answer so many questions and we gave up," he says.
Muhumuza says if people were respecting the law, they would not be growing crops in wetlands or along road and railway reserves like they are doing at the moment disguising themselves as people who are legally practicing urban farming. Urban Agriculture has taken the city by storm and the residents here offer ready market for the farm products. Some of the crops grown include yams, cassava, maize, potatoes, and a variety of vegetables.
Agriculture in Kampala is practiced mainly in valley slums where the poor live in informal settlements. NEMA however says urban farmers should be organised and stop-growing crops in wetlands especially those that act as effluent treating centres.
On the issue of garages, Mugisha admits that whereas NEMA has tried to encourage factories and petrol stations to put in place waste management systems, it has not done much for the garages.
"We have left the issue of garages to the planning authorities like KCC but as the industry takes root, we shall concentrate on this issue. "This is a land-use matter which I must admit that we have not handled. Garages should be away from water bodies and wetlands and they should be able to effectively manage their waste," he says. Mugisha says all petrol stations and factories have been instructed to put in place waste management systems.
He says garage for solid and liquid waste like used oil. Waste from garages like old oils, petrol and some emissions (gasses) and scraps of metal are dangerous to both the environment and human beings.
"They should put in place and implement waste management systems that collect and dispose off the waste properly or recycle it," he advises. He says if oil is separated from water, it can be recycled and used as an ingredient in making products like cement. Mugisha says.
NEMA will continue creating awareness to change the current situation. The major sources of environmental degradation and pollution in the city include solid waste, abattoir waste, sewage, industrial pollution, traffic pollution, atmospheric pollution, urban agriculture and rapid urbanisation and washing bays. Oil from vehicles especially those that are washed along the lakeshores also contaminates the water.

Original_Search_Title: FG, China, Arab Banks, Others Spend N66 Billion on Food Security
Headline: Nigeria: FG, China, Arab Banks, Others Spend N66 Billion on Food Security
Body_Text:
Lagos — Over N66 BILLION has been spent as a part of a joint effort by the federal government, the Chinese Government and a consortium of international banks in the country to boost food security.
The minister of Agriculture and Water Resources Sayyadi Ruma, gave this disclosure Monday in Abuja, at the opening of the 'inception workshop of NPFS' saying that government's plan to make provision for 'special intervention fund' for the development of the nation's agricultural sector.
It would be recalled that at the launch of the first phase of the NPFS in 2001, federal government committed the sum of $45.2 million to food security project; aquaculture and inland fisheries; animal diseases and trans-boundary pest control; marketing of agricultural commodities and food stock management and soil fertility initiatives.
In support of the initiative, the Chinese government also injected the sum of $22 million on a four year programme as well as deployed about 524 experts and technicians in a wide range of disciplines with a view to disseminating low cost production technologies to Nigerian farmers.
The trio: BADEA, ADB and IDB committed the sum of $18 million, $32 million and $30 million into the programme in 10 states of the federation.
Realising the importance of the programme to the development of the nation's agricultural sector, federal government also injected the sum of N45 billion or $364 million into the second phase of the five year NPFS programme.
Under the arrangement, the three tiers were to jointly provide for 60 percent of the total fund required while the balance of 40 percent was to be sourced from external donours.
Giving further insight on the 'special intervention fund', the minister said "the intervention was fundamentally because the annual budget of the federal ministry of agriculture and water resources is not likely to bring the desired resources for funding agriculture.
According to him "Nigeria therefore requires, like it has done in aviation, also come up with a special intervention grant for agriculture to take care of the major aspect of agricultural development which is indiscriminately linked to market access to agriculture of this country."
"The ministry in collaboration with Ministry of Environment, Housing and Urban Development has packaged a programme "towards addressing, fundamentally, the issue of land degradation in Nigeria." he added
Ruma, however stressed that the programme was aimed at "having the country leap forward towards increased production that would sufficiently reposition Nigeria's agricultural food security programme.
His word: "I am not talking about micro credit alone, I am talking about redesigning, repackaging, refocusing, recouping the food security programme to address the multidimensional challenges of agricultural development,"
Other aligning issues that would be addressed by the programme include storage of agricultural produces, processing, and marketing of agricultural products.
On concern over the inadequacy of mechanised farming and need to address issue of industrial linkage and market access to agriculture, the minister said Nigeria has close to 16,000 tractors on ground representing total density of about 0.2 and the minimum requirement of FAO is 1.50 density compared to India that has about 4 million tractors.
"Government is working towards increasing the tractor population in this country through a well structured redesigned tractor hiring system, because taking into cognisance of the fact that our resources at the moment, little as they are, may not give us the desired number of tractors that we want.
"The highest number of tractors we purchased 2007 is 1,000 tractors, which means it will take Nigeria 50 years to have 50,000 tractors, and which will mean an average of 75,000 farmers to one tractor. There is no way therefore we could have increase in production if the necessary technology mechanisation to agriculture.
"This would be the major focus of driving the food security programme forward in the Federal Ministry of Agriculture and implement our commitment and resolve towards also leveraging resources and working towards a very strong partnership with the private sector and other NDAs towards actualising this goal." he stated as a alternative to addressing the deficit
Earlier in her welcome speech, Ann Pepple, the ministry's permanent secretary posited that the programme has contributed to the sustainable improvement in national food security through ensuring rapid increase in productivity and food production.
on an economically and environmentally sustainable basis as well as reduce year to year viability in agricultural production.
Some of the successes recorded via the programme include "construction of boreholes, open wells, tube wells, rain water harvesting structures, wash-bores and water control structures.
The ministry, according to Pepple also distributed water pumps, milling machines, oil extractors, shelling, grinding and processing machines for rice, groundnut, melon, tomato, garri, spaghetti, oil palm, cassava and yam as well work-bulls and ox-drawn equipment to 109 sites across the country.

Original_Search_Title: Empower Women, Say Goodbye to Poverty
Headline: Nigeria: Empower Women, Say Goodbye to Poverty
Body_Text:
Lagos — The programme organised by Development Support Institute (DSI) in partnership with Esso/Exxon-Mobil at the Banquet Hall, Wellington Hotel, Warri, Delta State, was intended to equip the women in the rural area with basic skills so that they would be empowered to play major roles in the economic arena and contribute more meaningfully in the finance of their immediate family.
At the opening ceremony of the programme, the Executive Director, DSI, Mrs. Jacqueline Odiadi said, "With skills, women can set personal economic goals for themselves, have more respect and feel better fulfilled as human beings in the larger society".
Women, Odiadi said, constitute the very bedrock of the family and a woman who earns her keep, she added, can by far help bring up the children with all the additional support of the husband and men folk in general. DSI, she stressed, realizes all these and so has always made skills acquisition for women as one of its core objectives. Usually on its own and at times in partnership with donor groups, DSI has been able to deliver several services to women.
"We collaborate with a broad range of organizations foreign and local for this purpose", Odiadi noted. With the assistance of the German government, DSI was able to donate a borehole to women in the Berekodo Community, Eleko, Ibeju-Lekki, Local Government, in Lagos State very recently.
The borehole project commissioned at Berekodo community was conceived by DSI after a needs assessment of the community and was graciously funded by the Embassy of the Federal Republic of Germany in Nigeria.
Odiadi said that when DSI paid the initial visits to the Berekodo Community, it observed that the women and children were busy fetching water from two shallow wells, which were the only source of water in the community. As a gender oriented organisation, Odiadi noted that the experience served as a catalyst to persuade the partners, the German Government, to provide the women and children in the community their very first pipe-borne water.
This she will contribute to healthier living of the Berekodo community, by providing the community with clean water to drink, cook, clean and wash.
Expressing her gratitude to Esso/Exxon Mobil for partnering with DSI in the Niger/Delta programme, Odiadi said that the skills set down for the two-week programme were milliner- hat and bead making; tie and dye cloth making - local fabric (adire); fish farming - small scale ponds; snail and grass cutter farming; cassava processing and soap making. In addition to all these, she said there would be talk on basic entrepreneurial skills as well as reproductive health matters for the participants.
To encourage the participants to be on their own after the training, starter packs were provided at the end of the programme. To also demonstrate how seriously DSI viewed this skill acquisition programme, DSI intends to carry out post-training evaluation at the end to see how the participants are fairing and also conduct a furthers needs assessment on them.
Also last year in Lagos, DSI had a widely publicised two-day workshop on peace building in the Niger Delta region in partnership with Shell Petroleum Development Co. One of the key areas of agreement by nearly all the discussants at the event centred on the need to reduce the vulnerability of women to violent disruptions by making them viable as catalysts and players in the economic process. With basic skills as earlier mentioned, DSI believes that most people will see immediately which part of the economy they can function as goods or service providers.
Odiadi's advise for the Niger-Delta women, was that every one of them should take full advantage of the opportunity provided by the training programme as well as make best of it.
Delivering her address at the occasion, First Lady, Delta State, Mrs. Sheila Roli Uduaghan who was special guest, said that a major difference between the developed and rich nations of the world and their poor and developing counterparts is the extent and level to which women are empowered.
She noted that the United Nations Organization (UNO) report indicates that developing countries are much likely to bridge the gap if women are empowered and made partners of development in all spheres of national life.
"In Sub-Saharan African, the role of women in nation building is far from what is expected. This has been attributed to gender inequality and poverty. It is no longer news that poverty in Sub-Saharan African is colossal and has been recognized as the bane of economic, social and political advancement", she said.
She stressed that it was disheartening to note that the most vulnerable group to the impact of poverty is the woman folk whose access to education, employment, technology and decision-making has been curtailed by gender inequality in a male chauvinist driven world.
Consequently, Uduaghan said majority of women in Sub-Saharan Africa have been reduced to mere paupers and beggars whose livelihood must depend on the "crumbs that falls from the table of the their male counterparts".
She therefore noted that poverty reduction through women empowerment has been prioritized as a major cardinal point of her husband, Dr. Emmanuel Uduaghan, the governor of Delta State.

Original_Search_Title: FDC Challenges Were Inevitable From the Start
Headline: Uganda: FDC Challenges Were Inevitable From the Start
Body_Text:
Kampala — SOME analysts have attributed the declining fortunes of the FDC to infiltration by state agents. This analysis is wrong.
FDC was founded on sentiments of a few people whose anger was not stemming from any fundamental disagreements but rather precipitated by factors like dissatisfaction with one's area of deployment, no deployment or dismissal from office.
The first writings of Dr. Besigye in 2000 complained of issues like katebe or failure by Museveni to listen to historical leaders, and narrated how some senior NRA commanders used to eat chicken in the bush while others ate cassava. These were not fundamental disagreements but rather personal concerns.
It was not a case of departure from the Movement ideology that hitherto fought for policy issues. For example, Besigye's first pronouncements were not denouncing the Movement but rather castigating President Museveni. In 2001, Besigye and his agents insisted that they were still part of the Movement but only wanted to reform its methods of work. That is why his 2001 posters were dominated by the yellow colour, to emphasis that he was not an odd man out in the system so as poach on the Movement support.
And surely he did. Besigye and group insisted on re-focusing the Movement to its original bush struggle methods of work. That was evidence to show that ideologically and structurally the Movement was clearly on course. The problem was with individuals and not the system. But this was a contradiction. If Individuals were bad, especially the head and founder, how then would the system be good. That is why none of those grumbling resigned from the Government to prove to the world that they were dissenting on serious principles. They all formed opposition groups after involuntarily leaving the Government. Such a party founded on petty selfish reasons cannot sustain a political cause that appeals to the entire country and therefore would not last.
It was not wrong to oppose President Museveni, but this was a different type of opposition because it was not based on principle. MPs who had joined PAFO, hoping that it was a genuine platform for parliamentary advocacy, abandoned it on discovering its sole hidden agenda of fighting President Museveni.
The Reform Agenda group first opposed the proposal to change the name to FDC. They also opposed the new alliance of Besigye and the PAFO members, on the grounds that the majority of PAFO MPs had opposed Besigye and harassed his supporters in the 2001 elections and were now selfishly turning to him to use his slightly better position to fight their new war with President Museveni.
Besigye accepted them not on principle but to increase his numbers and also on condition that he was to remain leader of any anti-Museveni group no matter what shape and form it took. Some of the reform agenda people were right to reject these people. The so-called harassment of Besigye supporters in 2001 was largely occasioned by these PAFO MPs especially those who were soldiers, but at the same time became the first to rush and embrace him a few months after.
FDC was formed under an adhoc arrangement that saw individuals temporarily abandon their differences to pursue a common agenda of opposing and removing President Museveni the man. They are now stranded with their own hatred for President Museveni and their agenda doesn't seem to pay quick dividends given the resounding rejection they have registered from Ugandans since 2001.
Consequently, some of their progressive members are asking whether it is sustainable to build a party based on the agenda of eliminating one individual. These and other challenges are for FDC to address in order to re-build the confidence of its members, especially those who may have joined it without knowing its clear vision and broader agenda. FDC must demonstrate to its members and to all Ugandans they seek to lead that their assignment goes beyond pulling President Museveni from power.
Therefore, I don't agree with the suggestion that FDC has been killed by state infiltration. Political infiltration is always the game with power contenders but it never hinders progress of any serious political activity. The problem is lack of an agenda that appeals to the masses.
Prophesies of doom such as the prediction of tsunami did not help the situation either; it was a wrong approach that could not convince Ugandans that in Besigye there was a better president than Museveni. FDC should be assessed looking at how it was formed and what aspirations it had during its inception. That is the only way we shall understand why it is growing weaker.
To my colleagues Hon. Otto and Hon. Okumu, the only option available for you is to trace these inherent weakness and discuss them openly with Besigye. Struggle to project the party beyond the single item agenda of being anti-Museveni. But if the going gets tough, don't get going. Come over to NRM.
The writer is the MP for Kibale County, Kamwenge District

Original_Search_Title: Bigombe - a Peacemakers' Lonely Battle
Headline: Uganda: Bigombe - a Peacemakers' Lonely Battle
Body_Text:
Nairobi — ZACHARY OCHIENG previews the negotiator's upcoming book, 'Turning War into Peace: An Insider's Story'
BETTY BIGOMBE HAS BEEN involved in negotiations to end the Lord's Resistance Army insurgency in northern Uganda since the early 1990s. She has mediated in both public and private capacities, and has worked as a senior social scientist with the World Bank's post-conflict unit. Bigombe, now a senior fellow at the United States Institute of Peace is currently writing a book on the art of mediation. In her project report, she shares her experiences and insights from her work on the conflict in northern Uganda. She discusses the problems posed by intra-state conflicts, her unique role as a female mediator in Africa, and the role of the international community in conflict resolution.
The author says that what sustained her over the better part of two decades working at what was often a thankless task was her unshakeable belief that a negotiated settlement offered the only hope for sustained peace in Uganda.
The country's postcolonial history is one of recurrent coups, rebel movements, and a marked north-south divide. A nuanced approach is needed to stem the cycle of violence and insurgency that has defined Uganda's recent past. In this situation - one in which two parties are determined to destroy each other, yet are locked in a protracted stalemate - mediation is required to move toward peace.
Her first major initiative to bring peace to Northern Uganda, now known as the Bigombe I peace talks of 1992-94, saw Bigombe attempting to persuade the rebels to lay down their arms while at the same time connecting with the people of northern Uganda, and communicating their grievances to the government. She believes that many of the lessons learned in the process are universal and can be used to generate ideas for future mediators.
By the early 1990s, the insurgencies in Lango and in Teso had largely subsided, but the war in Acholiland continued to intensify as the armed movement led by Joseph Kony consolidated its military capacity. Until 1992, Bigombe's efforts had been focused on mobilising people through public rallies, seminars and town hall meetings to persuade them to reject the war and prevail upon the rebels to lay down their weapons while the army continued with its operations. After all, she had been sent to the north by President Museveni not to negotiate peace, but to persuade the remnants of the insurgents to lay down their arms and come out of the bush.
The LRA was at this point attacking various IDP (internally displaced persons) camps on a more regular basis than before. They had intensified their attacks on schools, abducting more children to serve as child soldiers. They were also making daring attacks on the National Resistance Army's positions. From 1988-1992, as Minister of State for the Pacification of Northern Uganda, Bigombe's aim was to listen to and try to understand why the LRA was fighting, what kind of support they enjoyed among the population, and what the source of their weapons was.
Right from the beginning, she realised that if she was going to make any progress towards ending the armed conflict, she needed to get a clear picture of what the LRA and their supporters wanted, since they did not seem to have any clear political agenda.
Her approach was therefore to listen to the grievances against the NRM government of the people of northern and eastern Uganda, where the LRA had garnered some support.
To listen to people's grievances meant going to the people wherever they were. It meant pitching camp in all the IDP camps, in small towns where people displaced by the war were camped out, and in small remote villages where the security situation allowed people to remain in their homes. These were not visits where she went in for a couple of hours and left - most lasted for several days. She writes:
"The IDP camps all look alike - mud huts clustered together as tightly as a patch of mushrooms. The huts barely measure 10 feet in diameter. They sit so snuggly together that almost every roof is touching another. Wisps of smoke drift through the conical thatched roofs from cooking fires smouldering within. Figures wander through the wattle and daub sprawl, clutching cooking pots, cups or hoes."
Going to stay in the camps exposed Bigombe to the suffering of the IDPs. She observed the daily struggle for survival in the squalid, congested camps where life was lived under constant threat of LRA attacks. Women lined up for hours at water pumps bearing the bold Unicef logo to fetch water in 20-litre jerricans.
