Some, tired of waiting in the long lines, walked to nearby wells to draw muddy, guinea worm-infested water. It was not safe to drink, but the people in the camps drank the water without even boiling it.
Boiling water was a luxury they could not afford, as it used up fuel wood made scarce because the trees in the area had been cut down and used for the construction of the camps and for the soldiers' huts. In some cases, what used to be forests or jungles had been reduced to open bare land. An additional reason for clearing the bush was to deny rebels hiding places.
The IDPs depended upon the UN World Food Programme to deliver food and other humanitarian assistance. Some people queued up as early as 7 am, though the food trucks usually arrived about mid-day. In the scrambled that ensued, mothers with babies strapped to their backs would be pushed out of the way by the stronger men. The distribution went on until evening and sometimes extended to the next day if the supply lasted that long.
Many people, mainly women, struggled to cultivate gardens near the camps to supplement the assistance they received from the WFP.
MEDICAL TEAMS Ocasionally came in to carry out immunisation programmes. On such occasions, women formed lines once again - this time to get their malnourished children immunised. The children, with their discoloured, wispy hair, wailed in pain while being injected. Sometimes the International Committee of the Red Cross came to distribute blankets and drugs. It was painful to see the people, especially the women, struggle to live with no sources of income, with poor sanitation, suffering from illnesses and with their movements restricted.
Bigombe writes: "As I walk into the camps I am greeted by the stench of latrines, competing with the very strong smell of kwete and lujutu (local alcohol made from cassava and millet) brewed and sold by women to supplement their livelihoods. I battle with huge bluish flies picking at my eyes. Under a tree, some young men are drinking kwete with long straws from one pot. Under another tree, young boys are playing coro, a game similar to scrap, but they are using seeds from the acacia tree and they make small holes in the ground. As I meander through the huts, I find men sitting on small school chairs in the shade, drinking lujutu, drinking from the same glass. Age and overuse has given the single glass being passed round a new colour. A fleet of children are chanting, clapping and fighting to be as close to me as possible. Many are naked, and the rest are wearing clothes that are tattered, barely covering their bodies. They are following me and my entourage.
On the other side of the camp, other children are playing war games using homemade toy guns. The guns are made of twisted metal and sometimes reeds. The older boys try to play soccer in the very limited space available. The balls are also homemade - bound up pieces of cloth, tightly wrapped up with plastic papers and rubber bands."
In the IDP camps, there are children and more children everywhere you turn - children whose futures are uncertain as very few manage to go to school. Even if they do not die before their fifth birthday (as many do, according to Unicef), most will never be productive members of their communities. The only thing that is certain is that poverty can be expected to continue in the region. Bigombe however, understands why this reality persists. She remembers Alex La Guma's book about life in the shantytowns in South Africa where black people lived. He described seeing fleets and fleets of children, and he explained that the only social activity that disenfranchised people have is sex, the only "free goods" at their disposal.
"The camp leaders - councillors and women leaders - walk me to the meeting venue. My arrival is announced to the people as we pass by, 'Welo me awora dong oo,' meaning, 'Our important guest has arrived.' The men, looking obviously drunk, promise to join us soon."
She notes that the constant drinking of alcohol in the camps is to a large extent to blame for the high levels of domestic violence and the breakdown of cultural values. In turn, the misery of life in the camps is used as an excuse for excessive drinking. Driven away from their land, the men are humiliated because they can no longer fend for their families as tradition dictates, so they resort to heavy drinking.
Frustration and despair prevail in the camps, where there is no real promise or hope for the future.
People just drift from one day to the next and moral degeneration takes over.
Under these circumstances, the burden of feeding families is left to women. What is more, it is women who take in orphans and tend to the sick. Because of the conditions in the camps, disease and malnutrition are rife, and children get fall ill frequently. The responsibility of caring for war casualties and the aged also falls upon the women.
Women are also vulnerable because of their sex. When they venture outside the camps to go to market or in search of work, they have to go through many roadblocks, or checkpoints, where they are sometimes subjected to humiliating body searches and interrogations. Often corrupt soldiers demand their goods or money and to survive they part with whatever they have for their own safety. They are often raped and/or beaten in the process.
BUT WHERE THE AUTHOR Expected to find bitterness and hatred, these women still smiled and persevered. What was most overpowering was their determination:
"They ululated, sang, clapped and danced as they lined up the paths leading to the camps to welcome me. From one camp to the next the horrors of women were the same. They described the abduction of their sons and daughters and the nightmare of not knowing whether they would ever see their children again. One woman who's daughter had been abducted said, 'I am scared the LRA might have already killed her, but sometimes I console myself that she is still alive, and is probably trying to escape, as I have seen some other children escape and come out alive. I pray to God all the time. When I am eating, I keep wondering whether she has eaten and what kind of food; when I am going to bed, I wonder whether the LRA allow her to go to bed, and what kind of bed. I think about her all the time, I hardly sleep, she is the first thing in my mind when I wake up, and the last thing I think about before I fall asleep.'
Another woman whose two young boys were abducted described her nightmares: 'I am tormented each time I see an army helicopter gunship fly past above me, on a mission to attack and kill the LRA. I feel extremely nervous that my boys might be killed in the process. I go through the same pains when I hear that LRA has killed people, or abducted some, or raped, or destroyed people's property, because I fear my sons have probably been turned into killers. We all know that children who are abducted are forced to commit all kinds of atrocities. I am devastated by the thought that I might now be a mother of killers or rapists. I brought my boys up to be good, disciplined children. Life will never be the same again."
Some women described how they were forced to watch their husbands and sons being killed, some described how they watched their daughters being raped. Some of the women were gang raped while their husbands and sometimes their children watched; they told the author of forced impregnation and lamented the loss of cultural values.
But while it was her harrowing experience of the conditions in the camps that gave her the emotional resolve to continue searching for a path to peace, the idea of seeking dialogue instead of a military solution was the result of a coldly logical analysis of the facts on the ground, based especially on the reality she uncovered of how the Ugandan army was "fighting" the war - a tale of almost unbelievable corruption and cynicism.
Bigombe then made a personal decision to talk peace with the LRA. This decision was not backed by any official government policy to end the war through dialogue. The decision was based on the belief that the NRA was not making enough effort to end the war - and that even if it were working on a military victory, a military solutions would not end civil strife or bring sustainable peace. When underlying sources of conflict remain unaddressed, there is a reasonable likelihood that conflict will flare up again. A political settlement gives the opportunity to address underlying causes of the violent conflict.
Some people argued that to engage with armed groups is to legitimise violence, or to give credibility to unreasonable or non-negotiable demands. This argument - that peace talks invite the legitimisation of irrational armed groups - was particularly applicable to the LRA, whose primary motivation to fight the government of Uganda was purportedly a divine call sanctioned by God through his "prophet," Joseph Kony.
Many people further argued that to talk peace with the LRA would mean that the brutality they unleashed on innocent civilians would go unpunished.
Bigombe remembers the parliamentary debate on the Amnesty Bill (1989), during which Charles Alai, Member of Parliament from Gulu district, said: "These are people who do not deserve amnesty; they have been committing heinous crimes; they deserve jungle law." Her response was simple: "So far the military option has not succeeded; we therefore need to create incentives that can attract the LRA to lay down their arms and come out of the bush, and amnesty is one way of doing so. There are those who were abducted and forced to commit atrocities - they are victims who became perpetrators. If the LRA leaders will not come out, we need to lure the others to come out of the bush."
Her conviction was that, even if LRA did not have a clear political agenda and had committed heinous crimes against an innocent population, peace talks gave northerners an opportunity to put their grievances with the NRM government on the table so that the government could address them.
Second, since the NRA was not effectively engaging the LRA, there was no point in persisting with a military solution. The NRA was merely responding to the atrocities LRA was committing. They responded to LRA ambushes, they responded to LRA raids on IDP camps to abduct or take food from the civilians, and they responded to LRA raids on health centres to acquire drugs. These responses came sometimes hours after the incident.
Though NRA commanders were reshuffled from time to time to improve their performance, each new postings raised expectations and hope only for them to be dashed within a short time.
Bigombe's resolve to initiate peace talks was further strengthened by reports she received about the conduct of some unscrupulous NRA officers who had exploited the war for their own monetary benefit. Interestingly, some of these reports came from senior officers who disclosed to her the corrupt practices that were rampant in the NRA and were weakening the war effort. These included the lack of adequate equipment and personnel, and tampering with records in such a way as to inspire overconfidence in a paper army. These and other revelations made her realise that the war could drift on forever. After all, members of the international community, especially countries with diplomatic ties to Uganda, had turned a blind eye to the war.
Other corrupt practices included the illicit sale of petrol that was intended to facilitate army operations.
The senior officers had their agents, known as "OPEC boys," at hand whenever fuel was delivered from Kampala. The OPEC boys paraded around with their merchandise in jerricans. They offered relatively cheaper prices than the petrol stations and therefore had no problem disposing of their merchandise quickly. The proceeds, minus a commission for the OPEC boys, were pocketed by individual officers.
Another common practice was the vandalisation of army trucks. Arrangements would be quickly made to procure and send new trucks. Bigombe remembers vividly the sight of a fleet of these brand new trucks arriving in the operation zones. It did not take long before these trucks were vandalised and the parts were sold off by the commanding officers.
There was also a brisk trade going on between the NRA and the Sudanese Peoples' Liberation Army (SPLA). Dry rations and uniforms intended for the NRA on the battlefront were sold by officers to the SPLA for either cash or gold.
If the aforementioned practices contributed to prolonging the war, they were nothing compared with the cases that were being investigated by the committee set up by the UPDF High Command in June 2003, or the revelations of the Sebutinde Commission, which documented the illicit importation of junk military helicopters, and the procurement of undersized uniforms and expired dry rations for the army.
The first cases revealed that senior army officers had created "ghost soldiers" in the Uganda People's Defense Forces (UPDF) and auxiliary forces and maintained these "ghosts" on the payroll. The commanding officers pocketed the salaries of the ghost soldiers.
The investigation discovered that some senior army officers from one unit alone pocketed about Ush11 million monthly (equivalent to almost $6,500). Salaries in one brigade were being paid for 6,500 troops, when in reality the actual number of troops was between 2,500 and 3,000. Colonel Nathan Mugisha, an officer interviewed by the committee, remarked, "The 4th Division (with headquarters based in Gulu) is a ghost division."
The report concluded, "Ghost soldiers were found to exist in all units examined by the committee, including the General Headquarters, Divisions: 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5, independent Units as well as Auxiliary Forces. The gravity of the existence of ghost soldiers is such that the operational efficiency of the UPDF has been undermined because the units are under strength and cannot cope with the task." The UPDF committee's investigation further revealed that, while a brigade usually consists of 736 officers and men, most brigades in the war zone where LRA was active had only about 250 officers and men each.
THE GHOST SOLDIER PROBLEM was so grave that a former army commander, Major General James Kazini, told the committee, "Uganda has no standing army; it depends on the politics of President Museveni to mobilise militia." Major General Kazini was himself seriously implicated in the creation of ghost soldiers and the Local Defense Units (LDU).
Auxiliary forces were recruited and haphazardly dismissed. Major General Kazini was himself accused of laying off 3,000 LDUs, whose monthly salary per was Ush40,000 (about $25) each. This amounted to Ush120 million shillings (approximately $67,000) per month, according to the High Command Committee's report. Another corrupt practice revealed in the report was what the committee termed "double reflections," that is registering one soldier on the rolls of more than one unit.
The committee also uncovered a high rate of desertions within the UPDF and the auxiliary forces that were never reported. Again, deserters' names were retained on the payrolls by unscrupulous commanders. The report talks of "beatings, insults, and abuse, withholding of salaries and excessive labour imposed on soldiers and LDUs by their commanding officers. Soldiers have been made to carry shop items belonging to their commanding officers in the so-called mobile canteens." The report continues, "This has in turn forced soldiers to desert so that their commanding officers and paymasters had access to their salaries. Such practices no doubt severely undermined the capacity of the army to deal with LRA.
One female soldier from the 5th Division, 87th battalion who was summoned by the committee as a witness, narrated the ordeal of her battalion. Her story indicates the dire conditions in which such profound corruption had left the UPDF. In December 2002, her battalion was attacked regularly by LRA.
Members of the battalion knew the attacks were commanded by Vincent Otti, the LRA number-two commander. UPDF had only one platoon (a fighting force of 20), and they were badly outnumbered by the rebels.
When they asked for help, a company (a force of 50) was sent to reinforce them. But the company had no communication devices and fell into an LRA ambush, where they lost nine of their comrades. The soldiers ran out of bullets. They called for a helicopter to come to their aid, but none was sent. The bodies of the nine dead were retrieved after a week, when they were badly decomposed. This almost caused a riot within the unit, and indeed caused desertions.
Given such scenarios, it was obvious to Bigombe that there was never going to be a military victory. It was obvious that a force of 250 could never do a job that should be done by 736 officers and men. She was encouraged by some senior officers to initiate peace talks with the rebels because they too believed that the war was not going to be won militarily.
In 1992, Division Commander Colonel Wasswa confided in her furthermore that the soldiers were tired of fighting. She in turn told him that it had been her long-time desire to persuade President Museveni to accept a peaceful settlement to the conflict through dialogue. Colonel Wasswa promised to support her if his advice was sought. The confession that NRA was tired of fighting was a dramatic change from the military propaganda that had gone on, but it did not surprise her. Some of the senior officers had fought alongside President Museveni when he waged the guerilla war that brought him into power in 1986.
After Museveni ascended to power, they had hoped to settle down and get married (most of those officers joined the NRA fighting forces when they were young men and not yet married). But when war broke out in north and northeastern Uganda shortly after Museveni came to power, these officers were forced to return to the bush and to put their plans on hold.
Even with the findings of the various investigations into corruption, Major General Kazini continued to misinform President Museveni that UPDF was on the verge of wiping out LRA, misinformed the president about the strength of the rebels and the number of weapons they still had, and grossly inflated the numbers of LRA fighters killed in battle. Sometimes, among the dead LRA fighters were abducted children, but all were termed LRA. Kazini persecuted those officers who did not condone his corrupt practices.
It is still a puzzle to Bigombe why the president believed all these stories and sometimes went ahead to declare publicly dates when the war would end. This he did not once or twice but many times. Sometimes he announced the names of LRA commanders who had been killed, only to realise later that the commanders were still alive. If you go by the numbers of announcements of the death of LRA commanders, then Vincent Otti was "killed" five times, Raska Lukwiya was "killed" three times, Joseph Kony had very many "narrow escapes and lost his tent and walking stick," and so on and so forth.
PRESIDENT MUSEVENI IS A smart man, by all accounts; so why did he allow these senior officers to fool him so many times over so long a time? He had the means to cross-check all this information, so why did he not do so? What was also strange was that whenever she tried to refute the information he was given, he never believed Bigombe.
He would always say, "But Shefe Ali, or Kazini, has just told me ?" It was very frustrating for Bigombe.
At the same time, it was generally believed that she was close to President Museveni and enjoyed his trust. There were a number of times that she felt snubbed. She would read in the newspapers, for example, that Prime Minister Eriya Kategaya or Foreign Minister Amama Mbabazi had been dispatched to country A or B to discuss the LRA issue. She was never informed, nor was she consulted. She has never bought the story that the president wanted the war to continue to keep him in power, or that he was benefiting from the war, or that he wanted to exact revenge on the Acholi people.
Thus, she made up her mind to persuade President Museveni to pursue a negotiated settlement. Her immediate challenge was how to make contact with the LRA to seek an end to the war. And she began to reflect on what a peace agenda would be like when the leadership's demand was to rule the country by the Ten Commandments.

Original_Search_Title: REES Building National Capacity in Cassava Commercialisation
Headline: Zambia: REES Building National Capacity in Cassava Commercialisation
Body_Text:
Ndola — CASSAVA has come to be appreciated in recent times as an effective food security crop and a viable alternative to maize, especially in times of drought.
The crop has been grown by most small-scale farmers, especially in the central and northern parts of the country, for domestic consumption and little was known about its commercial viability.
With numerous strides aimed at making agriculture a tool to fight poverty, the Government through its vision of enhanced household and national food security has continued to seek the support of cooperating partners in building national food capacity even in the area of alternative food sources.
One organisation that is helping Zambia to realise and utilise its development potential in rural areas is the Rural Economic Expansion Services (REES), which has provided management and training expertise to comprehensive agriculture programmes such as the Agriculture Support Programme (ASP).
The organisation has been actively involved in the promotion of cassava through the Cassava Intervention Programme, implemented through the Small Holder Enterprises Management Programme (SHEMP).
REES provides competent training and technical human resource capacity through its pool of consultants.
Cassava interventions have come in the areas of production, processing and marketing.
Serenje District was selected to implement the cassava intervention project because it accounts for up to 87 per cent of the Cassava produced in Central Province and also for its proximity to Lusaka and Copperbelt markets.
According to SHEMP regional business facilitator, Godfrey Munyoro, cassava trials were done to validate certain qualities using a value chain analysis in terms of nutritional value and it was discovered that cassava has up to three per cent protein content, hence the National Cassava Acceleration Task Force has now adopted and included this analysis.
This was an important finding because the country has been conducting research on the efficacy of using cassava as an alternative to maize in poultry feed.
The percentage of protein being one of the crucial nutritional elements in stock feed was very important.
As REES lead technical services consultant to the Cassava Intervention Programme, Brighton Mulonga observes that human beings and animals compete for maize, hence the use of cassava will reduce costs of production as it is more costly to use maize for poultry feed than cassava.
In times of drought or food deficit, preference is given to people and not animals, thereby causing stock feed industry to suffer.
To attest to the good quality of cassava in Serenje, Tiger Animal Feed - a commercial stock feed producer - had asked for five tonnes of cassava for poultry feed trials.
REES provided training to cassava farmers in Serenje.
The first training covered group development skills to enable the farmers to bulk the produce before selling.
The bulking process is important because it helps farmers access bigger markets and enjoy significant economies of scale, that is to say the costs of processing and moving cassava to the market reduces per unit.
Further, the farmers were taught how to harmonise the processing techniques to get the longest shelf life for cassava.
Jackson Chomba, a farmer in the Katikulula Resettlement Scheme in Chief Muchinka's area in Serenje, benefited from the training and is now able to harvest up to 100 x 50kg bags of cassava per hectare, which represents a significantly better output than maize which only amounts up to between 50 and 60 bags.
"In the training, we were taught how to use improved varieties of cassava to improve our output coupled with other techniques. Now that I know there is a ready market for cassava, I am increasing the number of hectares and so are many other farmers.
"The other advantage is that with cassava, we do not need to buy inputs such as fertilisers and hybrid seed as is the case with maize. In fact, this is a good way of moving away from depending on Government as most maize farmers are doing through the Fertiliser Support Programme.
"Because we grow Cassava at lower cost, we are even able to buy maize from other farmers with the extra cash. Cassava has turned out to be more profitable to grow than maize," Mr Chomba said.
The 65-year-old, Mr Chomba has six children and is the team leader for a group of 35 cassava farmers who are determined to increase their output in view of the increased demand for cassava in the Democratic Republic of Congo.
In the second phase, REES provided training to farmers in business development to enable them grow the crop for profit and not just for food security.
The traders have a bias towards crops they considered as having a ready market such as beans, maize and sweet potatoes and cassava often suffered as traders did not consider it a cash crop, hence REES ensured that the traders were also trained and shown market opportunities that exist for cassava.
The market opportunities for cassava are essentially in the Democratic Republic of Congo, especially during the rain season when prices shoot up from an average K45,000 to K80,000 per 50kg bag of cassava. In the feed mill industry and emerging markets such as Luapula where cassava trades well with fish which is later sold at a higher price.
The availability of this information to the traders who also attended market and trade training in cassava commercialisation has raised their interest and enthusiasm to deal in the commodity.
Cashwell Chisenga, a trader in Serenje, also attended the training and is now not only buying cassava but has acquired land to grow it as well.
"We were trained how to run our businesses and now I am even able to write a business plan.
"After attending the exposure visit to Kasumbalesa, we saw the high demand for cassava in the Democratic Republic of Congo.
"With the demand coming through from the feed industry, we know we have an assured market," Mr Chisenga said.
Mr Chisenga appealed to the Government to work on the poor road infrastructure so that traders like him could convince transporters to buy from farmers in remote areas of Serenje.
REES business development manager, Christopher Mpolomoka said the organisation would not relent in ensuring that the best personnel and expertise were availed to ensure that the national cassava commercialisation process was sustained as a huge success as the results generated so far were very encouraging.
He expressed delight that the farmers now had hands on experience in modern and efficient cassava production, processing and marketing techniques and were now managing to conduct business on their own, find and maintain markets.
Perhaps, a classic example of a farmer for whom this is true is Golden Kalunga who is on the rampage, buying cassava fields from fellow farmers because the quantities of cassava he is trading are outstripping his initial supply.
Mr Kalunga has found ready market for cassava in Luapula where he exchanges chipped cassava with fish and when he sells his fish, he buys maize and processes some of it for exchange for more fish within the province.
He is adding value to his products and earning even more in the process and the bottom line is cassava.
In order for cassava to be grown as an effective cash crop, the manner it is produced needs to improve and the farmers were advised to plant their cassava stems in a vertical manner and not a slanting manner as was done previously coupled with the use of improved varieties.
Mr Munyoro, the SHEMP coordinator, said another crucial set of training that was facilitated through REES was training in different processing techniques, as different markets require different skills in order to ensure low cynaid levels, as cynaid is the chemical that makes cassava bitter.
He explained that chipping cassava and drying it enables it to get rid of all the cynaid and can last for up to nine months without going bad.
Now that the market for cassava has been identified, one major challenge that the farmers are faced with is limited supply of planting materials of good quality that do not take too long to mature.
The Programme Against Malnutrition (PAM) had a national project where cassava planting materials were distributed so that farmers could plant it as a drought resistant crop, but it has since come to an end.
The farmers are now appealing to PAM to revive the project now that they have the necessary skills to grow cassava on large scale.
The efforts and initiatives coming from organisations like REES need to be supported as they are helping rural communities to facilitate their own development.

Original_Search_Title: Floods Force Displaced Back Into Camps
Headline: Uganda: Floods Force IDPs Back Into Camps
Body_Text:
Kampala — THOUSANDS of former internally displaced people (IDPs) in Lango sub-region fled back to the camps over the weekend after their homes were destroyed by floods.
The worst affected areas in Lira are Otuke, Moroto and Erute North counties. Parts of Dokolo, Amolatar and Oyam districts, too, have been hit badly.
A total of 20 bridges have been swept away by the water, including Amonmaka Bridge which connects Lira to Kotido district, with fleeing people using dug-out canoes to cross the flooded roads.
In Teso, 25 IDP camps - a total of 15,000 people - need to be relocated to dry land or uphill areas as disaster preparedness minister Musa Ecweru warns of cholera and malaria outbreaks in the flooded camps. Bad roads are hampering the delivery of relief items to the affected areas in Teso. Sub-counties like Magoro and Ngariam have been cut off due to flooded roads.
Agriculture experts predict that the East and the North will face food shortages for the next six months as crops like cassava and groundnuts are rotting in the water-logged fields and sorghum did not mature.
"We are threatened with a serious disaster and I hope the Government and the international community do something to save the situation," lands minister Omara Atubo said.
Poor harvest and delayed rains have also caused severe food shortage in the Karamoja region, with half a million people facing starvation, according to the latest report of Unicef.
"The sub-region in 2007 has faced severe food insecurity crises due to a poor harvest last year and the late onset of rains, leading to more than half the population, 500,000 out of 900,000, reliant on external humanitarian assistance," the UN agency stated in its August humanitarian situational report.

Original_Search_Title: 'North and East May Face Food Shortage'
Headline: Uganda: 'North and East May Face Food Shortage'
Body_Text:
Kampala — AGRICULTURE and weather experts have warned that the eastern and northern parts of Uganda, particularly Teso are likely to be hit by food shortage for the next six months.
"There is going to be a disaster because there will be no food for the four districts of Soroti, Amuria, Kumi and Katakwi," said Anuciata Hakuza, an official from the agriculture ministry.
Hakuza was discussing the implications of the weather forecast released at a press conference last Friday in Kampala.
"With the current floods and water-logged areas, root crops like cassava and groundnuts are rotting. It has not been possible to harvest crops like sorghum because the heavy rains made it difficult for such crops to mature early."
Hakuza said the rain came too soon in Eastern Uganda, when the farmers were not expecting it, making it difficult to prepare for planting. The rains will stop at different times in different places, according to weather experts.
In the East the rains are expected to stop in November, three to four weeks earlier than usual.
The catchment areas of Lake Kyoga, especially the low land areas, should expect more floods because the water table has risen.
Western Uganda is likely to have the most favourable weather conditions for food production according to agricultural experts. They advised farmers to plant fast-maturing crops.
Abushen Majugu, a consultant on weather issues said: "The rain is going to be higher than average and will continue until December in western Uganda."
Majugu said last year, there was El Nino, which was characterised by heavy rains. This has influenced the current weather patterns, that have seen the drier conditions of July and August become a rainy season.
Stephen Magezi, the acting commissioner in the meteorology department, said weather information and communication would soon improve as they expect an expert from the UK to enhance the dissemination of weather data.

Original_Search_Title: Coffee Producers Are the Biggest Losers
Headline: Uganda: Coffee Producers Are the Biggest Losers
Body_Text:
Kampala — Coffee producers in Uganda suffer from an unfair trade relationship with Europe, even though their beans produce some of the best quality coffee in the world, says the Ugandan coffee industry's governing body.
"The biggest loser is the person directly involved in coffee bean production," says Henry Ngabirano, director of the Uganda Coffee Development Authority. Moreover, Ugandan producers get an "unequal share of the revenue generated by coffee beans".
Combined, Ugandan growers and exporters receive 6 percent of the finished product's price, according to the Uganda Coffee Development Authority. "This shows what level of exploitation there is," Ngabirano says.
Coffee beans are Uganda's top cash crop and their production accounts for nearly half of the nation's economy. The country has become a leading Robusta producer since the political crisis erupted in Ivory Coast, a former top producer. Exporters said their earnings grew 65 percent to 17,9 million dollars in May this year, compared to last year.
This growth is due to higher prices and consumers in the North and in Asia buying higher-grade coffees. African countries have an advantage of high altitudes and smaller-scale farms, along with a natural abundance of organic farming that has made their coffee beans more attractive on the global market.
According to East African Fine Coffees Association director Philip Gitao, the demand for African coffee beans is at an all-time high. He says that African coffee was increasingly being used to upgrade traditional blends and to be marketed on their own as specialty brands.
The 1990s in Uganda saw a government policy shift on coffee production. The market was liberalized and hurdles in the way of coffee producers removed. The Ugandan shilling became fully convertible with any other currency and price controls were removed.
"The government saw coffee as having a role in poverty eradication," Ngabirano says. The number of coffee farmers surged during this time as "Ugandans began to see the business potential of coffee".
But farmers face a number of challenges, involving the unavailability of bank loans or government subsidies to jumpstart their farming. "What we are lacking as Ugandan farmers, is support," says Ronald Buule, a coffee bean farmer in central Uganda.
Although farmers form local associations to negotiate fairer prices for their raw beans from intermediaries, the profits they earn are still not enough to ensure that supply stores remain stocked and that they have sufficient tools, Buule says.
"Farmers work a lot but generate little money," Buule claims. Many coffee farmers are forced to heavily diversify their farms, growing other crops such as cocoa, cassava, bananas, oranges and vanilla and even raising poultry.
"It is a shame, really" that the retail coffee brands are not owned by Ugandan companies, though the raw material is bought from the country, Ngabirano says. Ugandan President Yoweri Museveni has said his country will only develop when it makes its own finished products instead of just selling raw materials.
The pending economic partnership agreement (EPA) between the European Union (EU) and developing countries will further exploit the producers, Ngabirano says. The EPAs require that the 77 African, Pacific and Caribbean countries offer reciprocal market access to their EU trade partners.
"The EPAs would have been better if European countries removed the subsidies on their domestic coffee industries," Ngabirano says, adding that since Uganda is a low-cost producer, "there would be no competition". He adds, "if the EU is really streamlining trade, they should address the issues of subsidies."
Uganda began organic coffee bean farming ten years ago, and now over 2,000 tons of organic coffee beans are produced a year. "We produce organic coffee in five regions of Uganda, two Arabica and three Robusta," Ngabirano tells IPS, referring to the different types of bean.
Farmers are certified by various European boards responsible for ensuring organic quality. "It is a protracted story," Ngabirano says of the organic certification process. Farmers should not have used chemicals on their plots of land for three years in order to move from conventional to organic production, a period that is called the transitional phase.
They are taught to avoid chemicals at all costs and how to properly substitute regular with organic manure. Outside analysts inspect the land after the three-year period to guarantee that all inorganic materials have degraded. But, according to Ngabirano, "in most cases, such inorganic materials have never been there, so we lose time (by being forced to go through the transitional phase)."
Inorganic fertilizers and chemicals are either unavailable or too expensive in many farming regions. As a result, out of one million coffee farmers, most are "organic by default", though not certified, Ngabirano says. Some 15,000 farmers are officially certified as organic producers.
But Ugandan coffee experts debate whether organic coffee bean farming is actually profitable. There is a 20 to 30 percent premium on top of the normal price, but farmers suffer a considerable loss in yield by not using chemicals and fertilizers on their crops. "We have not been able to establish as a fact which is better," Ngabirano said.
To complicate matters, Uganda is facing potentially harmful non-tariff barriers from the EU. Some European conservationists are saying that transporting products by air to sell in other countries increases pollution and is therefore bad for the environment.
Such talk could foreclose Uganda ever moving on to producing its own finished coffee products and sending them abroad to be sold.

Original_Search_Title: Floods to Get Worse
Headline: Uganda: Floods to Get Worse
Body_Text:
Kampala — For thousands of people in eastern Uganda, life has become a nightmare as they face Uganda's worst natural disaster so far this year.
Walter Okiring Elakas, a resident of Magoro in Katakwi district, is a sad man indeed.
Only a few months ago, he returned home after spending several years in refuge, having been displaced by the Lord's Resistance Army insurgency and Karimojong cattle rustlers.
And now the first crops he planted after returning home, with support from the Government's IDP resettlement programme, have been swallowed by floods just as they were about to mature.
Elakas' homestead, too, has been flooded to the extent that he now needs a canoe to reach it.
"My five acres of crops have been under water for more than a month and they have rotted," he cries. "I got a loan and took advantage of the resettlement programme to engage in intensive farming. All is lost now and I don't know how I'm going to repay the loan."
Like Elakas, several thousand people have been displaced by the floods since last month, while the majority are still stuck in the floods with nowhere to go, says Robert Ekongot, the LC5 chairman for Katakwi district.
Ekongot says that although no deaths in floods have been reported, the economic damage is grave and the flooding is likely to cause epidemics.
He regrets that the region lacks the capacity to promptly respond to emergencies because of the many insurgencies it experienced.
Ekongot describes the situation as catastrophic and horrible. He says in Magoro and Ngariam counties, seven out nine sub-counties are severely affected.
"The water levels are rising everyday. We have lost all the food for the last harvest and we can't plant for this season unless the government and other well wishers join us and provide cassava and potato stems," he says.
He points out that in Oongora camp, 3,000 people have been displaced, 800 people in Oolir camp and 1,200 people in Kipnyang camp are homeless.
The latrines in these camps are full and the residents have nowhere to help themselves.
"As I talk to you now, I'm with a mother who delivered last evening but she has nowhere to rest the baby because the whole place is flooded. I have 3,000 people to transfer today (Wednesday) but I have no means. I have no tents for shelter nor do I have food or transport for them," he said on phone.
According to the Minister of State for Disaster Preparedness, Musa Ecweru, a total of 300,000 people have been affected; 200,000 from parts of Teso and 100,000 from Bududa in Pallisa, Aloi and Omoro sub-counties in Lira district in the last few weeks.
Thomas Okoth Nyalulu, the Resident District Commissioner for Katakwi and Amuria districts, describes the torrential rains hitting the region,as the worst since 1972.
It rains day and night. The rivers and swamps are swollen. "We are in a dilemma," says Nyalulu.
As if that is not bad enough, the floods are threatening to cut off the Soroti-Mbale highway. Water has already started crossing the tarmac at the Awoja Bridge, eight kilometres from Soroti town.
Occasionally, unsuspecting motorists approaching from Kumi and Soroti are thrown into panic by the flow. Awoja Bridge lies at a bend.
In addition, the United Nations Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Assistance (OCHA) estimates that 4,000 households have been damaged.
The agency warns of the possible outbreak of diseases like cholera, skin diseases, respiratory infections and malaria.
In Amuria, most schools might not open when the new term begins next week, because of floods.
The district's LC5 chairperson, Julius Ocen, says floods have dug up faeces from pit latrines and spread them into the school compound.
He describes the floods as the worst he has ever seen in his life. "We are helpless. local leaders and our people are desperate."
Local governments are trying out desperate strategies to handle the disaster without adequate resources. In Soroti, for instance, schools are being requested to host families displaced by the floods.
Unfortunately, some of the schools have cracks and might collapse, says Ekongot, the district's chairman.
He adds that the affected community need warm clothes, temporary shelter and emergency measures to clean up the water sources to prevent out break of diseases.
"We have resolved to meet daily to devise ways of managing the situation. We are also making adjustments to our district budget to accommodate the urgent need to re-open all the blocked roads ," Ekongot says. They are also sensitising the locals to participate in managing the floods and to be aware of the catastrophe.
They have also urged them to try to relocate to safer places and not wait for the local leaders to help them because by the time they come in, it might be too late to saving their lives.
Ekongot appeals to President Yoweri Museveni and the donor community to see how best to save the lives of those affected.
In Amuria, the LC5 chairman, Julius Ocen, wants to divert money from other sectors such as health and agriculture, to support flood victims, but his hands are tied.
"Local government regulation does not allow lower local governments to divert funds already disbursed to the district for specific activities unless once again approved by the ministry," he says.
In Soroti, the district local council has diverted its meagre resources to feed 1,000 displaced people. Moses Edonu, the deputy LC5 chairman for Soroti, says the most affected sub-counties are Katine, Kamuda, Tubur, Arapai, Bugondo, Atiira and Gweri, where most residents have fled their homes.
Ecweru led a team that is taking emergency relief for 3,000 displaced persons.
He, however, does not have enough supplies to cater for the victims. "I'm expecting food, drugs and tents from humanitarian agencies that have responded to the problem," says Ecweru.
This, from the main man in charge of disaster response, implies that those who are displaced by the floods will continue to lack support for a while until aid agencies come in or Government diverts resources from other sectors.
As pointed out by RDC Nyalulu, the Teso floods are an eye opener, that the national mechanism for responding to disaster is not adequate.

Original_Search_Title: Floods Displace Thousands in East
Headline: Uganda: Floods Cut Off Teso
Body_Text:
Soroti — PERSISTENT floods have swept away bridges in eastern Uganda, displaced thousands of people and cut off Teso region from its northern neighbour Karamoja.
More than 100,000 people have been displaced from the districts of Amuria, Soroti, Kumi and Katakwi with five deaths reported so far.
As a short term measure, all road traffic to Amuria and Katakwi district headquarters has been diverted through Wera town.
The situation is further compounded by claims that the government is short on funding to manage emergencies.
It is the second time in two months that the region is flooding. The first incident was in August.
All roads have been rendered inaccessible making it more difficult to deliver even the little relief supplies secured from charities and the government.
"I have left my permanent secretary trying to patch up here and there. We do not have much money set aside for emergencies. We are trying to coordinate with humanitarian agencies like World Food Programme, World Health Organisation to help," Mr Musa Ecweru, the State minister for Disaster Preparedness, said yesterday.
"I urgently need food. I need medicines- especially anti-malarials. I need tarpaulins," Mr Ecweru said. "But if you asked me to name these needs in order of priority, I would say tarpaulins are the biggest priority followed by food and then medicines."
Mr Ecweru arrived in the region after the situation got out of hand.
STUCK: Residents try to lift a car that got stuck on one of the roads. Photo by Richard Otim
"The situation in Oogora, Katakwi District, has escalated to emergency levels. It has been raining consistently for over 48 hours. The entire place is flooded," he said yesterday.
Dire situation
Heavy flooding has particularly ravaged the sub-counties of Koliri and Malera in Bukedea, Omino in Kumi, Gweri and Kamuda in Soroti and the entire districts of Amuria and Katakwi. Mr Ecweru said in Abarilela Sub County, 100 people have been displaced.
In Katakwi, the sub counties of Magoro, Ngariam, Usuku and Ongongoja are the most hit. People in this area had just returned from camps where they had sought refuge from raids by the rebel Lords' Resistance Army.
The residents have taken refuge in schools, health centres and administrative centres.
In just one week, according to the LC5 chairperson of Amuria, Mr Julius Ochen, over 120 huts hav fallen. "What we are thinking of now is how to evacuate the 1,658 people," Mr Ochen said.
He said the residential huts of teachers at Amaseniko Primary School had also caved in.
"Where do we take these (affected) people? The need to seek for where to settle these families is very urgent," Mr Ochen said.
In Kapelebyong main IDP camp in Amuria District, sanitation has been made worse after a number of communal pit latrines sunk. This has created a high fear of a disease outbreak.
Despair
The flood victims are disappointed that the government's intervention has been slow. "It is absurd that the government is not coming in swiftly to tackle the disaster. We need more guidance and education on weather forecast and health risks related to such a disaster," Mr Ochen said.
Most of the people in IDP camps in Katakwi and Amuria have abandoned sleeping at night.
"In some huts, people literally sit all night. In Ocwiin, Obule-Ajet, for example, it is very difficult to access medical services that are far away from the camps," Katakwi chief administrative officer Nicholas Ochakara said.
Meanwhile, the district LC5 secretary for education, Mr Musa Aga Eceru said most schools in the district may not open in time for next term due to the rains.
"Schools are due to open next week (September 17). It means schools in Amuria will not open because the latrines and teachers' houses have collapsed," he said. He said the district is faced with a sharp retardation in health and education services.
Production of cassava, the main staple food in Teso has also been affected with much of the crop rotting in the waterlogged gardens.
Villages of Angole, Sugur and Amollo in Wera Sub-county, Amuria District are among the worst hit.
"There is need for items like tarpaulin to help the farmers dry the little that they have been able to save from the gardens because the grounds are perpetually damp," Wera LC5 chairperson Robert Adiama Ekaju said.
Aid trickles in
Ms Rose Nakabugo, the principal disaster management officer, said the Lutheran World Fellowship has offered blankets and mosquito nets to the victims. PSI Uganda and Red Cross have also helped, she said.
"We cannot deal with this problem alone, we need partners," Ms Nakabugo said.
She, however, said the situation had not yet been upgraded to an emergency one.
"An area can only be declared an emergency area if the administrative structures have stopped to function. But in this case, the administrative areas are still able to function," she said.

Original_Search_Title: UNBS Suspends Sale of Omo in Mbarara
Headline: Uganda: UNBS Suspends Sale of Omo in Mbarara
Body_Text:
Mbarara — UGANDA National Bureau of Standards and police are investigating a case in which fake Omo was manufactured and distributed in Mbarara markets.
UNBS has suspended the sale of the suspected adulterated 20gms Omo sachet in Mbarara until investigations are complete.
This follows an incident in which Police, last week, impounded 20 bags of Omo detergent alleged to be counterfeit from Raheel Traders Ltd on Buremba Road in Mbarara town.
A trader Mr Milton Kusasira had complained to UNBS that the Omo he bought on August 14 from Raheel, which he stocked in his wholesale shop was fake.
Later the proprietor of Raheel, Mr Savani Riyakat, and Unilever Sales Representative, Paul Waswa were arrested on August 27 in connection with the fake detergent and later released on bond.
Mr Kusasaira said his customers complained that the Omo was like cassava flour and could not form lather. On returning it to Raheel, Mr Kusasira says Mr Riyakat rejected it and referred him to Unilever offices in Kampala. I decided to go to police because they did not help me yet I have been their customer for six years," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Niger State National Domestic Trade Fair Comes With a New Face
Headline: Niger State National Domestic Trade Fair Comes With a New Face
Body_Text:
The 5th Niger State National Domestic Trade Fair organized by The Niger State Ministry of Commerce, Industry, Tourism and Cooperatives in conjunction with The Niger Chambers of Commerce, Industry, Mine and Agriculture (NCCIM) ended on a clean state.
The National Domestic Trade Fair is package as a platform to attract, encourage and support private investors to come and take the advantages of the condusive atmosphere of peace and abundant investment potentials in Niger State. The Theme "Building the National economy through Public and Private Partnership" popularly referred to as "PPP" is a step to reposition the State into the three most developed States in the Nation.
The State potentials that was exhibited at the 5th National Trade Fair includes the diverse mineral resources, the largest agricultural farm land in the Nation, the craft of the people and the tourists centers not forgetting the strategic location of the State.
The diverse mineral resources of the State are Gold, Iron, Clay, Silica, Kainite and Marble, Feldspar, Lead, Columbine and Tantalite are available for potentials industrialist. Agriculture indeed forms the bedrock of the economic life of the people specialized in cash crops farming like Cassava, Tomato, Yam, Rice etc.
The Power State of the Nation has the largest landmarks in Nigeria. The tourist potentials include Zuma Rock located along Abuja - Kaduna express road with a human face look inform of engraving representing the eye socket, the nose and the mouth on the side of the rock that face Abuja road. Other tourist centers like the Bida Glass work in Masaga village in Bida, the Gurara water falls located along Minna -- Suleja road to mention a few.
Culturally rich in Crafts souvenirs in form of Pots, Tea sets, flower vases etc. The DR. Ladi Kwali Pottery (a native potters) Center at Suleja town established in 1951 by late Michael Carder a British partner of international repute, also the Tatiko Craft Center located in Paikoro local government area of the State produces Pot jars, local buckets, Safes, Cooking pots.

Original_Search_Title: Mukono Farmers Blame Poverty on Low Vanilla Prices
Headline: Uganda: Mukono Farmers Blame Poverty on Low Vanilla Prices
Body_Text:
Kampala — GRIEF-STRICKEN, Hannington Luzze spent the weekend in the blazing sun, clearing his one-acre garden of vanilla and the bushes.
Like hundreds of peasants in the village of Mairikiti in Mukono, 33 miles south-east of Kampala, Luzze plans to replace the vanilla plants with other crops like potatoes and cassava.
"Everyone in this village has lost the initial great love for vanilla. The prices which were high are now too low that it is like donating the vanilla beans to the middlemen," the 42-year old said in an interview.
Vanilla prices suddenly shot up in 2003 with the price of a kilogramme at sh100,000 from sh35,000. The long-suffering peasants became rich over night.
Luzze who had never bought a bicycle bought a second-hand Carina vehicle and renovated his house.
People cut down their coffee trees and replaced them with vanilla. However, the prices suddenly went down and now, a kilogramme of vanilla costs between sh1,500 and sh2,000. The peasants are now reeling with poverty.
"I sold vanilla at between sh80,000 and sh100,000 a kilogramme and I bought whatever I wanted including a car. We started getting low prices from 2004.
When the prices fell, I was left with a huge debt burden. I had borrowed money to finish my projects. All these were unfinished on top of being hunted day and night to service the debt," Luzze said.
"I am now doing shoddy work like digging in other people's gardens and making bricks. I cannot even have sh20,000 in my pocket. You can imagine that at one time, there was a season when there was no single buyer for vanilla," he said.
The boom, however, lasted for a short time, leaving Ugandan peasants like Matia Ndabwalukanyi crying.
"I was a known coffee and cocoa framer but I cut all those crops and planted vanilla because the vanilla prices were very, very high. The money I got helped me pay tuition for my offspring. All that is gone and I do not even have the morale to weed the vanilla plants," Ndabwalukanyi (75), a retired teacher, said.
The U.S. is the largest importer of vanilla.

Original_Search_Title: Cocainemania in Tema
Headline: Ghana: Cocainemania in Tema
Body_Text:
The substance that was hidden in18 slabs in the shape of a Bible and seized by the Tema police command recently, have now turned out to be what is known in the Ghanaian parlance as Kokonte (cassava flour) after it was sent to the Ghana Standard Board for analytical report.
The police are of the view that the substances were a scam, which was being executed by some criminals to dupe some people on the pretext of selling cocaine to them.
DSP Samuel Kwesi Ofori, acting Director of Police Public Relations told The Chronicle in an interview that "whatever way one sees it, but from both ends they are all criminal acts."
He explained that both the suspected criminals and the people who were suspected to have been victims of the fraudulent acts when identified by the police would be arrested since they were all illegal.
He continued that on August 28,the Tema Regional Command together with the Organized Crime Unit of the Criminal Investigations Department (CID) forwarded the stuff for analytical examination however; it proved to be corn flour and corn dough DSP Ofori said the Astra vehicle in which the suspected cocaine was concealed is not registered emphasizing that the registration number it bore was fake.
"The chassis number when checked with the Customs Excise and Preventive Service (CEPS) indicated that the car was not registered.
He intimated that all efforts to get the informant in the case to disclose his identity has proved futile while the police have not been able to arrest any of the suspects yet. He called on the members of the public who have information on the transaction to release it to the police
It would be recalled that on August 24 The Tema police located a vehicle around the Tema Port containing 18 slabs of substances contained in an Astra vehicle.
Speaking to members of the media, the Tema Regional Police Commander, Assistant Commissioner of Police (ACP) Kudalor said the Police had had a tip off that the said Opel Astra car was carrying some quantity of cocaine was heading towards the Tema Port and was expected to arrive to board one of the vessels.The Opel Astra car was subsequently found specifically at around the premises of the defunct Meridian Hotel, near the Harbour area.

Original_Search_Title: We Must Step on Toes to Govern - Suswam
Headline: Nigeria: We Must Step on Toes to Govern - Suswam
Body_Text:
Abuja — Three months after he assumed office, the Executive Governor of Benue state, Gabriel Suswam says he has spent over N2 billion in service provision to the people.
In all this he says good governance requires that certain toes are stepped upon to achieve something for the general good. He spoke with journalists in Makurdi on some of the projects, and his plans for the future. Daily Trust's Suleiman M. Bisalla and Joseph Hir were there. Excerpts:
Your Excellency, you are just three months old in office and precisely 100 days as governor, how has it been so far?
If I have a mandate of four years I do not see how I should be judged in 100 days. But it is a culture that people have caught up with, so every person strives to give an indication that he can move forward. Fundamentally what you will be doing in 100 days is to strategize and find a way forward by putting plans together, as to what you need to do in the years ahead. So nothing much is actually expected to be achieved in terms of developments on ground.
First, we have to go through the process of awarding contracts. That alone takes time if you go through the due process. It goes through the usual process of evaluation, tender and all that.
But then I assumed with a background that I don't need to give any excuses. I spent eight years in the National Assembly where I was on top of most of the major committees in the House. I chaired the House Services Committee in 1999 before moving to the Committee on FCT. After that I chaired the Appropriation Committee, and later the Power Committee. So I was prepared to step into this hot office. I knew exactly what I was coming to do.
What specific areas did you identify as those needing immediate attention when you came in?
I noticed that what I basically need to focus on was rural infrastructure. This involves creating access roads for our rural people who are basically farmers, so that they can move farm produce to where they can market them. But we discovered it would not be possible for us to do this during the rainy season. So I decided that we will start with the township.
I also introduced something new to the people here by building walkways. Apart from that, I'm trying to build some of the decayed infrastructure across the state. If you check the judiciary, most of the courts are in very bad state. We are trying to work on some of the courts.
We have also looked in the direction of health, and we are putting strategies together to bring health facilities nearer to the people in the village. It is not about building hospitals or the infrastructure in the hospital. It is about manpower. You might build one of the biggest, well equipped hospitals, but if you don't have the personnel to man it, it is nonsense. So we are looking at how we can encourage the personnel to move to the villages.
In this regard I'm looking at the Jigawa state model. Jigawa has a model that is also practiced in Ghana, you have a main hospital in the local government, and then you have something like satellite hospitals. That main one is well equipped and you have experts there. Then you have the satellite ones which are also well equipped, and then you motivate people to stay there. Most personnel are not ready to go into the villages because of the absence of social infrastructure. We are therefore trying to provide functional quarters and facilities to enable the personnel who include medical doctors, feel that they are not missing anything that they were used to in the township.
We are starting this on a pilot scheme. There are hospitals already built by the former governor, about seven of them in seven different local governments. They are not functional because there are no personnel. We want to make them functional by sending doctors there, and persuading social facilities like electricity, water and so on.
We are not looking at building new hospitals, but making existing ones functional.
In the area of education, we have discovered that the environment under which our children learn is quite deplorable. We have done a comprehensive assessment of classrooms; the quality of teachers and so on. I discovered we need a lot of improvement. We have been able to access money from the Universal Basic Education. We have put together about a billion plus, including our counterpart funding.
We are building modern classrooms. The design is already on ground and we are starting the construction in the next two weeks or so.
We are going to have a computer classroom attached to the main classrooms. We will also provide modern furniture and a computer attached to a classroom.
At the level of primary schools, we are doing a pilot scheme starting with a few of them. But most of the Junior Secondary Schools are going to benefit from the project. We will provide like five to ten computers in each of these places.
But since they are in rural areas, how do we power the systems? We have actually got some experts in solar energy. They are being evaluated at the moment. We are going to use solar energy to power the systems.
We will achieve two things with this approach; first we can stop rural urban migration of our youths who are seeking to acquire computer knowledge. If we educate our children at the primary and secondary levels with computer knowledge, they won't need to come to town to do this. They can have this in the villages, stay there and also engage in some form of agricultural activities.
In addition to this, I will be going to the United States in the next few days, where I have an appointment with Bill Gates in Washington, in a collaborative effort to enhance computer education in Benue sate.
We have already given a contract in collaboration with them to build about four computer centers. Once they build them, they will also provide free computers to us. They will also issue us a micro soft license that we can use in the universities for our students to do research on the WEB, and collaborate with other educational institutions elsewhere in the world.
So our students will have access without traveling to America or Britain. They will sit here in their library and access any information they need.
In terms of education therefore I am IT compliant. The seriousness I have in this regard is what is prompting my meeting with Bill Gates.
What are you doing about the water problem in Makurdi?
I was coming to this. Those who live here know that our taps are not flowing. Like Okonkwo said in the novel, he was living on the bank of a river and washing his hands with spittle. We cannot afford to do that. We have a river here that flows through out the year but the taps are not running.
What I did when I came in was to assess the problem. I therefore invited the Chinese who came and evaluated the problem, and said there was a solution. The contract has been awarded and they are mobilizing to site. We hope that in the next four months all taps in Makurdi will flow non stop.
Also, the former governor had started putting up a television station. I want to complete that. I have just finished paying the money for them to complete the project. We have finished the structures, and some of the equipment have started coming in.
They were also in the process of expanding the Radio Benue, so that it can serve the entire state. I have also finished payment for that project, so that our people can listen to the station even at the extreme end of the state.
We also have a road that was more or less like jinxed. I am talking of the road from Otukpo to Apa to Agatu. It's one of the worst roads we have here. I have also paid the contractors and mobilized them back to site, so that that road can be finished for the people there to have a sense of belonging like all other people of Benue state.
Benue is referred to as a 'Food Basket of the Nation', what plans have you for the reform of this sector?
We have an integrated approach to this. This includes creating rural accessibility. We are also working towards creating a community marketing board, where there will be competitive pricing of commodities. What we have now is a kind of monopoly. Most buyers of our farm produce for now are from the East, and when they decide to bring down prices they can do it, because there is no competition in the market. So I'm trying to create competition using the marketing board, where farmers will know that they at least have an option. They will know that no matter the quantity they produce at anytime, we will be there to take the farm produce from them. They now have a choice, whether to go to the open market or the parallel market, so to speak, and sell their produce.
Then we are going to resuscitate some of the agro based factories that have collapsed. We have a couple of them like the Taraku Mill which is moribund. We have the fertilizer blending plant; we have the juice factory which is not functioning too. We also have the Cassava Factory. We want to make them functional.
To do this we intend to privatize and commercialese most of them, so that they will be efficient. Outside creating employment, they will also be places where farmers can always sell their produce to earn money, and solve some of their social problems.
I have also invited some people to build an international market, the design of which is compliant with our peculiarities here. They were here and they have taken the assessment, and have brought the bill of quantities. You find people selling everywhere now, because the market built in 1979 has become so small due to population expansion. So in order to keep the town clean, people have to move to the market. We'll also gradually do the same for all the major towns in Benue.
One problem besieging the entire nation and particularly Benue is the shortage of electricity, are there any plans to address this?
This is very vital. The entire country is now working on 4000 mega watts. We have large deposits of coal in Benue that can last over a hundred years.So when I came in I engaged some consultants who have done comprehensive feasibility studies as to how we can generate electricity from that coal.
We know we will be able to generate 800 megawatts and we are collaborating with some South Africans investors who will be here in the next two weeks. You know that for us to put up all those industries that can be profitable, we need this electricity.
We also have a large deposit of limestone and there was a cement factory already started by the last administration. There is also one that is outstanding. But we need electricity to do these. We are also talking to some Chinese who have already started the one in Zone C. So the electricity we intend to generate is very paramount to whatever we want to do.
In all these can you put a figure to what you have done so far in the last three months?
So far I have spent over N2 billion on the projects that I have done.
Many elected officers are battling for their seats at the tribunal, but it's like you are free of this, how did you do it?
We contested an election that was not so acrimonious. So it was easy for me to reach out to my opponents. After the election I reached out to them early enough, because political parties are only platforms. The common thing that all contestants aspire to do is to serve the people. So if we contested and I won, I feel we shouldn't exacerbate the situation as it were, other than concentrate in bringing development to the people.
I was able to persuade them to reason with me, that all we need is development, and if it is development, I do not need to be distracted.
I therefore appealed to them to join hands with me, so that we can work towards impacting positively on the people. They believed in me, and all of them decided to withdraw their cases from court.
Do we understand that to mean something like financial settlement or settlement out of court?
No, not one bit. Like I said while contesting we were relating, and we were conscious of the fact that only one of us will be governor at any point. There was not much dispute during the election. It was obvious from day one that the person who won election was going to win, because it was clear on the streets, in the villages and everywhere. Even the trees could feel it. So when I won there wasn't much dispute. That is why it was easy for them to withdraw their cases without any financial inducement.
You are one of the few governors that did not say how much you met in the treasury ,or what liabilities there were. You have just told us what you have done with funds in a short period, were you comfortable with the treasury you met?
I have always said that it is absolutely difficult to take over government and say there is money there for you. It is practically impossible.
Budget is a speculative document. When you budget, you are speculating that in a year this is what you are going to get. It's not that money has accrued on the table for you. As you move on revenue comes in.
The internally generated revenue here is nothing to talk about. What we have is about 95 percent coming from the Federation Account. So it becomes difficult for any governor to leave anything in the circumstance.
In the first place you are speculating on the revenue to work with. So if you leave office, it is what accrued after that, which is available to the person taking over.
If we must do capital projects, this is a fact, there is no way we will not borrow money. There was no money when I came in, but I know how government is being run. Even at the federal level you borrow money. So there is no money that I met, but then I know that government must be run. That is why the financial institutions are there. They collaborate with government to move it forward.
Again, there is no governor that will say he is comfortable with the financial state he met. But like I said in the budget, there is no money on the table, it accrues every month.
But what was worrisome is the amount of indebtedness and commitments we met. Most of them were those that had been started. If projects had been started and there is commitment on it, you have to contain with that. Those commitments are there, but as for money I didn't meet any money.
It is alleged that your predecessor is working against the move to reconcile the differences between the Senate President, David Mark and his contender in the April polls. There have also been mixed reactions regarding your role in the matter. Can you explain where you stand?
I believe that as prominent Benue sons we should reconcile among ourselves. I do not see why if we have a Senate President, and he has a case in court, we shouldn't sit among ourselves and reconcile out of court.
I believe that is possible. I also believe it is feasible and should be done. I don't think the former governor is working against the reconciliation of that case. I believe it is in the realm of speculation. On my own part, I truly stand for reconciliation of the matter.
We have the Senate President who is number three man in the country, and we stand to benefit from that. So in order not to distract him, it is better we sit among ourselves and settle the matter.
You were in the National Assembly for eight years, what do you think your people will remember you for while you were there?
I think they will remember me more for standing up strongly against the third term agenda, and also defending them when they were invaded by soldiers. I moved a motion and a panel was set up to investigate the remote and immediate causes of the invasion. So aside attracting federal projects, I think these two issues stand out.
I also went as far as going to the United Nations Human Rights Watch in New York to draw the attention of the international community, to the genocide that was being committed at that time. My people will fondly remember me for that.
What would you say are the problems you have faced in the last three months?
They are enormous. If you step out here you will see problems everywhere, you can feel them. I'm trying in my small way to address them. Like I said there is total decay of infrastructure and the collapse of the rural economy. And any society where the rural economy had collapsed is doomed. Once this happens people begin to drift and engage in all sort of vices. We were gradually drifting towards that. So what I'm trying to do is to resuscitate the rural economy and build back some of the decayed infrastructure. This is a big problem. But people are impatient. Because of the enormity of the problems, anything you do is like a drop in the ocean.
One fundamental problem is from the local government. I have embarked on repositioning and refocusing the local government. Before now people had associated local government with sharing money. Local government and money sharing were synonymous.
When I came in I decided to take some bold steps and totally restructure that tier of government. The local governments are now properly positioned for us to move forward.
But you know that people who are used to certain ways of doing things in the local government, are very unhappy with me, because is no money for anyone to share now.
Can you elaborate on the restructuring?
Before now local government chairmen celebrated what they called joint account meeting, and the chairman would pack the entire money of the local government in his boot. That was open to a lot of fraud.
The first thing I did was to stop the chairmen from coming to the joint account meeting, which is a function of the treasurers. The treasurers now come to joint account meetings. They look at what is allocated, take the paper back, and sit down with the local government chairman. They know their cash flow and then come back and take the money go and pay.
Also there are certain positions that people feel that by right they should stay there. For instance, the cashier is a job for a level five officer, but you find a level 13 officer doing the job. All cases of wrong placement had to stop. We have also moved persons from where they refused to leave for many years. We have introduced a lot of other changes.
Did you have to step on some toes because you didn't bow to pressure from some quarters?
Obviously, some people are not happy especially regarding the appointments we have made. We are about four million in Benue state, and I have just about 13 commissioners, and the pressure I had from people was much, so definitely there are people who are not happy. Also some people are very unhappy with some of the changes I made at the local government level.
But in taking political decisions, if you must work in the overriding interest of the people ,you must step on toes. If you are to take decisions on matters that affect the larger population, some individuals will not be happy.
Why did you not make the declaration of your assets public?
Incidentally, I was one of the first that submitted their forms to the bureau. I submitted it before I was inaugurated. Once you submit your form to the code of conduct bureau, it is as good as public.
I have been in the National Assembly in the past eight years, so you would expect that everything that I have is in the public arena. There is nothing that I have that is not known by people. I don't think that's a problem. Any person who contests what I have, can go to the Code of Conduct Bureau and access it. But I know I was one of the very first governors to submit the form. I have nothing to hide. If you go for research I think they will give it to you.
There is nothing that I have that is hidden. I have just a few properties. I have a house in Abuja. The government graciously sold the one I was occupying in Apo. I have one house here, I have one in the village. I have a few cars and bank account.

Original_Search_Title: I Begged My Opponent to Withdraw Petition at Tribunal - Suswam
Headline: Nigeria: I Begged My Opponent to Withdraw Petition at Tribunal - Suswam
Body_Text:
Lagos — While most state governors spent their first 100 days in office defending their elections at the tribunals, Governor Gabriel Suswam of Benue State has no case to answer as his opponents have withdrawn their petitions against him.
So to mark his 100 days in office, he held an interactive session with some journalists. Ike Abonyi who attended the session presents excerpts of the interview.
What is your overview of the first 100days in office?
The idea of the first 100 days in office, is basically a creation of the media because I don't know why so much importance is attached to a hundred days. If I have the mandate of four years, I do not see how I should be judged by just a hundred days, which is three months. It is a hype that everybody has caught up with that really, if we can do this much in a hundred days, in four years, we 'll do a lot. So it is for us to access ourselves, what we are able to do or what we intend to do.
Fundamentally, in a hundred days, what you are doing basically is to strategize and find the way forward to put your plans together as to what you need to do in the years ahead. So, nothing much is actually expected to be achieved in terms of cogent development on ground for three months. People have to go through the process of awarding contracts for capital projects. But then, you expect to see an indication that this person can do something.
When I assumed office, I was coming from a background where I couldn't afford to give any excuse. I had spent eight years in the National Assembly.
