I had been on top of most of major committees in the House. I chaired the House Select Committee upon the inauguration of the National Assembly in 1999 and thereafter I moved to the (Federal Capital Territory) FCT (committee), and then Appropriation and the Power Committee. So, when I was prepared to step into this very hot office, I knew exactly what I needed to do when I assumed office and I noticed that for us in Benue, as a rural environment, what I needed to focus on was rural infrastructure, basically rural accessibility, creating roads so that our people who are farmers can be able to move their farm produce to where they can sell them, because these are the things we do here in Benue. That is my concentration.
But you know that we cannot construct rural roads during the rainy season. So, we are looking at township roads and have decided to start there. Most of the roads are not motorable and I thought if we must have a state capital that anybody would be proud of, then we must do something. So, we are starting with the resurfacing of the township roads. We are introducing something that is totally new to the people here who are building walkways.
I am also trying to rebuild some of the decaying infrastructure. If you go to the judiciary, most of their courts are in very bad state. We have also looked at the direction of health, strategies and plans to bring the health facilities nearer to the people in the villages.
It is not just about the hospitals but also about the personnel. The biggest, most equipped hospital without the personnel is nothing. So, what am trying to do is how I can motivate and encourage medical personnel to go to the villages. I am looking at the Jigawa model. You know they have a model that is also found in Ghana, where you have a main hospital in one of the local governments and then there are satellite hospitals. Most of these people are not ready to go to the villages because one, they are used to electricity, used to drinking cold water from refrigerators, using air conditioners, keep them in the village and they don't see any of these things. So, how do you expect them to go to these villages, provide functional quarters for the health personnel? if the personnel is a doctor, he should live in a kind of environment where he can watch (Cable Network News) CNN and he will not be missing anything.
We 'll start this on a pilot scheme. There are hospitals that were built by the former governor, about seven of them in seven different local governments. They are not functional because we don't have the personnel there. I intend to make some of them very functional; let the doctors have electricity even if it is a generator. Let them have what people who live in the towns have. So, I am not looking at building new hospitals but improving and making the ones that exist functional so that we can look into areas that need more facilities and then move into those areas.
In the area of education, the environment or classroom of our children both at the primary and secondary school level is deplorable. I have done a comprehensive analysis of the classrooms and the quality of the teachers and there's need for improvement.
What we want to do is to build model classrooms. We have been able to access money from (Universal Basic Education) UBE and also from our own quota. So, we have put together about a billion plus, and we are starting the constructions next week Saturday (August 8 ) where there is a computer room attached to the main classroom, the classrooms will have the modern sitting chairs and desks done in rubber (somewhere in Ogbomosho) but are strong. We provide like five or 10 computers initially in each of these places and because they are in the rural areas, how do you empower them? I have gotten some experts in solar energy who have evaluated it and we intend to use solar energy to power these computers. With approach, we intend to achieve two things; first, stop rural-urban migration among our youth in the quest for computer knowledge. If people can get computer education in the villages, their parents will prefer they stay there. Then they will now engage in farming and they will be happy to stay there because their children are not missing out in anything.
There is an addition to it. I will be going to America in a few days. While I was in the National Assembly, I collaborated with Microsoft and brought a computer village in Katsina, that has internet access. I have an appointment to meet with Bill Gates in Washington; we have already given a contract in collaboration with them, to build about four of them. What will happen is that once it is built, we will be provided with computers free, and issue the Microsoft licence which our children in the university can use to link up on the net, do research, and collaborate with other educational institutions abroad. They will be able to access whatever information they need to access from their libraries here. So for education, we want to make it (Information and Computer Technology) ICT compliant and we are doing that with Microsoft. Because of the seriousness which we 've attached to this, I will be in Washington on 7th or 8th September.
On social services like water, in Makurdi, we don't have flowing taps. It's like Okonkwo (an actor) said that he was living on the bank of a river and washing his hand with spittle. We cannot afford to do that; we have a river here that flows throughout the year. I have accessed what the problem is; I invited some Chinese. The Chinese evaluated the problem and said there's a solution. The contract has been awarded and we have also mobilized. We hope in the next four months, all taps in Makurdi will flow non-stop.
We are also going to resuscitate some of the agro-based industries that are already in existence here; we have Taraku, which is moribund; we have fertilizer plants; we have a juice factory which is not functioning. We have cassava chip factory which is functioning but we want to make them all very functional. To this end, we are going to privatize and commercialize most of them so that they will be efficient. Again, beyond creating employment, it will be a place where farmers can always take their produce, make some money and settle their social problems.
I also invited people who are going to build an international market of a size that will be compliant with our people here not necessarily sophisticated. They have taken the assessment, they 've brought the drawings, bills of quantity. The market that was built in 1980 has become so small given the expansion in terms of population and now you find people selling every where. Because I want to keep this town clean, I want every person to go to where they are supposed to be selling in the market. We 'll gradually do that in each of the major towns that we have here.
The former governor was trying to put up Benue State television station. The project has started and I will complete it. I just finished paying the money; the structures are finished and some of the equipment have started coming in. The former administration was also in the process of expanding Radio Benue to be able to serve the whole state. I will also complete it so that if you are in the extreme end of Benue, which is Agatu, you can listen to radio. We also had a road that was the link from Otukpo to Akpa and Agatu. It's one of the worst roads we have here; I have also been able to pay the contractors and mobilize them back to site so that the people in that area can be linked up and have a sense of belonging.
There are some details I've not given here, but in summary, these are some of the things we are doing. We are strategizing so that we can put them on a pedestal that we 'll be able to take hold with high speed.
I'm looking at the possibility of having an alternative source of power generation. One of the sources is coal. We have a large deposit of coal here that can run for over a hundred years. Some consultants have done feasibility studies as to how we can generate electricity from that coal. We may be able to generate about 800 mega watts. So, we are collaborating with some South Africans. I already took a trip there to meet with the firms that are financing this project and some investors who will be here in the next two weeks. When I came back, the South African ambassador to Nigeria visited me here for us to collaborate more on this issue; so we are working towards not only mining the coal but generating electricity.
And you know that for Benue to put these industries in a state that they can be profitable, we need electricity. We also have large deposits of limestone here. There is another cement factory that was started by the former governor and there is also another one that is outstanding whose feasibility studies have been carried out.
Your colleagues in other states have been battling cases against them at the tribunals, but you are out of it with the withdrawal of your challenger. How did you do it?
We contested an election that was not so acrimonious, and so it was easy for me to reach out to my opponents. After the election, I decided to reach out to them early enough because political parties are only platforms. The common thing that all contestants aspire to do is to serve the people. If I contested and I won the election, I feel that we shouldn't exaggerate the situation, but to concentrate to bring developments to the people. So, I was able to persuade them to reason with me that all we want is development and not distractions. There is no need for us to remain in the court over time, dissipating energy on the court process other than channeling it to developing the state. I also represent their interest since I am not a governor of (Peoples Democratic Party) PDP. So, I appealed to them to let us work towards impacting the people. They believed in me and all of them decided to withdraw their cases from court.
Was there any financial inducement?
There was none. Like I said, while we were contesting, we were relating and we were conscious that only one person would be governor at any point in time. And if it was somebody that they believe had an antecedent, had a track record of performance, there was no need for squabbles. The election was done in a manner that there was no much dispute. It was obvious from day one that the person who won the election was going to win; The information was on the streets, in the villages, even the trees could feel it. So when I won, there wasn't much dispute which made it easy for them to withdraw without any financial inducement.
You appear not to be doing the same thing in the case involving the Senate President, where the former governor is allegedly working against him at the tribunal.
We are doing something to settle amicably out of court because the state needs the position. I don't think that the former governor is working against him (Senate President). It's all in the realm of rumours because of what happened in the past; but I do hope that the matter will soon be settled.
You were quiet over money you met in the treasury when you came in. What is the situation?
It is absolutely very difficult for you to take over government and say that there was money in the treasury and I will tell you why. When you budget, you are speculating that in a year, this is what you are going to spend. It's not as if the money is on the table, except at the federal level where you have excess crude money. So as you move in, revenue comes in. In this case, you have a situation where we have federation account where most of the money that states spend come from. In Benue State, 95 per cent of the money is from there because the entire revenue generated here is nothing to talk about. So, it becomes difficult for anybody to leave any money because you are speculating as to your revenue to work with. So, if we leave office, it is what accrues after that which comes to the person who is taking over and then if we must do capital projects, there is no way you would not borrow money. There was no money when I came in, but I also know how government is being run. Even at the federal level, you borrow money. So, I needed to put the numbers together for me to start something, which I did. I wasn't comfortable, but there is no governor that will tell you he was comfortable with the financial situation of his state.
What was worrisome was the amount of indebtedness or commitments which most of them had contended with. It becomes obligatory for you who took over to pay the outstanding monies for projects. So I didn't meet any money.
Why did you choose to abandon the law making business for governorship?
If you give me an opportunity, I 'll rather be in the National Assembly, but it ís beyond that as we are working on the mandate of the people and because within the executive, the positions are for four years, then the position goes to another zone. I was comfortable in the House because at the end of eight years of the former governor, the position had to go to another zone; in the zone I come from, the people who mandated me to go to the National Assembly felt I was serving them well and so they pulled me back to serve at the state level. If they asked me to go back and be a councilor, I will respect their wishes. If they had not given me that opportunity, remember I was a very young man then. I wouldn't be who I am today.
What do you think your people will remember you for after eight years in the National Assembly?
I think they will remember me more for standing up strongly against the third term and also defending them when they were invaded by the soldiers. I moved a motion for the panel that was set up to investigate the remote and immediate causes. Aside bringing Federal Government projects, these two principal issues stood out. I served as a very strong element against a proposed tenure elongation for the last administration. I was the one who raised the motion for a commission of enquiry to be set up; I went as far as the United Nations (UN) and the Human Rights Watch in Europe, to draw the attention of the International Community to the genocide that was being committed at that time. My people surely remember me for that.
After eight years, what are the major problems you have identified in the governance of the state?
The problems are enormous. If you step out here, you will notice the problems everywhere. In my small way, I am trying to address some of the basic and immediate ones. Like I said, there's a total decay of infrastructure and a total collapse of our rural economy, and once the rural economy of a society collapses, there is doom. Most of the states like Sudan , Chad and others failed because people failed to realize that what happened was that the rural economy of these societies collapsed. Once people begin to drift and there is nothing to do, they engage in all sorts of vices that make a society fail. We were gradually drifting towards that; what I am trying to do is to revive the rural economy and build back some of the decayed infrastructure.In doing that, you need an enormous amount of money which is not at hand. So, it is a big problem because people are impatient. You are making efforts and they think that you are not doing anything because the problems are everywhere; anything you do is just like a drop in the ocean.
I can say the fundamental problems were from the local governments, which is something that affects the nation. The reforms that are embarked upon are for repositioning and re-enforcing the local governments.
Before then, people had associated the local governments with sharing money. When I came in, I decided to take some bold steps and totally restructured that tier government. So, they are properly positioned now for us to move; but you know that as you do it, you step on toes, so people who were used to certain ways of doing things are very unhappy with me because there's no money for any person to share now. Every where you turn, there is a problem.
What happened was that Chairmen of local governments would celebrate what they called Joint Account meetings, and a chairman would pack all the money belonging to his local governments, put in his boot and drove around, drinking and spending the money; that brought about much fraud. The first thing I did was to stop the Chairmen from coming to Joint Account meetings which is the function of the treasurers. they (treasurers) are now attending that meeting. They will look at what is allocated, take the paper back, sit down with the local governments. There are certain positions where some people feel that they will not be moved from or from their local governments where they were posted.
People stay in one position for years; there were also situations where people who were not qualified, somebody from level eight given the job of a level 14 officer, and someone on level 14 would take the job of a level five officer.
Some people on level 13 insisted that they wanted to be cashiers which is a level five job. All that had to stop.
Now, we have changed heads of departments, moving them across the states, so that no person stays in one place more than it is required to prevent fraud in that place. We are also paying salaries until we are able to ascertain the exact salary bill of each local government and so these are some of the changes that we introduced.
In the last 100 days, have been so frustrated in the process of governance?
I was conscious of the responsibilities, which I was getting into, but there are times that I ask myself, why did I vie for this job? The pressure is simply unthinkable. But once God gives you a responsibility, he also gives you a will power and the wisdom to carry it out; God has been gracious to me that I've been given the will power to carry out these responsibilities without much problem, although at times it gets very hot.
Have you had to step on toes?
Of course, like in giving appointments, out of four million people, I have only thirteen commissioners and the support, which I garnered from people, was much. So, definitely there are some people who are not happy. Then, some of the changes I introduced in the local governments, some people are unhappy with them. Taking political decisions to work on the overriding interest of the people will make you step on toes. So, if you want to work for a cabal, then you have no problem. But once it's in the interest of the people, then you must step on people. That should be expected all the time.
Why did you not make the declaration of your assets public?
Well, I submitted my list to the Code of Conduct Bureau (CCB) before I was inaugurated. Once you submit your forms to the CCB, it ís as good as making your assets public. I was in the National Assembly for eight years, so everything I have is in the public arena. There's nothing I have that is not known by people. I don't think that there is a problem because any person who wants to contest what I have can go to the CCB and access it. Everything: bank accounts, properties are all there, there's nothing to hide. It's just the impression of the press that it must be public. I have few properties, a house in Abuja, I have one here and one in the village and I have bank accounts that have no money in them. So, there's nothing to hide.

Original_Search_Title: Country Can Earn N63bn Yearly From Neem, Says NAECIT Boss
Headline: Nigeria: Country Can Earn N63bn Yearly From Neem, Says NAECIT Boss
Body_Text:
Abuja — The Director-General of National Research Institute for Chemical Technology (NARICT) Dr. Ebeneza Okonkwo yesterday disclosed that Nigeria would earn N63 billion ($500 USD) annually from the exportation of Neem (Dogonyaro) Products.
Dr. Okonkwo who disclosed this in Abuja when he led a delegation to the Nigeria Export Promotion Council (NEPC) to deliberate on how Neem products can be turned into a money-spinner for the country if properly utilized, said that in Kebbi State alone there is 30 million Neem trees which can generate over $10billion annually.
According to him, if China can earn $2billion annually from 11 million neem trees, Nigeria can earn much more from the same products.
His words,"we have not gotten all machinery needed to extract this product from the tree, but if properly handled and exported, we will not get anything less than 500 million dollars (N63b).
"The demand for Neem is higher in Nigeria than India. Nigeria can earn $4 billion from the exportation of the products in five years. In Kebbi alone we have 30 million trees of Neem. You can imagine what we will make out of it if properly extracted and processed for exportation.
"Neem has been found to be wonder tree of 21st century. It is called a wonder tree because of the great potentials it has over our everyday life. There is no area of human endeavour you cannot find this wonder plant. It is found in agriculture, health, commerce, in building etc.
"Some countries in the world had now enjoyed the potential of this product, especially India. They have used it in various areas, and it has become one of their greatest export product. For example in 2004, India generated a foreign exchange of $2 billion just exporting a product (oil) from the Neem".
He pointed out that Neem products is very big enterprise and wealth creator, adding that luckily Nigeria has got so much of this plant.
"In fact the number of Neem plant we have in Nigeria far exceeds that of India, so if we can deliberately harness what we have currently in Nigeria, we may be earning much more than India has earned from this plant.
He explained however that their mission to the Nigeria Export Promotion Council (NEPC) is to see how this enterprise can be harnessed and promoted.
According to him, NARICT has established a plant in Kastina that is able to produce core products out of the seed alone. "We can produce the oil, organic fertilizer, which is the first of its kind in the country and also the pesticide which is able to control up to 200 pests in the past.
"We are now working on establishing a plant in Kebbi, Kogi and in Oyo state. We also have Neem plants in Zamfara, Borno and Anambra. We came to this place is to nurture the association so as to bring it in the area it can operate as a group and not independently", he explained.
In his remark, the Chief Executive of NEPC Mr. Aliyu Lawal said that Council will collaborate with NARICT/Neem stakeholders Forum and other relevant bodies to tackle various challenges in order to develop Neem for export.
"In this regard, the Council will organize training on the best practices on seed propagation, planting, harvesting, processing, pricing and packaging for export. We will also embark on aggressive publicity campaigns within and outside Nigeria just like we did for some other products such as Sesame Seed, Ginger and cassava", he said.

Original_Search_Title: Tribune, Yar'Adua and the Minister of Abuja
Headline: Nigeria: Tribune, Yar'Adua and the Minister of Abuja
Body_Text:
Abuja — Last Thursday, the Nigerian Tribune lent its formidable voice to those who have often criticized the Federal Government's administration of Abuja as being too partial to the northern region in which it is located.
The Tribune's criticism was that President Umaru Yar'Adua did not break with what seems to have been a long running tradition of appointing mostly northerners as Abuja's ministers.
In decrying the appointment of Dr Aliyu Modibbo Umar, who is from Gombe State, as minister, the Tribune seized the opportunity to defend former president, Olusegun Obasanjo, from the widespread charge that his eight-year rule disproportionately favoured his Yoruba kith and kin.
To take the first issue first, the newspaper said although it was not questioning the competence and disposition of the new minister, his appointment as a northerner could only have rekindled "a debate on whether it (Abuja) is a federal or northern capital territory."
To drive home its point, the newspaper reminded its readers that, "In the more centrifugal federalism of the First Republic.... the father of the current president, Alhaji Shehu (sic) Yar'Adua, was appointed the minister for Lagos territory in spite of the fact that the federal capital territory was then located in Southern Nigeria." (The name of the current president's late father was Musa not Shehu. The late Shehu, who was Obasanjo's second in command when Obasanjo was military head of state between 1976 and 1979, was the president's older brother). Presumably, Tribune had expected President Yar'Adua to return the favour of the southern politicians - Yoruba politicians in particular, as the original owners of Lagos - not objecting to his father being appointed a Lagos minister.
"It would have made sense as well as rejuvenated the flagging nationalist morale", said the newspaper, "for government to appoint a non-northerner as minister for the Federal Capital Territory, if only to break the ossifying but deplorable political tradition of treating the municipality as a northern capital territory."
At least, three criticisms can be made of Tribune's criticism of Yar'Adua's appointment of a northerner as Abuja's minister. First, the newspaper, like the critics it has lent its voice to, did not speak the whole truth. True, the majority of Abuja's ministers have been northerners, but that is only half the story. The other half is that there have been southerners who have been Abuja's ministers beginning from Mr Ajose Adeogun who was its first minister and ruled for three years from 1976. In addition, there were the late Mr Mark Okoye ('82/'83) and Professor Mariam Ikejiani-Clark ('93-'97), who were ministers of state, but even then were no pushovers, as anyone who had any business with the Abuja ministry would tell you. Then there was Dr Olu Alabi who, as Chairman of the Federal Capital Development Authority during much of Obasanjo's first term, was almost as powerful as the minister.
Altogether, Abuja has had 12 ministers of cabinet rank, inclusive of the current minister, and four ministers of state. Of the 12 cabinet ministers, only Alhaji Iro Danmusa ('82/'83), Malam Nasir el-Rufai ('03-'07) and current minister fit the stereo-type of the northerner as a Hausa-Fulani-Muslim. The late Mr John Kadiya ('79-'82) was from Plateau and a Christian. So also was Lt-General Jerry Useni ('93-'98), arguably its most powerful minister to date.
Alhaji Haliru Dantoro, the current Emir of Borgu, (Oct-Dec'83), the late Major-General Mamman Vatsa ('84/'85) and Major-General Gado Nasko ('89-'93), were from minority ethnic groups in the Middle Belt. Two, Arch. Ibrahim Bunu ('99-'00) and Engr Abba Gana ('00-'03), were Kanuri from the North-East, while Air Vice-Marshal Hamza Abdullahi ('86-'89) from Hadejia had a cross-breed ancestry of Hausa and Kanuri.
As for the four ministers of state, none was your typical northern Hausa-Fulani-Muslim. Two, Okoye and Ikejiani-Clark, were Igbo while the late Alhaji Idris Kpaki was Nupe from Niger State and Mr Solomon Ewuga was Eggon from Nasarawa State.
The devil, as the saying goes, is always in the detail. So if Tribune, and those who share its criticism of the Federal Government, had bothered to check the details, they would have seen from the variety of Abuja's ministers to date that the north as a whole did not have a complete monopoly of the Abuja's top job. Certainly, no one section of the north did.
In any case - and this is the second criticism that can be levied at Tribune - to focus on the top Abuja job is to mistake its symbolism for the substance. Even the most casual examination of Abuja's bureaucracy will reveal that the admittedly northern domination of the top job has never deprived non-northerners of their fair share of the city's administrative jobs or its contracts. Go to any of its departments or any of its area councils and you will find as many Igbo, Yoruba and other Nigerians working or getting contracts as you will find northerners. Chances are you will probably find even more southerners than northerners.
And what is true of Abuja's bureaucracy is even truer of its socio-economy. I do not have the figures, but the anecdotal evidence that we can all see suggests that those who constitute the main driving force of the city's private sector, whether it is property or information technology or the hospitality industry or the financial sector or whatever, are not northerners. This was something you could never say of Lagos when it was the capital territory.
The third criticism one could make of Tribune's position is that it conveniently chose to treat Abuja in isolation, just like it did with the city's top job. If the newspaper had taken a broader view of the issue, it would have noticed that there are other ministries that historically have been dominated by appointees from particular sections of the country. The newspaper was justified to frown at the northern domination of Abuja's top job, but the principle of consistency demanded that it also frowned at similar dominations of other ministries and parastatals by others.
Finally, to Tribune's defence of Obasanjo's nationalist credentials. "There is," said the newspaper, "much to criticize in the Obasanjo years, not least the former president's failure to translate the gains of Nigeria's unprecedented windfall earnings from oil into infrastructural uplift and all-rounded economic growth. Nonetheless, in the area of extending the country's nationalist imagination and playing up its federalist essence, it is difficult to fault him."
Really? Could the Tribune have forgotten so soon that the late Chief Bola Ige, the quintessential Yoruba champion if ever there was one, said he accepted to serve in Obasanjo's cabinet only because the president had promised him he would implement the Afenifere agenda? And did Obasanjo not deliver on his word by, among other things, replacing the much derided "government by Local Purchasing Order (LPO)", which was supposed to have favoured northerners, with a government by consultancy where his kith and kin are predominant? Did Obasanjo not site all but one of the seven or so new power generating stations in the south, particular his own South-West? Did he not, at the same time, deliberately neglect to refurbish and maintain all three decaying hydro-electric dams in the north which had served the entire country since independence?
Did he not initially neglect to invest in agriculture, the north's economic mainstay, and then when he decided to do so, did he not unduly focus on cassava which is mainly a southern root-crop?
Was one of Obasanjo's first acts not the wholesale sack of "politicized" military and police officers, the majority of whom were northerners, ostensibly to restore the lost professionalism and efficiency of those institutions? Yet, can anyone deny the charge that under Obasanjo, the same institutions suffered the worst form of politicization in the country's history because of the manner he divided and suborned their rank and file in order to massively rig the 2003 elections and, on an even grander scale, to also rig the last elections, not to mention his earlier pursuit of his diabolical Third Term agenda?
As the country's chief security officer, did he not look the other way when members of the much-dreaded Yoruba ethnic militia, the O'odua People's Congress, on several occasions killed and maimed other Nigerians, including members of the police force, with impunity?
Tribune would have been right to argue that generally speaking, the ordinary Yoruba or southerner was not much better off than his northern compatriot for all of Obasanjo's implementation of the Afenifere agenda because the former president was basically self-serving. But the fact that proportionately more Yorubas than other Nigerians benefited from his rule, at least at the level of the country's elite, is simply undeniable.
The Tribune itself talked about appointing a southerner as Abuja's minister as a means of rejuvenating (its own word) "the flagging nationalist morale". The question is did this morale start to flag only recently with Yar'Adua's coming to power barely three months ago?
The honest answer, as the editors at the Tribune know all too well, is that if there was any flagging in nationalist morale, it did not start with Yar'Adua. Of course, it did not also start with Obasanjo. Problem is he did nothing to stem its decline. On the contrary, he did everything through his strategy of divide and rule to make matters worse, much worse.
Tribune is justified to complain about northern domination of Abuja's top job but the solution is not to look at the city's top job or the city itself in isolation. The solution is to look at the whole picture across all the ministries and parastatals so as to arrive at an objective and correct diagnosis of the problem.

Original_Search_Title: Heavy Rains Destroy Bridge, Cut Off Region
Headline: Uganda: Heavy Rains Destroy Bridge, Cut Off Karamoja Region
Body_Text:
Mbale — Transport to Karamoja region via the Sironko-Nakapiripirit Road has been cut off following heavy prolonged rains and floods that have destroyed the main bridge along the route.
The 132.5km route through Cheptui Bridge in Bunambutye is the shortest gateway to Karamoja from Elgon zone. All vehicles destined for Nakapiripirit now have to go through Mbale - Soroti - Katakwi - Moroto and Nakapiripirit, which is almost four times the original distance.
Ministry of Works Executive Engineer Henry Matovu said the bridge collapsed on August 29 due to persistent heavy rains which eroded, scoured and undermined the foundation of the 45-year-old bridge.
"This is in addition to the dead load of super structure, vertical and horizontal loads by heavy trucks carrying gypsum from Moroto to Tororo Cement Industries placed on the abutment," Mr Matovu.
Mr Matovu told Daily Monitor on Saturday that the floods and heavy load forced the abutment (lateral supporting structure) to collapse and that the bridge deck also curved in due to lack of support. He said a team of engineers from the Ministry of Works had visited the bridge to assess the situation but that the road has been closed until construction is done.
He said because most of the bridges in eastern Uganda are as old as 1962, thorough assessment would be carried out by the engineers once the water level is down to ascertain their strength.
When Daily Monitor visited the scene, residents of Bulweta, Bunaburwa, Bududa and Bukhabuya villages in Cheptui were making a fortune by charging a fee to ferry merchanidise across the bridge to enable vehicles cross without a load.
Many roads remained impassable and a number of fields were still submerged, leading to destruction of crops like maize, ground nuts, millet, rice, cassava and sweet potatoes
Bunaburwa LC1 chairman Steven Kadondi appealed to the government to speed up re-construction of the bridge to enable farmers harvest crops which he said are rotting in the gardens.
"Many of us have gardens across the bridge and we also do business in Karamoja region but now, our crops are rotting in the gardens and our businesses are stuck," he said. Mbale, Sironko and Kapchorwa are the food baskets for Nakapiripirit and Moroto districts. This means Karamoja region could face famine if nothing is done soon.

Original_Search_Title: High Food Supplies Dampen Prices As Inflation Drops
Headline: Uganda: High Food Supplies Dampen Prices As Inflation Drops
Body_Text:
Kampala — UGANDANS spent less money on basic foodstuffs in August compared to six months ago.
This came about as a result of a 2.7 per cent price decrease in the price of staple foods in four major urban centres meaning that consumers were able to use Shs10,000 to purchase a double portion of the same goods.
"There was a consumer surplus meaning that consumers were spending less than they could have spent on the food items six months ago," Uganda Bureau of Statistics Principal Statistician Vicente Nsubuga Musoke said at a press conference on Friday.
Food prices
Food prices went down in all centres with significant contributions from staple foods especially matooke, sweet potatoes, and fresh cassava, cereals and fresh fish.
Prices of some fruits and vegetables like pineapples, oranges cabbages, tomatoes and onions also went down due to increased supply. Mr Nsubuga said that Masaka, Mbarara and Jinja towns registered the highest decreases in prices of food crops during the month.
"For instance people use to spend Shs10,000 to buy a bunch of matooke but during the month they were buying a bunch of matooke between Shs3,500 to Shs4,000 in Kampala and other centres," he said.
Due to seasonal factors leading to high production and high supply of food items in the market, Mr Nsubuga said that Uganda's annual headline inflation rate for the year ending August 2007 went down to 2.7 per cent from 4.2 per cent for the year ended July 2007.
At 2.7 per cent, this is the lowest inflation rate recorded in Uganda since 2004.
However, the Director for Macroeconomics, Mr Matthew Ssewanyana said that the annual underlying inflation rate, which excludes food crops items from CPI basket, stood at 6.7 per cent for the year ending August 2007.
Benchmark
Last fiscal year, Uganda's inflation closed at 8.1 per cent higher than the 5 per cent level set by the government as a benchmark.
"Since the fiscal year 2007/08 started, we have only moved two months and it is too early to take definite positions. We cannot tell whether the underlying rate, which government uses to measure the level of inflation in the country, will be higher than the targeted 5 per cent," Mr Ssewanyana said.
Mr Ssewanyana said that the reason why underlying inflation had maintained high levels was because the prices of manufactured goods rarely drop once they shoot up.
During the month increases in the prices of salt, bottled beers, soap and commuter transport were recorded in most centres.
"High energy costs and water tariffs have continued to increase the cost of production among manufacturers," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Hailstorm Destroys Homes in Bushenyi
Headline: Uganda: Hailstorm Destroys Homes in Bushenyi
Body_Text:
Kampala — FOUR families in Bushenyi district were left homeless on Saturday after a heavy hailstorm razed their houses. The incident happened in Bugarama village, Bumbeire sub-county.
Household property, banana and cassava plantations were destroyed by the heavy rain that was characterised with heavy wind.
The most affected homes included that of Jolly Muhanguzi, the sub-county's women chairperson, Mzee Rwandanguzi, Godfrey Mugizi and Nafutali Mujuni.
By Sunday, the affected families were collecting some of the damaged iron sheets and property from the neighbouring banana plantations.
"I have never seen this. We have nothing left," Muhanguzi said.
She appealed to the Government through, the Ministry of Relief and Disaster Preparedness, to assist the affected families.
"This disaster has hit us when we are preparing to send our children back to school," Mugizi lamented.

Original_Search_Title: Tackling Trade and Investment
Headline: Liberia: Tackling Trade and Investment
Body_Text:
Whether they decide to return home or stay in exile, Liberians in the Diaspora will play an important role in the reconstruction of the war-devastated country they left behind. Over the years, as governments stopped providing the basic necessities Liberians in the Diaspora somehow managed to keep the country going, through remittances and sometimes, through small economic ventures. Schools and clinics mostly remained opened because those who went to work relied on funds they received regularly from relatives "abroad."
Since coming to the helm of the government about two years ago, President Ellen Sirleaf has paid keen attention to this very important segment of Liberians, who, collectively, account for the greatest portion of Liberia's wealth. The entire educated middle class, that is, anyone who could afford a visa and a plane ticket, with a very few exception, left the country and the greatest majority headed for the United States. Mr. Sam Mitchell, the president of the Liberian Business Association says that if Liberians in the US alone could put work as a collectivity, they could jump-start the economy.
When he came to Washington, in 2004 as Liberian Ambassador to the US, Mr. Charles Minor was quick in establishing working relationship with the Diaspora, by organizing the first ever symposium on the future of the country that brought government and the Diaspora in the same room. It was a novelty, because throughout the years, Liberians in the Diaspora mostly went to their embassy to demonstrate against one government or another. Getting them together to discuss the way forward towards the reconstruction of the nation was a rare success.
These efforts, by both the President and the Ambassador culminated in the holding of a very successful Private Sector Forum in Washington, DC that brought together more 400 participants, with a great number of Liberian expatriates, longing either to return home or to undertake some type of economic venture. The economic potentials of the nations are well known to the people but government policies are essential in turning those potentials into wealth.
As a follow-up to the February 2007 Forum, the government Embassy decided to organize a series of conferences throughout the United States, in cities with a large concentration of Liberians. If major corporations, like Coca Cola, Mittal Steel, Firestone, and others are important in the development process, it is undeniable that the bulk of the economy will be sustained by small and medium enterprises and in that light, Liberians in the Diaspora have will an essential role to play.
In the next few weeks, and starting in Minnesota on September 8, going through California on the 13, Atlanta on Rhode on the 22, Rhode Island the 27 and ending in Washington, DC on October 1, 2007, Liberians in the Diaspora will have ample opportunity to put forward their suggestions, listen to government and private sector representatives from Liberia and map out a strategies as to how to undertake investments ventures at home.
Every year, Liberians in the Diaspora send home tens of millions of dollars. Unfortunately, that money went through a revolving door, spent mostly on buying imported commodities such as rice, fuel, and clothing and therefore never made its way in the local economy. The government could find a creative way, such as in Ghana or other Latin American countries where remittances strengthen the local financial market. It must also put in lace much needed creative monetary policies so that remittances are somehow injected into the local economy.
The government has been working on plans to restructure many of its ailing state - owned enterprises, which could all provide profitable investment outlets for Liberians in the Diaspora. If going home to start a new business may not always an option, Liberians in the Diaspora must be provided with alternatives where they create wealth at home without risking losing their hard-earned monies. The conferences in the various cities across the US must therefore be more about reflecting on new ways of transforming Liberian market rather the usual talk shop on how to send home second hand cars and used clothes.
From agribusiness to gold, diamond and to education through the production and sales of local textbooks Liberians in the Diaspora have an opportune time to rebuild their country on new foundations. For example, with the inclusion of Liberia in AGOA, the country qualified for the exportation of more than 5,000 different products to the US.
Packaging Liberian potato and cassava leaves, as well as its plums and papayas that rot year in year out could lead to a new economic boom for the farmers at home. The market is here, just among the 300 - 400 Liberians in this country who now buy those same products originating from other countries. Now that government has somehow has significantly increased civil servants salaries, remittances can find a new use in the development process. Investing in the new Liberia should not be exporting products from around the world, sometimes at the detriment of local labor markets. Recently, a young Liberian entrepreneur had to shut down his factory because he could not compete with the same product made in China. The nation is at a turning point and could go into either direction.
With their wealth of knowledge and financial capital as well as the business contacts that they can establish in this giant market that the US represents, Liberians in the Diaspora can turn things around in their home country and look at retirement from a different perspective. Working with a government committed to the development of private enterprise, there is no better time than now.
The various symposia will constitute a great starting point.

Original_Search_Title: Proposal to Restructure Agricultural Ministry in the Works - Investigation
Headline: Nigeria: Proposal to Restructure Agricultural Ministry in the Works - Investigation
Body_Text:
Abuja — A proposal for the restructuring of the Ministry of Agriculture and Water Resources is in the offing, an investigation by the News Agency of Nigeria (NAN), has revealed.
Impeccable sources in the ministry told NAN that the restructuring would entail the reduction of the departments from 11 to eight, with five technical and three service departments.
The sources who spoke on condition of anonymity, said the minister would present the proposal at the next Federal Executive Council (FEC) meeting.
According to the sources, some of the departments to be affected are the departments of rural development, land resources and cooperatives.
NAN reports that since assumption of office, the minister has been meeting with various heads of departments on how best to streamline and harmonise programmes and projects of the ministry.
NAN learnt that the minister was "not too pleased with what is on ground" hence the plan to harmonise activities for better performance.
In an interview with NAN on the development, Mr Mohammed Mogaji, the national president of the Cassava Produce Promoters and Exporters Association of Nigeria (CASPEAN), said the move would not do the local farmers any good.
He said: "If this plan is carried out, then the national policy on Integrated Rural Development will be thrown out."
Mogaji said that in a predominantly rural country such as Nigeria, integrated rural development must be seen as a major instrument for the attainment of the various "interdependent components of national interest".
He said that without a rural development department, it would be difficult to properly extend federal government's activities to the rural populace across the country.
He said the promotion of national interest through integrated rural development as an essential guarantee of national security would be affected. (NAN)

Original_Search_Title: Govt Sends Food Aid to Flood-Hit Teso Region
Headline: Uganda: Govt Sends Food Aid to Flood-Hit Teso Region
Body_Text:
Kampala — The government and a host of humanitarian organisations have started sending food consignments to residents of Teso following devastating floods that hit the region roughly three weeks ago.
The aid that includes posho, beans, cassava stems and potato veins has started arriving in some districts like Amuria,Bukedea and Katakwi.
The Permanent Secretary in Ministry of Relief, Disaster Preparedness and Refugees, Mr Martin Odwedo, told Daily Monitor on Thursday more aid was needed to assist the communities.
"What we have so far given out is a drop in the ocean because it was not only crops that were destroyed but even other facilities like houses, beddings and roads," he said.
Mr Odwedo said international humanitarian agencies like the Red Cross, World Food Programme, Food and Agricultural Organisation and the United Nations Children's Fund have already began distributing aid to the affected communities.
"As of now, the sub-counties of Malela (Bukedea District) and Wera, Abarilera (Amuria District) have already received their consignments of posho, beans and cassava stems," he added.
However, Mr Odwedo said he was not aware of how much food aid had been delivered since "we are different partners in this intervention". Thousands of people were recently rendered homeless in various parts of Teso following torrential rains that pounded the region.
Most sub-counties in the districts of Bukedea, Kumi, Soroti, Katakwi and Amuria were deserted due to the floods while roads and bridges got damaged making it difficult to reach the affected people. Daily Monitor has reliably learnt that President Yoweri Museveni will soon visit the region to assess the impact of the floods.
The State Minister for Relief, Disaster Preparedness and Refugees, Mr Musa Ecweru, who toured the region, told journalists on Thursday that it would require the involvement of all line ministries to avert famine and outbreak of water borne diseases in the region.
"The whole infrastructure has broken down something that cannot be handled by my ministry alone," he said.
He said apart from foodstuffs, the government was also looking for funds to purchase tents and household equipment for residents.

Original_Search_Title: Govt Aids Teso Flood Victims
Headline: Uganda: Govt Aids Teso Flood Victims
Body_Text:
Kampala — THREE weeks after floods wreaked havoc in Teso sub-region, the government and other relief agencies have started sending food consignments to victims.
The aid includes maize flour, beans, cassava stems and potato veins. So far flood victims in the districts of Amuria, Bukedea and Katakwi have receieved the aid.
The Permanent Secretary in Ministry of Relief, Disaster Preparedness and Refugees, Martin Odwedo however told Sunday Monitor on Thursday that more aid was still needed.
"What we have done so far is like a drop in the ocean," he said. He said since not only crops were destroyed the victims are in dire need of shelter and basic domestic utensils like saucepans and plates.
Mr Odwedo said international relief agencies like the International Committee of the Red Cross, World Food Programmee (WFP), Food and Agricultural Organisation (FAO) United Nations Children's Fund had began distributing aid in the devastated areas.
He said flood victims in Malela Wera, Abarilera have sub counties have received their relief consignments. The hostile floods that swept bridges and submerged roads made residents to desert villages in Bukedea, Kumi, Soroti, Katakwi and Amuria districts.
Sunday Monitor has learnt that President Yoweri Museveni will soon visit the region to assess the impact of the floods on the communities. Mr Tamale Mirundi, the President's Press Secretary on Friday confirmed Mr Museveni's impending visit to Teso. According to an inter-agency assessment last week over 50,000 people were displaced.

Original_Search_Title: Bageya Turns to Pigs
Headline: Uganda: Bageya Turns to Pigs
Body_Text:
Iganga — FORMER Busia resident district commissioner George Patrick Bageya has turned to rearing pigs on his 100-acre farm.
Bageya, who also is former Iganga district chairperson, said the pigs bought from Luuka county kept him busy after politics.
He has about 15 pigs, over 50 head of cattle as well as several gardens of maize, cassava and oranges on the farm located at Naibiri, Nambaale sub-county in Iganga district.
"I would rather stay here looking after my pigs than stay in urban centres after the end of my political deployment. Other politicians in my category prefer staying in towns until they run out of cash," he told The New Vision during a tour of his farm on Friday.
Bageya said it was better to do farming than hang around town to become a topic for gossip columns of media houses.
He boasted of having been a well-established entrepreneur before joining politics and had to continue after serving as a politician at several levels.
"I am not like certain politicians who take over districts and start amassing wealth overnight with little concern for the voters. Look at my posh house, it is 36-years-old and was never constructed using government money."
He attributed much of the political bickering in Busoga region to politicians' lack of personal assets to fall back to after service.
Bageya, who spends much of the day in his home office, keeps attending to the problems of his village-mates as if he were still a local leader.
"I began my political career as an LCI chairman. Although these people have another chairman, they still treat me as one," he said.
He said after serving the Government, he has embarked on serving the higher Kingdom of God.
"I now diligently serve my God. If you had visited on Sunday, you would have attended our prayer service in the hall located on the ground floor."

Original_Search_Title: 2 Die After Eating Cassava
Headline: Sierra Leone: 2 Die After Eating Cassava
Body_Text:
Freetown — Foday Dabo, 4 and Moinamah Bawoh, 3 were Wednesday reportedly pronounced dead after they finished eating cassava prepared by their parents. The incident took place at Torpombu 2 Section in Kenema, eastern Sierra Leone.
According to Chernor Dabo, the father of one the deceased, he went out with his wife in search of food in the township but they were not successful.
"When we returned home we decided to cook cassava for the day's meal so that the family cannot go to bed hungry," he said.
He explained that few minutes after the kids had eaten the food they started complaining of stomach ache.
"Before we could rush them to the hospital, Foday had already dropped dead," he said, adding that Moinamah Bawoh died shortly after they arrived at the hospital.
The couple also complained that they experienced serious stomach ache after eating the cassava.
They are presently responding to treatment at the Kenema Government Hospital while Police are investigating the circumstances that led to the death of the two kids.

Original_Search_Title: Mozambique And USAID Align Forces to Fight Cassava Root Rot Disease
Headline: Mozambique And USAID Align Forces to Fight Cassava Root Rot Disease
Body_Text:
Maputo — The Biotechnology Laboratory of the Mozambique's National Institute for Agricultural Research (IIAM), with the financial support of United States Agency for International Development (USAID) delivered in Maputo on Thursday 5,000 seedlings of a new cassava variety resistant to the root diseases, to be distributed among the peasant farmers in the northern province of Nampula.
This is part of a two-year project that will see over 50.000 seedlings distributed among the 200,000 peasant farmers in Nampula. It is aimed at dealing with problems associated with cassava root disease that is the cause of food insecurity in the region.
To materialize the project, USAID granted 580,000 USD to upgrade the IIAM Biotechnology Laboratory, and an additional sum of 705,000 USD to its partners NGO's, namely "CARE" and "Save the Children", for the construction of seedling incubators and fund seedling distribution in Nampula, the most affected region in Mozambique.
Speaking during the ceremony, the USAID mission director to Mozambique, Todd Amani pledged to continue collaborating with the IIAM in the pursuit of solutions to the problems threatening country's food security.
Severe yield losses due to root rots affect cassava supply to the local populations and weaken food security of rural communities, and impoverish the local communities.
The IIAM Biotechnology Laboratory developed the first batches of seedlings, of improved cassava variety, "Nachinyaya", in vitro.
According to Todd Amani, these plants are to be grown and multiplied in Nampula, and will help to mitigate the food insecurity among the local communities, particularly in the coastal areas.
"The researchers took seriously the concerns of the community leaders, local authorities and Agriculture Ministry, who found the need to develop technologies to grow and multiply plants which are not only resistant to the disease but also free from it", said Amani.
For her part, Mozambique's deputy agriculture minister, Catarina Pajume, said that IIAM would follow up to ensure achieving the desired results.
Over 200,000 people will benefit of this program.
According to Mozambique's CARE representative, Abdul António, this will come as a relief to the rural populations who have been fighting with the disease over the last seven years.
Cassava is cultivated in almost all the districts in Mozambique, where it is one of the major staple food crops, particularly in the rural areas.

Original_Search_Title: Need For Youth Empowerment and Career Development
Headline: Nigeria: Need For Youth Empowerment and Career Development
Body_Text:
Lagos — The challenges and opportunities confronting today's youths formed the thrust of a seminar organized by the Mgbidi-Amaka Elite, Nigeria, in Lagos last week.
With the theme Youth Empowerment and Career Development, three papers were presented, including a keynote address by distinguished personalities from Mgbidi community in Imo State.
In his paper, Managing Director/Editor-in-Chief of Champion Newspapers Ltd (CNL), Mr. Ugo Onuoha, said formal education was the best empowerment that could be given to Mgbidi youths, stressing that it was education that would prepare them for the challenges of today.
Stressing the importance of educating the youths, Onuoha appealed to the people to ensure that every Mgbidi child was given formal education regardless of the ability of the child's parents to foot the bill.
"What this means is that the education of all our children must be a collective responsibility and must not be left to the government or indigent parents alone.
"No Mgbidi child should be left out of school on account of the inability of his/her parents to pay for the up-keep of the child," he said.
The Managing Director of CNL went further to point the way of empowering the youths after their education by advocating that a data of all qualified Mgbidi youths be compiled and circulated through a network such that Mgbidi sons and daughters in positions of authority could influence the recruitment of the involved youths.
He said the poor state of the nation's economy which has seriously constricted job opportunities had opened a vista of opportunity for self-employment for the youthe while urging them to avail themselves of those opportunities.
"There are opportunities in small scale manufacturing and agriculture, fish and snail farming, poultry and piggery businesses and cassava cultivation have turned out to be gold mines for people. Some have ventured into pure water production while others have set up coaching classes which transformed into full-fledged schools," he said.
Challenging the adults of the town, including the association that put together the seminar, Onuoha said they all had strategic roles to play in actualizing the entrepreneurial potentials of Mgbidi youths.
He said, the provision of grants and soft loans to the youths would make it easier for those of them that accepted self-employment to become useful sons and daughters of the society.
He suggested the establishment of a revolving loan scheme for Mgbidi youths willing to set up their own businesses after their graduation.
In his words: "Those youths who cannot go it alone can come together and set up cooperative societies that I am sure will flourish if they are well managed and properly funded.
"For instance, we can, banding together raise venture money, which will be deposited with Mgbidi Community Bank from which our youths who are so minded can borrow at concessionary interest rates to set up cottage industries," he said
Emphasizing the relevance of information communication technology, (ICT), Onuoha urged the youths to embrace the trend, saying that they need to be computer literate besides the degree programmes and also to acquire skills in special areas of their disciplines.
"Our young ones should take advantage of the skills acquisition centres that are being set up these days to become very good at carpentry, furniture making, masonry, welding and such others that are now being dominated by young men from other West African countries," he stressed.
While frowning on parents who impose careers on their children, Mr. Onuoha posited that the young ones should be guided in choosing the right career, taking into cognizance their abilities, talents and inclinations.
He said those that make the wrong choice of career usually end up frustrated and unfulfilled.
In his own paper, Mr. Leonard Ezedike, Regional Manager of FirstInland Bank plc noted that the empowerment of the youths was a function of the interplay of various value chains, acting as agents of change.
He said the change agents consisted of parents' actions, self-empowerment initiatives of the youths themselves, government's policies and cultural orientations of the society.
Ezedike said the attributes of somebody with the right career development aimed at overcoming failure included determination, honesty, trustworthiness, faithfulness, humility, perseverance, kindness, openness, positivism and proactiveness.
Quoting a renowned motivational writer, Stephen Covey, Ezedike identified the seven habits that could catapult one from the low ground to the height of empowerment: Be proactive, begin with the end in mind, put first things first, think win/win, seek first to understand then to be understood, synergy and sharpen the saw.
In his exhortation, Revd Emeka Ngubo, General Overseer of Calvary Pentecostal Assembly International, Lagos traced the exemplary life and time of the Biblical Joseph. He urged Mgbidi youths to dream dreams and to focus on realising their dreams.
Revd Ngubo who is also chairman/Chief executive of Elim Motors advised youths to define their mission in life and then trust God to see them through.
He said the journey through life is littered with hurdles and obstacles but that those who keep their eyes on the ball and keep faith with divine instructions never fail.
Earlier in his keynote address, the pioneer president of the Mgbidi-Amaka Elite, Mr Chukwudi Ukwuegbu, noted that the day's event represented part of the legacy the founders of the association were passing to the youths.
He, however, advised that there was an urgent need for the revival of the association in order to reconnect the vision of its founders.
"But for the dream not to become a nightmare, and for the mission not to become a mirage, and vision of the founding fathers not to turn into an illusion, the Mgbidi-Amaka Elite Association of Nigeria needs revival.
"It needs a rejuvenation. It needs a renewal. Mgbidi-Amaka needs new members to revive, rejuvenate and renew the association," he said.
Recounting the vision of the founders of the association, Ukwuegbu who is the Deputy Registrar of the West African Examinations Council (WAEC), said their dream for founding the association in 1984 was to take Mgbidi to greater heights.
According to him, youth represented a phase of life when people gathered the bricks with which to build the mansions of their life later, a time to gather the twigs of life for the fire needed to warm the frayed limbs later in life.
Stressing the need for the association to involve God in all its activities, Ukwuegbu who was the association's president between 1984 and 1988, said any endeavour without God would collapse like the Tower of Babel.
"Our experience should warn us to warn our youths that any endeavour without proper spiritual understanding of the meaning of life and purpose of existence was utterly useless," he said.
He challenged the youths of Mgbidi to interact positively in order to gain better appreciation of their roots, stressing that it was the responsibility of the adults to guide the youths so that they could channel their creative energies into positive contributions in the development of the community.

Original_Search_Title: Government Supports Teso Flood Victims
Headline: Uganda: Government Supports Teso Flood Victims
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE Prime Minister's office is distributing food and planting materials to the people in Teso region whose gardens were destroyed by floods a week ago, writes Aidah Nanyonjo.
The items that included posho, beans, and cassava cuttings were given to residents of Wera, Abarilera sub-counties in Amuria district and Malera in Bukedea district.
Handing over the items at Wera Social Centre on Sunday, the state minister for disaster preparedness, Musa Ecweru, promised the locals medicine and mosquito nets to reduce the number of malaria cases that may result due to the floods.
"We have distributed some food, but it is not enough for all the affected sub-counties. All the victims should get food," Ecweru added.
He said the technical staff in the Ministry of Health would ensure that all the affected areas receive both qualitative and quantitative relief.
Ecweru added that the flooded roads still limit relief distribution because most roads are impassable. He thanked the Food and Agricultural Organisation for its support and asked other organisations to help.
The floods destroyed 29 classrooms, 28 teachers' houses and 15 pit latrines in different schools in Katakwi district.
The affected schools were Atoroma, Ngariam, Ocwiin, Abwanget Toroma Boys, Orimai and Magoro. Olelai, Abwokodia, Usuk Boys, Aakum, Okolimo, Ocorimongin, Omosingo, Adacar and Akoboi primary schools were also damaged.

Original_Search_Title: Coffee Producers Lose in European Trade
Headline: Uganda: Coffee Producers Are the Biggest Losers
Body_Text:
Kampala — Coffee producers in Uganda suffer from an unfair trade relationship with Europe, even though their beans produce some of the best quality coffee in the world, says the Ugandan coffee industry's governing body.
"The biggest loser is the person directly involved in coffee bean production," says Henry Ngabirano, director of the Uganda Coffee Development Authority. Moreover, Ugandan producers get an "unequal share of the revenue generated by coffee beans".
Combined, Ugandan growers and exporters receive 6 percent of the finished product's price, according to the Uganda Coffee Development Authority. "This shows what level of exploitation there is," Ngabirano says.
Coffee beans are Uganda's top cash crop and their production accounts for nearly half of the nation's economy. The country has become a leading Robusta producer since the political crisis erupted in Ivory Coast, a former top producer. Exporters said their earnings grew 65 percent to 17,9 million dollars in May this year, compared to last year.
This growth is due to higher prices and consumers in the North and in Asia buying higher-grade coffees. African countries have an advantage of high altitudes and smaller-scale farms, along with a natural abundance of organic farming that has made their coffee beans more attractive on the global market.
According to East African Fine Coffees Association director Philip Gitao, the demand for African coffee beans is at an all-time high. He says that African coffee was increasingly being used to upgrade traditional blends and to be marketed on their own as specialty brands.
The 1990s in Uganda saw a government policy shift on coffee production. The market was liberalized and hurdles in the way of coffee producers removed. The Ugandan shilling became fully convertible with any other currency and price controls were removed.
"The government saw coffee as having a role in poverty eradication," Ngabirano says. The number of coffee farmers surged during this time as "Ugandans began to see the business potential of coffee".
But farmers face a number of challenges, involving the unavailability of bank loans or government subsidies to jumpstart their farming. "What we are lacking as Ugandan farmers, is support," says Ronald Buule, a coffee bean farmer in central Uganda.
Although farmers form local associations to negotiate fairer prices for their raw beans from intermediaries, the profits they earn are still not enough to ensure that supply stores remain stocked and that they have sufficient tools, Buule says.
"Farmers work a lot but generate little money," Buule claims. Many coffee farmers are forced to heavily diversify their farms, growing other crops such as cocoa, cassava, bananas, oranges and vanilla and even raising poultry.
"It is a shame, really" that the retail coffee brands are not owned by Ugandan companies, though the raw material is bought from the country, Ngabirano says. Ugandan President Yoweri Museveni has said his country will only develop when it makes its own finished products instead of just selling raw materials.
The pending economic partnership agreement (EPA) between the European Union (EU) and developing countries will further exploit the producers, Ngabirano says. The EPAs require that the 77 African, Pacific and Caribbean countries offer reciprocal market access to their EU trade partners.
"The EPAs would have been better if European countries removed the subsidies on their domestic coffee industries, which runs to an estimated 365 billion dollars a year," Ngabirano says, adding that since Uganda is a low-cost producer, "there would be no competition". He adds, "if the EU is really streamlining trade, they should address the issues of subsidies."
Uganda began organic coffee bean farming ten years ago, and now over 2,000 tons of organic coffee beans are produced a year. "We produce organic coffee in five regions of Uganda, two Arabica and three Robusta," Ngabirano tells IPS, referring to the different types of bean.
Farmers are certified by various European boards responsible for ensuring organic quality. "It is a protracted story," Ngabirano says of the organic certification process. Farmers should not have used chemicals on their plots of land for three years in order to move from conventional to organic production, a period that is called the transitional phase.
They are taught to avoid chemicals at all costs and how to properly substitute regular with organic manure. Outside analysts inspect the land after the three-year period to guarantee that all inorganic materials have degraded. But, according to Ngabirano, "in most cases, such inorganic materials have never been there, so we lose time (by being forced to go through the transitional phase)."
Inorganic fertilizers and chemicals are either unavailable or too expensive in many farming regions. As a result, out of one million coffee farmers, most are "organic by default", though not certified, Ngabirano says. Some 15,000 farmers are officially certified as organic producers.
But Ugandan coffee experts debate whether organic coffee bean farming is actually profitable. There is a 20 to 30 percent premium on top of the normal price, but farmers suffer a considerable loss in yield by not using chemicals and fertilizers on their crops. "We have not been able to establish as a fact which is better," Ngabirano said.
To complicate matters, Uganda is facing potentially harmful non-tariff barriers from the EU. Some European conservationists are saying that transporting products by air to sell in other countries increases pollution and is therefore bad for the environment.
Such talk could foreclose Uganda ever moving on to producing its own finished coffee products and sending them abroad to be sold.

Original_Search_Title: Biofuels Likely to Boost Energy But Increase Hunger, Now Critics Warn
Headline: Kenya: Biofuels Likely to Boost Energy But Increase Hunger, Now Critics Warn
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Though biofuels are being touted as the solution to Africa's growing energy crisis, not everybody is happy with the rising demand for biofuel products.
Already, some environmentalists have raised concern about the potential threat to the continent's weak food security.
Although the use of bioenergy is yet to take root in Africa, there are fears that farmers might shift from growing food crops whose prices have remained low to biofuels which are currently attracting high prices in the world market.
Biofuels are made from living things or the waste they produce.
Wood energy
Bioenergy includes all agro-energy and wood energy resources. The former includes crops specifically grown for energy, such as sugarcane, cassava, sorghum, maize, palm oil, oilseeds and various grasses.
Other agro-energy resources are agricultural and livestock by-products such as straw, leaves, stalks, husks, shells, manure, droppings and any food and agricultural processing and slaughter by-products.
Wood energy resources, on the other hand, comprise charcoal, forestry residues, black liquor and any other energy derived from trees.
However, in recent years, the term "biofuel" has come to mean ethanol and diesel, made from crops like maize and sugarcane.
In Europe, for example, a whole new economy is developing, where huge investments are being made to turn cereal surpluses into fuels to be used in the motor industry.
Organic material
In fact, DaimlerChrysler and Volkswagen are among the major industries promoting the use of flexible-fuel vehicles - which can use synthetic fuel derived from crops and organic material as well as petroleum or diesel. The move is meant to reduce dependence on fossil fuels like petrol and diesel.
It is the new technology that has left environmentalists a worried lot. They argue that food aid for Africa's impoverished population may not be available in future as surplus agricultural production in the developed world will be diverted to produce bioenergy for the motor industry in Europe and North America.
According to Food and Agricultural Organisation (FAO), though bioenergy has the potential of improving the wellbeing of people in rural areas, some types may compromise food security and also cause environmental damage.
"Development of the sector will require special government attention to environmental and food security concerns," according to FAO, the specialised United Nations organisation, that leads international efforts to combat hunger.
The organisation argues that although there are many linkages between traditional agricultural biomass and food security, rapid development of liquid biofuels for transport has the greatest immediate impact on food security.
It says major agricultural producers, such as Brazil, the US, the EU and Canada are expected to reduce exports of basic feedstock commodities (for example, cereals or oilseeds) for use in domestic biofuels industries and also increase imports of biofuels.
"Liquid biofuels derived from food crops may have different food security implications than modern bioenergy systems based on waste materials," the organisation warns.
As competition for arable land and water for biofuel production increases, FAO predicts a sharp rise in world food prices.
"Countries that are importers of both food and energy could face some serious challenges over time," the organisation adds.
The concern about biofuels is not only confined in West. In Kenya, Climate Network Africa (CNA), an NGO dealing with environmental matters, agrees with FAO.
According to Ms Grace Akumu of CNA, biofuels without strict regulations can be catastrophic.
While local production of biofuels on a small scale of can boost local energy production, as is the case with ethanol, the international market can completely destroy available opportunities for sustainable production.
Ms Akumu cautions that the world could be aggravating the negative effects of climate change by converting millions of acres into maize, palm oil and soy fields.
"It is becoming clear that we could be making climate change even worse, driving more species into extinction, and at the same time, threatening food production," she adds.
The consequences of growing biofuel for export instead of growing food for consumption in Africa could be severe.
"Using potential agricultural land and water to grow biofuel crops will have a detrimental effect on food security in the continent that is already struggling to feed its more than 800 million inhabitants, Ms Akumu says.
Despite the concerns, the biofuel craze is slowly catching up in Kenya.
The objective of the biofuel programme is to boost the country's energy supply which has overtaken by rising demand.
Jatropha curcas, one of the success stories in the production of biodiesel in Latin America and Asia has already been introduced in several parts of Kenya.
An environmental organisation, Green Africa Foundation, is popularising the plant among peasant farmers.
Some in Nyanza, Eastern, Coast and Rift Valley provinces have began growing the plant. Other farmers have also began using the plant as a source of fuel for household needs, thus saving on paraffin.
The organisation now plans to help the farmers produce oil in large quantities for export to the developed world.
Alternative source
Despite the developments, Energy permanent secretary Patrick Nyoike said that Kenya has a long way to go in the production of biofuels.
"Ours is still small scale and is being encouraged as an alternative source of energy," he said.
A committee has been established involving representatives from the energy sector and Government to promote the production of biofuels.
Mr Nyoike said that 10 energy centres have been opened in various parts of Kenya to encourage the production of Jatropha curcas.
"Our objective is to form marketing structures for biofuels within the next two to three years," he said.
Mumias Sugar Company is leading the way in the sugar sector by using bagas to produce energy for domestic use and for sale.
The pro-biofuels group has rubbished the argument that biofuels are a threat to Africa's food security, especially countries like Kenya, Sudan, Tanzania, Eritrea, Burundi where about 20 per cent of the population is considered undernourished.
But the use of biofuels did not begin the other day. More than 50 years ago, Henry Ford and Rudolph Diesel designed cars and engines that ran on biofuels.
And even before World War II, the UK and Germany sold biofuels mixed with petrol or diesel made from crude oil. However, it was the availability of cheap oil that later drove the former products out of the market.
The pro-biofuel group, majority of them based in the western world and parts of Latin America, argue that what is holding back agriculture in the developing world is not shortage of land, but poor prices in the world markets caused by overproduction in Europe and the US.
The proponents also maintain that the demand for biofuels will help change the situation by lifting prices and by mopping up the surpluses to give third world farming a boost in terms of income.
The move, they maintain, will do more to alleviate starvation in Africa, Asia and Latin America than all the food aid programmes put together. This will help raise farm incomes in the rural areas, hence improving the standards of living among a population that bears the brunt of after effects of fluctuations of food prices.
In fact, Europe and Latin America are miles ahead in the production and use of biofuels. In the EU, for example, the product is already being promoted as an alternative energy source for transport.
Four years ago, the Europe Union set itself a target of increasing the use of biofuels in energy consumption to 5.75 per cent by 2010.
Despite a 2007 progress report showing that it is likely to only achieve a biofuels share of 4.2 per cent in that year, signs of a sharp increase in demand in future cannot be ruled out as the world continues to look for environment-friendly sources of energy.
