In fact, the European Commission proposed in its 2007 "Energy Package" to step up its effort and demanded a mandatory target of 10 per cent by 2020.
The United States has also crafted an energy and climate-change policy that aims at reducing US petrol consumption by 20 per cent in the next ten years.
All the targets set will lead to an increased demand for biofuels, probable result being more open land being cleared to grow biofuel crops and surpluses diverted to produce bioenergy.

Original_Search_Title: Sudden Rise in the Price of Beans
Headline: Burundi: Sudden Rise in the Price of Beans
Body_Text:
Bujumbura — The price of beans has suddenly risen today in all markets throughout Bujumbura. A kilogramme of beans of the yellow variety previously cost 650 francs, but the price has now increased to 950 francs.
The price of the variety called Kirundo has also increased by 60 francs. Stocks of beans in the central markets are empty. Following harsh weather, the yield of this year's harvest of beans was unusually low.
The price of beans normally drops during this period of the year, the time in which the beans are harvested. Beans are now imported from Uganda. Tanzania has put some restriction on the exportation of its beans after Burundian authorities removed customs fees on food products imported from abroad.
This sudden rise in the price of beans has also caused the prices of other staple foods such as potatoes and cassava to rise slightly as the demand for them has increased. Beans constitute the primary source of nourishment for the average Burundian.

Original_Search_Title: FDI Opportunities are Local
Headline: Africa: FDI Opportunities are Local
Body_Text:
Africa has traditionally not been on the radar screen of foreign direct investors. The reasons include the “Balkanization” of the continent and hence its small markets, its weak infrastructure and an image problem: in much of the world Africa’s image is dominated by pictures of civil war, sickness and famine.
There is truth to these pictures, of course, for some parts of Africa. But, they obscure a number of positive developments throughout the continent that, typically, do not capture the headlines. During the past few years, a number of civil wars have ended and reconstruction has begun; Liberia, with the first democratically elected female president in Africa, Ellen Sirleaf-Johnson, is an example. Through the African Peer Review Mechanism, an increasing number of countries are submitting themselves to a rigorous process of evaluation; hopefully, those that perform well will be particularly rewarded, e.g. in the framework of the G-8 process. Economic growth during the past two years has been respectable, and quite strong in a number of countries. Partly, this is because more governments have recognized that the private sector has a central role to play when it comes to wealth creation and economic development. Presidents from across the continent would agree with what the President of Ghana had announced for his country, namely the arrival of a “golden age” for business.
All this has created a new environment for foreign direct investment (FDI). But it is more than that: the role of FDI in economic growth and development is now widely acknowledged, and all countries in Africa, without exception, are actively seeking to attract it. For that purpose, virtually all have liberalized their national regulatory framework for FDI, making it much more welcoming than it was, say, 15 years ago, by, e.g., opening sectors to such investment, reducing obstacles to the operation of foreign affiliates and offering a range of incentives. This is not to say, though, that the regulatory framework cannot be improved and, perhaps even more importantly, implemented more thoroughly; but, overall, it is certainly welcoming.
National improvements in the regulatory framework are complemented and made stronger by international investment agreements, especially bilateral investment treaties (BITs) that protect foreign investors. In fact, the BITs density for Africa (12 BITs per country) is the same as that of Latin America and the Caribbean. Most recent free trade agreements and/or association agreements with, especially, Europe and the US, also include investment chapters; the Cotonou agreement, in particular, contains strong provisions aimed at promoting FDI in Africa. Finally, the EU, Japan and the US provide preferential access to a wide range of goods and services produced in most African countries, making it attractive for foreign investors to set up shop there and export to the world’s most important markets.
But there is more: virtually all African countries are actively seeking to attract FDI by setting up investment promotion agencies to attract foreign investors, beginning with image building and ending with after-investment services. Moreover, the rise of multinational corporations from emerging markets – especially China, India, and South Africa, but also Russia and Brazil 1 – creates new sources of FDI. African countries benefit from competition among firms for profitable investment opportunities, a development already noticeable in the natural resource sector.
To be true, African IPAs vary widely in terms of the resources they have at their disposal, but they are making strides, often being helped by international organizations such as UNCTAD, UNIDO, FIAS and OECD.2
These good-news stories need to get out to adjust the image of Africa to the new reality. In the specific area of FDI, moreover, African countries need to make stronger efforts if they wish to attract more FDI (especially outside the natural resources sectors) and, equally important, to benefit from it as much as possible. To do so, they need to go below the national level and identify concrete investment opportunities at the provincial and even city levels. This is all the more important as, traditionally, foreign investors concentrate in capitals, often leaving other towns outside the mainstream of economic development. This situation is aggravated by a trend in toward urbanization – typically because people flee rural poverty. Many of them end up in slums. The challenge, then, becomes to make cities viable – and that means they need to develop a vibrant enterprise sector that provides employment and hence helps people escape from poverty, thus promoting the MDGs. This is precisely where the Millennium Cities Initiative (MCI) comes in.
The Millennium Cities Initiative
The Millennium Cities Initiative is one of a number of practical initiatives launched to help implement the Millennium Development Goals (MDGs), for halving extreme poverty, as adopted by the Heads of State and Governments at a UN Summit in 2000 and re-affirmed in September 2005 at another such Summit. Anchored in the recommendations of the Millennium Project, the independent advisory body to the UN Secretary-General which was led by Professor Jeffrey D. Sachs, the Earth-Institute based Millennium Cities Initiative assists seven cities in six African countries to become viable economic units. The cities are: Akure, Nigeria; Bamako - Segou, Mali; Blantyre, Malawi; Kisumu, Kenya; Kumasi, Ghana; and Louga, Senegal. The MCI was launched in 2006 with the strong support of the Governments of the participating countries, and it benefits from the partnership of UNDP, including its MDG Support Team, which supports African countries to develop MDG-based national development strategies. The Millennium Cities Initiative also partners actively with the Millennium Villages Project  to localize the MDGs and establish a practical framework for supporting the escape from extreme poverty in Africa.
In the broader context of City Development Strategies being prepared, the priority of the MCI is to help to create employment, stimulate enterprise development and foster economic growth in the Millennium Cities, especially by attracting FDI. The latter effort rests on three pillars:
1. Analyses to inform foreign investors.
The value of these materials depends, of course, not only on their quality but also, and very much so, on reaching potential investors, including through a modest investment promotion capacity to be developed in each city. Accordingly, MCI’s second pillar involves:
2. Dissemination to potential investors.
Several avenues are being pursued or will be pursued:
The key is of course whether actual investment projects will take place and contribute to economic growth and development. The efforts described so far can be expected to do that. However, MCI’s third pillar approaches this challenge directly:
3. The development of products for export.
Thanks to the cooperation of International Trade Centre, teams of experts will work with the Millennium Cities and Villages to identify, for each of them, one or two products that can be developed immediately for the international market, thus meeting the toughest competitiveness test in a globalizing world economy. In due course, additional products will be identified, including by building especially on the efforts of UNIDO and KPMG.
In a parallel but related endeavor, the Business Alliance Against Chronic Hunger, led by the World Economic Forum and with the participation of major multinationals, seeks to develop business opportunities near Kisumu, building village-to-world market agriculture-based value chains for important products of the district.
All these activities are concrete, practical and result-oriented, and they build on the new reality in Africa. They involve a broad spectrum of supporters. Apart from those already mentioned, the Gates Foundation, the Rockefeller Foundation and private donors are involved. In many ways, the MCI team in New York and its representatives in each of the Millennium Cities is really a team of conceptualizers and connectors that helps the cities and various organizations to stimulate economic growth in an urban context. In that spirit, the MCI will also develop a Handbook which, based on its experience, will provide a framework for other cities in the developing world so that this effort can be scaled up to contribute to the implementation of the MDGs.
Conclusions
So what does all of this add up to? In spite of all sorts of limitations, the climate for FDI in Africa, as well as its regulatory framework and institutional infrastructure, today are better than they have ever been. This is reflected in the growth of FDI flows to Africa which reached some $35 billion in 2006 and they are expected to stay at this historically all-time high at least until 2010. To attract even more FDI, especially in manufacturing and services, countries will need to improve their infrastructure and identify and promote investment opportunities outside their capital cities.
In the end, all investment is local – hence local opportunities need to be brought to the attention of investors, and the local regulatory and business environment needs to be competitive. This also implies a broader lesson when it comes to Africa: investors need to differentiate and look for investment opportunities country by country, city by city, sector by sector – opportunities exist, and for all you know, your competitors are already taking advantage of them!
Karl P. Sauvant is Co-Director of the Millennium Cities Initiative and Executive Director of the Columbia Program on International Investment (a joint program of the Columbia Law School and The Earth Institute at Columbia University).  He can be reached at karl.sauvant@law.columbia.edu.  I am grateful for comments by Susan Blaustein, Joerg Simon, Stephanie Kage, and John McArthur.
1 See Karl P. Sauvant, ed., The Rise of Transnational Corporations from Emerging  Markets: Threat or Opportunity? (forthcoming).
2   For a recent discussion of FDI promotion efforts in Africa, see Jeffrey D. Sachs, “The importance of investment promotion in the poorest countries,” in Economist Intelligence Unit and Columbia Program on International Investment  World Investment Prospects to 2010: Boom or Backlash? (London, UK: The Economist Intelligence Unit Ltd., 2006), pp. 78-81.

Original_Search_Title: Floods Claim Lives, Property in Bayelsa
Headline: Nigeria: Floods Claim Lives, Property in Bayelsa
Body_Text:
Yenagoa — The problem of flood has been a part and parcel of life for the people occupying the geographical entity known as Bayelsa state for years.
However, the people have been disturbed lately by this awesome power of nature which has claimed lives and property as well as destabilizing villages and towns in the some parts of the country.
For a state with the longest coastline in the country, measuring approximately 800 miles and lying below sea level, the fear of the people is understandable as it has suffered far more reaching losses to life and property in the past than one could ordinarily imagine. It is on record that it's landmass on the Atlantic fringe is being swallowed up gradually by the surging ocean.
While it's semi-diurnal tidal regime ensures two tidal floods and two low ebb tides within the course of each day, wave action along the coastline results in both depletion and loss of sediments in the beaches. A classical example of this is Koluama I and Koluama II where the offshore facility operated by Chevron was once on land but is now an offshore well.
Also, some of its riverine settlements such as Peretorugbene, Algbere, Ofoni, Ayamasa, Sabagriea, Igbedi, Adagbabiri, Famgbe, Ogu, Fortorugbene, Agbura, Aguadama-Epetiama, Oporoma, Ndoro, Tombia, Sagbama, Peremabiri, Elemebiri, and Asamabiri Agalabiri among others are on the verge of being washed away by marine erosion if urgent remedial steps are not taken to tackle this yearly menace.
Although this year, the flood is yet to reach its peak, residents in the riverine enclave of the state are already apprehensive of a likely deluge as the month of September approaches when the rivers often overflow their banks.
This reporter, who went round some of the riverine settlements of Amassoma, Tombia, Gbarantoru, Sagbama, Sabagriea, Opokuma, Kaiama and Odi among others that could be accessed by road, observed that the greatest single problem in the physical environment of these communities is that posed by water.
The very low elevations, decreasing to below sea level in some parts of the state brings about an annual ritual of flooding in which the rivers overrun their banks and floodwaters spread submerging entire settlements.
In all the communities, residential and public buildings including schools, churches town and community halls are often submerged by flood at the peak of the rainy season between the ember months of September and October leaving in its trails pain and sorrow to the locals as they are forced to flee their homes abandoning their economic means of livelihood.
The situation is, to say the least, pathetic as several thousands of people are usually rendered homeless and compelled to relocate to neighbouring upland communities in search of shelter.
Investigations revealed that the hardest hit segments of the population during this period of the year are not the fishermen and the conventional formal occupations but the farmers, palm wine tapers, and distillers.
In some cases, the entire yearly harvest of the farmers such as cassava, sugar cane, yams plantain, rice and other perishable farm produce are lost to the flood leading to a sharp increase in the prices of these products during the dry season due to absence of modern storage facilities.
Lamenting the plight of the people, an indigene of Sabagriea, Mr. Seyeifa Uzaka noted with concern the speed at which the water level is rising this year warning that this could spell disaster for communities along the waterways considering the situation in other parts of the country especially in the north.
Mr. Uzaka was particularly worried that if the dams in the northern parts were eventually opened to control the flood in the northern part, then the communities in the state along the route of the River Niger and its tributaries would suffer greater ecological crisis.
"The economic frustration, loss of lives and property in submerged communities and farmlands is one of the major source of anguish and despair in the area," he lamented adding "As you can see," pointing towards the usually busy waterfront, "the entire area is deserted not because of anything but it might interest you to know that here our people are racing against time in anticipation of the September flood. Unlike other times when there are sacred days people don't go to farm the reverse is the case today as the people are on a daily basis going to their farmland to harvest their crops so as to avert the resultant losses that often accompany the deluge."
He further noted with sadness that sometimes during the peak period of the flood season, some sections of the community are submerged destroying life long acquisition and industry that gives the inhabitants economic means of livelihood.
In some areas of Ekeremor, Sagbama, Southern Ijaw and fishing ports in Nembe and Brass, close to the sea, some of the inhabitants are forced to flee their homes to distant upland areas from the riverbanks, creeks and waterways.
state government embark on land reclamation, shore protection, sand filling and canalization.
This projects, though capital intensive may appear insurmountable but the benefits derivable far outweigh the financial implication. Communities such as Otuokpoti in Ogbia local government and Sagbama headquarters of Sagbama council area could have been lost communities today but for the shore protection device put in place some 27years ago by the then Chief Melford Okilo administration when Bayelsa was part of the old Rivers State.
This problem of flooding is not limited to the communities in the riverine areas alone as residents of Yenagoa the state capital are also subjected to the harrowing experience of battling flood whenever there is heavy downpour because of the poor drainage system.
This situation is further compounded by the illegal erection of structures along the right of way of the natural canals, some of which residents have since converted to dump sites.
During this period, many residents in the low land parts of the state are seen scooping water from their homes with some of the streets not accessible thereby forcing the people to construct makeshift wooden bridges to link their abodes. While those that owned cars abandon them along the few tarred roads at the mercies of thieves.
The problem not withstanding, it is the general consensus of analysts that, with the required political will and proper planning, the perennial menace of flooding could be tamed in the state, as there is no problem without a solution.

Original_Search_Title: Consistent Policy Formulation Needed to Save Local Rice Farmers
Headline: Ghana: Consistent Policy Formulation Needed to Save Local Rice Farmers
Body_Text:
Accra — The National Coordinator of the Ghana Trade and Livelihoods Coalition (GTLC), Mr. Ibrahim Akalbila has said reversing the current low performance of the rice sector and agricultural in general will require serious commitment from government through consistent policy formulation.
He said it is unfortunate government is not showing enough commitments through proper policy formulation despite several projections and promises to increase local rice production and curtail imports.
The National Coordinator said this in an interview at a roundtable discussion to launch a research document dubbed "background research on Impact of Rice Policies on Rice producers in Ghana" in Accra.
Mr. Akalbila in supporting his claim of inconsistency in policy formulation said although government promised to cut down imports of rice by 30 percent cent and to allow private entrepreneurs to fill the gap on the local, the same government also pledged to reduce the average import tariff rate from 13.6 to ten per cent by 2005.
This he said showed a clear inconsistency on the part of the government and in a recent 2000 UNCTAD report it indicated that poverty is increasing unambiguously in primary commodity producing economies that have adopted the most open trade regime.
Mr. Akalbila called on government to exhibit enough commitment through the improvement of the technological base of the Ghanaian rice farmer and also subsidize the cost of producing rice in the country.
Launching the report, the consultant who prepared the document , Dr. William Ahadzie said in recent years some policy measures that centre directly on research and production ,supply of inputs , credit , processing , land development amongst other things have been taken up by government with the view of improving local rice production in the country.
Dr. Ahadzie said it is estimated that out of the seven million peasant farmers in Ghana , about forty per cent cultivate rice; so the promotion of neo liberal policies by the government has had adverse effect on both the farmers' families and the number of poor people who depend on rice as a stable food.
"Increased production costs resulting from the withdrawal of subsidies on farm inputs and the free trade policies that have allowed the importation of cheap rice have pushed peasant rice farmers to the brink of destitution", he said
He said the report examines government policy actions over the last several years with the view of identifying gaps or weaknesses so as to provide a sound basis for civil society to engage with government to reverse the observable decline in the rice sector which has an adverse threat to rural livelihood.
Dr. Ahadzie in highlighting the report called on government as a matter of national interest to re-craft a new policy for the Ghanaian rice farmer since the current trade policy regime does not favour increased rice production by local farmers.
He urged the government to ensure that in drafting policies they should take into consideration the fact that operators in the rice sector are not a homogenous group, but differ in size holding, production types and ecological zones and by gender.
"Neutral policies leave the extremely vulnerable peasant farmers grossly disadvantaged. New policies that reflect the needs of these different groups are essential", he said.
Dr. Ahadzie said the new policies by government should aim at assisting the Ghanaian rice farmer to generate surplus and also capacity building should be a key consideration of such a policy.
He said also issues of gender should be considered in the drafting of a new policy since the unbalanced power relations between men and women and the aspects of normative culture that inhibit women's access to production resources namely, technology , land and credit, especially and secure market must be factored into the formulation of the new policy.
The report called on government to provide domestic support for farmers since improving rice production is contingent upon the availability of inputs.
The report indicated that any effort to raise the productivity of local rice farmers will require making more inputs such as fertilizers, tractors, agricultural extension services and combine harvesters available to the farmers at affordable prices and in a timely manner.
It said current technologies for the cultivation of rice are inefficient and outdated and must therefore be replaced with more modern ones; also a support package that combines improved technology with agricultural inputs and education of smallholder farmers on new farming techniques and improved methods is essential.
The document further indicated that there is the need to increase the number of extension workers and also the poor state of road and communication to food growing areas is essential since it has hindered increase in rice production.
The document also recommended to the government to put in place measures that would allow it to offer subsidies to Ghanaian farmer, since studies have shown that the removal of subsidies has had a negative effect on rice production in the country.
The document indicated that the introduction of subsidies on agricultural inputs; such as fertilizers and insecticides are crucial to increasing crop yields. This is because the withdrawal of these subsidies in recent years put them beyond the reach of the poor farmer.
Also it called on government to as a matter of urgency support pro-poor policies since efforts in expanding commodity production in the export sector; must not let them lose sight of the domestic food security problems of local farmers.
"The concentration of effort on export commodities at the detriment of food security implies that there is increasingly a depletion of basic staples such as cassava, groundnuts, maize, millet, sorghum etc. Concerted effort must be made by developing countries in making the IMF and the World Bank understand that, there is no alternative to providing agricultural subsidies in developing countries."
It also advocated for government to finance farmer groups in agricultural communities, since experience in the country and elsewhere in the developing world indicates that financing individual farmers is a cumbersome process.
Also it recommended that investing in the repackaging and processing of local food for the domestic and external markets will enhance the market prospects of goods from the country.
It attributed the current low quality of locally produced rice to the often archaic methods employed during the processing and it is for such a reason that there are still large quantities of unsold stocks of local rice in the country, while there is massive importation from other countries.
The document called on the government to introduce anti-dumping policies into the country, since most of the countries that export rice into the country have employed one or multiple forms of intervention for certain key areas of their economies.
Such policies according to the document have led to the success stories of agricultural production in the USA, Japan and Germany since these countries use some of these policy interventions to achieve specific ends; some of which have been able to develop internationally competitive industries which do not require any protection.
"Rice and cotton have been made globally competitive through this form of protection and there is a lesson in this for rice production in Ghana. The government has the option of using its agricultural tariff of up to 125 per cent to reduce agricultural imports like many countries in the world do. This helps to protect the weak and vulnerable rice farmers." It said
The document also urged the government to eliminate unfair trade practices in the World Trade Organization and its allied institutions and also called on civil society groups to intensify their campaign against unfair trade policies.
The Coordinator of the General Agricultural Workers Union (GAWU) Rice Project, Ms. Glowen Kyei-Mensah made a passionate appeal to the government to, as a matter of priority rehabilitate and bring to international standards all old rice mills like the Nasia in the Northern Region and the Afife in the Volta Region to ensure that locally produced rice become acceptable in both the domestic and international markets.
She called on the Ghana Rice Inter-professional Body (GRIB) not to limit their activities to only the Greater Accra and Volta regions, but to expand it to the Northern, Upper East and West since they also need the group to enhance their business.

Original_Search_Title: Rural Areas Have Potential
Headline: Zambia: Rural Areas Have Potential
Body_Text:
ZAMBIA's rural areas largely remain potential for all sorts of investment. There is enough land, water and other natural resources yearning to be tapped.
Peasant farmers have consistently contributed to Zambia's thriving agricultural sector. High yields keep coming from family efforts that have grown maize, cassava, millet, sorghum and other crops.
It is just fair that such an important human resource in rural areas enjoys good health by having easy and quick access to health care. There is need for people in remote areas to get drugs easily.
With all the criticisms that has gone to Zimbabwe, one of the major successes that the government there is credited for even to day is the investment in the health and education sectors.
There is an array of health centres and hospitals throughout that country. Along health outlets are schools within reach too.
The call therefore, by the First Lady, Maureen Mwanawasa to invest in rural health programmes is valid and must be supported.
Government efforts need to be supplemented to open up rural areas not only to make them attractive but healthy too. The population there keep rising, but the growing numbers of rural inhabitants cannot match with the health facilities available.
Many times patients have died on the way as they are being rushed to a health centre.
With the kind of economic investment now emerging in rural Zambia, the business world should spare a thought for community service.
This thought should come in a way of putting up a clinic or health centre where ever a new investment is being set up.
One of the good things Zambia Consolidated Copper Mines (ZCCM) would be remembered for is the tremendous support to community service.
Most of the schools and and clinics that are in former mine townships were the initiative of the mining giant.
Even when its operations were in a rural outlet, ZCCM ensured a school and a health centre was built nearby.
Much as we agree that the emphasis by private investment is profit, it is fair to plough some of the profits into community support.
For instance if an investor in Northern, Western or Eastern province builds a school or a clinic, the Government would gladly provide the staff.
Zambia's rural areas need support, the people there must remain healthy for them to contribute to the current economic resurgence programme.

Original_Search_Title: The 13 Percent Economic Growth Rate
Headline: Nigeria: The 13 Percent Economic Growth Rate
Body_Text:
Lagos — A fallout of the recently concluded retreat of newly appointed Federal Ministers, advisers, and sundry other appointees, a retreat which the media has dubbed " routine", was the announcement that the President had given a target of annual economic growth of 13 per cent and had challenged the appointees to aim for that rate of growth for the national economy.
And after I watched a tour de force performance on Channels TV Sunrise Daily, by my friend Dr Doyin Salami, I thought I should avert my mind to the challenges of attaining that level of growth. The point of departure logically in this discussion is to enquire regarding the realism of this level of target.
Let me say as a student of Strategic Planning that we should not dissipate our energies regarding the realism that underpinned this level of expectation of growth in the national economy. One thing that is certain about any target, and indeed this particular target for that matter, is that there is no way the economy would achieve the exact target except if that achievement is contrived. In the normal course, if things are allowed to run their course, what is certain is that we would not achieve that level of growth rate baring a miracle.
I say this because if this projected rate of growth is put in the context of historical developments in this regard, it would be recalled that we just recorded a growth in the neighborhood of 6 per cent rising from an average over the years of about 3 per cent and when it is recalled that even the Chinese economy which is considered exemplary in this connection recorded a growth of about 10 per cent, the extent of ambition which has informed this target would be better appreciated.
But there is nothing wrong with being ambitious. If anything, it demonstrated the sort of passion which the President has for the country, and may God make his desire in this regard come through, Amen.
To stand a chance of achieving this rate of economic growth all hands must be on deck and what I would do in the rest of this paper would be to paint a broad brush of the import of this challenge as it pertains to various sectors of the economy. From the perspective of Fiscal policy there is the need to continue to pursue macro-economic stability. The challenge in this regard is that of continuity of policies and to ensure that where there would be a change such changes could be predicted.
It is reassuring that the President has served ample notice that he would play by the rules of the game. You cannot indeed talk of democracy where the rule of law is not the order of the day. Where the rule of law is not present you would then have arbitrariness, the rule of preference of the individual in power and that sort of governance cannot be honored with the description of democracy.
Before the President amply demonstrated his predilection for the observance of the law of the land, I was intrigued when the Secretary to the Federal Government in his rationalization of the reasoning that underpinned the reversal of the increase in the VAT rate said that the budget which is to all intents and purposes a law was formulated based on a VAT rate of 5 per cent, it was only proper that that rate was respected until the budget runs its course. And that line of reasoning was most instructive.
At the center, and for that matter, at the level of sub national governments, the budget should be the blue print for running the economy. And that is why most governments of the world take the preparation of their budget seriously. One of the problems we encounter in this regard is that we have not demonstrated convincingly the primacy and sanctity of the budget in running the affairs of the country.
The unfortunate impression that has been made hitherto is that the budget is at best an annual ritual which to be identified with best practice across the world the country has to endure. All you need to do is to enquire on the record of government in this connection! The worse offender in this regard is our attitude to the implementation of the capital budget. In fact we celebrate as success when we manage to implement more than 50 per cent of the budget.
We would advise that we get back to the three year rolling plan as this approach would end the trauma of abandoned projects which litter the national landscape and better accommodate projects which have an implementation period of more than one year. And where we have articulated other development plans, they should be implemented using the budget. The thrust would be not to implement, not even to countenance, anything that has not been included in the budget. Some of us have argued that because of the primacy of the budget, there should be administrative linkage between the Planning office and the Budget.
This is why at the level of the ministries both departments are often under the same authority as the budget should be the means for the implementation of all plans. Other budget details like the recognition of revenues, that is, the oil price based fiscal rule, and the extent of deficit and manner of its funding which the previous administration would appear to have gotten right should be continued with.
We must also emphasize the need to make sure that all expenditures count. The due process for incurring expenditures should be continued with and the effect of the activities of the EFCC in sending strong signals to those with the responsibility for managing the national resource that they would be called to account for their stewardship is salutary in this regard.
The monetary authorities have risen to the challenge of the new Act governing their activities. And as the Governor of the Central Bank correctly identified, the core function of the Central Bank is the maintenance of price stability. This stability would also relate to the maintenance of stability of the rates of exchange. The Governor in announcing the strategic initiative regarding the redenomination of Naira did rightly observe that the issuance and maintenance of the value of the legal tender is the responsibility of the Central Bank. In appreciation of the import of this responsibility the Bank served notice of its determination to commence inflation targeting.
We have had cause to argue in the past that in appreciation of this scope of the Bank's responsibility, that it should commence the process of divesting itself of the supervisory functions of the financial system. What is also important is that the reform agenda which the Central Bank had outlined for the financial system must be allowed to run its course. We should avoid unhealthy reversals and changes which would undermine the ability to stay the course. It obviously goes without saying that the latitude which the Bank would continue to enjoy would to a large extent be circumscribed by its record of performance. Where we have charged authorities with the attainment of any goals we must not do anything to constrain them unduly otherwise we give them alibis!
To attain the projected level of growth we must continue with efforts to attain some balance to the economic base of the economy. The present situation whereby the Country is overly dependent on the oil sector is insalubrious to say the least and it is not as if this fact is not know by all.
What is worrisome is the lack of progress in the attempt to address this imbalance. We must continue to make sure that oil continues to play its key role in the economy by ensuring that all negative factors such as the unrest at the Niger Delta and its impact on the nations productive capacity in this regard is addressed holistically as is being currently proposed. Agriculture continues to be the mainstay of the economy and must continue to receive deserved attention. In fact it is right to note that there is still scope for improvement in this regard particularly from consideration of the vast expanse of land which had not been put under productive use. We should continue to extend subsidy advisedly particularly with regard to the provision of extension services.
There are a number of initiatives in this regard which should be reviewed to ensure that their implication for the welfare of the citizens, which must be paramount, is taken on board. In this connection one can think of the cassava initiative which tended to deny our citizens of certain basic provisions. The progress which had been recorded with regard to the solid minerals must be continued with all determination so that the potentials in this area are fully tapped. In all this sectors we must avoid costly reversals except well informed.
There is the need to focus on the availability of essential infrastructure. In this regard one appreciates the President's focus on the energy sector. This sector was one area where all the promise made by the immediate past dispensation was kept in the breech.
The availability of requisite energy is paramount for the health and competitiveness of the national economy. Of late there seems to be an improvement in power supply. I don't know if this has anything to do with the area of Lagos where I live, that is Lekki phase one, or is it due to the high level of water reservoir because of the rains. But if the Nigerian economy is to be competitive the question of steady and regular availability of power supply must be taken for granted. We must guide against and exacerbation of the misery index as a result of incessant increases in the price of petroleum products.
-Chizea wrote from Lagos

Original_Search_Title: Fragile Historic Truce Holds Out
Headline: Uganda: Fragile Historic Truce Holds Out
Body_Text:
Kampala — In a freezing air-conditioned conference room, delegates still wore sleepy faces - the result of having spent the night haggling. But though drowsy and tired, they still put pen to paper on the August 26, 2006 truce document. 365 days later, this fragile truce still holds, raising the hopes of hundreds of thousands.
The deal followed an intense month of negotiation, walk-outs and reneging by the parties to the talks. "We could not easily agree to a truce before getting assurances that they could not use it to regroup, re-arm and reorganise," said UPDF publicist, Maj. Felix Kulayigye. But information availed to Sunday Monitor indicates completely different reasons. At the time, the army's 5 Division was hot on the heels of two of the five LRA ICC indictees.
UPDF had intelligence that Raska Lukwiya was locked-down in Mucwini Sub-county, Kitgum, while Dominic Ongwen was trapped in Pader. These were high value leadership targets. Desperate to save them, LRA's second-in-command, Vincent Otti had declared a unilateral ceasefire on August 4, 2006 and asked the Ugandan government to reciprocate.
Dr Rugunda stalled, saying they were waiting to see the effect on the ground .This prompted the rebel delegation to walk out the next day, August 5, 2006.
Seven days later, the rebels were convinced to return by chief mediator and Southern Sudan's Vice President, Riek Machar.
By then troops from 5 Division were within reach of Lukwiya, who they engaged and killed in Obem Village, 7km southwest of Mucwini.
Since then there have been several alleged violations of the truce by either side. This is what the parties say: "We simply ignored all provocations by the LRA for the sake of peace," said Maj. Kulayigye.
But LRA's Chief negotiator Martin Ojur says: "We declared a unilateral truce first, they killed our people; they have been using the media to attack us. We chose to restrain our forces for the sake of peace," Ojur said in a telephone interview from Juba.
UPDF's alleged truce violations
August 2006 -UPDF forces kill LRA's Maj. Gen. Raska Lukwiya
September 2006 -UPDF attack rebels, killing three fighters in Amuru District
September 2006- UPDF surrounds LRA who were assembling at Owiny-Ki-Bul, southern Sudan
September 2006 -UPDF led a convoy of diplomats and journalists to Owiny-Ki-Bul against the rules
October 2006 -UPDF troops in southern Sudan arrested LRA negotiator, Rei Achama who was delivering food to LRA rebels.
October 2006 -UPDF surround LRA fighters who retaliate by engaging resulting in the death of army Captain Sam Muagrura of 91 Battalion deployed in southern Sudan, 115km North West of Owiny-Ki-Bul.
November 2006- UPDF restricts movement of LRA rebels while leaving northern Uganda to Owiny-Ki-Bul.
February 2007 -Hostile propaganda by President Museveni who allegedly called the LRA the Devil's Resistance Army at a Nairobi press conference
February 2007 -UPDF kills two LRA soldiers near the Sudan-Uganda border
May 2007- UPDF soldiers along the River Nile blocked LRA rebels from crossing from Eastern Equatoria to Western Equatoria
Source LRA
LRA's alleged truce violations
October 2006- LRA attempt to hijack a boat in Mangala, southern Sudan but are repulsed by southern Sudan forces
October 2006 -LRA ambush UPDF troops of 91 Battalion, killing Capt. Sam Mugarura near the banks of River Nile
October 2006 -LRA stage multiple road ambushes on Juba-Nisitu, Juba-Gumbo and Nisitu-Jabuleni roads, killing 43 people and burning 12 vehicles. They also ambushed UPDF Intelligence Officer, Capt. Patrick Opira on Nisitu-Gumbo Road leaving one solder wounded.
November 2006 -LRA uproot cassava from civilians' gardens in Magwi, southern Sudan.
December 2006- LRA stage five ambushes on Torit-Korienglish Road, Jabul-Mugile Road leaving five civilians dead and four wounded. They also ambushed UPDF liaison officer, Richard Otto on Juba-Aru Road.
December 2006 -LRA rob business people near Boma and Tore and abduct an unspecified number of people.
January 2007-LRA stage an ambush in Kit and Manga, in southern Sudan wounding eight civilians and raided Luru Village for food and disarmed Southern Sudan soldier of his pistol and Submachine gun.
February 2007- UPDF 509 Brigade intercept a group of LRA rebels attempting to cross from southern Sudan into Uganda and kill two fighters.
April 2007- Seven civilian vehicles are ambushed at Kit, Aru Junction, Nimule-Mugule roads in southern Sudan killing 19 people.
May 2007- LRA commander Thomas Kwoyelo while crossing from Eastern Equatoria to Western Equatoria looted 100 goats and cows in Lariya and Adruba villages. Unspecified number of people killed. Rebels also raid the home of southern Sudan's Information Minister Samson Kwaje and disarm 13 militia men
Source UPDF

Original_Search_Title: The Passing Scene
Headline: Nigeria: The Passing Scene
Body_Text:
Now, what is with Professor Chukwuma Soludo? The man is too much, abi na weitin? That stunt (I mean, feat) of putting our banks on a reliable footing could have sent a more energetic man to the couch for, at least, a little bit of chilling down, or reeling in, or whatever bankers do when they need to relax. But the man just seems to keep stepping on it all the way down to the floorboard. We are hardly getting used to the new currency before he has called us out again for this new exercise called 're-denomination." And this promises to be quit a drill.
The naira, we understand, will still hold on to its value but what you see is not precisely what you get. This is because we would have "dropped two zeros", or moved by two decimals to the left" Why drop those hapless zeroes? Because they were becoming too heavy, that's why. And why move two decimal points to the left? Because those decimal points were on what the zeros rested and, in any case, we have been moving them to the right somewhat unconscionably in the recent past, powered by the swift passage of a visitation called SAP!
By the way, did you ever understand SAP? If you never totally got where it was coming from, at least I am certain that you got where it was going through, that is, your pocket The only thing that was seriously wrong with our economy was that we were neglecting other sources of economic development for the berserk exploitation of just one- oil. The fear was injected into our psyche that we needed to do something about this, namely, adjust the structure of our economy from a selling nation to a buying nation. Or, clearer still, reverse our time-honoured and natural role of the supplier of crude products to the receiver of manufactured goods. The bait was in the promise that we would be on the way to becoming an "industrialized" nation; the speciousness was in the twisted meaning of "industrialized".
One more lever, and that was the bogey of "inflation". The inflation in the economy, we were informed, was rising. Now, that is a spectre that sets the average Nigerian's heart galloping even faster than the rate at which any market can experience the undulating movement of an unbalanced economy. SAP, we were told would drown the inflation that was beginning to emerge. But even then, our naim was stronger than the pound sterling - twice the value of the US dollar. My tailor drove a Volkswagen beetle, which SAP later priced out of the market in no time. My messenger owned a motorcycle, an okada, which is now the favourite passenger vehicle in almost every city. You may not understand SAP, but your pocket knows a hole when it sees one.
The truth has been living with us these several years. SAP joined inflation and, hand-in-hand, they soared over the land. We have not become "industrialized", nor did we need SAP to be. We already had the Groundnut and Cotton industries in the North; the Cocoa and Cola-nut industry in the SouthWest, Cassava in the East; Palm products in the Delta and other natural raw materials that were only waiting to be developed into full-scale industries that would have propelled our economy sky-high, powered by the petro-dollars that was beginning to pour into the economy. That, in fact, was the fear of the Western nations. They held a meeting about it, and later emerged as the very promoters of SAP. It did not just happen.
One is disturbed by the similarity of the promises of SAP with those of the proposed redenomination of the naira. They say the "redenom" (let's make it nice and short) would improve the exchange rate of the naira; that it would promote export prospects; that it would bring down inflation. But, as a matter of fact, there is no indication that it would fulfill any of these heartfelt desires. Someone, I think rightly, called it a "mere compression of the currency".
If it is no more than that, why then are the Western powers again rushing in from the touchlines with such profuse offers of help and co-operation already? I smell a rat. That was how they supported SAP, and see where it has brought us? We are even exporting our crude oil to be refined for us so we can buy it at rates mounting higher and higher every day. "That is the sense in SAP".
Some of those who supported SAP and coined that slogan are now sounding a note of caution.
They already know the score. Others, who like to be counted among the "economic" avant-garde, are too cowardly to question what they probably, properly, do not understand. It is "The King's New Clothes", all over again. If only they would leave the stage for the "dyed-in-the wool" experts like Isaac Aluko-Olokun, whose voices we need to hear more clearly in these days of wonder.
Others have wondered if the entire venture of the "redenom" was not a sort of "hand-out", after all. Could one and the same mind have been cogitating on the project of the new currency, while formulating the policy of "redenomination", with the one major exercise coming so swiftly on the heels of the other? At best, it may only be accounted an afterthought. Of course, there are some people who thrive on what has been dubbed "restless intellectuality". The subjects here, however, affect the lives of not only this generation of Nigerians, but also even those of children yet unborn.
We should not be subjected to an apparently reckless intellectuality. One is still trying to learn more about the so-called proposal from whatever source. It isn't as though we could reject it. When it is proposed by the Central Bank of Nigeria, you can take it as already disposed, anyway.
They burnt those books. The books had been rescued from some arch-criminals who had, first of all, taken them to another West African nation where many copies were illegally printed. They were being smuggled back into Nigeria when they were intercepted. No doubt, the smugglers are felons, but the books remained as chaste as the meanings of the printed word in them. They should not have been burned. What the law prescribes is that a criminal should no benefit from the proceeds of his crime. The students who created the high demand which the books had been, howbeit, inappropriately provided to meet, were still waiting for them with a yearning yet to be satisfied.
You can do it with cocaine; you can do it with heroin. But. for goodness sake, you do not burn books. Those who did have a very low place in history. In this particular case, one would suggest that the tomes should have been returned to the original publishers or any library, where they could still serve the original purpose of enlightening the mind and ennobling the soul.
A good question has been asked about the "redenom" proposal. How would it help our oil industry? That is very important, and that is not being facetious, believe me. Any major project that is sure to have some serious effect on the economy can only be sensibly evaluated in the light of that impact. among other factors. No one seems to have a clear answer to that question, just as no one has come up yet with a widely accepted solution to the crisis in the Niger Delta, the turbulent womb of our nation's wealth.
The unrest started as a movement against environmental degradation, and the deprivation of a good life for the indigenes, who thus suffered through the exploitation of the resources which enriched other parts of the nation. The people of the area had a lot to be angry about as, through the course of the patently justified agitation, they also lost some of their leaders to "judicial" killings along the way. They subsequently found a very forceful way of attracting attention to their cause when the practice of kidnapping foreign workers was introduced. The effectiveness of this act increased their media stature, but also pointed the way to a gold mine in ransom payments. So the kidnaping was extended to small children, indigines and other targets that are far removed from political connections which such escapades had at the beginning.
And so, the former Governor of one of the States in the area was made the Vice-President of the Nation. One of the militant leaders, who was in detention, was freed. But while the people were still basking in the hope of good things to come, the sore burnt out red and raw in the form of an open gunfire encounter between rival groups, who turned the streets of a normally quiet town into a roaring battlefield. And, of course, some lives were reported lost.
The State Governor has imposed a curfew on the city of Port Harcourt that to all intents and purposes, is almost like an occupied city. He has opted to play host to soldiers for the next twelve months. On the contrary, some elders and acknowledged leaders of the area prefer tougher measures that would be no less than a declaration of emergency, to hand over the State administration over to the Federal government for a specified period. More ominous are the accusations of being at the root of the unrest flung at some of the politicians, including the State Governor himself, as the leaders of the warring groups, or "cults", as they have been described. So, where do we go from here?
That question is seriously directed at you. We are all in this together. What is your view about how to got out of this crisis- not who is right, but WHAT is right? Keep the texts short and sincere, please. You may have the answer. Or would you rather tackle how the "redenom" would affect the naira?
Here, is a memo to the Police Commissioner of Lagos State., M.D. Abubakar.
Re: Information to the Police by members of the Public.
Sir, permit me to congratulate you on the rapid strides you have taken within the short period of your assumption of office, in fighting against crime in the municipality. It is not an easy task. You will find it the more frustrating when even the members of the public you are striving to serve sometimes turn round to upbraid you for being high-handed, or insensitive, or proud, etc. (A gentleman by the name of Nuhu Ribadu, who may not be a complete stranger to you, could help you with some tutorials on how to deal with this particular problem since, under a deluge of such indictments, he has been able to remain focused.)
For instance, this touches slightly on the complaints that some policemen have been molesting some young ladies, or females really, about the way the way in which these women have chosen to dress, or rather, expose themselves. Do not mind them, the complainants, that is. They should indeed be called "Area Girls". Their appearance is not just provocative, but also prurient by design. There are parts of the human body, both male and female, identified as "private" parts, and they should be restricted to private viewing. Any deliberate act of uncovering these areas on the public domain is no less than "indecent exposure" and may be prosecuted as such.
This page is not taking a high moral note just for the sake of being seen as decent. This issue has been the talk in schools and colleges, and also in churches and other religious societies. The Police did not invent it .They only need to exercise some discretion in holding down what has escaped judicious restraint from all other institutions of public decency.
So, do not be intimidated, sir. The good Lord gave women big boobs and large bums to attract, in good circumstances, men who are interested, not to distract poor follows who are going about in the pursuit of serious business.
PS: Could you please do more publicity about the telephone numbers for Rapid Response?
We would suggest, along with other efforts, their prominent display at Police Stations forthwith. Thank you, sir.
Time out.

Original_Search_Title: Who Cares for the Aged?
Headline: Uganda: Who Cares for the Aged?
Body_Text:
Mukono — She is widowed and senile. At 95, Ms Bena Nakazi lives in complete uncertainty about what tomorrow may bring. Impoverished is what best describes the helpless old lady's life.
But regardless of the suffering that she has gone through, Ms Nakazi has had to persist. She doesn't live in total despair, as she has had to live each day the way it comes to avoid stressing herself every now and then.
And although some days might bring with them lots of gloom, the old lady says she is not about to give up. "I have always tried to find a way around my troubles," she says.
What constitutes the biggest part of the old lady's life is the dilemma of what to eat each day that sets in. And the moment she's able to find food, she considers her day a success. When Daily Monitor visited her, she was preparing a simple dish for lunch at her home down in the remote village of Kasanga, Ssi Sub-County in Mukono District.
Perched on a piece of Hessian square at the entrance of her back door, Ms Nakazi cheerfully peels the only cassava root that her small garden has luckily made available.
It is this single cassava root that ought to see her through this day and the day after, before she starts looking for food elsewhere. On her left are two cups, from which she sips what she would like to call tea.
But in actual sense, it's mere hot water with neither a beverage, nor sugar mixed in it.
"Hot water isn't good for humankind. But we have nothing to do to warm our bodies like on this cold morning," she says, "So we tend to fool our stomachs with hot water that what we are pouring in is tea."
Ms Nakazi says she has children but they do not help her. She says, instead they go to her home to get the food she grows.
Ms Nakazi is a reflection of the lives of hundreds of thousands of elderly people in Uganda. They are facing challenges at the time when they are powerless to make any meaningful change.
The 2002 National Census puts their number at 1.5 million. Although the figure is quite small in comparison to the other age sets, they are the most affected people in the country.
Recently the State Minister for the Elderly and Persons with Disabilities, Mr Suliaman Madada, revealed that the government would start paying a monthly allowance to the elderly. The inactive poor old people would get Shs18,000, plus Shs10,000 if they are looking after disadvantaged children.
Donors will fund the pilot project, which will start this financial year in six districts.
Surprisingly, there is no policy to protect the elderly. Most of them die in the most inhumane way due to lack of health care and basic necessities.
Ms Deborah Kaijuka, the Executive Director of Uganda Reach the Aged Association, says the problem of the elderly suffering originates from the fading traditional social support system.
"People no longer live in homesteads or extended families where young adults supported their elderly parents. Instead many young adults prefer living in nucleated families," Ms Kaijuka says.
A homestead was like a chain of three segments where the active young adults were in the middle supporting both their parents and their own children.
Ms Kaijuka explains that the system has changed and now the young adults have moved to urban centres seeking better life and jobs, leaving the elderly people in the rural areas.
HIV/Aids
Sister Monica of the Good Samaritans, an NGO supporting and managing Bakateyamba Home for the Elderly at Nalukolongo in Kampala, says the new challenge facing the aged is HIV/Aids. "Many elderly people who would have retired from work are now shouldering the burdens of orphans of their children who died of Aids," Sister Monica says.
Ms Kaijuka concurs with Sister Monica. "Aids has eliminated the active middle age group thus breaking the link of support," she says. "Even some older people are infected with HIV/Aids since the time the disease broke out, many were still in the 40s and were sexually active." Ms Kaijuka says without a policy, it becomes very difficult to mobilise funds from donors yet the donors don't consider the elderly when giving grants.
She says the resources they get for the elderly are just 'a drop in the ocean'.
According to a draft of the National Policy on Older Persons, there will be an institutional framework in both the central and local government funding of national projects aimed at helping the elderly people. Unless a policy on the elderly is drawn, older people like Ms Nakazi will live and die in poverty.

Original_Search_Title: Country Loses $7.6bn to Non-Exploitation of Cassava
Headline: Nigeria: Country Loses $7.6bn to Non-Exploitation of Cassava
Body_Text:
Jos — Nigeria loses over seven billion dollars due to her inability to tap the full potential of cassava in the country, National Cassava Exporters' Association zonal president has said.
Retired Air Commodore A. Okponachi who is the North Central zonal president of the association, disclosed this when its members paid a visit to the Plateau state governor, Jonah Jang at Government House, Jos.
Okponachi stated that cassava production could reduce poverty and create employment and solicited the support of the state governm-ent towards encouraging farmers to take up the venture.
The association boss, who presented a document on the potentialities of cassava farming to the governor, suggested that the state government organize an economic summit on cassava production for all stakeholders.
In his remark, Governor Jang urged Nigerians to pursue aggressive marketing of what are produced in the country, stating that Nigeria cannot continue to allow other countries to dictate how she will market her products.
While noting that more farmers were taking up cassava production, the governor enjoined the association to explore avenues through which the product can be better marketed. The Plateau state chief executive assured that government would work out strategies to protect farmers from the sharp practices of middlemen. He said the administration recognized the plight of farmers who he noted were often exploited by middlemen.
"We will ensure those who till the soil get value for their labour," Jang added.

Original_Search_Title: Zim to Buy Country's Maize at Reduced Price
Headline: Zambia: Zim to Buy Country's Maize at Reduced Price
Body_Text:
Ndola — GOVERNMENT has accepted to export 100,000 tonnes of maize to Zimbabwe at a reduced price for fear of the grain going to waste.
Agriculture and Cooperatives Minister, Ben Kapita said in an interview yesterday that the 100,000 tonnes was from the last farming season and Government with its counterparts in Zimbabwe had been negotiating on the prices for sometime now.
Mr Kapita said the maize would be exported at about US$195 to US$200 per tonne, the amount he said authorities in Zimbabwe were comfortable with.
"We have reduced the prices because we want to be competitive and we want everything to go. If we continue keeping it, the maize will get rotten," Mr Kapita said.
Zambia had recorded a surplus of 250,000 tonnes but in additional to last season's crop, there was currently 300,000 tonnes of maize for export.
Mr Kapita said he also took advantage of the just ended 27th Southern African Development Community (SADC) Heads of State and Government summit to discuss with his counterparts in member countries the best ways of helping nations with food deficits.
From the summit, Mr Kapita said, it was gratifying that member countries had recognised that Zambia and Malawi were able to export 10,000 tonnes each to hunger stricken neighbours.
Zambia exported the 10,000 tonnes of maize through the World Food Programme (WFP).
Mr Kapita said the summit noted that Zambia, Tanzania and Malawi had recorded maize surplus and therefore prodded them to export to countries that had deficits.
Meanwhile, the Food Reserve Agency (FRA) will continue purchasing maize and other designated crops until the end of September even in areas where targets have been met.
FRA public relations officer, Mwamba Siame said in a statement released in Lusaka yesterday that targets had been met in Mazabuka, Chadiza, Chipata, Sinda, Chisamba, Mwense, Kazungula and Gwembe districts.
The agency has so far purchased 251,750 tonnes of maize out of the targeted 400,000 tonnes, 440 tonnes of rice out of the targeted 1,200 tonnes and 10 tonnes of cassava out of the targeted 2,400 tonnes.

Original_Search_Title: Teso Faces Famine Threat Due to Floods
Headline: Uganda: Teso Faces Famine Threat Due to Floods
Body_Text:
Soroti — OVER 52 villages and hundreds of people in Teso Sub Region could face starvation in the next few months following prolonged rains and floods that have destroyed crops.
An assessment of the damages, compiled by respective disaster preparedness coordinators in the affected areas indicate that the low lying areas mainly in Amuria, Katakwi, Soroti, Kumi and Bukedea districts have been flooded.
The most affected areas in Katakwi include; Orungo, Abarilela, Obalanga, Morungatuny, Acowa and Wera, while areas in Amuria district are Usuk, Omodoi Magoro, Ngariam, Kapujan.
In Soroti, the worst hit areas are Katine, Kamuda, Tubur, Arapai, Atiira and Gweri sub counties. The prolonged rains which started in April up to date, have caused water logging in most low lying districts.
Available reports indicate that previous floods experienced in the region in 1962, 1966, 1978 and 2003 usually affecting the low lying areas within 2 to 5 km off the shores of Lakes Kyoga and Bisina lakes.
According to the report, most feeder roads in the region have also been affected and rendered impassable by the rains.
The roads in Amuria and Katakwi have been hit most by the heavy rains.
It further indicates that a number of acreages of fields have been submerged for a considerable period, leading to the destruction of crops like cassava, sweet potato and groundnuts.
Soroti district disaster preparedness coordinator, Sam Oinya told Daily Monitor in an interview that there is a generally reduction in agricultural activities.
"Some crops, especially cassava and groundnuts were prematurely harvested but the quality was poor for human consumption. In a few months time there will be shortage of food and there is need for an immediate intervention," Mr Oinya said.
He said in some areas such as Tubur in Soroti District, some livestock had drowned while in Katakwi some of the kraals had been filled with water.
The water logging has also contaminated water sources like spring wells and boreholes, forcing residents in the affected areas to use unsafe water.
"A number of pit latrines have collapsed while others have been filled up with water rendering them unsafe and leading to open waste disposal," Soroti district LC5 chairperson Stephen Ochola said.
He said the rains have caused water borne diseases like diarrihea in the affected areas.
The assessment report relased yesterday also indicates that higher incidences of malaria, cough and eye infections among others have been reported at health centres nearest to the affected areas.
Amuria District LC5 councillor for Wera Sub-County, Robert Adiama Ekaju said about 100 families in his area have been forced out of their homes and have had to spend nights at nearby churches s and schools because of floods. Residents are praying that the rains stop and they return to normal life.

Original_Search_Title: Govt to Tarmac Lwakhakha - Mbale Road
Headline: Uganda: Govt to Tarmac Lwakhakha - Mbale Road
Body_Text:
Mbale — President Yoweri Museveni has promised to have the Mbale-Lwakhakha border road tarmacked to ease transport and marketing of agricultural products within and across the border to Kenya.
Speaking during celebrations to mark the international youth day at Mbale sports ground on August 12, Mr Museveni said the programme would start in the next financial year. He said construction works on the 41km stretch that connects Mbale to the border with Kenya is in fulfillment of the pledge he made during the 2001 presidential campaigns.
"The government has delivered on most of the promises made during the campaigns and now there are these small pledges like tarmacking Mbale-Lwakhakha Road. This will be done soon. I think beginning next financial year," said Mr Museveni, adding that the government has embarked on construction and rehabilitation of major roads in the country.
Besides easing transportation of products like beans, maize, cassava, matooke, irish potatoes, tomatoes, onions and cabbages that make areas like Bumbo, Buwabwala and Bupoto food baskets for eastern Uganda, heavy trucks that have been going all the way to Malaba and Busia borders will be able to use the route.
Mr Charles Nasimolo, the LC5 councillor for Buwabwala, welcomed the move saying the tarmacking of Mbale-Lwakhakha road was long over due because it connects the region to Kenya where business in agricultural products has been booming.
"One of the things that had slumped business in agricultural products across the border with Kenya is the bad road network, especially on the Ugandan side but now with its construction, Uganda is likely to benefit from this trade across the borders," he said. Lwakhakha border is the shortest route to Kitale, Bungoma, Kiminini, Kimilili and other areas in western Kenya that also deal in agriculture.

Original_Search_Title: Returnees Among Thousands Displaced By Floods
Headline: Uganda: Returnees Among Thousands Displaced By Floods
Body_Text:
Kampala — Tens of thousands of people have been displaced by floods after heavy rain submerged whole villages in eastern Uganda, a senior government official said.
Musa Ecweru, assistant minister for relief, disaster preparedness and refugees, described the situation as "catastrophic".
"The region has had rains for the last two months and homes have been submerged, bridges swept away and roads have become impassable," he said. "Many people have sought refuge in churches and areas of raised ground, while others have left the affected areas to live with relatives."
In the sub-countyof Abarilela, 30,000 people who were displaced after Lord's Resistance Army raids in the area in 2003 and had just returned home were displaced again by the floods, he said.
"These people had just started cultivating their own food. The floods have again displaced them and all the food crops have been destroyed," he said. "Crops like cassava, millet and groundnuts that were due for harvest are rotting in the gardens."
The most affected districts include Amuria, Katakwi, Soroti, Kumi and Bukedea, where some homes have been under water.
The minister said some cases of cholera have been reported. "Plastic sheeting, food, medicine and mobile toilets are urgently needed to avert an epidemic. We are working with the health ministry to mitigate the problems," he said.
Kumi Member of Parliament Agnes Akiror Egunyu said that 2,000 families had become homeless in Ongino sub-county in Kumi district.
"We need immediate intervention in the form of food, medicine and shelter," she said. "People are living in schools and health centres and will not have any food until the next planting season."
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Country Has Surplus Maize for Other SADC Nations
Headline: Southern Africa: Country Has Surplus Maize for Other SADC Nations
Body_Text:
Ndola — ZAMBIA has the capacity to supply maize to all Southern African Development Community (SADC) countries with food deficits, Agriculture and Cooperatives Deputy Minister, Albert Mulonga has said.
In an interview in Lusaka on Friday where he expressed his concern over the food deficit in the region as observed on last week SADC Heads of State and Government summit, Mr Mulonga said his ministry was targeting to buy through the Food Reserve Agency (FRA) 400,000 tonnes of maize this year.
"Currently, Zambia has 150,000 tonnes of maize in its strategic reserves from the last farming season," he said.
He was optimistic that the 150,000 tonnes would sustain the country. However, the tonnage needed to be raised from 150,000 to 200,000 to enable the surplus to be exported to needy countries in the region.
The millers have been allowed to bridge the deficit gap by exporting part of their maize while farmers had been urged to grow more maize.
Mr Mulonga said the ministry was also looking at the promotion of cassava as another alternative to mitigate the deficit in the SADC region.
He said farmers would be encouraged to grow more of the crop, which would ground into cassava meal and later exported to other countries.
To mitigate future shortages of maize as a result of changing weather patterns, Mr Mulonga said his office would promote irrigation nationwide.
"Apart from maize and cassava, rice will be promoted to increase food production. So, our farmers should grow more rice and we will support them because we want to export rice in future," he said.
He pledged his ministry's support to the sector by giving technical skills on rice production to enable the local farmers meet the export demands of the crop.

Original_Search_Title: Floods Displace Thousands in East
Headline: Uganda: Floods Displace Thousands in East
Body_Text:
Kampala — THOUSANDS of people have been rendered homeless in parts of Teso following torrential rains that have pounded the region in the past days and weeks.
Most sub-counties in the districts of Kumi, Soroti, Bukedea, Amuria and Katakwi are deserted.
The rains have also hit Bududa, Bukwo, Mbale and Kapchorwa districts in eastern Uganda, with devastating effects.
A New Vision team, that toured parts of Teso over the weekend, found many roads impassable and bridges washed away. The team visited some of the worst hit areas in Amuria district, Wera, Abarilela, Asamuk and Acowa sub-counties.
It found that the floods had destroyed most crops in what is supposed to be a harvesting season, and had contaminated springs with human excreta.
"All our pit latrines in Abarilela IDP camps have been filled up. Water is now flowing down into the stream where people go for drinking water," said Abarilela sub-county chairman Max Ebalu. "We have advised the affected people to move to the nearby schools, health centres and churches."
He appealed for emergency supplies such as tents, food, mosquito nets and drugs.
The New Vision found about 500 people sheltered at Ongotoi Primary School. Others had sought refuge in Ongotoi Catholic Church and the sub-county headquarters. Many had already been displaced earlier by the LRA and the Karimojong.
"We are helpless," said Teresa Akello, who was displaced from Ongotoi village and has taken shelter in the primary school. "All our food crops have been destroyed. We don't know how we will feed our families."
The situation in the other sub-counties is worse. The chairperson of Acowa sub-county, Okwii Oria, estimated that about 16,000 people in his area had been left homeless, while Asamuk sub-county chairman Henry Abaiki Opolot reckons 14,000 people in his area were displaced by the floods.
The Amuria district chairperson, Julius Ochen, has been running statements over the local radio station appealing for help.
State minister for disaster preparedness Musa Ecweru over the weekend described the situation as "catastrophic".
"What we have been seeing in films and hearing in theatres has arrived in Teso.
The region has had rains for the last two months and reports from weather experts indicate more rains are yet to come," Ecweru, who is also the MP for Amuria, told the press at the Media Centre on Saturday. The rains are expected to continue until late September.
"Crops like cassava, millet and groundnuts that were due for harvest in Teso are rotting in the gardens and the stench hits you from a distance," Ecweru added.
He said plastic sheeting, food, medicine and mobile toilets were urgently needed to avert an epidemic. Ecweru was supported by Bukedea woman MP, Rose Akol Okulu, and that of Kumi, Jab Akiror Egunyu.
According to Egunyu, some 2,000 households had become homeless in Ongino in Kumi district.
"The situation is pathetic. We need immediate intervention in the form of food, medicine and shelter," said the MP who had just returned from the area.
Akol described the situation in Bukedea as a looming calamity. "People are taking shelter in schools and health centres and will not have any food until the next planting season."

Original_Search_Title: The Kanyama Lifestyle
Headline: Uganda: The Kanyama Lifestyle
Body_Text:
Kampala — ON the streets you can hardly notice anything unusual about him. He is like any other pedestrian.
The only time you will see his true colours is when you try gatecrashing. A body-builder, commonly referred to as Kanyama, is normally hired as a bouncer or bodyguard.
The Kanyamas are graded according to how much they weigh: Bantamweight (up to 65 kg), lightweight (66-70kg), welterweight (75kg), middleweight (76-80), light heavyweight (81-90kg) and heavyweight (91kg and above).
But what is their way of life?
Under normal circumstances, every Kanyama is registered with and attached to a particular gym after paying a membership fee.
Every gym has got rules for its members. Most of the rules are the same for most gyms. Breaking them attracts tough disciplinary action, which in most cases is expulsion from the gym.
"Discipline is the guiding principle of every bodybuilder. Under no circumstance is one allowed to fight in and out of the gym or beat up any member of the public," says Ssali Tamale, a coach at Powerhouse Gym, Nakivubo.
It is believed that if a Kanyama commits a crime, it is not only his personal reputation at stake. The image of both the gym and the entire bodybuilding fraternity is also dented.
"If a bodybuilder offends you, you are free to report him to the bosses of the gym where he trains from. He will be expelled," says Nixon Odic, a bouncer at Kampala Casino.
What if a member of the public offends a Kanyama?
"As a bouncer, I am supposed to throw out flukers and wrongdoers peacefully. However, if they resort to physical violence, I will have no option but to resort to self-defence. That is understandable," says Ivan Byekwaso, a bouncer at Al's bar in Kansanga.
If a Kanyama misbehaves or breaks any law, he is treated like any other Ugandan. The Kanyamas are not expected to participate in riots and demonstrations.
Because there are many gyms around, some Kanyamas, when expelled from one gym, end up in others, some of which are not keen in tracing their background.
When you take a look at a Kanyama's muscles, you will think that he has an unsual diet. "We eat normal food," says Apollo Mboijana, a bodybuilder from Kasubi.
However, not everyone who fills the stomach with food gains muscles. Particular attention is paid to the food's quality, quantity and the frequency with which it is eaten.
Kanyamas are generally supposed to eat 'hard foods' like cassava, boiled rice and posho. Eating before and after visiting the gym is vital.
Plenty of water is required to transport the food to the different body parts. Kanyamas can drink about 16 glasses of water per day.
Alcoholic drinks reduce a Kanyama's energy levels, but of course there are some who drink. Some Kanyamas don't even drink soda.
Do Kanyamas take muscle-enhancing substances?
"Everyone has his or her own secret to success. Something that inspires them.
