Some take substances while for others the muscles develop as a result of regular training, eating well and having enough rest," says Byekwaso, who emerged third at this year's Mr. Eldoret bodybuilding competitions in Kenya.
Bodybuilding is not a Kanyama's full-time occupation. They have other sources of income: "To register with us, one must either be a student or employed," Ssali says.
In addition to being a bouncer at Kampala Casino, 23-year-old Odic is a gospel artiste and a bodybuilding judge.
Ivan Byekwaso, 24, is a bouncer and the head of security at Al's bar in Kansanga. He is also a retired footballer.
What if you want to hire the services of a Kanyama?
You should go to a gym and talk to the administrators. They know who is best suited for what kind of assignment. The gym coach knows each and every member very well, so he can recommend someone who will not dent the gym's reputation.
"Not every bodybuilder is good or can guard. Some are dubious and others lazy," Odic says.
There is a myth that because of the stimulants bodybuilders take to enhance their muscles, they cannot live happily in marriage.
"If bodybuilding affects reproduction, then we would not be having wives or girlfriends and children," says Byekwaso, with a sheepish smile. He has a girlfriend and one child. "My girlfriend is supportive. We met when I was already involved in bodybuilding. Actually she fell for my body."
"I plan to set up my own gym. My wife has expressed interest in becoming its instructor and I have no problem with that. Currently, she trains in another gym," Ssali says.
However, as is the case with other sports, bodybuilders should not overindulge in sexual intercourse, especially prior to competitions. Just like a heavy drinker suffers from a hangover, bodybuilders believe that excessive sexual behaviour is followed by general body weakness.
Bodybuilders believe that they are always physically fit and rarely fall sick.
What pushes one to join bodybuilding?
Friends, movies, magazines and admiration for the muscles of bodybuilders have nurtured the current crop of Kanyamas.
"After seeing some Kanyamas, I started training slowly until I felt that the 'metals' had entered my head," says Sulaiman Sempa, the chairman of Nakivubo Bodybuilding club.
Bodybuilding competitions are held nationally and internationally for those willing to participate. "As the competition draws nearer, I must cut weight to fit in my category. In the off-season, like now, I eat a lot. To cut weight, I reduce on the quantity of food and water," says Byekwaso, who has participated in several bodybuilding competitions.
However, what discourages most bodybuilders from taking it on as a sport is that the pay is not good. Byekwaso emerged third at the Mr. Eldoret competitions held in Kenya last month and received a cash prize of sh60,000.
"I had spent sh50,000 on transport alone and yet we were also feeding ourselves. We only participate to learn from our counterparts and because we love it," Byekwaso adds. "At the 2006 Mr. Uganda competitions, we were not given anything."

Original_Search_Title: Returning Home After 12 Yrs
Headline: Uganda: Returning Home After 12 Yrs
Body_Text:
Kampala — When we first left our home, it was a difficult situation for us in the camp. Now returning home is like a dream come true. We only pray that the talks are concluded successfully.
AFTER 12 years of living in an internally displaced people's camp, Ms Regina Labongonye, 50, is trying to find her feet back home. Two months ago, together with her husband Peter Labongonye, 68, they started their journey from Awer IDP camp back to their home village-thanks to the government of South Sudan mediated peace talks between the Lord's Resistance Army rebels and the government of Uganda that have brought relative peace to northern Uganda.
The family moved away from their home village, about four and half kilometres from Awer camp in 1995 at the height of the now 20-year insurgency that has uprooted over a million people from their homes. It is the first time Ms Labongonye is visiting her home.
"When we first left our home, it was a difficult situation for us in the camp. Now returning home after all this time is like a dream come true. We only pray that the talks are concluded successfully," she says.
Before the war, Ms Labongonye says the family engaged in agriculture, growing mostly maize, groundnuts and cassava for both commercial and home consumption. "We were very successful farmers. We sold the produce in Gulu town. But when we went to the camp, we had to start all afresh," she says.
Life in camp
For the 12 years that she lived in the camp, Ms Labongonye led a life, which she says was very miserable. "We had to start from scratch. In the camp you had no freedom. Our movement was always restricted because of the rebels," she says.
Unlike previously when she would grow her own food and in excess, in the years she lived in the camp, Ms Labongonye relied on food supplies from the UN World Food Programme.
But this food was not enough. Ms Labongonye brewed local alcohol from which she would get money to buy household necessities like salt, soap and paraffin. Part of the money would also be used to cater for medical bills. Her husband used to cultivate a small piece of land that they rented from a fellow IDP whose home was within the camp premises. It is here that the family got food to supplement what was being provided by WFP.
Over one year into the peace talks, hope of IDPs returning to their long forgotten homes is beginning to be restored.
Radio announcements are being made encouraging IDPs to voluntarily return home, especially in areas that are near camp settlements. The announcements are mainly made through Mega FM, a community radio station located in the heart of Gulu district.
Ms Labongonye is one of several IDPs who have taken the first move to return home voluntarily. However, the home that she returns to is in ruins after the war left several villages and property destroyed.
"We grew a lot of sim sim and millet. Children were well fed and malnutrition was unheard of. But we are returning to nothing," she says.
Home today for Ms Labongonye is a small grass thatched house with only a papyrus mat, one blanket, drinking water pot, a few cups and plates and a sack full of groundnuts that had just been harvested from the camp.
Before they went into the camps, Ms Labongonye said the family had three huts. "All these were burnt when the rebels attacked the village. They left us homeless," she says.
Outside Ms Labongonye's new home, the grass was overgrown and there was no trace of any previous human settlement-though it was there before.
Mr Labongonye was clearing grass and cutting down trees that had surrounded the area. He said, "This village was full of life. We grew a lot of simsim and millet and children never starved. But look what this war has done to us! "This is a new beginning for us. I am going to clear the land and plant some beans and millet."
For the next three months, the Labongonyes and other returnees will be relying on WFP for food as they await their own harvests. The return ration that they will receive is part of what WFP is giving to IDPs in Gulu and Amuru throughout the month of August. WFP has been undertaking one of the biggest operations in food aid to the IDPs amounting to billions of shillings.
At least 122,600 IDPs in 20 camps in the two districts will benefit from the return rations. IDPs who choose to leave the main camp and settle either in smaller less congested camps or to their original villages will benefit.
According to the head of WFP in Gulu, Mr Bai Sankoh, the ration includes maize, beans, peas and vegetable cooking oil.
Ms Labongonye is only worried about the general lack of social services back home.
"The biggest problem will be where to access medical treatment in case of illness and school for our children. The health centre at Awer does not have all the necessary drugs so we have to move up to Lacor Hospital or Parabongo Health Centre," she says. Lacor Hospital is about 24km away from Ms Labongonye's home.
The couple's four children have remained in the camp where they attend the lone primary school. Water is already a big challenge for the returnees. According to Ms Labongonye, the nearest water source is about 2kilomotres from her home and there is no market in the village.
The presence of landmines that were planted by the rebels will also be another obstacle for cultivating. The foot high thick grasses that race through the villages were easy targets for planting landmines.
"You can access the fields but then there is fear of the landmines. There should be a programme to address this issue before people venture deep into their land," says Mr Labongonye.
But even in the face of a fragile peace, Ms Labongonye is happy to return home. At least the relative peace is a great comfort to her for now. "Our only prayer is that it holds," she says.

Original_Search_Title: Hakuna Matata; a Favourite Pork Joint
Headline: Uganda: Hakuna Matata; a Favourite Pork Joint
Body_Text:
There are two joints adjacent to one another - Hakuna Matata 1 joint to the right and the newly opened Hakuna Matata 5 to the left on the road to Makindye Hill.
Main Attractions: The place is an attraction, first with a beautiful signpost spelling out what to expect on the menu - roast pork, chicken, fresh juice and snacks, cassava and potatoes and introduces available local dishes.
Being the speciality, the joint has been christened the 'pork republic'. There is a plus in terms of hygiene; what with a spotless white floor tiles.
The green flowers and towering trees in these tranquil environs create a good contrast to the white tiles. You won't wait forever to get served. There are a number of waiters and waitresses to attend to you. In the neat environs, are seats under green umbrella shades. This is your ideal place to catch up with friends after work, or over the weekend for a night out.
Ambience: It is both relaxing and energising depending on the crowd. Fridays and Saturdays are bubbly with good music.
Price range: Beers and tuskers go for Shs2,000 and Shs2,500 respectively. Roast pork is Shs2,000 per stick. For juice and fruits, you have to part with between Shs1,000 and Shs3,000. Pocket-friendly snacks are also available.
The crowd: Mixed crowd, a predominantly middle class who come around to catch up especially during the evening hours.
A.O.B: If you're a fan of football, there is 24-hour Dstv showing so you won't miss your big games. And for fans of Big Brother, it is screened live.
Place: Hakuna Matata 5
Location: Makindye
Accessibility: The joint is located by the roadside as you ascend Makindye hill from the city centre.

Original_Search_Title: Kyaka II Camp Refugees Strive for Self Sustenance
Headline: Uganda: Kyaka II Camp Refugees Strive for Self Sustenance
Body_Text:
Kyenjojo — MILES deep inside Kyenjojo District is a small community that leads a totally different kind of life from their neighbours and other Ugandans.
This community is at Kyaka II Refugee Settlement Camp with a population over 16,000 residents. The refugees have made use of every inch of the 81-square miles of the camp land to an extent that the camp authorities are worried of space challenges.
Fleeing hardship in their home countries, these people left their means of livelihood and are strangers in a new country. However, they have not sat back to regret and idle away the time.
They have tilled the soil, the hills and valleys surrounding their camp and have huge maize, banana and cassava plantations. But the outstanding cultivators are the Bagegere tribe from DR Congo.
The Camp Commandant, Mr David Mugenyi, says the refugees' hard working trait has shocked many.
"It is so surprising that people around the camp who enjoy all the freedoms and have enough land come and buy food from the refugees," he said.
Besides tilling their land, Mr Mugenyi says the refugees also work as casual labourers in the gardens of the neighbouring communities.
It is from this work that many have been able to pay school fees, which is over Shs100,000 a term, for their children in the only secondary school and five primary schools in the camp.
The 16,000 refugees in the camp with 26 villages are mainly from DR Congo, the Central African Republic, Somalia, Sudan, Malawi, and Burundi. However, 3,000 Rwandans in the camp have been denied asylum and are yet to be repatriated home.
Kyaka II camp was established in 1983 to resettle thousands of Rwandans who had fled persecution in Rwanda. However, refugees from other countries have since then been resettled in the camp.
The representative of the United Nations High Commission for Refugees in Kyaka II, Mr Freeman Brent, says the commerce and trade in the camp has grown due to hard work and creativity of the refugees and their agricultural background.
"We are about to introduce an upland rice scheme in the camp because the refugees have proven to have the ability to handle such big projects which require hard work," he said. He said their success can also be credited to the agricultural extension services the refugees get from international agencies like the German Technical Cooperation (GTZ).
However, new arrivals in the camps still depend on food rations from the World Food Programme (WFP) before they can start supporting themselves. Other international relief agencies also supplement WFP to provide basic necessities.
"Each new refugee is given 15 Kg of posho per month and after sometime it is reduced to half. A refugee is able to harvest something, then we stop the rationing once one becomes self reliant and can provide their own food," Mr Brent says.
Authorities say the refugees can freely engage in agriculture because of Uganda's good refugee policy.
"Our refugee policy in Uganda is the best in the region. It mandates us to give refugees land to carry out agricultural practices," Mr Mugenyi said. "However, despite these incentives, some refugees still complain of poor treatment."
Mr Abdunur Mugenyi, the elected head of the refugees and a former administrator in Bunia town in Ituri District, complains that the refugees don't have access to clean water. "We have to fetch water from the same places where animals drink," he said. Mr Mugenyi says he fled his country after armed men attacked his home.
Unlike the displaced persons in the north who complain of lack most of the basic necessities, the refugees in Kyaka II lead a life better than many average Ugandans.

Original_Search_Title: The Sum of All Madness (2)
Headline: Nigeria: The Sum of All Madness (2)
Body_Text:
Lagos — To all intents and purposes, this strategy has worked once again. "Correct pricing of petroleum products" is a mantra which government after government in Nigerian has chanted.
Generally, they hinge their primary strategy for fiscal restructuring in Nigeria to this their 'inspired' ideology.
While Labour has done a whole lot in questioning the ceaseless increase in prices of petroleum product, it has only skirted around the issue of the rightness or wrongness of hinging fiscal restructuring in Nigeria to the reform of pricing structure of petroleum products. And while the former NLC boss, Adams Oshiomhole, had often debunked the argument that government was actually subsidizing pump prices in the strict sense of it while arguing that the common man ought not to be the loser for incredible recklessness of government after government in tackling refinery problems; he has never argued about the soundness of government's economic ideology.
This, of course, is not the role of the Nigerian labour movement, which has displayed far more bravery than almost any other body in the face of government's bully tactics; and those whose role, it is, is too cowed, too greedy or just too unsure to insist on the point.
In the upstream sector, inefficiencies are huge. Every bank manager in the country knows that a purchase order from a Nigerian oil company is about the most lucractive use to which their funds can be put, apart from round tripping. They will tell you behind closed doors that oil companies regularly pay double or triple the actual costs of their purchases. On the financial landscape, firms have sprung up in the last couple of years which claim to double or even tripple investment funds. These firms have partners amongst the 25 government approved mega banks, and are generally regarded as 'genuine'. While some have had their fingers burnt, majority have reported success.
When asked in what sector these investment firms are putting their funds, they and their clients simply declare: "Oil and Gas"
The result is that the Nigerian government, directly or indirectly, services the activities of the oil service industry while the Nigerian people who do not get their engines fueled by government have to pay for it.
It has always been the case that the politics, the geography and economics of oil has had a direct effect on the Nigerian economy as a whole. Today, in 2007, this is more the case than ever.
High school fundamental economics defined inflation as too much money chasing too few goods. Now more increasingly than ever, this phenomenon is showing face too vividly in the Nigerian economy. While the latest figures have inflation at the lowest ever and the Obasanjo economic team continues to pat itself on the back for taming inflation and bringing it under 10%, the naira continues to give its spenders a consumer problem. Services are rare or uncompetitive, while about the only goods available are those ones that might have found their way in through our ports.
When VAT taxes were recently tampered with and given the state of the petroleum and energy sectors, local production will continue to be kept down. Not even government rhetoric will ensure productive economic activity in an atmosphere in which every factor strives to repress this. Market prices are not allowed to determine prices and instability, due to importation of finished petroleum and other essential manufacturing products, are certainly deterrence to production.
Some years ago, Obasanjo began a mini revolution in the cassava sector of our local agricultural culture.
This, unfortunately, proved counterproductive. Garri, fufu and other domestic consumables have gone out of the reach of the ordinary man, while the role of Africa as the supplier of raw materials to an industrialized and increasingly service oriented world is reinforced so resoundingly.
What a difference it would have made had Nigeria, for all her cassava, produced the first ethanol powered airplane as a Brazilian firm has now done, or the first solar powered aircraft like Solar Impulse, which has been developed by Swiss adventurer, Bertrand Piccard, with the aid of 60 aviation experts. Today, Cadbury does not produce one chocolate bar in its Agidingbi Lagos factory. The palmers brand remains, for all our local cocoa production, the trusted and 'original' name in cocoa butter skin care.
It is a familiar fact that the anatomy of mineral exploitation-particularly oil exploitation reflects the essential structure of corruption and structural dysfunction in Nigeria. Using the oil production sector as an example, it is easy to show the dreadful failures of the present system which has seen a sector that contributes such a large percentage of our GDP employ less than 0.5% of its workforce. It is unfortunate that these tragic facts of our lives are not even recognized as such.
The great oil company scam of Nigeria in which foreign oil companies are subsidized by Nigeria at the expense of the hapless Nigerian has been going on for so long that it is easy to stop taking notice. And, perhaps, we have.

Original_Search_Title: Minister Advocates Duty-Free Export to China
Headline: Nigeria: Minister Advocates Duty-Free Export to China
Body_Text:
Abuja — Minister for Commerce and Industry, Engineer Charles Ugwu, has enjoined the Chinese government to give duty-free entry to Nigerian products.
Ugwu made the call yesterday, when he received a delegation from the Embassy of the Peoples Republic of China in Nigeria, led by the Political Counsellor, Mr Wang Lei, in his office in Abuja .
Ugwu said China is Nigeria's number one trading partner, adding that duty-free entry for Nigerian products to China will enable it export more of her cassava chips and sesame seeds.
A press statement signed by Chief Press Secretary to the Minister, Udo M. Akpan, quoted him as saying Nigeria and China have a long history of friendship and that the two countries have many things in common in the area of politics, economics and trade.
Ugwu called on Chinese investors to invest in Nigeria in the petro-chemicals industry, noting that the Chinese are very sophisticated in this are and very active in the plastics industry.
He said, "you are far ahead of other countries in this area. We plead with you to come and invest in petro-chemicals in Nigeria, because of the abundant gas deposit we have in the Niger Delta region."
Ugwu said through this investment, the Chinese can reach out to other ECOWAS countries in the region and assured that Nigerian government will create an enabling environment for them.
On the issue of Free Trade Zone that is being planned to take off in Ogun and Imo States, the Minister said that his Ministry will facilitate the process by removing all the bottlenecks to enable the project to take off. He said that he will take up the matter with the Managing Director of Nigerian Exports Processing Zones Authority. He stated that Nigerian Government places high premium on Chinese investments in the country and will do everything possible to encourage them because he wants the trade figure between the two countries to double in the next two years.
The Minister said that he will visit China very soon as he will lead a delegation from the Ministry to take part in the big trade exhibition that is coming up in China.The Minister lamented the influx of fake products and the issue of counterfeiting, saying the issue of copying brand names and trade marks of other companies that are successful in Nigeria as if they were original products was worrisome.
The Minister advised that both countries should set up mutual registration of brand names in order to forestall these problems.Earlier in his address, Mr. Wang sought for the intervention of the Ministry for the approval for the Free Trade Zone in Ogun State stating that the Chinese company is working in collaboration with Ogun State government to set up Free Trade Zone.He said that both countries enjoy very close relationship in Political and Economic spheres including cooperation in Agriculture, Science and Technology, Aviation and health.
Wang added that, the volume of Bilateral Trade has increased tremendously from $3.03 Billion to $10.6 Billion. However, he drew the attention of the Honourable Minister to the so called Taiwan Trade Mission in Abuja which he said was not in conformity with China Policy and requested that Nigeria government should correct the mistake

Original_Search_Title: Early Famine Warning Issued for Coming Months
Headline: Rwanda: Hunger Period Will Be Longer in Coming Months - Experts
Body_Text:
Kigali — Despite a good harvest recorded in parts of the country, some areas in Western, southern and Eastern Rwanda are expected to face a longer than expected hunger period by the end of the year, the Famine Early Warning Systems Network (FEWS) has warned.
The US aid agency funded climate experts say due to late rains that followed a dry spell early this year crops yields are likely to be slightly below normal as a result of the March given that Rwandan agriculture is subsistence-based and primarily rain-fed.
"Yields are likely to be particularly low in the west, where rainfall was especially below normal, and in Nyagatare and Bugesera districts, where farmers decreased the area cultivated in response to the late start to the rainy season.", their current Food Security Outlook assessment report released July 20 notes.
"As a result of the expected moderately below-normal production, food stocks for poor households will be depleted earlier than in a normal season, and the normal October to December hunger period will be longer".
The western and eastern parts of the country received the least rainfall, with rains between 60 and 70 percent of the average. The east, that usually experiences a very hot and dry weather for most of the year, was the worst hit and still remains affected.
Households in these areas, especially the poor, depend significantly on their own production for consumption and income and the production losses imply reduced food access.
About 34 percent of the food production in Rwanda is sold by farmers, according to the Ministry of Agriculture Strategic Plan for Agricultural Transformation in Rwanda.
While the severity of the October to November hunger period will be alleviated by the harvest due in September to October and related income-earning opportunities, the famine assessment says not all farmers will benefit, as many do not have access to the marshlands needed for production.
Another explanation for the impending food crisis is the fact that Rwandan farmers depend largely on water-sensitive crops such as beans.
The agency also says the quarantine due to an outbreak of foot and mouth disease (FMD) makes households in the Karangazi Sector of Nyagatare District highly food insecure.
The farmers are not able to sell animals and animal products, which are main sources of income in the area to purchase food and other essentials.
The specific impact of the low and late rains on overall national harvest is yet to be established by a Ministry of Agriculture-led harvest assessment mission conducted from June 11 to 25, 2007.
Higher prices
According to the assessment, compared to the same period last year, prices in Kigali markets are on average 11 percent higher, including substantial increases for cassava flour by 43 percent, Irish potato by 20 percent and beans by 18 percent. The price hikes reflect reduced production levels.
Cassava production has been strained by a limited availability of cassava planting materials due to Cassava Mosaic Disease (CMD), it says adding that the high prices for Irish potato are due to low domestic production resulting from lack of quality seed.
Low income families feed regularly on cassava flour and Irish potatoes that are grown on larger scale in northern Rwanda because of regular and good rains. It is rare that this area faces any food shortages as it supplies the other part with vegetables as well.
Food-for-work
The assessment says targeted assistance programs such food-for-work, cash-for-work should be scaled up during the October to December period. These activities increase household income and enable improved food access.
Food-for-work is a World Food Program (WFP) aimed at giving people food for the short term to cover up for the meager harvests.
For July 2007 to June 2008, food requirements to meet needs are estimated at 26,373 MT for all commodities.

Original_Search_Title: Moxico - Dilolo Lake Commune to Have Tourism Complex This Year
Headline: Angola: Moxico - Dilolo Lake Commune to Have Tourism Complex This Year
Body_Text:
Luanda — An 11-branch tourism complex is being built at Lago Dilolo commune, Luacano district, eastern Moxico province, to encourage tourism exploration in the locality that has 12,734 inhabitants, mostly of whom are fishermen.
The complex will have, among other branches, restrooms, cafés, an area of assistance to tourists, restaurant, kiosks, game room and a recreational area around Dilolo Lake, which with 18 kilometres of length and seven wide is considered as the third biggest in Africa.
According to district's administrator, Pascoal Mucazu, the undertaking has been experiencing some delays due to difficulties encountered in transporting construction materials, mostly coming from neighbouring Republic of Zambia, but he secured that it will be concluded this year.
After speaking on the need to reap major profits from this tourist site and turn the country more attractive to the foreigners and encouraging them to invest, the municipal administrator appealed to the Hotel and Tourism Ministry, the provincial government of Moxico and the civil society to intervene in the exploration of the lake.
The official stressed the existence of beauty scenery, plenty of fish, rice, cassava production, factors contributing to the valuing of the area and commitment to the growth of Moxico.

Original_Search_Title: The Pain of Innocents
Headline: Kenya: The Pain of Innocents
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Lucky Kituli is an attractive little boy with a beautiful smile full of life and energy.
At the age of two-and-a-half years, Kituli can neither sit nor walk; the little boy mostly spends a quiet time sleeping, sucking on two of his fingers. He smiles amiably as a stream of saliva trickles down his hand.
Kituli was born with a growth on his spinal cord, which would later be diagnosed as related to spina bifida.
The growth was spotted immediately after his birth in Mombasa. Apprehensive, his parents consulted a paediatrician and the prognosis was devastating: the problem would subject him to a retarded growth, unless an operation was performed in time.
Says the boy's father, Isaiah Kituli: "Upon receiving the news from the doctors, I had to think how I would save my son's life. I had no money and the only solution was to borrow from friends."
Doctors failed to make a certain artificial connection
He managed to collect a few thousand shillings from friends and well-wishers for the surgical correction of the anomaly.
Apparently, the operation was a success.
However, their dream to see their son growing up as a normal boy was nipped in the bud owing to what was later explained as a medical error.
In spina bifida, one of the 33 vertebrae (back bone sections) that form the spine fails to develop fully. As a result, a section of the spine is left exposed. During little Kituli's corrective surgery, doctors reportedly failed to make a certain artificial connection, which would have been essential for preventing cerebrospinal fluid from accumulating in the head.
With fluid progressively collecting in the boy's head, hydrocephalus resulted. Hydrocephalus refers to an excessive amount of cerebrospinal fluid within the skull, which often causes increased pressure.
Hydrocephalus eventually leads to brain damage
With the build-up of fluid, Kituli's head grew much bigger than is normal for a healthy growing child.
If left untreated, hydrocephalus eventually leads to brain damage, and can result in death. Symptoms include headache, deterioration of mental functioning, vomiting and loss of coordination.
Around Mombasa, spina bifida and hydrocephalus (each occurring on its own or with both existing together) are becoming a common concern, with affected families being unable to raise to the medical fees. Recently, four coastal parents came out after their children succumbed to the complications. The traumatised parents formed an association to offer support to affected members
Affected parents say discrimination forced them to act. Neighbours, they lament, ignorantly believe that the conditions are a curse or the product of witchcraft.
Association was formed last year
Their pain, the parents lamented, was unbearable. Families of children with the conditions are sometimes forced to keep them hidden to save them the trauma of stigmatisation.
"After attending several counselling sessions with experts and medical practitioners through the association, we have finally accepted our children's fate," the parents say. "We now view them like other children."
Through the association, parents ensure that children with special needs are recognised as needy, while parents and neighbours are persuaded to treat the children as human beings who have equal rights with regular children.
The Spina Bifida and Hydrocephalus Association of Kenya (SHAK) was formed last year, and comprises some 78 members.
The Secretary General of SHAK, Isaiah Kituli, says members are mostly parents with children who have growth complications. They are drawn from the coastal regions of Mombasa, Kilifi, Lamu, Kwale, Kaloleni, Mariakani and Malindi.
Perceptions can be turned around with education
He says that families with children diagnosed with hydrocephalus and spina bifida have for a long time braved discrimination.
With hopes that, through the parents' own efforts and teamwork these parents and their discriminated children could one day be re-accepted by society, the parents are willing to fight through the prejudice.
They believe that, with re-education, perceptions of the general population can be turned around.
In Kituli's opinion, the deaths of the four children were the indirect result of poverty.
Unable to meet the medical expenses or provide nutritious food to their affected children, they had to helplessly watch their children perish one after another.
Rukiya Omar from Lamu District, a parent whose son has hydrocephalus, says life has never been the same since her child was diagnosed with the condition.
She says that expenses (at-home maintenance as well as the cost of medication) are prohibitive. At times the family, which has had to sacrifice heavily, must go hungry if only to ensure that they give their child a day's relief.
Distraught parents sought medical advice
When they first noticed that Rukiya's child had the unusual problem, relatives and friends began talking behind their back, ignorantly seeing a link between the medical condition and the supernatural.
Distraught but doubting the widespread social belief that their child was somehow cursed, they sought medical advice.
"Most of us have been unable to meet medical expenses," Rukiya laments.
She believes that, with financial assistance to cover medical bills as well as a little social support (so that affected families do not feel ostracised in their own neighbourhoods), spina bifida and hydrocephalus can cease to be the emotional burden they have become.
"All we need is help to ensure that our children are equal and are as valued as other children who have various other disabilities," says the tearful mother.
The association meets once a month at the APDK-Port Ritz in Mombasa to deliberate and attend clinics.
Affected school-age children have had to remain at home
Members, however, lament that their meetings have become an additional source of stress: money spent towards meeting travel and logistic costs is money taken away from the affected children's survival kitty.
"Most of us come from districts as far away as Tana River, Lamu, Malindi, Kilifi and Kinango," the parents cry. "It has been hard to frequently attend the (necessary) meetings or clinics, since money has been our key obstacle."
They add that some of their affected school-age children have had to remain at home because, as they say, there are no special educational facilities for children with spina bifida and hydrocephalus.
"If other disabled persons have schools, why can't the government build us a school which caters for children with spina bifida and hydrocephalus?" the parents ask
Causes of the complications
According to medics, spina bifida and hydrocephalus are related to inadequate folic acid in the mother's diet during pregnancy. Gynaecologists explain that folic acid, a blood-building component, helps in the growth and development of the foetus. Dr Suleiman Marjan, a gynaecologist in Mombasa, says that insufficient folic acid in diet can compromise the growth of bones, thus resulting in complications such as head enlargement and the growth of tumours along the spinal column.
Marjan adds that the important ingredient usually comes as tablets that are rich in iron and folic acid.
These are given to the expectant mother as a dietary supplement. In the 13th to 20th week of pregnancy, the mother should be subjected to various medical investigations, including ultra-sound scans and blood-count tests, to ascertain that there is a standard level of folic acid in the blood.
"The ultrasound helps us to detect unwanted growths on the foetus. We advise women to take several medical tests during pregnancy."
Distinct but related conditions
Expectant mothers are advised to eat nutritious foods, particularly foodstuffs containing folic acid, such as red and white meat, red beans, cassava, yams, pumpkins, fruits and vegetables.
Caleb G, a Mombasa-based consultant surgeon, explains that hydrocephalus and spina bifida are distinct but related conditions.
The former refers to the enlargement of the head with the accumulation of cerebrospinal fluid under the skull; the latter refers to a discontinuity in the spine, and can lead to hydrocephalus.
Caleb says hydrocephalus can result from meningitis. Occasionally, children are operated on to prevent further growth and enlargement of the head.
"The operation must be conducted immediately after a symptom is spotted," he advises. "This can prevent further growth and complication."
During the operation, a tube-like gadget called a shunt is fixed to drain away cerebrospinal fluid and prevent its further accumulation in the brain.
Caleb adds that, in some cases, the complications are so extensive that nothing much can be done - meaning that the person may have to live with the problem. He says that children with spina bifida or hydrocephalus may lose their coordination to such extent that they lose complete control of their bowels.

Original_Search_Title: The Two Faces of Naguru
Headline: Uganda: The Two Faces of Naguru
Body_Text:
Kampala — KAMPALA'S residential areas like Wandegeya, Old Kampala and Nakasero are being swallowed by the spreading wings of the city. This has made places like Naguru Hill the option for the poor and the rich who wish to live near the city.
George Kibugujo, a commission agent, says getting a plot in Upper Naguru is as hard as the Biblical camel going through the eye of a needle. Prices range between sh20m to sh50m for a 50ft by 100ft plot, that is if you happen to get one.
"The advantage here is that the electricity poles are already in place, water is running, there is a low crime rate and the roads are good compared to Kololo," explains Kibugujo.
"The existence of Quality Supermarket in Ntinda, Ndere Troupe Centre in Kisaasi, Kadic health clinic and several schools give the place additional value."
However, unlike Kololo and Nakasero hills, which are strictly up market - Naguru has two faces. There is the striking Upper Naguru where having a tiled-roof on your house is a must. Homesteads have high wall fences to block the view of the shabby side of the suburb.
A walk in the neatly-tarmacked inter-connecting roads is a revelation of a serene atmosphere. The roads are littered with children learning how to ride bicycles and playing hide-and-seek behind 'whispering' palm trees. The children go to nearby plush schools like Kabira International and Kampala Parents schools. The little ones have a daycare centre in the neighbourhood. Here, trucks pick garbage daily. Water gushing out of huge pipes refilling swimming pools is audible to a pedestrian.
Most of the residents here drive four-wheel-drive vehicles. If you look carefully, it is not only their vehicles that are big, but their bodies too. The majority are trying to cut weight and this is where Kabira and BMK gyms in the neighbourhood come in handy.
Notices on gates scream: "This property is protected by Saracen Guards" making potential burglars flee like dogs with tails between their legs. Peeping into the compounds, is evidence that the shamba boys are doing their work. They are neatly trimmed like the beards of their bosses.
As you descend down the hill, you enter another world - that is Naguru Go-down and Naguru-Katale Zone. Here, children in tattered clothes compete for space with sheep, goats and rodents in the shanty homes.
Other than rent being affordable (between sh60,000 and sh100,000) for a bed-sitter, the value of property here is not any cheaper inspite of the area's ghastly appearance. The area is a small, closely-packed, dirty slum on either side of the road. There are small shops, butcheries, snack parlours, cassava roasters - all banded together. Cleanliness is unknown here. The area is carpeted with rubbish and broken glass. Semi-permanent houses are all over and face wherever the developers feel like.
Privacy is a word that does not exist here. A path may pass by one's door way.
Time check is 1:00pm and John Kachiga comes to the shop to buy two packets of Tyson (pineapple-flavoured) waragi (spirit), most probably for lunch.
What used to be posh Kampala City Council flats in the 1970s have become modern slums with algae matting the walls. They badly need of a coat of paint. The windows are gaping open with broken glass. The balconies are filled with broken chairs and rotten mattresses.
The tiled roofs of the houses facing Nakawa are in advanced stages of decay; timber doors and windows are falling apart and what used to be compounds are banana gardens.

Original_Search_Title: Grappling With Malnutrition And Post-Conflict Woes
Headline: Congo-Brazzaville: Grappling With Malnutrition And Post-Conflict Woes
Body_Text:
Brazzaville — Sitting at a small clinic in the Talangai area, north the Republic of Congo's capital Brazzaville, Elise Diamba holds the hands of her malnourished two-year old grandson.
"Gérard's mother stopped breastfeeding him when he was seven months," the 61-year old grandmother says. "He hadn't even started walking. Since then, his health has not been good."
Like many Congolese children, Gérard was left to the care of the grandmother after his biological mother could no longer feed him properly. Now diagnosed with kwashiorkor - a condition that results from inadequate protein intake - he is in desperate need of medical attention.
However Diamba, like the majority of the 3.1 million Congolese who live on less than a dollar day, has struggled to raise the Fcfa 50,000 (US $100) required for his treatment.
"I know I have to save the child," Diamba says. "But the doctor needs to first cure him and then I'll pay."
Gérard, who has a dozen other siblings, was just one of the children waiting for treatment at the clinic that morning. "I receive on average five cases of children suffering from malnutrition each month. We often manage to save them, but the treatment takes a long time and is expensive," explains Hermann Amboulou, who runs the small clinic.
Rampant malnutrition
According to the UN Children's Fund (UNICEF) representative to Congo, Koen Vanormelingen, seven percent of Congolese children are malnourished - a condition that affects their growth.
"These are children who do not have enough to eat; they are very poor. They have sometimes lost their mother and therefore cannot be breastfed," he says. "There are poor people in urban and in rural areas. Malnutrition is a clear sign of poverty."
Breastfeeding, he says, is key to reducing the number of malnourished children: "The most important factor is to sensitise mothers and young women to provide more breastfeeding to their children."
In the last decade Congo's socio-political problems, including conflict and political instability, have exacerbated food shortages and increased malnutrition rates. According to the UN, there are at least 100,000 displaced persons and thousands of ex-combatants in the country, including numerous child soldiers.
A 2005 UNICEF study found a significant lack of vitamin A and iodine in the population's diet, while 58.6 percent of pregnant women were anaemic. "The deficiency in iodine affects 10 percent of school going children: 14.6 percent of boys and 6.3 percent of girls," says Vanormelingen.
Apart from goitre (characterised by neck swelling) and cretinism, a condition characterised by severe brain damage in early life, iodine deficiency can also lead to learning disorders responsible for poor school performance, reduced intellectual ability and impaired work capacity.
According to the UN Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO), in spite of its enormous agricultural potential and nearly four million inhabitants, the Congo is considered a low-income food-deficit country.
"A national programme of food security that touches the whole of the country would change the situation," said FAO Director Jacques Diouf during a trip to Brazzaville in July 2007. "With the peace and the democratic process that has started, we will be able to restart a national programme and to have an economic impact throughout the country."
The case of the Pool
The 2005 report 'le Pool, une crise humanitaire négligée' said the Pool area - which includes the capital city Brazzaville and has seen most of the country's armed conflict - had the highest rate of malnutrition.
Remnants of civil war militias are still active in region, where basic human services and infrastructure such as schools, roads and health centres have crumbled. During the last major conflict that affected the Pool - which is also referred to as the country's 'granary' - more than 50 percent of farmers lost their tools, seeds and approximately 75 percent of their livestock, said FAO.
"Agriculturalists and pastoralists have limited themselves to self-consumption agriculture," the UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs said. "The Pool, which used to export agricultural products, now has to import food."
Farmers in the region are also recovering from the effects of the cassava mosaic disease, which devastated the crop in the region.
According to the UN, only two percent of Congo's arable land is exploited. Food production is insufficient to meet national requirements, and this has resulted in a heavy reliance on food imports.
The way forward
Congolese authorities have been working with development partners to improve the country's food situation.
Between 2003 and 2007, the government, the FAO and about 30 Vietnamese experts have been developing a pilot project on food security. Officials plan to take this countrywide after approval from ministers and the allocation of funds.
Using a US$17 million loan from the International Fund for Agricultural Development, the government will implement a seven year scheme targeting 50,000 farmers in the northern areas of the Plateaux, the Cuvette, and the Cuvette Ouest. There are also plans to build a modern fishing harbour in Pointe-Noire on the Atlantic coast.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Country's Per Capita Income Rises to $1,036
Headline: Nigeria: Country's Per Capita Income Rises to $1,036
Body_Text:
Lagos — DESPITE a slow down in economic growth in 2006, Nigeria's per capita income rose to $1,036.2 from $847.4 in 2005.
Per capita income means how much each individual receives, in monetary terms, of the yearly income that is generated in his country through productive activities. In effect, it is what each citizen would receive if the yearly income generated by a country from its productive activities were divided equally among the population.
The Central Bank (CBN) in its 2006 economic report released yesterday said: "A review of macroeconomic developments shows that the Nigerian economy continued on an expansion path in 2006, albeit, at a slower rate, demonstrating the sustained resilience. The economy recorded an estimated 5.6 per cent growth rate, down from 6.5 per cent in 2005. The economic expansion was commendable as it was broad-based, in spite of the several challenges which included: the slow down in the oil sector and the energy crisis that militated against growth in the economy. Overall, the country's per capita income rose to $1,036.2 from $847.4 in 2005.
"The total federally-collected revenue stood at N5, 965.1 billion in 2006, 7.5 per cent over the level in 2005. The revenue performance, which was 32.7 per cent of GDP, was largely attributed to the receipts from the oil sector occasioned by the sustained increase in crude oil prices in the international oil market. Revenue from the non-oil sector, however, declined by 13.7 per cent from the level attained in 2005. The overall fiscal balance narrowed from a notional deficit of N161.4 billion or 1.1 per cent of GDP to N101.3 billion or 0.6 per cent of GDP while the primary balance recorded a surplus.
"Growth in the economy was driven mainly by agriculture, wholesale/retail trade, building and construction, and services, which grew by 7.2, 13.7, 12.1 and 8.9 per cent, respectively. Industrial production, however, declined by 2.6 per cent, largely due to the fall in crude oil production.
"The agricultural sector continued its recovery in 2006, benefiting from the various Federal Government Initiatives and favourable weather. The efforts to increase agricultural productivity by increased fiscal incentives to the sector, review of import waiver as well as the promotion of increased use of agricultural machinery and inputs through favourable tariff policy and subsidies contributed to the healthy growth. Output of staples grew by 8.0 per cent, contributed by all the components.
Cassava production increased by 10.8 per cent compared with 8.0 per cent in 2005 and rice by 6.9 per cent. Growth in cassava production was driven by the urge to take advantage of the expanded export and domestic markets. The expansion in the markets resulted from the increased international demand for the product given its use in the production of various products, prominent among which was ethanol and the increase in domestic consumption occasioned by the compliance with the Federal Government's directive to Nigeria's flour millers to include at least 10 per cent of cassava flour in the production of bread and other confectioneries.
"The industrial sector benefited from the continued government privatisation policy in 2006 and a focussed effort on providing the enabling environment for core investors to take off and grow. The enabling environment was enhanced by the removal of legal hurdles associated with equity investment that hitherto hindered the inflow of foreign investment. The present fiscal regime allows foreign investors to freely repatriate dividends, profits and proceeds from their investments in Nigeria. Withholding tax was also reduced by 33.3 per cent for nationals of countries with which Nigeria had a double taxation agreement.
Manufacturing output declined by 1.5 per cent below the level in the previous year and average capacity utilisation rate fell from 54.8 per cent in 2005 to 53.3 per cent in 2006. The poor performance of the sector was attributable to the worsening power supply situation in the country, which further increased the cost of production, coupled with the influx of cheaper and sometimes sub-standard goods into the country. The cement sub-sector, however, improved substantially owing to the refurbishment of cement factories across the country. Regulatory activities by the National Food, Drugs Administration and Control (NAFDAC) and the Standard Organisation of Nigeria (SON) impacted on the sub-sector positively.
"Aggregate crude oil production declined from an average of 2.53 million barrels per day in 2005 to 2.23 million barrels per day. This was as a result of the persistent disruption of oil operations, especially, of the major Joint Venture Companies, in the Niger Delta Region. Nevertheless, some new oil wells started production, contributing about 265,000 barrels of crude oil per day, thus moderating the losses from the supply disruptions in the Niger Delta region. Aggregate export of crude oil, therefore, averaged 1.80 mbd compared with 2.08 mbd in 2005. The average spot price of Nigeria's reference crude, the Bonny Light (37API) increased from $55.43 in 2005 to $66.46 per barrel. Aggregate volume of gas produced in 2006 was estimated at 57,753.7 million cubic meters (MMm3) compared with 57,369.4 MMm3 in 2005. Of this total, 31.8 per cent was flared.
Solid minerals production increased substantially compared with 2005 as aggregate output rose from 4,471.1 thousand tonnes in 2005 to 36,031.2 thousand tonnes. The growth in production was broad based. The installed capacity of electricity generation increased by 2.2 per cent over the level in 2005. This was due to the commissioning of the 150 MW Omoku power plant by the Rivers State Government in December 2006. Progress was made in the construction of five other power plants under the National Integrated Power Projects with an aggregate installed capacity of 1765 MW/h spread across the country.
Electricity generation, however, declined by 5.1 per cent in contrast to the increase of 0.6 per cent in 2005. The decline in electricity generation was attributed to the low water level at the hydropower plants and the disruption of gas supply to the thermal stations following the vandalization of gas pipelines in the Niger Delta area. Energy consumption, therefore, fell in 2006. The aggregate energy consumed fell from 24.5 million tonnes of coal equivalent to 23.5 million tonnes in 2006.
"The external sector continued to expand, driven by the robust current account surplus as in the previous year. The overall balance of payments recorded a surplus of N1,772.7 billion, or 9.7 per cent of GDP, compared with N1,362.3 billion or 9.2 per cent of GDP in 2005. The improved performance was attributed to oil sector receipts, increased inward transfers and other financial inflows.
Following the further liberalization of the foreign exchange market through the introduction of the Wholesale Dutch Auction System, and the approval granted to the BDC operators to access the CBN foreign exchange window, which further enhanced the supply of foreign exchange to the market, the Naira appreciated in all segments of the market. This culminated in a convergence of rates across all the segments of the foreign exchange market, the first time in about two decades.

Original_Search_Title: Bie - Mecanagro Intends to Clear about 250 Hectares in Kuemba
Headline: Angola: Bie - Mecanagro Intends to Clear about 250 Hectares in Kuemba
Body_Text:
Kuito — The National Firm of Agricultural Mechanisation (Mecanagro) intends to clear about 250 hectares of land in Kuemba District, in the central Bie Province, for the coming agricultural season.
The information was given this Wednesday to Angop, in Kuito City, by Kuemba District's administrator, Bento Chilingueno, when putting into perspective the next agricultural season.
Bento Chilingueno informed that currently Mecanagro is bringing together, in the province's chief town (Kuito), all the necessary equipment.
He also assured that the peasants will get their agricultural inputs in due time, since the provincial government has already acquired them.
As regards the current harvest, the source indicated that it might be a little disappointing, because the heavy rains that hit this region have damaged some maze and cassava fields.
Nevertheless, he assured that the populations in Kuemba District will not face famine, since other crops will make up for the lack of the above mentioned products.
Kuemba District has about 82,886 inhabitants, most of whom are peasants who cultivate maze, cassava, rice, beans, potatoes, vegetables, as well as dedicate to hunting, fishing and apiculture.

Original_Search_Title: 7 Killed in Mpigi Crash
Headline: Uganda: 7 Killed in Mpigi Crash
Body_Text:
Kampala — SEVEN people were killed when a lorry and a bus collided head-on near Mpigi district headquarters on the Kampala-Masaka highway at 6:30pm yesterday.
Many of the victims died on the spot, while the injured were rushed to local medical centres after they were pulled out of the death trap by rescuers.
The Fuso lorry was heading to Kampala carrying cattle. The Mbarara-bound Isuzu coach belongs to Ibabu company.
Mangled bodies, carcasses of cattle and blood covered the accident scene. The vehicles were stuck together, and it took a breakdown to pull them apart. The villagers helped themselves to the carcasses.
Mpigi police commander Ivan Nuwamanya and the commissioner for traffic, Steven Kasiima, rushed to the accident scene. By 8:40pm, rescue work was still going on. Kasiima later said six of the dead were aboard the Fuso lorry which he said was from Ntugamo district.
The driver of the bus, Alex Kadogo, was the other victim, he said. Kasiima blamed the accident which occurred at Maziba Kalongo, on speeding and reckless driving.
He said Kadogo attempted to overtake a soloon car at a corner.
The bodies were laid by the road side and in a nearby cassava garden. Their identities could not be established by press time. The accident caused a massive traffic jam on the busy highway.

Original_Search_Title: Desopadec, Foreign Firm in 10,000 Housing Units Deal
Headline: Nigeria: Desopadec, Foreign Firm in 10,000 Housing Units Deal
Body_Text:
Warri — DELTA State Oilneral Producing Areas Development Commission (DESOPADEC) and a foreign firm, Trans Atlantic Integrated Developoment Corporation, LLC of New York have entered into a partnership agreement for the construction of 10,000 housing units within the next one year.
The foreign firm is also to build an ethanol plant, which would convert sugar cane and cassava to alternative fuel for cars. DESOPADEC chairman, Chief Wellington Okirika and Jack Heffernan, Executive Director of Trans Atlantic, confirmed the development in Warri when the latter visited the former. .
According to Okirika, "This visit by businessmen from the U.S. is being arranged by a few people who believe that there is now peace in the Niger Delta and Warri. So they are here to invest in the areas that have been described as a no go area.
"They want to build 10,000 housing units, ethanol plants, water treatment projects, we have a lot of problem with our water in this areas, especially in Escravos Area. They have a lot of project for agriculture, catfish and shrimp farming, among a host of other things.
"There is also Independent Power Plant, waste management, they are here to do business and they will invest their money in the development of our area."
He said the firm would partner with DESOPADEC to meet the development needs of the communities, adding that this is in line with the mandate of the commission.
On his part, Heffernan, who was accompanied by experts of the firm, including Mr. David Decker, who is reputed to have several years of experience in the construction of houses in the Caribbean Island, promised that his firm would turn the area around.
He said the purpose of the visit was for Trans Atlantic to be the early bird in the development of the region.
Sive projects that the President Umar Yar"Adua government would bring to the area.
He said the team was not deterred by the reports of insecurity in the area particularly the U.S. travel ban, saying the problems of the people was political. He said he was sure that DESOPADEC team would bring lasting solution to Delta State.

Original_Search_Title: Omotosho Power Plant to Inject 300mw By September
Headline: Nigeria: Omotosho Power Plant to Inject 300mw By September
Body_Text:
Akure — Hope of improved power supply to various parts of Ondo State was raised recently by Head of the multi-billion naira Omotosho Power Station, Engineer Samuel Itshekiri, who said the station would inject about 300 mega watts into the state by September.
Speaking while receiving a team led by Wife of the State Governor, Mrs Olufunke Agagu, Itshekiri said the station consists of eight latest Chinese Gas Turbines, with each having capacity to generate about 38 mega watts to the National Grid through the PowerHolding Company of Nigeria (PHCN).
According to him, the seven other turbines which would be handed over to the company in the next few weeks, would go a long way to boost the quality and quantity of power in the state.
Phase One of the project was commissioned by former president Olousegun Obasanjo at the tail end of his administration.
When fully completed, the station is expected to serve the people of Ondo, Ekiti, Edo and some parts of Lagos State.
While commending the Federal Government for the project, Mrs Agagu said it would positively impact on the socio-economic development of the state, as well as that of the nation in general and called on the people of the state to start preparing to make maximum use of the plant, in order to enhance their living standards.
Meanwhile, government has signed a $13million agreement for the establishment of cassava processing factory with the government of Thailand.
The Special Adviser to Governor Olusegun Agagu on Poverty Alleviation, Professor Adeduro Adegaye, disclosed this to journalists in Akure saying the government would focus more attention on wealth creation rather than poverty alleviation in the next four years.
The plant according to him would be established at Oba-Akoko in Akoko South-West Local Government area of the state for the processing of about two hundred tones of cassava per day.
He said the establishment of the plant would bring to five cassava processing plants in the state as there were four others in Okitipupa, Oba-Ile, Ogbese and Ondo with a total capacity of over 1,000 tonnes of cassava per day.The Special Adviser who noted that the present administration has impacted positively on the lives of the people of the state in the area of poverty alleviation said concerted efforts will be geared towards the creation of more wealth so that many people can become self- employed and be able to employ some other people as employers of labour.
He said enough opportunities will be created for the people to access the various wealth creation agenda of the government in the area of fish production, honey production and poultry farming, among others. He said there is no reason for anybody to be poor in Ondo State because of the abundant resources that are available, adding that enough awareness would be created for people to see that certain things which they don't consider as money spinner abound everywhere and could be used to their advantage.

Original_Search_Title: Soludo Wants On-Going Reforms Sustained
Headline: Nigeria: Soludo Wants On-Going Reforms Sustained
Body_Text:
Lagos — For Nigeria to be an economic powerhouse in the year 2020, current market oriented reforms must be deepened and sustained, Governor of the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN), Prof. Chukwuma Soludo has said.
This was the submission of Soludo while delivering a paper titled "Market Oriented economic reforms and the challenges of technological development in Nigeria," last Friday at the Bells University of Technology's 1st Eminent Persons' Lecture in Ota, Ogun State.
He noted that major market oriented reforms that were introduced by the Obasanjo administration, yielded positive results as the revolution in the telecommunication sector is powering productivity in the economy.
"Prices of commodities are going down, for example, the price of garri has fallen significantly due to the boom in the production of cassava. The phenomenal growth in the nation's capital market also attests to the achievement of the reforms. The market capitalisation of the Nigerian Stock Exchange (NSE) has grown to over N8 trillion. The big challenge now is that of sustainability, can we sustain the momentum?" he asked?
On the need to embrace technology, Soludo said the 21st century more than the 20th century belongs to science and technology, stressing that "countries are going to be distinguished not by their population or natural resources but by the level of their technological advancement.
On what extent Nigeria should rely on market-oriented instrument to drive its development, the CBN governor declared, "Our market oriented reforms will not be sustained unless it is accompanied by similar kind of revolution in building technology.
"Which market oriented reforms makes the difference? There are two growth insights that provoke the nexus of growth, the classical growth model and the new indigenous growth theory. The classical growth model believes the growth process in technology is assumed to be important and in the long run drive the growth process. The new indigenous model helps to accumulate more capital and grow faster. The message here is that the growth process of a country depends on the efficiency of both labour and capital. Any society that is able to accumulate a certain level of human capital will have its economy grow without a stop, there is no diminishing return in this system, the economy will grow infinitely," he said.
The indigenous model, he added helps to know that it is the level of human capital that you are able to accumulate that makes the difference.
The CBN boss noted that some of the growth that Nigeria has experienced is as a result of the deployment of capacity utilisation, increase in human capital and productivity enhancement.
"How then do we then accumulate technological capability? Why does market economy foster technological advancement than none market economy? The market economy is not without flaws, but there is no system known to man that is better than the market economy," he said.
He added that private market oriented economy has greater chance of fostering competition, "all scientific and technological inventions in the 20th century is as a result of market oriented economy. If you look at all sectors of the Nigerian economy, there has been a remarkable improvement either as a result of productivity enhancement or increase factor in human capacity. Creation and domesticating technological advancement which is deposited in individuals is what makes the difference."

Original_Search_Title: Up the Mountain
Headline: Uganda: Up the Mountain
Body_Text:
Kampala — IT is 5:00am. Seated on one of the many Kalita Transporters buses that ply the Kampala-Kasese route, I look on as more passengers come on board, clad in warm clothing and clutching polythene bags containing their breakfast.
It is my first time to go to Kasese, home of the Rwenzori Mountains. As I wait for the journey to begin, I contemplate what life must be like in the mountainous area that is one of Uganda's major tourist attractions.
After sitting through the ride and seeing many vehicles involved in accidents along the way, I am welcomed by a hot Kasese afternoon.
However, as I arrive in Kyanjuki valley, it soon turns into a very cold one with a heavy downpour.
Tomorrow, I will attempt to climb what is known locally as the 'world hill' because from its top, you can look over a broad expanse which includes parts of Fort Portal, Kasese, Bushenyi and the Democratic Republic of Congo.
This mountainous area is home to the Bakonzo and Bamba people, also known as Banyarwenzururu (people of the snow).
According to Noah Nzaghale, the general secretary of the Obusinga bwa Rwenzururu cultural institution, they started living up the mountains in 1919 during the early struggles for the Obusinga.
The Obusinga is a cultural institution that brings together tribes in the Mt. Rwenzori area under a king called the Omusinga.
The current king is Charles Wesley Iremangoma Mumbere, who will this month make 19-year-old Agnes Ithungu his wife.
The struggle that established the kingdom in the early 1900s was between different chieftains and Toro kingdom.
Nzaghale says the kingdom was forcefully assimilating the Bamba and Bakonzo, "taking away their freedom by turning them into slaves, forcing them to speak a language other than Lukonzo, taxing their agricultural produce and taking away their land".
To guard their freedoms, the Bakonzo and Bamba took to an armed struggle, fighting at first with rocks which they rolled down the mountain slopes at their enemy and later, as the years passed, with rifles.
Leading the battles, says Katya Aganatia, the representative in charge of planning on the Obusinga council, were three chiefs - Nyamustwa, Tibamwenda and Kapoli.
Tom Stacey, in his book, Tribe, The Hidden History of the Mountains of the Moon, quotes James Elliot, a doctor in the Colonial Service Cadet, Fort Portal in 1920 as saying: "The Bakonzo are people that live in the foothills and lower parts of the Mountains of the Moon.
They rebelled because they were being orphaned of the joy and clarity to be themselves, in their Rwenzururu."
"They were stripped and orphaned by the lords and nobles of Toro and their Bito aristocracy who were awarding themselves land," wrote Stacey.
I spend the night before my hike confined to my room, looking outside as it rains cats and dogs for the early part of the night. Finally a hot cup of coffee and the rhythmic sound of the waters of the Nyamwamba river hitting against the rocks send me to sleep.
In the morning I wake up to the same sound, thinking it is still raining. But the sun's glare through the window tells of a beautiful, sunny day - a day in which I shall talk with the people and climb to the 'top of the world'.
Kabolibo Jarom Marahi is our tour guide up Masule Hill, which forms one small arm of the Rwenzoris. The Bakonzo and Bamba people, he tells us, are short, which serves them well as it is easier for their bodies to store enough warmth for them to survive the cold weather.
As we climb, my eyes feast on the women tilling the land, the thick vegetation that grows between the mountainous ridges and the numerous steep hills that rise about 50km high.
In the valleys are the many cassava and sugarcane plantations, irrigated by the numerous rivers that flow from the mountain before joining the Nyamwamba which runs by the temporarily closed Kilembe Copper mines.
The cool breeze reminds us to walk faster or risk getting soaking wet and being washed back down the steep hill.
As we walk, our guide tells stories about the people of the mountains, their history and beliefs.
He talks about the local gods including Mulekya, the god in charge of peace, who was called upon whenever bullets were be heard or there was a rumour of war.
The people would offer hens, goats, local beer and bananas to Mulekya. Then they would sweep all the routes leading to the hills, symbolically chasing away the enemy. In exchange for the people's gifts, Mulekya would protect the region from war.
The gods are less frequently consulted today, Kabolibo says, adding that this has led to many problems, such as insurgencies and shortage of food.
From the Obusinga struggles that ended with the formation of the cultural institution in 1962 and the enthronement of Omusinga Isaiah Mukirania Kibanzanga I as king, the area had never suffered from any wars, until the rebel Allied Democratic Forces attacked in 2000.
Holding onto grass along the narrow trail, I struggle to keep moving up the hill, but the change in the soil texture is making it impossible for me to keep my grip. I soon fall and slide on the mixture of shiny, black soil that characterises the hill.
A coffee plant I come upon on my way down saves me from falling on the roof of a grass-thatched, mud-and-wattle house.
At this point, the whole group takes a break as the guide helps me back up the hill.
From up here you have a great view of small matooke and cassava gardens as well women and children carrying sacks of sand and traditional baskets - ebithiri - full of food.
In addition to Mulekya, the god of peace, there is Omusabire, a goddess who is called upon in case of bad weather. The goddess Omulembere cares for children, while the goddess Nyabahasa, protects twins and prevents them from bringing harm to others.
Kabolibo tells us that the mountain people have just six names for male children and seven for girls. If a man fathers more than that number, he must create names for the other children.
The children are named according to the order of their birth. A boy will be called, Mumbere if when he is born both his parents are alive or Baluku if just one parent is alive.
The others are Bwambale, Marah, Kule, Thembo and Mbusa, in that order. The girls will be named Musoki if both parents are alive or Masika if one parent has died, Biira, Kabuho, Mbambu, Ithungu, Kyakima and Nzabake.
However, a man will usually marry as many women as he wants and the set names will, therefore, apply to the first 13 children, though many women will insist on using the traditional names to name only the children they have produced. It is not surprising, therefore, to find a man with more than one child bearing the same name.
Hidden behind the hills, 2km from Kasese town stands a grass-thatched building which is the kingdom's assembly hall. Jutting out of the roof is a two-headed spear that symbolises the king - 0musinga - and the queen. Next to it is the Bwikira (palace) and in a small hut nearby is the royal regalia.
Inside the grass-thatched is a raised platform for the king's seat and benches for his chiefs to sit on as he addresses them.
On the back wall of the hall is the kingdom flag in four colours: "Yellow for the sun, green for the evergreen vegetation, white for the snow that characterises the Rwenzori Mountains and blue for the water bodies," says Nzaghale.
The institution's emblem, has small drums which represent the chiefdoms, a big drum and lion, representing the king, a colobus monkey for the peace-loving people, a sickle to signify the clearing of the land for cultivation, a crown for the king and a book to represent the Bible which guides the Obusinga.
Among the pillars of the Obusinga are clan heads, veterans of the war that gave birth to the Obusinga and chieftains, as well as an environmentally friendly way of life.
For instance, reveals Nathe Kahamba, the caretaker of the regalia: "If you excreted in water bodies, it was believed that spirits would attack you. It was also believed that if two unmarried people of the opposite sex sneaked off into the bush, they would catch an incurable disease.
This prevented the spread of sexually transmitted diseases. And if you went hunting, you waited for a line of animals to go by and then killed only the one that dropped out of the line. This helped preserve wildlife,"
The Banyarwenzururu also have an entertaining way of life that teaches history from generation to generation through dance and drama.
Through dancing, for example, they can tell a story of love and marriage or recount how the people gave moral support to warriors going to war and mourned a dead warrior.
As we continue trekking, I learn that the staple food in the area is bundhu - cassava bread - or yams mixed with cassava, served with beans or fish.
They also eat the soft cassava leaves, the shoots of yams, Irish potatoes and matooke.
The sight of people dressed in bright colours tells of a happy people, despite the fact that many of them live on the poverty line. Their houses are built out of reeds and mud, with roofs of ironsheets or grass.
The area is blessed with many herbs, right from those that treat malaria, stomach disorders, skin diseases, snake bites, to those that induce abortions. Some plants' leaves are pounded and used as soap.
Tired, sweating and with our legs unable to carry us any further, we descend Masule hill. At some points we have to use our hands to prevent us from rolling down the hill.
We retire without making it to the'world hill', but appreciative of what these people go through everyday and fascinated by the speed at which they climb the slopes.

Original_Search_Title: Love, Marriage, Money And Violence in a Corner of the Country
Headline: Nigeria: Love, Marriage, Money And Violence in a Corner of the Country
Body_Text:
Nairobi — On a 12-hour journey across Nigeria, the landscape, disparities in regional economies and the ritual of marriage provide OKELLO OCULI with interesting vistas of Africa's most populous nation
A Vintage car in use during a wedding in Kenya.
On the June 30, 2007 I travelled by bus from Abuja, Nigeria's capital in the centre of the country, to Port Harcourt on the oil-rich Atlantic Ocean coast. The bus was what the women in the group called a "motor" since it crawled painfully slowly compared to "buses" that sped past us.
Our sporty group was travelling to conduct the first ritual of a marriage between a young man from Kogi State (that borders the capital territory) and a girl from among the Izon (who the British colonials rudely called "Ijaw") people from Bayelsa State.
Bayelsa State made global news in 2006 with the drama-laden arrest of its ruler (state governor) by British authorities for possessing tens of millions of pounds Sterling in cash in his flat. He jumped bail. The Nigerian media probably created a computer photograph of him boarding a plane at Gatwick Airport dressed as a "high-society Lagos lady".
The girl's parents lived on the mainland in Port Harcourt. That saved us a repeat of an exciting trip I had earlier on made on a motor-boat along winding water alleys for one-hour to a place named Akassa. The bus trip lasted from 7am to 9pm with only one stop to fix a puncture and several short stops for the elderly passengers to empty their bladders. The crawl gave the mostly born-again Christians on board a long chance to chat with Jesus in songs claiming victories against hazards on the road, including those invoked by evil folks opposed to the marriage on both ends of the trip. Victories were proclaimed against the devil if he dared turn hearts of future in-laws against the wedding all the way to the very last minute before the girl gave her consent. More on that later.
The geography of the lands we traversed aroused a variety of emotions and thoughts. At the level of thoughts, the land was vast and covered in lush vegetation that ran from low umbrella-shaped treetops and short shrubs in central savanna areas where we started from, to sky-high tree trunks and numerous palm trees.
Palm trees were once among the six-member pantheon of agricultural Gods that sat over the fortunes of colonial Nigeria. The others were groundnuts and cotton from the Sahel zone in the far north. There was also cocoa and rubber in the south. The British were not interested in food crops like yam, cassava, simsim, beans, and a teaming variety of vegetables and condiments.
Today an ancient roaring trade in tomatoes, onions, thin hot pepper, slimy okra pods, smoked dry fish, long-horned live cattle and brown goats runs from the Sahel north to the south, but the British colonial agents of industries in Manchester and Liverpool had not been unduly interested in them. So, the international media ignored them.
Gods of colonial agriculture
The land remains very sparsely cultivated and populated outside of the Igbo-settled south-east. For its vast lands waiting to carry burdens of feeding stomachs all across Africa, this sector of Nigeria makes claims of Nigeria being a highly populated country most unconvincing.
The scrawny leaves on palm trees that struggle to stand out above wild bush thickets, are evidence of the long neglect that has failed former Gods of colonial agriculture from whom vigorous young men have fled to grab their share of wealth to be made, since the 1970s, from chains of trading in crude oil.
A short distance from Lokoja, capital of Kogi State, is the iron-ore rich Ajaokuta town. In the 1970s, the former Soviet Union was rewarded for supporting federal Nigeria against the breakaway war by Biafra (the State the Igbos wished for themselves), by getting a nod to construct a vast steel plant in Ajaokuta. The World Bank, the British, American, French and German governments would wage fierce diplomatic battles to abort the project. To the conservative feudal leaders of northern Nigeria, the capitalists waved a frightening spectre of communist influences and violent revolution if the Russians came to Nigeria in their thousands to build the plant.
