I had to call the appropriate Ijaw youth leader in-charge of such matters to issue a 48-hour ultimatum to the kidnappers of the British toddler, Margaret Hill, failure upon which they should move in and tell them that we are not ready for nonsense. So, we are working with an understanding.
Change of strategy: I have been going to the creeks, the thinking of these people is the founding document of the struggle, which is Kaiama Declaration, and I can assure you that all the militant groups are well informed about the Kaiama Declaration and we know what we are doing. Naturally, in every situation, there are those who cash-in to mess up things and we are taking up the challenge and ensure that we remove those elements that are trying to mess up our genuine agitation, which is where we are going. And my intention is to make sure that in the next few weeks, all the groups, as the IYC constitution allows, are put into the council of organizational leaders so that we can control things. By so doing, you will know if the Ijaws have taken hostage and the reason for it. We are saying that we are about changing strategy and those thinking that this hostage thing will be there for them to be making money will soon find themselves in the lurch, as the strategy would change and they would not be able to take hostages again.
Well, the Acting Inspector General of police, Mr. Mike Okiro, recently, alluded to the fact that 80 per cent of the hostage takers were not really from the Niger-Delta but how and why did the militants involved in the struggle allow infiltrators to take over?
I may not be able to quantify the percentage of those involved in kidnapping that are not Ijaws but I can attest to you that the ugly trend you have seen recently in this hostage taking thing, definitely, cannot be an Ijaw assignment because the militant groups in Ijaw are very focused. When they take hostages, they put the demands on the table before the world. They tell the world, this is what we are saying, develop the region, create jobs and all that.Yes, in the course of getting to release hostages, perhaps, I may not know, I have not been a negotiator in that direction, I don't know if money changes hands but I am sure that Ijaw struggle, hostage taking is part of it, if it becomes the only way that government will listen to us. I subscribe to it but that there are some elements who are not Niger-Deltans, who are non-Ijaws and are trying to rubbish us, the government and politicians are trying to call a dog a bad name in order to hang it.
So, they may have been funding some of these skeletal groups to discredit the strategy which we created to prosecute our struggle. So, we are changing our strategy and in a short while, the world will get to see that our strategy has changed and this one will not give room for infiltrators to participate in it.
Why it will be difficult to drop arms: I want to say categorically that the IYC, the militant wing, the generality of Ijaw people are peace loving people. Twenty years ago, if you touch an Ijaw man, he would tell you, "sorry, I did not know that you were going to pass here," but circumstances and events unfolding have put the Ijaw man on the defensive and all our actions, whether the one you say is right or wrong, are in the defence of our territory. Basically, what we are doing today is towards a goal. We may keep changing the strategy until we get to that goal. So, the issue of dropping arms may not arise if the Nigerian government is not sincere in itself.
But once we see genuine approach by the Nigerian state, we are peace loving people, what business do we have to do with arms if not for the way we are being treated? We were there all these years, we never carried arms. Circumstances and situations resulted in arms carrying. The Nigerian state itself introduced violence into the creeks, they came there with the military and the people had to defend themselves.
I am sure that with a genuine drive by the government and the people of this country to put back what is taken from the region to the region genuinely, talking about resource control, derivation and others, once they start doing these things, they don't need to ask anybody to drop arms. Willfully, everybody will drop his arms but if you want to use force and other negative things, it will not stand the test of time. You may get federal security agencies to protect the companies, oil workers and platforms but how long is that going to continue?
You said that you subscribe to hostage taking, which is to say that you subscribe to criminality because hostage taking is a criminal act, why?
Yes, criminality is an event, laws are made by men and when they are made, if you work contrary to the law, it is an offence, you have broken the law. The law says hostage taking is criminal but, to us, the law does not apply because whatever the Nigerian state is doing to us is more criminal than hostage taking. They have harassed us, they have molested us, they have undermined us, they have underdeveloped us, they have impoverished us, they have taken our resources, and taken us hostage too. They have made us so inhuman; they have dehumanized us to the extent that what we are doing is a reaction to the action meted on us.
So, the issue of criminality to my mind does not exist. Because I live in a country where I cannot even talk about my own God-given right and wealth, I can't even see it, have to go cap in hand to pick crumbs from what God has used to bless us. Our aquatic life is dead, you cannot plant yam in our region to grow, and our cassava cannot give good harvest. These underdevelopments and molestations in themselves are worse than hostage taking. So, if government is talking about the criminality in hostage taking, it should first of all address the fundamentals that led to hostage taking.

Original_Search_Title: Weekend of Fine Repeat Comedies
Headline: Kenya: Weekend of Fine Repeat Comedies
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Quick on the laughter lines of Marc Carmolleti's comedy Boeing, Boeing, Derek Benfield's Don't Lose The Place, which ended a fortnight ago, came yet another weekend of mirth last week, courtesy of comedies A Bridegroom for Sale and Tiga Miaki, the Kikuyu adaptation of Ray Cooney's How The Other Half Loves.
The common streak about them? They are all repetitions.
And while a group like Festival of Creative Arts have been setting a commendable standard in amateur theatre with its excellent casting, great scripts, superb sets, admirable marketing and good acting, the outfit has acquired an enviable following on the back of blasts from the past - When Did You Last See Your Trousers, She Ate the Female Cassava, A Fly in the Ointment and Look Who's Talking? which were all repetitions, albeit more professionally done.
Last week too, Heartstrings Kenya, a group that has been staging rib-cracking farces for the past 11 years, opened this month with Dennis Willis's comedy Bridegroom For Sale at the Alliance Francaise auditorium. The hilarious comedy was last staged nine years ago.
And next month, Festival will stage Benfield's riotous farce Bedside Manners "due to public demand" at the Alliance Francaise. The play was last performed by Mbalamwezi Players in 1998 and starred Suzanne Makena, Aleks Kamau and Tony Njuguna.
But why are theatre groups re-staging past hits?
"Because there are times when one wants a particular type of play or a specific theme and twist, but can't find it," says Sammy Mwangi, a director at Heartstrings.
"Then it could be that the play was not a hit when it was last performed and, as a director, you have seen it in a different light." Mwangi will next month direct Larry Shue's The Foreigner, which has not been performed before.
Eliud Abuto of Festival looks at redoing plays differently. "Local theatre has been suffering from lack of an audience for years. And re-staging hits is a way of bringing them back and thus creating audience loyalty after which one changes tact," he says. "Ray Cooney's Chase Me, Comrade and Francis Durbridge's Deadly Night Cap, which Festival will stage before the year ends, are not repetitions."
Titi Wainaina will direct fast rising theatre darling Francis Amos who will play Ferris, the manager of a hotel in Naivasha where Roger (Joshua Mwai) and Sally (Mbeki Mwalimu) arrive for a secret romantic escapade after leaving at home their spouses - Helen (Hellen Waithera), Roger's wife, and Sally's hubby Geoff (Derrick Amunga). But Helen and Geoff had also planned a steamy weekend together... in the same hotel!
Isn't repeating themed plays a way of short-changing theatre-goers while trying to remain relevant? "Kenyan theatre-goers are very discerning." says Mwangi. "And we gave them a raw deal when we staged A Fish Out of Water. We will never re-stage a play which is less than 10 years old."
Well, for sampling a fresh play perhaps one might consider watching Eugene Ionesco's classical The Bald Soprano, which ends today at the Phoenix Players.

Original_Search_Title: PDP Board of Trustees' Controversy: Why Sycophancy is the Name of the Game
Headline: Nigeria: PDP Board of Trustees' Controversy: Why Sycophancy is the Name of the Game
Body_Text:
Iro Dan Musa
You once said that Nigeria is too complicated for President Umaru Musa Yar'Adua to govern. That was before he was elected. Do you still hold the same opinion now?
First of all, I wish to congratulate President Umaru Musa Yar'Adua and also wish him well. It is God that bestows power on those he pleases. Well, I expressed my views at the time I did, using my experience in the last eight years since the inception of this democratic dispensation as an indigene of Katsina State where Yar'Adua hails from and who is also associated with his administration in the state since its inception to date. Having expressed my views, it is up to Nigerians to judge from subsequent events.
However, you must realise that I made that statement because I was aware that he did not sufficiently involve himself in national affairs (while serving as governor in Katsina State). He rarely attended the National Council of States (NCS) meetings, the National Economic Council (NEC), the Governors' Forum and even the National Executive Committee (NEC) meeting of the PDP at that time. I was also aware of how he governed Katsina State in the last eight years; having the tendencies to centralise and control the state administration, local government councils and the PDP administration (three-in-one) and the result/failure in that endeavour.
Can you review the eight-year administration of Chief Olusegun Obasanjo as the President of this country and tell us where he performed creditably and also where his administration failed.
I think Obasanjo has performed well in so many areas, but what one has to understand is that democratic rule is governed by the constitution and the requirements of the constitution and the stipulation of the constitution is that security and welfare of Nigerians are the primary purpose of government. In other words, the performance will be determined in terms of security and welfare that are available to Nigerians.
Unfortunately, in these two areas, there is less to show. But this is not to say that there are no areas that significant progress had not been made. Take for instance the financial sector. Definitely, Obasanjo has done very well. Take for instance again, the communication sector, certainly, he did very well.
Also if we talk of pension reforms, certainly he has done very well. I was giving you example with myself when only about four years ago I was earning N14, 000 per mouth as a pensioner. But as at today, I earn about N43,000 per month as a result of the review of pension scheme and I have been paid. Certainly, this is a significant development. Even in the area of agriculture, if you take some of the initiatives, for example the one on cassava; that is the Presidential Initiative on Cassava, you see this crop which is grown everywhere in the country, you know, awareness of its use and export value has been brought about by Obasanjo.
So, there are many other areas like that where certainly, he recorded successes. However, there are areas like the energy and power sectors where his performance certainly left much to be desired, like in the area of electricity. Also, in case of privatisation, he failed. I think this is my assessment but on the whole, some of these reforms, like I have been saying, have immediate pains but the benefits are of long term.
Obasanjo was a PDP president. How did his activities impact on the party?
Well, ideally, party president is expected to implement party programmes.
So, if he succeeded in implementing some of those programmes, it means that he impacted on the PDP. But I think the issue is interaction between the party and the president as the leader of the party, whether such interaction has resulted in promoting or impacting on the party. I can say that it was during his tenure that a reform committee was set up under my chairmanship and we amended the constitution (of the party), the manifesto and introduced so many measures that would ensure internal democracy and functionality of party organs. So, whether that has been done by the people who are managing the party on-day-to-day basis, is a different issue.
During Obasanjo's tenure, the party hadn't much influence on him but when he left; he became the chairman of the party's BOT with enormous powers to supervise so many things done by the current administration?
It is not true that the party hadn't much influence over him when he was in power. No, certainly during the tenure of Chief Solomon Lar as PDP Chairman of which I was part of, during the tenure of Barnabas Gemade of which I was also part of, we tried to work as a party. We only met Mr. President in meetings of appropriate organs of the party. Like at the National Caucus level, at the National Executive Committee level, we never asked for the permission of Mr. President to do anything or to hold any meetings. But I think things have subsequently changed. Maybe because of the style of the new leadership headed by Ahmadu Ali because they had the opportunity of having the new constitution; the new manifesto and the opportunity also to ensure that all organs of the party are functioning. Unfortunately, they over-centralised decision-making process thereby rendering other lower organs of the party ineffective.
You see, with that, you take decisions at the centre, of course, perhaps in consultation with the leader of the party, leaving the lower segments completely out of the normal functions as assigned to them by the party constitution. So, that was what really happened. But now that you have mentioned his role after leaving office, well, the very amendment, that was introduced to facilitate that, should be understood because this amendment was not part of our amendment during the constitutional reviews.
Our amendments were ratified by the convention of December, 2005 whereas they changed the composition; the functions of the BOT was decided by the NEC of the party in its 33rd meeting. Okay, whether this one has been ratified by the convention of the party, I don't know because I was not at the venue of that convention in December last year but certainly, the new role is very, very interesting and I don't like to say much on it because it has so many implications on the party, to the polity and so on and so forth.
I am not sure if many people really understand the implications. First of all now, the chairman of BOT has to be a former president of which he is the only one and it is only after his turn that any other one can be appointed. In other words, may be before he goes, another former president will emerge to become the Senate president and so it will continue to be former presidents' business. The most interesting part of this is the functions that now everything revolves around the BOT chairman. You know, this involves government policies and so many other things calling for the attention or needing the attention or intervention of the chairman of the BOT. How it will work, we don't know because we are just beginning so it will be too early to comment on it or to say how it is going to work. We only hope that things will be sorted out in the interest of the PDP, the government and the country.
You are a foundation member of the PDP, from G.18 to G.34 and all that. Do you think that what is happening in PDP now was envisaged by those who formed the party?
No. We stated our mission. We stated our covenant with Nigerians. Really, there is serious departure from our mission. In fact; that is why I said that I will remain in PDP. I will advise my colleagues to stay in the PDP so that we try to mobilise the people to show that PDP as a party, is a good party. PDP is not shegiyar uwar mai kashe digonta, (Hausa saying meaning: bastard mother that kills her children, as it is popularly known in the north), because we are its parents. I testify that it has a very good constitution.
It has a very good manifesto. I had the opportunity of being the chairman of the committee that amended the two documents, so the problem of the PDP as it is today, is the problem of the operators who were not around when the party was formed, who had no time to understand why the party was formed. So all it means is that the PDP constitution and manifesto have been thrown to the dogs. People do what they think pleases them or is in their own interest and not the founding fathers' interest. So, I think this is the tragedy facing the PDP now but PDP as a party, is a very good party and I will appeal to every Nigerian to come and join the party so that they will help us to rescue the PDP.
The PDP recently published the list of new BOT members but your name was not there. Why did this happen? Again, is it not ironic that you are asking Nigerians to come and join the party when you don't even know your own fate in the PDP?
Well, I don't consider my position as shaky. I will only appeal to the party to investigate the circumstance leading to my name being dropped and thereafter make its findings public. I think that is what I want because it is terrible considering my contributions to the party. Apart from the fact that I was one of the original founding fathers, I have contributed to this party in terms of writing memoranda, in terms of chairing critical committees where some other people failed. Most of these committees I chaired were simply because some people who previously headed them failed. I won't mention names but I will give you example with PDP staff manual. Before my committee was able to produce it, some other notable Nigerians were given the assignment, but they failed.
We had a failed congress in Kogi State in 2003 and I had to be drafted there to organise the successful one. We had also a failed state congress in Borno in the same 2003 and I was drafted there in order to organise the successful one. I headed the mediation committee on media war between Mr. President and the National Assembly. Even that name alone will suggest to you how important that kind of committee was. Look, there were so many committees like that and I also used my initiative to write memoranda to the party suggesting new initiatives. That is why I am surprised to read from a published list in one of the newspapers that my name is not among the members of BOT of the party.
The most insulting thing is that in that list, I saw the name of a member who left the party, who took the party to court, who declared for another party and even announced the expulsion of the leaders of PDP. But he is there and my name is not there. I really don't know, so what I want really is this investigation and public declaration of why my name is not there because if in the service of this country, there will be no regard for merit, no regard for patriotism, but only sycophancy, I think it is unfortunate.
Don't you think that your problem started from your home state, Katsina, since we learnt that you have not been in the same political camp with President Yar'Adua starting from the days of NRC/SDP up to the formation of the PDP in 1998 when the president joined the PDP with his K.34 group from Katsina?
Well, you see, this is a very long history that I cannot give here. You see, my plight started from the very day the PDP was launched on August 31, 1998 because, that very day, I was confronted by the K.34 under the leadership of the current president. He was then the President-General of K.34 (a political group from the 34 local governments of Katsina); now he is the president of Nigeria.
I was confronted by some people who wanted me to hand over the PDP flag which was given to me as the convener of the party in the state to President Yar'Adua, but that is now history. Whatever happened then, despite all that I have done for the party, in terms of benefits, who is benefitting from the party today? The truth is that Yar'Adua today is the number one citizen by the grace of PDP, by the grace of our contribution to the party, we in particular from Katsina. Therefore, as number one citizen, whatever happened, whatever anyone did to him assuming any person wronged him, I think with his present status, he is the greatest beneficiary of our labour, our effort.
I think he should rise above such kind of thing. I am saying so because I cannot see how my name would be removed and taken to Abuja without him knowing about it. It cannot happen. That much I know. For somebody who a zonal reconciliation committee was sent to his house and the result of that means removal of my name, I now ask again, what was the need of sending the committee to my house or to me? So that is why I am a bit skeptical about the recently inaugurated reconciliation committee. My only hope is that the chairman of the reconciliation committee, Dr. Alex Ekwueme is a man of honour.
I have confidence in him because right from the word go; he was the chairman of our G.34. He was also the first chairman of PDP steering committee and even the BOT. Of course we were together as he was the Vice-President during the defunct National Party of Nigeria (NPN) where I was a minister. So, I believe that with him, justice will be done. I was in the South-East as a member of the reconciliation committee anyway. He has the same reason as mine for staying in the party. You know, because, he really doesn't want what he built to be destroyed otherwise, you know he resigned as chairman of the BOT and so many appeal committees were sent to him but he refused to take the position again although he still remains in the PDP. And when we had an interview with him, his reason was the same as mine, that he doesn't want what he built to be destroyed.
You see, I have been involved in party administration right from the NPN days. Well, the election I contested was to the Constituent Assembly in 1977. It was there that we formed NPN. You see, one cannot say that there is no rigging or that rigging is not being done. I mean, it is the function of manoeuvring between political parties. If you have a political party that has very strong support base in an area, or you have weak parties that are not well organised, during the election process, every party, depending on their understanding of the process, starting from the electoral body's office where the election materials and other things will be distributed, down to the polling station, you know; because you know the process, there will be a lot of intervention at each level because of the interest of your opponents and electoral officers, security personnel will be used.
During the NPN days really, I cannot say that there was no rigging but it was done in a civilised way and it never reached this stage like 2003 and 2007.
Now, what happened?
I think one has to start from 1991 or 1990/1991 during the General Ibrahim Babagida's transition programme. You know, it started from the formation of political parties. So many associations tried to form political parties. They spent fortunes but these things were not accepted. At the end of the day, there were two government parties - NRC and SDP. So, I think that was the beginning of serious rigging of election or the excessive use of money in politics. I happened to be in the NRC from the backdoor.
I worked from behind the scene. I saw when the SDP, I mean I have to give the example of my state; that is what you are looking for. The first election we had during NRC/SDP was local government. And that election for instance, in Katsina at that time, we had 20 local governments. SDP won 14 local governments leaving us with six. Then we questioned the result... because we believed that we had more support than the SDP. Then we set up a fact-finding committee under my chairmanship anyway.
We toured the whole 20 local governments and then found out that the SDP winning was not from the votes cast. It was from the biro (pen). We (late Wada Nas, Senator Samaila Mamman and I) then reviewed everything and worked out our strategies. And at the end of the day, our candidate, Saidu Baba, won the governorship election. Of curse, the current president, Alhaji Umaru Yar'Adua was the SDP governorship candidate in the said election. So I was giving you the background because at the local government election, we found out that it was rigged, okay, we took corrective measures and we won the election.
You know that in 1993, the late General Sani Abacha suspended everything until 1998 when another fight started. Even in 1999, we had serious problems about the use of money and rigging. In the primaries, you know we had only three political parties and in our area here, we didn't even hear about Alliance for Democracy (AD) so, only the PDP and APP as it was then called competed. So where we had rigging and so on was during the primaries and our candidate or aspirant was defeated.
We then found out that there was a lot of rigging by our opponent, Alhaji Yar'Adua. But this time around, we are in the same party, the PDP. But I had to intervene because the rigging was so obvious. At that time, we used direct primaries. Direct primaries are just the same as general election where those at the grassroots elect their own candidates and the results are collated up to the last constituency depending on which office for which the primary was organised.
The point I am making is that the extensive use of money was introduced from the defunct Social Democratic Party (SDP) and the National Republican Convention (NRC). Now, we have another dispensation and starting from the primaries, we discovered this kind of thing because the PDP had to change from direct to indirect primaries. That was what we had at Jos convention where delegates now had to vote.
So, during the 1999 election, we organised it and there wasn't much use of money at that time or rigging because we didn't have power over security agencies, we didn't have control of the local government finances; so, this rigging has been graduating and by 2003, it started getting out of hand. Because this spectre I have been talking about... I talked about it during the presidential committee on the review of the 1999 Constitution... that I thought the way to curb rigging is single five-year tenure of office for the president and governors, will be better than this four-year tenure of two terms. This is because, the incumbents will do everything, they have the state government money at their disposal, they also have the local government money and the local government councils at their deposal and now that they are in power, they have the chief security officers of their states at their beck and call.
This means that they deal with the police, the army and the state security officers in their states. So, I think it was in 2003 that the thing started to get out of hand but all the same, there was election as proposed. In fact, I was following the process, right from National Electoral Commission (NEC) as it was then called up to declaration of results... so, all you can do as a party is to man or supervise the process but by the year 2007, I think that what happened in this country didn't look like elections - both the primary and the secondary elections. It was simply the use of power.
You know, in terms of state power, in terms of control of local government finances, in terms of control of local government councils, opportunity to having control of security operatives at the state level... So, really, the thing (rigging) went out of hand and what is of most concern is the level of injustice inflicted on people simply because people want to win elections. That is the most unfortunate thing. This has created bad blood. You know it concerns brothers, communities and so on really. It is unfortunate.
Each time you speak about PDP, you say the party is good but for the operators. Are you talking about the National Chairman of the party, Dr. Ahmadu Ali and other leaders of the party or what?
You people quoted me as saying that Dr. Ali should resign. No. What I said was that given the situation now, if I were him, rather than clinging to the post until the party's convention in November, I think he doesn't need people to force him to resign. He should simply resign to give opportunity to others. Now we have a president from the North, we have the Vice- President from the South-South and we have the Chairman of the BOT from South-West...
So what is really holding the emergence of the new National Chairman for these few months?
I think Ojo (Maduekwe, the former National Secretary) is going to take up a ministerial appointment so what are they going to do? You know, this is the kind of thing you can do voluntarily without anybody forcing you (to resign). I keep on talking about the operators. My own assessment/observation is that... where a leader tells lies...where a leader will just shut his eyes to genuine complaints from members of the party and even leaders of the party... you know what happened in Katsina. I gave you (Press) copies of a petition signed by the major governorship aspirants in Katsina State like the former Speaker of House of Representatives, Alhaji Aminu Bello Masari, Magaji Mohammed and the other fellow...
They signed petitions to the National chairman. I mean this is a fact. But if besides all these, a leader cannot take the right action; can- not act in the interest of fairness and justice, I think it is unfortunate. You don't need any evidence to know that even those who followed normal primaries and were elected are being removed and replaced by others who lost out. You cannot do this in a democracy. You have to try as much as possible to do justice especially as this is the whole purpose of PDP reform. It was designed to bring peace, social justice and internal democracy up to the grassroots level in conformity with our constitution which declared Nigeria as a country based on democratic principles and social justice, where all those in government must ensure that people particularly in governance and their security and welfare are given the required attention.
Has Ali failed or succeeded?
Use your own judgment.
You were one of those who called for the zoning of the 2007 presidency to the north. With Yar'adua now in power, do you think that this was a result of the PDP zoning policy?
No. Yar'adua did not become president because of the zoning policy of the party. The position was not zoned to the north. It is by God's wish that Yar'Adua became president. If the party zoned the position to the north, I could not have supported Chief Victor Attah. You know that aspirants from all over the country competed. So it wasn't as a result of zoning.
However, since Yar'Adua has been elected, all other positions in the party have to be zoned because one zone cannot produce all the national leaders. It is because of Yar'Adua's election that a new BOT chairman has emerged and also a new Senate President has emerged from other geographical zones.

Original_Search_Title: Restiveness in the Niger Delta - the Long-Term Role NDDC
Headline: Nigeria: Restiveness in the Niger Delta - the Long-Term Role NDDC
Body_Text:
Whereas there were intervention agencies before the Niger Delta Development Commission, NDDC, the general acknowledgment that the Commission has stamped its imprimatur on the region sits well in some quarters. However, what the Yar'Adua government needs to do to complement its welcome initiatives in the Niger Delta region is to engage government machinery with a view to ensuring that the NDDC gets its needed funding, even as it is desirable that the Niger Delta Regional Master Plan should be given fillup by the FG
The coming of the Niger Delta Development Commission is the needed tonic for the rapid growth of the oil-rich region.
Before its coming, the Niger Delta was a sorry tale of woes with about 70% of the people living below the poverty line; the industrial development level was very low; unemployment high; healthcare delivery poor, with the region's 20% mortality rate amongst the highest in the world; transportation system inadequate with movement and access to settlements being hampered by a poor road network and difficult conditions, especially in the riverine areas; absence of electricity supply in many riverine areas and regularly interrupted supply access all areas; telecommunication was poor; there was shortage of land for development; inadequate education facilities; ineffective waste management and inadequate sanitation facilities and unending social restiveness and conflict was the order of the day.
However, the NDDC has taken giant steps to redress some of these problems, and has constructed several kilometres. of roads in the oil-producing communities and States. It has also built many bridges, schools, hospitals and rural electrifica-tion.
However, the NDDC development efforts in the Niger Delta are also constrained by weak, inefficient and dilapidated institutional capacity. There is lack of adequate trained manpower, effective and transparent working practices and systems and established institutional norms and values.
Corruption is rife and societal institutions have decayed to such an extent that the challenge of developing an efficient, transparent and accountable civil service and a strong judicial, legal and regulations capacity is an outstanding priority task for the Commission.
For the Commission to, however, ensure an enduring legacy, it must rise to the challenge of ensuring the complete development of the region, by tapping the abundant human and material resources which the region is blessed with. If property tapped, the Niger Delta has enough potential to surpass even the socio-economic and human develop-ment objectives set out in the Millennium Development Goals (MDGs) and national strategies like the NEEDS. In fact, achieving these targets is considered the minimum possible development threshold for the region.
Besides the tremendous oil and gas wealth, the Niger Delta Region is endowed with a large number of working age population and potentially cultivable land and fishery resources. If properly exploited and combined with strategic programmes, the region will, in a short time, overcome the myriad of crises that have bedeviled it.
The so many other areas into which the Niger Delta Development Commission has to focus on to bring wealth and prosperity to people of the oil-rich region include the agric sector, the solid minerals sector, forestry and livestock production and processing.
In the agric sector, the Niger Delta people were farmers which produced 50% of the nation foreign exchange earnings before the oil boom of 1970s and the region produces a variety of cash and food corps. The cash crops are palm fruits from which palm oil and palm kennels are derived, rubber and cocoa, as well as groundnuts and pineapples.
Nigeria was once the world's largest producer of palm oil and the activity was largely centred on the Niger Delta region. The oil palm remains one of the most important cash crops in the region, and could serve again as a driver for the economic development fo the region.
The NDDC has a large role to play in this regard. It could help States, local governments or even be in corporate partnership with individuals to develop palm plantations which can rival the Bayelsa palm, Ada Palm and Rison palm owned by the respective governments of Bayelsa, Imo and Rivers States. The oil palm plantations owned by the respective States and private investors have proved to be not just profitable to the investors, but also available to the local economy job creation and infrastructure development.
The other cash crops produced in the region: rubber and cocoa should be given the same attention as outlined above for oil palm. These cash crops are international commodities which would fetch those who invest in them returns in foreign exchange.
For small-scale farmers, the NDDC should encourage them to cultivate food crops such as cassava, yam, plantain and cocoyam that could be developed more efficiently.
The NDDC should also encourage farmer to cultivate rice and fruits such as pineapples, oranges, grapes, lime, lemon, guava, pawpaw and bananas. A way to encourage the local farmers would be if the NDDC offers to buy a certain percentage of corps produced off the farmers. This would spur them back to cultivate the land, de-emphasise the focus on oil and gas and return the Niger Delta to its pride of place as the nation's food basket.
In the area of aquaculture, the Niger Delta is a labyrinth of creeks and rivers and is bordered by the Atlantic ocean. Despite various efforts in the past to develop the aquaculture, the potential of the sector remains unrealized. The fact that Nigeria is the largest importer of fish in West Africa is partly indicative of the failure of past attempt to harness the fish resources and the management to meet market demand. And this failure can be laid at the door-steps of the Niger Delta as it has more water bodies than other regions in the country.
The NDDC should encourage more people in the region to take to fish farming as there are benefits and income to be derived therefrom.
However, the Commission would have to, first, organise training seminars for the would-be fish farmers and help them start off financially, by way of loans, to entice the people.
On forestry, the NDDC should encourage the plant-a-tree" programme. The Niger Delta used to be a dense mangrove forest until illegal loggers started felling the trees.
Much foreign exchange can be earned from timber and the NDDC has to educate the people to plant trees for the benefit of upcoming generations and the region itself.
The only snag in the forestry business is that it takes long for trees to mature for sale and could be discouraging. However, the NDDC could, on its own, set aside areas as forest reserves and simply employ the natives to tend these forest reserves.
Solid mineral resources are the primary raw materials for industrialization. Yet, despite the richness of these resources in the Niger Delta, people of the region have not taken advantage fo their potential. There are abundant minerals that could be marketed in a wide range of industries.
The NDDC, in partnership with States and local governments, could help develop the available solid minerals for the industrialization of the region.
Limestone and other cement materials could be utilised by the local government industries in Edo and Cross River States. Glass sand/Silica sand could also be utilised by glass industries in Edo and Ondo States, while Bitumen/Tar could be used by petrochemical and fertilizer industries in Ondo and Delta States.
There are many other solid mineral found in the Niger Delta awaiting exploitation. There is no doubt that if there is a steady supply of these solid minerals raw materials, any industries that would make use of them would spring up in the region and, when industries spring up, roads will be built, electricity will be provided and the Niger Delta youths would be gainfully employed. This would turn off the fire in crude oil and gas and the incidents of pipeline vandalisation, hostage takings would be a thing of the past, leading to a crisis-free region.
All these bring us to the regional masterplan.
The masterplan, as envisioned by the NDDC, and which has become a Bible of sorts for its Managing Director, Mr. Ndutimi Alaibe, has the potentials to turn the fortunes of the people of the region around.
Some had wondered why the Commission did not prepare the regional masterplan at inception.
The response from the NDDC has been (and it makes good sense) that what was on ground in 1999 before then President Olusegun Obasanjo sent the NDDC Bill to the National Assembly required urgent and immediate measures.
In fact, the issue of accounting policy, which took quite a while before it was instituted by the Commission (and which had saved billions of Naira for the Commission and the federal government) was pilloried by some observers as not being necessary. Those who raised the complaints then insisted that the Commission should just get cracking and leave the issue of accounting policy aside. But, it was that same accounting policy, honed on due diligence, which saved the Commission from a fraud of half a billion naira.
The master plan, which took quite a while to be packaged, proposes a holistic approach. The beauty of the master plan is its integrative elements which chain-links the process.
Perhaps, the good intentions of President Umaru Musa Yar'Adua on how best to develop the region can actually start with his administration's endorsement and buying into the master plan.

Original_Search_Title: Man And Animals Not Living in Harmony in Akagera Park
Headline: Rwanda: Man And Animals Not Living in Harmony in Akagera Park
Body_Text:
Kigali — James Kabenga, who lives in Mucucu, has spent a lot of time and money on his dam in a valley near Akagera National Park. But lately, he has seen the results of his hard work disappearing."I have spent over Frw2 million on the dam now, but the buffaloes come at night and destroy it," says Kabenga. "Even last night they were there, just look what they did to the drinking mound (ikibumbiro)!"
Kabenga is one of many residents of Rwembogo, located in the Gatsibo District, angry over wild animals from Akagera National Park. Residents say the animals invade their properties, destroy their crops, graze their farms, or even kill or injure people and cattle.
Locals say the Rwanda Office of Tourism and National Parks (ORTPN) is ignoring their complaints about wild animals from the park, and not giving them proper compensation for animals which damage their property.
Statistics from the ORTPN indicate that this year alone, 5 people and 15 cows have been killed by wild animals in areas near Akagera National Park. The highest number was between 2004-2005, when 20 cows died.
Baboons are a problem around Nyungwe. Elephants, buffaloes and lions terrorize the people around the Akagera National Park.
ORTPN provides protection for the national parks, mostly against the local communities living around the parks. But now, the rural people can no longer access the park for water, pasture, firewood, medicinal plants, or any other of their needs traditionally attached to the areas. However, the wild animals from the park are at liberty to invade these people's farms, eat/destroy their crops, valley dams, and kill or injure people and cattle, among other things.
The genesis of the problem
After the civil war of 1994, Rwanda saw the influx of many Rwandans who had been living in exile returning home. To accommodate these masses, the Government decided that parts of some protected areas would be used to resettle them.
Akagera National Park was among the affected protected areas, and it became home to hundreds of human families. In 2001, the new park boundaries for Akagera were drawn and the law of 2003 establishing park boundaries in Rwanda confirmed them.
In 2004, the ORTPN started enforcing the ban on all activities except tourism in the park. Cows found grazing in the park would either be sold, to the benefit of the ORTPN, or the owner would have to pay Frw20,000 per animal. The people regarded this as an injustice because they believed they had made a harmonious relationship with the park's wildlife.
There is a valley dam at Munini village, about 30 Kms from the park in which about 12 hippopotamus live. These hippos pose a serious threat to people's lives, their livestock and their crops. This dam happens to be near the only road that connects the village to the administrative centre at Rwembogo, as well as the main Kigali-Nyagatare highway. People are afraid to go pass the dam after 7 pm for fear of the hippos, which is the time they get out of water to graze.
The people tell their suffering
Residents claim the unrestricted movement of wild animals from the park is one of the reasons they live in poverty.
People in Munini, a residential area (umudugudu) in Rwembogo, say they cannot grow cassava because of wild pigs and other animals from the park. Locals also have trouble growing maize because it happens to be a favorite dish for hippos.
George Gahondogo, a resident from Mucucu in Rwembogo, says the buffaloes are their number one threat. The lions kill in other areas like Rwemiyaga in Nyagatare District, but it is the buffaloes that are responsible for most cattle and human deaths or injuries in Mucucu.
Others like Gahondogo claim they are afraid to bring forward their complaints for fear ORTPN will punish instead of compensating them.
"We used to report our losses to the game rangers but we stopped after realizing it was in vain. Now we cannot even dare ask," says Bazimya Steven of Mucucu.
The ORTPN counters
According to Telesphore Ngoga, ORTPN's Community Conservation Manager, ORTPN currently does not compensate people for the damages caused by park animals because there is no law determining how to do that.
"There is yet no law determining things like who gets compensated, by what measure, proof of damage, where will the money come from, etc., but we are working on one," says Ngoga.
Ngoga, however, says that although ORTPN does not compensate the people, it does not just ignore them either.
"We extend some sort of assistance to various victim categories, in a gesture of good neighborliness," Ngoga says.
He explains that for every cow killed by park animals, the park gives the owner 20,000 Frw, as assistance. Where a person is injured by animals, ORTPN contributes for the medical bills, or up-keep in hospital. In case of a human death, ORTPN covers the burial expenses.
However, some residents are unaware of these policies. Gahondogo simply laughed at the idea that ORTPN gives assistance of any kind to victims of park animals.
"I have never heard any one assisted by ORTPN. Buffaloes injure people and kill our animals all the time but I never heard any one get money from ORTPN," he said.
Some victims do not get any help because they do not know when, where and how to claim it. After all, these are remote areas and their local leaders cannot forward their claims because there is no compensation obligation on the part of ORTPN.
Ngoga also expressed hope that the planned construction of an electric fence dividing the national park from peoples' lands will bring a lasting solution to most of the current complaints against the park.
"Experts from South Africa have already been here to inspect the park and we hope the feasibility study will be completed before the end of the year," says Ngoga.
Revenue sharing
The ORTPN gives 5 per cent of tourism revenue to districts with national parks to invest them in projects that benefit the people living there. It is supposed to benefit people who are affected by tourism.
Murara Fred Kazoora, the executive secretary of Rwembogo sector, says ORTPN is excellent at giving back to the community, but he feels they could do a little more to compensate residents.
When ORTPN captures people's cattle in the park, and owners have no money to pay fines immediately, Kazoora's office writes to ORTPN guaranteeing payment and the cows are released. However, when people bring claims about animal damages, his office cannot forward their claim to ORTPN because they have no compensation policy.
Still, Kazoora says the people could have a more positive attitude towards ORTPN.
"The people are beginning to understand that the park's animals are in fact also their own, it's more like your own cow killing another", he added.
The president's remarks
While addressing local government leaders of the Eastern Province at Rwamagana last month, President Paul Kagame remarked that the ORTPN should find a way of compensating people who incur losses due to wild animals from the park. Since ORTPN fines the people when they damage the park, ORTPN should also pay when the park affects the people, the president remarked.
The president's remarks have triggered renewed interest in the debates about the issue of compensating rural community members for damages by park animals. And this does not apply to the Akagera Park alone, but to all communities living around national parks in the country.
ORTPN says it is working on creating a compensation plan before the end of this year.

Original_Search_Title: Poverty-driven activities continue to thwart efforts at achieving sustainable management.
Headline: Cameroon: State and stakes, poverty-driven activities continue to thwart efforts at achieving sustainable management.
Body_Text:
Since the UN Conference on Environment and Development that took place in Rio de Janeiro in 1992 giving birth to the famous "text of application" in the name of Agenda 21, public awareness on the environment has taken a significant upward mobility. But just how this awareness has helped in the responsible management of our environment is the problem.
The State of Cameroon's environment today leaves much to be desired. A medley of human activities has, and continues to gag and muffle both the country's rural and urban environment.
Cameroon's rural environment covers mainly the forestry and agricultural sectors. The forest resources are estimated at about 22 million hectares of which 14 million are tropical rain forest located in the southern part of the country and the remaining 8 million located in the central and northern Savannah areas. The forest contains an estimated 1.5 billion M3 of timber. Prior to the oil boom era, forest had played an insignificant role in the country's economy. However, with the decline of the role of oil in the country's economy and the need to augment shortfall in state finances, the government had to turn to the forest, by increasing the exportation of timber.
Thus forest exploitation increased and it is estimated that about 200,000 hectares of forest are lost annually. Annual timber production is estimated at 2 million M3 and exports are in the order of magnitude of 1 million M3 per annum. Also, because of the high rate of forest exploitation, over 40 species of wildlife such as the black rhinoceros, gorillas and elephants are threatened with extinction.
Cameroon's forest according to authorities of the Ministry of Forestry and Wildlife, contributes 11 % to the Gross National Product and 20 % to the country's exports, occupying second position after oil. As government progresses with its policy of reversing the wanton exploitation of the product, the big question remains how to reconcile development with conservation. The laws are many and good. And as the out gone US Ambassador underscored, Cameroon has distinguished itself in its designation of protected area. "16.5 % of the territory has already been designated for protection", he said.
That notwithstanding, the rural environment continues to be affected by agricultural activities. In the mountain forest region, forests have been cut down and replaced with farmland. This is done through slash and burn system of farming. This involves clearing a plot, planting crops such as maize, beans, cocoyam, cassava and vegetables for a numbers of years. When the productivity of the soil declines, the plot is left fallow and degraded. After several years the farmers return to the plot. In some places the plot is left fallow for about 13 years.
The pressure on the forest is blamed on increased food insecurity resulting from rapid population growth, degradation of agriculture and arable lands, and ill adapted policies to support food production.
Human activities have contributed in altering the country's climate. The British High Commissioner to Cameroon Syd Maddicott expressed fears at the changing climate recently in the following words. "The phenomenon poses a threat to the economy, health, security, prosperity and the wellbeing of every human in the world." The effects, he said are already being felt in Yaounde where floods often take control of the central town. In his words, climate change occurs because of global warming that is blamed on the upsurge of carbonic gas emissions triggered by a rise of human activity.
In spite of the operation green sahel launched some years ago, the desert continues to advance towards the south.
The situation of Cameroon's urban environment is not yet alarming but disturbing signs are already showing their ugly head. The Douala metropolitan area alone has a current estimate of 2.5 million people and the fastest growing rate of urbanization. It is characterised by scarcity of land, water pollution caused by poor drainage system and acute shortage of drinking water.
The two major cities are characterised by poor sewage disposal and a steady growth in pollution-prone activities. The waste disposal from industries, especially in Douala has started having a toll on the coastal zone. The exploitation of the mangrove forests in the creeks is posing a big threat to the survival of the creatures endemic to the habitat.

Original_Search_Title: Innovate to Reduce Poverty - UN
Headline: Africa: Innovate to Reduce Poverty - UN
Body_Text:
Nairobi — To escape the poverty trap, the world's 50 least developed countries (LDCs) must narrow the technology gap between them and the rest of the world, the UN Conference on Trade and Development (UNCTAD) says in its 2007 report.
The director of UN-HABITAT's Monitoring and Research Division, Banji Oyelaran-Oyeyinka, said at the launch of the report in Nairobi, the Kenyan capital, said LDCs, most of which are in Africa and Asia, must increase the knowledge intensity of their economies.
"For example, how do you take a tuber like cassava or maize and transform it into a pharmaceutical product?" Oyeyinka said. "One must increase knowledge intensity to transform a low-value product into a high-value product; LDCs must use innovative designs in order to move from the laboratory to the market, especially in the agricultural field."
UNCTAD identified the 50 countries as least developed in terms of their low gross domestic product (GDP) per capita, weak human assets and high degree of economic vulnerability.
"LDCs must innovate their way out of poverty," says the UNCTAD report, which is subtitled Knowledge, Technological Learning and Innovation for Development.
The report makes practical suggestions and argues that knowledge-based development could be the foundation for a "re-invigorated and forward-looking partnership" for development in LDCs.
One must increase knowledge intensity to transform a low-value product into a high-value product
According to UNCTAD, at least 767 million people live in LDCs and 70 percent of their labour force work in agriculture. However, between 2003 and 2005, donors provided only US$22 million per year to support national agricultural research; $9 million for agricultural extension; and $12 million per year for agricultural education and training.
"This downward trend is particularly disturbing as agricultural research and extension are key priorities of the poverty reduction strategies of LDC governments," UNCTAD said. "Grinding rural poverty is mostly due to low levels of agricultural productivity as well as declining farm sizes. This makes science-based agricultural development urgent yet public investment in agricultural research in LDCs is at its lowest level since 1971."
National policies
Science, technology and innovation (STI) policies have been mostly neglected, according to the report, particularly in the context of structural adjustment programmes of the 1980s and 1990s and the Poverty Reduction Strategy Papers this decade.
To upgrade technological capacity, STI must become a priority for LDCs, which should focus on policies to leverage more technological learning from international markets.
"Knowledge is becoming more and more important in global production and competition, and there is a danger that LDCs will be increasingly marginalised if they do not increase the knowledge content of their economies and diversify them through learning and innovation," UNCTAD says.
It says governments and their development partners should focus on four key policy issues: integrating science, technology and innovation policies into national development and poverty reduction strategies; addressing stringent intellectual property rights; reversing the brain drain; and using knowledge aid - as part of official development assistance - to support learning and innovation.
Brain drain
UNCTAD says the emigration of skilled workers from LDCs is a serious barrier to using technology to help such countries to expand their economies.
According to UNCTAD, five LDCs - Cape Verde, Gambia, Haiti, Samoa and Somalia - have lost more than half of their university-educated professionals in recent years to industrialised countries. Overall, UNCTAD estimates that one million skilled people from LDCs lived and worked in developed countries in 2004 - 15 percent, considering there are at least 6.6 million people in LDCs with university-level education.
Lost 'human capital' can have serious consequences ... one million skilled people from LDCs live in developed countries
"Lost 'human capital' ... can have serious consequences," the report warns. "The work of skilled professionals is a precondition for upgrading the productive structures and the exports of LDCs and for improving the sophistication of domestic businesses, not to mention for improving domestic health and education, which benefit entire populations."
Foreign aid
On foreign aid, the report says official foreign aid has been much less effective that it should be because it does not recognise the essential role of technology in sustained economic growth.
It says high-income G8 countries almost all have programmes to promote innovation among domestic businesses. But technological transfer has largely been missing from aid programmes.
Technology acquisition in LDCs through international markets is weak, UNCTAD says, so aid has to play a bigger role in developing the technological capabilities of domestic enterprises.
"Donor priorities do not comply with these needs," UNCTAD says.
It added that reported annual technical cooperation commitments for improving governance in LDCs in 2003-2005 were $1.3 billion while reported aid commitments for agricultural extension were only $12 million.
Aid for science, technology and innovation in poor countries is an essential component of aid, "which is not a handout, but a hand-up", UNCTAD says. "Shifting the focus there could lead to a radical break with past aid failures."
The report calls for a rapid increase in official development assistance for agricultural research and development in LDCs, "particularly to support a 'Green Revolution' in basic staple foods".
"Not only do donors need to strengthen national agricultural research and extension systems, they must also re-invest in the network of international agricultural research centres under the umbrella of the Consultative Group on International Agricultural Research [CGIAR]," the report recommends.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]
Read the Report

Original_Search_Title: Opposition Party AC Launches Campaign for Delta Region Ministry
Headline: Nigeria: AC and Niger Delta Question
Body_Text:
Lagos — ACTION Congress (AC) made an unusual campaign for the Niger Delta last week. The party launched a crusade for the creation of a Ministry for the region.
At its National Working Committee (NWC) meeting in Abuja, the party contended that establishment of such ministry will bring an enduring solution to the lingering crises in the area.
However, though the party's chieftains made vigorous efforts to sell their idea, many people are yet to key to the suggestion. Perhaps, that is why many days after they expressed the view neither the federal government, the National Assembly nor the public has responded to it.
Such indifference to the suggestion is easy to decipher. For many AC got it wrong. Arriving at a panacea to the unsettling situation in the Niger Delta cannot be achieved through the establishment of a ministry for the region. It goes beyond that.
Instead, there is a resonating voice that the federal government should go beyond lip service and partner with the Niger Delta Development Commission (NDDC) to address the nauseating condition of the area.
Major oil companies operating in the region last week tacitly raised a flag for such arrangement. The companies, among other things had endorsed a new strategy that would make them jointly initiate and execute developmental projects in communities in the area. They called for an extra funding of NDDC by the government.
Really the degeneration of situation in the Niger Delta, has paved the way for several individuals and bodies to flaunt diverse remedies and options for addressing the problems of the area.
Of course some of the recommendations are salutory. But, arguably, of all of them, the recent unveiled NDDC masterplan seems to be the most realistic tool for confronting the many issues in the zone.
Even critics of the commission are yet to criticise the plan. The federal government has lauded it as a progressive step in the effort at fixing the region.
President Umaru Yar'Adua at a meeting with elders of the Niger Delta at Aso Rock expressed confidence in the ability of the plan to end touching tales of neglect and abandonment of the area.
However beyond such attractive comments from the presidency, watchers of events in the area believe the only approach to addressing the problem of the area remains a robust collaboration between the commission, the federal government, state, councils and other stakeholders.
For the Niger Delta to meet the yearnings of the people as a developed and crisis-free region and for the problems of widespread poverty; very low industrial development; poor health and high mortality rate; inadequate transportation with movement and access to settlements hampered by a poor road network and difficult conditions especially in the riverine areas; absence of electricity supply in many riverine areas; poor telecommunications; shortage of land for development; inadequate health and education facilities, ineffective waste management and inadequate sanitation facilities, social restiveness and conflicts be put to an end, there needs to be concerted efforts by the stakeholders in the Niger Delta in finding lasting solution to these problems.
These stakeholders include the Federal Government, the state governments; the local governments, the oil companies, the Niger Delta Development Commission, other private concerns and people of the local communities, including youths.
As the intervention agency set up by the Federal Government, NDDC needs to partner with the other stakeholders in ensuring speedy development of the oil-rich region.
The NDDC partnership with the Federal Government is very important as the latter, as the main policy maker, has a lot of roles to play in ensuring the growth of the region.
The lack of infrastructure continues to stifle the growth of the region and the provision of most of these infrastructure lies at the doorstep of the Federal Government.
Some of the infrastructure which the Federal Government needs to provide to make the job of the NDDC easier are power, telecommunications, roads and rail system.
In the area of power, it is noteworthy that the power sector in the Niger Delta is characterised by growing disparity between demand and supply. About 72% of the household in the region are said to be connected to the national electricity network. The rate of connection is even lower for some of the rural communities. A major oil producing states, Bayelsa, is not connected to the national electricity grid at all and this is worrisome as a great percentage of the nation's wealth is derived from the state.
Waste management is another pressing problem besetting the Niger Delta as the region is faced with a myriad of waste management-related problems. There is high level of environmental pollution from the oil, gas and other industrial activities and the intractable problems of solid waste management, especially within the over populated semi-urban and urban areas remain a source of concern. The role of the Federal Government in this area is enacting an enabling law on safe waste management, like it did by giving a deadline for gas flaring. Unless the federal Government is ready to enact the enabling laws and willing to enforce same, the problem of waste management in the Niger would not go away.
To open the Niger Delta for rapid development, the problem of telecommunications cannot be wished away. Agreed, there is now the Global Systems for Mobile (GSM) Communication, but the networks, owned by private companies are only available in the urban and semi-urban areas. Telecommunication Systems are absolutely absent in the rural and riverine areas. Since it takes a lot to deploy their equipment to communities, the GSM operators are very reluctant to deploy in the rural and riverine areas of the Niger Delta.
The NDDC and the states need to partner in the areas of agriculture and healthcare. In agriculture, the states governments need to take pro-active steps to develop the agric sector. The states have to make land available for a large rubber, cocoa and oil palm plantations. This would revive the peoples interest in cash crop farming as a partnership with the states and the NDDC, as expected would give birth to something fruitful.
Also, the states have to ensure that their statutory allocation to the NDDC are remitted so that the commission would be single-minded in its task of developing the region.
In the area of healthcare, the states and the NDDC can partner in building hospitals, equipping the hospitals and stocking them with drugs to meet the health care needs of the people.
Since the Local Governments are closer to the people, the NDDC and the LGs can partner on identifying what are the immediate needs of the people. The LGs should identity the areas to be opened up and it cannot build roads in its own, partner with the NDDC and build rural roads up to acceptable standards it can also build cottage hospitals.
Inspite of the position anybody may hold on issues concerning the Niger Delta and emerging trend in the area, nobody can concretely dismiss the intervention of NDDC in the region in the last eight years.
Though some people have raised some queries on the manner funds were expended, the truth however is that the commission has helped in varied ways to mark up developments in the area.
Before its coming, the Niger Delta was a sorry tale of woes with about 70% of the people living below the poverty line and unemployment high; healthcare delivery poor, with the region's 20% mortality rate amongst the highest in the world. The transportation system was inadequate with movement and access to settlements being hampered by a poor road network and difficult conditions, especially in the riverine areas. There was also absence of electricity supply in many riverine areas and regularly interrupted supply access all areas; telecommunication was poor; there was shortage of land for development; inadequate education facilities; ineffective waste management and inadequate sanitation facilities and unending social restiveness and conflict as the order of the day.
However, the NDDC can arguably be said to have done well in confronting some of these problems. It has constructed several kilometres. of roads in the oil-producing communities and states. It has also built many bridges, schools, hospitals and rural electrification.
However the NDDC development efforts in the Niger Delta are also constrained by weak, inefficient and dilapidated institutional capacity. There is lack of adequate trained manpower, effective and transparent working practices and systems and established institutional norms and values.
For the Commission to however ensure an enduring legacy, it must rise to the challenge of ensuring the complete development of the region, by tapping the abundant human and material resources which the region is blessed with. If properly tapped, the Niger Delta has enough potential to surpass even the socio-economic and human development objectives set out in the Millennium Development Goals (MDGs) and national strategies like the NEEDS. In fact achieving these targets is considered the minimum possible development threshold for the region.
Besides the tremendous oil and gas wealth, the Niger Delta Region is endowed with a large number of working age population and potentially cultivable land and fishery resources. If properly exploited and combined with strategic programmes, the region will in a short time over the myriad of crises that have bedevilled it.
The so many other areas into which the Niger Delta Development Commission has to focus on to bring wealth and prosperity to people of the oil-rich region include the agric sector, the solid minerals sector, forestry and livestock production and processing.
In the agric sector, the Niger Delta people were farmers which produced 50% of the nation foreign exchange earnings before the oil boom of 1970s and the region produces a variety of cash crops and food corps. The cash crops are palm fruits, from which palm oil and palm kennels are derived, rubber and cocoa as well as well as groundnuts and pineapples.
Nigeria was once the world's largest producer of palm oil and the activity was largely centred on the Niger Delta region. The oil palm remains one of the most important cash crops in the region, and could serve again as a driver for the economic development for the region.
The NDDC has a large role to play in this regard. It could help states, local governments or even be in corporate partnership with individuals to develop palm plantations which can rival the Bayelsa palm, Ada Palm and Risonpalm owned by the respective governments of Bayelsa, Imo and Rivers states. The oil palm plantations owned by the respective states and private investors have proved to be not just profitable to the investors, but also available to the local economy job creation and infrastructure development.
The other cash crops produced in the region: rubber and cocoa should be given the same attention as outlined above for oil palm. These cash crops are international commodities which would fetch those who invest in them returns in foreign exchange.
For small-scale farmers, the NDDC should encourage them to cultivate food crops such as cassava, yam, plantain and cocoyam that could be developed more efficiently.
The NDDC should also encourage farmer to cultivate rice and fruits such as pineapples, oranges, grapes, lime, lemon, guava, pawpaw and bananas. A way to encourage the local farmers would be if the NDDC offers to buy a certain percfn3etagte of corps produced off the farmers. This would spur them back to cultivate the land, de-emphasize the focus on oil and gas and return the Niger Delta to its pride of place as the nation's food basket.
In the area of aquiculture, the Niger Delta is a labyrinth of creeks and rivers and is bordered by the atlantic ocean. Despite various efforts in the past to develop the aquiculture, the potential of the sector remains unrealized. The fact that Nigeria is the largest importer of fish in West Africa is partly indicative of the failure of past attempt to harness the fish resources and the management to meet market demand. And this failure can be laid at the door steps of the Niger Delta as it has more water bodies than other regions in the country.
The NDDC should encourage more people in the region to take to fish farming as there are benefits and income to be derived there from.
However, the commission would have to first organise training seminars for the would-be fish farmers and help them start off financially, by way for loans, to entice the people.
One major reason why many people have given cold shoulder to a suggestion for the establishment of Ministry of Niger Delta is that it will lead to an unnecessary duplication. The funds that would have ordinarily been channeled to the execution of development projects would go into paying salaries and attending to the needs of officials of the ministry.
There is absolutely no achievement that NDDC cannot record if its sufficiently encouraged and supported to by the government.

Original_Search_Title: Maize Farmer's Big Day With Annan
Headline: Kenya: Maize Farmer's Big Day With Annan
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A family in Bungoma is over the moon after hosting a dream visitor at its farm last weekend.
Yesterday, the head of the family, 74-year-old Pharis Wekesa Masibo, was still elated as visitors kept streaming into his home to find out what had attracted former United Nations secretary-general Kofi Annan.
Mr Annan visited the retired primary school headteacher's 20-acre farm at Bukembe in Kanduyi Division to see his unique farming practice.
Mr Masibo, who was a headteacher for 44 years (between 1958 and 1989) after training at Siriba Teachers Training College (now Maseno University) is a small-scale farmer growing sugarcane, maize, sweet potatoes, bananas, groundnuts and beans.
"Every three years, I harvest 80 tonnes of cane for sale to Nzoia Sugar Company at Sh2,200 per tonne. Last year I harvested 100 bags of maize, small quantities of groundnuts and beans.
"For sweet potatoes and bananas, we sell some and use the rest for domestic consumption," the farmer explained.
Mr Masibo is the chairman of a group of 34 banana farmers intent on adopting new farming technologies to achieve higher yields.
They work with NGOs involved in the promotion of sustainable agriculture.
"We began inter-cropping maize with sweet potatoes, beans and groundnuts and we are reaping the benefits of improved harvests from small units," he told the Nation.
Half an acre of his farm is dedicated to trials of new seed varieties - 502 and 505 hybrids developed by Western Seed Company and Muguga 100 and 036, with appropriate fertiliser use for optimum yields.
Their performance in this agro-ecological zone is what Mr Annan went to see firsthand.
Mr Masibo said: "I was first told of the visit of Mr Annan last Thursday by some people from the Ministry of Agriculture and Sacred Africa and when he finally arrived here on Sunday, I was very touched. I felt very happy, my family too and everybody in this village.
It was an encouraging visit as we were able to articulate the challenges facing us - poor seed varieties, expensive inputs, particularly fertiliser and poor marketing systems that leave producers at the mercy of middlemen."
Married to Rhoda and Jerida with whom he has 16 children, Mr Masibo said more Kenyans should turn to agriculture for employment.
"If treated as a business, it is profitable once the issue of high seed and fertiliser prices is addressed.
"It is not for women alone who have been tasked with managing food reserves and farms as well.
"Men now need to minimise the time they spend idling in markets to work on their farms, and socialise only after work!" But the farmer said more investment in agriculture was crucial for its growth.
"The Government needs to pump in more money to empower farmers' organisations and expose producers to modern agricultural technology through visits to other parts of the world to witness first-hand use of latest techniques in production."
Emphasis should also be put on traditional food crops such as millet, sorghum and cassava, if families and the country are to attain self-sufficiency in food production, said Mr Masibo.
Addressing farmers at Mr Masibo's home, Mr Annan said a revolution that aimed at making the African continent self-sufficient in food was in the offing.
Mr Annan, who chairs the Alliance for a Green Revolution in Africa (Agra) board, said a partnership between his organisation and African farmers would increase agricultural productivity, ensuring food security and trade.
Africa has for long been ravaged by food shortages and famine as Asia was mid last century.
The Asian continent overcame food insecurity courtesy of a green revolution based on high yielding seed, increased fertiliser use and improved agronomic practices, spearheaded by Nobel laureate Norman Bollack.
All these practices were tied to one crop - rice - and irrigation.
Africa, on the other hand, was by-passed by the green revolution due to lack of political will, and today, only 13 per cent of its farmers use fertiliser and 20 per cent use improved seed varieties, says agriculture expert Eusebius Mukhwana.
Dr Mukhwana, the executive director of the Sustainable Agricultural Centre for Research Extension and Development (Sacred) Africa, said the problems on the continent were further compounded by subdivision of land into uneconomical units; expensive imported fertiliser; poor infrastructure and disjointed, haphazard, outdated policies, whose implementation was uncoordinated.
He told the Nation yesterday: "Liberalisation of the agricultural sector in the 1990s added more woes to the African farmer, who lacks a global view and entrepreneur skills, access to market information, thus paving the way for the middleman to ride roughshod over him.
"For the African green revolution to happen, we need to think out of the box because of the diversity of too many crops and agro-ecological zones.
"Consideration must be on key crops - maize, rice, wheat, cassava, sweet potatoes, bananas and profitable social working markets and probable subsidy on fertiliser and seed varieties," said Dr Mukhwana.
Mr Annan said he had on Saturday met President Kibaki, who was excited by the progress being made in agriculture.
Agra is poised to spend $150 million (about Sh10 billion) to improve seed varieties under its programme.

Original_Search_Title: Oni - We 'll Upstage Federal Government in Road Construction
Headline: Nigeria: Oni - We 'll Upstage Federal Government in Road Construction
Body_Text:
Lagos — How are you handling the strike action embarked upon by workers in your state?
We have agreed to pay the 15 per cent that the workers were agitating for. I am not however sure that we will meet any other demands beyond that. First, because there is a principle, it is either you are on the left or on the right side. The first principle that the workers had established is that, even though we are federating units, we are supposed to be independent in deciding what we will take or not take. They have decided to take a cue on whatever the workers are doing. That is the principle.
So, if that is the established principle, we negotiated and agreed on 15 per cent, which is across board.
