We have satisfied that and if they will bring additional demands, it means that the Nigeria Labour Congress (NLC) at the centre will also go into a continuous negotiation and get the whole of Nigeria to agree that another basis for negotiation has been formed.
But nothing is automatic. However, we have agreed on the 15 per cent demand after we felt that it was okay for us. Any other thing besides that, we don't think we can take it. You cannot eat your cake and have it. And it is not morally right to change your terms in the middle of negotiation. I don't think we are ready for that.
There were reports that you have planned to build some houses before the expiration of your first 100 days in office. How far have you gone in this pursuit?
Let me assure you that anything we cannot sustain, we will not try it. We are going to provide houses, no doubt about that. And in the next six months, there will be an investment, in my estimate, that will be more than N2 billion for housing delivery.
On this project, we are treating our housing corporation like any other developers. We have got a total of five serious developers now, including the Housing Corporation. And we are giving them land while they are coming with their designs for examination and criticisms. They will provide houses that people will buy.
The only subsidy we might be giving to make the houses more available is to tar the roads for them and extend water and electricity to the place. After all, we are providing such amenities even for the people on the streets. So, it is nothing extra.
What strategy have you adopted for the funding of this project?
No, we are not giving any developer money. They are bringing their own money. Developers are supposed to source for their own money and bring it here. It is their investment that they are putting in here. And theywill, at the end of the day, sell and make their money. We are just reckoning a minimum of N2 billion.
It could be more. But we are not providing anybody with anything other than a little support on infrastructure like roads, water and electricity.
Ekiti State has remained an agrarian state despite the growth in technological development. Does your administration have any plans to make the state a little bit industrialised?
The investment we are encouraging people to bring down here and the ones we are undertaking are not just in agricultural production, but it is also in agro-allied industries.
Very soon, you will see a lot of cassava plantations. Cassava will be processed here. It won't be only raw cassava. Some will be processed to ethanol; some will be processed to starch, while some will be processed to glucose and other syrups and so on.
We are going beyond just producing the raw cassava. We are going into manufacturing. And that is why we have inevented a terminology - Farmgate-for the agricultural project in Ekiti State. So, farmgate processing has been introduced to the consciousness of the people.
That means, when you have your plantation, you will establish your factory right there. For Farmgate, we will give manufacturers land within the state for their plantains and industries.
Will this government give the state a lift in terms of infrastructural development?
Our administration has declared an infrastructural battle. We have declared road revolution. And very soon, you will see what the cliché - road revolution means as declared by the Ministry of Works.
We are turning the total attention of the Ministry of Works on roads. We have just advertised the plan to consruct 27 roads so far in the history of Ekiti, totalling 340 kilometres at a cost that is close to N15 billion. And this is the equivalent of the total number of roads that the Federal Government has in Ekiti.
Federal Government's roads in Ekiti consist of about 348 kilometres. But we want to do 340 kilometres of roads. And this is more than one-third of the total roads that Ekiti has from both the federal and state governments.
Going for 340 kilometres at once has a reason. Every year like this, we want to turn attention on roads. And before the end of the raining season, we will award contracts for the dry months ahead. We don't want roads that people will work on and abandon for sometime. We want to award contracts when contractors will be willing to work and when they can deliver. And by the time the rain will be coming next year, they would have finished. We will prepare for who to tender for whatever roads we may want to do next year.
So, by August next year, the contractors will again move to sites and have time to work all round till another rainy season. And through road revolution, we are projecting to build roads, tarred by us in four years, that will be more than roads tarred in the history of Ekiti since 1922 when the first tarred road emerged.
The number of kilometers of roads tarred in Ekiti From 1922 till May 29 this year will be less than what we are going to do in four years. That is what our cliché, "road revolution," intends to achieve.
Are you also planning to attract foreign investors to Ekiti?
We are already getting a lot of foreign investors who have begun to talk to us. One way or the other, we are beginning to create a reputation before them that they seem to like. A lot of them are making contacts with us. But we want to talk about what is already on ground.
In the next two or three months, we would have signed a lot of deals. Many of them are coming.
What innovation do you want to bring into education?
Well, education, for us, is more than just a password. Knowledge is part of our culture. We are a people, who are knowledge prone. It is not just about western education, we have been knowledge prone from historical term.
There is an adage, whose origin was from the Ifa oracle litany, which says Alara has knowledge and Ajero understands it. (Alara and Ajero are two traditional rulers in Ekiti State). This will tell you that this terrain has been knowledge prone for sometime. So, that we are able to do well in education is never happenstance. It is in our blood. It is our ancestral heredity.
What we want to do now is to hold onto what we have and leverage on it. We want to make sure that we make substantial investment in education so that we can produce what is the best public sector education in Nigeria. We know that we can do well with education because, it is more natural to us. So, we are going to jealously and consciously guard that.
Can you reduce the influx of the people of Ekiti State into the urban centres through job creation?
It is fundamental that we are going to create jobs. We have a target to create 250,000 jobs in four years. It could be more but that is the target that we are trying to go for. Some of these jobs will be in entrepreneurial ventures. We are going to create an opportunity to have access to credit for some of the youth to be ale to do things on their own.
We know we have to create jobs. Yes, we know and there is no option or alternative to creating jobs. And that is why we are encouraging investors to come around so that when they have their manufacturing industries here, they will definitely create jobs for our people.

Original_Search_Title: African Bank GivesCountry $ 50 Million for Poverty Fight
Headline: Rwanda: African Bank GivesCountry $ 50 Million for Poverty Fight
Body_Text:
Kigali — The African Development Bank (AfDB) Group has approved a grant of US$ 50 million (Rwf 25 billion) to help finance the second phase of the Poverty Reduction Strategy Support Program (PRSSP II) in Rwanda, RNA reports.
Government adopted a modified poverty fight model termed as the "Economic Development and Poverty Reduction Strategy" (EDPRS) for 2007-2011. EDPRS followed the implementation of the 2002-2006 Poverty Redaction Strategy Paper (PRSP).
The Bank said Wednesday in a statement that the grant would be used to improve public finance management by improving budget programming and execution. The grant is in fact from the African Development Fund, a concessional window of the African Development Bank (AfDB) Group.
The money will also help create an enabling environment for business development and export promotion by improving the legal and institutional framework as well as strengthening financial intermediation and consolidating the banking system.
Early June, the Investment Facility for Africa (ICF) also injected US $8.9 million into efforts to strengthen the investment attraction capacity of Rwanda. Under the project 'Rwanda Investment Climate Project' the export promotion agency RIEPA will be reorganized. The financing will also help with starting-up of a commercial court and strengthen the standards bureau.
The ADB said today in Tunis (Tunisia) that through PRSSP-II, the ADF grant will support the implementation of the Government's PRSP program, in close collaboration with the World Bank, EU, DFID, Sweden, and Germany. These donor partners fall under the Budget Support Group that support Rwanda directly through budget top-up.
The ADB group began operations in Rwanda in 1974 and its cumulative commitment in the country stands at US$ 950 million for 113 operations. Its current portfolio in the country is currently made up of 16 active projects amounting to $250million (FRW 138billion), of which the education sector takes up 24%.
In June last year, Bank (ADB) provided US$ 24 million grant to government in support of the Education Sector Strategic Plan (ESSP) 2006-2010 aimed at developing the human resource capacity of the country.
Then in July, Bank earmarked another US$ 14.79 (Rwf 8.2 Billion) million to revive agricultural production in the drought-affected Bugesera Region.
The two-phased project aims at increasing agricultural production in the Bugesera Region by setting up irrigation infrastructure in a 650- hectare valley. It would also fund protection of water catchments basins and improve rain-fed farming on nearly 5,000 hectares of hills, as well as build the capacity of farmers and supervisory institutions.
The Bugesera region has of late been on the spotlight nationally and internationally following a hard hitting drought that left residents with barely anything to survive. It targeted 60,750 inhabitants (about 13,500 households).
At one point, the World Food Program Rwanda country office predicted a "social crisis" as hunger had forced people to eat grass and cassava leaves to survive.
Rwanda released its foreign policy last year in which said it would no long take any loans preferring to be given grants. Government said the motivation for the plan was because Rwanda did not have the capacity to accumulate debt.
As part of the same plan, government wanted all support channeled directly into its coffers - essentially to allow it make its own priorities. And indeed, most donor partners have been providing budgetary support.

Original_Search_Title: FAO/Moa Assists Over 100,000 Farming Families
Headline: Liberia: FAO/Moa Assists Over 100,000 Farming Families
Body_Text:
What can be described as the largest rice seed distribution exercise in recent times ended in June 2007 with 101,400 farming families throughout the country receiving 2,608 metric tons of high yielding rice seeds, which are suitable for both upland and lowland ecologies.
The Ministry of Agriculture/Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO) successfully carried out this operation in collaboration with UN and NGO partners with funds provided by the Central Emergency Response Fund (CERF) of the United Nations.
Prior to the launch of the 2007/2008 planting season, FAO in collaboration with the Ministry of Agriculture made an appeal for emergency assistance to cater for war-affected farmers, in particular returnees and those who did not receive rice seeds during the last 2006/2007 season and were in desperate need of rice seeds.
An average of 140,000 farming families including returnees who arrived during the second half of 2006 and vulnerable farmers that were excluded in the last season were identified as top priority for emergency assistance nation-wide.
However, the commitments of FAO, the Ministry of Agriculture and other partners in the sector at the time could meet the demand of emergency assistance for only 60,000 thus leaving a gap of 80,000 farming families.
15,500 farming families were targeted under Phase one arrangements of the Central Emergency Response Fund (CERF-I), while CERF-II, 80,000, bringing a total of 95,500 farming families who were initially targeted to benefit from these emergency assistance.
Based on increased demand from other sources, FAO and the Agriculture Ministry went beyond this number and serviced an additional 5,900 farming families.
FAO Resident Representative in Liberia, Dr. Winfred Hammond expressed the hope that in the ensuing year, the need for rice seeds would have been reduced by at least 50 percent and farmers would be in a better position to produce their own seeds and not go begging to procure rice seeds.
"In order to reduce the reliance on rice in our diets, the need to diversify our eating habits: we need to grow what we eat and eat what we grow, meaning our eddoes, cassava, plantain, yams are all food crops that we can produce to eat and sell the surpluses to earn income", Agriculture Minister Dr. J. Chris Toe observed.
This massive rice distribution exercise, which commenced in early March in Southwestern Liberia prior to the start of the rainy season ended mid last month covering all 15 counties.
Apart from providing the rice seeds, FAO in partnership with the Ministry of Agriculture organized two regional seed multiplication training workshops; one in Gbarnga Bong County and the other in Zwedru, Grand Gedeh County for lead farmers and agriculture technicians.
More than 80 lead farmers and selected agriculture technicians of the Agriculture Ministry nation-wide attended those workshops, which focused on building their capacities to improve the sustainability of local rice production and encourage the emergence of localized seed supply networks.
Most of the beneficiary farmers expressed thanks to FAO and the Ministry of Agriculture for the seeds and training and pledged their willingness to grow more food to feed the nation provided the support and market opportunities are made available.
The Ministry of Agriculture and FAO are currently supporting 60 seed multiplication sites throughout the country with a total area of 2,500 acres and 7,300 participating farmers.

Original_Search_Title: The Absence of Common Goal
Headline: Nigeria: The Absence of Common Goal
Body_Text:
Lagos — The biggest challenges in Nigeria is the absence of "for the good of all" since the day that Nigerians took their future in their hands by bidding farewell to the colonial masters from Britain. On May 1, 2007, The Guardian chose to publish the article contributed by Pat Utomi who seems to be a good man standing among the bunches that have just jostled for the flawed election in Nigeria. This man, who appears to be without blemish, wrote that he has henceforth dedicated his life to the struggle called Nigeria.
In my opinion, this is a very positive and healthy development. We have seen a few honest men in the past but the problem is that they never get to that vantage position where their good intentions can be tested nationally. This to me is the greatest challenge that Utomi faces. On face value, he wrote honestly and intelligently. I am not in a position to assess this man called Utomi but he has been around the corner long enough to be taken by his words. We may never be able to try such men as we should. In Nigeria, if you are not a thug or ruffian, you may not be well adapted to survive on the political terrain. Our democracy is not for decent people.
In our part of the world, we are involved in the selection of public office holders and an arrangement called kangaroo elections where we may or may not need to vote. It doesn't make a difference what we do on polling days. I am still very amazed that we have Nigerians who vote on selection days. Why do you vote when the winner of the election could be someone who is not even a candidate? You are 20,000 in your community and a total vote tally of 100,000 could emerge.
Do Nigerians know the meaning of boycott? You could even lose your life trying to cast a vote for some lunatic attempting to reach a certain political status! Why take such a risk? There are more than 50 reasons not to vote in Nigeria. The number is correlated with the number of ways that do-or-die politicians achieve their objectives. The 2007 elections in Nigeria is a new world record for cheating possibilities. All other African countries should never allow the Nigerian government to give them advice anymore on democracy or how to chose or run their government. The example of Nigeria can ruin Africa and the entire world.
The reason for all these catastrophes and retrogression in Nigeria is simply because there is absence of the common good. The politicians are selfish and on average business adventure to milk the populace. The blame is cyclic and the cycle itself is idiotism. The reason for politics in Nigeria is not to improve the state but for some nonentities to earn a living and siphon riches for personal gains. The Nigerian state is not set up to run itself like all modern systems are. Over the years, Nigerians generally have resorted to any means possible to be rich and live comfortably. This started with the rapid collapse of the infrastructure, non-maintenance of anything public/government owned. In short time, all social amenities hit the rock and the basic necessities of life became elusive. Eventually, only a few people live comfortably relative to the 140 million inhabitants.
Lack of common good bred by a fearful combination of both greed and corruption has ruined Nigeria. It is reported as one of the poorest countries in the world while on the contrary and in reality, Nigeria is arguably one of the richest countries in the world. There is abundance of natural resources in the country. If you study the geography of Nigeria, you will end up being confused since you will not be able to understand why average Nigerians should not be able to live on more than USD 100 per day if they so choose.
This is attainable since the intelligent minds have calculated that the wealth from the Niger Delta of Nigeria alone can sustain the entire Africa. If this is true, then it is extremely ridiculous that the people in the Niger Delta of Nigeria are among the poorest in the world. They are poor socially and ruined environmentally. Ask the foreign oil companies about that and how they have been aided by succeeding governments in Nigeria to trample on the local indigenes. Indeed, what I call "mass poverty" prevails in Nigeria.
Ask yourself, where does all the income from the oil go to? Why are there no refineries in Nigeria, the sixth largest producer of oil in the world? Why is agriculture no longer the main foreign exchange earner in Nigeria? Where are all the cocoa farms in Western Nigeria? Where are the groundnut pyramids in the North? Where is the Cassava from the East? Where are the products of the Ajaokuta Steel factory? Where lies the coal industry? What happened to the Hydro-Electric Power Generation system? Where are the graduate employment schemes of old? Do not attempt a full list of these potentials and don't even think about the human resources in terms of intelligence and availability! You will be more confused and disillusioned.
So what went wrong? Many things went wrong. The bottom line is the absence of the common good. The politicians are the worst culprits and the civil service was not left out as well. Nobody believed in the government any longer and people did what they liked. The results: prevalence of hunger, increase in road accidents, increase in general morbidity and mortality-due to diseases and a non-functional health system.
This takes me back to Utomi. Is he sincere? How many sincere people do we need to take Nigeria forward progressively? Shall we have more honest men to stand up in the fight to save Nigeria? Nigeria is collapsing and what she needs are the voice and actions of the people with common good.

Original_Search_Title: Saleh Offers 1,000 Acres for Starch Factory
Headline: Uganda: Saleh Offers 1,000 Acres for Starch Factory
Body_Text:
Kampala — MICRO-Finance minister, Gen. Salim Saleh, has given 1,000 acres of his land at Kapeeka in Nakaseke to Chinese investors to construct a starch manufacturing plant. The plant is expected to start production in July next year.
"I had put there veterans to grow food, but they resorted to felling trees for burning charcoal. Now there is not a single tree there," Saleh said while launching the Kapeeka Savings and Credit Cooperative Society.
The land is adjacent to the Uganda Peoples Defence Forces (UPDF) heroes' cemetery where the late Jet Mwebaze who died in a plane accident and other UPDF soldiers who died at the war front were buried.
He said the plant would process cassava from 4,000 acres of which 3,000 acres would be planted by outgrowers.
Saleh urged the Kapeeka residents to grow a lot of cassava to feed the plant.

Original_Search_Title: Wilson Road and Its Crooks
Headline: Uganda: Wilson Road and Its Crooks
Body_Text:
Kampala — Time check. 8.30a.m. In mid-town Kampala, Wilson Road is already a hive of activity. Forget that it is a rainy morning. No one seems to have been held up by the rain. Food vendors brave the drizzle, darting back and forth across the street.
Clutching flasks of tea and snack plates covered with polythene bags, they serve breakfast to their early regulars. Meanwhile, shop owners are opening up for the day. But there is this group of six youthful men that takes shelter on the veranda of the building that formerly housed a branch of Cineplex Cinema.
They are heavily bejewelled (in the flashy, cheap jewellery commonly known as bling bling), with several chains around their necks and others hanging off their trouser pockets and waists. They are clad in the latest trendy buggy jeans and tight casual shirts. Several punch keys on flashy mobile phones while others hold a heated argument.
On this rainy morning, I watch the group perched at the seat nearest to the window at Bon Apetit restaurant, where I am the first customer. As I take my breakfast, one of the young men leaves the group and struts across the street. He walks by the restaurant only to go by again, holding a car headlight.
He passes by a third time, this time without the headlight. When he eventually enters the restaurant, the fairly tall, dreadlocked, dark complexioned guy with a heavy-set face and bulging chest wears one of the heaviest scents of perfume I've been subjected to. He is the second customer in the restaurant and he takes the farthest seat opposite mine. Placing his order with a lot of impatience, he continues to punch keys on his handset, and taking brief breaks to throw me very inquisitive glances.
The sight of these young men is not a rare one around here. "We recognise most of them. We serve them tea sometimes. One day they go to those restaurants and take the most expensive breakfast on the menu, then the next day, that same person comes to me and orders dry tea and cassava. I think when they land a deal, they spend all the money in one day," says Annah Nanziri, a food vendor.
According to Nanziri, these men are always here. But unlike other people who are either hawkers or shop operators, these men don't have any business you can put a finger on. "Sometimes they are the first to arrive here in the morning. They don't operate shops and neither do they sell anything tangible. Some of them started coming here as young boys, but are now grown men. New members keep joining while others just disappear," she adds.
Wilson Road is one that owes its fame to this class of people. Generally referred to as Abafere (downtown slung for conmen), the sight of these young men is a nightmare to those who have had a close shave with them. To the passerby, these guys are the styled up, well-off youth, making their best attempt at looking busy. But to the neighbouring business owners, they are a crowd that would rather not be associated with.
James Akankwatsa, a newspaper vendor, narrates an ordeal that recently befell one of his colleagues at the hands of these 'refined' rogues. " Two of them came to my colleague and told him that someone in an office up in Kirumira Towers needed newspapers.
They guided him to the top-most floor. When they reached a certain corridor, they held him to a window and told him to choose between his life and all the money he had made, plus his mobile phone. They threatened to throw him out of the window if he didn't give the money up. Of course he chose his life. Now he goes without food because he is trying to recover all that he lost," he relates.
When the drizzle lets up, the remainder of the group, which looks to have hatched a plan from their argument, takes off in different directions. Betty Nabukenya, the pay phone attendant on the veranda next to where the group was convening, reveals that this is normally their place of rendezvous.
"They have been meeting here from the time the cinema was still open. Sometimes they would spend most of the day in the bar behind the building (DV 8), but it was also closed. Now this place is a venue for lunch hour prayers, so they can't go in. They just stand here or sit on the pavement and argue about football. Sometimes they talk about cars," she says.
The bafere have given Wilson Road such a reputation that it is one of those places where you can easily be conned of all your belongings in broad daylight. Since they don't look any bit like thieves and instead look like 'loaded' young men, people say you can never be suspicious of them at all. There is also a great paranoia that nothing genuine can ever be purchased from this side of town.
This perception however, in Eddie Nisha (a phone dealer)'s opinion, is simply not true. Nisha, who has been working from here since 1999, says that the bafere don't hang around here anymore. He adds that the Operation Wembley raids dismissed them though the street's reputation had already been tarnished.
"Those guys you saw there are genuine. They may look idle, but they work. Most of them are in the second-hand car business. One buys a car and they drive it around for a few days while they look for someone to buy it. When it is finally sold off, they buy another one. They earn their living," he says. But when you've listened to an experience such as the one that came upon the newspaper vendor, you are hesitant to share Nisha's opinion. In the eyes of many Kampalans, the bafere have become an experience people would sooner be ridden of.
Taking a quick look along Wilson road, all looks well; with most businesses picking up as the day progresses. But before most business owners and their customers can even imagine that all is indeed well, the street would have to be physically cleared of these lounging groups of youths.

Original_Search_Title: Kenya President Agrees to Host Green Revolution Headquarters
Headline: Africa: Kibaki Agrees to Host Annan's Organisation
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Kibaki has accepted a request by former United Nations Secretary-General Mr Kofi Annan that the Alliance for Green Revolution in Africa (Agra) headquarters be hosted in Nairobi.
The organisation, headed by Annan, aims at preventing poverty and hunger in Africa by increasing the productivity of small-scale farmers.
Annan, who arrived in Nairobi on Saturday morning, called on Kibaki at State House. Kenya has received grants worth $5 million (about Sh330 million) from Agra.
The organisation has also awarded about Sh19.8 million to the Kenya Agricultural Research Institute, to improve cassava and sorghum production in the semi-arid areas.
Helping small-scale farmers to sell surplus crop
Kibaki said agriculture was a priority and hoped the organisation would help local farmers access outside markets. He told farmers to add value to their produce to attract good prices.
The Head of State asked Agra to conduct research on crops that could thrive in Kenya and Africa. He said the farmers could use the knowledge to increase their yields.
Annan asked his team to focus on marketing. He said Agra emphasised on helping small-scale farmers to sell their surplus crop to earn from their sweat.
"The alliance will design programmes to regularly communicate with farmers. This will be in regard to farming techniques, dietary preferences, desired crop traits, local water sources and weather patterns.
"We will then design programmes that directly respond to their needs," Annan said.
Dr Peter Matlon, Agra interim President Rockefeller Foundation Managing Director, and Dr Akinwumi Adesia, an Associate Director of the Rockefeller Foundation, accompanied the former UN boss.
Agriculture PS, Dr Romano Kiome, was also present.

Original_Search_Title: Region's Crop Output Drops
Headline: Kenya: Region's Crop Output Drops
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Agricultural production in Western Province is on the decline, leading to abject poverty among farmers.
Many farmers were depending on Government relief food and donations, agriculture secretary Wilson Songa said. He urged farmers in the province to embrace new technologies to improve their productivity and income and promised that the Government was committed to improving their lot. Dr Songa was speaking at Busia Agricultural Training Centre during the province's farmers' field day.
Exchange ideas
The theme of the two-day event was "Empowering people through sustainable agribusiness and collaboration".
The official told the participants that the Government had increased budgetary allocation to the Agriculture ministry by 20 per cent in the last financial year.
He asked farmers to take part in field days as they provided opportunities to exchange ideas, learn about new technologies and make business connections.
Dr Songa said the Government was revitalising the agricultural sector to improve food security and reduce poverty.
He disclosed that the Government had reviewed policies, legal and regulatory frameworks to encourage private sector participation in conformity with the changing regional and global economic environment.
He said the Government had taken measures to improve agricultural extension services, research, inputs, quality assurance and had revived credit and financial services for farmers.
Coffee farming
Busia District produced 781,235 bags of maize, 185,350 tonnes of cassava, and 86,415 bags of beans in 2006.
District commissioner John ole Kepas urged farmers to grow coffee to benefit from improved prices, saying the district earned only Sh1.2 million from the crop last year.

Original_Search_Title: Deaedline Set for Biofuel Strategy
Headline: Mozambique: Deadline for Biofuel Strategy
Body_Text:
Maputo — The Mozambican government has given a deadline of two months for consultants from the American company Ecoenergy to deliver their proposal for a biofuels strategy and policy for Mozambique.
According to Antonio Saide, the national director for new and renewable sources of energy, in the Ministry of Energy, such a strategy document is a fundamental step in drawing up a viable programme for the production of ethanol and bio-diesel.
Saide told AIM that, if Mozambique is to become competitive in the biofuel market, it must look strategically at the area and then define clearly what it intends to offer.
"We must have our own strategies for each one of the biofuels", he said. "That's why the consultants must deliver in September their draft of a biofuel strategy for Mozambique, which should take into account the raw materials, competitiveness and the market".
The consultants have already produced one study on Mozambique's bio-fuel potential, which suggests that jatropha, ricin, African palm, and coconuts can be used to produce bio-diesel, while ethanol can be based on sugar cane, maize and cassava.
The challenge now is to identify which of these should be used, bearing in mind the viability of production and the quality of the end product. "We must assess the raw materials, study their potential, and the contribution that each could make to the programme, in order to decide which of them will be used to produce each biofuel", said Saide.
Ana Locke, financial advisor to the Agricultural Promotion Centre (CEPAGRI), told AIM that detailed work must be done to choose the plants that will be used as raw material for producing biofuels.
She pointed to problems with coconut palms: those in the southern province of Inhambane are old, and not very productive, and so new palm trees must be planted. As for the vast coconut plantations of Zambezia province, they were infected with lethal yellowing disease, although there is now a project, to be funded by the American millennium Challenge Account {MCA} to root out the infected trees and plant new ones.
It is thought that Mozambique could produce 40 million litres of bio-diesel and 21 million litres of ethanol a year. The government believes that a biofuel strategy can be sustainable, but so far there is no date for the start of biofuel production.

Original_Search_Title: Snail Farming As Source of Employment
Headline: Cameroon: Snail Farming As Source of Employment
Body_Text:
Snail meat popularly known as Congo Meat is consumed in many countries in the world, with some, considering it as a delicacy for the rich.Snail farming and research are already being practised in countries like Ghana, Nigeria, Benin, and Cameroon to a limited extent.
Nigeria is currently exporting snails to USA, Britain, France though the exportation is still very low.Despite the high demand for snails, the supply is solely dependent on handpicking from nature.
During the rainy season, the quantity of snails in the market is much compared to the dry season. But activities like snail hunting, bush burning, deforestation and agricultural pesticides are causing a decline in the population of snails.
As such, progress in snail farming could open new opportunities of increasing self-employment, demanding little capital investment and not labour intensive.
Rearing Snails
Snails can be reared under extensive, semi-extensive and intensive systems, depending on the location. Snails develop their activities at sunset and end at sunrise. The most appropriate period to rear snails is from March to October, which is the rainy season.
The choice of a site has to consider the wind, soil characteristic and the micro-climate. The site has to be protected from wind since wind favours the loss of water in the soil. Sandy and loamy or humus soils are the best for snail farming and the soil should have good characteristic to retain water.
Snail houses could be built from old tyres, drums and tanks, high fenced pens and low fenced pens. The house should be able to stop snails from escaping, accessible, well ventilated, spacious, affordable and durable. It must also be protected against predators and insect infestations.
For the feeding, snails feed on green leaves like paw-paw, cassava and sweet potato leaves. They could also feed on fruits like paw-paws, oranges, palm nuts, pear, amongst others. Calcium and phosphorus are very essential minerals in the nutrition of snails. Wood ash spread round the snailery could also be a good mineral supplement.
Medical Values
Snail meat is a good source of animal protein and the saliva contains the enzyme B-glucosinodase that is used pharmaceutically. They are excellent antidote for vascular diseases like heart attacks, cardiac arrest, hypertension and stroke due to the high calcium content.
Also, a mixture of snail blood and palm kernel oil in equal parts when rubbed on the body reduces the severity of stroke and severe headache. It is easily digested and good for stomach disorder, accelerates the movement of foetus, delivery and it is good source of iron for nursing mothers.
Snails are also used in native medicines like making the birth of a new baby relatively easier women and for inducing birth in overdue pregnancy. It is widely used to solve the problem of the placenta retention after birth.
The blush fluid from snails is used to stop bleeding and for circumcision of babies.
Snail Industry In Cameroon
The Director of Community Action for Development, CADEV, Tombel, Jacob Mbonteh Nzang, they have moved from snail farming to processing. He said the organisation has set up an industry in Tombel to process snail meat for national and international consumption.
The Director added that he has organised several workshops to train and evaluate Community-based Organizations, CBOs, on snail rearing and it is on the strength of this that the National Association of Snail Farmers, NASFARM, was formed with eight chapters.
In it's processing, CADEV shall buy snails from individuals and CBOs doing snail rearing, process and distribute it nationally. For exportation, he said the industry would process, dry, package and export it.
Mbonteh advised that snails lend themselves to both small and large-scale production and the future looks bright, and it's farming should be encouraged to avoid extinction.

Original_Search_Title: Why Fashola And Yar'Adua Need to Work Together
Headline: Nigeria: Why Fashola And Yar'Adua Need to Work Together
Body_Text:
Lagos — In trying to characterize the leadership style of President Yar'adua, a lot is being written about his quiet and slow mien and the general lack luster disposition which tends to signal a weak presidency. This may be right or wrong. The contemplative mood may be a sign of enormous inner strength or a sign of appreciation of the Herculean task ahead. The president may well, believe in the saying that actions speak louder than voice but it is of strategic importance to him to reckon early, the boisterous nature of Nigerians. Nigerians are highly knowledgeable and analytical people. The elites are vocal and comparative in their assessment of leadership. Being widely traveled, a typical Nigerian elite will easily reel out worldwide comparative facts on any issue whatsoever. Nigerians are also a long suffering people who have remained the happiest people on earth despite existing on less than one dollar a day.
To lead the Nigerian people requires dexterity and indeed innovativeness. You do not have to be the most brilliant of the 150 million people but you have to come with a unique style. Such a style may not be esoteric, complex or couched in sophisticated intellectual or academic agenda but something that should touch or connect with the ordinary people in the street. For instance, the late MKO Abiola put aside his mastery of business and economy and the fact that he was a wealthy chartered accountant of Nigeria, England and Wales and decided early enough to exhibit the humility of his origin and his large reservoir of native intelligence through his inimitable proverbs and wise sayings. He went further to touch the right cord by preaching the gospel of reparation - a reminder of the despoliation of Africa by our European colonizers. Though M.K.O Abiola was not allowed to assume his mandate, he surely would have been a charismatic leader.
Compare him to the out gone president Olusegun Obasanjo who despite his antecedents decided to suffocate Nigerians with lectures and lectures about just any topic in this world. Nigerians left him alone on the podium to wallow in his wisdom or ignorance and a whole world of opportunity to produce our own icon was lost.
A leader must be known for something unique. This uniqueness must be innate or derive from the leaders conviction and not through an image packaged by public relations experts. In this trying times and with all the credibility and moral problems foisted on us by an imperfect election, any person finding himself in position of
leadership should rapidly draw leadership lessons from world successful leaders and begin to cultivate the right physical, spiritual and intellectual image. It is only through this disposition that the leader can successfully hover in the horizon and dominate positively, the consciousness and world view of the led. Our new President therefore requires to take decisive steps in this direction. He should discard the shoes of Shehu Musa Yar'adua and Olusegun Obasanjo and go for his own unique design. If Umaru Yar'adua is truly a disciple of Aminu Kano, he should begin the process of restructuring and transforming PDP into a real masses party with programmes that will touch the masses. Such programmes must be in tune with our culture. For instance, for most rural communities, it is an anathema not to own a house. It is even worst if the house you purport to own was built by someone else. Therefore, rather than embark on futile Shagari voyage of building houses in the bush, all in the name of providing shelter the government should through mortgage institutions and community banks grant loans at highly concessional rates to citizens to build their own houses. The government can of course demarcate residential areas and give specific design patterns for the houses but it should have no business constructing houses for people. Agriculture is another area that offers unique opportunity for Yar'adua to excel. The vast land we have in this country should be cultivated. All empty lands should be converted to farm settlements. The industries must move into the farm settlements to produce and package the fruit juice, tomato puree, milk, yam and cassava powder. As a matter of priority, we should establish or reactivate assembly plants for the manufacture of tractors and other farm implements. Our university faculties of agriculture and engineering should be tasked to produce high yield crops and affordable farm implements while a collaborative effort of NAFDAC and Standard Organization of Nigeria should see to it that our processed products are of international standard.
While opening up rural areas for productive agro business, our cities must be cleaned up and made enabling for industries that will utilize agro raw materials from rural farms. The planning authorities must move in to standardize the structures in terms of strength of building materials and aesthetics. Trees, flowers and grass planting along pathways must be a compulsory aspect of building plan approval. Every building must be repainted at least every ten years. Our markets must be properly designated and located. Street trading should be banned. In its place should be standardized stalls in designated areas. Our roads should be widened and constructed to endure our terrain. New interlinking roads should be constructed. The management of solid waste should also be a priority. Local health inspectors should be deplored to ensure that dirt do not liter the streets. A policy for phased upgrade of health, educational and other infrastructure such as power and energy should be put in place. It is on this foundation that the economy can run efficiently and smoothly. Of course this has to be done in collaboration with state Governments. One state that is of crucial importance to Nigeria is Lagos State. The Governor of Central Bank, Prof. Chukwuma Soludo said it aptly when he warned that, "if Lagos fails, Nigeria fails". Therefore, political differences apart, the two leaders, Yar'adua and Fashola should work together to ensure that Lagos does not fail.
Back to their cap, may I suggest a design competition to give these two leaders their own unique caps. It is certainly not good for the political image of Fashola, that unlike his mentor, Asiwaju Bola Ahmed Tinubu he neither has a uniquely designed cap nor a unique pattern of wearing his numerous colours and designs. Same for the President, his cap appears to be too plain, too ordinary and too cheap looking. I also think he will need to wear reading glass for he appears often to be struggling with his sight. Our presidential handlers should start the work now. Things like this as small as they appear, has worked excellently well for leaders such as Jimmy Carter (his hair style) Mahatma Ghandi (his eye glasses), Mandela (his shirts), Zik (flowing gown, cap, glasses and eloquence) Awo (cap, glasses and eloquence)Tinubu (his cap) etc. Image they say, is everything.
Paschal E. Egerue is the Managing Director/CEO of Afribank Insurance Brokers Co. Ltd.

Original_Search_Title: Govt Accused of Secrecy On GMO Technology Bill
Headline: Kenya: Govt Accused of 'Secrecy' On GMO Technology Bill
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The process of preparing a Bill to regulate genetically modified technology in Kenya is shrouded in secrecy, a leading environmental Lawyer told The EastAfrican last week.
"There has been so much secrecy that most stakeholders do not even know where they should go to get a copy of the Bill," said Maurice Makoloo.
He explained that, under Kenya's environmental law, any proposed law or policy is supposed to be subjected to an environmental impact assessment, which would give all concerned parties a chance to interrogate its contents.
"But as for the Biosafety Bill, this has not been done? most researchers, lawyers and other stakeholders have been kept in the dark."
Similar sentiments were expressed by representatives of Kenya's small-scale farmers, faith-based organisations, NGOs and civil society groups who presented a memorandum to the Minister of Science and Technology, Dr Noah Wekesa, and Agricultural Minister Kipruto Arap Kirwa.
In the memorandum, the groups demanded the withdrawal of the Bill before it becomes an Order Paper for discussion by Members of Parliament. They also called for a national exercise of collecting views and the incorporation of the views into the Bill.
The EastAfrican has acquired a copy of the Bill, which - on the surface - appears harmless as it provides for the establishment of a National Biosafety Authority to regulate biotechnology in the country as contained in Section 5.
First published in 2005, the Bill is intended to "facilitate responsible research" into genetically modified organisms and to ensure protection in transfer and use of GMOs. It also provides for the establishment of the National Biosafety Authority and the National Biosafety Committee, which will regulate all GM activities in the country.
"No person shall conduct any of the following activities without the written approval of the Authority," it reads in part.
Dr Florence Wambugu, head of the African Harvest Biotech Foundation and a key pro-GM campaigner, says the Bill's objectives "are to ensure an adequate level of protection in the field of safe transfer, handling and use of genetically modified organisms."
The EastAfrican has established that the new Bill is the culmination of a long-running boardroom initiative by key biotechnology bodies and national research institutes, with support from the United States Agency for International Development (USAid) and giant biotechnology multinationals and their foundations.
These efforts are said to have begun in the early 2000s when it was felt that with the global expansion of research on genetically modified organisms (GMOs), Kenya needed a national policy and law to outline the direction of such research and to ensure safety of the public. The scheme also involved taking some MPs on a tour to Kenya's coast to "sensitise" them to biotechnology issues.
Seemingly, researchers have not been keen to wait for the Bill to become law before openly experimenting on the GM crops. For instance, with facilitation from US-based biotechnology multinational Monsanto and the Syngenta Foundation (associated with the Swiss-based biotechnology giant, Syngenta) researchers at the Kenya Agricultural Research Institute (Kari) had been experimenting on GM maize, cassava, sweet potatoes and cotton.
At the same time, the National Biosafety Committee allowed open trials of genetically modified cotton on farmers' fields in Mwea, Eastern Kenya, last month.
Interestingly, Monsanto offered the new cotton variety, which it hailed as having the ability to save farmers up to 32 per cent in production costs, as it does not require pesticides.
The cotton variety is said to be loaded with an organism termed Bacillus thurigiensis that is reputed to have in-built poisons that kill cotton pests (especially African bollworm).
But environmentalists now fear that the introduction of these toxins in areas that have not developed a natural ability to destroy them, could result in the decimation of beneficial plants and insects besides other hazards.
Observers are wondering why the concerned Kenyan authorities and individual scientists seem to be in such a hurry to introduce GMOs in the country. Some Kenyan MPs seem to have also bought into the pro-GMO lobby as they have in the past praised the benefits that genetically modified foods can bring to the country. Media reports published two years ago about the MPs' workshops at the Whitesands Hotel in Mombasa showed most were receptive to biotechnology, which they believed could uplift economic development in the country, and were also supportive of the Biosafety Bill.
Analysts have now taken issue with some of the Bill's provisions. On June 12 this year, Patricia Mbote, a professor of law at the International Environmental Law Research Centre in Nairobi, wrote in a newspaper article that though the Bill is fairly uncontroversial, it does not deal with the contentious issue of labelling. "But Kenya's main market for agricultural exports is Europe, where labelling requirements are strict and consumers are generally more sceptical of GMOs."
She felt that lack of provisions on labelling will jeopardise this valuable trade besides influencing parliamentary debate. Her sentiments were echoed last week by the Kenya Small Scale Farmers Forum (KESSFF), which was categorical that farmers in the country stand to lose them European market once their products are associated with genetic modification.
"Consumers in the international markets are now after organically produced foods, but here we are being pressurised to raise GM crops... What will happen to the market for our products is anybody's guess," said the treasurer of KESSFF Justus Lavi.
"Our fear is that we will end up losing our traditional seed varieties and get hooked to expensive varieties from Monsanto and other international biotechnology companies," said Gerald Ngatia, a member of the Kenya Biodiversity Coalition.
He added that once this happens, Kenyan farmers will not only be forced to buy the seeds, but also chemicals and especially pesticides emanating from these companies to ensure the survival of their crops.
Another contentious issue is the fact that the Bill does not address food coming into Kenya as aid. In the past, there have been claims that international agencies that distribute food relief in Kenya during periods of famine and other natural calamities could have triggered off the entry of GM products. For instance, there has been a lingering suspicion that food aid, especially maize, brought into the country by USAid and such bodies as the World Food Programme might be from genetically modified stocks.
Dr Wambugu conceded that food relief from the US is genetically modified. This feeling is strengthened by the fact that in the US, between 50 and 70 per cent of the maize grown is genetically modified. "There is also a policy in the two organisations not to discriminate between GM and non-GM foods when offering them as relief," said Dr Daniel Maingi of ANAW Biowatch, an anti-GMO organisation based in Nairobi.
"For the draft Bill to proceed, the debate needs to move beyond scientists and policymakers to encompass open public discussions with all stakeholders, including farmer groups," wrote Prof Mbote.
The Kenyan public, the anti-Bill lobby says, need to first be educated to understand what biotechnology is all about as well as the potential implications of consuming genetic modified foods. This is because evidence collected during past surveys suggests most Kenyans do not have any idea of what the two terms - biotechnology and genetic modification - entail.
"It is very important for decision makers, leaders, farmers, pastoralists, fisher folk, traders and consumers to distinguish the difference between the two terms and their implications before allowing the Bill to be discussed in parliament," says a statement released last week to the media by the Kenya Biosafety Coalition.
On her part, Prof Mbote calls for "unbiased information" to be provided on the benefits as well as risks of GMOs so that demand for GM regulations will be based on knowledge rather than on speculation or vested interests.

Original_Search_Title: Tourism is Country's 3rd Forex Earner
Headline: Rwanda: Tourism is Country's 3rd Forex Earner
Body_Text:
Kinigi — With tourism receipts of US$36million in 2006, the sector is Rwanda's third foreign exchange earner.
This marks a 30% increase from 2005. To build on the momentum, the country needs to improve its work ethic and professionalism in the service sector in order to be a competitive tourism destination, President Paul Kagame has stated.
Kagame was speaking during the annual gorilla-naming event, which has now been branded Kwiti Izina (Kinyarwanda word for 'to give a name') that took place at Kinigi, Musanze district in the northern province (formerly Ruhengeri) on June 30.
"Air transport, travel agencies, transport sector, banking, restaurants, hotels and the value chain requires a fundamental change to become a serious contender in the global tourism industry," he said. The first lady of Rwanda, Ms. Jeannette Kagame was also at the ceremony.
Some 23 baby gorillas were named by invited guests who included US Star Wars movie actress Natalie Portman and the chairman of Rwandatel (former Terracom Communications) Mr. John Dick who flew in from the US for the function.
The ceremony was punctuated by traditional dance and the poetry recital of a young child, Maire Aline Uwayezu won the hearts of many guests present.
A representative from the family of Mr. Steve Irwin, the famous Australian wildlife expert and television personality nick named 'the Crocodile Hunter'; Mr. John Stainton named a gorilla on their behalf. Irwin (RIP) passed away in September 2006 in an unusual diving accident in Australia.
High-ranking officials trekked to Kinigi to witness the event whose gorillas fetched $5million through tourist gorilla tracking out of the total $36million realized by the Rwandan economy last year.
Renowned Wild life Conservation personality Mr. Jack Hanna was among the 600 present. These hailed from government, private sector as well as regional and international guests.
President Kagame acknowledged the role played by the Rwanda Office of Tourism and National Parks (ORTPN) in community involvement, which has ensured a revenue sharing formula.
In Kinigi, a modern $1million lodge, Sabyinyo Community Lodge that is 100% owned through a community trust will be completed before the end of the year.
The president said that in partnership with the local community, conservation has been made possible in the past decade and this should be continued.
"Tourism is a vital economic driver but this should not be at the expense of environmental protection and wildlife conservation," President Kagame said.
In an effort to live to the theme, 'Caring for Wildlife concerns us all', Rwanda has upgraded Nyungwe forest into a National Park with an appropriate accommodation facility. The Nyungwe eco lodge is to be opened in 2008. In addition, the Akagera National Park has of recent experienced a boost in animal population. The so-called big five; lion, rhino, elephant, buffalo and leopard have begun reappearing, which will help boost visitors to the park and dollar receipts.
The director general of ORTPN, Ms. Rosette Rugamba told guests that the Kwita Izina event is an international event with a national identity and will be promoted and publicized on the international tourism calendar.
She said proceeds from the gorilla-naming event will be used to upgrade the Nyungwe and Akagera National Parks and the socio-economic well being of the community.
"We are helping the local community get involved in the business of handicrafts and have built 10 water tanks for water harvesting. Six private schools have also been constructed so that children no longer need to study under trees but can now study in proper classrooms," she said.
On the eve of the Kwita Izina ceremony, a cultural event was organized by the Musanze community in the northern province. It was dominated by folk songs, dancing, cassava and maize roasting, local brew and games. The aim was to show the communities that gorillas are an integral part of their welfare and thus further involve them in their conservation.
Kwita Izina was followed by a fundraising at Lake Kivu Serena that targeted $1.5million. This year's priority projects include installing an electric fence around Akagera National Park, constructing a community commercial complex in Kinigi for the communities to sell their crafts and resettlement of residents that were evicted from the buffer zone as well as planting tea around Nyungwe Park.

Original_Search_Title: Study Confirms Country As Regional Food Basket
Headline: Uganda: Study Confirms Country As Regional Food Basket
Body_Text:
Kampala — The volume of unrecorded agricultural exports leaving Uganda's borders into her five neighbouring states has confirmed the country as a major food basket for the region.
Findings of a survey conducted by the Central Bank, the Uganda Bureau of Statistics (UBOS) and the Uganda Revenue Authority (URA) indicate that Uganda has a comparative advantage in agricultural products.
"Informal cross-border trade between Uganda and her neighbours is significant, involving huge transactions of both agricultural and industrial products," said Dr. David Kihangire, the director for research at the Bank of Uganda (BoU).
Presenting the findings of the survey, which was conducted between 2005 and 2006, Kihangire said informal trade between Uganda and the five neighbours has increased considerably.
Exports he said increased by 15.7%, from $200m to $231m, while imports rose by 22.4%, from $66m to over $80m.
Results of the survey now show that Uganda's overall export earnings from both trading arrangements (formal and informal trade) amounted to $1.19 billion in 2006 compared to the combined export earnings of $1.01 billion in 2005.
The trade deficit resulting from both formal and informal is estimated at $1,444.0 million in 2006.
The survey involved monitoring trade over five months at the 14 main border posts with Kenya, Tanzania, Rwanda, the DR Congo and Sudan.
It found that agricultural products accounted for 48.7% while industrial products accounted for 51% of total informal exports.
Fish was found to be the main export product, earning the country Ush61b ($33m), followed by maize with a value of Ush50b ($30.3m), beans ($22.4) and groundnuts ($6.6m).
The survey also found that non-traditional cash crops had surpassed traditional cash crops in earnings.
Dominant among the informal imports into Uganda were the now banned polythene bags (kaveera), which accounted for one-third of total imports, and second-hand clothes, accounting for almost 9%.
The study recommended deepening trade liberalisation; with more appropriate national and regional trade policies to realise a large trade potential existing between Uganda and her neighbours.
Kenya continued to be Uganda's leading trading partner with exports and imports estimated at $96.9m and $63.9m respectively. Kenya was followed by DR Congo, Rwanda, Tanzania and Sudan.
Overall, Uganda had a comparative advantage in export of agricultural products, whereas Kenya export had a competitive advantage over industrial products.
Trade with Sudan has soared following the restoration of peace in Southern Sudan, but unrecorded exports to Sudan fell slightly from $9.1m in 2005 to $7.8m in 2006 due to insecurity caused by rebels along the border.
Informal exports to Sudan comprise of mainly industrial products like fish ($0.9m), soft drinks (0.7m), mattresses ($0.8m) and wheat flour while. Imports from Sudan were mainly agricultural products like beans, maize and sorghum.
Informal trade between Uganda and the DRC increased from $73.9m in 2005 to $80.5m. Total unrecorded trade between the two states was estimated at $91.7m, with a trade surplus of $69.2m.
Manufactured products stand out as the main export there at a value of $58m while agricultural products fetched $21.8m. Imports from DR Congo were mainly agricultural commodities valued at $8.9 million compared to imports of industrial products worth $2.4 million.
Uganda's unrecorded export trade with Tanzania increased substantially from $42.8 million in 2005 to $21.5 million in 2006. The informal imports also increased from $0.6 million to $4.3 million in the same period.
The leading agricultural exports to Tanzania were maize, beans, whose values were estimated at $5.8 million (30,288 tonnes) and $0.8 million (1,849 tonnes) respectively.
Unprocessed coffee ($0.9m) was the main imported agricultural product from Tanzania during 2006.
Recent inflows of unprocessed coffee from Tanzania and other neighbouring countries could be attributed to readily available market and favourable prices offered for the commodity under a liberalised trade environment.
Rwanda, a new entrant into the East African Community (EAC) increased her informal trade with Uganda with exports to Rwanda more than tripling from $7.3 million in 2005 to $25million in 2006.
Industrial products dominated the informal export commodities to Rwanda while Uganda majorly imported fruits, peas and hides and skins from Rwanda.
Maize was the leading agricultural export whose earnings were estimated at $1.6 million (7,697 tonnes). Others were Irish potatoes, sweet potatoes, cassava, beans, bananas and groundnuts.

Original_Search_Title: Woman to Serve One Year for Aiding Defiler
Headline: Uganda: Woman to Serve One Year for Aiding Defiler
Body_Text:
Jinja — Jinja chief magistrate Cissy Mudhasi last week sentenced a woman to one year's imprisonment in Kirinya Prison for aiding the defilement of a 14-year girl.
Aisha Nandego,33, a resident of Mafubira Trading Centre in Jinja district is said to have enticed the minor into the arms of a man only identified as Junior, by giving her cassava chips.
The girl told court that Ms Nandego told her about a man who was rich and loved her. Ms Nandego allegedly told her they would reap big if she accepted to date the man.
Although the girl declined, Ms Nandego could not hear of it.
She asked the girl to accompany her to the market only to lead her into the man's house.
It is said that after the two entered the house, Ms Nandego walked out leaving the girl inside. She then closed the two in the house at about 8pm. The girl said the man, whom she had not met beforethe incident, repeatedly and forcefully had unprotected sex with her.
Court heard that Ms Nandego unlocked the door at 10pm to let the girl out. She said Ms Nandigo took her to the man's room three other times. Court heard that Ms Nandego unlocked the door at 10pm to let the girl out.

Original_Search_Title: Scarcity of Cassava Flour, Wheat Hit Milling Industry
Headline: Nigeria: Scarcity of Cassava Flour, Wheat Hit Milling Industry
Body_Text:
Lagos — SHORTAGES of cassava flour, coupled with surging wheat prices at the world market are adversely affecting the operations of Flour milling companies in the country, according to Standard Flour Mills Limited's General Manager (HR/Admin), Festus Olarenwaju.
He gave this hint on the occasion of presentation of MANCAP (Mandatory Conformity Assessment Programme) certificate to the company by Standards Organisation of Nigeria (SON).
Fielding questions from newsmen on compliance with the 5 per cent cassava content in bread and other flour products by the company, he declared: " We have been complying. SON is always coming to inspect our factory to see the level of compliance, but supply of cassava flour is inadequate. Where is the cassava, do we have enough farmers, fabricators and processors?," he queried.
According to him, other challenges facing operators in the sector also include rising prices of wheat costing $20 per bushel and $180 - $204 per ton.
"In flour milling industry, almost 90 per cent of the raw materials are imported because Nigeria's climate is not viable for the production of wheat. The Federal Government tried it in Mambilla plateau years back but it didn't thrive for commercial quantity. So most of the wheat are being imported from U.S. but the U.S. finds it better to export to China, because China is an emerging world power," he explained.
Low capacity utilisation, and inconsistent power supply by PHCN, he said, are other challenges being faced by stakeholders.
"We have four big generating plants and it cost so much to fuel them. Doing business in the country is very challenging, nevertheless, this is our country, we have to edge on," he stated.
It's a thing of joy that we have been recognised as one of the finest flour millers in the country," said Executive Director, Mrs. Modasola Adetunji, who received the certificates of conformity on behalf of the Board and Management, said, "without regulation from SON, a lot of Nigerians would be in the grave due to consumption of hazardous products particularly in the food and beverages sector."
Acknowledging that it's no easy task to meet MANCAP requirements, she urged other companies to work hard in order to comply with the regulation because as she puts it, standards turn economy around for the good of all stakeholders - the companies, the country and the consumers.
SON Zonal Coordinator Lagos, Dr. Yunus Alaya said: "Only companies that embrace MANCAP are sure of the future. The aims of the conformity assessment for local manufacturers are to that ensure only safe goods are produced in the country, protect consumers' safety and Made -in-Nigeria goods are of competitive value. Our duty is to check standards right from raw materials to the point of production, distribution and sales," he said.
While commending the company for having the right equipment in place, he warned : "Certificates last three years. Consumers are paramount to us, and if we discover you have lowered your standards, these certificates will be withdrawn and fines or penalty imposed."

Original_Search_Title: Uganda On Migration Crisis List
Headline: Uganda On Migration Crisis List
Body_Text:
Kampala — UGANDA has been named as one of the countries facing displacement of people as a result of climate change, according to a new study.
The report, released recently by the British charity Christian Aid, says scarcity of water and crop failure as a result of global warming will trigger off movements of people in both Uganda and Sudan.
The organisation calculates that the effects of climate change will make at least one billion people homeless worldwide between now and 2050.
The charity points out that most of those on the move will remain in their own countries as internally displaced people, also referred to as "environmental refugees".
It notes that such people have no rights under international law and no voice, though in many cases their lives will be in danger.
The report's main author, John Davison, points out that countries have known economic refugees, people who flee the country and seek employment elsewhere, but that the real crisis facing the world is the emergence of environmental refugees.
Uganda issued its own report on climate change earlier this year.
The study predicted more frequent and severe droughts, less rainfall but more storms, landslides and floods, lower water tables and drying up boreholes, a reduction of coffee and cassava growing areas, as well as the spread of malaria and other infectious diseases for humans and crops.
The current problems of the pastoralists, such as the Basongora in Kasese, the Bakiga in southwestern Uganda and the herdsmen in Bulisa, western Uganda, are seen by some as examples of environmental refugees.
"This could be a result of population pressure; people are scrambling for the scarce resources. Climate change further complicates the situation," said Paul Isabirye, an expert in the Meteorology Department.
"As elsewhere in Africa, the population continues to grow as the environment deteriorates, exerting more pressure on a land that grows ever drier."
Climate experts in April issued the bleakest UN warning so far about the impacts of global warming, ranging from failing crops and hunger in Africa to species extinctions and rising sea levels world-wide. It predicted that sub-Saharan Africa would be hit hardest, calculating that some African nations might have to spend 5% to 10% of their gross domestic product on adapting to climate change.
"Climate change will hit pastoral communities below the belt," said Isabirye, who is also a Government representative at the UN negotiations on climate change.
"The conflict is already getting out of hand and the Government urgently needs to address this state of affairs."
The state minister for environment, Jessica Eriyo, said Uganda is developing a policy on climate change.

Original_Search_Title: Private Sector to Export to Oman
Headline: Rwanda: Private Sector to Export to Oman
Body_Text:
Kigali — Information from Rwanda Investment and Export Promotion Agency (Riepa) indicates that the private sector is to start exporting food stuffs to Oman.
"Several (members of the private sector) have come inquiring about the Oman market. Actually one wants to start exporting pineapples," Jean Guy Africa said. He is Riepa's director for traditional exports.
The private sector's interests in the Middle East market come after Riepa shipped 35 tonnes of assorted food products to Oman.
The products included cow ghee, honey, Agashya passion fruit juice, sweet bananas, cassava flour, and chili.

Original_Search_Title: Natural Fertilizers Vital, Says Dokolo Boss
Headline: Uganda: Natural Fertilizers Vital, Says Dokolo Boss
Body_Text:
Dokolo — THERE is need to promote the use natural fertilizers for sustainable agriculture to address the problem of food insecurity in the north, the Dokolo sub-county LC3 chairman has said.
Mr Peter Ejebu was speaking during celebrations to mark the National Organic Day at the sub-county headquarters on Saturday.
The function was organised by the National Organic Agricultural Movement of Uganda (Nogamu) under the theme; "Strengthening Standards and Certification Systems in the Organic Sector"
He said food insecurity and land conflicts were a major challenge to returnees IDPs.
"We appeal to well wishers to support farmers in post conflict areas to enable them emerge from poverty and compete with other regions in terms of better standards of living and development," he said.
Mr Ejebu urged farmers to embrace organic farming saying it is productive and does not affect soil nutrients.
Organic Agriculture is the growing of crops or stock farming where only natural fertilisers, pesticides and nutritional supplements are used in improving the productivity of the soils or controlling pests and diseases.
Organic farmers rely on crop rotation, crop residues, animal manures and mechanical cultivation to maintain soil productivity, supply plant nutrients and to control weeds, insects and other pests.
The Officer in Charge of Dokolo Police station Damascus Mahehe said Nogamu's intervention had begun bearing fruit since returnee IDPs are now producing food on a sustainable basis.
Nogamu was established to unite producers, processors, marketeers, trainers and stakeholders in organic farming.
Mr Mahebe said most people in the north have been confined in camps for so long and were ignorant of best farming practices.
Nogamu representative in the north George Oyenja said organic agriculture is growing at a fast rate in the region.
He said there are currently over 300 organic farmers in Lango sub-region alone.
"Most of them grow shear nuts, coffee, cotton, ginger, tomatoes, groundnuts, maize, beans, cassava among other crops," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Agriculture - Africa's Dependence
Headline: Africa: Agriculture - Africa's Dependence
Body_Text:
Kigali — Africa is rich in both natural and human resources, yet nearly 200 million of its people are malnourished because of inadequate food supplies.
Though dependent on agricultural activities, most Sub Saharan Africans live in dry or sub humid agro-ecological zones, and recent climate change models show that climate change will affect the rainfall patterns in these zones.
The impact is usually felt in the droughts, when perennial grasses are not able to use the sparse rainwater more efficiently but tap the nutrients stored in their roots.
However in the effort to improve on the yields, the farming systems now in use in varying degrees across Africa show the most promise for reducing malnutrition and improving agricultural productivity since they are directed to reduce soil erosion and depletion of soil nutrients.
These conclude the maize-mixed system, which is based primarily on maize, cotton, cattle, goats, poultry, and supplemental non farm work.
Cereal/root crop-mixed system, based on maize, sorghum, millet, cassava, yams,legumes, and cattle.
Irrigated system, based on rice, cotton, vegetables, rain-fed crops, cattle, and poultry; and the tree crop-based system, anchored in cocoa, coffee, oil palm, and rubber, and involves yams, maize, and non farm work as well.
Comprehensive strategies are needed across the continent to harness the power of science and technology in ways that will boost agricultural productivity, profitability, and sustainability.
This will ultimately ensure that all Africans have access to enough safe and nutritious food to meet their dietary needs
Within each of these systems, policy-makers, scientists, and farmers should explore all Science and technology options, including both conventionally bred and genetically modified plants; chemical and organic fertilizers; irrigation; and strategies that integrate pest, soil, and nutrient management.
In the near future, stakeholders also should take greater advantage of Information and Communication Technology, and encourage local production of agricultural machinery and equipment to reduce Africa's dependence on industrialized countries for such goods.
Farmers must be key players in all of these efforts.
African Centres of Agricultural Research should also be created and excellence to undertake basic research that can lead to the development of new technologies to improve agricultural productivity.
These centres, which would supplement work done by national agriculture agencies, should be owned and governed by Africans to help curtail the flight of the continent's agricultural scientists, who often seek opportunities in other industries or parts of the world.
Farmers should be taught to help identify relevant avenues of research for the centres.
On the whole, African governments should significantly increase investments in agricultural institutions.
Likewise, African governments, with support from donor agencies, should create a range of incentives and opportunities for scientists to stay and work in their countries.
Government leaders also must invest more in science and technology at all levels of education to create environments that can attract more students to these fields.
Vibrant market economies and effective economic policies are essential to helping poor farmers gain financial stability and improving food security, the report adds.
Governments must increase investments in rural infrastructure; such as roads, information and communication technology, and food storage technology. Plus, governments must ensure that appropriate food quality and safety standards and regulations are in place and enforced.
African nations should work together to support such standards, and also to eliminate regional trade barriers, increase research capacity, and expand Africans' opportunities in international markets.
Trade barriers with nations that are members of the Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development, which includes the world's largest economies, should be reduced.
And the members should help developing countries in Africa meet food quality and safety standards and enhance their ability to make sound decisions about agricultural and economic policy - with the goal of strengthening Africa's position in global agriculture markets.
Apart from agriculture, Africa is endowed with abundant and wide range of energy resources.
The region's renewable energy potential includes a wealth of biomass, solar and wind resource.
In addition, the region has over 1.1 million of exploitable hydro capacity; 59 billion barrels of petroleum; 8 billion cubic meters of natural gas reserves and, over 60 billion tons of coal.
So with economic development, more sustainable resource management and reductions of high fertility rates will all contribute to both poverty reduction and to increasing the capacity of Africans to cope with climate change.

Original_Search_Title: Business Has Strong Foothold in Ghana
Headline: South Africa: Business Has Strong Foothold in Ghana
Body_Text:
South Africans travelling in Ghana are not at a loss for home comforts as many South African companies have established a strong presence in the west African nation, writes Lavinia Mahlangu.
Familiar corporations in the retail and financial sectors have branches in Ghana, while even more consumer products from the Southern African Development Community nations have found their way to the stores of its counterparts in the Economic Community of West African States.
South Africa's Standard Bank has a local subsidiary, known as Stanbic Bank which offers only the Mastercard service in the country.
Other banks in Ghana offer only VISA services, as opposed to Stanbic Bank which accepts cards from both Mastercard and Visa, including their linked products such as Maestro and Electron, respectively.
However, it is not all sunny skies for the local venture in Ghana.
Stanbic Bank's parent company has been in the spotlight recently after making an unsolicited offer to buy shares in the Ghanaian government owned Agriculture Development Bank (ADB).
The ADB Bank is the umbrella bank for 123 rural community banks and supervises them throughout Ghana.
The ADB's branches in the capital Accra are now emblazoned with massive banners declaring "NO WAY Stanbic Bank".
The offer followed government's announcement to derive more value from the ADB Bank by possibly unbundling shares.
An official approach of how this would be carried out was, however, not revealed, although the government has privatised its entities for a profit in the past.
The new Accra Mall currently under construction between the capital and the nation's largest port in the town of Tema, boasts branches of macro-retailer Game and the Shoprite supermarket as flagship stores.
Branches of Woolworths, which is a multinational, are also present in Accra and Tema. These particular branches are backed by South African business interests.
On the high-street of Osu, the commercial and tourist hub of Accra, both Woolworths and MTN stores can be found.
MTN's operations in west and central Africa include Ghana, Nigeria, Cameroon and Liberia, amongst others. MTN entered the Ghanaian market following the acquisition of Investcom in 2006.
In the fast food industry, a massive new Steers restaurant, owned by South Africa's Famous Brands group is currently under construction, also in the vibrant Osu high-street.
