Another restaurant chain which can be found at Total filling station food courts around the country is the chicken outlet, Gallito's.
Total's "Bonjour" Food Court business model includes the South African Pizza Inn, Chicken Inn and Creamy Inn, as well as the convenience stores which stock Amarula Cream, the local "Saints" range of wines and Gallito's hot sauces.
There are almost boundless investment opportunities for South African businesses in Ghana, Charles Sam, the anchor of Good Evening Ghana and an investment consultant, told BuaNews.
"For instance Ghana has a massive timber industry but not a single local company manufactures something as simple as toothpicks here.
"We import every single toothpick you will see in homes, offices and the hundreds of restaurants here," said Mr Sam, who visits South Africa regularly for business purposes.
"This country is a great investment opportunity for South African companies to bring their operations here and manufacture for the regional market. They may even find it cheaper to manufacture products here and export them back to South Africa."
Apart from timber the other natural resources in Ghana are gold, industrial diamonds, bauxite, manganese, fish, rubber, hydropower, petroleum, silver, salt and limestone.
South African farmers could also reap large benefits by starting up operations to grow vegetables with a view to exporting them. Local vegetables are considered rather exotic to South African palates.
"Your farmers can invest here and grow cassava, plantain and yams for instance and then sell them in your designer supermarkets as premium crops," he suggested.
Ghana is quite an attractive investment destination boasting a growing, wealthy middle class as can be seen in the construction of literally hundreds of multi-storey manors in Tema.
The country's infrastructure includes 42 623 km of roads, of which 3 267 km's is tarred and 39 356 km is untarred according to 2004 estimates.
This indicates that much work and investment still needs to take place, although judging by the number of industrial cranes and domestic building sites, it would appear development is rapidly forging ahead in the west African nation.
Another major infrastructure achievement is Lake Volta, which is the world's largest artificial lake.
Kotoka International Airport is strategically located, being about seven hours from the United States and South Africa and about six hours from Paris.
According to estimates, Ghana has roughly twice the per capita output of the poorest countries in the ECOWAS region.
However, the country remains heavily dependent on international financial and technical assistance.
Gold, timber, and cocoa production are major sources of foreign exchange and the domestic economy continues to revolve around subsistence agriculture.
This accounts for 37 percent of GDP and employs 60 percent of the work force, who are mostly, small-scale landowners.
In terms of import partners, 2005 figures put Nigeria first at 15.2 percent, China 12.5 percent, the US at 6.3 percent, United Kingdom at 5.2 percent, South Africa at 4.5 percent, Brazil 4.1 percent and the Netherlands 4 percent.
These figures are promising and indicate that South Africa would do well to step up its investment efforts and take advantage of the opportunities Ghana's emerging economy presents.

Original_Search_Title: Justice Too Costly, Say Experts
Headline: Kenya: Justice Too Costly, Say Experts
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Many Kenyans have no access to justice, and where it is guaranteed it is often delayed. Poor infrastructure, corruption and ignorance have escalated injustice.
This was said during a live debate on KTN's weekly interactive show, Newsline.
Four panellists engaged the audience in a debate on whether the justice system works. Eighty-five per cent responded negatively.
Dr Ekuru Aukot, executive director of Kituo cha Sheria, singled out inefficiency as the greatest impediment to judicial services.
"Most lawyers and the Judiciary have used Kituo cha Sheria as a dumping site where they refer cases they do not want to handle," he said.
As a result, he noted, people are forced to camp at the institution's offices from as early as 5am. He accused lawyers of abandoning cases they feel would not benefit them.
Mr James Maina, a Mathare resident, said many Kenyans cannot access justice due to prohibitive costs and ignorance. He said ignorance has exposed many slum dwellers to suffering.
On the recent security operations in Mathare's Kosovo area, Maina said many people in the slums would rather bribe police than be arrested and charged in a court of law. He said Mathare residents fear courts.
But Prof Gilbert Ogutu of the University of Nairobi traced Judiciary to a society's quest to resolve disputes.
"At the Luo Council of Elders, there are cases we handle at the family court. A committee of people sit and discuss. You cannot sentence an hungry person to seven years in prison for stealing cassava or a hen."
Ogutu is also the secretary general of the Luo Council of Elders.
But in the case of statutory law, such a person, on pleading guilty, would be jailed.
Ogutu urged Kenyans to use cultural laws to settle family, land and children disputes since Kituo cha Sheria can only handle 30 cases a week.
Mr Charles Kihara, a lawyer, said court filing fee bars many people from seeking justice.
A member of the audience accused lawyers and judges of perpetuating corruption. "Most lawyers take bribes from clients to ensure cases do not go through," he said.
The Government was urged to employ more lawyers to help Kenyans who cannot afford the cost of justice.

Original_Search_Title: New Fuel Tax Will Double Food Prices
Headline: Uganda: New Fuel Tax Will Double Food Prices
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE fuel tax introduced in the 2007/08 budget will push up the cost of goods and services, the Uganda Bureau of Statistics (UBOS) has warned.
According to the latest monthly Consumer Price Index (CPI), the annual headline inflation rate for June surged to 4.3% from 4.2% in May.
Headline inflation is based on relative changes in prices of all goods and services, while the CPI measures the changing fixed basket of goods and services consumed by households.
"We have been experiencing a continuous rise in inflation since the previous month.
"In the next few months, we could experience a rise in inflation due to the introduction of fuel tax which will increase the cost of production that will affect the consumers hence a double rise in commodity prices," Vincent Musoke, the UBOS principal statistician, said.
The CPI report largely attributed the rise in inflation to high prices of sugar, milk, meat and poultry, washing soap, taxi and bus fares, fruits and vegetable in the past two months.
"The 1% increase between May and June is of great concern as the diesel shortage had not yet stabilised. This affected the prices across the index," explained Musoke.
However, rate is lower than the 7.2% registered in June last year.
"This means that consumers are spending more to purchase the same amount of goods they consumed in the same period last year," Musoke explained.
"Prices of staple foods especially matooke, Irish potatoes, fresh cassava, passion fruits, pineapples, charcoal and vegetables dipped in almost all centres due to increased supplies," he added.
Musoke attributed this to good rains in June, which ensured sufficient supplies.
"Farmers do not have good storage facilities.
"Therefore, these products had to be sold off immediately at low prices," he noted.
Kampala, Mbarara, Mbale and Masaka registered the lowest food prices in June, while Gulu had the highest, the CPI indicated.

Original_Search_Title: Two Asian Returnees' Nostalgic Tale of Love And Passion for Uganda
Headline: Uganda: Two Asian Returnees' Nostalgic Tale of Love And Passion for Uganda
Body_Text:
Kampala — Jamal ponders his return to Uganda while right is Arain with some of the Orphans he looks after at his orphanage school in Kyebando
IT is 35 years since the Asian expulsion from Uganda. One returnee is Dr. Vali I. V. Jamal, former Senior Economist at the UN and now owner of Café Viva on Kampala Road. Frederick Golooba-Mutebi lets us into the world of this academic-turned- restaurateur.
I knew the name Vali Jamal long before I stumbled upon a debate in the local press about his pillao. When I saw the name in the papers it struck a cord.
As a graduate student, I had read some of his publications, including his book Africa Misunderstood for my course. I decided to look him up.
Before I did, he wrote me an email about a column I had written on the coping strategies of Kampala's working poor, a subject in which he has had a longstanding interest.
He invited me for a chat and to sample his pillao. I am a pillao buff and have been a regular at Uhuru's for ages. Jamal's pillao, I must say is "different."
What I had intended to be a short visit ended up in a long conversation about his background, the 1972 Asian expulsion, years in 'exile', his eventual return, and life since then.
He has a B.A. from Cambridge, a PhD from Stanford, and worked for 25 years as a Senior Economist with the International Labour Organisation (ILO).
His grandfather arrived in Uganda in 1904 with the great Indian trading pioneer, Allidina Visram; his mother was born in Mbale; two younger sisters were born here; his eldest sister is buried here; he married in Mbale where his in-laws owned Budaka Ginners and the Eastern Province Bus Company.
His first job was at the Ministry of Commerce and Industry, where he was in charge of infant-industry protection. In 1967, after his post was Ugandanised, he left for the US with his new wife, to continue studying economics.
At the start of 1972 he returned to do research for his dissertation on the impact of cotton and coffee on Uganda's economy.
By then the couple had two young children. He took up a teaching post on the Master's course in Agricultural Economics at Makerere University.
When the expulsion came, it was traumatic. On some nights his entire extended family of 15 people camped at his one-bedroom flat at Makerere.
His younger sister, Gulzar, and her husband, Amin, who had wanted to stay on, were eventually forced to leave when they were shot at by thugs.
The family lost everything. His father owned a house on 44 Borup Avenue (now Malcolm X). One of his brothers-in-law owned the Odeon Bar, the Honey Pot café, and a house in Kololo. Another brother-in-law, Amin, had a 1000-acre sugarcane farm in Luweero.
They ended up in Canada.
Vali and his family returned to Stanford. After he completed his doctorate he taught junior college for two years.
In 1976 he joined the ILO, first in Addis Ababa, then Geneva, Bangkok, and back to Geneva where he worked with the renowned Kenya-Asian professor, Dharam Ghai.
In 1982 he returned to Uganda for the first time since leaving in 1972, courtesy of local businessman, Amir Kassam of Kassmobile.
"Despite the ubiquitous roadblocks, it was such a nostalgic homecoming. I walked to all my childhood places in Old Kampala which had been home to most of the established Ismaili families of the time, who later on became successful in Canada. Some have returned and re-established themselves here," Jamal says.
One thrill was to discover the desk he had used as a pupil 25 years back at Old Kampala Senior Secondary School, with the "VIVJ" he had carved on it.
Thereafter, he returned every two years on ILO missions. He was struck by the "new economics" of the Ugandan economy. Predictably, commerce and industry, once Asian-dominated, had collapsed.
So had formal employment as a source of livelihood, followed by the informalisation of the economy, precisely the subject of our first correspondence.
Later on he discovered that this was common in much of Africa, and went on to publish his findings in his book, Africa Misunderstood, in which he indicted the World Bank and the IMF's Structural Adjustment Programmes for Africa.
After all that, I asked, how could he return to Uganda to run a restaurant?
"After retiring from the ILO I worked as a founder member of the African Child Policy Forum in Addis Ababa. I came to work on child-related issues," he says.
He thought of starting an internet café, one where keyboards functioned properly and the letters were still visible. A restaurant-owner he knew from Vancouver's downtown Aga Khan Mosque got him interested in running a restaurant.
"At first the idea was to run an internet café, one attached to a real café. Then it was a "a café café - you know where they serve food only. Then it was "a pillao restaurant, you know the stuff everyone wants for lunch," he says. His would-be partner projected 5-figure sales per day!
He said renovations would take three weeks and cost $30,000 stead they took six months and cost a 6-figure sum "in major currency".
The craftsmen had a new excuse everyday: rain; no power; malaria; mother-in-law died; timber not dry yet; all his nails stolen; accident, etc.
Then the partner ordered an immense amount of appliances, many of which were inappropriate. And then the partner left. He had already set up something else.
Vali was left to continue on his own. He went on to use his pillao recipe which left food unconvinced. One called him a Johnny-come-lately in pillao-making. Another said, "So what's all the fuss about fried rice?"
At the upstairs balcony, he reminisces: "Imagine this street in 1972. It was full of Asian cars. On Sundays we filled up City Square.
On Saturday mornings we ate meat pies at this very shop. That's why I took this location. But who wants to come to Kampala Road these days with all the riots? To the Asians and the Whites the town centre is something of a no-go zone, even in daytime.
So trying to sell the second-best samosas, the best pillao, is not going to work. We are bringing out our traditional food in style. Quality counts, and I'll give quality."
"Could there be another Asian expulsion?" I ask. "The parameters are simply not there," he says. "The economy is even more skewed, but the ethnic element is missing. Attitudes, too, have changed. However, 90% of the Asians here today are 'parachute Indians', recent arrivals.
They do not know the history of this place. One of the things to come out of the April riots was that their attitudes are resented and some of the skills they are filling are not too scarce". That, in his view, presents a problem.
Vali eats at the Serena now and again. He goes to the mosque on big occasions; they do pillao there. One Friday evening he ran out of petrol right at the Wandegeya junction when he thought he had had the car refilled the previous day. The jam was of "legendary proportions."
His wisened sister asked him "what do you mean you filled up the car? Did you check?" He is now more careful about petrol stations and avoids Wandegeya junction.
Does he miss living abroad? He does, not least because he is far away from family. Are the children here? His son, Arafat, is the deputy chief of the UN refugee agency in Beirut. His daughter, Mina Farah, is disabled and cared for by her mother in Vancouver.
Fortunately his siblings have visited him. And his younger sister, Gulzar and brother-in-law, Amin are here. They repossessed their Luweero farm. He also has a cousin and her husband at Music Centre, next door to Viva Café.
I am vaguely discomfited by the thought of an economist of his experience "merely" running a restaurant. Does he ever contemplate returning to writing more economics?
"My youngest sister came over from Vancouver over the holidays and brought me some of my papers. Sure there are books there to be finished. It would be fun to up-date Africa Misunderstood and my 1976 dissertation with the pieces I wrote afterwards."
A cook book on pillao and other specialties as well, perhaps? He laughs out loud. "No! Well, OK, in the preface of an economics book I certainly would say I tried to cook a 'different' pillao in Kampala in the late 2000s. I would roll out the recipe even. Inshallah!"
RAFIK ARAIN HAS GIVEN THE HOMLESS A HOME
Rafik Arain was a child when former President Idi Amin expelled his parents and relatives from Uganda in 1972.
Together with other Asians, they boarded a train wagon and headed off into the unknown.
Amin gave them 90 days to leave. Like Arain, most of them had been born in Uganda. They had no where to go.
"I remember everything.
Everybody was crying. They were shouting final good byes to each other. I think that was and still is the most sorry time of my life," Arain says.
Before the expulsion, the Arain family had links in Kampala, Masindi and other major towns of the country. They dealt in general merchandise.
The Arains no longer called themselves Asians, they were Ugandans because they had been born here. "We were Ugandans. We had our hearts in Uganda and many of us had never visited India or Pakistan," says Arain.
"We left with nothing," Arain says.
They somehow found their way into the UK, with no money.
"We arrived in the UK with just 27 pounds," Arain recalls.
They started rebuilding their lives from scratch. Like the character of Asians, the Arains set on investing their small money. Through years of toil, they set up a successful business in the UK.
The Arain family runs a successful business within the electrical industry in Rochdale, a town near Manchester.
Coming back to Uganda
When Amin was over thrown in 1979, the new government of Milton Obote decided that some of the Asian properties should be returned to its rightful owners.
However, the Arains did not return to Uganda until the early 90s, after the NRM government allowed any Asian who wanted to return to come back.
"We returned to Uganda in the 90s, and noticed that many children were suffering as a result of the HIV/AIDS scourge," Arain says. The family decided to give something back to the community. "We decided to use some of the company's profits to assist children in Uganda," he says.
The Orphanage
Rochdale Greenland School and orphanage is located at Kyebando off Kampala-Busunju Road. The orphanage is home to 23 orphans, most of them street children.
Together with several other friends, they decided to establish the orphanage in August 2002. Rochdale councillor Paul Rowen and a friend Terry Mason, together with Arain travelled to Uganda and witnessed the ground breaking ceremony for the construction of the Orphanage.
"The buildings are designed to accommodate a small family unit with each house mother responsible for about five children.
The complex has got 10 buildings. These include dormitories, classrooms, a dispensary and a recreational hall. We are trying to give as much space as possible to the children. This is why everything is spacious," Arain says.
There is also a farm on which various foods, like Bananas, cassava and maize are grown. There are also cows and chicken for milk and egg production for the children.
There is also a mosque where both the children and local community go for prayers.
When I visited the school, the children were dancing calypso, a currently famed dance stroke. Some of them were dressed in white T-shirts.
They smiled and waved to the visitors, obviously happy to have been given a new life. The children are aged between one to 10 years. Pointing to one of the children, Arain says: "We got that one just near Barclays Bank in the city centre. He had been abandoned because he is deaf and dumb."
He points at another boy, called Timothy. "He was a baby when he was brought to Rochdale. He is now a boy and growing further."
According to Arain, on realising that Timothy needed special attention during school, he was taken to a special needs school in Entebbe. "He comes here during the holidays," Arain says.
"It does not matter from which religion a child belongs. We welcome all children," Arain says, when asked the criteria used in admitting orphans.
The children play on swings and rollers in the Orphanage quadrangle. There are plans to construct a larger play ground for them.
Given the fact that most of them are street kids, their behaviour is sometimes erratic.
"Some of them are very stubborn. We have to cane them sometimes," says one of the house mothers.

Original_Search_Title: Journey Into the Herbal Kingdom
Headline: Uganda: Journey Into the Herbal Kingdom
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE plants growing wild around your home could be the cure for that ailment you have, as Titus Kakembo discovered recently on a visit to the Uganda Wildlife Education Centre
It is a sunny Sunday. Clouds of lake flies are all over the place. Our destination is Uganda Wildlife Education Centre (UWEC).
Melissa wants to see the monkeys and gorillas imitate human beings at play. They make her laugh as they swing and climb.
Laughter turns to sadness when they snatch her box of biscuits and then a lollipop. Before Melissa can recover from the shock, a baby python crosses our path.
The hairs on the back of the little one's neck stand on end. I tell her that is the price human beings have to pay for trespassing on their habitat. We agree to the quit reptiles and animals section in favour of a walk in the forest or indulge in a bit of plant tourism, if you like.
Little do I know that I am in for a big surprise. I soon discover that many of the potted plants that we keep on our verandas for ornamental purposes as well as some that grow wild in the compound are medicinal.
For instance, Kibisi (senecio hadiensis) is a common cure for ebinyo, a dental ailment in children. The leaves are pounded and rubbed against the gum or mixed with water and then drunk.
"Dad, what is he eating?" Melissa asks as she watches our guide, Richard Othieno chewing furiously. "If you want to pass PLE in grade one you first of all need to read very hard. You may then come here and eat this plant," he answered.
"It's called Ngabo ya kyalo (cactus) in Luganda. It bears a prickly pear, which is believed to treat paranoia. It has lots of vitamin C and A, which help improve one's memory. It is also recommended for diabetic and asthmatic patients."
We move on to ibwakulanta/Ishangala (plectranthus) is a popular love portion in Buganda and Ankole. Not only does it help win hearts or to strengthen troubled relationships, but is used in treating treats sexually transmitted infections and coughs.
"Smear it on the fingers and then hug or shake the hand of the object of your passion. The next thing you know is that person swearing their undying love for you," our guide says.
Not far away Othieno stops by a tree called Omusali in Buganda and Atenum in Teso. "Its biological name is Garcinia buchanasis. It treats tubal problems, asthma and helps halt excessive bleeding experienced in women. It is a slow-growing tree which grows up to 10-12 metres when fully mature," Othieno explains.
"Given as cocktail with Segamwenge, it treats inflammations of the urethra or imbalanced urine flow which is common among elderly men and women," Othieno continues.
Next we come across a grass called Citronella. It is used to repel household pests such as mosquitoes and cockroaches.
Our ancestors, it is said, had massage parlours where it was used to relax the tensed-up muscles.
Pointing at Katukumu, a small leafy plant, Othieno tells me of how Asians love it for they claim that it helps sharpen their mental capacity. It is also used in treating skin infections, swelling of tissue, headaches and in making herbal tea.
However, Othieno says people ought to know what a plant looks like as well as its natural environment, the chemical content before they can distinguish it from others of similar appearance.
"Dutchman's Pipe (Aristolochia elegans) was renamed Kabayekera because President Yoweri Museveni used it while he was waging the guerilla war that brought him to power.
Kabayekera is used to treat malaria, asthma, snake bites, diabetes, hypertension and effects placenta expulsion from the womb," Othieno says.
There was indeed a shortage of medicines as war raged in the Luweero triangle. Curiously, when telling the story of battles, people highlight the tale of the legendary 27 guns, the eating of cassava leaves when there was no food, but they forget the role of herbs.
The World Health Organisationestimates that four billion people, 80% of the world's population, uses herbal medicine for some aspect of primary healthcare. Herbal medicine remains a major component in medicine.
Most of the prescribed drugs contain at least one active ingredient derived from plant material. Some are made from plant extracts whereas others are synthesised to mimic a natural plant compound.
Tired and with a story to write home, Melissa is now in love with plants on account of their ability to boost her academic performance. As for me I can't help but marvel at the pharmacy on my doorstep.

Original_Search_Title: Diaspora Trade Strengthens Communities
Headline: West Africa: Diaspora Trade Strengthens Communities
Body_Text:
Paris — In the northern Paris neighbourhood of Chateau Rouge, a casual visitor could be forgiven for a moment for feeling that one had gotten off the city's storied metro and ended up in Dakar or Abidjan.
Here, percolating soukous tunes from the Congo pump out of storefronts such as that of Rythmes & Musiques while women attired in weaved damask robes and men in West Africa's traditionally colourful dashiki vest go about their business in one of the city's most vibrant open-air markets.
But, say observers, "foreign" as they may seem to some French, it is also, empathically, as much the face of modern France as any of the postcard images of the Eiffel Tower or the Arc de Triomphe that tourists usually associate with the country.
France, which once controlled vast swaths of West and Central Africa as part of a colonial empire, and still wields considerable influence in the region, has in recent years been confronted with a vivid re-imagining of itself and concepts of what it means to be French as immigrants from that region make the journey north to find a home here.
There are currently approximately 4.5 million legal immigrants living in France, with up to an additional half million undocumented migrants. An estimated two million immigrants in the country are from north or sub-Saharan Africa.
The increased immigration from Africa to European countries such as France should not be surprising, some observers say, noting both the contraction of such traditional African industries as agriculture, and what many see as biased trade deals with more powerful countries in Europe and North America.
When Africans come to France, they bring many of their tastes with them. The tuna industry in France, for example, is almost entirely drawn from Africa: particularly the Côte d'Ivoire, which provides France with some 35 percent of its overall canned tuna imports.
France also yearly imports an estimated 229,375 metric tonnes of cassava, a root vegetable common to many kinds of African cooking, much of which is imported from Togo and Madagascar.
The Institut National de la Statistique et des Études Économiques (INSEE), France's leading body for statistics and economics studies, found in a report published in August 2006 that immigration from the region is on the rise.
By mid-2004, the country had a population of immigrants from sub-Saharan Africa numbering some 570,000, a 45 percent increase from the numbers found in a similar study in 1999, according to INSEE.
And along with this increase in immigration, the demand for the comforts from home like food, music and hair products will continue to increase.
The population from sub-Saharan Africa is also one of the youngest, the study finds, as a mere 4 percent of those arriving from that region are older than 65 years of age. That's compared to 54 percent of those over 65 immigrating to France from Italy, for example.
And it is in neighbourhoods such as Chateau Rouge where many West African immigrants, if not necessarily making their homes here, at least come together for a sense of community in what can sometimes feel like a forbidding, unknown place. Ever-steeper Paris rents have pushed more and more new arrivals out to suburbs beyond the Paris outskirts.
"We have not found things very easy," says Victor Osouno, a 36-year-old barber from Ghana who has been living and working in France for the past 11 years. As he speaks he is skillfully running an electric buzzer over the head of a customer, and his large hair salon is populated by men and women waiting to get their hair done.
Indeed, at nearby shops such as the Maison d'Afrique, which do a brisk business selling customers such traditional African delicacies as eggplants, yams and cassava, the tastes of home seem to hold a strong attraction.
"This is classic migration, there is a concentration of the population in areas where people from the same country already are," says Jean-Pierre Garson, head of the Non-Member Economies and International Migration Division of the Paris branch of the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD), a grouping of 30 rich nations. "When you arrive you try to connect with people you know who have come before."
The OECD itself, in its recent Economic Survey of France 2000 said that "the geographic concentration of poverty fosters its persistence, as people living in overwhelmingly poor areas lack access to social networks that might help them to integrate into the economy. The problems in question are made more complex by the fact that people of immigrant origin are over-represented in these areas."
"The community sticks together," Osouno says. "Someone goes to an African barber to get their haircut, goes to the food shops that have African food, not out of some kind of racism, but because the things they want they can easily find among the community."

Original_Search_Title: Art Inspired By Personal Tragedy
Headline: Uganda: Art Inspired By Personal Tragedy
Body_Text:
Kampala — HE does not have enough money to put up an art exhibition but his work heralds a budding talent.
Edward Ojara is a 20-year-old student who completed S.6 last year. He is a self-taught artist whose art has been largely inspired by his misfortunes.
"I lost my parents. This forced me to work hard to support myself through school. Since S.4, I have been selling my art to teachers and villagers to raise pocket money and school fees," he says.
With his meagre income of about sh30,000, he is able to contribute to his family's domestic needs.
Ojara's work carries with it a rare sense of maturity, mainly portraying human forms and wild animals. He adds value to his work by turning some of the sculptures into water jars and flower vases.
This ability to make his work practical is commendable.
He employs rudimentary methods and improvised material to finish his work. His main medium is clay, iron bars and unrefined wood.
Sometimes, he accentuates his work with dyes, cassava flour, timber husks and cement bags. Thus, his work often lacks fine finish and detail. This does not, however, rob his work of creativity and purpose.
Ojara came close to recognition last year when he moulded a clay sculpture of President Museveni.
"I wanted to thank him for initiating free education, which I benefited from," he says.
When he reached Boma Ground where the President was addressing people, the security was tight. By the time he reached the presidential podium, Museveni had left.
Ojara is now eagerly waiting to join university, hoping this will further his career.

Original_Search_Title: Government Approves Capanda Industrial Growth Point Development Plan
Headline: Angola: Government Approves Capanda Industrial Growth Point Development Plan
Body_Text:
Luanda — Government approves food self-sufficiency promotion plan and surplus for exports
Angolan Government Wednesday in Luanda approved the Development Plan for the Capanda Agro-industrial Growthpoint, in northern Malanje province, intended to promote food self-sufficiency and create surplus for export.
The plan has a public investment of USD 300 million, plus a private investment of USD 600 million, to be implemented over a period of eight years, with a production estimated at three million tons, according to the Agriculture minister, Pedro Canga, at the end of the sixth ordinary session of the Cabinet Council.
Pedro Canga said that the Capanda Agro-industrial Growthpoint reserves a development area for the productions of rural communities and foresees the creation of 63,000 jobs and annual revenues of over USD 500 million.
The project aims at the setting up of soya and cassava transformation industries, alcohol and sugar production, units for drying and storing grains, states a communiqué from the 6th ordinary session of the Cabinet.
To be executed in an area of 411,000 hectares, the plan will be carried out in three phases of a three-year period each. It will be managed by the Capanda Development Society, an entity whose establishment as an anonimous society with public funds was authorised by Cabinet Council.
Provincial governors attended the 6th session of the Cabinet Council.

Original_Search_Title: Split Hurts Mpigi District Revenue
Headline: Uganda: Split Hurts Mpigi District Revenue
Body_Text:
Kampala — MONITORING activities in a small area may seem simple, but this is not the case for Mpigi district, from which Wakiso was curved out five years ago. The district is faced with innumerable problems.
Local leaders find it difficult to run their activities after losing most of their revenue to Wakiso district. Mpigi is made up of Mawokota, Butambala and Gomba counties, with a population of 42,000 people scattered over its 16 sub-counties.
This financial year, the district estimates to collect sh19b. Out of this, sh900m is expected to come from the local revenue sources, while the balance of sh18b is expected from the central government and donors.
Some of the local revenue sources will come from dues on taxi parks, markets, landing sites, trading licences and boda boda transporters.
The chief administrative officer, Henry Makumbi, says the district revenue base has shrunk terribly. The abolition of graduated tax affected revenue collection yet the Government funding, he says, is not enough to finance the district activities.
"We used to collect over sh600m in graduated tax, but now we get only sh350m as graduated tax compensation from the Government," Makumbi says.
"The Government funding is little and conditional. We cannot channel it to our priority programmes," he adds.
Out of the sh18b expected from the Government, sh15b is for salaries, while the rest is allocated to the central government programmes like roads, education, agriculture and water.
"The Government should loosen the strings attached to the grants to enable the districts plan better," Makumbi says.
The vice-chairman, Frank Kawooya, says the splitting of the district led to loss of revenue. Three of their major markets at Kawempe, Kira and Zzana, were taken over by Wakiso.
"We had invested a lot of money in these markets, but we ended up losing them," he says. Apart from one market at Gomba, all the others are on private land.
"We cannot make permanent developments on private land. If funds were available, we would just buy them," Kawooya says.
According to Kawooya, landing sites, are another source of revenue, but the district does not own any.
The revenue from the markets and landing sites is shared between landlords, sub-counties and the district headquarters.
Unlike Kampala, whose budget can be supplemented by the property tax, local leaders in Mpigi are confused on how to implement the tax.
"People in trading centres sleep in the same structures they use for business. It is, therefore, not easy to levy property tax on such structures," says Kawooya.
The Government says that property tax can only be levied on commercial buildings.
Juuko Kasiita, the resident district commissioner, says the proximity of the district to Kampala has increased rural-urban migration, affecting agriculture and other developments.
However, despite these problems, the district chairman, Badru Kabega Mukalazi, says they have made some achievements.
He says 45.2% of the district has access to clean water and Mpigi is one of the six districts recognised in the promotion of health.
"There is at least one health educator in each village and we have a health unit in each parish," Mukalazi says.
He says the district provides farmers with seeds, especially the upland rice, cassava stems and potatoes, to increase food security.
The district also encourage residents to form co-operatives to benefit from Bonna Bagaggawale (prosperity for all) programme.
However, Mukalazi says the district still face challenges of inadequate planning, poor sensitisation of residents on population management, unemployment, inability of the local leaders to supervise their technical staff and to lobby investors.

Original_Search_Title: Chimaroke and the Burden of History (2)
Headline: Nigeria: Chimaroke and the Burden of History (2)
Body_Text:
Enugu — THE 'Loma Linda Housing Estate', with 324 flat apartments is clearly a phantom. Not a single flat has been delivered at the site to date. Interestingly as far back as September 2006, Chimaroke's propaganda machine had generated and was circulating simulated compugraphic visuals of the complex, awaiting commissioning. Any journalist, intent on independent investigation instead of the all-expenses- paid round trip that Chimaroke made popular, will find, as of a fact, that the Loma Linda site has largely not left the foundation stages. Indeed, a first time visitor to the Coal City will find it rather difficult to come to terms with the reality that, even as a former regional capital, Enugu can hardly boast of any form of public street lighting. At nightfall, the Coal City resembles the epicentre of darkness, the overpowering pall of a ghost capital submerged in a thick fog of insecurity and uncertainty. This is a city sentenced to darkness by the civilizing mission of a brain-drain democrat.
We can spend all the time in this world talking about Chimaroke's unique brand of "democracy-dividends", it must be emphasized however that the cornerstone of democracy is good governance and popular participation. Good governance consists in transparency, accountability, due process, respect for human rights, observance of the rule of law as well as the continuous expansion of the democratic space.
These ingredients which are catalysts of development and popular empowerment were regrettably in short supply under Chimaroke. The budgetary process was characterized by an opaque public expenditure management profile. Public works contracts in addition to the allocation of scarce values such as lands were not subjected to any form of competitive, open tendering process or due process mechanism. Only one firm undertook all the major public procurements and contracts in excess of N30 billion between 2003 - 2007.
The contract regime bore traces of an incestuous business relationship and could hardly stand up to scrupulous benchmarks of transparency. To cap it all, Chimaroke engaged in habitual abuse of his Oath of Office, by actively promoting nepotism, and clan hegemony in public appointments, contrary to the provisions and spirit of the 1999 constitution on the imperatives of federal character.
Good governance consists as well in the ability of a leader to cast a vision and to sow the seeds of long term socio-economic transformation of society. This was, sadly, again, absent in Enugu State under Chimaroke's charge. Although Enugu is an agrarian state, the former governor failed to come up with a single agricultural initiative in eight years to either harness the fertile land mass available to the state, or particularly to tap its potentials in the areas of rice and cassava cultivation. The state's overall tourism potential ratings, were left untapped and unharnessed. To further underline the impaired vision at work, the widely acclaimed Enugu Zoological Gardens, which used to sit on 48 hectares of land in the GRA, was in year 2000 shut and all the wildlife liquidated to make way for yet another of Chimaroke's numerous private estates. A similar fate befell the famous Enugu market garden, the sprawling agricultural seedling and horticultural nursery centre, which played a part in Malaysia's emergence as a major exporter of palm products.
Chimaroke was far too busy chasing after intellectual acclaim to ponder on the long-term economic prospects of Enugu State in the face of the inevitability of resource control? What is worse, Enugu State witnessed massive de-industrialization during the Chimaroke years. The few state-owned business establishments that could have constituted a critical base for the future industrial and technological take-off of the state's economy were, through neglect, mismanagement and sustained assets-stripping, allowed to go under and rot away. The roll call includes, but is not limited to:
Hotel Presidential, Enugu; Phoenix Hotels, Enugu; Ikenga Hotels, Nsukka; Ogbede Aluminum Company; Nigergas, Enugu,; ENTRACO; Sunrise Flour Mills; Nigersteel, Emene; Oghe Cashew Industry; NCFC; Universal Insurance and AVOP Vegetable Plant, Nachi (which was cannibalized and burnt down in suspicious circumstances in January 2006, while the state government turned a blind eye. Chimaroke did not deem it worthwhile to set up a Commission of Inquiry into the cause of the mystery inferno or to ascertain the extent of damage caused). This is to say nothing about Chimaroke's numerous abandoned projects like the Golf Course Estate, Harmony Estate, Ebeano Bye-pass, International Market at Ninth Mile and New Haven Shopping Complex .
The sustained impoverishment of the state and the ruination of its commonwealth contrasted markedly with the blossoming of the business conglomerate which he built up within the same period. It is difficult to place a value on this burgeoning private concern, Ebeano Plc. Suffice it to say that its asset base probably runs into several tens of billions. The catch here is that for every single public project that Chimaroke embarked upon during his tenure, he had executed two or three others for himself or in the name of his family estate. The custodian of all the loot is none other than Sister-Excellency, who, prior to September 1999, was a struggling housewife in America. To put it mildly, Chimaroke's thirst for propaganda and mass deception is matched only by his insatiable appetite for primitive accumulation. Enugu was working to the extent that it was transformed into the ruler's fiefdom.
It was little surprising therefore that all those who routinely scored Chimaroke high on overall performance in office were, for the most part, outsiders and total strangers to Enugu State. The list included ex-President Olusegun Obasanjo, and the likes of Chief Tony Anenih, Col. Ahmadu Alli, Bode George, in addition to the 13 European Union Ambassadors in Nigeria who allowed themselves to be viscerally manipulated by a practiced demagogue, out to exploit to the fullest the sheer photo-opportunity of their presence in September 2005.
It is instructive to recall that on the occasion of the 14th Anniversary of the creation of Enugu State on 27th August, 2005, the Enugu State Development Association (ESDA), the body that fought for the creation of the State, issued a communique endorsed by Igwe Charles Abangwu and Chief Enechi Onyia (SAN), in which it bemoaned the twilight of good governance under Chimaroke. The association scored the administration (at the time) below 40% on all indices except one: Propaganda and public relations where Chimaroke registered 99% (see Vanguard, Saturday, 27th August, 2005, p.32). How apt for an impoverished state that had by far the biggest public relations, media and propaganda budget out of the thirty-six states of the federation in all of the past eight years.
In the light of what has become common knowledge, Chimaroke's stewardship raises a number of fundamental questions about the future of the Nigerian polity. These questions touch on the integrity or lack of it as well as the sense of judgment of a number of principal actors in the Chimaroke saga.
Mr. Mbah, a social critic, writes from Enugu.

Original_Search_Title: Reaping From Intensive Farming
Headline: Uganda: Reaping From Intensive Farming
Body_Text:
Kampala — When we arrived at St Jude Family Projects at Busense, Kabonera sub-county, in Masaka District on June 2, 2007, the proprietor, Ms Josephine Kizza, had just returned from Brazil where she had spent almost a month addressing farmers and carrying out demonstrations in different parts of that country about organic farming.
She was resting after the long journey and it was Mr Juma Byarugaba, one of about 20 twenty farm hands who conducted us around the farm. We had in our party, Dr. Fred Ssewamala, professor of Social Work and Social Development at the University of Columbia, New York, Mr Vincent Ssemakula Ssettuba, the chairman of Rakai District and Ms Proscovia Nabunya, Programme Coordinator, SUUBI, an NGO that supports orphans in Rakai District. We also had two catholic priests, Father Paul Kafeero and Father Joseph Kato Bakulu of Matale Parish, where SUUBI has its headquarters.
Dr. Ssewamala mobilises funds for SUUBI in the USA and was back in Uganda with plans to expand the NGO's support to the orphans and had just met Ssemakula to concretise the plans. Ssemakula had suggested to Dr. Ssewamala that all the orphans under SUUBI's care should visit St Jude Family Projects as part of their training in self-employment skills.
Hence our trip to the farm! President Museveni has visited the famous 3.7 acre small-scale farm a number of times and has often publicly said that Ms Kizza earns a minimum of Shs50m from the farm every month. And our trip to the farm indeed left not the slightest doubt in our minds about the accuracy of the president's estimation. At the launch of Bonnabagaggawale on March 14, 2007, he took the entire cabinet to the farm.
The following day, all sub-county chiefs and commercial officers in every sub-county in Uganda paid a visit to the farm as a top-up of their one-month-training at Kyankwanzi in how to implement the Bonnabagaggawale programme. The president believes that other small-scale farmers have a lot to learn from Ms Kizza.
Plenty to learn
The farm has since the 90s been the destination of farmers seeking to make good money and to improve their livelihood out of small-scale farming. It is also the place to go to learn about environmental protection, rain water harvesting, fruit drying, organic farming, bee keeping, bark yard farming, irrigation, soil conservation, fish farming, and a whole range of other farming activities.
Today, it doubles as a training institution and a farm. Some farmers or extension workers who choose to spend a few days or whole weeks at the farm, learning about particular farming skills are provided with accommodation and meals at the farm at a fee.
Short time visitors that want to be taken around the various projects at the farm are each charged Shs5,000. Donor agencies and NGOs keep taking groups of farmers and students from as far as Tanzania and Kenya to St Jude Family Projects to learn about organic farming, while several others come from different districts in Uganda.
St Jude Family Projects comprises of 17 different money-generating projects, all on 3.7 acres of land, as Byarugaba disclosed at the start of our visit. The projects are divided into two categories, animal husbandry and crop husbandry. "But the most valuable commodity on this farm is the animals' and birds' droppings," Byarugaba said.
"Not the milk, or the eggs or the crop products that we sell. The droppings of animals and birds are its strength. It is from their droppings that we get the biogas, which we use to cook; it is the droppings we use to feed our fish, to make composite manure, and to fertilise the soil for effective crop growing."
Putting everything to use
There is so much to learn from St Jude Family Projects. The slurry from the biogas tank is directed into trenches that lead it to the various crop gardens on the farm where it serves as manure.
The farmer puts nothing to waste. Cow dung not only serves as a source of biogas or manure, but it may also be used to feed chicken. Yet, chicken droppings when treated may be used to feed cattle. Cow's urine is very good manure but it may also be turned into a natural pesticide. Cassava flour can be used to improvise for cement when the farmer wants to construct a simple house to store such items as seeds or small-scale farm implements.
Rainwater from the compound is collected and stored in a huge underground tank cheaply made out of polythene. The farmer may use the water to irrigate his crops or to feed his animals during the dry season.
A farmer who has easy access to water during the dry season is in position to sell his products at a higher price since he will be selling during time of scarcity. Also at St Jude Family Projects, the farmer learns about drip irrigation - a method of irrigation that allows slow oozing of water from containers to vegetable gardens. The farmer also learns about the preparation of 'plant tea' and liquid manure, which are both forms of irrigation as well as means of soil enrichment.
There are six exotic cows and about as many calves under zero grazing on the farm. There are some exotic diary goats and pigs and both local and exotic chicken. Farmers in the neighbourhood are encouraged to bring their local animals to the farm to mate with the exotic breeds.
The pigs on the farm tend to grow to monstrous sizes and some are said to weigh as much as 200 kilogrammes. The animals' droppings are used to make composite manure for the crops. St Jude Family Projects has a nursery for grafted fruit tree cuttings. One can get fast growing fruit trees such as mangoes, oranges, lemons, avocado, and passion fruit creepers.
The farm is planned in such a way that almost any tree or plant growing on it has a bearing to farming. The trees are either soil enriching or fodder for animals. There are such trees as calliandra calothyrsus, grevillea robusta and grasses such as elephant grass, Guatemala, Tanzania grass, and lab, lab. Mangoes and mutuba trees are also grown and their leaves are good fodder for goats apart from providing shade.
Others are just fruit trees. Some trees such as the neem tree are useful for repelling mosquitoes and other harmful insects. The trees and grasses are mostly planted along soil erosion control bunds and water conduction trenches. By continuously growing the trees, the farmer is never without wood for fuel.
Visitors to St Jude Family Projects also learn about fuel saving stoves and fireplaces. "All people who own cows have no excuse not to use biogas," said our guide, Byarugaba. On our way to the fishpond in the valley, we saw about 20 beehives from which the farmer harvests honey for export at about Shs9,000 per kilo.
In the pond, Kizza rears fish known in Luganda as male. How large they grow depends on how long she keeps them in the pond. Recently, Byarugaba said, it was decided that harvesting takes place after eight months. The buyers of her fish are large hotels and restaurants in Kampala at Shs1,500 per kilo. Cow dung, maize flour and rotting vegetables are good food for fish in the pond.
And how does she fight snakes and birds that prey on fish? Boiled eggs are placed along the pond's boundaries and when a snake swallows the egg, it cannot digest it and so it dies. Polythene sheets are also placed around the pond and most snakes are known to abhor polythene. Scarecrows such as damaged tapes are tied across the pond and they frighten birds as the wind blows, shaking the tapes and causing a peculiar sound.
No visitor to St Jude Family Projects should miss visiting the fruit drying section. There, one will learn about the difference it makes between selling jackfruit at Shs1,500 to buyers and taking it to Nakasero Market in Kampala, and drying it and exporting it to earn as much as Shs40,000. St Jude Family Projects exports dried sweet bananas, gonja, pineapples, jackfruit, tomatoes and mangoes to Europe.
So how did the two former school teachers, Kizza and her late husband Mr Kizza Aliddeki who had no prior training in agriculture at all, rise up to this level of farming? "Well, we were interested," said Kizza standing in the compound, in front of her beautiful grey-roofed mansion. She was an infant class teacher at Nkoni Primary School while her husband who held a diploma in education from Kyambogo NTC was a teacher of commerce and accounts at Lubiri Secondary School.
They once grew a large field of cassava but after failing to get a buyer for the crop, they decided to rear two pigs to consume the cassava. "From then onwards, we would attend farmers' seminars and visit other farmers, copying what we deemed best for us. Later, we went into cattle zero grazing, resigned from teaching, and became full time farmers." With time, they noticed they were attracting several visitors who wanted to learn from them. So it made sense to charge a fee to compensate for the time they spent taking the visitors around the different projects on the farm.
In the early 90s, an English visitor to the farm helped Kizza take up a year's training in Britain in organic farming. A few years later, her husband did a similar course in Denmark. And now what they built is something other farmers can learn from.

Original_Search_Title: Tobacco, a Favoured Yet Costly Crop
Headline: Uganda: Tobacco, a Favoured Yet Costly Crop
Body_Text:
Kampala — Although tobacco is widely grown and generate a high revenue in West Nile, poverty continues to hound many communities mainly because cultivation of the crop requires many inputs and doesn't do much to ensure food security
West Nile is perhaps one of Uganda's best kept secrets. Hidden away in the fresh green folds of the River Nile tributaries, the region made up of Pakwach, Arua, Koboko, Yumbe, Nebbi, Adjumani, Maracha, Ayivu, Vurra and Madi Okollo has some of that grasping beauty that makes you ask - where has this been?
The region is perhaps most popular for its tobacco growing culture and its warm people. However, its best asset - tobacco, has been its worst enemy, eating away the region's previously endowed natural resources, leaving the people spell bound in poverty and backwardness.
The thick natural vegetation that partially covered Murchison Falls national park down to the plains in Pakwach and Arua has slowly been destroyed as a desperate population searches for wood fuel to cure tobacco and burn charcoal for sell to earn a living. Driving through Arua, Maracha, Koboko, Yumbe, Terego and Moyo, the only sign of modern times are the towering tobacco curing burns that stand out in homesteads surrounded by hundreds of grass-thatched mud huts.
At one point on the trip, we had to ask what the people of West Nile eat, since the only gardens that were fresh and well cultivated were hundreds of acres of tobacco. According to the Member of Parliament for Terego in Nyadri district, Kasiano Wadri, West Nile Region alone produced 10 million kilogrammes of tobacco.
A kilo of high grade cured tobacco costs Shs1,800 according to the locals. The average price for tobacco is Shs1,500, which means for a good price, the area fetched Shs18b last year alone and at a throw away price, it must have walked away with Shs15b because all the tobacco was bought.
This income however is not reflected in the way of life here. So where did the money from the tobacco go? "The cultivation of tobacco is labour intensive and destructive to the environment as it requires wood fuel for its curing but we have no alternative," Wadri said.
Farmers harvest tobacco. To fight household poverty and promote food security, farmers are advised to grow other crops like rice and cassava and rear animals rather than depend solely on tobacco growing. Net photo
Recently, the vice president, Professor Gilbert Bukenya was in West Nile to mobilise and sensitise people about the prosperity for all programme. Shocked by the levels of poverty in the region, he urged farmers to diversify their crops for both income earning and food security.
What farmers should do
"I don't believe poor people owning small acreages of land should grow tobacco. This crop must be left for large-scale farmers who can afford to pay loans. This is not a poor man's crop. How can they say they earn so much money and yet there is so much poverty around?" he asked. Bukenya wants poor households and small landholder farmers to grow fast-maturing crops that can be sold and also used as a food resource.
"Rice grows very well in this region. I have been told by some farmers that even after they harvest the tobacco, they can grow rice immediately after and be able to get some food to eat. This region is also known for the best organic honey in the world. Why can't we support our people to harvest honey and grow these crops?"
But the Member of Parliament for Maracha in Nyadri district Alex Onzima says since tobacco is the main cash crop in the region, the government should intervene and subsidise the farmers. "It is the most expensive crop to grow and it's labour intensive. People have gone to school here because of tobacco but they also incur many loans. In the end, they get peanuts. In Zimbabwe, farmers benefit more.
The government here should subsidise farmers if they are to benefit from this crop," Onzima said. West Nile has some of the best honey with flavours from the numerous moringa trees. Its proximity to the vast water resource from the River Nile makes it a viable project to explore.
However, because of the destruction of the natural vegetation in search of firewood, most families that rear wild bees have lately been frustrated by strange small dog-like animals called bee bargers. The animals which used to survive on wild bees in the natural forests have now turned to domestic hives as most of their habitat has been destroyed.
"We used to harvest honey three times a year and would get 60 kilos of honey per hive. We sell each kilo at Shs6,000. However, our hives have been invaded by the bee bargers that eat both the honey and the bees," Ms Phoebe Awere of Aluyi sub-county in Nebbi district said. Awere who is an established bee keeper however, didn't have bees in most of her hives, something she attributes to lack of access to water.
While Bukenya's push for alternative cost effective crops for both income and food security has been highly embraced in the West Nile Region, access to high quality seeds and the Friesian cows remains a challenge.
Few options
Asraf Noha, 32, of Lodora village in Lodora sub-county, Koboko district says he owns two acres of land and has been growing tobacco for the last three years. "Here, we all grow tobacco, they give us loans to buy the seeds and we don't have to look for the market or even transport the tobacco. Tobacco firms always come for it, so we don't spend much," he says.
However, Noha spends much more than he cares to add up. In three years, he has only managed to buy one traditional cow. When he heard about the Friesian cow, he was crestfallen.
"It is true, the local cow does not give milk as much as the exotic ones. But exotic cows are very expensive. I can't afford that even if I desperately want it. Most of the money I get, I spend on fertilisers, spraying insecticides and maintaining the barn. The problem is we don't have access to other crops that we can make money from. Cassava is a recent crop and it's grown mainly because of the market in Southern Sudan. The rice seeds are not available, the only crop with a ready market is tobacco," he said.
The vice president's theory was simple. Tobacco is labour intensive and takes nine months to harvest. Rice takes three months and can be eaten and sold at the same time. Bees come as naturally as the weather allows.
"All you need is a hive that you hang up in a tree to trap the bees. You can have three activities on a two-acre piece of land. Rear chicken for eggs, or bees that don't even take space, have a fish pond, keep a Friesian cow or grow a quick maturing crop like rice," he said. According to him, incomes from any two of these activities can be saved in a microfinance association. If the farmers are in a group, this money can then help them access credit to improve their production or acquire a Friesian cow.
He too was concerned about the massive deforestation for charcoal burning which could degrade the environmental. Already, changing weather patterns have led to serious water scarcity for food production.
Local leaders say the harsh weather has led to long droughts and forced people to resort to charcoal burning to earn a living, destroying part of the natural vegetation of Murchison Falls National Park in the process.
According to the Save the Children UK report, chronic poverty is deeply entrenched with approximately 50 percent of the population having an income in the range of Shs500,000 a year. "To put this in context, if you live on $1 a day as a minimum required per person to be above the poverty line, 50 percent of the households in this zone comprising 6-8 persons live on less than a dollar a day.
Even after monetising all the food consumed from own cultivation (approximately Shs400,000), the entire household still has to live on about $1.5 per day (about Shs2,500). The possibilities of getting out of these conditions are constrained by the circle of poverty brought about by the precarious nature of their livelihoods," it said.
Bukenya who is promoting the fight against household poverty through improving basic incomes and food security, says Uganda is naturally gifted and people only need the correct information to enable them make the right decisions.

Original_Search_Title: Lunda-Norte - Government Grants USD 400,000 in Micro-Credits
Headline: Angola: Lunda-Norte - Government Grants USD 400,000 in Micro-Credits
Body_Text:
Luanda — About 408,183 dollars were conceded to farmers, nurses and teachers of the north-east Lunda Norte province, from January to June 2007, in the light of the programme of micro-credit and credit for consumptions of the Angolan Government, through the Savings and Credit Bank (BPC).
According to the manager of BPC in Dundo city, Adalberto Faria, 61 teachers, 59 nurses and a farmers' association made up by 19 groups, benefited from the credits.
Credit is granted to civil servants who receive salaries from BPC and later they suffer monthly discounts of 30 percent for a three-year period.
The director stated that BPC is also granting social credits to the whole civil services, what has been enabling the beneficiaries to purchase means of transport and repair or buy their residences in the urban area of the city.
The fixed amount does not surpass USD 2,500, considering that they are civil servants.
To the provincial director of agriculture of Lunda Norte, Domingos Mufungueno, the micro-credit project has already benefited 463 farmers since it was implemented in 2005, what has solved this sector's problems concerning the acquisition of agricultural inputs.
"This number corresponds to three associations that produce rice, cassava, maize and peanuts", he referred.
The micro-credit programme was launched in 2005 after a protocol signed by the Finance Ministry, BPC, Federation of Farmers Associations, Sol Bank and the Agrarian Development Institute to mainly help farmers, teachers and nurses in improving their social living conditions.

Original_Search_Title: More Must Be Done to Curb Pollution
Headline: Zimbabwe: More Must Be Done to Curb Pollution
Body_Text:
Harare — MABVUKU and Tafara residents have suffered quietly from dust emissions from the Circle Cement plant nearby for many decades with stakeholders oblivious to their pain and anguish.
Dust emissions from the Manresa plant coat vegetables, trees, grass, flowers, homes and clothes on the drying line with a thick layer of white cement dust.
On bad days, the air is heavy and living close to the plant is a nightmare.
"We have lived like this (air polluted environment) since the 1960s till now," said Ambuya Akilesi Diamond, from Old Mabvuku. People suffer from various respiratory ailments -- coughs and flu, some asthma. We are dying quietly here and I don't know what the Government and Circle Cement are doing to end our suffering," she said.
She is quite bitter that no one has shown concern over the health risks posed by the plant. She is not alone and many people in the Mabvuku and Tafara suburbs are frustrated that nothing has changed despite pledges by the company to curb air pollution.
"Pollution is still a big problem. People are being affected although there are no detailed studies that have been made in the area," said Stewart Sakarombe, of the community-based Environmental Group.
"Windows are always closed in most homes closer to the plant. Disease prevalence is unknown. We don't even know the chemical composition of the dust. We don't know how it affects people, animals and the environment. It's difficult to link the pollution to any particular disease affecting people in the area," he said. "There are no records but certainly many people complain about the dust emissions."
Circle Cement has, since 1954 when the limestone quarry was opened, made rich pickings at the expense of the local community.
In January last year, environmental experts said pollution was becoming a major threat to Zimbabwe, heightening the prospects of acid rain and respiratory illnesses.
World Health Organisation experts at a meeting with their counterparts from the Air Pollution Information Network Africa (Apina) warned that air in Harare had a heavy concentration of sulphur dioxide and nitrogen which were by-products of the burning of petrol, diesel and coal. Acceptable levels of sulphur dioxide and nitrogen dioxide are pegged at 50 micro grammes per cubic metre and 40 ug/m3 respectively but the concentration of these pollutants in the capital were estimated to be in excess of 73,5 ug/m3 and 55 ug/m3.
Environmental experts said the rising number of vehicles on Zimbabwe's roads, growing demand for energy, industrial processes, mining and mineral processing, agricultural and waste management were the major sources of air pollution.
They said thermal power stations emitted large amounts of sulphur dioxide while the industrial sector produced large quantities of methane, nitrogen oxides and carbon dioxide.
"There is need for combined efforts to curb pollution which has unabatedly increased," Prof. Sarah Feresu, director of the Institute of Environmental Studies at the University of Zimbabwe, was quoted as saying.
"Pollution is now an inescapable feature with long-term effects on human lives and the environment. The most visible impacts of increased air pollution are damage to vegetation, corrosion of buildings and materials and mortality due to exacerbation of respiratory illnesses," the environmentalist said.
Prof Feresu said air pollution led to numerous chronic health problems such as lung disease, cancer and genetic disorders.
"Scientific investigations carried out in urban and peri-urban centres showed that increasing amounts of potentially toxic particulate and gaseous pollutants were man-made and could be mitigated," she said. Prof. Feresu urged the industrial sector to install air pollution reducing equipment which included scrubbers, filter bags and electrostatic precipitators to trap emissions and scrub gaseous pollutants.
She bemoaned the fact that Zimbabwe did not have a systematic national air pollution monitoring mechanism apart from random pollution spot checks.
The city's Air Pollution Control Unit under the City Health Department carries out routine air pollution monitoring on eight sites, investigates complaints and controls open burning.
Lack of financial resources, equipment and skilled manpower has hampered efforts to carry out the monitoring activities.
Zimbabwe's cement industry produces more than 1,3 million tonnes of cement a year and is partly responsible for imbalances in the environment in addition to producing air pollution hazards.
However, environmental experts say in comparison with gaseous air pollutants, many of which are readily recognised as being the cause of injury to various types of vegetation, relatively little is known and limited studies have been carried out on the effects of cement dust pollution on the growth of plants. They say the cement manufacturing process is one of the chief sources of mercury in the environment.
"Very few studies have been done in Mabvuku and Tafara to study the composition of cement dust pollution," said a Harare-based environmentalist. "Studies elsewhere show that the accumulation levels of mercury in selected plant species around a cement factory is a possibility."
Mercury is a common toxic trace metal pollutant, bio-accumulation of mercury in plants and its entry into the food chain that results in long-term health hazards, is of major concern, experts say.
They say mercury has done considerable harm to the "adulteration" of the atmosphere. Residents in fields nearby the Manresa cement works, grow maize, sweet potatoes, cassava, vegetables, sugar cane and bananas along streams.
Fears abound that pollutants from the cement dust pollution could enter the food chain affecting the health of the residents.
But what has the company done to mitigate cement dust pollution?
Circle Cement managing director Isiah Bingwa said his company had invested US$10 million in the plant's dust control programme.
"Prior to the installation of a precipitator (dust collection equipment) cement dust pollution was a serious problem. Since we installed the machine which we imported from Germany there has been a massive change now," he said.
"On a normal operating day (without power disruptions) we are on average competing very well with European standards. Zimbabwean cement dust emissions are pegged at 120mg/cm3 and for Europe 50mg/cm3. We are around 80mg/cm3," Bingwa said.
He urged the residents of Mabvuku and Tafara to understand that Circle Cement had taken practical steps to mitigate air pollution in the area and that if power supply is consistent they should not expect any problems. "As long as we have consistent power supplies, we have no problems with emissions," Bingwa said.
During a tour of the plant to launch the Green Ribbon environmental week recently, Environment and Tourism Minister Francis Nhema said there was a marked reduction in cement dust emission compared to 2001 when he visited the plant.
"I commend Circle Cement for taking the initiative to reduce pollution by installing various systems that we witnessed during the tour with the aim of limiting emissions into the air during the manufacturing of their products," Nhema said.
"Every industry should ensure that they make such deliberate efforts to limit, mitigate or where possible, eliminate emission from any activities that influence global warming."
The Green Ribbon Week is an annual awareness campaign for the environment in Zimbabwe spearheaded by Environment Africa.
It coincides with World Environment Day which is commemorated every year on June 5.
"I asked the MD (Bingwa) whether there is an alternative to cement. For how long are we going to keep on mining? We have to look for alternative cement. As we increase in numbers we will not be able to sustain our development," Nhema said.
"We have to put our minds on this. Let's think about it."
Circle Cement has contributed immensely to the growth of the Zimbabwean economy through its infrastructure, roads and revenue generation.
It has been an important contributor to the construction of the Cahora Bassa Dam in Mozambique and to other economies in the region.
But all this has not been without social cost. The workers and residents in Mabvuku and Tafara who have been exposed to air pollution over the years have borne the brunt of the cement manufacturing process.
Circle Cement has to invest back into the community as well as rehabilitate the environment that has been scarred by its mining operations.
Its tree planting programmes, support for environmental programmes as well community welfare programmes are a step in the right direction.
The environment is everyone's responsibility.

Original_Search_Title: Boy, 5, Found Dead At Cassava Farm
Headline: Sierra Leone: Boy, 5, Found Dead At Cassava Farm
Body_Text:
Freetown — A five-year-old boy, Mohamed Sheriff, is reported to have been found dead at a cassava farm in Baraka village in Dama chiefdom, Kenema district.
PC 10322 Kamara BJP of the Police Media Unit in Kenema says the deceased was reported missing last Friday and was found dead on Sunday at 2pm.
He said a team of police personnel were dispatched to the scene and the deceased was conveyed to the government where he awaits transfer to Freetown for postmortem. Police sources also disclosed that the body was found decomposed.
The parents of the deceased are assisting police with investigations.
Five suspects have been detained while the investigation continues.
The people of Baraka are reportedly fearful, as the incident is being interpreted by some residents to be a ritual killing.
Just last month another child was also reported missing in the Kenema district of Gurahun Tunkia and has still not been found.

Original_Search_Title: Live-in in-laws: How Peaceful? (4)
Headline: Nigeria: Live-in in-laws: How Peaceful? (4)
Body_Text:
Hi, In-laws are generally perceived as "enemies" by couples. They are usually regarded as unnecessary interference in the scheme of things. Except in a few cases, many couples would rather keep their in-laws at arm's length. After all, too much familiarity breeds contempt. But as much as many couples would want to, the extended family practice in the African culture may not permit it. In fact, women (they happen to be more affected) who do not tolerate the infiltration of their partner's family members are often given social tags. The foundation of most relationships and homes have been terribly shaken due to the influence of in-laws. So, what happens when couples find it impossible to keep their extended family out of their homes? How do they survive, or maintain a balance? We spoke to some respondents who shared their experiences with us. Our fourth respondent, Tinu, (47), who says the first four years of her marriage was hell, continues her story below. Happy reading!
Ade's late night outings soon became a regular habit. He would stay out till the wee hours of the morning, returning when most decent people would have slept and woken up midnight to pray or do something useful. If I refuse to open the door, mama would be on hand to do it. She would insist that I am being wicked and wanted him to be attacked while waiting at the door. This is someone that was not attacked all the way from wherever he was coming from, but would now be attacked right at his doorstep. But, what did I expect from mama?
As far as mama was concerned, everything anyone does against me is alright. I must have been at fault, but everything I did was wrong because I am bad, and because one cannot expect anything better from Ijebu people.
As soon as my first child started speaking, the first few words she learnt were derogatory remarks about Ijebu people, courtesy of my mother-in-law. She would call anyone who annoys her Ijebu we mu gari, or Ijebu onigbe ndi. This is to tell you that Ijebu people are either backward or inferior to other people. That Ijebu people are so unsophisticated with their choice of food or taste buds so they can eat nothing else but garri (cassava). That Ijebu people are so unhygienic such that when they go to toilet, they do not bother to clean their buttocks. Other times, she would just say, Ijebu ologun, meaning that Ijebu people are synonymous with juju. You will be surprised what would come out of these children's mouth. And the brain behind it all was mama. It was as if I'd committed a crime by being an Ijebu woman. Why would she judge me by my race and not as an individual? Sometimes, I would just sit and imagine how many false stories she has been telling the children about me and my people.
The more I thought about my children, the more I had to also think about my husband who was now becoming more of a stranger to me than ever. We had become like cat and mouse in the same house, and I was beginning to avoid him like the plague, just to maintain the peace and not give mama further chance to run me down in front of my husband and my children. There was now a limit to how much complaints I could make, and who I could make the complaints to.
Then one day, my mother-in-law came home with two young ladies from her home town. She said they were her relatives and had just concluded their primary school education. Their parents were poor and could not sponsor their education any further, so she asked them to send the two girls to her in Lagos, promising to help them out one way or another. She, therefore, decided that the girls should be shared between her two sons. Since it was obvious that we both needed house helps, she decided that they should come and work for us, while we trained them in the craft of their choices in return. She then took one to my brother-in-law and left one with us. I resented the idea but there was little I could do since my husband had already agreed.
The young girls mama claimed had just finished primary school were already grown up and ready for marriage. The younger one who was left with us was 17 years. How could these sort of big girls just be leaving primary school? It was obviously not true. But who is to say otherwise when mama had announced? I could only stare at them in amusement. Little did I know that the whole plan will not be so amusing for long.
All that the girl did was to clean the house and follow my husband to the office. They said she was going to learn how to make soap, since my husband had a soap factory then. Soon, they said she was not learning again, but was going to replace the cashier who had just resigned. My husband and this girl now left home early each morning together and returned late in the night together.
The twins were already two years old by this time. Mama's troubles knew no bounds after this and never failed to ridicule me in front of this lady. Soon, mama started singing a new song. My reason for being antagonistic to her and her son was simply because of the young girl. I was plain jealous of her. She insisted that I should not worry too much as the girl would soon find her level and move out of my home. By this time, the girl who had been very respectful on arrival had become something else. She had stopped sweeping or even running any errands around the house. She only listened to mama and my husband. And since both of them were even out of the house for most part of the day, she was of no use to me. Even if she had been home, it was obvious she now considered me not important enough in the scheme of things in the house.
I didn't read much meaning to all their activities until one day this girl told me she was moving out as planned. One of her relatives had asked her to move in with them, so that she could get a proper job for her. I felt an uneasy relief because somehow, I was suspicious about the whole business.
About three months after her movement, the cat was let out of the bag. My sister-in-law visited me at the shop and made the revelation. She told me that Bola (my supposed housemaid) was pregnant for my husband and that he had rented an apartment for her in Surulere. She has, therefore, sent the other girl out of her home.
