Luckily for her, her husband had not touched his own girl, or, at least, she was not pregnant yet. My brother-in-law's wife told me that she overheard the discussion of the development when she visited her husband's office. She had been surprised to see Mama and one of her friends from her hometown. In actual fact, the two girls were the woman's relatives. She was the brain behind their coming to Lagos. She opined that she suspected mama to have asked the woman to recruit those girls for her sons, since she had never hidden the fact that she never liked us, her daughters-in-law. She advised me not to allow them bring the girl home, nor kick me out as was their intention. She would support me against them, no matter what, she promised.
That night marked another turn for the worse in our relationship. I could not hold back my anger and tears as soon as he entered. I had avoided mama all day, just so she would not suspect that I had already been informed of their game plan. I wanted to thrash things out with my husband alone. But you can trust mama to intervene in the matter that she was not invited. She did all the bragging on behalf of my husband and the lady. She told me I could do nothing as they have been able to overcome all my witchcraft and juju that I have been using over her son. That her son is now finally free of me and my hold on him. That night, Ade became a little spoilt brat. He could not even stand up for himself, but allowed his mother to go on ranting like a bitch at me. I called him all sorts of names. But it did not change what had already happened.
As soon as he was out of my grip, Ade made for the door and did not show up for several days.
I could not say that I was actually shocked on hearing the news. It was more like my fears were being confirmed at last. Somehow, things continued to degenerate between Ade and I, such that I felt that he had been bewitched by these women. Someone who used to be so caring and loving, had now almost turned into a total stranger. He would not come home for days, except to give me our feeding allowance and check on the children. I was forced to live in mama's company more or less, as it was now just myself, the children who had practically become her soldiers, and the great mama.
But despite everything, we still managed to make love once a while. It was not so much out of love, but for the fact that I just had to find some relevance in the whole mess. The more I told my sad story to relatives and friends, the more I was advised on the need to stick to my guns and remain in my matrimonial home for whatever it was worth. They opined that since he was still coming home even if only once a week, it meant there was still something to be salvaged in the union. I must ignore mama and concentrate on myself and my husband. I was now neck deep in a competition for my husband's favours.
On such days as God would have it, I took in again. However, I lost it mysteriously in the third month to the delight of my mother-in law. Prior to this time she'd been telling me bluntly to leave the children and pack out of her son's house since he now has a new wife. My continued stay in the house was forcing him to pay rent to some landlord. If I move out, they would be able to move into the house and cut unnecessary costs. Can you imagine all these coming from a mother? Soon, her outbursts started attracting neighbours, but I kept my cool as advised by friends and relatives.
To my surprise, mama managed to win over my sister-in-law, the same one who promised to stick by me against them, no matter what. That one now visited her almost on a weekly basis with foodstuff and provisions. They would talk for hours, exchanging jokes and recalling past events. Mama would never say anything good about me despite all I did for her. Right until I was forced to pack out, I was still washing all her clothes, while I'd resumed my pounding sessions right after giving birth to the twins.
One Saturday afternoon, I came home from my shop to take something when I noticed that mama had a visitor in her room. As I didn't want to disturb her, I went about what I came for. As fate would have it, I was stepping out of my room just at the same time the woman was leaving and so we jammed at the corridor leading to the exit door. The visitor was mama's friend, Bola's aunt. I knelt down on both knees to greet her and on rising felt a sharp pain in my abdomen. Immediately, I was in a pool of blood. I fell down again, reeling in pain and I know my mother-in-law must have been genuinely shocked. She begged the woman to assist her carry me, while shouting for help. I was taken to the hospital by one of our neighbours and thus lost the pregnancy. It was while in hospital, that I decided I'd had enough of mama's troubles. If I wanted my life and good health, it was better for me to let go. If God still wanted me to be married, he would see to how Ade would come back to his senses and become the man he ought to be.
On my arrival from hospital, my mother-in-law became distant and began visiting her home more often. My relationship with my husband became ice-cold. All forms of intimacy came to a halt as he seldom visited home. Most times, his manager brought our feeding allowance.
But for the fact that the children remained with me, I would have been driven out right after my return from hospital. Bola's baby turned out a girl and a lavish party was thrown to mark its birth. She was now the apple of my husband's eye as he obeyed her beck and call!
Mama too was the dotting mother-in-law who cooked and washed her clothes, besides caring for the child. After a full year of living on my own more or less with the children, my relatives advised me to come back home. If my husband still wished to marry me, he could file a new application asking for my hands. When I made this known to him, he made no fuss about it until he'd gone to consult his mother. Mama came to inform me that no one was against my leaving since my husband didn't want me again. The only clause was that I could not leave with the children. I was horrified, but on consultations with my family, I was advised to leave them behind as she could never harm them. It was obvious that she loved the children. Her grouse for whatever reason only God knew, was with me.
That was how I left my home and children after four years of marriage. Even some of my husband's relatives greed that it was the best thing to do under the circumstance. As long as I remained, my home and even my husband will know no peace. Mama wanted her children to have children, but no wives. Before my arrival, she and my sister-in-law were not so friendly because she was fond of causing trouble between the couple. More so, her husband was a stubborn person right from childhood. So she couldn't penetrate too much into their affairs. But the reverse was the case with my husband.
Anyway, as I said, God is always on the side of the patient and the oppressed. It may only take a while. Mama took over my children, while my husband pitched his tent with his new wife. But the love soon went sour and turned to hatred. Exactly a year after I left, the two of them broke up and Bola returned to her aunty's house with her child. At the peak of their problems, he'd packed into another rented apartment, refusing to come home to mama. The fall out of the two love birds caused a strain in my mother-in-law's relationship with Bola's aunt. Rumour even had it that mama had accused her friend of backing out of their agreement. Whatever it meant, only God knows. Anyway, he came back to look for me a few months after Bola's departure.
Bola too had left without her child who was also placed in mama's care along with mine. He told me that he'd been informed by one of my friends that I was staying with my uncle and wanted to know how I was faring. He asked why I'd not bothered to come and check on my children. He wanted to know also if I was seeing any man. Naturally, one would want to bluff and express anger and regret. My husband is a very simple and caring man, but for his mother and the influence and power she wielded over him.
In short, that was how he started visiting me again, and expressed the desire to reunite. But my parents suggested that my mother-in-law must accompany her son to come and ask for my hand again. Mama was forced to come when her son told her he would have nothing to do with her again if she failed to allow him. Anyway, before the end of the second year, I came back to my house. Mama was forced to return to her house too and we have all been living in peace, at least what appears to be peace, ever since.

Original_Search_Title: The Plight of Congolese Women
Headline: Uganda: The Plight of Congolese Women
Body_Text:
These women are unfortunate because neither the local community nor the UPDF wants them. They don't fit anywhere since they are not citizens or refugees and cannot even speak the local language
Their eyes shone in anticipation as they gathered one by one on the dirty veranda in Kasubi, one of Gulu's settlements, obviously curious about the strangers present. In one corner of the veranda lay dirty saucepans of leftover food and cassava leaves that are a staple in the Congolese diet. Their dark skinned malnourished children are a marked contrast to their bleached faces and dark limbs popularly referred to as Mirinda-Pepsi.
Like their lifestyle that has been branded immoral, this is the theme of many spiteful and hilarious songs composed by local artistes in Northern Uganda. But that is the least of their worries.
"Survival is very hard without real jobs or reliable sources of income. When I came here in 2002 with a Ugandan soldier from DRC, I had anticipated better life.
Unfortunately, the man disappeared after a few months. Since then, I have been struggling to sustain myself and two children with the little I can get, and help from fellow Congolese," Sola Colleta says.
Colleta owned a grocery shop back home, which she relinquished to her relatives. Now, she makes a living plaiting hair for as little as Shs500.
Seven-months-pregnant Lucy Naji weeps as she narrates how another man denied fathering her baby. She turned to him for support after being abandoned by the UPDF soldier who brought her to Uganda. "To get money, I am forced to use any means. If you meet a man who can offer it, you end up going with him, only for him to also abandon you," she laments.
These are typical experiences of scores of Congolese women in Northern Uganda, who trekked from the DRC to Uganda with the Uganda People's Defence Forces in 2001.
They dreamed of love and life, but once in Uganda, fate turned on them as their lovers either died or abandoned them.
Destitute, is what the women settled in communities on the outskirts of barracks and some IDP camps where they live in abjection, trying to make ends meet, are. Amuru, Pajimu, Alero, Pajule, Pabo, Kasubi, Konypacho, Koro, Kerulen, Kanyagoga are some of the places they have settled.
Food and water shortages, inadequate medical care and accommodation, hostility of the natives and insecurity are challenges they battle daily. It is difficult to estimate how many Congolese women eke a living on the fringes of society, but locals claim there are hundreds.
Destitutes
"When you are sick, no one can treat you unless you have money or documentation of marriage to a soldier. But most of us are not in official marriages. When someone dies, we can't even bury so some people are just thrown in the bushes," says Betty Langu who shares stuffy, cramped quarters with four other women and five children in Pabo IDP camp.
The Congolese women in this camp live in such desperation they are willing to talk to anybody who might offer some hope. Most are either pregnant, carrying infants or trailed by thin, filthy toddlers.Francis Odora, the LC 1 of Small Gate Kasubi where Colleta and Naiji live, reveals that most of the Congolese women have resorted to prostitution to make ends meet.
Hostile conditions
This, alongside their poor sanitation and aggressiveness has prompted negative feelings from the community. Their situation is appalling but we haven't been able to do anything to help them.
However, in spite of these vices, a few have managed to remarry and integrate. The community is overtly hostile, evidenced by name-calling (Congole), spiteful comments, songs and negative attitude toward them. One resident accused the women of spreading HIV. Local leaders also revealed that the prevalence of HIV/Aids is high in these communities.
"They should have known that the soldiers had wives at home. I hate them because they are promiscuous. They hang out in bars and give sex for money," says Sam Otoo, adding that they are materialistic, and eat meat and chicken when they get money instead of investing in a better life. They are high maintenance, he says, so men use and dump them.
"These women are unfortunate because neither the local community nor the UPDF wants them. They don't fit anywhere since they are not citizens or refugees and cannot even speak the local language. For this reason, no civil society or government has come out to help them," reveals social worker Sandra Oder, founder of Children's Environmental Health Network, an NGO in Gulu.
Oder has visited a group of Congolese women who were languishing in the 4th division military hospital in Gulu barracks.
"Many of them were terminally ill and were lying on the floor under the care of children, without food or medication. I volunteered to provide food for some weeks. But this can never solve all their problems," Oder says. She recommends drafting a formal system to recognise and manage foreign women in conflict areas, brought by armed forces.
However, UPDF Spokesperson for Northern region Chris Magezi, dismisses such claims as ridiculous. He says the UPDF in Gulu has a record of only 45 soldiers married to Congolese wives who are well catered for in the barracks and medically in the army hospital.
"These women making claims can best be described as one-time girlfriends whom we never, as an institution, brought here. If they voluntarily followed our battalion that came on foot, what did you expect us to do? Chase them?"
Magezi argues that the army does not marry as an institution and cases of abandonment are the personal business of the soldiers. This casts a shadow on issues of child support that the women desperately need sorted out. To him, this is an immigration problem. "Our (immigrations) is weak. How can they allow people to come into the country without documentation? This is endangering our security," he says.
Going back home
At the end of the day, having been rejected by the institution that is responsible for their migration to Uganda, they can only hope that one day, they will find their way home.
"If only I could afford it, I would go back home. Just look at me. I am a grown woman hassling with life here in Uganda yet I had a good clothes business back at home," Akombe Mbugu says sadly.

Original_Search_Title: Fuel And Electricity Push Up Inflation in Ghana
Headline: Ghana: Fuel And Electricity Push Up Inflation in Ghana
Body_Text:
GHANA MAY accelerate already expected tightening measures, including further interest rate increases, following a rise in inflation in May driven by a surge in non-food prices, especially petrol.
Inflation reached 11.0 per cent in May; the highest this year and the second month in a row it has been above the government's single digit target range this year. The comparable figure for April 2007 was 10.5 percent. The annual rate of inflation rose 0.5 percentage points.
The annual rate of inflation rose 0.3 percentage points from 10.2 per cent in March to 10.5 per cent in April having dropped in March by 0.2 percentage points.
Inflation is the rate at which the general level of prices for goods and services is rising.
The Bank of Ghana is wary of inflation, because of its impact on savings and macro-economic stability sustainability.
Latest figures compiled by the Ghana Statistical Service (GSS) indicates that Consumer Price Index (CPI) for May 2007 was 218.41 with respect to the base year of 2002 compared to 196.73 recorded in 2006.
According to the Acting Government Statistician, Prof. Nicholas Nsoma- of 44.9 per cent.
Within the non-food group, transportation contributed the highest to the upward movement of the index with 0.41 points where petrol was the highest contributor with 0.23 points. Housing, electricity and water group followed with 0.23 points.
These high movements, in Francis Nyoagbe, a financial expert with the NTH, were attributable to the recent fuel price increases and the shortage of electricity supply.
It would be recollected in what was described as the second fuel price hike in one month that the National Petroleum Authority of Prices of manufactured items have also increased because shortage of power to produce at full capacity. Shortage in supply pushes up prices.
Food and beverages group including potatoes also had the highest movement of 1.73 points with plantain, yam, cassava, and cocoyam recording the highest with 0.78 points, 0.31 points, 0.17 points and 0.07 points respectively.
High inflation causes increase in interest rate, which further reduces the present value of future cash flow thereby reducing the attractiveness of investment opportunities.

Original_Search_Title: Over 80 Percent of Kasese Residents Live Below the Poverty Line
Headline: Uganda: Over 80 Percent of Kasese Residents Live Below the Poverty Line
Body_Text:
Kampala — MORE than 80% of the households in Kasese district live on less that one dollar (sh1,700) a day. A report compiled last month by the Kasese local government and Kasese District Development Network, an NGO, noted that despite several poverty alleviation programmes, the household incomes had remained very low.
"The major cause of this is that the district entirely depends on agriculture yet bad weather has been frustrating the farmers for, a long time," observed Mathina Bwambale, one of the authors.
He said many farmers had received loans from banks and the poor harvest had forced them to sell their land to pay the loans.
The report also noted that 90% of the families do not use mosquito nets because they cannot afford them. This explains why most children dying below the age of five were victims of malaria, he said.
The other problems listed in the report were, poor nutrition, high maternal mortality, high fertility rate, early marriages, inadequate maternity services, poor health services and acute land shortage.
Bwambale encouraged local leaders to initiate self-help projects for the people. "It is only Dr. Crispus Kiyonga, who came up with a cassava-growing project."

Original_Search_Title: Jinja's Life Threatening Waste Sites
Headline: Uganda: Jinja's Life Threatening Waste Sites
Body_Text:
Jinja — We moved around Jinja town suburbs in search of an organised site for the disposal of waste materials by burial or a landfill. It was part of those key aspects of the field work training for environment journalists that had been organised by Makerere University with support from Swedish International Development Agency at Jinja's Hotel Triangle this month.
To our dismay, we ended up in one of the dumping sites in Masese Danida Village. There lay some hospital wastes like used syringes and expired drugs dumped in open area.
Plastic materials, rotting hides and skin and a chemical known as Parafomaldehyde, packed in bags of 25kgs were also dumped in open surface.
The chemical, a white crystalline powder, has been used as a fumigant worldwide for more than 30 years to decontaminate laboratory facilities and to disinfect sickrooms, clothing, linen, and sickroom utensils among other uses.
When heated, paraformaldehyde releases formaldehyde gas, which may be used as a decontaminant. Paraformaldehyde is also used in dentistry and in the manufacture of synthetic resins and artificial ivory.
However this chemical can be harmful to human health when swallowed, irritating to the eyes, respiratory system and to the skin. The bags where the chemicals were packed carried bold warnings "Keep away from source of ignition, do not breath dust". Other warnings read "Avoid contact with eyes, rinse immediately with plenty of water and seek medical advice".
The most disturbing thing is that they were openly dumped where women and children scavenge for various materials to earn a living. Some local women display their foodstuff like cassava on the Parafomaldehyde bag to dry under the sun.
Despite glaring warnings on the waste bags "Wear suitable protective clothing, gloves and eye protection, the women and children use their bare hands to scavenge from the garbage heaps.
The waste in the dumping site poses a health risk to the local people living around it as some could easily contract HIV from the used syringes as they shuffle the garbage bags.
The dumping site is on a hill, overlooking Lake Victoria. Yet just below the dumping site are banana and yam gardens. This brings to the fore the risk of toxins from metals being absorbed by the crops especially yam plants and others being washed down into Lake Victoria especially during the rainy.
The Jinja District Municipal Environment Officer Mr Ernest Nabihamba said that the area was gazetted for waste 23 years ago when there were no inhabitants. "People only started settling in the area, forming a slum as it is today between 1990-1994. Otherwise it is the wastes that went there first and not the people," he said. His argument is that it's the people who invaded the garbage.
He however said his office has demanded a landfill where such dangerous wastes in the municipality can be buried. But he regretted that his efforts to persuade the municipal leadership to have the waste management problem addressed has not yielded any meaningful results.
"We even have a grader (tractor) but dedicating it to compress waste is a problem. Instead, the dozer is sent for grading roads. Maintaining it in case it gets spoilt is yet another problem," Nabihamba said.
We found a yellow grader that was seemingly digging a pit for burying waste abandoned at the dumping site. Residents said the grader had been at the site for more than two months after it developed a mechanical fault.
Open dumping is not limited to Masese alone but also other areas. Sunday Monitor also visited another dumping site opposite Kirinya Sewage Works in Jinja municipality. For Kirinya, both the wastes that rot (biodegradable) and those that do not rot (none biodegradable) have been dumped up to the middle of the road.
The decomposing waste releases black liquid known as leachates. Leachates may contain various minerals, organic matter or other contaminants and can contaminate surface water or ground water.
Since there was no sign of collection and treatment of the leachate, the residents have to live with the risk of water pollution as the dumping site is near Lake Victoria.
For this particular dumping site, Nabihamba says " it was a mistake". He promised to make sure no more garbage is deposited there.
Open dumping of such dangerous waste is not unique to Jinja district. It is also taking place in other areas throughout the country. A similar dumping site exists in Kireka-Kira Town Council in a wetland on the western side of the Mandela Stadium near Kampala.
This open garbage site is at most 100 metres away from the fancy New Nassanga traditional cuisine spot. In the same wetland where dumping takes place, there is a natural water source which is used by the local community to draw water for mainly domestic consumption.
"We appeal to local authorities at all levels ( LCs III, II and I) and the Kireka community in Kira Town Council to desist from dumping in this wetland. They should know waste dumping is not only a health hazard but also negates the core of the government Poverty Eradication Action Plan and sustainable development to which every citizen is a custodian," says Mr Banada Nswa, the executive Director of Uganda Environment Protection Forum.
He says all citizens should press their respective leaders at all levels to have waste management issues on the priority list. He thinks this strategy would mitigate the problem of waste management especially in urban areas where the threat is higher.
But he says locals in sub-counties/divisions, too, need to exert pressure on their leaders to put a solid waste management plan in place.
If nothing is done to stop open dumping, there is a possibility of adding more pollutants into nearby water sources. For the case of Jinja, Lake Victoria, the second largest fresh water body in the world may cease being fresh soon. Such pollutants affect the volume and purity of the water in the lake and other sources.
A polluted Lake Victoria could occasion a big price to pay. The lake produces about 500,000 metric tonnes of fish annually and about 1,500metric tonnes daily.
Earnings from the lake fish catch are in the region of $400 million a year or slightly more than $1 million per day.

Original_Search_Title: Country Records Decrease in Maize Production
Headline: Zambia: Country Records Decrease in Maize Production
Body_Text:
ZAMBIA's maize production for 2006/2007 farming season has decreased to 1,366,158 tonnes from the previous 1,400,000 during the last season.
Agriculture and Cooperatives Minister, Ben Kapita, said this in Lusaka yesterday when he announced the crop forecasting survey for 2006/2007 agriculture season and the food balance status for the 2007/2008 marketing season.
Mr Kapita said the 4.09 per cent reduction in maize production would, however, not result in any food deficit as Zambia had a crop surplus of 250,000 tonnes.
Apart from the 250,000 tonnes surplus from this season, Zambia had another 400,000 tonnes as carry-over stock from the 2005/2006 farming season.
"Due to favourable production experienced in the previous season, the country has a maize carry-over stock of 433,031 tonnes in comparison to last year's maize carry over stock of 20,256 tonnes.
"When maize carry-over stocks are added to maize production for 2006/2007 agriculture season, total availability of maize for the country to cover the 2007/2008 marketing season comes to 1,799,188 tonnes," Mr Kapita said.
For the estimated population of 12.1 million people, total maize requirement amounted to 1,549,188 tonnes.
Results of the crop forecasting survey for the 2006/2007 agriculture season carried out in conjunction with the Central Statistical Office (CSO) showed that the country produced enough food, which when added to carry-overs from the last season could adequately cater for national consumption as well as leave a surplus maize for export.
The contribution from small-scale farmers to the national maize production for 2006/2007 agriculture season stood at 79 per cent, while that of the commercial farmers was at 21.01 per cent.
During the same season, cassava production rose to 1,185, 600 kilogrammes from the previous 1,059, 887 kilogrammes representing an increase of 11.86 per cent.
The minister said it was gratifying to note that cassava production had continued to increase and ordered officials at the ministry to secure the market for the commodity.
Rice production had risen by 31.18 per cent from 13,964 tonnes in the 2005/2006 season to 18,317 tonnes this year.
Mr Kapita said the production of cash crops such as soya beans had decreased by 4.53 per cent, while the production of barley tobacco had reduced by 30 per cent from 14,306,000 kilogrammes last year to 10,000,000 kilogrammes this year.
Virginia tobacco reduced from 20,000,000 kilogrammes last year to 15,562,000 kilogrammes in this year's harvest.

Original_Search_Title: Agagu Laments Political Violence in Ondo
Headline: Nigeria: Agagu Laments Political Violence in Ondo
Body_Text:
Abuja — GOVERNOR Olusegun Agagu of Ondo State has condemned the new wave of political murder and thuggery in the state, blaming the development on over-ambitious people.
Governor Agagu at a press conference at the auditorium of the News Agency of Nigeria (NAN) in Abuja shortly after a peace meeting with six governorship candidates in the state during the April 14, 2007 elections, said some people in the state were desperate to capture power at all cost.
He said, "we use this opportunity to condemn the new dimension of thuggery, murder, arson and violence recently introduced into the political landscape of our hitherto peaceful state. We must remind ourselves that elections were conducted in Ondo State in 1999 and 2003 without this new form of madness.
"Overambitious people have introduced violence in their desperate bid to capture power by all means. We thank God Almighty that this temporary insanity has abated and pray that it never rears its ugly head again in our dear state.
He however declared that, "we have set up the necessary machinery to curb the menace, give relief to those who lost their property and prosecute those so far arrested as perpetrators of the shameful acts."
The Governor said he has laid a solid foundation capable of turning around the economy of the state in the next four years, even as six governorship candidates of the opposition parties have agreed to work with him to move the state forward.
The six governorship candidates include at the press conference were: Ademola Adegboroye of the Action Congress (AC): Olusola Agbesua of Democratic Peoples Alliance (DPA), Otunba Dele Akingboye of Democratic Peoples Party (DPP), Professor Ibukun Arifalo of All Nigeria Peoples Party (ANPP) and Bankole Oluwajana of Alliance for Democracy (AD) as well as Pere Ganfo of African Democratic Congress (ADC).
According to him, the dream of his administration in the next four years was to change Ondo State from an agrarian and civil service state to a modern industrialised economy.
He said, "we reassure the good people Ondo State that whereas we used our first term to lay a very solid foundation and charted a course for profound and rapid transformation of the state, in the next four years, we shall drive at a fast but safe speed to move the state forward."
"Our dream is to turn the economy around in a manner that is unprecedented. Our infrastructure will continue to be upgraded as no effort will be spared in improving on our performances of the past four years".
He said, "we have already started this journey through the establishment of the Olokola Free Trade Zone, Oluwa Enterprise Park, Okitipupa Cassava Products Factory, Ifon Ethanol Pant and several others.

Original_Search_Title: Power Threatens N2bn Import Substitution Glucose Syrup Factory
Headline: Nigeria: Power Threatens N2bn Import Substitution Glucose Syrup Factory
Body_Text:
Lagos — NIGERIA's hope to earn ( $15 million) N2 billion annually from import substitution from glucose syrup production by Ekha Agro Farms Ltd is under threat as epileptic public power supply is adversely affecting the company's operations.
"The factory is currently operating at less than 10 per cent of its installed capacity due to a myriad of problems top of which is power. Power supply by PHCN to the factory is epileptic. Consequently, we currently run the factory only on diesel powered generators. The recent hike in fuel prices has further increased the cost of power generation," said Executive Director of Ekha Agro Farms Ltd, Mrs. Yemisi Iranloye.
"We are appealing to President Umaru Musa Yar'Adua, to do everything possible to ensure that the challenges of the power sector are given urgent attention," she said.
Other issues which have posed serious challenges to the factory, Iranloye, said include water supply, movement of cassava to the factory from the farms due to non-availability of trucks coupled with the increase in the price of petroleum products.
Despite the huge challenges, Mrs. Iranloye assured that the management of Ekha Agro Farms Ltd is unwavering in her determination to make a success of the first glucose syrup factory in the country so that the Nigerian economy will continue to enjoy the benefits of the investment.
The state-of the-art Cassava-based Glucose Syrup processing factory is the first of its kind in Nigeria and the second in Africa after that in South Africa.
It was designed and built by International Starch Institute, Denmark with funding from Bank of Industry Ltd, NEXIM Bank Ltd and NARCDB Ltd. Additional working capital was provided by Zenith Bank Plc and Intercontinental Bank Plc. The plant has an installed production capacity of 30,000 metric tons of glucose syrup per annum.
The major raw material for the factory is cassava and, according to Mrs. Iranloye, the commodity is abundantly available. While noting the immense support of IITA for the cassava industry in Nigeria and her glucose syrup project in particular, Mrs. Iranloye referred to the institute as a blessing to Nigeria having ensured the availability of high yielding and early maturing hybrids that thrives in all weather conditions.
With the advent of this factory, about USD $15 million is expected to be saved for Nigeria in Import Substitution; monies that will be freed for investment in other sectors of the economy. This can only be realised if the factory operates at full capacity.
The ultra-modern glucose syrup factory at Ibafo, Ogun State, was commissioned by former President Olusegun Obasanjo few weeks before the expiration of his tenure.

Original_Search_Title: Simba Celebrates With Bonanza
Headline: Nigeria: Simba Celebrates With Bonanza
Body_Text:
Lagos — Simba Group of companies, Kary-Go tricycle assemblers in Nigeria, has kicked-off a bonanza tagged 'Simba scratch and win' to mark its 20 years operation in the country. Customers who purchase the company's products within the bonanza period will stand a chance of wining prizes ranging from motorcycles, generators, rechargeable lanterns, branded caps and pen.
Speaking to Business Trust on the bonanza in Lagos, Vinay Grover, Managing Director, Simba Group of Companies said they embarked on it to show appreciation to its numerous customers, who have been with them since inception.
"We are appreciative of our customers' patronage. They are loyal customers and have been with us through thick and thin. So the only way to show our gratitude is to institute something like this, where they can benefit freely from us," he said.
Apart from the scratch and win bonanza, the Simba chief executive said the company's loyalty scheme as well as its annual dealers conference were instituted to give back to its customers.
Simba Group, apart from being a pioneer assembler of motorcycles in Nigeria also popularised the conversion of cassava grinding diesel engines to power diesel engines in the country.

Original_Search_Title: Southern Sudan - Reluctant Host for the LRA
Headline: Uganda: Equatoria  - Reluctant Host for the LRA
Body_Text:
Yambio — Zachariah Nzari says the rebels camped a few kilometres from his village in Southern Sudan overstayed their welcome the day they turned their guns on his people - looting property, destroying homes and abducting villagers.
That was in January 2006. Fighters with the Ugandan Lord's Resistance Army (LRA) entered Nzari's village of Nabanga in Western Equatoria, after looting and destroying 75 homes, took away four people to carry their booty.
"Their bodies were [later] found," said Nzari, the village chief of Nabanga, just a few kilometres from the border between Sudan and the Democratic Republic of Congo. "But the bodies of seven [other] people who went into the forest to collect honey have not been found."
The forest is now a no-go area for Nzari's community, yet they depend on it for honey and building materials. Also missing, the chief added, were six girls who were abducted; cereals, chickens, goats, radios and other valuables were looted.
The rebels, say locals in the state capital of Yambio, have continued to threaten them despite ongoing efforts by the Southern Sudan government to broker a peace agreement with the Ugandan state. The on-off peace talks, mediated by Southern Sudanese Vice-President Riek Machar, are set to resume in Juba.
While the world focuses on the talks, Nzari said, his people continue to suffer from their unwanted neighbours. "The Vice-President used to say there was going to be peace between the people of Uganda and the LRA, but we want peace between the people of Nabanga and the LRA first," he told IRIN.
The rebels, he added, should return the girls they abducted from Nabanga: "When the Vice-President came to Nabanga we presented him with their names on paper [and] he asked [Joseph] Kony [leader of the LRA] where are these girls. [Kony] said they were there in the bush with them at their camp. We want them to bring our people back, [then] just leave our country."
New report
Nzari was in Yambio on 13 June to attend the launch of a report, Reluctant Hosts, by the humanitarian agency, World Vision, highlighting the devastating effect of the presence of Ugandan rebels in Western Equatoria.
"It is not just northern Ugandan communities that have been affected - the international community should broaden its scope," said Sophie Gordon, author of the report, which describes how attacks on villages and towns in the region have continued in 2006 and 2007.
The report quotes the governor of Western Equatoria, Samuel Abu John, saying the local population had suffered a wave of abductions, displacement and looting during the 2006 long lull in talks when the LRA delegation refused to return to Juba for security reasons.
"We as the people who have been most affected ... are not being given the space to talk of what has happened to our people and participate in these talks in Juba," the governor notes in a forward to the report.
Much-needed development, following the north-south Sudan peace agreement in 2005, ending more than 20 years of war, has been limited by LRA activity. Cultivation of crops and schools was also disrupted.
"There is no official number, but we found 10 children abducted - of whom nine were girls - just in one county," said Gordon, adding that further reports of abductions had been made in the last months after the study was conducted.
Abu's deputy, Joseph Ngere Paciko, said more than 50 people had been killed by the LRA since they arrived in Western Equatoria in 2005. At least 16 children were abducted and their whereabouts still unknown.
"Although killings have reduced since the talks began, looting has continued; two weeks ago they looted in Mundri," he said, adding that it was frustrating to hear the LRA denying attacks the state authorities knew to have taken place. "We are giving a chance for peace but the LRA must stop harassing our people," he emphasised.
Another resident, Albino Clement, was more blunt: "We were not consulted about the LRA coming to our area. They should go to their land, to Uganda; if they stay here they should be like refugees, without arms."
Living in fear
World Vision said funding for protection and an early warning system for Western Equatoria was desperately needed to help prevent widespread looting, murder and rape in case of another LRA 'rampage' in that area.
It described the people of Western Equatoria state as living in an "atmosphere of fear" despite the ongoing talks in Juba.
The Vice President used to say there was going to be peace between the people of Uganda and the LRA, but we want peace between the people of Nabanga and the LRA first
"In February 2007, the LRA left its hideout in the DRC and travelled north into Southern Sudan, after peace talks between the government of Uganda and the LRA collapsed," said Seth Le Leu, programme director of World Vision Southern Sudan. "Its soldiers wreaked havoc on the populations of Eastern and Western Equatoria, repeatedly attacking communities, abducting, looting, allegedly raping and killing its citizens."
On the resumption of talks, he said: "This has not put an end to the fear felt by people in Western Equatoria. Under the terms of the renewed talks the LRA must assemble in one single place - which is within Western Equatoria. These communities have once again been forced to become the reluctant hosts of this movement. Men, women and children are living in constant fear of attacks."
According to the report, the LRA has also victimised humanitarian workers in the region. It cites the killing in November 2005 of two staff members of an international mine clearance agency south of Juba and an attack in February 2006 on a United Nations Children's Fund (UNICEF) compound in Yambio.
In December 2005, UN compounds in Yambio and Yei were attacked, with two staff killed in Yei. "The rise of attacks in southern Sudan has led to communities being harmed on two levels: their own communities have been pillaged and terrorised and their access to international aid and relief has been greatly hindered," said the report.
However, when peace talks between the LRA and the Ugandan government began in July 2006, the frequency of attacks in Western Equatoria greatly reduced.
Among atrocities listed in the report is the plight of abductees. They were normally loaded with looted goods, tied to one another, and made to walk in convoys to the DRC through the compounds of other residents in order to loot more goods and take more hostages. Goats were tied with ropes to the captives' waists and chickens attached to their arms.
Accountability issues
The current round of talks is focusing on "accountability and reconciliation", the third of five agenda points in a process that had stalled since December when LRA confidence in Machar's mediation dropped. Intervention by UN special envoy Joachim Chissano helped get the talks back on track in April.
The issue of accountability is particularly sensitive given the arrest warrants issued by the International Criminal Court against LRA leaders, which the LRA has said must be withdrawn. Discussions will look at a range of options in traditional, national and international justice to provide some measure of accountability and justice for victims of abuses committed by both sides during the long conflict.
LRA movement expected
But as the talks resume, Sudanese officials said rebel movement from Eastern Equatoria to the Ri-Kwangba base on the DRC border had not been completed as required, adding that 1,000 rebels were thought to remain in Eastern Equatoria.
Offers of help from Sudan with transport and pre-positioned food had been refused, a senior official said, because the LRA feared ambush by Ugandan soldiers. They also "don't want their numbers to be known", he said.
In Nimule hospital in Eastern Equatoria, Junal Logira, a father of eight, was recovering from an infected bullet wound. He recalls the night the LRA came to his house, in April, in the Labone area, east of Nimule: "Thank God I wasn't drunk that night." His wife woke him up when she heard shooting; he crawled outside and the family fled in different directions. He was hit in the ankle.
"The LRA fighters took all the food and some money," he said. "They uprooted my cassava crop and ran away."
Orieyn Okara, an elderly man displaced several times by the rebels, strapped a bag of sorghum rations distributed by an NGO in Nimule on to his bicycle, then angrily snapped: "Where is this Kony? I have lost all my 12 children ... he has done a lot of damage ... Have you found him? If you have, kill him."
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]
Read World Vision's Report

Original_Search_Title: NYEP-Catalyst for a Positive Change
Headline: Ghana: NYEP-Catalyst for a Positive Change
Body_Text:
IF THERE is any intervention that has provoked so much enthusiasm on the part of the government and which the people have accepted, it is the National Youth Employment programme (NYEP), which is providing a solution to the unemployment situation in the country.
The broad objective of the programme is to empower the youth to be able to contribute productively towards the socio-economic and sustainable development of the nation.
Needless to say, the programme provides vital catalytic ingredients for national development.
In implementing the NYEP, the government evolved ten modules targeting various employment areas.
The modules under consideration in phase one of the programme include Youth-in-Agri-Business, Youth-in-Trades and Vocations, Youth-in ICT, Community Protection System,
Waste and Sanitation Management Corps, Rural Education Teachers Assistants and Vacation Jobs. The rest are Auxiliary Health Care Workers Assistants, Paid Internships and Industrial Attachments and Volunteer Services.
The Youth-in-Agri-Business module is to provide employment for the youth in agricultural related activities and ventures and to encourage them to take up agriculture as an occupation.
The range of major agricultural activities covers crop production and cultivation, rearing and post harvest activities.
Under crop production, cultivation of vegetables like pepper, cabbage, onions, okro, tomatoes, garden eggs and mushrooms while yams, cassava, ginger come under roots and tubers.
Beneficiaries would also be encouraged to cultivate rice, maize, soya beans and sorghum as well as major cash and export crops like cashew, oil palm and coconut.
Under this module, anti-desertification Tree planting campaign and Plantation programmes would be embarked upon under an afforestation programme. Mass cocoa spraying, as it already persists in the cocoa industry, would be encouraged.
In animal production, Rabbitry, piggery, grass cutter rearing, goats and sheep raising and poultry including guinea fowls and others like snail culture, beeping and aquaculture would be introduced.
The module also takes care of post harvest activities including marketing and distribution and agro processing of various agricultural produce.
It is envisaged that marketing co-operatives would be formed to undertake the purchasing, packing and branding of various produce for distribution to consumers.
Some of the areas of agro-processing are: Oil palm and Shea Butter extraction, Gari processing, Cassava/corn dough kneading, Fish mongering/smoking, mango processing and Tomato processing.
The Youth in Trades and Vocations cater for the youth who engage in non-agricultural ventures like iron mongering and metal works, tailoring, fashion Design and dress making, batik tie and dye, block making, masonry, tiling, hair dressing and barbering, carpentry and woodworks among others.
The third module-Youth on ICT- is designed to provide employment to the youth by developing and sustaining a pool of knowledgeable ICT workforce in critical skills areas and professions and to support the ICT infrastructure of Ministries, Departments and agencies as well as other institutions in both the private and public sectors.
There is also this laudable Community Protection system which module is meant to enhance safety and security as well as strengthen law and order in the communities. About 815 of these agents have so far been turned out at the various police depots across the country to help the police do a good job.
In Kumasi, for instance yet another batch of 100 CPU's have began a month's training at the Police Training depot.
To ensure a tidy environment and maintain clean public facilities the Waste and sanitation management corps module has also been introduced to ensure availability of a strong and healthy workforce for the country.
Complementing this module is the Auxiliary Health Care Workers Assistants module whose beneficiaries would assist health professionals to deliver basic support services at health institutions such as hospitals, clinics and health posts particularly in the rural areas.
The basic objective of this module is to ensure total access to basic healthcare by most Ghanaians while providing opportunities for beneficiaries to upgrade their skills and progress to professional nursing courses.
The first batch of 15,000 youth is undergoing training to be redeployed to the Ministry of Health to various health institutions.
The graduates would go to assist the effective implementation of Community Health Services Programme in the communities.
The sixth module, the Community Education Teaching Assistants is to create opportunities for a pool of young men and women with at least second cycle level education who will help deliver pre and basic school level educational services in rural areas where teachers are constantly in short supply.
The other modules are Paid Internship and Industrial Attachment, Vacation Jobs and Volunteer Services.
While the Paid Internship would help formalize the institution and industrial attachments among in-and-out of school young men, the Vacation Jobs module would ensure that the students both in secondary and tertiary institutions during vacations are offered employment opportunities to earn some income and make the best of their leisure time and offer voluntary services to the communities as a means of contributing to the socio-economic development of the community.
In the case of Paid Internship, NYEP will identify a corresponding establishment and place the trainees.
Currently, 16,383 people are said to have been engaged in Youth in Agriculture module; 23,021 in Community Education Teaching Assistants; 15,500 Extension Health workers; 8,041 for Waste and Sanitation and 5,200 for Graduate Paid Internship among others.
The expectation is that the programme, which seeks primarily to create employment, would positively impact on the lives of the people.
Once it takes off, NYEP is bound to create a paradise in Ghana, as by the largesse of the government, the youth in particular shall not want.
That would obviously be a fulfillment of the positive change touted by the NPP government in its campaign for political power.

Original_Search_Title: Biotechnology Debated on Continent
Headline: Africa: Agri-Biotech - Safety First?
Body_Text:
Africa embraces a range of attitudes towards agricultural biotechnology, particularly transgenic crops. While genetically modified (GM) crops are commercially farmed in South Africa, an informal ban is in place in Zambia.
Biotechnology promises to solve many of Africa's problems, including an insecure food supply from a dry, harsh and unpredictable land. But the African Union (AU) believes that if Africa is to pursue biotechnology's promise it is going to have to do so as a cohesive whole.
Too many outsiders are pushing biotechnology agendas in Africa, says John Mugabe, science and technology advisor to the New Partnership for Africa's Development (NEPAD).
Mugabe believes that foreign interests, imposed on Africa, are creating a continent with no clear strategy.
He says the time has come for Africa to take back control of its biotech future.
Risk assessment
David Duthie, from the biosafety unit at the UN Environmental Programme (UNEP), says the problem is that many countries are confused about how to approach GM.
"African countries are really struggling with this," says Duthie. "They don't have access to [scientific] literature, they don't have scientific and technical elites to talk about the subjects. But they do have a lot of newspapers and a lot of media."
The chief concern of many countries is the safety of the environment and people's health. In accordance with the Cartagena Protocol on Biosafety, UNEP has been setting up procedures to help sub-Saharan African countries decide whether or not to import GM crops.
Cameroon, Kenya, Namibia and Uganda are working with UNEP to make their biosafety policies operational, while almost all other African states plan to have draft policies by December 2007, when the UNEP project is scheduled to end.
According to Duthie, UNEP has stayed clear of the pro- versus anti-GM fray.
"As a UN agency, we take a policy-neutral approach. We don't prescribe any particular policy or approach to safe use of modern biotechnology."
Caught between transatlantic differences
How a country defines 'safe' in the context of biotechnology forms the cornerstone of the debate. Germany and the United States -- both actively implementing biosafety policy and research programmes in Africa -- are in disagreement.
In the United States a transgenic product is considered to pose no new health risks if it can be assessed as 'substantially equivalent' to its unmodified counterpart.
But in Germany, which had led the formation of EU policy in Europe, the 'precautionary principle' is used. In the face of uncertainty, a defensive approach is taken even when causal links have not been scientifically established.
"From the EU standpoint, there is this question of 'what if?'" says José Falck-Zepeda, a research fellow at the US-based International Food Policy Research Institute.
Falck-Zepeda says there is no clear endpoint in that decision making process, whereas the United States is willing to live with a system that considers "safety as a matter of degree".
There are nations in Africa willing to live with this system too. The US-funded Program for Biosafety Systems has trained scientists in countries such as Ghana, Kenya, Malawi and Uganda to run field trials for GM crops in line with its own approach to biosafety.
Germany has directed its efforts toward persuading the AU, rather than individual countries, to adopt new biosafety regulations. Though the AU has no authority over its member states, it advises them on biosafety regulations.
Germany has, for example, funded an AU biosafety project, now in its second year, that focuses on building an Africa-wide biological safety system where member states are guided by a regional model law -- the African Model Law on Safety in Biotechnology.
The proposed law, which some say derives from the idea that the Cartagena Protocol cannot sufficiently safeguard human health and the environment in an African context, is conservative in its approach to biosafety. It puts the onus on exporting countries to pay compensation if any harm or loss of livelihood occurs as a result of introducing GM products.
A combined approach
Although a great deal of money has been invested in Africa through these projects, some think African nations have not benefited as much as they should have.
"The different projects may have resulted in more fragmentation," says Julius Mugwagwa, a researcher from the UK-based Open University and a former biotechnologist at the Biotechnology Trust of Zimbabwe, where he assisted in setting up a regional initiative (RAEIN-Africa) implementing a Southern African biosafety and environment programme from Namibia.
The Freedom to Innovate report, jointly published by NEPAD and the AU and put together by the High-Level African Panel on Modern Biotechnology, tries to reconcile these competing interests.
NEPAD's John Mugabe says it is about Africa taking back control of biotechnology and expanding scientific capacity -- laboratories, scientists, field trials -- beyond biosafety frameworks.
The report involved an all-African panel of experts, including Calestous Juma from Harvard University, the director general of Ethiopia's Environmental Protection Authority Tewolde Egziabher and representatives from the German-funded AU biosafety project, as well as scientists and representatives of nongovernmental organisations. They were charged with charting a strategy based on consensus.
Ismail Serageldin, director of the Library of Alexandria and co-chair of the panel, says the report offers "an alternative way forward from the paralysis that has characterised much of the work in Africa".
According to co-chair Calestous Juma, it is about developing long-term strategies that will give biotechnology efforts "a more pragmatic focus".
The Freedom to Innovate report emphasises the need for countries across Africa to unify their approach to biotechnology and regulation of risk.
Julius Mugwagwa says if countries don't work collaboratively as regional economic communities they will lose out.
He says regions want to be seen as one big market, so that investors won't have any problems with different systems in different countries. According to Mugwagwa, there will be economic losses if they don't harmonise.
He says the report reflects the continent's current enthusiasm for science, technology and innovation to "propel economies to a greater level".
But, he adds, whether this can be translated from an expert-driven report into sustained action at the implementation level is another question.
"A critical issue is how prepared are the regional economic communities at the policy, infrastructural, human resources and other levels to handle these responsibilities?"
Mugwagwa also questions the potential commitment of individual countries to the Freedom to Innovate report, especially those without the technical or policy capacity to contribute to regional biotechnology activities.
Some countries have been reluctant to let go of their sovereignty, but this may be changing. In March, West African states adopted a regional five-year plan of action for increasing food production through biotechnology.
Saving the orphans
The risk associated with incompatible biosafety requirements across the continent goes far beyond economic loss.
Small public-sector projects aimed at developing 'orphan' crops such as sorghum, cassava and pigeon pea -- largely ignored by big biotechnology companies -- may struggle to move forward through the sheer number of regulatory hurdles. These projects' limited financial resources would stretch further under one common testing and approval process.
Supporting these projects is vital, according to Frank Shotkoski from USAID (US Agency for International Development) who is currently involved in a Ugandan project on transgenic pest-resistant bananas.
Although agricultural biotechnology can't be a "silver bullet" solution for Africa, he believes it has "the potential to do more to bring Africa up to speed on the ability to produce food for its people than any other technology out there".
Field trials
Burkina Faso, Kenya, Malawi and Uganda are preparing for trials with Bt cotton -- engineered to carry the insect-killing Bt toxin. Kenya is pursuing transgenic maize, sweet potato and cassava. Nigeria is looking into Bt cowpea, and virus-resistant cassava is in the pipeline in Nigeria and Uganda.
There are other projects planned. The Harvest Plus project, funded by the Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation, for example, is fighting malnutrition with GM technology by fortifying the nutrient content of key crops such as sorghum, banana and cassava.
If Africa can forge a common path to protecting itself from any unseen consequences of GM technology without smothering innovation, it could find a pot of gold at the end of the transgenic rainbow.
According to Ismail Serageldin, Africa must look to the success stories and get inspiration. "These should not be the exception and they can be the norm," he says.

Original_Search_Title: RMRDC to Distribute Sesame Seeds in Kogi
Headline: Nigeria: RMRDC to Distribute Sesame Seeds in Kogi
Body_Text:
The Raw Materials Research and Development Council (RMRDC), has concluded arrangements to distribute improved sesame seeds free to 1,000 farmers in the North Central zone.
The Kogi RMRDC Co-ordinator, Mallam Sayuti Wali, told the News Agency of Nigeria (NAN), yesterday in Lokoja that the event would take place at the Children Amusement Park Lokoja, on June 14.
He said the Kogi First Lady, Hajia Zainab Idris, would perform the ceremony.
Wali explained that the seeds, which were of Sudanese variety and reputed to be of high yield, would be used for both domestic and industrial purposes.
When fully cultivated, he said, the government would purchase the produce from the farmers and eventually introduce them to other zones of the country.
This, he said, would enhance the farmers' income and popularise cultivation of the seeds.
"By the time the crop is fully entrenched in the agricultural system of the country, the improved and processed seeds would serve as substitute for cassava in the production of flour and wheat," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Boomtown Nimule Under Strain
Headline: Sudan: Boomtown Nimule Under Strain
Body_Text:
Nimule — Nimule, a town at Sudan's southern tip, has gained from being just a few metres from the Ugandan border, which provided a safety valve whenever the town was cut off from the rest of Southern Sudan by war.
Cross-border trade and population movement mean the Ugandan currency dominates the market, the Ugandan curriculum is taught in schools, Ugandan products are on sale everywhere, people listen to Ugandan FM radio stations and talk on Ugandan mobile phone networks. Thousands of people from the area still live in refugee camps in Uganda.
One Ugandan import, however, has brought nothing but fear, misery and death: the Lord's Resistance Army.
Nimule, its surrounding areas and roads, have been terrorised by the ambushes, looting and abductions by the Ugandan rebel group since the mid-1990s. The town hosts whole villages of people who have fled their home areas due to attacks by, or fear of, the LRA.
In addition, the town holds recently returned Sudanese refugees who had been living in camps in Uganda, and other long-term displaced communities that are unwilling or unable to return home, regardless of the LRA threat. But in addition to bustling commercial activity, there are social problems.
Swelling population
Nimule, situated at a bend in the Nile, is growing fast. A survey last year estimated the population at 45,000, but a local administrator, Shalfa Olweny Butrus, claims the town's population has doubled since.
The houses of displaced people - much like those in northern Ugandan camps - are scattered around the town, some on swampy land. Ugandan-style boda-boda motorbike taxis ply the dusty main street.
The latest community of displaced people to arrive in the town was already living temporarily in the Anzara settlement no more than a kilometre from Nimule. The Anzara people "originally came for safety - it turned out to be the opposite", said Gabriel Unzi Francis, a food monitor with the NGO Catholic Relief Services (CRS), which distributes food to Anzara and other needy communities in Nimule.
John Dick, a community leader from Anzara, said that on 20 February, two people were killed and three injured in a night-time raid.
Many households lost food and other property, he said. The settlement, not far from the Nimule airstrip, which is guarded by Ugandan and Sudan People's Liberation Army (SPLA) troops, has not been fully reoccupied, but people have started to go there by day, as security has stabilised for the time being, Dick said.
Abandoned fields
While Nimule does well from the constant flow of goods and people with Uganda, insecurity and displacement have left a diminished agricultural economy. The area could be very productive, according to agricultural workers, with crops including cassava, sweet potato, ground nuts and maize. But "people have deserted their fields" due to insecurity, Leonard Lagu of CRS told IRIN. It is a very difficult life for those who have to "depend on buying things from the market".
"We don't want to depend on this imported food," said Butrus. "When we see people depend on imported goods. I don't feel happy. If all become businessmen, who will buy?" he asked.
Social pressures
The roaring business atmosphere is also changing the social dynamics in the town and bringing new risks. Butrus is concerned about the rising number of bars and sex workers. "The worst enemy left is HIV," he said.
Drinking and inexperience contribute to a high number of traffic accidents on the improved road toward Juba. "All of them are learners," said Jeannot Wabulakombe, a doctor with the NGO Merlin, based in Nimule. In April and May, about 50 patients with serious traffic injuries were admitted to Nimule's hospital, one of the largest and best-run in Southern Sudan, soon to commission an X-ray machine.
We don't want people to depend on this imported food... if we all become businessmen, who will buy?
Typical health problems in the area include malaria, which accounts for 40 percent of outpatients. Diarrhoea, a sign of poor sanitation and hygiene, can develop into a dangerous outbreak of cholera or acute watery diarrhoea and the hospital is continually on standby - "it can come back at any time", Wabulakombe said.
The population is so mobile, Wabulakombe continued, that programmes requiring extended treatment, such as TB, face particular problems.
Returnees from Uganda and IDPs
An SPLA commander in charge of patrolling the road north of Nimule, Bol Dur, said the return of refugees from Uganda would help security, denying the LRA the uninhabited space that suited their tactics and need for unhindered mobility.
Local administration officials are also keen for refugees in Uganda to return, but remain wary of the demands they will place on limited local services and anxious about land disputes. Many refugees in Uganda and IDPs congregating in larger settlements because of the LRA risk will not return unless peace between the LRA and the Ugandan government is assured. Even those who have already returned face problems adjusting.
The difficulties of rebuilding a life in Sudan are daunting, compared with the constrained but relatively predictable existence as a refugee in Uganda. As a local aid worker in Nimule said: "In Uganda, you get free food, you dig, you are peaceful."
Nimule hosts a large population of displaced people of Dinka origin, who fled the civil war in the Bor region of Jonglei, to the north, in the 1990s. An NGO survey in April 2006 indicated that more than 20,000 people living in Nimule - almost half the population - were of Dinka origin. Over 2,000 left voluntarily with assistance last year. But the most recent organised operation to help more return to Bor fizzled out, local sources say.
Some of them are occupying land that returnees expect to reclaim, a local official said. "They don't want to get out," he said, confirming that a recent attempt to truck Dinka communities back to Bor had failed: "The vehicle went empty," he said.
A September 2006 report compiled by UN and NGO agencies said a concerted effort was needed to "avoid a simmering violence" in Nimule.
Local people say resentment toward the Dinka is based not only on land, but also the fact that key positions in the military, police and customs are held by Dinka, not by people from the local area, where the Madi ethnic group dominates.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Need for Zero Toleranceto Hunger, Poverty
Headline: Rwanda: Need for Zero Toleranceto Hunger, Poverty
Body_Text:
Kigali — Rwanda is a rural country with about 90 per cent of the population engaged in mainly subsistence agriculture. It is the most densely populated country in Africa. It is landlocked and has very few natural resources and minimal industry. Its main threat is primarily hunger.
Whereas the United States wants to build a system that will let it knock out incoming ballistic missiles, Rwanda should focus on "missiles" that would defend its people from hunger. We need to station radars all over the country to overcome the problem of hunger once and for all. Such efforts should be redoubled and refocused.
The case of the developed world is quite different from the one we experience in the poor and developing countries of Sub- Saharan Africa. Hunger is the most disturbing state a human being has failed to manage. A hungry person does not think, love or care but is full of hatred; he/she hates everything around him or her and is ready to commit any crime possible. Societies are only saved because the hungry are normally too weak to cause havoc to the expected degrees. Abraham Maslow's hierarchy of needs would help us in identifying priorities in fighting hunger as:
Physiological Needs
These include food, water, oxygen, etc. Anything the physical organism needs to survive is what should be our basic concern. Very fundamental life or death needs. You either eat or die of hunger and either drink water or die of thirsty. No short cut!
The former president of Tanzania, Benjamin William Mkapa once remarked:
"As a leader of a least developed country, I speak from experience when I say that poverty is too complex a phenomenon, and the strategies for fighting it too diverse and dependent on local circumstances, for there to be one silver bullet in the war on poverty.... Aid and trade are both necessary, but they are not enough on their own. Neither is good governance enough in itself. Above all, nothing can move without the direct participation of local communities. I fear that we lecture too much. This is not the best way (Tuesday, July 13, 2004 International Herald Tribune).
The fight against hunger need to be addressed in more specific manner and its approach must be a multidimensional one.
Can we have Zero poverty?
Rwanda is not food secure. Although we know better than ever what needs to be done, the complex situation of hunger equations are not even solved on paper. New and old food risks are looming in unholy alliances. So what needs to be done?
At this crossroads in the uncertain food situation, we cannot afford to make wrong turns. The government must back up their new declarations and promising policy initiatives on food security, with resources and policy action. Local authorities must get the support to play their critical roles. Agribusiness and the food industry must get the incentives to invest in poor people.
When you categorize the poor and the rich in the Rwandan context, you get the clear picture. How many are cash rich (Abakire-not our concern) and how many are food rich (Abakungu-our concern). We do not have the rich people acting as the food security of the whole country when there is a general famine and hunger.
Almost all families in Rwanda meet in markets to buy food to eat, be it in rural or urban areas, working class and/ or non working one, the relatively cash rich and the poor. Now the complexity of the problem is, who is the seller and who is the buyer, who is the producer and who is the buyer. How do we draw the dichotomy between the rural and urban people? This calls us to have a simple definition of the two terms for comparative purposes.
An urban area is an area with an increased density of human-created structures in comparison to the areas surrounding it. An urban area is more frequently called a city or town. Whereas rural areas are sparsely settled places away from the influence of large towns or cities. Such areas are distinct from more intensively settled urban and suburban areas, and also from unsettled lands. The two definitions leave Rwanda a unique country mainly because of its size. Rural areas in Rwanda do not have enough spaces and most of the look more of towns than villages. This contradicts the two definitions.
Right crop and right approach
Starvation is an unprecedented crisis, which calls for an unprecedented response. But why wait for crisis always? People do not know what they are supposed grow as crops to be food secure. You find many families planting cassava, potatoes, banana trees, etc. They all look like poorly maintained flower gardens! They therefore need help to be knowledgeable in terms of what to produce and how.
Our people are still in the traditional conservative agricultural ways. That is why they still produce the so called starch rich and heavy crops like cassava, sweet potatoes, etc. These were popular in the middle- age circumstances and where known as Ingandurarugo. The Kinyarwanda word meant the support of a family in food scarcity situations.
The government of Rwanda backed by the experience of the Chinese, has started an initiative to boost rice growing as a panacea to the ever increasing threat of hunger. But the initiative is not really promising and this calls for different alternatives.
The cereal crops of all nature should be given priority above all other crops. This calls for sensitising the population so that they understand the importance of the cereal crops. It calls too, for the change of culture-food.
Culture has to be dynamic and not static!
The use of all scientific methods of crop production should be applied. For sure there is no enough application of this scientific method in Rwanda. Scientists, agronomists and policy makers must be part and parcel of the next revolution in agriculture. The use of Genetically Modified crops should be not spared.
The hungry eat food dropped in dust bins or sleep without food for years and cannot be afraid if he/ she fed on food that is of GM nature.
We should not leave any bullet in the chamber as we fight against hunger and at the same time ant-hunger missile should be up to date. Then and only then will real progress down the road toward sustainable food security for all be achieved.
The writer is a lecturer at KIE

Original_Search_Title: Yar'Adua and the Nigerian project (2)
Headline: Nigeria: Yar'Adua and the Nigerian project (2)
Body_Text:
The recent wave of articles, stories and reports on what the Nigerian people expect from President Yar'Adua's term in office is a testimony of the pent up desires, which remained unfulfilled in the last eight years. The underlying theme in most of the literature on this subject is that of an enthusiasm or hunger, if you like, for a quick change or rescue from poverty.
The effusive proposals, ideas, exhortation, pleas and cautions to Mr. President in the media demonstrate the deep faith of many Nigerians on the eventual success of the Nigerian project, inspite of its apparent current state of failure. To all Nigerians, who can still keep such faith, I salute you and pray your dreams will come true!
I am a strong believer that Nigeria is a great country so bountifully endowed that it can only be a matter of time before we get our act together as a country and achieve our enviable potential as a nation.
In this regard, a series of articles, in this column in the recent past have been devoted to the search and identification of appropriate measures that can catalyze and support a self sustaining prosperous economy that would bring the majority of our people a much better life!
We have graphically demonstrated that the Nigerian economy would be positively transformed if we begin to use the instrument of dollar warrants or certificates to pay the dollar component of monthly federally distributable revenue.
This would crash commercial interest rates to below 10%, and together with a stronger naira to stimulate the Nigerian entrepreneurial spirit with positive chain reactions in areas of productivity, employment, income, social welfare and security.
We have challenged Mr. President to independently simulate this hypothesis with the current framework of the Nigerian economy and publish the results.
We have also urged President Yar'Adua to recognise that the adoption of a truly federal constitution is the sine qua non for justice, equity and peace in our nascent democracy. The Niger Delta will become a 'non issue'; groundnut pyramids will rise once more in Kano; oil palm, rubber, cocoa, cotton will once more become export earners, and millions of our people will be gainfully engaged throughout the country as each region discovers its dormant potential under a truly federal structure.
The threat of dictatorship will diminish and our wasteful 36 state structure can be reduced to six geographical regions with enormous savings that can be applied to improvement of local infrastructure and social welfare.
However, in the event that it may appear to be political suicide for President Yar'Adua to promote such a restructuring, we have recommended the development of specific physical infrastructure that would jumpstart the economy and improve the living standards of our people; for example, Mr. President should set himself a minimum target of 20,000MW of electric power generating capacity, and complete a comprehensive modern railway network before the completion of his first term in office.
We have also suggested that the destabilizing, debilitating, and the security problems caused by our huge burden of unemployment can be ameliorated by government commitment to the annual construction of at least 100,000 units of low income houses with predominantly local materials in each state for the next four years.
Nigerians were rudely jolted by the double barrel shots fired at the Nigerian people by the former President on the eve of his exit.
Indeed, if we should believe reports from the Federal Inland Revenue and the NNPC, VAT will further be increased to 15% by 2009, and petrol prices may rise by another N20/litre much sooner than that. The current irresponsible manner of infusing our nation's foreign exchange earning into the domestic economy has resulted in local petrol prices rising whenever crude oil prices rise in the international market; such cause and effect will continue to remove value from our incomes and cause distress to the masses so long as the government maintains this destructive 'monetization process'.
The glaring paradox also is that, while we are forced to pay higher fuel prices, the government continues to condone the flaring of our valuable gas resources, which, if appropriately harnessed, would cost a fraction of the price of petrol and moderate the inflationary spirals caused by price hikes of petrol, kerosene and diesel every now and then.
Even if President Yar'Adua does not recognize the huge economic benefit of the adoption of gas as a power source, he should, at least empathise with the people of Niger-Delta, whose lands and fresh water sources have been devastated by incessant gas flares!
Fortunately, our gas reserves will last, in spite of its current reckless waste, for many more decades, after the depletion of our increasingly expensive oil reserves; it would make eminent sense, therefore, for us to save our export and consumption of crude oil for the future when it would command much higher prices!
Mr. President should harness the full potential of this opportunity which could mean hundreds of billions of dollars for our generation and those unborn and consequently place an embargo on further gas flaring beyond December 2008. Before that date, our cars, lorries, generators, power stations, etc, should be retooled to also accept gas as fuel.
It is surprising that while over 20 licenses have been issued for new petroleum refineries (even though none so far constructed) there is no known application for gas liquefaction and distribution inspite of the tremendous people friendly cost and less damaging effect of this fuel on the environment.
The promotion and adoption of cooking gas for general domestic application will also reduce deforestation with positive impact on our traditional ecosystem and more healthful life for our people. In fact, this is a win-win project both in terms of revenue (from erstwhile flared gases) and a better life for our people and President Yar'Adua will have left a worthy and creditable legacy if he focuses on the adoption of gas for more of our energy needs.
The former administration quickly recognized the potential value of cassava for local consumption as well as for export; sugar cane is now also farmed for sugar as well as source of fuel, which becomes more potent when mixed in proportion with petrol.
