The economic benefits of both of these products together with other traditional export cash crops like groundnuts, cocoa, palm oil, cotton, rubber, etc should be methodically exploited by respective states with appropriate comparative advantage backed with a responsive and structured support and reward system by the Federal Government to ensure the achievement of our earlier glories with such produce.
Fortunately, the soil and climate have remained the same in the growing states and it would only require cooperation and commitment from the three tiers of government for us to once again have a multicultural export basket. Besides, Mr. President's attention to agricultural consolidation and expansion would put millions of Nigerians back to work and reduce the insecurity engendered by the increasing number of our youths who are jobless.
The cycle of income created by such employment will create its own demand and further stimulate commercial and industrial enterprise and lead to a generally better life for our people.
SAVE THE NAIRA, SAVE NIGERIANS!

Original_Search_Title: Food Fest At National Theatre
Headline: Uganda: Food Fest At National Theatre
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE thought of a food festival at the National Theatre had several tongues wagging over how much food would be on display.
What was meant to be an international food festival only showcased dishes from western, central and the northern regions of Uganda and some Nigerian dishes.
The presence of Fufu (Nigeria's staple dish made from fresh, steamed and pounded cassava) was the highlight of the day. Other Nigerian dishes included jollof rice, white rice, fried plantain and pepper soup.

Original_Search_Title: Logari's Offers Quality Traditional Dishes
Headline: Uganda: Logari's Offers Quality Traditional Dishes
Body_Text:
My first encounter with Logari's resto was several years ago when they were ensconced in a tiny space the size of postage stamp, somewhere in the Diamond Trust building. At that time, it seemed very much of a Jua kali operation with even something as rudimentary as tablecloths being absent. Nevertheless, the food was always an outstanding feature and the prices amazingly pocket-friendly.
Many years later, and having acquired far bigger premises in a very prime and key location, there is simply no let up in the quality of the food with the prices still very accommodating in this era of food and other related inputs costing a pretty penny.
The secret has to be based on turn over since the place is capable of accommodating a good 70 or 80 souls at any one time. Luo would appear to be the lingua franca and this should come as no surprise since the food is in the main from the North, as in Acholi land. Well, this is not a bad idea since the places that serve local food from Buganda all tend to be very parochial, not to speak of Indian or Chinese for that matter.
Last Saturday, in the company of my young brother and being in the vicinity of Logari's, we ventured forth and decided to have a meal. For some strange reason, the waitress could not fathom the request that we would share the millet since a double dosage would be more than we could both readily consume and sure enough she brought two orders.
Somehow, she got to understand the need to return one of them and so we ended up with smoked chicken, smoked fish and matooke, rice, sweet potatoes and a teaser of greens.
The rice was an awful let down being slimy and woefully small in portion whereas it was quite the contrary with the millet. The matooke was expertly done while the dry fish and chicken were in every sense of the word ample.
The trademark of the sauces at this joint is the interplay with a groundnut paste that is combined with ground sesame paste and this forms the sauce. Thus any chicken, meat or fish that have been charcoal-grilled are then stewed in this concoction.
People from that part of the world are very fond of this type of cooking and they never seem to tire of it. I would suggest that one orders a side dish of that formidable and wonderful sauce that is known as boo/gobe in ground nut and sesame paste.
For those who may not be in the know, boo/gobe are the leaves of a legume (cow pea leaves) that are best gathered when they are young and traditionally they are never washed. Rather, they are sun dried to get rid of the insects and then they are beaten so as to remove the sand. However, in a modern day kitchen, I would recommend that they be washed, dried and cut into small pieces.
Prompt service
I am glad to see that Logari's are doing well and continue to turn out food that is not only tasty but promptly brought to one's table. The décor is unimpressive and the waiters could be better attired and given a better command of what they are serving.
It would be nice to be informed that the only part of the chicken available is the back rather than have it served without any forewarning. One might wish to be served another dish. Oh well, such is the world that we live in that such common courtesy is the rule than the exception.
Place: Logari's Restaurant
Location: Buganda Road, opposite Central Police Station
Rating: X
Smoke-Free Zone: Not sure
Type of food: So called Afro-Nile
Price range: Snacks: chips and chicken Shs3,500, chips and liver Shs3,000, chips and sausages Shs2,500, plain chips Shs1,000, Katogo Shs1,000, sausages Shs600, chapatti Shs50. Lunch: smoked chicken Shs3,000, smoked beef Shs2,500, smoked fish Shs2,500, Angara Shs2,500, beef stew Shs2,500, chicken stew Shs3,000, goat stew Shs2,500, fresh fish Shs2,500, molokony Shs2,000, boo gobe Shs2,000, Malakwang Shs2,000 - all served with a choice of matooke, rice, millet, yams, pumpkin, cassava, posho, sweet potatoes
Recommended Dishes: Smoked fish, boo/gobe, smoked chicken
Service: The food gets there within minutes but the hired help tend to be sourly and generally not very helpful and as for those uniforms, surely they have seen better days.
Ambience: A lot better than when they used to be at Diamond Trust, still room for improvement.
Sound level: Not the best
Opening hours: Lunch everyday but never on Sunday.
RATING
XXXX: UNRIVALLED
XXX: EXCELLENT
XX: VERY GOOD
X: WORTH A VISIT
NONE: SATISFACTORY to POOR
These views are purely the reviewer's personal reaction to food, ambience and service with price being factored into consideration. The menu listing may change without notice.

Original_Search_Title: JCI Limbe Launches FCFA 3m Contest
Headline: Cameroon: JCI Limbe Launches FCFA 3m Contest
Body_Text:
The Junior Chambers International, JCI, Limbe Chapter, has launched an FCFA 3-million Best Business Plan, BBP, writing contest for young Cameroonians aged between 18 and 40.
The President of the Limbe Chapter, Takor Takor, broke the news of the competition during a press briefing recently.He said the competition was open to all interested young persons who have got some fresh business ideas that could be developed to the benefit the community around them.
As to the nature of the competition, Limbe JCI Public Relations Officer, PRO, Dona Forbin, said every competitor would be expected to explicitly put his or her idea(s) to paper, package and submit it either to the President at the DATA Enterprise Building downtown Limbe or to the PRO at the Limbe Council Chambers from June 1 to July 31.
"It could be a cassava transformation plan," she said. She further said that any competitor was free to submit more than one plan. "You must not be a JCI member," she said.Forbin also said that a jury shall sit after July 1, peruse the scripts and come out with a final verdict as to the best three projects.
The first prize would be FCFA 1,000,000, she said. In all, the PRO said some FCFA 3,000,000 would be shared to the winners in a grant awards ceremony programmed for September.
Takor Takor, 29, who is the Administrative Manager of the Data Enterprises in Limbe, said the aim of the JCI was to help the youths develop their leadership skills and be masters of themselves tomorrow.
"JCI is like a university. We encourage those between the ages of 18 and 40 to come up and test their entrepreneurial skills and develop them."We believe that any strong economy can only remain strong if it is matched by a strong private sector. That is why the economies of South East Asia are growing stronger and stronger by the day because of the strengths of their private sectors," said Takor Takor.
On a historical note, Takor Takor disclosed that the very first BBP competition took place in 2004 at the national level. This notwithstanding the Limbe Chapter had the pride of some of their members emerging as winners.
Past President of the Chapter, Carr Mafany, he said, earned FCFA 150,000 for a business plan on piggery, while one Rachel Angu picked the first prize of FCFA 200,000.As to how Mafany's piggery business has been faring, Henry Agah, 2006 President of the Limbe Chapter, said the project was flourishing.
"I know Mafany is a very rich man today thanks to that project," he said.Takor Takor said they would have to follow up the winners and ensure that they effectively use the money.
He also said since the competition requires only fresh business plans, there would be some experienced business persons or mentors to give guidance to the winners at any time they are in trouble of how to get

Original_Search_Title: Food Import Bills Reach a Record High
Headline: Africa: Food Import Bills Reach a Record High
Publisher: Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (Rome)
Body_Text:
Rome — Global food import bills are increasing, partly due to soaring demand for biofuels, according to FAO's latest Food Outlook report. Global expenditures on imported foodstuffs look set to surpass US$400 billion in 2007, almost 5 percent above the record of the previous year.
Rising prices of imported coarse grains and vegetable oils - the commodity groups that feature most heavily in biofuel production - account for the bulk of the increase. Import bills for these commodities are forecast to rise by as much as 13 percent from 2006, the report said.
More expensive feed ingredients will lead to higher prices for meat and dairy products, raising expenditures on imports of those commodities. In several cases, such as for meat and rice, larger world purchases are likely to drive import bills up.
In the case of sugar, generally high and volatile prices could lead to smaller import volumes, which is likely to result in a drop in the cost of global sugar imports, the report said.
Record-high international freight rates have also affected the import value of all commodities, putting additional pressure on countries' abilities to cover their food import bills.
Poor pay more
Developing countries as a whole are anticipated to face a 9 percent increase in overall food import expenditures in 2007. The more economically vulnerable countries are forecast to be most affected, with total expenditures by low-income food-deficit (LIFDC) and least developed countries (LDCs) expected to rise by 10 percent from last year.
"The food import basket for the least developed countries in 2007 is expected to cost roughly 90 percent more than it did in 2000," said FAO economist Adam Prakash. "This is in stark contrast to the 22 percent growth in developed country import bills over the same period."
Production up, but so is demand
World cereal production in 2007 is forecast to reach 2 125 million tonnes, up 6 percent from the reduced level in 2006 and higher than FAO's previous forecast in May.
"The prospect of a strong recovery in global cereal production in 2007 is a positive development, but total supplies will still be barely adequate to meet the expected rise in demand, not only from the traditional food and feed sectors but in particular from the fast-growing biofuels industry," said Abdolreza Abbassian, one of the authors of the report. "This means prices for most cereals are likely to remain high in the coming year."
FAO's tentative forecast for rice production this year stands at around 633 million tonnes, matching last year's record level, but with production still running short of consumption. Global rice reserves are forecast to shrink and higher price levels are anticipated.
Global cassava production in 2007 could surpass last year's record level, due largely to measures to increase utilization of the crop in the larger producing countries, especially for industrial usage, including ethanol production.
Oilseeds
Oilseeds and meal prices have continued to rise, largely due to surging feed grain prices. Unusually high maize prices are dragging up soybean prices as the two commodities are competing in both the feed and energy markets. First forecasts for the 2007/08 marketing season suggest that the steady growth in global oilseed production could come to a halt, however, as maize cultivation is likely to expand at the expense of soybeans.
Meat and dairy
Increased consumer confidence, following a reduced incidence of animal disease outbreaks in the past year, should result in a recovery in meat demand in developing countries in 2007, the report said. Global meat exports are anticipated to increase by 3.8 percent as trade bans are gradually lifted and markets return to more normal patterns.
Poultry prices have recovered after declining by 18 percent in early 2006, mainly because of outbreaks of avian influenza. By March 2007, export prices in the United States and Brazil, which together supply 70 percent of global trade, increased by 20 percent and 14 percent, respectively, from their 2006 annual averages.
FAO's meat price index has significantly recovered from a low in 2006 and, in March 2007, stood 7.6 percent higher than in March 2006. Moreover, rising feed prices are putting further upward pressure on meat prices, according to the report.
Prices of dairy products are currently at historically high levels. The FAO price index of traded dairy products has risen by 46 percent since November 2006. International prices for milk powders have increased most, as stocks in the European Union have disappeared.
The outlook for 2007 is for stronger growth in global milk supply, which may increase by 2.7 percent, sustained largely by expansion in those countries more responsive to international prices. Drought in Australia, suspension of milk powder exports by India, and Argentina's export taxes are restraining export supply in the short term. However, EU dairy policy reform is changing the structure of international markets as its export market share declines, creating opportunities for emerging exporters, the report said.

Original_Search_Title: Phone-Tapping, Mail Monitoring , Etc? Yeah, Right!
Headline: Uganda: Phone-Tapping, Mail Monitoring , Etc? Yeah, Right!
Body_Text:
Every once in a while, especially in the recent past, one hears tell of a security drag-net in some Western city in which terrorism suspects are a apprehended after lengthy periods of surveillance during which incontrovertible evidence in the form of intercepted telephone conversations, money trails and blood-curdling plots of mass murder are meticulously collected.
Confronted with such evidence in courts of law the suspects almost always own up and are put away for lengthy spells of imprisonment. During the last one year alone the London Metropolitan Police foiled several alleged plots to bomb significant landmarks in London.
Just last week four airport workers at the John F. Kennedy Airport in New York City were nabbed, allegedly for planning to blow up Airport fuel tanks and other strategic fuel pipes. If it had succeeded the plot would have caused an inferno of inestimable proportions.
In a world where all of us have become hostages of the blood feud between Israel and her backers on the one hand, and Palestine and her sympathizers on the other, there might be something to be said for the kind of universal and invasive surveillance first predicted sixty years ago by the novelist George Orwell, in the novel 1984, a drastic and terrifying portrait of the ultimate totalitarian world in which "Big Brother" - a euphemism for the State-watches and monitors every second of its citizens' lives via ubiquitous cameras and microphones, and where the penalties for the minutest transgression are draconian in the extreme.
George Orwell intended 1984 written in 1948 as a stern warning against the absolute and complete erosion of personal liberties and freedoms when the pursuit of political power becomes a consuming religion. As a result "Big Brother" today reigns supreme not only in the files and archives of the world's finest intelligence agencies but also in the intelligence networks of all cassava republics.
And so Uganda too is set to legalize clandestine surveillance by promulgating a law which will allow privileged security operatives to listen in on your telephone conversation, open and read your letters, and query any unusual money transfers on our bank accounts. If all that is going to prevent killing of innocent people anywhere in Uganda, then I am all for it.
Knowing the caliber of this country's intelligence operatives-from the General Service Unit of the 1960s, to the present Internal Security Organization and External Security Organisation of the last twenty-two years, nothing in Minister Amama Mbabazi's utterances reassures me that our cherished liberty will not be tampered with. I, for example, have always kept my E-mail a very tight secret, but this has not prevented conmen from sending me crazy schemes of instant wealth. And if one of them decides to send me an E-mail purportedly from Osama Bin Laden, what will happen to me?
Why not stick to the beaten path? Which is the cheaper option: to import obsolete gadgets at great expense for collecting information which is common knowledge in every public place, or to arrest suspects, clobber them into confession or commit them to some "safe house" to keep snakes and crocodiles company? What will happen to all that teargas? And all those water cannon vans? Who will the riot police beat?
In 1994 or thereabouts, Prince Charles was caught on audio-tape by an amateur phone-tapper telling Mrs Camilla Parker Bowles in a late night phone chat that he wished he was her tampon.
The eavesdropper sold this tape to a radio and television network regardless of the fact that the embarrassing contents had been made by the future king of the United Kingdom. That, of course, meant that the actual voice and transcripts of the conversation soon became available to all and sundry worldwide. Fellow citizens, brace yourselves for violations of a similar nature. "Big Brother" is watching you.

Original_Search_Title: Adopting Agricultural Biotechnology
Headline: Uganda: Adopting Agricultural Biotechnology
Body_Text:
By the time you read this article, it is likely that Genetically Modified (GM) bananas that have been imported from Belgium will be at Kawanda Agricultural Research Centre for field trial on their possible adoption in Uganda in a wake to introduce GM bananas crops.
Dr Yona Baguma, a Senior Research Officer National Research Organization (NARO) told agriculture extension workers, farmers' leaders and communicators at Lake View Resort Hotel on May 24 that the bananas were being brought for field trial in a week's time, from Belgium where a Ugandan engineered them during his PhD studentship.
"There are some GM bananas that are imported from Belgium that will arrive in Uganda next week. These bananas are going to be tested under confined field trials in Kawanda. The cultivars are not Ugandan so we are going to import the technology to see if it works in Uganda. If it works then we shall transfer it to the popular East African Highland banana cultivars. We have the capacity to this technology." Baguma said.
Government through NARO is building confined field trial sites at Mubuku (Kasese), Serere (Eastern) and Namulonge for testing genetic engineered Cotton, Cassava and crops hoping to adopt this technology after the trials on crops confirm they can grow well. Biotechnology is the modification and exploitation of living things or part of living things to produce new tools, goods and products for human use.
Through the advancement of technology, scientists have been able to develop more precise and powerful tools to produce crops and animals with selected trains that aim to benefit farmers and consumers.
Crops and animals are engineered to produce better ones, pharmaceuticals such as vaccines, antibodies and many other industrial raw materials. Baguma says application of agriculture biotechnology can help improve the nutritive value and productivity thus reducing, malnutrition, food insecurity and poverty with in Uganda. The substantial achievements, he said, being recorded with this technology in some developing and developed countries should not simply be ignored.
Most of the research about GM crops and commercial growing has been carried out in developed countries, mainly in USA, Argentina, Brazil Canada and Spain. Developing countries; India, South African and China among others have also spotted the potential benefits of GM crops and are now growing them.
Like her East African counterpart economy Kenya, Baguma said Uganda ought to put the policy in place and adopt agricultural biotechnology. And a delay in having a biosafety law will make Uganda vulnerable to illegal importation of Genetically Modified Organisms.
Lack of enabling policy
He said agricultural scientists at various centres in Uganda have been able to make genetically engineered cassava, maize and cotton among others but have not been exploited by farmers due to lack of an enabling policy. "Government should put in place biotechnology and biosafety policy to make Uganda utilize the technology for its national development." Baguma said. The policy that was drafted in 2004 is yet to be approved by the cabinet. The reasons for planting GM crops include but are not limited to increased food production, lower food prices, environmental conservation and improved food quality
By the year 2050, its projected that there will be 9.3 billion people living on this planet This means that less than 50 years world population is expected to increase by 3 billion. Feeding those people will mean massive changes in the production, distribution, and stability of food products.
Uganda's population is expected to increases from 28 million people to 47 by the year 2020. "We cannot sustain the country's food needs with conventional and organic farming. Our population is increasing and we need to supply them food in the next 50 years." Baguma said.
GMO proponents in Uganda say the crops do not only give better harvests, but also get effective protection from pests and diseases, bring more profit to the farmer as result of increased yields and conserve environment because of reduced pest cides use and tillage.
In the developed countries, GM crops have been integrated into commodity markets. According to a report prepared by the United States Development Agency (USDA) Advisory Committee on Biotechnology and 21st Century Agriculture (Opportunities and Challenges in Agriculture Biotechnology), in 2005, 52% of corn (maize), 87% of soybeans, and 79% of cotton planted in the United States were genetically engineered. Biotechnology however, requires some investment.
There is need for scientific capacity to assess the biosafety of the GM crops, economic expertise to evaluate their worth, regulatory capacity to implement guidelines for safe deployment and legal systems and punish transgressions in law. Therefore, the proposed National Biotechnology and Biosafety Policy should address such concerns especially building and strengthening national capacity in biotechnology research and development and providing a regulatory and institutional framework for biotechnology development and applications.
Much as the technology is becoming popular, there is a heated debate over genetically modified crops, featuring science, economics, politics and religion. In some countries, there have been increased risk management requirements as well as opposition to introduction of the transgenic seed varieties and the foods produced from those crops.
Some of the food processors and retailers have been reluctant to introduce food products developed from transgenic crops in markets due to perceived consumer resistance to genetic engineering technology, while other markets there is a requirement for mandatory labeling of food products.
The resistance stems from some consumers' perception that there are unknown risks associated with genetically engineered foods and an absence of obvious consumer benefits. The frequently asked questions are about quality of the out put, marketing and adoptability of the new crop varieties to the climatic conditions, and the crop impact on the human fertility.
Dr. Thomas Egwang, Director General Medical Biotech Laboratories Kampala says "Biotechnology will be an engine for Uganda's social economic development that would enable it catch up with the countries so far ahead of it." He said people who are ignorant about biotechnology are the ones bent on talking about its ill effects.
Despite the diversity of ethical issues in agricultural biotechnology, there is a need to understand beliefs and doctrines as this allows coexistence with in a cross societies, and prevents social conflict.
Technology's acceptance is based not only on technological soundness but also on how it's perceived to socially, politically and economically. A strong public awareness is therefore very necessary before the technology is finally taken up by the farmers in Uganda.

Original_Search_Title: Thirty-Five Tonnes of Foodstuffs Off to Oman
Headline: Rwanda: Thirty-Five Tonnes of Foodstuffs Off to Oman
Body_Text:
The Rwanda Investment and Export Promotion Agency (Riepa) has sent the first shipment of 35 tonnes of assorted food products to Oman. The products include cow ghee, honey, Agashya passion fruit juice, sweet bananas, cassava flour, and chili.
Vincent Karega, State Minister in charge of Investment Promotion at the Ministry of Commerce and Industry (Minicom), said the government is targeting the Middle East nation partly because it has about 3,000 members of the Rwandan Diaspora.
They want Rwandan products on their market, he said.
Karega said shipping agricultural products to the Middle East would encourage Rwandans to improve their production methods.
He also said it would help shift the emphasis from subsistence agriculture to commercial agriculture.
"This is a project under which we would want to change the lives and thinking of Rwandans towards production and eyeing the contract performance to enhance productivity," Karega said.
He said the shipments to Oman are also intended to target Omanis because of their standard of living.
"Their income, because of the wealth of their country, is very high," he said. "They will feel good about paying a lot of money to buy these exotic products."
Clare Akamanzi, the Rwanda Investment and Exports Promotion Agency (Riepa) Deputy Director in charge of Investment Promotion, said the agency will mobilise Rwandan farmers to increase production so that more products can be exported to the Middle East.
She said the order stems from a recent roadmap show in the Middle East, where Omanis expressed strong interest in consuming Rwandan fruits and vegetables.
The products will be transported by Silverback, a local cargo airline.
The shipment is part of the company's new development strategies to expand its market targeting local importers.

Original_Search_Title: Ohakim Declares War On Robbers
Headline: Nigeria: Ohakim Declares War On Robbers
Body_Text:
Owerri — Governor Ikedi Ohakim of Imo state has directed armed robbers in the state to repent and surrender their arms or recolate from the state.
Chief Ohakim who unfolded his New Face of Imo agenda in a state broadcast on Monday said that his government will address frontally the security situation of the state to create a conducive atmosphere for wealth creation which he said will be pursued vigorously.
He therefore begged all armed robbers operating in the state to turn a new leaf, surrender their arms and register at the new citizen's job centres that will soon be established by his government to provide jobs to them.
In the same vein he ordered those not willing to comply to quit the state because his government will use all means and power at its disposal to hound them out of the state.
Hear him: "if you are an armed robber listening to this broadcast, I have good news for you - provided you surrender your guns, ammunitions, cutlasses and all instruments deployed in your trade and surrender yourself to God. If you do this, your past will be forgiven and forgotten.
"If you are not willing to comply, then you better relocate from Imo state. And if there is no immediate marked decrease in armed robbery, you will have to come face to face with my government and there can be only one winner.
To this extent, governor Ohakim said his administration will in due course, set up a series of "neigbourhood watch" as part of civil security arrangement of his government but explained that the arrangement is not vigilante group.
The government nevertheless assured that the state will take the lead in our quest to set the South-East free from the shackles of poverty, infrastructural decay and underdevelopment.
According to him, the aim of his administration is to generate and create as much job opportunities as possible and also create enough wealth that can be shared by all without fighting, bitterness and rancour.
Unfolding his economic blue print, he disclosed that the launching pad of our economic take off is a radical, economic paradigm shift that will see the state moving from trading in goods to aggressive Imo enterpreneuship in the manufacturing industries for the production of high quality goods and products for export.
Government he said shall provide incentives to processing and canning industries that are designed to give value added dimension to our agricultural products such as cashed nuts and cassava.
He also stated that the state shall cease to be marginal players in the communications and oil and gas industries and shall no longer be patronizing mollified with crumbs. Handed to us from thwe huge oil revenue accruing to the Federal Government. He listed specific goals to be pursued by his regime in oil and Gas industry to include, ensuring that the state oil and gas potentials are maximally exploited to the benefit of the oil bearing communities and the state and with due regard to the preservation of the environment.
His government according to him will attract foreign investors to partner with us. For the establishment of Lube Oil and Petrochemical plants.
Besides, Chief Ikedi Ohakim stated that his administration will train and produce a hard core reservoir of professional oil and gas technocrats and technicians to participate effectively and in sufficient numbers in the strategic oil and gas industry, particularly upstream.
The governor who stated that the state economy is at the moment languishing in the doldrums of static underdevelopment however assured that he is going to put the state that he is going to put the state under the yoke of self reliance through self development.
Emphasizing on hardworking and innovation as the backbone of any economy, he explained that under his New face of Imo agenda the new work ethic for workers is zero tolerance for laziness, loitering, late coming, absenteeism and continue sick leave, warning that any hospital or medical doctor that issue fake sick leave certificate will face serious disciplinary action.
He disclose that his administration will soon launch a programme "Clean and Green Initiative" to restore Imo to its past glory as a clean state.
On education, Chief Ohakim disclosed that government will build three model schools in the three zone of the state and refurbish existing schools in terrible state of disrepair.
Urging the people of the state to be patient, patriotic and trust him he said that his government will soon constitute a council of Elders for the sate made up of elders statesmen and experience leaders whom he shall consult from time to time.

Original_Search_Title: From Humble Beginnings, Out of Poverty
Headline: Uganda: From Humble Beginnings, Out of Poverty
Body_Text:
Nabuule — FOR 12 years now, Amiina Nabuule has been financially independent. She shared her story with Anthony Bugembe
"My parents were very rich but refused to educate me. They considered boys' education more important. Girls were prepared for marriage.
I got married to a wealthy man in my home village, Kyaggwe in Mukono District. When I discovered that he was cheating on me, we separated. In 1995, I came to live with my sister in Makerere, Kavule. That was when I started frying snacks for a living.
Business success
I sell pancakes, samosas, cassava and potato pieces in addition to mangoes and bogoya (bananas). This business has been my only source of income.
I cannot borrow from any financial institution or women development group. Where would I get the security or money to pay back? On an average day, I make a profit of about sh4,000.
Work schedule
I wake up at 2:00am to peel the cassava and potatoes and mix the dough. Frying runs from 6:00am to 1:00pm. Then I attend to customers for the remaining part of the day up to 1:00am when I retire to bed.
Helping hands
My children are co-operative and understanding. Every morning before leaving for school, they walk to Kalerwe Market to buy cassava and the other ingredients I need. After school (4:00pm), they sell the snacks around Makerere University.
When I am sick, my children take over and run the business. I have trained them to handle the business.
The biggest portion of my earnings is used for feeding and paying school fees for my four children. My estranged husband refused to pay their school fees. I decided to do it on my own. The eldest is in S4, followed by one in P7 and another in P6. I also give each child sh100 daily to spend at school. Perhaps when the children are through with their education, I will build myself a house. I spend sh15,000 on rent every month.
Challenges
Sometimes not all the snacks are sold. At times some naughty university students eat them and refuse to pay.
Some guards also ask to be paid first before letting the children into the halls of residence or hostels.
I have gotten used to such disappointments.
If I hadn't, I would have wound up the business by now.
Working very hard, coupled with self-sacrifice, has made me successful. Despite my failing health and warnings from medics that I should not be exposed to too much heat, I continue to fry and sell snacks. It's our family's gateway to survival.
I also plan my time well. This involves waking up very early and retiring quite late. This has enabled me to remain financially independent for a long time.
Attracting and retaining customers is hard, but I have managed to attract them by establishing a good relationship with them. They address me as Jajja Muwogo (cassava), which to me is not an insult.

Original_Search_Title: From Clay to Cash
Headline: Uganda: From Clay to Cash
Body_Text:
Kampala — Joel Ogwang visited a community that earns an income from turning clay and metal into stoves.
HOLDING some wet clay, Mable Busingye's son, Isaac, imitates her. She is moulding pots and grills for charcoal stoves at her home in Kirinya village, Kito 'A' in Bweyogerere, opposite Mandela National Stadium, Namboole.
"He (Isaac) will be as hardworking as his father," Busingye says of the five-year-old.
She says she buys the clay she uses from vendors who transport it on trucks from Kakajjo, a nearby village.
For sh10,000, Busingye can get 20 bags of clay. Using stones, she crushes it into powder. And then she has to wet and soften it in preparation for moulding.
"I buy eight jerrycans of water to soften the clay," she says. Each jerrycan costs sh200.
Busingye, who goes about her work with a swiftness that is breathtaking, also parts with sh8,000 to hire men to trample on the clay mixed with water.
"I tried trampling on the clay to save the sh8,000, but didn't get a fine product," she says.
When the clay is mixed and ready for moulding, Busingye has to find a way to keep it properly since she can't use it all at a go. "This," she says, pointing to a kaveera (polythene bag) she is using to cover some clay, "helps keep the clay wet for up to a month."
Busingye makes at least 50 grills daily. When she is done moulding them, she has to dry them. This has to be done in a shade because "if you lay the grills under direct sunrays, they crack".
Whenever a grill develops a crack, it is destroyed.
About 90% of the households in this village make pots or stoves. Like any other venture, the stove business involves specialisation. "I make only the metallic exterior and buy the clay grills which I insert, then sell a complete stove," says Hassan Ntale.
A P5 dropout, Ntale sells each stove at sh4,500. His customers come from Kampala, Mukono, Wakiso and Bweyogerere. Ntale, who earns sh150,000 a month, says: "My customers buy in bulk and sell each stove at between sh5,000 and sh10,000."
Ntale and his wife live in a two-roomed mud-and-wattle house, for which he pays monthly rent of sh25,000.
"I will be living in my own house by the end of this year," he says. Ntale also pays fees for his four siblings, in addition to supporting his mother in Mubende.
Ntale's neighbour, Jane Takuwa, usually buys a tipper-full of clay at sh15,000.
Her 12 sons and daughters do the crushing, wetting and softening of the clay. "Why should I hire someone when I have men and women around?" she asks.
Takuwa's family make stoves which are entirely made of clay.
The family moulds 400 stoves a week. "I make 60 a day," says Julius Muwereza, Takuwa's eldest son.
The stoves are dried in a grass-thatched house. "It is very cool in there," Takuwa says.
"This keeps them from cracking."
The stoves take a week to dry, then Takuwa has to smoke them. She spends sh70,000 on firewood to do that. "It takes four hours to smoke all 400," says Rashid Musoke. "We normally do it at the weekend."
"They can easily break during the smoking," says Takuwa, who has spent 12 years in this business. She sells each stove at sh500.
"Much of the money goes to feeding and medication," she says. She also caters for her eight grandchildren.
"Your income is so small compared to the size of your family, can't your children find alternative jobs?" I ask.
She pauses for 30 seconds, before she retorts: "Do you have one for them?"
Her sons, she says, tried to do petty jobs in Bweyogerere, but quit because of exploitation.
Yusuf Mukiibi says he tried to work at a construction site, but got disgusted and left. "I was mixing sand and cement, but one time my boss said he couldn't pay me because he was, after all, giving me free learning experience," he says.
Middlemen buy the stoves at sh500. They re-sell them to retailers at sh700. Muganga, a trader in Mukono town, who buys the stoves at sh7,00, says: "I sell each at sh1,000. But during the Christmas season, I sell them at sh1,500 each."
In spite of the free primary and secondary education, many children in this community don't attend school.
"We only survive on making stoves. If my children were away at school, who would make the stoves, and how would we survive?" Takuwa asks.
Kato, a father of six, says by the time he makes 45 stoves, his back is aching. "If I had something else to do, I would have quit (this business)," he says.
Middlemen also make the business a challenging one. "Sometimes they take the stoves on credit and don't pay for a whole month," says Musa.
Ntale says his business is risky. "I almost lost my finger while cutting the metallic frames," he says.
In spite of their hard work, most people's living conditions have not improved.
The majority live in mud-and-wattle houses, roofed with papyrus mats and polythene bags. Only the so-called tycoons have houses roofed with iron sheets.
Sadly, a lot of hard-earned cash is spent on smoking and consumption of alcohol.
To boost their earnings, some residents grow cassava, maize and potatoes.
It is tough going, but they know success doesn't come cheap, so they are not giving up.
A driver himself, Bosco Mabonga's wife makes sigiiris. "Her earning is too little but the good thing here is that she's kept busy," he says.
John Sebugwawo, who makes pots, says the costs of clay rise by every day that passes.
He earns sh150, 000 from selling pots who prices range between sh1, 000 and sh18,000 depending on the size.
"We have a group of five people. We pool funds and buy the ebbumba," says the father of eight. "It's very expensive buying it alone."
Smoking and alcohol consumption of, especially Malwa, is high. This drains their hard earned cash.
Each family here has an average of four children. "Every child is an additional burden. I don't know what I was thinking by producing eight children," says Sebugwawo.
They may not look successful, but there is clear commitment to work by residents of Kirinya 'A' zone. They say success doesn't come cheaply, so they aren't giving up.

Original_Search_Title: Agreement on Agricultural Research
Headline: Mozambique: Agreement on Agricultural Research
Body_Text:
Maputo — The Mozambican government and the European Commission (EC) on Friday signed in Maputo a protocol under which the Commission will disburse 4.3 million euros (about 5.7 million U.S. dollars) to fund agricultural research, primarily on crops such as cashew, cotton, cassava and potato.
Mozambican Minister of Agriculture, Erasmo Muhate, and the European Commission delegate in Mozambique, Glauco Clauzuola, signed the protocol.
According to Saturday's issue of the Maputo daily "Noticias", the research activities will be carried out by Mozambique's National Agricultural Research Institute, the National Cotton Institute and the Cashew Promotion Institute.
Speaking during the signing ceremony, Muhate explained that selection of those crops took into account their relative importance for improving the living standards of the Mozambican population, as well their significant contribution to the country's Gross Domestic Product and balance of payments.
According to the minister, cotton and cashew are traditional sources of foreign currency in Mozambique and make a major contribution to the income of peasant families, while cassava, because of its drought-resistant qualities, is an important crop to ensure food security.
Potatoes are also among the group of crops that are currently the subject of research, due to the growing demand in urban centres. Also, the Mozambican authorities believe that expanding domestic production of this crop will reduce the level of imports.
For his part, Glauco Clauzuola, explained that this funding would contribute to food security, improve production levels and the income of small farmers.
Clauzuola stressed that the success of this programme will depend largely on guided research, the production of improved seeds and the promotion of new technologies.

Original_Search_Title: Inflation Closes At 8 Percent
Headline: Uganda: Inflation Closes At 8 Percent
Body_Text:
Kampala — Uganda's inflation rate for the fiscal year 2006/07 will close at a high of 8 per cent, buoyed by higher prices of water and electricity, fuel, food items and other essential commodity goods.
The Uganda Bureau of Statistics Principal Statistician, Mr Vicente Musoke Nsubuga told journalists at a press conference on May 31 that it was "too late" for the inflation rate to reduce to the 5 per cent benchmark set by the government.
"We are left with only month to the end of the fiscal year and there is no way the inflation can come down to 5 per cent even if all things settle at zero. It is already too late," he said.
He said the higher inflation rate was attributed to the prices of goods and services, which he said have been high from the beginning of the year. He said government policy in measuring the overall inflation rate relies mainly on underlying inflation, which has been high throughout the year.
The Consumer Price Index (CPI) indicates that the underlying inflation dropped to 8 per cent down from 8.9 per cent registered in the month of April. Mr Nsubuga said May, fuel prices and transport fares went up in all centres following an increase in fuel prices on the international market.
In addition, an increase in the prices of washing soap was registered in some centres because of reduced supply. But on the other hand, the annual headline inflation rate for the year ending May 2007 dropped to 4.2 per cent compared to 6.0 per cent that was registered for the year ended in April 2007.
The decline in the inflation rate was mainly attributed to lower prices of most staple foods, some fruits, milk and charcoal in most centres in May. The monthly headline inflation rate for May also went down by 0.2 per cent compared to an increase of 2.6 per cent that was recorded in April.
Mr Musoke said most centres registered reductions in prices of a number of food items.
The figures show that prices of staple foods especially matoke, sweet potatoes, fresh cassava and fruits such as passion fruits, sweet bananas and oranges were lower in some centres due to increased supply.
The centres of Mbarara and Masaka registered the highest decreases in prices of food crops.

Original_Search_Title: Inharrime District to Process Coconut Oil
Headline: Mozambique: Inharrime District to Process Coconut Oil
Body_Text:
Inharrime — The district of Inharrime, in the southern Mozambican province of Inhambane, is to start refining coconut oil within a month.
So far, the district has been exporting raw coconut oil to various places within the country and to the southern African region.
"We are now setting up a refinery, which must be completed within a month", said the factory's owner, Naimito Mussa.
Mussa was speaking on Thursday to reporters accompanying President Armando Guebuza on his tour through one of the major copra producers in the country.
Mussa did not give any figures for the costs of this investment, but he said that, when the refinery starts functioning, the state will gain a lot in terms of taxes.
He said his company has the capacity to process one tonne of copra per hour, which will give half a tonne of coconut oil.
The factory will employ 60 workers from Inharrime, and guarantees to purchase its copra from local producers. It is buying copra at prices varying between 4 and 4.4 meticais a kilo (there are about 25.9 meticais to the US dollar at current exchange rates).
One of the main constraints that used to face industries in Inharrime was the lack of a reliable power source. This has now been solved with the extension of the Mozambican national electricity grid, based on the Cahora Bassa dam on the Zambezi.
A second industrial unit in Inharrime produces cassava flour, using technology imported from Brazil and Germany. About half a tonne of cassava flour a day is produced, which is mostly sold to local health units.
The importance of these two factories was stressed by Inharrime traditional leaders when they briefed Guebuza shortly after his arrival.
The fact that the government has distributed cattle to many families in Inharrime also deserved a positive remark on the part of these leaders, as well as from participants at the rally addressed by Guebuza on Thursday.

Original_Search_Title: How Trade is Mending Fences Along Rwanda-DRC Border
Headline: Congo-Kinshasa: How Trade is Mending Fences Along Rwanda-DRC Border
Body_Text:
Kigali — Rwandans living on the border with DRC in Rusizi District Western Province are doing brisk business with their Congolese counterparts. This can explain why almost all Rwandan banks Banque Commercial du Rwanda (BCR), Banque de Kigali, BANCOR, BCDI and most of all Banque National Rwanda (BNR) are found at the border town of Kamembe.
The Congolese, armed with their hot dollar, buy almost every foodstuff from the Rwandan side of Kamembe town, namely: meat, cassava flour, tomatoes, onions, banana, rice and fish among others. This explains why food is expensive along this border area compared to other parts of the country, including Kigali. In fact, the entire town of Bukavu in DRC (almost the size of Kigali) is fed by food from the Rwandan border town of Kamembe.
The Congolese Merchants along this border area bank their money in foreign currency in Rwandan banks on daily basis, after transacting their businesses. Asked why they don't bank the money in their Bukavu Banks, the Congolese who preferred anonymity says,
"Rwandan banks are more secure than our own banks on the other side of the border in Bukavu."
One may ask where the food to feed the entire population of Bukavu and Rwandans along the border comes from. Rusizi District produces a variety of foodstuffs like banana, rice, onions, "Isambaza" fish, Cassava and Irish potatoes from Gisenyi in Rubavu District among others. To meet the high demand of food at this border town, other Rwandan Provinces supplement the demand. These include the Eastern Province, which supplies cattle for meat from Umutara, the Northern Province, which supplies more Irish potatoes from Ruhengeri and the Southern Province that supplies cassava flour and beans.
Many neighbouring countries also supply food to this tiny but densely populated town of Rusizi District. These include Burundi, which supplies cassava flour and "Mikeke" fish; Tanzania supplies "Indagara" fish, which comes all the way through Rusumo border via Kigali to Kamembe town. Uganda is the major source of maize flour and industrial processed long lasting milk. This is why one meets many heavily loaded trucks and pickups while driving to Cyangugu
On the other hand, the Rwandans residing along the border cross to Bukavu to buy industrial products such like "Bitenge" textile materials, radios and TV sets; Nido powdered milk, Mobile phones, Wines and Liquors, Sugar and "Amamesa" type of cooking oil among others. Many wholesale and retail traders can be seen crossing Rusizi 1 and 11 to Bukavu in the morning hours and return at mid-day or late in the evening with heavy luggage, containing various industrial products. Asked why they don't buy these goods from Kigali, one resident who preferred anonymity said:
"Kigali is far and the transport costs cannot make me realize any profits."
Besides business transactions along border areas, the Rwandan International Airport at Kamembe serves as a gateway to the outside world for Congolese residents at Bukavu and even beyond. Many use Rwandair express to Kigali to connect to other international Airlines such as SN Brussels Airline, Ethiopian Airline and Kenya Airways, to Dubai and other European destinations.
In addition to business transactions, many Rwandans residing along the border cross to Bukavu to study in secondary schools and other institutions of higher learning there.
It is a common sight to see students crossing to Bukavu in the morning hours and return home in Rwanda in the evening.
There are many Universities at Bukavu that offer full and part time education services to both Congolese and Rwandan students. Bukavu University (UOB), African Evangelist University (UEA), Bukavu Institute of Education (ISP), Rural Development Institute (ISDR), Bukavu Catholic University (UCB), Institute of Finance and Commerce (ISECOF) and Bukavu Medical Institute (ISTM) are among the institutions that offer services.
This has led to the mushrooming of hostels that provide accommodation for the growing number of students. Many Rwandans resident at the border go to study in DRC town of Bukavu because it is near than other Universities located in Butare, Gitarama or Kigali. Besides, many people who operate their businesses at Kamembe and other border areas find it convenient to study at Bukavu than other parts of Rwanda.
The many economic activities that bind Rwanda and DRC along this border town have helped to improve the political, economic and social relationship between the two countries. However, the Interahamwe militias who committed Genocide in Rwanda in 1994 are still operating in Kanyola of Walungu zone, about 20 KM from Bukavu town. They pose a big threat to the security of both Congolese and Rwandans along the border areas.
On many occasions, these Interahamwe militias have been reported on DRC RTNC (Radio Television Congolese), as having raped women, raided and killed Congolese people and committed other atrocities. Their inhuman activities are a threat to the relationship between Rwanda and DRC.The African Union and the International Community need to intervene to get rid of the Interahamwe remnants still active in the South Kivu region of DRC.

Original_Search_Title: Zim, Indonesia Sign Empowerment Pact
Headline: Zimbabwe: Zim, Indonesia Sign Empowerment Pact
Body_Text:
Harare — ZIMBABWE and Indonesia on Monday signed an agreement on women empowerment in Jakarta to strengthen co-operation on women's economic capacity, exchange of information, knowledge and technology.
Indonesian Embassy spokesperson Mrs Ita Mappajarungi said the signing of the Memorandum of Understanding between the two countries was a follow-up to the letter of intent on the same issue which was signed in August last year when Indonesian State Minister of Women Empowerment Professor Meutia Hatta Swasono visited Zimbabwe.
Prof Meutia Hatta and her Zimbabwean counterpart, the Minister of Women, Gender and Community Development, Cde Oppah Muchinguri, signed the agreement.
Prof Swasono said women in Africa and Asia comprise more than half the total number of women in the world and the welfare of the community depended on their achievement.
"This is one of the reasons why women should be given bigger opportunities to improve their lives," she said.
"The empowerment of women is an effort to eradicate discrimination against women and to strengthen women's capacity so that they can play a bigger role in the development of the community."
Cde Muchinguri said the agreement offered Zimbabwean women a chance to learn from Indonesian women's skills and the technology they use to add value to raw materials.
Women in Zimbabwe, she said, could, for example, learn how cassava is processed into different kinds of ready-to-eat food in Indonesia, thereby enhancing their income.
Cde Muchinguri said women in Zimbabwe had scored many successes over the past 27 years.
This progress, she said, included the increasing number of educated women (90 percent of literacy among women), the passing of the Domestic Violence Act and women's rights over land.
She also hoped that the agreement could be a foundation for the enhancement of communication and strategic co-operation in the field of development and women empowerment.
Prof Meutia Hatta and Cde Muchinguri plan to establish a joint working group to formulate priority programmes which will cover empowering women economically, promoting women's role in politics and public office, gender mainstreaming and protection of women.
Minister Meutia Hatta added that this co-operation will become an important part of the New Asia Africa Strategic Partnership.
She also hoped that the meeting of the NAASP will be held simultaneously with the Asia Africa Conference Meeting.
Cde Muchinguri is leading the Zimbabwean delegation which arrived in Jakarta, Indonesia on May 24.
The delegation consists of several senior members of her ministry and the Minister of Lands, Land Reform and Resettlement Program, Cde Flora Buka.
During their stay in Indonesia, the delegation will visit Mother and Child Health Centres in Jakarta, handicraft centres in Bali and hold meetings with the board of the Association of Indonesian Women Entrepreneurs in Jakarta and business women in Bali.

Original_Search_Title: Save the Mother!
Headline: Uganda: Save the Mother!
Body_Text:
Nairobi — ELIZABETH NAKKU WAS pregnant when one day, as she was walking to market in Kireka, a suburb of Kampala, she collapsed.
Good Samaritans took her to a local clinic, but the nurses there declined to handle the patient and, instead referred her to Mulago Hospital.
Nakku, 28, had had a successful first delivery with assistance from a traditional birth attendant and so had assumed all would be well with the second pregnancy.
When she was diagnosed at Mulago, the medics said her problem had something to do with poor diet, leading to anaemia, a condition caused by iron deficiency. A blood transfusion was then administered.
Loss of iron in women increases during pregnancy and iron tablets are administered if the condition is not serious, says Victo Nabuule, a midwife at the obstetrics and gynaecology emergency annex ward at Mulago Hospital.
"Most pregnant women do not know what to eat," she said. "Some suffer from malaria and become anaemic, they bring them here when they are 'paper white' and very weak."
Iron deficiency prevents oxygen from being carried in the blood.
Ms Nabuule said lack of a balanced diet makes pregnant women weak and vulnerable to infection. It can also make them give birth to unhealthy babies and suffer excessive bleeding during childbirth.
Excessive bleeding from pregnancy related complications in Uganda accounts for about 26 per cent of deaths in childbirth.
On average, 16 women die of pregnancy-related problems every day in Uganda, said Dr Olive Sentumbwe-Mugisa, an official of Family Health and Population at the World Health Organisation.
Although the problem of maternal and child mortality cannot entirely be solved through nutrition, scientists believe biotechnology can reduce the number of children lacking Vitamin A and Uganda's maternal mortality, which stands at 505 deaths per 100,000 live births.
BIOTECHNOLOGY MAY ALSO play a role in combating diseases such as HIV/Aids and it is set to become integral to future advances in medicine, particularly in vaccines.
Ugandan scientists have now embarked on a biotechnology project to increase micronutrients in staple foods like bananas, maize, cassava and sorghum in order to give pregnant women, HIV- infected people and young children a chance to eat a balanced diet.
Dr Geoffrey Arinaitwe, a plant biotechnologist at Kawanda Agricultural Research Institute, said under the project, genes will be introduced into banana cells to increase the micronutrients.
"All bananas have micronutrients in very small quantities," said Dr Arinaitwe. "This project will increase by tenfold the amount of nutrients in bananas, which I believe will prevent early childhood deaths and maternal mortality."
The bananas will look like normal bananas on the outside but with increased yellowing of the pulp.
The project, which started a year ago, will fortify bananas to make them an important sources of Vitamin A, B and iron.
In sub-Saharan Africa more than three million children under the age of five suffer from blindness due to lack of Vitamin A.
Deficiencies of iron, Vitamin A, protein and zinc are ranked among WHO's top 10 leading causes of death through diseases in developing countries.
The project, supported by the Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation and the Queens University of Technology in Australia, is working on improvement in food quality for health.
Ugandans do not get enough iron in their diets from the two main staple foods - bananas and maize - that they consume. The per capita consumption of bananas for Ugandans is 1 kg per day.
More than 50 per cent of the blood collected from donors in the country is used to save the lives of anaemic children, while 25 per cent of transfusions is done on pregnant women with childbirth complications.
Vitamin A deficiency is prevalent in the developing world and mostly in countries with the highest rates of child mortality like Uganda. It leads to blindness and weakens the immune system.
Bananas are sources of potassium, contain vitamin C and B6 and provide soluble fibres.
Heads of state at the African Union summit meeting in Addis Ababa this year endorsed a 20-year biotechnology action plan calling for co-operation among African nations in specific regions to bolster biotechnology research and address biosafety issues.
In 2006, the global area of approved GM crops grew to 102 million hectares, the first time more than 100 million hectares was under such crops in a single year.
The number of farmers growing GM crops exceed 10 million, 90 per cent of them being small-scale, resource-poor farmers from developing countries whose income from these crops contributed to poverty alleviation.
In the same year, the number of countries planting GM crops increased to 22, up from 17 in 2004; of these, 11 are in the developing world.
Forty per cent of the global GM crop area in 2006 was in developing countries where growth was higher - at 21 per cent - compared with 9 per cent in industrial countries.
The most important potential benefit of GM crops will be their contribution to the Millennium Development Goals of reducing poverty and hunger by 50 per cent in 2015.

Original_Search_Title: Reflections - Nigerians Since 1999
Headline: Nigeria: Reflections - Nigerians Since 1999
Body_Text:
Lest We Forget' reads the banal headline which ably and serially chronicles the landmark achievements of 8 years of Obasanjo administration in critical sectors of the economy that include agriculture, finance, health, Niger Delta, information, education among others.
A visitor from the outer space reading these colourful advertised achievements would undoubtedly assume that Nigeria is already one of the leading 20 economies of the world and that we are indeed in the promised year 2020 and not 2007! With 7 per cent growth rate in agriculture, emergence of rice pyramid and Nigeria as the world's largest cassava producer, the impression is that Nigeria has banished hunger.
In fact uncritical reading shows that we are already in the age of food security and self sufficiency. The statistics from Obasanjo Legacy mill with respect to health and Niger Delta are even intimidating enough. With 100 million Euros to "equip" and "upgrade" 8 teaching hospitals to "Five Stars" status and 200 Ward Health Centres, (WHC) a reader would have been made to believe that Nigeria has met critical four Millennium Development Goals (MDGs) on health, namely Goal 4 that seeks to reduce child mortality by two third between 1990 and 2015, Goal 5 that seeks to improve maternity health by reducing maternity mortality rate by two third and Goal 6 that Combats HIV/AIDS, malarial and other diseases.
The former civilian President Alhaji Shehu Shagari during the weekend singled out the growing militancy in Niger Delta as the Nigeria's biggest challenge. Former President Shehu Shagari must have missed out the advertised pull out on Obasanjo Legacy with regards to Niger Delta which shows that since 1954, Niger Delta had never had it so better. According to the ad, the past eight years had witnessed a "New Deal" of improved revenue (26% to 44%), dualization of roads, and electrification of 396 communities and the launching of N20 trillion development plans. With this alluring statistics of progress and development, Niger Delta emerges to us more as a success story than the "biggest problem" that President Shehu Shagari discerned. And that's the bane of the past eight years.
The bane of governance under President Obasanjo is that governance had been with some "Hard" factors as blocks and bridges, renovations and foreign reserves building, bank consolidation and cassava production, and not with critical "Soft" issues such as peoples and their welfare, leadership and followership, Nigerians and their problems.
As important as hospital blocks and stones are, the critical question is that how have Nigerians fared in physical and social wellbeing in the past eight years? It would be recalled that high profile accident-victims and patients have been jested out of the country for treatment since 1999 up to date, despite hospital renovations and commissioning.
In fact it is today an acceptable fact that high profile Nigerians die abroad. The recent list of late included former Chief Justice of Nigeria, Justice Mohammed Bello, General Abdulkarim Adisa, legendary musician Chief Osita Osadebe, Sheik Lemu etc. If the notables had such a raw deal to die abroad, then the plight of the unknown Nigerians is better imagined. This is the grim reality of the collapse of the public health sector under Obasanjo which makes advertised achievements some legacy to be mere illusion.
On education, a day before the exit of President Obasanjo university students are at home and not in school on account of crisis of academic staff compensation that this government incompetently could not resolve. It will go on record that in 1999 whatever it is worth, Obasanjo met university students at school and he is leaving them out of school. In spite of his advertised licensing of private universities, (as if they are substitutes for public federal universities) it is an open knowledge that Nigerian parents compete today to get admission for their children in Ghana, South Africa which says so much about the so-called educational reform of this government.
It is based of these critical "Soft" issues that a government should be judged and not some miserably advertised "Hard" factors. Of what relevance is teaching hospital where teaching is not taking place? Of what relevance are renovated hospitals that lack patronage on the account of crisis of service delivery? The advertised achievements of Obasanjo administration are miserably below expectations such that the "achievers" themselves have to miserably remind us "Lest We Forget".
If the transformation and changes are so significant and dramatic in the areas of education, health and energy, we need not be reminded for the people themselves would be the real advertisers. The truth of the matter is that there has been considerable welfare loss for Nigerians in the past eight years.
Nigerians since 1999 are worse off not due to lack of movement in growth rate, government revenue, GDP, road building, telecom expansion, bank consolidation etc. but in spite of those things that have been done. The bane of the last eight years is bad, unfair and unjust leadership which presides over so much opportunities and excitement yet leaving behind, mass poverty despair, factory closures, power outages, hopelessness and general national apathy.
South Africa's Nelson Mandela is today (even after long years of retirement) a model case in global leadership. But Mandela has not been assessed based on miserable "Hard" factors like dualization of roads, mobile telephones, renovated hospitals, catching of party chiefs (anti-corruption) because those things are taken for granted under a good governance anyway. Mandela has been assessed and commendably judged by critical "Soft" issues such as offering leadership that reconciled a multi-racial society to move from violence to peace and social justice, to move from idle capacity to productivity, poverty to prosperity.
It is a sad commentary that President Obasanjo who came in with such unprecedented national acclamation and support is bequeathing to his successor a divided political class, controversial electoral outcomes, multiple litigations, nationwide strikes, avoidable militancy in Niger Delta and general crisis of legitimacy.

Original_Search_Title: Remembering the Obasanjo Years
Headline: Nigeria: Remembering the Obasanjo Years
Body_Text:
Port Harcourt — "Let me make a solemn pledge before all of you, before the whole world and before God, that I will devote all my energy and all I possess in my power to serve the people of Nigeria and humanity."
"Nigeria has no business with poverty. With our human and material resources, we shall strive to eradicate poverty from our country"
- Olusegun Obasanjo.
The above quotes capture the strength of mind and zeal with which General Olusegun Obasanjo set to pilot the affairs of Nigeria. So much has been said about his personality, leadership style, successes and failures. As the only privileged Nigerian alive or dead to serve Nigeria for 11 years by May 29, some of the more discerning perceptions about him take polarised views of what seems to be their opinions on his divisive personality.
There are those who believe that Obasanjo is the worst thing that has ever happened to this country. Those who hold that belief, and quite passionately at that, do so in spite of the cheering comments made elsewhere especially among the comity of nations as evident in the message of commendation and congratulations to Obasanjo (for breaking transition jinx) and Yar'Adua (for emerging victorious in the April election). These letters came from no less a nation than the G8 countries.
When Obasanjo emerged from solitude in 1998 and subsequently emerged a top contender in the Presidential race in 1999, as a fresher then in the University, I had argued with my senior colleagues that Obasanjo was the man to beat. As a reasonable student of African history, I had insisted that Obasanjo had enormous goodwill and charisma. He gained international respect through his efforts to end white minority rule in South Africa and Zimbabwe, supporting neighbouring states such as Angola and Mozambique.
After that epoch making interventions, the retired General became an intellectual of sort through such platforms like the Eminent Persons Group, Transparency International and his African Leadership Forum with great, thematic, lucid and seminal publications to his credit -- 'My Command', 'Not My Will' and lately, 'This Animal Called Man' among others. He also accepted places on numerous international commissions after which he retired to his second home at Otta where he enjoyed his image as a man of the people.
I also went further to remind my listeners that he was the first Nigerian Military leader to hand over power willingly to civilians against all odds -- a feat that was replicated by General Abdulsalami Abubakar 20 years after. His ebullient personality and popularity cut across all continents traversing languages and cultures. For instance in Universite Nationale d'Abommey Calavi in Benin Republique, students of Government and Politics wrote long essays as finalist on the leadership ethos of Obasanjo as well his viewpoints as encapsulated in many of his celebrated books and essays -- in one of these occasions, as a French Language student in Benin Republic, I was privileged to tell my Beninese Friends how Obasanjo abhorred dictatorship, snag in leadership, injustice and elitist regime.
A Nigerian fellow also reasoned that the masses would have it so good in view of the fact that Obasanjo just emerged from the dungeon of human depravity.
One thing we cannot take away from Obasanjo's eight-year rule is his administration's genuine attempt at effecting serenity in the nation's economic sector and the empowerment of the country's business heavyweights. With the liberalization of the capital inflow in the economy, we can still go ahead to mention some decent attempts by the Obasanjo's administration to strengthen the private sector with confidence built among the business class.
There was also spirited endeavour at curbing the waste of resources. For instance, the fight against corruption has become one of his major efforts as far as changing the status quo is concerned. The anti-corruption czar has also become an epitome of generational-shift - miracle which has also confirmed my long standing belief that; only young men and women can redefine our collective destiny not gerontocrats.
However, this singular but unpatriotic act of neglecting the poor has remained the albatross of Obasanjo's eight-year rule despite what seemed like a good intention as captured in the above quotations. The administration has failed to effect a remarkable change in the areas of road construction, the condition of an average Nigerian has worsened with Mr. President's inability to provide electricity and basic infrastructure despite the huge resources sank in these sectors.
Also another area that affects common man that was utterly neglected was the area of security of lives and property. The situation is frighteningly disturbing. A friend recounted his grief-stricken story on how they were attacked along Benin -- Ore road in quick succession with different gangs operating freely within a 'captured territory'. The same horrible experience repeated itself while they were going back.
Also Obasanjo's days paid less attention to the rule of law. The inability of his administration to prosecute and detain the self-confessed electoral fraudster of Anambra state and the 'political juju man of Ibadan politics' leave so much to be desired. Rather, these detestable characters and marauders were gratified by Obasanjo whose penchant and brazen romance with detestable and amorphous characters has watered down his professed good intention to rebuild Nigeria of our dream. This is sad!
In Nigeria, the economic situation is so inclement, mixed up, and excruciatingly strangulating with so much confusion in the air. The people are hungry, wages are not being paid. One always wonders if the outgoing administration is aware of this. The masses are daily harassed by a combined forces led by landlords and school owners because no low-cost houses any more for retrenched civil servants and public schools have become a breeding ground for charlatans; in fact they are a complete right off. Unemployed youths in an effort to assuage their hopelessness take up arms to wreck havoc and destruction on the weather-beaten and terminally frustrated populace.
While unemployment is not a yardstick to be a rascal, the situation calls for a state of emergency. Obviously these issues were constantly being addressed by newspaper editorials and columnists. If Obasanjo had paid attention to these words of wisdom, he would have been guided in adopting masses - oriented agenda.
Despite the huge resources embedded in the Niger Delta, the region has become a case of long disputes between the Nigerian state and the region which obviously means something is fundamentally wrong. With all the grandstanding about the regional master plan and the visionless NNDC, the incoming administration must accept wholesale the way Obasanjo accepted in the power sector, that all the while, wrong approaches have been adopted in solving the region's problem.
That tells any neutral observer that the retrogressive impulse in the region today is as a result of a bottled-up anger, boiling frustration, agonized powerlessness, misguided priorities as the case of NNDC and consequential irrelevance of the people which when put together brewed the present state of lawlessness, hostage-taking, pipeline destruction and bombing of senior government officials' properties.
To check the aforementioned, all efforts must be put in place to encourage the agricultural sector in the region. The people must go back to the farms and the rivers for their traditional trades. It is disheartening to note that despite the outgoing government huge investment in agriculture in other part of the country, Niger Delta is neglected. It is worthwhile to extend such integrated agricultural programmes particularly the cassava initiative to the Niger Delta with full intensity.
As Napoleon Bonaparte rightly said, ability is of a little account without opportunity. The next administration can create leadership opportunities for the young, strong and vibrant in Niger Delta and elsewhere as against the inter-generational perpetuation of gerontocracy and 'babacracy' that characterised the outgoing administration. The little opportunity Obasanjo gave the vibrant youth class proved to be very effective.
As the duo of Yar'Adua and Goodluck Jonathan plan to stir the ship of Nigeria to the next level, Nigerians are eager to witness a government agenda that will have the masses as its driving force. If there is any area that Nigerians may not wish the incoming administration to consolidate on, it is the National Poverty Eradication Programme (NAPEP). That agency has failed woefully.
Guaranteed electoral reform that secures complete independence of INEC is non-negotiable. That ultimately will guarantee free and fair election in the future. A radical approach to youths empowerment by creating an enabling environment for leadership generational change, generate 10,000 mega watts of electricity for industrial development to create employment opportunities and finally check the spate of armed robbery and assassinations via a holistic police reform. With the foregoing, I wonder what my response will look like when confronted with questions about the new Obasanjo.
Happy retirement Sir!
James wrote in from Port Harcourt

Original_Search_Title: 'Farmers Not Meeting 10 Percent Cassava Requirement for Bakeries'
Headline: Nigeria: 'Farmers Not Meeting 10 Percent Cassava Requirement for Bakeries'
Body_Text:
Abuja — Farmers in Nigeria are currently not meeting the 10 percent requirement for cassava flour content stipulated for bakeries by the federal government. The government last year directed bakers use 10 percent of cassava in their flour.
Minister of Commerce and Industry, Dr.Aliyu Moddibo Umar who made this known at a one-day national convention on cassava stakeholders at the weekend in Abuja said the supply of the commodity has been poor because the private sector "had not demonstrated commensurate efforts by establishing more processing facilities to turn the cassava into industrial raw material for the use by industries".
He revealed that flour millers have already made N500 million available to entrepreneurs to access and set up cassava flour processing mills with a "guaranteed market for their products".
Dr. Moddibo said cassava production has increased since the government directive, yet "we are unable to meet even the 10 percent requirement for cassava flour content for bakery nor process enough cassava chips and pallets to satisfy the export market".
He said one of the issues militating against the cassava industry is adequate publication on the commodity and trade networks.

Original_Search_Title: Group Chides ING Proponents
Headline: Nigeria: Group Chides ING Proponents
Body_Text:
Lagos — The criticisms trailing the call for an Interim National Government (ING), following alleged widespread irregularities of the April general elections, continued at the weekend, when a group, the South east Alliance Mandate (SEAM) described the proponents of the idea as coup plotters.
The group, at a press conference addressed by its Chairman, Chief A.E.S Njoku, said such a call was uncalled for, just as he urged Ndigbo both within and outside the country to distance themselves from such "evil machinations".
"We condemn totally the call for the constitution of an Interim National Government (ING) as unconstitutional and tantamount to a coup and call for a violent overthrow of a legitimate government either out-going or in-coming. The call also potrays the proponents as anti-democratic elements and intrinsic military apologists since ING is associated with military dictatorship or war raven nations-Nigeria can never be one," he warned.
Speaking alongside Chief Paul Ogbonna, Njoku said "the call by the same segment of people for the cancellation/annulment of the just concluded nation-wide elections is not only anti-democratic, but a declaration of state of anarchy and second Nigerian civil war in the country".
