According to him, "we implore Ndigbo in particular and Nigerians in general not to allow themselves to be used as canon fodders on the purported mass action/protest by some segment of civil society and unsportman opposition parties".
The chairman explained that "Ndigbo and other Nigerians who are largely victims of the first civil war and the 1993 election annulment should dissociate themselves from this evil and sinister call by people, who wear democratic garments but with the heart and blood of despotism, dictatorship and incivility in them. Do not be deceived a second time".
While commending President Olusegun Obasanjo for laying a solid foundation for the growth and development of the nation, Njoku noted that his reform mechanisms has put the nation on a sound socio-economic and political footing within the comity of nations.
"We also want to express out gratitude to the out-going President of the Federal Republic of Nigeria and Commander-in-Chief of the Armed Forces, Chief Aremu Olusegun Obasanjo (GCFR) for the wonderful reform programmes witnessed within the eight years of his administration vis-avis introduction of GSM, bank recapitalisation, debt relief, anti-corruption crusade, fight against fake drugs, agricultural development that has placed Nigeria as one of the largest producers of cassava in the world today, and most importantly, enduring democracy by the coming handover on May 29, 2007 from one civilian administration to another which is the first of its kind in the annals of history in Nigeria," Njoku recalled.
Decrying the recent mudslingings against the personality of the Deputy National Chairman (South) of the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP), Chief Olabode George, Njoku said instead, George has contributed immensely to the growth of the party in Lagos and across the nation.
According to him, "Lagosians and PDP followers must not forget the position of the party in 1999 prior to the ascension of Chief Olabode George to the leadership. For instance, PDP in 1999 had no single member in the Lagos State House of Assembly and other South Western States, talkless of producing governors and national Assembly legislators".

Original_Search_Title: The Forgotten People of Lamu
Headline: Kenya: The Forgotten People of Lamu
Body_Text:
Nairobi — With a population of less than 2,000, the nearly extinct hunter and gatherer Boni community has remained almost the same for centuries, writes Athman Amran.
Whistling melodiously, he looks around. After a short while he gazes into the horizon.
Noticing my perplexed expression, he smiles and says: "That is one of the ways of communicating with the birds that help us to locate honey."
Mr Nuri Abala is a Boni, a nomadic tribe of hunter-gatherers.
Boni women and children at Kiangwi village, Kiunga Division in Lamu.
The Boni love honey. Usually, the small and rare bird responds to the whistling by taking off in a particular direction as the honey hunter tracks it.
Finally, it would settle on a tree and dive up and down - an indication that it had found honey. The hunters extract the honey from beehives with their bare hands.
Community is on the verge of extinction
They do not light a fire to smoke the bees out as other people do. They believe they are resistant to bee stings.
The Boni are on the verge of extinction. They are scattered in small communities of about 200 to 300 people in five locations in Lamu.
The people considered to be of pure Boni blood are estimated to be less than 2,000.
Abala is an elder in a 400-member strong community in Kiangwi, an almost inaccessible village in Kiunga division, Basuba location in Lamu East constituency.
The village is next to the Dodori National Reserve. Other Boni villages are in Mararani, Mangai, Basuba and Milimani locations, each with about between 200 and 400 inhabitants.
These villages are scattered from Pandanguo in Lamu West to Witu in Lamu East.
A nomadic tribe, the Boni are constantly on the move. The Government has tried to encourage them to settle in one place but they have resisted because of harsh living conditions and superstition.
The only available vehicle is an army truck
To access Kiangwi we took a powerful two-engine speedboat from Kizingitini village on Pate Island past treacherous mangrove forests.
It took a skipper who knows the area well and a one-hour to reach Kiangwi. It is easy for one to get lost in the mangrove forests or the vast Indian Ocean.
There is an alternative land route but it is arduous. The only available vehicle is an army truck, which takes one hour from Mokowe, in mainland Lamu, to Bargoni village.
This is followed by a ten-hour walk to the village. The truck often labours on as it traverses marshland and bushes.
And it is not always available to transport villagers. Villagers in the entire Lamu archipelago travel by boat or donkey.
Most residents walk from one village to the next for hours. The only motorised vehicles in Lamu are the District Commissioner's Land Rover, a tuk-tuk (Scooter taxi), an ambulance and a tractor. There are about one or two privately owned motorbikes.
Boni are suspicious of visitors
Boni elders, Mr Nuri Abala and Mr Lafa Suruva talk to The Sunday Standard at a remote village of Kiangwi, Kiunga in Lamu. Pictures by Athman Amran
One can walk for up to 15 hours from Kiangwi village to Mokowe.
On our arrival at the village, curious, skinny and half naked boys and girls crowded the beachfront. Visitors are a rare phenomenon.
At hand to guard the villagers were two shabbily dressed "home guards" armed with G-3 rifles. They escorted us to the village.
The Boni are suspicious of visitors and it took time to convince them that we meant no evil.
But they were reluctant to discuss their way of life. The Sunday Standard learnt that the community at Kiangwi abandoned their migratory ways five years ago.
Before then, communities moved when a tribesman died. The tradition is fading. Other reasons for moving include the search for fresh water and hunting.
Living on the edge of starvation
Although they live close to the sea, some old men and women do not eat fish as they prefer wildlife meat.
"Some of us now eat fish but traditionally the Boni were hunters," Abala explains.
Using bows and arrows, the Boni hunt for wild pigs, antelopes, dikdiks, buffaloes and elephants. They are fearless and walk fast and stealthily.
On a hunting expedition they can follow their prey for more than two days without rest.
With the number of wild animals dropping, some of the communities are now living on the edge of starvation.
The people who have settled down are attempting small-scale farming. They prefer to grow cassava, potatoes and millet.
According to traditional lifestyle, Abala says, women stayed around the home to gather potatoes, wild nuts and leaves while the men hunted.
Strict marriage traditions
Strict marriage traditions are one of the reasons why the Boni are threatened with extinction. It is only recently that they have started to intermarry, especially with the Bajunis.
The Bonis speak Hargan, a language that is close to Somali. Only particular clans are allowed to intermarry.
Some of the clans, which inter-marry are Anurbala, Awirbade, Awirdina, Uta, Berchira, Mdoyo, Itane, Argalo and Nyurto.
Before marrying, an individual must prove he is a "man" by killing either a lion, buffalo or an elephant.
Then he is given the title of "Miso" and he is usually greeted "Nawala Miso" which identifies him as a hero. Following a kill the hero is feted with the beating of drums and dancing. Young women smear his head with coconut oil. He is then adorned with beads around his wrists and neck. He is ready to marry.
But heroism does not end there. The man has to kill a buffalo the first time his wife becomes pregnant. The wife eats the meat to ensure good health for her and the baby.
Women have to undergo circumcisation
With the second kill the man now gets an additional title of respect - "Ofya" and is addressed by the title "Nawala Ofya".
For a woman to be worthy of a brave man, she has to be circumcised.
Mr Alfa Suruva, a Boni elder, says a woman can only be eligible for marriage after circumcision. A marriage does not take place before the man invites village elders for tea to make known his intentions.
This could be done when the woman is aged only five. Then the man pays "bride price" in instalments in the form of packets of sugar, maize flour, tobacco, meat and other foodstuffs until the agreed amount is completed.
But the man could lose the girl if one of the relatives changes their mind. To forestall such an eventuality, the man would plan with his peers to "kidnap" the woman while on her way to fetch water or to buy vegetables.
Traditions are dying out
If the alarm is raised and the "kidnappers" are caught, the chances of ever marrying the woman diminish.
But if the abduction succeeds, the parents of the bridegroom visit the parents of the bride pay for the "blood". This is because the woman would have been deflowered.
The kidnapping can be forceful or it could be stage managed by the couple, especially when they love each other.
A woman who is married without being kidnapped has a special wooden stool in her homestead. Both Abala and Suruva say the traditions are dying out.
Some of the residents we interviewed would not openly acknowledge that they hunt because they fear getting into trouble with the Government. The State has pushed the Boni out of Dodori forest, where most of the wild animals are found.
At Kiangwi, the Government has ensured the Boni settle down by building a djabia (an underground rainwater storage facility) through the Constituency Development Fund.
Money rarely changes hands
But due to scarcity of rainfall, the water from the facility is not adequate. Each household gets a free 20-litre can of water once a week.
The other sources of water are two boreholes, whose water is saline. Despite these efforts, life in Kiangwi village is not easy. There is no shop and money rarely changes hands. Maize flour, sugar and tealeaves are rare.
Only the few who dare to walk long distances to Bargoni come back with the commodities after selling crabs and cucumbers. Some of the Boni women sell tobacco in Lamu Island.
Children do not go to school. The nearest school is at Mangai in Pate Island, a five-hour walk away.
A new nursery school block near the beach is abandoned. Teachers from other parts of the country refused to teach in the area.
The sick do not go to hospital since the nearest health facility is in Lamu Island. It is accessible by walking for about 15 hours to Mokowe before taking a 20-minute boat ride.
On landing at the Lamu jetty, a patient has to walk for another 45 minutes to reach the Lamu District Hospital.
"If one of us gets seriously sick we just wait for him or her to die," Suruva says.

Original_Search_Title: Seeds of Capacity Building in Agriculture
Headline: Ethiopia: Seeds of Capacity Building in Agriculture
Body_Text:
Addis Ababa — The International Day of Biodiversity was commemorated around the globe last Tuesday.
Plant diversity around the world is under threat from modern crop improvement, habitat loss and disasters, both natural and man-made. Many scientists argue that guaranteed funding for the world's gene banks is essential to preserve biodiversity and to secure food supplies for the future. In this article I deal with efforts to fight hunger in Africa by preserving seeds and by boosting research into improved crop varieties.
In the face of impending climate change, many fear that Africa, already the world's poorest continent, will be hit hardest in its ability to produce food. Success in preventing food shortages in Africa will be achieved only if farmers maintain a wealth of seed diversity that can cope with ever-changing rainfall patterns. In recognition of this, new initiatives are emerging that will hopefully bring about a green revolution, and ensure food security in Africa.
Starting from seeds
Seed banks have been identified as part of the solution. They preserve seed diversity, and can provide the raw genetic material to develop improved plant varieties.
In April this year, the Global Crop Diversity Trust and the United Nations Foundation announced a joint initiative to safeguard 21 of the world's most critical food crops by preserving their seeds.
The Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation, whose five-year plan aims to provide African farmers with improved and adaptable crop varieties, has emerged as a major source of funding for the initiative, putting forward US$37.5 million in grants.
The initiative will cover many 'orphan' crops important to the poor but largely neglected in modern plant breeding such as sorghum, millet, yam, cassava and cowpea.
The initiative will also fund a comprehensive global information system that will allow plant breeders everywhere to search gene banks worldwide including existing banks in Ethiopia, Rwanda and the southern Africa region for traits needed to combat new diseases and cope with climate change.
"The initiative will secure at-risk collections [of important food crops] in poor countries and document their astonishing diversity, making it available to meet the food needs of the poor," said Cary Fowler, executive director of Global Crop Diversity Trust.
Bumper crops
Current initiatives are not just about saving current crops; there are also plans to improve them.
A new partnership between the Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation and the Rockefeller Foundation has allocated US$150 million to improving seeds, including cassava, millet and sorghum, through conventional breeding to increase their yields and make them suitable for Africa's unpredictable rainfall patterns.
This work will decrease farmers' dependence on hybrid maize seeds, which need sufficient rainfall to grow and already do not yield enough maize.
The partnership is working with policymakers in African governments, non-governmental organizations, African centers of excellence and donors to bring about a green revolution.
At the Rockefeller Foundation meeting on biotechnology, breeding and seed systems for African crops on 26 March this year, Venancio Massingue, the Mozambican minister of science and technology, said, "Seed breeding is key to the modernization of our economies through agriculture, and to providing jobs both in rural and urban areas. This is why science improves the lives of people."
Crop Diversity Trust
Drought-tolerant crop varieties are already starting to emerge. Mick Mwala, head of the University of Zambia's crop sciences department, says they have already come up with new wheat varieties that are drought-tolerant.
One of the partnership's two initiatives, the Alliance for a Green Revolution in Africa, will help breed improved seeds and distribute fertilizers to improve soil health in Africa, as well as supporting projects to improve water resources and the distribution of farm produce to the market.
The second initiative, the Programme for Africa's Seeds System, will help distribute these improved seeds and adaptable crop varieties to smallholders.
Chemical controversy
Scientists involved in the initiatives believe that improving seeds to resist drought and using fertilizer are the most effective ways of ensuring a good harvest.
But this approach has caused a stand-off with Africa-based non-governmental organizations who claim that Western countries are pushing for a corporate-controlled, chemical system of agriculture in Africa.
In a signed statement, several non-governmental organizations such as Ethiopia's Africa Biodiversity Network, Uganda's Centre for Development Initiative, the Kenya Organic Agriculture Network and Kenya Genetically Modified Organisms Concerned attending this year's African Social Forum in Nairobi, Kenya (25 January), rejected the Gates-Rockefeller initiative.
They called the initiative a "new foreign system that will encourage Africa's land and water to be privatized for growing inappropriate crops for export, bio-fuels and carbon sinks, instead of food for African people".
But Roy Steiner, the Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation's senior programmes officer, told SciDev.Net that the foundation's focus is to bring about a sustainable green revolution through seed breeding and improvement in Africa.
"We need to find ways to interact with small scale farmers. It is a long road but we have to make progress. The possibility is there and the potential is there," he said.
People power
Another obstacle to the success of these initiatives is the scarcity of qualified African scientists to create these new seed varieties.
Africa faces problems with funds to train enough scientists, and to provide them with attractive salaries and contracts.
Brain-drain is also decreasing the size of the science community. Many qualified scientists have already migrated to greener pastures.
The Regional Universities Forum for Capacity Building in Agriculture (RUFORUM) is trying to address this problem.
The organization made up of 12 eastern and southern African universities, led by Zimbabwe's African University and Kenya's Kenyatta University, will launch an initiative in August this year to provide postgraduate programmes in agriculture-related fields such as aquaculture and fisheries, agricultural resource economics, food science and nutrition and dry land resource management.
Trust
They hope to secure financial resources to support more scientists to masters and doctorate degree level. Those scientists graduating under RUFORUM-sponsored programmes will be given jobs in research institutions, boosting research capacity.
The development is part of current efforts by African institutions of higher learning to build capacity for Africa within Africa.
RUFORUM regional coordinator, Adipala Ekwamu, said the 12 African universities are collaborating to accelerate agricultural research and biotechnology development in Africa. Up until now, RUFORUM had been supporting training in agriculture-related fields only to a Master's degree level.
"We need a new institutional framework to make universities more responsive to emerging challenges in the region and to respond to those challenges in a national and regional development paradigm," said Ekwamu.
The success of RU Forum's initiative, however, still depends on how much African policymakers support the organization financially, he said.
"We are not asking for gigantic funding but a little that will keep our programmes moving. Sixty per cent of our scientists in the region will soon retire and so we need funding to continue training more," Ekwamu said.
Seeking support
So far ministers from Malawi and Mozambique have publicly said they are in support of science and technology as the only means to improve people's lives.
Massingue said his government has set aside over US$30 million for seed and fertiliser distribution, and will work side by side with RUFORUM to increase the amount of research and training for scientists.
Kainja Kaluluma, Malawian Minister of Women and Child Development, said science and technology is "our engine in national development and the Malawian government will support scientists" and that the government would give increased support to research, technology and training.
In an era where economies are driven by scientific and technological developments, no single country in African can ignore science and still expect to thrive.
The continent of Africa has the basics - land and water - to produce enough food for its people. Combined with initiatives to train scientists, to develop seeds and to improve farmer's access to this technology with support from African policymakers, the battle against hunger in Africa could be won.

Original_Search_Title: Professors And Bishops Have Become Soldiers of Fortune
Headline: Kenya: Professors And Bishops Have Become Soldiers of Fortune
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Is it possible that one can hope to find a corrigible institution in a generation that seems to love to bury itself in avarice, debilitating hypocrisy and dishonest doublespeak?
We read in the Good Book about Saint Peter saying to a cripple, whose habit and practice it was to sit and beg outside the Beautiful Gate of the Temple in Jerusalem: "Silver and gold have I none, but this I say to you - in the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth, rise up and walk."
But, today, are our holy shrines and academies equally enjoined in factional pursuit for silver and gold, with the tribal leader as the head hunter and a later-day untouchable tin god? Do silver and gold seem to be as precious to the churchman in every corner of the republic and to the university academic on every campus as they are to the ordinary unschooled hedonist?
It would seem that the Church of Christ in Kenya today would mind less that someone was committing sin than it would mind about who was committing the sin. If it is 'one of our own' then it is all right. That seems to be the philosophy. Pious greed and learned avarice, therefore, travel on hand in hand as spiritual and intellectual hunter-gatherers of material fortune turn a blind eye where they should be keeping the nation informed on what ails it.
For my part, I have this week been lumbered with a plethora of irate intellectual email. My sin is that I dared wonder on this page last week why our scholars should display the same primitive appetites as the ordinary hunter-gatherer - the lot who collects and hoards even what he has no use for. If the scholar should be a hunter-gatherer, one would think that he would hunt in the jungle of knowledge and store in the granary of scholarship?
But not so any more. Plato of ancient Greece once drew clear distinction among three segments of society. There were those whose lot it was to gloat and eat. They drew their life force from raw instinct and raw appetite. They hunted, gathered and ate. Sometimes they even looted. Plato saw them as the limbs of society. They were innocent and rarely concerned with anything beyond what sheer instinct could address.
Next to these hunter-gatherers was a lot driven by emotion and raw passion. These are Plato's second category of the human soul and of society. Their life force comes from hot passion. Very simply put, they act and react purely on the basis of sensation. Reason and logic have no place in their scheme of things.
Finally, we have the philosopher-kings, the thinkers and the voice of reason in society. These would be the men and women of the academy. Their lot is to exercise the mind - to think for society and to rule, for, as the poet Okot p'Bitek would say, the intellectual is the ruler. He rules people's minds and hearts.
But how will our literati claim to be our philosopher-kings when they succumb to the same appetitive drive and to the raw passion of the ordinary man and woman? How can they be kings when they resort to such mundane 'reasoning' that because everyone else is greedy and tribal they are also entitled to descending to that level?
Kenyans have missed the train of transformative leadership and reform at virtually every boarding point. What do our scholars and spiritual superiors have to say for themselves? In 1992 some university professors and churchmen were peddling themselves as Youth for Kanu. Others called themselves scholars for Kanu while others offered prayers for Kanu. But they were basically hunters of fortune salivating after Kanu's election moneybags. And where they were not following Kanu's moneybags, they were worshipping outside empty tribal shrines opposed to Kanu. Without any serious persuasion beyond wanting to see their tribesman in the Big House on the Hill, they accused Kanu and Daniel arap Moi of all manner of ills, both real and imaginary.
The problem was not that what Moi and Kanu were doing was bad. The problem was that it was not their tribal surrogate in charge. Today Moi is a retired old man, eating yams and cassava in Sacho. What do we say about the continued bloodletting in our country? In the 1990s we blamed Moi for everything that went wrong. While defeating his Uhuru Project we sang everywhere that all was possible without Moi. With hindsight, we now know just how so wrong we were. The men and women of letters and our spiritual superiors who should tell it to us, as it is, have become conspirators. They have taken their place on the tribal queue, waiting for the droppings from the tribal high table. The latter day professor and the bishop are largely soldiers of fortune, just like your regular dogs of war.
Like the ordinary yodel from the tribe, the professor and the bishop are money and hero worshippers. They alternate between an empty tribal cave and a decadent tribal shrine. They are as protective of the tribal chief priest as is the ordinary tribal yodel is. Like the later, they believe that the tribal chief priest will bring bounty to them and to the tribe, at the expense of the rest of the citizenry. The quest for truth and righteousness wherever they may be found has been replaced by the quest for hedonism wherever it may be found. We are left to say with Alan Paton of South Africa, Cry the Beloved Country.

Original_Search_Title: Open Letter to Umaru Yar'Adua (2)
Headline: Nigeria: Open Letter to Umaru Yar'Adua (2)
Body_Text:
Enugu — THESE days, Nigerians spell words and punctuate very badly, and these Nigerians include holders of PhD in English. Even government literature contain words like "gotten", "offense", "program", "organization" and "labor". And Ministry of Transport has suddenly become Ministry of "Transportation"!
This is almost unfortunate. Can we not have standards, which we and our visitors must abide by in our own country? When people went to study or live in America, Europe and other places in the world, was the idea for them to come back with junks and disorganise Nigeria?
If we are not careful in this country, we will not be able to understand one another any more as everyone will be speaking and writing whatever and however he likes and claim that he was taught so by some alien. And before you know it, one of those junkies will come up with the "smart" idea to do away with examinations and tests altogether. Without rules and standards, you couldn't have examinations and tests, anyway. And law and order would be meaningless. We must banish the "anything goes" culture from Nigeria.
It is erosion or lack of standards that has made it impossible for us to be law abiding, to produce things, and for things to work here. Without removal of cultural junks, intellectual junks, material junks, ideological junks, theoretical junks, junk attitude and behaviour, and other junks from Nigeria, nothing will work and we will continue to consume only that produced by others, however, unsuited their products might be to our environment and aspirations.
Your Excellency, Nigeria has no foundation; the country still needs to be designed and anything existing but out of place in this design would either disappear by itself or must be pulled down. For example, as we have British English standard in Nigeria, no one (not even computers) should be allowed to come here or influence us from outside with non-British English spellings, construction or pronunciation. And for as long as we produce oil and gas in Nigeria, we must never have shortage of these energy commodities. And that no one will export cassava when the Nigerian market is short of cassava products like garri and fufu. And that electricity supply must never be interrupted.
The foundation I talked about, therefore, can be seen as a set of guiding principles which, if followed faithfully, would produce a great country. Without such guiding principles and faithful adherence, we would continue to grope in the dark.
I urge you not to see the next four years as any other four years in the history of Nigeria - years of contractors and public servants colluding to waste our resources on white elephant projects and meaningless jamborees. I urge you to seek to remake Nigeria, starting with a real constitution based on sound principles and written for Nigerians (not for a man or a group of men) and for eternity. And structures of all tiers and arms of government that will work, i.e, encourage production, stimulate demand and consumption, and promote love, and happiness among Nigerians. With simple but principled steps, life for Nigerians can easily become meaningful and enjoyable.
I am sure none of what I have said here would accord with the wish list of your coordinators and the groups of solidarity visitors or courtesy callers you have been receiving since you were declared winner of the presidential contest. But, I hope your eyes will be on history and not necessarily on the demands of individual Nigerians. I wish you a history-making tenure.
Dr. Ogbuagu, a Development Philosopher, writes from Iheuwa Centre, Enugu.

Original_Search_Title: Banana Wilt Worries Kyenjojo Residents
Headline: Uganda: Banana Wilt Worries Kyenjojo Residents
Body_Text:
Kyenjojo — Farmers in Kyenjojo fear for famine following an outbreak of banana wilt disease. Bugaaki Sub-county councillor Resty Kabasomi asked the council to quickly address the issue.
"The disease is threatening to destroy all our banana plantations," Ms Kabasomi said.
She was speaking during a council meeting recently.
The councillors asked the production department to deploy staff in Bugaaki sub-county to advise farmers on how to control the spread of the disease.
In his report, however, the production coordinator Baziraki Ntawera said measures to control the disease were in place. Over 80 per cent of residents depend on bananas for food and income.
Meanwhile, farmers in Kabarole District have received improved planting materials from Kawanda Agriculture Institute.
The National Agricultural Advisory Services coordinator for Hakibaale Sub-county Michael Businge told journalists recently that they distributed 72 bags of cassava cuttings and 120 bags of potato veins.
According to Mr Businge, the materials were distributed to farmers under the government programme of ensuring food security in households.

Original_Search_Title: Jonas Mva Mva,  "We Have Reduced Use of Chemicals by 40 % "
Headline: Cameroon: Jonas Mva Mva,  "We Have Reduced Use of Chemicals by 40 % "
Body_Text:
Jonas Mva Mva, project manager of Tree Crops Production, Marketing and Livelihoods program.
How far have you gone with the implementation of the Tree Crops production, marketing and livelihoods program in Camerioon?
This program, it should be noted, is made up of four components; three of which are already being implemented. The first component is the setting up of farmer organisations. In the Centre Province, we have already built eight cooperatives and in the South West province, four, giving a total of 12 cooperatives with a minimum of 6,000 members. The second component has to do with production. Here, we have two main activities: extension of the farmer-fiscal approach where the farmer is at the centre and is an extension worker in his own area of activity. Through this approach, we have been able to reduce the use of chemicals by training farmers in integrated crops and pest management to ensure that they are able to reduce the cost of chemicals used in maintaining their cocoa farms and increase their yields. Within this sub component, we are able to reduce the use of chemicals by about 40 % and increase the yields by about 5 to 10 % in farms. The second major activity in this component is the supply of farmers with good quality planting materials. So far we have trained about 200 farmers within the cooperatives we are working with in opening cocoa, oil palm and banana nurseries. We have equally trained about 50 farmers on fruit trees seedlings. The third component is income generating activities. This include increasing cocoa producer income by improving the marketing tools, setting up quality control centres between cooperatives and engaging more women and youths into the cooperative activities.
The last component which we have just started has to do with micro credit. This would enable them to understand why they need credit and the credit institutions to know who their counterparts are. So far, this is what we have achieved in one year.
Why did you choose cocoa, oil palm and banana/plantains knowing that there are several other crops farmers can rely on for income?
We had to go through a baseline survey on what is bringing more income in the rural areas. From our survey, we discovered that in the Centre and South West Provinces, cocoa provides about 70% of revenue to about 50% of rural farmers. This is followed by oil palm, banana/plantains and food crops. We did not see the necessity to get to food crops like cassava, ground nuts, etc because there are already many projects on them. When the farmer is no longer very active, it is more important for him to continue to get money from cash crops. Through the Tree Crops Production, marketing and livelihoods program, we like to demonstrate that farmers can effectively prepare their retirement on cash crops.
What criteria did you use to choose the Centre and South West Provinces?
Being located in the Centre Province, we thought that it would be easy to create the innovations we have discussed above by being closer to the farmers. Statistically speaking, we saw that the South West Province is first in cocoa production in Cameroon followed by the Centre. Against this backdrop, we intend to extend to the South which occupies third position.
What contribution is your program going to make to ensure increase production especially in cocoa?
The important element of this program is diversification. With the new variety of cocoa introduced by research centres such as IRAD, we no longer need to have a density of 1,200 trees per hectare as in the past. The present variety enables farmers to produce more even with 800 trees per hectare. We are telling farmers that rather than staying in monoculture, they can increase production by regenerating the farms and revenue by introducing new crops in the same farms. This can help farmers to support their needs in case of drop in prices of cocoa. They wouldn't need to cut down cocoa trees like we saw in 1982 when prices dropped drastically.
How will this program pull the youths into cocoa, oil palm and banana/plantation production?
During the baseline survey we did, youth said they would like to move away from the old system of farming. They said they would like to become more professional. In this project we have introduced a component that handles problems related to entrepreneurship. We think that by setting enterprises in rural communities where people will be able to become real entrepreneurs in their own farms, and as members of cooperatives this will attract the youths. Take the example of UCCAO in the West Province. When this organisation was well managed, youths did not want to go to town because they had their revenues from there. With the system we are introducing, youth will become managers themselves.

Original_Search_Title: Nigeria Begins Glucose Syrup Production From Cassava
Headline: Nigeria Begins Glucose Syrup Production From Cassava
Body_Text:
Lagos — MORE than two decades of importation of glucose syrup, the country has began local product of the raw materials from cassava, courtesy, Ekha Agro Farms Limited, Ibafo, Ogun State. Glucose syrup is used in large quantities in the manufacture of candies, biscuits, pharmaceutical products and as adjuncts in breweries.
The factory is said to be the first of its kind in Nigeria and the second in Africa after that of South Africa. It was designed and built by International Starch Institute, Denmark, at a cost of N2.5 billion and has an installed output capacity of 30,000 metric tons of glucose syrup per annum.
Glucose syrup production from cassava is subdivided into three process areas of liquefaction, saccharification, and purification
"With the advent of this factory, about US $15 million, equivalent to over N1 billion, will be saved for Nigeria in import substitution, monies that will be freed for investment in other sectors of the economy, said the Managing Director of Ekha Agro Farms Limited, Mr. Osazenaye Osarenkhoe.
To meet the demand of the companies, the Managing Director said that the factory requires about 400 metric tons of cassava tubers to produce about 120 metric tons of the syrup, while stressing that to meet this demand, the firm has employed over 20,000 Nigerians through the network of out-growers and contract farmers charged with the responsibility of producing over 120, 000 metric tons of cassava tubers needed by the factory,
"over 50 Engineers, Biochemist, Microbiologists and highly trained technicians have been employed to manage the factory. Also, 68 Agronomists, Agricultural Engineers and specialist in other fields of agricultural practice are employed directly on the company's own farms", adding, "more cheering is the fact that far in excess of 20,000 Nigerians are employed by the vast network of out-growers and contract farmers producing the over 120,000 metric tons of cassava tubers to meet the huge demand of the factory for the commodity,"he noted.
While also stating that it has not been a bed of roses, the Managing Director told President Obasanjo that Power supply to the factory has been a major challenge. According to him, "unfortunately, the voltage of the power supply by PHCN to the factory is too low and cannot power-up our equipment. We are told that a battery of 2500KVA x 33 and 2000KVA x 11 are needed by PHCN to stabilize the power supply. We currently run the factory on diesel generators 24 hours daily.
All efforts to get Nigerian Gas Company Ltd (NGC) supply gas to the factory have not been successful. Our boilers are designed to run on gas and we are in the process of taking delivery of 3 units of 600KVA gas generators. It is our hope, that you will use your good offices to get NGC to connect our factory to their gas pipeline and PHCN to install the much needed transformers".
Commending Mr. Osarenkhoe for his vision and tenacity in pursuing his dream to a reality, President Obasanjo deplored the prevailing high interest rates in the country describing them as disincentive to investment in the manufacturing sector. He also promised to get the Federal Ministry of Agriculture and Water Resources to assist the factory in alleviating its water needs. Also commending the management of the company to have chosen Ibafo as the location for the factory, the Ogun State Governor, Otunba Gbenga Daniel assured that he will fulfill his earlier promise of constructing the access road to the factory.

Original_Search_Title: Foundation Supports Indigenous Farmers
Headline: Nigeria: Foundation Supports Indigenous Farmers
Body_Text:
Lagos — The BATN Foundation recently demonstrated its absolute commitment to agricultural development of Nigeria, by commissioning its first pilot irrigation facility recently in Jima, Bida Niger State.
At the commissioning and donation ceremony of the Irrigation System meant for dry season vegetable production, the Area Director, BAT Nigeria & Director, BATN Foundation, Mr. Simon Welford, said the objective of investing in the project is to empower the benefiting farmers with a tool to facilitate farming during the dry seasons so that the farmers can be fully and productively engaged even when the rains are over.
The irrigation facility, designed to water three hectares of farmland, is equipped with a borehole with 1.5 hp submersible pump powered by a 7.5 kva diesel engine and four water storage tanks with 20,000 liters water storage capacity with irrigation pipes.
Stressing the community's need for the facility, the Area Director said, "At BATN, we believe that agriculture is the bedrock for socio-economic development of many nations. That is why the BATN Foundation has since its inception placed great emphasis on sustainable agricultural development as a veritable means not only of promoting self sufficiency in food supply, but also as a means of empowering Nigerian Communities to earn income and reduce poverty".
The BATN Foundation has successfully trained and developed Nigerians in cassava, maize, watermelon and rice enterprises. Only recently, it added fish farming to the portfolio of profitable vocations that it supports.
In addition, the Foundation has through the establishment of cottage industries across Nigeria responded to stakeholder requests to provide support in the area of food processing with the aim of adding value to their farm yields.
Congratulating all the farmers of Jima Community who actively participated in the projects, Welford said, "we are crowning our empowerment drive in Jima with the donation of this facility and I would implore you to nurture and use it for sustainable production of rice and vegetable"
The BATN Area Director, also thanked His Royal Highness, Alhaji (Dr.) Yahaya Abubakar, for his support of BATN Foundation's Projects in Jima and also for motivating the beneficiaries and workforce that diligently ensured the success of the Foundation in Niger State.
The Executive Director BATN Foundation, Mr. Foluso Koku at the commissioning and donation ceremony said the irrigation system was a value-adding component of BATN Foundation's Jima Community Agricultural Development Project.
Koku noted that the objective of the Foundation's agricultural development initiative in Niger State was to establish model rice plantations in Jima through training of farmers on how to apply best practice in rice cultivation.
Benefiting farmers in Jima, the Foundation Executive Director said, have successfully undergone several pre-season, mid-season and post harvest training on rice production, during which they were introduced to improved and high yielding variety of Foundation Rice - WITA-4.
The Rubati Nupe, Alhaji Moh'd Dyadya, who represented His Royal Highest, Etu Nupe, Alhaji (Dr.) Yahaya Abubakar CFR, said the occasion was unique in the sense that it marked another mile stone in the agricultural development, progress and also the advancement of the Doko people in Jima, Niger State.
"Today, is a day to remember, because it is a day when all our dreams, expectations and hopes have come to reality. We all needed a dam in this locality but this modern irrigation system is a sign that we are moving forward for further development and this is a step forward for the good things to come from BATN Foundation".
He however, expressed the hope that the good tiding will not be limited to only Jima area, adding, "we want it to be extended to other parts of the emirate so that farmers all over the emirate will see the impact of this organisation".

Original_Search_Title: Seeds of Capacity Building in Africa's Agriculture
Headline: Africa: Seeds of Capacity Building in Africa's Agriculture
Body_Text:
In the face of impending climate change, many fear that Africa -- already the world's poorest continent -- will be hit hardest in its ability to produce food.
Success in preventing food shortages in Africa will be achieved only if farmers maintain a wealth of seed diversity that can cope with ever-changing rainfall patterns.
In recognition of this, new initiatives are emerging that will hopefully bring about a green revolution, and ensure food security in Africa.
Starting from seeds
Seed banks have been identified as part of the solution. They preserve seed diversity, and can provide the raw genetic material to develop improved plant varieties.
In April this year, the Global Crop Diversity Trust and the United Nations Foundation announced a joint initiative to safeguard 21 of the world's most critical foods crops by preserving their seeds.
The Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation -- whose five year plan aims to provide African farmers with improved and adaptable crop varieties â-- has emerged as a major source of funding for the initiative, putting forward US$37.5 million in grants.
The initiative will cover many 'orphan' crops -- important to the poor but largely neglected in modern plant breeding â-- such as sorghum, millet, yam, cassava and cowpea.
The initiative will also fund a comprehensive global information system that will allow plant breeders everywhere to search gene banks worldwide â-- including existing banks in Ethiopia, Rwanda and the southern Africa region â-- for traits needed to combat new diseases and cope with climate change.
"The initiative will secure at-risk collections [of important food crops] in poor countries and document their astonishing diversity, making it available to meet the food needs of the poor," said Cary Fowler, executive director of Global Crop Diversity Trust.
Bumper crops
Current initiatives are not just about saving current crops â-- there are also plans to improve them.
A new partnership between the Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation and the Rockefeller Foundation has allocated US$150 million to improving seeds -- including cassava, millet and sorghum -- through conventional breeding to increase their yields and make them suitable for Africa's unpredictable rainfall patterns.
This work will decrease farmers' dependence on hybrid maize seeds, which need sufficient rainfall to grow and already do not yield enough maize.
The partnership is working with policymakers in African governments, nongovernmental organisations, African centres of excellence and donors to bring about a green revolution.
At the Rockefeller Foundation meeting on biotechnology, breeding and seed systems for African crops on 26 March this year, Venancio Massingue, Mozambican minister of science and technology said, "Seed breeding is key to the modernisation of our economies through agriculture, and to providing jobs both in rural and urban areas."
"This is why science improves the lives of people."
Drought tolerant crop varieties are already starting to emerge. Mick Mwala, head of the University of Zambia's crop sciences department, says they have already come up with new wheat varieties that are drought tolerant.
One of the partnership's two initiatives, the Alliance for a Green Revolution in Africa, will help breed improved seeds and distribute fertilisers to improve soil health in Africa, as well as supporting projects to improve water resources and the distribution of farm produce to the market.
The second initiative, the Programme for Africa's Seeds System, will help distribute these improved seeds and adaptable crop varieties to smallholders.
Chemical controversy
Scientists involved in the initiatives believe that improving seeds to resist drought and using fertiliser are the most effective ways of ensuring a good harvest.
But this approach has caused a stand off with Africa-based nongovernmental organisations who claim that Western countries are pushing for a corporate-controlled, chemical system of agriculture in Africa.
In a signed statement, several nongovernmental organisations -- such as Ethiopia's Africa Biodiversity Network, Uganda's Centre for Development Initiative, the Kenya Organic Agriculture Network and Kenya Genetically Modified Organisms Concerned â-- attending this year's African Social Forum in Nairobi, Kenya (25 January), rejected the Gates-Rockefeller initiative.
They called the initiative a "new foreign system that will encourage Africa's land and water to be privatised for growing inappropriate crops for export, biofuels and carbon sinks, instead of food for African people".
But Roy Steiner, the Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation's senior programmes officer, told SciDev.Net that the foundation's focus is to bring about a sustainable green revolution through seed breeding and improvement in Africa.
"We need to find ways to interact with small scale farmers. It is a long road but we have to make progress. The possibility is there and the potential is there," he said.
People power
Another obstacle to the success of these initiatives is the scarcity of qualified African scientists to create these new seed varieties.
Africa faces problems with funds -- to train enough scientists, and to provide them with attractive salaries and contracts.
Brain drain is also decreasing the size of the science community -- many qualified scientists have already migrated to greener pastures.
The Regional Universities Forum for Capacity Building in Agriculture (RUFORUM) is trying to address this problem.
The organisation -- made up of 12 eastern and southern African universities, led by Zimbabwe's African University and Kenya's Kenyatta University -- will launch an initiative in August this year to provide postgraduate programmes in agriculture-related fields such as aquaculture and fisheries, agricultural resource economics, food science and nutrition and dryland resource management.
They hope to secure financial resources to support more scientists to masters and docterate degree level. Those scientists graduating under RUFORUM-sponsored programmes will be given jobs in research institutions, boosting research capacity.
The development is part of current efforts by African higher learning institutions to build capacity for Africa within Africa.
RUFORUM regional coordinator, Adipala Ekwamu, said the 12 African universities are collaborating to accelerate agricultural research and biotechnology development in Africa. Up until now, RUFORUM had been supporting training in agriculture-related fields only to a masters degree level.
"We need a new institutional framework to make universities more responsive to emerging challenges in the region and to respond to those challenges in a national and regional development paradigm," said Ekwamu.
The success of RUFORUM's initiative, however, still depends on how much African policymakers support the organisation financially, he said.
"We are not asking for gigantic funding but a little that will keep our programmes moving. Sixty per cent of our scientists in the region will soon retire and so we need funding to continue training more," Ekwamu said.
Seeking support
So far ministers from Malawi and Mozambique have publicly said they are in support of science and technology as the only means to improve people's lives.
Massingue said his government has set aside over US$30 million for seed and fertiliser distribution, and will work side by side with RUFORUM to increase the amount of research and training for scientists.
Kainja Kaluluma, Malawian Minister of Women and Child Development, said science and technology is "our engine in national development and the Malawian government will support scientists" and that the government would give increased support to research, technology and training.
In an era where economies are driven by scientific and technological developments, no single country in African can ignore science and still expect to thrive.
The continent of Africa has the basics -- land and water -- to produce enough food for its people. Combined with initiatives to train scientists, develop seeds and improve farmer's access to this technology -- with support from African policymakers â-- the battle against hunger in Africa could be won.

Original_Search_Title: The Party Mechanism in the War Against Political Corruption
Headline: Nigeria: The Party Mechanism in the War Against Political Corruption
Body_Text:
The People's Democratic Party (PDP) held a retreat at the weekend in Abuja for its elected members. Nuhu Ribadu, the Chairman of the Economic and Financial Crimes Commission (EFCC), was among the resource persons who presented papers at the retreat. Below is an excerpt from his remarks:
I want to start with a statement of appreciation to the leadership and members of the Peoples Democratic Party [PDP] for this invitation to your policy retreat for the newly elected officials. I noticed the punch line in your invitation that this retreat is intended for the capacity enhancement for your elected officials within a general philosophy of helping them become "true change agents". I seriously commend this gesture and in doing so I also want to assure you that the EFCC welcomes and considers what you are doing here today one of the most useful service you are offering to the reform process of our fledging democracy. For all the elected officials I say a big congratulation, and I urge you not to betray public trust. The excitement and the anxiety I see here are understandable but I wish you all Godspeed in your new journey.
The political party as a mechanism of popular mobilization, programme development, consensus formulation and discipline matters a lot in a democratic political system. After all, it is the only constitutional institution for legitimately securing power and for forming governments, and this point alone gives it a pre-eminent role in the ultimate definition of the character of the democracy that subsists in the country. For this reason therefore, the party is ultimately the mirror image of the political system, and political systems grow or fall in relation to how healthy their party systems are. The failure of a party is always a prelude to the failure of a government and in many respect of the state. This is why we must take the whole business of party building serious and no resources channeled in this direction are a waste. If we have a situation where parties display palpable internal organizational weakness, and where their moral structure is slimy, there will not be a viable democracy because that would be akin to planting cocoyam and expecting to harvest cassava.
From the following it is obvious therefore that the challenges before the party system are enormous. Every age reveals the major challenge that defines the identity of its institutions, and in the case of nations and governments, the political party bears the burden and the responsibility to understand the contours of these challenges and provide effective responses that will translate to social progress. Party building therefore, the way I see it is serious business. Sadly however, in contemporary Nigeria, there are ample examples to convey the point that it has become more of a medium to valorize conventions of patronage, of promoting individual status over programmes and ideology, and above all of finding shelter for those who failed in other noble callings and merely seek a quick path to wealth and prestige.
Let me challenge this policy retreat with the proposition that today, the major challenge that Nigeria faces is corruption. It is the major obstacle to the emergence of our progress and, unarguably, it represents one of the major barriers to democratic development in the country. This is not a play on words, because it is damn serious business for a country that has made about half a trillion dollars from oil in less than five decades to be still battling with the most basic challenge of offering its citizens a decent life.
Ladies and gentlemen, how defensible is it that Nigeria has earned revenue from oil far in excess of the whole international aid that had flowed to Africa since its independence yet all we see around us are the institutional decay and in the grim reality where two-thirds of our 140 million Nigerians live in hopeless poverty. Where is the outrage? Development cannot be the high rises daily popping up to define the new skylines of Port Harcourt, Kano, Lagos and Abuja. It cannot mean the sophisticated automobiles that many of us drove here today, or the expensive lifestyles that has come to characterize elite living in the country. To be sure, it must mean a whole lot than all these. For the world's fifth oil producing nation, there must be something paralyzing if not breathless about the idea of governance that is exemplified by the squandering of our huge human and natural resources.
Since you will all be in office in a manner of weeks please take time to reflect deeply on the social fault lines in the country: on the education sector, look at the state of infrastructure in each of our 36 states and the 774 local councils in the country, take an honest look at the many failed national projects like the refineries, the ambitious River Basin projects of the eighties, take a look at the Nigerian Airways, the National Shipping Lines, the motor manufacturing sectors, and I can go on endlessly. What honestly comes to your mind? I pose the question this way to help us come to a collective awareness that all is not well with the state of the nation in the special sense of building and sustaining our democracy. Let us be honest and frank to ourselves, ladies and gentlemen, the major factor responsible for this catalogue of failure, I make brave to say, is corruption. There is no doubt about this fact. Let us be clear about it, this is the context of governance you will be inheriting and either you like it or not, the summation of this argument in part is that there must be some value and truth to the public disgust regarding the quality of leadership that got us to this point, a leadership so inept that it could only hang on to power through nepotism, ethnicity, religious bigotry, waste and general indiscipline...
Ladies and Gentlemen, we cannot be dispassionate about political corruption. When we elect politicians or empower political parties, it is in the expectation that they would act in the greater community interest. Having elected them therefore, we yield two major resources to them: the power to control public assets and the power to take decisions that impact on our lives. With the authority of this privileged mandate, politicians or parties can promote a season of blissful prosperity, or set out on a wrecking mission of social and economic damage if they proceed in the spirit of greed, or in the service of godfathers who bankroll their ascent to power.
But the world is changing and the global environment of governance is changing. Sadly many elected officials in the country do not know this. The international community is growing in the image of zero tolerance to corruption and this is even reflected at the level of treaty making and cooperation among countries. In the past it was easy for politicians from developing countries to stash away stolen funds into safe havens of the West but even that is very difficult today. The growing intolerance for corruption is part due a reflection of the global response to failure of governance in national jurisdictions and one of the mechanisms of the anti-corruption war is strict scrutiny of public leaders and their immediate relationships.
Let me draw three quick references to illustrate the point: First the United Nations Convention against Corruption [UNCAC] to which Nigeria has signed and ratified unequivocally calls for: "enhanced scrutiny for those entrusted with prominent public functions and their family members and close associates". Secondly, the same is true of the Financial Action Task Force [FATF] which calls for intensive scrutiny of what it characterized as the Politically Exposed Persons [PEP]-,-that is public officers, their friends and their relatives. Lastly, current international standards of freedom of expression provides that "journalists ought to dig deeper on political figures, and that public figures cannot even use criminal defamation laws as shield against scrutiny, and that, indeed, public officers should tolerate a higher degree of criticism than ordinary citizens." I use these references to draw attention to the new architecture of the international environment regarding issues of ethics in government, accountability and the probity of public officials. Nigeria cannot be different and this will help you understand the level of rigour that the war against corruption entails.
Our experience in the law enforcement environment shows that there are two main ways to do something positive on political corruption: first by focusing initially on corruption during the electoral cycle; and secondly, by implementing, to the letter, mechanisms that can help prevent corruption by politicians who are in power. We are all aware of the claims and counter-claims regarding the just concluded elections. First I want to emphasize that a lot of the problem can be traced to the corrupting role of money in politics. Electoral experts maintain that although money is indispensable for parties and candidates in elections, anti-corruption efforts in the electoral arena ought to target funding sources to ensure that they are neither corrupt nor potentially corrupting, so that people do not lose interest in the political process and lose trust in government.
Many here will agree that our electoral laws are robust and in sync with international standards in many respects, but the question is poor enforcement! Even the best laws become parodies if there are no effective enforcement mechanisms. As I said early in the week at Ilorin, the time has therefore come to enforce the laws that can help stem the distortions of money in politics which in turn bred political corruption.
I do not want to suggest that our political finance laws are beyond reproach and in this regard I will urge the next class of parliamentarians to take a priority look at our existing electoral instrument and increase the level of transparency in political financing by introducing disclosure requirements. If the public is significantly empowered with respect to who gave how much to whom, for what purpose and when, Nigeria would have significantly tightened the loose ends in the current provisions of the law which currently focuses more on restrictions on spending and restrictions on contributions rather than on disclosures. At the end of the day however, as I said above, a good set of political finance regulations is ultimately of little use if it is not properly enforced. Effective enforcement, in my opinion, will require the collegiate effort of inter-agency collaboration among agencies dealing with tax status, money laundering issues, electoral management and the civil society to the table; I must say at this point that we are in discussion already on how to address this matter. My experience from the anti-corruption work we have done so far assures me that the political will is high up in the conditions for success of this type of work and I trust that the anti-corruption agencies in the country today are encouraged by the comments from the President-elect that his administration will make anti-corruption a priority programme.
Corrupting as political financing is, it pales into insignificance against the background of vote buying which I consider perhaps the most flagrant manifestations of political corruption that has troubled the history of electoral practice in this country. Vote buying, in a nut shell, is a practice through which politicians bribe voters directly. The cynical irony of this practice is that it specially targets the most vulnerable sections of the community. From the perspective of law enforcement, this practice is typically stimulated by tainted money which is often linked to criminal sources. This is not ignoring the sometimes obvious fact that such funds have been sourced from public coffers. As we all now know, even the child on the street has sufficient evidence of what happened in the just concluded elections.
Distinguished Ladies and Gentlemen, if we think seriously about the future of our country, the challenge before us today will be to accurately measure the dangers of political corruption and move fast to stem its rancid consequences. There are three areas to explore in the effort at bringing perpetrators of political corruption to book. These are:
Regarding the specific case of political immunity, experience shows that political immunity is at the heart of many of the corruption cases involving politicians. The just concluded elections open a wide opportunity for the Nigerian law enforcement community on what to do with those who have hitherto leveraged their criminality with the shield of constitutional immunity. At the sub-regional meeting of police chiefs in West Africa last March in Abuja, I brought up the same issue and asked that we urgently create a community corridor of shared criminal intelligence and information resources to address what we at the EFCC genuinely believe could be a new season of fugitive flight of politicians across the border for those whose immunity expires after May 29. Two months after that March proposal, I am pleased to announce here that we have recorded modest but appreciable progress in our effort to frustrate the ambition of those whose criminal impunity had been so far protected by constitutional immunity. However on this matter, as in many law enforcement practices, let me emphasize the role of the broader community of citizens as a critical pivot in assisting institutions like the EFCC through useful information.
Allow me please, at this point, to comment, in general, on the question of immunity by stating that while there are reasonable grounds for granting some degree of immunity to politicians, immunity however should only protect the office that the politician holds - not the politician. Immunity cannot be a shield against justice, and thankfully while it can protect a set of officials from prosecution, it does not protect them from investigations. I hope that there would soon be constitutional opportunities through which immunity can be lifted for serious crimes such as abuse of power or gross mismanagement in office.
As far as extradition is concerned, legal instruments that call for better international cooperation to bring corrupt officials to justice, such as the UN Convention against Corruption, the AU Convention, the ECOWAS Protocol and the Commonwealth Treaty, within the framework of the Mutual Legal Assistance Treaty [MLAT], would make it easier than it used to be for governments to ignore the pleas of foreign courts regarding extradition.
The flip side of how to bring corrupt politicians to justice is the related question of how to repatriate the wealth they stole. Some baby steps have been taken in the context of new money laundering laws in many national jurisdictions, and the UN Convention against Corruption has created the template which could, through the mutual legal assistance, obligate countries to return pillaged assets to requesting countries.
Ladies and Gentlemen, in conclusion I want to leave us all with the thought that in spite of the current weaknesses of our democracy, we have a rare opportunity today to oil its tracks, and retread its paths by using the opportunities of the judicial corridor to correct the distortions we perceive in the just concluded elections. In this regard, the major weight will be on the judiciary that must show courage and independence and, above all, help dispel the growing worry among some Nigerians that judgments and justice can be purchased. The challenge in my mind before the tribunals today is to strongly make the point that those who purchase their electoral victory at discounted or counterfeit prices are denied the proceeds of that crime.
Locally, we believe that increasingly criminals and offenders in government are finding out that they cannot keep proceeds of crime for deferred personal or family use. By denying offenders these proceeds of crime, the justice system makes the correct point that there will be no safe havens and that there will be a zero tolerance for corrupt acquisition. This therefore is the rational kennel that the election tribunals must crack in deciding on the cases from the just concluded elections. We must unequivocally call on these election tribunals to do for political corruption what new international mechanisms of anti-corruption have done for financial and economic crimes: denying offenders the proceeds of their political crimes; deny them safe havens; and deny them immunity for their criminal impunity.

Original_Search_Title: Exploiting the Innocent Children
Headline: Uganda: Exploiting the Innocent Children
Body_Text:
Reports about child abuse and neglect are numerous in our media. The government has done little citing inadequate legislation yet a look at certain protocols Uganda is a state party to reveals that there are mechanisms in place under which these problems can be addressed. Ms Asiimwe examines some of them:-
Until I interacted with Uganda Child Rights NGO Network (UCRNN) recently, I did not know that Uganda is signatory to the Optional Protocol to the Convention on the Rights of the Child on the Sale of Children, Child Prostitution and Child Pornography.
Uganda signed this Protocol in August of 2002. The Protocol enjoins states to protect children against sale, child prostitution, and child pornography. Part of the responsibility of States that sign up to these UN instruments, is to report on progress of implementation. Uganda, through Ministry of Gender, Labour and Social Development, recently prepared its first periodic report. By and large, our government concedes, that it has not done enough.
The Optional Protocol has not been domesticated yet, meaning that it has not yet become part of our law. There are a few provisions in our Penal Code that touch on child prostitution, but they are not adequate. The government points out that sale of children, child prostitution and child pornography are prohibited under the Sexual Offences Bill. However, this Bill has been sitting on the shelf since 1999.
The government also concedes that it has not done any sensitisation about the Protocol and says that it does not have enough resources to address the problem. As a result, child protection is rarely a priority issue .
Compounding the problem is that there is inadequate information about the magnitude of sale of children, child prostitution and child pornography. Information obtained from Police Headquarters by UCRNN is that they do not have adequate information.
The challenge is that as these crimes go on unchecked, our children continue to suffer the consequences of sexual violence and exploitation. UCRNN highlights a few case studies from a report done by Save the Children (Uganda) in 2006: "I saw a woman from Matanyi who had a baby and a young girl of about six. They all looked very hungry. She was selling her older daughter. One woman bought her at Shs30,000.
However, this woman pleaded in tears with the mother who had sold her daughter to her have the baby as well for Shs15,000. But the mother said 'I cannot sell you my baby. I have sold you her sister in order to feed her (my baby). Since I now have some money, let me feed her until she grows. Then I may give you this child as well."
An LCI secretary narrates this story, "One day I went to Ochorimongin. I disguised myself as an Iteso and joined the others to take ajono - a local brew - on the eve of the Friday market. In the home where we were drinking from, there were two women - SL and LJ and a number of girls aged 14 and above...what I saw broke my heart.
These women had gathered the Karimojong girls and they were using them to cater for their survival. One of the Itesot men came and asked the women to let him have one of the girls to work for him. The woman demanded a fee and the man promised to pay 4 - 5pieces of cassava tubers for 4-5 hours of work. Another cyclist came in and asked to have a girl, for a 'wife'. This cyclist mobilised other men to come and take 'wives'"
"This morning (18/07/06) I saw many young children leaving in a bus going away. Very young children between the ages of 7-15 are leaving to search for food. There is a generational extinction coming to our area. During holidays because there is no food provided, children go out to look for food. They only come back when school resumes. When children come home and find their parents have gone to look for food, they also go away.
I was also informed about EG from Teso. EG collects children from Matanyi and parades them under a tree in Ochorimongin nicknamed 'supermarket'. People come all the way from Mbale, Kapchorwa,, Iganga and Jinja to this 'supermarket' to buy girls.
When asked why he is selling children he says, 'The Karimojong stole our cattle and that means we have already paid bride price for these children so they are ours.' EG demands Shs3,000 as a 'transport refund' for each girl taken" - Chairperson LC2.
The vulnerable children of Uganda will never know our government cares for them until it puts the right measures in place to protect them.
The writer is a member of the Daily Monitor Panel of Experts, a lawyer and a member of Fida Uganda with extensive experience in policy research and advocacy on human rights and governance.

Original_Search_Title: Dora Akunyili - Safeguarding National Health
Headline: Nigeria: Dora Akunyili - Safeguarding National Health
Body_Text:
Lagos — JUST recently, Nigeria was honoured with the Universal Salt Iodization (USI) compliant certification for recording 100 per cent salt iodization at factory and 98 per cent at both household and retail levels in the country. The USI certification award makes Nigeria the first African country to reach such an enviable target in the fight to eradicate Iodine Deficiency Disorder (IDD).
In giving Nigeria the award which took place in Istanbul, Turkey, the Global Forum on Micronutrient said the country has made unprecedented progress in the campaign against the endemic problem of Iodine Deficiency Disorder (IDD). The feat was made possible through pragmatic regulatory and enforcement action of the National Agency for Food and Drugs Administration and Control (NAFDAC); and such has been the destiny of the agency since April 2001 when Prof. Dora Nkem Akunyili was appointed its Director General. The six years of her stay have seen unprecedented achievements so much so that NAFDAC has become a household name in Nigeria and a well- respected organization among members of the international community.
NAFDAC was established by Decree 15 of 1993 (as amended in 1999). The Decree was passed as an Act of the National Assembly CAP No 1, Laws of the Federal Republic of Nigeria 2004. The laws establishing the agency gave it powers to control and regulate the manufacture, importation, exportation, distribution, advertisement, sale and use of food, drugs, cosmetics, chemicals, detergents, all drinks, including packaged water and medical devices. The agency is therefore saddled with the responsibility of safeguarding public health in the country. Thus, NAFDAC is expected to achieve by ensuring best quality, safety and efficacy of all medical, food product and all other products used in the country.
Prior to her appointment as head of NAFDAC, the agency recorded poor in performance, which led to high incidence of fake drugs, unwholesome food and other substandard products which put the lives of the people at risk and actually resulted to preventable deaths of millions of people. The development naturally attracted biting criticism from the public who accused the Agency of non-performance.
Some of the problems that NAFDAC was bogged down with before Prof. Akunyili took over the mantle of leadership included expired products and those without expiry/best before dates, products labeled with the intention of extending their shelf-life; non- iodized or insufficiently iodized salt; improperly processed and unregistered packaged (pure) water; beer without alcoholic content indication and low quality soft drinks. Others included deceptively labeled juice drinks, bakers using potassium bromide as bread enhancer. (The chemical is known to cause cancer, kidney failure, loss of hearing and break down of vitamins cases), palm oil mixed with Annatto dye to enhance its colour and increase in volume and substitution of edible salt with industrial salt.
In the same way, tins of baby milk filled with cassava flour and sweetened with sugar filled the Nigerian market, green fruits like mangoes, bananas and pineapples were forced to ripen through the application of heat generated from sacks of carbide, a product that causes diarrhoea, nausea, gastro-enteritis and stomach ulcer. Foods were spread with insecticides to protect them during storage. Consequently the insecticides have their residues retained by the foods, a situation that is dangerous to health. There were substandard and gelatinous cosmetics products in Nigerian markets, including body creams containing dangerous bleaching agents like mercury and hydroquinone, packets and empty bottles of designer perfumes were imported into Nigeria and filled with locally diluted concentrates while toothpastes without or with insufficient fluoride were sold to the people.
Because of inefficient regulation by the then NAFDAC, consequences of fake/counterfeit drugs increased in dimension leading to the following negative situations: Fake drugs embarrassed Nigeria's health care providers and eroded the confidence of the public in the nation's health care system, local drug manufacturers closed shops due to undue competition (even some multi-national companies left Nigeria).
Within the same period, Nigeria was rated as one of the countries with the highest incidences of fake/counterfeit drugs. This led to the rejection of drugs produced in Nigeria by West African countries. Estimates of the extent of counterfeit drugs in circulation in Nigeria ranged from 48 per cent to 80 per cent in 2001. The surveys were conducted by Pooles (1989), Lambo (1990), Ochekpe et al (1993) and Tailor et al (2001).
When Prof. Dora Akunyili took over the leadership of NAFDAC in April 2001, with the collaboration of the World Health Organization (WHO) and the United States Department for International Development (DFID) the Agency was able to make an overall assessment of the problems confronting the drug sector in the country and came up with the following findings:
That there were drugs with no active ingredients; clones of fast moving drugs; drugs with active ingredients different from what is on the label; herbal preparations mixed with orthodox medicines; expired drugs or drugs without expiry dates, or re-labeled with the intention of extending their shelf-life and drugs not registered by NAFDAC yet in circulation.
Having identified the level of unwholesomeness of food and drug products with which the people of Nigeria were fed with and the great danger they pose to the health of the nation, Akunyili declared war against those perpetrating the acts that were responsible for putting the lives of the people on the line and to wipe away those products from the Nigerian market. Today, six years into the battle, Nigerians and, indeed, members of the international community have awarded her excellent marks; a few of the reasons for their judgments are enumerated below. From 2001, when she became the Director General of NAFDAC, the Agency has, as at April 2007, carried out over 120 destruction exercises of counterfeit and substandard food and drug products valued at N21 billion.
These involve impounding of trailer loads of fake drugs caught by NAFDAC officials, raids on drug depots and shops, outright ban on markets that were identified as centers for fake drug sale, closure of illegal packaged water manufacturing outlets, smashing of fake beer manufacturing plants, arrest of drug marketers and impounding of container load of rotten fish. Others include:
*Closure of fake vegetable oil manufacturing plants and fake wine production companies.
*Sanitization of food and drug industry through well-regulated environment. This has saved the lives of million of Nigerians and non-Nigerians residing in the country, including women, children and unborn babies. The sanitization of the regulation process has created confidence in the Nigerian drug industries, thus attracting foreign investors.
