It may occur spontaneously (on its own) as in miscarriage or it could be induced. Spontaneous abortion may be complete or incomplete, may be a missed abortion or inevitable or threatened while induced abortion may be complete or incomplete
From the beginning of time, when women make up there minds against a pregnancy they have been known to go to any extent to end it. Stories abound on the use of several dangerous methods like herbs, clothes hanger, cassava sticks, bamboo shoots, lime, potash , heavy alcohol to mention but a few to procure abortion. This has usually left them with serious morbidity and very frequently mortality.
Abortion procedure may be in two methods-surgical and medical methods. The surgical methods are through vacuum aspiration, which may be manual, pedal and hand pump or electric, sharp curettage (dilatation and sharp curettage), dilatation and evacuation, the World Health Organisation (WHO) recommends the manual vacuum aspirator. Medical methods are by misoprostol and mifepristone.
Abortion may be safe when the procedures are performed by trained health care providers with proper equipment, correct technique and sanitary standards, while it is unsafe when the procedure for terminating an unwanted pregnancy is done either by persons lacking the necessary skills or in an environment lacking the minimal standards or both, according to the WHO.
Complications arising from unsafe abortion could be classified as immediate and late. Immediate complications include bleeding, shock abdominal pain, fever, infection, uterine perforation, intra abdominal injury and death. Late complications include pelvic abscess, intestinal obstructions, pelvic adhesions, infertility, chronic pelvic pain, cervical incontinence, cervical stenosis, psychological problems, uterine synaechiae, dyspareunia and chronic vaginal discharge
According to Dr. Akuabia Nzeribe, gynaecologist and obstetrician, properly provided services for early abortions, save women's lives and also avoid the often substantial cost of treating preventable complications of unsafe abortions.
One can not discuss about unsafe abortion and leave out the issue of maternal mortality, which has been described as the " death of a woman while pregnant or within 42 days of termination of pregnancy, regardless of the site or duration of pregnancy from any cause related or aggravated by the pregnancy."
Apart from the direct causes of maternal mortality which were highlighted earlier, some of the indirect causes include socio-economic factors, poverty, illiteracy ignorance political factors, cultural reasons and health system factors. These causes are being compounded by what some medical doctors described as the three delays models: delay in seeking medical care, delay in reaching health facilities and the delay in obtaining services at the facilities.
Interestingly, reduction of maternal mortality has been one of the major goals of several recent international conferences and has been included in the Millennium Development Goals (MDGs). However, because measuring maternal mortality is difficult and complex, reliable estimates of the dimensions of the problem are not generally available and assessing progress towards the goal is difficult.
In recent years, new ways of measuring maternal mortality have been developed, bearing in mind the needs and constraints of developing countries in particular. As a result, there is considerably more information available today than was the case even a few years ago.
Nonetheless, problems of under-reporting and misclassification are endemic to all methods, and estimates that are based on household surveys are subject to wide margins of uncertainty because of sample size issues. For all these reasons, it is difficult to compare the data obtained from different sources and to assess the overall magnitude of the problem.
In response to these challenges and in order to improve the information base, World Health Organisation (WHO), United Nations International Children Emergency Fund (UNICEF) and United Nations Population Fund (UNFPA) developed an approach to estimating maternal mortality that seeks both to generate estimates for countries with no data and to correct available data for under-reporting and misclassification.
A dual strategy is being used which involves adjusting available country data and developing a simple model to generate estimates for countries without reliable information. The approach, with some variations, was used to develop estimates for maternal mortality in 1990 and 1995 and has been used again for generating these estimates for the year 2000 and subsequent years.
On the basis of the present exercise, the estimated number of maternal deaths in 2000 worldwide was 529,000. These deaths were almost equally divided between Africa (251,000) and Asia (253,000), with about four per cent (22,000) occurring in Latin America and the Caribbean and less than one per cent (2,500) in the more developed regions of the world. In terms of the maternal mortality ratio (MMR), the world figure is estimated to be 400 per 100,000 live births. By region, the MMR was highest in Africa (830), followed by Asia (330), Oceania (240), Latin America and the Caribbean (190), and the developed countries (20).
The country with the highest estimated number of maternal deaths is India (136,000), followed by Nigeria (37,000), Pakistan (26,000), the Democratic Republic of the Congo and Ethiopia (24,000 each), the United Republic of Tanzania (21,000), Afghanistan (20,000), Bangladesh (16,000), Angola, China and Kenya (11,000 each), Indonesia and Uganda (10,000 each). These 13 countries account for 67 per cent of all maternal deaths.
However, the number of maternal deaths is the product of the total number of births and obstetric risk per birth, described by the MMR. On a risk-per-birth basis, the list looks rather different. With the sole exception of Afghanistan, the countries with the highest MMRs are in Africa.
The highest MMRs of 1,000 or greater, are, in order of magnitude, Sierra Leone (2,000), Afghanistan (1,900), Malawi (1,800), Angola (1,700), Niger (1,600), the United Republic of Tanzania (1,500), Rwanda (1,400), Mali (1,200), Central African Republic, Chad, Guinea-Bissau, Somalia and Zimbabwe (1,100 each), and Burkina Faso, Burundi, Kenya, Mauritania and Mozambique (1,000 each).
The maternal mortality ratio is a measure of the risk of death once a woman has become pregnant. A more dramatic assessment of risk that takes into account both the probability of becoming pregnant and the probability of dying as a result of that pregnancy cumulated across a woman's reproductive years is the lifetime risk of maternal death. The table shows that the lifetime risk of death is highest in sub-Saharan Africa, with as many as one woman in 16 facing the risk of maternal death in the course of her lifetime, compared with one in 2,800 in developed regions.
The purpose of these estimates is to draw attention to the existence and likely dimensions of the problem of maternal mortality. They are indicative of orders of magnitude and are not intended to serve as precise estimates. In addition, these estimates can help to stimulate greater awareness of and attention to the challenge of measuring maternal mortality. Following the publication of the 1990 and 1995 estimates, a number of countries have been undertaking special studies to assess the completeness and adequacy of their vital registration and health information systems. For other countries, particularly where the only source of data is from sisterhood surveys, the estimates can be used to highlight the potential pitfalls associated with such indirect measurement techniques.
The margins of uncertainty associated with the estimated MMRs are very large, and the estimates should not, therefore, be used to monitor trends in the short term. In addition, cross-country comparisons should be treated with considerable circumspection because different strategies are used to derive the estimates for different countries, making it difficult to draw comparisons.
In recent years, the issues of unsafe-abortion and maternal mortality have remained controversial and have been able to win large number of sympathisers, depending on the divide the person is. The pro-life or anti-abortionists as well as the church frown at the procurement of abortion, saying that it is tantamount to committing murder (the unborn). However, some medical experts and local and international organisations that are campaigning for safe abortion and reduction of maternal mortality in the country say that a woman should have the right to terminate a pregnancy if it is the appropriate choice for her.
Medical experts like Nzeribe and Dr. Ejike Oji, the country director of Ipas International believe strongly that a woman should be given the opportunity to make a choice about her reproductive life. That a woman should be able to choose to keep or terminate a pregnancy and if she wants to terminate it, the services of a professional and not a quack should be made available to her, while the professionals or experts would be allowed by the law of the land to render such services to her.
Both Nzeribe and Oji told THISDAY in Owerri recently that there is need to make information available to women on unsafe abortion and safe abortion so that they can make informed choice whether to go for termination of an abortion or not. They also agreed that the existing law on abortion should be reformed and made more liberal so that care givers would be able to render services to women who chose to seek for their services without being constrained by the law. Oji and Nzeribe further argued that what is killing the women is lack of information available to them. For example, they said if Franca and other women/adolescents had been empowered with information on their sexual and reproductive health and rights, some of these deaths would have been reduced drastically.
Unfortunately, the existing abortion law is not favourable to women's reproductive health and the attempt to amend the law to make it liberal and promote other women's reproductive health last year was thrown out at the National Assembly largely due to ignorance and other moral grounds that on their own have not in anyway promoted women's reproductive health.
At various occasions, the National Council of Women's Societies, Nigeria have called for the reform of the existing laws on abortion to include more indicators for the safety of both the service and the women, especially in cases of rape, incest and other health conditions. They also called for the sponsor of relevant health and social bills that support women's reproductive health rights.
It is rather unfortunate that while the nation play politics with the laws that supposed to promote women's health, women, especially the adolescents are dying in large numbers. Like Ejike asked recently: "must we continue to allow our women die emasse as a result of the opposition of some few Nigerians?"

Original_Search_Title: Empowering Farmers as Antidote to Food Insecurity
Headline: Ghana: Empowering Farmers as Antidote to Food Insecurity
Body_Text:
Accra — The world today is more threatened by declining food supplies than it has ever witnessed in history, particularly in Africa, where millions of food crop farmers are rendered jobless as a result of policy failures.
Ghana, a country that had in the early 1970s, produced enough food, i.e. rice, maize, yam, cassava, tomato etc to feed its citizens and had excess for export, is today ranked among war-torn nations like DR. Congo, Cote d'Ivoire, Rwanda, Uganda and thirteen other least developing countries, by the 2006 Social Watch Report, as suffering from food insecurity.
The problem of food insecurity is however not a natural one. In other words, the inability of Ghana or any other African country to feed its populations is not because they do not have the capacity to produce enough food to meet the needs of their populations. Neither is it a fact of poor agricultural endowment. Rather, hunger and famine in the continent is the direct outcome of the global economic system, which is so arranged in a manner as to intrusively and ruthlessly obstruct, and indeed, prevent countries like Ghana from employing the strategies, which were used by Western nations to lay the foundations of their economies.
Ghana for instance was asked in the early 1980s by the Bretton Woods Institutions; the International Monetary Fund (IMF) and the World Bank not to provide agricultural subsidies to farmers because subsidies distort the competitive principle of a market based economy and makes farmers inefficient. This, Ghana complied and in return, received loans for its structural adjustment reforms.
Two and half decades down the line, the influx of highly subsidized and inefficiently produced agricultural products from the west has collapsed not only Ghana's agriculture, but all of its productive sectors.
The attendant effect is that all food crop producing zones, especially the northern savanna, now have to depend on food rations in order to survive a full year. Rural urban migration is now on the ascendancy, crowding city centres and providing a fertile ground for crime, destitution, and immorality with the concomitant result of increasing rates of infectious diseases such as the deadly HIV/AIDS pandemic among others.
Challenging the foregoing status quo requires mass mobilization alongside the empowerment of farmer-based organizations, unionized and non-unionized wage earners, individuals and organizations etc to demand a policy rethink.
And this is what civil society organizations under the umbrella of the Ghana Trade and Livelihood Coalition (GTLC) seeks to achieve. According to the National coordinator, Mr. Ibrahim Akalbila, an empowered farmers group is what is required to resist obnoxious trade and economic policies that have been the bane of Africa's quest for economic freedom.
"When farmers are empowered, they will be able to ask the politicians who go to them to for votes to give them policies that will protect their interests. If the politician does not, the empowered farmers will not give him their votes," he told a gathering of peasant farmers who poured onto the streets of Accra in November last year to protest against the new trade deal, which the European Union is seeking to build with countries in the African, Caribbean and Pacific bloc, called the Economic Partnership Agreements or EPAs.
In an interview, Mr. Akalbila intimated that capacity building is critical to farmers' success at blocking bad policies. "If farmers are aware of the issues and know the channels through which they can get those issues addressed, they will be able to protect their interests," he said.
According to Mr. Nasiru Adams, President of the Peasant Farmers Association of Ghana (PFAG), poverty and the myriads of problems confronting Ghana and the developing world are the direct effects of the weak entitlement positions of farmers and food crop growers.
"If they had knowledge of what their entitlements are and how these can be claimed and protected, we will not be here today with our arms on our heads while poverty rides. Our poverty is one of policy. Why can't we tell our governments not to impose them on us?"
Through its "Citizens Voice in Trade Policy & Food Security," the General Agricultural Workers Union (GAWU) of the Ghana Trades Union Congress (GTUC), is seeking to promote sustainable citizens' engagement with policy makers and government on trade and food security related policies.
GAWU thinks an empowered and well-informed citizenry, ready to engage policymakers in policy dialogue to expose how trade policies affect their livelihoods and interest, is the only solution to the threat of food insecurity.
It is also about the only means to upholding good governance and the constitutionally guaranteed rights and freedoms of citizens.
GAWU's advocacy project is therefore grounded in both national and international legal commitments. The project's emphasis on the development of the local rice industry is also based on the recognition that indeed, there cannot any talk of food security when the rice industry is allowed to collapse completely. This is because not only is rice produced in almost every region of Ghana; it has also become the staple food of many Ghanaians.
Therefore boosting local rice production means creating employment in every corner of the country. It also means enabling citizens to put the energies at their disposal and the fertile lands that surround them into productive use. That brings about feelings of self-worth, because instead of being reduced to idle fellows who must wait for manna and rations from whoever cares to provide it, they now own the decision of how, when and what to produce and in what quantities.
But beside the fact of having secured access to adequate and readily available food supplies and the self gratification that the development of the local rice industry may bring, the multiplier effect of increased incomes and the reinvestment of those incomes in education, health, housing etc by farmers would mean accelerated progress towards achieving a middle-income status as stated in Ghana's medium term development objectives; the Growth and Poverty Reduction Strategy (GPRS II) document.
Panos and Cordiad features

Original_Search_Title: Who Succeeds Kufuor?
Headline: Ghana: Who Succeeds JAK?
Body_Text:
ADDRESSING A high-powered delegate's conference in the heart of London during his recent visit to the United Kingdom, the President set out his benchmark in the form of advice to his party members, wishing that his successor would have loads of commonsense, experience and national appeal to voters.
After more than six years as a father of the party and President of Ghana, President John Agyekum Kufuor cannot be faulted in his advice to party members who have the privilege to elect his successor. Though he stands the risk of being accused of 'interference' and taking sides, the President was very careful with his words in handing down the advice.
Superficially, the party looks more colourful, more united, more resilient and more organised than when it was lumbering in opposition, but behind the façade, there are deep-rooted cracks, animosities and divisions, which could tear the party asunder.
The party, like any other political movement, is also not free from perception of corruption, cronyism, tribal innuendoes, dissatisfaction and power struggle. However, the biggest question of all, which will make or break the party in the near future, is who takes over Kufuor's mantle for the 2008 do or die battle? It is against this background that I think President Kufuor's advice should be looked at with all the wisdom and courage that the party can muster.
One thing that all the 16 or so candidates vying for the flagbearership agree on, is that the 2008 elections will be hotly contested and savagely competitive, hence the serious need to elect a leader who can put the NDC where it belongs.
Nana Addo Dankwah Akufo Addo is certainly one of the popular names, which readily pop up when questions about who succeeds President Kufuor come up. In popular spots, drinking bars, hairdressing parlours and shopping alleys, whether in Kumasi, Koforidua, Tamale, Nkawkaw and Hohoe, Akufo Addo is a household name. They see him as the 'natural successor' to JAK.
Nana was born into the Danquah-Busia tradition, highly educated, speaks French and English proficiently; a strong factor which will help in our relationship with other African States, now that we are talking about African integration. As a successful lawyer and a high profile Minister of Foreign Affairs, his numerous supporters see him as a matured and experienced politician, who can put the NDC on a stretcher for good. "If there is any man in NPP, who has developed himself seriously to occupy the Osu Castle in future, it is Nana Akufo Addo. Prof Mills cannot match Akufo Addo in any future elections", a political watcher noted at University of Ghana.
At 64 or so this is his time. His solid achievement in Foreign Affairs has put him in good stead to exploit the array of foreign contacts and networks he has systematically built for the benefit of the nation.
Nevertheless, his opponents say he is old and belongs to the old school. He is arrogant, bossy, boisterous and too ambitious - with such traits, he cannot be a unifying force, they claim. Extremists in the party did not forgive him when he was given a black eye by Tsatsu Tsikata at the Fast Track Court, when he was then the Minister of Justice and Attorney General. His chances of carrying the whole of Eastern Region are being contested by three equally strong candidates - Honourables Hackman Owusu Agyeman, Owusu Agyepong and Osafo Maafo.
His campaign is yet to hit the ground running but until then keen watchers say he has an uphill task to cross the road to Osu Castle.
The cool and unassuming Alhaji Aliu Mahama, though yet to announce his candidature officially, is reported to be preparing to join the fray. His foot soldiers are vividly busy all round the country, marketing him as the next President. The Appiah Menkas and the Haruna Attas, are highly convinced that after serving his apprenticeship as Vice President of the nation, he can safely take the baton from the President, without muddying political waters.
Unashamedly liberal and generous, Alhaji Aliu's strongest point is that he has served the party and the President with all the loyalty he could muster. With the talk of easing the NPP from the alleged domination of the party by Akans, who is a better fit than the Vice President - to take up the running and direction of the party? Relatively young and untainted with alliances within the NPP, Aliu seems to be the right candidate to sustain and expand the achievements of President Kufuor, his supporters vigorously contend.
The Vice President's downside is that he is not a popular figure in his home: in biblical parlance, his own people hold him with contempt. The imbroglio between the Andanis and the Abudus has dented his credibility and sapped his popularity in the North. As a relative newcomer in the NPP, he seems to lack the support of 'kingmakers', who have to remove stumps and thorns for his climb to the Presidency. He can achieve his goal if the current President throws his support behind him, according to key analysts.
Dr. Addo Kufuor has all the credentials to be the next President of Ghana. Having served as Minister of Defence for six years, his rare achievement is that he has literally lifted the army from the gutter and transformed it into a disciplined and formidable organisation, well respected within and without. It is also a fact that he has put centripetal and centrifugal forces in the army in check. If for the past six years, there has not been any military coup and Ghanaians are enjoying their democratic dispensation in peace, Addo Kufuor takes more than modest credit for that.
Talk about maturity, academic qualification, political achievements, he is second to none - but his problem is that Ghanaians will be setting a precedent for "dynastic rule" if he becomes the next President. There seems to be strong ill -feeling against Dr Addo Kufuor contesting the forthcoming NPP primaries because the party will crack if he is seen as trying to succeed his brother. "Let's give chance to everybody in the country to become President", a strong NPP member said in Accra.
Asked about Poland, where two brothers are ruling the country as President and Prime Minister, the anti -Addo Kufuor agitators say Ghana is not Poland and that Addo Kufuor should give way to other candidates to succeed President Kufuor.
In Tamale, they call him 'Mr. President'; in Kumasi Kejetia, they call him 'Chief' at Kpando, they call him 'Fia' and at Sunyani they call him 'Adwumawura'. The popularity of Alan Kyeremanteng, soft spoken, cool and collected, has surged in recent times, especially amongst ladies and young men in the party and even beyond in all the Regions, thanks to his looks, which have assumed magnetic characteristics these days.
Alan's idea of Empretec and his conviction that the private sector has the capacity to transform Ghana into a prosperous nation is held in high esteem by the youth, hence their hope in his candidacy. The failure of the Presidential Initiative on Cassava has been used as a yardstick against him but the success in other initiatives -oil palm, tomato and others have forced his opponents into sudden retreat. His recent rise in popularity polls has, not unexpectedly, woken up slumbering foxes, vultures and jackals in the party to hunt him down, using all kinds of tricks to 'block' his candidature but they seem to have hit a buffer.
Born in Kumasi with Fanti/Ashanti parentage, the 49 -year old Alan is 'supremely well qualified to build on the huge achievements of President Kufuor and to keep the NPP solidly united", Edmund Larbi told me at Cape Coast, only last week.
Like Nana Addo, Alan was born into the Busia - Danquah tradition. He is also a lawyer and strong believer in liberal capitalism but like Alexander Hamilton of the United States{US first Secretary of State}, he strongly believes that those who through no fault of their making are poor and downtrodden, should be helped by the State to climb the social ladder. His personal record of philanthropy, which has saved thousands of waywards from the streets and poverty-ridden alleys, attest to his warmth.
Untainted by alliances and splinter 'fiefdoms within the NPP', his opponents say he is media shy and has cocooned himself from the grassroots. They claim it is "not easy to see him" and "he does not pick his telephones" when he is needed. Others also see the temporary slow down of the Presidential Initiative on cassava/starch as his main weakness.
Yaw Osafo Maafo, the former Finance and Economic Planning Minister, is certainly a power in his own right who cannot be ignored easily unless he decides to cut short his ambition to move to the Osu Castle.
Dubbed the 'Sarkozy' of Ghanaian politics, his splendid performance as Minister of Finance and later Education is still as solid as ever. He is a real leader, intelligent, matured and knowledgeable, whom many say, could put the NDC in political coma easily, but simmering 'grudge' within the party could land him on a bed of thorns for the rest of his life if he decides to push his ambition. He has explanation to do on why he was removed as Minister of Education and Sports last year.
The hullabaloo surrounding the stadia contract to the Chinese and the Ghana Publishers/ Macmillan contract case, which the State said it is "going to appeal" are just but two issues, which could easily be albatrosses around his neck.
Hackman Owusu Agyeman is a nice man who can easily fit into Kufuor's shoes. Very loyal and a major linchpin of the party who commands respect, both locally and internationally, he is politically matured, having served as Minister of Foreign Affairs and Interior. His weakness is that he is relatively old, about 64 years. As a Minister responsible for Water Resources, his inability to ensure regular supply of water to majority of Ghanaians, could be a problem when he is nominated as NPP flagbearer.
Several political analysts say Professor Frimpong Boateng could win the 2008 elections if NPP nominates him as their flagbearer on grounds that he is nationalistic and a hard working nice man, who has sacrificed his comfort abroad to help his country. He is also squeaky clean and has not tainted his hands in the rough side of politics.
A cardinal fact in politics is that it is a profession, which is not good for nice people. Many politicians know him more as a CPP sympathiser than being a member of the NPP. He seems to have no kingmakers and foot soldiers in the party to 'evangelise' on his behalf, hence his candidature is more likely to be scuppered by extremists within the party, and leave him frustrated before he realises what he is actually doing. Good luck.
Prof. Mike Ocquaye has all the tools and experience to call on Ghanaians to vote for him as President of Ghana in 2008 but in all the constituencies, which matter most to NPP, he is not popular. Perhaps he has not been using the media or has cocooned himself from the people. He has a lot of travelling to do at the constituencies and media houses, if he really means to become the flagbearer.
I really doubt if his age and health will allow him.
Until Dr Kofi Konadu Apraku's recent promise that Ghanaians will not see darkness again when he becomes the next President of Ghana, many NPP members/ supporters thought he had given up his ambition. His strengths are that he is a founding member of the party, mature and a strong campaigner.
He is yet to recover fully from his removal from the Ministry of Trade, in the formative years of the NPP administration, to his dumping at the scrap-yard of NEPAD/Regional Cooperation. His chances seem to have evaporated; he has no chance this time.
Papa Owusu Ankomah is a real gentleman, who rose through the Ministries of Justice, Interior and now Education and Sports unscathed. Relatively young, he has the world at his feet. On the side of the coin, his popularity does not go beyond Sekondi/Takoradi.
Dan Botchwey is a young politician who can cause an upset. As a successful party organiser, he takes credit for NPP successes in the last two general elections, which brought the party to power. His asset is that he knows very well almost all the relevant delegates who will select the new flagbearer, but his downside is that he is yet to fully recover from his dismissal from the Information Ministry. The rancour and bitterness, which followed in the trail of his removal is still in his head.
Fourteen- day fame seekers - Nobody knows these two returnees from America - Agyako Kyeremanteng and Dr Arthur Kennedy. They might have been successful as a banker and a medical officer respectively, but in the villages, districts, and constituencies, much is not known about them.
It is doubtful whether they actually know the objective problems facing the people in these areas.
Kyeremanteng Agyako has so far proved himself as a knowledgeable presenter of issues to only few people in the party, while Arthur Kennedy seems to be feebly using the media to publicise his manifesto. Nkrabea Effah Dartey and Kwabena Agyepong seem to be advertising themselves for political appointments after 2008. The NPP will be doing good service to themselves if they forget them now and concentrate on the right candidates. It is their democratic right to vote and be voted for but they should not waste our precious time and resources.

Original_Search_Title: Hoima Farmers Get Improved Cassava
Headline: Uganda: Hoima Farmers Get Improved Cassava
Body_Text:
Hoima — The National Agriculture Advisory Services in Hoima has distributed improved cassava cuttings to farmers to promote food security in homes.
While distributing the cassava cuttings on Wednesday, district Naads coordinator Scola Bwali said the improved Akena breed is disease resistant, matures in one year, has high yield and is sweet.
Dr Bwala said the cuttings were procured from Dokolo district at Shs30 million and the breed is resistant to the new cassava brown streak disease that has been reported in the districts of Masindi and Kibaale.
She said each parish would get cassava cuttings that can plant gardens on 42.5 acres of land. Hoima district is made up of 52 parishes, 11 sub-counties and two town councils.
Dr Bwala said the Naads Secretariat in Kampala gave Hoima the Shs30 million after the district emerged the best countrywide in implementing Naads programmes last year.
"The district council recently passed a resolution that every home must have a mandatory garden of cassava, sweet potatoes and millet to sustain food security in homes," she said.
"You have to sign for the stems received. This is to allow the production department make a follow up to realize value for money."
District vice chairman Edward Billy Kyamulesiire advised residents to change their negative attitude towards work.
District Agriculture Officer Catherine Kaija cautioned the beneficiaries to avoid mixing the new stems with the local breed in the same gardens to avoid the cassava mosaic.
The disease has been prevalent in the district for several years.

Original_Search_Title: FDB Denies Giving Permit to Two Drug Barons to Import Cocaine
Headline: Ghana: FDB Denies Giving Permit to Two Drug Barons to Import Cocaine
Body_Text:
THE HEAD of Quality Control Laboratory at the Ghana Food and Drugs Board (FDB), Rev. Jonathan Yaw Martey yesterday told an Accra Fast Track Court trying two alleged drug barons, Kwabena Amaning alias Tagor and Alhaji Issah Abbas, involved in the much-talked- about missing 77 parcels of cocaine that he did not know the accused persons.
In his evidence to the Court presided over by Justice Jones Dotsey, Witness said that FDB does not issue licenses to individuals to import narcotics drugs into the country but rather corporate entities which required the consent of the International Narcotics and Drugs Board.
He held also that he did not issue any permit to ship cocaine into the country, adding that the FDB since its establishment in 1992, had not issued permit to any of the accused persons to import cocaine into the county.
Responding to questions during cross-examination, Witness admitted working on cocaine samples for analysis at the Ghana Standards Board.Describing the nature of cocaine, Witness informed the Court that it appears in a white crystallized powder depending on its quality.
Mr. Martey had also denied reading or hearing in the media that some cocaine sent for testing had turned into Kokonte powder (cassava flour).
Tagor and Alhaji Issah are facing four and three counts respectively of dealing in narcotic drugs, conspiracy to deal in prohibited business, namely narcotic drugs, supplying, buying, and crediting, contrary to the laws of the Narcotic Drugs Control Board and have both pleaded not guilty to the charges.
Earlier, the two had been charged together with Victor Kisseh, alias Yaw Billah, Alhaji Moro Mohammed, and Kwabena Acheampong who is now a prosecution witness in the same case.
The case has been adjourned to May 15 2007 for continuation of trial.

Original_Search_Title: Fragile Peace is Threatened After Disasters Strike
Headline: Kenya: Fragile Peace is Threatened After Disasters Strike
Body_Text:
Nairobi — It has been four years since the Pokot and the Marakwet embraced peace. But the fragile relationship is threatened by the effects a livestock disease and drought.
A veterinary officer examines goats in Marakwet before they were sold through barter trade.
two communities - which engaged in cattle rustling for many years - had started a form of barter trade at the boundary between Marakwet and Baringo East districts.
But due to the Rift Valley Fever - which killed thousands of cattle in the region - and drought, which led to low harvests, the communities have few goods to exchange. And the traditional method of determining the value of the goods has also been affected by scarcity of some products and the changing demand for others. For instance, though the demand for meat has reduced due to fears over the disease, the demand for vegetables has gone up.
Toll on livestock
Incidentally, the fever took its toll on the Pokot livestock while the drought reduced crop production in the highland parts of Marakwet District.
"Trade in livestock products has not been good after the outbreak of Rift Valley Fever although the region has been cleared of the disease," says Mr Richard Cheboi, a trader at Kilos market on the common boundary of the two districts.
"Farmers did not realise good harvests last season posing a serious threat to the future of our business."
The trade was revived four years ago after a series of peace meetings between the Marakwet and Pokot communities living along the Kerio Valley.
Before the trade ties were revived, scores of people from both sides of the valley lost their lives and property running into millions of shillings due to cattle rustling and banditry that gone on unchecked for many years.
Exchange products
Among the most active traders are members of the Pokot community from Kolowa division in East Baringo who are mainly pastoralists. They exchange animal products with their Marakwet counterparts who are farmers.
The Pokot mainly give meat and milk in exchange for cereals such as maize, sorghum and millet. Sometimes, they also get vegetables, oranges, paw paws, mangoes, bananas, cassava and sweet potatoes.
Kilos, Arror, Kobulwo, Mogil, Mon and Tot markets - all of which lie on the boundary of the two districts - are the most popular areas where the trade is conducted on Thursdays and Saturdays.
"Collapse of the trade will adversely affect the socio-economic status of the two communities," said Ms Mary Chemwor, a cereals trader from Arror division in Marakwet District.
In some cases, the herdsmen take live animals which are then slaughtered at the various markets and the meat exchanged for agricultural produce.
To a newcomer in any of the markets, the most unusual sight would be the method of determining the value of the goods to be exchanged. Since there are no weighing scales, the sellers and buyers use their hands to weigh either the meat or the cereals.
There are ready experts who can determine the value of various products with few complaints from either parties involved in the trade.
Mr John Kawerweren of Mon market is one of them. He said: "Traditional methods are used to inspect the meat and as to its weight, the arm acts as the weighing machine".
Experts examine the shape of the intestines of the slaughtered animals to determine if the meat is fit for human consumption. The meat is never inspected by health officers.
"If the carcass, especially the liver, is eaten by white ants immediately after the animal is slaughtered, then it is a clear indication that it is fit to be eaten," said Mr Kawerweren.
But members of both communities admit that the outbreak of Rift Valley Fever had a negative impact on the barter trade resulting in increased prices of cereals and vegetables whose demand shot up drastically.
"Apart from helping to improve the economic status of the two communities, the trade has been a major reconciliatory factor that has contributed to stamping out cattle rustling and banditry in the entire Kerio Valley region," said Marakwet East MP Linah Jebii Kilimo.
Reconciliation
The MP has been credited for playing a leading role in the reconciliation of the two communities.
She said: "The two communities have been depending on livestock as their main source of income and they are likely to suffer unless the Government and other humanitarian organisations come to their rescue by helping to revive the trade".
Both communities are, however, optimistic that the trade will pick up again following pledges by some humanitarian organisations to help them restock their cattle. Farmers also expect a bumper harvest this season.
"We do not want to go back to tribal conflicts. The trade has helped unite us and we would like to see it pick up as soon as possible," said Mr Julius Barketwo from East Baringo.
To the two communities, nothing goes to waste including hides and skins from slaughtered animals.
Animal skins
The region is one of the few areas in Kenya where residents, especially women, dress in well treated animal skins popularly referred to as sabat ab arran.
The cloth also forms part of the barter trade business and is in high demand since the process of producing it is tedious. It is also part of the rich culture of the two communities which has been preserved over the years.
As the two communities struggle to overcome the social and economic challenges through their informal trade, many are hoping that the exchange that has helped them embrace peace will not collapse.

Original_Search_Title: Poverty, Not Investors, is Mabira's Biggest Enemy
Headline: Uganda: Poverty, Not Investors, is Mabira's Biggest Enemy
Body_Text:
Kampala — WHEN people talk of government listening carefully to people's cries about the request of Mehta to get part of Mabira for sugarcane growing, a question comes to my mind: Do our people have the big picture about development and environmental protection? Nobody in Uganda can teach the NRM to do what we have done best: listening to the people.
Listening to the people, however, should be done with caution because the overall responsibility of governing Uganda was delegated to us for five years. The delegated powers of the people come with responsibilities. We need to guide our people correctly lest we are blamed for generations to come.
Democracy is double-edged; the people should be empowered to make their feelings known to the leaders while at the same time the leaders have the responsibility to guide the population on issues of a strategic nature. In the Bible, there are two instances when eminent people who had delegated powers failed to guide the people and made very serious mistakes.
One was when the people asked Pilate to release Barabas (the murderer) and crucify Jesus. The second was when Aaron succumbed to the pressure of the people, in the absence of Moses, and joined the lot in honouring the Gold Bull Calf instead of the Lord (Exodus 32, 1-3 5). The Government will of course listen to the people, but it is also its responsibility to share with them the big picture concerning development.
With Uganda's GDP growth averaging 6% per annum in the last 10 years, and projected to increase further if some structural bottlenecks like inadequate power and poor infrastructure are finally settled, there will be more "clashes" between development and the environment.
This should not be surprising because it has happened in all countries - developed and developing. What should be of everybody's concern is, how do we strike a balance between development and environmental protection? The biggest enemy of the environment is poverty. Therefore, to sustainably protect the environment (forests) the first line of defence should be enhanced efforts to fight poverty.
Fighting poverty is not an easy task. With a population growth in excess of 3% per annum, and a fertility rate of seven children per woman in the child bearing age bracket, we need to be highly innovative if we are to break out of the vicious cycle of poverty.
Even if all our efforts to control birth rates were to be successful, Uganda's population, about 30 million today, will more than double in the next 20 years. Any visionary person, especially leaders, should be able to anticipate the implication of such population trends and ask themselves where the 60 million Ugandans will be employed in the next 20 years.
The developed countries and the emerging economic giants of the day have all had to contend with this issue and in all instances these countries chose not to be the conservation paradises of the world. They chose development.
For Uganda's 60 million people in the next 20 years, there should be a plan to transform the economy so that the additional population finds employment in the modern sector (manufacturing and services). Lack of such a plan will mean that the 60 million people will be scrambling for land and the forests we want to protect will end up being cassava gardens and overgrazed land. No government shall be able to restrain a big number of unemployed and landless peasants from encroaching on any forest, including game parks.
As President Yoweri Museveni has repeatedly said, the future of Uganda lies in processing. Processing of agricultural products will require strategic investors ready to invest in large scale plantation agriculture and in turn organise small scale farmers into out-growers' schemes to feed the factories with raw materials. For example, the Mukwano Group and Kinyara sugar factory rely heavily on outgrowers for tea and sugarcane in Toro and Bunyoro respectively.
Acasual cost-benefit analysis of the impact of peasant farming and plantation large scale agriculture on the environment is revealing. In Mubende District, for example, there is a high population of peasants who are destroying the environment with overgrazing and poor farming methods.
Apart from feeding themselves, how much do these peasants contribute to the national coffers? On the other hand, even if Mehta or Madhvani 'destroyed' part of the natural vegetation, they would contribute more to the national coffers and offer employment to thousands of Ugandans. Such big investors have enabled us to finance 56% of the national budget and to offer free universal education for one's first 12 years in school. We also sponsor 4,000 students in public universities and our capacity to improve our infrastructure has been enhanced.
With enhanced taxable capacity, Uganda will continuously become a better destination for investors because of improved education levels, infrastructure and government capacity to cushion investors against the high initial costs of production through provision of modern industrial parks (one is to be opened at Namanve by 2010).
This is the future of Uganda; a country where majority of the population will be employed in the manufacturing and service sectors. Only then shall we talk of sustainable environmental protection. At that stage, agriculture will be handled by a minority of the population using better farming methods to protect the environment. There will be less pressure on our forests because the majority of our people will be employed in the manufacturing and service sectors in towns.
Former governments misled the people that the future of Uganda lay in peasant agriculture. It is only recently that the structure of the Ugandan economy started to change. By 2002/2003 agriculture accounted for 38% of GDP compared to 52% in 1991/92. On the other hand, services and industrial production increased as a share of GDP from 49% in 1991/92 to 62% in 200 1/2002. That is the way to go.
We either "functionally destroy" the environment with development or become the "conservation paradise" of the world amidst poverty. Poverty will ultimately destroy the environment.
The writer is the Minister of State for Luweero Triangle

Original_Search_Title: Kasajja Makes a Fortune From Pork
Headline: Uganda: Kasajja Makes a Fortune From Pork
Body_Text:
Kampala — SEEING a Land Cruiser with a customised number plate, you would expect to see a managing director. But the man had a humble beginning, with only sh12,000 as capital. Hakuna Matata, which literally means 'there is no problem', is the name for a chain of popular pork joints in Kampala. They belong to 33-year-old Moses Kasajja.
"Give me pork for sh6,000 and 'escorts," one man shouts out at one of the famed pork joints. The pork is served on wide platters. The 'escorts' include vegetables and cassava. The topics discussed are football, politics and business.
Hakuna Matata pork joints are the brain child of Moses Kasajja,. His story is one of patience and perseverance. He started in Luweero, moved to Maddu in Mpigi in the late 1980s and finally to Kampala. In Kampala, he sold clothes at St Balikudembe Market, before he found his niche in pork.
"I started with around sh12,000 in 1990," Kasajja says. At the time, he had four poles and papyrus that created a shade.
It was the typical village pork joint: Dirty men without shoes, chopping a small piece of pork. The joint had no name. Kasajja says: "It was very difficult to see people of class eating pork in the open.
Many people bought the pork, roasted it and ate it at their homes. I worked harder and saved a lot," he says. "One of the biggest problems in Uganda is that people do not want to save. They think that one must first have millions before he or she starts saving."
"I expanded the pork shade in Makindye, added a few chairs and another shade. From my savings, I was able to establish the chain of pork joints." As the business expanded Kasajja's patrons found problems directing their friends to the joint. The issue of the name then came in.
"Hakuna Matata' appealed to me. It is soothing when one says, there is no problem," he explains. He operates five pork joints. Two in Makindye and the other three in Kabalagala, Nakulabye and Kiwatule.
Kasajja dresses colourfully. When The New Vision visited him at one of his joints, he was dressed in a basketball outfit of the Los Angeles Lakers. He looked like a basketball player.
The pork joints are painted white with a little brown and red. "White signifies cleanliness," he explains.
Like the rest of the walls and floors of all the joints, the toilet walls are covered with white tiles. There are cleaners in the toilets all the time.
Animal paintings also feature at Kasajja's joints. At one of them in Makindye, there are big statues of cattle, crocodiles and lions. Around them is a fountain. Because Kasajja loves perfection, he says it takes long to construct the joints. "I grew up seeing cattle. My grandfather had so many and I loved them," he says.
By any standard, Kasajja is a successful man. "One of the reasons I have succeeded, is because I supervise my businesses," he says. Kasajja and his wife wake up at 6:00am everyday.
"We buy pork from Wambizzi near Nalukolongo," he says. On a holiday or a weekend, each of the five joints slaughters about 10 pigs. A pig weighs about 25kgs. Therefore, an average of 250kgs is sold at every joint. Once the pork is delivered at the joints, the fat is removed.
Years ago, there was a belief that roasted pork was the reserve of the poor. "Today, I think that pork is the reserve of everybody, including the rich and famous," Kasajja explains. He says unlike chicken, goat and beef that are roasted everywhere, pork is exceptional.
His customers include professionals and non-professionals.
At Kasajja's pork joints you will find an assortment of drinks, pool tables and luxurious unisex hair salons.
He never had formal education. Unlike most business people, Kasajja attributes his success to savings. "I have a dream of owning a building in the centre of the city. You can only do it if you have support from the bank," he says.
Adjusting his LA Lakers unscripted cap, he says failure to go to school was not an obstacle, but a challenge. He has been able to view many things with a much wider lens.
"All of us have unique factors that can help us succeed whether we get an education or not. I discovered these traits and adopted them," he adds. Kasajja employs at least 150 people. He loves cars. Among his fleet of vehicles is a Land-cruiser with a personalised number plate. "I like watching motor sport. I also do martial arts," he says.
Kasajja was born to Michael Kyeyune, a resident of Wakiso district. Kasajja married in the early 1990s. He has six children, the oldest of whom is in S2. Kasajja is a happy man, thanks to pork.

Original_Search_Title: Life Getting Harder in Lofa County
Headline: Liberia: Slavery Life Hits Lofa
Body_Text:
Fresh and reliable reports from Lofa County speak of extreme hardships being faced by its citizens, as a result of job scarcity and unavailability of basic necessities that they can hardly afford to purchase.
People of Salayea, Zorzor, Voinjama, Kolahun, Foya and Vahun districts are reported to have also employed trekking long distances to reach various destinations, due to high transportation fares charged by commercial drivers, something that they can hardly afford daily.
According to Mr. Joseph Johnson, a citizen of the county who arrived in Monrovia recently, the majority who rely on agricultural production for sustenance have been forced to engage into barter system, since they lack the means of generating financial resources through employment, in the maintenance of family members.
Noting that the various districts are over-populated, he attributed the inflow of citizens from the republics of Sierra Leone and Guinea to the population explosion that has exacerbated the worsening plight of permanent residents, thereby creating a state of "wonderland."
Noting that farmers have been forced to secure contracts in the form of brushing, felling trees or cutting palm nuts for a day at the cost of LD$60, inadequate to satisfy domestic needs, Mr. Johnson who was nearly in tears while explaining the many hardships facing citizens, lamented over conditions of mainly youths who have now appeared pale and elderly out of the hardships.
"Do not even pray to be involved into court matters as male, since your wife or wives would be forced to equally secure contracts similar to their male counterparts, in order to go through the case and as well take of domestic problems," he indicated.
He was however succinct in pointing out that in the Foya District, it was only through the efforts of Vice President Joseph Nyumah Boakai that an oil palm mill has been reopened to provide limited job opportunities for citizens, with the bulk outnumbering the employed equally seeking contractual jobs thereat to sustain their lives.
Vice President Boakai who immensely contributed to agricultural development in the county before ascending to the national leadership, is noted to have also helped in the expansion of activities of the Liberia Produce Marketing Corporation (LPMC), at which he became Manager of the Voinjama Estate, Managing Director and subsequently Minister of Agriculture.
Similarly, as a result of the establishment of the multi-million dollar Lofa County Agricultural Development Project (LCADP) in Voinjama, the respective districts in the county boomed with rice, cocoa and coffee production, contributing revenue generation of the nation.
Further elaborating on the state of affairs in Lofa, Mr. Johnson, a professional driver who is still unemployed, also disclosed that whilst farmers are eagerly prepared to embark upon their normal core of life, ecological factors have served as impediment during the last and current planting seasons to realize substantial harvest.
He pointed particularly to drought emanating from the long period of the dry season that scientifically contribute to stunted growth of plants in the lowland as major reason, hoping that the rains would come soon enough for the fertility of the fields.
"Can you imagine individuals from neighboring Sierra Leone walking from Kwendu to Foya Airfield on market days with only two pieces of cassava to sell for between LD$5-10, simply to buy "make-it-thick" for soup," he sadly noted.
He cautioned citizens from the county currently residing in Monrovia to beware of their visits to the county to see family members, since their needs are indeed greater and must therefore make adequate allocations to assist them.
Interestingly, Mr. Johnson spoke of citizens often besiege "strangers" from Monrovia with hundreds of letters to be delivered to family members in the city, with most seeking urgent assistance to improve their conditions.
The latest reports from Lofa County come amidst continuous pronouncements by the Unity Party-led administration, headed by President Johnson-Sirleaf, to improve the socio-economic wellbeing of citizens.
It also comes at a time that the international community is exerting every effort at relieving the Nation of its huge external debt obligations, hoping that the democratically-elected government would be able to effectively and efficiently maximize the limited revenue currently being generated in addressing priority needs of citizens.
In its 2005/2006 fiscal budget, an amount of US$100,000.00 is said to have been allocated to the various political sub-divisions of the country, through the Ministry of Internal Affairs, to undertake development programs.
Regrettably, however, reports of misdirection of funds were experienced in some sub-divisions, inviting protruded eyes and rabbit-ears to future expenditure to local administrators for development purposes, without first receiving project proposals from county project planners or assistant superintendents for development.
Amidst the plight of citizens in Lofa County, however, it is recalled that many non-governmental organizations had over the years been operating in the county, although it remains to be seen what were the practical developments implemented.
Peace Winds Japan and the International Committee of the Red Cross (ICRC) are regarded as having become practical in delivering food and agricultural supplies, as well as providing building materials for citizens in reconstructing their lives.
Meanwhile, in spite of the periodic visits by lawmakers from the county at the National Legislature, unless their advocacy for growth and development is practicalized through action, citizens are of the conviction that mere words would prove them as "jokers."
They are appealing to Vice Pres. Boakai and the lawmakers to visit the various districts, to ascertain the worsening plight, since they fear any explosion that may have adverse consequences on the national life.

Original_Search_Title: Machaze Made Wrong Use of District Money
Headline: Mozambique: Machaze Made Wrong Use of District Money
Body_Text:
Maputo — Machaze was the worst district in the central Mozambican province of Manica in terms of the use of the seven million meticais (about 280,000 US dollars) allocated to every district last year for local development projects, according to the Manica provincial governor, Raimundo Diomba, cited in Wednesday's issue of the Maputo daily "Noticias".
Diomba was addressing an extraordinary session of the district government during a recent visit to Machaze.
He noted that all projects for which the money was used have nothing to do with the set objectives, which are mainly for food production and projects for the creation of jobs and income generation.
He noted that in Machaze, most of the money was used to build or rehabilitate social infrastructures and roads (usually considered a responsibility of the central or provincial governments), and to purchase domestic utensils.
However, Diomba said that, taking into account that this is a new initiative, and most district authorities were not yet clear about what the money was for, "nobody will be punished" for this misuse of funds.
He stressed it was necessary to avoid such irregularities in the future, because then appropriate measures will be taken in order to ensure that this money is used to make the district a pole of development.
During his visit to Machaze, Diomba said that the poverty that affects Manica, and the country at large, can never be eradicated until the leaders and the population start being creative in search of alternatives to face the problems of hunger and poverty.
He called for the introduction of drought resistant crops, such as cassava and sweet potatoes, and the use of low lying lands close to river banks for the production of vegetables.
He also recommended the production of the shrub jatropha for bio-diesel and cashew nuts because the Machaze soils are good for those products, which have a high commercial value.

Original_Search_Title: Landslides Bury 2 Homes in Bududa
Headline: Uganda: Landslides Bury 2 Homes in Bududa
Body_Text:
Bududa — Landslides have buried two homes in Bukigai sub-county, Bumakuma parish, Bududa district leaving 12 people homeless.
According to the residents, the landslides buried two children of John Makuma. They are George Nadelenga, 12, and Moses Kamoti, 12, who were rescued and taken to Bududa hospital.
A resident, Boniface Wakube lost property worth millions of shillings. The landslides also buried coffee, banana, cassava and maize plantations in the district causing fear of famine.
When Daily Monitor visited Bududa Hospital on Monday, Nadelenga, Kamoti and their mother Clemensia Namome were undergoing treatment at the paediatric and female wards respectively.
Ms Namome said the landslides occurred at about 11.30pm on Saturday night after a heavy downpour accompanied by hailstorms and buried their sitting room, children's bedroom, and kitchen. She said they survived because part of the room they were sleeping in was not buried.
"The landslides happened at around 11.30pm when we were asleep but what woke us up was the sudden noise from our children who had been buried. When we got out, everything was buried and the place looked like a dug garden," Ms Namome said. She said they made an alarm that attracted other residents who helped to dig the ground to rescue the children.
When Daily Monitor visited Bumakuma, residents had deserted the village. Bukigai sub-county chief John Bosco Kuloba said the heavy rains also flooded Manafwa and Tsutsu rivers sweeping away crops.

Original_Search_Title: Modibo - We Must Appreciate Local Products
Headline: Nigeria: Modibo - We Must Appreciate Local Products
Body_Text:
Lagos — How do you connect to your constituency at the grassroots?
I was born in a town called Gumu in Gombe State in the northern part of Nigeria. I grew up partly and started my primary school in Gumu. Periodically, I go home, and I try as much as possible to blend in and be near my people.
To be really honest with you, wherever I go, be it New York, London or any other place, I really like that village I was born in (I mean town now) because it is really not a village anymore, it is the best place. It is better to be with your people but I have really missed these opportunities because I have not spent more than three days in my hometown for up to five years or so.
But these past elections afforded me an opportunity to be at home for almost ten days with almost all the people I used to play with. It humbles you before God, your maker, and it also make you grateful because the same people you grew up with are still there in the village not because they didn't try, not because they are lazy, not because they don't have certain qualities; just because God decided to chose whom he will favour.
After all, whether you have money or cars or lands and houses and the other person does not even have a place to sleep, the ultimate thing is that all of you are going to die and will be going to the under ground.
What special skills and qualities have been helping you in the discharge of your responsibilities?
The exposure and the educational preparation have really prepared me to take these responsibilities that have come my way. Thank God that I have gone to the best institutions in the world. I have studied under the best teachers and also worked under the best and ablest individuals. Lessons from whomever I have worked with have really enriched me.
When I was in the University of Abuja, the Vice Chancellor was one of my mentors. I picked a lot of good work habits even from my colleagues. I recall, of course, Professor Isa Mohammed; he is meticulous and a disciplinarian and these have impacted on me because he was well organised and focused.
And Alhaji Shehu Musa, Makama Nupe, even before I worked for him, I had always been with him. He was taking me as one of his sons. The first thing I learnt from him is humility, someone who is so disciplined, so simple in life and unassuming. He does not take money to be the ultimate thing in life or the ultimate goal of a public servant. He lived a simple life that I became so close to him. When he gave me the opportunity to work under him, he exhibited those moral qualities that I imbibed in myself.
So Shehu Musa had a great impact on me and, of course, when I came into the Presidential Villa working directly under the Heads of State of the country; from General Abacha to General Abubakar to President Obasanjo and then mingling and having opportunities to listen when they meet with General Babangida, Alhaji Shagari, General Gowon, Chief Shonekan and General Buhari. I pick all these wonderful qualities from these individuals, which have helped me a lot.
By working in the Villa for almost 15 years and having the privilege of covering meetings, doing nothing but taking notes and listening to arguments and perspectives of a wide section of the public, of former military administrators, governors and ministers and captains of industries and businesses, I learnt a lot. I have really learnt so much from President Obasanjo, from the whole issue of integrity, determination, hard work, patriotism and quick mind and intelligence, trying to solve a problem as quickly as possible in leading a nation.
However, I am still in the classroom. I am still learning. So these are the things that I was highly privileged to gain which is only the will of God and not one's intelligence or smartness but only the favour of God that has placed me in this type of environment that can I can pick so much experience and so much knowledge.
By your own account, you are a privileged person. What are your contributions to the system?
I believe, in my own little way, I have contributed as a student, because during my studies in the United States, particularly during my undergraduate years. I participated fully in the student movement and activism. We started a newspaper. Actually, without being biased, we are the first people to use the word or the nomenclature 'African-American' because, in the late 70's and early 80's, African Americans still referred to themselves as Blacks.
Of course, they graduated from being 'Negro' to 'Blacks'. Nevertheless, we (especially those students who are really into the writings or the life of Malcolm X, for instance) concluded in our student years that every black person is of African descent. So wherever you find yourself in the world, you are from Africa.
We were really pushing for it in those days and quite sometimes the words started being used and you see in the whole of the United States and elsewhere people of African decent are either referred to as African Americans or people from the African continent or, if you want to make a distinction, you can say continental Africans or Africans in the Diaspora so that level we have delivered.
I left the US since 1992 and that is about fifteen years now. I believe if you go and ask what Alhaji Modibo has done, we were good ambassadors of Nigeria because we led good and exemplary conduct. We lived well; we didn't go into 419 businesses. So we became a shinning example of good Nigerians there and we were respectful of others. We know what we were there for - to study and come back home and then impact our knowledge on our community.
At UCLA, I was the president of African Activism Association. Even as I am talking to you, the association still exists at UCLA and we even have a journal called UFAHAMO which you can get, if you go to Google. Though I didn't start it because the African Activism Association has been there since the 1960's when Anelka Falderal and Franz Fanon used to come there.
UCLA gave me the opportunity to be tutored by distinguished Nigerian scholars that you don't get the chance for them to teach you here like Chinua Achebe, Ade Ajayi.
When I was a student, they used to come on Sabbatical or fellowship and stayed a couple of months. And coming back home here, I believe that we have brought a sense of diligence into government. We have brought hard work and, being given the opportunity to do your best, that may translate into why some people will say, this gentleman is very lucky; look at him he just came back from the US, in 1992, to be specific, and he is already a full minister.
I have always said and I have stated it in an interview before that Einstein said that success is 99 per cent perspiration and 1 per cent luck. So whoever you see succeeding, has worked very hard for it. We are still working very hard for it because you cannot get success on a platter of gold. If I did not work for it and I am just thrust to it, I would not appreciate it.
I believe that whatever I did during the Constitutional Conference got me noticed because I didn't apply to get a job in the Presidency. After my assignment, I went back to the university and somebody called me and said, Do you know that there is an interview for you with two other people with doctorate degrees and they are looking for an assistant in the Office of the Head of State.
The panel that interviewed me said, Are you not Dr. Modibo? You put on your glasses. And I said, Yes. And they said, Three of you are short-listed; we want you to pick the appointment. From that time, people who know me know that I cherish hard work and creativity and I give my 100 per cent time in whatever I do. So I think that is it. We are making a difference. When we came to the Ministry of Commerce, when I was given this portfolio, everybody said, What is in the Ministry of Commerce? They have killed the ministry.
But I can tell you that we have in our modest way brought it back. I think we have woken from sleep, we brought in this policy called Commerce 44, where Nigerians, if you work hard in 11 agricultural products, 11 solid minerals, 11 manufactured products; we will go places because these are products that abound in our country.
If we exploit and work hard on cocoa, cotton, sesame seed, cashew, gum Arabic, ginger and floral culture. These flowers that grow in parts of Nigeria that we take for granted will give us a lot of money. We have two or four regions, in the South-South, in the Port Harcourt area, flowers do very well. Ethiopia is going deep into flowers.
These are the things we have addressed to Nigerians, the opportunities that abound in our country and that is what commerce is all about. Commerce is not giving import licence. I am sorry to say that previous regimes absconded into giving import licenses. You bring goods from abroad. No, we check our goods from outside.
We make Nigeria prosper; we make Nigerians get the opportunities that they never thought existed. Let me give you an example. When you even take cocoa and cotton, because these are cash crops you and I grew up with, or the cassava that Mr. President is talking about, are things our people took for granted. We just use cassava as garri and forget that we can use it as bio-fuel. You can do ethanol out of cassava, you can do starch, you can do glucose and there are a whole world out there like China that are looking for these things.
Nigeria seems to be the largest cassava producer in the world because in all the 36 States of the federation and Abuja, it is a crop that can grow anywhere. That was not like when you say cocoa can only grow in the South West or in the South or cotton is largely in the North. But God is so wonderful that he put these golden trees all over Nigeria.
Gum Arabic is something that is just in the tree; you don't even labour for it. It just came out from the tree fetching millions of dollars, like last year we made almost like a hundred million dollars from gum Arabic; the same for cashew. Forget even the market abroad, check us Nigerians, approximately 144 million people. We need to patronise our textiles.
That is why, since I became minister, I resolved to wear made-in-Nigeria materials Insha Allah, like what I am putting on now. One is from Funtua, Katsina State, and the other is Nichemwax from Lagos. This is our cotton; these are our fabrics. By the time we go into that we stop patronising other people's products. If it is the drinks we are going to do on our own, we have the population that can sustain any market.
These are the things that I am proud to say I have rekindled and I hope the crusade will continue. Surprisingly, there is a company that is doing lace in Lagos. It is just that Nigerians don't know. The other day, I went into Balogun market in Lagos and bought made-in-Nigeria shoes and they look perfect even though they put the label 'Davina, Made in London'. Actually, they took me to the factory in Shomolu.
When I queried them, I said, Why are you putting 'Made in London'? They said, Oga, nobody will buy it if we put Made in Nigeria. So it is that unpatriotic attitude that we have to change where young men and women of this country will appreciate our products and it is our generation that has to do it.
We blame the older generations that they have done this and that. They have done their own bit. They are in their 60's and need to rest. It is we that need to carry on the torch. We have to move forward and do wonders. If you gauge our talents, look at services, one of the items we put on Commerce 44.
Last year alone, Nigerians sent in four billion dollars from abroad and these are Nigerians that are just professionals, trained in Nigeria but now working abroad. We should be proud of that and explore more of those opportunities.
What are the advantages of the merger of the commerce and industry ministries?
The beauty of it is that it is the natural thing to do. You know, in the olden days, the colonial government had it as commerce and industry. It was the same thing in the First Republic. You will get the details from the Commerce 44 publication, where you can see the list of the past ministers.
I insisted that we must put the pictures of all former ministers. Sometimes, that is even a statement in itself because we just did the turn over of the ministers, which is unbelievable, because we have up to 30 or more ministers over a period of 40 years of our independence, which is not a good message.
Sometimes, you need to have a measure of consistency unless you have a terribly bad minister. I was talking to my Indian counterpart and I asked him why India is suddenly springing up in industry, trade and commerce.
He said that the minister he took over from, who just died, spent 15 to 20 years as the minister and he was really doing it right. So for Nigeria, we should learn from there. Is it working? That is the issue. Like the Americans would say, If it ain't working, why keep it?
So the merger of commerce and industry is a natural thing to happen in the sector because they go together. You produce and you market or you look for the market and you push production. It is like what we have done with cassava with the policy of the government of 5 per cent inclusion of cassava flour in flour mills and the flour they use in making bread.
You know the flour millers don't like it and don't want to go into that territory. But when I came, Mr. President insisted that I close the ones that have not complied. (They were closed) and were opened after they had complied. Now, believe it or not, they are making great progress.
We are not oblivious of the fact that there are some hiccups. The flours cost about N75,000 to N95,000 because the processors are just getting into the business.
But we told them that by the time you buy it the price will begin to go down. They compare it with the ton of wheat flour which is about N45,000.00; so it is cheaper. We said no, we came from somewhere. If there is nothing wrong in putting cassava in bread, we insist. You either comply or you forget it. Thank God, they have.
That is why Nigerians didn't hear us shutting them down for the second time because 80 percent of them have complied. And there is no need to disrupt their operations. We also need to periodically check if they are not complying and we are going into other territories. Companies like the Unilever have started to apply local products in their soap called 'Alanblasia'.
What plans do you have for your life after government?
After government, I have always said I will have a series of things, not one thing in particular. I love to still go back to the classroom, even if it is not full time. I will like to pursue my journalism profession. I think it is a noble profession, particularly broadcasting.
It is not like you guys who push pen all the time. We play with images and sounds; broadcasting is so wonderful. The print media labours too hard. You connect images through your prose, your sentences and many things. But we put one little picture and the message is sent. So I love broadcasting skills and I even have an outfit right now, as I am talking to you.
I have a production company. I have always had one; I have a sound production company, I have a video production company. But I will like to get hands on and get involved and do documentary.
There was even a time I think I wanted to do freelance, but right now it is too late for me. I had a thought of going to the rural areas and getting footage to sell to the CNN or National Geographic Channel. I think I have missed that opportunity. I think still to get involved in Nollywood, though I was not trained in programming and production. I was more in broadcasting but still, I will like to get back to television, one way or the other.

Original_Search_Title: Agriculture will Be Mainstay of Nigerian Economy - Aishatu R. Ibrahim
Headline: Nigeria: Agriculture will Be Mainstay of Nigerian Economy - Aishatu R. Ibrahim
Body_Text:
As the first Nigeria National Agricultural Show comes under way from the 13th through 18th May 2007, Nasarawa State Commissioner for Agriculture and Natural Resources, Hajiya Aishatu Rufai Ibrahim, explains the rationale behind sponsoring the show by Nasarawa State Government and the attendant benefits to the people of her state. She also says that agriculture is the future of Nigeria economy. She spoke with Ahmad Tahir: Excerpts:
How would you rate agriculture in Nigeria at the moment?
I think for too long, we have merely been paying lip service to the revival of agriculture as the mainstay of our economy. Before oil was struck, you know that the Nigerian economy was sustained by agriculture. Over the years past governments have tended to neglect the sector and over relied on oil. Some of us fail to appreciate that oil may finish someday, and we will be forced to revert to agriculture as the saving grace. We do not have to wait for the day our oil resources will dwindle and possibly fail to sustain us, we must act now to bring back the past glories of agriculture, and use it to complement our oil earnings. I think the present administration has taken bold steps towards changing our orientation on agriculture and reversing the trend.
Why did your state took it upon itself to sponsor the National Agricultural show, when the federal government is there to do this type of thing?
First of all, let me correct the impression that Nasarawa State Government undertook to sponsor the First Nigeria National Agricultural Show because of the failure of the federal government to do so. We are just complementing the initiatives of the federal government in the quest to achieve food sufficiency and attain the 10% growth in the Agriculture sector each year. Principally we were motivated by the giant strides the federal government has taken to revive Agriculture in the country. As a federating unit, we have the responsibility to partner the federal government in any area we deem appropriate.
As you may be aware, the idea of the show was conceived by Governor Abdullahi Adamu in London when he led some of us to the royal Agricultural show in the United Kingdom last year. Nigeria did not make an impressive outing, by the estimation of the Governor at that forum. As a farmer and one who is conversant with trends in the Agricultural sector in this country, Governor Adamu was of the view that Nigeria has more to show the world than what was showcased in London. Even against the Background of Nasarawa State's poor financial status, he was moved by that patriotic zeal to sponsor a similar event in Nigeria if only to correct the erroneous impression created at the London Fair. I want you to note however that we accepted to sponsor the maiden edition to kick start an annual event that could be sponsored by others in the future.
