What roles do the All Farmers' Association of Nigeria, AFAN and the Federal Ministry of Agriculture have to play as co organizers?
Well, organizing an event of this magnitude is a collaborative exercise. When the idea of organizing the show was muted, we sought the opinions of the All Farmers Association and the Federal Ministry of Agriculture and Water Resources as stakeholders in the Agricultural sector. We have been doing all the planning for a successful show together. They are the ones that know the target participants outside Nasarawa state, and they have the requisite manpower to assist us in organizing the show. On our part we have availed them the benefits of our own local experimentation in staging Agricultural shows locally.
I hope you know that it is not a local affair of Nasarawa state alone, so it would not have been possible to have a national Agricultural show without involving the real farmers and the body with the statutory responsibility of coordinating Agricultural activities in this country. To some extent, the two bodies should be seen as central in the show, although the idea still remains essentially our own.. I want you to appreciate that in the situation we found ourselves in the UK; it takes one with a patriotic spirit to point out the short comings and go ahead to offer his state resources for the sake of the country. Governor Adamu spoke our minds when he intervened to save the country that embarrassing outing, by organizing the Nigerian show. We hope that countries that were in the UK with us will have an entirely different impression by the time they see what we have to show at this meet.
What do you hope to achieve by staging the Agricultural show at this point when the administration is exiting?
Government is a continuous affair, and the benefits from a particular administration do not have to be savoured by leaders of that administration. We are staging it for the befit of Nigerians and not a particular regime. As for what we hope to achieve, the theme of the show and the spelt objectives, which is "Enhancing Agricultural Mechanization and Technology", summarizes it all. We seek to showcase the country's agricultural potentials and achievements. We want to expose our farmers to new trends in improved agricultural technologies. The show will tend to place Nigeria on the path of food sufficiency and food security.
Again our objective is to encourage the exposition of farmers to the advantages of the value addition to their farm produce. We hope to attract global attention to the vast agricultural potentials of Nigeria, as well as the unique endowments of the different ecological zones of this country. We will inculcate into farmers the sense of participation, ideas and exploration of market opportunities. Finally, we are encouraging the development of relevant and appropriate skills in producers, processors, marketers and other related entrepreneurs.
There are a lot of things we want Nigerians to know about agriculture, especially the fact that it remains Nigeria's saving grace even with the celebrated oil wealth we have. Nigerians have to understand that agriculture still contributes 40% of our GDP, 5% of our foreign exchange earning and employs about 80% of the 70% active workforce we have in the country. Our ultimate target is to achieve the 10% annual growth in the agricultural sector and meet the NEEDS goals. In a nutshell, the idea is to bring back the glories of Agriculture as the mainstay of our country's economy, and to show the world the true side of our agricultural endowments.
You must have envisaged some benefits for the government and people of Nasarawa State in accepting to sponsor the fair. What are some of the benefits to your own people and government?
Nasarawa state as perhaps you know is richly endowed in solid minerals and agriculture. Even without singing our own praises, you will agree with me that we have more than it takes to reposition Nigeria as an agricultural giant. We have been staging our own domestic agricultural shows over the years, and our farmers have been benefiting from it. This particular show will provides us the opportunity to expand beyond the local level.
We have been looking for the opportunity to tell the world that we are the true food basket of Nigeria. We have initiated many research activities which are beginning to yield results and we will have the opportunity at this show to cross ventilate our ideas with that of others who may have done researches in other fields too. We hope to tap from the experiences and technological know how of others who have gone beyond our levels, and use same to advance our own plans.
More importantly, our farmers will have the opportunity to test their own sophistication against others. We have always wanted our farmers to acquire new trends in agricultural mechanization. This show will bring in people with superior technological exposure than our people, and we are bound to benefit from them. This is in the event that our foreign participants bring in their own ideas and implements.
Talking about the nifty gritty of the agricultural show, who are the target participants, and how is it going to run?
It was initially slated to hold in April, but logistics and some exigencies compelled us to reschedule it for the 13th to 18th of May. As it is all arrangements have reached advanced stages and the new date remains tentative. We are targeting individual farmers and farmers associations, agro allied companies, local Governments, State Governments and Federal Institutions and agencies. We also expect the participation of some foreign countries. We have extended invitations to their embassies and we hope that they will be there.
For each category of participants we are charging modest fees, as a means of encouraging people to participate. We are not hopping to make gains from the show; we are more interested in starting the process, which is expected to continue. We are trying our best to ensure a good outing at this maiden edition so that future sponsors can be encouraged. We hope to raise the stakes at the next edition, and we have received assurances from the Federal government that its level of involvement will be more than this.
It is expected that for him to show so much interest in promoting a national event of this nature, your Governor, Abdullahi Adamu must have scored a plus in the promotion of agriculture in his own state. What are the things he has he done in his eight years reign as governor?
Dr Abdullahi Adamu has been in the vanguard of promoting agricultural activities in Nasarawa state sometimes through personal efforts. Through his initiatives, we have been able to launch the state into competitiveness. As a farmer himself, he has brought his experiences to bear on a lot of agricultural projects. You may perhaps have heard that Nasarawa state has been chosen as one of the state for the Federal government pilot project in the farming, processing and marketing of cassava.
In the past eight years, we have promoted the processing of some produce, Abdullahi Adamu has established agro allied based industries, and has encouraged the formation of farmers' community associations. Today, Nasarawa state has come to be recognized as a hub of agricultural activities. Amongst some of the legacies Abdullahi Adamu will be leaving in the state include, the establishment of a fertilizer blending plant in Lafia, the state capital. He has purchased 39 units of tractors, refurbished 21 others, and distributed to farmers' cooperative societies. Another set of 25 units of mini tractors are ready for distribution to dry season farmers on loan basis. All dormant nurseries and orchards across Nasarawa state have been reactivated. We have established Beniseed processing industries, rice mills, fish ponds, forest seedling nurseries, and the Nasarawa state Agricultural Development fund, also established by Governor Adamu has been responding promptly to farmers financial needs.
Through the Ministry of Agriculture, Governor Adamu has developed grazing reserves in seven locations across Nasarawa state, and has also demarcated cattle routes. He has in addition established a beef processing factory at Masaka in Karu Local government Area. Other activities worth mentioning are the purchase of farm inputs, extension services, adaptive researches, seed multiplication, and Fadama development. The totality of all these achievements is what makes us beat our chests to say we have done well in agriculture. You can therefore see that our accepting to sponsor the National Agricultural fair was after all justified in many respects.

Original_Search_Title: Odds That Worked Against Ali's Wife in Delta
Headline: Nigeria: Odds That Worked Against Ali's Wife in Delta
Body_Text:
Asaba — The Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) in Delta State no doubt has shot itself in the foot and by all indications its members are feeling the heat. This is given the controversial North senatorial district result announced recently by the Independent National Electoral Commission (INEC) in the state. The outcome of the election has raised much dust among other polls that took place in the state placing both officials of the state government and INEC on the part of suspicion.
Most remarkable and disturbing too has been that members of the PDP fold have at several fora dismissed being dogged by internal wrangling over who controls the party machinery since the Chief Emmanuel Ogidi- led state executive took over from the party's former helmsman ,the taciturn Captain Pius Sienebe.
It is not surprising that members of the ruling party are licking their wounds for suspending the evil days and for keeping deaf ears to properly heal themselves of the festering infirmity of factionalisation.
Presently the party is locked in shock and freezing nightmare over the inexplicability of its loss of the Delta North seat at the National Assembly. The coveted seat before April 25 engulfed by power tussle within the leadership of the party in the state and those of the National Executive Committee of the party in Abuja. Both sides in the war which had ruffled feathers over interests and loyalty problems between Aso Rock ,the state and the party's national secretariat.
The fierce battle of the political titans in deciding the pace of events in the zone now poses members with the challenge of having no option than to live with the new reality.
Already, preparations are high for the new crop of elected lawmakers to the senate to take their shot at the hallowed chambers without a PDP member from the area moment on June 3.
The party in the state can no longer pretend that it was not visited by the political holocaust. Behind this was the apparent ethnic divide and undue quest for power amongst the three senatorial zones. At the centre of the entire drama was the demand of the North senatorial district that power should be ceded to them from the Central zone, the area that had been the saddle of the exalted seat at the Government House Asaba since the creation of the state in 1991.
Also not left in the power shift agitation is the South zone. Both the North and South had in 2003 formed what was then known as Equity 2003 that collapsed under the weight of executive frown. On their part, the central had consistently posited that Government House is their farm and they are the farmers in it. The crescendo of the ills of the ethnic and political war of attrition occasioned by endless cries of marginalization and inequality in the distribution of the revenue accruing from the federation account to the multi-lingua Big Heart -oil rich state gave rise to undue intrigues and dog eat dog battle among the different forces in the state.
The staccato of tunes that followed the agitation was deepened when it was learnt that the incumbent state governor, Chief James Ibori plotted to install his first cousin and former Secretary to the state Government ,Dr. Emmanuel Uduaghan as his possible successor. Outstanding in the then unfolding political drama was the entry of Chief Godwill Obielum into the governorship race of the state. The Ushie -Ndokwa born politician and retired Assistant Commissioner of Police as well as businessman with his strong Aso Rock connection and rumoured support from the Rivers State government had in December 2005 upturned the structure of the seating Delta state governor. The Obielum bang more than anything created a deep crack among the ranks of the state executive members. Like a rheostat and a stormy petrel, Obielum's swift move also shook the foundation of the state chapter of the PDP .With the monumental support garnered then as the only force with the zeal, fire power and will to put a sting on the incumbent governor, the governorship aspirant was said to have tactically plotted to pull the carpet off the feet of executives of the party at the wards and local council levels. He first set surprises at the December 2005 revalidation of voters exercise held at the Grand Hotel and Resort Center ahead of the 2007 polls in the state. The Outcome of that gimmick at the venue of the revalidation exercise led to the emergence of two state chairmen of the party in the state with one headed by Chief Emma Ogidi and controlled by the state government while Dr. Eniforo Mallam Obi chaired the other faction that received the strong backing of Chief Obielum and key actors of the party at the national level not disposed to Ibori's choice of his brother as the next governor of the state.
The perceived mid-day penetration of the perceived strange political added into the ranks of the PDP members in the state was seen as an affront on the state chief executive. Ibori with the master stroke backed by the deep pocket quickly deployed all known strategies to crush all oppositions on the path to the realization of his political game.
The twist in the political arrangement in the state aided the red-eye schemes by unseen forces from internal and external quarters in the determination by the powers that shaped the state to secure their future as plans for the elections advanced then. It was that astute political design that thinned down the number of governorship aspirants from the three senatorial districts who jostled for the number one seat of the state under the PDP at the Ogwashi-Uku controversial guber primaries on December 10, 2006.
Despite the intrigues and suspicion among members of PDP in the state the sore point of the entire political drama was the appearance of Dr. (Mrs.) Marian Nneamaka Ali, wife of the national chairman of PDP, Senator Ahmadu Ali. The woman, backed by his husband made frantic effort to secure the support of leaders, elders and groups from the North senatorial zone of the state. However, in the heat of the crisis of confidence and personality clash between camps loyal to both Obielum and Ibori, the Asagba in Council, Asaba installed the traditional Odu title of Enyi-Kpakpandu (the Big Shining Star) on Mrs. Ali, preparatory for the realization of her ambition as the next senator to represent the North zone. This is in apparent negation of the contending interests of Hon. Ned Nwoko and Hon Pascal Adigwe. The installation of Mrs. Ali was seen as an opportunity for Ibori to perfect his then supposed plan "A" to whittle down her ambition for the seat of Delta considering her alliance with his(Ibori's) perceived political rival, Obielum. The plot by all indications paid off with the sponsorship by the state government of the colourful installation ceremony attended by who is who in the Nigerian socio-cultural and political spheres. No doubt, the Alis did not see the game plan on the political wall of the state. But following the sad and dramatic exit of Chief Godwill Obielum from the governorship race amongst others, the battle intensified at the senatorial primaries where Mrs Ali under the PDP umbrella won though, in a controversial circumstance against other contestants like Hon Ned Nwoko of the PDP, one Chief Mbulu and Senator Patrick Osakwe also of the PDP. Foreseeing the dark political future that awaited Uduaghan ahead of the then approaching general elections, Ibori was said to have made a deft move to encourage aggrieved members of the party whose ambitions were thwarted by the crisis within the PDP in the state to re-launch their ambition under the umbrella of other parties. This game plan worked for Senator Osakwe under the ACCORD party after he lost to Mrs. Ali at the primaries held at the Oshimili Council Arcade , Asaba. This last minute tactics by Ibori was seen as a masterstroke to assist the governor secure the loyalty of the Ndokwa people having lost their support during the vexed revalidation of voters card and their switch over to Chief Obielum as the man to rally round. With these began the political vendetta between the forces considered as leftists and rightists in the state.
Most vexed was the violation of the secret deal between the Alis and the state government when it became obvious that Ibori would lose the battle to gain the full support of the Igbo speaking people of the state. The state government was said to have single handedly sponsored the traditional tiltle installation ceremony of Enyikpakpandu. This ,the state did with overnight construction of Enyikpakpandu Street near government House and the completion of the multi-million naira bungalow for Mrs. Ali in the state capital. The hosting of the traditional ceremony, the road and the building were said have cost the state over two hundred and forty one million naira albeit it took pains to explain that the sum was over stated by the media.
Again following a petition and graphic analysis of his travails in the hands of the Alis and the police in the state, filed by the Presidential Aide on Cassava Initiative and one of the senatorial aspirants to the North senatorial seat, Honourable Ned Nwoko to President Olusegun Obasanjo, the battle shifted from the state to Abuja between the PDP national boss ,Senator Ahmadu Ali and the president. President Obasanjo, according to Aso Rock sources, was reportedly said to have threatened Senator Ali to remove his wife's name from the party's list of candidates particularly as winner of the primaries for Delta North. This was received with rebuff by Ali who in return asked the president to nullify his daughter's claim to the Ogun state senatorial seat before he (Ali) could do same in Delta. Then, began the fierce fight by the elephants on various fronts. Undaunted, Hon. Ned approached the High court Abuja to slug it out with the Alis over their claim on the senatorial seat. The odds worsened for Mrs. Ali as Chief Obielum, the dogged governorship aspirant, whom she had hoped would facilitate her ambition, left her in the lurch following his failure at the guber primaries in the state. Governor Ibori's U-turn in the ensuing drama began the evil process that led to puncture the dream of Mrs. Ali for the senatorial race. Ibori was quick to realize that the North zone was instrumental to his 1999 victory at the polls with over 85 per cent votes from the area and as such cannot dismiss them especially the two strongholds of Ndokwa and Anioma.
Mrs. Ali was said to have later relied so much on the ability of her spouse to use his might as the national chairman of PDP to deliver her victoryat the poll. She forgot so soon too that the powers that be in the state have much resources to soften all hard grounds to secure whatever is needed. This to give Osakwe the edge to get the state government strong support having served the Delta North people and the state two times since 1999 loyally at the senate and by the new mandate hopes to preside the affairs of the area for another four years. The world is watching how the elections petition tribunal will handle the case arising from the controversies in the just concluded polls across the country. However the pendulum it is quite unlikely that Osakwe will be injured.

Original_Search_Title: Taking Care of Orphans
Headline: Uganda: Taking Care of Orphans
Body_Text:
Kampala — YOU are the only personwho can look after my daughter," a woman who was on her deathbed told Agnes Nambuya. Then she stared at Nambuya and took her last breath.
When the woman was buried, Nambuya's relatives and friends discouraged her from adopting the child. "They told me not to adopt the girl because she would infect my family members with HIV," Nambuya says.
Despite having seven of her own children, Nambuya adopted the girl in 1997. Unfortunately, that girl died in 2001. The former counsellor at The AIDS Support Organisation (TASO) Jinja branch, felt a call to help.
"Orphans suffer a lot. Most of them have no relatives to take care of them," Nambuya says.
In Jinja, they either seek for help from TASO staff or live on the streets. Jinja town has several such children living on the streets.
"Unfortunately, the district authorities have done little to address the children's plight," Nambuya notes.
As a TASO employee, Nambuya used to set aside sh60,000 every month to support such children.
"I used to earn sh600,000 per month and would set aside sh60,000 to buy bedsheets, shirts and dolls for these children," she says. To her dismay, some TASO officials used to take these things from the children.
"I was hurt. But this did not stop me from supporting these children," Nambuya says.
She went on to adopt seven other children and brought them to live in her home. This did not go down well with her friends, and more so, her husband.
"He always told me that what I was doing was an additional burden upon the family.
But, as time went by, he welcomed what I was doing," she says.
"We are now a family," says the 61-year-old. In most communities, children orphaned by HIV/AIDS are stigmatised.
But Nambuya's home was the exception, "My children learned to live with them. They embraced and played with their new brothers and sisters and did house chores together.
There was hardly any difference between these adopted children and my biological children".
Nambuya was born to Stella Mutonyi and Daudi Musamali in 1946 in Mbale District.
She went to Nyondo Demonstration School before joining Nabumali High School for her O'Level.
"My parents were peasants and therefore couldn't afford to pay my school fees. So in 1958, I joined Mbale Tutorial College, to study secretarial management," Nambuya says.
Upon graduation, she joined Nyondo Teachers' College as a secretary. She got married a year later. Shortly after, she got a job at Nile Institute on Jinja Main Street.
Nambuya then joined TASO in 1989 as a volunteer and was appointed a counsellor two years later. She held this position for 10 years and in 2001, tendered in her resignation.
"It was a painful decision. Everyone was surprised," Nambuya says.
At first, Naome Gonor, the Jinja TASO chief, didn't accept her resignation. "Like any other person, she thought I was joking. But I had made up my mind and wanted another challenge," Nambuya says.
Nambuya was given a sh12m retirement package. She wanted to buy a plot of land for building an orphanage in Jinja, but failed to get one.
A broker found her a plot of land in Maligita village on Njeru-Kayunga highway.
"I had never been to this village. I had been told that it had many robbers, drunkards and witches, but I decided to go," Nambuya says.
She bought a house at sh8m and used another sh1m to buy a nearby plot of land. Today, this building houses 101 orphans. It is the Agnes Children's Home.
During her first Sunday in the area, Nambuya attended prayers at Lwakitaka Gospel Church.
By coincidence, an American evangelist had come along with 30 other preachers to minister at the church.
Something strange happened. One preacher rose and said, "God has sent a woman amidst us to start an orphanage. She is, however, finding difficulties. Can she rise up," Nambuya recalls.
When Nambuya got up, the pastor said, "You are a humble and dedicated woman. God has chosen you for this task."
He told Nambuya to see him after the service, but this wasn't to be. "People mobbed him. Being a Mzungu, they wanted free money from him," she says.
A week later, she met the evangelist, who identified himself as Duncan Hill. He said he was working with an American NGO called Uganda Orphanage Fund.
"Hill told me that God had sent him to help me.
He started visiting the orphanage on a weekly basis, offering financial and moral support. He built two classrooms and an office and promised to build another classroom block but his involvement with a Ugandan jeopardised our relationship.
"Hill used to give this man money to buy food from Kayunga. But he always claimed that he was buying the food from Nakasero Market and that it was was expensive," Nambuya says.
Nambuya says she tried to dissuade Tamale in vain, instead he started blackmailing her.
"Hill eventually confronted me and accused me of swindling money. He even abandoned the building project he had started and returned to America," Nambuya says.
"We were left in a crisis. Feeding became a problem. In fact there was a time when the children went hungry for the whole day," she adds.
Tough times call for tough measures and so Nambuya had to think fast.
Together with the children at the orphanage she planted maize, beans and cassava on a two-acre piece of land. They produced enough food to last six months.
Former Ntenjeru South MP Rev. Kefa Sempangi gave them more food and the Community HIV/AIDS Initiative donated sh3.5m to the orphanage. "We were able to buy three-inch mattresses, bedsheets, blankets and some rice and sugar," Nambuya says.
At the same time she wrote to International Care and Relief in Kampala for support. In partnership with the World Food Programme (WFP), the organisation started supplying food to the orphanage.
"We signed a deal with WFP. They will supply us with beans and maize till 2008," Nambuya says.
The children eat porridge for breakfast and beans and posho for lunch and supper. All of them refer to Nambuya as their mother. Indeed like a mother, she embraces every child as though they were her own.
"Mummy, Kato ate my food yesterday," reports Bernard Erima, a former street child in Jinja town.
Wearing light-green shirts and blouses with blue skirts and shorts, the children run to their dormitories to pick up their plates as soon as the gong is sounded for lunch.
Children in P1 and P2 queue first. "They are young. As a mother, I can't allow them to be served last," Nambuya says.
Teddy Kitongo, 54, has been a cook at the orphanage since 2004, meanwhile, Hawa Namwasa, 23, teaches lower primary classes.
"The pupils are very stubborn and since the Government banned canning, we find it hard to enforce discipline," Namwasa says.
The children make necklaces after classes. These are sold and the proceeds used for buying other necessities for the orphanage.
The pioneer set of children at the orphanage will sit for their Primary Leaving Examinations this year.
"We do not have the resources to pay for their secondary education. However, we have plans to start a vocational section at the school.
We will be able to teach the children how to operate sewing machines, make handicrafts, as well as carpentry and catering," Nambuya says.
The children that Nambuya started off with stay at her home during holidays while the rest go back to their guardians. Nambuya says she wants to be remembered as someone who gave hope to children who had none.

Original_Search_Title: Sampling Gulu's Hangouts
Headline: Uganda: Sampling Gulu's Hangouts
Body_Text:
For a weekend get away, Gulu mostly serves up low-grade accommodation, hearty but costly meals and a vibrant nightlife that extols area musicians. The wearisome four-hour journey from Kampala by road has few delights - skewer served goat at Kafu, the roaring Karuma falls and cute monkeys on the homestretch.
That will ordinarily make you crave arriving at a hotel where you will be given a welcome drink and hot towel. Acholi Inn, for a long time one of the shining pieces of the Uganda Hotels consortium will offer you neither.
But then again, no one cares about the finicky traveller in this tranquil town emerging from a civil war. Still, Acholi Inn is the choice venue for high-profile events like the recent Miss Uganda regional final.
But the open sky has its drawbacks where the promise of a starry night can quickly turn into one where rain clouds hang low and the chilly weather leaves contestants wearing gooseflesh. Punctuality is not a virtue here. A 7p.m. starting time on a poster will be read as 9.30p.m., the actual time revellers will arrive in droves and pay Shs5,000 uncomplainingly.
Apart from that and a restaurant that serves meals a la carte, there is nothing about this almost rundown place that could use a facelift.
The tragic thing is that you will most probably not find room at the inn especially if the inter-university games officials are in town. The pretty young things that stroll along the town's shop-lined streets must be due to the existence of a university in the district.
Gulu Sunset Hotel on Lagara Road is the next alternative for budget accommodation. But it's only after one checks into the three-storey facility that one comes face to face with the odds upcountry facilities face.
A three-day power outage meant National Water and Sewerage Corporation could not pump water, an occurrence that renders the "self-contained" useless if guests cannot use flush toilets or have a shower.
The hotel got around this mishap by ferrying water in 20-litre plastic jerry cans and rationing one for each guest. Heaven knows how they carry them up the narrow stairway. There is no better lesson in using resources sparingly than that. I did not have an arresting view from my hotel room window and the overnight blare from a nearby nightclub meant I could hardly get any shuteye.
Most of the Gulu streets are a cluster of shops with no surprising food finds but lunch can be had at the adjacent Diana Gardens, a hangout that could pass for a sports bar.
Its strategically placed TV sets screening DStv Supersport soccer matches positions it as the ideal place to catch up on the Premiership or La Liga action. The soccer lovers here are a surprisingly very disciplined lot.
At least they did not bang or step on the tables while celebrating a goal. Diana Gardens serves up a bland buffet at Shs4,000, one where the local malakwang (a slimy vegetable soup) delicacy usually eaten with layata (sweet potatoes) is absent.
Be sure to try out the grilled pork served with either French fries or crispy cassava chips. It is an evening snack to delight the palate. It must be an ideal venue for private parties as the expatriate community that was holding an outdoor birthday party there can attest.
The shiny metallic beverage flasks at Peyero Guest House (next to Diana Gardens) will lure anyone craving a tea or coffee but the waitresses' quirky behaviour here is in its own league. When we pointed out that the milk in our African tea tasted curdled, we were shrugged off with a "that's all the milk we've got" response. Trust the "call the manager" demand to always work. A lady apologised profusely about her employee's unbecoming behaviour and offered us "black tea" on the house.
Going dancing
If you carried your dancing shoes, be sure to head to Havana (entry is free) and although you will not be met with salsa dancing and Cuban cigars, it is a place that "happens" and also one where you will at least find Smirnoff Red Ice (there was a scarcity of Smirnoff Black Ice that weekend). Havana that Saturday seemed to be living in a time warp.
The DJ played 80s South African bubblegum music; Yvonne Chaka Chaka, Brenda Fassie, Pat Shange Sipho "Hotstix" Mabhuse - the works. Like I said, local artistes rule and for almost two hours, the DJ played synthetic larakaraka/ding-ding and bwola beats that almost brought the house down as locals replicated the captivating dance routines on the discotheque floor.
Colonel Walter Ochora, the flamboyant former district chairman who never ceases to tell all and sundry that it was he that announced the July 1985 coup that exited Obote, was absent. Everyone knows him as a fixture on the town's revelling scene.
It's a relief though that Gulu is not quite like Kuito, the bullet-ridden Angolan city that was for a long time the poster child for civil war devastation.
And the best advice for any Kampala dweller heading up north would be to pack your own Smirnoff Black Ice if you feel you will miss its crispy taste while you are there.

Original_Search_Title: Financing Misery (2)
Headline: Ghana: Financing Misery (2)
Body_Text:
Accra — Nana Kodjo Minnah, Chief of Teberebie told this writer that local communities who depended on subsistence farming are now bereft of land in their communities.
"Land replacement is critical to restoring the livelihoods of the displaced farmers but nobody knows whether the company will give the farmers land, the situation poses immense and immediate threat to food security in the area," he declared
Nana Kodjo Minnah also stated that the immediate restoration of lands and livelihoods for displaced farmers and the provision of clean water for the communities affected by the Anglogold Ashanti should also be an immediate concern of the International Finance Corporation.
"They are the ones financing Anglogold Ashanti and they need to be concerned. They have to put in place measures to protect and improve the sustainable access to safe water of the affected communities, this is urgent, he explained.
Nana Kodjo Minnah further disclosed that local community consultation, participation and involvement in decision-making processes were poor. "Each time we complain the officials of Anglogold Ashanti tell us we have heard and that is all. They have formed the community consultative committee about a year ago. but the problems created by the company in the communities are still there and getting worse," Nana said.
Blasting Causing Wall Damage
One long-running battle between the communities and Anglogold Ashanti has centered chiefly over the intensity of blasting by the company and the cracks that are tearing buildings in the communities apart.
"As for the cracks in our buildings I will be glad if you could go and see them for yourself so that you appreciate better what we are going through here. Please get up and let me go and show you the cracks in the buildings, seeing is believing," Nana Kodjo Minnah said to this writer.
Pointing at a crack in one of the buildings, Nana said almost all the houses at Teberebie have cracks due to the intensity of the blasting from Anglogold Ashanti mine. He explained that many of the buildings in the village are routinely damaged by the impacts of the blasting and many of them have already collapsed all as a result.
"We have spoken to the management of the company about this problem for years but nothing has changed. The noise from the blasting is still deafening and the vibration earth shaking," he stressed.
4-8 Kilometer Walks for Children to School
Another source of friction between the Teberebie community and Anglogold Ashanti is the plight of children aged between 5 to 12 years who now have to walk long distances from their communities to school at Teberebie. They walk between 4 to 8 kilometers as a result of the destruction of a shortcut route to Teberebie by the mining operations of the company.
"I have made every effort to tell the management of Anglogold Ashanti to address this problem by buying a bus for the Teberebie school to convey the children to and from school but it seems they are not interested in buying the bus," said Nana Kodjo Minnah.
He explained that when children from the neighboring villages such as Wangarakrom, Badukrom and others get to school very tired and they are not able to learn anything. They then walk the same distance back after school, reaching their homes very tired.
Headmaster Graham Sees Many Problems
In an interview, Jonathan Graham, Headmaster of the Teberebie Kindergarten, Primary and Junior Secondary school confirmed the escalating problems in the community and the lack of commitment on the part of Anglogold Ashanti to find lasting solutions to community concerns.
"Even the children in this school have no water to drink; all the boreholes in the Teberebie community where the school is located are spoilt, so the kids just run into the bush when ever they are thirsty and drink from dirty streams. These are children and they are thirsty, we try to stop them but to no avail," said Graham.
"This is just not fair, it is counterproductive, we are toying with the future of these children, the children are now dropping out of school because the walking distance is too much and you know the devil finds work for the idle hands so you can imagine what we are doing to these kids who have decided to stay at home because of the distance from their homes to school," Graham fumed.
Madam Justina Akrokoli, the head of the Kindergarten department of the Teberebie Kindergarten, Primary and Junior school , added that the children are always tired and sleep in class because of the long distances that they have to walk to school.
Employment, Land Issues
He mentioned that unemployment was also a major problem at Wangarakrom, the youth cannot find jobs and there are no lands for them to farm either and the company doesn't want to employ them.
Interactions with residents of Abompuniso one of the communities affected by the mine also revealed that securing replacement land appeared to be a significant issue for people without sustainable source of livelihood in the community.
The local residents charged that despite the growing anger and frustration the mining company had refused to provide affected farmers with a long-term solution to loss of arable land. "Promises by the company that replacement land will be allocated to farmers have been in vain and it looks like we will have to wait forever," said one resident.
They explained that access to land is a necessary prerequisite to restore livelihoods and economic wellbeing of the project-affected population and the company should as a matter of urgency find a lasting solution to this problem. They also called on Anglogold Ashanti to improve the sustainable access to water of the affected communities.
At Mile 8, also a village affected by mine operations. Owusu Takyi, a farmer complained about the high unemployment levels in the community. He said the mines had taken over their lands and as such they had no farms and source of livelihood. He disclosed that lost access to land was a major threat to food security at Mile 8.
No Jobs at the Mine
"The officials at Anglogold Ashanti always insisted that we were unskilled. If they are serious about giving us employment they can train us. Are they saying that we are so dumb that we cannot even be watchmen?" Owusu Takyi asked.
"My illiterate brother who lived here at Mile 8 with me is now working as a security man at a private company in Accra, the big city. The other one who has also not been to school just like me also drives a truck belonging to a mining company in the Ashanti region," he said.
Yaw Tanoh, also a farmer at Mile 8, chipped in, "They just don't want to employ us so they find all sorts of excuses, and they think we are only fit for weeding and cleaning their compound."
Burden Placed on Women
At Mile 8, spending several hours a day collecting water is not uncommon and women and girls shoulder this burden of hauling water. The women complained that the laborious task of searching for water was taking a toll on their education, health and income generation.
They complained that they did not have access to safe and sufficient water to drink, or for cooking and washing. The quality as well as the quantity of water in the boreholes that the company had provided them was also a major problem.
"We no longer have streams here and the borehole that the company constructed to provide an alternative drinking water source for us is no longer used by the community because when you boil yams, cassava or plantains with the water from the borehole the food, together with the water, turns black or purple," said Akosua Ada a resident of Mile 8.
Scientists contacted by this writer attributed this to the high iron content in the water, which they said was high.
At Nkwanta, also one of the villages affected by the company's operations, residents said they have had their share of unfulfilled promises, lack of community involvement in decision-making processes, despoliation of their environment and the destruction of livelihoods by mine operations of mine. They complained that their streams have been polluted and they urgently need measures to be put in place to protect and improve sustainable access to safe water.
Lack of Information a Common Concern
In almost all the affected communities visited, the common complaint was that despite their rising frustration, impoverishment and anguish, there hasn't been sufficient dissemination of information concerning the monitoring of the impacts of the company's mining operations. They told this writer that they have requested that copies of the results of the laboratory analysis of water quality samples collected in their communities be made available to them so that they know which well, borehole or stream is clean and which is not and the source of the contamination but they have not received any satisfactory response from the company. They also called on the company to make public, results of blast and vibration monitoring in their communities.
They condemned the notion that understanding the potential impacts of mining requires a level of technical knowledge which is beyond most affected local residents. "There are people in these villages who can read and write, we also have educated relations in Accra who visit us here from time to time, they can read and understand the reports so we want the company to give us copies of the report," said Emelia Amoateng. Residents of the affected villages, however, lauded efforts by the Opportunities Industrialization Center International to provide alternative livelihood training programmers for the youth and unemployed in the communities around Anglogold Ashanti Mine. But they contended that this cannot provide substantial employment because the center's training is inadequate and there is no market for graduates because not much market surveying was done before the training program.

Original_Search_Title: It Has Been a Dog's Life for Farmers in Rural Areas
Headline: Kenya: It Has Been a Dog's Life for Farmers in Rural Areas
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Small-scale farming is undergoing profound transformation - from subsistence farming to mixed-enterprise and market-oriented agriculture
This transition is in recognition that farmers' household needs cannot be satisfied by farming in isolation, and must make adjustments to improve production and marketing. Small-scale farming systems, where a household cultivates a small piece of land, are a recent phenomenon in Kenya like elsewhere in Africa.
Africa, especially eastern and southern regions, has undergone a series of pastoralist migrations. Once new lands suitable for agriculture were secured, early migrants practised farming, but on relatively small portions, and grazing fallow rotations as a means of replenishing lost soil nutrients. The early farmers cultivated sorghum, millet, cowpeas and pigeon pea.
They gathered a wide range of wild green vegetables and indigenous fruits. Livestock was viewed as wealth and complex patterns of communal grazing and gift giving developed. As population increased, larger portions of land were placed in cultivation and rotational fallow intervals decreased until, in densely populated areas such as central Kenya and Western |Province, communal grazing ceased.
At the earliest stages of European-Arab contact, new crops were introduced and African farmers adopted them. They included maize, beans, groundnuts and cassava, allowing for greater land use. This process was interrupted in many parts of East and southern Africa when white colonial farmers displaced Africans from the best agricultural land and, in many cases, forced them to become labourers on their new plantations.
This invasion was short-lived, ending for the most part with independence. It left behind a mixed legacy of cash crops, new farming methods, some infrastructure and semblance of land tenure. But many traditional crops and farming practices were lost and land re-allocation was in many cases worse than anticipated. It is in this backdrop that small-scale farming today has developed in the past 40 years.
Independent African governments sought to "jumpstart" their economies in the 20th century through parastatals that would develop major crops and at the same time regulate agriculture and other industries. Their priority was to boost export crops such as coffee, pyrethrum and tea as a means of securing foreign currency for industrial development.
Because of skewed investment in agriculture, basic needs of small-scale farmers were overlooked. The lack of commitment to the cause of poor rural farmers is partly responsible for the failure of the much-touted Green Revolution in Africa in the 1970s. This has led to chronic food insecurity and episodic famine as population increased.
Indeed, many African countries established agricultural extension services, marketing boards, farmers' cooperatives, loan facilities, faculties of agriculture and national agricultural research institutes, but their services were weakened as government-planned economies failed in the 1980s, services and sectors were poorly coordinated and most parastatals fell into mismanagement. They became major drains of public resources and were mostly overstaffed.
During the four decades of independence, little has changed for small-scale farmers except that their numbers increased, farm sizes diminished and seasonal food shortage intensified. This led to the poorly understood and documented phenomena of "hunger months", a period when households lack sufficient food.
In the past 10 years, governance has been improving as a result of democratisation, but few of the benefits have reached small-scale farmers, especially in rural areas. Because of years of frustrations, many farmers were demoralised, others left for 'concrete or green' pastures in urban centres and cities, but the majority sought to make the best of their difficult situation. As a result of weaknesses in agricultural extension, farmers organised and joined community organisations in efforts to access information and new farming technologies and products.
Farmers recognise the benefits of improved crop varieties and the need for soil fertility replenishment, and make modest investments depending on the availability of cash and credit. Some recent adjustments to farming systems include adoption of higher-value vegetable crops, establishment of trees on farms and orchards, better rearing of animals such as chickens, pigs, dairy cows and goats as well as the formation of value-adding cottage industries such as dairy processing plants, activities that have great potential for improving farm productivity and incomes.
Most farmers' organisations consist of between 10 and 30 members. While the membership is suited to community discussions, hosting visits by local development organisations such as NGOs and Government extension agents or farmer field days and technical demonstrations, it lacks sufficient mass to achieve economies of scale through collective action.
Groups of this size are unable to hire professional staff, contract services or buy farm inputs and market produce. The services require good organisation required if small-scale farmers are to improve their income and living standards.
Farmers' groups should be consolidated to offer extension, input and marketing services. Government and development agencies should work with farmers to manage such an organisation. This will facilitate negotiation and policy dialogue and bring the plight of small-scale farmers to the top of the country's agenda.
The writer is an agriculture expert

Original_Search_Title: Alternative Crops Take Root
Headline: Eswatini: Alternative Crops Take Root
Body_Text:
Mbabane — After years of cajoling by nongovernmental organisations and Swaziland's ministry of agriculture to plant drought-resistant crops and diversify from maize, the staple food, small-scale farmers are finally heeding the message.
"My maize all died in the heat, or it was stunted and the cobs were so small they were only good to give to the cattle. But look at my sorghum! It is doing well," said Nonhlanhla Thwala, a widow in Lubombo, the country's eastern region.
The drought that devastated crops in the first quarter of the year seems to have been the catalyst for small-scale farmers to re-examine their passion for growing maize and experiment with other plants, a move that food security officials hope will prompt an interest in alternative crops.
Swazis view all alternative crops, meaning any crops that are not maize, with a degree of suspicion, to the extent that farmers will gamble on the chance of good rains rather than plant a non-maize crop. It is a habit that has proved hard to break, despite a 15-year cycle of poor rainfall in the Lubombo Region. About 80 percent of economically active Swazis are involved in small-scale farming.
This stubborn resistance to planting alternative crops is surprising, as at one time sorghum was the staple food of Swazis. "Maize was not always here; the white people brought it," Thwala said. "Before, we grew sorghum - we cook it, and we brew a traditional
All these years the agricultural officer advised the community to plant something other than maize. It is when I remembered the sorghum field of my grandparents that I did so beer from sorghum, called 'umqombotsi'."
Despite the role of sorghum in Swazi culture, it has taken years of inadequate seasonal rain to convince Thwala to plant something other than maize - "maize is easier to handle and cook!" - and dedicate a portion of her two-hectare field to sorghum.
The contrast is startling. In one part of her field there is a pathetic display of stunted maize, bordered by a solid column of thriving sorghum plants, each crowned with a bountiful inverted pyramid of seeds waiting to be harvested.
"All these years the agriculture field officer advised the community to plant something other than maize. It is when I remembered the sorghum field of my grandparents that I did so," Thwala said.
In the northern Hhohho region, the efforts by the agricultural ministry to turn subsistence farming into thriving small businesses by growing cash crops encouraged Ambrose Kunene to grow mangoes, oranges and avocados.
"The maize did poorly this year; my storage bin will be full by only a quarter. Usually it is half full in bad years, and full in normal years. The fruit will save us - the avocados I planted. I can sell them. The oranges and mango trees need some more years to mature, but I thank God for the avocados," he said.
To feed his family, Kunene relies on another crop that used to be widely cultivated but lost popularity when years of good rain resulted in bumper maize harvests. "My jugo beans have done well; they need little water. I am also happy that my watermelons did well; they received rainfall at the right time, when they were germinating," he said.
Kunene points to the local type of watermelon - pale green, the shape and size of a cantaloupe - resting on its vine, surrounded by emaciated stalks of maize that did not survive the rainless summer.
Abdoulaye Balde, country director for the World Food Programme (WFP), commented, "There are places in the country, like the Lubombo Region, where rainfall has not been sufficient for some time to grow maize. Weather patterns are changing, and we may never see good rains in those areas. There is no reason to grow maize everywhere in Swaziland when people can grow cassava and other alternatives."
Cassava is still grown sparingly in rural areas, but because it requires little space and reaches maturity quickly, it can often be seen in towns, growing on vacant lots or reaching above the garden walls of homes.
Prudence Zwane, 25, a secretary who works in Swaziland's second city, Manzini, grows vegetables to augment her family's diet. The drought has led to food price increases, and a bag of maize is expected to cost twice as much as it did in 2006.
"My tomatoes and lettuce did not do well, because I was at work and they were not watered properly, but the cassava grew nicely on its own. We use it for the starch portion of our meals," Zwane told IRIN.
The planting of diversified crops has also coincided with a shift in cultivation techniques. "I planted early this year, at the first rains. Everything I have is because I planted early," said Musa Ndlovu, 62, who farms in Shiselweni Region, in southern Swaziland.
Shiselweni usually enjoys good rainfall, so farmers have sometimes planted as late as December, but in recent years the rainfall pattern has changed, a consequence attributed by some climatologists to the effects of global warming.
Ndlovu had read the signs of changing weather patterns, and put his maize seed in the ground after the first soaking spring rains; by January, when the rain stopped, his crop was ready for harvest.
Abagale Dlamini, an agriculture ministry official in the Shiselweni Region, recognized Ndlovu's foresight compared to that of his neighbours, who planted late and got poor crops.
"He planted early, but there were other factors. He used fertiliser, and he managed his crop very well, weeding at the right time and being scientific about his business. These are lessons we are trying to instill in Swazi farmers," said Dlamini.
After years of declining food production nationwide - in 2006 a quarter of Swaziland's roughly one million people had to receive some form of food assistance - this year, harvests worse than ever have brought the urgency of food production home to farmers.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Storm Razes Fort Portal Maize Gardens
Headline: Uganda: Storm Razes Fort Portal Maize Gardens
Body_Text:
Kampala — A storm destroyed several banana plantations in Fort Portal Municipality last week, reports Bizimungu Kisakye.
The West Division deputy LC3 chairperson, Grace Luyimbazi, on Monday said the storm blew off roofs of houses and also razed cassava and maize gardens after a downpour.
The most affected villages are Nyabushozi, Kyamukelege and Kitere. "We are preparing a report which will be submitted to the Government in order to get some help. The affected residents might face famine as a result of the storm," said Luyimbazi, who is also the secretary for production. The area MP, Stephen Kaliba, said he had asked the Ministry of Relief and Disaster Preparedness to intervene.

Original_Search_Title: Inflation Rises Again to 8.9 Percent
Headline: Uganda: Inflation Rises Again to 8.9 Percent
Body_Text:
Kampala — INCREASES in fuel prices leading to higher transport cost and the impact of high utility charges have sharply increased the inflation rate.
The Consumer Price Index (CPI) released by Uganda Bureau of Statistics (Ubos) for April indicates that Uganda's underlying inflation - the price of items excluding food items - has risen from 8.5 per cent in March to 8.9 per cent in April.
On the other hand, the CPI figures reveal that the annual headline inflation for the year ending April 2007 rose to 6 per cent from 4.5 per cent for the year ended March 2007. The annual headline inflation measures the changes in prices of all items including food items.
Speaking at a news conference at Statistics House on April 30, the Director of Macroeconomics, Mr Matthew Ssewanyana said since the beginning of the fiscal year 2006/07 there has been persistent increase in Uganda's inflation rate both underlying and annual indicating that the economy is having internal shocks.
"With just two months remaining to the end of the financial year, we do not expect the inflation rate to go down in this fiscal year. We note that the economy has been experiencing high inflationary pressure, and we do not expect that the inflation rate will settle at 5 per cent or below," he said.
"Unless everything registers a zero or negative rate, then we cannot expect a low inflation rate for the year that is about to end," he said.
Though the government wants the inflation rate to be below the 6 per cent mark, it remained stable at 8.9 per cent in January and February 8.9 and 8.5 in March.
He said that the increase in electricity and water tariffs twice during the year had a lasting impact on high costs of utility services.
Mr Ssewanyana said the reasons for the rise in headline inflation included the rise in food items such as fruits, vegetables, meat, poultry, fish milk and sugar, fuel and utilities compared to the same period last year.
On the monthly changes, he said that the monthly inflation rate for April rose further by 2.6 per cent compared to an increase of 0.4 per cent in March, which he said was the highest monthly increase since March 2006.
"Prices of vegetables, especially beans, onions, cabbages and tomatoes as well as fruits (passion fruits and oranges also increased in a number of centres," he said.
Other items, which registered higher prices, include fish, milk, rice bread and sugar. Mbarara registered the highest increase of 14.6 per cent followed by Masaka and Kampala at 7.1 per cent and 6.1 per cent respectively.
Other price movements during the month of April were registered petrol, and paraffin which went in various centers.
In addition, prices of washing soap, new materials and rental charges also rose in some centres officials attributed the in crease rental as result of increase in electricity and water.
Under the period under review, food prices increased by 5.7 per cent.
All centers except Mbale registered increases in prices of staple foods, especially matooke, Irish potatoes and cassava.

Original_Search_Title: Entrenching Positive Leadership
Headline: Nigeria: Entrenching Positive Leadership
Body_Text:
Lagos — NIGERIA today is a veritable 'failing' state, if not actually a failed state. With at least 70 per cent of its population living below the poverty line, an average life span of just a little over 43 years and infant mortality and maternal mobility being among the worst in world, hardly can anyone be in doubt that we are in dire straits. Not only have we displayed our inability to perform a task as ordinary as counting ourselves, we are exhibiting to the entire world our gross lack of capacity to effectively manage the transfer of power from one administration to another through the ballot box.
In almost every department of human endeavour, Nigeria has been found wanting. Despite being the world's eighth largest oil exporter, we have been unable to be assured of absence of queues at the filling stations. Nor can we guarantee 24-hour supply of electric power at any point in the country while criminals seem to have taken over in our cities and villages with police response to the cries and anguish of citizens muffled, weak-kneed and despicably few and far between. Not only is corruption rife in the land, people in leadership positions across the country seem to have lost every sense of shame. The immunity of our uniformed personnel in their inter-relationship with their hapless compatriots is unrelenting. The near total collapse of social infrastructure throughout the country has reduced our daily existence to a most primitive level. The sense of disillusionment and hopelessness that has gripped the land is reflected in the long queues of our youth (and some elderly ones, too) at foreign consulates in quest of visas in a bid to escape the nightmare that Nigeria has since become. Even football, which used to be possibly the only unifying factor in the country has fallen on bad times, so much so, that the Super Eagles are now anything but super. In sum, everything seems to have gone awry and there does not seem to be any light at the end of our miserable tunnel.
Undoubtedly, Nigeria is incredibly blessed. Aside from having the largest population on the African continent, Nigerians possesses an abundance of talents. There is hardly any area of human endeavour that Nigerians have not produced experts in. With 80 universities and numerous other institutions of higher learning, we have nearly two million students pursuing different courses that can clearly make a difference in Nigeria's underdevelopment if carefully deployed.
As far as natural resources go, Nigeria is Africa's leading oil producer and the world's leading producer of cassava and niobium, a strategic mineral. In fact, there is hardly any mineral on the Mendeleey table that Nigeria is not richly blessed in. Our rich tropical forest contains flora and fauna that are the envy of others. With gas reserves enough to last over 300 years and a vibrant and intelligent population, Nigeria is clearly destined for greater heights. It is on account of this, that the eyes of foreign investors are riveted to the country despite its harsh economic and political environment.
Claude Ake never tired of observing the deleterious consequences of Nigeria's "disarticulate" economy, by which he meant an economy which produces what it does not consume and consumes what it does not produce. He argued that except, unless and only to the extent that Nigeria is able to solve this riddle would the country's future be assured. I agree with him absolutely. However, the problem with Nigeria, as Chinua Achebe argued, was that of leadership. According to him, Nigeria might have all the human and natural endowments necessary for an effective take off, but without the requisite leadership, all is liable to come to naught. I also agree with him.
In view of the foregoing, the task of identifying and empowering a leadership possessing the requisite leadership values for transcending the privations and distortions that haunt Nigeria constitutes, perhaps, the most important issue confronting the country today. In the midst of false prophets and all manner of pretenders, it is getting increasingly difficult to identify elements possessing the wherewithal to move the country forward. Nevertheless, the truth of the matter is, for as long as we fail and, or neglect to enthrone the right kind of leadership, for that long would Nigeria continue to wobble round and round like a barber's chair, with all motion, no movement. But then, what is the right kind of leadership that we are talking about?
Leadership is a combination of both vision and mission for the betterment of the existential conditions of the masses of the people. In other words, the ability to transcend immediate privations and articulate a blueprint for rapid socio-economic transformation, undoubtedly, constitutes a major ingredient of leadership in contemporary Nigeria. More important, positive leadership entails not only the ability to 'talk the talk" but also being able to "walk the talk."
Accordingly, a leadership which make loud noises on patriotism and self-sacrifice but see nothing wrong in taking the first flight out to consult foreign doctors at the first signs of a headache or muscle pull is not one deserving of the appreciation of the generality of Nigerians. A leadership which deems it fit and proper to salt away huge sums of unearned income in foreign locations or finds it necessary to dispatch its children and wards to far-flung corners of the world for their upbringing and education is definitely not one that can lift Nigeria out of its current morass. A leadership that sees nothing wrong in appropriating national heirlooms and the collective patrimony in the face of mass poverty, privation and alienation is totally unbefitting of being entrusted with the destiny of the nation.
The point being made is that leadership should be by example and not just by precept. If a leadership fails to share the experience of the people, then it should be considered as having been bereft of any claim to presiding over the affairs of the people. In fact, the days of such a leadership would surely be numbered and when the inevitable occurs, there would be no tears shed. On the contrary, the people would actually rejoice in expectation that its replacement would ameliorate their niggardly existence.
As our people say, fish starts getting rotten from the head. So, a leadership manifesting clear symptoms of leadership failure simply cannot endure. Such a leadership is apt to instigate restlessness among the people such that before too long, it would be confined to the ash heap of history. Failure by a leadership to properly articulate the way ahead and put round pegs in round holes could, more often than not, be fatal to its tenure. Furthermore, hardly is anything more deadly to any leadership today than lack of probity and transparency. Corruption and non-accountability constitute the bane of leadership in many parts of the so-called Third World. Therefore, any leadership that craves positivism must be upright, honest and accountable.
Regrettably, for a variety of reasons, ballot-box democracy has not always ensured that the best candidates occupy leadership positions in our society. Quite often, the most gifted in intellect or perception are not always the most endowed, materially speaking. Nor are the best and brightest enthused by the rough and tumble of what goes in a country such as ours for politicking. Thus, more often than not, we had ended up with mediocre people in public office. Admittedly, meritocracy, by and large, operates in the private sector but that is not to discount the impact of influence-peddling and the old boy network in leadership selection even in that sector.
What it all boils down to is that we can be oblivious to the emergence of leadership positions in society only to our peril. If our votes count, (and there is no reason why they should not count), our people must be fully ready to exercise their franchise at the polling booths in order to ensure that the political leadership that emerges at the end of the day reflect our value preferences. It is only by so doing that we would be able to hold government accountable to us, the people. If the notion of a social contract is to mean anything at all, the political leaders must be constantly reminded of their manifestos or stand accused of political swindle. We need to make sure that the days are gone when the electorate merely cast their votes and then go to sleep. A good done of skepticism of the people vis-a-vis their leader is quite healthy for democracy and good government. As long as the people remain docile, accepting without complaining whatever their leaders dish out to them, for that long would their interests be discounted in the scheme of things.
Leadership is all about service to the people and not self-aggrandizement as many of our leaders think. In our peculiar circumstances, we should cease and desist from perceiving our leaders as bosses instead of servants. This carry-over from colonial times needs to be jettisoned in favour of an attitude that is 21st century-compliant such that we become ready, willing and able to stand up to our leaders whenever they falter or depart from the pursuit of the greatest happiness of the greatest number of our people.
It is generally agreed that ours is a great country with tremendous potential to actualise its manifest destiny if only we are able to get the leadership question right. It is true that past history has not been particularly cheering news to many Nigerians but that should be no reason for despair since we need constantly to keep hope alive and believe in our ability to effect a turnaround in our affairs.
The problems of poverty and underdevelopment are such that only a gifted and imaginative leadership can be in a position to confront and transcend them. Whether or not we can identify and install such a leadership is a matter lying in the womb of time. Meanwhile, the next elections would afford us another opportunity to have a rendezvous with history and we can only hope the people would make the right choices.
-Prof. Oyebode, a former Vice Chancellor of University of Ado Ekiti, is currently head of Jurisprudence and International Law, University of Lagos.

Original_Search_Title: Cleaning And Beautifying Kampala is for Us All
Headline: Uganda: Cleaning And Beautifying Kampala is for Us All
Body_Text:
Kampala — I WANT to begin by thanking the residents of Kampala for the numerous and very encouraging responses I received on my first article on the subject of how we can work together to improve Kampala city's standards of hygiene.
It showed me that this city can be clean because the people who live in it care and can change their attitudes and personal habits to ensure that it does.
This time, I would like to, one more time, dwell on the matter of personal responsibility with regard to the cleanliness of our homes and our neighbourhoods, because "charity begins at home" as the saying goes. We will, next time, tackle the issue of the city authorities charged with garbage management, as well as other matters in the public domain.
As I said before, we must begin by sorting out, and in our homes, the type of waste we generate, and decide how it should be disposed of. There is the obvious and most sensitive waste which is common to all of us - the waste generated by our bodies: urine, human excreta and the water we use whenever we wash our bodies.
A good number of Kampala city residents have indoor plumbing and it is supposed to almost automatically and cleanly dispose of this type of waste. Unfortunately, systems break down, especially if they are not maintained properly. It is very important for the householder, or the landlord, to keep their sewage systems well maintained so that this human waste can be transported to a safe place where it can decompose safely and even enrich our environment.
Left to float above the surface of our roads, as is often the case, it becomes a hazard to our health and an ugly, smelly testimony of our inability to manage our waste. Here, I would particularly like to appeal to landlords of some of the old buildings in town to ensure that their sewage systems are repaired or up-graded, to avoid the spillages we commonly see on our city streets. I would also like to appeal to the users of such toilet facilities not to flush material that does not dissolve in water down their toilets.
Then there are large areas of our city where residents use the pit latrine. Constructed properly, and in the right location, the latrine is an efficient way of human waste disposal, if the householder cares to maintain it and clean it and its environs properly. As a matter of fact, the people in the countryside, who have no modem sewage systems and have to rely on pit latrines, do manage them very well and they are cleaner than the city dwellers. Here, we must task the landlords to ensure that their properties have adequate pit latrines and bathrooms, and that when the pits are full, they are promptly emptied, for the sake of the people they charge money to live in their houses.
I have said that there must be laws and by-laws to ensure that communities and individual householders observe strict standards of hygiene. And I have been informed by Local Government that there are laws and by-laws on our books. If such by-laws exist within the city of Kampala, then they must begin to be strictly enforced. Simply because you have built a home and enclosed it within a wall does not give you the right to make the entire neighbourhood suffer with your broken-down sewage system! You are free only so long as your freedom does not encroach on the freedom and well-being of your neighbours. We can only be called a "community" if we learn to respect and uphold the rights and freedoms of all those in our neighbourhood.
The second type of household garbage mainly comes from food-processing and food-preparation. The types of foodstuffs we eat tend to produce a good deal of waste - for example, banana, cassava and potato peelings are quite bulky. Fresh vegetables and fruits also generate waste product. This is not necessarily a bad thing, depending on how we handle these waste products. The wise householder will see value in these bio-degradable products and make manure out of them for his or her flower bed or kitchen garden. The objectionable thing you must not do is to let the house-help throw them outside your hedge, in that "no man's land" which quickly becomes the dumping ground of all the neighbours. Rather, teach your house worker to sort out garbage, burn that which does not rot, and make compost with those which degrade. Then, your problem will be the stuff that is harmful to the environment, such as the bottles and the plastic bags.
I am aware, of course, that wastes such as plastic bags and plastic bottles are a menace. While the authorities are still struggling to find long-term solutions to these problems, what should the ordinary resident of Kampala do with them? The first thing you can do is stop littering the city or your residential area with these items! Everyone can do this. Secondly, get back into the habit of carrying a natural-fibre basket to market, even to the supermarket, for your shopping.
You do not have to carry 10 plastic bags home each time you buy food! This way, you will also support the women who weave these baskets. Happily, I have learned that there is now an industry in Kampala which buys used plastic bottles and "buveera", if they are clean, for re-cycling. This is excellent news, and we may soon see the boys around town actually collecting such items and earning some money from them.
I would appreciate your responses and input to this discussion, using the contact below. I am convinced that if we think together, we will defeat the menace garbage represents in our city.
The writer is the First Lady of Uganda

Original_Search_Title: A Thousand Days Savouring the Land of a Thousand Hills
Headline: Uganda: A Thousand Days Savouring the Land of a Thousand Hills
Body_Text:
Kigali — A lot of the trees are sporting new leaves, mostly a dull brown with a dash of sprightly red. The mature leaves of seasons past are brilliantly green. The trees look like a ragtag guerrilla army in new fatigues. The jackfruit - common to large parts of Africa - is perennially in season. The big, green, goose-bumped fruit has a sweet, slightly sickly smell. Here in Uganda, adventure is waiting.
The rains are almost upon us. The clouds gather darkly and forbiddingly silent. The hills on the horizon seem to be competing with each other: one range enjoys the warm kisses of the glorious sunrise while the opposite hill mulls moodily in the shadow of dark clouds.
The smell of rotting molasses is so strong it easily sticks to one's body. The long rains are almost here, but the changing weather patterns have brought an unpredictable and erratic behaviour to the climate. One minute the sun blazes murderously; the next, dark clouds brook no nonsense as they slap heavy raindrops on the land.
The fallow land has been tilled, as usual. Dotting the landscape are the red Flaming Trees. They grow in a bulbous shape. At the end of their branches, brilliant red flowers blossom to create a fiery montage. Even Moses would mistake these trees as a thousand Burning Bushes. The brilliant hued flowers remind one of the poets' ode - that 'the pain of remaining in the bud is far greater than that of blossoming'.
As we approach the Katuna border post, riding on the side of the hill, the land is still relatively flat. Then we cross into Rwanda, and the land suddenly buckles up, creating a snarl-up of hills as they hug each other, leaving little room between them. This is Rwanda, Land of a Thousand Hills. It is a paradise tucked away in the heart of Africa.
It is now 5.30 in the evening. We wind and snake across the hills in Rwanda. This is the country that is known by its pet name 'Land of a thousand hills', coined by a white adventure writer, so I'm told. It is also famous for its 1,000-year-old monarchy and, incredibly, for the infamous 100-day Genocide. Yet all of these have become interwoven into a tapestry of African history.
The lush green carpet on the river valley floor holds one of the world's sweetest blends of tea. Rwanda, a tiny nation with the highest population density in the world, is at the heart of Africa. A nation which suffered a near-mortal wound, but steadily on the fast lane to full recovery.
Fast-forward to Kigali, the capital city.
The beauty hidden between the romantic hills is too much for this space. Maybe another day....Kigali's immaculate boulevards are lined with fruit trees, the most common being avocado. Apparently, former strongman Juvénal Habyarimana had a thing for these nutritious fruits - or so goes the tale. And, recently, the city authorities planted grass and palm trees which are watered every evening to give a lush green 'garden' between the lanes.
It is the season of mourning. The Genocide Commemoration Week for the massacre, 13 years ago, of a million innocent souls. The 100 days of absolute evil that characterised the worst excesses of Man are here.
The Parliament Building stand defiantly opposite the Ministry of Justice (Minijust), its pock-marked face a grim reminder of the hellish period. Gaping holes remain a part of its architecture, the bygones from tank mortar and RPG rounds, the smaller freckles from small automatic weapons. Here and there, there is a jagged edge, a gash on the beautiful, predominantly brown building.
Opposite, on a fallow piece of land, an acacia tree comes to life. Hundreds of swirling moths make this their home among the thorns of the evergreen tree. It is also prime real estate in this temperamental weather, alternating between clammy hot and humid to cold and damp tropical rainstorm. It is a product of the El-Nino phenomenon.
This year, the moths are far greater in number than anyone can remember. The swallows and weavers fly ecstatically above the Parliament Building in a feeding fest. They plunge, twirl and drop in the light breeze as they scour a unique buffet.
Just like most African cities, Kigali is hot and humid. These conditions are just right for the propagation of Rwanda's mascot - Amavubi (wasps). Rwanda's national football team is known as the Amavubi Stars. Most rafters, ceilings and hidden cleavages host a nest of these aggressive stingers.
The Kigali Serena is an imposing and immaculately kept building. The cream of Rwandan society is gathered here for the opening ceremony of the Rwanda Film Festival, or Hillywood as it has come to be known. Former US Ambassador to the UN, Andrew Young, is among the gathered guests who include the Minster of Sports, Youth and Culture.
The event has been given immense political support as Rwanda gears up to become a leading filmmaking destination. The government has gone the extra step of putting filmmaking on its political agenda by setting up a film commission.
The theme of the festival is 'sharing stories'. The premier film, 'Rwanda Rising', explores the nations from corner to corner, searching for success stories in this land. The Ambassador Young is the producer. It's his sophomore film.
"The difficult we do immediately, the impossible just takes a little more time," Mr Young tells the assembled audience, adding that "Africa is the hope of the world in the 21st Century".
