The film features the voices of Phylicia Rashad, Jasmine Guy, Forest Whittaker and Quincy Jones. It is easy to tell that the former diplomat has been bitten by the African Bug. Denise Gordon winds up the night with soul performances from her new album.
A haze has settled on Kigali, sitting lazily on the city, creating milky hues. It is 9 am and it's already hot and humid. The view from Gaculiro is heavenly. One of the most upmarket estates, it could easily be mistaken for an upscale American suburb.
The venue of today's showing is a newly constructed church within the estate. It is heavenly and divine in its craftsmanship and design. It is built of brick, with bright earth colour finishing. A blue fountain at the bottom steps gives it a divine touch.
A lone Mercedes Benz zooms past the immaculate black tarmac. Several male and female domestic servants get on with their chores, conversing and catching up on the latest gossip. Two house girls chat excitedly as they ignore the exasperated cries of the infant on one of the girl's back.
A young lady leisurely walks past a group a group of working men. The constant monotonous din of hammer on stone subsides, then stops.
Their collective gaze is drawn to the young lady wearing black Stone Wash jeans. They hug her supple bottoms as she wiggles them past their hypnotised gaze with calculated precision. Her small bosom and tiny waist seem to play tricks on the men's minds. With smooth skin and small dreamy eyes, she looks regal. The common belief is that Rwandan women are the prettiest in the world. Maybe these men know the answer....
This evening we head to Remera Market to buy food in bulk. Just like any market, Remera is a cacophony of noises, hagglers and idlers. With a taxi park nearby, the constant movement is a headache.
Remera has a breathtaking view of Kigali International Airport. From our vantage point, the airport seems to be the only flat piece of land. But looking closely, it is half a hill, shaved, flattened and landscaped straight. This was the trigger for the Genocide, with the felling of the jet carrying the then presidents of Rwanda and Burundi. A white couple has come to shop for second-hand clothes, seemingly for a charity.
St Famille, adjacent to the main road, is the oldest church in Rwanda. But it also happens to be the most infamous of them all. Built in 1913, its doors have been modernized to iron and painted brown. St Paul's Hall - in St Paul's Pastoral Centre - is just a walking distance below.
Inside the brick cathedral, the texture is accentuated by the white stripes painted in between the bricks. It feels like a remote outpost in Outer Mongolia.
The altar is huge and magnificent. A giant watercolour painting of Jesus Christ looking sombrely down on his worshipers and standing above the Kinyarwanda words "Umuryango Mutagatifu" (Holy House). The benches are wooden and fixed on iron rails cemented to the ground.
At the height of the 1994 Genocide, several thousand Rwandans took refuge in the church, in the naïve belief that the holy house would keep them safe. The Rwandan priest in charge of the church handed them over to the militia and, to this day, the massacre of helpless people still haunt it. As one priest asked after the Genocide, "We (the Church) have been in Rwanda for a 100 years; what have we been doing?"
Today's agenda is specifically for women and their aspirations to have a voice in the male dominated industry. The highlight film is a 15-minute feature entitled "Nkundira Umwana" (Love My Child), by the Abiyubaka Forum, a group of survivors of the Genocide. It is their interpretation of the events of that period and the brutal scars, as well as the offspring sired in the violence.
Maison des Jeunes de Kimisagara is on the edge of the city. The building is lined up on one side by small mud-brick shacks that are built on the hillside. By night, once the wicker lamps of the hovels come on, one can easily mistake this for a skyscraper. At the top of the hill is the main prison - simply called Gereza ya Kigali (Kigali Prison). But many know it by its year of construction, loudly inscribed above its formal name: "1930". Just 1930.
Here we watch such movies as Kibera Kid, set in the sprawling Kibera Slums of Nairobi, reputed to be Africa's largest. Poverty and Africa are synonymous no matter where you go; the squalor and mire is the same. Even the causes of it are the same - poor leadership, corruption and economic mismanagement.
A series of animated films from all over Africa thrills this young audience. My digital camera's batteries are dead, so I decide to hitch a lift to town with some of the organisers of the festival. One particularly tall and lanky fellow called Jimmy is so 'long' I am always tempted to ask him how the weather is 'up there'.
At the Nyabugogo Bus Park I hunt around for some Duracells. There are none but a multitude of imitation brands. In my desperation I decide to buy a pair of what is on offer. But they go dead even before I take the first picture of the Agasake Bank building at the base of the hill. Because of their cheap prices, imitation brands have a ready market in Africa. Entrepreneurs and investors cannot possibly compete with these low-cost products.
I walk into a restaurant, order for a soda and deep-fried cassava. That is my lunch for today.
Legends Restaurant hangs on a cliff overlooking the snaking roads down below. In the valley are some industrial premises. We sit to watch 'Death of Two Sons', a documentary film made by Harvard University. Amadou Diallo, a strong black man, went to New York City with the same dream as other immigrants - achieve his dreams.
Amadou came from Guinea in West Africa - a tiny, predominantly Muslim nation at the edge of the rainforest. He already had a first degree when he went to America. One night as he came home from work, he was shot 19 times as he tried to retrieve his wallet.
He died at his front door, under a hail of 41 bullets. The Street Crimes Unit of the New York Police Department was responsible for the fatal death. Their motto? 'We own the night'.
At about the same time Diallo moved to New York, a peace corp volunteer left for Guinea. They never knew but the volunteer's neighbour in Guinea happened to be a relative of Diallo. A few weeks after Diallo's shooting, the volunteer and his friends decided to go hitch-hiking into the interior.
They boarded a public service vehicle, the old ramshackles which are usually overloaded. They were involved in an accident with a tanker. The volunteer died on the spot. The driver of the car was charged and convicted of dangerous driving. He was sentenced to three years in jail.
In New York, the trial of the officers climaxed on 4th February 1999. Each of the officers was acquitted of all charges relating to the fatal shooting and death of Diallo. The uproar brought the ire of African-American activist Rev Al Sharpton. These were some of the films on show in Hillywood.

Original_Search_Title: The 'Lost Tribes' and the Bliss of Innocence
Headline: Kenya: The 'Lost Tribes' and the Bliss of Innocence
Body_Text:
Nairobi — To arrive at the secluded Milimani village in Boni village, Lamu District is a humbling experience. The residents, known as the Waboni, have never tasted some of modernity's basics, yet this does not hamper them from enjoying life.
Ambala Ware Wakati, a homeguard at Boni, and his mark IV rifle. The village is almost crime-free.
At the end of a two-hour sail from Lamu, Milimani village, home of the Waboni, peeps from behind coconut trees. You can get to the village through Kilifi, but it is a much longer route. Beyond the village, all you see is the Indian Ocean. Eager women wrapped in khangas appear from the huts to welcome visitors, a rare occurrence we come to learn.
There is no electricity and no TV in the village of about 300 residents. A small portable radio is their source of information and link to the rest of the world. It sits on the lap of a young boy. Once in a while, someone from Lamu brings the dry cells that power it.
The nearest hospital is 100km away, while a walk to the shops takes a whole day. When this writer displayed a cell phone, young men standing nearby referred to it as kijidude, a Kiswahili word for gadget.
Too bad there was no network to show how the gadget works. The majority of the villagers have no idea what a cell phone looks like or what it is used for.
So isolated is Boni village that the residents have no knowledge of major events taking place around them. Although they are Muslims, they are not part of the Maulid festivals in the antique island of Lamu that mark Prophet Mohammed's birthday.
Huts in the village are made of local material structured in unique frames. They are mud-walled and the roofs are thatched with makuti.
Tribe of bad luck
Although the land is dry, the residents grow bananas, cassava and other food crops. The only animal in sight is a chicken foraging for insects. Most of the food is bought from Lamu. Occasionally, the village gets donations of food and clothing from the Red Cross.
Depending on who you ask, there are different versions of why the Waboni live in isolation. Although a few Waboni live in Lamu, they too live on their own.
"The Waboni are also known as Wasanya who are feared in the coastal towns as people who bring bad luck to those they interact with," said Sina Neno, a Giriama elder who lives in Lamu.
But another version has it that since they know the bad luck they bring, the Waboni are only comfortable among their own. Call them forgotten, unwilling to embrace change, isolated or plain ignorant, but the villagers of Boni embrace each day as it comes.
Two tribes known as the Wabajuni and the Waboni live in the village. Both speak the coastal Kiswahili language. Other coastal people refer to them as "the lost tribes of Somalia" because of their Bantu origin - the majority of the Somali are Cushitic.
Boni neighbours the islands of Manda, Lamu and Pate. If you sail past Boni to the north, you are likely to arrive in Somalia, while a longer trip to the south takes you to Zanzibar.
It takes two hours to sail to Boni from Lamu Island by dhow, but you have the option of using a speedboat if you choose. It is better to set sail in the morning to avoid stormy waters later in the day when it is more windy.
No schools
Boni is a place not frequented by many. Speaking in Kiswahili, women lead the way into their village. A group of young boys offload bags of cement from a different boat unperturbed by our arrival. The cement will be used to build two boreholes at Sh2 million.
Celtel, the mobile phone company whose awaited services might be the only modern communication means here, sponsors the borehole project. The borehole is a welcome gesture, as the villagers previously drew water from the sea.
Other villagers stare at visitors while seated or standing in groups outside their huts. The village gives a rough idea of what the Coast might have looked like before it was invaded by the Portuguese, Omani Arabs and the British.
It is Wednesday. While other children would be in school, those at Boni play in the village, doing nothing in particular. They have no school to attend.
At one end of the village is a deserted building that once served as a nursery school. The only teacher who was available took off one day and never returned. "Our children are getting married without even learning how to write down their names," says Mangai, an elder in the village.
The most learned person in the village reached Standard 8. Mangai fears that children in the village may never see the inside of a classroom.
The borehole project and fishing keep most of the men busy, while the women attend to children. The scores of children in the village belie the frequency of maternal deaths.
"When complications arise during pregnancy, the best we can do is pray. It is safer to stay in your house during childbirth than to trek to the hospital," says Fatma Warr.
The villagers live like a big family and everyone knows the other. In one of the huts, a patient is suffering from hernia. He endures the pain, saying he does not want to go to hospital. It is too far, he says.
Apart from Somali robbers who frequently attacked the village many years back, stories of intruders are rare. Forty-year-old Ambala Ware Wakati, who has been a home guard for eight years, clutches an old Mark IV rifle. He and 56-year-old Warre Soroba, are two of the seven home guards in the village.
Barefoot in their tattered police uniforms, they have never set eyes on Internal Security minister John Michuki. Their only worry is that no one pays them for their trouble.
"Here we are with Government property protecting our people but with not a coin to show for it," says Warre. They do not remember the last time they used the guns, as the village is almost crime-free, but they would like some money for their upkeep.
Both have families of eight children and their job is to keep vigil for any attack. "If taken to Nairobi, we can deal with the criminals we hear are a giving the police a headache. When it comes to handling guns we are next to none," says Ambala.
As the rest of the country endures campaign violence, elections are the least of the Waboni's worries. They do not have identity cards, so they will not vote.
"We would like to vote like the rest because we are also Kenyans but only one person in this village has an identity card," says Abu Chiaba, an elder.
Big dreams
A 22-year-old man, pushes through the crowd. "My name is Adi. When a school will be built in my village I will study to become the president of the country so that I can assist my people," he says in Kiswahili.
I ask him a few questions about the country. "I know Nairobi. It is where there are many thieves that you cannot walk without holding tightly to your belongings," he says.
He has also heard that there are tall buildings in Nairobi and if he gets there, "my first stop will be at the house where the President (Mwai Kibaki) lives".
Although he cheekily refers to the President as 'Kabaki', he cannot describe him as he has not seen his picture. Although he wants to be Kenya's next president, he has never voted and he has never ventured out of his village.
After speaking to us, he slips away from the crowd, relieved that he has made his dreams known.
Seated outside a hut is 14-year-old Yai. Like Adi, she has never been to school. She appears withdrawn and answers questions with the help of her mother. She does not know what she would like to do in future, but she hopes to go to school.
As a token of appreciation to the visitors, the women dance to Erma and Bere tunes, songs that symbolise happy times.

Original_Search_Title: The Poor After April 2007
Headline: Nigeria: The Poor After April 2007
Body_Text:
Lagos — Arondizuogu is one of the largest rural communities in Imo State in South East Nigeria even as a sizeable percentage of the inhabitants are reputable subsistent poor rural farmers who are mostly engaged in the production of palm kernels, cassava tubers and a variety of fruits. But a major impediment facing these poor rural farmers is the total lack of basic infrastructural facilities like good road network, health facilities and potable drinking water even though Arondizuogu community harbour tributaries that flow into the legendary Imo River.
Shortly before the 2003 general elections, the question that was on the lips of most rural poor in Arondizuogu was how far can the political office seekers honourably and truly implement their campaign promises because of the realization that the promises made by the current office seekers to better the rural communities were not implemented.
Eight years after the advent of democracy in Nigeria, the majority of the rural poor interviewed in Arondizuogu in Imo State, Kafanchan in Kaduna State and Auchi in Edo State as part of effort to put up a well researched write up, told me that they are yet to witness any strategic change and that poverty has even increased. These set of people I interviewed told me pointedly that they have lost faith in the capability and willingness of political office seekers to better their living condition. These apprehensions were eventually vindicated when the United Nations Development Programme issued a country report last year and asserted that rural and urban poverty has increased astronomically in Nigeria. A conservative estimate of seventy five percent of Nigerians are said to be living below the extreme poverty benchmark.
On March 14th 2007, the European parliament deplored the high rate of poverty in Nigeria and called on government to take drastic action to better the living condition of millions of people in the country. Excerpts from the full text of resolution B6-105 (EPP-DE) obtained by this writer from the website of www.ukgaynews.org.uk shows that the European parliament in clear terms urged the federal and state governments in Nigeria to implement result - oriented measures to check the rising trend of rural and urban poverty. The parliamentarians in their words stated that' "whereas despite efforts made in recent years by the Nigerian government to promote human rights and to stem corruption, and despite some improvements in respect for civil and political rights, a number of urgent and basic human rights issues remain to be addressed and the country remained marred by corruption, arbitrary arrests, torture, and extrajudicial Killings and political violence. Whereas ethnic and religious divisions, as well as widespread poverty, are major causes of chronic inter communal violence". In 2006, the United nations development Programme (UNDP) reported that' "though Nigeria's Gross Domestic product per population in 2004 was $560 United States dollars (a little over the poverty line of $1 US dollars per day), the situation has not changed in the last 30 years. While less than half of the population has sustainable access to improved sanitation, the number of people with access to clean drinking water has dropped from 48 to 46 percent over the last 14 years while the average life expectancy for other low developing countries is 58.7 years, Nigeria's stands at an abysmal 43.4 years."
Specifically, in the state of food, insecurity in the world for 2006 a report by the food and Agricultural organization Sub-Saharan Africa accounts for thirteen percent of the population and twenty five percent of the under nourished people in the developing world. It is the developing region with the highest proportion - one-third-of people suffering from chronic hunger. Hunger in sub-Saharan Africa is as persistent as it is widespread. Between 1990-92 and 2001 to 2003, the number of undernourished people increased from 169 million to 206 million".
The report stated that Nigeria is among the few in sub-Saharan Africa that saw only a marginal reduction in the numbers of undernourished people while the prevalence declined.
The report also suggested pragmatic ways of ending this terrible high rate of hunger and accordingly stated that growth in food production is in deed key to hunger reduction in sub-Saharan Africa. Productivity - driven increases in food production have been shown to have strong positive impact on the rural economy, leading to increased food availability and reduction of food prices in local Markets. At the same time, the enhanced incomes of small holders the main producers of staples - provide a stimulus to rural economic activity by generating increased demand for the products of other sectors that are either linked to agriculture like processing and agricultural services or supply consumption goods to farmers.
The food and Agricultural Organization rightly suggested among others that public investment is essential for Agricultural growth.
Public investment in infrastructure, agricultural research, education and extension is indispensable for promoting agricultural growth. Writers of the report by the food and Agricultural organization rightly stated that actual public expenditures on agriculture in many poor countries do not reflect the importance of the sector, particularly in those with high prevalence of undernourishment. This affirmation is the truth in Nigeria where this writer observed that several farming communities have not benefited from the numerous pro-agricultural programmes and policies introduced by the current administration. Several farmers do not know how to access credit facilities to expand their farming activities just as the total absence of infrastructure like motor able roads and storage facilities means that these farmers can only remain subsistent farmers with the capacity to feed a few months even as hunger and poverty continue to ravage millions of Nigerians.
The poor living condition of most Nigerians has made it imperative that the political office seekers need to tell Nigerians in very clear terms the strategies and modalities to be truly adopted by them to better the excruciating living condition of millions of Nigerians. What is the place of the poor after the historic April 2007 elections? If one may ask. Umaru Musa Yar'adua the erudite scientist and governor of the North - West state of Katsina who is the Presidential candidate of the Peoples Democratic Party said he will definitely implement measures to end poverty in Nigeria.
Yar'adua is not alone because other presidential candidates like professor pat Utomi a distinguished academic, Chris Okotie a lawyers and cleric and Orji Kalu have all out lined what they considered as workable measures to end rural and urban poverty in Nigeria.
Yar'adua who sounded more passionate about the need to abolish poverty through deliberate revolutionary pro-poor policies of government said if he is elected in April 2007 he will ensure that poverty becomes history. According to him, the abundant natural and human resources in Nigeria would be galvanized by his federal administration to end the cycle of poverty that has entangled millions of Nigerian families in the unfortunate web of insecurity of life and property.
Introducing a divine dimension, Yar'adua emphasized thus; "By God's grace, poverty in Nigeria will become a thing of the past because of God's endowment to us. PDP will lead Nigeria into an enviable nation".
Yar'adua anchored his priority programmes on a seven point all -encompassing agenda to include power (energy), agriculture, industrialization, education, human empowerment, poverty eradication and redemption of Nigeria's past glory.
In a recent publication carried by a national newspaper in Nigeria, the World Bank President Mr. Paul Wolfowitz said the best way to tackle rural and urban poverty is the immediate improvement of governance and fighting corruption through the due process of the law.
The American born Wolfowitz rightly observed that, " governance is about having transparent governments, accountable governments that's why parliaments are so important as a check on the executive; that's why the free press is important as a vehicle for conveying information to the society; that's why civil society and the private sector is so important because increasingly, governments are responding to the pressures that come from their own people."
The poor in Nigeria want to see a post April 2007 that will bring about economic prosperity, transparency and an accountable government that will do more in improving their living conditions.
Onwubiko, an Abuja based Journalist is a federal Commissioner in the Nigerian National Human Rights Commission.

Original_Search_Title: Joy As Local Invention Works Wonders On Pest-Infested Farms
Headline: Kenya: Joy As Local Invention Works Wonders On Pest-Infested Farms
Body_Text:
Nairobi — He is an ordinary, peasant farmer who has leased land for horticulture to earn a living in Matanya area of Laikipia District.
But to local farmers, Robert Ngari, 45, is a dependable researcher. He is more dear to them than the Government's agriculture officers are.
Thanks to him, many landowners are returning to their land, which they abandoned over the past three years following a massive invasion by millipedes.
Ngari has a local invention to control the arthropods. Now farmers can once again venture into horticulture, which is fairly lucrative.
A millipede, he discovered, does not die easily when sprayed with poison, unlike other pests and insects that attack growing plants.
Swallow poison
"You must ensure that the millipede swallows the poison for it to die. Otherwise, spraying it with chemicals is useless. I even tried to sink millipedes in a container of water mixed with diazinon (a poisonous solution used to control pests), but they did not die," he says.
At first he tried several types of edibles to see whether the insect would consume them.
"The food that millipedes seemed to like most is maize germ, which we usually give our livestock and chicken," the farmer says. After adding diazinon to it, the millipedes devoured the mixture and started dying.
Ngari's new invention is good news to horticulturists, hence their return to the area. Matanya is semi-arid. Horticulture farmers depend on the perennial Burguret River for irrigation.
Since millipedes are restricted to moist places, where they feed on organic matter, it is mainly within the valley where this river courses through that they thrive.
The area is usually wet. Mulching to protect shooting plants from the scorching sun provides shelter to millipedes.
Last three years
"We started seeing them (millipedes) about three years ago. Soon they were countless. They quickly destroy tomatoes and French beans just as they start to shoot," says local farmer Julius Wahome.
The farmer had been selling tomatoes in Mombasa over the past 10 years. He temporary stopped doing so after the millipede invasion, but has now resumed the business.
Mr Wahome, who has a three-acre piece of land, recalls: "The millipedes could not be managed. They came in large numbers. We first noticed them during the rainy season of 2004. Each time it drizzled, they seemingly sprouted from the ground. They invaded growing crops and our homes."
Besides ripening tomatoes and French beans, the millipedes also destroyed maturing cabbages and shooting cow peas.
No crop was safe from them except maize, the farmers say. They add that even ordinary beans and Irish potatoes, which are grown for subsistence in the area, were not spared.
Leaves of bean stalks and Irish potatoes were "cleaned out by the creatures," thereby damaging the whole crop. The millipedes also damaged mature potatoes by drilling holes into them and partially eating them.
For three years when the pests reigned supreme, farmers were in pathetic state.
The maize crop, the only one spared by the millipedes, was not reliable, owing to insufficient rains. Beans and Irish potatoes used to do better in the area as they didn't take long to mature, unlike maize.
Currently, for instance, the maize crop that was planted in October has failed, owing to a prolonged dry spell from January to this month. But beans and Irish potatoes in millipede-free farms have flourished.
"We can't plant beans or Irish potatoes in many areas, yet the two crops used to do fairly well here," says Simon Mureithi.
Mr Mureithi, who says he was born in the area in 1948, adds that the farms had become useless to them despite their previous good productivity.
He started farming in the area 27 years ago, soon after he got married. He says they have never seen such an invasion of millipedes.
"It is a serious crisis. Even sweet potatoes and cassava are suffering." Following the millipede attack, peasant farmers like Mureithi have had to hire land elsewhere to grow beans and Irish potatoes, which are their staple food.
"We are now planting maize and nappier grass in Matanya for our animals."
A few years ago, the main problem farmers in the area encountered was the occasional rogue elephant. The area is sandwiched by Mount Kenya on the eastern side and the Aberdare ranges on the western side. It is an elephant corridor.
But elephants are no longer a nuisance following the erection of an electric fence around the Aberdare National Park.
The millipede invasion was, however, a more gigantic problem. Elephants used to invade the farms only twice a year. But millipedes, which lie beneath the surface of many farms, strike every time it rains, or when irrigation is done.
Laikipia district crops officer Stanley Mutuota says millipedes are a headache, mainly due to their large numbers. But he says the challenge they pose is not as big as the farmers claim it is.
"These organisms (millipedes) are essential to soil productivity. They improve aeration by turning the soil around, besides helping in soil structure formation," he says.
Crop rotation
"With proper crop rotation, farmers will not have any problem with millipedes," he says.
"Those who heed our advice and practise crop rotation and husbandry will not complain of millipede infestation," he says.
But farmers in the area disagree. They say that although they own about three acres each, which is very little, they have tried crop rotation and other practices, to no avail.
Before discovering the local remedy to the millipedes, Ngari had tried crop rotation, without much success.
The father of three says he left his one-acre coffee farm in his rural home in Mathira Division five years ago. When the prices of coffee started to fall on the world market, he decided to try horticulture in Laikipia District. The land there is more expansive.
"I made a small fortune in the first few years from tomatoes, cow peas and French beans," Ngari recalls.
But when millipedes started becoming a nuisance, he followed the advice of agriculture officers to the letter.
Plant water melons
"Crop rotation does not seem to work against millipedes. I was advised to plant water melons on a farm I had hired by agriculture experts," he recounts.
That was in 2004. He says he sourced seedlings of the fruit from Mwea Division of Kirinyaga District. All the 40 seedlings he planted on a pilot basis were destroyed by millipedes, he recalls.
He was devastated. "I could not go back to my rural farm and its abandoned coffee trees. So I had either to move out of Matanya to continue with horticulture farming, or to start another occupation," he says. Necessity is the mother of invention. Ngari came up with a solution to the problem. It is now working wonders on many farms.
The millipedes are still in large numbers, but farmers now have a deadly weapon against them. It is not meant to wipe them out completely, they say, but merely to control them. This allows the young plants to sprout. Farmers resume the control measures when the plants, fruits and vegetables start to mature.
"The Government should come up with a solution to the millipede peril," they say.

Original_Search_Title: Return Banned Insecticide to Curb Malaria, Experts Say
Headline: Kenya: Return Banned Insecticide to Curb Malaria, Experts Say
Body_Text:
Nairobi — As the country turns its attention on malaria, which kills over 90 children daily, debate on whether to reintroduce a chemical banned in the 1980s is gaining new currency.
Dichloro-diphenyl-trichloroethane (DDT) was very effective against disease-carrying mosquitoes, but was quite harmful to the environment.
Since Monday this week, the Government has been carrying out indoor residual spraying of synthetic pyrethroid insecticide in 16 malaria pandemic districts (11 in Rift Valley, three in Nyanza province and two in Western province. The exercise is one of the major ones to mark this year's World Malaria Day.
It is expected to last two months, with 680,000 households benefiting.
The spraying is also in response to last year's warning by Kenya Medical Research Institute scientists that malaria cases are likely to increase by 80 per cent in the early part of this year.
Officials of the Malaria Control Programme have, however, ruled-out use of DDT in this year's exercise, citing the current, prohibitive policy.
DDT has been shown to work effectively in eradicating the malaria parasite, with World Health Organization releasing guidelines on its application and the United States funding arm, USAID, agreeing to fund countries ready to use the insecticide.
Even with these developments, the government has decided to trend carefully on the issue after countries in Europe, the biggest buyers of horticultural crops from Kenya, threatened to impose an embargo on Kenyan goods if DDT is used.
Quietly however, sources within the Ministry of Health intimidated that discussion around whether or not to change the policy on the use of DDT have been going on at the highest decision making levels.
While this is happening, Kenya's neighbour, Uganda, has successfully managed to change its policy on the use of DDT as one of the interventions in malaria control, after convincing the very countries in Europe.
The Ugandan argument was based on a study done to show that DDT was safe in the control of malaria when used for residual indoor spraying.
Dr Myers Lugemwa of the Department of Medicine, National referral Hospital, Mulago and one of the researchers behind the study says they conducted the research to prove DDT can be used safely by malaria endemic countries to eradicate the disease.
"Developed countries got rid of malaria by 1967 with use of DDT. Similar benefits have been registered by some Asian and African countries, and there is no reason why we should not use it," says Dr Lugemwa.
"Compared to the amount of DDT used as a pesticide in agriculture, the one used for disease control is negligible to have any adverse effects."
To him, when the chemical is applied strictly for indoor spraying, it poses very little environmental threat, if there is any.
DDT is an insecticide used in control of disease carrying vectors like malaria parasites. It is a persistent organic compound, meaning it can stay in the environment for over 10 years, long after its initial application.
In this long period, it can find its way into the food chain, a viable route to enter the human and animal body system.
During an interview with the Nation while attending a recent workshop on malaria in Nairobi, Dr Lugemwa said: "We are ready to share with our Kenyan brothers on how to move on this issue and the strategies to apply when using DDT in malaria control."
B. Byarugaba, W. Byarugaba, G.Bimenya, and L. Okwi, were the other scientists from Makerere University's Pathology Department, Faculty of Medicine, who conducted the study whose findings were released last year.
In the study, they found low levels of DDT in parts per billion (ppb) in the human blood samples, urine, soil, beans, and fish, even after being exposed to the chemical for a long time.
Parts per billion are units commonly used to express contamination ratios and help in establishing the maximum permissible amount of a contaminant in water, land, or air.
In two of the fish species that had ingested the insecticide on the shores of Lake Edward in Kihihi, the concentration was 22.98 ppb for Bagrus and 4.92 ppb for Protopterus, against the between 200 and 800 ppb known to be detrimental to reproduction.
In Kihihi and Nyarusiza, ppb in human plasma was 52.41 and 9.65, respectively.
Soil samples in the study sites returned less than 7.28 ppb, while urine samples had 11.66 ppb (Kihihi) and 6.91 (Nyarusiza), all described by the scientists as low levels.
Concentration in crops such as Bananas, Potatoes, and Cassava, which had also been exposed to the insecticide, was zero, notes the study titled, probing the persistence of DDT and its effects in residents and the environment of Kihihi sub-county in Kanungu district, Uganda.
Conducting the study in Kihihi was a deliberate move by the scientists because DDT and its derivative, diphenyldiethylene (DDE), was used successfully in the area 45 years ago to control malaria.
Volunteers involved in the study were females and males aged over 40 years, and who had come into contact with the insecticide as spray men or those whose houses were sprayed.
People whose houses were regularly sprayed with DDT and who were still alive presented with no traces of the chemical in their urine or blood when tested during the study.
Even those who did the spraying of the chemical and who participated in this study as volunteers too had not suffered the side-effects propagated by the opponents of the DDT, according to Dr Lugemwa.
There were also no associated environmental damage or human defects of infertility, impotence, neurological abnormalities and cancers, adds the study.
"DDT used in Malaria control persists in the environment and in human tissues at harmlessly low levels," the study concludes.
Says Lugemwa: "The whole debate around the insecticide is politics. The very people cautioning us not to use it applied it to eradicate malaria in their countries and continue to make huge savings due to the absence of the disease."
"The scientific approach we used was to convince those who insist on having data that shows the chemical is safe for use."
Giving the example of South Africa, the Uganda researchers say Kwazulu Natal was able to reduce malaria related deaths from 300 in 2000 to just 1 in 2003, after using DDT.
Likewise, Sri Linka reduced malaria deaths from 80,000 in 1935 to 17 when they applied the insecticide in their malaria prevention programme.
The United States on the other hand is said to have used over 800,000 tonnes of DDT before they banned it. By this time, they had managed to eradicate the disease.
Lugemwa argues that what people should concern themselves with are the levels of the insecticide to be used in the control of malaria.
Indeed, the Stockholm Convention on Persistent Organic Pollutants (POPs) and the specific recommendations of WHO which guide the use of DDT allows its production and use for public health purposes only.
But the convention is very strict on the issue of the amount used. Countries opting to use the chemical as an insecticide are required to submit information to WHO and the Secretariat of the Stockholm Convention on the amount of DDT used and conditions of such use every three years.
The Uganda scientists' findings were also bolstered by previous studies which have too shown the insecticide to be less harmful to human and animals when used in certain permitted levels.
A number of studies in rabbits have indicated normal reproduction even after the animals were fed on the insecticide for a longer period.
In the publication, "Chlorinated Hydrocarbon Insecticides", Smith A.G. says dermal exposure to DDT has not been associated with illness or irritation in a number of studies.
Again in A review of Selected Persistent Organic Pollutants by scientists at the Canadian Network of Toxicology Centres, it is observed that studies conducted in human volunteers with DDT in dosages considered safe returned encouraging results.
"No volunteer complained of any symptoms, or showed any sign of illness that did not have an easily recognizable cause clearly unrelated to the exposure of DDT," say the scientists in the review publication.
In the Ugandan study, the scientists recommend that the Uganda government may safely use the insecticide in the control of malaria using the Kihihi model. It should also effect an awareness programmes about how it should be used and the accruing benefits.
Environmentalists have however stuck to their guns that use of DDT has negative effect on environment and wildlife, which is manifested in the form of a dysfunctional reproductive and immune system, cancers, and developmental disorders, among other things. For Kenya, it is still a wait and see stance.
- An AWC-Feature

Original_Search_Title: Witnessing the Ame University Funeral
Headline: Liberia: Witnessing the Ame University Funeral
Body_Text:
These are excerpts from a cleric and a school pupil's conversation somewhere around the administrative building:
School Pupil: "Are you serious Bishop, by the end of the tenure you will be a millionaire?"
Cleric: "That's exactly what I mean; I'm not discussing illusion my young man, I soon as am out as Bishop, I am in as a Liberian millionaire.
School pupil: "Oh, Great idea! Great idea! So, by what means?"
Cleric: "Little inquisitive chap, you do not have to be a UL student to decipher how much power I've to turn the wheels of fortune. Oh, you think I spent all my money to become Bishop for nothing. The country running ooh, my pekin (small brother)!
School Pupil: "You are leaving vague expressions in my mind. Who you care to comment?
Cleric: "To be frank, everything is now 'spoils of war' and the 'documents pass through my office before going to the oversee partners.
I waited in vain anticipating the early morning crow of the few hens and roosters left in the community. I rolled in bed, or rather changed position from one end of the mattress to the other, keeping this motion a pattern whenever a particular place soaked-wet with perspiration. I kept at this tactics until every spot on the pallet soaked deep with sweat as if immersed in a bucket of water. As a matter-of-course, in the ensuing discomfiture, the only alternative to the thinking mind would be, slipping off the mattress onto the cold flood for temporary comfort. Dishearteningly however, the congestion of the room's square, and the fact that the four-inch mattress filled every space in the tiny room negated this option. The only additional space in the US$50 a month downtown rented room, was occupied by a table that had a lamp, a pile of books and a single bowl used to mix my gari (cassava dough). And so, my fate would be left to be decided by renegade mosquitoes operating within my premises without permit, singing their strange songs as they feasted on my most vulnerable parts.
I rolled again to the next end of the pallet and jammed the wall, taking advantage of a little dry spot. My ears remained open, eager to hear the fowl's announcement of coo-coo-lea-coooo! But instead of hearing the crowing of a cock, the merciless stinking of the lethal insects brought to my mind a fresh reminder of my being a product of the AMEU, an Institution once associated with all the positives; now with a chief mastermind of negations and from all the many diabolic unearthing, it would appear as though it is a part of our culture to import all the unwanted religious toxics there, and as far as our happy history of this great revolutionary learning laboratory is concerned, the unraveling contretemps are the perfect doings of a cleric who has now come to undo what great bishops of the 14th Episcopal District of the AME Church had collectively done in the running of the fastest growing degree granting institution , my dear African Methodist Episcopal University.
These are the woes that baffle even the least literate, my eighteen-month old boy being amongst them. He once questioned, "It is true that all the good people in the AME Church are dead. What has happened to all the white bishops from America? Why did the Church leave all the good people there and tote all the bad things to come bring trouble to us?" These were not mere questions thrown to test the brightness of a father, rather, my boy sought cogent and satisfactory answers in the midst of torturing agony; his tears filled his eyes and pour over like a pit less carrier. I was still in deep sleep as my boy became infuriated. He cried restlessly without comfort and then he started chewing his book bag leaving the threads fully exposed. Without any available corridor for solace, he did the unbelievable: started the masticating the threads left over. His shouts compelled gathered birds to be strewn to the four winds of the heaven. But that was many years back though memories are still afresh as though it was just yesterday. These are the compelling factors motivating my fight against eco-social ills. Almost my entire generation got sacrificed on the desecrated altar of merciless dogmatism. And I must fight to prevent others from being sacrificed; this is how I honor their memory, not vice versa.
I still remained in bed however, hugged to the wall awaiting the announcement of a particular rooster that refused to crow. There were similarly other colleague roosters and hens, too in this neighborhood. You could find them everywhere a year ago until just now. Someone has said that the prevalence of unparalleled corruption at the AME University under the current regime has led to a conspiracy theory against chicken kingdom! Even chickens can no longer be allowed to be domesticated in the midst of the absence of everything. Dogs, too, are in harms way as scores of animal thieves roamed all over the place, carrying hammers and sticks carefully fixed to hustle a 'whuu' (Vai name for dog) for a hot market! I still waited, half awaked, half asleep and it was beginning to look like the chickens were on a go-slow. But no, the ones in my community had simply been quieted due to the fear of some "lynchpin" the era and we were left without a crier to wake us up daily. But all was not lost, a nearby mosque took up the responsibility and the early morning prayers of their religious leaders console us to forget the fowl's disappearances.
I was now up, I anxiously put my phone on, being opportune to be a faithful subscriber of Lonestar Cell, my phone has a clip art coding system, I received over 50 messages signaling people wanting to call me. The first caller was an unknown student, only identified as Fred. "The bombshell has been dropped". He echoed. Alieu, the Student leader at the University and his co were suspended for two semesters" There were other callers, one after the other, all sharing the already acquired information. There were voices filled with intermixture of facts, some bit of fantasy, a little more of sentimentalism, and a lashing at friends and foes alike. Almost all of the callers, except two took a clear position on the unfolding institutional burlesque. On the other hand, as the maelstrom whirkpooled across the land, the conversation became entrenched in the minds, mouths and midst of the ordinary students. And day, by day, it seemed to be gearing up to unimaginable proportions to the disadvantage of the conspirators.
First, the factor of timing heavily weighed against the masterminds of the subterranean intrigue. In their underestimation of the influence and inveigles of Alieu Nyei, they unleashed the game plan in manners and forms totally detached from the most rudimentary political wisdom. Had they not been overly emotional, unnecessarily overcharged and, having worked out too much with fist punches bigger than their boxing gloves, they may have at least succeeded to a level we can not determine. The cleric should have known that the premature hatching of a conspiracy to kick Alieu Nyei and others out with polo boots would have created a countermeasure. To think that they expected Nyei to play freeze like his name and form a cheering squad to go into applaud for his own demise, is a monumental flabbergast. This is their dilemma; two semesters or more will be left as an open space where anything could happen before the set date to present the resolution. In his calculation, once you get the board to reach a decision, it becomes the "word of God that goes out and cannot return void." But I am sure they have now learned in the strictest way that the legitimacy of any decision is determined at conscious, not in the cabal chambers of an existing parallel throne! More so, when you gave two semesters to an Alieu, it will simply be melted by descending heat waves and in the process, every space is occupied through expansion. So, the current countermeasures are just a part of the expansions of the melted Alieu, Bility, Charles and others that has just now transformed into tormenting heat waves causing a lot of discomfort for the disciples of lawlessness.
From another crotch, the concourses of the unethical warriors proved to be astonishingly misguided and unequivocally consigned to mediocrity. This fact became evident when the cabalists refused to change strategy even though their mode of operation and the very axis, upon which the conspiracy spanned, had become public knowledge. When "The School's Funeral; beware the ides of March" was envisaged and written, it was not an attempt to build an embankment for the shielding of an individual, it was an objective drive to prevent the paralysis of a shrieking cleric who receives over Thirteen million Liberian Dollars annually that amounts to 90% of the AMEU's running cost that it is diverted to someone's personal coffers. The schemes of an unsuspecting religious mischief maker who sub contracted a spinster, Christiana, who is on records for calling the Student Leader an Islamic Muslim fundamentalist thereby fueling religious tensions and sanctioning the campaign for suspending Alieu on the basis that he is Muslim leading students at a church run university. More so, when the church' annual conference is about to be staged, the cleric is embarrassed that his shady deeds will soon come to light. This is the key motivator behind the purported scam to dislodge student advocates who are bent to seeing the growth of the institution.
Hence, the objective mind will easily see the nature of the plot revealed. As early as 2005, the Bishop's first casualty was Professor Forh, now Congressman, who he managed to muscle out to resign as Vice President to set the agenda to empty the coffers, it is baffling how 'distinguished members of the school's board who allow their thoughts to be subpoenaed to please the cleric. This is absolutely unfortunate! Outside of that terrain, the devil-may-care approach of these clerical cronies, oops! I mean, Board members who appear to have placed themselves on oath to satisfy the bishop and save their sitting per diems, which leaves us wondering as to whether they still deserve the honorable appellation.
As I view this matter from a vantage pedestal, my hands and mouth overflows with shocks, surprises and utmost suspense. That a cleric will stoop so low and remains an eminent danger in tearing to pieces what other bishops have helped to construct disappoints us all. Unlike the satire of pigs which portraits the dirt-ridden animal as having no understandable value for the gold ring pierced in its nose, this is an assumed "man of God" who must realize who he is! Pluralistic Liberians in an ever changing multicultural environment have good reasons to raise concerns in the unfolding institutional embarrassment. Lord, save us from evil! Sadly, the mawkish approach of this honorable cleric in the unfolding hodgepodge deserves the condemnation of every well-meaning son of the soil. Whilst it is their right to discipline students involve in riots, this was just a wakeup call, a mere genuine protestation to bring to the attention of administration that the COUNTRY WAS RUNNING and parents could not afford the 80% down- payment. Whatever the outcome of the mediation presently going on between the Education Ministry and the Bishop is, I will do a formal communication to the A.M.E. Church in South Carolina, USA requesting the replacement of the bishop, and the delay in reinstating the students will only be paralyzing the Bishop's faded objective in becoming a millionaire.
Thank goodness, some members the US-based Church Council are here to witness the conference!

Original_Search_Title: Coke Turns to 'Kokonte'
Headline: Ghana: Coke Turns to 'Kokonte'
Body_Text:
The cocaine racket that has hit the Police administration has taken a ludicrous twist following the outcome of forensic tests carried out on recently seized drugs.
The Chronicle has gathered that tests conducted on the substance, which was believed to be cocaine that was captured in the operation conducted in Tema by a group of officers from the Monitors Unit at the Accra Regional Police command, have proved negative.
Sources at the Police Headquarters hinted the paper that the parcel of the substance, when sent to the Crime Laboratory for a forensic test had turned out to be what is known in Ghanaian parlance as Kokonte (cassava flour).
It would be recalled that the paper had reported that some officers at the Monitors Unit had gone for an operation in Tema upon a tip-off and returned to their command base with a parcel of what was supposed to be the narcotic substance that had been captured in the operation.
It later emerged that the actual quantity that was captured by the peace officers was more than one parcel and so questions were raised about the whereabouts of the remaining parcels.
Subsequently, a civilian, Richard Ackon Mensah, was arrested by officials of the National Criminal Investigations Department, attempting to sell cocaine at Nyamekye, a suburb of Accra, which he later confessed was given to him by a member of the Monitors Unit, Lance Corporal Russell Ekow, who was part of the team, which went for the operation in Tema.
Meanwhile, at the same time Ekow had been given a 10-day period to produce a suspect named Sebastian, who had been arrested in the Tema operation, for negligently allowing him to escape while he was escorting him from the Labadi Police cells to the regional Headquarters in Accra.
At the moment, officials of the Accra Regional Police Headquarters have decided to keep mum on the issue on the grounds that the whole issue about the cocaine racket had been referred to the national Criminal Investigations Department (CID) Headquarters for investigations.
Some of the top hierarchy in the Accra Regional office had expressed ignorance on the drug racket with the explanation that they were not investigating the cocaine issue but rather were investigating how Ekow who went for the operations had negligently left Sebastian loose.
About two weeks ago, the Accra Regional Commander, DCOP Douglas Akrofi Asiedu a.k.a. Charge, told reporters of the paper at his office that he was not aware of issues related to the cocaine but said what he was aware of was that Ekow had been given a ten-day ultimatum to produce the suspect. "So what I know is about the jail break for which he was to face service enquiry," he added.
DCOP Akrofi said he had heard the CID headquarters had arrested Ekow on a drug-related matter but that was not under his domain.
The Accra Regional Crime Officer, Mr. Boi Bi Boi, when contacted last Sunday, was emphatic that he was not aware of the details of the cocaine case and that it was a matter in the hands of the National CID.
The Accra Regional Police Public Relations Officer, Clemence Dompreh told The Chronicle in an interview that he was not aware of the cocaine testing negative but what he was aware of was that the case was under investigations by the CID.
"I am not aware about the cocaine testing negative. What I am telling you is that I am not aware of anything you are asking me of. The fact I know about is that the case is being handled by the CID headquarters" he told The Chronicle.
Meanwhile, Chronicle sources have expressed shock at the attitude of the police insisting that there had been a directive, which instructed that all forensic reports should be forwarded to their various Regional headquarters, where such requests were made from.
Suspicions are high that the cocaine turned into Kokonte in the hands of the police, since part of the cocaine that was captured by the CID, when it arrested Richard at Nyamekye proved positive at the spot testing.
It is believed that either the parcel that was submitted by the officers on the Monitors Unit were substituted with the Kokonte and presented as part of the exhibits found in the operation or the cocaine turned into Kokonte after submission to their bosses in the office.
The Chronicle has received a secret signal from the Police headquarters that the Inspector General of Police (IGP), Mr. Patrick Kwarteng Acheampong is fuming at the cocaine racket in the police service and has therefore instructed the CID to investigate the issue meticulously without fear or favour.
The Sources said the Police Chief was ready to deal with any officer who is involved in the case.
Chronicle further efforts to contact the Operations Director of the CID, ACP Charles Tokor proved futile as he was said to be engaged when this reporter got through to his cell phone on two occasions. One Alhassan, who responded to the phone call on one occasion, assured getting back to the paper but as at press time, no one had called.
Meanwhile, seven officers from the Monitors Unit at the Accra Regional command were yesterday, again escorted by their colleagues to the Criminal Investigations Department (CID) headquarters for interrogation on their alleged involvement in the racket.

Original_Search_Title: Bill Gates Funds $37.5m Initiative
Headline: Africa: Bill Gates Funds $37.5m Initiative
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Bill Gates Foundation has announced the funding of a crop biodiversity initiative with $37.5 million.
The initiative, aimed at safeguarding 21 of the world's most critical food crops through securing their seeds, was a joint programme of Global Crop Diversity Trust and the United Nations Foundation.
It is also aimed at boosting the implementation of the new Food and Agriculture Organisation (FAO) international treaty on plant genetic resources.
In a statement made available in Abuja, the director of the trust, Mr Cary Fowler, said the grants would finance research into the world's most important food crops that were critical to the poor, but largely neglected by plant breeding.
Some of the orphan crops are potato, yam, cassava and coconut among others.
"Although some orphan crops like yam cannot be grown from seeds, but needed to be cultivated from cuttings, roots or cell cultures thereby making their conservation more complex and expensive, but it would be included because it is important to the poor," he said.
The initiative will fund 400,000 samples of more than 2,000 species of 150 crops amounting to 85 per cent of the diversity of all agricultural crops.
It would also develop state of art genetic bank management software system and enter at least 50 priority collections for 100 different traits of hidden genetic resources.
He said the efforts would be made to secure more than 95 per cent of the underfunded and endangered crop diversity in genetic banks of developing country .
Fowler said the new technologies were expected to reduce conservation cost by 75 per cent and improve the security of collections of such crops.
He said the initiative would also transform communications for plant breeders and farmers around the world to search genetic banks for traits needed to combat new diseases and
cope with climate change.
The President of the Gates foundation, Mr Sylvia Burwell, said the efforts was to help hundreds of millions of rural farmers and their families to overcome poverty and hunger rested in part
on food security.
"There can be no food security without first securing the basis of food production," he said.
He said crop diversity was vital part of the solution to many of the world's great challenges from environmental conservation to climate and food security.
Burwell said plant breeders across Africa would be able to adapt their crops to varieties that would grow in different climate conditions.
"Out of the total grant,15 million dollars would go to the trust's endowment while the proceeds would be used to maintain the collections of the 21 most important crops," he said.
The UN foundation was created in 1998 with entrepreneur Ted Turner's historic #1 billion gift to support UN causes and activities.
While the Global Crop Diversity Trust was to ensure the conservation and availability of crop diversity for food security worldwide.

Original_Search_Title: 1972 All Over Again? Well, Not Quite!
Headline: Uganda: 1972 All Over Again? Well, Not Quite!
Body_Text:
Dr Jamal is a Uganda Asian returnee. His grandfather was already in Arua by 1904. He originally lived here from 1946 to 1972, with breaks abroad for furher studies and career. He says:
"First and foremost, I extend my condolences to the three families that lost their kith and kin on that fateful Thursday. The increasing references to the Idi Amin period (Mehta, do you want another Amin?) were ominous, but I must say that during that whole three months of the [1972] expulsion, local folks never menaced us. And nobody was killed. It was scary, because Amin used every dictator's trick to frighten us into packing up, with statements like All Asians Must Go (Uganda Argus, August 21, 1972). I still have a copy of that newspaper. His directive encompassed even those who had taken up Ugandan citizenship. Their papers were thrown into dustbins during bogus verification exercises at the Immigration Department.
So the demonstration on the other Thursday surprised me because of the amount of anti-Indian violence it unleashed. I was walking to my restaurant from the Dastur Street corner after my habitual commute on the matatu when I got surrounded by a crowd of youths. Luckily, I was oblivious of what had happened in town, just an hour ago, otherwise I would have made to run, and then one word - "Mehta" and.....!
Any person of brown skin was a target, and, this, I learnt later, included an Arab acquaintance of mine. A black woman was also mistaken for an Asian because of her fair skin -- and escaped a beating by an inch! There is a picture of her carrying her even-fairer-skin child. Next day, I moved about freely, so I am not sure how deeply felt the resentment against Asians is - and whether some hotheads for their own benefit did not whip up the masses.
The President, the UIA, the Chamber of Commerce, and political parties made statements deploring the racial aspect of the riots and assuring all residents of Uganda, of whatever race, of their security. From a noble grievance - the arrogant giveaway of one-third of our last remaining forest, to the sugar company (the least efficient of the three!); dictating terms to government, and the government going like this was the best thing that ever happened to Uganda - sprang two subplots: that all Asians supported Mehta and all of them are very rich!
Neither of the subplots was true. The Indian Association in a post-riot meeting disassociated itself from the Mabira give-away, going so far as to criticise the illustrious Mehta family (a century of cane-growing in Uganda). And not all Asians are rich. Wasn't Devan Rawal dragged off the common-people's transport, the boda boda? Don't I ride the matatu? You don't see that many Indian cars, do you?
What people have not realised is that what is referred to as "the Asian community" is quite different from what it was pre-1972 and that there is a vast gulf in incomes within that amorphous community. There are now around 20,000 "Asians" in Uganda. Almost 90 per cent are recent immigrants from the sub continent, filling semi-skilled positions in industry, trade, banks, and hotels. Many of them have graduated upward to owning shops. Some of the new immigrants are part of a wave of investors from India.
Uganda Investment Authority boss Maggie Kigozi, in a thoughtful comment on the riots, pointed out that India was now number 3 in the list of foreign direct investment (FDI) in Uganda.
Then, around 2,000 or so of the original Ugandan Asians have returned. Two or three notable ones never even left Uganda; many others returned as soon as peace reigned, but mark: only 2,000 out of 75,000 who were expelled have returned; the vast majority having dug roots in the UK and Canada where they have been praised for their diligence.
These 2,000 Asians are often in the news (I like to think) for their contribution to Uganda's economic development in industry, banking, hotels, services, etc. Last week, as a hark-back to the Amin days, they were in the news as perennial exploiters of the common people. And the whole Asian community cowered indoors. The fact of the matter is, the top of the income distribution profile in Uganda is now mixed; it includes scores of Africans, and poverty is higher than it was in 1972.
In 1976 in my PhD dissertation at Stanford University (topic: The Role of Cotton and Coffee in Uganda's Economic Development) I showed that one per cent of the people who were Asians owned almost 40 per cent of the "modern economy" - commerce, industry, plantations, and professions.
Now the income profile is even more skewed, but it includes scores of Africans who are often richer than the top Asians. Many of the very rich (of all races) have been benefited with even more flagrant land give-aways. There is pent-up anger there -- because the working people are being left behind.
Poverty was never at the-now one-third level in 1972. Granted, it was at one-half when the NRM government took over, but trickle-down is just not happening fast enough. Growing and glaring inequality is a worldwide phenomenon in these days of unfettered globalisation. Another global phenomenon that rings a bell is destruction of forests.
Most of Brazil's forests were destroyed in just two centuries from 1500 to feed the sweet tooth in Europe, powered by slave labour. And now the rest will be finished in just the next two decades to fill car tanks with ethanol and bio-diesel, powered by equally cheap labour.
Deforestation and cheap labour are almost synonymous with sugarcane. Ninety eight per cent of the forests in Malaysia and Indonesia will be gone in the next decade to make room for palm oil -- again to power tanks in Europe. (some of those 4WDs in the West are like army vehicles.) Fossil oil is finite and fast depleting.
No amount of land planted to bio-fuels can ever fill the gap, but that is not going to deter entrepreneurs from peddling sugarcane, palm oil, maize, soya, sorghum, sweet potatoes or cassava as the answer. In the course of this, if forests are destroyed then that is the way of business.
If because of the warming, the Arctic snow melts - then why, good, we shall be able to dig out the oil there! Even chip-oil (i.e. oil used in cooking chips, etc) will be offered. I, as an owner of a restaurant, could be one of those entrepreneurs vending recycled cooking oil, but let me assure readers not all others will be brown!"
Mr Jamal was the Senior Economist of the International Labour Organisation from 1976 to 2001. He has a BA from Cambridge University and a PhD from Stanford University. He has also published extensively on the Uganda economy (see www.google.com).

Original_Search_Title: Uncertainty Persists As Citizens Go to the Poll
Headline: Nigeria: Uncertainty Persists As Citizens Go to the Poll
Body_Text:
Abuja — Vice-President Atiku Abubakar and other presidential candidates of the Opposing parties are contesting today's polls. Dr Adionyi Onukabar Ojo, a media aide to the Vice-President, said so in a telephone interview.
A youth puts up a fresh election poster for a senatorial candidate over posters that have been torn down in Nigeria's commercial capital, Lagos, on Thursday. Nigeria holds presidential and senatorial elections today. Photo/ REUTERS
The confirmation of Mr Atiku's participation has removed one question over the Nigerian elections; the question of whether the Vice President would boycott the elections. Such talks have been rife since Monday, when the oppositions parties began calling that the previous Saturday's state governorship and legislature polls be cancelled owing to massive irregularities. But other question marks still remain and new ones have even arisen.
First, is the question of security. In Kano, the heartland of the Moslem North and its commercial city, a running battle has been on since Monday between what Nigerian journalists have dubbed Moslem fundamentalist. Soldiers have been called out to patrol Kano streets and engage the religious militia in gun battle. Since Monday, the taps in Kano have remained dry as the militia seized the city's water works.
The insurgents have so far targeted law enforcement agents; they have attacked police stations and mowed down policemen, and their family members. So far, 50 people, including 20 policemen, have died in Kano and the toll keeps rising by the day. The militia numbering more than 500, are said to be made up of not only Nigerians, but also people from Chad and Niger Republic. That they are well-organised and that the Nigerian security officials knew nothing about them is a source of worry. This is because if other members of the group or another group are in other states and cities, today's presidential polls may be jeopardised far, what has kept the matter from spreading is that the militia has not attacked non-Moslems. If the fight turns against non-Moslems, it would spread far and wide.
Secondly, though the elections have not yet taken place, their credibility have already been substantially damaged. Meeting on Wednesday and Thursday, the main Opposition parties almost agreed to boycott the presidential and the national legislature elections after the malpractices and the wanton violence that trailed the state elections last Saturday.
An insider said the only voice against boycotting the polls was that of former military Head of State and now All Nigeria Peoples Party's flag bearer, Gen Mohammadu Buhari. He argued that though he knew the elections would be rigged, boycotting them would make the other parties lose the right to challenge the outcome in both the legal and public opinion courts. So the trouble is that the Opposition parties are canvassing for an interim national government. Perhaps this explains why the police have banned campaigns this week.
Then there is the Interim National Government. Ever since President Obasanjo's tenure elongation attempt failed, many have accused him of programming the elections to fail so that he could lead an interim government and organise the elections later.
Yet, there are things above the capacity of the Senate to rectify. One of them is the court case that is already on to nullify the elections. One of the parties, the National Democratic Party, instituted it. The ruling is being awaited with trepidation.
Also, it's not clear whether Mr Atiku's name and picture are actually on the ballot papers. It was only last Monday that the Supreme Court ruled that INEC lacked the powers to disqualify anyone from contesting any election. Unless there was a contingency plan and INEC had printed two sets of ballot papers before the judgement, it would be difficult for INEC to discard with all the presidential ballot papers it had printed before to print new ones bearing the name and logo of Atiku's Action Congress party, as well as Mr Atiku's name and picture. If any of the four is missing, then Mr Atiku and his party will predictably head for the courts to stop nullify the elections.
Thus, Nigeria is walking the famished road not once, not twice, not thrice but for the fourth time. The first time was in 1964, when the flawed elections led to the military intervention in 1966. The 30-month civil war started a year later. The second came in 1983 polls and the army struck on New Year's eve. The third was the annulment of the 1993 elections by the Gen Ibrahim Babangida regime, followed by an interim government that the late Gen Sani Abacha tossed aside months later and replaced it with his murderous five-year rule from which his death freed Nigeria in 1998, making way for the return to civilian rule in 1999. Will this be the fourth time? Unfortunately, the signs are ominous as novelist Chinua Achebe warned last week in a letter to the Press titled the "Clouds Are Gathering".
Last Saturday's elections showed that Mr Achebe was right, after all. First, six candidates were barred from contesting the polls on flimsy grounds. The Supreme Court ruling of last Monday did not just benefit Mr Atiku; it obviously included the six governorship candidates who will surely head for the courts too to seek by-elections or outright cancellation of the elections.
Killings, mayhem
Killings, mayhem and protests have continued to trail those State government elections. By Monday, a curfew had been imposed in six out of Nigeria's 36 States as wanton destruction descended on 20 states. In Ibadan, soldiers were called out to patrol the streets on the election day, but that could not entirely stopped lawlessness.
Such incidents were not limited to large towns and cities. Mr Okonkwo Nwadiafor was an ad-hoc electoral officer for last Saturday's elections in the village of Ubulu-Uno village. He told of how at 6pm, gun shots rang out right beside his polling booth, bringing the tension that had been building up to a climax; he and the other officials scampered for safety. He ran into a cassava farm and from on to a thick forest. Three hours later, he arrived the electoral commission office at Ogwashi-Uku, minus the ballot boxes and other electoral materials. He did not know how really lucky he was until he read the following day's newspapers; some of his colleagues were killed as mobs. That day, hundreds were killed countrywide.

Original_Search_Title: Banana Wilt Destroys Crops in Kasese
Headline: Uganda: Banana Wilt Destroys Crops in Kasese
Body_Text:
Kasese — KASESE district authorities have expressed concern over looming famine in the district due to the out break of the banana wilt Disease.
The disease broke out in parts of Kibuga parish, Karusandara sub-county, Busongora County where over 20 acres of banana plantation have been affected.
The district agriculture officer Teddy Munyanzikwiye confirmed the outbreak in an interview recently.
Wayfoward
"We have already formed the District Banana Wilt Bacterial Taskforce to handle the situation," Ms Munyanzikwiye said.
She said the district has instituted aquarantine on the movement of bananas from the affected sub-county to other areas in the district as a way of combating the disease.
Ms Munyanzikwiye also said the district taskforce will soon embark on sensitising the masses on control measures.
The beer bananas commonly known as kayinja have been the most hit by the out break.
Over 20 other districts have been hit by the disease since 2005.
Scarcity of food
Meanwhile, most families in the district are having one meal a day, according to the district agricultural officer.
Ms Munyanzikwiye said this follows poor harvesting in the district for the last two seasons.
Food security
She said the district's staple food which is cassava had been hit by cassava mosaic.
She appealed to the residents to grow food security crops like millet and potatoes along side cash crops like coffee and cotton.

Original_Search_Title: Labour Seeks Two Years Grace for Floor Mills Over Cassava Fortification
Headline: Nigeria: Labour Seeks Two Years Grace for Floor Mills Over Cassava Fortification
Body_Text:
ORGANISED labour in the food and beverages industry has described the two months grace given to Flour Mills Industries in the country to comply with the 5 per cent fortification of Cassava Flour as unrealistic saying most bakers are complaining of less yield and short life span for Flour fortified with cassava leading to either closure or skeletal operations of majority of flour mills companies.
Under the umbrella of the National Union of Food, Beverages and Tobacco Employees (NUFBTE), labour lamented that in addition to negative effect on operations, the jobs of members of the union are under threat.
NUFBTE in a communique at the end of its National Executive Council (NEC) meeting held in Enugu recently, appealed t.o the government to grant the flour mills industries a grace of two years to comply with this directive.
In a communique signed by Comrades John Oyenemere and Idowu Yussuff, President and General Secretary, respectively, the union said: "The NEC in-session viewed that the two months (2) grace given to Flour Mills Industries to comply with the 5% fortification of Cassava Flour as unrealistic as bakers are complaining of less yield and short life span for Flour fortified with cassava, this has made majority of the Flour Mills Industries to either close down or go on skeletal operations thereby putting the jobs of our members on the line. The NEC in-session therefore appealed to the Federal Government to grant the Flour Mills Industries a grace of two years to comply with this directive." On the epileptic power supply across the country, communique said members ofNEC expressed sadness that since the past years, the government. could not provide stable power to the citizenry despite billions of Naira spent on power.
According to t.he communique: "The NEC in-session is dissatisfied over the epileptic power supply which went down from 3000 mega watts m 1999 to 1000 mega watts in year 2007 in spite of 600 Billion expanded on power generation. The NEC in-session therefore call on the Power Holding
Company of Nigeria (PHCN) to take stringent measures to ensure adequate electricity supply to ensure the smooth operations of our industries so as to restore the hope of our employers in building this ,nation. Since Electricity\Energy supply for production has been so costly in our industry, the NEC in-session is therefore appealing to the Federal Government to approve a tax-relief for companies running their operation, on Diesel so as to ease their financial burden. The NEC in-session having considered all areas, calls on the general overhauling 'and total review of all tax laws in the country which are outdated and begging for review. It is on this note that workers believe that whatever increment is made on salaries and other Fringe Benefits may not be meaningful."
Commenting on the political situation in the country, the communique added: "The NEC in-session icondemned -in totality the shoddy, unconstitutional insensitivity, double standard, political meddling and very outrageous decisions INEC had taken to satisfy the over bloated ego of its paymasters and their nefarious intentions and therefore advised INEC to thread softly and avert the eventual disaster that is I waiting to happen which will be a great cost to our democracy."

Original_Search_Title: Mabira - Poverty is the Foe
Headline: Uganda: Mabira - Poverty is the Foe
Body_Text:
Kampala — When people talk of government listening carefully to Ugandans' cries about the request of Mehta to turn some part of Mabira to cane growing, a question that comes to my mind is: Do the majority have the big picture about development and environmental protection?
Of course nobody in Uganda can teach the National Resistance Movement to do what we have done best: Listening to the people. Listening to the people however, should be done with caution, because the overall responsibility of governing Uganda was delegated to us for the next five years.
The delegated powers of the people come with responsibilities. We need to guide our people correctly lest we are blamed for generations to come. Democracy is double edged; the people should be empowered to make their feelings known to the leaders at the same time the leaders have the responsibility to guide the population.
Some of you who are Bible readers know the story of how Aaron succumbed to the pressure of the people, in the absence of Moses, and joined them in honouring the Golden Bull Calf (Exodus 32, 1-35). Government will listen, but it is also its responsibility to share with the same people the big picture.
With Uganda's GDP averaging 6% per annum in the last ten years, and projected to increase if some structural bottlenecks like inadequate power and poor infrastructure are finally settled, there will be more "clashes" between development and the environment. This should not to be surprising, it has happened in elsewhere.
