What should be of concern is how do we strike a balance between development and environmental protection? As most of us may know, the biggest enemy of the environment is poverty.
Fighting poverty in a country like Uganda is not easy. With population growth in excess of 3% per annum, fertility rate of seven children per woman, we need to be innovative. Even if all our efforts to control birth rates were to be successful, Uganda's population, standing at close to 30 million today will more than double in the next 20 years!
Any visionary should anticipate the implications of such population trends and ask themselves: where will the 60 million Ugandans be employed. Developed countries and emerging economic giants all had to contend with this issue. They chose development.
Without a plan definitely means that you will have 60 million people scrambling for a fixed land mass and the forests we so dearly want to protect today will end up being cassava gardens and overgrazed unproductive land. No government shall be able to restrain he unemployed landless peasants from encroaching on any forest.
We need to transform our economy so that the bulk of our people shall be employed in industry. Like the President has said on several occasions, the future lies in processing. Processing will require strategic investors ready to invest in large scale agriculture and in turn organise small scale farmers into out-growers to feed into the nucleus plantations (factories). The case of "Mukwano" (Tea growing in Kabarole) and Kinyara (Sugar-cane-in Bunyoro) are there for all to see.
Consider the case of my district, Mubende with peasants all over the place destroying the environment with overgrazing and poor farming methods. Then consider Mehta or Madhvani, even if these two "destroyed" some part of the environment, but because of their better farming methods and processing capacity, they substantially satisfy the demand for sugar in Uganda contributing considerably to the national coffers, not to mention employment.
We are now able to finance our budget to the tune of 56%, we can now afford to have education for all (for 12 years in school), we can sponsor 4,000 students in public universities and our capacity to improve our infrastructure has been enhanced.
Uganda will continuously become a better destination for investors because of improved education levels, infrastructure and government capacity to cushion investors against the higher initial costs of production through provision of industrial parks.
At such a stage agriculture will be handled by a minority using better farming methods to protect the environment. The land pressure that is becoming a problem in Uganda is a result of former governments' policies that sought to preserve the peasantry.
It is just recently that we have seen the structure of the Ugandan economy starting to change: by the year 2002/2003 agriculture accounted for 38% of GDP compared to 52% in 1991/92 -on the other hand, services and industrial production increased as a share of GDP from 49% in 1991/92 to 62% in 2001/2002, that is the way to go.
Given the foregoing, the debate over Mabira should be wider to capture the wholesome development aspect of Uganda. We have to choose between the two extremes, by striking a balance between whether we should "functionally destroy" the environment with development or become the "conservation paradise" of the world amidst poverty- which poverty will ultimately destroy the environment whether we like it or not.
The writer is Minister of State for Luwero Triangle.

Original_Search_Title: Need for Community Involvement in Development
Headline: Nigeria: Need for Community Involvement in Development
Body_Text:
Lagos — Ohief Jegede Titus Oladimeji, hails from Ido-ajinare community in Ekiti State. He is a retired teacher who was in the profession for forty years, presently he is the community development chairman in his community at the Ekiti West Local Government Area.
The trip to his community was quite a bumpy one, as the road that leads there was almost inaccessible. Chief Jegede, an old man in his seventies said that though they are located in a secluded area, education is their main industry as they have all the professionals in the education sector ranging from lawyers to doctors, amongst others.
"This community produced the first science graduate in the whole of Africa. We know what education is and we know that we have to train our children", he stated. He described what his community looked like before United Nations Children Fund (UNICEF) came to them. "Before UNICEF came to our aid, we had a crude source of water supply which was mostly from streams and there was no hygiene. Some rich people amongst us though, boiled their water. But after UNICEF came, we have all benefited because we enjoy the water we are now drinking. Water-borne diseases were very rampant, but now there is a gulf of difference and that is why we should be grateful to them", he explained.
Community-driven development, as described by Mr. Tayo Adesoji, communications officer, UNICEF, is what would help the community get involved in its developmental activities. Here, he said the communities are consulted to give their advice on issues that affect them. But, what we are concerned with at UNICEF, he said, is mostly the sustenance of a programme that is put in place. What we do here is ask the people what they want, what they lack and in so doing we have made them a part of development and if we (UNICEF) are no longer there they can carry on.
"Communities are picked from questions that are asked of their deprivation and the kind of development here is providing a basic necessity for the people in the community", he said.
He added that communities are expected to have an early child care center, child-friendly primary school, portable water, safe means of excreta and waste disposal, women skill acquisition center, youth recreational facilities, community development associations and a structure put in place for management of these development programmes.
UNICEF, Chief Jegede said, had sent representatives on a visit to his community three years ago. A dialogue ensued between members of the community, the community development officer, Ekiti West, Mr. Oluwadare Johnson Olurotimi, and the UNICEF representatives and the conclusion was that the community was in dire need of assistance. Their little request then, he added, was satisfied.
Jegede said that the programme by the UNICEF organisation started with the day care programme, Early Childcare Centre (ECC) where teaching materials were provided for them. Also, toys, plastic chairs, television sets, video compact disc players and a generator were also made available.
"Provision for portable water supply was made, motorcycles for easy movement and drugs were also supplied to the maternity center," he said.
However, on the case of good health facilities, Jegede, lamented that they are still suffering. He stated that they have only one maternity clinic that is poorly managed with no staff at all. "The doctors assigned to the clinic come at random and only when they actually feel like. The nearest hospital is about 15 kilometres away. So we are pleading on this ground that our clinic should be equipped", he added.
Another need, he highlighted, was the need for an accessible road. Apart from education, he said, agriculture is one thing that they have focused on. According to him, the community has surplus food supplies.
The community still adds its contribution in their little way, Jegede explained. The community members contribute their quota through its Ido -ajinare day, a day that is set aside for celebration. "Here the sons and daughters of the community are all present to celebrate. For instance, the community hall is a project that is being sponsored by the community and we have said at its completion, it shall be used as a recreation hall", he said. Supply and if the road is repaired, then transportation of these supplies to the market and other communities would be easy.
Olurotimi said that he started working with the project in November, 2002. He has worked on the CD programme for Alafia and Ido Ajinare communities, and has attended various training workshops organised by UNICEF.
He commended UNICEF, saying that he has gained so much experience in terms of man power development through the training workshops. "I have been exposed through my interactions with UNICEF officers. In fact, I can't quantify my knowledge in terms of health and community development programmes", he said
The challenges he has to face, he added, is from the local government management saying that they don't support the programmes financially.
In the Iloje community, Owan West local government area of Edo State, community development chairman, Chief Jolly Odigure, described the genesis of their community development programme. He said that their CDD project started in 2003, with twenty-five members in the committee. "When UNICEF came, we agreed on the project to be carried out. UNICEF then promised that they would support us, and this started in 2003. We had our meeting and contacted the community elders", he said.
In 2004, Mr. John Oviagbede volunteered his house to be used as health centre. There was full operation at the hospital with immunisation services delivered and basic treatment administered and also, permanent staff was brought here. Before this, there was no health post in the community as the nearest hospital was the General Hospital which is about 10 kilometres from here located at Uzeba. Then in 2005, we contacted one of our sons, Julius Ihonvbere and appealed to him to assist us in building a health centre which he agreed to do as we already had the permanent site ready but no structure. This he obliged to and in addition, he equipped the centre with ten beds, ten mattresses and twenty eight plastic chairs. Also a television set and a borehole was also dug near the center", he explained.
Since the intervention of UNICEF, Odigure said they have not had a record of child death unlike before when it used to occur frequently. They were provided with ECC materials. "The life of the people has changed as regards the health, now, we do not have to trek a distance to get children immunised or delivered", he added.
UNICEF, he added, has also provided them an engine used for grinding cassava, with which the little income generated is put into an account for sustenance of these programmes.
According to Odigure, in the area of education, there has been development. "With the assistance of UNICEF we established an adult literacy centre and supplies of writing materials, pencils, slates, chalks, rulers and toys were given to us by them.
Since 2003, the ECC centre has been functioning, we have had 25 students graduate from the 2004/2005 session while in the 2005/2006 session, 15 pupils have graduated. Also, UNICEF gave us a motorcycle.
"On the area of water supply, before UNICEF came to our aid, there was no clean water, but now we have a borehole. Though we still appeal to UNICEF to help us with the other borehole that was promised us", he stated.

Original_Search_Title: Supermarkets Edging Out Small Farmers
Headline: Uganda: Supermarkets Edging Out Small Farmers
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE emerging markets especially in urban areas are predominantly sourcing food products from other countries, a Makerere University official has said.
"This implies that local farmers and producers are being crowded out by foreign competitors," said Dr Gabriel Elepu, a lecturer in the Department of Agricultural Economics and Agribusiness.
"Even if the growth in market share of supermarkets in agri-food retailing may be slow, this trend is likely to continue," he said in a report, The Growth of Supermarkets and the Implications for Agribusiness in Uganda.
"Therefore, the Government needs to identify ways of supporting farmers and agri-businesses to meet the needs of modern supply chains and also assist traditional agri-food marketing systems in competing with supermarkets," he said recently.
The research, funded by the Uganda Programme for Trade Opportunities and Policy (UPTOP), was aimed at analysing supermarket food procurement systems, sources, requirements and procurement problems.
He said that research has shown that supermarkets especially Shoprite and Uchumi offered a wide range of food products including fresh, staple and processed foods, fresh fruits and vegetables most of them imported.
"For example, apples and oranges are imported from Kenya and South Africa while staple foods like rice are from Pakistan and India," he said at a two-day workshop in Kampala.
Sources for fresh fruits including apples, mangoes, melon, pineapples, banana, oranges, onions, cabbages, lettuce, beans, carrots and peas among others are Uganda, Kenya, South Africa, Israel and India.
Supermarkets source rice, maize and millet flours, cassava, Irish and sweet potatoes from India, Pakistan, Vietnam, Kenya and Uganda.
Processed food like cereals, confectioneries, dairy products, beverages, soft drinks, tinned food, spices, dried and packed fruits are from Uganda, Kenya, South Africa, Denmark, New Zealand, Italy, Tanzania, United States and UK and Thailand and England.

Original_Search_Title: Cassava Brown-Streak Hits Mukono
Headline: Uganda: Cassava Brown-Streak Hits Mukono
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE cassava brown streak disease has hit Mukono district, threatening a famine outbreak. Over 6,000 acres of cassava in the affected sub-counties face destruction.
The disease is characterised by the rotting of the root tuber and stunted growth though the plant may look 'healthy' with green leaves and stems.
In the long run, however, the plant succumbs to the virus and collapses. "At first, I thought it was the usual (cassava) mosaic," says Fred Wamala, a farmer.
Cassava mosaic causes yellowing of the leaves, shrinking of the stems and in the long-run, drying of the plant.
It attacked farmers in Mukono a couple of years ago, but a successful campaign led by the district agricultural department restored productivity.
Doreen Kataama, the district agricultural officer, identified the most hit areas as Kasawo and Seeta-Namuganga sub-counties.
She says farmers in the two sub-counties grow cassava on a commercial basis.
The disease cannot be cured, therefore, an affected plant must be identified and destroyed immediately.
"And if cooked, it has a sour taste. It cannot be used for making alcohol," says Betty Mukwana, the deputy resident district commissioner.
During his visit to Seeta-Namuganga sub-county, Hillary Onek, the Agriculture minister, urged farmers to destroy the plants forthwith. "We (the Government) are looking at availing the affected farmers with virgin stems for planting," Onek said.

Original_Search_Title: Support One Another in Times of Need, Women Told
Headline: Zimbabwe: Support One Another in Times of Need, Women Told
Body_Text:
Harare — WOMEN should stand up in support of each other in times of need, especially during election times, a cabinet minister has said.
The Minister of Women Affairs, Gender and Community Development Cde Oppah Muchinguri told businesswomen in Marondera yesterday that there was need for women to promote each other. The women, who included farmers, miners and health workers were gathered at Marondera High School. Cde Muchinguri also spokes strongly against gender imbalance and domestic violence.
"Women should support each other during election time. District co-ordinators should conscientise people in rural areas concerning Government activities to address gender imbalance. After 27 years as a Women's League, looking at our background women were seen as nobodies and no woman could earn a salary which was more than her husband's. They were not even allowed to open a bank account or to own their own property," said Cde Muchinguri.
Cde Muchinguri said the constitution of the Zanu-PF Women's League makes it mandatory for women to mobilise and support each other. Mashonaland East Governor Cde Ray Kaukonde paid tribute to women for their concern over the welfare of their families. "The Government empowerment of women is gathering momentum. Projects being implemented by the Ministry including cassava and jatropha cultivation as well as mining should be hailed", said Cde Kaukonde.

Original_Search_Title: USAID Director Sees Nothing Wrong With Biotechnology
Headline: Ghana: USAID Director Sees Nothing Wrong With Biotechnology
Body_Text:
Accra — The West Africa Mission Director of USAID, Dr J. Cheema has said that biotechnology is one of the tools that can help African countries to achieve sustainable agricultural growth.
According to Dr Cheema, African agriculture needs to grow faster not only to feed a growing African population, but also to raise incomes and boost trade if the continent is to improve its position in the world economy.
"However, it is not stand-alone solution and must be pursued in conjunction with other improvements in agricultural management such as seed delivery systems and appropriate regulations in order to encourage the private sector investment" she said.
She said this at the third ECOWAS Ministerial conference on biotechnology and biosafety in Accra, which adopted a five-year action plan on biotechnology and biosafety.
Dr Cheema said that effort and regional cooperation are required to maximise productivity and to ensure that biotechnologies are developed and used safely.
She said some experts have estimated that Africa's agriculture needs to grow at around 6 percent to achieve the Millennium Development Goals.
"As a new technology, a lot of questions have been raised about the safety of products of biotechnology but Africa believes it is important for their nations to develop regional regulations" she stated.
According to her, Africa's successes lies in soybeans, cotton and maize, while some of the technologies used in United States may have significant economic potential for the continent.
Dr Cheema said, there are other technologies, which have potential for West Africa such as genetically improved cassava with resistance to cassava mosaic diseases, and technologies to accelerate development of new varieties of crops and diagnostics for livestock diseases.
Speaking at the programme, the Agriculture Officer of Plant Production and Protection Department in Rome, Dr Kakoli Ghosh said biotechnology has increasingly presented many countries with a new policy in the economic development.
According to Dr Kakoli, a strong action oriented biotechnology policy and approach is needed to complement conventional technologies like plant breeding and diseases management systems to allow it to become apart of an integrated and comprehensive agriculture programme.
She stated that, her organisation is not aware of any scientific data on potential negative effects of GMOs on human health or the environment. However, the reluctance of GM companies to label their products sold n supermarkets has given consumers cause to worry that the industry has something to hide.

Original_Search_Title: Ondo NDP Guber Candidate Plans Big for Cocoa
Headline: Nigeria: Ondo NDP Guber Candidate Plans Big for Cocoa
Body_Text:
Akure — National Democratic Party (NDP), governorship candidate in Ondo State, Rev Luyi Rotimi has said that the best way to revamp the ailing industries in Ondo State is to promote domestic use of cocoa production.
Rev. Rotimi governorship who was a former participant at the stock exchange said that if voted into power in the state, he would ensure full participation of Ondo State in the stock exchange market to finance the state capital project.
Rev. Luyi who advocated for a Holding company to support and strengthen the industrialization of the state, pointed out that investments in properties and valuable stock of blue chip companies would ensure industrial growth of the state.
He promised to re-activate all old farm settlements in the state to encourage youths in Agriculture programme, adding that the cocoa plantation, the rubber plantations and oil plantation in the state would be re-planted within the first year of his administration.
The government, he said, would liase with the local government to encourage the planting of rice and cassava across the 18 local government areas.
As part of efforts to ensure stable power supply, he promised to embark on independent power projects to ensure increased power supply to support domestic and industrial use of the state.
He also promised to re-activate the acquired oil bloc of the state, the Owena Oil and Gas Limited and build a refinery for which the state has unused license.
He promised to return the state schools to boarding system with the hope to providing qualitative education for students of the state.
Rural telephony especially the effective linking of local government headquarters would be effectively pursed, he stated, promising that if voted into power the NDP government would pursue the development of games village and the construction of an Olympic size stadium for Akure, the Ondo state capital.
On the development of the coastal riverine areas of Ondo State, Rev. Luyi said the NDP government would collaborate with International Development Agencies to develop the riverine areas of the state.

Original_Search_Title: Heavy Rains Hit Mukono
Headline: Uganda: Heavy Rains Hit Mukono
Body_Text:
Mukono — HEAVY rains that have hit Mukono district caused widespread disaster on Easter Monday.
Property worth millions, several plantations and buildings were destroyed when a storm ravaged Walusimbi and Bajjo villages in Nama and Goma sub-counties respectively.
Over 10 houses were destroyed and over a hundred residents were left homeless. An eyewitness in Walusimbi in Nama sub-county Mr Sulaiman Matovu said three homes in Walusimbi were de-roofed including Mr. Kayondo, Kawooya and Namuvule's homes.
Several banana plantation , cassava, potatoes, coffee were washed away. In a similar incident four people were admitted to hospitals in Seeta because of the effects of the heavy rains.

Original_Search_Title: Nyako - A Patriot in Politics
Headline: Nigeria: Nyako - A Patriot in Politics
Body_Text:
Lagos — It is not my habit to eulogise people, least of all, those in the arena of politics. However, when one sees men of extraordinary character immersing in the murky waters of Nigerian politics, one cannot but take notice of them. One of such people is Murtala Alhaji Haman -Yero Nyako, a patriot, an Admiral and gentleman.
Unknown to many Nigerians, Admiral Murtala Nyako then a Navy Lieutenant first came into the limelight in the Nigerian Navy while in action between 1967 and 68 during the civil war as the commanding officer of a Russian built Naval Patrol craft, N.N.S Ekpen. His exploits and tactical manoeuvers with his boat into the unswept channels of mined rebel territory between Bonny and Port Harcourt so much impressed the Officer in Tactical Command (OTC) and Senior Officer Afloat (SOA), Commander later Admiral Akin-Aduwo that he had to send a special signal to the Naval Headquarters commending the courage and bravery of young Lt Murtala Nyako. Reports by Officers in Tactical Command were taken most seriously by the Naval Headquarters, more so, coming from Commander Akin-Aduwo, reputed for his courage and no- nonsense operational efficiency in war front.
However, behind the iron curtain military strategist that he is, the man Murtala Nyako ordinarily is a pleasant personality to inter-act with, a down-to-earth honest person who discusses issues logically and persuasively, he is humble, kind- hearted and very humorous. He hates dishonest and corrupt people.
As a young Naval Commander, he was appointed Military Governor of Niger State by the late General Murtala Muhammed in February 1976, which was newly created from the then North- Western State. When he assumed office in Minna, the State capital, there was neither adequate office accommodation nor residential accommodation for the thousands of civil servants and their families returning from Sokoto. Nor was there adequate infrastructure on ground to support them. Basic amenities like electricity and potable water were lacking. Electricity supply came from an old and epileptic generating plant which after few weeks, broke down plunging the state capital into total darkness for 5 days. Water supply on the other hand was nothing to write home about as the existing water works were in complete shamble.
Murtala Nyako, together with his team of young commissioners which included technocrats like Sidi Aliyu Enagi, Sallahu Agaie, Idi Kuta, now a senator, Adbullahi Bisalla, Umaru Mashegu along with dedicated civil servants like late Abdulkarim Lafene, late Sulaiman Takuma Mallam Awwal Ibrahim now Emir of Suleja, Engr Akbar and Engr A.A. Lapai just to mention a few worked diligently round the clock to make Minna habitable. With a devastating efficiency, Murtala Nyako re-built the water-works, making water supply available 24 hours in Minna. Electricity supply was also pursued with the same vigour. Several visits by the Governor to the NEPA headquarters in Lagos and 24 hrs detention of NEPA District Manager in Minna yielded fruits, as more powerful generating plants were installed in Minna and other major towns of Niger state.
On housing, within six months, Nyako constructed 500 housing units for both senior and junior civil servants in Minna the state capital using indigenous local contractors and ensuring proper mixture of cement to sand ratio.
While solving the basic problems of accommodation, water and power supplies, Nyako also tackled the issue of indiscipline. First, he launched a campaign against late comers to the office, official corruption, shoddy jobs by contractors and son on. Governor Nyako would be at the gate of a ministry or government parastatal located either in Minna, Bida, Kontagora or Suleja as early as 7 am waiting for late-comers. Once it was 7.30 am, the gate would be ordered closed. Late comers would then be made to fall in together irrespective of rank or status. The Governor would then address them reminding them of their responsibility to the state and the nation, he would warn them of serious consequences they would face if such offence was repeated. He was a delight to watch.
Once, a state commissioner confessed that he was on his way to office when he noticed the Governor and his entourage already at the gate to his own ministry, so he simply made a round- about turn to save himself the embarrassment of being made to line up with junior officers of his ministry for coming late. Such was the degree of discipline that Nyako instilled in the civil servants in Niger state. So that by the time he left the state you could walk confidently in to any of the ministries at 7. 15 am and found everyone already on seat.
Nyako was absolutely incorruptible as the Governor of Niger state and till date does not own even a plot of land in Niger state. Compare this to what is happening in most states of the federation today, where according to EFCC reports, some chief executives treat public funds in their trust as personal wealth leaving the citizens of such states in utter penury, lacking in all social amenities including potable water.
After the selfless service in Niger state he returned to the Nigerian Navy where he rose steadily to become the Chief of Naval staff (CNS) in 1990. He revolutionised the Nigerian Navy by creating an engineering Command called Fleet Maintenance Corps to be headed only by naval engineers. Before then all naval commands were headed only by officers of the seaman department.
This revolution yielded fruits as the naval engineers took up the challenge and became more dedicated to duty. Today naval ships are no longer sent abroad for refit. The indigenous naval engineers carry-out both minor and major repair works on naval ships locally at the Naval Dock yards in Lagos and Port-Harcourt. They have become so efficient that even merchant vessels now patronize them. Hitherto, all major refit of ships were carried out in Europe costing the nation millions of scarce foreign exchange which is now saved, thanks to the patriotic zeal of Admiral Murtala Nyako.
Upon retirement from service in 1993, Nyako diligently pursued the noblest of all professions - farming. Within a couple of years, Nyako's farm located near his country home in Mayo-Belwa, a few kilometers from Yola became the talk of the town as one of the most successful farms in Nigeria. Sebore Farms as it is called, is reputed to be exporting mango and other fruits to Europe thus earning for the nation some foreign exchange.
But the Admiral did not stop there. He wanted every Nigerian farmer to be as successful as he is and so he began to spend his time and energy to organize Nigerian farmers who were in numerous and uncoordinated farmers associations scattered across the country. He succeed in obtaining the Federal Government support for the scheme thus today all the Nigerian farmers now operate under a common umbrella called All Farmers Association of Nigeria (AFAN) of which Admiral Nyako is the national President. The association is so well organized that its organogram clearly shows its national, state and local government officers. Every farm product has its national, state and local government officials, so that if you were a cassava or rice farmer at a remote village you have an officer at your local government area in charge of that crop who you could contact for necessary assistance.
One of the greatest problems that the Nigerian farmer could face is sourcing adequate finance to support his farm. Admiral Murtala Nyako speaking in the farmers language to the President Chief Olusegun Obasanjo who is also a farmer per excellence did not find any difficulty in convincing Baba to make funds available in soft loans to Nigerian farmers. The Federal Government has since last year set aside a whopping N50 billion in designated banks to be given to farmers as loans with interest not exceeding 8 percent.
It was all these achievements by Admiral Murtala Nyako that made the people of Adamawa state to invite him to come and rescue them from the clutches of vision less leaders who have for the past eight years failed to provide the people of Adamawa, with the basic necessities of life vis housing, health care, good roads, good schools and potable water, in spite of billions of Naira collected from the federation account on their behalf.
I have no doubt that with Nyako as the Governor, Adamawa state would in less than two years become the state to beat in terms of development. Wishing the patriot the best of luck. And as sailors would say, Bon voyage.
Mohammed is a commentator on public affairs.

Original_Search_Title: Ugochukwu to Promote Cassava in Ikwuano
Headline: Nigeria: Ugochukwu to Promote Cassava in Ikwuano
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) governorship candidate for Abia State, Chief Onyema Ugochukwu, has said that his government will utilise the vast fertile land in Ikwuano local government for massive cassava cultivation if voted into office.
Chief Ugochukwu, who stated this during a campaign rally in the area, said the venture would not only help in creating employment and revenue for the people but also attract ancillary industries that will boost the state's economy.
The PDP frontrunner described Ikwuano, comprising Oboro, Ibere, Ariam Usaka and Oloko, as a super agrarian area, saying that Ikwuano indigenes and Abians in general would greatly benefit from the scheme, as cassava is in high demand for both domestic and industrial uses.
Ugochukwu who described the state of agriculture in Abia State blamed it on Orji Uzor Kalu's bad administration, promising that his government would right the wrongs of the outgoing administration.
He implored the people to remain and vote for the PDP in all the forthcoming poll stating that his government would surely deliver and rebuild Abia.
While thanking Ikwuano indigenes for coming out en masse to receive him and his entourage, Chief Ugochukwu lamented the state of roads in the area, promising to build roads in Ikwuano if voted into office.
He used the occasion to introduce his running mate, Hon. Chinwe Nwanganga, Senatorial candidate for Abia Central, Hon Nkechi Nwaogu, House of Representatives candidate, Chief Chijioke Nwakodo and Abia state House of Assembly candidate, Prince Henry Eleogu and called on the people to vote for them and the PDP in all the elections, promising that their votes would be protected.
Speaking on the occasion, a member of the House of Representatives, Chief Emeka Atumah, said that Ikwuano people have no other candidate but Ugochukwu and called on his people to vote him and the PDP.
PDP chieftain, Col Austin Akobundu (rtd) and former PPA governorship aspirant in the state, Chief Henry Ikoh, both lauded Chief Ugochukwu's leadership qualities, pointing out that they were sure Ugochukwu would rebuild Abia.
Earlier, Chief Ugochukwu had undertaken a tour of the four clans that make up Ikwuano - Oboro, Ibere, Ariam-Usaka and Oloko, where he paid courtesy call on their royal fathers and council of rulers, who in turn prayed for him and his quest to govern the state.

Original_Search_Title: Nyako - a Patriot in Politics
Headline: Nigeria: Nyako - a Patriot in Politics
Body_Text:
It is not my habit to eulogise people, least of all, those in the arena of politics. However, when one sees men of extraordinary character immersing in the murky waters of Nigerian politics, one cannot but take notice of them. One of such people is Murtala Alhaji Haman -Yero Nyako, a patriot, an Admiral and gentleman.
Unknown to many Nigerians, Admiral Murtala Nyako then a Navy Lieutenant first came into the limelight in the Nigerian Navy while in action between 1967 and 68 during the civil war as the commanding officer of a Russian built Naval Patrol craft, N.N.S Ekpen. His exploits and tactical maneuvers with his boat into the unswept channels of mined rebel territory between Bonny and Port Harcourt so much impressed the Officer in Tactical Command (OTC) and Senior Officer Afloat (SOA), Commander later Admiral Akin-Aduwo that he had to send a special signal to the Naval Headquarters commending the courage and bravery of young Lt Murtala Nyako. Reports by Officers in Tactical Command were taken most seriously by the Naval Headquarters, more so, coming from Commander Akin-Aduwo, reputed for his courage and no- nonsense operational efficiency in war front.
However, behind the iron curtain military strategist that he is, the man Murtala Nyako ordinarily is a pleasant personality to inter-act with, a down- to - earth honest person who discusses issues logically and persuasively, he is humble, kind- hearted and very humorous. He hates dishonest and corrupt people.
As a young Naval Commander, he was appointed Military Governor of Niger State by the late General Murtala Muhammed in February 1976, which was newly created from the then North- Western State. When he assumed office in Minna, the State capital, there was neither adequate office accommodation nor residential accommodation for the thousands of civil servants and their families returning from Sokoto. Nor was there adequate infrastructure on ground to support them. Basic amenities like electricity and potable water were lacking. Electricity supply came from an old and epileptic generating plant which after few weeks, broke down plunging the state capital into total darkness for 5 days. Water supply on the other hand was nothing to write home about as the existing water works were in complete shamble.
Murtala Nyako, together with his team of young commissioners which included technocrats like Sidi Aliyu Enagi, Sallahu Agaie, Idi Kuta, now a senator, Adbullahi Bisalla, Umaru Mashegu along with dedicated civil servants like Late. Abdulkarim Lafene, Late Sulaiman Takuma Mallam Awwal Ibrahim now Emir of Suleja, Engr Akbar and Engr A.A. Lapai just to mention a few worked diligently round the clock to make Minna habitable. With a devastating efficiency, Murtala Nyako re-built the water - works, making water supply available 24 hrs in Minna. Electricity supply was also pursued with the same vigour. Several visits by the Governor to the NEPA headquarters in Lagos and 24 hrs detention of NEPA District Manager in Minna yielded fruits, as more powerful generating plants were installed in Minna and other major towns of Niger state.
On housing, within six months, Nyako constructed 500 housing units for both senior and junior civil servants in Minna the state capital using indigenous local contractors and ensuring proper mixture of cement to sand ratio.
While solving the basic problems of accommodation, water and power supplies, Nyako also tackled the issue of indiscipline. First, he launched a campaign against late comers to the office, official corruption, shoddy jobs by contractors and son on. Governor Nyako would be at the gate of a ministry or government parastatal located either in Minna, Bida, Kontagora or Suleja as early as 7 am waiting for late-comers. Once it was 7.30 am, the gate would be ordered closed. Late comers would then be made to fall in together irrespective of rank or status. The Governor would then address them reminding them of their responsibility to the state and the nation, he would warn them of serious consequences they would face if such offence was repeated. He was a delight to watch.
Once a state commissioner confessed that he was on his way to office when he noticed the Governor and his entourage already at the gate to his own ministry, so he simply made a round- about turn to save himself the embarrassment of being made to line up with junior officers of his ministry for coming late. Such was the degree of discipline that Nyako instilled in the civil servants in Niger state. So that by the time he left the state you could walk confidently in to any of the ministries at 7. 15 am and found everyone already on seat.
Nyako was absolutely incorruptible as the Governor of Niger state and till date does not own even a plot of land in Niger state. Compare this with what is happening in most states of the federation today, where according to EFCC reports, some chief executives treat public funds in their trust as personal wealth leaving the citizens of such states in utter penury, lacking in all social amenities including potable water.
After the selfless service in Niger state he returned to the Nigerian Navy where he rose steadily to become the Chief of Naval staff (CNS) in 1990. He revolutionized the Nigerian Navy by creating an engineering Command called Fleet Maintenance Corps to be headed only by naval engineers. Before then all naval commands were headed only by officers of the seaman department.

But the Admiral did not stop there. He wanted every Nigerian farmer to be as successful as he is and so he began to spend his time and energy to organize Nigerian farmers who were in numerous and uncoordinated farmers associations scattered across the country. He succeed in obtaining the Federal Government support for the scheme thus today all the Nigerian farmers now operate under a common umbrella called All Farmers Association of Nigeria (AFAN) of which Admiral Nyako is the national President. The association is so well organized that its organogram clearly shows its national, state and local government officers. Every farm product has its national, state and local government officials, so that if you were a cassava or rice farmer at a remote village you have an officer at your local government area in charge of that crop who you could contact for necessary assistance.
One of the greatest problems that the Nigerian farmer could face is sourcing adequate finance to support his farm. Original_Search_Title: Who Succeeds John Agyekum Kufour ?
Headline: Ghana: Who Succeeds John Agyekum Kufour ?
Body_Text:
Accra — Addressing a high-powered delegate's conference in the heart of London during his recent visit to United Kingdom, the President set out his benchmark in the form of advice to his party members. - wishing that his successor should have loads of commonsense; experience and national appeal to voters
After more than 7 years as a father of the party and President of Ghana, President John Agyekum cannot be faulted in his advice to party members who have the privilege to elect his successor. Though he stands the risk being accused of 'interference' and taking sides, the President was very careful with his words in handing down the advice.
Superficially, the party looks more colourful, more united, more resilient and more organised than when it was lumbering in opposition, but behind the façade, there are deep-rooted cracks, animosities and divisions, which could tear the party asunder.
The party, like any other political movement, is also not free from perception of corruption, cronyism, tribal innuendoes, dissatisfaction and power struggle. However, the biggest question of all, which will make or break the party in the near future, is who takes over the Kufuor's mantle for the 2008 do and die battle? It is against this background that I think President Kufuor's advice should be looked at with all the wisdom and courage that the party can muster.
One thing that all the 16 or so candidates vying for the flagbearship agree is that the 2008 elections will be hotly contested and savagely competitive, hence the serious need to elect a leaders who can put the NDC where it belongs - terminal coma.
Nana Addo Dankwah Akuffo Addo is certainly one of the popular names, which readily pops up when questions about who succeeds President Kufuor come up. In popular spots, drinking bars, hairdressing parlours and shopping alleys, whether in Kumasi, Koforidua, Tamale, Nkawkwa and Hohoe, Akufo Addo is a household name. They see him as the 'natural successor' of the JAK.
Nana was born into the Danquah-Busia tradition, highly educated, speaks French and English proficiently, a strong factor, which will help in our relationship with other African states, now that we are talking about Africa integration. As a successful lawyer and a high profile Minister of Foreign Affairs, his numerous supporters see him as matured and experienced politician, who can put the NDC on stretcher for good. "If there is any man in NPP, who has developed himself seriously to occupy the Osu Castle in future, it is Nana Akuffo Addo. Prof Mills cannot match Akuffo Addo in any future elections", a political watcher noted at University of Ghana. At 64 or so this is his time. His solid achievement in the Foreign Affairs has put him in good stead to exploit the array of foreign contacts and networks he has systematically built for the benefit of the nation.
Nevertheless, his opponents say he is old and belong to the old school. He is arrogant, bossy, boisterous and too ambitious - with such traits, he cannot be a unifying force, they claim. Extremists in the party did not forgive him when he was given a black eye by Tsastu Tsikata at the Fast Track Court, when he was then the Minister of Justice and Attorney General. His chances of carrying the whole of Eastern Region are being contested by three equally strong candidates - Honourable Hackman Owusu, Owusu Agyepong and Osafo Maafo.
His campaign is yet to hit the ground running but until then keen watchers say he has uphill task to cross the road to Osu Castle.
The cool and unassuming Alhaji Aliu Mahama, though yet to announce his candidature officially, is reported to be preparing to join the fray. His foot soldiers are vividly busy all round the country, busily marketing him as the next President. The Appiah Menkas and the Huruna Attas, are highly convinced that after serving his apprenticeship as Vice President of the nation, he can safely take the baton from the President, without mudding political waters.
Unashamedly liberal and generous, Alhaji Aliu strongest point is that he has served the party and the President with all the loyalty he could muster. With the talk of easing the NPP from the alleged domination of the party by Akans, who better fit than the Vice President - to take up the running and direction of the party?. Relatively young and untainted with alliances within the NPP, Aliu seems to be the right candidate to sustain and expand the achievements of President Kufuor, his supporters vigorously contend.
The Vice President's downside is that he is not a popular figure in his home: in a biblical parlance, his own people hold him with contempt. The imbroglio between the Andanis and the Abudus has dented his credibility and sapped his popularity in the north. As a relatively a newcomer in the NPP, he seems to lack the support of 'kingmakers', who have to remove stumps and thorns for his climb to the Presidency. He can achieve his goal if the current President throws his support behind him, according to key analysts.
Dr. Addo Kufuor has all the credentials to be the next President of Ghana. Having served as Minister of Defence for almost 8 years, his rare achievement is that he has literally lifted the army from gutter and transformed it into a disciplined and formidable organisation, well respected within and without. It is also a fact that he has put centripetal and centrifugal forces in the army in check.. If for the past 7 years, there has not been any military coup and that Ghanaians are enjoying their democratic dispensation in peace, Addo Kufuor takes more than modest credit for that.
Talk about maturity, academic qualification, political achievements, he is second to none - but his problem is Ghanaians will be setting precedent for "dynastic rule" if he becomes the next President. There seems to be strong ill -feeling against Dr Addo Kufuor - contesting the forthcoming NPP primaries - because the party will crack if he is seen as trying to succeed his brother - let's give chance to everybody in the country to become President", a strong NPP member said in Accra.
Asked abourt Poland, where two brothers are ruling the country as President and Prime Minister, the anti -Addo Kufuor agitators say Ghana is not Poland, Addo Kufuor should give way to other candidates to succeed President Kufuor.
In Tamale, they call him Mr President, in Kumasi Kejetia, they call him 'Chief', at Kpando, they call him 'Fia' and at Sunyani they call him 'Adwumawura'. The popularity of Alan Kyeremanteng, soft spoken, cool and collected, has surged in recent times, especially amongst ladies and young men in the party and even beyond in all the regions, thanks to his looks, which has assumed magnetic characteristics in these days.
Alan's idea of Empretec and his conviction that the private sector has the capacity to transform Ghana into a prosperous nation is held in higher esteem by the youth, hence their hope in his candidacy. The failure of the Presidential Initiative on Cassava has been used as a stick against him, the success in other initiatives -oil palm, tomato and others have forced his opponents into sudden retreat. His recent rise in popularity polls has not unexpectatedly arisen slumbering fox, vultures and jackals in the party to hunt him, using all kinds tricks to 'block' his candidature via inferior tactics but they seems to have hit a buffer.
Born in Kumasi with Fanti/Ashanti parentage, the 54 -year Alan is 'supremely well qualified to build on the huge achievements of President Kufuor and to keep the NPP solidly united", Edmund Larbi told me at Cape Coast, only last week.
Like Nana Addo, Alan was born into the Buisia - Danquah tradition, he is also a lawyer and strong believer in liberal capitalism but like Alexander Hamilton of United State {US first secretary of states}, he strongly believes that those who through no fault of their making but are poor and downtrodden, should be helped by the state to climb the social ladder.
His personal record of philanthropy, which has saved thousands of waywards from the streets and poverty-ridden alleys, attest to his warmth.
Untainted by alliances and sprinter 'fiefdoms within the NPP', his opponents say he is media shy and has cocooned himself from the grassroots. They claimed it is "not easy to see him" and "he does not pick his telephones" when he is needed. Others also see the temporary slow down of the Presidential Initiative on cassava/starch as his main weakness, but it does not up, since other Presidential Special Initiative Projects are working.
Yaw Osafo Maafo, the former Finance and Economic Planning Minister, though one of the old guys, is certainly a power in his own right who cannot be ignored easily unless he decides to cut short his ambition..
Dubbed the 'Sarkozy' of Ghanaian politics, his splendid performance as Minister of Finance and later Education is still as solid as ever. He is a real leader, intelligent, matured and knowledgeable; whom many say, could put the NDC in political 'coma' easily, but simmering 'grudge' and backbiting within the party, could end him on bed of thongs for the rest of his life, if he decides to push his ambition. He has loads of explaining to do on why he was removed as Minister of Education amd Sport last year.
Hackman Owusu Agyeman, dubbed 'the Ashanti man from Koforidua' is a nice man who can easily fit into Kufuor's shoes. Very loyal and a major linchpin of the party, who command respect, both locally and internationally. He is politically matured, having served as Minister of Foreign Affairs and Interior. His weakness is that he is relatively old, about 64 years. As a Minister responsible for water resources, his inability to ensure regular supply of water to majority of Ghanaians, could be a big problem when he is nominated as NPP flagbearer.
Several political analysts say Professor Frimpong Boateng could win the 2008 elections if NPP nominates him as their flgbearer on grounds that he is nationalistic and a hard working nice man, who has sacrificed his comfort abroad to help his country. A medical brain par excellence, he is full of business and scientific ideas, he is also squeaky clean; who has not tainted his hands in the rough side of politics.
A cardinal fact in politics is that it is a profession, which is not good for nice people. Many politicians know him more as a CPP sympathiser than being a member of the NPP. He seems to have no kingmakers and footsoldiers in the party to 'evangelise' on his behalf, hence his candidature is more likely to be scuppered by extremists within the party, and leave him more frustrated before he realise that a success in medicine may not necessarily mean a free visa to politics.
Prof. Mike Oquaye has all the right and experience to call on Ghanaians to vote for him as President of Ghana in 2008 but in all the constituencies, which matter most to NPP, he is not popular. His series of anti-coup writings in the NDC/PNDC era endeared himself to thousands of Ghanaians but it seems these same Ghanaians have forgotten. His failure to ensure regular flow of water in the Kwabenya/Aboakobi Constituency as he promised has mobilised his own people against his candidature.
Perhaps he has not being using the media as he cocooned himself from the people. He has a lot of travelling to do at the constituencies and media houses, if he really means to be the flagbearer. I really doubt if his age and health will allow him.
Until Dr Kofi Konadu Apraku's recent promise that Ghanaians will not see darkness again when he becomes the next President of Ghana, many NPP members/ supporters thought he had given up his ambition. His strengths are - he is a founding member of the party, matured, and a strong campaigner.
He is yet to recover fully from his removal from the Minsitry of Trade in the formative years of the NPP administration to his dumping at the scrapyard of NEPAD/Regional Cooperation, which was unmercifully scrapped as a ministry on its own. His chances seem to have evaporated; he has no chance this time.
Papa Owusu Ankomah is a real gentleman, who rose through the ministries of Justice, Interior and now Education and Sports, unscathed. Relatively young, who has the world at his feet. On the side of the coin, his popularity does not go beyond Sekondi/Takoradi, but political watchers say Papa could be a good material for vice President.
Strong and highly prolific, Dan Botchwey is a young politician who can cause an upset. As a successful party organiser, he takes credit for NPP successes in the last two general elections, which brought the party to power and in recent times, he is fast becoming David Cameron of Ghanaian politics. His asset is that he knows very well almost all the relevant delegates and proletariats, from Accra to Zabzugu, who will elect the new flagbearer, an asset that could be exploited to his advantage. Many people remember him in the 1980s, when he became a pain on the head of the military junta led by J.J.Rawlings - when he was a leading member of the National Union of Ghana Students.
His downside is that his dismissal from the Information Ministry by the President in the last reshuffle has politically sown a double seed of rancour and bitterness in his mind. His relation with the President is not one of the best, and this is likely to be extended to elements close to the Presidency. Cash for his campaigns could be a problem, and the possibility of the old foxes in the party ganging up against his rise cannot be ruled out.
Nobody knows these two returnees from America - Agyarko Kyeremanteng and Dr Arthur Kennedy. They might have been successful as a banker and a medical officer, respectively, but in the villages, districts, and constituencies, much is not known about them.
He has ideas all right but Agyako Kyeremanteng has no political experience and seems to be you be crawling to win the minds and hearty of the party executives. Kyeremanteng Agyako has so far proved himself, as a knowledgeable presenter of issues to only few people in the party but who knows whether he can charm the party executives before the congress. Time will tell.
Dr Arthur Kennedy, on the hand, is fondly remembered as the student leader, who took the baton, to the greener pastures, when students of Ghana needed him in the battle against military dictatorship in the country. His use of the media, instead of going to the country, makes political watchers wonder whether he is advertising himself for a cabinet position or preparing himself as Anti-Corruption crusader, after 2008 elections. No chance.
Nkrabea Effah Dartey and Kwabena Agyepong seem to be advertising themselves for political appointment after 2008. Both of them are nice chaps who have contributed their quota to democracy and the growth of NPP. They should forget the Presidency now and help defeat NDC.
The fact that more than 16 or so people are jocking and jostling for power within one party underscores the scale of divergence and divisions in the party under the façade of democracy. Certainly, this is a major source of worry, but if there is any body in the part that is more worried and frustrated than ever, the lot falls on Peter MacManu, who at the end of the forthcoming congress will have to unite the winners and losers, the old and young guards and heal all the festering wounds affecting the fabric of the party.
He equally has the additional duty to also proactively manage and reconcile the seemingly power struggle between the hundreds of ambitious district chief executives who are fighting to oust their sitting MPS and the guerrilla war going on in the circles of party executives.

Original_Search_Title: Five Small Towns to Benefit From R3.2 Billion Biofuel Project
Headline: South Africa: Five Small Towns to Benefit From R3.2 Billion Biofuel Project
Body_Text:
Nelspruit — The small towns of Hoedspruit and Ogies in Mpumalanga will benefit from a R3.2 billion biofuel project, funded by the Industrial Development Corporation (IDC) and the Central Energy Fund (CEF).
Hoedspruit and Ogies are two of five towns in South Africa identified by the IDC as investment areas in terms of biofuels production.
The other three towns are Cradock in the Eastern Cape, and Makhathini and Pondoland in KwaZulu-Natal.
IDC project manager Noel Kamrajh said this week that the projects in the Eastern Cape and Mpumalanga were currently at a detailed engineering study level.
"Both studies are due for completion in September, and construction is likely to start in January next year," Mr Kamrajh said.
The Hoedspruit plant will make 100 million litres of biofuel from sugar cane in the first year, while the Ogies plant will make 150 million litres from maize.
Mr Kamrajh said as part of the community empowerment strategy, maize would be bought from local farmers and traders.
The Cradock plant will produce 90 million litres of biofuel per year from sugar beets, while the Pondoland project will produce 150 million litres from sweet sorghum and sugar cane.
The Makhathini plant will produce 100 million litres a year from cassava and sugar cane.
Kamrajh said the towns would probably produce 1 billion liters of biofuel a year and contribute 1.3 percent to the gross domestic product.
The biofuels will cost between 50 to 70 US dollars a barrel.
The IDC will have a 49 percent stake in all the projects while the CEF and outside partners will share a 51 percent stake.
The biofuels sector is among those prioritised for development by the Accelerated and Shared Growth Initiative for South Africa, including tourism and business process outsourcing.
AsgiSA plans to raise annual economic growth to 6 percent between 2010 and 2014, as well as halve poverty and unemployment by 2014.
Biofuels, which can be mixed with conventional petrochemicals such as diesel, are seen as a viable and environmentally responsible resource for the provision of fuels, as fossil fuels take millions of years to form and significantly contribute to carbon emissions.

Original_Search_Title: I Want to be Driver of Change, Says Fayemi
Headline: Nigeria: I Want to be Driver of Change, Says Fayemi
Body_Text:
Lagos — Action Congress (AC) governorship candidate Dr Kayode Fayemi would not hesitate to tell anybody that his entrance into politics was by design with the aim of changing the face of Ekiti state. In this interview with Toba Suleiman , Fayemi spoke on the present state of decay in his state and his programmes to make Ekiti state an economic viable state, he also spoke on other things.
If you are to review your campaign so far, What Was the reaction of the people towards the exercise?
It is clear that we are in election mood. People want to give all the candidates the benefits of the doubt.
They want to ensure that we are able to sell ourselves to them and that we at least demonstrate why they should vote for us.
Over the last one month, I have had course to visit 12 local government of about 120 towns and villages, and in almost all the villages and towns that we visited, the reception was nothing, but overwhelming.
Though, there were areas, where we have limited reception, either for reasons of bad planning or inadequate mobilization of the communities and constituencies. But clearly, everybody admits that this brand, Action Congress (AC) is a reliable brand.
The Action Congress is a unique selling point, and the candidate of our Party is held above any other people running this race.
But much more importantly for me, the highlight of the various visits has been the -additional knowledge I have gained about the problems in each community and constituency. Of course, there are things that I have always known. But there are also things that I only discovered, about some of our communities. Let me give specific examples.
I was at Iyemero, Itapaji, Oke Ako in Odo - Oro axes, and even though we are talking about harmattan, when I went there, the leaves were still lurch green. It's like an all year round forest. Water is there abundantly. The Itapaji Dam is an amazing gift to Ekiti.
I believe that we have a lot of work to do on that.
But what is more significant is that I have, based on evidence before me, shifted my focus from infrastructure being number one priority that we are going to pursue in government for youths employment.
Because everywhere I went, every community I visited, I am extremely concerned with the number of our young people who don't have anything doing.
And I believe that, it is a key to unlocking the crisis that we are currently experience in the State. The challenge for us is how best we tackle the question of unemployment in Ekiti State.
Yes, our people still want free education, which we should give them, they still want free health services, they want solid infrastructure development.
But in almost every community I visited, the issue was jobs, jobs, jobs. And for us, part of the reasons why we were on tour was not just to introduce ourselves to our people, but also to familiar ourselves with them.
And in discovering that we now that it is the major problem we have to respond to very quickly.
So, that's an aspect of the tour that has been going on, that is very, very useful.
The other issue is that we conducted our tour in a very specific manner, We have not left out any town or villages, or farm stead. We visited everywhere. And it is the reminiscence of what I did during the primaries when I went round the 177 wards, in the State how we are going to every community, paying homage to traditional rulers in the communities, and then familiarize our ourselves with the problems of people of the communities.
We are also organizing women rallies, this is the unique aspect of our own campaign, my wife, the Deputy Governor and the Women Leader of our Party, they led the Co - Leaders in the State round each and every local government. They visit the markets, they visit the communities. They share the pains and the pangs of women.
They look at the priority of the women, because we have a pact with those women. And we want to share that pact with them specifically.
So that's the unique aspect, which we have introduced on the campaign tour. And we've seen an enthusiastic turn out of women in that regards.
So, it's been hectic, it's been very informative, and we believe it is the best way to really meet the people and we believe that the awareness is clearly improving.
In the course of your moving round the State, have you been of able to discover the statistics or the poverty rate among people of the State.
I have taken pain to study this very closely. And I have also related to bodies that are responsible for monitoring these things. I have related with the National Directorate of Employment (NDE), which does the sole monitoring of the unemployment figure in Ekiti State.
And I can tell you, the figures are alarming even, worse than what NDE or any other government body is showing me as labour statistics of unemployment rate.
I actually think that outside the 14,000 people who work in the civil service and in the public sector generally in Ekiti State there is no any other business in the State again.
The sizeable number of people who are paid employment, or in self employment, generating living for themselves in Ekiti State, is actually not up to 400,000 in a population of 2.38 million. You know what crisis we are talking about. So, we have a huge responsibility on our hand.
And that's why I said to you that one of the things that came out of my tour is a complete shift, well, don't let me say complete shift, because we are still going to work on infrastructure, because infrastructure too lead to employment. If you can get our people back on the field tilling the land, if you can get them back on the road, working on road construction, they will earn a living that way.
But what I'm trying to say is that we will re-order our priority in line with what we found on the field, which revealed unemployment as the greatest threat in Ekiti, which induces poverty, misration and hopelessness among our people.
So, for me, if you have a population that is something close to 68% unemployment, it is dangerous because what it seems to me is that you and I cannot be innocent by-standers. The fact that oh, I did not steal anybody's money, I built my house with my own money, is not good enough.
We all have to be interested in building our communities together. I cannot say okay, there is no electricity I put generator in my house, there is no water, I put borehole in my house.
There is no security; I hired one, at the end of the day, that won't pay, because those who are standing outside looking at the beautiful cars coming into this place would have nothing to lose.
They would get to a point when something would break and we are all going to be victims when it eventually breaks.
When I talked about doing something fundamental about employment, it is also in my self-interest. It is not that about helping other people. It is about helping me, helping my family. So that I don't become a victim of the legitimate pains of other people who are less than fortunate to be in the situation I found myself.
Ekiti is one State that has no business, absolutely no business, my brother. I have gone round the State, I have been round Ero, I have been round Itapaji. I was in Egbe, and I always make it a point to go to all these Dams.
I don't know how many states have this kind of water Supply that this State has. And yet, there is no water everywhere. There is hardly anything to drink.
And there is a multiply effect either in irrigation, or in hydro-electric power, or in agriculture plantation, or in the revival of our farm settlement, every part of Ekiti has something that it specialize in. if you come to this belt, it is tuber, it is yam, it is cassava.
If you go to Ise-Emure, Aramoko, you go to Iloro, Ikoro, Ekiti - West axis, you know that's the Cocoa belt of Ekiti, Cash crops.
If you go to Igbemo, Are, Afao we all know what comes out of there. That is the rice belt of Ekiti. If you go to the SouthWest, Ilawe, Ogotun, Igbara-Odo, you know that you are dealing with plantain, banana belt.
And you can tie an-agro allied sector facilities to each of these specialized places. So, for me, the key to poverty eradication in Ekiti is agriculture, but not just subsistence faming because that won't get us anywhere.
We've done it repeatedly, we have not benefited from it. We have the duty to revive all the farm settlements. We have a duty to provide solid extension services to our workers, and our farmers, we have a duty to help develop large farm cooperatives.
And we also have a duty to help them market. To create a solid enabling environment for our farmers to market their goods. We can break the jinx of remaining a subsistence farming community, which we must break.
Let me give an illustration, when I first heard it myself, I was shock. I had cause to discuss with a friend, who is a commissioner for agriculture in Lagos State, and he said to me that average people consuming food worth about N1.5 to N2 billion in Lagos, and I said it's impossible.
And he gave me very simple statistics, that let's assume that everybody in Lagos eats with N100. And using the current NPC figure of 10 million people for Lagos. N100 of 10 million people you get N8 billion.
Even, if you eat without meat you pay N50, so if you eat twice, you pay N100, what does Lagos produce, nothing, and it's purely an aqua-culture.
They do not produce yam tuber in Lagos, there are no tomato anywhere in Lagos. Where does the food they eat in Lagos come from?
What stops Ekiti from cornering, we are not even ambitious, a quarter of the marker N250 million daily, just imagine that N250 million coming daily to Ekiti given you N7.5 billion every month.
If you people get that kind of money, you will see a difference in this society. You will see the manifestation of the dignity of average Ekiti people.
And I believe that is the key to resolve the crisis of poverty in Ekiti State.
Every other thing can go along but they are much longer term projects. Of course, we have the vision to use information technology; I am the driver of developmental change in Ekiti. That's going to take a while to achieve.
So the problem of poverty is a key issue and very critical. It is a major crisis, and we are going to put everything that we have into tackling the poverty syndrome.
What is your expected role of the Security Operatives during and after the April Polls, with specific regards to the recent violent attacks in Iyin-Ekiti
We are not unaware in the Action Congress of the various machinations of the PDP particularly, to harass to intimate to manipulate the forth coming election.
But as we have always told our people, internal vigilant is the prize we pay for our liberty. We will conscientize them, we will raise their consciousness; we will provide them with tools and incentives to defend their votes. We will also call the attention of the International Communities towards what is happening in Ekiti. Don't be surprised Ekiti is one place where you are probably going to have the highest number of observers in this election per capital.
In addition to that, we have our own strategies, what happened in Iyin was unfortunate. And I think it's just demonstrated the State of desperation PDP has found itself. You will recall that specific names were mentioned including my own name, as being responsible for an assassination attempt, the so called assassination attempt on the gubernatorial candidate.
I wouldn't want to elaborate on what we are doing on that, but it is clear to us that this was just a figment of imagination cooked up by the PDP, propaganda machines in otter to save face about their own popularly in Ekiti State, particularly in Iyin-Ekiti, where they had no reception.
There was no warm reception for them, when they came to campaign there. And that's why they have to resort to this sort of under hands captive.
What is important to us is to sell ourselves to our people. It is critical to us to raise the confidence of our people.
Because, right there people say to me, Dr. Fayemi, if this is going to be determined on a one on one vote, you can go to sleep, and I say to them no. Even if that is the case, we are not going to go to sleep, because it's not over, until it's over.
For us we will do everything to ensure that we cooperate with these security agents, and with the electoral commission. We will not stand arms akimbo, if our people are being harassed or intimidated. And I will like to think that the leadership of the police and the security services in Ekiti State are mindful of the huge potentials of conflagration in this State.
But I must say that we hear all the stories, we hear about all the meetings going on. We hear about all the attempts to utilize cults and utilized members of self determination groups like OPC to rig the election.
It's not unknown to us. But we will cross that bridge when we get there. We will make adequate preparations for protecting our voters, protecting our votes, and for ensuring that our people's votes count this time.
What is unique in Dr Kayode Fayemi, and what makes you better than others, and when you get to power do you think you will be a mind of your own?
What is unique in Fayemi, it is leadership. And let me define leadership as I understand it. Leadership for me is not the strongman who is in charge. You know, some people would say oh, we need a strong man; we need a tough man to do this thing.
Ekiti cannot be ruled by just nobody, these are the kind of comments that people often made. So, leadership is about empowering others to do what would ultimately help them change their own environment. It is all about doing things for them, it is all about giving them the tools, giving them incentives to do that. That is what I have. I am not a manager. I want you to really understand the distinction that I am making. I am a leader, I am not a manager. There are several managers in the Ekiti State Civil Service. I am a visionary, which is what I bring to the table, which others don't have.
In fact, they acknowledged that they are managers, that they are not visionaries. One of them who is my friend among the candidates even say that he is a manager of men. Anyway, we need managers. Managers don't transform society, leaders do, visionaries do.
And because I know what it takes to transform society, which is why I have always, been in the position of servicing every society to transform them. That's why I get calls to places like Sierra-Leone, to places like Liberia, to places like Rwanda, Basket places in dire need of reconstruction.
And it is the ideas of how to do this thing that I believe I have. I have the exposure, I have the training, I have the intellectual capacity. And I have the practical delivery skills to ensure that that vision that I have get translated into concret reality.
I don't know many people in the field today in Ekiti State, infact, I am not aware of any of them, with greatest respect to them that have the kind of exposure that I have had in life. That possess the kind of practical experience of development issues that I have been opportuned to have, or even the integrity that I demonstrated.
And that is why, at the risk of being immodest, when people are talking oh, some people are sponsoring Fayemi. Some people are doing this, I laugh, because deep inside them they know that this person is different, so the slogan says. That is why their party, particularly PDP tried everything for me to come and join PDP in their hours of need.
If I don't have a mind of my own if all that it takes for me to be governor, it is far easier for a Kayode Fayemi that is in the ruling party in this country, with Federal might to become governor.
Because I also come with a huge douse of integrity than to team on a platform that is popular, a platform of people that can only be defended by the people but not with any access to Federal might.
The only reason why I am where I am today is that I have the courage of my conviction. Everybody knows that I am associated with the Federal Government in this century, which is ruled by the PDP.
In fact, I have the greatest respect for President Olusegun Obsanjo, but ideologically, we are poles apart. And there is no way I can be in the name political party with people whose ideology I don't share. I come from a long, long tradition of progressive politics.
My father is a community leader in this town. He has paid his dues as a politician, rights from the Action Group (AG) days as to the SDP days; I think he retired on the SDP days. He had never left the progressive camp, and I have a lot to share about his life, I believe that a society does not operate in a vacuum. I believe that intervention is very critical in transformation of the lives of our people.
I do not believe that everything private is good, and that everything public is bad. I believe that you can create an enabling environment for public/ private partnership, but one that gives a solid base in terms of regulatory framework through government and to governmental institution in order to protect our people, and create a safety that for the marginalized and the oppressed.
So, I know that my track record makes it very clear if there is any one who has conviction, or if there is anyone who has maintained politics of issues in Ekiti, I think I am the one, And it is because for me it is not a do or die affair. It is about service, and not just service but service to humanity.

Original_Search_Title: Civil Society Rejects ECOWAS Plan for Biotech
Headline: West Africa: Civil Society Rejects ECOWAS Plan for Biotech
Body_Text:
Lagos — The planned introduction of Genetically Modified Organisms (GMO) crops by the Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS) ministers into the region has been rejected by a coalition of civil society organisations at a one-day conference in Accra, Ghana, last week.
The conference, which was organised by Environmental Rights Action/ Friends of the Earth, Nigeria and Friends of the Earth, Ghana, also condemned the planned introduction of GMO cassava in Ghana following the failure of similar trials in Nigeria and the non- conclusive outcome of the over three year-confined trials of BT Cotton in Burkina Faso.
The conference timed as a parallel event to the ECOWAS Ministerial meeting on biotechnology has the theme: "Checking the GMO Policy Thrust in ECOWAS" and it was attended by NGO representatives from Ghana, Nigeria, Sierra Leone, and Cameroun as well as scientists, consumer groups, students, farmers and journalists.
A resolution adopted after the conference noted that the GM debate was not about food production only but matters of food sovereignty, good governance and democracy and as such, ECOWAS governments needs to rise to the occasion by rejecting any attempt to privatize the region's food chain by a few corporations.
The participants stressed that an all inclusive process devoid of biotech industry interference be put in place by ECOWAS to produce an acceptable Agricultural Action Plan for West Africa.
It demanded that ECOWAS governments should reject industry lobby to open every space for GMOs through laws that provides for the declaration of GMO-free zones.
They further stated that ECOWAS ministers must develop a policy that prioritizes the development of local agricultural systems and look away from dependence on the biotech industry and their sponsored organizations who been profit fixated see the people merely as markets.

Original_Search_Title: Kagulu Hill Rediscovered
Headline: Uganda: Kagulu Hill Rediscovered
Body_Text:
Kampala — Situated north of Kamuli District, Kagulu Hill towers manificently over Busoga. It conceals a rich cultural and historical heritage, writes Roderick Ahimbazwe
THE Chinese say the journey of a thousand miles begins with the first step. Similarly climbing up Kagulu Hill is not a simple feat. But once you take that first step, you embark on a journey into the history of Busoga.
Panting and sweating, under the hot afternoon sun, together with my guides, we made our way to the top of the hill. Kagulu is about 10,000 feet above sea level. It is situated north of Kamuli District.
On average, it takes about an hour to climb to the summit of Kagulu Hill. Kagulu is the birth place of Basoga. According to legends Kagulu had two wives called Kagwese and Mawale. These are two smaller hills that sit next to Kagulu.
In the sixteenth century the first people to arrive in Busoga settled on Kagulu Hill. It is believed that they migrated from Bunyoro Kitara kingdom of the Bachwezi. According to Francis Makiika, a resident, the first settlers were led by a prince called Mukama, who was nick-named Omukama Namutukula because he was very light-skinned.
Makiika says Omukama Namutukula came to Busoga through Lake Kyoga at Iyingo landing site and first settled at Iyingo in Budiope about three miles from Kagulu. He was accompanied by his wife, called Nabalongo. Namutukula married other women who produced the first generation of Basoga.
As a result over 75% of Basoga trace their roots from Bunyoro and the two tribes share some norms and other cultural aspects. Makiika says Namutukula's sons gave rise to the clans in Busoga. It is from these clans that the Kyabazinga (king) is chosen.
"These sons were; Zibondo, Gabula, Wakoli, Nkono Kitimbo, Tabingwa and Ngobi," Makiika says. It is also believed that Mukama Namutukula did not die but just disappeared. "Namutukula left Busoga in obedience to God's command that go out to the world and multiply," Makiika says. Namutukula's relics can be found on the hill.
