On the northern side of the hill one can see the remains of the boat that brought Namutukula from Bunyoro to Busoga along the Nile through Lake Kyoga. There is also a spear which locals say belonged to Namutukula.
According to Makiika, Zibondo Babani gave birth to Bulamogi, Gabula to Bugabula, Nkono Kitimbo to Bukono, Tabingwa Nyiro to Luwuka, Wakooli to Bukooli and Ngobi to Kigulu. These are said to be the founding fathers of Busoga Kitimbo gave birth to Mawerere and Kyabazinga Sir Wilberforce Nadiope came from this lineage.
Folklore has it that when Mawerere died, his head was preserved and that to this day the skull is still there. Locals however, say that only one person from the Ngo clan called Basuuyi, the official keepers of the head, is not able to view the head. Every year the installation of a Gabula or a chief is done on the hill. Locals from the surrounding areas go to the hill and Make merry.
They spend about three days on the hill, drinking, feasting and dancing as they congratulate the new Gabula. But the festival has gradually disappeared due to poor management.
As the people of Budiope advocate for a district, there are fears among Basoga from Kamuli that, this will lead to the demise of these festivals. As you ascend the hill you pass through an open tunnel with rocks on either side. Stairs were built to the summit of the hill in 1972. Part of the stairway has been overrun by grass.
One of my guides, Living Balyejusa recalls the day when the Deputy Speaker of Parliament, Rebecca Kadaga, visited the hill. He says, she was so scared to climb a section of the hill and decided to call off her visit.
Once on the top you see stones believed to be royal seats for kings who came to visit the hill. Next to these stones is an open space where the cultural rituals are performed.
There is also a pool of water in which people used to bath to cure them from diseases. However, the water in the pool has since receded in size. Marriage, the death of a royal or the birth of children used to be comemorated on the hill.
Makiika says people would sound drums called nduhyi to commemorate the special occasion. They would shout, "Bunyoro Kitara," in respect of their ancestors. Eight-three-year-old Kayizi Eseri, who has lived in the area for over 60 years, says the stairs were built on the orders of Uganda's former President Idi Amin.
He says Amin had wanted to establish a communication centre on top of the Hill. "Amin wanted to communicate with his people easily, especially those across Lake Kyoga in Teso region," Kayizi says with a wide grin on his wrinkled face.
A lone dilapidated building stands on top of the hill. The building has no semblance to the once beautiful appearance it commanded. What is left of this grand scheme is a Radio Uganda mast that is said to have been over 100 feet tall.
Kayizi recalls the day when a helicopter brought some dignitaries to visit the hill. "We had never seen a helicopter before and we were so excited," he says. Kayizi recalls hyenas that used to live in the caves on the hill would terrorise locals.
"These hyenas used to eat our animals and it was not until 1983 when we ganged up and killed them that we experienced peace," Kayizi says. For a fee one can be taken for a tour into the dark confines of the caves.
Just below the hill lies Kibirekyeza trading centre. It has 10 main buildings. The residents, according to Balyejusa and Makiika, take visitors on guided tours of the hill at a fee.
Most visitors to the hill are mainly whites from the UK and Holland. The soils around the base of the hill are very fertile and farmers have planted crops like maize, ground nuts and cassava.
It is rumuored that there are plans to build a hotel on the slopes of the hill, Makiika however, rubbishes claims says bickering among Kamuli's politicians can't make it possible. As one descends from the summit, one appreciates the value of history.

Original_Search_Title: You Can't Uproot a Ugandan Pumpkin
Headline: Uganda: You Can't Uproot a Ugandan Pumpkin
Body_Text:
Kampala — YOU can take someone out of Uganda. But true to the Song of Lawino proverb, you can never uproot a pumpkin from its homestead. So is the case with UK Senga Betty Mandazi's home which is a magnet for Baganda living in London. "Some how I have to adjust to some things like the English law which prohibits child beating.
But the protection makes the kids so unruly. It makes them indulge in extremes like drugs, crime and early sex," she observes.
"I wish my two girls could sift out the good in English and the good in Buganda to make one nice mixed grill of cultures," she says with a grin. Contrary to expectations, after living in UK for decades luganda was the first language for her children and she sticks to the spare the rod and spoil the child belief.
"I see people coming to UK and in thirty days they speak English which is harder to understand than what the Queen of England or PM Tony Blair speaks.
The more reason even if my children were born here they must communicate with their grannies in Luganda when they go back home." In her modest home located in East London you stumble into people from all walks of life.
The doors to Senga' s home are like that of a Police Station-never shut. At one time it is the first son Kato Lule(former president YusufLule's son.). In the long queue are members of the royal family, entertainers, computer wizards, accountants and priests.
After a heated banter of catching up with Ugandan politics, entertainment, sports and business, aunt Mandazi whets her guest's appetites with a bottle of wine.
Then she invites all to the dining table. Come summer and there is BBQ carnivore party. It is an occasion to welcome summer. Inspite of being in the heart of London, like a home woman it is "Karibu" (welcome) tradition in this cosmopolitan side of the world also.
In this Ugandan Londoners home there are no banana leaves to steam the bananas, cassava or potatoes. But on sneaking into the kitchen it is evident she took an extra mile to use polythene papers to improvise.
At table dig a fat chunck of steaming banana bread. They roll it into a ball. Thumb a hole in the boulder. Then fill it with thick groundnut stew before posting it down the wine oiled throat. The stuff slides down so smoothly.
It is so soft one need not to chew it. Come to think about it. The food tastes better when you chop it with the African fork (hands.) Oiled by enva endirwa (greens.) There all you need is a huge appetite. Nakati or egobe is cooked, dried and pounded for a longer shelf life.
Meat is salted and smoked in the firewood kitchen in Uganda before being flown to UK. bananas from Jamaica. With this concerted conservatism in a foreign land Senga does not spare the boys and the girls a word of caution. "Like the old times you have to seek partners. Marriage is still a respected institution in Uganda.
And if you get a lady -treasure her like gold. Those little gifts like a ring, necklace or handkerchief make the petals of a lovers heart flutter with joy.
Take her out when Cameleon, Ragga Dee and are having shows in Club 9.. ." Senga does not spare the gorgeous girls any barbs. "Divorce is not fashionable. Mubigumire (toughen up to cushion marital hard times. )
Always respect your husband," sighs Senga under her breath. "It does not matter -even if you have a better job or more money.
There is a limit to equal rights." She shrieks with laughter. "Nature put it that men dominate. That is why they were hunters and protectors while we stayed home to take care of the home."
As if agreeing with Senga, the British law announced recently that husbands can beat their wives provided they can say sorry. But she does not spare the boys the rod. She advises them to find suitors and marry. "The family is the pillar of our survival," Senga tells the boys in the habit of sowing their wild oats abroad.
"You should be proud of the good side in our society and blow it out of proportion. Muwase, muzale-baana bange (marry and bring forth children)" she counsels youths in the Diaspora. "A bakajja mumpe details zamwe njabafunira ababeezi (New comers to UK just give me your contacts and I will put you in touch with good people.)
Going generic Senga Mandazi finally calls on her guests trapped in the foreign lands to stick to their customs. "The name Baganda comes from the word ganda (stem) it was because the English and Swahili adulterated our language and we got to be called Baganda," she gives a mini history lesson.
These messages punctuate enormous cartons of Stella Artua, Genius and Uganda Waragi being uncorked. And as she takes more beer the lessons get more precise and less articulate.
"Each of you belong to one of the 50 clans. Don't think because you are in Europe you can inter marry or eat your totem. The message will get home by phone or intemet- it's taboo."
All in all it was such a jolly good party.

Original_Search_Title: We Shall All Miss Mr. President
Headline: Nigeria: We Shall All Miss Mr. President
Body_Text:
Abuja — Umaru, are you dead?!!!"
"No, Mr. President, I am alive."
"Umaru, I say are you dead?!!!"
"No, Your Excellency, I am very much alive."
"Nigerians you hear? I say you hear? Umaru is alive. God curse the liars," (he continued in a Yoruba sing-song or, as Hausa kids would say, Allah Ya tsinewa mai karya).
In a few days hence, Mr. President will finally, hopefully, retire to his Otta Farm for a well-deserved rest. We thank Mr. President in advance for the great sacrifice of serving us for eight good (and some not so good) years, but we thank him more again now for the even greater sacrifice of leaving us alone.
But we shall all miss this President, much as his chickens and pigs have missed him all these eight long years when he took up residence in Aso Rock, so far away from Otta Farm. We congratulate the chickens and the pigs for Baba Oyoyo!
Yes, we are going to miss the President. Whatever our opinion of Baba, he has been highly entertaining. Had the Third Term Project succeeded, we would have continued to be so entertained for the next four or more years. For starters, Baba has been such an avid and expert dancer. He misses no opportunity to show us his expert steps: at Christmas, Sallah, Easter, Wester.
And the President so loves the masses that he once whipped a policeman to show he cared. Or don't you remember the Kogi policeman? For those who don't, something happened early in the life of the President's First Term that would leave Nigerians shocked and awed by what manner of Leader God had saddled us with.
To recap, the President was in Kogi State on a State Visit. As he spoke at an occasion, there was the usual policeman making sure none of the lumpen proletariat moved near the Most Excellent Person. The law-and-order keeper was using a whip (bulala, koboko) to ensure everyone stood in line, both literally and metaphorically. As he whipped away, the President noticed the commotion, left off his prepared speech and yelled at the top of his voice to the policeman to stop what he was doing.
But the policeman failed to hear, such was the din. Baba then bodily left the podium, babbar riga agbada and all in tow, rushed to where the policeman was busy doing his duty, physically snatched the whip from his hand and proceeded to give the law-and-orderer the whipping of his life. The host Governor, he of the heavily-embroidered babbar riga, the President's then ADC and all seated dignitaries had left their seats and had rushed to the scene to behold the First Wonder of the First Term. From that day, Nigerians knew they had a most caring person as Leader. A Defender of the Masses.
The President is legendary for the legend that he is quick to anger, slow to forget, almost never forgives, and is highly impatient. Much early in the First Term also, a northern journalist we know so well was invited to the then novel Breakfast Meeting with the President. When this particular journalist rose to ask a question that very early morning, the President called him by name and tongue-lashed him to no end, live on network television. The diatribe included "I know who sent you..." but I don't remember whether he added "I will deal with you." Were that journalist superstitious, to be so upbraided so early in the morning would have made him give up the profession.
And we shall miss the President because he has always being his own MC and Chairman of any occasion. If anyone were appointed to be chairman of an occasion, or even a Master of Ceremony, that person would have nothing, absolutely nothing, to do. The President would do the intro of the meeting, do the body, the conclusion, the joke, the laugh, everything. Members of the audience, they of the convoy mentality, are always left with the applause, and another round of applause!!! How so entertaining!
How we will also miss being represented at the highest level everywhere in the world. The President never tires of traveling. He has such energy to represent Nigeria personally at any global, continental or regional fora, even where other countries send the equivalent of a Local Government Chairman to represent them.
The President is so generous with his (or is it our) time that he would represent us in meetings where a junior minister or even lower would have sufficed. Indefatigable lawyer and Senior Advocate of the Masses, Chief Gani Fawehinmi, used to keep tab on the President's frequent travels, and even published a book on that subject. But I think even the Chief must have by now given up; such is the frequency of VIP Movement.
Because of VIP Movement, Nigerian and foreign air travelers have suffered greatly delays, anguish, fear, near mishaps and the indignity of having to remain in the air for hours on end to allow the President depart or land in Lagos or Abuja airports especially. Many travelers are said to always include in their embarkation prayers that "may the Lord not to make the President wish to travel at the same time."
And the Baba being a widower and misses Mama so much always shows through and through. The other day, on that his highly-entertaining FRCN Network Radio early morning phone-in programme The President Explains, a certain young man (man, I say) called and asked an apparently pleasant question. And the President started with: "If you were a woman..." live on primetime network radio. It apparently shows it has been a great sacrifice for the President to remain a widower to serve this nation, and we thank him for this. O we shall miss Baba so, as he misses his wife so...
And all Hausa speakers will miss the President. He has been most entertaining when he speaks that military barracks Hausa. The President is so courageous in his Hausa. We Hausa speakers appreciate it when the President speaks Hausa.
At the tail-end of this Second Term, Baba had just dropped another bombshell by making another declaration: Mass Communication, Sociology and such similar subjects are no good. Whoever studied these courses, according to Mr. President, has wasted his time and the time of Nigeria.
Were I not a Mass Communicator myself, I would have rushed to defend our academic turf. But no matter. The President recently matriculated as an undergraduate student of the Open University. Is it philosophy or religion he is going to study? Over to you Open University lecturers: don't harass this student for he has great potential.
We are proud of the President for promoting our national attire. You never saw the President at any forum, be it ECOWAS, AU, EU, UN, D8, G8, G whatever, but he would be bedecked in our national costume. But one thing no one ever remembered to draw the attention of the Excellent One was that silky babbar rigas are notorious for not sitting quietly and respectfully on the shoulder; and they have the tendency of getting ensnared and hooked on other people's chairs as the wearer passes by.
On a final note: the President told all our farmers to grow cassava, that the world is looking for cassava. That it would be the next crude oil or similar attraction. Nigerian farmers apparently took this exhortation to heart as the gospel truth: most have grown lots of the crop, some at the expense of even food crops.
There is so much cassava in the polity, a near glut, waiting for foreign buyers as the President promised time and again. Mr. President will do well to take all these cassava with him to Otta, as our "widower's mite" to him in retirement.

Original_Search_Title: Climate Change Hits Uganda
Headline: Uganda: Climate Change Hits Uganda
Body_Text:
Kampala — The Semliki River shifted almost one kilometre into Uganda as a result of erosion, causing a border dispute with Congo
CLIMATE experts warned on Friday morning that global warming will cause faster and wider damage than previously forecast, ranging from failing crops and hunger in Africa to species extinction and rising sea levels.
It predicts that some African nations might have to spend 5 to 10 percent of gross domestic product on adapting to climate change.
Climate change has also started hitting Uganda. According to the first ever government assessment, obtained exclusively by The New Vision, the impact is dramatic.
Temperature rise resulted in an increase in infectious diseases. Malaria increased throughout the country but has reached "epidemic proportions" in southwest Uganda, according to the report titled 'Climate Change, Uganda National Adaptation Programmes of Action'.
Frequent droughts have resulted in lowering of the water table, leading to drying of boreholes, with the rural poor and the cattle corridor most affected (see map on p2). Rains are decreasing in amount, yet they fall in concentrated heavy showers and storms, leading to floods in lowlands and landslides in highlands.
Increased temperatures affect agricultural crops like coffee, cassava and soya and lead to the emergency of new pests.
The ice caps on Rwenzori Mountains have receded to 40% of their 1955 recorded cover and are set to disappear within the next two decades, affecting wildlife species and increasing the erosive power of River Semliki.
"Climate change is a serious risk to poverty reduction and threatens to undo decades of development efforts through destruction of infrastructure, property and lives," warns former state minister for environment Lt. Gen. Odongo Jeje in the introduction.
"Indeed, climate change threatens to frustrate poverty eradication programmes and the Millennium Development Goals."
The report was compiled by a team of 33 experts of the ministries of health, agriculture, wildlife and environment led by Philip Gwage, the Assistant Commissioner in charge of meteorology.
Southwestern Uganda, where temperatures have risen by 0.3 degrees in a decade, is cited as one of the hardest hit areas in terms of disease outbreaks.
The highlands, which were malaria free, are now invaded by the disease. People living in highlands have not developed immunity for malaria and are therefore more susceptible to it. The report noted an increase in malaria cases of 43% in Ntungamo, 51% in Kabale and 135% in Mbarara.
"There was a general increase of malaria incidences throughout the country, particularly in southwestern Uganda where it reached epidemic proportions."
In the semi-arid areas, tick-borne diseases have become rampant because of higher temperatures. The tsetse fly belt has expanded, while meningitis and eye infections have increased.
"Severe droughts resulted in frequent dust storms and associated respiratory and eye infections in low lying areas," according to the report. It noted an increase in such infections in Nakasongola, Lira and Rakai.
Erratic rainfall
The study further indicated that across the country, rainfall has been more erratic and the amount of rain has gone down. Landslides occurred in the highlands while flooding occurred in the lowlands, leading to the spread of waterborne diseases.
"A large proportion of the rural poor do not have pit latrines. Floods pose serious pollution problems to sources of drinking water, with potential danger of outbreaks of water-borne diseases such as cholera, typhoid and dysentery," the report noted.
Although rainfall is expected to increase in some areas as a result of climate change, evaporation will also increase due to the rise in temperatures, thus undoing the gains.
Droughts have become more frequent and severe, the report observed, with not less than seven droughts between 1991 and 2000.
"Drought is the single most important and widespread disaster in Uganda. It is increasing in frequency and severity, particularly in the semi-arid areas of the cattle corridor. The rural poor, whose livelihoods are dependent on natural resources, are most affected."
Prolonged droughts have affected food production and led to almost perpetual dependency on food aid in some parts of the country, according to the experts' findings.
"A typical example is in the arid areas of Karamoja where the World Food Programme supplies virtually all the food."
Long spells of drought have also resulted in the drying up of boreholes and the lowering of water levels of lakes and rivers. The study observed that River Rwizi in Lake Mburo National Park, which feeds Lake Victoria, dropped by four metres in a few years. The fall in water levels will continue to impact on hydro-electric power generation, water supply and bio-diversity, the report warned.
"Severe drought resulted in drying of water sources, leading to serious water shortages. This compromises personal hygiene, escalating water-borne diseases like cholera outbreaks."
The areas expected to be hit hardest by drought include the cattle corridor, covering about 30 districts, from Ntugamo, Rakai and Mbarara in western Uganda over Luwero, Nakasongola, Apac and Lira to Karamoja in north-eastern Uganda
"This area had a prolonged drought in 2000 that caused severe water shortage, leading to loss of animals, low milk production, food insecurity and increased food prices," the report noted.
Coffee affected
Droughts and temperature rise will also lead to a change of crop growing areas. Current temperatures and rainfall permit the cultivation of coffee in most parts of the country. With a temperature rise of 2 degrees, areas suitable for coffee cultivation will be reduced to Kabale, Fort Portal, parts of Nebbi, Kapchorwa and Mount Elgon.
"Other crops like cassava and soya may also be sensitive to temperature increases."
The disappearing of the glaciers on the Rwenzori Mountains will probably be the most visible symbol of climate change. Apart from the nostalgia associated with the sight of snow and its seasonality, the melting of the ice caps has a negative effect on both the water catchment and eco-tourism. It has already increased the erosive power of River Semliki, shifting it almost one kilometre into Uganda, causing a border dispute with Congo.
Gorillas threatened
The warming of mountainous areas will also drastically affect wildlife species. The Mountain Gorilla, of which half of the world's population is in Uganda, is under threat. Equally endangered are the Rwenzori leopard and the Rwenzori Red Duiker, that usually live at altitudes above 3,000, corresponding with colder climates.
"Unique species of chameleon are also found on the mountains, including the three-horned chameleon, whose range is shifting upwards as a result of rising temperatures," according to the report.
The dwindling of wildlife will certainly affect tourism. Wildlife-based tourism was recorded in 2004 for the first time as the country's leading foreign exchange earner, bringing in $300m (about sh5.4b).
Climate change is caused by environmental destruction - cutting down trees - as a result of industrialisation and population pressure. Trees absorb carbon dioxide, found in emissions from cars, planes, factories and charcoal burning. Carbon dioxide traps the heat escaping from the earth. With more carbon dioxide being released and less trees to absorb it, more heat is being trapped, leading to global warming.
At a glance
- Temperatures rising
- Droughts more frequent and severe
- Water table down, bore holes drying
- Gorillas and leopards endangered
- Coffee and cassava areas reducing
- Diseases spreading
- Malaria up
- Less rainfall but more storms, floods and landslides

Original_Search_Title: Surviving Informal Work - And Politics
Headline: Southern Africa: Surviving Informal Work - And Politics
Body_Text:
Cape Town — While informal work is insecure and low-paying, it has become the primary way in which most Southern Africans earn a living. Without such work, the meagre livelihood options in the region dwindle even further, as shown by a number of recent studies.
The situation in Kinshasa, the capital of the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC), is a case in point.
The collapse of the copper market has made Kinshasa the hub of economic activity in the DRC. The city has grown dramatically and now hosts seven million inhabitants. In the past decades, a high percentage of Kinshasa's residents have been forced into the informal sector to make a living.
Levels of poverty and inequality in the city have been analysed by Tom de Herdt and Wim Marivoet from the Institute of Development Policy and Management at the University of Antwerp in Belgium; they compared two income and expenditure surveys which were conducted in 1975 and 2004.
The DRC's per capita gross domestic product currently stands at one-fifth of what it was in 1974 due to successive economic setbacks and civil war. These events also changed household expenditure patterns in significant ways between 1975 and 2004.
The average household spent 0.8 percent of its budget on education in 1975. This has gone up to 5.6 percent by 2004, which reflects the de facto privatisation of schools during the 1990s. Schools have been turned into places where the government levies "taxes to finance the department of education" according to De Herdt and Marivoet.
Similarly, the state's withdrawal from housing and transport provision has led to increased private spending on these items. The result is that households spend less on food: from 62.8 percent in 1975 to 57.8 percent of the average household's budget in 2004.
But a comparison of calorie consumption only shows a five percent decrease among households during the same period. De Herdt and Marivoet contribute this to a switch to food stuffs that are more nutritious. Cassava has been replaced with maize and rice, which are cheaper and have more nutritional value.
De Herdt and Marivoet also found a slight decrease in levels of inequality when comparing the Gini coefficient of 1975 with that of 2004. This index of economic inequality has moved from 0.43 to 0.41. Zero indicates absolute equality and one absolute inequality. Moreover, the percentage of poor people has only grown slightly, from 60.7 to 60.8 percent.
The percentage of affluent people has increased from 16.7 percent in 1975 to 19.8 in 2004, according to De Herdt and Marivoet. The growth in affluence is due to the shrinking of the middle income group.
These changes happened against the backdrop of increasing numbers of Congolese people working informally. Only 12 percent of households in Kinshasa earn their income in formal occupations.
A further 11 percent derive income from formal and informal work, while the rest (76 percent) are engaged in informal work for their income. The majority of poor people (66 percent) are in the informal economy, compared to 16 percent of the affluent.
A gender analysis shows that the informal economy has taken a toll on women, this as female-headed households nearly doubled from 11 to 20 percent between 1975 and 2004, according to De Herdt and Marivoet.
In the DRC, a class of women known as "femmes libres" ("free women") have historically been active in the informal production and trade networks. Some 40 percent of them were affluent in 1975. But, this group has dwindled as female-headed households on average had lower incomes than male-headed households in 2004.
Female-headed households were "over-represented among the poor and under-represented among the affluent", suggesting that while the shift to informal work has pulled more women into the labour market, it has "pushed them towards the lower layers", De Herdt and Marivoet pointed out.
Moving to a different scenario, studies have found political interventions in the informal economy destructive to livelihoods.
When Malawi's government declared minibus "callboys" illegal in January 2006, for instance, many were forced into destitution. Previously, their work of recruiting passengers by calling out to them had earned the callboys about 107 dollars a month. By comparison, a mid-career teacher earns 43 dollars per month, according to Richard Tambulasi and Happy Kayuni from the University of Malawi.
The two researchers note that callboys are not "boys in the strict sense of the word as they have families and responsibilities".
Most callboys in a study by Tambulasi and Kayuni had finished primary school, but did not attain a higher level of education. They were unable to find formal employment in a country where only 25 percent of school leavers are absorbed by the formal economy, Tambulasi and Kayuni pointed out.
The government banned callboy activity amidst accusations that callboys were criminals. According to Tambulasi and Kayuni, callboys are mostly not criminals and frequently help passengers to get to their destinations. Pleas to the government to scrap the ban have been ignored.
As a result, many callboys have sunk into poverty, unable to find other ways to make a living. Some have even resorted to crime to stay afloat, Tambulasi and Kayuni's study observed.
These and other findings were discussed at a conference on the informal economy held last week (Mar. 26-28) near the coastal city of Cape Town, South Africa.
The conference was arranged by the Programme for Land and Agrarian Studies, a research organisation attached to the University of the Western Cape, and the Isandla Institute, a non-governmental organisation that studies urban settlements. Both are based in Cape Town.

Original_Search_Title: The JDPC Manifesto
Headline: Nigeria: The JDPC Manifesto
Body_Text:
Lagos — To all intent and purpose the Justice, Development and Peace Commission (JDPC) is a religious and non-governmental organisation. Established by the Catholic churches in Nigeria its objectives are centred on justice for all, peace, human rights and human development through "self actualisation". It is said to possess an active human rights programme and thrives on democracy monitoring and empowering the people on their political rights.
These are noble deeds and demonstrate the vision of the church in general to immerse itself in issues relating to social justice alongside its pastoral duties a la liberation theology. The JDPC happens to be one of the NGO's that would monitor the forthcoming elections and towards this end has been quite active in conducting programmes such as election training workshops etc as part of its contributions towards democracy and good governance. It has consistently shown itself to be a broad minded organisation whose values are deeply rooted in the welfare of the people. It was therefore quite surprising to read a document published by the JDPC Ijebu-Ode Catholic Diocese titled "The People's Manifesto "in which it outlined what it saw current position of things in Ogun State .
Amongst other gross acts of misinformation, the document spoke about "mass retrenchment even in the government sector". It is on record that on the inception of the current administration in the State, there was actually a lifting on the embargo placed on employment by the previous administration led by Chief Olusegun Osoba.
The State Government has deliberately pursued a "No Sack" policy within the government sector and made employment generation one of the key components of its development agenda. In 2003, it embarked upon the recruitment of over 4,000 unemployed youths. The recruitment exercise targeted both senior and junior cadres in the public and civil services and was spread across the State's three senatorial districts. As opposed to the easy option of retrenchment, the government also adopted the policy of rightsizing by allocating surplus staff to areas of need. This meant that surplus workers from agencies such as the Ogun State Broadcasting Corporation (now known as Gateway Radio), Inland Revenue Board, Gateway Transit and the Water Corporation were sent to work where their services were required.
The State Government's approach to employment was the driving force behind the establishment of the Ogun State Employment Generation Programme (now known as the Bureau of Employment Generation). This was done very early on in the life of the administration in 2003. As implied in the name, the agency has set up skills acquisition programmes in agricultural and non-agricultural/vocations targeted at unemployed youths. To date the programme has conducted several skills acquisition training programmes and through the assistance of the Ogun State Agricultural and Multi-purpose Credit Agency (OSAMCA) many have been set up in small scale businesses.
To further demonstrate its commitment towards vigorously addressing unemployment the government very recently established the Ogun state Unemployed Graduate Scheme (OGUGRAD). This is the first social welfare scheme in Nigeria . It recruits graduates from all over the State into a scheme that provides training in new skills, pays a monthly stipend and sources for employment for them. Another area of misinformation contained in the JDPC document is reference to a style of governance that "precludes electorate from fair participation in budget formulation". Again it is on record that the State Government on its inception in May 2003 adopted a transparent and participatory budgeting process. It has taken every step to ensure that the budget process is open and accessible by the citizenry. Through a series of consultative budget sessions conducted within the nine Federal Constituencies in the State, various interest groups such as trade associations, trade unions, traditional rulers, community leaders, students, faith-based organisations and women groups have an opportunity to put the needs of their members across to the Government. It is worth mentioning here that the scope of the consultative sessions for the 2007 budget was widened to include civil society, organised private sector, and the House Committees.
Besides this Bottom-Up approach towards the budgeting process across the State, the Bureau of Management and Budget also holds open interactive sessions with the Government Agencies to ensure that they have the same policy focus as far as the poverty reduction strategies of the State are concerned. Still in keeping with its commitment to transparency and accountability, the State Government has introduced another measure to the budget process. Early this year it held a Budget Breakdown Session for members of the public and other stakeholders such as members of the Manufacturers Association of Nigeria, the media, financial institutions, organised private sector, civil society etc. The Session reviewed the previous years' budget and pointed out the highlights of the 2007 Budget. For the record, it must be mentioned that the JDPC was an active participant in budget consultative sessions conducted by the State Government. It is also worth knowing that government officials attended a people's budget session hosted by the JDPC as well. In short there has been a close interaction between the two sides on this issue. In the light of this, it is not clear why the Commission has chosen not to acknowledge any of the Government's efforts to promote an open budget process in its document. It is also baffling why the Commission failed to take into cognisance any of the progress recorded by the government agencies.
Verifiable progress made in road maintenance, mass rehabilitation of public schools, the School Net Programme which creates IT laboratories in public secondary schools, efforts in encouraging mechanised farming through introduction of tractors, agricultural programmes in Cassava, Ofada Rice and Cocoa, the resuscitation of the farm settlements and creation of new farm estates etc did not receive any mention in the document.
The traditional role of civil society has been to stand as a defence line for the people. Constructive criticism is always welcome as a means of keeping a government on its toes. It is however not very wise for a widely - respected NGO to use its clout in a society to misinform the public. This sort of behaviour has been condemned in the past as a ploy by some NGO's to seek funding from their international partners.
With the elections around the corner, it is the responsibility of an NGO to act as an umpire, shun any kind of partisanship and to ensure that their platform is not inadvertently used to promote disaffection. The ideal approach would have been for the Commission to actively engage the Government, get the correct information and evaluate before making its recommendations. That way it will be truly seen to promote "justice and peace".

Original_Search_Title: Diplomats Assess Nation's Agric Research Potentials
Headline: Cameroon: Diplomats Assess Nation's Agric Research Potentials
Body_Text:
All diplomatic roads led to Nkolbisson yesterday with Ambassadors of some ten countries and representatives of the European Union, UNDP and the World Bank visiting Cameroon's leading research structure, the Institute of Agricultural Research and Development (IRAD). It was an occasion to discover what Cameroonian researchers are capable of producing and probably raise questions as to why such potentials have remained so undeveloped at a time Cameroonians need them more in the drive towards poverty alleviation.
The whole idea of getting the Ambassadors of countries and Representatives of organisations, many of whom have disbursed big sums of money to support research programmes in Cameroon, came from the French Ambassador, George Serre. The idea was hatched on 19 January this year when the French diplomat visited IRAD, saw the research potentials of the institution and expressed the wish they could constitute a veritable base for the development of Cameroon.
The success story of IRAD was best told by the nine exhibition stands visited by the Ambassadors. The results of the four research programmes carried out by 72 scientists of the Department of Annual Crops research, left no diplomat indifferent. The four programmes cover Roots, Tuber and Plantains involving crops such as, cassava, yams, cocoyams, sweet potato, and banana; Cereals like maize, rice, sorghum and millet; Grain, Legumes and Vegetable Crops such as Ground nuts, common beans, cowpea and soybean and Industrial Annual Crops like cotton, sugarcane, and tobacco.
Other exhibition stands included; Animal and Fishery Production; Farming Systems and Socioeconomy, perennial products, and Forest and Environment. Of particular attraction was the stand of the Ministry of Scientific Research and Innovation/ IRAD on improved seeds supported by the HIPC project. This project consist in producing, multiplying and distributing seed varieties to the poorer segment of the population.
The visit was occasion for the General Manager of IRAD, Dr. Zok Simon, to make a comprehensive presentation of the institute. IRAD, he said, is a public establishment assigned with the challenging task of carrying out research aimed at promoting agriculture, which remains a base for economic development. "To carryout this mission, IRAD has engaged the services of qualified and devoted staff made up of 275 researchers, 114 technicians and about 1,000 permanent and temporary scientific and administrative support staff", he said.
Without being exhaustive, he cited the example of countries and organisations that have financed research projects in Cameroon. These include: Germany, Brazil, Canada, USA, France, United Kingdom, Japan, Italy, Netherlands, European Commission, World Bank and the African Development Bank.

Original_Search_Title: Farmers Opt for Fishing Rod to Boost Their Food Security
Headline: Kenya: Farmers Opt for Fishing Rod to Boost Their Food Security
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Residents of semi-arid Waseges location in Lower Subukia which suffers from perennial food shortage, have embarked on fish farming.
They have been relying on Government relief food during the dry spell.
The fish ponds, of which 20 have already been built and 80 others are in the pipeline, will provide income, generate employment and improve food security.
The farmers will be breeding tilapia, the most popular fish species in the local market.
Agricultural officers and Provincial Administration officials say the ponds will enable farmers harvest rain water to irrigate their farms during the dry season after harvesting fish.
Rain harvesting is expected to minimise the number of conflicts with neighbours over the use of water from rivers Waseges and Igwamiti than run through the area.
Irrigate tomato farms
Communities living downstream have been complaining that the rivers dry up due to farming by their counterparts in the upper areas who use the water to irrigate their tomato farms.
The World Wide Fund for Nature had assisted Waseges residents and villagers around Lake Bogoria form Lake Bogoria Water Resource User Association.
The chairman of the World Wide Fund for Nature, Mr John Kiptek urged the communities to plant more trees to protect the river from drying up. He said building water pans would prevent conflicts over the resource.
Agricultural officers say farmers would be using water from the fish ponds, which would be rich in nutrients.
Fish farming is among agricultural activities carried out by farmers' groups under the Kenya Agricultural Productivity Project.
The Government with support from the World Bank, has set aside Sh1 billion to educate farmers in 20 districts across the country on new production methods. The project's national co-ordinator, Dr Samuel Muigai, told Nation they aim to improve the country's agriculture through generation, dissemination and adoption of new technology.
He said the project would be implemented in three phases spread over 12 years during which farmers would also be helped to acquire equipment that would enable them add value to their produce on a cost-sharing basis.
Dr Muigai was speaking to Nation at Lari primary school in Waseges location when he launched the project.
Local farmers intend to build more than 100 ponds.
Experts have been using the Lari primary school pond to train villagers in fish farming before they embark on individual projects.
Nakuru district project coordinator Peter Kimani said farmers had formed groups and identified activities they would engage in.
Mr Kimani said his organisation has been paying allowances to specialists and trainers assisting farmers.
He said there were 95 such common interest groups in the district spread across Kamara, Mbogoini, Gilgil and Olengurone divisions.
The groups are involved in fishing, bee-keeping, livestock rearing and cassava, sorghum and passion fruit production.
Others rear chicken and rabbits and grow sunflowers, mushrooms and vegetables. He said different groups were educated in areas of interest with the trainers expected to provide a plan of action on production, processing and marketing.
District irrigation officer Anne Kagiri said farmers would use the ponds to breed fish between April and October of each year.
She added that the farmers could then use the water to irrigate their farms from November to March. The officers noted that an estimated 16 other ponds were under construction in the area.
Ms Kagiri said farmers were only required to use cow dung or fertilizer in water to spur growth of algae, which the fish feed on.
"When cow dung or ordinary fertilizer is put in the water, the water starts fermenting and is rich in nutrients, which allows the growth of algae," she said.
"After the fish is harvested, the water which is rich in various nutrients could then be used to irrigate tomato farms."
A senior fisheries officer in Rift Valley Province, Mr Simon Macharia, who attended the launch, said fish farming was the most suitable way of harvesting and conserving rain water for use during the dry spell.
"The water pans also control the flood level during the wet season. The area is normally affected by floods during the rainy season only for the residents to be affected by drought within a short period," Mr Macharia said.
He said farmers would now conserve flood water, which normally ends up in Lake Bogoria.
Mr Macharia said farmers had bought fingerlings from the Sagana fingerling production centre.
He advised them to buy cat fish, which was essential to control prolific growth of tilapia. "The total cost of the 900 tilapia fingerlings is Sh2,700 while 100 cat fish fingerling cost Sh500. Their packaging cost Sh200. One therefore needs Sh3,400 to introduce 1,000 fingerlings in a small pond," Mr Macharia said.
He said the fingerlings, which were injected into the pond, weighed 20 grammes and farmers were expected to harvest at least 1,000 tilapia weighing between 200 and 250 grammes within six months.
At current market rates, the officers said a farmer would be expected to earn between Sh10,000 and Sh45,000 per harvest.
Wiumiririe Goshenline Irrigation and Fisheries Association chairman Mwaura Gakunga said members hoped to improve their livelihoods. He noted that an estimated Sh30,000 was used to build a demonstration pond. The Government had also spent over Shl30,000 to train farmers.
Mr Gakunga told Nation that the military had the machinery and skills on fish pond construction. The project, Mr Gakunga said, would benefit close to 2,000 people in Waseges location.

Original_Search_Title: My Plans for Warri North Local Government--Aganbi
Headline: Nigeria: My Plans for Warri North Local Government--Aganbi
Body_Text:
For so long, Warri North Local Government of Delta State, either by omission or commission, has not really got the attention it deserves in this democracy. One man that has vowed to make the difference if offered the privilege to occupy the chairmanship seat of the council is Andrew Aganbi. He believes that the four communities that make up Warri North LG- Abuegborodo Community, Koko Community, Ureju Community and Ebrohimi Community would soon witness tremendous change if he is allowed to fly the flag of Peoples Democratic Party, PDP.
He is not short of ideas that would bring about total transformation to the council. According to him," my vision is the restoration of our Local Government Council, that is Warri North LG."
Soliciting for the approval of the good people of the council, Andrew Aganbi said in his plans:"My mission is to solicit your approval for my ambition, to put myself forward through this great party of ours, PDP, as your candidate and to have your co-operation in seeing to it that victory is ours at the polls.
"My manifesto is not a record of unachievable promises as you have been used to hearing in the past decades of our political history, it is a record of well-planned progressive programmes, which if we all work harder and together, can easily be achieved to the joy of all members of the community of Warri North Local Government Council."
On food and health, Aganbi said he would put in place machinery for food sufficiency through agriculture and fishing and would cooperate with the Ministry of Health for well-equipped health cottages in all the communities while seeing to it that the only hospital in the Local Government Council Area is well-equipped to serve both the land and riverine communities.
He said education would be given top priority, explaining that he would cooperate with the Ministry of Education for well-equipped schools and colleges in the Local Government Council Area.
Aside education and good health for the people, transportation being factor that has greatly affected his people to no end, the former Calabar Kitchen boss, disclosed that he has drawn up an effective network of water transportation for the council. In his programmes also, roads would not be left out. He gave examples of such roads as between Abuagborodo and Koko, between Koko and Ogheye that would be accorded priority attention.
On some communities that have been deserted for quite sometime now, he said he would cooperate with state government to ensure restoration of all deserted towns.
Pledging unalloyed support for Dr. Emmanuel Uduaghan, the Peoples Democratic Party gubernatorial candidate in Delta State as the man specially anointed to move the state forward. Andrew Aganbi explained that the fierce opposition of respected Niger Delta leader Edwin Clark to Uduaghan's candidature was misplaced.
"It all borders on nothing. When Uduaghan made his intention known, Chief Clark supported him. When he got the flag, he blessed him publicly. I don't know why he is opposing him now. I am sure if we are to look at the capacity and credibility of all those in contention, Uduaghan remains the best. And I believe he was specially chosen by the Almighty God to lead the state to another level, promising never to let his people down at any point in time," he said..
Aganbi equally said the fish markets of Eghoro, Brecibi and Ogheye would get his attention in addition to holding a dialogue with state government and other interested agencies to establish Cassava, fish/sea food and palm oil industries.
Andrew Aganbi who also oversees Agura Hotel in Abuja promised that "I will not let my God down, I will not let my family down, I will not let you down, I will not let my party down if it pleases God and you to see me as your worthy candidate and to establish me on the seat of the Chairman of the council."

Original_Search_Title: Region to Boost Food Crops With Biotechnology
Headline: West Africa: Region to Boost Food Crops With Biotechnology
Body_Text:
Banana production could benefit from biotechnology in West Africa, say scientists
The 15 members of the Economic Community of West African States have agreed to use biotechnology to increase food production in the region.
Ministers of agriculture, environment, science and technology met to discuss the issues surrounding biotechnology in agriculture at a meeting held last week (28-30 March) in Accra, Ghana.
They adopted a regional action plan for biotechnology development for 2006-2010, which stresses the use of public-private partnerships to increase investment in biotechnology, and the need to put safety measures in place at national and regional levels.
The plan calls for a network of biotechnology experts to be established, and the promotion of networking between centres of excellence in biotechnology and the West and Central African Biosciences facility planned by the New Partnership for African Development.
The ministers also agreed to set up an independent fund for assessing the socio-economic impacts of using genetically modified (GM) organisms.
A regional policy for managing intellectual property rights to help with the acquisition, development and distribution of biotechnology knowledge and new technologies will also be developed.
But the meeting drew criticism from non-governmental organisations, scientists, farmers and consumer groups who met at a parallel meeting to criticise the 'biotech industry interference' in the process, and the lack of provision for GM-free zones.
However scientists contacted by SciDev.Net welcomed the plan.
Baboucarr Manneh, a Gambian researcher at the biotechnology unit of the Africa Rice Center in Benin, said it has the potential to improve agricultural productivity in West Africa.
He pointed to a number of biotechnology applications needed in West African countries, such as the use of antibodies and biopesticides against crop, animal and human diseases.
Manneh indicated the necessity of producing disease-free plants such as bananas, cassava and fruit trees, and plants resistant to environmental stresses.
He also highlighted the importance of improving the nutritional qualities of existing crop varieties and animal breeds.
To address public fears about biotechnology, the ministers agreed to establish a network of national information and communication units responsible for raising public awareness of biotechnology.
The Economic Community of West African States includes Benin, Burkina Faso, Cape Verde, Côte d'Ivoire, Gambia, Ghana, Guinea, Guinea-Bissau, Liberia, Mali, Niger, Nigeria, Senegal, Sierra Leone and Togo.

Original_Search_Title: Unfulfilled Promises of the PDP And Obj
Headline: Nigeria: Unfulfilled Promises of the PDP And Obj
Body_Text:
Minna — The PDP is supposed to be the Peoples Democratic Party and General Olusegun Obasanjo, the democratic leader elected through democratic process of an election, but eight years after a purported mandate that was given in 1999 and stolen in 2003, Nigerians are still suffering and wallowing in untold hardships. The economy is in shambles; the socio-economic policies of the government have led to mass retrenchment and emboldened the unemployment figure. The neglect and inefficiency of the power sector even after the government claims to have pumped about 6.2 million dollars into the sector had led to the closure of industries and companies thereby leaving many Nigerians unemployed. The nation's heritage in petroleum, telecommunications and national assets have been personalised in the name of privatisation and deregulation. All the virtues that the PDP and Obasanjo are claiming, and the brazen disregard and disconnection from the norms and tenets of a working democracy have qualified the PDP as the persons' dictatorship party and Olusegun Obasanjo as a maximum dictator with political colouration, for the president's style or mannerisms in leadership have never been found to be in tandem with those of a democratic leader. So what we have as the PDP is the congregation of bootlickers doing the biddings of the political dictator.
In 1998, the PDP and General Olusegun Obasanjo promised to address and fix the electricity problem the nation has since been bedevilled with within the first six months. They promised us electricity and power supply and yet what we have are endless power outages and blackouts. They've simply refused to solve the problem yet the government is claiming to have pumped about 6.2 million dollars into the sector. So what happened to the largesse? And why has the problem not been solved? The best we could be given thereafter is PHCN, which Nigerians have construed as problem has changed name or please hold candle now.
Could the PDP and Obasanjo not see the plight of Nigerians who have been made jobless as a consequence? How is it possible that they've not seen the number of textile industries in Kaduna, Kano and other parts of the country that were made to close down because of the inefficiency of the power sector? How did they expect to lure us to vote for them with the promise they made in 1998 to solve the power problem in six months only to remake the promise eight years after? Are Nigerians or PDP so forgetful?
Agriculture: The PDP and Obasanjo made us to believe that having a farmer as our president will be advantageous, because he would revolutionise our economy from a mono-product economy to the lost glory of an agriculture-driven economy. Eight years after, what we've been hearing is cassava this, cassava that, ethanol this, blah! blah!! blah!!! The middle class has since disappeared and families in Nigeria are either rich or poor with about seventy-one percent (71%) as the latter, so the question for the PDP and Obasanjo is how have having the PDP as the ruling party and Obasanjo as the president impacted positively on the agricultural sector? What advantage or benefits have accrued to the sector, economy, or Nigerians by having the PDP and Obasanjo, the supposed farmer, as president?
Economy: Till today, the Nigerian economy is still a mono-product economy. Let us forget about the propaganda on cassava exportation, or World Bank/CBN collaborated venture in cassava revolution. Does the economy appear diversified or revolutionised? The only revolution that has happened is in the economic fortunes of Otta Farm, which has resulted in monetary turnover of thirty million naira monthly according to the former media spokesman of Obasanjo, who's now a minister, Fani-Kayode. Imagine just what good it will do to the Nigerian economy to generate thirty million naira monthly as a result of some form of revolution or diversification to the credit of the PDP and Obasanjo.
Transportation: The PDP and Obasanjo promised us in 1998 to resuscitate our railway mode of transportation with a world class functional railway system. Eight years after, no single rail track has been laid down, and no train has been bought as a result. Our roads have since been ridden with potholes and serve more as death traps.
Health: We are still waiting for their promise of an affordable and functional healthcare delivery system, and housing for all pledge.
The truth and reality is there for everyone to see, and the stark truth is the Peoples Democratic Party as a ruling party has failed Nigerians and Obasanjo as a leader has failed us too. The failure at the federal level has also been replicated in most states of the federation, The PDP and Obasanjo's pari pasu, cannot deny it, much less to Nigerians that they have failed us. There is no single primary election conducted by the PDP that has been adjudged to be free and fair, even the presidential primaries election was organised or staged to deliver the candidature of Governor Umaru Yar'Adua, having used the EFCC to intimidate some aspirants to sheathe the sword of their aspirations. There is no element of democratisation in all the primary elections conducted by the PDP yet, the party prides itself and Obasanjo as the greatest things to have ever happened to Nigerians.
The same things they are promising us now were all they promised us eight years ago. They must believe that Nigerians have short memories or that we all suffer from amnesia. Oh yes, they must think we cannot remember or we are all forgetful; or what is the explanation for repackaging the same promises, pledges, lies? As the factor of democratic importance, we must ask ourselves a question and answer it with utmost honesty and sincerity. Did the PDP and Obasanjo deliver on any of their campaign promises of 1998? I don't believe a word of the PDP or its leader's! They are all liars; they lied to us eight years ago and they are doing the same thing now. Nigerians, challenge the PDP and Obasanjo to go through their electoral promises (manifesto) of 1998 and tell us just one promise they were able to deliver. They lied and they are now lying again!
The thing about lying is that once you tell it, you have to tell several others to nurture and cover the first one. The PDP lied to us eight years ago and they are now reconstructing and repackaging some lies to lure us to them again. Their campaign promises are nothing but lies; they qualify for melodramatic promises. I don't believe them and I believe many Nigerians are with me in my thinking.
In Islam, the PDP and Obasanjo had exhibited all the three attributes of hypocrisy
(1) When they talk, they tell lies(2) when they promise, they fail to fulfil and (3) when they are entrusted with trust, they betray! I challenge the PDP, Obasanjo and Nigerians to tell me which of these characteristics the PDP and Obasanjo have not exhibited. I make bold to say this: PDP and Obasanjo, I don't believe you and your campaign promises. They're all LIES!!!

Original_Search_Title: Country's Increasing Face of Poverty
Headline: Nigeria: Country's Increasing Face of Poverty
Body_Text:
Abuja — Arodizuogu is one of the largest rural communities in Imo State in South East Nigeria even as a sizeable percentage of the inhabitants are reputable subsistent poor rural farmers who are mostly engaged in the production of palm kernels, cassava tubers and a variety of fruits. But a major impediment facing these poor rural farmers is the total lack of basic infrastructural facilities like good road network, health facilities and potable drinking water even though Arondizuogu community harbour tributaries that flow into the legendary Imo River.
Shortly before the 2003 general elections, the question that was on the lips of most rural poor in Arondizuogu was how far can the political office seekers honourably and truly implement their campaign promises because of the realisation that the promises made by the current office seekers to better the rural communities were not implemented.
Eight years after the advent of democracy in Nigeria, the majority of the rural poor interviewed in Arondizuogu in Imo State, Kafanchan in Kaduna State and Auchi in Edo State said that they are yet to witness any strategic change and that poverty has even increased. These set of people interviewed stressed that they have lost faith in the capability and willingness of political office seekers to better their living condition.
These apprehensions were eventually vindicated when the United Nations Development Programme issued a country report last year and asserted that rural and urban poverty has increased astronomically in Nigeria. A conservative estimate of seventy five percent of Nigerians are said to be living below the extreme poverty benchmark.
On March 14, the European parliament deplored the high rate of poverty in Nigeria and called on government to take drastic action to better the living condition of millions of people in the country. Excerpts from the full text of resolution B6-105 (EPP-DE) obtained showed that the European parliament in clear terms urged the federal and state governments in Nigeria to implement result - oriented measures to check the rising trend of rural and urban poverty.
The parliamentarians in their words stated that' "whereas despite efforts made in recent years by the Nigerian government to promote human rights and to stem corruption, and despite some improvements in respect for civil and political rights, a number of urgent and basic human rights issues remain to be addressed and the country remained marred by corruption, arbitrary arrests, torture, and extra-judicial Killings and political violence.
Whereas ethnic and religious divisions, as well as widespread poverty, are major causes of chronic inter communal violence," in 2006, UNDP reported that' "though Nigeria's Gross Domestic product per population in 2004 was $560 a little over the poverty line of $1 US dollars per day, the situation has not changed in the last 30 years. While less than half of the population has sustainable access to improved sanitation, the number of people with access to clean drinking water has dropped from 48 to 46 per cent over the last 14 years while the average life expectancy for other low developing countries is 58.7 years, Nigeria's stands at an abysmal 43.4 years."
On food insecurity, the 2006 report by the Food and Agricultural Organisation (FAO), sub-Saharan Africa accounts for thirteen per cent of the population and twenty five per cent of the under nourished people in the developing world. It is the developing region with the highest proportion - one-third-of people suffering from chronic hunger. Hunger in sub-Saharan Africa is as persistent as it is widespread. Between 1990-92 and 2001 to 2003, the number of undernourished people increased from 169 million to 206 million".
The report stated that Nigeria is among the few in sub-Saharan Africa that saw only a marginal reduction in the numbers of undernourished people while the prevalence declined. The report also suggested pragmatic ways of ending this terrible high rate of hunger and accordingly stated that growth in food production is in deed key to hunger reduction in sub-Saharan Africa.
Productivity - driven increases in food production have been shown to have strong positive impact on the rural economy, leading to increased food availability and reduction of food prices in local markets. At the same time, the enhanced incomes of small holders, the main producers of staples - provide a stimulus to rural economic activity by generating increased demand for the products of other sectors that are either linked to agriculture like processing and agricultural services or supply consumption goods to farmers. FAO rightly suggested among others that public investment is essential for agricultural growth.
Public investment in infrastructure, agricultural research, education and extension is indispensable for promoting agricultural growth. Writers of the report by FAO rightly stated that actual public expenditures on agriculture in many poor countries do not reflect the importance of the sector, particularly in those with high prevalence of undernourishment.
This affirmation is the truth in Nigeria where it is observed that several farming communities have not benefited from the numerous pro-agricultural programmes and policies introduced by the current administration. Several farmers do not know how to access credit facilities to expand their farming activities just as the total absence of infrastructure like motor able roads and storage facilities means that these farmers can only remain subsistent farmers with the capacity to feed a few months even as hunger and poverty continue to ravage millions of Nigerians.
The poor living condition of most Nigerians has made it imperative that the political office seekers need to tell Nigerians in very clear terms the strategies and modalities to be truly adopted by them to better the excruciating living condition of millions of Nigerians. What is the place of the poor after the historic April 2007 elections? If one may ask. Umaru Musa Yar'adua the erudite scientist and governor of the North - West state of Katsina who is the Presidential candidate of the Peoples Democratic Party said he will definitely implement measures to end poverty in Nigeria.
Yar'adua is not alone because other presidential candidates like Prof. Pat Utomi a distinguished academic, Chris Okotie a lawyer and cleric and Orji Kalu have all out lined what they considered as workable measures to end rural and urban poverty in Nigeria.
Yar'adua who sounded more passionate about the need to abolish poverty through deliberate revolutionary pro-poor policies of government said if he is elected in April 2007 he will ensure that poverty becomes history. According to him, the abundant natural and human resources in Nigeria would be galvanized by his federal administration to end the cycle of poverty that has entangled millions of Nigerian families in the unfortunate web of insecurity of life and property.
Introducing a divine dimension, Yar'adua emphasised thus; "By God's grace, poverty in Nigeria will become a thing of the past because of God's endowment to us. PDP will lead Nigeria into an enviable nation". Yar'adua anchored his priority programmes on a seven point all -encompassing agenda to include power (energy), agriculture, industrialisation, education, human empowerment, poverty eradication and redemption of Nigeria's past glory.
In a recent publication carried by a national newspaper in Nigeria, the World Bank President Mr. Paul Wolfowitz said the best way to tackle rural and urban poverty is the immediate improvement of governance and fighting corruption through the due process of the law.
The American born Wolfowitz rightly observed that, " governance is about having transparent governments, accountable governments that's why parliaments are so important as a check on the executive; that's why the free press is important as a vehicle for conveying information to the society; that's why civil society and the private sector is so important because increasingly, governments are responding to the pressures that come from their own people."
The poor in Nigeria want to see a post April 2007 that will bring about economic prosperity, transparency and an accountable government that will do more in improving their living conditions.

Original_Search_Title: Soroti Displaced Aided
Headline: Uganda: Soroti Displaced Aided
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE Christian Children's Fund Soroti office has donated resettlement kits worth sh221m to formerly displaced persons, reports John Omoding.
The state minister for disaster preparedness, Musa Ecweru, officiated at the function at Katine-Tirir Primary School on Monday.
Over 500 ox-ploughs, 500 pangas, 1,000 hoes, 2,500kg of improved groundnuts seeds, 1,000 insecticide-treated mosquito nets, 1,000 goats, 500 bags of cassava cuttings and 1,000 tree seedlings were given out.
Ecweru called upon charities to declare their programmes to district authorities so that those that are not genuine can be weeded out.
He noted that when he visited Lira recently, he was shocked to learn that some officials of charities had lived in hotels for three years and were paying sh150,000 per day.
He explained that at the end of one year that meant each of them spent sh54m, resulting in sh162m after three years.
Ecweru said he had a list of such organisations and would present them to the Cabinet and Parliament.

Original_Search_Title: Hoima Eagerly Awaits Oil Boom
Headline: Uganda: Hoima Eagerly Awaits Oil Boom
Body_Text:
Nairobi — HE WALKS WITH CONFIDENCE along the murram road to his hotel near the shores of Lake Albert. Occasionally, people stop him for a chat. The women wave and the teenagers bow. A prominent man in his community, he could become a crucial player in the oil boom everyone is expecting.
Ofungi Kasangaki is the Local Council (LC 1) chairman of Kyeihoro village, in Buseruka sub-county of Hoima district. The setting of Kyeihoro is similar to Kaiso, Tonya, Kiamboga and many others on the shores of the Albert.
This is the area in western Uganda where foreign prospecting firms have announced discoveries of oil deposits. Figures are hard to come by, but by one estimate, there could be as many as 250 million barrels of the black gold in the ground here.
Like Kaiso and Tonya, Kyeihoro is a fishing community. It is 80 km from Hoima, the nearest big town, which is 203 kilometres from the capital Kampala.
Most of the people living in Kyeihoro depend on the lake for survival. Fishing is the main activity and they exchange their catch for food from the neighbouring villages, mainly cassava, sweet potatoes, and maize.
Ironically, the good murram road that connects to the lake has meant that fish stocks are getting depleted. At least three fish collecting trucks come to the landing sites every day to pick up the night's catch.
Most of the people who live here are members of the Alur community from Nebbi district in West Nile region. With a population of about 1,500 people in an area of about 12 square kilometres, only 15 per cent of the residents are Banyoro. The rest are Congolese and Bakinga.
Prospecting for oil is still underway, with commercial production not expected for several years.
The Congolese who cross the lake occasionally while fishing and those who have fled the war in nearby Ituri region have settled here too. Most of them are members of the Lendu ethnic group from the Democratic Republic of Congo and have intermarried with Ugandans. Residents say they are living in peace so far.
Marine officials, however, have a problem with the Congolese fishermen.
"They do not enforce strict fishing gear requirements on their side of the lake," says Mandy Robert, a maritime officer at Kyeihoro. "This is depleting the fish stocks."
"The Congolese population has increased. We cannot estimate the percentage increase but I think that the endless war in Congo sparked off the exodus to Uganda," says Harriet Musinguzi, Hoima District Information officer.
The struggle between the Congolese, Alur and the Banyoro could mark the beginning of the oil curse in this region. All of them feel they have a right to belong. Already, however, the Alur and Congolese are taking up jobs with the oil companies.
Some of the people here have mixed ethnicity. Ofungi, whose second name is Kasangaki, says he was born in Bunyoro. Kasangaki is a name from Bunyoro while Ofungi is from the Alur. He says this is his home but talks fondly of Nebbi as the land of his forefathers.
Ofungi, like the entire local community here, has big dreams of an "Oil City," Many people here believe the fishing villages will be turned into an oil city with magnificent hotels, high rise buildings, shopping malls, white sand beaches, leisure parks and so on.
THE OIL CITY WOULD OF course be inclusive of the neighbouring Kabwoya Game reserve whose animal population has doubled. Many animals can be seen roaming in the reserve in the evenings.
Joselyne Nyangoma, the environment officer for Hoima district says the buffalo, warthogs, Uganda Kob, antelopes and other animal populations have increased in Kabwoya. She attributes it to the heavy deployment of soldiers who are guarding the oil wells and who have also checked the previously rampant poaching.
The reserve has a safari lodge and a runway where jets carrying tourists land.
The government dismisses talk of an oil city and says there is nothing like that on paper. All that is being done, the government says, is planning for the area that is undergoing development.
"We are making a development plan for the Albertine Graben region. There is physical planning for the townships, infrastructure upgrading and development for the place," said Ernest Rubondo, assistant commissioner of the Petroleum Exploration Department in Entebbe.
BUT IF THE DEVELOPMENT plan comes to pass, will people like Ofungi who have set up small businesses survive? Will the development plan take them into account? Won't the displacement be the first oil curse in Bunyoro?
Government officials say some people may indeed be displaced and relocated elsewhere and that the oil policy in the offing will take care of all these issues.
So far, people like Ofungi have managed to put up simple hotels, which are big investments by the standards of the region. They are respected people in their community and can function as a link to the oil exploration companies.
Ofungi has helped several community members get odd jobs around the Tullow oil camp in Kyeihoro.
"When there is work to be done, the companies ask us, the local leaders, to recommend workers. The educational background is not considered, it is your sweat," said Ofungi. The pay is usually Ush5,000 ($2.8) per day, sometimes more."
This, by local standards, is good money, and it may improve with time when oil activities increase in the area. For now, Tullow Oil is trying to improve infrastructure for the community; roads, a wooden classroom block, health centre and borehole as well as a Lake Albert rescue project. John Moley, Tullow Oil Uganda country manager, says his company, which has operated in the area for three years, is building infrastructure to benefit the community.
The few children who have not abandoned school for fishing have benefited from the school facilities developed by the company.
Tullow has also decided to put some teachers on its payroll to improve the quality of education.
Many locals are afraid of relocation. While there is a current mini-boom, many are wary about the future, especially if they have to be relocated to make way for the oil exploration and exploitation.
But officials in Hoima district are upbeat. District officials are planning to develop the district's infrastructure as soon as their percentage of the revenue comes through.
The percentage the district is entitled to is not yet known as the oil policy has not been finalised. A committee has, however, been set up to take care of the area's interests.
"Since the colonial days, we have not had a tarmac road in the district," says Yunus Mugabe, speaker of the district council. "When we get the money from the Fund as stipulated in the draft policy, we shall carry out our development plans for the three year duration that we usually develop."
The town has big plans for the oil money, but interestingly, many people are ignorant about the location of the wells. They cite impassable roads - that have since been greatly improved - for not making the journey there. They also cite the stringent process one has to go through to visit the wells.
The traditional King of Bunyoro, Solomon Gafabusa Iguru I, has twice been denied entry to the oil wells area during his travels around his kingdom.
In Hoima, where most of the Banyoro live, many frown at the idea of "foreigners" taking their jobs down at the camps. But some are content with the booming business. Mugabe says those in the hotel business are doing well. "The hotel rates in Hoima are twice what they were some two years ago. Most of them are usually fully booked by evening."
BUT SO FAR, MICROFINANCE is the most lucrative business. Microfinance institutions have multiplied, giving a chance to whoever needs credit to start up money-making businesses.
In less than six months, the number of microfinance institutions has gone up by 60 per cent and their interest rates are as high as 20-25 per cent, says Mugabe.
The microfinance firms are probably attracted by the belief that the oil will trickle into the pockets of the local people and that they shall pay off their loans. For them, and many of the residents in this part of western Uganda, the countdown to the oil boom has already started.
.

Original_Search_Title: Salem Irrigation Project - a Small Breadbasket
Headline: Namibia: Salem Irrigation Project - a Small Breadbasket
Body_Text:
Rundu — The Salem Irrigation Project is where most of the traditional vegetables commonly known to the Kavango people are grown and harvested.
In fact, it looks like a miniature breadbasket with a hive of activity.
"This is epungu, which is maize in Rukwangali, omdiga is mainly cassava, kachama is sweet water melon and also what is commonly known as Kavango spinach or Mutete is grown here," said Vice-Chairperson of the Salem Irrigation Project, Johannes Lumbala.
The Salem Irrigation Project started before independence, with the aim of producing food.
Today, the 34-hectare plantation serves as a small breadbasket for more than 40 men and women ploughing and harvesting the fields.
"We feed our families with these vegetables by selling them at the marketplace in Rundu," said one woman placing the heavy sack of potatoes on the ground.
"If you want to survive or make a living, you must work hard and not just wait for government to throw in a helping hand," said the woman, preparing to get ready with another empty sack that needed to be filled.
On the field, there was a large stretch of maize, watermelons, tomatoes, pumpkins, cabbages and potatoes. This is testimony that the Salem Irrigation Project is progressing well.
However, Lumbala said there are a couple of challenges that the labourers deal with on a daily basis.
"We struggle with marketing our goods sometimes. You'll be shocked that if you come here in July this year you will still see these sacks of vegetables here - with no market to sell it to," said Lumbala pointing at the maize fields that will be ready by the end of this month (April).
The problem is that the various marketplaces in Rundu are already glutted with traditional greens, making it difficult for these women to sell their goods.
"Sometimes the food becomes rotten because we don't have buyers around here. It's a very good community project, but without any government support," he added.
