Yet, there is some hope for the project's harvested maize crops as milling companies, like Pentagon Milling in Rundu, are ready to buy such quality products from local subsistence farmers at a fair price.
The multi-million-dollar milling plant at the town which buys maize and wheat in the Kavango Region wants to enhance the government's green scheme project.
Most of the work at the Salem Irrigation project is done manually. There is need for water irrigation machines and a storage facility.
"Ploughing is mostly done with horses, donkeys and oxen. We need a tractor and two water irrigation machines to make our work easier," added Lumbala.
He is, however, optimistic that the project will continue to be a flagship of community development for the people of the Kavango.

Original_Search_Title: Chukwumerije Raises Hope on Mineral Deposits in Abia
Headline: Nigeria: Chukwumerije Raises Hope on Mineral Deposits in Abia
Body_Text:
Umuahia — THE senator representing Abia North, Comrade Uche Chukwumerije has raised the hope for the exploitation of the mineral deposits said to abound in Abia State, especially in Abia North Senatorial Zone.
Chukwumerije who is seeking re-election on the platform of Progressive Peoples Alliance (PPA) while campaigning for the April 21 election in his senatorial zone said that details and locations of various mineral deposits in the state have been compiled by the state Ministry of Environment, adding that he had also written the Federal Ministry of Solid Minerals on the Salt deposits discovered in Bende.
He attributed the delay in the commencement of exploitation of the minerals, to the spate of redeployment of ministers of the ministry and perhaps the attitude of the central government to matters that concern the South East insisting that it is only by electing the PPA government and legislators at state and federal levels that would be more responsive to the needs and affairs of the South East and Abia North that exploitation of the mineral deposits would be given priority.
Senator Chukwumerije also vowed to ensure the completion of an approved airport at Uturu for which he said about N50m was appropriated in the budget, just as he promised to influence the establishment of an International Trade Centre in Isuikwuato in his Abia North zone, adding that the zone would also benefit from the Cassava programme for which he sponsored over 10,000 rural people in Abia North on Cassava Value Added Training conducted by the National Root Crops Research Institute [NRCRI] in each of the five local government areas in the zone.

Original_Search_Title: PDP Does Not Implement What It Preaches - Ekpenyong
Headline: Nigeria: PDP Does Not Implement What It Preaches - Ekpenyong
Body_Text:
Lagos — Former Deputy governor of Akwa Ibom State now progressive Peoples Alliance (PPA) governorship candidate Obong Chris Ekpenyong in this interview with DENNIS UDOMA talks tough on political issues, his stakes in the socio-economic transformation of the state if giventhe mandate as a governor in April, 2007 elections.
Excepts:
What informed your decision to dump your former party (PDP) for the Progressive Peoples Alliance (PPA)?
Thank you. Yes! As you know, I was a member of PDP from the founding stage, as a matter of fact from G-34 to when it changed to Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) and by my contribution, I became the Deputy Governor of the State on the platform of PDP twice in 1999 and 2003 elections with Governor Victor Attah.
As it were, I left the office two years ahead of the end of the tenure and finally contested election as a governorship aspirant on the platform in order to offer my own vision because the vision that I had with the governor (Attah) was subsumed by his vision because he was the first among equal.
PDP processes of selecting a flag-bearer particularly in Akwa Ibom State were not transparent. So, there was no election conducted, the entire exercise was a sham, it negates the principles of democracy, it did not at all conform with the reforms agenda of PDP-led government at the centre neither does it conform with the conditions of all the sectors; from economic to politics which the party claims it is doing. It doesn't at all and in that case, it beats my imagination that we could only be preachers of words and not doers of what we are preaching. So, it is that, that informed my looking for an alternative platform that will perform what he or she is preaching, that will enunciate and articulate as well as ensuring that whatever is articulated or enunciated for the Nigerian Public is implemented for the good of a common man, elite and all Nigerians.
You mean you have found that in PPA?
Yes! By that name and even the nomenclature alone connotes and stated that you must seek first the kingdom of God and its righteousness so that all other things would be added unto you.
The political platform that I am standing on now is called, Progressive Peoples Alliance (PPA). You must be progressive, you must be prosperous before you can look for power. I have witnessed for about 8 years people talking of power to the people whereas people are hungry and we enunciated all forms of poverty alleviation, small scale entrepreneurship which is difficult to access funds. Talking about agriculture which N50 million was made available for people to access agriculture which will open windows of opportunities to the teeming masses of the nation, none! How many of the people of Akwa Ibom State have accessed that N50 million, none! Even myself as a farmer could not access. It so, how much less the subsistence farmers. So, it looks to me that we are preaching what we are not implementing.
That is, the PDP?
Yes! The PDP, it is preaching what they are not implementing. So, I have gone into a platform which talks about that poverty must be exterminated before you look for power, there must be prosperity before
you talk about power to the people. You can only manipulate or handle power when you are sufficiently equipped with intellectual capacity in industry, agriculture and food sufficiency, security of individuals and corporate security of the nation. You can only talk about it when there is abundant wealth, sufficient enough for at least 80 per cent of Nigerians. So, that is why I love this party, that is why I believe in this party and that is why I believe that I can also use this platform because for me, I believe in prosperity, industry, wealth creation, empowerment of Akwa Ibom people as the basis for growth and development.
It is often said in many circles that Akwa Ibom is a One Party State, i.e. PDP control State and that it would deliver a block vote of 100 percent in the forth-coming elections. What are your comments?
I think it's just an imagination, they are dreaming and they have hurriedly forgotten that we made PDP what it was. A lot of us that time, I was in government and I worked assiduously to defeat APP in
1999. I worked assiduously to defeat ANPP, NDP and other parties in 2003. So, they are just dreaming, they are day dreamers. Now that I am out of that party, others are out of that party including the entire Akwa Ibom people have understood the intrigues, the desire for a change that there must be a change.
Obviously, there is going to be a change in Akwa Ibom State, it is not going to be PDP, they are just dreaming to have said that Akwa Ibom is a one party State. It is a state we have PPA, AC, LP, ADC and ANPP parties and all these are being facilitated by strong individuals who have integrity, vision and I know there is going to be a coalition. The coalition of all the political parties fighting PDP in this state. Of course that is not all, I alone can fight PDP to a standstill and of course I have done that. The entire people of Akwa Ibom have jettisoned PDP. They are just dancing naked and when the election comes up on 14
April 2007, I am going to stand and they will know that PDP was not in existence any longer.
The State PDP Chairman recently said the party was not campaigning rather, it was celebrating their successes, what do you make of the statement? in Uyo also, the Nigeria Labour Congress (NLC) in the State declared support for Akpabio. Are you comfortable with all these strategies?
Well! I don't want to talk about . Otu Ita Toyo because he has never stood for any election. I personally imposed Otu Ita Toyo on the party in this state and he never stood for any election one day. And
so, he can just brag because he is a none performer. He does not believe in the tenets of democracy and so, you only allow him to dance the naked dance that he is dancing now. He would be taught a proper lesson at the appropriate time, so I don't have to join issues with him.
One, he is not transparent, he is highly corrupt. As at the time I left PDP he has not paid my N1 million which he extorted from me which was one of the cardinal things that actually pulled him out of the PDP. He was suspended and I worked for his re-installation. And so, all those things, Akwa Ibom people will get to know as we flag-off our party's campaign this month and most of those characters will be given their due position.
Then talking about NLC, I don't want to believe that NLC had endorsed anybody because I was in NLC and they all jumped, danced and clapped hands for me excepting that, comrade Udo Kirian who is no more the Chairman of NLC. I am just happy that he has now seen the true side of life that there is nothing that is permanent, that if there is anything that is permanent, it is change and today; he is no more the NLC chairman and I am not too sure that the new NLC chairman would buy the idea that somebody had been endorsed.
NLC supposed to be a watch-dog, a protector of the workers interest working in pari-pasu with the government. So, you cannot take sides with a political party because it never knew which political party will form a government.
So, I think he did that for his selfish interest and at the end of the day, when we get to the field, I just believe we will be at the Philippi. So, I would not want to join issues with NLC because I am also a comrade, I am part of the workers. I have always been a worker and a protector of the workers. So, I as a workers, I as a member of the NLC has never endorsed myself neither did I endorse any other political competitor. So, he had no right as my own representative as at that time to say he has endorse somebody because there was no meeting at any level where the NLC met to say this is the candidate of our choice. If there was going to be any agreement, it was going to be myself that the NLC would have adopted.
Your sudden defection to PPA has really given the electorate an opportunity of choice among the competitors in the state. What are your programmes for the people you intend to govern?
My goodness! I am so glad, I am a new face if I may borrow the word of my presidential candidate, Dr. Orji Uzor kalu; "there is a new face of a new Akwa Ibom, I am a new face of a model Nigeria. To the best of my knowledge, I have 22 programmes or agenda for Akwa Ibom people but they are cued into four broad based areas. I am going to change the lives of Akwa Ibom people. The Independent Power Plant (IPP) which we were told would be inaugurated this month by Mr. President and I hope he has inaugurated it, if not, we still await its inauguration would provide the basis for economic development.
Like I had said in various fora, after the inauguration by the grace of God given the mandate by the people of Akwa Ibom on the 14th of next month and consequently sworn-in as the governor of this state on the 29th of May, my first area of interest would be to ensure that the Independent power plant after being inaugurated by the preceding government, the succeeding government will work assiduously to commission it. Commissioning means that we would ensure that the power that is generated is transmitted and distributed to Akwa Ibom environment and that the Power remain stable and unfluctuated. That alone would attract investors, that alone will enable me to ensure a sporadic emergence of industries in the 329 wards in this state where I would have spent at least N50 - 80 billion in each of the cottage industry because it is going to be uninterrupted source of power and therefore suggests, there is going to be windows of opportunities for people to work. Unemployment would be a thing of the past. When you have industries where you have employment opportunities for people means you have created wealth. So, there is going to be wealth, that is going to mean prosperity to the entire citizenry of Akwa Ibom State.
When I finish from there, I will then extend my hands so that I can have a dependent. There should be another sector of economy that would generate revenue.
So, I would move to agriculture to receive a prime vision in my administration, it would offer windows of opportunities to those unemployed youths. They will partake in farming (cassava, cocoa, palm oil) and we shall produce cocoa into various forms which we would use in feeding children in primary schools Since three would be free education from primary school to secondary levels, in so doing, we would have had another sector of wealth creation through agriculture.
Service delivery in health care will be a thing of the past because agriculture is going to help me provide the raw materials for Pharmaceutical industries since there are going to be Allied industries which I will create within the three Senatorial Districts. Of course, we have our industrial estates which were created by the government in which I was a party to it, is not just going to be overgrown with weeds, we will put them into proper usage by providing the infrastructural facilities along side what has been provided now.
So, that will bring a multiplicity of prosperity along the line. When I finish that, why should I allow Akwa Ibom person to travel to London for health services. So, I will improve on the health-care facilities, not just having the buildings but having the health-care facilities and the human capacity in them to man the facilities installed for health care delivery.
So, healthcare would be well taken care of, Education would be made qualitative and functional with basic infrastructural equipment available for free and compulsory education from primary 1 - 9 and I shall ensure that secondary education would also be free but for the tertiary education; unless I have my President, Dr. Uzor Kalu on top since he said that at the federal level he would ensure free education up to the University level. But for me, I would ensure that there is free education up to secondary level.
Tertiary education shall be competitive, any good or brilliant student would have to compete for scholarship and bursary. Again, anyone that stopped at secondary school would never regret because there is going to be confidence in the re-orientation of such people. He could branch into any vocation - like furniture production and so on, he would have a ready market for it and support base.
There shall be micro credit facilities provided to assist the small and medium scale entrepreneurs who are now diversifying in carpentry/furniture making, tapping our rubber plantations which are lying waste, we will tap it for the production of foams. These are some of the things I want to provide to Akwa Ibom people.
Talking about Education, do you think it would be possible for you to provide free-education up to the level you envisaged without abandoning other sub-sectors.
I don't think education would take so much. Again, there is going to be internal generated revenue attached to the federation account. When I have industries working optimally with stable source of power supply to attract investors into the state,don't you know that there is going to be an increase in the internal generated revenue. We will generate enough revenue to sustain our programmes Of course, we will cut down areas of waste. So, providing free education will not be a problem along side with the development of infrastructural facilities.
How will your government achieve the much talk about industrialization of the State?
My government will achieve industrialization as soon as I have the Independent Power Plant at Ikot Abasi working. Why we don't have industries working today in Akwa Ibom and in Nigeria is as a result of high cost of Power generation.
It is a shame. I thought Nigeria said they are going to work practically to ensure that we have or we produce 300 mega watt of electricity. It's a shame.
A nation that was decades ago agitating for the independence of South-Africa as a black nation that should be governed by the blacks, praying oh! Free Mandela, little did they know that within the five years of Mandela stay in government, till today power production in South Africa stands at 49,000 mega watts but-Nigeria that says it is the giant of Africa, the Police of Africa is producing 1,700 mega watts of electricity.

Original_Search_Title: Research Council, Ghanaian Experts Aim High Quality Cassava Flour
Headline: Nigeria: Research Council, Ghanaian Experts Aim High Quality Cassava Flour
Body_Text:
Abuja — The Raw Materials Research and Development Council (RMRDC) is partnernering with Ghanaian experts on the production of high quality cassava flour in Nigeria.
The idea is achieve the 10 percent requirement for high quality cassava flour for producing bread and confectionaries.
RMRDC in partnership with several stakeholders Thursday started the training programme with the assistance of technical experts from Ghana for Nigerian industrialists involved in the sector.
Already, the target of government is to popularize cottage level production of high quality cassava flour and glucose syrup from cassava. Among other things, participating industrialists are being introduced to a simple production process recently developed in Ghana by the resource persons.
The training is designed to train the trainers on the production for high quality glucose syrup. It is expected that participants that would be trained at the programme would in turn train others at zonal and state levels.
The Ministry of Science and Technology recently constituted a committee of stakeholders to produce a blue print on cassava development. Part of the activities of the committee included a study tour of selected cassava producing countries to find out what they are doing with cassava and explore areas of possible collaboration with other countries.
In a message to the participants, Director General of the Raw Materials Research and Development Council, Prof. Peter Onwualu stressed that the Council believes in the establishment of small scale, and cottage level processing centers to produce the needed high quality cassava flour. According to him, "This is because cassava processing is a traditional business of the local populace in this country. Processing centers for the high quality cassava flour production should therefore be such as to fit into the cultural practices if the people. "In this regards, the Council has been making a lot of efforts to identify the technology that will easily adapt to this situation. This technology has now been identified.

Original_Search_Title: 'I 'll Turn Enugu Into a Mega Economy in West Africa'
Headline: Nigeria: 'I 'll Turn Enugu Into a Mega Economy in West Africa'
Body_Text:
Lagos — As the April 14 gubernatorial election draws nearer, Enugu State governorship candidate of the Accord Party (AP), Barrister Ugochukwu Agballah, said if elected, he would introduce economic programmes involving the linkage of agriculture to industrialization, thereby bringing about an economy that is the strongest in the West African sub-region.
As a contender for the seat of power in the Enugu State, what really is your vision for the state?
My vision for Enugu State is to reposition the state economically to be a sub-regional power; to be one of the strongest economies in the West African sub-region. That is my aim. But to do that, we need to rehabilitate the infrastructure because there is infrastructural collapse in Enugu State.
In Enugu, we have over 300 communities, but over 80 per cent of the communities do not have access to good roads. During my tour, I noticed that virtually all the communities do not have functional water scheme. Even in about five per cent of the areas where you have boreholes, it is not reticulated. So, I intend to introduce a programme that will make sure that people have access to water. Because in eight years of this administration, there was not one single investment in enhancing access to water - which is very embarrassing.
Thirdly, many communities do not have access to electricity - communities like Awgu, Uzo-uwani, parts of Ezeagu, Nkanu East, even in Enugu urban, there are some communities that do not have access to electricity. For instance, in Enugu, places like Ugwogu and some communities around the Nike area - Nkwubu, Ugwueme, Nkoromo do not have access to electricity, even though they live within the Enugu metropolis. So we have an infrastructural breakdown in Enugu. The infrastructural collapse in the State was precipitated by the refusal of government to invest in infrastructural rehabilitation in Enugu State. So, inspite of the media hype in Enugu, there was no investment in infrastructural development in Enugu State.
So many of the local governments don't have access roads. They include: Nkanu East, Isiuzo, Awgu, Udi, Ezeagu, Igbo-eze South, Udenu, parts of Nsukka local government, parts of Igbo Etiti local government, Aninri and parts of Igbo Eze North. In Enugu State, there are about 40 communities that have been cut off completely from civilization. These communities cannot be accessed, and it is a terrible situation. During my campaign tour, I discovered that what people need is infrastructural rehabilitation. What the people desire is to have good roads, electricity. The greatest needs of the 300 communities I visited were all similar. These needs include access to road, followed by electricity and thirdly water.
What is your programme on agriculture, considering its importance to the people of the state?
Initially, before I went on my ward tour, my blueprint was anchored on first, trying to expand agriculture. But after my tour, I had to review the blueprint because of the harsh human condition I saw our people in. I now knew about the need to remodel our blueprint. So, our emphasis will now be on infrastructural development; secondly, security; thirdly, agriculture - agro-allied industrialization, education, health, sports development in that order.
In agriculture, we did a research and discovered that Enugu State loses an average of N530 million to food stock from the North. Traders in the North bring in food stocks - all the tomatoes, onions, pepper, beans. 70 percent of the yam, goat, cows are from the North. We calculated how much we are lose to be N530m which is the amount Enugu residents spend on these items. To me, the capital flight is much.
First, we are going to put measures that will enhance agriculture - food production. The first measure is to build roads to food producing areas. To do this, we have identified very important food producing areas. We have identified the communities that have extensive agricultural business. In these places, we intend to give access roads to first. Secondly, we are going to make a nice agricultural production, that is why the North has advanced more than the East because of the type of land they have - and because tractors are being used successfully in the North. But tractors have not been successful in the South-east, especially in Enugu because of our terrain and our vegetation.
Here, we have expanses of trees, so we need to do stumping. We have designed a mini-tractor that we make available to our farmers. They will no longer be involved in agriculture that is full of pains - as in cases where they use hoes, cutlasses. We want tractors that will turn around and cultivate because of the trees. This is because for us to do stumping in Enugu, it will cost us a lot. So, the measure now is to have these small tractors in places where we carry out extensive clearing and stumping of areas where we have a large portion land because here, 80 percent of our people are involved in agriculture.
To do this, we intend to put in place a state agricultural mechanization company which will be involved in producing mechanized implements and machineries used by our farmers. We shall also invest in preservation and storage. We are not going to be involved in direct production of agriculture because it has failed, but we will set up demonstration farms in all the local governments and communities. We are going to have what you call farm extension workers in every community in Enugu State.
We shall also set up a fertilizer application and regulation agency because of the research discovery that our use of fertilizer is abysmally low. We use 10kg per hectare while in advanced countries, they make use of 110kg - especially in Asia, Europe and America and some parts of Africa - like South Africa where they make use of 110kg. In Enugu, we use less than 10kg - we use 5 - 6 kg per hectare. This has led to low output in agriculture. We are also going to do soil test in Enugu State to determine the type of fertilizer applicable to given geographical areas.
We shall then tell the communities that this type of fertilizer is suitable for your soil. That will be the function of the Fertilizer Application and Regulation Agency. It will be well founded by government, government will also make sure that farmers get their chemicals, insecticides at subsidized rates rather than investing in farming implements.
In Enugu, government has not invested in agriculture. In some states in the North, we have instances of government giving 1,000 tractors to farmers. So we are going to redress this. We did a research on why there is poverty in Africa and discovered that it is because there is no linkage between agriculture and industrialization. So, we are going to put in place policies that will link agriculture with industrialization. For instance, we are going to start with cassava. We have identified 28 crops that have the capacity of eradicating poverty in Enugu and because of time, I will not be able to give you all the details. But one of them is cassava. Enugu produces four million metric tons (MT), So, when I am voted in, I will put in place measures to enhance this to not less than 10 million MT in the first and half years. We intend to double that every year - to have about 20 million MT in our first-four years.
To link cassava to industrialization, we are going to put in place an ethanol producing industry - because etanol is the fuel of the future. Ethanol is being used as a blend in gasoline for usage by cars. This January, the European Parliament passed a law saying that every car manufactured in Europe in 2020 will go on hybrid fuel, and that gives great prospect for ethanol. Even as I speak to you, China is positioning itself as the world largest producers of etanol. That is why they budgeted $54bn buying cassava chips from Africa. And Africa is making the mistake their founding fathers made to be a supplier of raw materials to China.
In the 60's, we were the largest supplier of raw materials to the Western economies. There was a time we were number one supplier of rubber, cocoa, cashew nut which were raw materials in the Europe. We never linked up these agricultural products to industrialization. If we had established rubber, cocoa and cashew processing plants, we would have eradicated poverty. This is the reason for our poverty. We are still making mistake with crude oil . In the case of cassava, we want to position Enugu as etanol supplier to the world. It is possible to do it in Enugu because we have the agricultural base. So, if I link this and develop the economy, Enugu will have many etanol producing plants, making use of our cassava.
Also with cassava, we will produce animal feeds - since livestocks that are fed with animal feeds do better. We can also generate electricity from the waste. We can also produce starch. There are about 200 end use of cassava and we intend to utilize all of them, so as to have a cassava economy in Enugu State. Another agricultural produce we are looking at is neem which grows wide here, and the pharmaceutical and chemical needs are diverse. Today, India makes over $2bn processing neem and exporting it to the whole world. We are going to expand the production of this product - because it is a thing that small scale industries can be encouraged to get into.
Also, cashew, the world cashew business is valued at $10bn; 40 percent of this comes from Nigeria. Another idea I intend to use in expanding the economy of Enugu is Information Communication Technology (ICT) because what some governors have used in setting up telecommunication companies is the amount we need to hoist a satellite that will put Enugu on the super highway. And if we do that, we would have to a large extent empowered our youths, because IT is the revolution of the millennium. It is the only way we can get Africa out of underdevelopment and poverty.
In my tour, I did not see any school with a functional library or ICT equipped. So, my administration will build three modern schools that will be like Mea Mather Elizabeth Nnamani Secondary School. If somebody could build Mea Matter using state funds to make it a model in Enugu, then we have state funds to have at least three Mea Mathers in Enugu in our first year.
On security, we have divided Enugu into 10 security zones where we will position surveillance cameras which are not very expensive - at high brow areas. We have noted the logistics need of the police and discovered they need 300 vehicles. We are going to provide that during our first year in office. These vehicles will be linked with central computerized communication network that will make it possible for the police to co-ordinate activities. We are going to provide other modern technology to enable the police combat crime. We do this because we know that if the right measures are put in place, it will be impossible or risky for people to commit crime.
Looking at the Ebeano political group of the PDP and other candidates in the race, what are your chances in the election?
Well, I have great chances because first of all, you must remember that I was the one God used in setting up that Ebeano family. But when they began to walk on paths that were unacceptable to my faith, values and ethics, I decided to resign; because the style of governance of the Nnamani administration was unacceptable to me. Because of that and the way he has ruled, and the position of a candidate of the party, I don't think they stand a chance in this election. Secondly, you must also agree with me that I defeated (Governor) Chimaroke (Nnamani) in 2003. I will defeat whoever he brings out this time. The people of Enugu never vote for parties, they vote for individuals. And they have bought into my programme. .And that is why today, I am the object of attacks. I have had two attacks since the campaign started. But I have restrained from replying because I feel it will inflame the sentiment and tension in the state. Yesterday (Monday, March 26) the PDP governorship candidate, Mr Sullivan Chime, and his party chairman, Chief Onyioha Nwanjoku circulated a petition to the Police and the press, alleging that I am campaigning for Yar'Adua, that I should not campaign for Yar'Adua, that my campaign for Yar'adua is an attempt to deceive, that I am an impostor. But it is a clear indication of a sinking ship, of a frustrated candidate. Because of the fear of people to buy into his candidature, he has resorted to propaganda, blackmail. And that petition is also a sabotage of the Yar'Adua presidential ambition, because it is obvious that Sullivan Chime is petitioning the police to stop me from campaigning for Yar'Adua. That means he is not in support of Yar'Adua to be President of Nigeria. Chief Nwanjoku is also not in support of Yar'adua to be President of Nigeria because if they do, they should encourage parties to campaign for Yar'Adua. As I speak to you, I have gotten 14 political parties that have agreed to declare their support for Yar'Adua. I have gotten five governorship candidates that will join me in campaigning for Yar'Adua in Enugu State. And it is in the spirit of adoption of Yar'Adua by my party, Accord. That adoption is a thing that has been applauded by all well meaning people in Enugu State, but it is in the character of the Ebeano family to sabotage the national leadership of the party, to sabotage President Olusegun Obasanjo, to sabotage the leaders of Enugu State. And they resort to intimidation, subterfuge and cheap propaganda, but I will not join issues with my friend and brother, Sullivan Chime. When I win, I will accommodate them, because I am going to win.
Can you give an insight into the kind of working relationship that your party, Accord, has with PDP?
My party adopted Yar'Adua as its presidential candidate, and this was done publicly at Yar'Adua Centre, Abuja, in February this year. I believe the leadership of PDP was there. It is in the spirit of the new Nigeria that we are talking about. Yar'Adua is a man of principle, integrity and honour. It is not like in Enugu State where the governor is leaving behind a debt profile of close to N5bn. Yar'Adua is leaving behind an excess reserve of N6.5bn.
What do you expect in return for adopting Yar'Adua?
I will not tell you that on the pages of newspaper. It is not for public consumption . We were not coerced to adopt Yar'Adua. Nobody lobbied us to do so. We adopted him out of our own volition. Just watch and see. But one thing I know is that we have a working relationship, and Sullivan Chime and Nwanjoku are saying that relationship should be a subject of police investigation. So we quietly referred their petition to the national headquarters of their party for necessary disciplinary action.
You took active part in 2003 election under the All Progressives Grand Alliance (APGA), when you alleged massive electoral fraud that affected your ambition. As a governorship candidate for yet another election after about four years, what is your party doing to ensure that you do not encounter the same experience you had in 2003?
We don't discuss our strategy on the pages of newspapers.
Are you confident that the April election would be free and fair?
No. There will be rigging. Of course, the Nigerian politician wants to rig election. What you have to do is to make sure you safeguard your votes. As I speak to you, they are already planning to rig the 2007 election in Enugu. We have facts that there is a plan. But to foil that is the reason we are working against it. That is why we are making sure that the plan fails, and it will fail.

Original_Search_Title: When PDP Train Landed in Kebbi
Headline: Nigeria: When PDP Train Landed in Kebbi
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) last Wednesday officially flagged-off its presidential campaign and presented the flagbearer, the Katsina State governor, Alh. Umar Musa Yar'adua and and his running mate, Dr. Good luck Jonathan to the people of Kebbi State. Thousands of people abandoned their work on that fateful day to catch glimpse of the candidates while the venue of the ceremony Haliru Abdu stadium was filled to the capacity.
The PDP presidential campaign team arrived Birnin Kebbi, Kebbi State capital from Sokoto. The team which was led by the PDP national chairman, Senator Ahmadu Ali was received on arrival at Sokoto/Kebbi boarder by the state governor, Alhaji Adamu Aliero, members of the state executive council, the PDP presidential campaign co-ordinator in Kebbi State, Alhaji Bello Ibrahim Gwandu and PDP leaders in the state. The first point of call by the presidential team on arrival was the palace of the chairman Kebbi state council of chiefs and the emir of Gwandu Alhaji Muhammadu Iliyasu Bashar before proceeding to address the mammoth crowd at Haliru Abdu Stadium in Birnin Kebbi.
Although president Olusegun Obasanjo did not attend the rally, many PDP stalwarts who spoke at the occasion including the national chairman of the party,, the party presidential candidates, Alhaji Ummaru Musa Yar'adua, the deputy chairman (South) and Director general of Yar'adua/Jonathan campaign organisation, Chief Bode George, PDP north-west chairman, Dr. Bello Muhammed, PDP North-west zonal presidential campaign coordinator and Kaduna state governor, Alhaji Ahmadu Makarfi, Kebbi State governor, Alhaji Adamu Aliero, PDP state Chairman Alhaji Saidu Maigishiri PDP gubernatorial candidate Alh. Saidu Dakingari, Kebbi state PDP presidential campaign coordinator,, Alh. Bello Ibrahim Bello Gwandu and Alh. Isah Muhammed, the Director general, Saidu Dakingari Campaign Organisation among others. The PDP presidential running mate and governor of Bayelsa State, Dr. Good luck Jonathan and all the PDP gubernatorial and senatorial candidates in Sokoto, Kebbi, and Samfara states also graced the occasion.
While addressing the rally, Yar'adua promised to ensure justice and fairness and tackle most the nation's problems through its seven programme agenda which he said would solve the problem of power supply, unemployment of youth and ensure food surplus, good road network and health care delivery among other things. He said he will give priority to agriculture, education and transform the rural areas within few years.
Said Yar'adua: " I have a lot of things to do for this country if elected as next president. Backward never forward ever Insha Allah".
The PDP candidate had earlier promised during a courtesy call on the emir and in an interview with newsmen after the rally that he would present a bill to establish National Council of Traditional Rulers and tackle the problem of insecurity.
Yar'adua said the PDP government under him would continue to accord respect to the traditional institution as the custodian of our culture and heritag and urged traditional rulers to pray for peaceful conduct of the 2007 election.
Said he: " Your royal highness. I will like to call on traditional rulers to pray for the peaceful conduct of the coming election. Our government will continue to respect the traditional institution and I promise to present a bill to the National Assembly for the national council of traditional rulers if elected as the next president".
On the problem of insecurity in the country, he explainedthat he would adopt practical approach and endeavour to employp the best talents to addresse the problem. " I will adopt practical approach to solve the country's insecurity problem if elected as the next president Insha Allah. There was insecurity problem in Kaduna State before Governor Ahmed Makarfi was elected. Kaduna has been governed by military governors but they had never been in control of security situation as is the case during the tenure of Makarfi. What is important is to have the brain to tap the talent that abound. Whoever is saying you need military background in order to tackle Nigeria security problem is just expressing his personal wishes and opinion. And this has no relation to reality".
He explained that it was not in the constitution that anybody who wants to be president should have military background. The criterion for governance is spelled out in the constitution. Who can govern and who cannot govern. And the constitution did not say you must have a military background before you can tackle security problem or be a teacher before you can tackle education problem".
The national chairman after presenting Yar'adua/Jonathan to the gathering, said the duo were the winning team for the PDP and will not disappoint the nation if elected. According to him, the PDP-led administration in the past eight years had improved on the life of down trodden in the society and the good policies and programmes of the Obasanjo administration must continue.
He used the occasion to call on farmers in Kebbi state to cultivate 0cassava farm to enhance their economic status. Ali revealed that the Federal Government had earmarked N 5 billion for the purchase of cassava from the farmers across the country and urged the farmers in the state to cultivate the commodity to enable them benefit from the cassava scheme. " Cassava has now become a hot commodity for export in the country and I will advise farmers in this state to support the Federal Government initiative on cassava to boost the nation's foreign reserve," he said.
The state governor, Alh. Adamu Aliero reiterated that he joined the PDP in order to enable the people of the state participate actively in the national politics. He said the merger of ANPP and PDP had reduced tension and political clash in the state, adding "More benefits will come to our people when the PDP controls both the federal and the state governments".
Besides the PDP chairman, Yaradua, George and Makarfi all praised Aliero for defecting to the PDP. They described him as a man with foresight who loves his people and do not want them to suffer. Ali said "this development is historic and in the best interest of your people. The PDP will win in the northwest especially Sokoto, Kebbi and Zamfara states".
The PDP govership candidate, Alh. Saidu Dakingari promised to continue with programmes of Aliero's government and lay a solid foundation for the economic growth of the state if elected as the next governor.
The Yar'adua/Jonathan presidential campaign coordinator in the state, Alh. Bello Ibrahim Gwandu in his welcome address, said Kebbi State was the home of PDP and called on the people of the state to vote en masse for the Yar'adua/Jonathan Ticket who will win the election in Sha Allah.
The rally was with a lot of thrills, which climaxed with the presentation of the Kebbi state PDP gubernatorial candidate Dakingari and his running mate, Alh. Ibrahim K. Aliyu to the general public and a meeting of PDP stakeholders and , in Kebbi State at an interactive session with Yar'adu. at the banquet hall of presidential lodge, Birnin Kebbi before the presidential team departed to Zamfara State.

Original_Search_Title: Tobacco Industry Going Up in Smoke
Headline: Malawi: Tobacco Industry Going Up in Smoke
Body_Text:
Blantyre — Tobacco prices and production levels are dropping amid pressure from the anti-smoking lobby and the general downturn in agricultural produce markets. But Malawi has still not made adequate progress in promoting crops to replace its primary foreign exchange earner.
Green gold is the term that Malawians use for the country's tobacco. The nation derives up to 70 percent of its foreign exchange earnings from the crop and 80 percent of the country's labour force works in the tobacco industry. Historically, the leaf has been regarded as an economic lifeline in a country without rich mineral endowments.
The southern African country is a major tobacco exporter in the world, accounting for five percent of the world's total exports and two percent of world's total production. In terms of burley tobacco, Malawi produces about 20 percent of the world's total, according to the World Bank.
The Tobacco Association of Malawi (TAMA), which promotes and protects tobacco farmers' interests, says that the leaf also accounts for 13 percent of the country's gross domestic product and makes up 23 percent of the tax base.
The crop is treasured because of historical associations. Commercial production can be traced back as far as 1889 when it was introduced by settlers from Virginia in the United States.
However, in recent years the tobacco industry has been struggling for survival. It is fighting global anti-smoking campaigns led by public health activists, backed by the World Health Organisation. Poor auction prices and a dearth of buyers are also among the challenges that Malawian tobacco producers are grappling with.
Caught in the middle of these challenges are the country's small-holder tobacco farmers. One of them is 55-year-old Dongo Msiska. All his life he has known nothing but tobacco cultivation.
At the age of 24, he took over a 50-hectare farm from his father. Since then the livelihoods of his family and those of his 33 employees have depended on the production of the leaf.
In the past three years, Msiska's income has dwindled rapidly in the face of poor auction floor prices. He has since been forced to cut production by half.
"I could not afford to buy enough production inputs with the little money I got for last year's crop. I had to reduce production and have since had to let some of my workers go," he says.
Msiska's woes were a result of last year's catastrophic decline of 15 percent in tobacco sales. Sales figures from the Tobacco Control Commission (TCC) indicated that the crop raked in 162 061 893 US dollars in 2005 but only 137 834 528 US dollars in 2006.
Malawian president Bingu wa Mutharika, himself a farmer, has admitted that the tobacco industry is not as viable as it used to be.
He and the ministries of agriculture and trade have urged tobacco farmers to consider diversifying production to cotton, cassava, pigeon peas, ground nuts, soya, dairy products, beans and rice as alternatives to tobacco.
Up to 40,000 farmers have heeded the calls to diversify in the past six years and have since abandoned tobacco production, according to the TCC.
But despite his calls for diversification, Mutharika has continued to fight for the survival of the tobacco industry. Major buyers of Malawi's tobacco were last year ordered by the president to leave the country or offer better prices on the auction floors. He accused them of running a cartel and fixing prices.
Mutharika imposed a minimum price of 110 cents per kilogram and, for higher grade leaf, 170 cents per kilogram but the buyers boycotted the market, forcing the government to concede defeat. The president has no kind words for the buyers and has since branded them "thieves" and "exploiters" for defying his price setting.
Meanwhile, Malawi is pursuing a deal that addresses issues of collective marketing as well as value-adding with the other tobacco-producing countries of Zambia, Tanzania, Zimbabwe and Mozambique. They aim to position the industry better in the world markets so that the countries can reap more from the crop, says the trade ministry.
The government is optimistic that handling the problems dogging the tobacco industry at a regional level will yield more positive results than working in isolation.
Despite the pressure on the industry the majority of Malawian farmers, through TAMA, have resisted a diversification strategy that excludes tobacco because the commercial value of the crop still remains the highest.
TAMA executive secretary Felix Mkumba argues that the leaf has a readily available market which is well guaranteed since everything that they produce is still sold, despite the low prices. "Accepting total diversification will be suicidal."
David Mkwambisi, a lecturer at the Bunda College of Agriculture at the University of Malawi, disagrees with TAMA. He does not consider tobacco to be the backbone of the country's economy anymore.
This is because the government and stakeholders have failed to introduce measures which would enhance crop production, he argues. The growers have been penalized with taxes which have not been ploughed back into the sector.
Therefore Mkwambisi contends that the global problems besetting the tobacco industry are not the primary concern. Domestic politics is the source of much of the tobacco industry's difficulties.
He also has questions about how the government is approaching the issue of diversification. "Even though cotton was identified as a crop to replace tobacco, nothing has been done to promote cotton.
"Why did the president rush to announce the diversification towards cotton? Do we have markets for cotton, cassava, soya and beans? Why should we expand cultivation to those products if we have not found the markets yet?" asks Mkwambisi.
He says that even if tobacco was not facing the current public health campaigns, it is extremely bad planning to depend on one crop for economic growth. "As a country we have been standing on a slim edge economically by relying on tobacco only," warns Mkwambisi.
Meanwhile Malawi's tobacco production levels are plunging. The TCC has indicated that tobacco yields this year (2007) are down by 18 million kg from 158 million kg last year. The country's international buyers are demanding 170 million kg, which means that the supply from Malawi is short by 30 million kg.

Original_Search_Title: Why FG Introduced Bio-Fuels, By Kupolokun
Headline: Nigeria: Why FG Introduced Bio-Fuels, By Kupolokun
Body_Text:
Enugu — Group Managing Director, Nigerian National Petroleum Corporation (NNPC), Engineer Funso Kupolokun, yesterday gave insight into why bio-fuels were introduced into the nation's energy mix, explaining that it was aimed at integrating the agricultural sector with the energy sector.
Kupolokun also said yesterday, at the on-going 18th Enugu International Trade Fair, that the idea of establishing the $350million Nigerian Content Support Fund (NCSF), was to create an avenue through which indigenous companies can source funds to compete with their foreign counterparts.
Represented by one of the Directors in the Corporation, Engineer Austin Oniwoh said the effective combination of efforts of the agricultural sector and the energy sector would have profound impact in increasing the nation's Gross Domestic Product (GDP), in addition to the creation and spreading of wealth among a very large segment of the Nigerian populace.
He said in pursuit of this, the NNPC has engaged the Federal University of Agriculture, Markurdi, as a consultant to develop high yielding, short gestation cassava and sugar cane breeds.
"The seedlings will be made available to both small and large scale farmers for cultivation and subsequent supply of the produce to ethanol distilleries, to be established in various parts of Nigeria," he said.
Kupolokun said the bio-fuels initiative will have the twin effect of protecting the nation's environment as green fuel, and spreading wealth amongst the poor rural farmers and the mechanised urban farmers who hitherto have been distant beneficiaries of the oil and gas economy.
He also said it was to ensure that Nigerian entrepreneurs were put in a position to compete on a more level-playing field with foreign firms that led government through the NNPC and a consortium of Nigerian banks, to establish the $350million NCSF fund through which serious Nigerian oil service providers can have access to single-digit borrowing as their foreign competitors.
He invited entrepreneurs from the South-east and members of the Enugu Chamber of Commerce, Industry, Mines and Agriculture (ENCCIMA), to take advantage of the fund.
Kupolokun also said dividend of the liberalisation of the downstream oil and gas sector has engendered effective and active private sector inclusion and participation in bulk products importation, distribution and expanded retailing,
resulting in the much desired wealth creation across the products supply chain.
He said NNPC has now shifted emphasis to creating the same enablement of Nigerian entrepreneurs in the upstream sector through the Nigerian Content initiative, adding that the
Corporation's management has been actively engaged in promoting and monitoring local inputs to all on-going and new projects in the sector.
"Our target of achieving 45per cent Nigerian content in 2006 was largely realized, while concerted efforts are on to achieve the targeted 70 per cent in year 2010. The Nigerian Content initiative seeks to significantly improve the quantum of composite value
added or created in the Nigerian economy through the effective utilisation of Nigerian human and material resources for the provision of goods and services to the oil and gas industry," he said,

Original_Search_Title: Agip to Open Office in Bayelsa
Headline: Nigeria: Agip to Open Office in Bayelsa
Body_Text:
Yenagoa — The Nigerian Agip Oil Company (NAOC) may have succumbed to pressure from the Bayelsa State government to establish its presence in Yenagoa the state capital as the Italian oil giant has indicated its preparedness to set up a functional and administrative office in the capital city.
In an interview with newsmen in Yenagoa shortly after the presentation of improved planting materials to Bayelsa State Ministry of Agriculture, NAOC's Public Affairs Division Manger, Mr. Naaman Dienye said preparations are in top gear for the company to establish its presence in Yenagoa.
The planting materials presented to the ministry include 5000 yam seedlings, 500 cassava bundles, 1000kg of maize, 1,500 plantain suckers, rice paddy, oil palm seedlings, melon, sweet potato vines and 100g of Okro.
He noted that the presentation of the improved planting materials is part of the company's social responsibility programme to empower its host states and communities through agriculture.
"Though NAOC is an oil exploring, exploiting and production company, we love diversifying our activities, especially empowering communities where we carry out our operations," he said, in reference to Agip Green River programme adding that, "since the company cannot employ everybody, this is one way the host communities in the Niger Delta area are being impacted".
According to him, Nigeria is in a good position to improve its Gross Domestic Products through agriculture and reiterated NAOC's preparedness to set up demonstration farms as well as supply farmers with quality farm inputs.
Receiving the materials on behalf of the government and farmers, the state commissioner for agriculture, Mr. Gilbert Adeh thanked NAOC for the gesture promising that farmers would make judicious use of the items.

Original_Search_Title: Evil Life for Students After 4.30
Headline: Kenya: Evil Life for Students After 4.30
Body_Text:
Nairobi — With his shirt hanging loose, a rolled book thrust under the belt at the back of his pair of trousers, Juma Onyango* walks majestically on the streets of Mombasa. He is accompanied by friends. The boys chant as they stroll, oblivious of the fact that their loud voices attract the attention of other pedestrians. Eavesdropping passers-by stare with dismay. The boys are talking about a new female teacher at their school. To them, she is easy prey.
According to the boys, the teacher, "Miss Hot Cake", is simply stunning. They hope to take her out someday. "I will drive her in my Pa's Land Cruiser, take her to the finest hotel in town, and shower her with real love," one of the boys boasts.
As they head towards Mama Ngina Drive Garden, the lads humour themselves with self-important talk, describing their teacher's body curves in lurid detail, and discussing her pretty looks, all aloud. Their desire forces them to make a bet: who among them will ask her out first?
Time passes. The clock ticks. Finally, they arrive at their destination, Mama Ngina Drive Garden. This garden is one of the popular resting hotspots at the Coast, ideal for a boring weekend. From the lush garden, one can have a panoramic view of the Indian Ocean and ships entering and leaving the Port of Mombasa. At a distance, fun lovers are treated to a spectacular view of the Likoni Ferry channel, and catch a glimpse of the ferry as it cuts through the deep sea, loaded with people and vehicles.
The garden not only serves as a resting place, but discotheques and casinos are also found there, with vendors selling cassava crisps and coconut juice. During weekends, the road leading to Mama Ngina Drive is jammed with private vehicles. Young boys and girls from rich families can be seen driving their parents' cars, moving back and forth as they play loud music in a brazen, ostentatious manner.
Mama Ngina Drive has a rich history, having hosted events from crime to love stories to suicide missions. The garden was at one time riddled with crime, and tales abound about how thieves hid in the bushes and caves along coral rocks, before they were flushed out. Today, on working days, the garden has a different kind of visitors. Under the giant baobab tree, which acts as a shed from the scorching sun, one will find a group of middle-aged men chewing khat ('miraa'), and lovers catching up on old times. Those lucky enough to have cars, park them overlooking the ocean, in order to get a good look at the vast blue water.
Onyango and his friend hide behind one of the baobab trees to smoke Marijuana (bhang). After taking several puffs, blowing a cloud of smoke to the air and letting his chest rise and fall showily, he becomes furious when he realises that intruders are eyeing him and his friends. "What are you looking at?" he asks with a tone of defiance. "You wanna have a puff?"
After a friendly chat, the boys open up and talk of their escapades at Mama Ngina Drive after school. After class, most of them are tired, they explain, and need space to "loosen up" before going home. They claim that, at home, parents do not give them space to watch TV or visit friends. Instead, parents expect them to study. Onyango says it is only during schooldays that they get a chance to go out and have a good time without a chaperone. At home, he is overburdened with chores like washing.
He carries on: "When I get to the house, I am not allowed to visit any friends. I do the washing, cooking, sweeping, ironing and caring for my siblings.
" Asked why he smokes marijuana, he says he needs to induce a mindset in which it becomes easier to put up with his stepmother's hatred and mistreatment, as well as the frequent beatings he receives from his alcoholic father.
"I feel good and stress-free after a few puffs," he adds. "When I get to the house, I am full of energy ready to endure all the insults."
Boys smoke bhang during schools events
Onyango reveals that, on weekends, particularly on Saturday' s between 2pm and 7pm, students from various Coastal schools spend time at the beach or at Mama Ngina Drive, accompanied by their boyfriends or girlfriends. Not more than once, girls carry from home an extra outfit, shoes and make-up kits, convenient for transforming their looks from those of students to those of reckless lowlifes. And when the Saturday preps are over, they change and head to the beach or some other entertainment joint.
Onyango continues: "Parents are not aware of these things. We pretend to be on a school trip or group discussion to get the excuse of getting home late, after we have quenched our thirst." Students, he says, take advantage of special-day celebrations, like public clean-up days, wildlife days, sports days, science congresses, and drama and music festivals to drink liquor, use drugs and engage in early sex.
This boy began using bhang during such schools events. "It was fun and we enjoyed it at first," he reveals. "But now I can't seem to stop myself. I have tried all ways out of the habit but in vain."
Students flirting with prostitutes
Students of a Mombasa high school walk home after school. While many do not engage in vice, some others turn to sites reputed for sleaze and evil. Picture by Omondi Onyango
Today, on every school day after 4.30pm, Onyango and his peers roam the town, dressed in school uniform, drinking liquor or using hard drugs. Around the Coastal region, the practice is endemic. More than once, students have been seen flocking the town centre, moving from shop to shop as others are spotted at the beach, almost naked.
In downtown Mombasa, especially along Moi Avenue, male students spend time in pubs and hookers' dens, drinking and flirting with prostitutes. As dusk approaches, some walk out of seedy lodgings, hand-in-hand with the prostitutes.
In some mixed boarding secondary schools, girls openly fight over boys. After class, students often walk hand-in-hand in town, visit discotheques together, many times concealing their identities with the 'bui bui' (a veil).
A parent, Duda Omar, says that touts, who play loud music, have DVD shows in the vehicles. These are popular with students of both genders. The vehicles carry an excess number of students, playing lewd music and entertaining students who abscond from class. "One time, a friend caught her daughter enjoying herself in a bus, without caring that it was school time," he recalls. He however says that since the police began a crackdown on the public service vehicles that were playing the banned music, students have almost stopped hiding in them.
At home, children of between age 10 and 15 years have sinful and shocking stories to tell of how they spend their time after school. Shockingly, they are already conversant with romantic TV films, with some already having put to practice what they watch with their peers.
Boys want to kiss
A teacher at a local school says the school has had several cases of parents being summoned after male pupils tried to kiss their female counterparts at school. Speaking on condition of anonymity, the teacher says such children watch questionable movies with the full knowledge of parents.
Not all students or pupils engage in the vice, nonetheless. The teacher expounds: "We have some pupils whose parents are keen on their children's education," he says. Such pupils, he explains, do well at school.
An example is Musa Juma, a 12-year-old student in a Mombasa school, who says his parents have never given him room to watch TV or play at his studies' expense. During the school day, Musa is already seated in class by 6.30am for tuition. "During lunch break, we eat in a hurry because, by 1pm, we are expected back in class for the afternoon preps, until 2pm when normal classes resume." On Saturday, he attends school. On Sundays, he is at the Madrassa (or Islamic school).
Youths like Juma, however, have bad role models all around. Not long ago, a local FM station DJ had to rebuke an 11-year-old boy after the boy called at 11pm, asking the DJ to play a love song for him. The radio programme was 'Ngalawa ya Mapenzi'.
Mombasa students don't take education seriously
Peter Mayoyo, a senior teacher at Mombasa High School, says because Mombasa is a tourist destination, students are easily lured into Western-style evil, especially where they live in homes that are inundated with undue privilege and freedom. Mayoyo adds that many Mombasa students have never taken their education seriously, especially in families that are financially well off.
"With them, it is just a bother that they also have to attend school," he says. "A big number of them are sure to inherit fortunes from their wealthy relatives after school, or to get employed at their parents' businesses."
Mayoyo also points an accusing finger at the media for showing near-pornographic movies, arguing that some TV and radio stations carry programmes of themes and taste that should not be viewed or listened to by people falling under a given age. Pupils and students, he says, imitate what they see, much to their detriment. He at the same time blames the woes on cheap access to the Internet in urban centres.
On his part, Thedius Mkalla, head of Ziwani School for the Deaf, says peer pressure and modernisation have made what he terms "dot.com students" to go for wicked cultural practices.
Mkalla says poor upbringing has greatly contributed to the current behaviour of students in schools, as parents are less concerned about their children's education.
Also to blame has been evening tuition in upper primary school, which persists despite a government ban on tuition in public primary schools. A spot check shows that many pupils from public primary schools check out of school at 3pm and spend a good part of the afternoon and evening playing football. Not all such students' whereabouts can be accounted for by parents or guardians.
*Some names have been changed

Original_Search_Title: New Cassava Disease in Mukono
Headline: Uganda: New Cassava Disease in Mukono
Body_Text:
Mukono — After five years of relief from the cassava Mosaic disease, another disease has emerged in Mukono district.
The disease which poses a threat of famine and poverty was identified by the Agriculture officer of Mukono Doreen kataama as cassava Brown Streak virus.
The disease causes rotting of roots.
It is caused by a virus spread by white small flies that suck water from cassava leaves and stems.
They move from infected plants to uninfected ones.
The attacked plant remains green but the roots rot.
"The entire district is affected but Seeta Namuganga and Kasawo sub-counties are the most affected because cassava is grown on a commercial basis," Ms Kataama said.
Seeta Namuganga sub-county agricultural officer Steven kasumba said the sub-county has been producing between 15 and 24 tones of cassava every day but last year's produce reduced to 445 kilogrammes because of the disease.
Ms Kataama said the virus was identified in 1945. She said there was no drug for it but advised farmers to plant resistant species of the crops.
"There two type of resistant cassava at Namulonge Agriculture Research institute, Akena and Type 2961," she said.
However, these two types do not mature quickly.

Original_Search_Title: Flour Millers Give Cassava Growers N500 Million Life Line
Headline: Nigeria: Flour Millers Give Cassava Growers N500 Million Life Line
Body_Text:
The Flour Millers Association of Nigeria (FMAN) has given N500 million to the Cassava Empowerment Fund to actualize the federal government's policy on 10 per cent cassava inclusion in flour.
A member of the association, Mr Babatunde Odunayo, last Friday in Abuja said that the money would serve as life line for cassava growers, processors and other key stakeholders in the cassava initiative, including research institutions.
Odunayo, who made this known at the meeting of the National Cassava Development Committee, said the money would attract eight per cent interest.
He said the money was deposited at the Nigerian Agricultural, Cooperative and Rural Development Bank (NARCDB) early in the year while applications were being processed "to present cheque to successful applicants in few days time."
The association member, said millers required more than 200,000 tonnes of cassava annually to feed the mills around the country.
Odunayo noted that in the long run if there was enough to feed the mills it would save the country the foreign exchange being spent on imported wheat and also reduce import bill.
"Nigerians will then feel proud that we have a unique made in Nigeria bread with local content," he added.

Original_Search_Title: IT World - Nigcomsat Ready May - Minister
Headline: Nigeria: IT World - Nigcomsat Ready May - Minister
Body_Text:
The Nigerian communication satellite will be launched on May 14, 2007, Minister of Science and Technology, Professor Turner T. Isoun has said.
Isoun said the satellite had already been completed and moved to the launching site.
Professor Isoun who addressed journalists at a workshop organised for science correspondents in Abuja said the purpose of the workshop was to bridge the existing gap between the scientists and journalists especially in the space technology.
He said it was important to get journalists involved in the activities of the science and technology if the sector has to grow.
He said going to space is the fundamental decision by the federal government in its desire to uplift the wellbeing of Nigerians through using space technology, saying in the fast eight years, Nigeria has recorded success in the area of space.
He said their administration had raised the level of space technology from zero to number one in the sub-saharan Africa.
In his remark, the director general of the National Space Research and Development Agency (NASRDA), Professor Robert Boroffice said part of the mandate of the NARSDA is to promote space education and manpower development to actualise the gains of satellite technology through appropriate skills acquisition.
He said more Nigerians are being trained in all aspects of satellite technology in order to create the critical mass of experts needed to actualize 'our own goal of building future satellite in Nigerian by Nigerians'.
He said the earth observation satellite- NigeriaSat-1 launched in 2003 is doing well in the orbit and had met over 250 requests for data by researchers and students in universities.
According to him, satellite data derived from NigeriaSat-1 has been used in addressing the following national problems such as monitoring health and yield determination of cassava, Fadama land information system for rice growing, gully erosion, desertification, deforestation and other environmental impact assessment of oil producing in Niger Delta.
"In addition, a telemedicine network that will link university teaching hospitals and other primary health centres is on progress," he added.

Original_Search_Title: Agip Restates Commitment to N. Delta Dev.
Headline: Nigeria: Agip Restates Commitment to N. Delta Dev.
Body_Text:
Lagos — Management of Italian oil major, Nigerian Agip Oil Company (NAOC) says it is committed to boost Agricultural production in the Niger Delta in order to create revenue for the people and make them self reliant.
Public Affairs Divisional Manager of the company, Dienyi Feyi stated this in Yenagoa during the distribution of improved planting materials to the Bayelsa state ministry of agriculture.
Feyi stated that Agip's principle to empower communities in its areas of operation to enable them has something. 0
"We realize, we cannot employ everybody, so we took a firm promise to help the people where we operate. We have been going round the coastal states to fulfill that promise made by management," the manager explained.
He explain that the country was leaving out an integral part of her overall contributions to the Gross Domestic Product (GDP), due to the over concentration on oil, but noted that agriculture is a bigger earner with a genuine investment in the sector; which would readily complement the nation's rich oil resource.
As an exploration and production company, Feyi pointed out that Agip was already looking at key areas in agriculture, saying it has a rice farm in Bayelsa that is doing well, extension services and fish farms in other areas; all of which are appendages of the 'Green River' project.
The Public Affairs Manager stressed that the state government has the appropriate organs to put the materials to good use and stated with emphasis that the firm expects a feedback on the success or otherwise of the project.
Responding shortly after receiving the improved planting materials, the state Commissioner for Agriculture, Mr. Gilbert Adeh thanked Agip for the gesture and promised that as good farmers, government would take advantage of the materials to boost the agricultural potentials of the state, especially now that she was looking at areas such as aquaculture and fish farming.
The materials distributed include, 500 bundles of cassava stems, five thousand seed yams, one thousand kilogram of maize, 1,500 plantain suckers, 500 sweet potato vines, 500 oil palm seedlings, 500 Telfaria pod and 500 kilograms of rice paddy among others.

Original_Search_Title: Hardships Fertile Ground for Invention
Headline: Zimbabwe: Hardships Fertile Ground for Invention
Body_Text:
Harare — IT is said Indian legend Mohandas Gandhi devised a list of what he termed the "Seven Blunders of the World."
One of them was "Science without Humanity" This statement in many ways showed Gandhi's splendid and lasting possession -- mental excellence.
Top Japanese inventor Professor Yasuyuki Fujimura who visited Zimbabwe recently is walking in Gandhi's path of simplicity, humility and concern for humanity.
His passion today is to help find solutions, using simple technology, to the numerous problems facing people in the developing world.
Prof and Dr Fujimura, as the Japanese call him, came to Zimbabwe together with Engineer Satoh Yohji, a consultant, Mr Ichiro Wachi of the Tokyo International Language and Technology Centre and Jeffrey Ng of the Microware Systems based in Singapore to help find ways of supporting the country's IT system in disadvantaged schools.
Prof Fujimura and the Minister of Science and Technology Development, Dr Olivia Muchena, visited schools and communities in Mutoko.
The visit gave Prof Fujimura a lot of insight into the challenges facing rural people when it came to accessing potable water and computers, particularly in schools.
Talking to him after the visit to Mutoko and the Eastern Highlands, revealed his passion and love for invention that makes life easier for "everyday people".
"Innovation is very necessary in this country. My impression about people in this country is that they are peace loving and hard working," he said.
"After the Second World War we were so poor; we had nothing and we had to find ways of surviving. When I visited Mutoko with the minister I found that people have difficulties in accessing water and power for computers in schools.
"The problems are similar in some ways with our experience after the Second World War but inventors can help find solutions to the challenges. Innovation not only helps make life easier for people, it also gives courage and hope to people to persevere in their chosen environment," he said.
Concern for the people who are suffering is one of his major motivations.
"It's not about money. If you don't have anything and you are going through hardships, that creates a fertile ground for invention," Prof Fujimura said, "Invention is about offering solutions to people facing hardships."
Prof Fujimura, who has more than 1 100 inventions which are patented in Japan, is the president of Labo Non-Electric company and Invention Labo Company, and professor at Kyushu National University and Osaka City University.
His discussion on his career was important and inspiring as it offered deep insights and invaluable reassurances about the power of positive thinking in an age driven by high tech and consumerism.
When he was 38 years old, his eldest son suffered from asthma. The deep cough, difficulties in breathing and pain his son endured affected him greatly that he had to invent an air purifier to help his son.
This (asthma attack on his son) was a price Japan paid for concentrating on high tech without respect and concern for the natural environment.
The rapid industrialisation of Japan had caused excessive pollution of the air and environment.
"I don't want developing countries to copy the technology of industrialised countries.
I want people in the developing countries to invent simple technology using locally available materials to answer some of their problems," Prof Fujimura said.
Since then, he said, his company focuses on research and development to create products that are friendly to the environment and beneficial for the health of children.
No companies were interested in air purifiers in the mid-1980s when Prof Fujimura quit a secure job with a major company to set up his own business to market the fan-less, ionic air purifier Clear Veil.
To date, his company has produced more than 2,5 million units of air purifying machines for millions of children suffering from asthma.
"Through this invention, I started to be aware of this problem the world over. I also became aware that there are more problems in developed countries than in developing ones.
Since then, I got the motivation to help people in need in Mongolia, Nigeria and Latin America," he said.
Scientific inventions, he said, must have a human face and help people cope with life in their chosen environs.
"I became concerned about the health of children when I found my son had developed an allergy.
"The environment and children's health were neglected when the economy was booming. I asked myself whether the two should be sacrificed in the name of economic development," he said, as he emphasised the need to put the environment first.
"In a way, I saw Japan 20 years ahead there. I believed Japan would not be able to sustain its high economic growth much longer. I was also convinced that people would not just sit back and watch the Earth being destroyed, but do something to help the environment."
Prof Fujimura is now at the forefront of popularising his "Leap Frog Theory" that advocates the creation of independent and sustainable industries in developing countries.
"I decided to go low-tech and work on inventions that will assist people in their day-to-day lives," he said.
"We need to promote technology that is sensitive to the ecology, that is safe to humans, that is human and addresses the concerns of the poor too."
In Mongolia, Prof Fujimura invented a non-electric refrigerator for the nomadic population in that country and is now working to develop simple radios and televisions using local materials.
"Nomadic Mongolians must continue to live with nature and if I invent television and radio, this can help them retain their traditions without moving to the urban areas," he said. "The non-electric refrigerator gave them courage and hope. I feel very happy when I invent something that gives people hope."
The nomads pay two sheep for the refrigerator and Prof Fujimura said he gives the concepts for free to the communities via a locally based people-centred company.
For Nigeria, he invented battery recondition units and solar fish dryers to help communities that faced numerous challenges when it came to drying fish they caught.
His battery recondition model can help cut Nigeria's battery import bill from US$10 million per year for new batteries to US$4,5 million every three years for reconditioned batteries. He is now working to develop devices for cassava and oil processing as well as solar water sterilisers.
In Latin America, he is helping rural communities with simple coffee roasters and other gadgets effective and more appropriate for developing countries' agricultural sector needs.
"Invention is not a discovery but its combining local materials and known concepts. If invention is new then it's a discovery. Most communities need concepts and business models for simple technology which is environmentally friendly," he said.
For Zimbabwe, he said, he will work to device a gadget that will tap solar energy and convert it for use in computers that were distributed under the Presidential Computer Programme.
"Solar energy uses direct current (DC) and computers use DC so maybe its possible to by-pass the Alternate Current connections by putting a switch that can move power directly to the computer," he said after learning of the challenges facing many rural schools that have no electricity.
Prof Fujimura was born in 1944 in Manchuria (mainland China). His family returned to their homeland after World War II.
"We were so poor after the World War Two. Every year was a year to produce something for survival.
"I had no toys, so I had to make my own toys. I got interested in using my brains to create something that could make me and my people happy," he said.
"This was the basis of my interest in invention."
He completed his PhD in Physics at Osaka National University in 1973 when he was 28 years old.
"At that time, I wanted to be a professor but I found the life of an inventor very interesting. So here I am, I've been an inventor for 31 years."
Prof Fujimura said his visit to Zimbabwe was inspiring.
His colleagues described him as a gifted person willing to share his ideas and concepts with people in the developing world without being driven by the profit motive.
"He has made us think invention. It's the mindset that we need to change.
