Describing secondary schools in the state as mere 'corner shops', he promised to upgrade two of the schools in each senatorial zone to a one stop shop of modern knowledge while engaging the expertise of such Enugu born eggheads as Pofessor Nnaji to turn the state into a technological wonderland.
Amidst intermittent cheers from his supporters, Oscar Egwuonwu, the candidate of the Democratic Peoples Party (DPP) admitted that all the candidates had spoken well and were in the danger of repeating themselves. He declared that the problem with the state was like that of the country, not the lack of ideas but lack of morals. Thanking the church for organizing the forum,he said it marked the 'beginning of democracy in Enugu state since it provided a moral platform for the eventual winner of the election to launch his activities in government. He said the greatest challenge facing the state was to rid it of corrupt elements and corrupt orientation stressing that it required miracles to bring that about.
Shipping magnate and the candidate of the Labour Party Mr. Okey Ezea promised to bring his experience and successes in both the private and public sectors to bear on his governance of the state. He said the main trust of his policies would be the eradication of poverty and wealth creation through the reintroduction of mechanized agriculture, qualitative and purposeful education and industrialization. Noting that Enugu people had suffered enough; he said his administration would bring back the glory of the state and the cheer on their faces.
Nnamdi Anigbo, the candidate of the Progressives Peoples Alliance (PPA) injected some comedy into the session when he challenged Peter Okonkwo's claim to being the 'David' of the race saying that as the youngest of the candidates, the situation properly suited him to be the 'David with the small pebble' to conquer the 'Goliath' of corruption and backwardness in the state. He promised to redirect and reorganize the youths towards engaging in productive and constructive activities adding that he would also empower the women and the aged.
The last of the speeches was delivered by Chief Charles Egumgbe, the candidate of the United Nigeria Peoples Party (UNPP) who came fully armed with statistical data from a study he had commissioned on the imperatives for the rapid development of the state. He said with the state earning more than N3.5 billion every month from all sources, there was enough money to pay civil servants on the 25th of every month, pay retirees their pensions including backlogs as well as their gratuities. He said there was also enough to give a subvention of at least N50m monthly to each of the tertiary institutions owned by the state government while constructing at least one road in a local government each month. He said a study he had commissioned on the grounded industries in the state including those established as far back as the first republic, showed that all they required was the will power to commit the requisite funds and efforts to revive them and make them the giants and major employers of labour they used to be.
After the candidates had taken a few questions from members of the congregation, Catholic Bishop of Enugu Dr Anthony Gbuji who sat patiently throughout the session capped it with a homily on the need for political leaders in the country to seek the face of the Lord as they go about their duties in the service of the people. Reminding them that an elected person owed the electorate a solemn duty to fulfill his promises, he said it was only by seeking the wisdom of God and striving to do his will that they can succeed.
Noting that only one of the candidates would emerge as winner from the lot, the Bishop urged the eventual losers to demonstrate their commitment to improving the welfare of the people by rallying round the winner and supporting his efforts to improve the lot of the people.
He also prayed that the winner would be wise enough to involve the losers in his administration and offer them the opportunity to implement their visions for the people in capacities other than that of the Governor.
Despite the shortcomings, most people in the state view the exercise as having been worthwhile as the church at least, managed to humble the would be Governors before the electorate by compelling them to speak and take random questions from the people on a territory that was at once neutral and sacrosanct.

Original_Search_Title: Dar Licenses Independent Power Producer to Serve Lindi And Mtwara
Headline: Tanzania: Dar Licenses Independent Power Producer to Serve Lindi And Mtwara
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Tanzania has licensed Artumas Group and Partners Power Company Ltd to generate electricity to be supplied to Lindi and Mtwara, two of the country's least developed regions.
The company - involved in the exploration of gas - has been granted a provisional electricity generating licence to operate an 18MW power plant.
According to the director general of the Energy and Water Utilities Regulatory Authority (Ewura), Haruna Masebu, the Mnazi Bay gas to electricity project will generate sufficient power to supply small and medium industries in Lindi and Mtwara regions.
"The Licensing of Artumas is a great relief to power consumers as well as a blessing to the Tanzania Electricity Supply Company (Tanesco), which used to operate expensive diesel generating units in the area," said Mr Masebu.
The ferrying of fuel for the generators by Tanesco was also expensive because of the poor road network in the two regions.
He said Artumas will apply for a permanent licence as soon as it completes its commissioning tests and before Ewura rules on licensing and other incidental matters are out and operational.
This was the first such licence to be issued by Ewura. Under the Interim Power Purchase Agreement, Mtwara Transmission and Distribution Company will enter into a contract with a franchise to use Tanesco infrastructure.
By December 2006 the number of power consumers in Mtwara stood at 11,217, while that in Lindi was 6,436 however, there is potential for growth because the population of Mtwara, going by the 2002 census stood at 1,128,523 while Lindi had 791,306 people.
The managing director of AG&P Power Ltd, Peter Gathercole, said that this was one of several licences Artumas will require to provide a long awaited energy solution to the two regions.
"With our partners, the Tanzania Petroleum Development Corporation (TPDC), we are confident that when the gas development and further electrical transmission and distribution licence applications are made and issued, the significant gas reserves within our concession can be shared with a much greater section of the Tanzanian population, outside the franchise area," said Mr Gathercole.
The gas at Mnazi Bay becomes the second facility to produce electricity from locally sourced gas.
The first was gas from Songo Songo Island, which is transported to Dar es Salaam via a pipeline to Ubungo Power Plant, where Songas generates power for sale to Tanesco.
Development and generation of reliable power in the coastal regions of Mtwara and Lindi, is expected to speed up economic development, especially value-addition production.
Mtwara region is the leading producer of cashewnuts in the country besides crops such as sorghum, millet and cassava. The region boasts a seaport, which connects it to the outside world.
Lindi shares Mtwara's economic activities.
The government said the two regions have been lagging behind for a long time and that calculated efforts will be made to bring their development at par with other regions.
Fishing - which is a crucial economic activity in these two coastal regions will receive a shot in the arm with the supply of reliable power.
The generation and supply of electricity is also expected to act as a mitigating factor in the rural-urban migration from the two regions, mostly to Dar es Salaam.

Original_Search_Title: Group Criticises Federal Governments' Cassava Policy
Headline: Nigeria: Group Criticises Federal Governments' Cassava Policy
Body_Text:
THE Association of Nigerian Cassava Starch Manufacturers (ANCASM) has criticized the Federal Government policy on cassava, saying the policy is being sabotaged through government's activities, reflected in what they described as conflicting policies.
"The policy of the government on cassava, laudable as it is, is being undermined by other counter measures by the same government and how some unscrupulous few exploit these conflicting policies of government to sabotage the Nigerian economy", chairman of the association, Mr. Dele Ogunlade said in Lagos.
While expressing displeasure over the development, Ogunlade said ANCASM was set to prove how the measures have turned the cassava policy into a nightmare and consequently, a threat to the economic lives of key players in the up-stream aspect of the cassava starch supply chain, who according to him, are farmers and processors. He observed that the presidential initiative on cassava has led to situation that would not benefit neither the farmers nor processors, rather, they are being confronted with problems.
He noted: There are serious problems in the sector, and they are threatening the success of the cassava revolution. This sector which has never found it easy doing business in the country is now under considerable stifling pressure.
The sector may buckle in soon, unless the government shows some understanding of the responsibilities it owes to us. On the ten per cent cassava in bread flour, he expressed fears over the success of the project on account of lack of necessary cassava flour processing plants.
He argues that although cassava is already being produced massively, the starch industry that would have mopped up the cassava is in a state of coma because of the influx of smuggled cornstarch.
Against this background, he said cassava farmers and starch processors were in distress. The association therefore, urged government to ban the importation of cornstarch and similar powder starch.

Original_Search_Title: Why Nigerians Should Accept White Farmers-Idachaba
Headline: Nigeria: Why Nigerians Should Accept White Farmers-Idachaba
Body_Text:
Professor Francis Idachaba, Vice Chancellor of the Kogi State University, Anyingba, is a renowned professor of agricultural economics. At a ceremony in Ayingba recently, Idachaba presented a three-volume book to the public titled 'Good Intentions are not Enough: Collected essays on government and Nigerian agriculture'. He spoke to Weekly Trust shortly after the ceremony on a number of issues in the agricultural sector.
How can government ensure that farmers actually benefit from agricultural policies instead of the middle men as happens in most cases?
I think the way to tackle the problem of unintended beneficiaries is to be more thorough in the design of policy and programme. And to ask at the beginning who are the likely unintended beneficiaries, why will the target beneficiaries not get the benefit and what can be done to ensure that intended beneficiaries actually get the benefit of policy so that we can begin to cut the wings of the intended beneficiaries because they are powerful, well-connected and are always waiting in the corridors of every government. They move from government to government. At the design stage, we need to sensitize the stakeholders, the civil societies and the media so that when policy of government is announced, the media should help us to identify who the unintended beneficiaries would be and how to block the loopholes. Otherwise they would always emerge.
Why did you give our book the title: Good Intentions are not Enough?
Good intention are not enough because government policies and programme have been based on eloquent statement of what they want to do for small farmers, and eloquent statement of what they want to do for consumers without taking note of the fact that unless you track implementation nothing can be achieved. I have an analytical framework that talks about policy variety the same way we talk about biological variety of seeds. When you are designing biological varieties of seeds you test the varieties to different ecological environment to see how robust your varieties are to environmental stress. If you extend this analytical framework to policy as in policy variety, then you design them and make them robust to environmental stress. The environmental stress here can include political stress, administrative stress, funding stress and stress from the emergency of unintended beneficiaries. The unintended beneficiary will always emerge and put the programme under stress. If the design is not robust enough, then they will tap away the benefit of policy.
How do you think you can use your professional experience to the benefit of local farmers around you?
We are coming up with the design of collaborative platform between our faculty of agriculture and Agricultural Development Programme (ADP) of the state. We have had a few discussions and we want to move the discussion to the next level. We are looking forward to the faculty of agriculture identifying some villages for agricultural extension and then in collaboration with the state ADP. We can then make our knowledge available to the villagers while working with the ADP. This will enable us translate what we are doing in the research laboratories in the faculty of agriculture to concrete things of value. We have our major crops in our ecological zone like palm trees, cassava, cashew, maize etc. The university will collaborate with the ADP and the villagers in selected villages which we intend to identify.
The government recently approved N50 billion loans to farmers. What is your advice to government and the farmers over this loan?
First on accessibility to loans. We have moved away from the small scale farmer with a quarter of a hectare trying to write a feasibility paper to get a bank loan to a situation where farmers are encouraged to form groups because the recovery rate from groups is much higher that the recovery rate from an individual farmer. That is the lesson of experience. You find that the recovery rate of Grameen Bank of Professor Muhammad Yunoos who won the Nobel Prize recently has been very good because they used group. There is this lady in Akure who is also organizing something similar to the Grameen Bank. She calls it Rural Credit Scheme based on our local contribution method. The more the farmers form groups the easier for an individual within the group to obtain loan from the bank and other agencies. On the N50 billion naira loan, I think the problem is the capacity to formulate bankable proposal. There is money sitting out there. What we need to do is develop the capacity to prepare proposals -that would be appealing to the bank to make them give out these monies. There are not shortages of bankable loans. The agricultural bank and commercial banks have been giving loans in the past. Once the banks can have proposal that are well written, they are willing to give loan. The issue is how to do you get groups of farmers who actually knows how to write proposals that will appeal to the banks.
How do you think upcoming lecturers in the faculty of agriculture can have access to research grants?
There are two or three things to say here. The first point is that the university has a responsibility under my administration to provide some money for research. I have already approved N5 million for agricultural research to be disbursed to the six faculties based on the research proposals that they write. So that they can be begin to execute research programmes from the funds they get from this allocation. The second point is this: It is an illusion to think that government through the university would provide all the money you need for research. In my 31 years at Ibadan, the number of times that I got research fund from the university couldn't have exceeded two times in 31 years. That is what we called senate research grant. The 99.9 research grant I got at Ibadan, was from my own entrepreneurial grant seeking activities. You have to formulate proposals. In those days from Ibadan, it took me three hours to get to Lagos to go and sit in front of the office of director of agriculture or a permanent secretary for one research proposal on rural infrastructure. It took me two years to get the funding. In between we had four different directors but I never gave up and eventually I got the fund. In those days in 1975, N9000 was a big money. I was able to publish articles in the Indian and Canadian Journals from that research grant. We will encourage them with the grant from the university. But we don't want them to be under the illusion that the university is going to provide the bulk of their research money. The bulk of their research money must come from their entrepreneurial activities. They must formulate proposal. They must go to ADP in Lokoja. They must visit directorate of rural development, the banks, NGOs and so on to get funding.
Some people believe that without agricultural revolution we cannot develop in Nigeria. What is your view on this?
You are quiet right. Unless we can transform our agriculture, all other things we do will amount to nothing. This because we eat and we are 140 million plus and we cannot continue to depend on food import. We will have to work to produce the food we eat. That means we have to work to produce the food we eat. That means we have to transform our agriculture from the present near subsistence level to commercial scientific agriculture. The Indians if you remember in the 1950s were plagued with famine and everybody was laughing at the Indians.
Norman Bullock, who won the Nobel Peace Prize, now breeds a short wheat variety in Mexico and they were taken to India and applied in different agric ecological zones and they planted the wheat. That was the origin of what is called the green revolution. That was why the man won the Nobel Prize. You can have a revolution without shedding blood. You can have a revolution by bringing about new variety of plant. What we need in this country is new variety, good policy and particularly rural infrastructure. I know the situation of road in the rural areas. Most of the roads can only be accessed by motorcycle. During harvest time and rainy season, because of the problem of transportation, you find a lot of food rotting away. We really need to transform our rural areas through infrastructural revolution.
What is your reaction to the influx of Zimbabwean farmers into Nigeria?
We can be sensitive about white farmers coming to farm in Nigeria. But that is because we are sensitive about issues of land. If we cast our mind back, we will realize that white people are here in our factories, services, breweries, food and beverages, companies, transport and oil and gas. People are not shouting that why are white people in oil and gas even though it is a strategic resource to Nigeria. The anxiety about white farmers is only because of our peculiar attachment to land. Otherwise, what is the distinction between white people being in farming and being in oil and gas and other sectors provided you don't alienate or throw away Nigerians from the land? If you open the economy, you cannot say that agriculture is excluded and white people cannot come in.
What is your take on genetically modified foods?
It is a Very Controversial Topic. It Does Hold a Lot of Promise. You Know the Controversy in Europe. America Has Wanted to Export Gm Foods to All Part of the World. Europe Has Been Cautious. We Also Need to Be Cautious. This is Because the Time is Short. We Are Not Aware of All the Consequences of Gm Foods Yet. But I Don't Want US to Get Over Sentimental And Take an Ideological Position That Gm Crops Are of No Use. We Should Take Each Case On Its Own. for Example Cotton, Tomatoes, Onions Etc. If You Go to the Usa Today, You See Some Onions As Big As Football. This is the Essence of the Gm Foods High Yield And Easy Way to Become Self-Sufficient. We Should Take Each for Its Individual Merit. for Example Banana Which is an Area That Has Generated a Lot of Research in the Gm Foods We Should Find Out the Experience And the Impact to the Environment Based On Research in Other Part of the World. We Should Not Rule Out Gm Foods Entirely.

Original_Search_Title: IRAD Prepares Farmers For Improved Potatoes, Maize Seeds
Headline: Cameroon: IRAD Prepares Farmers For Improved Potatoes, Maize Seeds
Body_Text:
A training workshop was organised in Bambui to that effect.
Maize and Irish potato production in the North West will soon improve with the Research Institute for Agricultural Development (IRAD) to thank for building the capacities of farmers. The organisation credited as a viable partner in rural development and a frontline actor is the development of Cameroon's agriculture was at the centre of a workshop in its Bambui station on March 6th 2007. In effect, some 14 elite farmers assembled from all the seven divisions were drilled on how to ensure a bomber harvest this time around with improved seeds.
Participants later took home lessons on maize seed production; Irish potatoes seed production, cultural techniques in maize and Irish potatoes, crop protection, maize and Irish potato post harvest etc. Setting the tone for the workshop, Dr. Chris Wanzie, IRAD Bambui station boss stressed the importance of producing foundation seeds for seed multipliers to in turn serve farmers. According to him, a good harvest is the result of good seeds. Against this backdrop,Dr. Wanzie said IRAD is committed to serve North West farmers with the best to ensure a bomber harvest.
The workshop for trainers of trainees was also a rare moment to improve working relations between farmers and the Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Development with IRAD as the liaison. Meanwhile, Dr Ngoko Zachee, Plant Pathologist and provincial service Head for seed production said the workshop offered an opportunity to update the knowledge of elite farmers on maize and potato seed production. He stated the commitment of the government to improve production with HIPC funds made available in 2006 to ease the production of eight tons of Irish potatoes, 1200 seedlings of coffee, one hectare of cassava cuttings ready for farmers and 600 kgs of foundation maize seeds.
In all, it also emerged that the workshop is a way forward towards poverty alleviation while farmers had the opportunity to update their knowledge on maize and potato seed production. The farmers also had the opportunity to communion with Researchers on the field. Resource persons included Dr. Kenga Richard, Njualem Dominic, Bame Irene, Delphine Mapiafell, Dr. Ngoko Zachee, Dr. Tchamo Pierre and Tangi Davie

Original_Search_Title: Elders Move to Reconcile Clark, Uduaghan
Headline: Nigeria: Elders Move to Reconcile Clark, Uduaghan
Body_Text:
Warri — SOME Ijaw leaders in Gbaramatu in Delta State and the Uduaghan Reconciliatory Movement (URC) have launched separate moves to reconcile the Ijaw leader, Chief Edwin Clark and his group with the PDP gubernatorial candidate in the state, Dr. Emmanuel Uduaghan before the gubernatorial election, next month.
Founder of the URC, Mr. Stanley Jakpa and the president, Mr. George Ogaga told Vanguard in an interview that the group had concluded plans to visit Chief Clark at his country home, Kiagbodo in Burutu Local Government Area of Delta State.
Chief Clark was said to be in a meeting when Vanguard called to get his comments on the trouble-shooting initiatives but a source close to him said: "It is not a batle of Chief Clark alone, there are other leaders from the other senatorial districts and he (Clark) alone cannot sit down to hold any peace meeting on Uduaghan with anybody or group".
URC founder, Mr. Jakpa, however, said the group recognised that the reconcilition is not with Clark alone but with all aggrieved persons.
According to him: "It is not only Chief Clark, there are other leaders like Senator Fred Brume, Chief Benjamin Okumagba, Chief Patrick Bolokor and Chief Newton Jibunoh who are aggrieved, we are going to meet them to reconcile them with Uduaghan".
Ijaw leader from Okerenkoko, Prince Clement Bebenimibo told Vanguard, yesterday, that Ijaw leaders of Gbaramatu have resolved to broker peace between Chief Clark and Dr. Uduaghan because there was no need for the South senatorial district, comprising the Ijaws, Isokos and Itsekiris to be disunited as one of their sons is the gubernatorial candidate of the strongest party in the state.
"Dr. Emmanuel Uduaghan is Chief Clark's son, our leader, Clark is not against Uduaghan as a person, and he is only against the system that brought him in. We don't want this matter to be continued to be discussed in the newspapers, so we are going to settle it", he said.
Bebenimibo asked those making provocative statements against Clark to stop the write-ups; as such statements incense the elder statesman, who took it to mean that they were abusing him for speaking his mind.
He said that Uduaghan hails from a riverine area as the Ijaws and "we know he is capable of transforming the riverside areas of the state", adding that people should stop carrying the propaganda that he was coming to execute an Itsekiri agenda, as he has shown over the years in government that he is peace maker and would rather use his position as governor to ensure enduring peace in the state.
Meanwhile, Dr. Uduaghan has promised to build a higher institution in Ndokwa land if voted into power as governor in next month's gubernatorial election. He stated this on Tuesday during his campaign tour to Utagbe-Ogbe and Aboh in Ndokwa West and Ndokwa East local government areas.
He also assured that the PDP administration under him would do more roads in the area, empower the women with micro-credit scheme and establish a cassava processing factory in Ndokwa.
At Aboh, he promised that in conjunction with the PDP government at the federal level, the PDP government in the state would ensure that the people enjoy electricity from the Okpai power plant, as well as drive pleasurably to the area on a good road.
Member of the House of Representative, representing the area, Mrs. Mercy Almona-Isei said, however, that the Agip Oil Company has recently awarded a contract for the extension of electricity to nine contiguous communities to the power plant.
Uduaghan was accompanied on the campaign tour to Ndokwa by Mrs. Nkoyo Ibori, wife of the governor, Chief James Ibori, his wife, Mrs. Roli Uduaghan, director general of the campaign, Dr. Ifeanyi Okowa, chairman of the campaign organization, Chief Ighoyota Amori, Secretary to the State Government, Mr. Ovie Omo-Agege and others.
Yesterday, he visited Oleh and Ozoro in Isoko South and North local government areas and today (Thursday), his campaign team would storm Orerokpe and Isiokolo in Okpe and Ethiope East local government areas.

Original_Search_Title: Heavy Storm Hits Mbarara
Headline: Uganda: Heavy Storm Hits Mbarara
Body_Text:
Kampala — HEAVY rains and strong winds destroyed several acres of banana plantations, uprooted trees and blew-off roof tops in Nyakayojo sub-county, Mbarara district on Sunday.
Teo Kataratambi, a banana farmer, said: "The rain lasted for about 30 minutes. It destroyed 15 acres of my banana plantations and eucalyptus trees."
He revealed that strong winds usually hit the area in March.
Kataratambi, who is also an area councillor and chairperson for Nyamiyaga matooke growers association, said about 150 farmers had been affected.
"We have been selling over 20 lorries of matooke weekly. Unfortunately, the disaster occurred when we had not paid school fees for our children. This is going to have a negative financial impact because matooke is our security."
He also foresaw a food shortage, arguing that they were selling bananas to buy other foodstuffs.
The Rwampara county MP, Charles Ngabirano and Nyakayojo LC3 chairperson, Jomo Mugabi, on Saturday visited the area to assess the extent of damage.
Ngabirano observed: "There will be a food shortage because people depend on bananas as a source of income. We need to inform the Government about this tragedy."
Recently, a hailstorm hit the district ravaging banana, cassava and potato gardens in Kashaari county.
Weather experts attribute the storms to a lot of heat and energy accumulated in the dry season that causes violent storms.

Original_Search_Title: Ssebunya Uses Banana Peelings to Make Charcoal
Headline: Uganda: Ssebunya Uses Banana Peelings to Make Charcoal
Body_Text:
Kampala — Proscovia Ssebunya was the second runner-up in the New Vision Woman Achiever's Award 2007. Carol Natukunda visited her at her home in Kyebando to see what she does to tackle the energy problem and conserve the environment ...
Things we deem impossible can, many times, be very easy to achieve. The news that you can make charcoal out of banana peelings made Proscovia Ssebunya laugh in disbelief.
"I thought it just couldn't be. Imagine peelings, mere peelings!" she recalls of her skepticism then.
That was way back in 1995 while working with Black Power Limited, an energy saving company based in Kasangati, on Gayaza Road.
"I had a colleague who had just returned from abroad on a study course on energy. He told us you could actually use coffee husks or banana peelings to make charcoal briquettes. At first it didn't make much sense, but when we did the experiment, I knew it was the way to go," she says.
Ssebunya then decided to adopt the new technology which she has used for about 12 years now. As she explains, the experiment is a lot more affordable for an average family.
"When you have your coffee husks or peelings, you carbonise them by burning them up in a limited air. You make sure that they do not turn into ash. Then you break them into smaller pieces and mix them up with clay or soil. But you have to balance; if you have a basin full of peelings, you mix it with a basin full of clay."
"After that, you use a binding material to mould them together. I normally use starch from cassava or some little water to bind and mould them together. You can use your hands to mould if you like. After molding, you put them under a shed for three days to dry. On the fourth day, they are ready to use as charcoal briquettes," Ssebunya explains.
She asserts that the process produces a hot, long-lasting, virtually smokeless fire. "I have found it a solution to firewood problems. The charcoal you make remains hot for a very long, long time, because soil takes a long time to extinguish."
But Ssebunya's achievement would, perhaps, not be helpful to society, if she were to keep it to herself. Since 1997 when she joined the Young Women's Christian Association (YWCA), she has had to train other women on how to adopt the same technology. Ssebunya boasts of training about 400 women in Kyebando, Katwe, Makindye, Kanyanya, Naguru, Kinawataka and the women who were members at YWCA itself. Todate, she arranges training in groups of 20.
And she has touched so many hearts in the process. "I think this is a calling for her. Not many people voluntarily teach you something without asking you for payment," says 30-year-old Grace a mother of five, who has benefited from Sebunya's training.
Another woman in Kyebando asked me: "You mean you have never had about her? Come and visit the whole of Kyebando central zone, and you will be surprised that every home is talking about her tactics. What I like about her training is that it has not only helped me to solves the fuel problem, but also to get rid of heaps of garbage from the peelings."
Ssebunya also recalls one woman, who never stops to thanking her every time they meet. "That woman came to my training when she was stressed. She was really needy. Her husband expected her to cook, yet firewood was not available," she says.
However, that was not all for this 40-year-old mother of five. By the end of 1997, Ssebunya decided to switch to stove making, something which she felt would give more meaning to her charcoal. "You have to make something accessible and appropriate. Otherwise people will shun it right away."
At her home in Kyebando, Ssebunya displays a charcoal stove which she has used for about 10 years. "It is older than my daughter," she laughs. The stove is made of clay inside, metal outside and has two 'doors'.
Her firewood or rocket stove, is made of metal outside, with an insulating material inside, which she says comprises 'vermiculite and cement.'
"The firewood you use for the rocket stove lasts for about five days. Because the insulating material gets heated up and becomes red hot. A person like my old mother only uses small branches to cook her meal; it is that easy," she explains.
Ssebunya feels stove-making should be a job for every woman, if the energy crisis is to be eliminated.
"Yes, we must eliminate the crisis. We do not have an alternative, because it is us or our daughter who collect firewood. We are the cooks and we know which stoves are good for us. We can't afford to leave it to the men. Maybe you want a stove that will enable you to cook while standing; the man won't know. We are capable of doing it ourselves," she stresses.
She is also optimistic that more women would be able to participate in other developmental activities once they have settled the energy problem in their homes.
Ssebunya sells a small charcoal stove at sh18,000, while the medium and the small one goes for sh20,000 and sh22,000 respectively.
"I am a ceramist by profession from Kyambogo University, " she says.
"When I had just started making charcoal briquettes, I would return home very dirty. My son said, 'Mummy, you shouldn't stop us from playing in the mud again! You are doing the same'," she recalls fondly. "But I am glad they now understand, that this is what has made me what I am today," Ssebunya says.
Ssebunya works as the energy officer with the Integrated Rural Development Initiatives, an energy project based in Nsangi, Mpigi district.
Does she ever have time to rest?
"Rarely," she admits, "but I encourage the women I have taught to go and teach others. We must eliminate the energy crisis," she concludes.

Original_Search_Title: Noczim Set to Buy Over 10 000t of Jatropha Seed
Headline: Zimbabwe: Noczim Set to Buy Over 10 000t of Jatropha Seed
Body_Text:
Harare — THE National Oil Company of Zimbabwe expects to buy between 10 000 and 15 000 tonnes of jatropha seed from farmers.
In an interview, Noczim acting director of technical support services Engineer Innocent Masunungure said the State enterprise had already started collecting the seed from various parts of the country where the tree is grown in abundance.
"We have conducted a survey and within the next three months we are expecting to collect between 10 000 and 15 000 tonnes of seed," Mr Masunungure said.
He said jatropha seed was being bought from farmers at $60 000 per tonne and urged them to continue collecting the seed.
Areas that Noczim, the sole buyer of the seed, has started visiting include Mutoko, Guruve, Chivi, and Uzumba Maramba Pfungwe. Government, through the Ministry of Science and Technology, is in the process of setting up plants in UMP, Mudzi and Mutoko, where the seed would be processed into bio-diesel oil.
Jatropha is a drought-tolerant plant producing seed with an oil content of between 30 and 40 percent. The oil has great potential for making bio-diesel while the by-product could be used as organic fertilizer.
Jatropha is also a high value crop and Government has since specified the tree, banned its export, and adopted the tree along with cassava as major development crops for the next five years.
Cassava is a high-energy plant rich in starch and ethanol. There are also measures to raise production of sugar cane, castor bean and soya bean, among others, as crops which could be used to develop bio-diesel.
The Government has stepped up efforts to increase jatropha farming and has identified over 122 000 hectares to be put under the plant.
By 2010, the Government is targeting to have substituted 10 percent of the country's annual diesel consumption with bio-diesel.
Many countries in the world, including France, Germany, South Africa and Egypt, are already powering motor vehicles with bio-diesel, a cheaper and more environmental friendly alternative to fossil fuel.

Original_Search_Title: Pallisa Farmers Get Business Centre
Headline: Uganda: Pallisa Farmers Get Business Centre
Body_Text:
Pallisa — FARMERS in Pallisa district have acquired a one stop centre aimed at empowering rural communities to provide and maintain their own services on a commercially viable basis.
Pallisa Agri-Business Training Association (PATA), which is located in Kadama sub-county, was formed under the Sasakawa Global 2000 One Stop Centre initiative.
The Eastern Private Sector Development Centre (EPSEDEC), Sasakawa Global 2000 and Uganda Women Girl Child Concern (UWOGICO) joined efforts to help the farmers come up with the initiative.
Speaking to Daily Monitor on Thursday, EPSEDEC Chief Executive Officer Nelson Kyagera said the centre was inaugurated in the last week of February. "The mission of this one stop centre will improve people's lives through capacity building and increased farm productivity. There will also be promotion of commercial farming, improved farm technology and food security," he said.
Mr Kyagera said with support from UNDP, EPSEDEC has been offering requisite skills to farmers. The farmers were trained in group formation and management, strategic planning, financial records management, savings and credit and enterprise selection.
PATA brings together 885 smallholder farmers the majority of them women (523), while the men are 362. These are drawn from farmer groups in eight sub-counties.
According to Mr Kyagera, since the formation of PATA, the farmers are now engaged in selected agricultural enterprises such as bee keeping, cassava and sorghum production, processing and packaging.
"The activities have helped improve household income," he said. adding that a micro credit facility is also part of the venture.

Original_Search_Title: Hailstorm, Landslides Hit Sironko
Headline: Uganda: Hailstorm, Landslides Hit Sironko
Body_Text:
Sironko — An unusual rainfall pattern accompanied by a heavy hailstorm has ravaged coffee plantations, banana, cassava, cabbage and potato gardens in Sironko district causing fear of famine among residents.
The heavy torrential rains also caused a landslide at Namugabwe in Bukahengere that left two houses with property worth millions of shillings belonging to two families, completely buried.
Fenekasi Waduwa and Fabian Wangadya's houses with over two acres of banana and coffee plantations were completely covered by the land slide in Bunamoli village.
In Buteza, Busulani, Buyobo and Zesui sub-counties, churches, mosques, schools and people's houses lost roofs, leaving over 30 families homeless.
Several community roads were blocked as the culverts were swept away. When Daily Monitor visited the scene of the incident on Thursday, the villages were still covered by ice.
Residents of Bunamoli, Bunambozo, Nakungu and Bumukone villages in Buteza sub-county who looked shocked, said the storm started about 1 pm and lasted for three hours up to 4.30pm on Wednesday.
Business in several areas came to a standstill as residents scampered for their dear lives while others ventured out to save their property. "For fear of landslides, many of us could not stay inside our houses and yet we could not dare the heavy torrential rains accompanied by hailstorms," said Silver Wangadya, one of the residents.
Whereas many residents regarded this as a new occurrence in Sironko, elders said it was a usual occurrence. According to the district LC5 councillor for Buteza sub-county Stephen Mafabi, plantations belonging to about 150 families were destroyed.
Sironko LC5 Chairman Wambi Kibale said the district is still compiling the magnititude of damage caused by both the landslide and the hailstorm to the sub-counties of Zesui, Buteza and Busulani.

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo - More Can Be Done Before May 29
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo - More Can Be Done Before May 29
Body_Text:
Lagos — Fct
President Olusegun Oba-sanjo has said his administration can still do a lot in the three months left for a hand over on May 29, this year.
President Obasanjo told a delegation from Benue State led by Governor George Akume during a thank you visit, following his recent two-day visit to the state, that whatever the administration could not accomplish would be handed over to the incoming administration.
"We still can do something even in this injury time. We can do what we can and leave the rest to the incoming administration," he said, pointing out that governance was a continuous process.
The president said that he was impressed by the pilot cassava processing plant built through a collaborative effort between the Benue government and the government of the Netherlands.
He said the Federal Government had requested the Netherlands to build more of such plants in the country.
The president said the Federal Government would collaborate with the Benue State Government to assist the large number of ex-servicemen in the state to go into farming and suggested that
a large piece of land could be cleared for distribution to them.
He said government had projected about N75 billion for the settlement of pensions of ex-service personnel and other civil servants.
The president, however, expressed regret that the problem of proper documentation had continued to hamper the smooth payment of those entitlements.
President Obasanjo said his administration would look into the state government's suggestion to dam the River Katsina Ala from which about 750 MW of electricity could be generated and the recurring incidents of floods downstream checked.
He said Benue State would benefit from at least five North-South roads as part of Federal Government's national inter-model transportation programme, which, he added, would cover roads,
inland waterways, air transportation and railways.
Earlier, Akume had thanked the president for the visit and requested that the Makurdi-Lafiya road be dualised.
The governor commended the move by the Federal Government to construct five major bridges to open up the state and an inland water port in Makurdi, among others.

Original_Search_Title: Primary Teachers - Worth of Glory And Honour
Headline: Rwanda: Primary Teachers - Worth of Glory And Honour
Body_Text:
Kigali — If there are professions that deserve constant praise in our societies, then they are teachers. Primary school teachers in particular, play a pivotal role in shaping the young into reasonable citizens of tomorrow. I, being a former village primary teacher, have got experiences to share in addition some village primary school teachers in Rwanda that I talked to. Their stories are both interesting and awesome.
I started teaching in a primary school on a voluntary basis. I asked the headmaster (in a Umutara-based primary school) to let me volunteer to which he gladly nodded. We used to wake up in a chilly morning, take a cold shower in a makeshift bathroom made of banana fibres, pick our books and off we leave for the school.
As a new entrant in the teaching arena, there are a few things that took me time to get accustomed to; keeping on an empty stomach to 1.00pm, rushing back to our small house made of grass and mud only to eat cassava mixed with some crushed ground nuts and take a big mug of water and identifying students so congested in a class. People always used to give those stares of surprise I was the only physically endowed man in terms of seize.
The place, however, was not a total bore because there are so many humourous and awesome stories which teachers, students and the villagers could relate.
Here is a story that would make you feel like visiting such areas under any circumstances. I was having a chat with one teacher Eric (not the real name), and when I asked him what it feels like being a village Primary teacher, he had this to say:
"You know am actually a young man of 25 years of age and at first I took myself as an important man in the village. But the misconception is slowly dying down. When I started teaching, I could be invited to represent my school in government meetings and felt real big for that. The authority of ordering pupils to do what I commanded made me feel equally great. You know when you are young and you are given the whole week to command the school as a duty master, you feel so big. Another interesting thing for a young teacher is to see himself determining promotions, repeating, suspension and expulsion of pupils in a school. As you mark the papers you actually think that all is in your hands and this gives a kind of superiority complex that most primary teachers enjoy. And I used to get one or two free bottles of beer at least three times in a week from parents. All these coupled with the fact that when one stays for a given job for long tends to love it, makes me what I am today.
"But, for sure, I am getting to terms with life facts. I wasted a lot of time teaching in a primary school. It's high time I thought of continuing with my studies or resigning for a petty business. I did not know that some people earn millions every month. I thought that they are all like me! Your environment can enslave you for ever. I am now 25 and I do not know if I will ever get money, marry, build a house, drive a car and all those that makes life meaningful."
He concludes as he sweeps his last drop in the glass in a rather thirsty manner. I had to order one more to keep him in the mood.
If you are to make an analysis of the tale, do it in any way you want.
Having got a young male part of the story that was overwhelmingly interesting, I decided to go to the female young teacher for the other part of the story. It was not so easy to get a female teacher for a rendezvous (audience). There were few obvious cultural reasons; one, young female primary teachers either stay in their parents' homes or share one house. They not visit bars. When they drink, they do it indoors for it is against the Rwandan culture for a young female to drink beer in an open place. Two, if you proposed to meet a girl when she is alone, then you may stand to be misunderstood. So it took me more energy than anticipated but finally I convinced one of them to come and visit me and my fellow teachers in our house. She came one Saturday afternoon.
Mary however, gave me her part of the story concerning how she saw herself as a primary teacher in a village:
"I completed 'O' and 'A' levels. So now I teach in a Primary school. As to how I feel about the job, it is fine with me. I wake up early in the morning go teaching. At the end of the month we are paid our salaries that are of course not enough to make our ends meet. But we keep going. You see when you are a teacher, you do not expect much in terms of salary. The job, to many of us, is actually a transition to another profession.
"To be sincere most of us, girls, you see here teach, are waiting to get married to some men who earn a reasonable income for a home. And we feel we can get better jobs in future. Teaching cannot be the end of life because it will not allow you enjoy life. When we go to Kigali, we see how our age-mates dress and we envy them. It tells us much about what we miss in life. Otherwise, our parents and relatives keep on supplementing on what we earn as we search for better means."
She concludes shyly as she mentions that parents and relatives have a hand in getting them potential husbands (suits).
What implications, then, does Mary's story reflect? The implications may be far reaching depending on the interpreters or the analysts. Nevertheless, female teachers seem to be more comfortable than male teachers in village Primary schools. This is evidenced from the latitude of hope from the two parties. Male teachers seem to be more desperate than female teachers. Female teachers can be supported by their parents when working and have hopes of getting married to well to-do families. This partly explains the difference.

Original_Search_Title: Inflation Eases in Feb
Headline: Uganda: Inflation Eases in Feb
Body_Text:
Kampala — UNDERLYING inflation, which measures price changes excluding food crops, has maintained high spirals; registering an 8.3 per cent rate.
According to the Uganda Bureau of Statistics (UBOS), underlying inflation was recorded at 6 per cent in February compared to 8.9 per cent registered the previous month.
UBOS attributed the rates to increased prices of eggs, which went up in Kampala by 10 per cent and that went up during the month in most centres.
Addressing a press conference at Ministry of Finance, Planning and Economic Development headquarters on Wednesday, the Principal Statistician Price Consumer Index at UBOS Vicente Musoke Nsubuga said the high cost of rearing chickens in the country had led to increase in prices of eggs.
"Secondly, there is high pressure on Ugandan eggs from southern Sudan market where there is a high demand leading reduced supply of eggs for the domestic market," he said.
However, Mr Nsubuga expressed optimism that although there is high inflationary pressure in the country, the economy is steadily recovering and growing.
On the other hand, the annual headline inflation rate for the year ending February 2007 dropped to 6.6 per cent from 8.9 per cent that was recorded for the year ended January 2007.
Mr Nsubuga said the decrease in the annual headline inflation rate was a result of a continued drop in prices of a number of food items (staple and fruits).
He also attributed it to reductions in prices of sugar in all centres (down to an average of Shs1,900 in February compared to Shs2,100 in January) combined to bring down the inflation rate.
Food prices during the month went down by 3.5 per cent. Mr Nsubuga said most centres registered reductions in prices of food items, especially matoke, sweet potatoes, fresh cassava, passion fruits and pineapples due to increased supplies.
However, prices of most vegetables and some cereals went up in most centres due to reduced supply.
Mr Nsubuga said the high increase in demand by educationals institutions for cereals especially maize, rice and beans coupled with exports to Southern Sudan had contributed to pushing up the prices of these commodities.

Original_Search_Title: Bread - Cassava Policy to Generate 6m Jobs
Headline: Nigeria: Bread - Cassava Policy to Generate 6m Jobs
Body_Text:
Abuja — Fct
The policy of the Federal Government requiring flour millers to include 10 per cent of cassava, described as the "ultimate future crop" is expected to generate six million jobs from up and downstreams and 374 small and medium enterprises.
This was disclosed yesterday by the Special Assistant to the President on Food Security, Mrs. M.O. Adetunji, while presenting a position paper at a stakeholders' forum on the production of high grade cassava flour for bread and other confectioneries and investment in cassava processing plants.
She said the 374 SME's that will be created, going by the demand of the flour mills, will each employ 28 people while millions of jobs would be created in the upstream sector among the farmers.
"Opportunities will also be opened to marketers and distributors of the products," she said, "while input dealers and fabricators will have regular demand."
She said the initiative was in line with the economic status of cassava in improved economies of the world such as Brazil, Thailand, Europe, where the crop has assumed the centre stage in trying to ensure food security for the nation.
"A country like Brazil has long been taking 100 per cent cassava bread and has gone a step further to diversify the use of PMS (premium motor spirit) the (the) form of ethanol to drive their vehicles, which can be sourced from cassava," the special assistant told participants who included bakers and flour millers.
The Director-General of Raw Materials Research and Development Council, Prof. A.P. Onwualu, suggested that to achieve the government's plan of making cassava a source of foreign exchange earning as well as a resource for local industries, it was necessary to develop the domestic market, diversify the use of the product in industries, among others.

Original_Search_Title: Peasants of Belize Improve Cultivation Work
Headline: Angola: Peasants of Belize Improve Cultivation Work
Body_Text:
Luanda — The harvest of the last campaign in the Municipality of Belize, in the northern Cabinda Province, increased significantly due to a programme carried out by the farming authorities, which changed the practice of subsistence agriculture for a more ample and qualitative farming production.
The information was given by the assistant administrator of Belize Municipality, José Manuel Grilo, when speaking to the press about the socio-economic situation of his locality.
He explained that the programme includes three projects, one of agrarian foment conceived by the Agriculture Provincial Board, another one by the oil company Chevron/Texaco and the third one which is for the ex-military of the former rebel separatist group FLEC, integrated in a process that preceded the recently signed Memorandum of Understanding for Peace in Cabinda.
According to José Manuel Grilo, the authorities have stimulated the creation of test fields for the cultivation of cassava and beans, whose results were positive.
The harvest was enough for the consumption of the peasants, for the storage of seeds and also some was used for commerce.

Original_Search_Title: Dust Over Obasanjo's Benue Visit
Headline: Nigeria: Dust Over Obasanjo's Benue Visit
Body_Text:
Makurdi — Recently, President Olusegun Obasanjo paid a two-day visit to Benue State. During the visit, he attended the launching of the Musa Yar' Adua presidential campaign and commissioned some projects executed by the George Akume administration.
From 1999 to 2006, there were many reasons given as to why Obasanjo had visited the state twice for the purpose of seeking the people's votes and not for the commissioning of projects.
One of the reasons was that Governor Akume had not embarked on meaningful projects for the president to commission while the second one was that the relationship between Akume and Obasanjo was strained because the former Minister of Environment, Dr Iorchia Ayu, who was said to be Akume's godfather was dictating the pace of the administration. It was also said that the relationship became more strained because Governor Akume stood against Obasanjo's third term agenda.
As the status-quo remained, Governor Akume continued with his administration until it dawned on him that his influence in the 2007 elections may be seriously undermined if he failed to mend fences with President Obasanjo.
Consequently, the governor reconciled with those opposed to his administration within the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) and started sending emissaries to the president on the need for the two of them to meet. Hon. Terngu Tsegba of the House of Representatives, former ALGON President, Mr Baver Dzeremo and a host of other powerful PDP members including General Geoffrey Ejiga were deeply involved in the reconciliation between Akume and Obasanjo.
Chief Barnabas Gemade who was opposed to Akume because of the role played by some of his younger ones, reached a truce with him after it was discovered that he (Gemade) could serve a useful purpose in the reconciliation process.
During the PDP gubernatorial primaries of December 2006, Benue became over charged due to the allegation that Governor Akume threw his weight behind Mr Gabriel Suswam, a member of the House of Representatives. Using his political prowess, Akume went into reconciliation with some of the aggrieved aspirants and pacified others.
With this situation, the move to lure Obasanjo into coming to Benue State started. The governor led a powerfull delegation to the presidency to tell the president that Benue was ready for him and that he was ready to part ways with Ayu. Indeed, he demonstrated this by removing some of Ayu's loyalists from the administration but whether he has finally parted ways with Ayu or not is a matter for another discussion.
The fact is that Benue had been a PDP state and has been upper most in President Obasanjo's mind. This coupled with the influence of the State Coordinator of the Yar' Adua Campaign, Comrade Abba Moro, made the President of the Federal Republic of Nigeria and Commander-in-Chief of the Armed Forces, Chief Olusegun Obasanjo, to approve the visit to Benue State.
Even as approval was given, the issue of security became a matter of priority. Programme for the visit especially actual time for arrival, therefore, shifted forward and backward in order that confirmation would be made as to whether Benue was safe enough for Obasanjo to land. The fear was over the inability of the administration to deliver dividends of democracy to the people.
Eventually, Obasanjo came to Benue State on February 22, 2007 and left the following day after attending the launching of the Yar' Adua campaign and commissioning a few projects. Many may ask: what is the significance of the president's recent visit to Benue State?. The answer lies in the peace that has returned between Benue and the federal government even as the people of the state had felt so bitter over the military invasion of Zaki-Biam and the privatization of the Benue Cement Company.
Another significance of the visit was that Governor Akume who was almost drawn to the Action Congress was able to detach himself and assert his independence.
Further more, the governor who was almost called a failure because Obasanjo did not visit the State to commission projects, has won back the people's confidence.
Apart from the Fertilizer Blending Plant, Cassava Processing Factory and the College of Health Sciences commissioned by President Obasanjo during his visit, Akume has embarked on other projects like the Fruit-juice factory, Igumale Cement Factory, Wannune Tomatoes Processing factory and BENKIMS Plastic Industry.
Obasanjo's recent visit indicated that he was still a friend of Benue but he has to work hard to influence Yar' Adua(if he wins at the polls) to favour Benue so that what was lost could be regained. This is because his promise during his recent visit that he would use three months to put Benue on the 330 KV national grid, construct a bridge across River Benue at Oweto in Agatu local government and implement the Greater Makurdi Water Works project, looks elusive.
The visit strengthened Governor Akume's political leadership of the state which became shaky in the wake of the undue pressure exerted on his administration by a few members of the PDP.
The visit may also have brightened the governor's senatorial ambition which was threatened by the division between him and Ayu and the Gwer local government crisis which erupted when the PDP House of Assembly candidate declared at the primaries was replaced by another one.

Original_Search_Title: Nakasongola Asks for 'Bonna Bagaggawale'
Headline: Uganda: Nakasongola Asks for 'Bonna Bagaggawale'
Body_Text:
Kampala — NAKASONGOLA district officials have asked the Government to begin the implementation of the Bonna Bagaggawale (prosperity for all) programme with their district.
"There is no doubt that the programme will be successful, if we are given chance," Fredrick Bandumba the district vice-chairman, told micro finance state minister Gen. Salim Saleh, recently.
Saleh was in the district to launch a cassava processing plant owned by Nakasongola District Farmers Association (NADIFA) at Wabigalo sub-county. The association has 40,000 members.
"All the sub-counties have organised themselves and we are anxiously waiting for the funds," Bandumba said.
He said the district had a number of investment ventures including cattle rearing and cassava growing.
Saleh commended the association, saying: "NADIFA has fought food insecurity and they are potential candidates for the Bonna Bagaggawale programme."
He, however, informed residents that whereas prosperity for all is intended to address all the structural constraints faced in production, the communities also need to do something for themselves.
Saleh said the Government wants to ensure that people can access loans at affordable interest rates. However, he showed concern that 17 districts in the country do not have any financial institution operating in their area.

Original_Search_Title: RDF Donates Cassava Stems
Headline: Rwanda: RDF Donates Cassava Stems
Body_Text:
Kigali — Southern Province: Ruhango - The 47th Battalion of the Rwanda Defence Forces (RDF) which operates in Ruhango District has donated cassava stems to residents of Kinihira Sector in the district. Christine Uwamahoro, the sector Executive Secretary, received the donation from Maj. Jomba Gakumba, the battalion commander on Thursday.
She told The New Times: "A full Isuzu truck of the (cassava) stems which can be planted on a hectare has been given to residents and this is helpful in promoting food security."
She said that the cassava mosaic virus had destroyed a lot of cassava stems in the area, adding that the grant will help save the situation.
Maj. Gakumba also said that the military is pro-active in assisting residents in receiving resistant stems to eliminate hunger from local communities. He revealed that similar assistance was recently extended to residents in Ruhango Sector.

Original_Search_Title: Apilo Uses Waste Paper to Make Charcoal Balls
Headline: Uganda: Apilo Uses Waste Paper to Make Charcoal Balls
Body_Text:
Kampala — IT was becoming increasingly difficult for the women of Mbale to fulfil their natural role of putting food on the table for their families. It was almost impossible for a family to have the normal three meals a day.
Children were going hungry, adults were starving, not because they lacked food, but because there was no firewood, and charcoal was too expensive.
It was then that Judith Apilo founded an organisation, the Uganda Gender Rights Foundation (UGRF) in Namatala slum, to equip other women with energy conservation skills.
UGRF, a local NGO, started its programmes in 2004 after the Government put restrictions on forest encroachment in the district. "Some local people from Namatala kept coming to us for financial assistance. They wanted money to buy firewood to cook for their families as the forests had been gazetted.
Namatala, the biggest slum in the district, has no trees. It is heavily populated and covered by shanty structures. There is no space for even a wood lot," says Judith Apilo, the UGRF founder and director.
"A friend from the US, Dr Joyce Lockard, working in Mbale, came up with an idea after she saw many people coming to my home for help. 'Why don't you start a charcoal briquette project to help the women? It will help many of them to meet their basic needs and make a living as well'," she proposed.
Apilo said their main setback was the acquisition of technical knowledge. "But luck was on my side. I recalled seeing a gentleman in industrial area carrying a metallic cylindrical kiln that burns wood chippings into charcoal.
The kiln partially burns six sacks of wood chippings into charcoal. The wood chippings are got free of charge from the carpentry workshops within Mbale. After the kiln has been filled, fire is lit underneath using waste paper. The kiln retains enough heat to burn the chippings to the required level (not to ashes). The fire is left burning the whole night. By morning, the chippings have cooled down. They are then mixed with cassava flour and water.
"We do not have to look for wood to burn the chippings. We use waste paper which we collected from anywhere, and this is free. The wood chippings are also free. We get them from carpentry workshops.The only thing we spend money on is the kiln, which can be used by made people and for a long time," explains Apilo.
"I looked for the man. He accepted to train the women. Lockard contributed some funds for our initial training and the purchase of the kiln and for sometime, contributed $200 every month to the project.
In November 2006, Apilo started the training project with only 10 trainees. The first trainees helped train other women and now, she has about 40 graduates, who pass over their newly acquired skills to 10 other women every month.
"We take on the disadvantaged and school drop-outs aged 15 to 60 years. Most of them come from Namatala. The training is voluntarily," Apilo explained.
"Because of lack of funds, none of those trained has taken on serious commercial briquette making," she adds.
The partially burnt charcoal chippings got from the kiln are about 90% (40kg) of the wood chippings. It is then mixed with about 10kg of cassava flour and about 40 to 60 litres of water to give 1,200 briquette balls, about four basins. Cassava is used because it contains a binding substance. A 100-kg sack of cassava flour costs sh38,000. Cow dung was also tested and proved to be good. But it is scarce in this part of the country. Anthill soil has also been found useful as a binding substance instead of cassava.
After attaining the right mixture, the women start molding briquette balls with their palms to the recommended size. The briquettes are then put out to dry.
But up to date all that has been made, has been used up by the members, though plans are under way to start selling some briquettes after they have carried out energy pricing.
Jennifer Namwanu, a resident of Sissye Cell, Namatala slum said she no longer sleeps on an empty stomach. "The project has shown me everything is possible. I can now mold my own briquettes using anthill soil instead of cassava. Once ignited, they take longer than the charcoal or wood to turn to ashes."
"I use six or 10 briquette balls to cook sauce, food and water. At times, I can re-use the balance for the next day, which is impossible with charcoal. I have just prepared my beans. The briquettes do not emit smoke," said Namwanu. "My neighbours have requested me to sell them some briquettes."
Joyce Kayinza, an elderly lady and the leader of the women said the project had opened their eyes. "When they slumped a ban on tree-cutting in the district forests, the price of charcoal rose to sh25,000 a sack. We could not afford this.
The charcoal used here comes from Lira, Apach and Busoga because there there are no trees here, no space to plant some. The project has been a blessing to us," she narrated.
"It is so helpful and we are planning to expand and train more women if funds allow," said Alfred Alianu, the UGRF training officer.
He said they were looking out for a development partner who could provide them with kilns. "The material to make briquettes is in plenty here. We have the wood chippings which would otherwise be thrown away, cassava is in plenty and we have anthill soil, but we do not have enough kilns. We are also looking out for funds to secure a charcoal crusher and briquette-pressing (molder) machine, instead of using hands."
"If we can get another kiln, things will be better. We shall forget all our worries. Many people go without food. If I can get about five kilns that can be spread out in different cells, there will be a lot of improvement.
The group is comprised of widows, orphans and people living with HIV/AIDS and other disadvantaged people from all tribes across Uganda.
UGRF training centre is located in Namatala, industrial division, Mbale Municipality. It was primarily established to address the needs of marginalised, disadvantaged, women and girls, who bear the brunt of scarcity of fuel for cooking and lighting in their homes.
The project also sensitises and educates women and families to combat domestic violence, empowerment, vocational tailoring skills and improve household incomes. It has a section of drama, vocational training, briquette making, poultry and organic agro-gardening projects.

Original_Search_Title: Hunger Looms in Mbarara After Storm
Headline: Uganda: Hunger Looms in Mbarara After Storm
Body_Text:
Kampala — OVER 2,000 people in Mbarara district are threatened with a shortage of food after a heavy hailstorm destroyed their gardens on Saturday.
The LC3 chairman for Bubaare sub-county, Robert Kanene, yesterday said over 200 acres of banana plantations were destroyed in Kashaari county.
"We estimate that about 400 people were affected, but the number might increase to 2,000."
He added that the storm also destroyed cassava and potato gardens in Kamushoko, Kashaka, Rwengyenyi and Nyamitooma parishes.
Kanene noted that residents of Kashaari, who were the most affected by the storm, depended on bananas as their major source of income.
He observed that the area produces over 65 lorries of bananas (matooke) monthly.
"Each lorry transports 250 to 350 bunches of matooke."
Kanene explained that LC1 chairpersons from the affected parishes had met the area Member of Parliament, Urban Tibamanya.
"We will send a report about the hailstorm to the Red Cross and the Ministry of Disaster Preparedness for assistance. We need to grow beans, cassava, potatoes and Irish potatoes."
The Kashaka parish LC1 chairman, Benon Mpamiza, said the daily matooke market at Kashaka trading centre had been affected by the hailstorm.
The hailstorm, which lasted for one hour, covered villages with ice stones that looked like snow.

Original_Search_Title: I've Restored Peace in Ekiti - Olurin
Headline: Nigeria: I've Restored Peace in Ekiti - Olurin
Body_Text:
Lagos — EKITI State administrator, Brig Gen. Tunji Olurin (rtd) weekend disclosed that his administration has succeeded in restoring peace to the state through economic empowerment of the people.
Gen. Olurin, who was appointed administrator of the state in the wake of political crisis, which engulfed Ekiti late last year revealed this in a chat with media executives.
Describing the Ekiti crisis as not only politically but economically motivated, Olurin, a former Economic Community of West African States Monitoring Group (ECOMOG) commander, said the first step he took on assumption of office was to address the economic frustration of the people.
This, he explained was achieved mainly through empowering indigenes of the state who hitherto depended on few available government jobs, to go into self-employment.
He said on realizing that the state lacked big investments and corporations that could offer job opportunities for the teeming population, his administration embarked on resuscitation of all agricultural ventures through the National Poverty Eradication Programme (NAPEP).
"We decided that the best thing was to tackle head on the poverty situation and do anything we could to bring succour to the people and that is why we have decided to resuscitate all the agricultural ventures through NAPEP so that farmers could get money to borrow to go out to the field to practise their profession."
Gen. Olurin also disclosed that as part of measures to support the farmers, his administration has put up a cassava plant to ensure farm produce could be exploited maximally for the people's benefit.
"We decided to set up three plants in three senatorial districts for cassava products like cassava flour, gari and starch. And we are doing this project to prove to other people that even if they form unions they can do the same thing by going for one of the agric loans to borrow money as a union to set up such plant anywhere in the state," he said.
The Ekiti administrator also explained that apart from the agricultural ventures, he has set up an economic council, which brought people of varied experiences to attract investors to the state.
"We must get investors into Ekiti State to see what we have as raw materials, to see what they can manufacture, and the multiplying effect of that will help to spring up the economy in the state," he said.
Gen. Olurin also pointed out that his administration has in the area of electricity generation brought in 33KVA from Ilesa to ease power supply in the city just as a 132KVA transmission line project is being pursued so that the state would have stable power supply.
On the political scene, he said that his neutrality in the political affairs of Ekiti State has offered all the political parties a level playing ground unlike what obtained in the past.
"I had to use my peacekeeping experience to make sure that I reassure the people of my neutrality and tell them that I am there to create an enabling environment for everyone to be able to operate according to the law without fear or favour and without intimidation," he said.
He said he is on the side of law in the recent dissolution of local government councils in Ekiti State.
Gen. Olurin further revealed that his action was motivated by credible security reports that the sacked chairmen and councillors were bent on fomenting trouble and causing a breach of the peace.
In addition, he said the councils still believed that it should be business as usual in terms of not accounting for tax payers' money.
Gen. Olurin said he regards his current assignment in Ekiti State as sacred and so would not allow himself to be distracted by pockets of trouble makers.

Original_Search_Title: Hailstorm Destroys Crops in Mbarara
Headline: Uganda: Hailstorm Destroys Crops in Mbarara
Body_Text:
Kampala — MBARARA looked like a European winter landscape last Saturday. An unusually heavy hailstorm ravaged banana plantations, cassava and potato gardens in Kashaka, Kashaari county, causing fear of food shortages.
Trading centres, forests and hillsides were covered for hours with carpets of ice stones that looked like snow.
Surprised residents said the storm started at about 1:30pm and lasted for one hour. Businesses in several areas were brought to a standstill, while some ventured out to feel the ice.
"I could not leave my house for fear of being hit by hailstones," said Kashaka parish LC3 councillor Emmanuel Mwijukye. "Yet I also feared staying in the house in case it collapsed." He appealed to the Government to assist the affected villages with food aid and said he was compiling a list of needy families.
"I never experienced this before. I thought the world had come to an end," commented another resident, Kafuruka Bukenya. At Kashaka Girls SS in Mbarara, the roof of one of the teachers' houses was blown off while the school's banana plantation was destroyed. The students said their compounds and dormitories were covered with ice. "We should go home. It is too cold here," said Agatha Karungi, a Senior Six student.
The LC1 chairman of Kashaka trading centre, Benon Mpamiza, said banana plantations belonging to 60 families were affected by the hail storm, while some residents had goats and hens killed.
"We are appealing to the Government to provide us with emergency foodstuff like posho. Alternatively, we might have to plant quick maturing crops like beans within the destroyed plantations," Mpamiza added.
For hours vehicles on the Mbarara-Kasese highway parked by the road due to poor visibility and fear of slippery roads. Some places like Kyatamba, Rwengyenyi and Nyamitoma had fields covered with thick layers of ice.
Though hail storms are rare in the tropics, they can occur occasionally in the dry season, according to the Meteorology Department.
"In the dry season, you can have a lot of heat and accumulated energy which leads to violent thunder storms, accompanied by hailstorms," explained meteorologist Aloysius Kagoro. Asked whether it was a result of global warming, Kagoro said it was going to be discussed in a climate change meeting in Nairobi.
Hail is produced inside storm clouds and is made of solid ice. Hailstones can grow to dangerous sizes. Some can be as big as tennis balls or even a grapefruit. The largest can weigh a little under 1kg and fall at more than 50 metres per second.
Hail storms can devastate entire fields of crops in a few minutes, flattening and bruising the growing plants. They can also cause serious damage to vehicles and glass roofed structures. One of the heaviest hail storms occurred in Sydney, Australia, in 1999 when 20,000 properties, 40,000 vehicles and 25 aircrafts were damaged, causing loss of $1.6b.
More thunder storms and possible hailstorms are expected in the western, central and eastern part of the country, according to the weather forecast of the Meteorology Department, while the northern region will mainly be sunny and dry.

Original_Search_Title: For Baba, No More Perks
Headline: Nigeria: For Baba, No More Perks
Body_Text:
Kaduna — Last Wednesday, Nigerian senior citizens who fought gallantly to make Nigeria what it is today, out of frustration and rage, staged a big protest holding Abuja, the seat of power still for a couple of hours, leaving commuters to groan under a terrible traffic situation. Less than 24 hours to this incident, President Olusegun Obasanjo had handed in a Bill seeking many perks for himself after May 29. But does Obasanjo deserve many more perks?
Military pensioners on the Valentine's Day during rush-hour, blocked off the Abuja-Keffi Road, one of the major entrances to Nigeria's capital, stalling traffic. For about five hours, the world was at standstill for commuters caught up in the massive traffic jam. Abuja was completely cut off from the South Eastern and North Eastern parts. Many missed work hours just as scores could not keep their appointments.
Grouse of the senior citizens was prolonged non payment of their gratuities and pensions. That day, they preferred death as some of them had already died or were nearing death awaiting pay of their legitimate entitlements.
Scrooge wants more
Interestingly, a day to this demonstration, President Olusegun Obasanjo had sent to the National Assembly a Bill seeking a jumbo pay and many perks for himself less than 100 days to his exit as president. Obasanjo wants the National Assembly to pass a law that would allow him have a residential home built for him in any location of his choice in Nigeria at the expense of the country.
Similarly, if the Bill gets the nod of the National Assembly, Obasanjo would be paid 300 per cent furniture allowance and collect existing annual allowance and fringe benefits. He would get three cars with one serving as a pilot car. He wants himself to be guarded by three State Security Services (SSS) officials and two policemen.
What is funny about this Bill is that while he wants life-comfort for himself, many who come from the same military background as him are left at the mercy of the elements at the not-far-away Mogadishu Cantonment. Aso Rock and the Mogadishu Cantonment are all within Asokoro District.
Ex-servicemen...or just ex-men?
Many of the ex-servicemen who have been called from the 36 states of the federation to converge on Abuja have been staying in Abuja in appalling conditions. They sleep in the open around the Military Pensions Board in Wuse Zone 4 and the Mogadishu Cantonment for numerous weeks if not for months waiting when their pensions or gratuities will be paid. Those who have fallen ill are picked and dumped at the Mogadishu Cantonment Clinic with little or no medications. Those who run out of money resort to eating wild fruits or cassava from nearby farms. Due to overstay in Abuja, some of the retirees have received news of mishaps in their villages but it appears there is nothing they can do.
In Wuse Zone 4, the senior citizens used an uncompleted building as shelter but for the relentless demolition of unwanted structures, many of them are thrown in the open.
