And the ex-servicemen, we know they have their own wahala. Some do not have genuine claims for pensions but once they hear that invitation is issued out, they come. Quite often, you could see them squat by the side of the ever-busy Herbert Macaulay Road; just watch their down below, you would see them pass some dark or yellowish pulp, doing it with careless abandon.
A ghastly ghostly problem
But in an interactive session with the House of Representatives Committee on Defence last Tuesday, Finance Minister Nenadi Usman announced that verification of pensioners delayed payment. According her, 50,000 ghost names were on the list reiterating that "The number of pensioners was trimmed down from 320,000 to 270,000". She also told the House Committee that most of those who went wild that Wednesday were not yet on the list for current payment. Why do we have such a huge number of ghost pensioners? Why are those not on the list long informed that they were not on the list? Is it impossible to make the list of bonafide pensioners available in all the states of the federation? Clearly, a lot of things have officially gone wrong.
So on that Wednesday, senior citizens refused all entreaties to open the way until the Minister of Defence Thomas Aguiyi Ironsi begged them to. The minister exonerated himself from all blame. According to him, the trouble started when the Accountant General of the Federation asked the military pensioners to come to Abuja. That he was not consulted when that decision was taken.
One might ask, who gives the Accountant General the leverage to toy with the lives of our senior citizens? Does he think he could wake up one morning and simply issue directive that all retirees should travel long distances on road with all the inherent risks to Abuja just to get their pensions without adequate means to pay them promptly when they arrive? Don't we have data for those who retire from the force yearly in all the states? Don't we have military pensions' and finance ministry's offices nationwide? Would the Accountant General be happy if he were a pensioner and treated the same way? What really does it take to get pensioners audited that our senior citizens will be kept waiting in Abuja for weeks/months to get audited?
This is how daily we subject our fellow country men and women to hardships. Even when the president of Nigeria, nay president of PDP recently declared in Abeokuta that April polls are a do-or-die affair, one was not so cheesed off as this revelation by the Defence Minister that the Accountant General single-handedly ordered our aged retirees to come to Abuja.
Scams, scams and more scams
Is this how low rascality has taken our land to? Daily, crass rascality in high places kills our people in hospitals, on the highways/airways, in the creeks; even in our homes. Rascality has rendered Nigeria to senseless spending of our scarce resources under a president that does nothing but globe-trotting; doing almost everything at his whims.
Yesterday, it was National Identity Card scheme. Where is the scheme now? Majority of Nigerians cannot say how the so called National Identity Card looks like; huge money gone down the plughole!
That CNN headache
American journalists were invited to come and see and hear how a baba and a Turaki are locked in mutually hostile altercations. The Americans were reportedly disappointed when they went to Ajegunle (in Lagos) and came face-to-face with the poverty and squalor of Lagos slums; a country where leaders are falling over each other just to exploit the perks of the high office. And only last week, Obasanjo ordered the termination of his contract on the Heart of Africa Project with the Cable News Network (CNN). The decision was the fall out of the CNN's recent airing of a programme on the Niger Delta.
Part of the statement released by the Information and Communications Minister Frank Nweke (Jnr) penultimate Thursday reads: "the government of Nigeria considers it improper to continue to run a promotional material about the country in a medium that has consistently even unprofessionally depicted it and her people in a denigrating manner."
According to Nweke's Media Advisor Niyi Ibietan, the termination of the contract is to protest "CNN's unethical and subversive reportage of the Delta hostage-taking situation." If Jeff Koinange did something amiss in that his report, Obasanjo is guiltier. Obasanjo has been minister of petroleum for almost throughout his nearly eight-year administration, yet he has not effectively tackled the energy related crises in the country. He is worse than an albatross hung on Nigeria's neck.
A recent report attributed to Herman J. Cohen published in the International Herald Tribune says "Obasanjo's role in Nigeria's internal affairs has been far less laudable. His energy policies have done little to alleviate Nigeria's crushing poverty and social unrest...He kept the oil portfolio for himself so that he could use Nigeria's vast oil wealth for political ends."
Cohen, former American Assistant Secretary State for Africa, now member of the Board of Directors of the Council for a Community of Democracies and the Constituencies for Africa accuses Obasanjo of engaging in "undemocratic tactics". Yes, a man who short-circuits a party's primary election to impose his own nominee as the party's candidate is infuriated that some people are rebelling and some meticulous journalist has done his job.
Just as Obasanjo is demanding apology from CNN, so also are Nigerians terribly dehumanized are asking for his apology. Obasanjo must apologise for the Odi killings, for the Zaki-Biam killings, for his Third Term adventure, for the wastage of our resources, for needless detention of some folks, for his undemocratic utterances and acts. Obasanjo must apology for desecrating our hallowed Aso Rock and he should honourably leave that place now so it could be cleansed properly before a new leader enters.
Particularly, Obasanjo must apologise to our senior citizens for hoarding their pensions and subjecting them to much hardship and humiliation.
What about numerous others in the country, dying silently and "instalmentally" but cannot come to Abuja to register their grievances? Needless to say Obasanjo needs to apologise to the poor rural person who is constantly being neglected/maltreated.
To the National Assembly, they must not pass that Obasanjo's Bill. He deserves no more perks. Let him go to Ota and one day be called to come to Abuja to collect his alawi. Do unto others what you want done to you!
But does he really need it? Proceeds from Ota Farm alone can take care of Baba as well as his offspring including those unborn. But that Bill must not be passed by the National Assembly; definitely not for Baba to benefit. The National Assembly will be viewed as abetting exploitation and subjugation of Nigerians if they do put their seal to that document. We believe in our distinguished people. Like the Third Term plan, we know they will give it the perfect antidote. If all leaders were like this, many societies would opt to be without leaders.

Original_Search_Title: Catholics Flock to Shrine for Virgin Mary Vision
Headline: Uganda: Catholics Flock to Shrine for Virgin Mary Vision
Body_Text:
"The Virgin Mary is there, that one in a sparkling white cloak holding her hands together," 9-year-old Bella Priscilla Pirwoth called out as she pointed to the top of a pine tree near Nyapea Catholic Chapel.
A flock of children joined her chorus as bewildered adults perched on the apron of the church building craned their necks to catch a glimpse of the apparition. Such scenes have been common since early January at the chapel known locally as Awedoke and sandwiched between the residence of the Sisters of Mary Immaculate and the quarters of the five diocesan priests.
The late Bishop Negri and his fellow Italian Verona Fathers founded the chapel in 1933, and Catholics in the region will celebrate its diamond jubilee next January.
But a year before the celebrations, the phenomenon has become a major attraction, pulling thousands of people from across Uganda as well as neighbouring southern Sudan and the Democratic Republic of Congo.
Father Emile Onegwa, an elderly priest, says the vision of the Virgin first appeared to him inside the chapel on July 21, 2005, then again on February 4 last year. This year, he said, the vision has appeared around January 9 on a pine tree a short distance from the old building where the maiden batch of aspirants, or candidates to be nuns at Mary Immaculate, professed decades ago.
A nun joins other faithful in prayers at the chapel. Photo by Tabu Butagira
The rattle of arriving vehicles, the blare of prayer calls and the sounds of joyous worship fill the air in this previously hushed and forested neighbourhood about 10 kilometres northwest of sprawling Paidha town.
The chapel itself is only a few kilometres from the DRC Justine Ringtho, chairman of the six-member local Apparition Committee, said only about 2,000 of an estimated 900,000 believers who had flocked to Nyapea by February 22 had actually seen the apparition. He did not explain how he knew this but said he was among those who had seen it.
"She appeared in white and blue gown with a crown on her head surrounded by bright shining stars," he said. During an outdoor mass Thursday in front of the chapel, Fr. Onegwa told the crowd of hundreds that now was the right time for them to look deeply into themselves.
"The Virgin Mary has come to grant us salvation. Let us pray hard so that she helps us to overcome all difficulties and we start our journey to heaven peacefully," he said. The crowd remained quiet, then burst into deafening ululation and clapping.
Lent, the Christian period of introspection and repentance that precedes Easter began last Wednesday. Bella Pirwoth riveted the crowd with her testimony, then drew laughter when she gently teased her tearful mother Rose Mary Pakech for failing to see the apparition for the second day after travelling from Arua and offering constant prayers and reciting the rosary.
"Since Mum, you have failed to see the apparition, don't blame yourself. Just keep heart and thank the good Lord for keeping you alive," Ms Pirwoth, a pupil at Awindiri Primary School, said.
Paul Bedijo, a teacher of French at St. Aloysius College Nyapea, refuses to give up the struggle after three unsuccessful attempts to see the apparition. "I am a staunch Catholic and believe that Mary the Mother of Jesus has a role to play in our lives," he said as he jostled with children to catch a glimpse.
'"If She has appeared somewhere, I want to see Her for it will be a clear confirmation and evidence of what I believe in." The selective manifestation of the apparition is already causing emotional and social ripples in the local community amid wide-ranging inferences that it only appears to the faithful. There is a growing belief that since children are largely faultless, they are able to see it.
Desperate believers, mainly women, have turned to praying in front of statues of Virgin Mary installed inside the chapel, and others are keeping vigil on the chapel grounds.
At least 10 of the 300 some people gathered at the Nyapea Chapel Thursday professed to having seen the apparition.
But the bishop of Nebbi Diocese, Rt. Rev. Martin Luluga, has repeatedly declined to comment on the perceived miraculous happenings, explaining that such matters are "very complicated, and I have also not seen it yet".
Stephen Ocopcan, who is compiling a list of the progressive events of the apparition, said some of those individuals who have failed to see it are so distressed they are claiming to have seen the divine sight for self-fulfilment.
"I know it (apparition) is a miracle that happens to different people at different time and for different purposes. People are not seeing it because they are unhappy, ever longing for material possession without much time to renew their faith," said Ocopcan, who claimed to have seen it. "Apparition requires and brings change in people's lives. It is not a fashion."
The influx of those hoping to see the apparition has imposed additional demands on church administrators who have increased the number of weekday masses from one to three. As a result, offertory collections at the chapel have shot up from Shs15,000 to Shs 50,000 a day.
And some local businesses are booming. The demand for rosaries has grown tremendously. Stanley Gobson Omung, the chief supplier, said since last month he has sold 680 sparkling rosaries at Shs500 each, earning Shs340, 000.
Makeshift markets have sprouted up around the chapel where vendors sell all kinds of food including anyoya mix (boiled maize/beans), cassava, bananas, avocados and sugar cane.

Original_Search_Title: Sad Tale of a Star
Headline: Uganda: Sad Tale of a Star
Body_Text:
Kampala — Fifty-one years ago, he represented Uganda at the Melbourne Olympics. Today, Patrick Etolu is not as athletic. He walks with a stoop of shoulders that sit on a frail body. Knock knees slow his movement as he drags the right leg.
At 72, Etolu wakes up at 6:00am to attend to his garden, the source of his livelihood. Born in Asingei, Madera in Soroti District to Lawrence Etengu and Maria Aliano, Etolu had the average villager's childhood. As his parents dug in the garden, he played with sticks; jumping over them as he stacked them as high as possible. What started as a joke soon turned professional.
Throughout his primary school at Madera Boys, Etolu practised high jump after class everyday. He hastily adds that it didn't interfere with his studies and boasts that he always emerged best among his peers. Soon after P6, Etolu joined a teachers' training college in Soroti.
The high jump competitions were seasonal and this helped him balance teaching and his passion for athletics. During one of his sojourns at the district level, the principal of the TTC, Fr. Hildas, transported Etolu to Kampala so he could participate in national competitions. By now he was so accustomed to winning that he expected nothing but the best.
Etolu's attitude soon turned into complacence and indifference. No one could beat him. He shares a running joke among his peers that the height at which they failed to clear the cross bar was where he started. His antics won him a place on the first Ugandan Olympics team in 1956.
Sadly, he finished 12th overall.
"I don't know what happened," he says. Did he feel let down? Etolu says he felt no emotion because he had got accustomed to winning, but blames the inadequate training he received. At this point, Etolu does not remember the month or date the Olympics took place.
All he remembers are "glittering lights."
"The place was so colourful. There were so many lights. It wasn't like where I am living now," he gestures around his compound.
We are seated on wooden fold-up chairs under a mango tree. A few goats graze in nearby grass while others eat at cassava laid out to dry. Etolu sports tattered green Corduroy pants, a green shirt and with cracked feet and scaly skin in red slippers.
Etolu remembers someone donning him with something around his neck but has no idea what it was. In fact, his memory of the Olympics and his life before today is hazy. He does not remember the dates when he was in school or when he attended competitions in the US, UK and Nairobi.
He narrates that last year while on his way home from his brother's place, unknown thugs attacked him around 10:00pm, beating him to near death. He was with his wife, Grace Ironga, when the thugs broke into the house.
They didn't ask questions or even make off with property. Instead, they beat him up with sticks.
He points at scars on his cheeks, forehead and nose where he was stitched. He says he did not recognise what the thugs were wearing but reckons the attack may have been inspired by a land issue. Etolu, however, maintains that he owns very little.
At this point, Etolu's youngest of seven children, Patrick Etolu Jr., interjects saying the incident happened in 1997. Etolu shrugs when he's corrected. However, Etolu's younger brother, 65-year-old Faustine Egonu, tells me later that the incident actually happened in 1989.
Egonu says thugs entered his brother's house and using a panga, cut him on the head, back and face. They found him bleeding and speechless on the floor, where after he was rushed to hospital.
"But the old man's condition was not promising. For one month, he (Etolu) used sign language to communicate," Egonu says.
Contrary to Etolu's act of indifference, Egonu says in his welcome speech from the Olympics, his brother said he was proud to have come 12th out of many participants.
"He amused us when he said that European ladies could show any other part of their body but not their breasts," Egonu said.
The qualifying height for high jump was 6 feet. Etolu was six feet one inch (6' 1") tall. His record best is six feet eight inches (6' 8"). Etolu was so accustomed to jumping without shoes that he only wore the left shoe for grip on the slippery ground.
"It was out of choice," he says.
He explains that they used the western straddle where one's feet go over the cross bar before the rest of the body, unlike today's Fosbury flop, where the head and shoulders cross the bar first.
After the olympiad, Etolu returned to Uganda to teach at Ngora Boys in Kumi. He also found time to coach athletics at Soroti TTC. One of his students, George Odeke, excelled and became National Athletics Coach. The first of eight children retired in 1986, concentrating on agriculture.
His greatest disappointment is that since he came back, government has given him nothing.
"Not even a chicken or ajon (local millet brew)," he says.
All he got was a state dinner in the Olympic team's honour, which also included Benjamin Ndugga, a sprinter and Lawrence Ogwang, a long jumper.
He would have preferred that government built him a permanent house to save him from a leaking grass-thatched hut, which his wife can't fix.
"Akii-Bua was presented with a house and given a car with private plates," Etolu complained.One more frustration is the pension he gets, which can hardly pay all his children's fees and maintenance. Egonu explains that his brother used to earn sh15,000 a month. Then policy required that all pensioners open accounts. He opened one for his brother who continued receiving pension until 2000.
"Mysteriously the name disappeared from the list of pensioners in 2001," Egonu notes.
"For three years and five months, he was getting nothing. He was surviving like a flying bird."
After much consulting and writing letters, Etolu resumed getting pension in 2004. It's now at sh86,000.
One consolation is that the zone, in which Etolu lives, was named after him shortly after his Olympics experience.
He may have forgotten much else about his life, but Etolu remembers his birth date: March 17, 1935. Etolu retires at 8:00pm after enjoying adere (local brew served in a calabash).

Original_Search_Title: Regional Demand Keeps Food Prices Up
Headline: Uganda: Regional Demand Keeps Food Prices Up
Body_Text:
Kampala — CONSTANT demand for beans, maize flour, rice and groundnuts in the neighbouring countries has kept prices high, the weekly survey has shown.
Robert Mugisa, a trader at Nakasero Market explained: "Most of the food items are going to Kenya, DR Congo and southern Sudan yet the supply is low because most harvests got spoilt during the recent rains."
He elaborated: "There is high demand in these countries especially southern Sudan which has the best market rates."
Southern Sudan is increasingly becoming the target for organisations and individual traders looking for new markets in the Great Lakes region while Uganda has predominantly remained the major source for foodstuffs for the regional population.
"This has forced traders to buy directly from the farmers, leaving us with little supply, " Mugisa added.
He explained that this, coupled with high demand in schools for posho and beans, had pushed food prices up."
Beans was sold between sh1,300 and sh1,400 from sh1,000 a kilo, while maize flour was sold at sh800 from sh700 a kilo.
Rice was sold at sh1,600 from sh1,500 a kilo and maize went for sh300 a kilo.
Groundnuts and simsim were sold at sh1,800 a kilo.
However, millet flour began flocking the market since it is the harvest period and a kilo was sold at sh700.
Irish potatoes, cassava and sweet potatoes kept flocking the market and a heap was sold between sh300 and sh600 from the previous week.

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo Warns Electorates Against Rigging
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo Warns Electorates Against Rigging
Body_Text:
Makurdi — As President Olusegun Obasanjo lead a cream of PDP national leadership to flag off the party's Presidential and gubernatorial campaign in Benue state yesterday, he warned electorates particularly supporters of PDP that those alleging that PDP has perfected plans to rig the forthcoming elections have something under their sleeves and should be watched during the elections.
President Obasanjo who arrived the IBB Square Makurdi, venue of the occasion, in company of Benue state governor, George Akume, national Chairman PDP Chief Ahmodu Ali and other big wigs of the party at 5:45 pm was greeted with a very loud ovation by the mammoth crowd that had assembled waiting for him under the scotching sun, for close to nine hours.
Obasanjo challenged those alleging that PDP is ready to rig the election to come out with concrete proofs, asking "For what reason should we want to rig. Those who are saying others may rig are the ones who want to rig the elections; I want you to watch out seriously because they would want to steal your votes.
"When there is no wolves and you are crying wolves, there is something behind it, when there is no thief in the compound and you are shouting 'thief, thief' maybe you are the one who want to steal. We, the PDP, are people who will keep our words and our promise, just hold us to our words and we will do what we have promised to do.
The President who commended the leadership of the state governor, Dr. George Akume who he described as one who has lived up to the expectations of the people, warned the people of the state to be wary of politicians in the state who are out to deceive them and urged them to resolve to remain in the mainstream party for more dividends of democracy.
He said, "I want to say to you people of Benue how happy we are to meet you as we have met you solid, united and ready to so us proud as you have always done. The history of the people of this state is a history of incommutability, history of courage, history of resistant to whatever is regarded as evil.
"We have to move forward, which is part of what majority of the people of this state fought for in the past, you should be in the mainstream of political lives of this country and that you have won. What you have won you must jealously guard, don't let anybody take it away from you because it can be taken away by those who would deceive you".
Obasanjo who said he was in the state to join in the party's campaigns going on in the state said, he would conclude his assignment in the state by commissioning a number of projects executed by the Akume administration today, before leaving the state for other official assignments.
According to him, "From what I have seen, your governor, Chief Akume has done some wonderful job especially in the are of education and in the area of agriculture. I will be commissioning a cassava power plant, one of a kind in this country, and I will be commissioning a fruit juice plant, again one of the kinds in this part of the country. These two important projects speak volume for a state that is regarded as food basket of the Nigeria.
He however, expressed disappointment with the people of the state for not appreciating his administration's effort towards improving the power outage since he assumed office, saying, "When we came to power the light here in Makurdi was just like candle light, and I was also here in one occasion and I couldn't even read with the light. We then improved on the power with 132 KVA from Aliade to Makurdi, but that is not enough now and we are bringing 330 KVA from Cross River to Makurdi, and from Makurdi to Jos".
The President said his administration has resolved to surprise the people of the state with another package as arrangements have gone far into awarding the Oweto Bridge to a reputable firm very soon as well as the water problems of Makurdi about to be finally solved, stressing that if he has no interest of the state at heart how could his administration thought of dividends of democracy from the federal level to these points.
Speaking earlier, the national Chairman of PDP, Chief Ahmodu Ali who commended the resilience of the people of the state to remain permanently in PDP cautioned them never to succumb to evil antics of some politicians who would like to deceive them from the mainstream party.
Ahmodu Ali who reminded that he represented Benue as a Senator of the Federal Republic twice before the carving out of Kogi from Benue remarked that he knew the people of the state as peace loving, focus and dynamic in whatever they chose to believe in and do.
The PDP Presidential candidate, Alhaji Umoru Yar'Adua who described Benue people as astute politicians that could be relied upon any moment of the day, called for a minute silent prayer for the late Joseph Tarkaa, saying he is a politician well respected nationwide and one with humility and love for all Nigerians.
He pleaded with Benue people to vote enmasse for PDP in the forthcoming general elections so that the state could enjoy all the vision and projects of the government at the federal level.
Governor George Akume who gave the vote of thanks at the occasion, applauded the leadership of Chief Olusegun Obasanjo and promised overwhelming PDP victory from the state in the forthcoming elections.
Notable PDP stalwarts from the state who spoke at the occasion all eulogized the exemplary leadership of Chief Olusegun Obasanjo and urged the PDP Presidential candidate, Alhaji Umoru Yar'Adua and his running mate Chief GoodLuck Jonathan to try as much as possible to consolidate on the foundation already laid for them by the outgoing administration.
Those who graced the occasion include governors of Taraba, Rev. Jolly Nyame; Mr. James Ibori of Delta; Olusola Saraki of Kwara and the deputy governor of Enugu state.
Others include Paul Wantaregh Unongo, Generals Lawrence Onoja and Geoffrey Ejiga (rtd), former national Chairman PDP Chief Barnabas Gemade, Amb. Iorwuese Hagher, Prof. Dora Akunyili and Chief Bola Ige among others.
Highpoint of the occasion was the flag off campaign for the PDP Presidential candidate and his running mate as well as the official presentation of PDP gubernatorial candidate Mr. Gabriel Suswam and his running mate Chief Steven Lawani for Benue state.

Original_Search_Title: Government Reviews Jatropha Price
Headline: Zimbabwe: Government Reviews Jatropha Price
Body_Text:
Harare — GOVERNMENT has reviewed the price of Jatropha from $35 000 to $60 000 per tonne in an effort to encourage farmers to boost production.
In an interview, National Oil Company of Zimbabwe acting chief executive Mr Isaac Mhaka said the State was also considering reviewing the price to be in line with oil prices to encourage farmers to venture into jatropha farming.
Jatropha is a drought tolerant plant that produces seed, which has between 30 and 40 percent oil content.
The oil has great potential for making bio-diesel while the by-product could be used as organic fertilizer.
"We have reviewed the prices of buying seed from $35 000 per tonne to $60 000 to encourage farmers to venture and contribute in the bio-diesel project, which could help the country solve its fuel woes," Mr Mhaka said.
He said Noczim, which is the sole buyer of jatropha curcas seed will start buying the seed from March this year in various areas throughout the country.
The areas include Mutoko, Guruve, Lupane, Checheche, Chivi and Masvingo.
Jatropha is a high value crop and Government has since specified the tree, banned its export, and adopted the tree along with cassava as major development crops for the next five years.
Cassava is a high-energy plant rich in starch and ethanol.
There were also efforts to increase production in sugar cane, castor bean and soyabean among others as an initiative to grow crops, which could be used to develop bio-diesel.
Many countries in the world such as France, Germany, South Africa and Egypt were already powering motor vehicles with bio-diesel that was considered to be cheap and more environmentally friendly than fossil fuel.

Original_Search_Title: A Trip to the Past
Headline: Kenya: A Trip to the Past
Body_Text:
Nairobi — It's poor, and residents are more Ugandan than Kenyan. Welcome to Mageta Island.
A forty-five minute "voyage" to Mageta Island from Lake Victoria's Usenge beach is an adventure that only likens to Jonathan Swift's Gulliver's Travels.
First time visitors to the island often term their excursion an "exploration" but the residents here see nothing special about the island apart from its "inconveniences."
"We are cut off from activities in Kisumu and the rest of the country. We receive news only from Ugandan TV and radio stations," complains Mr Ouma Kach, our boatman for the day.
The British administrators used Mageta Island as a colonial detention camp before independence. Passengers pay Sh80 from Usenge beach to the Island. An additional Sh80 takes one to Oyamo Island. Food has to be brought from the mainland at additional cost.
Transport charges have often discouraged many a small-scale businessmen like Moses Owaki, a fisherman, who would want to sell their wares on Mageta, Oyamo, Ngede and other islands.
Mageta is popular for Omena (dagaa) fishing and its rich colonial history. The prices of commodities like onions, tomatoes, bananas, maize, rice, and beans almost double upon landing on the islands, as boat operators cite increasing petrol costs.
The island has a population of more than 20,000. People move about on the isle on foot and bicycles. Residents here engage in sorghum, cassava and fish farming for subsistence.
Fishermen lose their catch to pirates
Passenger boats from Usenge mainland and other beaches are few and operate at intervals of 30 minutes.
Commuters have to wait for more than one hour for the vessels to return after their first trip to Mageta from Usenge.
Of the three islands, only Mageta has schools - Mageta, Mahanga and Mitundu primary schools and Mageta Secondary School. Pupils from the other two islands have to travel by boat to schools at Mageta.
That Friday, we took 45 minutes from Usenge beach to Mageta Island by motor boat. Enroute, we passed another island without human habitation called Sirigombe.
Kach told Transport that the island is full of poisonous snakes.
Fishermen on the islands, who lose close four tonnes of their daily catch to pirates and Ugandan security officers, are waiting with bated breath for the conclusion of talks on a common East African market.
A common market will see the breaking of boundaries and free movement of persons among the three East African states.
"We are forced to sell our catch on Ugandan beaches at a throwaway price, or be arrested," said Owaki.
Unexploited tourism attraction
Kenya claims only six per cent of Lake Victoria waters with Uganda and Tanzania enjoying the rest.
Plans are under way open up the islands.
"The Government has already inaugurated a Sh10 million murram road on Mfangano Island," says Mr Richard Abura, Assistant Director of Information at the Ministry of Roads and Public works.
The challenge to the Government, however, is to start ferry services between Mbita and Mfangano Island - a three-hour journey by boat.
Hiring a private ferry to transport road construction materials from the mainland costs the Government Sh1 million, says Abura.
Bicycle taxis (boda boda) could soon thrive as they are easier to transport from the mainland.
However, residents of the three Lake Victoria islands can only wait for the first public service vehicle to start business on the island.
According to the Kenya Tourism Board Managing Director, Dr Ong'ong'a Achieng, the island is an unexploited tourism attraction.
Says Ong'ong'a: "Infrastructure is the key to turning our islands into tourism sites."

Original_Search_Title: Between Obasanjo And Herman Cohen
Headline: Nigeria: Between Obasanjo And Herman Cohen
Body_Text:
Former American Assistant Secretary of State for Africa, Mr. Herman Cohen in an article published on page 12 of February 18, 2007 by the Sunday Trust Newspaper, decisively took on President Olusegun Obasanjo. To the casual or undiscerning readers, the article may well passed off as an altruistic intervention by the retired American Diplomat in the ongoing efforts in Nigeria at transiting from one civil democratic government to another. However this is far from being so as very incontrovertible but classified facts point to the sad fact that our dear Mr. Cohen, having been fed with misinformation and outright lies set out to do the bidding of known powerful political forces in the country.
The American set out to tar President Olusegun Obasanjo as a non-achiever who is bent on clinging to power beyond May 2007 at all cost. Now, even the worst critics of President Obasanjo will not accept his labelling as a non - performer. Even persons in opposition have been caught in public saying: 'yes we know Obasanjo has tried but we want to form the next government.' But if Cohen chose to believe the lies he has been fed, then we must address the issues. I must state here that it will be impossible to itemize or list exhaustively the achievements of President Obasanjo in this one write up. I am only attempting a rough sketch.
On assuming office, President Obasanjo began an intensive and extensive cleaning up exercise. This involved sending very important bills to the National Assembly. These included the bills setting up the Economic and Financial Crimes Commission (EFCC) and the Independent Corrupt Practices Commission (ICPC). He went ahead to establish the Due Process mechanism, which ensures that Nigeria and Nigerians get proper value for their money when contracts are awarded or a supply is made to the Federal Government. In the first 18 months of the Due Process regime, The Federal Government saved a staggering N52 Billion. Today, the due process culture has been institutionalized and government saves billions of naira daily. The President was also prompt to raise the wages of the nation's workers given that when he assumed office in 1999, the minimum wage in the federal civil service was N3, 000. This has been severally raised and the least paid worker in the Federal Civil Service enjoys a wage of over N10, 000. In the same regard, this government has more than tripled pensions for retired workers. Profound reforms in the civil service have placed the nation's workforce at par with their counterparts in the developed world.
Across the county, network of new roads dot the landscape and the newly established Federal Road Maintenance Agency (FERMA) is currently ensuring that the roads do not suffer neglect just as was the case in the past. In the same regard, water projects have been sited in virtually all the local government headquarters in the country. Indeed in villages and towns across the country our people are living better lives owing to transforming effects of rural electrification projects. The government has achieved increased agricultural production. In the light of this unprecedented increase, government has since the year 2000 been expanding storage facilities across the country. It is an incontrovertible fact that the Cassava Policy of the government has transformed thousands of the nation's farmers to millionaires.
On the issue of security, the government policy of increased recruitment in the Police Force and other security agencies have begun paying off. No matter what anyone would want to say, Nigeria is today better policed than ever before. Yes the desired equipment needs of our security forces may not have been completely met but concerted efforts to that effect is yielding positive results.
On the vexed issue of electricity power supply, it must be placed on record that the Obasanjo government inherited a fundamentally flawed situation where no new power station had been built in over thirty years. Not even one. The few power stations that were in place were virtually dead. The combined electricity generation in the country when President Obasanjo assumed office in 1999 was about 1,500 mega watts. Nearly eight years after these same decrepit power stations have been reworked and capacity has since been upped to over 4,000 mega watts. Even then President Obasanjo has repeatedly held that the target of the administration is 10,000 mega watts. And we are very nearly there. In the next two months or so, at least three of the 15 power stations being built by the Obasanjo government will come on stream. The failures currently being experienced in electricity supply is one of the consequences of past misrule. No sane person expected President Obasanjo to redress in just eight years, the colossal failings of past inept leaders, spanning several years.
The health needs of our people are now being adequately met owing largely to huge investments in the all - important health sector. At the last count, eight Teaching Hospitals had been up-graded, re-equipped and refurbished to give five star treatments to Nigerians. In the same regard, Federal Medical Centres across the country have been totally re-built, re-energized and rejuvenated. Even more heart - warming is the fact that this government has established at least one primary healthcare centre in each of about 8,000 wards in the country. Reforms in the health sector have seen to the aggressive tackling of the HIV/AIDS scourge and the childhood killer diseases. Our children and pregnant women are today some of the best vaccinated in Africa. As early as 2003, Nigeria already attained more than 80 percent in the immunization of her infants.
The revolution in the telecommunication sector inspired by President Obasanjo saw to the astronomical rise of subscriber levels for fixed lines from 473,316 in 1999 to 1,027,707 by September, 2005. For mobile lines, the figure rose from 35,000 in 1999 to 16,078,817 by September 2005. This revolution in the telecommunication sector has led to direct foreign investment in our economy to the tune of $10 Billion by 2006. Ongoing expansions in the telecommunication sector are expected to yield even more for Nigeria and Nigerians.
Sweeping reforms in the education sector have revived the nation's schools at all levels. Through the Universal Basic Education programme launched by the President, billions of naira has gone to the re-working of schools across the country. For example, in 2005 alone, over N30 Billion was provided as UBE intervention funds to states of the federation. Information and Communication Technology (ICT) have been deployed to schools across the country. Our Universities are alive and well again and once again our graduates can now conveniently compete with persons with similar qualifications elsewhere.
Internationally acclaimed economic reforms of the Obasanjo government have given our nation a formal economy, a private sector - driven economy that brings out the very best in the hardworking and creative citizenry. Today, legitimate entrepreneurs are exceeding targets even as they are breaking new grounds. Thanks to our pepped - up economy, the middle class is back and waxing ever stronger. It has suddenly become unattractive for educated and hardworking Nigerians to live abroad. In their thousands they have begun returning home and are reaping bountifully from the gains of economic reforms of the Obasanjo administration. The success of the economic reforms has restored the faith of the international community in our economy. This achievement more than anything else, was instrumental to the successful agreement to wipe off Nigeria's debt with both the Paris and London Club of Creditors.
But even more important, the anti - corruption crusade of the administration has taken roots. For the first time in the history of Nigeria, efforts are being made to bar corrupt persons from aspiring to be elected to exalted political positions. This is a move being applauded by Nigerians of all ages except of course the powerful persons that have for years been milking our treasury and can now afford to pay for the services of a retired very senior American Diplomat like Mr. Cohen, to fight their political wars against their country and people.
Now reading through Cohen's acerbic piece one would easily make out his love for Alhaji Atiku Abubakar. He emphatically concludes that a deliberate plot is on to exempt him from contesting to be Nigeria's President in the next election. Cohen must understand that just like is the case in his beloved United States of America, Nigerian laws are no respecter of persons. Nigeria is serious about combating corruption and for us the starting point may well be in carefully exempting persons already indicted for corruption from occupying or seeking to occupy exalted political offices. Alhaji Atiku Abubakar, like all Nigerians know, has been indicted by an Administrative Panel set up by the Federal Government, for corruption. For the records, Atiku Abubakar was thoroughly investigated by the EFCC and the Commission found him seriously wanting and consequently indicted him for corruption. President Obasanjo set up an Administrative Panel to review the findings and recommendations of the EFCC on Atiku Abubakar and others. This panel of very eminent persons was unable to fault the findings and recommendations of the EFCC. The Panel upheld the recommendations of the EFCC and indicted Alhaji Atiku Abubakar and others for corruption.
It is understandable that Alhaji Atiku Abubakar would not accept this position of the law and would want to battle to upturn things. So, it is not difficult to locate how our dear Ambassador Cohen entered into the fray. But the surprise is that Cohen, who ordinarily should have access to all the high - ranking officials of the Federal Government, would rely on outright falsehood and misinformation as well as misrepresentations, in his bid to help his good friend, Alhaji Atiku Abubakar. Had he asked, we would have given him the true picture of events in Nigeria.
Comments that the primaries of the PDP were short circuited to ensure that persons the President could control while out of office emerged are not only vexatious but illiterate. Such comment coming from Cohen clearly shows how low he is willing to sink in his bid to share in the ill - gotten wealth of his Nigerian paymasters.
Mallam Uba Sani is the Special Assistant to the President on Public Affairs

Original_Search_Title: Country to Introduce Genetically Engineered Banana
Headline: Uganda: Country to Introduce Genetically Engineered Banana
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Uganda could soon introduce genetically modified bananas after a successful genetically engineered sweet banana variety proved resistant to pests and diseases.
The technology will improve the quality of banana, an important food and cash crop whose production has declined due to diseases, especially the banana wilt disease.
Genetically engineered bananas will also contribute to food security and improve household incomes. Almost 24.5 per cent of Ugandan household's income is contributed by bananas. Some 70 per cent of farmers grow them as a staple food as well as for brewing local liquor.
Scientists estimate that if the technology is applied to other varieties, the country could save up to $8 billion it is said to have earmarked in the next five years for fighting the banana bacterial wilt disease.
The disease is currently ravaging the country and spreading to Rwanda, the Democratic Republic of Congo and Tanzania.
The genetically engineered variety was developed by Geoffrey Arinaitwe, a Ugandan scientist based in Belgium who has now applied to the Uganda National Council for Science and Technology (UNCST) for a permit to import it to Uganda.
"The Council has already cleared it for field testing after importation from Belgium.
This innovation will pave the way for research on other varieties to make them resistant to diseases," said Arthur Makara, the biosafety desk officer at the Council, the country's leading institution for science, technology and innovation development.
The tested banana type will be brought to the Kawanda Research Institute (Kari), which has just completed construction of a greenhouse to field test bananas for resistance to bacterial wilt and black sigatoka fungal disease, said Andrew Kiggundu, a plant biotechnologist at Kari.
The bacterial wilt is highly destructive, wiping out at least 90 per cent of the fruit on the trees it affects. When it affects a tree, it becomes poisonous to both humans and animals.
Scientists say the commonest way the disease spreads is through bees, which pick up pollen from one banana and transport it to another.
Mr Kiggundu said more crops have been earmarked for testing before the end of this year at Namulonge Research Institute and the National Agricultural Research Organisation (Naro).
Other crops that are slated for field testing at Kari are genetically modified cassava, which is resistant to the mosaic virus, sweet potatoes rich in Vitamin A content and BT cotton.
"The varieties we have developed have increased resistance to pests, are nutritive and fast growing," said Mr Kiggundu.
But scientists have warned that although the genetically engineered crops are being developed, they may not reach farmers any time soon as the country has no legal framework to enable their distribution.
"We have new technology but we are being left behind because there is no policy in place," said Dr Charles Mugoya of the Association for Strengthening Agricultural Research in Eastern and Central Africa (Asereca).
"The GMOs will increase farm incomes and minimise environmental impact as there is pesticide reduction. We need technology to drive our food production," said Dr Mugoya during the launch of the 2006 Global Status of Commercialised Biotech report in Kampala.
The government is in the process of putting in place an enabling law to allow the distribution of the GMO bananas.
The National Science, Technology and Innovation (STI) policy, which will guide the activities and distribution of GMO resources, is awaiting approval by parliament.
"We in parliament will do everything possible to ensure that the biotechnology policy is approved and biosafety bill enacted into law," said John Arimpa, vice chairman of the Parliamentary Committee on Natural Resources.
Uganda has three banana varieties - sweet bananas, plantain and the East African highland bananas (matooke), which are only grown and consumed in East Africa.

Original_Search_Title: Hunger in a Land of Plenty
Headline: Guinea Bissau: Hunger in a Land of Plenty
Body_Text:
Bedanda — Driving through lush farmlands, forests and mangrove swamps south from Guinea-Bissau's coastal town of Buba, it is hard to believe that the roughly 100,000 people who live and farm here could not find a way to feed themselves last year.
But the fact is that malnutrition shot up to beyond what the United Nations World Health Organisation (WHO) calls the 'emergency threshold' and relief agencies shipped in over 1,000 tonnes of food aid in 2006.
And although the crisis is over for now, next time it could be far worse.
"We have a disaster waiting to happen," said World Food Programme (WFP) representative José Pita-GrÃ's. "The root causes of the problem aren't being addressed and the population is more and more at risk."
The 2006 crisis occurred mostly in Guinea-Bissau's Quinara and Tombali regions, which are normally the breadbasket of the country. For generations, people here have grown local varieties of rice, which scholars say existed before Europeans arrived.
Agroscientists say people in the area have developed complex farming techniques. "Southern Guinea-Bissau can be considered a reservoir of both rice genetic diversity and farmers' germplasm management skills," according to agronomist Marina PadrÃ£o Temudo at Lisbon's Institute of Tropical Scientific Research.
The soil and climate are also ideal for growing cashews and in recent years Guinea-Bissau has become the world's sixth largest producer. Cashews account for 85 percent of the country's export earning.
Local and imported rice is now readily available in Babu market but experts say food security is fragile and hunger could return. (Buba, capital of Quinara Region in southern Guinea Bissau, 26 Jan 2007)
From July to October, before the new rice is harvested, people sell cashews to traders, who mostly come from India. For years the traders had been buying a kilo of cashews for 250 CFA (about US 50 cents) which is the same price as a kilo of imported Asian rice, and locals were happy to barter.
"We like to eat rice but it's easy to grow cashews," said Fatou Nasamha, a women farming roadside near the village of Bedanda. "We just have to pick them off the tree."
So what went wrong?
Experts focus on two main problems:
First, in September 2005, high tides spilled seawater over the banks of the traditional system of dykes, flooding the rice fields around the mangroves and destroying thousands of tonnes of rice.
At this point people had enough to eat but farmers had lost the rice seeds they would need for the next season, said Rui Jorge Fonseca, a representative for the Food and Agriculture Organisaion (FAO) in Guinea-Bissau.
"The worst part was there was no system of alert; no one started preparing ahead of time for the crisis," he said.
The government only made a request to FAO for seeds in May 2006, about the time the seeds should have been planted, Fonseca said.
The FAO immediately started the process of importing 400 tonnes of seeds but they didn't arrive before August when the rainy season started setting in. "Much of it came too late," said Fonseca. "One seed variety spoiled because of humidity and farmers couldn't get the seedlings to grow."
Paulo Sambu, a farmer in the southern town of Buba, told IRIN he gave up and started eating the seeds. "There was nothing else we could do," he said.
Disaster by decree
At the same time a separate crisis started brewing, this one around cashews.
It had nothing to do with natural forces. In fact the cashew harvest for 2006 was one of Guinea-Bissau's best ever.
But 2006 was also an election year and the government wanted to please voters. It decided that farmers could raise the price they sold their cashews from 250 CFA a kilo to 350 CFA.
The Indian traders refused to pay.
The government told the farmers to hold out and the Indians would eventually come around. Instead, the traders just went elsewhere.
Rice became scarcer; the price of cashews started dropping and then it crashed below 50 CFA.
Pretty soon farmers couldn't sell their cashews at that price. Tens of thousands of tonnes just rotted.
People became desperate. "We invented a kind of soup made from cashews and cassava but nobody wanted to eat it," the chief of Bedanda village, Nhinna na N'tchama, said. "So to get rice we sold our tools, our clothes and all our domestic animals."
And the price of rice started to rise.
By July the FAO had issued a report stating that "the majority" of the population was facing chronic food insecurity.
By August malnutrition in five out of nine regions of the country reached levels of 10.9 percent, according to a WFP survey.
Ten percent and above is considered 'acute'.
WFP set up nutrition centres with various partners and brought in over 1,000 tonnes of food aid.
"It was logistical nightmare," Pita-Gros of WFP said. "The rainy season had already started and our big 30-tonne trucks were getting stuck. Even smaller trucks couldn't get through to some areas. We had to do some of the distribution by canoe."
"We were all taken by surprise," he said. "We just weren't expecting to see these kinds of problems in a country with such abundant natural resources."
Solutions
Who to blame depends on who is speaking. International aid officials say the government's policy on cashew pricing was the leading cause of the crisis. Government officials and local relief workers say the crisis could have been averted if the international organisations had responded faster and more effectively.
But what everyone agrees on is that the problem can really only be solved if farmers diversify what they produce and people vary their diet.
"Guinea-Bissau is capable of producing plenty of sweet potatoes and cassava but people here don't like to eat them," said the FAO's Fonseca.
Depending on just one cash crop - cashews - is like playing Russian roulette, he said. "The world price could collapse or traders could decide to go do business somewhere else and we could have a humanitarian catastrophe," he said.
Already Indian traders are saying they may not return to Guinea-Bissau this year because the government has created new rules forcing them to buy cashews through local middle men rather than directly from farmers.
"I just hope that people will see what happened last year as a sign that they have to be prepared for what could go wrong in the future," Pita-Gros said.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: 'Operation Saviour Has Saved Our Lives'
Headline: Zambia: 'Operation Saviour Has Saved Our Lives'
Body_Text:
Ndola — OPERATION Saviour is the Government's answer to the relief supplies distribution puzzle brought about by sudden floods being experienced in some parts of North-Western Province.
Hundreds of people living on the banks of the Zambezi river and its tributaries in Zambezi, Chavuma and Mwiinilunga districts now live under constant threats due to the floods caused by heavy rain.
With the next harvest four months away, both locals and some relief aid experts say the situation could deteriorate in the absence of a sustainable intervention mechanism.
With the entire major road network blocked in areas worst affected by the floods, the Disaster Management and Mitigation Unit (DMMU) under the Office of the Vice-President has teamed up with the Zambia Air Force (ZAF) coming up with an ambitious relief supplies distribution initiative code named Operation Saviour.
A total of 600 tonnes of maize, among other relief food, is expected to be airlifted to flood victims in Zambezi, Chavuma and Mwinilunga districts.
Apart from airlifting relief foods to flood affected areas, the initiative aims at ensuring that flood victims were helped to lead normal lives through the provision of social amenities.
Zambezi's Matondo-Nyachikayi ward councilor, Caiphas Muzanga, said that hundreds of people in flood-affected areas had lost goods worth millions of Kwacha.
Mr Muzanga said the worstdestruction of houses by floods was in the east and west bank of the Zambezi river.
He named some of the areas as: Machikayi where 132 families had their houses and property destroyed by the floods.
In Kanyenge area 50 families had their dwellings destroyed by the floods.
Mr Muzanga also stated in Matondo area 38 families were now living in an emergency situation after their houses and properties were destroyed.
He said that the floods affected hundreds of cassava and maize fields.
" We now have a situation where crops are rotting. The rice which was recently planted has been washed away by the water before it even germinated," the councilor said.
In most maize fields the crop has been swept away such that some rotten steams were now floating in water.
He said that in some places people have abandoned their dwelling places opting to settle in satellite relief centres.
An on the spot check on the west bank of the Zambezi river reveals that some of the most affected area were: Chinyama Litapi, Kalengwa, Milomboyi and Nyachikayi among others.
The Government has engaged PM Development agency to distribute relief food and other emergency needs to those affected by the floods in Zambezi and Chavuma districts.
PM Development Agency, coordinator, Mark Hilmorst, said the coming in of the Zambia Air Force (ZAF) under Operation Saviour has made a difference as far as ensuring that no life was lost during the emergency situation caused by the floods.
" We now have a situation were relief food is being airlifted to flood affected people in Mwangi, the furtherest point on the west bank near the Angolan border thanks to the efficiency of the Zambia Air Force relief operation," Mr Hilmorst said.
He noted that Operation Saviour was instrumental in bringing together some village headmen and other opinion leaders who were part of the leadership of the satellite relief supplies distribution committees.
He observed that apart from the relief food there was urgent need to supply the flood victims with other necessities.
Things urgently needed are tents, mosquito nets, malaria treatment, diarrhoea treatment and water purification substances.
According to PM Development agency findings, infant mortality rate has increased by more than 70 per cent on the west bank of the Zambezi river because of the floods .
Mr Hilmorst said that it was difficult to quantify the amount of relief needed by the flood victims in Zambezi and Chavuma districts.
He said the demand for the relief supplies was still high as the number of flood affected places continue increasing due to the heavy rain being experienced in the Zambezi river banks.
Some flood victims on the west bank have commended Operation Saviour for saving them from starving to death after their crops and animals were destroyed by floods.
Mwangi satellite relief distribution committee, member, Edwin Kayishala, said despite being in one of the remotest parts of the North-Western Province no human life was lost due to starvation after the floods.
The Zambia Air Force using airplanes and helicopters was now airlifting relief food and other supplies.
"The crops have been completely destroyed. Lakes and valleys covered by weeds have now been turned into bleeding grounds for mosquitoes," Mr Kayishala said.
The TIMES findings on the west bank revealed that strong water currents moving from the Zambezi river pouring into Lake Mwangi, river Lungevu and Lutembwe, among others, were now infested with weeds making it difficult for the locals to do fishing which was one of the major sources of income.
Mwangi area agricultural assistant, Patrick Muwichi, said that all the small-scale farming activities which used to be supplemented by fishing had now grounded to a halt due to the floods.
Mr Muwichi said the Mulunga Settlement scheme, which was the most productive farming area in Mwengi had almost all its crops destroyed.
He said that most of the few peasantries who owned some cattle have had their livestock dying due to the deadly contagious Bovine Pleural Pneumonia (CBPP).
"We are now facing a situation where the people can not grow their own food.
We can not communicate with other places within the west bank and the rest of the country because the only tool of communication in the area the radio message is no longer receiving signals due to the heavy rains," Mr Muwichi said.
He said the coming in of the Operation Saviour has mitigated the effects of the floods on the lives of the affected people in Zambezi and Chavuma districts.
He said that Operation Saviour had brought relief to the people who now feel that they belong to a country, which has a Government, which cares for its citizens regardless of where they were.
Mr Muwichi said: "As people affected by the floods on the west bank we salute the brave men and women in uniform who are risking their own lives dropping relief food and other supplies from moving planes and helicopters making sure that we survive. Operation Saviour has truly saved our lives."

Original_Search_Title: Fuel Shortages, Stark Realities And Present Challenges (2)
Headline: Nigeria: Fuel Shortages, Stark Realities And Present Challenges (2)
Body_Text:
THE government and NNPC have since continued to plead and preach that the concerns of these aggrieved communities can be addressed through dialogue and constructive discussions and not by confrontation and destruction. The issue of vehicular pressure, in absolute terms, is very real and worrisome. Today, in Nigeria, the ambition of every young man or woman is to be a car owner. This is a legitimate desire though, as a result of poor development of reliable mass public transportation. The rail system in Nigeria which was developed by the colonial masters mainly for the evacuation of agricultural produce and minerals in the 20s has received unimaginable and scandalous neglect by successive Nigerian governments since independence in 1960. Same goes for the waterways and the airways in which basic and necessary facilities are poorly developed, rendering them ineffective for mass commuting from location to location. As a result, virtually all movements have to rely on land vehicles plying ill-developed roads.
In most other countries of the world, both developed and developing, effective mass transportation is accomplished by way of efficiently run rail-line network systems. A trip to some other West African countries will highlight the importance of the rail system. In East Africa, we are all familiar with the Tanzanian or Kenyan rail wonder, which traverses mountainous and remote regions, making travels for the citizens less hazardous and cumbersome. And we dare not to talk of the highly sophisticated South African rail system.
In India, we are told that the rail system in that country caters for about 16 million passengers on a daily basis. Japan, on its part, is known for its very fast and efficient rail network which caters for its illustrious and precision conscious workforce. In the UK and other European countries, the underground rail system network and the overland train and tram systems are remarkable for easing public mass transportation. Hence, most private vehicles are not routinely put on the road because of preference for the rail system. Professors, top government functionaries and celebrities can all be seen boarding these trains as they are comfortable and efficient and timely. But, since the 80s, we have dined and wined with the Indians and the Chinese over our rail system and recently, some other groups have been invited for the resuscitation of this same Nigerian rail system. In Lagos, we started hearing of the introduction of the Metro-line since the late 80s but up till this moment, no significant progress has been recorded. Instead, we have continued to rely on "tuke-tukes" (mini buses) which, at best, convey 14 to 24 persons, as being our own understanding of mass transit. What a misnomer! We cannot continue like this if we must improve on the welfare and comfort of the citizenry with regard to transportation.
There are obvious challenges if we must embrace the culture of care for the average Nigerian who suffers unquantifiable losses as a result of fuel shortages or for the Nigerian who cannot afford to buy his or her own personal vehicle and pay for roadside fuel at whatever cost. Tackling these challenges headlong will contribute towards reducing the vehicular pressure on fuel consumption. We must therefore begin to focus on those factors responsible for both seasonal and impromptu petroleum products shortages. We must again begin to remind ourselves of the current global trend in sourcing for alternative sources of energy, especially renewable energy sources such as biomass, solar and hydraulic, wherever we find them available and applicable.
These are challenges that are readily surmountable once the political will is mustered. Our refineries must be maintained adequately so as to function optimally and yield products as per design. In the recent past, some Nigerians were granted licenses to build refineries. The licenses must be encouraged to build and the refineries on stream. In the event that there is need for importation to augment local production, opportunities should be given to the oil majors with wide international connections to assist in massive importation, to ensure that the landing cost is comparatively reasonable. But, above all, there must be the concerted effort to seek ways of introducing alternative products which will be environmentally friendly and cost effective.
The NNPC must be commended for its efforts towards diversifying fuel energy sources. Over a decade ago, NNPC introduced gas powered cars at the local trade fairs in Lagos, Enugu and Kaduna. The CNG (compressed natural gas) project is to convert cars run on petrol to natural gas. It has proven to be more fuel efficient and more engine friendly. Of course, we all know that gas is more abundant in Nigeria than crude oil and it is safer for our delicate environment, therefore, this option will reduce the dependence of all car users on petrol for vehicular travels.
Bio-fuels are receiving global attention as alternatives to petrol and in Nigeria, we have the opportunity to go into bio-fuels production. In the past two years, the NNPC has been championing projects in this direction. With the creation of a Renewable Energy Division (RED), manned by seasoned and articulate engineers, reasonable progress has been achieved in such a short time. In the main, local farmers are being encouraged to go into mechanized farming of cassava and sugar cane for conversion to ethanol.
It is quite obvious that the concern posed by fuel shortages is being vigorously addressed by the government and NNPC. Credit must be given to the originators of the petroleum products mega-stations which are blazing the trail of honest calibration and price compliance, to enable Nigerians acquire the culture of price discipline. Recently, also, floating mega-stations were introduced for the riverine areas of the Niger-Delta creeks, which hitherto could not be provided with petrol stations on land. When fully deployed, these floating stations will alleviate the age-long difficulties experienced by the people of this region. Through dialogue and collective discussions, the issue of sustainable community development of the Niger- Delta region could be accomplished, thereby eliminating the sabotage of facilities and disruption of operations.
Existing refineries must be maintained and be in top form for full production, while new ones in the pipeline are brought on stream. There should be appropriate and affordable pricing so that the temptation to make quick money is discouraged. Above all, government must ensure that mass transportation is given priority attention in the transport ministry by way of resuscitating and modernizing the rail system, nation-wide. When all these measures are put in place, mass movement of people, products and goods will become more effective and efficient, and at the same time discourage individuals from relying on personal transportation efforts. It is my conviction that all these will check fuel shortages and allow government focus on and tackle urgently needed infrastructural development of our nation.

Original_Search_Title: Pork in the Sudan
Headline: Uganda: Pork in the Sudan
Body_Text:
Kampala — This is something different. You actually travelled all the way to Sudan in search of pork?
No, we did not get into the Sudan itself, but we were pretty close. I would venture to say that we were less than an hour's drive from the border.
Getting to Classic Cuts is a bit of a gamble. It is one of those places that only the locals appear to know about because getting there from the central business district of Arua requires a person to have a degree in Geography. It is all about turning left, then right, then over a small hill and the farther you drive out, the more you ask yourself whether there is indeed a pork joint ahead.
And once you get there?
The first impression you get is that you are pulling up at a homestead. The second impression you get as you clamber out of the car is that the folk of the homestead are taking an afternoon nap.
And the third impression you get when life begins to stir is that this is not a pork place, but most certainly a homestead and that the man of the homestead is hosting a barbecue for his friends.
Talk us through the place.
Just picture a homestead. That is exactly what Classic Cuts looks like. There is a main house that is the focal point of the place and right in front of it are a number of trees under which the patrons sit.
Behind the main house are the kitchens - one for preparing the pork and the other a modern kitchen where they manufacture their own sausages and to oue surprise, beef burgers.
I presume you placed orders for kilos upon kilos of pork?
That we did considering the size of the group and I am sure that there were well over 10 kilos of special roasted pork. Way before the pork was served, by the smell emanating from the kitchen, it was obvious we were in for a treat. They did not disappoint for when put before us the pork looked very succulent. Once we started eating, all was well. The pork was very tender, in fact they would give Zanzi in Ntinda a run for their money. We then washed down the pork with a couple of beers and were set for the rest of the day.
THE GOOD POINTS: The pork without a doubt!
THE BAD POINTS: Remembering how to get there, as it is quite a drive out of Arua town.
THE PRICES: Beers cost sh1,500. Special roasted pork is at sh4,500 per kilo while ribs and roasted rib chops go for sh3,000 per unit. Extras such as cassava, avocado, tomatoes and salads will set you back sh1,500. Whole chicken costs sh10,000 while drumsticks, the breast and the wings (500gms) go for sh6,000. Beef burgers cost sh5,000.
THE KABOOZI: "How close are we to Sudan?"
THE STAFF: Not exactly the best, as they seamed to be made up of family members.
THE TOILETS:Pit latrines, so I guess I need not go any further on them.
THE CROWD: On the day we were there, it was full of MTN staff: PRO Tina Byaruhanga, Chris Ireland, Robert Afema and Guspa Mbowa, to name a few.
Classic Cuts is located in Sudan Zone, Mvara. Open all week from noon till customer demands. For reservations: 0752612573

Original_Search_Title: Butare Market Vendors Cry Foul Over Poor Sanitary Conditions
Headline: Rwanda: Butare Market Vendors Cry Foul Over Poor Sanitary Conditions
Body_Text:
Huge — Vendors and residents in Butare Town, Huye District are complaining of poor sanitary conditions in Butare Market, currently under rehabilitation. Several vendors who talked to The New Times complained that since the district authorities started rehabilitating the market last month, sanitary conditions inside the market have drastically deteriorated. According to the vendors, many of them have since either been forced out to trading in the street on the outskirts of the market or squeezed to one side of the market, making it very difficult to effectively supervise cleaning.
"The situation in the market is very bad and may result into an outbreak of diseases. Trading in the market is uncontrolled and everything is scattered with no one supervising the cleaning of the market," Vedaste Muvara, a vendor, told The New Times.
He added: "It's difficult for cleaners to sweep in such an overcrowded place."
Many other traders say the market's poor sanitary condition is due to overpopulation in areas that are not under reconstruction.
"They used to splash water in the market before sweeping, but today they don't due to the overpopulation," said Jean Hategeka, who sells clothes in the market.
He said there is a dusty smell and it is too hot in the market as vendors have little space for their activities.
But traders say the most challenging sanitary problem for them is lack of toilets.
"Imagine a whole market has no toilets, and this complicates the whole situation.
Those that were used before were too old and are being rehabilitated," a tomato vendor, Mariya Mukaneza, said.
Vendors said they are very much concerned, calling on the district authorities to wind-up the rehabilitation works to save their businesses and lives.
Another problem, they say, is the new stands in Rwabayanga Avenue near the market created by some vendors who failed to get space in the main market.
At Rwabayanga traders' stands which are near the market's main dustbin, women are selling maize and cassava floor in very bad conditions.
Some stands are very dirty, but foodstuffs and other products for sale are still placed on them.
"You can see, we are selling our goods near the dustbin and this affects our clients. Only poor people are buying our food," said Denise Uwitonze.
She said the district allowed them to work from Rwabayanga as they await completion of the reconstruction works at the market.
Uwitoze further said that the authorities are even demanding taxes for her previous stand in the main market so that she could occupy it when the reconstruction is complete.
Market cleaners hired by Huye District authorities concurred that the major cause of the hygiene problems is overpopulation in the market. According to one cleaner only identified as Mukama, the market was clean before the rehabilitation works started.
"But for us we do our best. Only that people continue dirtifying the market," she said.
Eric Uwimana, another cleaner, said good hygiene cannot be expected in the market unless the rehabilitation is completed.
When contacted, the district official in charge of Infrastructure, Jean Marie Vianney Munyaneza, said the first phase of the market's rehabilitation will be completed by next month. He however called on vendors to ensure proper sanitation in the market.
He said the completion of the first phase will help the market have toilets again, bring about 250 traders back to their previous stands and solve the sanitation problems.

Original_Search_Title: Normalcy Returns to Mogadishu Barracks
Headline: Nigeria: Normalcy Returns to Mogadishu Barracks
Body_Text:
Calm appears to have returned to the Mogadishu barracks after Wednesday's protest that resulted in the blockage of the Abuja-Keffi expressway and left motorists stranded for close to five hours.
Motorists fearing another round of protest yesterday morning left their homes very early in the morning to cross the army barracks.
This reporter observed orderliness in the payment process of the ex-soldiers when he visited the barracks yesterday morning. The payment staff appeared to be busy at their duty posts, while ex-soldiers as well as the next of kin of deceased soldiers queued to collect their pensions.
Those whose names were not in the lists were also seen writing complaint letters to the military pension's board.
Some of the ex-servicemen spoken to were very optimistic that the government would settle their pensions before the end of next week.
"We believe that by next week our entire pension would be given to us," a retiree, Corporal Samson James said.
Other retirees said they were giving the government another chance as any disappointment would lead to a great protest.
"If by any chance government faults in its promise to pay us before next week Friday then they should expect another greater protest," one of them said.
Retirees yesterday lamented their living conditions as some of them said they have been sleeping in the open at Mogadishu barracks since January 8 and have exhausted all the money they came with.
Mohammed Muta, a retiree said he has been feeding on fruits from the bush since he ran out of money while retired Master Sergeant Agono Samuel said most of the retirees, including himself, go to the bush to uproot cassava from people's farms in order to feed.
"We roast these cassavas and eat them like that just to survive," he said.
Samuel used the opportunity to take the reporter round the barracks premises to show how they slept and fed in the open.
"If you visit the military clinic inside the barracks, you would see a lot of sick ex-soldiers there," he said.
He lamented further that he has lost his job in Aba because of the number of days he has spent in Abuja trying to get his pension.
It would be recalled that when the defence minister visited the ex-soldiers on Wednesday morning he expressed shock at their plights and promised to see President Obasanjo over the issue.
The minister, Ambassador Thomas Aguiyi Ironsi, also directed the chairman of military pension's board to ensure that the ex-soldiers as well as the next of kin deceased soldiers are given water to drink.

Original_Search_Title: Manufacturing Falls Behind
Headline: Namibia: Manufacturing Falls Behind
Body_Text:
Windhoek — The manufacturing sector needs to increase its share of the Gross Domestic Product (GDP) to 30 percent for Namibia to achieve its targeted economic growth of 7 percent.
The concern remains that the current 10.7 percent that the sector contributes is lower than what is needed for the country to achieve its developmental goals as stated in the country's road map - Vision 2030.
The Vision 2030 document expects the manufacturing and services sectors together to contribute 80 percent of Namibia's GDP, and the export of largely processed goods to account for not less than 70 percent of Namibia's total exports.
But since 16 years ago, Namibia has had very little industrial growth, so much so that it continues to import most manufactured products, mainly from neighbouring South Africa, because the sector is characterized by structural weaknesses and operational constraints that include high input costs relating to electricity, transport and port charges.
"The manufacturing sector has an unusually low share of Namibia's national output, employment and exports compared to countries such as Singapore, South Africa and Ireland," says a summary paper, "Assessing the Potential of the Manufacturing Sector in Namibia".
The report, compiled by the Research Department of the Bank of Namibia, was to identify and assess potential products within the sector that could increase growth and also to suggest possible policy options to increase manufacturing output to achieve the goals of Vision 2030.
The bank conducted research and field surveys with selected government ministries and agencies, the private sector and 26 manufacturing companies in Windhoek, Arandis, Swakopmund and Walvis Bay.
The study identified high input costs, availability of quotas for the fishing industry, low throughput for the meat processing industry and unfair competition from well-established South African companies as some of the constraints facing the local manufacturing sector.
Other problems facing the sector include a small domestic market, which leads to the lack of economies of scale, low levels of productivity, the negative impact of HIV/AIDS, lack of advertising opportunities for Namibian manufacturers, unavailability of highly skilled personnel and the processing of and acquiring of work permits.
The BoN paper identified new products which Namibia could consider producing, including plates, pencils, desk chairs, large-scale tiles, marble, granite, karakul pelts, mineral water bottles, processing Devil's Claw, cassava for fuel production, metal products, auto components, unique wood, palm and clay, ready-made fish meal and leisure garments and many others.
