From the Kamwokya Town Centre entrance, you would have to count five joints to your right to get to Guzzlers. Though quite small, Guzzlers also hosts football fans to its 30-seater interior with games on Dstv.
Just at the entrance, is a muchomo roasting point for different types of meat, and this supplies to all the hangouts in the centre. Sanitation standards are commendable save for the noise at night, which culminates from different kinds of music being played from the different joints.
Whereas there is security in the confines of the centre, you might need to take care of the security of your car if you park outside.

Original_Search_Title: Cassava Producers Cry Out, Say Glut is Back
Headline: Nigeria: Cassava Producers Cry Out, Say Glut is Back
Body_Text:
CASSAVA Produce Promoters and Exporters Association of Nigeria (CASPPEAN) has raised an alarm over the resurfacing of Cassava glut in the country, even as it said that access to credit windows for its members should be looked into by the Federal government.
In separate appeal letters to the President Obasanjo by the Minister of Agriculture and that of State for Agriculture, that were made available to Vanguard in Abuja, the association, which noted that the conditions for accessing these facilities were too difficult for existing and intending entrepreneurs, said the demand for collaterals that centre on properties with Certificate of Occupancy situated in urban areas are scaring and frustrating.
According to them in a letter signed by its President, Markus Magaji, "for example, the demand for collaterals which centre on landed properties with Certificate of Occupancy situated in urban areas, not even just urban areas, but their emphasis are on State capitals. For where an entrepreneur has a Right of Occupancy, such an instrument is never accepted by banks as collateral".
Mr. Magaji, who called for a presidential directive to banks to honour any Right of Occupancy issued by any arm of government as collateral for the approval and disbursement of credit facilities, said there was the need for a presidential directive to State governors to process and approve applications for Certificate of Occupancy for industrial purpose within thirty days from the date of submission. He added that a presidential indemnity should be issued to banks for any application for industrial project that is considered viable.
According to the association, if there must be growth in the production of cassava in the country, there was the need for government to makeprovision for electric transformers which should be made available to industrial areas or project sites geared towards ensuring stable power supply, even as it called for the involvement of all cassava processors in backward integration as it would allow for the setting up of buffer farms to augment cassava supply to their processing plants.

Original_Search_Title: Daniel's Agenda for the Future
Headline: Nigeria: Daniel's Agenda for the Future
Body_Text:
Lagos — While on the campaign trail three and a half years ago, Governor Gbenga Daniel of Ogun State launched his Contract with Ogun People. It was a blue print for introducing a new phase of development into the State. Indeed it covered the various sectors and spoke about much-needed developmental initiatives as well as a new style of governance- 'Business Unusual'. The underlying philosophy of the document was to transform Ogun State into an industrial hub through the creation of investment opportunities and promotion of socio-economic infrastructures. It also aimed to tap into the large reserve of natural and human resources as vehicles towards stimulating economic growth.
A review of the past three and a half years and indeed from the facts on the ground, it is clear that the promises contained in the Contract with the Ogun People have undeniably been fulfilled and in some sectors the Administration has surpassed expectations. The first sign of transformation in Ogun State begins along the Lagos-Sagamu Axis of the Lagos-Ibadan Expressway. Travellers can't but notice the activity that's springing up on the way into the heartland of the nation. These days' private developers are tapping into the vision for the State and leading the way in creating industrial and residential schemes. Private sector initiatives that have responded to the Administration's development initiatives include cocoa and cassava processing, rice farming, ceramic production, new towns development, estate development, power producers and ICT park developers.
The creation by the Administration of an enabling environment for private entrepreneurs to thrive in is a clear demonstration that it is not oblivious to the pivotal role which it- the administration- has to play in the scheme of things. Within the State, the Government has continued to use infrastructural development to drive its vision to usher in industrialisation and commerce. These include road construction and rehabilitation, installation of streetlights, tackling urban and rural water supply etc. Other projects that have been initiated by the State Government to drive development, employment generation and wealth creation include the Gateway Stadium, Ijebu-Ode, Olumo Rock Tourist Complex, OK Free Trade Zone, the Agro-cargo Airport , Gateway Trailer Park etc.
A sectoral analysis shows that the administration has recognised the benefits to be made from placing emphasis on sectors such as housing, education, and roads etc as key drivers of development. In the housing sector, this is exemplified by several residential schemes that have sprouted up in various parts of the State under public-private initiatives.
It is important to note that the spate of developmental projects in the State can be clearly identified within the context of life changing projects and life sustaining projects. While the Administration continues to focus on the life sustaining projects that would drive development and promote industrialisation and commerce, it has not excluded of the so-called traditional life changing projects which meet the everyday needs of the people, from its development agenda.
From the available facts, it is also very clear that Governor Daniel has also demonstrated that governance is all about stewardship towards the people. A good example of this lies in the widely-acclaimed public budget hearings. The open budget process which the State Government has adopted ensures that its budgets are truly people-oriented. It is on record that this approach is conducted through consultation sessions with stakeholders round the State. The Sessions enable the people of Ogun State to have a say in the budget process and sets a model of participatory democracy.
This approach which has been adopted from the beginning of the Daniel-led administration recently received an added dimension. The State Government introduced what it termed a Budget Breakdown Session in which it disclosed to the members of the public detailed information on the 2007 Budget tagged Budget of Economic Independence. Again, the session was attended by members drawn from various walks of life including corporate world and other stakeholders who came to listen to the outline and directing principles of the Budget.
It is instructive to note that Governor Daniel has certainly refused to make assumptions about the needs of the people. Rather he embarked on a Community Development Assessment tour- similar to his 2002 Ward to Ward tour- of the State's 236 electoral wards to listen again to the concerns of the people and assess their needs once again. The outcome of this assessment has been carefully woven into the vision Daniel has for the State and the result is the Agenda for a Secured Future.
What is the difference between the Contract with the Ogun People and the Agenda for a Secured Future? Both documents project a vision to "broaden the economic base of Ogun State under an innovative and effective government that is business- friendly; collaborates with its people; and responds to the needs of stakeholders". The facts show that indeed the administration has successfully fulfilled its contract with the people and has actually in several cases initiated projects that were not originally included in the Contract. Several new projects include the establishment of Ogun State Employment Programme, four ICT-based institutions, Gateway Bitumen Company, Gateway Fertiliser Company etc. These initiatives are all part of a broader plan to create a new independent Ogun, one that thrives on industrial development; a State that partners with the private sector and is ultimately not dependent on the Federal Allocation.
The Agenda for a Secured Future goes a step further. It aims to build on and consolidate the gains made in the past three and a half years. It will certainly be taking off from a solid foundation. In the words of Governor Daniel the Administration has a clear vision to "construct a lasting, magnificent monument that is sure to liberate our people from want, need, hunger and starvation". It essentially is the people's document with an underlying philosophy to "ensure that the people are healthy, well-educated, gainfully employed, and live happy and fulfilling lives."
How realistic is the Agenda? It certainly provides a very real, practical and forward-looking setting for achieving sustainable development. It preaches self-reliance and seeks to break into even more pioneering grounds and attain novel heights for the State. Above all, it opens doors to greater possibilities for the indigenes of Ogun State . If driven and managed with the same amount of zeal as its predecessor, the Agenda for a Secured Future is sure to be a winner.

Original_Search_Title: Fuel Shortages, Stark Realities And Challenges (2)
Headline: Nigeria: Fuel Shortages, Stark Realities And Challenges (2)
Body_Text:
Lagos — In most other countries of the world, both developed and developing, effective mass transportation is accomplished by way of efficiently run rail-line network systems. A trip to some other West African countries will highlight the importance of the rail system. In East Africa, we are all familiar with the Tanzanian or Kenyan rail wonder, which traverses mountainous and remote regions, making travels for the citizens less hazardous and cumbersome. And we dare not to talk of the highly sophisticated South African rail system. In India we are told that the rail system in that country caters for about 16 million passengers on a daily basis. Japan on its part is known for its very fast and efficient rail network which caters for its illustrious and precision conscious workforce.
In the UK and other European countries, the underground rail system network and the overland train and tram systems are remarkable for easing public mass transportation. Hence most private vehicles are not routinely put on the road because of preference for the rail system. Professors, top government functionaries and celebrities can all be seen boarding these trains as they are comfortable and efficient and timely. But since the 80s, we have dined and wined with the Indians and the Chinese over our rail system and recently some other groups have been invited for the revival of this same Nigerian rail system. In Lagos we started hearing of the introduction of the Metro-line since the late 80s and up to this moment, no significant progress has been recorded. Instead we have continued to rely on "tuke-tukes" [mini buses] which at the best, convey 14 to 24 persons, as being our own understanding of mass transit. What a misnomer! We cannot continue like this if we must improve on the welfare and comfort of the citizenry with regard to transportation.
There are obvious challenges if we must embrace the culture of duty of care for the average Nigerian who suffers unquantifiable losses as a result of fuel shortages or for the Nigerian who cannot afford to buy his or her own personal vehicle and pay for roadside fuel at whatever cost. Tackling these challenges headlong will contribute towards reducing the vehicular pressure on fuel consumption. We must therefore begin to focus on those factors responsible for both seasonal and impromptu petroleum products shortages. We must again begin to remind ourselves of the current global trend in sourcing for alternative sources of energy, especially renewable energy sources such as biomass, solar and hydraulic, wherever we find them available and applicable. These are challenges that are readily surmountable once the political will is mustered.
Our refineries must be maintained adequately so as to function optimally and yield products as per design.
In the recent past a number of Nigerians were granted licenses to build refineries, they must be encouraged to build and put them on stream. In the event that there is need for importation to augment local production, opportunities should be given to the oil majors with wide international connections to assist in massive importation, to ensure that the landing cost is comparatively reasonable. But above all, there must be the concerted effort to seek ways of introducing alternative products which will be environmentally friendly and cost effective.
The NNPC must be commended for its strive towards diversifying fuel energy sources. Over a decade ago, NNPC introduced gas powered cars at the local trade fairs in Lagos, Enugu and Kaduna. The CNG [Compressed Natural Gas] project is to convert cars run on petrol to natural gas. It has proven to be more fuel efficient and more engine friendly. Of course we all know that gas is more abundant in Nigeria than crude oil and it is safer for our delicate environment, therefore this option will reduce the dependence of all car users on petrol for vehicular travels.
Bio-fuels are receiving global attention as alternatives to petrol and in Nigeria we have the opportunity to go into bio-fuels production. In the past two years the (NNPC) has been championing projects in this direction. With the creation of a Renewable Energy Division [RED], manned by seasoned and articulate engineers, reasonable progress has been achieved in such a short time. In the main, local farmers are being encouraged to go into mechanized farming of cassava and sugar cane for conversion to ethanol. The ethanol is planned to be blended with petrol to produce a fuel that is more efficient and cleaner for our environment. The beauty of this project is its integration of agriculture and the petroleum industry for the resuscitation of our farming culture, enabling us to grow more food while at the same time reducing the pressure on petroleum products. Other long term benefits of this project include the production of bio-diesel from palm oil, to support our Small and Medium Scale Enterprises [SMEs], which are adversely affected by irregular and epileptic power supply.
It is quite obvious that the concern posed by fuel shortages is being vigorously addressed by the government and NNPC. Credit must be given to the originators of the petroleum products mega-stations which are blazing the trail of honest calibration and price compliance, to enable Nigerians acquire the culture of price discipline. Recently also floating mega-stations were introduced for the riverine areas of the Niger delta creeks, which hitherto could not be provided with petrol stations on land. When fully deployed these floating stations will alleviate the age-long difficulties experienced by the people of this region. Through dialogue and collective discussions, the issue of sustainable community development of the Niger Delta region could be accomplished, thereby eliminating the sabotage of facilities and disruption of operations.
Existing refineries must be maintained and be in top form for full production, while new ones in the pipeline are brought on stream. There should be appropriate and affordable pricing so that the temptation to make quick money is discouraged. Above all, government must ensure that mass transportation is given priority attention in the transport ministry by way of resuscitating and modernizing the rail system, nation-wide. When all these measures are put in place, mass movement of people, products and goods will become more effective and efficient, and at the same time discourage individuals from relying on personal transportation efforts. It is my conviction that all these will check fuel shortages and allow government focus on and tackle urgently needed infrastructural development of our nation.

Original_Search_Title: Rebel Activity Fuels Insecurity in Northeast
Headline: Central African Republic: Rebel Activity Fuels Insecurity in the Northeast
Body_Text:
Kaga-Bandoro — People in the town of Kaga-Bandoro in northeastern Central African Republic (CAR) continue to live in fear as the region remains volatile.
They claim they can no longer enter the town because soldiers mistake them for rebels.
"They killed two civilians at the market on the grounds that they were rebels," Col Jean-Christophe Bureau, the town's prefect, told IRIN on 17 January.
Bureau said civilians were helping the rebels. "The rebels are living with civilians in villages but villagers cannot reveal their presence to security forces," he said.
The insecurity is a result of an army crackdown in the area that began in October last year and is aimed at routing rebels loyal to the Armée populaire pour la restauration de la république et de la démocratie (APRD). The APRD has since spread its activities to Ouandago, 48km northwest of Kaga-Bandoro, and Gribingui, of which Kaga-Bandoro is the provincial capital.
Local aid workers say the attacks amount to human rights abuses.
"It is not the first time such killings took place in the town," a clergyman, who requested anonymity, told IRIN. He said a student had also recently been killed by a soldier who had escaped prosecution.
Even those needing to go to Kaga-Bandoro say they are too afraid to enter the town. Dimanche Dague, 21, from Goumourou, 30km northwest of Kaga-Bandoro, said: "I cannot go to Kaga-Bandoro for fear of being arrested by the army there."
Meanwhile, Mandogo Regis, 21, from Patcho, 25km northeast of Kaga-Bandoro, told IRIN that troops had spies at the entrance to the town to warn the military of people trying to enter.
Food stocks running low
According to the International Rescue Committee (IRC), villagers around the town do not have access to basic goods such as salt, sugar, medical care and food. The price of food and other goods has risen five-fold.
Civilians have been most affected by the conflict
"There is no meat in the village and we are relying on cassava leaves and other vegetables to survive," said Georges Yandiba from Zando, 15km northwest of Kaga-Bandoro.
He said the villagers' goats and other livestock had either been driven away by soldiers or eaten by rebels, and anyone found with meat was accused of supplying the rebels.
The IRC reported that 54 villages had been burnt down between October and January, displacing at least 20,000 people who have sought refuge in the bush. These people are exposed to malnutrition and water-borne diseases, and are dying of preventable and treatable diseases due to the lack of medical supplies.
One clergyman in Kaga-Bandoro told IRIN that villagers were forced to choose between two evils in the battle for survival.
"Despite harassment, civilians in villages prefer to stay with rebels rather than with regular troops," he said, adding that although the rebels were taking food from villagers, it was better than being shot at by the army.
Rebels in the northeast
The Union des forces démocratiques pour le rassemblement (UFDR) rebels are active in the northeast of the country. Last November, they captured five towns and controlled them for about a month before the regular army, backed by French troops, regained control. These towns are Birao, Sam-Ouandja, Ouanda-Djalle, Ouadda and Ndele. Like APRD rebels in the northwest, UFDR fighters are pushing for talks with the government over power-sharing in the CAR. They captured the towns to pressure the government to agree to their request for
Despite harassment, civilians in villages prefer to stay with rebels rather than with regular troops talks.
In one of the bloodiest attacks on the region, 15 school-children were allegedly killed in November last year in an air strike by French troops on Gordil, in Vakaga Prefecture. The French troops had been asked by the government to quell a rebellion in the area. It is believed they mistook the students for UFDR rebels.
Two students are still missing; another three survived the air strike with injuries.
In a separate incident in the region, at least 20 villagers in Gordil, who fled into the bush to avoid French air strikes, have disappeared.
"The people have seriously suffered the consequences of the rebellion," said Ousmane Ramadane, the interim mayor of Gordil.
He added that the army had also killed a former local member of parliament. Ramadane said the army had accused the former MP, Alias Zakaria, of providing financial support to UFDR rebels. However, the army has not officially commented on why the MP was killed.
"He was gunned down in the village in front of his relatives by the national troops," Ramadane said, adding that a further 11 people had been gunned down in Serekobo, in Vakaga Prefecture.
He went on to describe how military operations have disrupted education in the region as school-children hide in the bush with their families. As a result, 17 primary schools and one secondary school in Ouandja commune have closed. Crop production has also been halted.
"Since June last year, when we ran into the bush to escape violence by the national troops, we have abandoned our farms," Ramadane said. "We are going to run out of food and face starvation this year."
Health concerns
The lack of hospitals and drugs is another concern. "There is an acute problem as far as access to medical care for people in this region are concerned," Norbert Elounga, a doctor with the World Health Organization (WHO) said.
"The medical coverage is really poor, there are no medicines in hospitals and patients have to pay a lot of money to be treated," he added. He said children in the area had not received vaccines for the past couple of years.
We are going to run out of food and face starvation this year
The rebellion has also fuelled ethnic tensions, and fighting has begun between the Goula and Youlou ethnic groups. The Goula are blamed for the violence in the region.
"The national army arrived in Ouandja with some civilians of the Youlou ethnic group who located and burned some of the houses belonging to the Goulas," said local resident Abdoul Fadjir.
Diplomats have observed that the situation in CAR is similar to that in Sudan's Darfur region.
Homes in are systematically burnt down with people living in the open, without shelter, food, drinking water and basic health services.
The rebels and the army are also reported to use rape as a weapon. Pierre Raveneau, a local doctor, said rebels had raped eight women, and that a pregnant woman had been raped by four gangs.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Bread Price And Nigerians' Vulnerability
Headline: Nigeria: Bread Price And Nigerians' Vulnerability
Body_Text:
Lagos — Democracy is when the indigent, and not the men of property, are the rulers. Aristotle? In a democracy the poor will have more power than the rich, because there are more of them, and the will of the majority is supreme. Aristotle? I love Nigeria and it is a fact I never cease to highlight every-time that I can, though it is sad that most times Nigeria does not love me, or better put, the forces that make up Nigeria for reasons of greed, lack of direction, and sense of it, all combine to frustrate that relationship of love for nation. There are times one gets tired of writing, commenting, suggesting and discussing matters that are Nigeria and Nigerian, but I and many will not fail, if not for us but for the future, we shall not give up. I know a sizable number of Nigerians that do not read, talk, see or smell Nigeria, they have since consigned their beloved nation to the confines of a nation of lost hope.
For me and many, I believe that we can only keep giving the best that we can, and watch, before we pray that par-adventure a miracle is on the horizon. That perhaps, the indigent would rule, or have a say, and that possibly, the poor will afford bread on their table because they are deserving. The price of bread is the essence of this essay, ordinarily one would ask what has the price of bread got to do with Nigeria, in the light of other serious national issues that require discuss and solution to, one is talking of bread. The political firmament is hot with intrigues, betrayal, and many more and one chooses to discuss bread. Pardon me until we are done with this piece. I have decided to write on bread for as many reasons that we can find for the failure of the Nigerian State.
The price of bread in Nigeria helps us to understand the anatomy of vulnerability in Nigerians. few weeks back in delivering judgement, the presiding Judge that was in charge of the Reverend King saga in Nigeria made a comment in the over 50-page judgement that caught my attention and I have in the next few lines sought to use the term he used to chart with us.
The term he used was "vulnerable elements"....I would not quote him for fear of being misquoted, and for fear of contradiction, but in not too many words he said that Nigerians were largely made up of vulnerable elements and that is why people like Reverend King thrive in their so-called spiritual business. It is in this light that I proceed. The price of bread in the last few weeks has been on the increase and Nigerians have kept mute, bread is a staple food, a meal that is close to 90 per cent of Nigerians take every other day, from Aso Rock to Ajegunle, the only difference is the type of bread, but be it, wheat bread, oat bread, cake bread, buttered bread or the popular Agege bread, all is bread, a friend would say. The rising cost of this food item can be traced to many factors, and they include the rising cost of flour, the cost of production, in a system where the basics such as electricity, transportation are all but operating in a scatter-scatter fashion. Also at this point is the question of what has government done...nothing is the answer.
On ground is the fact that sometime not far away in other to find a solution the government initiated the cassava flour project with research institutes like the Raw Materials Development Company and the Federal Institute of Raw Materials Research but after millions were expended, it all ended up as zero, all zero and because of the vulnerability of Nigerians nothing happened, no one was brought to book for failure.
In Algiers, the Algerian capital, sometime ago, the price of same bread was increased and the citizenry took to the streets, the president of the nation had to officially go on air to announce a drop in price. And so the riots were quelled, mind me, I am not advocating a riot because of the increase of bread price in Nigeria, but I ask for how long and how far will our vulnerability go? Let's leave bread alone as the Holy Books say that, "man shall not live by bread alone..."
Sadly the price of bread has skyrocketed, the price of salt, rice, beans, maize, water, fuel, everything on the price index has recorded increases in percentages most times over 200-300%. But Nigerians move on doggedly, no one has thought it fit to do anything. In a few weeks we are expecting the launch of redesigned Naira notes and it is coming on the heels of the usual economics garb that means nothing to the ordinary man. Banks have merged, the cost of funding for small businesses has not dropped, all the promises associated with the consolidation and merger are still a mirage and vulnerable elements of the society bear the malfunction that our society has turned to. Only last night I sat up late to really get to the bottom root of the mathematics of increased salaries and I nodded my head at how vulnerable we are as a people.
If the government has increased wages and salaries by 15% of what benefit is it if the lowest paid civil servant on the Federal pay is 7500, that increase is barely 1000, and then all his allowances, and leave grant are consolidated, meaning that it is now included in the monthly take home pay rather than paid in bulk, what is the benefit when prices go up by 100, 200, 300% in the market, of what significance is it that transportation is up by more percentage, how does it translate to regular power supply when indeed supply has dropped and tariff has increased. In the market, market people have increased goods, so also services, as the activities of middlemen go into play, no one acts as a stop gap or regulatory body, so every body is seen as a federal worker whose salaries have been increased. The fuel situation is not about easing but sadly in our vulnerability Nigerians are not mourning the unthinkable price at which the commodity is being sold but we are requesting that the product be available and one wonders our line of reasoning.
The increased price of bread is a reflection of our national life, a national life where the basics of life is taken for granted and no one shows concern. When the price of bread increases incessantly and we keep quiet, it shows our state of amnesia as a people. Prominent persons are murdered and the story ends happily thereafter, and millions of ordinary men die and in Shakespeare's words not even the star notices their demise to accidental discharge of police bullets, bullets of assailants called robbers, and not forgetting those that end up on the other side of life due to the insensitivity of leadership that is supposed to fight their cause. The Chima Ubani of these world died on the altar of fighting increased fuel price, today we do not even see the fuel. Bola Ige died and till today it is still politics as to who was responsible, and as vulnerable as we are, it's business as usual. It is all like the increase of price for bread, it just comes and slips by us, we hardly notice, we are mute, deaf and blind to these events and consciously and unconsciously they go a long way to moulding the shape of our society.
Our vulnerability has given rise to the likes of Reverend King that promise all sorts of paradise in guise when it is only obvious that hell beckons, many Kings are out there exploring single girls desperate for a husband, and many a husband and wife in dare want of babies, we cannot throw away the vulnerable millions who beseech the mosque, church and all manner of places just to hear a somebody shout hallelujah by the grace of Almighty and pass the offering plate one more time. In analyzing the anatomy of our vulnerability, the same reason why Obj was good for the nation is the same that makes Buhari good, and mind me no offense meant and no judgement either, but let us not forget that we were warned of the implications of electing Obj in 1999, those that overlooked were reminded in 2003, but as we remained vulnerable we are given the punishment of watching the transformation of Gabriel into a Judas, for all that General Obasanjo stood for that was good, where is it today.
Our vulnerability almost saw the emergence of an IBB. Our vulnerability as a people see us prescribe Andrews Liver Salt for headaches, and Panadol brand of paracetamol for stomach disorder, we get it all wrong, a man without attending a law school practices because in Nigeria it is about being smart and he gets away, a mad man wears white apron and officially kills because he is allowed a scissor in the nation's general hospital. Only months back the body of a top state government functionary was declared missing in the morgue in the National Hospital...In Jos Plateau, the Deputy Vice Chancellor of a Federal University has been missing without trace for over three months now, do wonders end, no they do not, especially when the people remain in a state of paranoiac vulnerability, so anything can go.
Bread is common, so common that on the 1st of January while traveling, my car gave me problems in a village kilometers after Pambeguwa on the Kaduna-Jos road, all I could get was locally baked bread, it was obviously not made of flour, I guess maybe wheat, because it would be easily accessible in the village. The point I make here is that for N40 it was a sweet experience, and I topped with a bottle of Pepsi-cola drink, the mallam selling told me that the bread used to be sold for N20 and that the price was just increased, the villagers had no option, no one questioned the why of the increase. These days, the sight of several loaves of bread as a sign of safe journey is gradually fading as people manage to travel with the high cost of transportation, so the money for bread is just a frivolity not worth the pain. So we come back to kids at home with stories rather the Onithsa Queens Bread, nor the Kaduna Saba Bread of the past.
The sing-song "Buy Agege Bread, Bread Agege re" that woke many Lagosians in the morning may be a thing of the past just like the good old days, when the news vendor, with his horn, is now becoming extinct as people cannot afford the paper and rather would rent to read. In concluding I only recently decided to read all of Obasanjo's New Year, Independence and budget Speeches and I asked where is the Nigeria in those speeches, holding my jaws, I said how vulnerable can a nation be? And I can bet as he bids farewell, in our vulnerability as long as he did not break the nation, and as long as he leaves come May 2007, we care less, some are even suggesting we give him a nobel, just like Anini, and Adelusi the great armed robbers of the past deserve post humous merit awards.
After all, we give thieves chieftaincy, and honorary degrees in our vulnerability, if the price of bread is good. So we are confined to Buhari, Yar'adua, and co. and without our consent again. We cannot say that what we get we deserve neither can we say that we do not deserve we shall get. The price of bread has increased for the wrong reasons, nothing that goes up price wise in Nigeria comes down and that is the physics of Nigeria. The words of Abraham Lincoln is most deserving of my end-line "I care not much for a man's religion whose dog and cat are not the better for it". Of What benefit is a reform that cannot accommodate the price of bread. May the Almighty Allah be our provider and make us firm as a people to fight our cause ?

Original_Search_Title: Country Plans New Refineries with Venezuela, Iran
Headline: Nigeria: Country Plans New Refineries with Venezuela, Iran
Body_Text:
Lagos — Minister of Petroleum Resources, Edmund Daukoru says Nigeria is negotiating with several oil companies including state-run firms from Venezuela and Iran, on the establishment of two refineries that will cost about $6 billion.
"Iran and Venezuela are involved in these discussions. There are also majors (international oil companies) Total, Shell, and Chevron," he said.
The capacity of each refinery may be around 200,000 barrels. "We are at early stage ... We also have to get around the domestic situation. We have price caps here in Nigeria on diesel and gasoline and, obviously, that's a concern to investors," the Minister further said that India, which has already been looking at other energy investments in Nigeria, is also looking at the possibility of taking stakes in the refineries.
U.S. oil major Chevron Corp. (CVX), Anglo-Dutch Royal Dutch Shell PLC (RDSB.LN) and France's Total SA (TOT) are also considering investment stakes in the projects, Daukoru said.
The negotiations also entail discussions with some of the companies about building production facilities to convert cassava, a root vegetable that is a staple food in Nigeria, into ethanol.
Nigeria has long had insufficient crude refining capacity to meet domestic demand, forcing it to spend hundreds of millions of dollars a year on importing refined oil products.
But Daokoru stated that "We're hoping to have a decision by the end of the year (on who will build the refineries and when)...but it depends on how we resolve the domestic situation (with price caps)," he said.
According to him, the country's four state-run refineries have for years operated at reduced capacity, due poor management and maintenance and sabotage.
He said the country has tried to boost its refining capacity by requiring companies bidding in some of the country's recent oil exploration rounds to also invest in refineries.
Daukoru said he expected to announce in coming weeks a formal date for the country's next bidding round on oil exploration blocks.
The round will involve at least 20 onshore and offshore blocks, Daukoru said. In January, he said.
Nigeria planned a bidding round involving up to 60 oil blocks and also said talks were underway with India's state-run NTPC Ltd. on a deal involving a new power plant and Nigerian supplies of liquefied natural gas to India.
Nigeria's oil production in January averaged 2.1 million barrels a day, down from December, Daukoru said, without elaborating.
Nigeria produced about 2.25 million b/d in December, according to OPEC's monthly oil market report released in January.

Original_Search_Title: FG Re-Opens Sealed Flour Mills
Headline: Nigeria: FG Re-Opens Sealed Flour Mills
Body_Text:
The federal government yesterday in Abuja directed that the flour mills closed down by one of its agencies be reopened.
They were closed last year for not complying with government regulations that 5 percent of their products be made of cassava for the purpose of raising more market for cassava in the country. 14 flour mills were closed down between June to December last year.
Speaking to newsmen yesterday in a press briefing, the minister of commerce and industry Dr. Aliyu Moddibo Umar said the President Olusegun Obasanjo has given the millers more grace period to comply with the policy.
Dr. Moddibo said the millers now have: "up to 1st of April to comply otherwise they will be closed again." He said the policy is part of the reform program of the government to develop agriculture and generate employment for Nigerians.
The mills that will be reopened are Crown, Interstate, Standard, Dangote and Honeywell Flour Mills all in Lagos. Also to be re-opened are Ideal, Life, Niger and Bendel Flour Mills of Kaduna, Anambra, Edo states respectively.
The government's initial deadline for compliance to the policy was June last year. Standard Organisation of Nigeria and other stakeholders in the sector said it is a good development that millers are given more time to comply.

Original_Search_Title: Enveloping Ecology of Poverty
Headline: Nigeria: Enveloping Ecology of Poverty
Body_Text:
Abuja — There have been various attempts to deal with grinding poverty in Nigeria. But it seems the attempts have not in any tangible way yielded positive results, by way of bring succour to the people, in terms to concrete economic palliatives. This worrisome trend undoubtedly is a worrisome development, bearing in mind the fact that; the fundamental reason why people are in government is to see to the welfare and wellbeing of the masses. Although, this very position is indeed a very contentious one in Nigeria, where public office holders have abandoned the people and are helping themselves with public funds. And, as they are getting richer, the masses are being pauperized; a case in point is that of a prominent politician who could not boost N20, 000 in 1998, but today is perhaps the richest individual in the whole of Africa, courtesy of relentless, but clandestine pilfering of the resources of the state, yet he pretends he is sanctimonious wog.
In Nigeria today, the issue of poverty is a recurring decimal. Poverty has really become a sing-song. Spiritually poverty is a curse. Socio-economically, it is a state of extreme want, need and deprivation. When the present democratically elected government led by President Olusegun Obasanjo came on board in 1999, one of the cardinal programme and philosophy of his administration was poverty reduction which had by then become endemic. To this end, he created the Poverty Alleviation Programme (PAP) which became a lame duck, and at the end of the day N15billion was sunk into programme and nothing concrete came out of it. Hence, National Poverty Eradication Programme (NAPEP) was put in place; however, it has not been able to meet the aims and objectives for which it was created, instead, it has been a drain-pipe on government's lean purse.
This led the government to set up another poverty reduction initiative called Investment led Poverty Reducing Employment which has equally failed very woefully. The point here is that any government programme that does not have the people as the aim and essence will be nothing, but a tantalizing mirage. Yes, poverty in Nigeria did not start with the present government in 1999; it has been with us for quite a while now. For instance, in 1986, according to official figures, three million Nigerians were unemployed. Of these, those below 25 years accounted for 73.1 per cent, young school leavers accounted for the bulk of the employed with 60.3 per cent and 46.2 per cent in the urban and rural areas respectively.
The Gross National Product has been declining. The decline in the housing sector averages about 10 per cent, that of manufacturing 6.4 per cent and 5 per cent for construction, wholesale and retail sectors.
However, the agriculture sector has been improving by an annual average of 2.2 per cent. Expressed in real terms, by an average of 36.7 per cent. No doubt is the largest producer of cassava, albeit, much of this is lost to waste because of the absence of good storage facilities. In spite of this, the country go a begging to imperialist creditors for debt forgiveness that was partially given on very stringent conditions that were not favourable to the country, even at that, the present government has stated accumulating another rounds of debt, the so-called economic reform policy of the government is not working because the people are not benefiting from it in any way, because, poverty looms large in the country. Therefore, the reforms should be reformed. It is also important to note that, the United Nations Development Programme(UNDP) in 1995 estimated that at least half of Nigeria's teeming population are regarded as undernourished, the 71 million ill-fed Nigerians constitutes 25 per cent of the 200million under nourished Africans alone. It is equally important to note that, the Gross Domestic Product(GDP) of Nigeria in 2001 was $41billion, that of South Africa was $113billion, Malaysia $188billion, Korea $9,460, United Kingdom $25,120 and United States of America $34,000.
It is very clear therefore that, Nigeria is not only grossly underdeveloped, it is poverty ridden. Hence, it would be proper the government of the day could create conditions in which Nigerians could be very productive and self-employed. All the present government is arguing about foreign investment but Nigerians should be able to invest in their own economy that is when real development can actually take place. However, this is not the case, for example, according to the UNDP report 2002, 70 per cent out of every 120 Nigerians live below the poverty line. Also, the report emphasized that, Nigeria is one of the 20 poorest countries in the world. Nigeria also ranked 57th in terms of Gross National Product (GNP).
Equally, the country is 124th in gender related development and 151st in Human Development Index. Nigeria's contribution to global Gross Domestic Product being mere 0.22 per cent and also ranking 187th in GNP per capita income. In the Human Development Index report 2002, out of the 173 countries whose living conditions were assessed, Nigeria ranked 148th behind Madagascar and Haiti. The African country with the best condition is the Island of Seychelles which occupied the 47th position in the reports ranking, coming a distant second was Libya in the 64th position, while Cape Verde, Algeria and South Africa were ranked 100th, 106th and 107th respectively. According to official report conducted in 2002, it was reported that 91 per cent of Nigerian household are poor, in fact, 89 million Nigerians are living the international poverty benchmark of $1 a day. The poverty level figure which translates to about 70.2 per cent of the country's population makes Nigeria the country with the highest concentration of people living in extreme poverty in Africa.
Also, the country's health system performance was ranked 187th amongst the 191 member states by the World Health Organization (WHO), and public expenditure on health in Nigeria is less than $8 per capita, compared to the $34 recommended internationally. This is supported by the worrisome 2004 Human Development report that placed Nigeria in the 151st position out of the 177 ranked in order of poverty. Also, in the latest World Bank report Ghana is rated far ahead of Nigeria in such areas as corruption, rule of law, good governance, infrastructural development, democratic ideals/ethos etc.
Yet, the 1999 constitution of the Federal Republic of Nigeria provides in section 16(1b) that, government shall control of the national economy in such manner as to secure the maximum welfare, freedom and happiness of every citizen on the basis of social justice and equality of status and opportunity, while section 16(2d) states suitable and adequate shelter, suitable and adequate food, reasonable national minimum living wage, old age care and pensions, and unemployment, sick benefits and welfare of the disabled are provided for all citizens.
But government has not been able to do any of this, and since 1999 to date, Nigeria has never had it so good, in terms of revenue from oil as result of upsurge in prices at the international market. So, how can Nigeria that is the sixth largest oil producing country in the world be one of the poorest countries in the world? It means therefore that the Obasanjo's government has failed irredeemably. In the country today, it is indeed an enveloping ecology of poverty. Grinding poverty is ubiquitous and if adequate care is not taken, it can grind the country to a halt; hence, it is time to vote for a better Nigeria, for all Nigerians.

Original_Search_Title: Food Crisis Looms for 2 Million  Flood Victims, UN Warns
Headline: Burundi: Food Crisis Looms for 2 Million  Flood Victims, UN Warns
Body_Text:
Two million people hit by recent floods in Burundi need $12 million in urgent aid to tide them over until the end of the so-called 'lean season' in June and avert a food crisis, the United Nations World Food Programme (WFP) warned today.
"If we do not bring urgent help to the most vulnerable, the consequences could be devastating for the recovery of a country still weakened by 13 years of civil war," WFP Country Director Gerard Van Dijk said, noting that 2 million people represented 25 per cent of the small East African nation's total population.
"Given how long it takes to get food aid and other assistance to Burundi, we have no time to waste; we need international support now in order to help people until June," he added.
Last week UN agencies and non-governmental organizations (NGOs) appealed for $132 million for their work in Burundi during 2007, a figure that includes the present urgent requirement of $12 million.
An assessment in January led by WFP and the UN Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) found that heavy rains and floods had destroyed 50-80 per cent of the November harvest and much of January's crop of beans, sweet potatoes, maize, sorghum and rice across large parts of the country.
It recorded increasing use of survival strategies with some people cutting down to one meal a day and resorting to 'famine foods', such as cassava leaves or bitter banana. Mortgages of fields, the sale of assets and people being forced to leave their homes are worrying signs of an impending major food crisis.
The late arrival of rains at the end of last year combined with cassava disease and recent floods have had a devastating effect on the food stocks of the poorest families in Burundi. "All these elements are signs of a looming food crisis," Mr. Van Dijk said.
Due to a shortage of food stocks, WFP and its NGO partner, CARE, delivered reduced food rations to 400,000 people, most of them affected by the floods. WFP also had to cut rations by 25 per cent for refugees, returnees, Burundians expelled from Tanzania and children in its school feeding programme.
"The floods have hit the country at the worst possible time; although peace prevails, living conditions remain extremely difficult, with most Burundians having to focus on just meeting their basic daily needs," Mr. Van Dijk said.
In the most affected areas in the north and centre of the country, the traditional 'lean season,' when food from the last harvest runs out, began in September 2006 and will last until the end of June 2007, when the next harvest is due. Burundi is still emerging from the civil war that killed more than 300,000 people and displaced more than 1 million others.

Original_Search_Title: WFP & NGOs Warn of Looming Food Crisis Facing 2 Million Flood Victims
Headline: Burundi: WFP & NGOs Warn of Looming Food Crisis Facing 2 Million Flood Victims
Body_Text:
Bujumbura — WFP and non-governmental organisations warned today that two million people hit by recent floods in Burundi need urgent assistance until June to avert a food crisis.
This follows last week's joint appeal for US$132 million by UN agencies and NGOs for their work in Burundi during 2007.
The assessment found increasing use of survival strategies with some people cutting down to one meal a day and resorting to 'famine foods', such as cassava leaves or bitter banana.
Mortgages of fields, the sale of assets and people being forced to leave their homes are worrying signs of an impending major food crisis.
Devastating combination
The late arrival of rains at the end of last year combined with cassava disease and recent floods have had a devastating effect on the food stocks of the poorest families in Burundi.
"All these elements are signs of a looming food crisis," said Gerard Van Dijk, WFP Country Director in Burundi. "If we do not bring urgent help to the most vulnerable, the consequences could be devastating for the recovery of a country still weakened by 13 years of civil war."
Two million people are 25 percent of the country's entire population.
Urgent needs
As part of the UN-NGO joint appeal, WFP urgently needs US$12 million to cover food aid requirements in Burundi until July.
Burundi's president has declared seven of the country's 17 provinces as disaster areas because of the damage caused by flooding.
Due to a shortage of food stocks, WFP and its NGO partner, CARE, delivered reduced food rations to 400,000 people, most of them affected by the floods. WFP also had to cut rations by 25 percent for refugees, returnees, Burundians expelled from Tanzania and children in its school feeding programme.
No time to waste
"The floods have hit the country at the worst possible time; although peace prevails, living conditions remain extremely difficult, with most Burundians having to focus on just meeting their basic daily needs," said Van Dijk.
"Given how long it takes to get food aid and other assistance to Burundi, we have no time to waste; we need international support now in order to help people until June."
In the most affected areas in the north and centre of the country, the traditional 'lean season' (when food from the last harvest runs out) began in September 2006 and will last until the end of June 2007, when the next harvest is due.
Civil war
Burundi is still emerging from 13 years of civil war that killed more than 300,000 people and displaced more than one million in the region.
WFP's NGO partners in Burundi as part of the recent UN appeal are: Solidarites, World Vision, CARE, German Agro-Action, Caritas Burundi, International Medical Corps and Catholic Relief Services.

Original_Search_Title: Census And the Igbo - Arguing the Wrong Points
Headline: Nigeria: Census And the Igbo - Arguing the Wrong Points
Body_Text:
Lagos — I would not be making this intervention were it not for what I have observed in the debate so far since the census figures were released. The reason is that we knew ab initio that the census was destined to be another fraud foisted on the nation to sustain a lie. The enumeration itself was a fiasco and we said so here. I have always wondered why we have a slide movie attitude to a larger national problem. Oh, today it is a disastrous national identity card, and we raise hell that it failed. Next, it is voters' registration, and we cry to high heavens that it is a fiasco. Ah ha!
The census comes with its historic lies and fraud and we split hairs about it. What of the election? When last did we have an undisputed election? Even the near perfect one in 1993 was annulled and the nation was dragged through needless agony for that. Why did Nigerians expect that the census would reflect correct population figures? We were all witnesses to the disaster that the headcount was. In any case, Nigeria itself is one huge fraud run by racketeers. Why then would the census meant to sustain the fraud be different, even if conducted by an Obasanjo? Would you sow yam and reap cassava?
I have been a bit concerned by the drift of the protest from the Igbo of the South-east. If care is not taken, the Igbo will play into the hands of their enemies. Many people have argued against the figure awarded to the South-east. They are right to say that the figure was absurd, like the figures awarded to other geo-political zones. These figures have no basis in geography or rational observation.
The enumeration, in the first place, was a fiasco, not withstanding that the Chairman of the National Population Commission, Mr. Samu'ila Makama, admitted that they captured only about 90 percent of the population. Let us even take his word for it that only 10 percent of the population was not counted. In matters like this, 10 percent error margin is very significant. We may ask to which geo-political zone was the uncounted 10 percent awarded? There is no arguing that the census figures were inaccurate. That is an established fact.
But what I feel concerned about is that the Igbo are not looking at the story the figures are telling us. In the first place we must all remember that the census did not set out to establish the population of each ethnic group in Nigeria nor did it set out to establish how many Christians, Animists or Muslims there are in Nigeria.
It was a very stridently agued issue before the exercise that ethnic and religious parameters be included in the headcount. But the government refused. The Ohaneze Ndigbo even threatened that the Igbo would boycott the census, if these two information were not included in the census. But suddenly, Ohaneze relented and became more interested in Obasanjo's third term project.
The point therefore is that the census figures are not ethnic mapping figures. You can reject it an inaccurate figure but do not imply that even the inaccurate figure ever intended to refer to the population of the Igbo, Yoruba, Hausa, Christian or Muslim.
The figure of 16 million awarded to the South-east, therefore, is not saying that there are 16 million Igbo in Nigeria. The figure, to my understanding is simply saying that only 16 million Nigerians are living in the South-east.
That figure also includes Yoruba, Hausas and other Nigerians living in the South-east and who were counted there. My family and I were counted in Lagos. So were Nigerians of other ethnic origin who harkened to Governor Bola Tinubu's appeal and stayed back in Lagos to be counted.
Therefore the figure that Gov Tinubu is contesting vis-à-vis the Kano figure, includes all of us who were counted in Lagos. The Kano figure, right or wrong, includes other Nigerians who were counted there. Therefore, it is wrong for the Igbo to infer that the 16 million refers to Igbo population in Nigeria. To so imply is dangerous in that Nigeria can run away with the impression that the Igbo have accepted that they are only 16 million and begin to use it. Whenever the Nigerian nation is bold enough to find out how many Igbo, Yoruba, Hausa, Christian or Muslim are in Nigeria it will have a headcount that would reflect so.
The more important question to ask is: why is it that Nigerians, including the Igbo, have found the South-east unattractive to live in? The answer is simple, especially if you notice that nearby Rivers has witnessed population increase due to the oil industry while the South-east has manifested a denudation (at least that is what the census figure is telling us). The South-east has no economy to sustain people. It is still regarded by the Nigerian state as a war zone and those who remain there are no more than war prisoners.
The policy of the Federal Government since the end of the civil war has ensured that that geo-political zone remained economically depressed and its citizens flee into exile in their own country. It began with the territorial amputation of Igbo land and sequestering of the oil-bearing lands and sea access in the care of the "minorities".
Only God knows how safe the oil wells taken from Igbo are today, but what that act ensured was that the economic activity that would have sustained the Igbo was now in a territory where they are regarded as strangers and their real estate regarded as "abandoned property". People go where there is an economy. How can anybody live in the South-east where there is no electricity, no road, no water, no industries, no jobs and no federal presence?
What Igbo leaders, if any group in Igboland can truly be so described, must do is to actively challenge the federal and state governments to make the South-east part of Nigeria a zone of economic activity. They can do this if the Igbo can make their votes a bargaining power, not what is thrown away on sentiment or to please middlemen and jobbers.
They must re-examine if the strategy of a big national party can make Igbo votes count in a skewed federation like Nigeria or whether such votes can serve better purpose if warehoused in a small party. In other words, the Igbo must take their destiny in their own hands and desist forthwith from continuing to be purchasable merchandise. As for challenging the figures of true Igbo population, that is something that can be done in less than three weeks at minimum cost.
There is no part of the world where the Igbo reside that they are not organized into unions. Through these unions and the Eze Igbos, the census of the Igbo outside Igboland can be obtained. In Igboland itself the aladinmas and awuruawus or amaramas can in two days collate the figures kindred by kindred. This is very important so that if Nigeria ever gets to counting the ethnic groups, there will be empirical basis to challenge the figures if they are suspected to be fictitious. For now, they should not ascribe to the census what it was not intended to be.

Original_Search_Title: Crash Kills Four Traders
Headline: Uganda: Crash Kills Four Traders
Body_Text:
Kampala — AN over-loaded truck over-turned and killed five people near Kahangye bridge on Thursday evening.
Thirteen others were seriously injured at the boarder of Rukungiri and Ntungamo districts.
Rukungiri CID chief Henry Mbabazi told journalists that the Fuso lorry which was carrying 26 passengers and sacks of rice, beans, cassava, peas, ground nuts, lost control as it was negotiating a corner before the bridge.
He said the lorry was coming from Bikurungu Market in Bwambara and was heading to Kabale.
The dead were three men from Kabale as well as a woman, Dainah Mwesigwa and her three-year-old son from Kazindiro, Bugangari in Rukungiri. The bodies were taken to Nyakibale Hospital mortuary.
The injured were rushed to Nyakibale and Mbarara regional hospitals in critical conditions.
Five people got minor injuries and three, including the driver, Dege Mishaga, escaped unhurt.
They were identified as Patrick Tukamuhabwa, 26, Gerevasio Kiteta, 33, Owen Tumuhimbise, 21, Ian Asiimwe, 16, Delison Musinguzi, 28 , Drake Twinamatsiko, 38, musinguzi Tumwine, 22 and Muhamood Bamuranga, 26. This is the third accident on the same spot in less than three weeks, due to a sharp bend at the spot.

Original_Search_Title: Over 2,000 Fulani Herdsmen Invade Nkwanta District
Headline: Ghana: Over 2,000 Fulani Herdsmen Invade Nkwanta District
Body_Text:
Nkwanta — The Nkwanta District Assembly in the Volta region is currently battling with Fulani herdsmen with over two -hundred thousand cattle, which move about indiscriminately and destroyed farms.
As a result, the Assembly through the support of the Regional Security Council, deployed the military and the police from Ho to the area to drive the Fulanis and their animals away, which was code named 'Operation Cow Leg'.
The Nkwanta District Chief executive, Hon. Joseph Booker Yaw Denteh, who made this known to The Chronicle in an interview at Nkwanta said the presence of the Fulani herdsmen in the area posed a major security threat to life and property.
The DCE said due to the large number of animals, the security agencies were finding it more difficult to drive them away but subsequently had to adopt tougher infantry tactics to overpower the herdsmen who normally give commands to the 'army of cattle' to attack the security officers .
Mr. Denteh said the Assembly in collaboration with the security personnel undertaking the operation, embarked on taking inventory of farms destroyed, evaluated them and made the herdsmen to pay for them before they were finally driven out of the District to the Republic of Togo.
He said the operation was becoming more difficult each day because the Fulani herdsmen were at separate locations and identification of such locations was very difficult as the military at times spent the whole day without signs of their presence.
Mr. Denteh said the Assembly therefore cautioned chiefs and people in the area to report the presence of the Fulani herdsmen to the security agencies and warned that any chief or group of people who would collaborate with the Fulani herdsmen and harbour them in any part of the District would be dealt with when caught.
The DCE explained that the activities of the Fulani herdsmen in the District was draining the scanty resources of the Assembly as monies that could be used to embark on development projects were being used to drive the herdsmen away.
Contributing, a farmer and victim of Fulani herdsmen at Kabiti, William Dzramado said the Fulani herdsmen invaded the village, destroyed crops like rice, cassava and potatoes adding that the nuisance caused by the Fulani herdsmen and threat of life compelled some of the people to abandon their villages to stay in other communities.
He said the Fulani herdsmen normally carry along knives, AK 47 rifles and other dangerous weapons, noting that anybody who complained about their activities was threatened with these weapons and at times the Fulani herdsmen chased them out of their farms to the village.
Mr. Dzramado explained that some of the affected villages where the Fulani herdsmen concentrated their activities like Teacher-Akroa, Nolikpui were so deprived that there were no access roads which made police officers feel reluctant to visit whenever cases of the activities of Fulani herdsmen were reported to them. He said the only solution to the plight of most of the people who could no longer suffer silently the activities of the Fulani herdsmen was to leave their villages, to Kpando-Torkor, Dzemeni and Atimpoku among others to start a new life.
Mr. Dzramado commended the Nkwanta District Assembly for its efforts to drive the Fulani herdsmen away and hoped that those who left their communities would come back when peace finally returns to the area.

Original_Search_Title: Fuel Shortages, Stark Realities And Challenges (1)
Headline: Nigeria: Fuel Shortages, Stark Realities And Challenges (1)
Body_Text:
Lagos — The issue of fuel shortages poses a great concern to the government, the Nigerian National Petroleum Corporation (NNPC) and indeed the entire Nigerian nation. This concern stems from the fact that Nigeria, as a country, is ranked 6th in the world crude oil production ladder. It therefore appears inexplicable that a situation such as this should befall our country, whereas less endowed nations do not experience same magnitude of shortages. To understand this, and we do need to, we must take a look at the wider picture and have a holistic consideration of the prevailing factors attributable to this unpleasant predicament. It is only when this assessment has been done that we can appreciate and tackle the challenges posed, and thus be in the position to reduce to the barest minimum any future occurrences.
Nigeria surfaced in the global petroleum scene in the late 50s with the discovery of oil in commercial quantities in Oloibiri, in the present Bayelsa State of the Niger Delta. This was after almost 50 years of initial detection of petroleum in the form of seepages in the coastal western Nigeria. Concerted efforts by the early explorers, namely Shell DÃ¡rcy, the precursor of the present day Shell Petroleum Development Company (SPDC), culminated in the production and first shipment of crude oil from Nigeria in 1958. This led to the attraction of other foreign based companies into Nigeria to make subsequent phenomenal discoveries. Prior to this period, petroleum products for domestic consumption, were mainly imported into the country by the colonial masters, utilizing revenue generated from agriculture and agricultural produce. It will be interesting and quite revealing to look back to check the records, for comparison sake today, to find out how many motor vehicles were plying our roads at that time. However, suffice it to state that relatively noticeable vehicular traffic was restricted to the big cities and these could be enumerated rather easily and readily.
As production levels of crude oil increased, so also was there an increase in the level of generation of oil based revenue and a concomitant quantum leap in economic growth and development. Many more agencies, organizations and individuals became more prosperous and affluent and could readily afford to import and utilize various forms of vehicles for business and pleasure. The consequent increase in the demand for petroleum products led to the realization of the then government that there was the need to refine crude locally to complement the importation of products which was then the norm. The first indigenous refinery was then built and commissioned in the mid 60s in Alasa-Eleme, near Por Harcourt, Rivers State. The end of the civil war in 1970 saw the sole dependence on petroleum as the prime revenue earner, attracting all and sundry to blue collar jobs and contracts seeking, with the abandonment of our traditional farming, fishing and other supposedly menial means of livelihood. The climax of the new life came with the 1974 nation-wide upward revision of salaries in the public and private sectors of the economy, which raised the appetite of the rural dwellers to join their urban cousins in search of the golden fleece. Thus commenced the urban drift and concomitant pressure on the cities, to accommodate both the employed and the unemployed, the busy and the loafing. This trend also led to a drastic change in life style and taste, particularly the introduction of emphasis on cars, flashy cars and more cars. The situation in Lagos became so scandalous that the government of the day had to ration the usage of cars by commuters by introducing the "odd and even" plate number phenomenon in order to alleviate unending traffic jam and chaos in that city.
While all this was going on, existing infrastructures and major means of mass transportation remained static whereas urban migration and opulent lifestyle continued to grow. The demand for fuel to cater for the explosion in vehicular traffic far outstripped the import level and what quantity the only available refinery then could supply. Thus in the late 70s, government saw the wisdom in NNPC management recommendation for the building of more refineries in the country in Warri and Kaduna. For the effective and efficient storage and distribution of products, NNPC also embarked upon the construction of depots all over the country, to receive petroleum products from the refineries via an interconnection of pipeline systems traversing about 6000km of terrain and communities.
These efforts on the part of government and the NNPC were quite commendable at the time and as could be noticed nation-wide, actually improved on products availability in the 80s. Another phase of severe products shortages appeared in the late 80s into the 90s, casting doubts on the efficacy of the palliative measures introduced by the NNPC. This necessitated the need to uncover the factors leading to the persistent scarcity in the midst of sustained importation and delivery from the four refineries.
The root cause analysis undertaken by the NNPC management in the face of these shortages revealed a cocktail of factors, chief of which included the following: smuggling, pricing, state of the refineries, pipelines vandalization and vehicular pressure.
Smuggling products from within Nigeria across to our less endowed neighbours was found to be lucrative, attractive and unfettered as a result of low pricing in Nigeria and higher prices elsewhere. It was thus a quick means of making money by unscrupulous Nigerians, a situation which was also bolstered by the porosity of our international borders.
The refineries, on their part, could not undergo the mandatory periodic turn-around maintenance as a result of budgetary constraints, which led to reduced production and dependence on higher levels of importation to meet local consumption.
This period also witnessed the expression of concern by the Niger Delta communities over their perceived neglect by previous governments, inspite of their region generating the oil wealth. Thus the youths took up arms against the industry operators whom they regarded as proxy for government, in their quest to share in the oil wealth. Their militancy resulted in the disruption of operations and the vandalisation of facilities, particularly crude oil pipelines and petroleum products delivery pipelines. Both facilities once vandalized, turned out to be easy access to petro-dollars as the stolen crude readily found its way into waiting barges heading for the high seas, and the products illegally tapped from the pipelines became an all-comers eldorado.
However, the vandalisation of such facilities has had in its wake unfortunate and regrettable loss of Nigerian lives, which must be discouraged by all and sundry. The government and NNPC have since continued to plead and preach that the concerns of these aggrieved communities can be addressed through dialogue and constructive discussions and not by confrontation and destruction.
The issue of vehicular pressure, in absolute terms, is very real and worrisome. Today in Nigeria, the ambition of every young man or woman is to be a car owner. This is a legitimate desire though, as a result of poor development of reliable mass public transportation. The rail system in Nigeria which was developed by the colonial masters mainly for the evacuation of agricultural produce and minerals in the 20s, has received unimaginable and scandalous neglect by successive Nigerian governments since independence in 1960. Same goes for the waterways and the airways in which basic and necessary facilities are poorly developed, rendering them ineffective for mass commuting from location to location. As a result, virtually all movements have to rely on land vehicles plying ill-developed roads.
In most other countries of the world, both developed and developing, effective mass transportation is accomplished by way of efficiently run rail-line network systems. A trip to some other West African countries will highlight the importance of the rail system. In East Africa, we are all familiar with the Tanzanian or Kenyan rail wonder, which traverses mountainous and remote regions, making travels for the citizens less hazardous and cumbersome. And we dare not to talk of the highly sophisticated South African rail system. In India we are told that the rail system in that country caters for about 16 million passengers on a daily basis. Japan on its part is known for its very fast and efficient rail network which caters for its illustrious and precision conscious workforce. In the UK and other European countries, the underground rail system network and the overland train and tram systems are remarkable for easing public mass transportation. Hence most private vehicles are not routinely put on the road because of preference for the rail system. Professors, top government functionaries and celebrities can all be seen boarding these trains as they are comfortable and efficient and timely. But since the 80s we have dined and wined with the Indians and the Chinese over our rail system and recently some other groups have been invited for the revival of this same Nigerian rail system. In Lagos we started hearing of the introduction of the Metro-line since the late 80s and up to this moment, no significant progress has been recorded. Instead we have continued to rely on "tuke-tukes" [mini buses] which at the best, convey 14 to 24 persons, as being our own understanding of mass transit. What a misnomer! We cannot continue like this if we must improve on the welfare and comfort of the citizenry with regard to transportation.
There are obvious challenges if we must embrace the culture of duty of care for the average Nigerian who suffers unquantifiable losses as a result of fuel shortages or for the Nigerian who cannot afford to buy his or her own personal vehicle and pay for roadside fuel at whatever cost. Tackling these challenges headlong will contribute towards reducing the vehicular pressure on fuel consumption. We must therefore begin to focus on those factors responsible for both seasonal and impromptu petroleum products shortages. We must again begin to remind ourselves of the current global trend in sourcing for alternative sources of energy, especially renewable energy sources such as biomass, solar and hydraulic, wherever we find them available and applicable. These are challenges that are readily surmountable once the political will is mustered.
Our refineries must be maintained adequately so as to function optimally and yield products as per design. In the recent past a number of Nigerians were granted licenses to build refineries, they must be encouraged to build and put them on stream. In the event that there is need for importation to augment local production, opportunities should be given to the oil majors with wide international connections to assist in massive importation, to ensure that the landing cost is comparatively reasonable. But above all, there must be the concerted effort to seek ways of introducing alternative products which will be environmentally friendly and cost effective.
The NNPC must be commended for its strive towards diversifying fuel energy sources. Over a decade ago NNPC introduced gas powered cars at the local trade fairs in Lagos, Enugu and Kaduna. The CNG [Compressed Natural Gas] project is to convert cars run on petrol to natural gas. It has proven to be more fuel efficient and more engine friendly. Of course we all know that gas is more abundant in Nigeria than crude oil and it is safer for our delicate environment, therefore this option will reduce the dependence of all car users on petrol for vehicular travels.
Bio-fuels are receiving global attention as alternatives to petrol and in Nigeria we have the opportunity to go into bio-fuels production. In the past two years the NNPC has been championing projects in this direction. With the creation of a Renewable Energy Division [RED], manned by seasoned and articulate engineers, reasonable progress has been achieved in such a short time. In the main, local farmers are being encouraged to go into mechanized farming of cassava and sugar cane for conversion to ethanol. The ethanol is planned to be blended with petrol to produce a fuel that is more efficient and cleaner for our environment. The beauty of this project is its integration of agriculture and the petroleum industry for the resuscitation of our farming culture, enabling us to grow more food while at the same time reducing the pressure on petroleum products. Other long term benefits of this project include the production of bio-diesel from palm oil, to support our Small and Medium Scale Enterprises [SMEs], which are adversely affected by irregular and epileptic power supply.
It is quite obvious that the concern posed by fuel shortages is being vigorously addressed by the government and NNPC. Credit must be given to the originators of the petroleum products mega-stations which are blazing the trail of honest calibration and price compliance, to enable Nigerians acquire the culture of price discipline. Recently also floating mega-stations were introduced for the riverine areas of the Niger delta creeks, which hitherto could not be provided with petrol stations on land. When fully deployed these floating stations will alleviate the age-long difficulties experienced by the people of this region. Through dialogue and collective discussions, the issue of sustainable community development of the Niger Delta region could be accomplished, thereby eliminating the sabotage of facilities and disruption of operations.
Existing refineries must be maintained and be in top form for full production, while new ones in the pipeline are brought on stream. There should be appropriate and affordable pricing so that the temptation to make quick money is discouraged. Above all, government must ensure that mass transportation is given priority attention in the transport ministry by way of resuscitating and modernizing the rail system, nation-wide. When all these measures are put in place, mass movement of people, products and goods will become more effective and efficient, and at the same time discourage individuals from relying on personal transportation efforts. It is my conviction that all these will check fuel shortages and allow government focus on and tackle urgently needed infrastructural development of our nation.

Original_Search_Title: 'Big Brother' at the Senate?
Headline: Nigeria: 'Big Brother' at the Senate?
Body_Text:
MEMBERS of the Senate came to work on Thursday, February 1, 2007 to find out that some security cameras and other devices had suddenly been installed inside the Chamber of the Senate. What first started as an unusual distortion of the output of the Public Address System (PAS) drew the attention of erudite academician-turned lawmaker, Senator Daniel Saror, who noticed, to his dismay, that the Senate was once again being bugged for the second time in the eight-year young life of the federal legislature.
From all indications, just as in the first case in 2006, the authorities of the Senate were not informed of the necessity for the bugging, not did they give their consent to it. Therefore, the motive for the act, widely attributed to the State Security Services (SSS), could not be immediately ascertained.
