The Executive, the only branch of government that can give the security agencies directives to this effect, therefore left the Senators and the public at large to figure out for themselves the motives behind this strange and anomalous pussyfooting act.
Bugging any of the chambers of the National Assembly without the permission of its members is indeed, sacrilegious. That arena is usually referred to as a "hallowed chamber" because that is where the laws of the land are made, including the sacred act of constitution amendment. It is also in the chamber that impeachment of erring executive elected officials, another sacred process (forget that it has been bastardised of late, also through the nefarious machinations of the Executive Branch) is carried out. Indeed, the Senate of the United States, from where we copied aspects of our presidential democracy, is imbued with some judicial powers. It is in the chamber that the Senators try erring executive officials impeached by the House of Representatives.
When such trials, which reflect the traditions of the courts, result in indictments, such officials stand removed from office. Therefore, bugging the Senate or any legislature is as consternating as bugging the courts. Anybody who can bug the legislative chamber, if not put back to his constitutional tether, will one day bug the courts, including the Supreme Court. This is why the matter must be thoroughly investigated and the source of that odious directive identified and appropriately sanctioned. This will serve not only as a deterrent but also assure the world that we are not a cassava republic democracy willing to submit to the oafish whims of any self-conceited megalomaniac.
In addition to identifying the source of this irritation, it is also important to know the role the Sergeant-At-Arms played or did not play in the saga. He is the officer entrusted with the keeping the sacred instruments of the legislature, including the Mace, holy. He is answerable to Nigerians as to who planted those bugs. We want to know if the Sergeant-At-Arms knew about those bugs. If he did, why did he not inform the authorities of the National Assembly? If he knew, it is a clear case of disloyalty to the National Assembly for which he should lose his right to be the Assembly's custodian. But if he did not; it can be read as a sign of incompetence. The Sergeant-At-Arms and staff of the National Assembly should have been the ones to discover the bugs and inform the Distinguished Senators.
Now, we need to pause and try to figure out a few puzzles surrounding this bugging affair. Why would the Executive branch remain interested in monitoring the Senate through these surreptitious devices, less than four months to the end of the Obasanjo presidency? Usually, when an administration has only four months to hand over power to a new group of elected leaders, it is involved in rounding off its act. Bugging the Senate does not appear to me as an act of winding down operations. Rather, it is smacks of a move by the Executive to keep an eye and ear on the Senate. It is both an offensive and defensive tactic. What is the necessity of it, with only 106 days to the expiration of the Obasanjo presidency?
When one sees these sorts of strange happenings at such inauspicious moments of our transition, one is persuaded to begin to wonder if those who loudly suspect that the Executive is up to no good do not have a valid point? The Vice President, Atiku Abubakar, has been harping on the message that President Obasanjo is still plotting to foul the transitional processes to stay longer in power, following the defeat of tenure elongation. Because he is at loggerheads with the President, his words could be taken as mere sour-graping; part of the mud being thrown. Pray, if the bugging of the Senate is not part of the speculated act of sabotage by the Executive against the transition programme, what else could be the intent of it?
WHY else do they need to monitor the Senate? As a mere harassment gambit, line with the programme of destabilisation that has been going on since 1999, resulting in five Senate Presidents within five years? If the president carried out those maneuvers in the past to consolidate his hold on power, what does he need consolidation for, just days to the lawful end of his days in power?
Or could it be that the Presidency is taking this rumour about plots to impeach the President and Vice President over their unsavoury roles in the Petroleum Technology Development Fund (PTDF) that serious? Even if that is the case, do they need bugs to unearth such plots? What of the human spies and legislative hackers, such as Senate Deputy President, Ibrahim Mantu, who have more than proved their worth? Or may be Mantu and his co-travellers have been set aside since they failed on the tenure elongation mission?
What ever be the answer to these and other yet to be asked questions, we really need to watch the next four months. The enemies of our democracy are determined and formidable. They neither slumber nor sleep. It is dangerous to fall asleep.
World gas cartel is okay
EVEN though top natural gas producers from around the world have
been shying away from the possibility of a world gas cartel in the likeness of the Organisation of Petroleum Exporting Countries (OPEC) it would seem to even lay persons like this writer that it is an event that is bound to take place one day.
In recent months, two of the world's biggest gas producing countries, Russia and Iran, have been smooching each other on this topic, even though they always come out to deny they are championing it.
In fact, Russian President, Vladimir Putin's European Union aide, Sergei Yastrzhembsky, blamed the West for the speculation because they are always looking for "evil" in Russia. What is evil about countries that produce a strategic product like gas coming together to derive the kind of benefit such an alliance has given OPEC countries since the early 1970's? I am of the view that Nigeria should look into this issue and see if we should not also horn into it to derive maximum benefit from our own vast gas deposits. Let's do it.

Original_Search_Title: Save Our Souls, Mr. President
Headline: Nigeria: Save Our Souls, Mr. President
Body_Text:
Lagos — I hate to disturb your peace, Mr. President, but what else can we do when Nigerians are not living in peace? That is why I have decided, in my own humble cubicle, to write you these few lines today, hoping that someone close to you will read and summarise to you before sunset. The situation in the country today is "dire". I know you hate that word, because in your own books, Nigerians have never had it so good. Things are getting better, as your supporters usually sing. We have debt relief, we have cassava explosion, we have 10,000 megawatts, we have gas and oil or oil and gas, we have minimum wage, we have foreign reserves, we have everything, according to your supporters. I have no intention of saying anything contrary. But I intend to draw your attention to the fact that the situation in Nigeria is dire. Heed this SOS and save our souls.
No, Your Excellency, I am not talking about the regular supply of darkness by NEPA/PHCN in the last two weeks. I feel very sorry for Nigerians who don't have generators and those who have generators but don't have fuel. As for me, I have been generating, consistently, 2.1 KVA per day in the last three years and I am able to watch my English Premiership matches, work on the computer and have some light around the house for a few hours in a day. I pity some 139 million Nigerians who don't have generators. And, no, Your Excellency, I am not talking about the fuel situation. I understand we now import every litre of fuel we consume in Nigeria because of circumstances beyond NNPC's control. Accept my sympathy, Mr. President, but that is not the SOS I am talking about.
Have you been listening to news lately? Just recently, a young woman was killed at Apongbon, Lagos. According to reports, Mrs Clementina Saduwa, the Product Manager of Ericsson Nigeria and a loveable mother of two, was on her way home from work at 8 pm when her car broke down on the Apongbon Bridge. The boys swooped on her. In panic, she wound up her side glass. Angered, the boys attacked her with a sharp object and she later died in the hospital. Her husband became a widower instantly, and he must be having a hell of time explaining to their kids that "mummy" would never hug, cuddle and kiss them again, because "mummy" would never come home again. That is just one of the thousands of cases we read about all the time. Hardly does any day pass without this "dire" situation confronting us.
What's your business with that, Your Excellency may ask. A lot, Mr. President. I don't know if you glance through the papers, but there is insecurity in the land. The spate of robberies should worry you. I can list a thousand and one reasons why the population of armed and violent robbers is on the increase, but that is not my aim today. What you are doing about the dire situation is what I and many other Nigerians are worried about. I am not a crime buster or a crime expert, but something tells me that there is something we are getting wrong. We can increase the strength of police force from 50,000 to one million and still achieve no results. We can import ammunition from Russia and Ukraine and still achieve little or no results. You know, as well as I do, Mr. President, that there is certainly something we are not getting right. Given the human and material resources we have lost as a nation to armed robbery in recent times, I would have thought that by now, some drastic and far-reaching measures should have been taken if indeed we value human life in our country.
Take Apongbon, for instance. That is at the heart of the financial capital of Nigeria, full of young professionals who are daily contributing their quota to the development of this country. They close from work at 5 pm and are sacred to death to leave Lagos Island because of the robbers-cum-murderers who lie in wait for them at strategic corners. I am not even talking about the bad patch of road at Apongbon, which I am sure would not cost us the entire foreign reserves to repair, especially as we are talking about the financial capital of Nigeria. Your Excellency, everybody knows that Apongbon is a dangerous spot. Everybody, including the Inspector General of Police. Yet, for whatever reason, there is no extra effort to provide security for the place. Instead, you have hundreds of policemen and women stationed at Allen Roundabout, Ikeja, and Maryland, also in Ikeja, waiting for any motorist to breach the traffic light so that they can pounce of him and strip him to the last kobo in his pocket. The dark spots all over Lagos, and all over Nigeria, that need security are abandoned to the robbers, while the lucrative places are over-policed. What this means, Mr. President, is that we have no respect for human life in this country.
Like I almost said, we can attribute the spate of violent crimes in recent times to harsh economic conditions which I know you would not agree with since you, as the Father of Modern Nigeria have solved all our problems. However, in as much as I agree that there are crimes all over the world even in the best of economies, what I cannot stand is the fact that we have folded our arms and accepted it as part of God's design for our lives. We have got to a stage where the Inspector-General of Police will tell us, as he did during Christmas, that these things are "normal" and should be expected. No, this is unacceptable. Remember, Your Excellency, when your daughter was attacked by armed robbers some years ago. Didn't the police get to the root of the matter? Didn't you order the Nigeria-Benin Republic border shut for some days? Didn't the police arrest the culprits and the ring leader? You know the right thing, Mr. President, when it affects you personally, and so I completely disagree with those who think you don't.
Violent robberies are not limited to Lagos. They are everywhere, including Abuja. I would have expected a state of emergency to be declared right now in the area of security of lives and property. I would have expected that proper measures would have been taken by now to forestall as well as combat robbery operations. To start with, what has happened to police intelligence? There were times in the past when the police took information gathering very serious. There were serious efforts geared at locating the abodes of robbers and questioning landlords who house people of dubious character without raising questions on their means of livelihood. Nigerians were given hotlines to call and information boxes were put in place for Nigerians to report persons they suspect to belong to the underworld.
Also, why is it that in the year 2007, our police force does not have a fingerprint system in place in order to be able to solve crimes? Isn't it ridiculous that police arrest suspects, charge them to court, get them jailed and yet there is no record of finger prints? People go to jail and come out without their fingerprints being taken. We held a national census in which Nigerians, including criminals, were supposed to have been counted. No attempt was made to seize the opportunity to gather fingerprints.
We are currently doing voter registration for people aged 18 and above. The process includes taking the fingerprint and photograph of every eligible Nigerian. I would expect this to be of interest to the security agencies, but I'm sure another contract would need to be awarded to gather fingerprints afresh instead of utilising what we already have. Even if the fingerprints are gathered, where are the laboratories to analyse them? I'm aware that the federal government has been talking about building a forensic lab. I won't be surprised if this is just a PR gimmick.
As I confessed earlier, Your Excellency, I am not a security expert, neither do I know much about crime control. I think, Sir, that it would be in the interest of Nigeria and Nigerians if we can gather the best of security experts, urgently, to tackle this problem. It certainly wouldn't take a genius to figure out that dark spots should be well policed.
As for what to do with sophisticated robbers with sophisticated weapons, I'm sure those who become millionaires and billionaires by taking up strategic positions in the police force should spare a few thoughts on how to tackle them efficiently-since that is what they were employed to do. I hate to see those pot-bellied police chiefs eating pepper soup and drinking beer while robbers take over the running of affairs on the roads and in our homes. I hate to think that Clementina Saduwa, and hundreds of other innocent Nigerians, would have been alive today if only their country had offered them simple protection. It hurts, Your Excellency. Save our souls, Mr President.
Impeachment as Tea Party: The merchants of impeachment are back to work. We have been told that the governor of Adamawa State, Mr. Boni Haruna, is on his way to the "gallows". Adamawa lawmakers, we are told, have decided to impeach him from Abuja in order to secure the release of their Speaker and four colleagues from EFCC cells. In Ibadan, combatants loyal to Ali Must Go's Garrison Commander, Lamidi Adedibu, are also sharpening their knives in readiness to behead Governor Rashidi Ladoja. Even the House of Representatives briefly toyed with the idea of impeaching President Olusegun Obasanjo-a move that was called off obviously because the movers might have realised that they were about to play into Obasanjo's hands as the general elections draw near.
Senator Ken Nnamani, Senate President, issued a note of warning at a dinner with media executives in Lagos recently. Impeachment, he said, is like the death sentence: you don't pass it except there is no other reasonable option available. At the slightest provocation in Nigeria, the impeachment axe is dangled by the lawmakers as if it is a toy. The immediate past Chief Justice of Nigeria, Justice Alfa Belgore, noted as much when he said recently that the lawmakers should stop abusing the impeachment clause in the constitution. He said politicians have not been following the laid-down rules and procedures in trying to carry out impeachments. "You can just wake up in the morning and say yesterday, we saw the governor laughing, we must impeach him," he said, while cautioning lawmakers that in the 250 years of American democracy, impeachment has taken place just once.
Belgore knows what he is saying. He was the CJN when the most bizarre cases of impeachment took place, notably that of Governor Peter Obi of Anambra. It was a clear abuse of the constitution, right from the way the impeachment notice was peddled around, the timing of the legislative hours and the venue of the sitting. The Appeal Court is now expected to rule that the process that led to the impeachment is arrant nonsense. "The judiciary as an independent body must live up to expectation to check the excesses of desperate politicians," Belgore said, challenging the judiciary to be truthful at all times.
This impeachment nonsense must stop. And the courts must protect our constitution no matter whose ox is gored.

Original_Search_Title: What is Minagri's Policy On Crop Cultivation?
Headline: Rwanda: What is Minagri's Policy On Crop Cultivation?
Body_Text:
Kigali — The Eastern province governor, Theonest Mutsindashyaka was last alleged to have issued a directive banning the cultivation of sweet potatoes in his controversial bid to improve agriculture in the province.
The ministry of Agriculture immediately issued a statement denying the governors' decree.
"That's what happens when politicians take the job of technocrats," remarked Mbanda, a farmer in Rwamagana.
The Minister of state for Agriculture, Dr. Daphoroes Gahakwa said, "The ministry cannot abolish potato growing because the crop is a major tool in fighting malnutrition and enhancing food security in the country"
However, she said that the ministry discourages growing potatoes in wetlands because the heaps of potatoes channel water drainage away from the wetlands and allow water only to pass through, leaving the wetlands dry.
Gahakwa said the ministry does not facilitate the growing of sweet potatoes in the country because it is a staple food and therefore farmers grow potatoes naturally without any input from the ministry. For this reason, the ministry discourages potato growing in wetlands but advises farmers to grow the crop on hill tops and flat lands.
The acting Secretary General in the Ministry of Agriculture, Ernest Ruzindaza said, "sweet potatoes are a historical crop in Rwanda that has fed a majority of Rwandans in all provinces since time immemorial, for that particular reason, it is not a priority crop in the ministry, because it is inherent in farmer's natural selection of crops to grow.'
He says the Ministry promotes growth of cash crops like tea, coffee and other food crops like peas, Soya beans, wheat, cereals and horticultural crops.
Policy on crop growth
To correct this impression, there's need to highlight what exactly is the policy of the Ministry of Agriculture on which crops are allowed, which ones are promoted and which are important for agricultural farming.
Agricultural crop production in Rwanda can be grouped in three categories: Food crops (leguminous, cereals, roots and tubers, banana); the traditional cash crops (coffee, tea, and pyrethrum), and the new cash or export crops (fruits and vegetables flowers, spices etc.)
According to the Strategic Plan for Agricultural Transformation national Agricultural policy plan, the farming sector is subdivided as follows in regard to need and regional productivity;
Rice production is conducted in rice schemes, found in the provinces of Cyangugu, Butare, Gitarama, Kigali-Ngali, Umutara and Kibungo. The study for the marshlands utilization master plan identified 48.000ha of marshlands, which can be used for rice production.
The national rice production covered 28 per cent of rice needs in 2001, 50 per cent of needs in 2002 and 60 per cent of need in 2003. Importation of rice costs the country an average of Frw305 billion annually, which can be greatly reduced if the national production capacity is increased.
The traditional export crops (coffee, tea, and pyrethrum) are particularly important to the nation as foreign exchange earners and to the farmers as monetary income.
Coffee is grown by 400,000 farmers in 60 districts, which were identified as the most suitable for the crop in the context of regional specialisation and intensification of coffee growing. The number of associations working with OCIR-CAFÃ is 80
Tea constitutes the second traditional export crop. Tea production covers an area of 12.800 ha, in industrial blocks and village small holdings.
The criteria of choosing the crop to be supported takes into account the importance of potential production and intensification, generation of revenues in the national as well as regional and international market possibilities of transformation conservation, and storage.
Food crops occupy 92 per cent of the total cultivated land; while coffee and tea occupy respectively 6.3 per cent and I.6 per cent of the total cultivated land. The average cultivated land in the 1997-2003 period was 1,428,033 hectares. Crops grown on this land included cereals, leguminous crops, bananas, roots and tubers, (sweet potatoes, cassava and Irish potatoes)
Nutritional content of crops
Compared to FAO (Food and Agricultural Organisation) standards of average food requirements per person per day, Rwanda's agricultural production only partially covers needs: 87 per cent for calories, 70 per cent for proteins, and 22 per cent for lipids, the nutritional deficit is a general one, for energy and proteins, the deficit in fats is chronically critical and has to be urgently addressed.
The food situation and the level of coverage of food needs, which are regularly established on a seasonal basis throughout the country, show different forms of disparities. The seasonal variability is linked with the cycle of seasonal crops. The geographical variability shows evidently the most fragile provinces as far as the food situation is concerned.
For the majority of Rwandans, daily food intake largely consists of bananas, roots and tubers (sweet potatoes, cassava, and Irish potatoes), which contribute 60 per cent of calories. Leguminous crops constitute the principle source of proteins and a considerable source of fats. Beans alone account for 40 per cent of protein source, peas, soybeans and groundnuts are present. The last two are the source of fat. Consumption of cereals (sorghum and maize) is poor despite the fact that they are energy generating foods.
Cereals contribute only 15.4 per cent of the energy needs. Animal foods contribute little in the diet; they contribute just above 2 per cent for calories, 6 per cent for proteins, and 40 per cent for fats. Although, vitamins and mineral salts are important for a balanced diet, fruits and vegetables are only eaten in small quantities.
The nutritional situation is precarious if one considers the fact that the rate of infantile malnutrition (poor weight/underweight) recorded in 2002 was 24 per cent. Provinces with the higher rate of chronic malnutrition are Gikongoro (50 %), Kibuye and Butare (48 %) and Byumba (47 %).
In order to fill the gap between production and needs, Rwanda and her partners have to resort to importation and food aid. In 2002, 16 per cent of the total expenditure on imports was on food, equivalent to 46 million US dollars, without counting food aid, which equally represented a significant amount.
What needs to be done?
There needs to be promotion of specific crops to add to the agricultural out put of the country for example;
Wheat: These are included because of contributing to reduction of importations so as to attain production of around 39000 tons in the year 2010. It is in this frame that the following actions have been developed.
Provide support establish flour mills in Gikongoro and Byumba and launch production in these regions
Reinforce the research program on wheat suitable for bread making.
Offer facilities to the private sector in view of ensuring pattern ship with producer organisations for availing inputs and supervision of production.
Support the training of local bakers in view of promoting consumption of bread and other products from wheat flour.
Promote food technology programs aimed at giving maximum value to wheat flour.
Irish potatoes: These will allow satisfying national demand, mainly by increasing yields. It concerns in particular to reinforce the diversified research of varieties; improve the quality and the productive potential in general;
Increase the production capacity of seeds to grow (we presently have 30% of natural needs) in view of satisfying the national and sub regional market.
Support profession of production of commercial seeds and guarantee the quality.
Reinforce the commercial capacities of organizations of producers' kind commercial participants in view of guarantying regular supply and good prince to producers;
Beans: These aim at increasing production and the yields of this crop which remains of great importance in Rwandans' food, notably as regards proteins. Thus, the following will be undertaken is:
To develop research programmes in order to improve the quality of the climbing bean seeds (early maturing, colour, productivity etc.)
To provide increased availability of support sticks via accelerated development of agro-forestry
To promote cultivation in rotation with maize or potato
Inc conclusion, national policies consider the agricultural sector to be a springboard for the fight against poverty. Economic growth in the primary sector should raise rural households from their situation of generalised poverty.
This economic growth would equally initiate progressive development in secondary and tertiary sectors. This would create off-farm employment. However, most of the agricultural farms are still subsistence-oriented.
The Agricultural Development Strategy should reconcile market-orientation with the search for food security and the preservation of the environment. On the other hand, it is important to put mechanisms in place, which guarantee that different categories of agricultural farmers, especially the most vulnerable, benefit from the economic growth that is being advocated.

Original_Search_Title: Facility for Trade Through Mobile Phone Launched
Headline: Ghana: Facility for Trade Through Mobile Phone Launched
Body_Text:
An Internet facility that allows farmers and traders in agricultural commodities in Africa to transact their business through the use of mobile phone Short Message Service (SMS) has been launched in Accra.
The facility, known as tradenet.biz, is a website developed in Ghana by Busylab with an 11 million-dollar USAID support. It is intended to enhance trans-border trade between farmers and traders in Africa.
The USAID support was provided under a three-year market information systems project meant to improve the regional agricultural marketing systems in Africa to help increase intra-regional trade in agricultural products.
The services of tradenet.biz are completely free for users, except the normal sms messaging charges by the mobile phone service providers.
Mr Mark Davies, the architect of the website, said the service was operating in 12 countries and 300 markets in Africa and in some countries in South America.
He said the tradenet facility basically provided a platform for sellers in agricultural business to display their profiles and information on their commodities, prices and locations on the Internet, with the view to attracting potential buyers through the net.
He said the facility also offered individuals and traders associations the opportunity to establish their own website within the tradenet platform at not cost to constantly display their commodities and prices.
"Potential buyers looking for a specific commodity only need to compose SMS message on their mobile phones stating the code of the commodity in question and the country from which they want the results and send it to tradenet number '1344' for Areeba users or 024649999 from any other network and get instant results."
Mr Davies said traders could also register to receive regular SMS alerts on commodities from markets of their choice.
The tradenet platform comprises both regional and country portals. Regional portals display commodities and prices from a collection of countries from a particular region in Africa and country portals display markets in a particular country.
It lists prices of commodities from local markets in various countries. For instance, it has prices of commodities from Nima, Agbobloshie, Techiman and other local markets in Ghana.
He assured potential users that the platform was effectively protected from fraud. Dr Kofi Debrah, Chief Coordinator of the USAID project, said since the introduction of tradenet, various participating buyers and sellers had chalked a trans-border trade value to 350 million dollars.
He said the facilities had helped several farmers across Africa to sell their commodities to sellers outside their countries and therefore prevented their commodities from getting rotten on the farms.
"This promises to be an effective means for Busumuru Kofi Annan (former UN Secretary-General) to realise his dream for food security and promotion of agricultural business in Africa."
Dr Debrah said the facilities, however, faced a few challenges such as the high level of illiteracy and poverty among farmers and traders in Africa, which made it difficult for them to acquire computers, mobile phones and even be able to use them.
He said the project had therefore included training for the literate children of farmers and traders to assist their parents use the facility.
Mr Hugh Dake, an official of ECOBANK, assured traders that the bank would provide the necessary funds for them to be able to trade among themselves more efficiently.
A cassava farmer from Nigeria gave testimony of how she reached several buyers across the continent through the tradenet platform and recommended it to farmers on the continent.

Original_Search_Title: NNPC Set to Blend Ethanol With Petrol
Headline: Nigeria: NNPC Set to Blend Ethanol With Petrol
Body_Text:
Abuja — The NNPC will in the next two months commence blending of petrol with ethanol as part of the country's alternative energy development strategy.
NNPC Group Managing Director (GMD) Funsho Kupolokun said the project would offer Nigerians the option to either buy ordinary petrol or petrol blended with ethanol.
"We will blend 10 per cent of ethanol with petrol as a start and gradually move to achieve 40 per cent blended petrol by the turn of the decade," Kupolokun told the
News Agency of Nigeria (NAN), yesterday in Abuja.
He said that the nation would rake-in more than 150 million dollars from the bio-fuel initiative.
According to him, the initiative will free more crude for export as well as reduce exhaust emissions in the country.
"It will also be a platform that will enhance the linkage between oil and gas sector with the agricultural sector," Kupolo-kun said.
He said a robust fiscal legislative framework needed to be put in place in order to sustain the envisaged linkage between the oil sector with the agricultural sector.
NAN learnt that the NNPC received a grant of 70 million dollars from the renewable energy and energy efficiency partnership of Germany to support detailed feasibility studies at the target locations.
The corporation had already established a large farm in Jigawa for sugar cane production and a farm in Benue for cassava production.
"We have already rehabilitated our reception facilities in Apapa preparatory to importing ethanol," Kupolokun said.
The NNPC had last year promised to sponsor research in the Benue State University,
Makurdi, to boost the production of Cassava in the country.
The corporation is seeking core investors for some joint venture projects to fully develop the bio-fuel industry in Nigeria.

Original_Search_Title: People Who Drive Ntungamo District Amuru District - Movers & Shakers
Headline: Uganda: People Who Drive Ntungamo District Amuru District - Movers & Shakers
Body_Text:
John Wycliffe Karazaarwe is the LC5 Chairman of the district. He is the President of Uganda Local Governments Association (ULGA) and former Vice President of Uganda Local Authorities Association (ULAA). He has helped in setting up several primary schools around the district and several roads.
The First Lady Janet Museveni is Ruhaama member of Parliament. Her main goal is development. Hardly a year in Parliament, she has helped bring power to Kahunga trading centre and Kiyora Primary Teachers college and repaired the Ruhaama road as her maiden projects. She has presided over several fund raisings around the district.
Maj Gen Mugisha Muntu is the longest serving army commander in Uganda. He also fell out with the government and is currently the secretary for mobilisation in the opposition FDC party. He is straight forward and has integrity. He is a legislator in the EastAfrican Community Parliament. He is a principled man.
Steven Tashobya is the MP Kajara county. Education is his main goal in Ntungamo. He distributes scholarstic materials and weekly exams to many schools in the district. He also supplies schools with improved cassava stems and seeds from NARO Namulonge in a bid to boost food security in his area.
Mwesigwa Rukutana is the MP for Rushenyi constituency and State Minister for labour. He is a lawyer by profession. He has done more business than politics. He is the proprietor of the only radio in the district-Ankole Radio.
George Karamira is the FDC district chairman. He has steered the opposition party through hardships in a district considered to be the NRM leaders tap root. He was the LC5 councillor for Itojo sub- county in Mbarara district council and later in Ntungamo district council. As councillor he transformed Itojo with better roads and schools. He is also the former headmaster of Ruhanga Seventh Day Adventist SSS.
Wilberforce Mugyenyi is the LC5 Chief Whip and councillor representing the male youth. He is an inspiration to the Ntungamo youth with integrity.
Peace Musiime represents Itojo sub-county in the district council. She is the former Secretary for Security in the LC5 council. She is known for making prompt decissions.
Reginah Karibwende Kinyatta is the deputy district speaker. She was a civil servant at Ntungamo district administration before joining politics.
Denis Singahache is the district speaker. He was very instrumental in the team that spearheaded the creation of the district in 1993.
Beatrice Rwakimaari is serving her second term as woman MP. She is very instrumental in mobilising her constituents for developmental projects. She started her NGO called ARISE that has helped fight Aids and fostered good health around the district.
Barbra Cynthia Bindeeba is the LC5 female Youth councilor. She is an inspiration to female youth in her district as far as dressing is concerned. She recently advocated for a dress code in the council.
Vincent Rutagonya is the LC5 councillor for Kayonza sub-county. He is also the Chairperson for the Social services/Works committee.
Dan Buteera is among the longest serving LC5councillors has been on the LC5 executive committee in different capacities. He is a retired medical assistant and is serving as the secretary for health and security. He has supported and improved the health sector.
Urban Banturaki is very influential in the LC5 council. He represents Ntungamo town council and is the former chairman of the Tender Board and a businessman.
EDUCATION
John Tumuhimbise is the director of Mother Care Primary School, a private school in Ntungamo town. It is among the best performing schools in the district. She is a teacher by profession
Prof Rwakishana Bibangamba is the vice chancellor of Kakoba University. He is a former lecturer at Makerere University. Back home, he is an inspiration in the education sector as he is generous with his counsels to students. He once stood Ruhaama MP seat but failed.
CIVIL SERVICE
Andrew Mawejje is the chief administrative officer. Many councillors are impressed by his performance. He introduced a Funds Allocation Committee to promote transparency and equity in the distribution of district resources.
Charles Tusingwire is the clerk to council and also acting district information officer.
RELIGION
Mzee Asanasiyo Kampororo, President Yoweri Museveni's primary school teacher. He is very instrumental in Church Of Uganda development in the district. He is the main pillar behind in mobilising the set up of Kyamate COU.
Protaz Tugurihwayo is the resident district commissioner. He has worked tirelessly in fighting corruption in the district. He has caused arrest of many district officials implicated in corruption and dubious deals. He has exposed several dubious deals between some district officials and Tender Board that lead to shoddy work. He has worked hard towards maintaining security in the area.
BUSINESS
Jacob Kafureeka aka JakaJex has succeeded more in business than politics. He represents Itojo sub- county in the LC5. He runs JakaJex businesses in Ntungamo town. He was the first mayor of Ntungamo town council and during his time, he implemented projects like water in town and the council building. He has a diploma in public administration.
Raghbir Sandhu is the managing director of Pearl Flowers Ltd. The $11 million about shs20 billion flower farm is among the top exporters in the country for roses. It is planning to take on 800 employees very soon. The company aims at growing high quality flowers for export while fostering community welfare and environmental protection. He believes the farm will help investors realise the potential of many climatic zones in Uganda.

Original_Search_Title: 100 Days in Office - Why Olurin Shunned Celebration
Headline: Nigeria: 100 Days in Office - Why Olurin Shunned Celebration
Body_Text:
Lagos — ACTS have emerged on why Ekiti State Administrtor, Brigadier-General Tunji Olurin (rtd) did not celebrate his 100 days in office last Friday.
Daily Champion learnt that Olurin opted out of the frenzy ceremony because he preferred his work to speak for him than making fuss over it.
We further gathered on the day he clocked 100 days in office, Olurin left Ado-Ekiti, the state capital for Lagos State to attend a crucial meeting.
This, according to source, is characteristic of the Administrator, who has always preferred to do things without making much fuss abut them.
However, it is not that there was nothing to celebrate, but sources close to the administrator l said that it was one of Olurin's aides who drew his attention to the date last Saturday. An unperturbed Olurin was said to have retorted: "Is that so? I did not even remember. That means we should be packing our luggage gradually because our six months tenure would soon be over".
Sources said that the administrator has had a very busy schedule in office in the last 100 days. During this period, he had to grapple with the appalling security situation in the state which was almost getting out of hand by the time he resumed work in Ado-Ekiti on October 19, last year.
But having restored normalcy to the state in the first few weeks, Olurin identified poverty as having played a significant role in the tension that gripped the state in the last days of the Fayose administration. Sources said, the administrator has been doing everything to reduce the level of poverty by empowering people to go into self-employment.
To this end, loans have been made available to farmers through the National Poverty Eradication programme, (NAPEP), Also since the state is an agrarian economy, the Olurin administration had been supporting agricultural ventures by installing cassava plants to enhance the production of cassava products like cassava flour, garri and starch.
According to one of his aides who does not want his name in print "the administrator is doing this to prove to other people that if they form unions, they can do the same thing by pulling resources together to set up such plants".
Besides, the administrator will soon set up an Economic Council through experts and those who have clouts and can reach beyond the shores of Nigeria will be opted to source for foreign investors to the state. This is because, according to the aide, "the administrator believes the government cannot go it alone to create factories, so the option is to attract investors to the state to utilize available raw materials for manufacturing purposes and spring up the economy of the state".

Original_Search_Title: Ekiti: 100 Days of Emergency Rule:... Olurin Crosses the Half Mark
Headline: Nigeria: Ekiti: 100 Days of Emergency Rule:... Olurin Crosses the Half Mark
Body_Text:
Lagos — Last week Friday marked the 100th day in office for Brig. General Adetunji Olurin (rtd) as the Administrator of Ekiti State. But the day went by without the usual frenzy and ceremony with which political office holders especially in the executive arm of government have always observed the day.
It was learnt that the Administrator left Ado-Ekiti, the state capital, early on that day for Lagos to attend to crucial matters of state.
This, according to sources, is characteristic of the Administrator, who has always preferred to do things without making much fuss about them.
However, it is not that there was nothing to celebrate, but sources close to the Administrator disclosed that it was one of Olurin's aides who drew his attention to the date late on Saturday evening. An unperturbed Olurin was said to have retorted: "Is that so? I did not even remember. That means we should be packing our luggage gradually because our six months tenure would soon be over".
Sources said that the Administrator has had a very busy schedule in office in the last 100 days. During this period, he had to grapple with the appalling security situation in the state which was almost getting out of hand by the time he resumed work in Ado-Ekiti on October 19, last year.
But having restored normalcy to the state in the first few weeks, Olurin has gradually transformed the fountains of knowledge state. He was said to have identified poverty as having played a significant role in the tension that gripped the state in the last days of the Fayose administration.Sources said, the Administrator has been doing everything to reduce the level of poverty by empowering people to go into self-employment.
To this end, loans have been made available to farmers through the National Poverty Eradication Programme, (NAPEP). Also since the state is an agrarian economy, the Olurin administration had been supporting agricultural ventures by installing cassava plants to enhance the production of cassava products like cassava flour, gari and starch.
According to one of his aides, "the Administrator is doing this to prove to other people that if they form unions, they can do the same thing by pulling resources together to set up such plants".
Besides, the Administrator will soon set up an Economic Council through experts and those who have clouts and can reach beyond the shores of Nigeria will be co-opted to source for foreign investors to the state. This is because, according to the aide, "the Administrator believes the government cannot go it alone to create factories, so the option is to attract investors to the state to utilize available raw materials for manufacturing purposes and spring up the economy of the state".
Perhaps, far more than transforming the lives of the people, the general atmosphere of peace and tranquility now prevailing in the state had been attributed to the efforts of the Administrator who has brought his wealth of experience in peacekeeping operations in several parts of the world to bear on the governance of the state.
When contacted to find out why the Administrator opted not to celebrate his first 100 days in office as had been the practice in the country in recent time, Ayo Akinyemi, his Chief Press Secretary said: "Just as you may have known, the Administrator believes that his achievements will speak for themselves rather than resort to noise-making and merriment. He is one quiet, easy-going, but tough person who does nor blow his trumpet", he added.

Original_Search_Title: Harvesting Season Hits Peak
Headline: Uganda: Harvesting Season Hits Peak
Body_Text:
Kampala — INCREASED supply of foodstuffs has led to a reduction in prices, a weekly survey of Kampala's major markets has shown.
Robert Mugisa, a trader at Nakasero Market, said harvesting had reached a peak. "We are receiving plenty of maize, beans and groundnuts, forcing us to reduce prices. The supply has enabled us meet schools' demand without causing a shortage."
There were fears that purchase of foods by schools would cause a shortage, leading to higher prices.
The schools' traditional foodstuffs are maize flour, beans and rice. The first term for primary and secondary schools is going to start on February 5.
Paul Muyigi, a trader in Mukono, said there would be increased supplies and low prices until March when the new planting season begins.
"Farmers are selling commodities to take back children to school," he said.
"Farmers will be preparing for the new season and will retain more commodities," he predicted.
Maize flour was sold at sh600 from sh750 a kilogramme, while beans fell to sh700 from sh900 a kilogramme.
Groundnuts also declined to sh1,500 a kilogramme from sh1,800.
Cassava flour was sold at sh350 from sh450, while maize was sold at sh250 from sh400.
A bunch of matooke was sold at between sh5,000 and sh7,000.
Twenty passion fruits were sold at sh500.
Other foodstuffs in plenty were Irish potatoes, bananas, pineapples, tomatoes and onions.

Original_Search_Title: Child Trafficking On the Rise in Karamoja
Headline: Uganda: Child Trafficking On the Rise in Karamoja
Body_Text:
Kampala — CHRISTINE Nangole does not want to go back home. She would rather remain in Kampala, even though she is frightened that the man who brought her from Matany village, Bokora county in Moroto district, wants to destroy her innocence. Although far away from home, Nangole is determined to defend her chastity.
Like hundreds of other girls and women who flee Karamoja every year, this tall, skinny 13-year-old girl was flushed out of her home in what had turned into a do or die contest. Do? Yes, run, as fast as your little legs can take you or get onto any vehicle that will put more distance between you and home. Die? Yes, at home, people die of starvation.
Surprised that in Uganda someone can die of starvation?
The Karimojong culture is strong. It has captivated these pastoral peoples through generations with unrelenting purpose in the face of civilisation. This culture will haul anyone guilty of premarital sex over the coals, unless it is done during the event where a young man chases the girl he has chosen for a wife and 'mounts' her. He then marries her.
Karamoja faces endemic cycles of armed cattle raids and counter-raids and successive intervals of long spells of drought, creating serious water and food shortages.
As they leave home, Karimojong women and girls often move in groups. These groups are a common sight in towns like Busia, Tororo, Mbale, Katakwi and on the streets of Kampala. However, the girls are increasingly being picked from home, which means the person who picks them is the only one they know when they reach 'this other side of the world'.
A report, A Chronic Emergency: Child protection issues among communities in Karamoja region 2006, warns that the children being picked from Karamoja are a case of increasing child trafficking in Uganda.
The report, drawn from a study carried out in the districts of Kotido, Moroto and Nakapiripirit by Save the Children, Uganda, cited insecurity and perennial hunger as the main reasons why children in Karamoja are vulnerable to trafficking.
Parents were being given as little as sh5,000 for their children to be taken away. This means the children are bought with their parents' cooperation. The report warns that Uganda may be experiencing child trafficking on a much larger scale than is thought.
Other children are taken with the promise of being paid with commodities like cassava, potatoes, maize, posho and beans which are supposed to be sent back home to their parents. Some children are paid with the food they eat and the spot they rest their heads at night. Respondents on the study said it is common for the buyers to renege on their terms and refuse to pay the children.
"This is the reason for the seemingly noticeable sense of helplessness on the part of the district leadership out to stem the trade," the report adds.
Nangole is a confident girl, but flashes of doubt dart across her eyes casting a dark shadow on her light skin.
A man who works for World Food Programme brought Nangole to Kampala with the promise of sending money and food to her parents at the end of every month as payment for the girl's work. In 2004, the same thing had happened to her sister, who has never returned home and yet Nangole's parents still gave her away.
"My sister is also in Kampala but I have never met her. I have been in Kampala for over a year now. I sent for my parents some posho and beans during my first months away from home, but things changed after Uncle Robert started coming to knock on our bedroom door at night while whispering for me to open. I refused to open because I share the bedroom with younger children. When I told his wife, whom I call mummy, he denied it.
"In the daytime, he would find me alone and fondle my breasts. Sometimes I would open for him the gate and he would again fondle my breasts.
One day, I refused to go and open the gate and told mummy all these things. Uncle Robert got cross and threatened to return me home.
"Mummy said I could stay but Uncle Robert was not happy. He refused me to eat at home, so I would work but mummy would just steal for me food. Uncle Robert would slap me when he met me anywhere in the house.
"In the end, I told mummy that I could not stand the torture and I would rather go back home even though home is not good. Uncle Robert was happy and the next day, we set off for Karamoja.
On reaching Jinja, he said we would proceed the next day. But at night he came again knocking on my door. When I refused to open, he threatened to abandon me in Jinja. I didn't know what I would do if he did that but I refused to open," she narrates in English which she learnt while playing with other children.
According to Save the Children, the victims' job descriptions keep changing as the employers find it convenient. Young girls hired to handle household work soon find themselves playing concubines, sexual slaves or prostitutes for sale by their masters.
The report also points to the chilling but possible fact that some of the children are sacrificed in ritual killings and for organ harvesting.
The practice often involves direct child sale, child theft, smuggling and kidnap. The destinations for trafficked children include Katakwi, Busia, Iganga, Jinja, Mbale, Moroto, Tororo and Kampala. Others end up in Kenyan towns like Eldoret, Busia and Malaba.
Despite efforts by local district leaders, the report says there remains unabated movement of children out of Karamoja to their 'employment points'. Attempts at closing in on the perpetrators of this illicit trade are frustrated due to the nature of its operation.
Unlike previous years when the practice peaked in the months of May to August, the high numbers of children leaving Karamoja continued through 2006 into 2007.
The Police was quick to dismiss reports of child trafficking as false and promised to make independent investigations. The Inspector General of Police, Maj. Gen. Kale Kayihura, said: "We disagree with whoever compiled findings about exporting Karimojong children to Kenya. There is no child trafficking at the moment and we are yet to release our concrete findings to end the hullabaloo," Kayihura told some sections of the press.
Despite this denial however, the findings in the report seem to corroborate another recent report by the US Department of State (The Protection Project Report 2006), which cites Uganda as a source and transit point for trafficked children.
The State Department report says Uganda is one of the countries yet to pass strong anti-trafficking legislations.
Uganda does not have a specifically comprehensive law on anti-trafficking.
The penal code defines penalties for trafficking-related offences, including procuring a woman for prostitution, detention with sexual intent, sex with a minor under 18, dealing in slaves, and compelling unlawful labour.
While the penal code covers the full scope of trafficking persons, the two reports decry the lack of precedents related to the offence.
Like the US report, the Karamoja study notes a conspicuous inability of the law to punish people caught in this illegal trade.
Livingstone Ssewanyana, the Foundation of Human Rights Initiative Executive director, advises the Government to put in place programmes that will address employment problems as one of the ways to respond to problems of child trafficking.
Hosea Muruka, an advocate of the High Court in private practice, says there is no data on the trafficking in Uganda and neighbouring countries.
"That is why there is a degree of unawareness about its existence. In order to solicit the support of international organisations and lobby the Government, it is imperative that concrete information and data is collected on the magnitude, forms, means and methods of trafficking in Uganda," Muruka says.
The report calls for immediate and increased early childhood development interventions for children below five years. "There is also need to carry out an assessment to establish what the children were doing and the legal status of people keeping them," the report adds.
The report called for the re-examination of the disarmament exercise in the region, which, while well-intentioned, has also exhibited serious human rights abuses.
There is also need to establish early warning systems to provide timely information on potential natural and man-made disasters such as drought and cattle rustling activities.
Nangole has been mistreated, threatened and has gone hungry but no punishment is worse than home.
If only she could get the opportunity to work to make things at home better.
If Uncle Robert allows for her to remain in his home, will she give in to his illicit advances, one wonders.

Original_Search_Title: Food Production in Northern Region Increases
Headline: Ghana: Food Production in Northern Region Increases
Body_Text:
Food Security in the Northern Region has been slightly enhanced following an increase in production last year, Mr. Sylvester Adongo, the Regional Director of Agriculture, has said.
Mr. Adongo told the GNA in an interview in Tamale that the region produced 1,073,102 tonnes of yam and 10.64 tonnes per hectare last year as against 1,012,892 tonnes and 10.24 tonnes per hectare in 2005.
He attributed the achievement to improved yield, planting at the correct time and the use of new technologies.
The region produced 809,970 tonnes of cassava and 86,167 tonnes per hectare and 89,661 tonnes of groundnut and 0.85 tonnes per hectare in 2006 as against 678,754 tonnes of cassava and 77,724 tonnes of groundnuts in 2005.
Production of maize fell slightly last year to 136,227 tonnes as against 142,602 tonnes in 2005.
Mr. Adongo said improvement in crop yield and quality was partly due to the engagement of the youth in agriculture through the National Youth Employment Programme initiated by the government.
He said loans were given to farmers for livestock production and that some butchers and women who process local milk also benefited from the credit facility.
New varieties of maize including the "obatampa", "okomoasa", "dodzi" and "mamaba" and rice varieties like; "tos" "GR 18" and "digan," are all doing slightly well.
Groundnuts varieties such as the "Chinese" and "mampinta" as well as soybean and cowpea varieties are being promoted in the region.
Mr. Yakubu Iddriss Sheref, the Regional Monitoring and Evaluation Officer, told the GNA that mixed cropping had been introduced with cashew and mango to help improve yield.
He said the Ministry of Food and Agriculture had introduced good storage facilities and chemicals for good preservation to prevent post harvest losses.

Original_Search_Title: What's the Recommended Dose of Polio Vaccine?
Headline: Nigeria: What's the Recommended Dose of Polio Vaccine?
Body_Text:
Abuja — "In part, the totalitarian state is sustained because individuals terrorise themselves - they become accomplices in their own tyrannization, censoring what they say and even what they allow themselves to think and feel-" Willa Appel, Cults In America: Programmed For Paradise, 1983.
Once again, one cannot help but stoke the embers of the polio controversy by asking the question of the dose of polio vaccine being shot into our children routinely like food. I was incensed when I read in the Thursday, 25th January 2007 edition of Daily Trust an advert from Rotary International calling on people to submit their children as guinea pigs for another nebulous round of immunisation, which we know no end of.
I deemed it important to add my voice since I am not a Muslim who can be labelled an 'Islamic extremist' that is opposed to 'modernisation' as has been the direction of the debate in the past. The whole polio controversy has been so slanted by religious dogmatism and blackmail, covert intimidation and brazen coercion that one is incensed that otherwise rational minds have turned their backs to reason. How else can one feel when the bases of scientific enquiry are being jettisoned in the face of nothing but a triviality called polio, relative to calamities such as HIV/AIDS, tuberculosis, malaria and malnutrition?
I was one of the journalists on hand when the press briefing that stimulated the polio controversy held at the Arewa House in Kaduna. Dr Datti Ahmed, a medical doctor and Muslim leader, alerted the public of plans to inject children with vaccines that contained agents that could make the children unable to experience childbirth when they grow. He explained that the initiators of this idea feared that the projected population of certain Third World countries was a threat to the First World. Not a few journalists in the hall scoffed at him. I was studying pharmacy in the university and I understood what he meant; that if you inject a foreign body from another living being (such as a vaccine), the human body has a capacity to develop a means of attacking and neutralising that substance with what scientists refer to as antibodies. These antibodies are not produced for negligible quantities of the foreign body, however. The quantity must be large or made significant with repeated introduction of the substance into the body, which may explain the endless administration of the vaccines.
One stands the everlasting risk of being accused of engaging in pseudoscience for making these observations, but like several professors on my side of the divide have argued, we are open to a healthy debate on the matter. Why is it that, all of a sudden, we have a drug that has no limit to its administration? You are told how many times to give anti-rabies injection to your dog every year; you are told how many times to take Paracetamol for a headache before consulting a doctor; or how many times to take Chloroquine to stop malaria. Why is the case of polio different? Every advert for polio, including the one that crystallised this strong reaction from me, insists that "every child from 0-59 months" must be immunised EVERY TIME the immunisation days come. There is more to this than meets the eye. We have every reason to think like this.
When the then dean of ABU Pharmacy School, Dr (now Prof) Haruna Kaita took samples of the oral polio vaccines (OPV) from Nigeria to India for analyses and declared that the vaccines were contaminated, those of us who saw the thorough, scientific nature of the work he and his colleagues did, presented in over 200 pages, laughed when the federal government brought a one-page report of their 'scientific analysis'. Kaita asked, finally, why NAFDAC, whose job it was to screen vaccines, had not screened them. He was not insisting on his own results; he was asking that due process be followed by having NAFDAC certify the vaccines. Only then did we know that Dora Akunyili had a South African lab result that showed that the vaccines were contaminated, but the federal government forbade her from releasing it. That was under Dere Awosika, an influential pharmacist who was head of the National Programme on Immunisation (NPI). Dora never did, which was why all the vaccines that came out under that programme had no NAFDAC certification or number. Our children were thus given fake vaccines, since NAFDAC defines a product as fake if it has not been certified by the agency whether it comes from the best factory in the world. It was only last year after Awosika's replacement with Abebe that Akunyili disclosed during a courtesy visit that she hoped that the coming of Abebe marked the end of the era when NAFDAC was not allowed to screen polio vaccines. Can anyone imagine that coming from our own superwoman Akunyili?! It shows that toes are bigger than toes and you cannot afford to step anyhow.
To counter Kaita's analysis of the oral polio vaccines, the federal government sent a team of traditional rulers with a few medical practitioners to South Africa and Indonesia, ostensibly to go and screen polio vaccines. These people didn't know that some of the analyses to verify the presence and quantities of some of the impurities required many days in the lab; they made an itinerary that may not even complete the analysis in one laboratory. When they came back, they were transparent enough to publish their results in The Punch newspaper to show the level of their ignorance about the matter. Their results said six out of 12 samples contained the sex hormones that Dr Datti Ahmed had alerted the nation about. The newspaper headline was: "Report Confirms Safety of Oral Polio Vaccine," and it reported, "The equipment showed that 6 samples out of 12 samples had trace levels of Estradiol above Minimum Quantifiable Level" (The Punch, February 27, 2004).
Yet, do you know what the team said in their advert? They said the vaccines were safe for consumption because the levels of the contaminants in the vaccines were 'negligible' ("Based on all evidence before us, including interactions held with professionals and scientists in various countries, it is the considered opinion of the Committee that the Oral Polio Vaccine, when used as recommended in the Polio Eradication Programme in Nigeria is safe"). That was 50% failure for a pharmaceutical product and some persons say, "That's fine; no problem"? That was the last time I felt the way I am now: furious. We know that all pharmaceutical products that record as much as 0.5% failure are usually trashed. But this is a 100 times that, and we are told that it is safe and in some states parents are to give their children the vaccines or be refused certain rights and privileges like sending their children to certain schools. The lives of innocent Nigerians are being put at risk! If it were the lives of adults who are the ones making the choices, it would have been easier to comprehend.
When sex hormones are discovered and they are called routine impurities in negligible amounts, the next question a pharmaceutical analyst would ask is, "Is this impurity expected in this product, judging by the channel of its production?" The answer determines what to do. For instance, if you produce garri from cassava, chaffs of the cassava are expectedly contaminants. Same with cow fat in milk, and sand in rice milled after spreading the parboiled rice and husk on the bare floor. However, polio vaccines are grown and harvested in the kidneys of some species of monkeys. Kidneys are not sex organs and do not traditionally produce sex hormones, which are substances expected from other organs such as the scrotum or ovaries. How come then that sex hormones were CONSISTENTLY found in the samples taken by Haruna Kaita, NAFDAC and the FG team? Even if one could excuse the trace quantities as either false-positive results or products of accidental contamination, how come they are found in basically uniform amounts, even though the vaccines were imported from different countries? And how come Awosika, the then head of the NPI, never allowed the body saddled with the duty of certifying drug products fit for consumption in Nigeria (NAFDAC) formal access to perform their statutory duties? Was there anything amiss? Maybe.
We are worried now, anyway. Immunisation officials have been strutting about the streets without asking questions about our children's ages, but are busy instilling drops of the OPV in their small orifices. Whether the officials have instructions as to the dose of the vaccines, they are not telling that to parents. They are not saying, "If you have given your child this vaccine four times, which is the end; the child is protected." No one has said a thing like that to my hearing. I write this publicly so that I may be corrected and so that the relevant authorities may be corrected. If there is a point I am missing, it is not the fact that all the polio adverts have omitted stating the dose; that one is obvious. From now on, perhaps we can start hearing of the dose and start submitting our children a little less reluctantly. It is many years now that have gotten me this convinced. However, I write because I have a settled point of view, but the fear that I may not be right is worth nursing.
Okenyodo, a pharmacist and journalist, is the National Publicity Secretary (North) of the Association of Nigerian Authors (ANA)

Original_Search_Title: Mayor Implores Residents On Food Security
Headline: Rwanda: Mayor Implores Residents On Food Security
Body_Text:
Kigali — The Mayor of Huye District, Aimable Twagiramutara has appealed to residents to store more food to reduce poverty and eliminate the culture of begging common in the area.
In an interview with The New Times, on January 29, Twagiramutara said that increase of food supplies is the crucial solution for the increasing exodus of residents from villages to Butare Town.
He explained, "In abide to control the continued exodus of beggars from rural areas to town; the district authorities are commitmented to increasing food supplies in rural sectors through mobilization of residents to grow more valuable food crops and construct storage facilities. We therefore call on all people to grow more food to save themselves from poverty and begging syndrome."
He however attributed begging of some residents in town to lack of self respect laziness and dependency left behind by the first missionary priests who lured convicts into Christianity by giving them cheap items.
"Laying a hand for something is worse off than hunger itself. Yes, lack of food is one of the factors. However, issues of laziness and lack of self respect can be attributed to rural -Urban exodus," the mayor added.
Being a King's home coupled with missionary legacy of giving out materials in turn for convicts into Christianity, some Butare residents are still caught up in Master-servant mode of living, a factor authorities have tried to break but with serious challenges.
Early in the morning, ably young women carrying babies on their back, handicapped and youth, trek long distances from their homes to Butare town for begging.
Contacted for comment, the provincial Governor, Eraste Kabera said food insecurity is responsibility for social and economic instabilities in some parts of the Province.
Storing food through granaries, farming in accordance to geological compositions and increase of drought persistent food crops are some of the strategies Huye district is vehemently pursuing to control food inadequacies.
In line with government's program of fighting poverty by giving a cow per poor family, 223 cows have been given to needy families that have fulfilled all requirements.
To increase peoples' purchasing power, coffee has been identified as Huye's fortune hence; residents have been argued to learn a lesson from Maraba's specialty coffee success story both at regional and international markets.
In 2006, at least 50060 coffee seedlings were planted and this year, over two million coffee seedlings are to be planted as a long term strategy of fighting poverty at house hold level.
Butare was most hit by 1994 Genocide, living hundreds of thousand children orphans and helpless widows.
To remedy accommodation problem, a factor also cited for the increased elderly beggars, Huye district is to construct 400 houses for vulnerable groups.
To effectively contain hunger, Twagiramutara said the district has designed special programme of encouraging residents to grow rice and cassava.
One beggar who admitted of having cultivable land said poverty is the major cause of their movement from village to Butare town. According to the district officials, Save Sector of Gisagara District is the one with many beggars.
Butare Police has insisted that district authorities provide alternatives for the beggars and street children because they cause public disorders and insecurity in town.

Original_Search_Title: Agriculture - Fertile Ground For Investment
Headline: Cameroon: Agriculture - Fertile Ground For Investment
Body_Text:
Apart from the consistent supply of agric tools to Cameroon, many Chinese companies have flowing into the country.
The commissioning last Friday, 26 January, of the Chinese rice and cassava production, processing and marketing project, SINO-CAM IKO in Nanga Eboko, Upper Sanaga Division by the Minister of State for Agriculture and Rural Development, Jean Nkuete, alongside four other members of government, tells the story better of the excellent relationship between Cameroon and China in the agriculture sector.
The influx of Chinese companies is the logical follow up of the sixth Cameroon-China joint commission which met in Yaounde in October, 2005 and the deliberations at the China Africa summit of last year where President Paul Biya actively took part. Agriculture stands out as one of the major areas of concern for China. On several occasions, businesses in this country have expressed their desire to invest in the sector. This interest has its roots as far back as 1971 when the two countries established diplomatic links. "We are thinking about sectors such as forestry, oil, gas, agriculture, livestock, etc", the Chinese vice Minister of Commerce, Wei Janguo had stated during the joint commission in Cameroon.
During the same occasion, he said there are several things that China and Cameroon can do both in Cameroon and China. "Chinese enterprises can transfer technology to Cameroon", he said. In fact, he emphasized on assembling of machineries. In effect, a Chinese tractor assembly factory has since been earmarked for Kribi. Pending the installation of the factory, the country has been providing assistance to Cameroon through the supply of farm tools which are usually given out to the Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Development to give out to farmers through farmer groups in a bid to enhance mechanised agriculture in Cameroon.
Cameroon on her part has demonstrated her determination to facilitate things for Chinese investment in the country. In an interview with China news last September, PM Inoni said Cameroon has published an investment law, which encourages foreign investment in the country's agriculture, transportation and water projects. "This is also applicable to Chinese investors," he said, adding that agriculture could be a priority area for bilateral cooperation. Agricultural industry contributes one fifth of Cameroon's total gross domestic product (GDP) and employs 60 percent of its labourers. However, the lack of technical expertise has severely hindered its development; hence the presence of Chinese technocrats remains a plus.
Statistics show that China has provided Cameroon about 100 million U.S. dollars of economic aid and bilateral trade volume exceeded 190 million dollars in 2005.

Original_Search_Title: Choir Leader Defiles Nine Children
Headline: Rwanda: Choir Leader Defiles Nine Children
Body_Text:
Kigali — The choir leader of Bethsaida Kids' Choir Jean Baptist Tuyiramye Safari, 26, of ADPR Kabagali in Kibaza Cell of Kacyiru Sector in Gasabo District has been alleged to have defiled nine members of the choir he leads, impregnating four of them. The news of the alleged acts of defilement came in the limelight after one of the defiled girls, only identified as Anike, bled profusely after an botched abortion. She is admitted at the Kigali Central Hospital/Muhima Maternity, where she is in a coma.
Anike, a primary six leaver that was set to join secondary, was, on 11th January, sent to Gitarama to look for a school but, having realized that she was three months pregnant, instead went to Tuyiramye. The latter advised her to carry out an abortion. The rudimentary methods that were used at first failed, until a one Sarah tried to use a cassava stem to extract the foetus, leading to severe bleeding.
It is alleged that Tuyiramye used to tell the female choir members that he had to find out whether those that were serving God had their virginity intact, defiling them in the process.
"We used to practice from the church. But as time went on, he [choir leader] told us that we had to change the venue and, instead, practice from his home where he stays alone with his young sister," one of the defiled choir members who preferred anonymity told this reporter.
Church leaders found at the temple told The Sunday Times that they were shocked.
"We trusted him so much. Most of us have always looked at him as a model Christian. There is a time when I found him punishing one of his choir members, saying that he saw her standing with boys. So I thought that the guy is serious and I started respecting him, only to be iced by his defiling acts," said Berchimas Habiyambere, whose 13-year-old daughter is a victim.
Tuyiramye is now said to be in hiding either in Southern Province or Burundi. Police are investigating.

Original_Search_Title: Prisoners Form HIV-Positive Association Behind Bars
Headline: Burundi: Prisoners Form HIV-Positive Association Behind Bars
Body_Text:
Bujumbura — HIV-positive inmates in Mpimba Prison in Burundi's capital, Bujumbura, have found that the best way to survive the hardship of being incarcerated in the overcrowded facility is by banding together to support each other.
Originally named 'Mboshwe Kabiri', Kirundi for 'detained twice', the association for prisoners living with the virus later settled on a more uplifting name, 'Turemeshanye', meaning 'support one another'.
Georges Ndabihawe, chairman of the association, told PlusNews the group helped each other access food, drugs and psychological support. The organisation was especially useful because many HIV-positive prisoners' families gave up on them, particularly when they were not expected to survive a long sentence.
The jail was built to accommodate 800 prisoners but currently holds more than 3,000, and food is very hard to come by. The diet of 350g of beans and 350g of cassava flour per day was "not even enough for a child", said Martin Yabu, who is HIV positive.
"Doctors tell me to eat well, to cover myself up correctly; sometimes I feel like laughing at them," said Vincent Tugirimana, who is also HIV positive. He has been incarcerated since 2000 on a charge of murder but is still awaiting trial.
Tugirimana has always managed to get something to eat when taking his morning dose of antiretroviral (ARV) drugs, but for the evening one, he said, "it is only God's will".
According to Baselisse Ndayisaba, coordinator of the Burundi chapter of the Society for Women against AIDS in Africa (SWAA), the problem does not lie in the quantity, but the poor quality of the prison diet.
To help positive inmates supplement their diet, SWAA Burundi helped Turemeshanye start a small restaurant in 2006 as an income-generating activity. The inmates take turns to work in the restaurant and are thus able to supplement their diet.
The women's organisation also provides ARVs, and twice a week brings a doctor to Mpimba to treat opportunistic infections. The government's treatment programme bypasses prisoners, and the group is now lobbying for inclusion in the national rollout.
Despite the existence of the 60-member Turemeshanye, Ndabihawe noted that stigma remained high in the jail and many inmates were reluctant to join the group, as this would confirm their HIV status to the rest of the prisoners. "Those who accept to come in the open have no choice," he said. "Rather than dying unattended, they accept their status."
SWAA Burundi is tackling ignorance about HIV in the facility and regularly shows documentaries, which are as much to educate inmates about the pandemic as they are to prevent those who are negative from contracting the virus.
Most of Turemeshanye's members have been trained as peer counsellors, and chairman Ndabihawe said the group also aimed to make sure negative prisoners stayed negative.
"Some inmates may develop high-risk behaviour such as homosexuality," Ndabihawe said, adding that poor living conditions forced some female inmates to sell sex for money. Condoms are not distributed by prison officials, but SWAA offers them to prisoners who request them, who are mostly female inmates.
A New Year's Day proclamation by Burundi's President Pierre Nkurunziza that the elderly, children and people suffering from incurable diseases would be released early from prison has given the HIV-positive inmates some hope, but until then, Turemeshanye will keep lobbying the national AIDS control council for better food and access to drugs.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Directive On Sweet Potato Ban Was a Joke - Mutsindashyaka
Headline: Rwanda: Directive On Sweet Potato Ban Was a Joke - Mutsindashyaka
Body_Text:
Rwamagana — The Provincial Governor, Theonest Mutsindashyaka has clarified that his recent comment on banning sweet potato in the province was a mere joke, meant to encourage people to grow more valued crops.
In an interview with The New Times on Wednesday January 24, Mutsindashyaka however insisted that sweet potatoes should be grown for domestic consumption but not relied on as a cash crop to generate people's incomes and welfare.
The Governor was reacting to The New Times story that was published on January 23, under the headline Mutsindashyaka bans sweet potato growing. While meeting district agricultural and district leaders recently, the Governor was quoted as having directed residents to stop growing sweet potato and told the officials to ensure the implementation of the directive. However, the issue has since generated mixed reaction from the public.
"I was not well interpreted as my comment never meant banning sweet potato growing but only advised people to consider not relying on it as a cash crop to improve their incomes. I said many things and that one was just a joke meant not to cause any negative implications.
