It was a statement aimed at encouraging people to consider more valuable produce," Mutsindashyaka clarified.
The governor stressed that he only advised farmers to think of growing crops like cassava which have high yields and command a high price on the market than sweet potato.
"Banning sweet potatoes is not part of my mandate. I am an implementer and not a decision maker. My stance on sweet potato growing was made in good faith only meant to advise farmers to think of Agriculture in terms of the cash it generates in homesteads. This is in line with the Vision 2020 as regards revolutionising the agricultural sector which is depended on by over 90 percent of the population," he illustrated his point.
Agriculture in Rwanda is still largely subsistence, characterised by small holdings and low soil productivity due to over cultivation and low use of modern inputs. This sector is one of the seven major sectors targeted by the Economic Development and Poverty Reduction Strategy (EDPRS).
Vision 2020 spells out that by then, subsistence agriculture should have been replaced by fully monetised commercial agriculture. This necessitates producing high value and market oriented products. However, according to agricultural experts, over 60 percent of the population in the country depend on sweet potato as their chief food for survival.

Original_Search_Title: Supplies Stabilise
Headline: Uganda: Supplies Stabilise
Body_Text:
Kampala — A STEADY supply of foodstuffs has kept prices stable, a weekly survey has shown.
Robert Mugisa, a trader at Nakasero Market, said the supplies had continued to increase despite heavy rains upcountry.
"We are receiving plenty of foodstuffs and expect more as the harvesting season reaches a peak," he explained.
"If the rains continue at this rate, transportation may be hampered because the roads will become impassable," Mugisa said.
Paul Muyigi, a trader in Mukono, said there was plenty of maize, cassava, matooke, beans, soya and groundnuts in the markets.
"The supplies are the schools' traditional foods. That may lead to high demand," Muyigi said.
The new primary and secondary schools' term will start in February. Maize flour and beans dominate most of the schools' menu.
A kilogramme of beans remained at sh850, maize flour sh700 while groundnuts were at sh1,800.
Meanwhile, sugar prices have fallen to sh2,000 from sh2,500 a kilogramme due to increased supply.
A bunch of matooke was between sh5,000 and sh7,000.

Original_Search_Title: FEC Approves N35b for Healthcare Centres in LGA, Others
Headline: Nigeria: FEC Approves N35b for Healthcare Centres in LGA, Others
Body_Text:
Abuja — The Federal Executive Council (FEC) has given conditional approval for the construction of N35 billion comprehensive healthcare centres in the 774 Local Government Areas in the country.
Minister of Information and Communications, Mr. Frank Nweke (Jnr), while briefing the media after the council meeting on January 24 said the construction of the health centres was an initiative of the Association of Local Governments of Nigeria (ALGON) on behalf of the local governments under a Private-Public Partnership (PPP).
He further explained that council approved the project with the condition that the consent of each chairman of the 774 local governments including approval by the local government legislature is obtained and the execution must not be in breach of any law or any provision of the constitution.
The minister said each unit estimated to cost N4.8 million would have a primary health centre, staff quarters and equipment. Adding that after meeting the stipulated conditions the Ministry of Finance would deduct the fund at source and pay the contractors in proportion of the work done.
According to Mr. Nweke (Jnr) Council also adopted new measures to stem the incessant Military-Police clash problem while a committee to advise it was established under the Minister of Defence, Ambassador Thomas Aguiyi-Ironsi to ensure that the crisis never happens again.
Other measures taken to end clashes by the military, paramilitary and civilian population include:
- Civilian membership of military and paramilitary mess is to be encouraged
- Troop-carrying vehicles are to be made available to the military to minimise their use of civilian vehicles
- Peace committee is to be put in place immediately by all the nation's local and state governments in order to check the outbreak or escalation of such incidents
- Joint training courses are to be encouraged for the military and the police, at all ranks, to help lower the feeling of superiority by any
- Government is to take pro-active steps to enhance the roles of military and police agencies.
- Decoration of civilian vehicles in military emblem must be discontinued.
- Disciplinary measures for officers of the Nigerian military and police found to have performed below expectations thereby taking positions prejudicial to service discipline.
The council also approved the following:
- Approval for the supply of 253,062 packs of anti-retroviral drugs for the treatment of Human Immuno-deficiency Virus/Acquired Immune Deficiency Syndrome (HIV/AIDS) at a total cost of N3.071 billion.
- N520 million for the supply of 2.8 million doses of potent and safe artemisin combined therapy for the treatment of malaria. Both are to be supplied by local manufacturers.
- N3.9 billion for the construction of transmission lines from Apo sub-station-Gwagwalada sub-station, Gwagwalada sub-station -Kokuabba and the Kokuabba-Apo sub-station
- Construction of the new transmission lines from New Abeokuta-Otta-Ogba-Papalanto at a cost of N4.9 billion
- Production of a master-plan for the development of the Inland Water Transportation in Nigeria
- Construction of an inland port at Lokoja at a total cost of N2.3 billion with completion period of 11 months
- Rehabilitation of the Lagos Port Harbour Mauls by a Chinese firm at a cost of N16.5 million
- Rehabilitation of the Nasco Dam, Niger State at a total cost of N25.1 million.
- Payment of $5million pledged to the International Fund for Agriculture Development (IFAD)
- Ratification of the ECOWAS Convention relating to small-scale arms and that of the United Nations (UN) Resolution on global arms trade treaty.
- The enforcement of five per cent cassava content in all the nation's flourmills.

Original_Search_Title: Help People to Feed Themselves, Governments Urged
Headline: Africa: Help People to Feed Themselves, Governments Urged
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Eighty percent of Africans who experience frequent food shortages live on poor land in rural areas - neglect of such communities is one of the main causes of hunger on the continent, campaigners for the right to food said.
Speaking at a seminar on food security at the ongoing World Social Forum in the Kenyan capital Nairobi, Michael Windfuhr, the human rights director of the German charity, Bread for the World, said 50 percent of the hungry were small farmers who lived in areas without reliable infrastructure or credit facilities.
"Governments should try to use the maximum available resources in order to enable people to feed themselves," Windfuhr said on Wednesday, citing cases of forced evictions where people lose access to land and water and lose their livelihoods.
Other speakers said governments often seemed unaware of people's right to food. Mike Anane, coordinator for FIAN, an international NGO that campaigns for the right of people to feed themselves, said violations of the right to food in Ghana also resulted from poor water management.
People have become so dependent on maize ... that they say there is no food if there is no maize
"Entire rivers and streams are diverted by mining companies towards their tributaries, thus depriving these communities of water for irrigation purposes," Anane said.
In addition, he said, the dumping of mining rock waste on farmland also rendered the land unfit for cultivation.
Promotion of traditional African food crops and implementation of the African Union's Maputo Declaration, which urges governments to increase funding to agriculture to 10 percent of national budgets, would help improve food security, according to Anne Maina, advocacy officer for Zambia-based Participatory Ecological Land-Use Management (PALUM). The organisation is an umbrella body of grassroots civil society groups in eastern and southern Africa.
Lack of clear-cut government policies on agriculture could also undermine efforts by the people to feed themselves, she said.
Another fundamental reason for food insecurity in eastern and southern Africa, Maina said, was the collapse of agricultural marketing boards due to corruption and mismanagement. She cited Malawi and Kenya, where some marketing boards had foundered, exposing farmers to exploitation by middle-men.
Over-dependence on maize, which needs a lot of rain, as the main food crop in southern and eastern Africa was another factor that undermined food security in the region, she said.
Photo: Siegfried Modola/IRIN
A crowd of participants at the World Social Forum
"People have become so dependent on maize that they say there is no food if there is no maize, yet there are other [drought-resistant] food crops they can depend on," Maina said. These include cassava, yam and pumpkins.
Lack of women's access to land for cultural and traditional reasons was another hindrance to the right to food, said Huguette Akplogan-Dossa, national coordinator of Contrôle Citoyen, a local nongovernmental organisation in Benin affiliated to the international NGO, Social Watch.
Since 1999, the organisation has been helping women in Benin negotiate access to land and micro-credit financing, thereby enhancing food production.
"Local authorities have begun to understand the need for women to have access to land," Akplogan-Dossa said.
Windfuhr said states were obliged, under the United Nations International Covenant on Economic, Social and Cultural Rights, to take necessary action to mitigate and alleviate hunger even in times of natural or other disasters.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: FEC Rejects Relocation of Barracks
Headline: Nigeria: FEC Rejects Relocation of Barracks
Body_Text:
Abuja — The Federal Executive Council (FEC) has rejected the relocation of all barracks away from civilian populated areas as a means of stopping the constant clash between the military, police and civilians.
It however, after going through a white report on the clash between the police and soldiers between May 22 and 25, 2005 during which lives and property were destroyed, decided to discontinue the use of military stickers by civilians, encourage more interaction between all the groups and make available vehicles that would carry troops instead of using civilian vehicles.
The Council also approved the setting up of a Committee to advise the government on how to ensure discontinuance of such incidence.
The Council meeting which was presided over by President Olusegun Obasanjo also approved a N27.1 billion contract (N35 million per unit) under a Private-Public sector Partnership (PPP) for the construction of comprehensive healthcare centres in each of the 774 Local Governments in the country as a means of bringing government's involvement closer to the people.
The Minister of Information and Communications, Frank Nweke disclosed these while briefing State House Correspondents alongside the Minister of Commerce and Industry, Dr. Aliyu Modibbo.
He added that President Olusegun Obasanjo has directed the immediate enforcement of an order he gave earlier on the use of five per cent cassava content in all the nation's flour mills.
While emphasising on the need for all Nigerians, whether in the military or civilian to respect each other, Nweke added that the Council also resolved that civilian membership of military and paramilitary Mess be encouraged; a peace committee be put in place immediately by all the nation's local and state governments in order to nip in the bud, any outbreak or escalation of such incidents; joint training courses encouraged for the military and police at all ranks in order to help lower the feeling of superiority by member of the forces; and the government takes pro-active steps to enhance the roles of military and police agencies.

Original_Search_Title: Punishment for Excellence?
Headline: Nigeria: Punishment for Excellence?
Body_Text:
Lagos — Each time democracy is put on the firing line or under the guillotine, about to be beheaded, we expect good people to quickly speak out. The situation in Owan East Local Government Council in Edo State portends grave threat to our nascent democracy. It must be checked quickly.
David Thoreau (1817-1862) was essentially right when he argued rather convincingly, that in a situation where just people are imprisoned unjustly, the only just place for any just person is also the prison. And in these short years, we have come to realize that the world on its own does not have problems. Rather, we humans keep introducing bundles of problems into the world. It is politically alluring to hop on the problems of the common man for, indeed, it cannot be said that the common man has no problems. He has excruciating problems.
But it is also true that the "uncommon man" has a lot of problems and that these problems often times grow with him. The "uncommon man" is more the subject of envy as he begins to progress; he also becomes more attractive to the assassin's bullets while armed robbers have every cause to dare him on a continuing basis. In fact, his undoing could be that he is offered a job, which another person wanted or that his promotion is too rapid.
All the same, shall we wake up in the morning and pray in the face of the foregoing, that we should not be successful? God forbid! Even where the successful ones are attacked daily, we must all continue to pray for success.
This is where Hon. Pally Iriase comes in. How did it all start? At the inception of the current democratic experiment, Iriase was elected to represent Owan East Constituency in the Edo State House of Assembly. He was one of the few viable and articulate members of the House. He soon became a ranking member of that legislature.
To the initial chagrin and disappointment of his friends, he announced that he was to leave the legislative job and move to the Executive arm. He was going to seek election as Chairman of his home Local Government, Owan East. Asked why he would want to engage in what looked like a demotion from the State level to a lower rung of the ladder, Iriase said, "I seek power to provide the greatest good to the greatest number of Owan East citizens". In an attempt to convince him to remain in the legislature where he had made a very impressive outing, he was even reminded of the philosophy of the late sage, Senator Chuba Okadigbo, "I would rather dine with kings and queens than with local government councillors". He retorted: "What am I doing here when my people back home have no roads? Why should I be here when my people are in perpetual darkness? What am I doing here. It was from him we heard that Local Governments could tar roads and provide electricity to the people. At first, it sounded like the usual campaign rhetoric.
The darkest part of the night is just before dawn. When the Local Government elections were delayed for about one year, it was beginning to appear to Pally's friends that the man had taken a dangerous gamble. His chairmanship aspiration was finally realized when he was sworn in on 3rd May 2004.
Little did anyone know that this was going to be the beginning of an all-round revolution in Owan East. There is no space here to catalogue the achievements of the Iriase-led administration in the first two years. We shall only attempt to highlight a few of them. In the area of energy, it is a delight to know that many communities have been electrified: ERAH (Ugbovbighan and Olenlo)"over 10 km of high and low tension stringing plus three substations; IGUE-SALEH and IVBIOMION COMMUNITIES
(Ikhin, Arokho, Ohanmi, Uroe and Iruoke) - over 45km of high and low tension stringing plus 5 substations); Extension of Electricity from Uokha to Igbira Camp; Installation of 110KVA Perkins sound-proof plant at the Council secretariat; installation of 200KVA transformer at the Council Secretariat to further boost electricity supply; 300KVA Transformer instated at Ihievbe to boost electricity supply.
In the area of roads, enormous work has been done: seven roads have been fully tarred as follows: Amoya (2.5km x 7.3m); Johnson Street (1km x 6m); Azama College (0.6km x 6m); Otuo Township Road (3.4km x 7.3m); Ekeinde Street (1.2km x 6m); Ojealaro Street (1.2km x 6m); Ihievbe market Road (1.2km x 6m); Construction of Ohigbai Road Bridge; Construction of 502m concrete drainage in Afuze; Construction of concrete drainages on Otuo Township Road, Ihievbe Market Road, Iyakhara-Warrake Road, Afeho Crescent and Ikhueran Road. All other roads not yet asphalted are constantly bulldozed, graded and properly maintained.
The Iriase-led Administration has neglected no sector. All the educational and health institutions, which were in a rather deplorable state at the inception of the Pally Iriase-led administration, have been given a face-lift and made functional. It is worthy of note that in the area of agriculture, the Council has acquired a tractor and a slasher and has cultivated 10 hectares of experimental cassava farm on Uokha Road. Farm inputs, including chemicals are constantly supplied to cocoa farms.
It is also instructive to note that the Iriase-led Administration inherited a debt of N125 million when it came in and this debt has been fully defrayed. Right now, there are numerous ongoing projects at various stages of completion. President Obasanjo must hear this: There is a real revolution taking place in Owan East and this revolution should change his earlier categorization of the 774 Local Government Headquarters throughout the Nation as Stealing Centres. Pally Iriase has successfully removed Owan East from this category.
While most of the people of Owan East are happy with the wind of development in their area, there are some who feel endangered by Pally Iriase's towering political image. To these ones, the man must be stopped by all means and at all cost. Once upon a time, the Edo State House of Assembly bestowed on itself the executive power to remove an erring Chairman by a resolution supported by two-thirds of its members.
It soon dawned on the House of Assembly that time was fast running out and that it had not experimented on its bogus law. Then a petition must be induced from Owan East Local Government. In the process, all the laws of mathematics and natural justice must be subverted. We hear that the petition was sponsored and signed by three out of the eleven Councillors from the Local Government: James Igbaromah (Ward I), Joseph Ojeaga (Ward II) and Andrew Osigwe (Ward IV). The petition was rushed in on the 8th of January, 2007.
We also hear that immediately the petition was received, members were recalled from their recess for an "emergency session". In less than ten minutes, it was all over. That was how long it took the Assembly to break its own law! Instead of the two-thirds vote required to remove the Chairman, the 11 members plus the Speaker who were in attendance did the job. Perhaps inadvertently, the Speaker promoted Hon. Ekhator (PDP/Ovia North East) over himself by making him Chairman of the Committee, which in the same stretch and in the same chamber removed Hon. Pally Iriase (oops, he was not removed. He was merely suspended for the remaining part of his tenure). Under the pseudo committee system, there was no need to hear from Iriase. He was already guilty as charged! What a smart way to punish excellence! We are still lost in the democratic jungle. At least, the 18th century American settlers knew more than that when they openly proclaimed, "Taxation without representation is a tyranny". Let history remain the best judge.

Original_Search_Title: Mtsindashyaka Bans Sweet Potato Growing
Headline: Rwanda: Mtsindashyaka Bans Sweet Potato Growing
Body_Text:
Kigali — The provincial Governor, Theonest Mutsindashyaka has issued a directive banning the cultivation of sweet potatoes in his controversial bid to improve agriculture in the province.
Mutsindashyaka, who issued the directive last Friday, said the rationale for this decision is that Potato growing can never improve household incomes.
Addressing agricultural officers from all districts and Sectors in the Province together with Vice Mayors in charge of Economic affairs in districts, Mutsindashyaka warned agricultural officers against allowing farmers to plant potatoes in this coming season.
He said: "If we have to improve the incomes of our people, we have to advise them or at some point force them to follow ways that will lead them to this desired end. Not a single person in this area can attest the fact that potatoes increase household income. People should be encouraged to plant cassava instead because of its cash value."
This directive follows yet another controversial but well intended policy of planting crops in rows. In an interview after the meeting, majority of the agricultural officers revealed that the policy has been met with stiff resistance from farmers.
However in districts like Kayonza, Rwamagana and Bugesera where the policy of planting in rows has been successful, production per acre of land has more than doubled.
According to one agricultural officer who preferred anonymity, implementing the directive on the ban of sweet potatoes is next to impossible.
"People have survived on potatoes for centuries. They have always turned to them in times of hunger because of the short period they take to mature. It will be hard for us to implement this directive," he said.
Analysts of Mutsidashyaka's policies as regards revolutionizing agriculture in the province appreciate the fact that if well implemented, they can make a tangible improvement in household incomes.
In Bugesera District, according a report from the province, the number of people below the poverty line has fallen from 89 percent to 57percent, after a successful implementation of the initiative to regulate cereal trade and joining cooperatives.
Analysts however appreciate that some of these decisions are rushed are forced on people without their will. This is the sole reason why they are met with strong resistance.
According to one opinion leader in the Province, people have to be consulted before such decisions are made. They should also have an input for them to take ownership of the policies which in the long run are meant to benefit them.

Original_Search_Title: Fadama Contractors Advised to Complete Jobs
Headline: Nigeria: Fadama Contractors Advised to Complete Jobs
Body_Text:
Owerri — Imo State Project Coordinator of Fadama II Project, Mr Nnaemeka Anyanwu, has advised Service Providers (contractors) to the Fadama Community Associations and Fadama Users Groups (FUGs), to complete on-going projects, to enable beneficiaries achieve their overall goals.
Anyanwu, who gave the advise during an interactive meeting in Owerri, said essence of the meeting was to take over on implementation process, with the view of improving on current performances of all the Services Providers and beneficiaries.
He said the decision to call for the meeting was informed by the recent monitoring visits to sub-project sites by the State Fadama Development Committee (SFDC), and the State Fadama Development Office (SFDO), stating that some Service Providers failed to complete some of the sub-projects given to them by Fadama Users.
The Fadama II Development Project, Anyanwu stated, is part of the state government's strategy to reduce rural poverty by empowering beneficiaries to implement viable economic enterprises in the rural communities.
According to him, these economic enterprises include small-scale irrigation, fishery, feeder roads, poultry, agro-processing, snailery, bee-keeping, advisory services and capacity building, adding that with the full support of all stakeholders, including the state government, 70 cassava processing plants have been established.
Others, he said, include 65 oil mills, 52 boreholes, 20 coldrooms, 53 poultry sub-projects, six feed mills and over 60 kilometre feeder roads.
The Fadama II project in the state has also funded rehabilitation of 15 markets, 25 piggery projects, six VIP latrines, bridges and small scale irrigation systems in and around the 11 participating local government areas of the state.
He commended some of the Service Providers who have successfully completely their sub-projects at record time and urged them to obtain their certificate of completion from the State Fadama Development Office (SFDO) to facilitate final payment.

Original_Search_Title: Bill Gates And the Green Revolution to End Hunger in Africa
Headline: Africa: Bill Gates And the Green Revolution to End Hunger in Africa
Body_Text:
Kaduna — The world richest man and co-chair of the Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation (BAMGF), Bill Gates, was in Nigeria recently to monitor the country's progress in the fight against poverty and diseases. Weekly Trust x-ray Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation (BAMGF)'s effort in this regard so far.
Microsoft founder and co-chair of Bill and Melinda Foundation, Bill Gates, was in Nigeria last week with his wife under the auspices of their foundation to monitor the Nigeria's progress in the fight against HIV/AIDS and poverty. Gates had an audience with President Obasanjo and pledged their foundation's continued assistance to Nigeria in the fight against poverty and diseases such as tuberculoses, polio, and HIV/AIDS.
Since the Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation (BAMGF) was established in 2000, over 60% of the foundation's money has been spent on sub-Saharan Africa and Southeast Asia, leaving 40% for the rest of the world.
According to Nigerian Professor Idris Muhammed, a professor of medicine: "The Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation (BAMGF) has established and funded an 'AIDS prevention initiative in Nigeria' (APIN) tied to the Harvard School of Public Health (HSPH), which has studied the disease from several perspectives. BAMGF has funded the production and publication of what has been described as the best documentation of AIDS in Nigeria under the auspices of APIN."
According to the information available in the Bill Gates website Bill and Melinda Gates philanthropy also expects to improve healthcare in Nigeria through their intervention in the meningitis vaccine project, in which the foundation has invested $70 million. Cerebro-spinal meningitis, a major killer in Nigeria, killed hundreds of Nigerians in1996 when it ravaged Kano state.
The Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation opened another ground-breaking chapter on September 14, 2006 when it announced four grants totalling $68.2 million to accelerate research on neglected tropical diseases, including hookworm, leishmaniasis and trypanosomiasis, which kill or disable millions of people in the world's poorest countries every year. One of the grants will support a new medical journal devoted to neglected tropical diseases.
Â· Infectious Disease Research Institute (IDRI) will receive $32 million to develop a new and affordable therapeutic vaccine to safely treat leishmaniasis, a debilitating and often fatal parasitic disease that affects more than 12 million people in developing countries. Existing treatment for leishmaniasis requires a long course of toxic, painful and expensive injections. The grant will support a six-year programme to develop the vaccine and conduct clinical trials in India, Sudan and Brazil where leishmaniasis is common.
Â· Sabin Vaccine Institute (SVI) will receive $13.8 million develop a vaccine to prevent hookworm, which affects more than 600 million people worldwide and is a leading cause of anaemia and malnutrition among children and women of childbearing age in many developing countries. The only currently available method to control hookworm is by repeated drug treatment, which can lead to drug resistance. The grant will support the human hookworm vaccine initiative which SVI is to develop in partnership with other research institutions in the US, Europe, Australia, and Brazil.
Â· University of North Carolina (UNC) will receive $21.3 million to develop effective and inexpensive drugs for treating the late stages of leishmaniasis and trypanosomiasis. Trypanosomiasis, otherwise known as "sleeping sickness," infects over 65 million people in sub-Saharan Africa every year and kills 300,000 people. Treatment is currently expensive, difficult to administer, and often toxic or ineffective. UNC will lead a consortium of researchers from the U.S., Europe, and Kenya to develop new and better drugs for the two diseases.
Â· Public Library of Science (PLoS) will receive $1.1 million to launch PLoS Neglected Tropical Diseases, a new open-access, peer-reviewed medical journal covering the science and policy advocacy for neglected tropical diseases. While other medical journals have paid increased attention to neglected diseases in recent years, few have focused on neglected tropical diseases. The new journal will provide an important forum for scientists from developed and developing countries alike to share the latest information on neglected tropical disease research.
The Green Revolution to End Hunger
Sub-Saharan Africa is the only place in the world where people have less food year after year. Farmers in the region are today forced to contend with challenges their parents never dreamed of. They have no choice as the population grows but to cultivate their land more intensively, which takes nutrients out of the soil and increases disease and insect infestation. Add the increasingly volatile weather to the equation and tens of millions of Africans are condemned to live on the edge of starvation.
Starvation has long threatened the people of most developing countries. Starting in the 1940s however a concerted effort to improve crop yields, known as the "Green Revolution," swept through much of Asia and Latin America and gave small farmers there a measure of security for the first time ever. In 1970 Norman Borlaug, a Rockefeller Foundation scientist and pioneer of this effort, was awarded the Nobel Prize for his work on the Green Revolution. By the 1980s the Green Revolution had doubled the amount of food produced in the developing world.
The Rockefeller factor
The Rockefeller and Ford foundations initiated the Green Revolution in partnership with the governments of developing countries. The idea was to make a comprehensive approach to the improvement of agriculture ranging from the seeds planted by farmers to the markets where their surplus crops are sold.
Rockefeller Foundation and its partners sponsored the breeding of hardier seeds that responded better to fertilizers and irrigation, and nurtured a group of agricultural experts to ensure the new seeds were well-suited to the local soil and climate of the developing countries. Governments working with the foundation made judicious investments in agricultural infrastructure to link farmers to more and better markets where they could buy improved seeds and fertilizer and sell their surplus.
The Rockefeller Foundation started a cutting-edge work in Mexico which soon spread to dozens of countries in Asia and Latin America. However the Green Revolution never reached most of Africa, where sixteen of the eighteen of the most undernourished countries in the world are located.
While much of the world has benefited from agricultural innovation, three-quarters of smallholder farmers in Africa still use the same unimproved seeds they used 50 years ago and have little or no access to fertilizers. Science has passed them by completely.
Africa's overdue revolution
On September 12, 2006 the Rockefeller Foundation and the Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation launched a new partnership to help Africa start its own Green Revolution. The Alliance for a Green Revolution in Africa (AGRA) will initially invest $150 million in what agricultural scientists call seed systems: developing appropriate seeds to attain the best yields in the diverse environments of Africa and working to ensure that these high-quality seeds are delivered to the farmers that need them. The hope is that the combination of Rockefeller and Gate's resources as well as Rockefeller's years of experience will eventually address the whole range of issues that has made agricultural development such a challenge in Africa.
The partnership's first initiative will focus on:
Â· Breeding crops that are better adapted to the variety of local conditions in Africa. The goal is to develop a hundred new varieties in five years.
Â· Training African breeders and agricultural scientists to spearhead the process in the future.
Â· Guaranteeing reliable ways to get high-quality, locally adapted seeds into the hands of small farmers through seed companies, public organisations, and a network of thousands of agro-dealers and small merchants responsible for providing supplies and knowledge to Africa's farmers.
The Ultimate Goal
The Green Revolution did not extend to Africa in the first place because the continent's agriculture was much more diverse than that of Asia or Latin America. In India, for example, developing a few new varieties of rice and wheat radically improved the productivity of much of that country's agricultural sector. Scientists would however have needed to develop improved varieties for at least ten different staple crops including maize, cassava, sorghum and millet to sustain the Green Revolution in Africa.
In addition, Africa's lack of infrastructure severely limited farmers' access to improved seeds and the markets they needed to sell their crops.
India's transportation networks were significantly better in the 1950s than Africa's are today. Moreover African governments have yet to make the kind of investment in agriculture that could radically improve the yield. For example, irrigation projects are usually sponsored by governments, at least in part, but only two percent of the agricultural land in Africa is irrigated. In India that figure is closer to 85 percent.
The breakdown of the grant for the Alliance for a Green Revolution in Africa is as follows:
$28,667,000 over five years to increase African food security by funding a network of crop breeders to improve Africa's crop varieties;
$16,000,000 over five years to increase access of smallholder farmers to improved crop varieties using a variety of production and distribution strategies;
$13,333,000 over five years to increase the number of African agriculturists trained in practical methods of breeding and seed systems development;
$17,333,000 over five years to provide operational support grant to the programme for Africa's seed systems;
$24,667,000 over five years to improve the access of poor farmers to agricultural technologies in Africa;
$200,000 over one year to support a conference on the productivity and profitability of the crop-based production systems of the world's poor and undernourished; and
$166,318 over six months to explore the best way to support development of private seed companies.

Original_Search_Title: E/Region Chiefs Plan to Form Retaliation Force
Headline: Ghana: E/Region Chiefs Plan to Form Retaliation Force
Body_Text:
...to battle Fulani herdsmen
Frederick Teye with pellet-riddled body Inset: Awudu with head wound
UNLESS GOVERNMENT curtails the harmful activities of Fulani herdsmen, some chiefs and their subjects in the Eastern Region have now decided to face these herdsmen squarely.
The Chronicle's investigations noted that if the Fulani herdsmen, who are heavily armed and terrorizing farmers in the rural areas are not stopped by the security agencies, some chiefs and their subjects would soon retaliate.
These Fulani herdsmen are destroying peoples' farms, pollute their water bodies, causing serious bush fires, raping and caning women and children as well as burning down houses of farmers who complained of their activities As most of the Chiefs and their subjects in the Upper Manya Krobo District, Yilo Krobo, Fanteakwa, Kwahu South and Afram Plains have not seen much security measures to curtail the activities of the Fulanis, an attempt was being made to form a retaliation force to fight these Fulani herdsmen.
These became evident when this reporter upon complainants went to assess the situation at Dedeso Wroko in the Fanteakwa District where the people have been plunged into perpetual fear due to these wild acts meted out to the residents by the Fulanis.
Investigations revealed that due to this most of the healthy, strong and energetic youth in the village have abandoned their farming activities and migrated to the cities since they continue to suffer human rights abuses at the hands of these Fulanis.
As this reporter went round the village and some few communities, some demolished structures were discovered to have been caused by the Fulanis in reaction to the residents' complaints of their wild activities there.
The paper also took note of few farms which were burnt by the Fulanis so that when the rains set in, green pastures wouldl grow for their cattle to graze on.
Speaking to The Chronicle, a victim of Fulani brutalities, Mr. Joseph Awudu, a farmer and government appointee at the Fanteakwa District Assembly, narrated how he sustained gun shot wounds on his head by a Fulani.
The victim, who has cultivated eight acres of maize and cassava at Akumasu Mahem near Dedeso Wriko said he and his brother, Frederick Teye went to harvest their farm produce on October 24th 2006 when a Fulani shot them.
According to him, they spotted a cow around 6:30 am on their farm and in the attempt to drive it away, about 70 cattle emerged from nowhere so therefore they invited a third person to help them identify the owner of the animals.
He said while driving the animals from the farm, several gun shots were fired from 30 yards away, which unfortunately hit the three of them making them, sustained various degrees of wounds.
When the victims were taken to the Akomasu Police station, they were given Police medical forms to attend hospital to have the bullets removed from their bodies.
The second victim hard five bullets removed from his body leaving seven more bullets in his body yet to be removed.
It was learnt that Frederick Teye was still seeking herbal treatment to have the remaining seven bullets removed from his body but it is learnt that the removal of one bullet costs Â¢2 million hence Teye has to pay seven million cedis to have his seven bullets removed.
Tawiah, who was the third victim The Chronicle learnt, had three gunshots but so far two bullets have been removed with one still in his body.
Further investigations revealed that the Tafo Divisional Police did well to arrest one of the Fulanis who claimed it was his father who pulled the trigger and currently had been granted a Police enquiry bail.
It is further learnt tha when the Policemen from the Tafo Divisional station with their colleagues of the Abuoso station to effect more arrest of the Fulanis, they recited words to the animals which attacked one of the Policemen by name Constable Teye in the process. Both Teye and Awudu appealed to the Police to conduct identification parade for them to arrest the other culprits.
Commenting on the issue, the Chief of the area, Osabarimah Bosompem Ayiripe II, observed that the Fulanis have not sought permission from them but only come to harass them.
The Chronicle recalls that it is almost six years now since the Fulanis started harassing farmers and appeals by Chiefs to the authorities for redress have fallen on deaf ears.
Osabarimah Ayiripe appealed to the government to pass a law prohibiting the Fulanis arming themselves and come out with a proper plan for cattle rearing in the country.

Original_Search_Title: Ondo, NNPC Sign Pact On Ethanol Production
Headline: Nigeria: Ondo, NNPC Sign Pact On Ethanol Production
Body_Text:
Akure — ONDO State government has signed a Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) with the Nigeria National Petroleum Corporation (NNPC) on the production of ethanol from cassava tubers.
Already, pre-feasibility studies on the joint venture has commenced on selected sites of Okeluse forest reserve in Ose council area of the state.
The Agriculture Commissioner, Mr. Ayo Ifayefunmi stated this in Akure while briefing newsmen on the activities of Governor Olusegun Agagu in the past forty months in the state.
Ifayefunmi said that the state was collaborating with the NNPC following the initiatives of the Federal Government on agriculture.
According to him, the cassava ethanol project is meant to diversify the use of cassava, saying "it is to produce ethanol from cassava tubers and also generate power which will be supplied to the national grid to serve neighbouring communities.
The Commissioner added that the state government had forwarded to the NNPC the signed agreement for endorsement.
In the year under review, Ifayefunmi said that government had committed N716m to the purchase of fertilizer, N34 m in the rehabilitation and construction of 18 farm service centres.
The commissioner noted that government had opened various credit window facilities for farmers in the state and that over N400m was available under the Trust Model Fund TMF.
Under this, the commissioner pointed out that individual farmers can access N250, 000 while cooperatives/companies can avail themselves N10m.
Ifayefunmi sad that the interest rate was eight percent repayable within a period of 18 months.

Original_Search_Title: Zim to Host Regional Sanitary And Plant Health Workshop
Headline: Zimbabwe: Zim to Host Regional Sanitary And Plant Health Workshop
Body_Text:
Harare — ZIMBABWE is expected to host a regional sanitary and plant health workshop next week in a bid to enhance crop protection and harmonise import and export regulations within Southern Africa.
The country's plant protection experts yesterday said the three-day African Union-organised workshop is critical to Zimbabwe, which now has a significant number of people involved in farming.
"Many people are farmers and there is now need to safeguard our crops and ensure consumers are exposed to safe food crops," an agriculture expert said.
The workshop, however, comes against a backdrop of reported cases of smuggling of crops such as cassava without following proper channels that incorporates scientific investigations by the Agriculture Research and Extension Services.
Zimbabwe is also trying to enhance good agricultural practices to improve food security and ensure crops meant for export are not rejected.
"Crops should, however, be rejected based on a scientific research that explains cause of rejection.
"We are concerned about the success of agriculture, which could help boost levels of foreign currency," animal health expert, Dr Obatolou Ushewokunze, said at a Press briefing yesterday.
She said the country needed to adhere to international standards provided by the World Health Organisation and various international bodies that Zimbabwe is affiliated to.
Dr Ushewokunze said issues related to trade are more critical to the country following the destruction of milk that had been exported to Zambia by some individuals.
She said there is need for countries to adhere to the World Trade Organisation regulations before destroying imported goods.
"The bulk of trade barriers are going to fall away following regional efforts to work together.
Unless the concern is scientific and evidence based, no one has the right to stop proper importation of crops or crop products.
"Critical is the training of our inspectors to be able to identify pests brought in by some plants or seeds that could threaten our crop production."
Trade barriers that have stifled development in the export and importation of crops include import duty, tariffs, import and export licences, quotas, subsidies and an uproar on suspicions and lack of understanding of what entails genetically modified material.
Dr Ushewokunze said evidence regarding harmful genetically modified material should also be substantiated by scientists.
Every year Zimbabwe loses a significant quantity of its seed crops that include wheat and several other small grains to migratory quelea birds.
Efforts have been made to see how best to harvest and destroy the birds without causing huge damage to the environment.
The workshop is also expected to deliberate on best practices to protect plants without also causing great harm to the environment and safe guard the health of animals and farm workers.

Original_Search_Title: ICRC Allocates $70 Million for Humanitarian Work
Headline: Liberia: ICRC Allocates $70 Million for Humanitarian Work
Body_Text:
The International Committee of the Red Cross (ICRC) says it has allocated US$70 million for its humanitarian operations in the country.
Making the disclosure on Tuesday at a News conference in Monrovia, the head of delegation of the ICRC Monrovia, Mr. Tobias Epprecht, said the money would be used for several projects to be undertaken by his organization this year in meeting the humanitarian needs of the Liberian people.
Mr. Epprecht pointed out that the ICRC would continue to restore contact between children and adults who have been separated from their families due to the war and endeavor to reunite Minors (less than 200 remaining cases with their love ones).
ICRC according to Mr. Epprecht, will implement projects to respond to the protection needs of children reunited with their families and to visit detainees throughout Liberia followed by oral and written interventions wherever applicable.
On the issue of preventive measures, he noted that ICRC would continue to assist the Ministry of National Defense in providing training to the new Liberian armed Forces and brief the UNMIL battalion officers arriving in the country on international Humanitarian Law (IHL) and its activities, the training of the Liberia National Police on IHL and to develop IHL dissemination sessions with the University of Liberia with the aim of including it in the University's curriculum.
Commenting on the issues of Water and Sanitation, the head of ICRC delegation said his organization will continue to focus on the community components such as training of water committee, pump mechanics, hygiene promotion sessions, work with partners to establish a distribution network of manual pumps as a response to their frequent break down.
ICRC says it will continue its ongoing support to the already supported 12-health facilities in Lofa and Grand Kru Counties and open a new health structure in Kpotomai, Lofa County.
On its relief operations, Mr. Epprecht added that 10,000 vulnerable households (very poor and recent returnees) in Lofa would receive seeds, tools and foodstuff for the next farming season. He said this operation has already started in January and will consist of 500 metric tons of food and 10,000 tool kits.
Concluding, Mr. Epprecht disclosed that some 1000 vulnerable households in Lofa will receive support to construct a traditional shelter, the agricultural rehabilitation projects will take place in Lofa (swamp and cash crop rehabilitation) while the hand operated cassava mill project will continue in Grand Kru County, respectively.

Original_Search_Title: We're Committed to Building a New Delta - Uduaghan
Headline: Nigeria: We're Committed to Building a New Delta - Uduaghan
Body_Text:
Lagos — Peoples Democratic Party's governorship candidate in Delta State, Dr. Emmanuel Uduaghan, yesterday said his choice as the party's flagbearer is a commitment to building a new Delta State in which all ethnic groupings are united for the development of the state.
Uduaghan, who spoke with a select group of journalists in Lagos while presenting a 3-point agenda, said Delta state is presently peaceful but noted that the biggest challenge "is to maintain this peace" and by extension save the Niger Delta region from restiveness.
According to him, "every ethnic group will get the respect it deserves in the state if I become the governor from May 29, 2007. If I'm unable to build confidence among our people as far as ethnicism is concerned, I would have failed as a governor.
"I say this because I cut across all the ethnic groupings. I'm an Itsekiri but I speak Urhobo and I also cut across other minority groups. What would build confidence in the state Urhoboman to have confidence in Itsekiri man and vice versa," he said further.
Noting that ethnic bitterness had been played up since he emerged as the PDP candidate, the former commissioner for finance stressed the need for compromises among the various ethnic groups.
Itemising his 3-point agenda under peace and security; infrastructural development; and, human capital development, the former secretary to the state government, promised to strenghten government organs.
"We would fund the community development centres (CDC) to enable them perform more than they are doing presently. The PDP presidential candidate, Alhaji Umar Musa Yar'Adua, has assured that his administration would ensure more funding at the grassroots and I believe this is in line with our plans for Delta.
"We would build on what Ibori has done in the energy sector while focusing more on agriculture through the production of cassava and of course farms produce. We will also encourage fishing through ponds and improve on yields from river fishing," he assured.
Urging his co-aspirants in PDP to sheate their swords and work for the collective interests of the PDP in Delta State, Uduaghan said the process of reconciliation is on-going in the state, adding that, he would not give up on this.
Asserting that the process that produced his candidature was not rigged, Uduaghan however advised that those who are disatisfied with the process should not resort "to insulting the person of the state governor, Chief James Ibori, because he remains the leader of the party and he is still the sitting governor.
"People should not be tempted to insult his (Ibori) person while pushing their cases," he counselled.

Original_Search_Title: How to Minimise Cholera Miscarriages
Headline: Uganda: How to Minimise Cholera Miscarriages
Body_Text:
Kampala — CHOLERA causes miscarriages, nurses at Mulago Hospital's cholera treatment camp, have revealed.
Winnie Bukenya, a nurse at the camp, said that since the cholera outbreak on October 31, last year, no pregnancy affected by cholera has been saved.
Cholera is an acute, diarrhoea illness, caused by infection of the intestine with the bacterium Vibrio cholerae. Symptoms are watery diarrhoea, vomiting and leg cramps. Rapid loss of body fluids in an infected person leads to dehydration, shock and death if not treated.
The nurses said a pregnant woman's condition gets complicated when she contracts cholera.
About 1,000 Ugandans have been affected by cholera since its outbreak last year and 20 have died, according to health statistics.
Dr. Baterana Byarugaba, the in-charge of the centre, said most pregnant women brought to the cholera camp are never on time to save their pregnancies and most of them are brought when in comma.
When a pregnant mother gets cholera, she immediately starts vomiting and passing watery stool, depriving the unborn child of nutrients to feed on.
Pregnant patients suffer low sugar and lose mineral salts as a result of severe diarrhoea and vomiting.
"If her blood sugar is below 40mg/deciliter, it means the unborn child's sugar is less, leading to death," Byarugaba said. "What befalls the mother also befalls the child," he added.
Byarugaba explained that due to loss of mineral salts like potassium and chloride, the unborn child develops muscle spasms and convulsions that lead to death.
"The cells of the unborn child are rapidly growing and need a lot of energy. That is why they die," he said.
Pregnant patients get drained fast because they lose most of the body nutrients yet they have another dependant inside.
Byarugaba advises that a pregnant patients be immediately put on drip to rehydrate the body.
During treatment, she should feed on soft food to prevent intestinal obstruction. This is because severe diarrhoea makes her intestines fragile. Hard foods like cassava and sweet potatoes cause constipation and clog the intestines that are lacking lubrication.
Byarugaba further advises that should they succeed in saving the child, pregnant patients should take lots of drinks like juice on discharge. They should especially the oral rehydration salts prescribed by medics.
He urges that personal hygiene for pregnant women should be at the highest and they should be rushed to hospital immediately they pass out watery stool.

Original_Search_Title: FG Reforms Irreversible - Obj
Headline: Nigeria: FG Reforms Irreversible - Obj
Body_Text:
Abuja — FEDERAL Government's current economic reform will be irreversible from May 29 this year when President Olusegun Obasanjo's successor will emerge.
Obasanjo, who declared this at the opening of the third Business Roundtable in Abuja said that the reform that was resisted initially by the people has now been embraced by all.
He maintained that he was not moved by the initial resistance to the reform programme when he introduced it .
"There is certain amount of resistance to reform. It is understandable, as human beings would want to stick to the things they are used to rather than plunge to the unknown".
He reflected on what the economy was at the time he assumed office as the president in 1999 and noted that it was not a good period for the nation.
According to him, "it was so bad that some people said I must be mad to have accepted to be Nigerian president".
He said that "foreign exchange was so bad, debt overhang, a pariah state that nobody wants to talk to Nigeria. Besides, infrastructures were run down while human infrastructure was more than run down".
The president pointed out that some people believed that in 1999, Nigeria was at the verge of breaking up with splinter militia groups.
However with the reform embarked upon by the present administration, he said, "we have reached the stage where people now feel that reform is imperative".
President Obasanjo noted that the reform programme has become part of the campaign of the leading presidential aspirants in the forthcoming presidential election in April.
"We are in a situation where the reform is no longer one person or government reform," he said adding that the reform is gradually becoming a national agenda and a policy where the government can draw up programme.
He also noted that the price of garri might not come down because the farmers too want to come out of poverty as they would not be producing cassava for local consumption but for export.
The president said that the nation has set a target, saying that for agriculture, a particular crop has been targeted for attention.
To achieve the set target, he said four committees have been set up for marketing, research, production and processing.
Obasanjo said that the cocoa in the past year grew from 150,000 tonnes production capacity to 500,000 tonnes while rice production increased from 150,000 to 2 million tonnes.

Original_Search_Title: Talking Point - "Sit Down Jo"
Headline: Nigeria: Talking Point - "Sit Down Jo"
Body_Text:
Is it the case that even age cannot temper some of the more undesirable traits of our President that he must continually embarrass his fellow citizens with his willful lack of finesse?
A so-called stakeholders meeting on the economy held last Tuesday, 9th January, in Abuja. President Olusegun Obasanjo gave an address at the event. As has become the case with this President, it was a less than exciting speech, delivered with the usual perfunctoriness with which one conducts a dining room banter. In the circumstance, all went well. That is until the President decided to take questions from the, we must hope, august assembly. The President was in charge and conducted the event in the fashion of a village school headmaster speaking to his errant and cowed teachers or students.
He would conclude the session by reminding the audience of the need to go to the back of the hall for 'item 7', necessary refreshments to revive their sapped energy, listening to some humdrum address. It might be asked why the President of a nation had to take up the role of a secondary school food prefect at a public function. Of course he is the President, the one and only Baba Iyabo who must address the needs of his household. But then I'm ahead of my tale. So let's return to the speech.
Those who stood up to speak couldn't avoid the urge to massage the President's ego, praising his achievements since he came into office. He acted the role of the indulgent Baba to the hilt until it came to the turn of an Abuja-based gentleman who heads a cassava-exporting association. His first error, apparently, was to have introduced himself as a 'comrade'.
The President asked him to introduce himself again and upon hearing the man call himself 'comrade' what-not, the President cut him short and proceeded to remind him, in a half-chiding half-derisive tone, that he must be the only comrade left in the world. It was obviously, for him, an opportunity to take pot shots at one of those khaki-wearing types, irritating bugaboos who had given him sleepless nights. But this time our cassava-exporting comrade was well turned out in a three-piece suit.
A sure but 'uncomradely' sign of the changed times, you might say. Neither Mao tse-Tung's successors in China nor his Nigerian re-incarnate, Adams Oshiomole, who has been clambering the soap box to become Edo State's governor still answer to the comrade title, the President told the clearly embarrassed man. But President Obasanjo was not done yet as he went forth and back to the theme of the man's irritating title. In the end, he let him take his seat and the man must have been thankful for nobody was now sure if the President wouldn't order the cassava farmer's arrest for being so proud of an otiose and disgusting title.
If the cassava exporter went away with a spank on his wrist not so the next gentle man, who stood to ask his own question to which he certainly expected an answer. He wanted to know why farmers are unable to access loans from a dedicated N50 billion set aside for that purpose. Quick as lightning, the President didn't allow him to finish his remark before brushing aside his comments. Resorting to language appropriate only among fighting area boys and with disgust written all over him, the President ordered the man to 'sit down jo!'
The man whose opening words followed the same adulatory tone with which others had praised the President's programme must have wondered what hit him. In his well-creased agbada he must have perspired like a slaughter-bound Salla ram. How was he to explain to, say his ten year-old child, what he had done wrong to warrant such peremptory put-down from the President? The embarrassed silence of members of the audience was agonizing to see. So must have been others watching from the quiet of their homes. How could they explain such unseemly display of anger, a total lack of control by the President of the largest black country in the world?
Is it the case that even age cannot temper some of the more undesirable traits of our President that he must continually embarrass his fellow citizens with his willful lack of finesse? It is, in my view, cowardly for any person in a position of power to continually thrust it in the face of those with whom he relates in a clear attempt to intimidate them. President Obasanjo has refused to change his ways ever since he first happened on national consciousness. Considering he has always had his way until Sanni Abacha, another oaf gone amok, almost did for him, Obasanjo might have persuaded himself he was above control.
That sense of hubris led him home, almost to his death, in 1995 when against national and international warnings he returned to answer to coup-planning allegations. But for the support of the people, some of them friends of his, like Bola Ige, who got silenced in controversial circumstances during his adminitartion, Obasanjo would have ended his earthly sojourn in Abacha's gulag. Yet, it's some of these people Obasanjo now see fit to ridicule at the slightest opportunity in public. Certainly the shame of such conduct lies, not on victims of such wanton use of power, but those who put such power to disgraceful use. We only pray in the last few months of his presidency, we won't see our dear President attending a State function with an iron-plated koboko in his hand. I didn't hear your Amen!

Original_Search_Title: Health First!
Headline: Uganda: Health First!
Body_Text:
Nairobi — HEALTH CONCERNS Triumphed over economic fears last week in Uganda as the government approved the use of the controversial insecticide DDT to control malaria, which kills 100,000 people every year.
The decision to use dichloro diphenyl trichloethene - an effective mosquito killer, banned in many countries for its ability to accumulate in the food chain over a long period of time - marks the end of wrangling between health officials and environmentalists.
The officials were frustrated by their inability to fight malaria, while environmental activists and agro exporters were worried about the insecticide's potential impact on the environment and the economy.
Officials say DDT is needed to reinforce the fight against malaria, which kills over 100,000 Ugandans annually and is becoming increasingly resistant to a growing number of drugs.
The head of the National Environment Management Authority (NEMA), Dr Henry Aryamanya-Mugisha, said, during a press conference to announce the decision that the approval was granted after a review of an environmental impact assessment submitted by the Ministry of Health in July last year and a subsequent public debate attended by concerned parties and experts last November.
The assessment recommended that the insecticide be applied using the indoor residual spray method to avoid a spillover into the environment and the food chain. NEMA said that the application of DDT can be well managed and monitored but conceded that there are challenges involved like spraying in mud houses in which the majority of Ugandans live, lack of co-operation from the public and the logistics of such a large exercise.
DDT was last used in Uganda 46 years ago by the World Health Organisation for malaria control in Kanungu district in western Uganda.
In 2005, biochemists and pathologists from Makerere University studied the effects of the chemical and found it in people's urine, in soil and in foods such as fish, beans, cassava, and Irish potatoes.
However, Dr Gabriel Bimenya, the principle investigator for the study and who also heads the Pathology Department at the university, said during the presentation of the report that there is no empirical evidence of any disease directly associated with DDT.
"The decision to reintroduce DDT was reached last month," Dr Aryamanya-Mugisha said, "and notification has now been sent to the lead implementer - the Ministry of Health and other stakeholders, including the Prime Minister's Office, Ministry of Agriculture, Animal Industry and Fisheries, Ministry of Trade, Tourism and Industry, Ministry of Water and Environment and various non governmental organisations."
He said the approval is conditional on a primary consent from international authorities - like WHO, the secretariat of the Stockholm Convention on Persistent Organic Pollutants and the Rotterdam Convention.
These allow the use of DDT but within strict public health guidelines.
However, Uganda is also a signatory to some protocols like the one on Lake Victoria, which binds the East Africa Community against the use of DDT, citing its impact on the environment, particularly aquatic life.
"We also want a multisectoral committee to participate in the implementation and monitoring of the use of DDT. Officials to this committee will come from Ministries concerned with health, agriculture, trade and inline NGOs," he added.
THE OTHER CONDITIONS SET by NEMA include adequate public awareness for at least three months before starting DDT application; that DDT should be brought into the country by the Ministry of Health alone and that it should directly supervise the insecticide's transportation, storage and, application by trained staff and later dispose of the used equipment, according to the NEMA Waste Management Regulations of 1999. If all goes according to plan, the Ministry of Health could start spraying the insecticide by the end of March.
Agro-exporters, who previously opposed the use of DDT, said their fish, flowers and other agricultural produce risks being banned from markets in the West if contaminated with DDT.
Ben Naturinda, acting executive director of the Uganda Export Promotion Board, told The EastAfrican that even if the chemical is approved and well managed, there is no guarantee that its application will not harm organic exports.
"There is a difference between regulatory issues and consumer policy, and we don't know how consumer associations will react to this," he said. "We cannot object to national policy, but we call for caution and full involvement in applying and monitoring the application of DDT."
Officials from the European Union delegation to Uganda have previously told The EastAfrican that while there would be no official ban from the EU on Ugandan produce, specific consignments found with traces of DDT would not be let through. Consumer organisations could also avoid Ugandan produce.
The exporters point to the late 1990s when the European Union banned fish imports from Uganda over fears that fishermen were poisoning fish in the process of catching it.
The ban was later lifted after stringent regulations were introduced into the industry, and Uganda currently exports over 35,000 tonnes of fish worth more than $144 million per year - 80 per cent of it to the EU market.

Original_Search_Title: Movers & Shakers: Holding Sway in Moyo
Headline: Uganda: Movers & Shakers: Holding Sway in Moyo
Body_Text:
Politics — The Moyo LC 5 chairman leads the district of 267, 000 residents. He was Under Secretary in various ministries including the Judiciary. He also served as the Academic Registrar of then Institute of Public Administration (Uganda Management Institute). Mr Iku-Dollo, serving his second tenure at the district's top political job.
Anne Auru is the district woman MP. She won on the Independent ticket. She worked as a district councillor for Moyo town for eight years before joining Parliament. She has already secured scholarships for 15 students pursuing diploma and certificate courses at various institutions in the country.
Hilda Abio Eriga
The youthful Ms Eriga is the LC 5 councillor for Moyo Sub-county. She is also the district vice chairperson and concurently holds the powerful portfolio of finance secretary.
Ms Eriga is a campaigner of the rights of the girl-child. She and leads a simple lifestyle mingling freely.
Phillip Idro
The former envoy to China is credited with promoting Ugandan coffee abroad. He contested for the West Moyo parliamentary seat on NRM ticket during the February elections but Dr Apiliga defeated him. Ambassador Idro pioneered FM radio investment with Moyo FM that has since collapsed. He was also formerly the Moyo district chairman and is said to own a huge rice factory in Iganga. Mr Idro who nolonger involved in active politics was also a former Director General of the Internal Securty Organisation (ISO)
Hassan Kaps Fungaro
The Obongi county FDC MP has unlimited vigour in criticizing the government. He recently joined the radical crusade for northern Uganda to secede to form the Nile State due to alleged marginalisation. Mr Fungaro is a good orator and this oratory skill saw him easily win the seat.
CIVIL SERVICE
Samuel Tako
The 36-year old former Geography/GP teacher at Metu SS switched jobs to become Assistant Cao. He says the job is hectic due to Moyo's little revenue bag and meagre funding from the central government. He is currently pursuing a Master's degree in Development Studies at Uganda Martyrs University-Nkozi to sharpen his skills in public administration.
Rev. Fr. Sabino Anyanzo chairs the District Service Commission. He always decries the high level of unemployment, especially among the youth but says it is tough attracting professionals to Moyo because the district is considered too remote. The Coordinator of Moyo Episcopal Vicariate previously served for 12 years as the director of Social Services in Arua Diocese. He likes relaxing at the Multi-Purpose Training centre.
George Amoli
Mr Amoli was an accountant at Metu SS. He is now the acting Chief Finance Officer (CFO). As CFO, He faces an uphill task in balancing the allocation of the district's Shs9.2b budget. Mr Amoli is a strict financial disciplinarian.
Herbert Alule-Amudra
The love for trees and environmental protection has propelled Mr Alule-Amudra to the position of acting head of the district natural resource department. His method of work is so unique that he befriended encroachers in Laropi forest reserve, charmed and evicted them in April, 2006.
AGRICULTURE
Lulu Albert Izama is a towering man who prides himself in his massive agricultural success. "I was brought up as a leader and my farming initiatives are to show people that they can manage even without government support," he reminisces. Mr Izama, an influential opinion leader and member of the district land board, owns 80 acres of upland rice, Epuripur sorghum, cassava, simsim and potatoes in Orujo
Anyanzo Thomas Abbey
In pursuit of the government policy on modernisation of agriculture, the Naads Coordinator in Moyo works tirelessly to organise, train and empower the 293 farmers' groups in the district
CORPORATES
Robert Mwanika
The West Nile regional manager for Eagle Air is also the prietor of StarLite Restaurant in the town centre.
LEISUERE/HOSPITALITY
Santa Anzo of the Arapapa (butterfly) fashion house fame is widely known in the district by name but not face. She is a daughter of the district chairman Iku-Dollo. Her parents describe her as a bold, open-minded and assertive girl whose charisma has enabled her break even in the country's turbulent fashion and design industry.
Rose Kojoki Regina
The slender lady oversees the day-to-day management of Moyo Multi-purpose Training Centre. With a constant smile on her baby face, Rose has perfected the art of service with a smile. Ms Kojoki supervises 28 other employees running accommodation, restaurant, conference and DSTv entertainment services at the Arua Diocese-owned facility.
Emmanuel Leju rose from humble beginnings as a village mason to town tycoon. This has earned him immense respect. He deals in hardware under Star Party Enterprises Ltd, owns a fleet of trucks and is the sole agent for Daily Monitor in Moyo. His business exploits straddle across the border into South Sudan.
Rev. Fr. Grace Waigo
He served as administrator of Arua diocese for 15 years. The bespectacled priest was very effective in mobilising interviewees for this series.
MEDICAL SERVICES
Dr Seraphine Adibaku
The District Director of Health Services has been in the medical service for 23 years and holds a Masters degree in Public Health. During his 7-year tenure, the number of health centres in Moyo increased from 18 to 35 and the district scooped the coveted Ministry of Health Annual Health Performance Award.
Dr Richard Mangwi
At 37, he is already serving his fourth year as the Medical Superintendent of the 100-bed capacity Moyo Hospital. He is a jolly and welcoming doctor who is however stressed by inadequate funding and limited personnel since the hospital's catchment area extends to South Sudan.
Dr George Bokka
He hails from Obongi County and currently works with Unicef as the National Programme Coordinator. Dr Bokka won meritorious award during his professional fellowship. He is a down-to-earth man of medicine.
RELIGION
Monsignor Pierino Madrwa is the Episcopal Vicar for Moyo Vicariate that covers Moyo and Adjumani districts. He shepherds over 200, 000 Catholic faithful and is revered and hugely influential. Fr. Madrwa has been a priest for 37 years and is poised to become a bishop when the area becomes a diocese soon.

Original_Search_Title: Floods Damage Crops in North-West
Headline: Zambia: Floods Overwhelm Chavuma, Zambezi Districts
Body_Text:
Ndola — MAIZE, rice and cassava fields have been flooded in Chavuma and Zambezi districts in North-Western Province following a heavy downpour.
The rain has also destroyed some houses and rendered roads impassable.
The heavy rain had been pounding the province in the last few weeks, leaving villagers and authorities worried that people in the affected districts would experience hunger this year.
The district disaster management teams, which at the weekend were reinforced by the provincial team, moved in to assess the damage.
It would take a week before the extent of the damage could be known.
Chavuma District Commissioner, Frobisher Fulayi, said yesterday after assessing the damage that the floods were severe this year and had submerged fields and some houses had collapsed.
"What we are seeing now is what we usually see in March. Most plains are covered, and the water is just spreading in the bush. We are still assessing the damage. We will only know the extent when we receive the reports," Mr Fulayi said.
The floods hit both the west and east banks of the Zambezi river.
The provincial team led by Permanent Secretary Richard Salivaji had to use a helicopter to assess the damage.
On Saturday, the team assessed the damage in Zambezi where some areas were reportedly cut off from the rest of the district because of the floods, while yesterday the team was in Chavuma.
Zambezi District Commissioner, Rosemary Muntabowa, said the situation in the district was similar to that in Chavuma.
"The situation is bad. Water is still rising. These floods are just too early. If we get this now, you can imagine in February and March. Most of the fields have been submerged," Ms Muntabowa said.
She expressed sadness that the farmers in the district would have little to harvest despite working hard this season.
Meanwhile, 13 more houses have collapsed following a heavy downpour in Ndola's Kantolomba township, bringing the total number of houses damaged in the area to 74 in the last one week.
Kantolomba ward councillor, Nathaniel Mwange, said 13 houses collapsed at the weekend as a result of continuous rains. A total of 61 houses collapsed in the township last week.
Mr Mwange said three families were spending nights in the cold as they did not have relatives or friends to offer them shelter.
He said he would arrange for them to be temporarily accommodated at the local police post.
The heavy rains experienced recently had also made the roads in the township impassable.
Ndola District Commissioner Dennison Chisunka was today expected to visit the area.
About 45 squatters in the same township have refused to move to Twapia township where the Ndola City Council gave them free plots.
The squatters were told to leave the area to give chance to the ministry of Education to complete a school project that started in 2003.

Original_Search_Title: Traditional Institutions in Sustaining Obasanjo's Reforms
Headline: Nigeria: Traditional Institutions in Sustaining Obasanjo's Reforms
Body_Text:
Lagos — "Personally, I have always supported the advocacy for a partnership between the government and the traditional institution in the country". These were the words of President Olusegun Obasanjo recently stressing the need for the traditional rulers to collaborate with government for the socio-economic development of the nation. This is indicative of the fact that the traditional institution is important and indispensable although the countryÃ­s 1999 constitution does not append any particular function to it. Since the pre-colonial days, the traditional institution has been playing a significant role in the administration and maintenance of law and order in the country. Before the advent of colonialism, traditional rulers who wielded a lot of powers and influence ran the various independent states.
During the colonial period, the indirect rule system was employed by the Britons to reach out to their subjects and it was effective especially in the Northern part of the country. Today, the traditional rulers have been assisting in resolving communal disputes, administering customary practices and regulating the conduct of people in their domains. They have been of tremendous help in tackling the intractable issue of youth restiveness. It is a fact that the traditional rulers are closer to their subjects and they exert more influence on them than the government.
Therefore, the President Obasanjo administration having achieved much in the last seven and half years will definitely need the cooperation of the traditional institution to sustain its laudable reforms that have once again set the country on the path to recovery and sustainable development. One of the ways by which the traditional could help in sustaining the reforms is through enlightenment and education of the people beginning from the grass roots. The governmentÃ­s anti-corruption war comes handy because Nigerians must change their perception about governance and acquisition of wealth. Before the advent of the present administration, most Nigerians had come to equate public office with acquisition of wealth. It was a general belief that once a public officer one could do anything with government funds without accounting for anything. But the effective anti-corruption stance of the Obasanjo is gradually making Nigerians aware that public office for service and it is a trust of the peopleÃ­s wishes and aspirations.
Therefore, as traditional rulers who are close to the people, it is incumbent on them to help in the revival of our good cultural values, which do not welcome corruption and anti-people policies. Politicians who steal government funds could be rejected by their communities and even ostracised to serve as a deterrent to aspiring politicians and leaders. They could help by stopping the award of chieftaincy titles to people with questionable wealth and even withdraw those already given out when it is discovered that such individuals have betrayed the trust reposed in them by the people. In addition, many people were of the belief that ìgovernment property is nobodyÃ­s propertyî so it could be handled with contempt but the Obasanjo administration has been able to drum the message into Nigerians that government property is for all of us and should be treated with utmost respect and honour. The traditional institution can use its closeness to the people to further imbue in the citizens their rights and obligations to the society. People could be educated on how to protect public utilities: paying of tax, electricity bills and water rates, maintenance and preservation of public institutions etc.
For example, the reforms in the banking sector may not be complete if the people are not guided on how to handle the nations currency because we would continue having mutilated notes. Again, most people had before 1999 lost faith in the banking industry but with the recapitalisation and other changes. Government can enforce the reforms but the traditional rulers would be needed to bring the message down to the people. They are the natural representatives and are more accessible to the people.
