The Obasanjo administration has also done a lot in the reformation of the electoral process and the traditional institution could play a vital role in sustaining and building on it. Before now, most Nigerians were of the view that politics is a cash and carry game and hence they give their support to the highest bidder but the experience of the present administration has shown that it is a game for decent people who have the interest of the nation at heart. Beginning from the ward level, district heads and chiefs could educate their subjects on the ills of voting in people of dubious characters into government and this has to start with the forthcoming elections. Through this, people will stop celebrating people of questionable characters. This will remould the character of our politician.
Again, the traditional rulers could help in shoring up support for the government reform programmes in different areas. For example the cassava revolution could be taken up be the traditional institution by encouraging their subjects to embrace the idea because it is laudable and of immense economic benefit to the people and the nation at large. Most people have abandoned farming and the rural areas for the non-existent white-collar jobs. This is largely responsible for the overcrowding and increased crime rate in our towns and cities. The reform in the sector especially in the transport sector would need the support of the people to be accomplished. For example, the revitalisation of the railway system requires the support of every Nigerian because the contractors would need the cooperation of hosting communities for the job to be done well and completed on time.
Most importantly the war against HIV/AIDS and immunisation against killer diseases being embarked upon by the present administration could only be sustained if people are properly educated. The influence of the traditional institution in this respect cannot be over emphasised. Therefore, for the current reforms, be it in the housing, health, economy, social or political sector to be sustained, the traditional must be involved and it must be seen to play a supportive role through mobilisation support and cooperation from the people.

Original_Search_Title: Cities Worth Visiting
Headline: East Africa: Wonders of East Africa
Body_Text:
Kampala — THOSEwho travel to foreign lands return home to tell the tale, so goes a Luganda saying. Besides, sharing experiences after a journey is irresistible. Not only does society demand it, but the thrill of the story is overwhelming if the listeners are available. Jude Katende was among the journalists covering the Pearl of Africa Music Awards, which were last year expanded to include Kenya, Tanzania and Rwanda. He shares his experience.
SOME of us had been to Kenya countless times before, so we were not as eager as the first-timers for this leg of the journey last October. But we anxiously awaited the trips to Rwanda and Tanzania.
The first stop was Kenya's capital. Nairobi is a fast city. The all-day chill has not slowed the city and people and businesses are brisker than in sluggish Uganda.
Unlike in Uganda, where people borrow newspapers or 'hire' them, in Kenya it is common to see people of all ages buying and reading their own papers. This explains why one paper's daily circulation beats all the Ugandan papers' combined sales.
Just as busy as the newspaper stands are the shoe shiners. In Kampala, we like to leave our shoes at the shiner's and wear lugabire (sandals made out of car tyres). In Nairobi, however, clients perch themselves on raised chairs and bury their faces in a magazine or newspaper as the shiner does his thing without the client ever having to remove their shoes.
This system, seen even in developed countries, is both time-saving and secure. In Uganda, stories abound of shiners fleeing with clients' nice shoes or hiring them out for a fee.
The Kenyan capital is full of numerous showrooms full of brand new cars. Even upcountry areas like Kisumu and Nakuru have new cars on sale, while here, Kampala seems to be the only centre for showrooms with brand new cars, with the bulk of our cars on sale being reconditioned cars in bonded warehouses.
Kenya's organisational structure speaks volumes. In Uganda, almost everybody wants a bit of Kampala and with all the vital services crammed here, Kampala looks like a big slum.
Go to Nakuru, Kisumu or even Naivasha and you will see that these towns give Kampala a run for her money.
Nairobi is fascinating; there are lots of magnificent buildings that would give ours a knock-out in an architectural design bout.
Unlike in Kampala, in Nairobi there are many tall buildings with car parking space and plenty of space for further construction. Most government buildings are in the same area for easy co-ordination.
Though there are some posters on the walls, they are not as many as those that fight for attention and soil Uganda's buildings. Nairobi has something our City Council authorities could pick a leaf from - many notice boards in busy areas.
It is not all good, however. The streets are dangerous. Daylight robberies and muggings are rampant, while carjacking is worse. And the notoriety of taxi drivers is worse than in Uganda. They are boisterous, rough and abusive. You have to pay your fare almost immediately you get on board, lest you are thrown out.
The only good thing about the public service vehicles is that they only stop at designated points.
Tanzania
It takes nearly 13 hours to travel from the Kenyan border to Dar-es-Salam. The August journey was tiring. Right from the border at Namanga to sand-filled Dar-es-Salaam, travelling by road was a hellish experience.
It got worse with the incessant police roadblocks after every three kilometres. Fatigue, coupled with our poor Swahili, only made us appear like lumpens.
Our drivers, however, knew the language so they bailed us out many times, although the sight of one of them dozing at the steering wheel told us we were in danger.
His driving mirror let us in on his unfortunate little siesta that sent shivers down many of our spines. "Oh, we're going to die!" one female cried out loud.
The driver heard our fears and after that fought hard to stay awake.
At first sight, Dar-es-Salaam seems to be sparsely populated, but it has a population of three million - much higher than Kampala's two million. Nairobi has more than three million residents.
When it rains Nairobi is flooded with black muddy water; Kampala, brown mud and filth; while Dar-es-Salam is washed with sandy water.
There is sand almost everywhere, as are pine-like trees and palm trees.
Potatoes, cassava and fruits are planted in the sandy soils.
The Tanzanians are also sluggish people, and so the capital wakes up late and lazily works under the searing heat of the sun. It is common to see men walking bare-chested with rivulets of sweat running down their bodies.
Swahili is the main language, with just a handful of locals speaking not-so-good English.
There are so many Somalis and Asians of Indian origin. There are many push and pull carts, used to ferry goods the way we use bodabodas here. They are usually loaded with avocado, bananas, and coconuts, among other goods.
Like in our Nakivubo Mews in Kampala, there are plenty of wares on the streets. Dar-es-Salaam doesn't seem to ever run out of car spare parts because almost every street has spare parts on sale, ranging from rims to steering wheel covers and headlamps.
The city is suffocating under a large fleet of taxis. Conductors precariously hanging out of the vehicles as they ply city routes calling for passengers. They are as bad as their Nairobi counterparts.
To unwind, one needs to make a trip to the Indian Ocean Coco beach with its huge expanse of white sand. People jog and play as the breeze from the ocean cools them.
Rwanda
So much has been said about this little central African country which is now part of the East African Community.
Often described as the land of a thousand hills, its geographical landscape is similar to that of western Uganda as are its people.
Arriving in the Rwandan capital Kigali in July, we were struck by the beauty of the tall Rwandese ladies.
Acting as ushers at the magnificent Intercontinental Hotel, their warm smiles lit unseen bonfires in our hearts, assuring us that we were in the right place.
After the cocktail, attended by many Rwandan artistes and businesspeople, we headed for the Nyira Rocks joint, where we were told kimansulo (strip tease dances), courtesy of a Ugandan dance group, Stingerz, reigned. However, we discovered that it was the usual Kampala nightclub comedy spiced up with karaoke sessions. The karaoke and comedy shows went on with plenty of tributes to the Ugandan PAM awards contingent. We basked in the limelight.
After Nyira Rocks, we left for Planet Club at the Kigali Business Centre building. Ugandans thirsting for waist and rib-shaking sessions took to the floor and went jiggy with Rwandan revellers. Ugandan, Kenyan and Western music ruled the night and the Ugandans formed a ring and got pretty close to the Rwandan women on the floor, who hardly left the Ugandan circle.
Rwanda is rather cold. Even when the sun is out, the mornings are chilly.
The capital is a small, clean city with well-tarmacked roads. You can hardly spot a pothole, unlike in Kampala where they keep popping up as if they are in a contest. And in Kigali, where repairs have been necessary, the patching has been done neatly.
Forget the genocide. That was over 10 years ago. The people we encountered were hospitable and had picked up the pieces from the 1994 tragedy.
Luganda, Swahili, English and Kinyarwanda languages are spoken in Rwanda. Radio stations play a variety of music, but mainly Western and Ugandan hits.
Thursday morning looked like Sunday morning, suggesting business is slow here. It is common to see youths moving around with landline phones as if they were mobile phones.
For them, any place is a call centre. Their bodabodas are huge bikes of the TVS 125 and Freedom Topper type. Two passengers cannot sit on one motorcycle here, unlike in Uganda, where some even carry three passengers. Special hire taxis are available, including brand new Toyota models. The taxis bear yellow stripes and the Toyota Hiace (kamunye) is a common vehicle here. Drinks are rather pricey, with Mutzig and Primus beers ruling the bars.
Back home, musing over the beauties, buildings and roads in the four East African cities, I wondered whether home is best. Besides, it offered me the opportunity to tell the tale.

Original_Search_Title: Mityana Farmers Get Seeds
Headline: Uganda: Mityana Farmers Get Seeds
Body_Text:
Kampala — Mityana district has given out seeds to farmers to enable them grow enough food in the next season.
The district LC5 vice-chairman, Ibrahim kateregga, on Wednesday said the donation would ensure food security and improve on peoples' income.
Kateregga, who is also the chairperson of the production and marketing committee, said this was in line with the presidential directive to ensure food security.
He disclosed that the funds for the donation were got from Local Government development Programme and Plan for the Modernisation of agriculture. The package included cassava cuttings, beans and maize.
Kateregga explained that the town council did not benefit from the project because it is not a beneficiary of the Plan for the Modernisation of Agriculture.
He said 60% of the people in the district, including the local leaders, do not have enough food in their homes.
"We need to grow enough food and remain with more for commercial purposes. As of now, our people are used to subsistence farming," he concluded.

Original_Search_Title: LCS Can End Poverty, Mukwaya
Headline: Uganda: LCS Can End Poverty, Mukwaya
Body_Text:
Kampala — LOCAL council (LC) leaders can end poverty in homesteads, trade minister Janat Mukwaya, has said. She was addressing Mukono district councillors recently.
Mukwaya criticised LCs who are only interested in their sitting allowances being increased.
She said the Government's role is to design programmes, which must be implemented by local governments.
"There is no reason why Ugandans should continue eating cassava and salt when we have the National Agricultural Advisory Services and Plan for Modernisation of Agriculture. What are our councils doing?" she asked.
The minister urged all district council representatives to be role models among their electorates.
"It is illogical for a councillor to lead a crusade against poverty in homesteads when they (LCs) don't have plantations." Mukwaya said.
She urged district councils to embrace the Government policies intended to empower Ugandans. "There should be a chain through which government programmes reach the grassroots. District councillors are the middlemen," she added.

Original_Search_Title: Why We May Drop More Candidates, By PDP
Headline: Nigeria: Why We May Drop More Candidates, By PDP
Body_Text:
Abuja — THE Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) yesterday declared it would drop its candidates found to have damaging security reports and court judgement against them.
It said it will not field such candidates to save the party the risk of having such candidates thrown out in court if the opposition stumbled on such evidence and use it against them.
The party's National Publicity Secretary, Mr. John Odey who disclosed this yesterday in Abuja, while speaking with journalists at the venue of the party's workshop for its candidates, said the party would substitute candidates who have problems between now and February 13, 2007.
He however, declared, "but for now status quo ante remains but if there is need for a change, obviously we are going to change the candidate".
The party spokesman who said the party was yet to resolve the inconclusive last month's primaries in Anambra, Enugu and Imo States Anambra, noted that obviously we cannot have two lists from Anambra State because as a party we are going to present a candidate for each of the constituency and the state".
In the case of Imo State where two acclaimed winners of the gubernatorial primary, Senator Ifeanyi Araraume and Chief Charles Ugwu were currently attending the PDP workshop for its candidate, he said, both of them are members of the party and are not the only candidates present at the workshop.
candidate that still has a problem between now and February 13, 2003, obviously, we will substitute that candidate".
Pressed on Imo crisis, he said, "I have said that as far as Imo State is concerned, there are controversies and there are problems and once these are sorted out we will obviously choose one candidate out of the candidate out of all those who contested to fly the flag of the party".
"We will be able to give you the list but one thing again is that from state to state we are resolving issues and some candidates have security reports against them and some we were also told they were allegations that they have court judgement against them".
"We cannot wish away some of these allegations without verification and once we verify truly and fairly we will be able to decide who fly the flag of the party".
"One thing is clear, if we do not verify and field wrong candidate, obviously the opposition will take them to court and we don't want the risk of having our candidate thrown out by the court".
In his paper at the workshop which enters its second day yesterday, immediate past Special Adviser to Mr. President on Policy Matters, Professor Julius Ihonvbere urged the party's candidates to conduct themselves in the manner that would give value to the party.
"You, the candidates of the party must now, through personal conduct, pronouncements, actions, interactions, engagements with others outside our party, and adherence to those lofty values, give life and meaning to the Code and Index".
"For a thorough job to be done by the screeners, we need to give them sufficient time for the job. Screening almost 100 aspirants in two days is an uphill, if not impossible task. We can only build confidence in the process by giving every one a fair hearing".
"Of course, we must mass produce and circulate both documents so that leaders and followers can own and deploy them in their political work. The party members can use the documents to hold their leaders accountable whenever they speak or act outside its preachments".
"The party leaders must be ready to show example by sanctioning any candidate found to have doctored the information with which he or she was cleared and must be ready to deal ruthlessly with those that try to undermine the worth of the code and index. This is the only way in which members and non-members will take them seriously".
"Finally, the future of our party and nation lies in our hands. We need to search our souls and see if we have done things that we can defend before God and man; if we can really say loudly that love and care for the people, community and country are our primary motivators for office".
Minister of Agriculture, Mallam Adamu Bello in his presentation said there have been serious drives towards diversification and increased public and private investments in agriculture since the inception of this administration in 1999.
He said, new agricultural policy and three subsectoral policies-Rural Development, Cooperatives and Fertilizers were consequently approved, followed by launching and vigorous implementation of several presidential initiatives.
According to him, achievements made under the current efforts was that crop production recorded tremendous increase with the production of maize, millet, sorghum and rice increased by 38%, 29%, 31% and 28% respectively in 2006 compared to 1999.
He said that of cassava, cowpea, palm-oil, groundnut and cocoa increased by 50%, 38%, 44%, 32% and 242% respectively.

Original_Search_Title: DFP's Big Plans
Headline: Ghana: DFP's Big Plans
Body_Text:
The Democratic Freedom Party (DFP) has plans to produce gari in large quantities for export to generate adequate revenue for the country when given the mandate to rule the nation.
Apart from the immense economic returns the nation would get, the move would help solve the unemployment problem confronting the nation as it would offer millions of the jobless youth in the regions permanent employment.
To ensure the fulfilment of this dream, the party plans to provide each of the ten regions, a giant gari processing factory and encourage cassava growers to produce more to feed the proposed industries by offering them a better deal.
Mr. John Amekah, Deputy General Secretary of the DFP disclosed the plans when he addressed a cross-section of people, including farmers, fishermen and DFP regional executives at Awutu-Panim, near Ayensuako, in the Central Region.
The meeting was to explain the aims and objectives of the party to people in and around Awutu-Panim, who are mostly cocoa and food crops farmers.
Mr. Amekah attributed the failure of the Ayensu Starch Factory at Awutu-Bawjiase to lack of proper planning by the initiators, and explained that when DFP takes over the administration of the country and sets up the proposed gari factories, cassava growers would be worth their salt.
He said the country could produce millions, if not billions of tonnes of gari to famine-threatened African countries annually to generate enough revenue to cushion her economy if succeeding governments had thought of it and initiated moves towards that decades ago.
Besides, the commodity could also be exported to some Western and Middle-East countries for greater economic returns if proper feasibility studies were carried out in those countries to secure a lasting market for the commodity.
Mr. Amekah described as untrue, the perception by some leading politicians that no political group can solve the numerous socio-economic problems facing the nation single-handedly.
He maintained that, "the mere fact that the two leading political parties, NDC and NPP have, as yet, not been able to secure the right antidote for the country's economic woes, does not imply that no political group could do so".
The Deputy General Secretary said that the DFP has got the answer to the country's economic and other predicaments and asked the electorate to give the DFP the nod in the forthcoming Presidential and Parliamentary elections in 2008.
He said the DFP would unite the people, irrespective of their political, religious and ethnic backgrounds, to ensure effective national cohesion and progress all the time.
Professor E. S, Okyne, a founding member of the DFP, who chaired the function, also assured Ghanaians of the party's determination to salvage the nation from its socio-economic predicament.

Original_Search_Title: South West - Hon. Efite Rehabilitates "Ndongo Bridge"
Headline: Cameroon: South West - Hon. Efite Rehabilitates "Ndongo Bridge"
Body_Text:
Abandoned for more than 13 years after it collapsed, a solid "Ndongo Bridge" will soon be a reality thanks to the ongoing rehabilitation works being financed by Hon. Andrew Efite Engange.
At a cost of CFA 26,681,778 francs from Hon. Efite's micro-projects money from the National Assembly, the "Ndongo Bridge" is a dream come true to the Tiko population who (for years) have been cut-off from the Tiko General Hospital, Mungo villages and some CDC camps such as Small and Big Ekange, Sone, Ebunjie, Mondoni and Missellele.
Apart from facilitating transportation, when completed the bridge will ease access to farms and the transportation of farm produce from villages to the historic Tiko market. It should be recalled that farms in the villages near and around the bridge are noted for producing foodstuff such as plantains, cassava, yams, egusi, cocoyams, beans, potatoes, etc. Moreover, thanks to the bridge, children will have access to schools in Tiko; the CDC will easily carry its latex to its factory in Tiko and will enjoy other advantages. However, vehicles above four tons will not be allowed to use the bridge.
Talking to CT, the charismatic and no-nonsense Hon. Efite said the decision to construct the bridge stems from his profound concern for the "suffering masses of Tiko" who had been completely cut-off with the collapse of the "Ndongo Bridge". "People had to pay thrice the cost of circumventing the destroyed bridge to areas across the Ndongo river. My decision is also an attempt to alleviate the poverty of the people and in attaining the 2015 Millenium Development Goals. I think this initiative is my own way of demonstrating to the people of Tiko that l have them at heart," Hon. Efite explained. Construction work kicked-off on April 4, 2006 and is expected to be over soon. Hon. Efite has engaged a local contractor with local labour. The bridge spans through a length of 38 metres, has a height of 8.30 metres and a width of 8.20 metres.
The collapsed bridge was constructed by the CDC in the 1940s. "This is a dream come true. I never expected this bridge to be rehabilitated in my life time. Hon. Efite should not only receive our blessings, but he is a man of the people," a villager said, as he examined the current rehabilitation work of the "Ndongo Bridge". The famous "Ndongo Bridge" is not only vital for the population of Tiko, but it is highly significant. Its rehabilitation therefore, has indepth social and economic ramifications to the people of the area.

Original_Search_Title: WFP Seeks Shs221b to Feed IDPs in North
Headline: Uganda: WFP Seeks Shs221b to Feed IDPs in North
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE United Nations World Food Programme is seeking $127 million (about Shs221 billion) to buy food for displaced people in north and north-eastern region, this year.
According to statement dated January 5 the money which is being sought through the UN Consolidated Appeals Process will benefit 3.5 million internally displaced people and refugees in the north and eastern regions.
"For 2007, WFP seeks US$127 million under the UN Consolidated Appeals Process to provide relief and recovery assistance to internally displaced people, refugees and drought-affected people in Karamoja," the statement reads.
Last year WFP purchased and distributed 162,000 metric tons of food valued at $41 million. A total of 73 per cent of this food was distributed to IDPs and refugees in the country while the rest went to other WFP programmes in the Great Lakes region, the statement says.
WFP said in 2006, it helped 230,000 IDPs in Acholi and Lango to return to their homes owing to the relative security registered in the region.
The statement said most of the returnees moved voluntarily, while others formed satellite camps where they are already undertaking farming.
WFP says it helped returned families to cultivate 600 acres of land in Pader and at the Loro Prison farm in Apac district and supported 1.4 million IDPs in Lira, Gulu, Pader and Kitgum districts under the Food for Life project. The project involved giving return packages and relief food to the returnees to support malnourished children.
WFP gave a three-month resettlement food package to families who gave up their relief ration cards in 2006. In addition, it gave out seeds worth $ 208,000 to returnees and displaced people in camps - most of whom can now grow some food to meet a part of their family's needs.
"Some of these families have spent years in the camps, and some of their children have never known a normal life. It's enormously encouraging to see people feel safe enough to go home. WFP has worked hard to provide the practical help they need in those vital first few months," WFP Deputy Country Director in Uganda Alix Loriston said.
WFP said vulnerable people, such as widows with young children and the disabled continue to receive 100 percent rations, meeting all their minimum kilocalorie requirements.
The statement adds that this year, families who will return home will continue to get help from WFP which will provide potato and cassava seeds to 24,000 households in Lango and Acholi in the planting season.
Mr Loriston said generous support from donors, the government and non-governmental organisations helped WFP to continue its support for the 1.2 million people who remain in the camps, deprived of the most basic essentials, including food.

Original_Search_Title: WFP Helps 230,000 IDPs Resettle
Headline: Uganda: WFP Helps 230,000 IDPs Resettle
Body_Text:
Kampala — FOLLOWING an improvement of security in Acholi and Lango sub-regions, 230,000 Internally Displaced Persons (IDPs) have returned to their homes.
According to the World Food Programme, the IDPs, who voluntarily moved out of the camps, received resettlement packages.
While many moved back to their homes, others formed satellite camps where they could farm, said a Friday statement from the World Food Programme in Kampala.
The deputy country director, Alix Loriston, said the organisation gave the IDPs resettlement packages that were enough for three months.
The organisation also gave the returnees and other people still in the camps agricultural seeds worth $208,000.
"Some of these families have spent years in the camps, and some of their children have never known a normal life.
"It is enormously encouraging to see people feel safe enough to go home," Loriston concluded.
Over 1.5 million people were forced into camps due to the insurgency by the LRA rebels led by Joseph Kony .
Loriston said: "Despite these gains, food assistance represents more than half of their daily intake of 2,100 kilocalories a day." He explained that this year, families that returned home would continue getting help from the World Food Programme, which seeks to provide potato and cassava seeds to 24,000 households in Lango and Acholi region.
"In 2006, the World Food Programme helped people dig and stock 160 fish ponds in Lango and Teso sub-regions.
"For 2007, the World Food Programme seeks $127m under the UN Consolidated Appeals Process to provide relief and recovery assistance to IDPs" added Loriston.
He said the generous support from donors, the Ugandan Government and non-governmental organisations helped them continue its support for the 1.2 million people who are still in camps.
Loriston said they would in partnership with the Government of Uganda, UN agencies and NGOs assist 3.5 million people.

Original_Search_Title: Huíla to Rebuild Over Sixty Bridges This Year
Headline: Angola: HuÃ­la to Rebuild Over Sixty Bridges This Year
Body_Text:
Lubango — Sixty four bridges in the southern HuÃ­la province will, this year, undergo repairing works foreseeing the improvement of road circulation, announced Friday the local director of Public Works, RosÃ¡rio Ima Panzo.
The official said that these bridges were identified in the whole province as needing a serious and urgent intervention, with highlight to those over the Cassongue, Catapi, Cuvunge (Caluquembe), Cutenda and Cassava Rivers.

Original_Search_Title: Heavy Rains to Go On Till Mid January
Headline: Uganda: Heavy Rains to Go On Till Mid January
Body_Text:
Kampala — The department of Meteorology has warned of more heavy rains until mid January to be followed by a normal dry spell.
The heavy rains that started late last year appeared to have stopped in some parts of the country but the weather experts say it was due to some disruption.
"The rains were disrupted by a series of tropical cyclones near Madagascar but as they die out, we expect the rains to continue until mid January after which it will be followed by a normal dry spell expected to go on till March," Principal meteorologist Aloysius Kagooro told Daily Monitor yesterday.
However, the former Assistant Commisioner for Meteorology currently on special assignment at the department, Mr Abushen Majugu said the coming rains are just a continuation of the moderate elnino conditions, which started in 2006.
Majugu said farmers should make plans for the expected normal dry season and plant crops like cassava, potatoes that can withstand the dry spell.
He said the department of meteorology would update the public on the weather pattern to enable them make adjustments where possible. A team of experts are currently sensitising the public in Tororo district about the rains and how to utilise meteorology information for planning.
"We are done with sensitising people in Manafa, Kapchorwa, Sironko and Kamuli districts," Majugu said. "Next week, we shall move to districts in south western Uganda and later central western." The sensitisation exercise is expected to end with the central region.
The rains, which started late last year, have already caused havoc in some parts of the country. In some areas, roads were washed out and made impassable while in others, floods caused massive destruction of crops and other property, including lives of people and animals.
Some places, including Kampala experienced outbreaks of cholera often associated with floods.

Original_Search_Title: FG Pays $1.4 Billion Out of the London Club Debt
Headline: Nigeria: FG Pays $1.4 Billion Out of the London Club Debt
Body_Text:
Abuja — President Olusegun Obasanjo has said $1.4 billion London Club debt has already been paid, with the final balance of $900m to be paid by March 2007.
President Obasanjo made this disclosure on January 4 at the State House, Abuja when the National Economic Intelligence Committee (NEIC) presented the 3rd Quarter, 2006 Report.
"We have paid off $1.4 billion of the amount we owe to the London Club, and the balance of about $900 million will be paid by March, this year, effectively wiping clean, the debts we owe", he stated.
Commenting on parts of the Report, which expressed concern at high interest rates and inflation, the President agreed that interest rates were still high compared to other economies, though he acknowledged that progress had been made in the last seven years.
He challenged Nigerian farmers to take up cassava farming, to forestall price increases, which would arise from higher demand that is already evident in the markets noting, "The massive importation of machines for the production of starch, flour, and other cassava products clearly indicates that we need to raise cultivation of cassava".
He condemned cases of violence in the country, and urged Nigerians to desist from attributing any violence to political causes, emphasising that "we should move away from giving ourselves a bad name unnecessarily. Many of the cases of violence are economic or even social, and we should not classify them otherwise".
The President while responding to NEIC's concern about the Niger Delta, said implementation of the Regional Master Plan prepared for the region last year would commence this year adding that development has to be an admixture of human and infrastructure to be meaningful for comprehensive societal growth and called for more practical training and skill acquisition.
Dr. Ibrahim Ayagi, Chairman of NEIC, had earlier told the President that recent surveys showed that "the anti-inflationary policy of government had been very effective" with food-related inflation down to 9.0% in September 2006 from 22.9% in January of the same year. He commended the stable foreign exchange rate and described the 2.32% differential in the parallel market as "a strategic achievement".
On expenditure, Dr. Ayagi said recurrent expenditure from January to September, 2006 was N861.22 billion, while capital expenditure was N72.76 billion, and expressed concern at the "low" utilization of capital releases by ministries, departments and agencies.
The NEIC report commended the President for committing about N210 billion in the Niger Delta through NDDC and the "basic characteristic of transparency" with which this was done, it also expressed concern at the over emphasis on physical projects to the neglect of human development.
The violence in the Niger Delta, lack of a nurturing partnership between NDDC and its host states, and the "unsatisfactory performance of consultants" handling regional projects were the other areas of concern expressed in the report.

Original_Search_Title: Flood-Hit Tororo Locals Get Food Aid
Headline: Uganda: Flood-Hit Tororo Locals Get Food Aid
Body_Text:
Kampala — ABOUT 1,500 people affected by floods in Paya sub-county, Tororo district, have received relief items from the Prime Minister's Office.
The LC5 chairman, emmanuel Osuna, said 8,750kg of posho and 1,250kg of beans were delivered to the affected families.
"They also got 10 bales of blankets, three bales of secondhand clothes and five bags of secondhand shoes," Osuna said on Wednesday.
The items were distributed to people in barinyanga, Sopsop, Nawire, Sere, Paya and Perper parishes.
The floods that hit the area in November destroyed 469 acres of cassava, potatoes rice and cotton and displaced 823 families in Paya and Merikit sub-counties.
Osuna said the World Food Programme had pledged to deliver relief items in April after the assessment of the disaster.
"By then, we expect food stocks to have run out. Most people will be preparing gardens for the first season's planting," Osuna said.

Original_Search_Title: We've Fulfilled Our Promise - Udenwa
Headline: Nigeria: We've Fulfilled Our Promise - Udenwa
Body_Text:
Lagos — Imo State Governor, Chief Achike Udenwa, Wednesday, reviewed his eight year administration in the state and declared that he had kept faith with the promise to kick-start monumental infrastructure and human capital development in the state.
Governor Udenwa made this declaration, when he addressed Imo Citizens from all walks of life, during this year's Imo Stakeholders Forum at the Multi Purpose Hall, Owerri, the state capital.
"We have kept with our promises to leave Imo State better than we met it in terms of human capital and infrastructural development," he stated.
The forum which was started in 2004 has become annual feedback interactive session to review the governor's vision and stewardship and chart the way forward.
Speaking at this year's forum, which is the last in the life of his administration, Chief Udenwa, expressed satisfaction that he had reversed negative attitudes both in the civil service and the larger society thus transformed the state economic fortunes to a beehive of commercial activities and development potentials.
This, he explained, had been through the tripod vision anchored on Agriculture, Industrialization, and Reorientation.
He said apart from massive provision of electricity and potable water these years, other tangible sign posts of his achievements including the ultra modern state secretariat, which according to him is ranked among the best in the country.
In the area of road development, Udenwa said that his administration had completed 48 roads with a total of 272 kilometers just as 38 others were at various stages of completion, even as he assured that the on-going ones would be commissioned before he leaves office in May, this year.
"Our target is to get all on-going projects completed so as to leave no room for the sad phenomenon of abandoned projects," he stated.
Other achievements, he listed included the Orlu Regional Market, to reposition Imo as a key centre for economic and commercial activities; the Imo State University Teaching Hospital, which would soon graduate its first set of medical doctors, and the take-off of the College of Engineering of the Imo State University (IMSU) at Umuna Okigwe.
The state government, he further said had also re-equipped the General Hospital at the new Owerri and three others in the oil producing Local Government Areas (LGAs) of Umuokanne, Egbema and Oguta; as well as revitalized ailing industries especially the Imo Concord Hotel, Adapalm, the Imo Modern Poultry and the state Modern Shoe Industry.
Besides, his administration had also created enabling environment for agricultural activities to thrive even as it had established oil mills in various parts of the state, rice processing mills in Ihitte-Uboma and Ideato North LGAs and a cassava processing complex at the Michael Okpara College of Agriculture and Technology to give added impetus to cassava production.
Governor Udenwa also listed gains of the reorientation programme and the Imo Free Trade Zone (FTZ), which he described as the biggest economic venture by his administration.
According to him, the venture which is being established in conjunction with a Chinese consortium, when completed, would inject $500million into the state's economy. He commended the Chinese, who are already on ground and owners of the land in Ngor Okpala LGA and prayed that the project be sustained by the incoming administration.
He identified peace and tranquility in the state as a major hallmark of his administration, adding; "We need leaders who will ensure that nothing is done to jeopardize the peace for which Imo State is admired by all."
High point of the forum which was well attended by Imo people was the interactive session when the governor answered questions from the citizenry.
Many commended his road rehabilitation programme and prayed for the construction of a ring road to reduce congestion on the Aba-Owerri Onitsha road, while the low point of the administration was on environmental/refuse management in the state capital.

Original_Search_Title: W.K. Kellog Foundation Releases Grant to IITA
Headline: Nigeria: W.K. Kellog Foundation Releases Grant to IITA
Body_Text:
Lagos — W.K. Kellog Foundation in the U.S. has given a grant of US$599,800 to the International Institute of Tropical Agriculture (IITA) to promote NEPAD Pan Africa Cassava Initiative (NPACI) for sustainable economic growth, food security and poverty alleviation in Africa.
A release by Mr Taye Babaleye, IITA Public Relations Manager, said that the amount was to complement IITA's response to promote the production of cassava, a drought tolerant crop, to reduce the risk associated with the dependency on maize as major food-crop in some drought-hit countries.
The release said that in 1997/98, a severe drought hit Katimba village and devastated maize farms in Southern Africa, putting the lives of 27 million people in Lesotho, Madagascar, Malawi, Swaziland, Tanzania, and Zambia at high risk of severe food shortage.
It explained that IITA, in conjunction with two other organisations, initiated the Masinda Cassava Club in the village as pilot processing centre to turn cassava into industrial starch.
"This was aimed at adding value to cassava production in the village," it said, adding that after one year, the Club sold about 40 Mt of starch, worth over US$20,000.
It explained that prior to the establishment of the factory, cassava had no real monetary value in Katimba village, but that farmers now make over US$800 from just one hectare of fresh cassava sold to the Club.
According to the release, the grant will help the NEPAD/IITA facilitation unit being established by NPACI/SARRNET at Chitedze Research Station.

Original_Search_Title: Nigeria Exits London Club
Headline: Nigeria Exits London Club
Body_Text:
Abuja — President Olusegun Obasanjo has disclosed that Nigeria would exit the London Club of creditors by March when it would pay the final tranche of about $900 million to the club and the country would thereafter become debt free.
Speaking yesterday while receiving the Third Quarter 2006 Report of the National Economic Intelligence Committee (NEIC), at the State House Abuja, the President said Nigeria has so far paid $1.4 billion to the London Club in debt repayment.
"We have paid off $1.4 billion of the amount we owe to the London Club, and the balance of about $900 million will be paid by March, this year, effectively wiping clean, the debts we owe", he stated.
Commenting on parts of the Report that expressed concern at high interest rates and inflation, the President agreed that interest rates were still high compared to other economies.
He however said appreciable progress had been made since his administration came on board over the issue.
While responding to NEIC's concern about the Niger Delta, the President stated that the implementation of the Regional Master Plan prepared for the region last year would commence this year.
After receiving the report from the Chairman of NEIC, Dr. Ibrahim Ayagi, the President also condemned cases of violence in the country and urged Nigerians to desist from attributing any violence to political causes.
"We should move away from giving ourselves a bad name unnecessarily. Many of the cases of violence are economic or even social, and we should not classify them otherwise", he emphasised.
The President then threw a challenge to Nigerian farmers by asking them to take up cassava farming to forestall price increases which would arise from higher demand that is already evident in the markets.
"The massive importation of machines for the production of starch, flour, and other cassava products clearly indicates that we need to raise cultivation of cassava", he stated.
Speaking on the need to ensure development, the President said "development has to be an admixture of human and infrastructure", for it to be meaningful for comprehensive societal growth and called for more practical training and skill acquisition.
Meanwhile, the NEIC report disclosed that recurrent expenditure from January to September 2006 was N861.22 billion, while capital expenditure was N72.76 billion.
It however expressed concern at the "low" utilisation of capital funds by ministries, departments and agencies.
Earlier, Ayagi had told the President that recent surveys showed that "the anti-inflationary policy of government had been very effective", disclosing that food-related inflation was down to 9 per cent in September 2006 from 22.9 per cent in January 2006 .
He commended the stable foreign exchange rate and described the 2.32 per cent differential in the parallel market as "a strategic achievement".
While commending President Obasanjo for committing about N210 billion to the Niger Delta through the Niger Delta Devel-opment Commission (ND-DC) and the "basic characteristic of transparency" with which it was done, the NEIC Report expressed concern that more emphasis are placed on physical projects to the neglect of human development.
Other areas of concern raised by the NEIC include violence in the Niger Delta, lack of a nurturing partnership between NDDC and its host states, and the "unsatisfactory performance of consultants" handling regional projects.

Original_Search_Title: FG Pays $1.4bn London Club Debt
Headline: Nigeria: FG Pays $1.4bn London Club Debt
Body_Text:
Abuja — President Olusegun Obasanjo said Nigeria has already paid $1.4billion London Club debt with the final balance of $900million to be paid by March this year.
He said this when the National Economic Intelligence committee (NEIC) presented the third quarter of the 2006 report to him at the state house in Abuja yesterday.
According to him, "we have paid off $1.4billion of the amount we owe the London Club and the balance of about $900million will be paid by March this year, effectively wiping clean the debts we owe."
He expressed concern on the high interest rates and inflation in the economy, adding that the interest rate is still high compared to other economies, though he acknowledged that progress had been made in the past seven years.
He encouraged the Nigerian farmers to take up cassava farming, to check inflation.
"The massive importation of machines for the production of starch, flour, and other cassava products clearly indicates that we need to raise cultivation of cassava," he said.
He condemned cases of violence in the country, and urged Nigerians to desist from attributing any violence to political causes.
Responding to NEIC comments about the Niger Delta, the president said implementation of the regional master plan prepared for the region last year would commence this year.
He added that, "development has to be as a mixture of human and infrastructure," to be meaningful for comprehensive, societal growth and called for more practical training and skill acquisition.
Earlier, the chairman NEIC Professor Ibrahim Ayagi had told the president that recent survey showed that "the anti-inflationary policy of government had been very effective," with food-related inflation down to 9.0 percent in September 2006 from 22.9 percent in January of the same year.
He commended the stable exchange rate and described the 2.32 percent differential in the parallel market as a "strategic achievement."

Original_Search_Title: FAO to Improve Lives of Returnees
Headline: Uganda: FAO to Improve Lives of Returnees
Body_Text:
Kampala — Nutrition of about 13,800 internally displaced persons and returnees is to improve in the war affected sub regions of Acholi, Lango and Teso.
The Great Lakes Regional Cassava Multiplication Initiative vowed to massively propagate and distribute disease free planting materials to these regions.
The National Agronomist of Food and Agriculture Organisation (FAO) Emergency Coordination Unit in Uganda Jimmy Owani said this will ensure food security among households whose livelihood depend on cassava.
He said the project wants to improve the nutritional status of the vulnerable returnees in the Great Lakes Region.
He said that for the case of Uganda, FAO is working in collaboration with National Agriculture Research Organisation(NARO)'s Cassava Programme among other stakeholders to implement the project and that sofar, more than half of the planned acreages have been covered.
T he great Lakes Regional Cassava Multiplication Initiative is a one-year project (June 2006-May 2007).
The project is currently implemented in Uganda, Rwanda, Burundi and Democratic Republic of Congo.
It mainly targets war affected population, households affected with cassava mosaic disease and other complex emergencies like the dry spell.
The targeted districts in Uganda are Kitgum, Pader, Gulu, Soroti, Kaberamaido, Katakwi, Lira and Apac.
One million euros (about Shs23.4 billion) initiative funded by the European Commission Humanitarian Office (ECHO) is being spearheaded by the Food and Agriculture Organisation of United Nations in collaboration with stakeholders in four African countries.

Original_Search_Title: Decongestion Reaches 50 Percent in Gulu, Amuria
Headline: Uganda: Decongestion Reaches 50 Percent in Gulu, Amuria
Body_Text:
Kampala — OVER 50% of internally displaced people (IDPs) in Gulu and Amuria districts have moved to smaller camps, closer to their homes, as part of the government's decongestion plan.
At the peak of the insurgency, the two districts had a combined IDP population of 518,000, living in 53 camps.
In recent months, about 259,000 moved to at least 72 new settlements, nearer to their land, according to the Prime Minister's office.
In Gulu, some of the sub-counties they returned to are Paicho, Bungatira Bobi, Lalogi and Kony's home town of Odek.
In Amuru, the new resettlements are in Pabbo, Alero, Lamogi and Amuru.
Pabbo camp was one of the oldest and biggest in Gulu, hosting over 60,000 IDPs.
Today, the population has gone down to 20,000. The rest spread out to Bira, Otong, Olinga, Gengari, Pabbo Kal and Pogo parishes.
When visiting the new settlements, the returnees were found repairing their old homes, cultivating sorghum and cassava, while others were harvesting sim sim and beans.
The main factors prompting people to move closer to their villages were the peace talks, the IDP registration exercise and the reduced assistance in the main camps, local authorities said.
At the new Olinga camp, on the busy Gulu-Nimule road, 3,824 people have resettled.
"We were originally staying in Pabbo," explained camp leader Coonok Okwonga. "But we were tired of living in that overcrowded camp. At least here we can access our land." He, however, noted that they were in need of safe water, teachers, garden tools and drugs.
He also remarked that they had not yet harvested, so they still depended on food hand-outs of the World Food Programme.
Bira satellite camp received 4,000 returnees in the past weeks.
"All of us came from Pabbo, We have built 840 houses so far," said camp leader Donato Onen.
Pointing at a UPDF detachment nearby, he added: "We feel secure here. We are happy that we have returned to our land."
Roasting guinea fowls at a fire on the bridge over river Ayugi, Ben Ouma, a 29-year-old father of 11 children, said he fled to Pabbo camp when the rebels killed his father, an RC (local council) chairman in 1987.
"Now we can sleep (peacefully)," he said. "We have planted and next year we shall have no hunger." In the new Gengari camp, there was excitement. "We have lived in exile in Pabbo for too long," a group of youngsters said.
According to camp leader, Albert Achellam, over 10,000 people had moved back and 1,249 new houses had been built.
"We have planted and even harvested some of them. We expect a bumper harvest next year. Our children are at Palwong Primary School. But we need medicine," he said.
A thriving market sold charcoal at sh10,000 a sack, sorghum at sh200 a kg and beans at sh400 a kg.

Original_Search_Title: Kyeremanten Leading NPP Field
Headline: Ghana: Golden Boy Allan Tops the Bill
Body_Text:
CHRONICLE POLITICAL Intelligence can confirm that Mr. Allan Kyeremanten, 48, the Minister of Trade, Industries and President's Special Initiative (PSI) is streets ahead of New Patriotic Party (NPP) aspiring candidates duelling in the congested field of 19 for the opportunity for a face off with the NDC's Professor John Evans Atta Mills, and others from the smaller parties yet to elect a presidential candidate.
The very first Minister-aspirant to burst unto the field, Mr. Keremanten, who dropped an opportunity to contest the Komenda-Edina-Eguafo-Abirem (KEEA) Parliamentary seat, has been able to sustain the blistering pace he set with his maiden visit to Elmina in the first quarter of 2006.
Allan at the time touched down at the area with his brother Steve,his unofficial campaign manager, introduced himself to the Chief of Elmina, Nana Conduah, and his abusuapanyin in tow.
The dapper Allan has been able to exploit his fractional maternal linkage (mother is half Fante and half Asante) to his Fante roots, an essential political asset these days of ethnic political permutations.
Chronicle's intelligence monitoring of most of the NPP aspirants by this paper's correspondents in the paper's, fully-established editorial offices in nine out of the ten regions, has confirmed to head office that in the face of all the claims being cabvassed, the Allan's chariots of war is in front line standing so far, but with nearly one year to go, anything can happen to upset everything, something his insurance executive brother in Accra, is dreading.
KEEA POLITICS Data supplied from the Central Region bureau show that, some supporters of the Convention People's Party (CPP) have erected a protective wall to ward off the onslaught of Allan in the KEEA Constituency, which is the sole seat in the country where the 'inclusive 'Government' candidate Paa Kwesi Nduom is currently 'king'.
Candidate Kyeremanten's recent high profile activity, during the celebration of this year's Bakatue festival, when he was dispatched to attend by President Kufuor to represent him, was seen as a confirmation of the widespread speculation that Allan was the President' choice.
To his credit, Allan, has been able to secure the support of the NPP Constituency Chairman, Mr. Appiah Korang, a tough principled man, the constituency secretary, Asmah and his elder brother, who is the DCE, Mr. George Frank Asmah, a former correspondent of The Chronicle, who worked for the paper while studying for his degree at the University of Cape Coast.
What is unclear is the position or allegiance of the Regional Minister, Nana Ato Arthur, a native of Abrase, in Abirem within the minority KEEA Constituency, since the constituency chairman and his Regional Minister have a very tested relationship.
The biggest problem confronting the area is a chieftaincy affair, which has pitched the long-time friend of President Kufuor, Nana Conduah, the chief of Elmina and godfather of Dr. Kwesi Nduom against one of the implacable foes of the chief, Nana Asmah, a noted kingmaker in Elmina, who incidentally happens to be the father of the Asmah brothers.
The CPP knows that any exponential growth in the support base of the NPP in the area could potentially spell trouble for Dr. Paa Kwesi Nduom, the Minister for Public Sector Reforms, but would have an egregious political downside because it is guaranteed to tip the seat straight into the waiting hungry arms of the main political rival, the NDC, which kept the seat from 1992, 1996, 2000 through Dr. Ato Quarshie, the former Minister of Roads.
KUFUOR STOPPED ATO ARTHUR's RUN It took the intervention of President Kufuor to stop Nana Ato Arthur, then the District Chief Executive, from running on the ticket of the NPP for the seat in 2001, before Dr. Nduom could halt the advance of the NDC. But with President Kufuor now virtually in his last year in office, no one can stop the burning ambition of Arthur to mount a parliamentary assault on Nduom's seat.
Allan's forays into KEEA and the open declaration of Nana Ato Arthur, now the Regional Minister, for the parliamentary seat, and key KEEA executives\delegates, may be providing Allan with a vital bridgehead for his political annexation of the Central Rregion, arguably the most prized asset in the election.
KALAMARI Chronicle intelligence has also established a super secret political ring erected by Allan with the code name, Kalamari, a political network which envelopes the nation, and is financially nourished and empowered by Allan. Virtually every NPP regional chairman, from Volta through Upper East to Central, is said to belong to the Kalamari, which boasts of some leading Ministers in the Allan train as well.
Allan also boasts of a close alliance with the Honourable Joe Ghartey, the dynamic and youthful Attorney General and Minister for Justice, who is also the MP for Essikado Ketan, a helpful combination that has strategically reduced the potential impact of Papa Owusu Ankomah, who has declared to fellow MPs that he is running for President.
Owusu Ankomah, 48, crucially needed the Joe Ghartey support because he worked from the chambers of Ghartey and Ghartey in Accra when he had to leave his practice in Takoradi to take up appointment as Minister.
Another substantial Minister associated with the Allan ticket is the workaholic and resilient Hon. Stephen Asamoah Boateng, Minister of Local Government, and Enviroment. Steve and his DCE for his Mfantseman West constituency, Mr. Quainoo-Arthur, are both from Kromantse and are strong Allan loyalists, an indication of the quality of support that Allan enjoys considering the powerful pull that these relevant loyalists can command.
Allan's former Deputy, Honourable Osei Ameyaw, the MP for Asuogyaman, is also a very powerful ally to the Kyeremanten Kalamari network.
Chronicle intelligence has however established that Allan has begun to entertain doubts on the loyalty of Osei Ameyaw; because of the entry of the powerful former two-term General Secretary Mr. Dan Botwe into the presidential contest.
Dan Botwe's maternal link is Asuogyaman, and the highly regarded Dan was a major catalyst for Osei Ameyaw's victorious assault on the otherwise impregnable NDC strongold from the party's domination since 1992.
Billonaire Osei Ameyaw, has now improved his standing in the constituency, being one of a handful of MPs who do not even collect their pay checks from Parliament, but turn it over for projects that benefit the constituency. Independently, Chronicle could not gather whether he is still loyal to Allan even though he is a regular in the Dan Botwe side. He could not be reached for confirmation when Chronicle called and most of the respondents to our calls refused to be quoted for obvious reasons- they do not want to be tagged.
Curiously, even though, NPP political pundits maintain that a Kufuor endorsement is a kiss of death, the Allan ticket appears to be on the ascent, buoyed by a finely tuned message of youthfulness and enterprise and his careful omission of record of catastrophic failures as a Minister and ambassador.
On Tuesday however, a Peace FM programme however had someone touch on the sensitive, potentially destructive record of his tenure as Minister in charge of Presidential Special Initiatives (PSI). It was like pin prick in a bloated balloon- a suggestion that $40 million might have gone to waste on the cassava/ Starch project under his watch though.
'Strangely, nobody seems to know that it was Allan who brought failed projects like the American supermarket chain, and the CNCTI scandal, which has been foisted on to the balance sheet of Hon. Yaw Osafo Maafo', observed a delegate from one of the opposing camps in the Ashanti Region, where our Ashanti regional bureau confirmed that the 39 constituency voter-rich region was divided over Allan's bid.
According to our intelligence, constituency executives and polling station chairmen have been expressing disappointment with Allan's stance of distancing himself from his genuine Asante roots. His father was one of the President's mentors, along with the late Victor Owusu, and remains a doyen of the Kumasi Asante folklore, having been a Minister in the Second Republic of Dr. K.A Busia's government.
There is no clear front-runner in the region, which is famous for its independent free-wheeling spirit, which has enabled the principal political adversary, the NDC to secure three seats.
Allan's technique of canvassing for delegates from polling station to polling station, instead of meeting only the regional executives has also caught on widely, and pushed equally hard-working aspirants like Dr. Addo Kufuor to adopt the same expensive but effective strategy.
The Northern Region is an open field with Vice President Aliu, Nana Akuffo Addo, Dr. Addo Kufuor and Allan, fighting for slices of the open fields of an increasingly resurgent NDC maintaining a grip on the turf.
'Lately, Arthur Kennedy has been making inroads at KEEA', observed one confirmed delegate, loyal to Allan, of the equally youthful 47-year-old former student leader/medical practitioner (He has resigned his lucrative job in the US). Dr. Kennedy is from Asebu in the Central Region, with the Minister for Fisheries, Hon. Gladys Asmah, grew up in Kumasi, but according to the Allan loyalist, 'Allan has sponsored and secured all the delegates from that constituency'.
With tomorrow's constitutional review conference, it is expected that all the giants of the party will assemble at Koforidua and provide a veritable pool of delegates for mass fishing.
It is expected that probably the party's leadership would borrow a leaf from the NDC's 'family forum' format, when the ground rules for non-adversarial combat would be laid out to head off a potentially poisonous campaign at which candidates may be slashing at one another to get to the front line of the queue.
Issues, and only issues and track record of performance would eventually be the deciding factor.

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo Hits Atiku Afresh
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo Hits Atiku Afresh
Body_Text:
Abeokuta — IN apparent reference to the current face-off between himself and Vice-President Atiku Abubakar, President Olusegun Obasanjo, yesterday, charged in-coming political office holders to pray for loyal deputies that would deputise for them in the real sense of the word.
President Obasanjo spoke at his ancestral home, Ibogun-Olaogun, in the Ifo local government area of Ogun State while hosting Governor Olugbenga Daniel, state commissioners, People's Democratic Party (PDP) chieftains and traditional rulers at a New Year party.
He said the peace being experienced in the state was made possible by the co-operation of everyone including traditional rulers.
His words: "I want to thank all of you because when we talk about peace and development in Ogun State, they were achieved through your cooperation. Yes, somebody leads. The governor leads as the political head of the state; as the chief executive of the state.
"But how far could he have gone if he did not have the support of all and sundry? Particularly of the commissioners, particularly of the deputy? May you never have a deputy that will not be a deputy."
Assessing the performance of his administration at the ceremony which was attended by Governor Ahmed Makarfi of Kaduna State, President Obasanjo said Nigeria was making steady progress and was moving in the right direction.
He advised Nigerians to place emphasis on the education and skill acquisition by their children and wards, adding that the country would lag behind if emphasis was not on skills. He warned that parents should not just enroll their children and wards in school but should ensure that they stay in school to learn and acquire the right skills.
"Education is the best legacy you can give to your children. It is what nobody can take away from them. If you build houses and your children are not educated, those who are educated will buy the houses from them. If you leave factories for them and they are not educated, they will run those factories down and the educated ones will buy those factories," he said.
Also speaking, Governor Daniel commended the reforms of the Obasanjo administration, noting that the economy had improved tremendously. The cassava revolution programme of the state government, he said, had started yielding positive results with increase in the production of the crop.
In attendance at the reception were the Alake of Egbaland, Oba Adedotun Gbadebo; the Awujale of Ijebuland, Oba Sikiru Adetona; the Agura of Gbagura, Oba Halidu Laloko; the PDP Chairman in the state, Chief Joju Fadairo and state commissioners as well as top government functionaries.

Original_Search_Title: Odili Explains Rivers' N30bn Investment
Headline: Nigeria: Odili Explains Rivers' N30bn Investment
Body_Text:
Port — WITH a determination to boost its internally generated revenue (IGR), Rivers State Government has invested over N30 billion in key sectors of the economy such as banking, insurance and petrochemicals.
Governor Peter Odili who disclosed this said the move has facilitated the recruitment of graduates from the state in the affected sectors and premised the action on the need to diversify the state's sources of revenue.
Besides, he said this will maximize investment and employment opportunities for indigenes of the state.
The governor spoke Monday during the annual state banquet held at Government House, Port Harcourt to mark the New Year.
He advised those who for selfish reasons conspire with outsiders to discredit the state and destabilize the system to turn a new leaf and join hands in making the state most attractive, investment-friendly and enviable economic bloc in Nigeria.
Odili maintained that the government is confident in its actions in the conduct of public affairs, expressing optimism that since the government is on the side of truth, there was nothing to be afraid of.
He noted that the banquet which is the eighth and last to be organized by his administration has provided him another opportunity to appraise the performance of his government, adding that the journey which started on May 29, 1999 has been long and fruitful.
According to him, graduate recruitments in these sectors accelerate growth on family and business levels, because income are easily spread among the people, as well as serving as an intervention for immediate family needs.
Odili pointed out that it was equally for this reason that the state Sustainable Development Programme, (RSSDP) a federal government supported initiative, was embarked upon.
"The RSSDP roll out plan is ongoing as many implementers of the Quick Result Projects are on the field across the state in such areas as aqua-culture, micro-credits, cassava growing and processing", he said.
Odili said that although government has made notable impact in agriculture, housing, rural water supply, sports, civil service welfare, empowerment and Capacity-building among others, its effort at creating qualitative capacity towards job and wealth creation was of primary concern.
"We have a subsisting agreement with Unilever on the Allanblackia Plant as an alternative source of oil for margarine and soap making", Governor Odili said, assuring that from inter cropping of the plant to harvesting, the economic benefits of the plant to many farm-families, and business interest, would be overwhelming.
He appealed to the people of the state to support government to complete ongoing projects in the state saying that 'the New Year shall herald a period of greater hard work"
Some of the achievements of the administration according to him, are in the areas of power generation, road construction, education, agriculture, housing, rural water supply, sports and civil servants welfare among others, saying that the achievemnts are there for everyone to see.
He commended the people of the state including traditional rulers, political and religious leaders, the business community, women and youths as well as political parties for their solidarity and support, particularly during the period of his campaigns for the presidency.
He thanked President Olusegun Obasanjo for the able manner he has steered the ship of state and congratulated the PDP presidential flag bearer, and Katsina State Governor, Alhaji Umar Musa Yar'Adua for his emergence, and for choosing Dr Goodluck Jonathan of Bayelsa State as his running aate.
Odili who observed that the gesture has shown that the South-South has become an issue that cannot be pushed aside in the political calculus of Nigeria , enjoined the people of the state to work assiduously for the success of the PDP in the forthcoming elections.

Original_Search_Title: Inflation Soars to 11 Percent
Headline: Uganda: Inflation Soars to 11 Percent
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE annual headline inflation for the year ending December 2006 has hit a 19-month high at 11.3%, the principal statistician at the Uganda Bureau of Standards has revealed.
Vincent Nsubuga stated that the increase was due to higher prices for food, merchandise and services. November's inflation was 9.2%.
"Prices for many foods like cassava, sweet potatoes and irish potatoes went up by 2.4%," he explained.
Nsubuga was releasing the Consumer Price Index.
He noted that the increase in food prices was due to prolonged drought and heavy rains that made the roads impassable.
Nsubuga added that the heavy rains destroyed the crops, making the produce rot in the gardens, which led to low harvests. The demand from neighbouring states was high because they were hit by drought thus increasing the prices.
Nsubuga also attributed the rise in inflation to high sugar prices, electricity tariffs, water bills and transport fares.
However, prices of petrol and paraffin went down due to a fall global oil prices while the shilling appreciated against the dollar.

Original_Search_Title: Agricultural Projects for 2007 Identified
Headline: Cameroon: Agricultural Projects for 2007 Identified
Body_Text:
A workshop was organised in Yaounde recently to assemble proposals from peasant organisations.
The National Research and Extension Programme (PNVRA), a joint programme of the Ministries of Agriculture and Rural Development, and Livestock, Fisheries and Animal Industries, will during the 2007 agricultural season, step up its actions towards peasant farmers. It took the authorities of the organisation and other stakeholders three days running in Yaounde, from 18 to 20 December, 2006 to synchronise the needs of farmers and build up an empirical programme to be executed during the next farming season.
In effect, the whole process of identifying projects for 2007 started in all the operational units of PNVRA from 14 to 28 November this year with farmer organisations expressing their technical and material needs to rural development support structures. The Yaounde workshop was therefore venue to harmonise and rationalise the different support that such bodies might bring in to producer organisations for the implementation of their projects for 2007.
As explained by the National Coordinator of PNVRA, Mbili Oloume Jean Pierre, the workshop was equally occasion to draw a balance sheet of activities for 2006. "Globally, we had a satisfactory balance sheet for 2006, however, we spotted out some of the areas which should be improved upon", he said. This, he said, include the need to step up the level of coverage for the agricultural extension zones where there are presently several vacant posts. "The regular recruitment of senior workers as temporal workers by the minister of Agriculture and Rural Development has not completely solved the problems", he said.
That notwithstanding, the National Research and Extension Programme has put up a highly ambitious action programme for 2007 with the aim of significantly improving the situation of peasant farmers. According to the programme, the number of peasant organisations with whom PNVRA will deal will increase to 971 up from 6152 in 2006. It will assist 139,390 producers as against 85003 in 2006. Action will hinge specifically on 83 projects some of which are already in process.
Producer groups have shown interest in the following areas: food crops such as maize, 83090 tons, beans, 6048 tons, potatoes, 34,195, cassava, 314,000 tons, groundnuts, 4,817 tons, etc. Production has also been earmarked to increase for perennial and fruit crops such as arabica coffee, 400 tons, oil palm 5,333, banana plantain, 129,694, robusta coffee, 1,415, and cocoa 80,169.
Today, PNVRA has 1,628 operation zones, 1,164 which have agents. This means 467 zones are vacant. Each zone is made up of eight villages and village assembles. So far, the State, through PNVRA, has been reinforcing the activities of micro finance establishments so they can finance activities of farmers. Presently, some 330 motor bikes, and 14 vehicles financed by the HIPC fund, are being awaited. These will be shared to extension workers to enable them reach farmers on the field.

Original_Search_Title: 'The EU Is a Bandit in Trade Negotiations'
Headline: Ghana: 'The EU Is a Bandit in Trade Negotiations'
Body_Text:
Accra — A lively debate has been taking place in Ghana for some time now over the likely effects that an economic partnership agreement (EPA) with the European Union would have on the country. This has been thanks in no small part to the work of Tetteh Hormeku, one of Africa's most vocal campaigners on trade issues.
The European Commission, the EU's executive arm, is adamant that nearly 80 African, Caribbean and Pacific (ACP) countries must sign interim EPAs - relating to trade in goods - by the end of this year, with talks on a range of other issues, such as services liberalisation and investment rules continuing into 2008.
Calls by West African governments for the deadline to be extended have been rejected by EU officials.
A 48-year-old qualified lawyer, Hormeku has worked with advocacy group Third World Network Africa in Accra since 1994. He grew up in Old Ningo, a farming and fishing centre 50 km from the Ghanaian capital, where his parents grew tomatoes, cassava and peppers.
These were "useful" crops during his childhood, he recalls, but their importance to the national economy has declined because the International Monetary Fund, the World Bank and the EU have insisted since the 1980s that the country's markets be prised open to often cheaper food from abroad.
By removing most remaining tariffs on agricultural imports from Europe, an EPA would make the situation of Ghanaian farmers even more precarious than it already is, Hormeku fears, thereby exacerbating poverty in this low-income country.
Hormeku spoke to IPS's Brussels correspondent David Cronin during the latter's visit to Accra.
IPS: What is the latest state of play with the EPA talks between your government and the European Commission?
TH: I was almost heartbroken when I heard on Tuesday (4 December 4) that a European Commission delegation was here and that it had brought a draft interim agreement with it. The delegation said on Tuesday that "we have a flight tonight" but they then postponed their flight.
This was an incorrect approach if they actually wanted us to discuss the interim agreement. They should have a submitted a copy of the agreement at least a week in advance. You don't wake up Tuesday and say "here's the agreement you have to sign".
The talks broke down in the end because of a proposal made by the EU on abolishing export taxes. Export taxes are used by developing countries to encourage more processing at home. In Ghana, we have export taxes on cocoa and scrap metal -- to discourage the export of scrap metal.
IPS: Can you explain why the EU wants to have the export taxes applied by African countries eliminated?
TH: Kenya has an export tax on raw leather. In 2005, the EU tried to force Kenya to give up its export tax on leather but Kenya told it to get lost. When it failed with Kenya, it tried to introduce new rules in the WTO (World Trade Organisation) negotiations. But everybody --China, India, and so on -- told it to get lost.
The reason for the EU's position lies with the Global Europe strategy (a policy document which emphasises the Union's desire to remove any obstacles European firms encounter while doing business abroad). In order to ensure Europe's manufacturing competitiveness, you have to ensure a continuous flow of raw materials.
IPS: Were you pleased that the Ghanaian government did not accept the agreement proposed by the EU?
TH: I was pleasantly surprised, not pleased.
IPS: Although the EU had been undertaking talks aimed at securing an EPA with the West Africa region, it has more recently been trying to clinch deals with individual governments in the region. How do you feel about that?
TH: The EU delegation shouldn't have come here to Ghana. ECOWAS (the Economic Community of West African States) had set up a negotiating team. The EU knows that and yet it comes to Ghana and goes to the head of state.
This would be like bypassing (European trade commissioner and British politician) Peter Mandelson by going to (British prime minister) Gordon Brown. It would be like saying to Brown, "we have a problem with Peter Mandelson, you have to intervene".
This exposes the cynicism of the European Union. First, they are saying an EPA is for promoting regional integration. But when they are not getting their way, they behave in a way that undermines our own regional integration.
IPS: Do you think Mandelson has a colonial mindset?
TH: I wouldn't put it in terms of a colonial mindset. His mindset is that, what he thinks should be good for you is good for you. He had that attitude in the British government (in which he was secretary of state for Northern Ireland). He had that attitude to the Irish, when dealing with Ireland.
IPS: A "sustainability impact assessment" undertaken on the Commission's behalf has outlined some of the negative effects that an EPA could have on Ghana. For example, it predicts that agriculture will suffer once tariffs are removed on food imports from Europe. Are you disappointed that the Commission has ignored this study?
TH: In May 2003, there was a meeting with Pascal Lamy (the then EU trade commissioner) in La Beach Hotel (in Accra). I asked him what he would do if the sustainability impact assessments for West Africa say it does not need to be liberalised. He said: "I don't care, I'm here to promote Europe's interests".
IPS: The EU constantly points out that it is the largest aid donor in the world. So the picture you paint of the EU would come as a shock to Europeans who believe it is trying to help developing countries.
TH (holding a beer bottle): People who believe that, have too much faith in European institutions. The EU is a bandit in international negotiations. It is no different to the Americans. Or the only difference is that the Americans say "I want your beer, baby, and if you do not give it to me, I'm gonna shoot you". The Europeans say "I want your beer because it is in your own interests to give it to me".
*The interview was conducted before Ghana signed an EPA with the EU on December 13.

Original_Search_Title: NEPC Leads 44 Coys to Dubai Trade Fair
Headline: Nigeria: NEPC Leads 44 Coys to Dubai Trade Fair
Body_Text:
Abuja — The Nigerian Export Promotion Council (NEPC) is leading over 44 companies to participate at the Dubai Trade Fair.
The Acting Executive Director and Chief Executive Officer of NEPC, Mr. Aliyu Lawal disclosed this at a press briefing in Abuja that the Council will lead a delegation of 44 Nigerian Companies to participate in the fair otherwise known as Global Village Shopping Festival which holds between December 13th, 2007 and March 1st, 2008.
"The objectives of the Fair are to enhance the penetration of Nigerian goods into the international market, attract foreign investments in the development of the nation's non-oil export sector as well as expose the Nigeria business community to current trends in international trade among others," he said.
According to the Market and Product Development Department (MPDD) of the Council, the Nigerian companies who participate at the Fair would use the opportunity of the Fair to exhibit some Nigerian products such as: leather, clothing and textile, beauty products, charcoal, arts and crafts, solid minerals and cassava chips and flour.
He said the Fair, which attracts over four million visitors from over 39 countries will further afford the Nigerian investors the opportunity to interact with their counterparts from other participating countries with a view to seeking for market opportunities for Nigerian in the non-oil export products.
