But though Dr Kedera said Kephis does its own independent tests - and has, at one point in the past been taken to the World Trade Organisation (WTO) by US-based seed companies for delaying the registration of some of their seed varieties - doubts linger over its capacity to test all the products entering the country through the various points of entry.
"Moreover, it is possible that finished products coming into the country could have transgenic traits, but testing for this is beyond Kephis's mandate," Kedera said.
Much of Kenya's agricultural research is handled by KARI, which has been involved in developing a series of hybrid maize and other crop varieties since its inception. But of late, KARI has taken to openly experimenting with GM seed varieties of different crops brought in by Monsanto and other companies.
Recently, KARI announced that it was about to release into the market a GM maize variety that is resistant to stem borer. The latter is a pest estimated to cause losses of up to 15 per cent of the entire Kenyan maize crop.
Besides GM maize, KARI has also been engaged in open trials of sweet potatoes, cotton and sorghum varieties in Kakamega in Western Kenya, Embu in Eastern Kenya and other places. This has been taking place even though the country has yet to put in place legal checks and balances to regulate GM products.
There are fears that through wind action, the trials could end up contaminating hybrid varieties of maize in areas where KARI has been doing the trials.
This fear is reinforced by recent reports from Mexico that the government there has announced that the DNA of some wild varieties of maize in areas close to where GM maize is grown had been found by researchers to possess GM traits.
But Dr Kedera ruled out the contamination of hybrid maize varieties by GM traits, saying Kephis has insisted that the maize seedlings under test should not be allowed to flower and that all trial materials be destroyed to avoid any possibility of contamination. He told The EastAfrican that Kephis had to order the termination of trials that had been going on in Kiboko in Eastern Kenya as the scientists involved "had not followed the laid down regulations."
Genetic engineering is defined as the deliberate alteration of plants' or animals' genetic makeup for purposes of doing away with unwanted traits or to produce traits that are desirable to biotech researchers. Such engineering has the potential of raising productivity and helping the plant cope with environmental or pest hazards.
In Kenya, the trials are conducted for the purpose of producing crops resistant to striga (witch weed), grain and stem borer and other pests.
Many of the genetically modified versions on trial are said to be "armed" with the capacity to withstand such pests or herbicides that kill striga and other weeds.
While many scientists see nothing wrong with genetic modification, there are those who say that science has not fully grasped the implications of injecting "salien" genes into edible plants and animals and prefer to approach the matter with caution. Some, especially anti-GM activists, see these experiments on "Frankenfoods" as deeply evil and dangerous.
GM maize was first raised in 1997 in the US and Canada and has since spread to about 21 million hectares with over 50 per cent of the maize produced in the US being modified to resist insects/pests or to tolerate herbicides.

Original_Search_Title: Life Before the Polythene Bag
Headline: Uganda: Life Before the Polythene Bag
Body_Text:
Kampala — For many Ugandans it is hard to imagine what life will be like, following the ban on certain types of buveera (polythene bags). In Kireka, a Kampala suburb, bachelors are wondering what they will take their Rolex home in. I mean, what is a Rolex without ebigenderako, wrapped up in a transparent little piece of environmentally unfriendly plastic? Obviously the people who make the budget decisions are not bawuulu (bachelors).
It is difficult to imagine a trip to the shops or the market that does not involve kaveera.
The question on the way forward leads one to ask: what did we do before the kaveera came along and revolutionised our worlds?
Banana leaves
Back in the day, banana leaves were not only confined to the steaming of matooke. I remember buying kabalagala wrapped neatly in a banana leaf. Sometimes they passed the leaves over a fire, so that they would make softer and more flexible packaging. In many places, banana leaves were the preferred packaging for meat as well.
Kikapu
Which housewife did not possess a couple of sturdy bikapu (shopping baskets woven out of palm fronds) for the market days?
You carried your kikapu along and filled it up as you went along, the harder and possibly dirtier sweet potatoes and cassava going at the bottom and the lighter or more vulnerable purchases, like tomatoes, at the top.
Once upon a couple of decades ago, excited boarding students would line up at the school gate, to see whose parents lugged in the biggest, or the largest number of bikapu.
Today a student would rather starve, than receive a kikapu-brandishing Mzee; however appealing the contents might be. It is no longer "cool".
Gunny bags
These were sacks known locally as kutiya, and in Swahili as guniya. They were made out of sisal. Sisal was also used to make kiondo - a type of basket which Kikuyu women slung over their foreheads. Gunny bags were really handy among rural folk, who used them as travelling bags, suitcases, curtains, mattress covers, and even garments.
Paper bags
These khaki-coloured paper packets, made by a paper factory in Jinja were widely used back then. Flour, salt, powdered milk and most especially sugar were sold in them.
Paper
Where would the world be without newspapers? Even back then, smaller purchases were wrapped in paper - most especially old newspaper. Used stationery paper, mainly from government offices, was also very handy.
Instead of disposing of them in the incinerator, office cleaners would smuggle out old documents and sell them in downtown Kampala. That is how confidential government files ended up in Owino Market.
There was also a type of oil-absorbent white paper, but that was rare and only found in really upmarket places. I bought lots of colourful 'ball gum', simsim balls and Patco sweets neatly wrapped in paper. Though that popular myth about how simsim balls are made almost cured me of my addiction.
I recall with nostalgia the plastic paper used to wrap Tip Top Bread. It was quite handy for covering our primary school exercise books, and its bright orange, blue and lime-green patterns (for sweet, sliced and salty respectively) were quite eye-catching.
PIL
Then, a sturdy polythene bag called PIL started making its way into our hands and our homes.
They were large, rectangular and mostly came with a calendar printed on both sides. Even handier were the plastic handles with a clasp that could close the bag.
Then the distributors unleashed a PIL kaveera with not only a calendar, but the colours of the Uganda flag as well. Slowly bikapu were replaced with the trendier, easy-to-carry kaveera. It wasn't long before other buveera entered the market, and buveera became less sturdy, more disposable, cheaper and thinner.

Original_Search_Title: Country Now Kenya's Leading Food Basket
Headline: Uganda: Country Now Kenya's Leading Food Basket
Body_Text:
Kampala — Ugandans and Kenyans have diminished border restrictions and now enjoy mutual informal trade that has seen Uganda emerge as Kenya's leading food basket.
Kenya reciprocates by being the leading exporter of manufactured products to Uganda.
According to the Informal Cross Border Trade Survey Report (ICBT) 2006 released in Kampala on July 4, agriculture accounts for 80 per cent of our informal exports to Kenya.
The report was compiled by the Uganda Revenue Authority, Bank of Uganda and Uganda Bureau of Statistics (Ubos).
Speaking at the launch of the report, Mr Male Mukasa, the Executive Director of Ubos said: "ICBT survey generates valuable information that investors and policy makers will find useful for trade policy formulation," Mr Mukasa said.
The report shows that Uganda exported goods worth $96.9 million (Shs156.9 billion) to Kenya of which $75 million (Shs121.5 billion) was from agricultural produce. Uganda's informal imports from Kenya are estimated at $63.9 million (Shs103.5 billion) of which $55.8 million (Shs90.3 billion) goes to manufactured commodities.
Maize, sorghum, millet, peas, beans, bananas form the bulk of goods exported to Kenya, while industrial products such as polythene bags, sugar, bread, and wheat move in the opposite direction.
The survey was carried out at border posts of five neighbouring countries namely Sudan, Kenya, Rwanda, Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC), and Tanzania.
Uganda, in general, informally exported goods worth $231.7 million (Shs375.3 billion) and imported goods worth $80.6 (Shs130.5 billion).
The country, therefore, registered a trade surplus of $151.1 (Shs244.7 billion), underlining the importance of cross-border trade to Uganda's economy. Recorded official exports, on the hand, are valued at $962.1 million (Shs1.56 trillion) while imports are worth $2,557.3 million Shs4.14 trillion, representing a deficit of $1,595.2 million (Shs2.5 trillion).
Kenya took the highest market share for both informal imports and export, followed by DRC, Tanzania, Rwanda, and Sudan.
Although Kenya and Uganda are members of the East African Community that is currently working toward a political federation, there are still boarder restrictions that oftentimes hamper free movement of goods across countries in the region.
In towns of Busia and Malaba, "the market days of Wednesdays and Saturdays register huge flows of goods from either side," the report reads.
"Informal traders engaged in agricultural produce hire cyclists to transport the produce across the border to avoid delays and cumbersome documentation at customs."
However, this segment of international trade has often been ignored in national accounts, according to the Bank of Uganda Director of Research Dr Asimwe Kihangire, despite its importance in influencing monetary policy. It is, for instance, estimated that there is 40 billion of Ugandan currency circulating in Southern Sudan
The survey is therefore meant to capture informal cross border trade, which ordinarily would not feature in the country's trade statistics.
According to the report, DRC is emerging as Uganda's next most important trading partner.
While exports to Kenya fell from $107 million in 2005 (Shs173.3 billion) to $96.9 million (Shs156.9 billion) in 2006, Exports to DRC increased from $73.9 (Shs119.7 billion) in 2005 to $80.5 (Shs130.4 billion) in 2006.
In contrast to Kenya's scenario, Uganda mainly exports industrial products to DRC valued at $58.0 million (Shs93.9 billion) but imports more agricultural products such as palm oil and cassava.
Trade with Rwanda more than tripled between 2005 and 2006, while trade with Tanzania also registering a dramatic increase.

Original_Search_Title: Eat, Drink But Avoid the Loos
Headline: Uganda: Eat, Drink But Avoid the Loos
Body_Text:
Kampala — STENCH in the toilets? Explain. Never before, have I smelt such stale urine in my life! The concentration is so strong that you may think that you just walked into an ammonia manufacturing plant.
Oh dear! I see we have started off on the wrong foot then? Anyway, take us through the place.
Basaliza Pub is about 10 minutes out of Fort Portal on Kampala Road. The signpost is not clearly visible but if you time your arrival for about four in the afternoon, when you see a good number of cars parked on the roadside (on the left), that is the place. And Basaliza Pub does not stand out that much. It looks like any pork kafunda that one would find in Ntinda, Wandegeya or Nakulabye.
Some bufunda look tacky on the outside, but once you walk in, they amaze. Does Basaliza Pub amaze on the inside?
It depends on the definition of the word amaze. They have a pool table at the entrance and beyond that, sofa-style seating. At the very rear of the pub by the television set hangs some netting that closes off the executive wing. Basaliza Pub does not have any walls but pillars which support the iron sheet roof. It is, therefore, possible to see right through the rotunda bar and the kitchen beyond it.
Basaliza Pub has to be the first pub that I know of that has an executive wing! Do go on.
Unless you can speak Rutooro, communication with the staff can be a problem, especially when it comes to ordering pork. To get out of our situation, we headed for the kitchen, picked the sticks we wanted and settled down to a beer as we waited to be served.
When the pork eventually arrived, was it to your liking?
This is where Basaliza Pub showed some signs of improvement. When the pork arrived with all its dressings (tomatoes, avocado and cassava) it looked good. And good enough to get the mouth watering. While not on the same level as say that of Zanzi's it is a must-try for the visitor to Fort Portal.
THE GOOD POINTS:
The pork.
THE BAD POINTS:
See 'The Toilets'.
THE PRICES:
Beers are at sh1,800 and pork at 1,500 a stick.
THE TOILETS:
The stench is so strong. As soon as I put my head round the corner, the smell of last year's stagnant urine literally killed my nose. The option? To go to the bush! For the ladies it's a case of holding one's bladder until you get to some place decent.
THE CROWD:
On the day we were there, it was full of locals though we did see Martin Sebuliba of Celtel there.
THE CROWD:
"Is there a chance that King Oyo might pop in for a rib or two?" and "How do I know which Amooti to call when they are four of them with the same name?"
*BASALIZA PUB is located in Fort Portal on Kampala Road. Open all week from mid-morning till late. Reservations: 0772 997 337, 04834 25063.

Original_Search_Title: Nso Fon Exhorts Elderly to Be Self-Reliant
Headline: Cameroon: Nso Fon Exhorts Elderly to Be Self-Reliant
Body_Text:
Overwhelmed by the successes registered by the Community Development Volunteers for Technical Assistance, CDVTA, that helps the elderly find new ways of earning a living, His Majesty, Fon Sehm M binglo of Nso, has appealed to the elderly people of his Fondom to close ranks with CDVTA so as to become self-reliant.
Welcoming the Director of CDVTA, Francis Njuakom Nchii, and some of his staff, who were in Kumbo, recently, to install the Kumbo Chapter of the organisation, Fon Mbinglo told the elderly that only lazy elderly people are still looking up to their children for micro assistance to sustain their lives at old age.
Fon Mbinglo told close to 200 old people that there is life in abundance at old age and cautioned them that those who want to die too soon are those who sit and keep banking on their siblings.
According to him, most terminal diseases such as heart attack and hypertension are caused by the neglect of the old by their children who are living in affluence in towns. He said he was quite satisfied with the activities of CDVTA in fighting poverty and had to apply for their services to be implanted in Kumbo Central.
"If you do not want to go to your grave early, group yourselves so that you can be easily assisted when the project takes off," Mbinglo advised.The Director of CDVTA explained to the elderly in Nso that his organisation will help improve their lives by giving them something to wake up to daily.
He said CDVTA has one of the leading organisations in the area of the elderly with excellent elderly projects in the villages of Anyajua in Boyo Division, Oshie in Momo and Limbo in Bui Division.
Njuakom said they have donated blankets, warm clothes, bush lamps, farm seeds of maize, beans, potatoes, cassava, yams, groundnuts and onions amongst others, to needy populations.He said the Oshie old people have FCFA 400.000 in their credit union accounts thanks to CDVTA.
Bui Divisional Delegate of Social Affairs, Emmanuel Cheneboh, while acknowledging the complementing role CDVTA is playing in alleviating poverty, said the elderly in Kumbo are not poor.On behalf of Nso Development Association- NSODA, the Secretary General, Blasius Nformi, promised full support and urged the CDVTA to go beyond Kumbo.

Original_Search_Title: Minister Thrilled By Leap in Agricultural Production
Headline: Nigeria: Minister Thrilled By Leap in Agricultural Production
Body_Text:
Lagos — Agriculture and Water Resources Minister Adamu Bello has described 2006, as a year of agricultural renewal for the country.
Speaking in Abuja at the 2006 ministerial press briefing. Bello said the sector received global boost by playing host to many national and international conferences and summits in the year.
He said, continued implementation of various Presidential initiatives, notably rice, cassava, vegetable oil, cocoa, cotton, livestocks and tropical fruits, contributed to the renewal.
Giving the over view of the initiatives, vBello said the demand for Nigeria cassava, vhas increased the production appreciably.
He said the production capacity in 1999 was 32.69 million metric tonnes before the cassava initiative.
By 2006, the production increased to 50 million metric tonnes, adding that government was determined to increase the production with the target earning of five billion dollars from exports in seven years.
Government, he added, set a target production of 150 million metric tonnes of cassava per annum by the end of 2010.
Bello said that as at 2006, annual production of cassava tubers had increased from 33 million metric tonnes in 1999 to 49 million metric tonnes in 2006.
Within the period, some 43 newly-improved cassava varieties were produced.
The Minister said a committee was put in place to restore, improve and revive cocoa production in the country.
He said within a short time, the committee revived cocoa production, which increased from 170,000 metric tonnes in 1999 to 500 million metric tonnes in 2006.
Bello said the demand for the nation's maize was high, with a projected estimate of 14 million metric tonnes.
He put current production of the commodity at seven million metric tonnes, stating that to increase the current level, a committee was put in place last year to work out programmes that would lead to the doubling of maize production by 25 per cent.
Bello also said to achieve the target, over 5,000 metric tonnes of improved maize
seeds and 60,000 litres of agro-chemicals had been produced for distributions to farmers.
He said: "As part of efforts to boost cotton production, and revive all the textile industries, and in pursuit of the one million metric tonnes target, a Presidential committee was set up last year."
Bello said through federal government intervention, N50 billion was facilitated as textile rehabilitation fund to give the sector a major boost and revive the textile industries nationwide.
He said by the initiative, the output of cotton seed, had increased from 150,000 metric tonnes in 1999 to 350,000 metric tonnes last year.
The minister said more than 1,800 hectare was cultivated for varieties of cotton, adding that the cumulative effect of the various Presidential initiatives boosted
Nigeria's ranking as one of the leading producers of various commodities.

Original_Search_Title: Palm Oil Development and Youth Empowerment in Delta State
Headline: Nigeria: Palm Oil Development and Youth Empowerment in Delta State
Body_Text:
Lagos — Dr. Uduaghan has stated that his mission for Delta State is, "To use our human and natural resources to launch an era of rapid and sustainable social and economic development that will transform us into the most peaceful and industrialized state in Nigeria..." I am not a farmer, but I believe in bio-fuel and see its development as one of the strongest opportunities to achieve the above stated mission. This mission statement signals another welcome and critical initiative.
Developed and developing nations are rapidly adopting 'sustainable energy,' achieved in part by encouraging electrical plants to use bio-fuel. Bio-fuel is defined as any fuel with 80% minimum content by volume of materials derived from living organisms harvested within the ten years preceding its manufacture. Agricultural products specifically grown for use as bio-fuels include palm oil, cassava, sugar cane, corn, and soya beans etc. In a small number of instances, palm oil is used in place of diesel fuel, without further refinement. But as the United States and many European countries push for more green energy, they are increasingly importing palm oil from the tropics, since there is simply not enough plant matter for fuel production at home. The demand for palm oil in the United States and Europe has soared in the last two decades, first for use in food and cosmetics, and more recently, for fuel. This versatile and cheap oil is used in about 10 per cent of supermarket products, from chocolate to toothpaste, accounting for 21 per cent of the global market for edible oils.
Enamored by government subsidies, energy companies in the developed nations have actually designed generators that run exclusively on palm oil, which in theory, would be cleaner than fossil fuels like coal because it is derived from plants.
The emerging market in bio-fuel is another opportunity to transform Delta State into a more peaceful and industrialized state. Delta State has one of the largest untapped palm oil potentials in the world. The export of palm kernels began in 1832 and by 1911, British West Africa alone exported 157,000 tonnes of which about 75 per cent came from Nigeria - the region encompassing Delta State being the major producer. As of today, palm oil production in our state has been ignored by the relentless focus on crude oil. The total output has fallen drastically, denying the state billions of dollars in potential revenue. Palm trees proliferate throughout the state with well known production centers in such towns as Ajagbodudu, Agbarho, and Otegbo, for example. The revitalization of this production sector is, therefore, well within reach.
One of the problems facing the Niger-Delta region today is youth restiveness. It is therefore incumbent on the Uduaghan administration to address this problem by developing and improving palm oil production as a means of empowering our youths and at the same time, achieving his mission of transforming Delta State into a more peaceful and industrialized state. At a symposium organized by the Concerned Deltans Inc., USA, at the Petroleum Training Institute Effurun, Delta State, about youth restiveness, the youth leaders in attendance stressed the need for vocational training in the area of farming among other trades. The past is clear enough and offers a framework for the future. There is the need for partnership among the state, business, labour, youth, and communal organizations, emphasizing human and technical development of palm oil production.
In conjunction with other vast natural resources of Delta State, the advancement of palm oil production will be of seminal importance to the overall development of our state. This initiative will drive revenue growth as a result of exports, increase employment for our youths, and generate electrical energy to power our industries.

Original_Search_Title: Bonna Bagaggawale is More About Votes
Headline: Uganda: Bonna Bagaggawale is More About Votes
Body_Text:
Kampala — VETERAN politician Jaberi Bidandi Ssali, the former local government minister, is busy with his new party, which he hopes will one day rule Uganda. Charles Etukuri interviewed him on political trends.
You formed a political party the People Progressive Party (PPP). How is it performing?
I did not form any political party. I have never formed one. After I left the National Resistance Movement (NRM) I joined the National Progressive Movement led by Dr. John Barya which later transformed into PPP. I was entrusted with the responsibility of being interim chairperson and we are busy now putting down roots for the party branches. In the next few months we should be able to convene a delegates' conference in accordance with our constitution, to elect the substantial leadership.
Why isn't PPP fielding candidates in by-elections?
Two reasons: the first being that we have not put in place party organs in accordance with our constitution. These organs have to sieve and approve candidates. So I have no way of initiating sponsorship in the by-elections. Secondly, we are not expected to reap where we did not sow. Until we know we have reached the ground and we are able to approach the electorate through the structures of our party, it would be unrealistic for us to say that because there is a by-election we must participate.
But then you have not even supported your colleagues in the opposition who have fronted candidates.
It's our principle that as long as the PPP has not sponsored any candidate, any member of PPP is free to vote for a candidate of his or her choice.
What is the future of the PPP?
First of all, we are focused and registered. We have aims and objectives written down, we have a constitution and our own distinctive approach which is: Let us prepare the guns for 2011. Our guns are the votes and who holds the votes? The people. That is where our focus is.
Do you hope to head this country or take on the incumbent come 2011?
We are building a party and a party has a procedure of sponsoring a candidate. If by 2011, the need arises to sponsor a candidate, I will look at who among us in the party is interested and I will definitely offer myself to the party. There is no question about it.
Is the multiparty dispensation on the right track?
I think it is. It is wrong to assume that after so many years of the absence of political parties, one year after their re-introduction they should be run and organised at the ideal level. No. It will take time to build the institutions within each party. It will take time before the Government accepts that political parties are not enemies or destructive opponents, but part of the system that helps a good government.
We saw the re-activation of the G6 - the coalition that brings together six political parties - over the issue of the proposed Mabira giveaway. Do you see this becoming a more common occurrence in the future?
You see parties coalesce to achieve specific interests or targets. For example, those six had a common interest and had a common expression on their dissatisfaction with the way the President handled the Mabira issue. In future they could choose to coalesce over something else. It could include other parties or it could be less than the six. It depends on the particular interest or issue.
Under our circumstances, where the central government wants to remain in power at all costs, then the idea of a coalition can't be ruled out.
Why didn't PPP join other parties over Mabira?
First of all, we are a distinctive party and came out with our individual view. If we had kept quiet or condemned those who came out in the way they came out, then we would have been questioned. The other parties came out in their own way. We don't agree with the approach they took, but we came out, expressed our view, wrote to the President and he responded to us in writing.
Your colleagues demonstrated and engaged the Police in running battles. Do you find that a disagreeable approach?
You see our view is that it is a democratic right for either a political party or any group of civil society to demonstrate on the streets within the parameters of the law. But when it comes to violence, then the PPP becomes very reluctant to become party to that approach because one of our very important aims or principles is adopting a non-confrontational policy in trying to achieve what we want to achieve.
Some people say that the introduction of multiparty politics has affected the smooth running of the local governments.
I believe that where we have a proper operation of the multiparty system, it really should not affect the structure of the local government. After all, at the time when I was in the ministry after the introduction of multipartyism, we had districts where the chairpersons were in the opposition, but the system worked. All that matters is following the law governing the local government in the country.
In your youth you were a member of the Uganda People's Congress (UPC) youth wing. With the re-introduction of multiparty politics, we have seen similar wings come up, but they are becoming too militant. What is your take on them?
Political parties at the moment have youth groups. They don't have well-organised, structured youth organisations, and I think that as we move on they will definitely be organised to the level that we were organised.
Some of us were leaders in these organisations, but they were organisations which were first of all provided for in the party constitution and organised according to it. Today no party is so organised as to say that it has a distinctive structure of the youth wing. One hopes that as we move on, this will eventually come out.
What we have now are youth groups - some call them brigades - not emanating from an electoral process right from the grassroot level to the top leadership.
Bonna bagaggawale (wealth-for-all scheme) is taking shape. What is your opinion of it?
It is a political gimmick. How can you expect every citizen of the country to get rich? The Government comes out with what appears to be a popular policy, not necessarily for the sake of developing the people concerned, but to capture their votes, and you may find that it will take a bit of time before this money is released.
What are the flaws in the programme?
Personally I don't think they are focused. The other day the President was saying it is not like entandikwa (start-up funding) which people received hoping that they were being thanked for voting for him. But if somebody fails to pay money advanced to him (under bonna bagaggawale), are you going to sell his kibanja? If there is no mortgage in the form of kibanja, how are you going to ensure that they pay?
If you lend a common person so much money deep in the countryside, for example for farming purposes, how are you sure that in the first instance the money will be directed to the activity it was meant for? How do you make sure that even after growing cassava and maize the market will be there and that he will have enough produce to sell and pay you and retain enough to make him rich.
The idea is okay; helping the ordinary person and introducing reasonable credit to the lower level of society, but the focus is not really to help them but make NRM mobilise for votes.
If the PPP was in power, how differently would you have handled this programme?
The PPP has got to discuss, but I have my personal view which I want to sell to the organs of the party when they are formed. It is that our approach to fighting poverty should stem from an approach which says: Let everybody in the country start fighting poverty from where he or she is and with whatever he or she has.
That is where it should start and then how does the Government come in? It comes in to say alright, if County X is mostly groundnut, bean or maize-growing, then this money you want to dish out to individuals should be used as a catalyst to sustain the policy you are trying to use to help the people.
What do you mean?
For instance, the people of Kaberamaido grow mostly groundnuts. So I would say that for Kaberamaido, the Government has offered sh200m to be borrowed, at a very low interest rate, by any trader who wants to use it for purchasing groundnuts from Kaberamaido.
I am very sure that the money would not lie idle. Traders would come and borrow the money.
In the first season, the whole amount may not be used because of the limited number of sacks produced, but the following season if a farmer found what was grown last season sold out, he would now seek to double the area he used and the one who did not plant groundnuts would be persuaded to do likewise because the person who cultivated and sold got money.
One of the most important aspects of agriculture here, especially why people have given up hope in this direction, is because they go to their gardens, grow produce, harvest it and then they have no market for it.
The most important thing is for the Government to create market for whatever is grown in these areas. As a starting point you let the society take the initiative to produce and you bring in the money and the assistance to make sure that whatever is produced is purchased.
That is the sort of approach I would think is more sustainable and it is not something to be achieved in two or three years. It is a programme that should cover a number of years.
During the riots over Mabira we saw rioters targeting Indians. Do you think there are underlying tensions between Ugandans and Indians?
I think it was just a coincidence that on the day of the strike the driver who hit the band members was an Asian and whoever was buying Mabira, happened to be somebody of Asian origin. People were demonstrating against Mehta and here was an Asian who had knocked down people and then there was another unfortunate Indian who was riding his motorcycle through the crowd after the accident. Mob justice is what applied there and I don't think these people moved from their houses to go to the streets to demonstrate against the Asians. It was just a coincidence and I don't what to look at it as a racial issue. In fact, for us we said that the incident should open the eyes of the leadership of this country.
How?
The way they are governing this country, they way they are condoning corruption and nepotism in future could trigger a situation like that one which turns the focus on race to tribe, ethnicity and so forth.
It should be an eye-opener to this government that whatever policy they put in place, especially with regard to the provision of services and also with regard to the al__cpLocation of resources, should not reflect any of the aspects like tribalism, nepotism and corruption.
Do you share the opinion of the Forum for Democratic Change that the Government policy of favouring the Indian investors over local investors could have triggered the racial sentiments?
I don't know. I think there are other factors other than the Government preferring or giving special treatment to Asians as opposed to the other investors.
You have been an active player in the politics of this country for over a half a century. How would you like to be remembered?
I want to be remembered as a politician who used the office of leadership to serve the people with sincerity, commitment and with no pecuniary interests.

Original_Search_Title: Guests in Accra
Headline: Ghana: Guests in Accra
Body_Text:
Kampala — A traveller's ability to communicate with the locals at his destination is important, as I learnt recently on a short stay in Accra, Ghana. Boiling Ghana, I should say, but that will come later.
We arrived at Kotoka International Airport at about 3:00pm. The airport is named after a military general who shot dead Kwame Nkrumah, Ghana's first president and father of the nation. Kotoka himself was later assassinated in what you can call a palace coup.
We were driven to the out-of-town Earlbeam Hotel. We checked in and washed off the sweat gathered between disembarking from the bus and entering the hotel (gosh, it is hot and humid in Ghana!) and then, a Kenyan man I had met on the plane, and I decided to go see the town.
We did the best to make some sense of the Twi-influenced English of the hotel staff, asking them about the features to look out for in the neighbourhood.
"Look out for the Achimota Overhead and the Kwame Nkrumah Sseko," a manager told us. We wanted to ask what the overhead and the sseko were, but he had turned to other matters.
Outside the hotel, we asked another man what the overhead was. He said that's what it was, an overhead. He said if we boarded a taxi, we would not miss it. And the sseko?
He didn't have another word, but with an exaggerated swing of the hand, he made it clear what it was: a roundabout. They are not called roundabouts there; they're called circles.
To board the taxis, you need the Ghanaian cedi, and we had American dollars on us, so went to a nearby forex bureau.
When the lady at the till gave the man ahead of us his money, he fished out a kaveera (polythene bag) and packed the cedis into it. I thought: this guy must be going to buy a car or a house.
You think the Ugandan shilling is weak? For a dollar, Ghanaians pay 9,200 cedis! So when I asked the lady to change $20 for me, she gave me more money than could fit in all my pockets.
Ghanaians keep right on the road, and for someone who has grown up in East Africa where everybody keeps left, you could be knocked down as you cross the road. Instinct tells you to look left, right, and then left again.
Reality, however, has many huge taxis and other vehicles coming at you at top speed. But cross the dual carriageway we managed to, and boarded a taxi to the overhead. What is this overhead, we kept wondering.
We should not have bothered our heads, because when we saw it, we didn't need to ask: it was a flyover. Not the flyovers like the ones being built over the Northern Bypass, my dear.
These are giant works in concrete, multi-lane roads above roads, casting a deep shadow under them enough to require lighting, day and night.
Business never stops at the Achimota Overhead, and one of the items on sale there are used transporter saloon taxis, the type commonly called special hire in Kampala.
The majority of these taxis are Opels, sprayed in maroon, yellow and black.
The conventional commuter taxis, however, are big estate Benzes, whose staff are mostly young men the majority of whom are no older than.
The conductors travel with their trunks sticking out of the car, and immediately they see a catch, they shout a Twi phrase to the driver, who steps on the brakes, swerves off the road and pulls up the handbrake, all at a go. It is a hair-raising experience travelling in those taxis.
The average Ghanaian owns a better and bigger car than the rich Ugandan. Everywhere you go, you see giant Benzes, Fords, Cherokees, Chevrolets, BMWs, Volkswagens, Hummers and a sprinkling of Pajeros and Prados.
After taking a few pictures at the overhead, we boarded another taxi, to the Kwame Nkrumah Sseko. This is where the real life was.
Life at the circle is a beehive of activity.
Taxi touts literally try to drag potential travellers into the cars. Young girls balancing trays of fish deep-fried in okra oil, buns, banku (balls of mingled cassava flour) and oranges dash here and there.
Hawkers vending mobile phones, clothes, watches and other wares traverse the place. Idlers while the time away, doing what they do best: nothing. Travellers passing through the town cast a hasty glance here and there, before shooting across the street at the speed of light. You need to be alert, to make it anywhere.
I was famished by that time, and told Joe, my Kenyan friend, as much. He suggested we look for a not-so-posh place to taste what the ordinary Ghanaian eats as s/he struggles to make it through the day.
We found one such place and placed our orders: two balls of banku each, with fish in okra oil soup. When the food was brought, I realised I should have been less adventurous.
The sauce was pure pepper in pure oil, with a chunk of fish sitting in there.
But hunger has its own power, so we struggled with the piripiri and oil, and when we were done, each of us was dying for a drink. I asked for soda.
"Soda? We don't have it," the waiter said. But in his fridge were all brands of Coca-Cola and Pepsi products. I said: "But you do, it's there!" That's when Joe said: "In West Africa they don't know soda.
That's an American term! They either call each brand by its name, or you ask for a soft drink."
So I asked for a Fanta, to fight the burning sensation of the piripiri.
Eating done, we went out to take pictures.
And take pictures we did. We took pictures of the phone (mainly Nokia, Samsung and Motorola) kiosks by the roadside; of posters of the Black Stars, the national soccer team on sale by the roadside; of double-decker buses snaking their way through the hold-up; of long lines of the Benz taxis, parked and waiting for passengers; of women selling bangles, ropes, wire gauze and other such wares, as well as foodstuffs like deep-fried fish, yams and roast meat; of people walking, lounging and wooing each other on the overhead overhanging the circle... We took pictures.
Zooming in and out; peering and bending; standing on tiptoe and moving backwards. I was thus engaged when a hand tapped my shoulder. I turned, to see a young man of about 26 or so, in a blue uniform not unlike the kind worn by most private security organs here in Kampala, only his was rather threadbare.
"May I see your permit?" the guy asked. My heart sank like it was tied to a 50kg-weight. In an instant I was surrounded by four of these guys, and they were towing and shoving me, telling me I either show them the permit to take pictures of Ghanaians, or they take me to The Station.
Joe was also surrounded, and the two of us were led down the overhead, to a waiting Police car near the sseko. There, we halted, trying to plead our case. We said where we come from, people may take pictures of open places like roundabouts (what are roundabouts?
Circles... sorry, ssekos) and other such places. Only gazetted places are illegal, but these are clearly marked as such. Where do you come from? East Africa. Oh-oh! So you take our pictures, to go and show the world there that Ghanaian police is lousy?
That was their problem, that we had taken pictures of the Police without their permission. But we had not. I had taken pictures of the sseko, the fountain therein, the Ghana Commercial Bank towers behind it, and, there in the background of one of the pictures (as I later discovered), was the police car, looking like a dot.
So close to an hour later, after we had argued back and forth, us quoting the Ugandan and Kenyan law, and them insisting Ghana is a free country, where the legal system would release us if they found us innocent, the leader asked us to step aside.
By this time, the cameras had been grabbed and hidden in the little deep-blue Opel saloon.
"The problem, my brothers," he said calmly for the first time, "is that the Police here are serious. You can't take a picture of me without my permission. If you ask, I say yes.
If you don't, trouble." He then asked me to delete all the pictures, and Joe to tear the film and give him the offending pieces. Then we were released.
Day One over.
Another day Joe and I took a stroll to the beach. As we walked, we saw men and women stop, step aside and pee. Yes, pee on the streets, without a single symptom of shame or fear. And the women don't even bend or squat!
They hold the panty aside and let out the gush! Other pedestrians step aside and go on their way.
But that was nothing. At the beach, we found a line of men squatting in the sand, apparently watching the waves come and go. We were wrong.
As more men came and passed the squatting squad, they went a few metres to the left and, letting down their pants, they too squatted and - oh God, how do I say this without feeling what should have been their shame? - and they did potty!
There, on the beach. So when the waves come, they sweep away what should have been proof of really nasty manners.
And they know it's wrong, too. For while Joe was still taking pictures, a teenage urchin came upon us, asking who had allowed us to take pictures "of our dirty beach". He actually asked Joe to cut the film and hand it over to him, or else he would call the Police. We knew the story, so we did not wait.
But an urchin? Man!
Then there is the city, Accra. Accra is divided into two amazing parts. On the western side is the business area, very like our Nabugabo, Kisenyi or Market Street areas.
Tall buildings interspersed with two-to-three-storeyed structures, sandwiching narrow, filthy, crowded streets, where all manner of wares are sold.
Taxis, private cars, trucks and buses jostle for the space, creating jams the size of infinity. The noise is prohibitive, as are the prices of the simplest of the wares. Even with the inflation in the country, a simple shirt going for 150,000 cedis is the upper limit! We refused to ask about the decent ones.
Then on the eastern side of the city, you find the other twin, who looks like the rich neighbour's son. This is where you find financial institutions, the High Court, the Kwame Nkrumah Museum, foreign embassies and government ministries and departments.
Boy, is this side of town beautiful. Gorgeous buildings, neat grounds, no crowds, a serene environment. But the grandmother of them all in beauty is the Independence Square, still under construction.
It is a sprawling place, sitting on maybe four square acres, with stadium-like stands and pavilions scattered here and there.
The podium, on which the president will be standing to address Ghana, is something that confounds attempts to describe it. I wanted to reach for my camera to take pictures, but saw the seemingly inattentive sentries and remembered the Sseko incident, so I just let the fingers caress the camera.
Joe did not even reach for his bag where his giant Pentax camera was. He is much older and senior in these things, you know.
Ghanaians are very patriotic people. The fact that they just celebrated 50 years of independence makes it even more pronounced.
Ordinary Ghanaians have flags on or in their cars; they fly flags in their compounds; they speak of John Kufuor, the president, the way Balokole speak of Jesus, and they think the sun rises in Ghana and then shines on the rest of the world.
Talking about Balokole, every street corner has a church structure, and the structures are as flamboyantly built as the people behave.
The most dominant church in Accra seems to be the Winners' Chapel, which has branches almost everywhere. And the pastors - Ghanaian pastors are small gods and goddesses.
They wield plenty of power and respect, and people talk about them with awe.
Finally (what better place to talk about this than at the very end of the story) are the coffins.
Ghanaians have style, and this shows even in their coffin designs. When a man who loved his bottle passes on, they buy him a coffin in the shape of what kept him busy all his life - the bottle.
They also have coffins shaped like aircraft for frequent 'fliers', shoe shapes, heart-shaped coffins and the conventional shape for people who lived plain lives. Ghana, a place you can't bury without a sounding eulogy.

Original_Search_Title: On President Kabbah's Achievements
Headline: Sierra Leone: On President Kabbah's Achievements
Body_Text:
Freetown — Towards the end of June 2006 the President, Alhaji Dr Ahmad Tejan Kabbah, delivered his last annual address at the state opening of parliament to an audience that included senior members of government, the police and the military, members of the diplomatic corps, the chief justice and high judges, the Mayor of Freetown, and members of the press and clergy.
The annual address is to be his last, given that elections are to be held this year and parliament will be dissolved before then. President Kabbah used the occasion to elaborate on his views of the government's major achievements over the past ten years. They include the end of the civil war and the re-establishment of constitutional authority over the whole of the country, as well as strengthened border security.
Immediately after the cessation of hostilities, the programme for the disarmament, demobilisation and reintegration of ex-soldiers and the repatriation of refugees; the holding of a number of free and fair elections since 1996; the promotion of freedom and growth of the media; post-war economic recovery, underpinned by the restoration of macro-economic stability and the implementation of economic reforms; the achievement of a food-security rate of 69% by 2006; refurbished health centres and schools; and the introduction of fiscal reforms and the establishment of the Anti-Corruption Commission were established, pursued with reasonable attempt to sustained.
The International Monetary Fund's take on the issue under review is even clearer. In its 2006-07 fiscal policy report summary on Sierra Leone, IMF observed that Sierra Leone's fiscal policy is expected to focus increasingly on boosting revenue, because continued efforts to improve fiscal discipline will be expected by donors.
The institution also predicted sustainable tide in economic and social growth. This is against the backdrop that fiscal revenue is expected to rise over the forecast period as tax revenue increases in line with the establishment of more businesses; issuances of mining and other licences; management of the National Revenue Authority and greater imports and exports to contribute more to customs levies.
There were, however, some challenges the Kabbah government faced. For instance the country's porous borders and the associated loss of revenue through the smuggling of diamonds and other minerals initially limited total revenue growth. For this reason the government, having suspected that there would be continued pressure to increase spending, especially given the huge scale of post-war rehabilitation and rebuilding work as well as the vast level of demand for social spending was concerned, embarked on serious institutional and policy reviews.
It was under this apparent economic policy review that the state fund for the aged and vulnerable was launched. In June President Kabbah launched a Le5.3bn (US$1.8m) Social Safety Net Scheme; a fund that is meant to assist the poor and vulnerable groups, particularly those who are old and incapacitated. More specifically, the beneficiaries are reported to comprise the disabled, widows and widowers, the aged and children up to 15 years who have been orphaned or separated from their parents by the civil war.
This was significantly reflected in IMF's outlook for 2006-07 policy trends of the country. Thus the justification for IMF to approve a new three-year, US$46.3m poverty reduction and growth facility (PRGF) in June 2006 to support the government's 2006-08 economic programme. It also happened that the previous PRGF expired in June 2005. it was therefore evident that new PRGF was expected because the IMF appeared to be broadly satisfied in its final review of the last PRGF, and donors showed a strong commitment to the country's development with the pledge of US$800m at the November 2005 Consultative Group (CG) meeting.
Given that bolster, the reviewed government's economic policy came to aim at reducing poverty, tackling corruption, maintaining tight fiscal and monetary policy, building institutional capacity, and improving domestic revenue collection. Once this foundation was laid policies would support the three main priorities identified at the Consultative Group meeting: fostering good governance, creating employment and improving food security.
Although satisfied with the solid foundation so far created, the government knew that there were certain things remaining on track with a new PRGF for at least six months, together with the presentation of the new poverty reduction strategy paper (PRSP) in June 2005, to make Sierra Leone eligible to reach completion point under the IMF-World Bank's heavily indebted poor countries (HIPC) initiative.
With reference to the country's domestic economy, food production was said to have grown substantially. Data sourced from the Bank of Sierra Leone (BSL, the central bank) show that food production has risen substantially in Sierra Leone since the end of the civil war in January 2002. For instance production of rice, the staple food in Sierra Leone, has increased by around 80% since 2001, while that of maize has increased by 30%, groundnuts by over 200%, sweet potato by more than 300%, cassava by 600%, and coffee and cocoa by more than 1,000%.
This was a major breakthrough for a post war government given that agriculture, the mainstay of most of the rural population, was devastated by the civil war that took place between 1991 and 2002. The war lasted longest in the eastern borderlands, where cash crops such as coffee, cocoa and palm products are grown, while the spread of conflict to the north also affected the production of rice. You would also realize that by the time the war ended, much of the rural population was left dependent on food aid. This was a major setback.
However, there was a strong rebound in agriculture since then and this was due to a number of factors among which was the resettlement of refugees to their former homesteads. By mid-March 2004 the government estimated that 90% of Sierra Leoneans who fled the country during the civil war had returned home, and many more people are reported to have returned home since then. An estimated one million internally displaced people were also said to have been resettled at the time.
Perhaps most important a factor is the distribution of seeds, tools and other technological inputs via donor- and government-funded programmes. The cultivation of a new rice variety in Sierra Leone, under the New Rice for Africa Project (Nerica), which has a high yield and a short growing period was unprecedented. This has been a particularly significant development. This feat resulted in reduced demand for food imports, which fell by 30% between 2003 - when they peaked - and 2005.
Also a boost to the country's domestic economy is the commencement of bauxite and rutile shipments. The UK-based Titanium Resources Group (TRG), which owns both Sierra Mineral Holdings and Sierra Rutile, reported that exports of both bauxite and rutile have commenced during 2006. In the company's Trading and Operational Update in July 2006, shipments of 400,000 tonnes of bauxite and 15,500 tonnes of rutile are reported to have taken place to date.
The first shipment of 38,500 tonnes of bauxite from the Sierra Mineral Holdings mine took place in February, and the first shipment of 7,000 tonnes of rutile from the Sierra Rutile mine took place in May.
In its update, TRG stated that rutile shipments had been below the expected level of 20,000 tonnes and attributed this to a lack of sufficient equipment, but said that commissioning problems had now been resolved. The company also reported that it was increasing its rutile and bauxite exploration programmes, as well as its drilling capacity in line with this, and that it had secured additional exploration concessions in Sierra Leone, including the Rotifunk rutile deposit.
Again construction activity increased considerably in 2005. The establishment of peace and the high rents paid by a large international peacekeeping and humanitarian community triggered a mini construction boom in the capital, Freetown, and provincial towns.
According to the BSL, building permits issued for Freetown rose from 453 passed in 2003, to 667 passed in 2005, most of which were for residential buildings.
The central bank also noted that in 2005 growth in the construction industry was being fuelled by the construction of new roads, government administration buildings, schools, churches and mosques, as well as by the rehabilitation of roads and buildings that had been damaged in the civil war. Construction growth has, however, remained constrained by the government's lack of capacity to use all the donor funds that it receives for reconstruction and development programmes.
However, President Kabbah admitted that, despite these efforts, much more remained to be done. Some of the reason for this, according to him, was because the opposition was not proactive and innovative. What he meant was that the opposition was not supposed to be "dwelling inordinately and reactively on nitpicking on what the SLPP government says or does." But some critics dismissed this statement saying such a statement served not just as an attempt to give a positive assessment of his legacy, but to paint his party in a positive light ahead of the 2007 elections.
Whilst recognising some of the government's achievements, particularly with regard to the restoration of democracy, the opposition strongly criticised the government for not making significant inroads into fighting poverty, which remains at very high levels.
Numerous other shortcomings were also highlighted by the opposition, such as "continued problems with services, such as electricity and water supply; the frequent use of the 1965 Public Order Act, which the government uses to quell independent dissents; and slow progress in tackling corruption."

Original_Search_Title: 'We Deserve Landslide Victory'
Headline: Nigeria: 'We Deserve Landslide Victory'
Body_Text:
Lagos — Being governor for four years, you must have some experiences to share with us. It has been quite interesting and to me I see it as a challenge from God. I see that God has made it possible for me to scale through all those hurdles and trials up to present moment. I said it is a challenge because after all I'm just one person out of 3.3 million people in Kogi State, and God made it possible for me to be elected governor of the state. I believe that I have to do something to make sure that I actually did what God has sent me to do. That's the way I see it and I will not go against that confidence that the people of Kogi State have reposed in me. I believe I have to work very hard so that the people will be able to see that my coming has been a thing of joy and glory to them. I would say I enjoy it because of what I've passed through. Beside the experience I've got, my private sector experience has to a large extent helped me in administering the state. I truly thank Almighty Allah for the progress we have made since we came in 2003.
What would you say has been your biggest challenge as a governor?
One of the biggest challenges I think we faced as government is in fashioning out ways of bringing the three main ethnic groups in the state together. I think that was the first thing I worked on and I'm happy that at least everybody is now free to say whatever they want to say, they are free to discuss and interact with other ethnic groups. I'm talking about the Okuns from the West, the Igbiras from the Central and the Igalas from the East. We were able to bring them together to work harmoniously as members of the same family. I believe that is one good thing I will always live to remember and, you know, without peace there will be no development. So, that is the first thing and I'm happy we were able to put that in place - today where people sit down anywhere to discuss and interact without fear that somebody is watching me, or somebody wants to take my information somewhere. That has stopped since I came in.
What will say is your biggest achievement?
My biggest achievement, I believe, is in the education sector. We did marvellously very well, both in the primary and tertiary education. For instance, look at the University in Anyigba. If you notice what happened to the university, it was about to be closed when I came in. Out of 27 courses, we were denied accreditation in 21, you know what it is. But I'm happy that the Kogi State University is known as the best state and private university in Nigeria today. I think with that alone, I am convinced that I have done something. We will continue to be the leading university because I'm looking forward that until it is mentioned that Kogi State is the best in Nigeria, I will continue to work very hard, which I know we will get there with the rate we are moving now.
And again, we have done very well as far as roads construction, rehabilitation and maintenance is concerned. Virtually, every local government has two or three tarred roads now, which we didn't have before. Apart from that, we have the other urban roads being opened up so that our farmers can have access to the metropolitan cities. It will help them in terms of bringing their goods into the market which develops the economy. That's another area we have done very well. Above all, there is this human management which people seem not to see. It is an area that even if I'm no more the governor, people must always see to remember me - that is to make sure that workers have their salaries as and when due. We have been able to pay workers their leave bonuses and allowances, pay them their increment yearly, give them what they are supposed to have, for instance, car loan, motorcycle loan and so on. In short, making sure that you give them what they deserve. We have offered our workers some of the benefits they are suppose to get. I'm sure if we are able to give then what they deserve, they will be very happy to even do the work because I'm happy that the workers themselves are happy going to work. I remember when we came in, there was nothing like that, by 9.00am you see people, but around 10, 11am, you won't find anybody in the office. So, I continued to wonder what kind of life we were living. But now, you can see people remain in the office till the close of work, which shows that they are now happy doing what they are doing and of course, giving them what they are supposed to have. Before I came in, there was nothing like press, not even typewriters. For instance, there was a day the Ministry of Justice wanted to type a highly confidential material, but because they had no such facility, the officials went to a commercial place to do the work. It is unbelievable! I remembered I met this Mungo Park typewriter here, that one that makes a lot of noise as if one is at the railway station. But all these are no more. And I think it's one of the areas we have done very well.
Healthcare is one area that governments always battle with. What has your experience been in that and other areas?
Apart from the state hospital we are building here which I believe is going to be one of the best in Nigeria because we have all the equipment to make it what it should be, we have over about 25 new cottage hospitals, since I came in, with doctors, nurses and everything. And some of the cottage hospitals which have been are being modernised. Also some of the rural clinics have also been upgraded to cottage hospitals. I believe health is wealth. It is when you are well that you can start looking for other things. That's an area I concentrate more. And of course, in agriculture apart from the awards I've won, some other states may make a similar claim but I think Kogi State is the food basket of Nigeria now. Is it in rice production, cassava, millet? We hope by next year we start exporting them outside the country. We already have our customers ready, so we will start doing that because we have enough now. I bought over 1,700 tractors for farmers and in most cases we give some to cooperative societies free of charge, especially the youths because we want to encourage them. If you ask them to buy they will tell you there's no money, now take it free of charge and go and work and earn a living. I'm sure it will really encourage them and we can see what is coming out of it. So, it's something that we must continue to do because it will help us to boost agricultural production in the state. In addition, we bought tricycles for the farmers and also for commercial purpose. Most of these things are actually free of charge because we want to encourage people especially the youths. That is an area I really want to pay full concentration this time around because our youths are so many now, there is no family that doesn't have more than six, seven, eight youths sitting down doing nothing. And to me, I see it as a time bomb and we must start doing something now before it explodes. For me, in Kogi State, that is an area I'm going to focus seriously this time around. And I pray that God gives me the wisdom on how to go about it.
What are your chances like in the coming election?
I'm not God. God is the decider. But from the look of things I don't think I even need to campaign. We, the Peoples Democratic Party in Kogi have been involved in electioneering campaign to thank the people for their support in the last four years. All we have been doing everywhere we go is to thank the people and also ask them to vote for the PDP because we have better programmes to offer them. All we are doing is just thanking people for the support they have given my administration. So, I know it is going to be victory for PDP by God's grace.
What do you make of the challenge that is coming from AC and ANPPP in the state?
To me I've not seen any challenge, but then, it is supposed to be there. It is expected that other parties have to compete with PDP. I think that is in order because until we have such challenges, democracy that we are talking about, will not be interesting. So, I am enjoying every bit of that. You need people to really oppose you, people that can put you on your toes, you need an opponent that will make you stand up and work harder. But notwithstanding, I'm not relenting in my effort to ensure that victory is ours come April 14. What I am gunning for is a landslide victory, not half measures.

Original_Search_Title: Agricultural Grants, New Face of Social Responsibility
Headline: Nigeria: Agricultural Grants, New Face of Social Responsibility
Body_Text:
Lagos — For many years, Conocophillips had concentrated its social support efforts in the Lagos area, its primary place of operation. In Lagos it has for many years expended substantial financial support in the area of health and child education, particularly disadvantaged children. It provided infrastructure to schools, gave out grants to disadvantaged students, and supported efforts at eradication of HIV/AIDS.
However, the company recently reviewed its policy and decided to expand its social support efforts beyond Lagos. The search took it to Kaduna. As was later explained, it was not by happenstance that Kaduna was chosen. Managing Director of Conocophillips, Mr. J. Todd Creeger explained that the choice of Kaduna was principally because of the company's relationship with the Emir of Zazzau, Alhaji Shehu Idris who is a member of its advisory board.
"We have been looking for a way to expand outside the Lagos area since that is the place we operate from in Nigeria. We have a relationship with His Highness, the Emir of Zazzau and he has been talking to us about agricultural support project. In the last couple of years, we've been trying to put together a framework on how we can cooperate with them," Creeger explained to THISDAY.
The grant awards, which are a pilot project, are intended to support developmental growth to the recipient communities, in addition to providing the ability for them to improve their businesses. The grants were to support the cooperatives with the procurement of fertilizers, provide irrigations, buy new seeds and such other projects.
"It is not a refundable loan, it is a grant, there is no requirement that they pay back. What we would like to see them do with the money is to reinvest them and grow their businesses from small to big thriving businesses," Creeger stressed.
From the financial standpoint, each of the ten cooperative societies was given the sum of five hundred thousand naira (N500, 000.00) each. The beneficiaries included Hayin Mandare FCS, (Giwa LGC), Igabi MPCS (Igabi LGC), Ang/Makama Danalawan MPCS (Ikara LGC), Ang/Sariki Pambaguwa FCS (Kubau LGC) and Jaja Cassava FCS (Kubau LGC). Others are Gimi Gari MPCS (Makarfi LGC), Muchia II Women's MPCS (Sabon Gari LGC), Ang/Zakara FCS (Zaria I LGC), Tukur, Tukur (Youth) MPCS (Zaria II LGC) and Ung/Katuka (Zage Zagi) Women's MPCS (Zaria LGC).
What about the strategy put in place by the company to monitor compliance with the application of the money? Creeger explained that Conocophillips has put in place some requirements such that the District Heads will monitor and ensure that the money is used appropriately.
The Managing Director stated that the expectation of his company is to see the judicious utilisation of the funds, such that Conocophillips will be encouraged to channel more resources to accommodate more cooperative societies in the immediate future.
"We will like to see some measurable progress so that later we can expand the programme and include some more cooperatives," Creeger explained, noting that, "I will challenge the recipients of the funds to grow their businesses. These grants are good way to sustain development in these areas."
Explaining the processes involved in the selection exercise, the company's Business Development Manager, Mr. Bawo Ogbetuo stated that the company appointed a consulting firm, which undertook the short-listing of the applicants. He said altogether, 114 applications were received from which the 10 cooperatives emerged.
According to him, some of the eligibility criteria used by the consultants included track record of the applicants, their ability to manage previous loan facilities, their contributions to past agricultural loans, against others.
On how to monitor usage of the facility, Ogbetuo explained that, "We look forward to how the funds would be grown. We'll do periodic visits to see how the funds would be managed with a view to growing the grants in future."
Responding, the Emir expressed profound gratitude to Conocophillips for the grants, which he noted was intended to enhance agricultural production in Kaduna State. A visibly elated Emir described Conocophillips as "one of the reputable oil and gas companies that operate in this country." He stated that apart from running their businesses successfully, Conocophillips provide assistance to the nation's economy, particularly the agricultural sector by giving grants to agric societies.
He disclosed that aside from the grants, Conocophillips is also providing financial assistance to the Beth Torrey Home, school for the handicapped, Kaduna noting that, "the company is always willing to assist in many more humanitarian ways."
While wishing Conocophillips success in their business operations in the country, the Emir urged the beneficiaries to utilise the money judiciously so that it would increase in the near future, noting that, "If that happens, then we can confidently go back to the company and ask for more."

Original_Search_Title: Too Many Kids
Headline: Uganda: Too Many Kids
Body_Text:
Kampala — IN the 1980s the issue that brought President Yoweri Museveni into prominence on the world stage was not the bush war, but his stand on AIDS. Unlike any other African president at that time, he openly declared to the world that Uganda had a problem and needed to face it squarely.
A decade later Uganda published statistics which showed the prevalence of AIDS had dropped dramatically and although there is still a huge problem in controlling the spread of the disease, it is acknowledged that much progress has been made.
There is another issue which is of tremendous importance to Uganda's development, yet it is barely highlighted by any politician, it does not appear in any political manifesto and the problem itself is aggravated by the pronouncements of local leaders.
The issue is the high fertility rate of women in Uganda, with a resulting population growth spiraling out of control. I am sure there are some Ugandan men who feel this is an indication of being strong in that department.
When Idi Amin was interviewed about fathering scores of children, he said, "I am a good marksman," and there appear to be many Ugandan men who are good marksmen. According to the statistics, the current fertility rate of 7.1 per woman in Uganda will result in a population of 150 million people by the year 2050. This is staggering. We are already experiencing the effects of the rapid population growth yet our population is only 25 million; the National Forestry Authority reports rapid deforestation by the encroachment by expanding communities, the cutting down of trees for the production of charcoal and the de-gazetting of forests to produce sugar and palm oil.
The consequence is that our forests are shrinking by 2% per year, which will leave us treeless within a few decades. This will result in climate change, adverse effects on weather patterns, soil degradation, crop reduction and increased poverty.
Rapid population growth in itself is not the issue. The issue is whether the country has the ability to cater for the increase, in terms of sufficient infrastructure to provide education, healthcare and employment. For example, Ireland has a young, well educated population which been very marketable to companies who wished to establish hi-tech industries. However the total population of Ireland is only five million while Uganda's increase in population is more than this in just the past decade.
Uganda's economic growth rate is around five to six percent - respectable figures considering everything. With the possible discovery of oil, this growth rate may increase and if the revenues from oil are well spent on education and infrastructure, we should create more employment and lift more of our population out of absolute poverty.
However alongside the economic growth of 5-6%, we have inflation of say 5% which eats up the progress. On top of this we have a current population growth of 3.7%, which gives us more mouths to feed and more people to educate and train for a meaningful livelihood.
When the rate of population growth outstrips the growth of resources to provide for them, we simply increase the number of people living in poverty. We then hope that those people will earn a livelihood in the informal sector, but there are only so many young men who can be employed as boda boda riders or taxi turn-boys.
Some will argue that Ugandans never starve, as there is always some cassava in the ground in this green and pleasant land and that we should multiply in order to be strong. However, we have all seen poverty and we do not want a Uganda where the majority of people are peasants and our natural resources are increasingly degraded because of population pressure.
As a generalisation, people have smaller families as they become wealthier. One would think the reverse would be true since a rich person can afford to support more children. However, the young educated people in Kampala seldom produce more than three children, because they want all their children to go to the best schools and they know how much that costs.
It is the fathers in the village who want myriads of children, but that same man often takes little monetary responsibility for the children he has fathered. Hence these children grow up half educated and repeat the cycle of poverty, where the economic growth rate cannot keep pace with the birth rate.
Those politicians who encourage their people to multiply as fast as possible are simply leading future generations into poverty.

Original_Search_Title: A Trip Into Toro Culture
Headline: Uganda: A Trip Into Toro Culture
Body_Text:
Kampala — IT was my first time to travel aboard a Kalita Transporters bus from Fort Portal to Kampala. Considering that I am accustomed to the Mbarara-Kampala buses that stop only at designated places I found the many stopovers on this route unnecessary and time wasting.
It was already 6:00pm and the bus still needed more passengers! Why didn't these people converge at one point so that they could all board at once, I exclaimed loudly!
Mid-way through my whining, I turned to the passenger seated next me. His expression said it all. He was angrier than I was. Eyes red, he was ready to burst with anger. He opened his mouth to yawn and I was enveloped in a cloud of bad breath. Looks like he had been on the bus for a week.
Turning my head to the window for fresh air, I saw him. Clad in a checked shirt, black tailcoat, khaki trousers, banana fibre hat and wearing a mini basket around the neck, and holding another containing coffee beans, several pieces of mulondo (a cream-coloured root) and herbs, John Kamwanyi aka Magezi gaa Toro (Wisdom of Toro) go onto the bus.
At first I thought he had a mental problem. However, I soon realised how wrong I was. Like a roadside preacher, the strange-looking man suddenly began stressing to the passengers the importance of the culture of Toro and why the pasengers should all strive to be proud of their origin, Toro - he was assuming that everyone on board was a Mutoro. Before he could go any further, the bus stopped for, I don't know how long. Just as the passengers began complaining of how late it was getting, Kamwanyi, taking on the role of a tout said in Rutoro: "Mutembe, mutembe abagenda Kampala. Emotoka enu, nimugira bagiretiro oha?" meaning, "Could all Kampala-bound passengers get into this bus. You are the reason it's here." The conductor just looked on.
As the journey got underway, Kamwanyi pulled some mulondo roots from his basket and began talking about their medicinal value. "This root creates an appetite for food when chewed before a meal. It increases sexual prowess, just like viagra and helps to treat bad breath," he explained as he handed out free samples and offered to sell at a giveaway price of sh100 per root. At the next stop, Kamwanyi bought some pineapples and roasted cassava.
Generosity is one of the virtues of the culture of Toro, he said adding: "Since I couldn't afford to buy for all of you, I will share my food with the children and their mothers only."
His next topic was why people should desist from eating fried foods and go back to the Toro way of cooking where a little ghee is used to spice up food. "Fried foods are the source of many health problems arising to day. They are responsible for high blood pressure, cholesterol and obesity. Personally, I eat steamed foods and that is why I have not fallen sick in many years. But if you must continue eating fried foods, then you must take some of Toro's herbs, which I have properly dried and ground so that after a meal, you take them to clean your body system," he explained.
Continuing with food, he showed us Toro's traditional plate he called olwabya. This kind of plate does not get worn out as many others do. Actually it doesn't need washing with soap, as it doesn't retain any grease. However, to make everything easy for myself, I chose to have three of these plates fixed on a piece of wood. I did this to make sure that I have one for food, main sauce and additional sauce at all times and yet easy to carry with me everywhere I go. "I never use any other plate. It's always my lwabya or no food," he asserted.
As part of our culture, this wood, bowl like plate is our traditional plate that even at cultural functions is used to serve and eat food. It makes food tasty!

Original_Search_Title: Craving for Local Food in the UK
Headline: Uganda: Craving for Local Food in the UK
Body_Text:
Kampala — Absence makes the heart grow fonder, so goes the old adage. And true to it, Ugandans living and working in the UK are growing fonder of local dishes that are taken for granted while at home.
Today travellers destined for the little island are reluctant to tell those relatives abroad about a pending trip. They dread being asked to carry along malewa (bamboo shoots) mukene (a tiny silver-coloured fish), semutundu (Catfish,) or nakati (vegetables.)
While starving for those little snacks, Senga Mandazi in East London has become a household name for baking the best pancakes (kabalagala) in the UK. She uses cheap cassava flour from India and bananas from Jamaica and spices them with hot pepper. To celebrate the onset of summer, Mandazi prepares sumptuous local dishes and crowns the party with scarce bites like grasshoppers, roast groundnuts and roast goat ribs.
