Lovers of the frothy millet beer have it ferried from Teso or Ankole in powder form. Other conservative folks have it delivered through the post office. There are also consumers who take the raw materials and brew it in summer when the weather is warm enough to process the brew.
Rose Oredo of Katakwi District brews Ajon (millet beer) and her home is a crowd-puller for homesick folks eager to guzzle the traditional beverage.
"When I go on holiday I return with the baked flour and all the other brewing ingredients. In summer the sun makes it brew faster just like back home," she says.
Where to shop
The pioneer provider of Uganda foodstuffs, Mugalu and Sons in East London is now facing stiff competition from many other providers. Competitors like Salabed Money and Goods Transfer Stations sell groundnuts, ndizi (sweet bananas,) roast coffee berries and semutundu.
More moneyed dealers like Blue Mountain have also opened up shop in Hallesden.
Pakali's Shop at Seven Sisters is another must-visit. In store for the conservative sons and daughters of the soil is Maganjo maize flour which costs sh15,000 for a 2kg pack. Three fingers of matooke go for sh3,000.
The other place to shop is Owino Market. Here you'll find entula (cherry tomatoes) and dried and pounded vegetables like nakati, timpa as well as yams.
The market also stocks matooke, maize flour, smoked fish, pounded groundnuts, sesame paste, sweet potatoes, pork, beans and jackfruit.
Those with a craving for emolokony (cow hooves) wait for the weekend market in Brickstone.
Restaurants
When it comes to choice, it's quits with cheaper European/Asian meals served at McDonalds, Nandos and Lahor Karahi. For a special treat Ugandans converge at Muena's in East London where one can tuck into a Ugandan buffet comprising steamed bananas, smoked beef, millet bread and offals. The menu also includes dishes from western, eastern and northern Uganda. A plate costs sh21,000.
For the kyeyo (odd jobers) who work awkward hours, Live n'Let Live is the ideal places to dine. It serves mouth-watering and generous portions of local dishes that leave you belching with satisfaction. Diners are even allowed to have seconds. There is a barbeque too and it will set you back sh15,000.
Afro Food in Isleworth sells Ugandan beers at sh10,000 a pint whereas similar brews from the UK cost sh3,000.
However, pound-laden kyeyos will do anything to eat their treasured home delicacies.
Matooke is steamed in polythene paper because there are no banana leaves.
Ugandans living in UK travel all the way from North London to East London to buy a bag of Maganjo maize flour. Beer guzzlers will part with sh10,000 for a bottle of Nile Special or Bell although Stella Artois is just across the counter at sh3000. Smoked beef mysteriously finds its way onto Ugandan tables although strict laws bar the import of beef products.

Original_Search_Title: Mbarara Gets Hailstorm Relief
Headline: Uganda: Mbarara Gets Hailstorm Relief
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE Ministry of Relief and Disaster Preparedness on Wednesday delivered over 170 bags of maize and 90 bags of beans to families affected by last week's hailstorm in Mbarara district.
State minister Musa Ecweru, while handing over the items to the resident district commissioner, Clement Kandole, said iron sheets will also be given to Kashaka Girls Secondary School.
Other affected families will receive bean seeds, quick-maturing cassava stems and potato vines.
He noted that he visited affected parishes in Bubaare sub-county and will compile a report for use by the Ministry of Agriculture and the national agricultural advisory services secretariat.
The Kashaka trading centre LC1 chairman, Benon Mpamiza, explained that banana plantations belonging to 60 families were destroyed, while some residents lost their goats and hens.

Original_Search_Title: Bio-Fuel - Experts Call for Caution
Headline: Zambia: Bio-Fuel - Experts Call for Caution
Body_Text:
Ndola — ZAMBIA spends in excess of US$500 million a year on the importation of crude oil to meet its national demand for fuel needed in running vehicles and industries.
And with the dilapidated machinery at Indeni Oil Refinery, Zambia has had to incur even more costs in efforts to ensure that the country has enough fuel stocks to propel its economic activities.
The Government has for years now struggled to find means of reducing the costs on fuel and also avoid shortages that arise as a result of frequent closures of the only oil refinery plant based in Ndola.
First it was the idea of creating oil reserve tanks that has to date not been a reality before the Government looked at other options.
The focus has from last year shifted to the production of bio-fuels that have proved to be cost-effective in other parts of the world such as Brazil.
The Government, through Energy and Water Development Minister Felix Mutati started by courting Zambia Sugar in enticing them into production of ethanol, which is a critical component of bio-fuel.
And the Mazabuka company has shown commitment and pledged to invest $150 million to expand its sugar cane production in order to produce even more ethanol as a by-product of sugar production.
Further, Mr Mutati has undertaken several tours of commercial farms where he has gone to learn how much Jatropha is being grown and to also find out more knowledge on the plant, which is important to the production of bio-fuel.
It is envisaged that although bio-fuel cannot completely replace fossil fuels, it can help the Government reduce costs on crude importation if significant quantities are produced within the country.
However, the latest minister's tour of Sherrif Estates in Serenje and Golden Valley Agricultural Research Trust (GART) on the Great North road in Lusaka reveals that the country needs to be conscious on its path to embrace bio-fuel as a supplement to fossil fuel.
At Sherrif Estates where a farmer is growing Jatropha and producing diesel from sunflower and groundnuts that he later uses to run his vehicles, tractors and other machinery at the farm located 30 kilometres from Serenje boma, he learnt that the Government has to be careful in embracing Jatropha.
Owner of the farm, Roger Sherrif, told the minister that he was currently producing bio-diesel which he used for his fleet of vehicles and other machinery without adjusting them and that it had proved cheaper than buying from filling stations.
The farm is currently producing 2,000 litres of diesel a day and, with the availability of more raw materials, there is capacity to produce even more fuel.
Mr Sherrif led the minister and his delegation to his fields where he has grown Jatropha and proved that although the plant was poisonous to animals, he used his cattle for weeding and they did not eat Jatropha leaves.
He agreed that he was ready to partner with the Government in the production of fuel as long as certain protective measures were put in place to benefit the farmers, producers and finally consumers.
He said the Government should ensure that tax on bio-fuel was as low as possible, especially when it was being sold to consumers so that it could also compete with mineral fuel.
"In Britain, tax on bio-fuel is half that of mineral fuel while no tax is levied on the same fuel used for agricultural purposes," he said.
The farmer said lack of material and engineering skills were also prohibitive in the development of bio-fuel while tax on imported ethanol should also be reduced.
At GART, experts also reminded Mr Mutati that there was need for the Government to fully understand Jatropha and its effects when grown on a large scale.
Head of research and development, Douglas Moono, said experts had keenly followed the excitement on Jatropha and production of bio-fuels, adding that there was need for adequate research on the plant.
Mr Moono said there was need for caution in the way the Government was trying to come up with the production of bio-fuels, especially effects on the environment and the disposal of remains needed to be taken care of.
He said there was also need to regulate the importation of Jatropha plants until research was done to find out their suitability to the Zambian situation.
"As Zambia we need to know the effects of Jatropha remains, especially that on the international level it has been agreed that by 2020, 20 per cent of mineral fuels should be replaced by bio-fuels," he said.
The danger was to allow any one to grow the plant without any control measures that would protect animals and the environment.
Bio-energy can succeed in Zambia if energy crops do not impact on food security but provide opportunities to benefit small-scale farmers who may be engaged in growing Jatropha.
A large scale bio-energy sector can be built upon organising many small-scale farmers through outgrower schemes while energy cropping can be environmentally sustainable.
He added that GART was aiming at reaching out to 120,000 small-scale farmers in Jatropha growing while encouraging the growing of other crops such as cassava and other seasonal crops as a way of ensuring food security.
Mr Mutati, who agreed that although the Government was excited with bio-fuel prospects, said it was wary of many factors and was slowly coming with a policy that would also cater for a legal framework.
He said there was a lot of anxiety in the country about Jatropha but the Government first wanted to strengthen partnerships with various stakeholders so that no stone could be left unturned.
He said all players needed to have enough knowledge on the issue and, as a result, the Government had this year set aside K600 million for research on Jatropha and production of bio-fuels.
"As we remain excited, we should also be mindful of how we are going to dispose off Jatropha residues, protect animals and the environment," he said.
The minister said that Government had also sent a team of experts to India so that they could learn how bio-fuels ended up in vehicles and machinery, especially that Jatropha was a labour-intensive plant.
He added that bio-fuel was supposed to cushion fuel imports and the Government's goal was to replace mineral fuel initially by 10 per cent and later 20 per cent.
He, however, said that there were several other challenges such as ensuring peasant farmers had enough food since Jatropha took three years to be harvested although the plant could live for over 35 years.
He said the Government would definitely come up with a policy on the dos and don'ts and also come up with a proper taxation regime to make bio-fuel as cheap as possible.
He said currently, it cost $1.00 to produce a litre of bio-diesel and, with low taxes and having a decentralised production and selling system, the fuel would be affordable to many people.
The Government has also put up an inter-ministerial committee that is currently discussing possible challenges and effects to the country once bio-fuels are fully embraced in the next three years.
The land is plenty and water is available for Zambia to grow Jatropha and, subsequently, produce bio-fuel that could no doubt help the country save on the importation of crude oil which is ever costing the country more resources.
However, there is need for caution in the manner the country is trying to come up with an alternative source of fuel so that there are no devastating effects to cost the country even more.

Original_Search_Title: Maximising Gains of Agric Insurance
Headline: Nigeria: Maximising Gains of Agric Insurance
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Administration of Chief Olusegun Obasanjo since inception in 1999 has given agriculture priority. There is no doubt about that", that was the view of Mr. B. D. Pembi, MNIPR, MIPRA, Head, Corporate Affairs, Nigerian Agricultural Insurance Corporation, Head office, plot 590, Zone AO, Central Area, P. O. Box 3754 Abuja; recently, when we brought up a discussion on Agro - based industries, potentials, riches, wealth and natural resources, we have in this country.
Inscribed glowingly on his complimentary card is "ploughs you back to prosperity". We were talking against the background that the attention given cassava has yielded positive result, the bid to enhance abundant production of rice and stop its importation, the provision of pesticides, insecticides, fertilizer, high yielding hybrid seeds, purchase of tractors, ox - ploughs, soft loans, Fadama I, II and III projects, environmental projects, and other accessories like extension services, grants e.t.c. But if these have no safety strategies to guarantee its insurance against any risk - there could be problems in safeguarding the future. This is where the Nigerian Insurance Corporation comes in. Before unveiling the advantage of liaising with this corporation as a frame let us take a brief look at the history of NAIC.
The Federal Government of Nigeria launched the Nigerian Agricultural Insurance Scheme (NAIS) on 15th December 1987, as part of Government's efforts to enhance/sustain food production in Nigeria . It was realized that most efforts to promote food production have not yielded much results, due largely to incidence of inclemental weather conditions and the effects of natural hazards like floods, drought, pests, diseases, fire e.t.c. To address this problem, the Nigerian Agricultural Insurance Scheme was established to provide protection to farmers from the effects of such natural hazards.
This prompted the need for a specialized Agricultural company to provide insurance cover to farmers was informed by Government's concern over the vacuum created due to the unwillingness of conventional insurers to accept Agricultural risks, which they considered too risky. The implementation of the scheme was thus initially vested in the Nigerian Agricultural Insurance Company limited, which later turned into a corporation in 1993 by enabling decree no. 37 of1993.
The objectives of the scheme were and still to provide financial support to 'farmers, where losses to crops and livestock arise from natural hazards. It is to induce provision of credit by financial institutions, as the insurance cover is an added collateral. Again, it is to promote agricultural production by giving farmers confidence to accept new/modem innovations and inputs. It is also to minimize or eliminate the need for Government to provide adhoc assistance to farmers during agricultural disasters.
At inception, the scheme provided cover to only two crop items namely, maize and rice, two livestock items namely cattle and poultry and some commercial businesses like farm buildings, machinery and equipment. The cover has since been extended to cover more items namely: 21 crop items, nine items of livestock and 12 types of commercial business. And at the present, efforts are being intensified to increase the number of items under cover to include fisheries, perishable vegetables and more horticultural products.
In addition to the above types of cover (NAIC) also designs specific covers to suit peculiar customer needs. For example, it has designed and implemented a special type of cover for Agriculture or produce marketing loans as follows. Fire and special perils cover for property used as collateral security (Private and Business Premises) for marketing loans. Others are the burglary insurance cover for produce/goods in storage~ fire and special perils cover for produce, goods or other materials in storage, warehouses e.t.c. Again, there are goods in transit (GIT) policy and cash in Transit / Safe.
Similarly a special Insurance package has been prepared for the beneficiaries of the National Poverty Eradication Programme (NAPEP). Therefore, prospective clients can always approach any NAIC office, for the possibility of arranging special types of covers to meet their needs.
NAIC has operational presence in the 36 states of the Federation and the Federal Capital Territory . There are 5 Zonal offices to supervise these state offices. Plans are under to open more field offices in the Local Government Areas and Districts.
The same was established for the benefit of all categories of farmers, namely Small, Medium and Large-Scale holdens either in group or as individuals in addition to individual clients. The scheme operates a mandatory cover which applies to the following; All agricultural loans from banks taken for all items insurable by NAIC; all agricultural and agro-related projects / programmes which are assisted, supported or fully funded from public funds, all direct and on-lending loans taken by Federal, State or Local Government for rebursement to farmers or I and agriculture projects for implementation; all forms of loans for agricultural marketing purposes, by all banks and non-bank lending agencies, and all direct on-lending and Investment loans disbursed by the Nigerian Agricultural Co-operative and Rural Development Bank (NACRDB).
The perils under cover for the crop sub-sector are drought, flood, windstorm, fire, pests and diseases. For livestock, the perils covered are death and injuries due to accident, disease, fire, lightening, storm and flood. Losses caused by negligence or willful damage are not covered. Clients are always encouraged to study the conditions on their policies noting all exceptions and exclusions.
For crops, the duration of cover ranges from germination to physiological maturity. However, tree crops such as cocoa and rubber are covered for one year at a time. Livestock, cattle and small ruminants are covered for one year at a time.
For poultry, however, the duration of cover is from one-day-old to 52 weeks for layers and breeding birds extendable 72 weeks and from day old to 10 weeks for boilers. The duration of all other cover is one year except where specified.
Sum insured in the volume of risk for any particular insured enterprise.
For crops, the sum insured is the approved input basic costs while the sum insured for livestock is equal to the value of the animal at the commencement of the insurance plus the sum of the approved input costs. For conventional insurance, the sum insures always the value of the items to be insured.
The approved premium rate for the scheme ranges between 5% and 8% of the sum insured for crops and 3.5% - 7.5% for livestock. The applicable rating varies with the agro-ecological zone of the country.
The Nigerian Agricultural lnsurance Scheme is subsidized to the tune of 50% by the Federal and State Government in the proportion of 37.50/0 and 12.5% of the premium payable. This subsidy is applicable only on food crop and livestock items while commercial rates are charged for non-food items. Claim payment is one of the most important function of any insurance outfit. In NAIC, claims are treated and paid with dispatch. Insured are always encouraged to report claim incidence promptly to enable verification and commencement of processing. The indemnity for crops is based on the approved input costs, less the value of the crops harvested (if any). For the livestock indemnity is the value of the animal, at the commencement of the poliCY:1 plus the approved inputs costs. NAIC offices Nationwide are the main reporting points of claims.
The scheme continues to gain acceptance as evidenced by the increasing number of new participating farmers and most of them that suffered losses have been indemnified. The value of risk taken by NAIC since inception as at the end of 2003 was N80billion, which were adequately covered by local and international Re-insurance treaties.
The vast scope of coverage, the huge volume of risk taken up by NAIC and the 50% subsidy given by the Nigerian Government makes the Nigerian Agricultural Insurance Scheme recognized by many countries and the Food and Agricultural Organization (F AO) of the United Nations as a major contribution towards self sufficiency in food production and as a scheme to be replaced in other parts of the world.
Farm size is normally determined by surveying, pacing, measuring, using a linear measuring equipment. Cost of production for crops is determined by summing up the cost of all operation involved in production of the crop. For example, the cost of production for maize is determined by adding the total value of listed operations.
Therefore with all these, an insurance analyst said that an insurance company means nothing to the insured, if in the event of a loss, it is not there on time to pay the necessary claims or it comes up with undue technicalities to avoid paying the claims?
This is where the Nigerian Agricultural Insurance Corporation is different from others and is always there for the farmer, NAIC, unlike other conventional insurance must within reasonable time pay the appropriate compensation sufficient to keep the farmer in business after suffering a loss. Any delay could force the farmer out of business in no time. The Nigerian Agricultural Insurance Corporation is living up to its responsibilities, so says, the Managing Director, Mr. Kwaga Kwatri Yusuf. The Chief Executive, who emphasized this position at the beginning of the year, charged staff to be committed - he restated that prompt payment of claims is always the cardinal philosophy of the corporation. It has to be pointed out here that financial institutions like the Nigerian Cooperative and Rural Development Bank:1 (NACRDB), Union Bank, First Bank, DBA and others have been forthcoming in lending to farmers as a result of the existence ofNAIC which is 100% owned by the Federal Government.
This success story of NAIC so far is largely informed by the calibre of personnel recruited to manage the corporation. Currently, Mr Kwaga Kwatri Yusuf is the Managing Director; His choice by the Federal Government was based on merit, experience and professionalism. A chartered insurer since 1990, Mr. Kwaga began his insurance career at the Nigerian Reinsurance Corporation in 1982. He left to join Niger Insurance PIc in 1985 as Branch Manager. Six years later due to hard work, he left as Northern Area Manager and joined Nicon Insurance Corporation in 1991 as zonal manager. Five years after, he was promoted Assistant General Manager in charge of Northern operations. Given his professional skills, the Industrial and General Insurance Company (IGI) decided to hire his services in 1997 with the designation of Assistant Director, a position he held until 2003, when the Federal Government came for his services and made him Executive Director in charge of operations.
A year later, following the exit of the former Managing Director, the Federal Government again counting on his dynamism, ability, innovation decided to appoint him as acting Managing Director, given a well decked, an asset base and highly competent team; tested professionals - the Nigerian Agricultural Insurance Corporation has a role to play in revamping agriculture. This is time to tap the advantages.

Original_Search_Title: Help for 300,000 Flood Victims
Headline: Burundi: Help for 300,000 Flood Victims
Body_Text:
Bujumbura — About 300,000 Burundians affected by floods since November 2006 are due to receive seeds from the United Nations World Food Programme (WFP) to tide them over until the next harvest season, a WFP official has said.
"We aim to prepare people for the next harvest season and inject food relief to affected zones in the north and northeast of Burundi," Guillaume Foliot, WFP programme officer, said on Wednesday.
He said the agency would provide the aid between 15 February and 15 March, under its seed protection ration (SPR) framework.
Heavy rains have damaged crops and destroyed infrastructure in several regions since November 2006, prompting the government to set up a national solidarity fund to support flood-displaced people in the provinces of Muyinga, Kirundo, Cankuzo, Karuzi, Kayanza, Ngozi and Muyinga.
In addition to the WFP, the government, the Burundi National Red Cross Society and other relief agencies have also provided food and non-food aid to the flood-affected people.
The Red Cross had "already assisted 844 of the 1,187 flood-displaced at Gatumba in Bujumbura Rural", said Venerand Nzigamasabo, the officer in charge of rescuing and managing catastrophes at Red Cross Burundi.
Nzigamasabo said the non-food aid comprised buckets, blankets, mosquito nets, soap, clothes and chemicals for purifying water and disinfecting water sources. The Red Cross has also carried out hygiene education campaigns in the affected areas.
In the northwestern province of Cibitoke, Red Cross Burundi has assisted 300 households by distributing beans and vegetables, and it is scheduled to help a similar number in the northeastern province of Ruyigi.
Meanwhile, in a joint statement last week, the WFP and relief agencies in Burundi said they were having difficulties targeting those in need of urgent relief. They warned that two million people affected by recent floods in the country required urgent aid until June to avert a food crisis.
The agencies' statement followed last week's launch of the Consolidated Appeals Processes (CAP) in which the UN and agencies sought US$132 million to support their work in Burundi in 2007.
In an assessment in January 2007, the WFP and the UN Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) found that heavy rains and floods had destroyed 50-80 percent of the November harvest and much of the January harvest of beans, sweet potatoes, maize, sorghum and rice across large parts of the country.
The WFP country director Gerard van Dijk warned of devastating consequences if urgent help was not brought to the needy.
"Given how long it takes to get food aid and other assistance to Burundi, we have no time to waste; we need international support now in order to help people until June," he said.
The January assessment established that the vulnerable people had resorted to survival tactics such as having one meal a day and opting for drought-resistant food such as cassava or bitter bananas.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Ekiti - 100 Days of Emergency Rule
Headline: Nigeria: Ekiti - 100 Days of Emergency Rule - Olurin Crosses the Half Mark
Body_Text:
Last week Friday marked the 100th day in office for Brig. General Adetunji Olurin (rtd) as the Administrator of Ekiti State. But the day went by without the usual frenzy and ceremony with which political office holders, especially in the executive arm of government, have always observed the day.
It was learnt that the Administrator left Ado-Ekiti, the state capital, early on that day for Lagos to attend to crucial matters of state.
This, according to sources, is characteristic of the Administrator, who has always preferred to do things without making much fuss about them.
However, it is not that there was nothing to celebrate, but sources close to the Administrator disclosed that it was one of Olurin's aides who drew his attention to the date late on Saturday evening. An unperturbed Olurin was said to have retorted: "Is that so? I did not even remember. That means we should be packing our luggage gradually because our six months tenure would soon be over".
Sources said that the Administrator has had a very busy schedule in office in the last 100 days. During this period, he had to grapple with the appalling security situation in the state which was almost getting out of hand by the time he resumed work in Ado-Ekiti on October 19, last year.
But having restored normalcy to the state in the first few weeks, Olurin has gradually transformed the fountain of knowledge state. He was said to have identified poverty as having played a significant role in the tension that gripped the state in the last days of the Fayose administration. Sources said the Administrator has been doing everything to reduce the level of poverty by empowering people to go into self-employment.
To this end, loans have been made available to farmers through the National Poverty Eradication Programme, (NAPEP). Also since the state is an agrarian economy, the Olurin administration had been supporting agricultural ventures by installing cassava plants to enhance the production of cassava products like cassava flour, garri and starch.
According to one of his aides, "the Administrator is doing this to prove to other people that if they form unions, they can do the same thing by pulling resources together to set up such plants".
Besides, the Administrator will soon set up an Economic Council through experts and those who have clouts and can reach beyond the shores of Nigeria will be co-opted to source for foreign investors to the state. This is because, according to the aide, "the Administrator believes the government cannot go it alone to create factories, so the option is to attract investors to the state to utilize available raw materials for manufacturing purposes and spring up the economy of the state".
Perhaps, far more than transforming the lives of the people, the general atmosphere of peace and tranquility now prevailing in the state had been attributed to the efforts of the Administrator who has brought his wealth of experience in peacekeeping operations in several parts of the world to bear on the governance of the state.
When contacted to find out why the Administrator opted not to celebrate his first 100 days in office as had been the practice in the country in recent time, Ayo Akinyemi, his Chief Press Secretary said: "Just as you may have known, the Administrator believes that his achievements will speak for themselves rather than resort to noise-making and merriment. He is one quiet, easy-going, but tough person who does nor blow his trumpet", he added.

Original_Search_Title: Demand for Funeral Cloth Goes Up After X'mas
Headline: Ghana: Demand for Funeral Cloth Goes Up After X'mas
Body_Text:
Demand for funeral clothes have shot up after the Christmas and New Year festivities while traders at the various markets in Accra are recording low sales for other products.
Ms Irene Renner, textiles dealer near the Rawlings Park in Accra, said there was high demand for funeral cloth after the Christmas festivities because most families had postponed their funerals till the New Year.
She quoted the prices for full piece funeral cloth as 230,000 cedis and half piece for 125,000 cedis and that of the ATL wax cloth full piece as 320,000 cedis and the half piece for 160,000 cedis.
Although Ms Renner did not quote any figures, she said sales had soared up although the prices for the clothes had remained the same before and after the Christmas holidays. "During the Xmas period no one even asked for the price of funeral cloths, now they are back", she said.
The Ghana News Agency (GNA) during its rounds in Accra found that even though prices of goods and services had generally remained stable there were few other items whose prices were fluctuating depending on their demand.
A crate of tomatoes that was sold for 800,000 cedis during the Christmas period remained the same while "Olonka" full of tomatoes, which sold for 50,000 cedis, had now come down to 35,000 cedis.
Ms Linda Akuakor, a tomato seller at the Tema-Station market in Accra attributed the low patronage to the lack of money in the system and parents cutting down on expenditure as school had just re-opened and they had to pay school fees.
At the Makola market, an Olonka of gari is being sold at 9,000 cedis; medium sized tin for 4,500 cedis and the margarine tin for 1,500 cedis; while an Olonka of maize is going for 8,000 cedis showing no appreciable fall or increase from the pre-Xmas price
The prices of plantain, yam and cassava had remained the same on the market. Four or five fingers plantain, depending on the size sells at 5,000 cedis; a big tuber of yam goes for 17,000 and five pieces of cassava cost 5,000 cedis.
A full sack of onions is being sold for 800,000 while twelve pieces of the same item is going for 10,000 cedis.
Mr Michael Asamoah, an onion seller in Accra, hinted that it was likely that the prices of the commodity would fall further because of low sales.
Meanwhile a 25 kilogram-bag of rice (Texas Star) that was sold for 370,000 cedis before and after the Christmas has shot up to 380,000 cedis while 5kg of sugar is being sold at 48,000 cedis.
A 25kg of sugar (Argentina) is being sold at 330,000 and a 25kg of (Brazil) sugar goes for 250,000.
At the Okaishie lane in Accra, a box of frytol cooking oil is being sold at 180,000 cedis whiles a box of key soap, sunlight soap and 500 grams of Omo are being sold at 149,000 cedis; 133,000 cedis and 120,000 cedis in that order.
Generally prices quoted by dealers in electrical appliances visited by the GNA showed no change in prices from that of last year.

Original_Search_Title: From Ogoniland With Tears, Frustration
Headline: Nigeria: From Ogoniland With Tears, Frustration
Body_Text:
Lagos — Mr. Sorle Deekae's main wish is to wake up someday and find his air breathable, his waters free of blackish, oily filth and he and his fellow youth gainfully employed in productive ventures. Deekae, a youth leader in late Ken Saro-Wiwa's hometown of Ogoniland, is not likely to have his wishes fulfilled soon.
The peace initiative began by President Olusegun Obasanjo to reconcile the Ogoni with their long time foe, Shell Petroleum Development Company (SPDC), has not progressed well and the people, represented by the Movement for the Survival of Ogoni People (MOSOP), have asked for any other oil company instead of Shell to work in their communities.
So Deekae's community is still as undeveloped, perhaps even worse than when his late famous and activist leader, publisher and writer, Kenule Saro-Wiwa was alive. There have been a few token improvements: a water borehole was built in the community before the burial of Ken Saro-Wiwa's father but the borehole has long been abandoned to rats and detritus since there is no generator to power it; an 8-feet-wide tarred road was also done at the same period but since no drainage was built, the elevation of the road from the tarring is causing massive flooding on both sides, which have now become like flood plains, in the wet season.
"It would have been better for the road to have remained untarred," said Deekae, who has a secondary school education and speaks impeccable English but works as a mechanic.
Touring this community made famous by the pipe-smoking and powerful writer Ken Saro-Wiwa is like going back to many ages past. The pristine forests and vast farmlands, lush vegetation and healthy-looking cassava plants attract but equally repelled by the grinding poverty of the people.
Even after 11 years of the writer's death, there is still an abundance of dilapidated mud huts, some unpainted cement-block bungalows, and other homes of uncertain make. Ken Saro-Wiwa owns a very modest one-storey house in the eastern part of the town and the King, HRM Menebua Kehnwigbara Karikpo, owns another equally modest one-storey house in the town centre. The people are semi-urban folks with faces lined by the daily grind of hard life, and vacant faces devoid of radiant joy. You will look in vain for the carefree, semi-drunk village comedian that featured in some of Ken's fictional prose.
Electricity was connected to Bane and much of the larger Ken-Khana communities only two years ago, but there has been no light since the end of 2005. Youth leader, Deekae does not know why. "Must be some problem with the gas turbine," he says. "We really don't know."
There is one girl's school, Miriam International School hidden behind high walls; there is no secondary school for the boys, and of the two primary schools in the community, one has completely broken down. One part of the collapsed colonial-style school building and much of the roof has so caved in that the structure now poses a safety risk. The nearby Anglican Church houses the school pupils in one of its semi-open halls.
Driving from Eleme, Ogoniland's outermost town near Nigeria's oil capital city of Port Harcourt, you pass the Onne junction leading to the fertiliser company, and then you begin the journey into the heart of Ogoniland. After the hubbub of Port Harcourt and the commotion in Okrikaland, Ogoniland looks like a delicate patch of peace. Its lush vegetation, wide savannah-like plains with few trees exude healthy greenery. There is a wide network of roads leading to the major areas of Bori, Kaima, Gokana and Ken-Khana. At Kwa Wa near Saro-Wiwa's place, a hastily painted signboard reads: "Haven of Peace".
However, not long ago, there wasn't any peace in this patch of the Niger Delta whose soil is blessed, some say cursed, with some of the 2.5 million barrels of petroleum per day that the nation produces. That lack of peace in the 1990's led to dire consequences for the Ogoni. Deekae and his more than 500, 000 Ogoni kinsmen hold Shell responsible for the state murder in 1995 of their kinsman, Ken Saro-Wiwa. The late activist was the first in recent years to put the Niger Delta problem on the world stage, documenting "vast abuses" by Shell, setting up the Movement for the Survival of the Ogoni People (MOSOP) and crafting an Ogoni Bill of Rights which he presented to the Nigerian State and the UN in November 1990.
Their demand for greater resource control, more political autonomy, adequate representation in state institutions, and stoppage of further damage to their ecology brought serious repression on them by the then military dictator Gen. Sani Abacha.
Shell denies responsibility for Saro-Wiwa's death but admitted they had "inadvertently" contributed to the crises in the region and "were prepared to help".
That help has been in the form of scholarships, micro credit loans, training and capacity building. Shell also announced recently that they were moving from using community leaders as distributors of their development schemes to empowering youths directly. But all these are too little too late; say the youths of the area.
"Shell has sown so much destruction and bad blood among us that it would take a lot for us to trust them again," admits Deekae when told that Shell is prepared to negotiate. The 20 young men with Deekae nod their heads vigorously in agreement with him.
Yet a healing is possible, they concede. First they point to the very deep gully erosion, more than 3-storey deep cutting the community into two and threatening the palace of their king. The gully is wider than a dual carriage expressway and stretches for miles, from the northern part of the land near the waterside, where it has destroyed the picturesque bathing beach, to the city centre, running parallel to the palace and currently stopping near the tarred road.
There appears to be an underground tunnel extending deep from it to unseen depths further into the town, perhaps indicative of paths it would open up. Trees have grown from the bottom and you can easily see their tops, like mere shrubs on a rural farm road. Staring at the wide mouth of the gully, you are reminded of a massive crocodile's open jaws.
The sigh speaks volumes and is everywhere in Ogoniland. As you are shown numberless huts damaged by the regular floods in the wet season, you come across what can only be described as a colony of old women near the Saro-Wiwa homestead. Their ages are indeterminable and they crouch or stoop outside their very long mud-and-thatch huts.
Their dwellings, without exception, are coming apart from the foundation due to the floods and the walls are in various stages of collapse. Parts of the roofs have caved in and puddles are everywhere. The picture is one of abject misery and it takes all your courage not to shout out in frustration at this unnecessary condition of penury in a land of plenty.
Deekae and his fellow youths live with this grinding poverty, yet one wonders why they cannot rebuild the old women's huts. "We're resettling them," they reply, "but we don't have much resources."
It is to the credit of Saro-Wiwa's teachings and influence that these poorly educated, unskilled, unemployed youths have not taken to the violence of the rest of the Niger Delta. Highly disciplined in their allegiance to MOSOP, many still remember the military siege that engulfed their land and took their parents and siblings lives in the 1990's.
That siege has only changed uniforms. Instead of military fatigues there are the police and the MOPOL uniforms manning about seven checkpoints between Onne Junction and Bori, less than an hour drive away, each collecting its own "toll" from buses and commercial motorcycles (Okada) riders.
For one way of the journey your driver has parted with N400 in bribe and another N400 to union officials. Of the N2, 100 total fares he collected from passengers he's now left with N1, 300 to buy fuel, oil, feed, maintain the vehicle, settle the bus owner, and if he's lucky, have something to keep aside for his family's upkeep. Whatever that "something" amounts to, it's his own share of the $300 billion experts say the country has earned since oil production began in these lands in 1957.
As one leaves Ogoniland traumatised by what he has seen, he remembers one of Ken's smuggled notes from prison before his execution. He had written: " the most important thing for me is that I have used my talents as a writer to enable the Ogoni people to confront their tormentors. I was not able to do it as a politician or a businessman. My writing did it. And it sure makes me feel good! ... I have the moral victory." You cast aside your gloom and pick up your pen; after all, you are also a writer.
-Ike, a poet and journalist writes from Abuja

Original_Search_Title: Rural Women in Need of a Helping Hand
Headline: Cote d'Ivoire: Rural Women in Need of a Helping Hand
Body_Text:
Abidjan — From morning, Thérèse Allangba starts checking on members of the Cooperative of Women Farmers of Marahoué (Coopérative des femmes exploitantes agricoles de la Marahoué, COOFEEAMA), based in Bouaflé, capital of the Ivorian region of Marahoué. These women work in teams of five to supply leading markets with food.
"I ask those who are responsible for bringing the attiéké (a local meal made of cassava flour) to hurry up. As for vegetables and fruit, I tell people in charge of these to make a record of the products that are going to go out," she tells IPS on one of her demanding days.
"We are late. Since five o' clock this morning, three trucks of food should have made their way to Abidjan, and we are still running behind," adds Allangba, who coordinates the activities of the cooperative on a daily basis.
The main women's organisation in Bouaflé and its surrounds, about 200 kilometres north of the economic capital of Abidjan, COOFEEAMA comprises more than a thousand women -- most based in rural areas.
In 2005 it made a net profit of close to 30,000 dollars. "We expect, at the end of 2006, about 50,000 dollars," Allangba notes.
According to her, these results are the fruit of efforts by educated women to teach those in rural areas how to manage themselves. "When we started the project, many women were reluctant (to take part in it). But we showed them that with the cooperative, not only could they increase their earnings -- they could also be financially independent," she says.
But obtaining funds for the endeavour was problematic: "Even getting the minimum of credit was a nightmare."
Allangba says women who have no guarantees or people to stand security for them do not obtain loans, while illiteracy also undermines their prospects of getting credit. This is despite the fact that the loans requested only amount to between 100 and 300 dollars each -- to maintain a market garden, perhaps, or buy fertilizer.
Estelle Tra Lou, a producer of plantains (a fruit similar to a banana) and member of COOFEEAMA, has experienced similar problems.
"In the course of my first experience in a cooperative, we asked a local bank for assistance to buy a cassava mill. Not only did it request a guarantee, it also asked us for bank statements from our husbands, because we did not provide any guarantee," the mother of four, who is the only bread winner in her family, told IPS.
These difficulties come despite the fact that rural women play a key role in the food security of Côte d'Ivoire, according to the Ministry of the Family and the Child -- which also notes that although these women make up 25 percent of the Ivorian population, they account for close to 30 percent of national food production.
"Unfortunately, this participation by the rural woman in development is not understood because of its informal character," says Euphrasie Yao, director for the promotion of gender and equality in the Ministry of the Family and the Child. And as they do not benefit from any technical assistance, she observes, these women remain financially dependent on men.
Notes Allangba, "If we had people like Muhammad Yunus, there is no doubt that the task would be a little easier."
Known as the banker to the poor, Yunus is a Bangladeshi economist who received the Nobel Peace Prize last year along with the bank he founded in 1983 -- the Grameen Bank, meaning "village bank". This was in recognition of his efforts to help millions of people, mostly women, escape poverty by providing them with micro-credit through the Grameen Bank.
"We expect that this will set an example here," says Allangba.
According to the Ministry of Economy and Finances, Côte d'Ivoire had only two micro-finance institutions in 1995. By 2004 the number had grown to 70; these were making use of 116 savings banks that had collected about 80 million dollars for some 500,000 beneficiaries.
But, says Tra Lou, while "We do not say that these establishments do not exist they do not play the role that we expect of them, especially towards women."
This view isn't accepted by all. "In our records, there are several women's associations that have benefited from our assistance," says Marcel Aka, manager of a micro-credit institution. "The procedures to obtain credit have even become more flexible. As long as we are sure of being reimbursed, we will give credit."
Still, claims Christiane Bitty Kouyaté, much remains to be done. An advocate, she is also president of the Women's Platform to Win (Plate-forme des femmes pour gagner) -- a non-governmental organisation based in Abidjan.
Kouyaté calls on government and donors to focus on enabling rural women to be productive and on extending micro-credit to them, while improving the procedures to obtain finance still more: "Not demanding too much paperwork would be a good step."
Allangba agrees. "People should know that in some way, we are taking part in the development of the nation. Daily, we produce almost 10 tonnes of food to feed a part of the country, so we must also be helped."
Côte d'Ivoire has been divided in two by an armed rebellion since September 2002, with rebels occupying the northern half of the country. They claim to have taken up arms to fight against the alleged exclusion of people in this part of the nation.
Just before the crisis, in January 2002, the first bank for women was set up in Abidjan -- with 20,000 clients.
The outbreak of war did not permit branches of this institution to be established in different regions of the country to assist rural women. Nonetheless, the bank still exists in Abidjan; according to its officials, it has loaned about 200,000 dollars to some 2,800 women over the past four years.
